stem: During the modified Hardinge (direct lateral) approach to the hip, the surgeon splits gluteus medius and dissects proximally along its fibres. To protect the structure that innervates the anterior third of gluteus medius and gluteus minimus, by what maximum distance is proximal splitting of the abductor mass conventionally limited above the tip of the greater trochanter?

options.label: A
options.text: 1 cm

options.label: B
options.text: 3 cm

options.label: C
options.text: 5 cm

options.label: D
options.text: 8 cm

options.label: E
options.text: 10 cm

explanation: The superior gluteal nerve runs transversely between gluteus medius and minimus, on average about 5 cm proximal to the tip of the greater trochanter. In the direct lateral (Hardinge) approach, splitting of the abductor mass is therefore kept within roughly 5 cm of the trochanteric tip to avoid denervating the anterior fibres of medius and minimus; injury produces a persistent abductor lurch (positive Trendelenburg). The nerve is the terminal branch of the lumbosacral plexus that leaves the pelvis through the greater sciatic foramen above piriformis with the superior gluteal vessels, then runs forward on the deep surface of the muscles.
learningPoint: Keep the abductor split within ~5 cm of the greater trochanteric tip to protect the superior gluteal nerve in the direct lateral hip approach.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Surgical approaches: hip
Hoppenfeld, Surgical Exposures in Orthopaedics — lateral approach to the hip

stem: A surgeon performs a Smith-Petersen anterior approach to the hip. The superficial internervous interval lies between sartorius and tensor fasciae latae. A patient subsequently reports a patch of numbness and dysaesthesia over the anterolateral thigh. Which nerve has most likely been injured?

options.label: A
options.text: Femoral nerve

options.label: B
options.text: Lateral femoral cutaneous nerve

options.label: C
options.text: Anterior division of the obturator nerve

options.label: D
options.text: Superior gluteal nerve

options.label: E
options.text: Nerve to rectus femoris

explanation: The Smith-Petersen approach uses the superficial interval between sartorius (femoral nerve) and tensor fasciae latae (superior gluteal nerve). The lateral femoral cutaneous nerve (L2-3) emerges near the anterior superior iliac spine, usually just medial to and beneath the interval, and is the structure most at risk superficially; injury or traction produces meralgia paraesthetica — numbness/dysaesthesia over the anterolateral thigh with no motor loss. The femoral nerve lies more medially within the femoral triangle and is the deep structure at risk, but causes quadriceps weakness rather than an isolated cutaneous patch. The obturator and superior gluteal nerves do not supply this skin territory.
learningPoint: Anterolateral thigh numbness after an anterior hip approach = lateral femoral cutaneous nerve injury (meralgia paraesthetica); identify and protect it near the ASIS.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Surgical approaches: anterior approach to the hip
Hoppenfeld, Surgical Exposures in Orthopaedics — anterior (Smith-Petersen) approach

stem: A 9-year-old sustains a physeal injury in which the fracture line passes transversely through the physis and then exits proximally through the metaphysis, leaving a triangular metaphyseal (Thurston-Holland) fragment but sparing the epiphysis. Within the histological zones of the growth plate, through which zone do such physeal separations characteristically propagate, and which feature explains why most of these injuries do not cause growth arrest?

options.label: A
options.text: Zone of proliferation; because the germinal cells lie on the metaphyseal side and are removed with the fragment

options.label: B
options.text: Zone of provisional calcification within the hypertrophic zone; because the proliferative/reserve (germinal) layer on the epiphyseal side and its epiphyseal blood supply are preserved

options.label: C
options.text: Reserve (resting) zone; because the epiphyseal artery is sacrificed but collateralises rapidly

options.label: D
options.text: Zone of ossification in the metaphysis; because the perichondrial ring of LaCroix regenerates the plate

options.label: E
options.text: Zone of proliferation; because the metaphyseal vessels supply the germinal cells and are preserved

explanation: This is a Salter-Harris II injury, the commonest type. The plane of cleavage runs through the hypertrophic zone, specifically the zone of provisional calcification, which is mechanically the weakest layer because of its large, mature chondrocytes and sparse matrix. The reserve and proliferative (germinal) zones lie on the epiphyseal side of this plane and derive their nutrition from the epiphyseal artery; because both the germinal cells and their blood supply are left intact, longitudinal growth potential is usually preserved and growth arrest is uncommon. By contrast, interruption of the epiphyseal blood supply — as in some SH III/IV/V patterns, or in physes covered by articular cartilage such as the proximal femur — is what predisposes to arrest.
learningPoint: Physeal fractures cleave through the hypertrophic zone (zone of provisional calcification); SH-II spares the epiphyseal-side germinal cells and their epiphyseal blood supply, so growth arrest is uncommon.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pediatric orthopaedics: physeal anatomy and injury

stem: A surgeon plans posterior C1-C2 fixation. Considering the course of the V3 (suboccipital) segment of the vertebral artery, which anatomical feature most increases the risk of vertebral artery injury when placing a C2 pedicle screw, and where does the artery lie relative to the C1-C2 complex?

options.label: A
options.text: A 'high-riding' vertebral artery with a narrow, medially-eroded C2 isthmus; the artery runs in the foramen transversarium and arches over the posterior arch of C1 in a groove on its superior surface

options.label: B
options.text: A medially positioned artery crossing the C2 lamina; it lies on the inferior surface of the posterior arch of C1

options.label: C
options.text: An aberrant artery passing anterior to the C1 anterior arch; it does not relate to the transverse foramina

options.label: D
options.text: A vertebral artery that enters the foramen transversarium only at C7; it is therefore absent at the C1-C2 level

options.label: E
options.text: A laterally displaced artery lateral to the C2 superior articular facet; the C2 isthmus is widened and protective

explanation: After ascending through the foramina transversaria of C6 to C2, the vertebral artery turns laterally through the wider C2 transverse foramen and then sweeps medially and posteriorly to lie in a groove (the sulcus arteriae vertebralis) on the SUPERIOR surface of the posterior arch of C1 before piercing the atlanto-occipital membrane and dura. A 'high-riding' vertebral artery — where the transverse foramen sits high and medial and erodes the C2 pars/isthmus — narrows the safe bony corridor and is the principal risk factor for arterial injury during C2 pedicle/pars screw placement; preoperative CT measurement of isthmus height and width is mandatory. Options describing the artery on the inferior surface of C1, anterior to the C1 arch, absent at C1-C2, or protectively lateral are anatomically incorrect.
learningPoint: Assess for a high-riding vertebral artery on preoperative CT before C2 pedicle screws; the V3 segment lies in a groove on the superior surface of the C1 posterior arch.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Spine: cervical anatomy and instrumentation
British Association of Spine Surgeons (BASS) — cervical spine surgery principles

subtopic: Pelvis — anatomy of the corona mortis
stem: While performing a modified Stoppa (anterior intrapelvic) approach for an acetabular fracture, a surgeon encounters torrential bleeding from a vascular anastomosis crossing the superior pubic ramus. This 'corona mortis' is most commonly an anastomosis between which two vessels, and roughly how far lateral to the pubic symphysis does it typically cross the ramus?

options.label: A
options.text: External iliac and superior gluteal vessels; about 2 cm from the symphysis

options.label: B
options.text: Obturator and external iliac (or inferior epigastric) vessels; about 4-9 cm (commonly ~6 cm) from the symphysis

options.label: C
options.text: Internal pudendal and femoral vessels; about 1 cm from the symphysis

options.label: D
options.text: Superior and inferior gluteal vessels; about 10 cm from the symphysis

options.label: E
options.text: Median sacral and obturator vessels; directly at the symphysis

explanation: The corona mortis ('crown of death') is a vascular communication between the obturator system and the external iliac (or inferior epigastric) system running over the superior pubic ramus on its posterior (intrapelvic) surface. It crosses the ramus on average around 6 cm (range roughly 4-9 cm) lateral to the pubic symphysis. An arterial, venous, or combined connection is present in a high proportion of hemipelves (venous variants being the most frequent); uncontrolled bleeding from it is a classic hazard of the Stoppa and ilioinguinal approaches and of pubic ramus screw placement, hence the name. The other vessel pairings and distances are incorrect.
learningPoint: Identify and ligate the corona mortis (obturator-to-external iliac/inferior epigastric anastomosis) crossing the superior pubic ramus ~6 cm from the symphysis during the Stoppa/ilioinguinal approach.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pelvis and acetabulum: surgical anatomy
BOAST — The Management of Patients with Pelvic Fractures

stem: In the developing limb bud, a specialised thickening of ectoderm at the distal tip directs outgrowth and patterning along one axis, while a cluster of mesenchymal cells at the posterior margin secretes a morphogen that patterns the digits. Disruption of the posterior signalling centre is associated with mirror-image polydactyly. Which structure is the posterior signalling centre, and which axis does it pattern?

options.label: A
options.text: Apical ectodermal ridge; patterns the dorsoventral axis via Wnt7a

options.label: B
options.text: Zone of polarising activity; patterns the anteroposterior (radioulnar) axis via Sonic hedgehog

options.label: C
options.text: Apical ectodermal ridge; patterns the proximodistal axis via FGFs

options.label: D
options.text: Zone of polarising activity; patterns the proximodistal axis via FGF8

options.label: E
options.text: Progress zone; patterns the dorsoventral axis via Sonic hedgehog

explanation: Three signalling centres pattern the limb bud along three axes. The apical ectodermal ridge (AER) drives proximodistal outgrowth through fibroblast growth factors (e.g. FGF8) acting on the underlying progress zone. The zone of polarising activity (ZPA), a posterior mesenchymal cluster, secretes Sonic hedgehog (Shh) and patterns the anteroposterior axis (thumb-to-little-finger / radioulnar) — ectopic anterior Shh produces a mirror-image ZPA and mirror polydactyly. Dorsoventral patterning is governed by ectodermal Wnt7a (dorsal) and Engrailed-1 (ventral). The stem describes the ZPA/Shh anteroposterior axis; the other options misassign the centre, morphogen, or axis. Note that option C is a true statement about the AER but does not answer the stem, which asks specifically about the posterior signalling centre.
learningPoint: AER/FGF = proximodistal; ZPA/Sonic hedgehog = anteroposterior (mirror polydactyly if disrupted); Wnt7a = dorsoventral.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pediatric orthopaedics: limb embryology and morphogens

subtopic: Lower limb — named nerve compression syndromes
stem: Theme: peripheral nerve lesions of the lower limb. For each clinical scenario, select the single most likely nerve (or specific branch) involved from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Common peroneal (fibular) nerve at the fibular neck

options.label: B
options.text: Deep peroneal (fibular) nerve

options.label: C
options.text: Superficial peroneal (fibular) nerve

options.label: D
options.text: Tibial nerve in the tarsal tunnel

options.label: E
options.text: Saphenous nerve

options.label: F
options.text: Sural nerve

options.label: G
options.text: Femoral nerve

options.label: H
options.text: Obturator nerve

explanation: Localise lower-limb nerve lesions by the precise motor and sensory map. A prolonged kneeling/squatting posture, a tight below-knee cast, or a proximal fibular fracture compresses the COMMON peroneal nerve as it winds round the fibular neck, giving foot drop (weak dorsiflexion and eversion) with numbness of the dorsum of the foot and first web space and a preserved ankle jerk and inversion (answer A). An 'anterior tarsal tunnel syndrome' from tight footwear or dorsal osteophytes compresses the DEEP peroneal nerve under the extensor retinaculum, producing first web-space numbness with little or no motor loss to the long extensors (answer B). The SAPHENOUS nerve, the terminal sensory branch of the femoral nerve, is at risk during medial knee surgery or long saphenous vein harvest and produces medial leg/ankle numbness with no motor deficit (answer E). The TIBIAL nerve or its branches, compressed behind the medial malleolus by a ganglion or post-traumatic fibrosis, causes burning plantar pain with a positive Tinel's sign posteroinferior to the medial malleolus (answer D). The superficial peroneal nerve (lateral leg/dorsum sensory symptoms with intact dorsiflexion) and the sural, femoral and obturator nerves are distractors here.
learningPoint: Localise lower-limb nerve lesions by the precise motor/sensory map: fibular neck (foot drop), anterior tarsal tunnel (deep peroneal, first web space), tarsal tunnel (tibial, burning sole), and the saphenous nerve as the femoral sensory branch over the medial leg.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Anatomy: peripheral nerves of the lower limb
Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Foot & ankle: nerve entrapment syndromes

emiLeads.stem: A 35-year-old labourer spends a full day laying flooring on his knees. He develops a painless right foot drop with weakness of ankle dorsiflexion and eversion and numbness over the dorsum of the foot and first dorsal web space. The ankle jerk and inversion are normal.

A long-distance runner reports numbness limited to the first web space of the foot, worse in tight lace-up trainers. There is a positive Tinel's sign over the dorsum of the ankle beneath the extensor retinaculum, but dorsiflexion and eversion power are preserved.

Six weeks after a long saphenous vein stripping and a medial arthrotomy of the knee, a patient complains of a band of numbness running down the medial side of the lower leg to the medial malleolus. Motor examination of the leg and foot is entirely normal.

A patient with a ganglion behind the medial malleolus describes burning, tingling pain on the sole of the foot that is worse on standing and at night, with a positive percussion sign posterior and inferior to the medial malleolus.

subtopic: Upper limb — surgical approach to the humerus & radial nerve
stem: A surgeon uses a posterior approach to the humeral shaft, developing the interval through the triceps to expose the bone. The radial nerve and profunda brachii vessels are identified running in the spiral (radial) groove. Relative to the deltoid tuberosity and the lateral epicondyle, where does the radial nerve cross the posterior humerus, and which triceps head is most useful as the landmark leading the surgeon down to it?

options.label: A
options.text: It crosses immediately proximal to the olecranon fossa; the medial head of triceps is split to find it

options.label: B
options.text: It runs in the spiral groove between the lateral and medial heads of triceps, roughly at the junction of the proximal and middle thirds; the interval is developed with the long and lateral heads superficial and the medial head deep

options.label: C
options.text: It lies subcutaneously along the medial border of the arm throughout; no triceps landmark is needed

options.label: D
options.text: It pierces the lateral intermuscular septum 2 cm distal to the lateral epicondyle; the anconeus is the landmark

options.label: E
options.text: It crosses anterior to the surgical neck of the humerus; the long head of triceps leads to it

explanation: In the posterior approach to the humerus, the radial nerve with the profunda brachii artery runs in the spiral groove on the posterior surface of the humerus, deep to the lateral head of triceps. The classic strategy is to identify the interval with the long and lateral heads superficial, retract them, and recognise the obliquely-running fibres of the medial (deep) head; the nerve lies on the bone roughly at the level where the proximal and middle thirds meet (around the spiral groove / lateral head origin). The confluence of the triceps aponeurosis is a useful distal landmark, with the nerve lying a few centimetres proximal to it. More distally the nerve leaves the groove to pierce the lateral intermuscular septum approximately 10 cm proximal to the lateral epicondyle, entering the anterior compartment — a critical zone in distal-third humeral fractures (Holstein-Lewis pattern) and during plating. The other options misplace the nerve or the relevant muscular landmark.
learningPoint: In the posterior humeral approach the radial nerve lies in the spiral groove deep to the lateral head of triceps near the proximal/middle-third junction, then pierces the lateral intermuscular septum ~10 cm above the lateral epicondyle.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Surgical approaches: posterior approach to the humerus
Hoppenfeld, Surgical Exposures in Orthopaedics — posterior approach to the humeral shaft

stem: A 28-year-old man sustains a closed mid-shaft femoral fracture treated with a reamed, statically locked intramedullary nail. At 16 weeks there is abundant bridging callus on three of four cortices. Which statement best describes the dominant mode of bone healing that produced this callus?

options.label: A
options.text: Primary (direct) cortical remodelling via cutting cones across a contact gap

options.label: B
options.text: Secondary (indirect) healing with endochondral ossification driven by interfragmentary strain

options.label: C
options.text: Distraction osteogenesis with intramembranous bone formation under tension-stress

options.label: D
options.text: Creeping substitution of necrotic bone without a cartilaginous intermediate

options.label: E
options.text: Appositional periosteal growth independent of mechanical load

explanation: A locked IM nail provides relative (not absolute) stability, allowing micromotion at the fracture. This interfragmentary strain stimulates secondary (indirect) bone healing: a soft cartilaginous callus forms and is converted to woven then lamellar bone by endochondral ossification. Abundant external bridging callus is the radiographic signature of this process. Primary healing (A) occurs only under absolute stability (e.g. compression plating) and produces no visible callus, advancing by Haversian remodelling/cutting cones. Distraction osteogenesis (C) is intramembranous bone formed under gradual tension (Ilizarov), not the situation here. Creeping substitution (D) describes revascularisation of avascular/necrotic bone (e.g. osteonecrosis, graft incorporation). Perren's strain theory: tissue differentiation is governed by the strain at the gap — granulation tissue tolerates up to ~100% strain, cartilage an intermediate range, and lamellar bone forms only at <2% strain (a tissue ruptures if local strain exceeds its tolerance).
learningPoint: Relative stability (IM nail/bridging plate) heals by callus/endochondral ossification; absolute stability (lag screw + compression plate) heals primarily without callus.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Basic Science — Bone Injury and Repair
Perren strain theory; AO Principles of Fracture Management

stem: During a research project you examine the biomechanical contribution of articular cartilage matrix components. Which structure-function relationship is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: Type II collagen fibrils are oriented perpendicular to the surface in the superficial zone to resist shear

options.label: B
options.text: Aggrecan (proteoglycan) bears the predominant compressive load by generating osmotic swelling pressure

options.label: C
options.text: Chondrocyte density is highest in the deep zone, reflecting the greatest metabolic demand there

options.label: D
options.text: The tidemark separates the superficial and middle (transitional) zones

options.label: E
options.text: Water content is lowest in the superficial zone, increasing toward the calcified layer

explanation: Aggrecan is a large aggregating proteoglycan whose glycosaminoglycan side chains (chondroitin and keratan sulphate) carry a high fixed negative charge. This draws in water and counter-ions (Donnan effect), generating swelling/osmotic pressure that resists compression; the collagen network restrains this swelling. So proteoglycan governs compressive behaviour while type II collagen governs tensile/shear behaviour. In the superficial zone collagen runs PARALLEL to the surface (tangential) to resist shear and friction (A is wrong). Chondrocyte density is HIGHEST in the superficial zone and lowest in the deep zone (C wrong). The tidemark separates the deep (radial) zone from the calcified cartilage, not superficial from middle (D wrong). Water content is HIGHEST superficially (~80%) and falls toward the deep/calcified layers (E wrong). Cartilage is avascular, aneural and alymphatic, nourished by diffusion from synovial fluid, which underlies its poor intrinsic healing.
learningPoint: Proteoglycan (aggrecan) resists compression via osmotic swelling; collagen resists tension/shear — and the tidemark marks the deep zone/calcified cartilage junction.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Basic Science — Articular Cartilage

stem: A 4-year-old previously well child presents with a 2-day history of refusing to weight-bear, fever of 38.8C, and tenderness over the distal femoral metaphysis. Inflammatory markers are raised and MRI confirms acute osteomyelitis. Before culture results return, which single organism should empirical antibiotic therapy primarily target?

options.label: A
options.text: Kingella kingae

options.label: B
options.text: Staphylococcus aureus

options.label: C
options.text: Streptococcus pneumoniae

options.label: D
options.text: Salmonella species

options.label: E
options.text: Pseudomonas aeruginosa

explanation: Staphylococcus aureus is the most common cause of acute haematogenous osteomyelitis across all age groups beyond the neonatal period and must be covered empirically; UK practice typically uses high-dose IV flucloxacillin (with clindamycin or a glycopeptide if penicillin-allergic or MRSA risk). Kingella kingae (A) is an increasingly recognised cause specifically in children under ~4 years and is fastidious (improved yield with blood-culture-bottle inoculation and PCR), but it is not the primary empirical target over S. aureus. Salmonella (D) is classically associated with sickle-cell disease but S. aureus still predominates even there. Pseudomonas (E) relates to puncture wounds through trainers/IV drug use. Pneumococcus (C) is less common. Metaphyseal localisation reflects sluggish flow in the hairpin metaphyseal capillary loops; in children under ~18 months transphyseal vessels allow spread to the epiphysis/joint.
learningPoint: S. aureus is the empirical target in acute osteomyelitis at any age; consider Kingella kingae in under-4s and Salmonella in sickle-cell, but still cover staph.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Basic Science — Musculoskeletal Infection
BSAC/BOA guidance on bone and joint infection

stem: A 70-year-old man develops a chronic prosthetic joint infection 14 months after a total knee replacement. Cultures grow a coagulase-negative staphylococcus that is sensitive to multiple antibiotics in vitro, yet prolonged systemic therapy alone fails to clear the infection. Which property of the infecting organism best explains this treatment failure?

options.label: A
options.text: Production of an exotoxin that inactivates beta-lactam antibiotics

options.label: B
options.text: Formation of a glycocalyx biofilm on the implant harbouring metabolically dormant bacteria

options.label: C
options.text: Plasmid-mediated extended-spectrum beta-lactamase production

options.label: D
options.text: Intracellular survival within osteoblasts evading all antibiotic classes

options.label: E
options.text: Acquisition of the mecA gene conferring vancomycin resistance

explanation: On an implant surface, organisms (especially coagulase-negative staphylococci) secrete an extracellular polysaccharide glycocalyx and form a biofilm. Within it, bacteria adopt a slow-growing/dormant 'persister' phenotype and are shielded by the matrix and a diffusion barrier, so they tolerate concentrations of antibiotic far above the planktonic MIC despite the lab reporting 'sensitive'. This is why eradication of established (chronic) PJI almost always requires removal/exchange of the prosthesis plus antibiotics rather than antibiotics alone, and why rifampicin (active against biofilm/adherent staphylococci, always combined to avoid resistance) is favoured. The in-vitro sensitivities are measured on planktonic bacteria and do not reflect biofilm tolerance. mecA confers methicillin (not vancomycin) resistance (E wrong); ESBL/exotoxin mechanisms (A, C) would alter the susceptibility report, which here was sensitive. Intracellular persistence (D) contributes to chronicity but biofilm is the dominant implant-related mechanism.
learningPoint: Implant biofilm makes 'sensitive' organisms clinically resistant — chronic PJI usually needs implant removal/exchange, not antibiotics alone.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Basic Science — Musculoskeletal Infection / Biomaterials

stem: A 68-year-old woman with established postmenopausal osteoporosis and a previous vertebral fragility fracture is reviewed in the metabolic bone clinic. Considering mechanism of action, which agent works principally by inhibiting the RANK ligand (RANKL) pathway to reduce osteoclast formation and activity?

options.label: A
options.text: Alendronate

options.label: B
options.text: Teriparatide

options.label: C
options.text: Denosumab

options.label: D
options.text: Strontium ranelate

options.label: E
options.text: Raloxifene

explanation: Denosumab is a fully human monoclonal antibody to RANKL. By binding RANKL it prevents engagement of RANK on osteoclast precursors, mimicking the action of endogenous osteoprotegerin (OPG), thereby suppressing osteoclast differentiation, function and survival — a potent antiresorptive given 6-monthly subcutaneously. Note its effect is not retained after stopping: discontinuation causes rebound high-turnover bone loss and multiple vertebral fractures, so therapy must be continued or followed by a bisphosphonate. Bisphosphonates such as alendronate (A) bind hydroxyapatite and impair osteoclast function (nitrogen-containing types inhibit farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase in the mevalonate pathway) — antiresorptive but not via RANKL. Teriparatide (B, recombinant PTH 1-34) is anabolic via intermittent PTH receptor stimulation. Strontium ranelate (D) has a dual/uncoupling effect and is now rarely used. Raloxifene (E) is a SERM acting on oestrogen receptors. The RANK/RANKL/OPG axis is the master regulator of osteoclastogenesis.
learningPoint: Denosumab = anti-RANKL antibody (mimics OPG); never stop it abruptly without follow-on therapy because of rebound vertebral fractures.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Basic Science — Bone Metabolism (RANK/RANKL/OPG)

stem: A 72-year-old man has an isolated, markedly raised serum alkaline phosphatase with normal calcium and phosphate. Radiographs of the pelvis show coarsened trabeculae, cortical thickening and bony expansion of the right hemipelvis. Which histological feature is most characteristic of the underlying disease?

options.label: A
options.text: Wide unmineralised osteoid seams with prolonged mineralisation lag time

options.label: B
options.text: Disorganised mosaic pattern of lamellar bone with prominent cement lines

options.label: C
options.text: Trabeculae lined by numerous osteoclasts with marrow fibrosis (dissecting osteitis)

options.label: D
options.text: Non-caseating granulomata within the marrow space

options.label: E
options.text: Sheets of small round blue cells replacing marrow

explanation: The clinical picture — isolated very high ALP with normal calcium/phosphate and expansile, coarsened, thickened bone — is classic Paget disease of bone. Its hallmark histology is a chaotic 'mosaic' or jigsaw pattern of lamellar bone created by repeated cycles of accelerated, disordered resorption and formation, with irregular prominent cement lines. The disease passes through an early osteolytic phase (giant multinucleated osteoclasts), a mixed phase, and a late sclerotic/burnt-out phase. Wide osteoid seams with increased mineralisation lag time (A) define osteomalacia. Marked osteoclastic resorption with marrow fibrosis/'dissecting osteitis' and brown tumours (C) describes osteitis fibrosa cystica of hyperparathyroidism. Non-caseating granulomata (D) suggest sarcoid/TB. Small round blue cells (E) indicate a tumour such as Ewing/metastasis. Complications of Paget include high-output cardiac failure, deafness, pathological fracture, secondary OA and rare osteosarcomatous transformation (sudden pain/rising ALP). UK management uses a potent IV bisphosphonate (zoledronate) for symptoms.
learningPoint: Isolated high ALP with normal Ca/PO4 + expansile bone = Paget; the mosaic lamellar pattern with cement lines is pathognomonic.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Basic Science — Metabolic Bone Disease
NICE/Bone & Tooth Society UK guideline on Paget disease

stem: A 38-year-old man on long-term high-dose corticosteroids for SLE develops insidious right groin pain. Plain radiographs are normal. Which investigation is the most sensitive for detecting early (pre-collapse) osteonecrosis of the femoral head, and what is the earliest specific sign?

options.label: A
options.text: Plain radiograph showing a subchondral lucent 'crescent sign'

options.label: B
options.text: MRI showing a serpiginous low-signal line demarcating the necrotic segment

options.label: C
options.text: CT showing the 'asterisk sign' distortion of trabeculae

options.label: D
options.text: Bone scintigraphy showing diffusely increased uptake throughout the head

options.label: E
options.text: Ultrasound showing a joint effusion

explanation: MRI is the most sensitive and specific modality for early osteonecrosis (avascular necrosis) and detects disease before radiographic change. The earliest characteristic finding is a serpiginous (geographic) low-signal-intensity line on T1 demarcating viable from necrotic bone, and the 'double-line sign' on T2 (inner high-signal granulation/hyperaemic margin, outer low-signal sclerosis) is essentially pathognomonic. The crescent sign (A) is a SUBCHONDRAL fracture seen later on plain film and signifies impending collapse (Ficat III) — it is not early. The CT 'asterisk sign' (C) is less sensitive than MRI. A bone scan (D) classically shows a photopenic ('cold') centre with increased peripheral uptake ('cold-in-hot' / doughnut), not diffuse uptake, and is less sensitive than MRI. Effusion (E) is non-specific. Steroids and alcohol are the commonest atraumatic causes; pathogenesis involves impaired blood supply/intraosseous hypertension and marrow fat changes. The Ficat-Arlet and ARCO systems stage disease; pre-collapse hips may be considered for core decompression.
learningPoint: MRI (T1 serpiginous line / T2 double-line sign) detects osteonecrosis before X-ray; the crescent sign is a LATE subchondral fracture predicting collapse.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Hip — Osteonecrosis (Ficat/ARCO)

stem: A 55-year-old man presents with an acutely hot, swollen first metatarsophalangeal joint. Aspiration is performed and polarised light microscopy is requested. Which combination of crystal morphology and birefringence confirms gout rather than pseudogout?

options.label: A
options.text: Rhomboid crystals, positively birefringent (blue when aligned parallel to the compensator)

options.label: B
options.text: Needle-shaped crystals, negatively birefringent (yellow when aligned parallel to the compensator)

options.label: C
options.text: Needle-shaped crystals, positively birefringent (blue when parallel)

options.label: D
options.text: Rhomboid crystals, negatively birefringent (yellow when parallel)

options.label: E
options.text: Amorphous non-birefringent material with abundant cholesterol clefts

explanation: Gout is caused by monosodium urate crystals: these are NEEDLE-shaped and NEGATIVELY birefringent under compensated polarised light — appearing yellow when their long axis lies PARALLEL to the axis of the red compensator (and blue when perpendicular). Pseudogout (calcium pyrophosphate dihydrate deposition, CPPD) crystals are RHOMBOID/brick-shaped and POSITIVELY birefringent (blue when parallel, yellow when perpendicular) — option A. A useful mnemonic: urate is 'negative and needle'. Option D mixes pseudogout's shape with negative birefringence, and C mixes urate's shape with positive birefringence — both wrong. Crucially, septic arthritis can coexist with crystal disease, so a hot joint should still have synovial fluid sent for urgent Gram stain, culture and cell count before attributing it solely to crystals. UK acute gout management: NSAID, colchicine or corticosteroid; urate-lowering with allopurinol is offered after the acute episode (and continued if already established).
learningPoint: Urate = Needle, Negative; CPPD/pseudogout = rhomboid, positive — but always exclude coexisting sepsis on the aspirate.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Basic Science — Arthritides (crystal disease)
NICE NG219 Gout; British Society for Rheumatology gout guideline

stem: Biology of musculoskeletal soft-tissue healing. For each clinical scenario, select the SINGLE most appropriate matching concept from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Wallerian degeneration

options.label: B
options.text: Neurapraxia

options.label: C
options.text: Axonotmesis

options.label: D
options.text: Neurotmesis

options.label: E
options.text: Inflammatory phase of healing

options.label: F
options.text: Proliferative (fibroblastic) phase with type III collagen

options.label: G
options.text: Remodelling phase with type I collagen

options.label: H
options.text: Enthesis (direct fibrocartilaginous insertion)

options.label: I
options.text: Creeping substitution / ligamentisation

explanation: Nerve injury (Seddon): neurapraxia (B) is a focal conduction block from segmental demyelination with an intact axon and complete recovery in days–weeks (e.g. 'Saturday night palsy'). Axonotmesis (C) involves axonal disruption with preserved endoneurial tubes, so the distal axon undergoes Wallerian degeneration but regeneration follows the intact tubes at roughly 1 mm/day with a good prognosis — typified by an advancing Tinel sign. Neurotmesis (D) is complete transection of the nerve including its connective-tissue sheaths; spontaneous useful recovery does not occur and surgical repair is required. Wallerian degeneration (A) is specifically the antegrade breakdown of the axon and myelin DISTAL to a transection/disruption, clearing debris (by Schwann cells/macrophages) to permit regrowth. Soft-tissue/tendon and ligament healing proceeds through overlapping phases: inflammatory (E, haematoma and cytokines, first days), proliferative/fibroblastic where disorganised type III collagen is laid down (F, weeks), and remodelling where type III is replaced by stronger, aligned type I collagen over months (G). Ligamentisation/creeping substitution (I) is the cellular remodelling a free tendon graft undergoes after ACL reconstruction (necrosis → revascularisation → repopulation), with a transient mechanical weakness. These leads map cleanly: a wrist-drop fully recovering in 6 weeks = B; the distal degeneration after a sharp laceration = A; preserved endoneurial tubes with Tinel advancing = C; early scar strength from disorganised collagen = F; the remodelling of an ACL graft = I.
learningPoint: Match nerve injury by sheath integrity (neurapraxia→axonotmesis→neurotmesis) and remember soft-tissue healing swaps weak type III for strong type I collagen during remodelling.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Basic Science — Nerve Injury (Seddon/Sunderland) and Soft-Tissue Healing

emiLeads.stem: A 45-year-old wakes with a wrist-drop after falling asleep with his arm over a chair; nerve conduction shows focal conduction block with preserved distal responses, and full power returns by 6 weeks.
After a closed traction injury the axons are interrupted but the endoneurial tubes remain intact; an advancing Tinel sign tracks recovery at about 1 mm/day.
Following a glass laceration completely dividing the digital nerve, the axon and myelin distal to the cut break down to clear the way for any regeneration.
Six weeks after a tendon repair the bridging scar consists largely of disorganised, mechanically weak collagen that is later replaced by aligned type I fibres.
A hamstring autograft used for ACL reconstruction undergoes avascular necrosis, revascularisation and cellular repopulation, passing through a phase of reduced mechanical strength.

stem: During a viva you are shown stress–strain curves for cortical bone tested at two different rates: one slow and one fast (high strain rate). Which statement best describes the behaviour of cortical bone as the strain rate increases?

options.label: A
options.text: Bone becomes stiffer and stronger but more brittle, failing over a shorter strain range

options.label: B
options.text: Bone becomes more compliant (lower Young's modulus) and fails at lower stress

options.label: C
options.text: The Young's modulus is unchanged because bone is a purely elastic material

options.label: D
options.text: Bone fails earlier in its elastic region with no change in stiffness

options.label: E
options.text: The ultimate tensile strength falls while ductility increases

explanation: Bone is viscoelastic, so its mechanical response is rate-dependent (and it also exhibits creep, stress-relaxation and hysteresis). At higher strain rates cortical bone behaves in a stiffer and stronger manner — both the elastic modulus and the load to failure rise — but it becomes more brittle, with a reduced strain (elongation) to failure, and tends to fail in a more comminuted pattern (relevant to high-energy injuries). At low strain rates it is comparatively more compliant and fails at lower load over a larger strain range. A purely elastic material (option C) would, by definition, show a rate-independent modulus, which is not true of bone. Option E is the reverse of the true behaviour: at high strain rate strength rises and ductility falls.
learningPoint: Bone is viscoelastic: higher strain rate → stiffer, stronger, but more brittle (shorter strain to failure) with more comminution.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Basic Sciences: biomechanics of bone
Nordin & Frankel, Basic Biomechanics of the Musculoskeletal System

subtopic: Fracture fixation — relative stability / working length
stem: A comminuted mid-diaphyseal femoral fracture is treated with a locked bridging plate spanning the zone of comminution, with the aim of relative stability and healing by callus. The construct is felt to be too stiff, producing very little interfragmentary motion and a risk of delayed union. Which single change would most effectively reduce construct stiffness and increase strain at the fracture to promote callus formation?

options.label: A
options.text: Increasing the plate working length by leaving the innermost screw holes empty on each side of the fracture

options.label: B
options.text: Adding a lag screw across the comminuted segment through the plate

options.label: C
options.text: Placing additional locking screws in every available hole close to the fracture

options.label: D
options.text: Exchanging the titanium plate for a stiffer stainless-steel plate of the same dimensions

options.label: E
options.text: Using bicortical screws in place of unicortical locking screws near the fracture

explanation: In bridge plating the goal is relative stability with controlled interfragmentary motion to drive callus. Working length — the distance between the two innermost screws either side of the fracture — is a key determinant of construct stiffness: lengthening it (by omitting the screw holes nearest the fracture) reduces axial and bending stiffness and increases the strain experienced at the fracture, encouraging callus. A lag screw (B) creates absolute stability/compression and is inappropriate across comminution. Packing screws close to the fracture (C), a stiffer steel plate (D) and converting to bicortical fixation near the fracture (E) all increase, not decrease, stiffness. A construct that is excessively stiff produces too little motion and can cause delayed/non-union; one that is excessively flexible produces too much strain and also impairs healing — the surgeon tunes working length to land in the optimal strain window.
learningPoint: Lengthening the plate working length lowers construct stiffness and raises interfragmentary strain to promote callus in bridge plating.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Basic Sciences: principles of internal fixation

stem: Two total hip replacements use a metal-on-conventional-polyethylene bearing. Implant X has a 28 mm femoral head and implant Y a 36 mm head, with otherwise identical components and identical linear (penetration) wear rate per unit sliding distance. Compared with the 28 mm bearing, the 36 mm bearing would be expected to generate:

options.label: A
options.text: A greater volumetric wear (more polyethylene particles) for the same linear wear rate

options.label: B
options.text: Less volumetric wear because the larger head reduces contact stress

options.label: C
options.text: Identical volumetric wear because the bearing materials are the same

options.label: D
options.text: Lower volumetric wear because a larger head lowers the coefficient of friction

options.label: E
options.text: No measurable wear because fluid-film lubrication is achieved

explanation: Volumetric wear of a polyethylene bearing is approximately proportional to the linear (penetration) wear rate multiplied by the area swept, which scales with the square of the head radius (V ∝ r² × linear wear). So for the same linear wear rate, a larger head sweeps a greater surface area and produces more volume of polyethylene debris — the osteolysis-driving particle burden rises. This is the classic trade-off of larger heads with conventional polyethylene: they improve stability and range of motion but increase volumetric wear (which is why highly cross-linked polyethylene was adopted to allow larger heads). With metal-on-polyethylene the lubrication regime remains boundary/mixed rather than full fluid-film, so option E is wrong. Contact stress and friction arguments (B, D) do not overturn the area-driven increase in volumetric wear.
learningPoint: For the same linear wear rate, volumetric polyethylene wear rises with the square of head diameter — larger heads make more debris.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Basic Sciences: tribology and bearing surfaces
National Joint Registry (NJR) — bearing surface outcomes

subtopic: Implant materials — modes of failure / taper corrosion
stem: A patient with a well-fixed, well-positioned metal-on-polyethylene total hip replacement using a cobalt-chromium head on a titanium-alloy stem develops new groin pain with rising serum cobalt and chromium levels and a fluid collection at the head–neck junction on MARS MRI. The acetabular bearing is intact. Which mechanism best explains the metal debris in this construct?

options.label: A
options.text: Mechanically assisted crevice corrosion (fretting corrosion) at the modular head–neck taper junction

options.label: B
options.text: Third-body abrasive wear of the polyethylene liner

options.label: C
options.text: Adhesive wear of the metal-on-polyethylene articulation

options.label: D
options.text: Stress shielding of the proximal femur

options.label: E
options.text: Fatigue fracture of the femoral stem

explanation: A metal-on-polyethylene hip should not generate a metal-on-metal articular debris load, so raised cobalt/chromium with an adverse local tissue reaction localised to the head–neck junction points to taper (trunnion) corrosion. Micromotion at the modular junction breaches the passivating oxide layer; the crevice environment becomes acidic and oxygen-depleted, driving repeated depassivation and corrosion — mechanically assisted crevice corrosion, also termed fretting/trunnionosis. This releases cobalt and chromium ions and particulate debris that can cause a pseudotumour/ALTR even with an intact bearing. Polyethylene wear mechanisms (B, C) liberate polymer, not metal ions. Stress shielding (D) is a bone-remodelling phenomenon and stem fatigue fracture (E) would show implant breakage, not isolated taper-junction corrosion. Surveillance of patients with raised ions follows MHRA guidance.
learningPoint: Raised Co/Cr with an ALTR at the head–neck junction in a metal-on-poly hip = taper (trunnion) corrosion — mechanically assisted crevice corrosion.
references: MHRA — Medical device alerts on metal-on-metal and taper corrosion
Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Basic Sciences: corrosion and implant failure

stem: Theme: properties of bone grafts and graft substitutes. From the option list, select the single material that best matches each described property or scenario. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Fresh autologous iliac-crest cancellous graft

options.label: B
options.text: Fresh-frozen structural allograft

options.label: C
options.text: Demineralised bone matrix (DBM)

options.label: D
options.text: Synthetic porous hydroxyapatite / tricalcium phosphate ceramic

options.label: E
options.text: Recombinant human bone morphogenetic protein-2 (rhBMP-2)

options.label: F
options.text: Autologous bone marrow aspirate concentrate

options.label: G
options.text: Calcium sulphate (plaster of Paris) pellets

emiLeads.stem: The only listed graft that simultaneously provides all three of osteoconduction, osteoinduction and osteogenic cells, and is regarded as the biological gold standard for grafting.
A purely synthetic, osteoconductive-only scaffold that contains no living cells and no inductive proteins, used as a void filler; the slowly-resorbing form provides a porous lattice for ingrowth.
An allograft-derived product processed to expose bone matrix proteins so that it is principally osteoinductive but provides little structural support.

A recombinant growth factor that is potently osteoinductive on its own but has no osteoconductive scaffold or osteogenic cells, and carries a regulatory warning about off-label use in the cervical spine.
The listed option that supplies osteogenic/osteoprogenitor cells but essentially no structural scaffold, often combined with a carrier to add osteoconduction.

explanation: Grafts are characterised by three properties: osteoconduction (a passive scaffold for ingrowth), osteoinduction (signalling that recruits/differentiates host cells, classically BMPs) and osteogenesis (viable bone-forming cells). Fresh autograft (A) uniquely delivers all three and is the gold standard. Synthetic ceramics such as hydroxyapatite/β-TCP (D) and calcium sulphate (G) are osteoconductive only. DBM (C) is processed allograft that exposes matrix proteins (including BMPs) to make it chiefly osteoinductive, with negligible structural strength. rhBMP-2 (E) is a pure osteoinductive growth factor with no scaffold or cells and carries well-known cautions about off-label cervical spine use and life-threatening swelling. Bone marrow aspirate concentrate (F) provides osteoprogenitor cells (osteogenic) but no meaningful scaffold. Structural allograft (B) is mainly osteoconductive and provides mechanical support but is non-viable.
learningPoint: Classify grafts by osteoconduction/osteoinduction/osteogenesis: only fresh autograft offers all three; ceramics are conductive-only, DBM and rhBMP-2 are inductive, marrow concentrate supplies cells.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Basic Sciences: bone grafting and graft substitutes
MHRA / manufacturer guidance on rhBMP use

stem: A 67-year-old postmenopausal woman undergoes dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) after a low-energy distal radius fracture. The lowest T-score (lumbar spine) is -2.7. Using the World Health Organization densitometric criteria, how is this result best classified?

options.label: A
options.text: Osteoporosis

options.label: B
options.text: Normal bone mineral density

options.label: C
options.text: Osteopenia (low bone mass)

options.label: D
options.text: Severe (established) osteoporosis

options.label: E
options.text: Osteomalacia

explanation: The T-score expresses bone mineral density as the number of standard deviations from the mean of a young healthy adult reference population. The WHO densitometric thresholds are: normal ≥ -1.0; osteopenia between -1.0 and -2.5; osteoporosis ≤ -2.5. A T-score of -2.7 is in the osteoporotic range. Crucially, the WHO category of severe (established) osteoporosis is defined as a T-score ≤ -2.5 PLUS one or more fragility fractures. This patient has a T-score of -2.7 AND a low-energy (fragility) distal radius fracture, so she meets the definition of severe/established osteoporosis — the single best classification here. Had there been no fragility fracture, the answer would simply be osteoporosis (A). The Z-score (not the T-score) is used in premenopausal women and younger patients. Osteomalacia (E) is a qualitative mineralisation defect, not a DEXA T-score category. UK practice (NICE/NOGG) would then formally assess fracture risk and guide treatment.
learningPoint: WHO DEXA: normal ≥ -1.0, osteopenia -1.0 to -2.5, osteoporosis ≤ -2.5; a T-score ≤ -2.5 PLUS a fragility fracture = severe (established) osteoporosis.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Basic Sciences: metabolic bone disease and imaging

stem: Theme: appraising a diagnostic and therapeutic study. A new bedside test for scaphoid fracture is evaluated against MRI as the reference standard. In 200 patients, 40 truly have a fracture; the test is positive in 36 of these and is also positive in 16 of the 160 patients without a fracture. For each statistical concept described, select the single best matching term from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Sensitivity

options.label: B
options.text: Specificity

options.label: C
options.text: Positive predictive value

options.label: D
options.text: Negative predictive value

options.label: E
options.text: Number needed to treat

options.label: F
options.text: Absolute risk reduction

options.label: G
options.text: Relative risk

options.label: H
options.text: Type II error

emiLeads.stem: The proportion of patients who genuinely have a scaphoid fracture in whom the new test is positive — here 36/40 = 90%. This property is unaffected by disease prevalence.
Of all patients with a positive test result, the proportion who actually have a fracture — here 36/(36+16) = 36/52 ≈ 69%. This value falls as the prevalence of disease in the tested population falls.

In a separate randomised trial, a drug reduces the rate of symptomatic VTE after hip fracture from 10% to 4%; the reciprocal of the 6% difference (1/0.06 ≈ 17) gives the number of patients who must be treated to prevent one event.
A trial concludes there is no significant difference between two implants, but it was underpowered and a true difference was in fact missed — failing to reject a false null hypothesis.

explanation: Constructing the 2×2 table (TP 36, FN 4, FP 16, TN 144): sensitivity = TP/(TP+FN) = 36/40 = 90% and is a property of the test independent of prevalence; specificity = TN/(TN+FP) = 144/160 = 90%. Predictive values depend on prevalence: positive predictive value = TP/(TP+FP) = 36/52 ≈ 69% and falls as prevalence falls, while negative predictive value = TN/(TN+FN). For the therapeutic lead, absolute risk reduction = 10% − 4% = 6%, and the number needed to treat is its reciprocal (1/0.06 ≈ 17). A type II error (β) is failing to reject a false null hypothesis — concluding 'no difference' when one truly exists, the hallmark of an underpowered study (a type I error is the opposite: a false-positive rejection of a true null).
learningPoint: Sensitivity/specificity are prevalence-independent test properties; predictive values are prevalence-dependent; NNT = 1/ARR; type II error = missing a real effect.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Basic Sciences: biostatistics and evidence-based medicine
CEBM (Oxford) levels of evidence and diagnostic statistics

stem: A 17-year-old presents with several months of well-localised pain in the proximal tibial diaphysis that is worse at night and reliably relieved within 20-30 minutes by ibuprofen. Radiographs show a small (<1 cm) lucent nidus surrounded by dense fusiform reactive cortical sclerosis. CT confirms a central nidus. His symptoms persist despite regular NSAIDs and he wishes to proceed with definitive treatment. Which is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: CT-guided percutaneous radiofrequency ablation of the nidus

options.label: B
options.text: Wide en-bloc resection of the involved cortex

options.label: C
options.text: Curettage and bone grafting of the lesion

options.label: D
options.text: Long-term NSAIDs only, with no further intervention offered under any circumstances

options.label: E
options.text: External-beam radiotherapy to the nidus

explanation: The clinical picture (young patient, night pain dramatically relieved by NSAIDs) with a sub-centimetre lucent nidus and surrounding fusiform reactive sclerosis is classic for osteoid osteoma. Pain is prostaglandin-driven, and although many lesions eventually 'burn out' over years, when symptoms persist and intervention is wanted the modern standard of care for an accessible lesion is CT-guided percutaneous radiofrequency ablation of the nidus, which is minimally invasive, has cure rates around 90-95% and avoids the morbidity of open surgery. Wide resection and radiotherapy are inappropriately aggressive for a benign lesion; curettage risks missing the small nidus and is no longer first-line; ongoing NSAIDs alone are reasonable while awaiting burnout but do not constitute definitive treatment when symptoms persist. (Open or CT-guided surgical excision is reserved for lesions where percutaneous ablation is unsafe, e.g. immediately adjacent to the spinal cord or a major nerve.)
learningPoint: Osteoid osteoma = night pain relieved by NSAIDs + small (<1 cm) nidus with surrounding reactive sclerosis. For persistent symptoms, the definitive treatment of choice for an accessible lesion is CT-guided radiofrequency ablation; surgery is reserved for sites where ablation would endanger neural structures.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Orthopaedic Oncology: benign bone-forming tumours
British Orthopaedic Oncology Society (BOOS) guidance on benign bone tumours

stem: A 15-year-old boy has 6 weeks of worsening pain and swelling around the distal femur. Plain radiographs show an aggressive metaphyseal lesion with cortical destruction, a spiculated 'sunburst' periosteal reaction and a Codman triangle. He is referred to the regional sarcoma service with a suspected high-grade primary bone sarcoma. Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.label: A
options.text: Staging investigations and biopsy performed by the specialist sarcoma centre, then neoadjuvant chemotherapy before limb-salvage surgery

options.label: B
options.text: Immediate wide local excision at the referring district general hospital to avoid delay

options.label: C
options.text: Open biopsy at the referring hospital, then transfer to the sarcoma centre for definitive surgery

options.label: D
options.text: Primary above-knee amputation as the definitive oncological procedure

options.label: E
options.text: External-beam radiotherapy to the primary lesion followed by surveillance imaging

explanation: The radiographic features (metaphyseal, aggressive, sunburst spiculation, Codman triangle) in an adolescent are typical of osteosarcoma. UK practice mandates that all suspected primary bone sarcomas are referred to a recognised sarcoma centre, where both the staging (whole-bone MRI, CT chest, and bone scintigraphy or PET-CT) AND the biopsy are carried out by the treating team, because a poorly planned biopsy tract can seed tumour and compromise subsequent limb salvage. High-grade osteosarcoma is managed with neoadjuvant (preoperative) chemotherapy, then limb-salvage resection where feasible, then adjuvant chemotherapy. Excising or biopsying at a non-specialist hospital risks contamination and worse outcomes; amputation is not automatically required when limb salvage is achievable; and osteosarcoma is relatively radioresistant, so radiotherapy is not the primary local treatment.
learningPoint: Suspected primary bone sarcoma → refer untouched to a sarcoma centre; the specialist team performs the staging and the biopsy. High-grade osteosarcoma is treated with neoadjuvant chemotherapy, surgery (limb salvage where feasible), then adjuvant chemotherapy.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Orthopaedic Oncology: osteosarcoma
NICE / BOOS guidance on sarcoma — referral of suspected bone sarcoma to specialist centres

stem: A 62-year-old man presents to his GP with a painless lump in the posterior thigh that he first noticed 3 months ago and feels is slowly enlarging. On examination it is approximately 7 cm, firm, deep to the deep fascia and immobile. Which is the most appropriate initial management?

options.label: A
options.text: Urgent direct-access ultrasound (within 2 weeks), and refer on a suspected-cancer pathway to a sarcoma service if findings are suggestive or remain uncertain with ongoing clinical concern

options.label: B
options.text: Reassure and review in primary care in 6 months if the lump persists

options.label: C
options.text: Arrange an outpatient marginal excision of the lump at the local hospital for histology

options.label: D
options.text: Aspirate the lump in clinic and send the fluid for cytology

options.label: E
options.text: Routine (non-urgent) referral to general surgery for excision biopsy

explanation: A soft-tissue lump that is larger than ~5 cm, deep to fascia, enlarging or painful must be regarded as a sarcoma until proven otherwise. NICE NG12 recommends an urgent direct-access ultrasound (within 2 weeks) for an unexplained, increasing soft-tissue lump in an adult, with referral on a suspected-cancer pathway if the scan is suggestive of sarcoma, or if it is uncertain and clinical concern persists. This lump has multiple red flags (>5 cm, deep, enlarging). Watchful waiting risks dangerous delay. Crucially, the lump must NOT be excised, marginally removed or aspirated outside the specialist centre — an unplanned 'whoops' excision contaminates tissue planes, mandates wider re-excision and worsens limb-salvage and oncological outcomes.
learningPoint: Deep, >5 cm or enlarging soft-tissue lump = sarcoma until proven otherwise: NICE NG12 urgent (2-week) direct-access ultrasound, then refer to a sarcoma service — never an unplanned local excision or aspiration.
references: NICE NG12 — Suspected cancer: recognition and referral (soft-tissue sarcoma)
Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Orthopaedic Oncology: soft-tissue sarcoma
BOOS guidance on the management of soft-tissue sarcoma

stem: A 68-year-old woman with known metastatic breast cancer has a painful 3 cm lytic deposit in the subtrochanteric (peritrochanteric) region of the right femur. She reports increasing pain on weight-bearing. The lesion involves more than two-thirds of the cortical diameter. Using the Mirels scoring system, what is her total score, and what is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Mirels 12 — prophylactic internal fixation is indicated before the bone fractures

options.label: B
options.text: Mirels 9 — manage with radiotherapy and analgesia alone, fixation not indicated

options.label: C
options.text: Mirels 8 — definitively a low fracture risk; reassure and discharge

options.label: D
options.text: Mirels 6 — prophylactic fixation is mandatory

options.label: E
options.text: Mirels cannot be applied to lytic lesions, so the score is uninterpretable

explanation: Mirels' score predicts the risk of pathological fracture from a long-bone metastasis using four parameters, each scored 1-3: site (upper limb 1, lower limb 2, peritrochanteric 3), pain (mild 1, moderate 2, functional/mechanical 3), lesion type (blastic 1, mixed 2, lytic 3) and size relative to cortical diameter (<1/3 = 1, 1/3-2/3 = 2, >2/3 = 3). This lesion is peritrochanteric (3), causes functional weight-bearing pain (3), is lytic (3) and exceeds two-thirds of the cortex (3), giving the maximum total of 12. A score of ≥9 (out of 12) predicts a high fracture risk and prophylactic internal fixation is recommended before the bone breaks; a score of 8 is borderline (individualise / consider fixation), and ≤7 can usually be managed with radiotherapy and analgesia. This maximal-risk lesion should be fixed prophylactically. (Option D is internally inconsistent — a score of 6 is low risk — and Mirels is specifically designed to be applied to lytic, blastic and mixed lesions alike.)
learningPoint: Mirels (site, pain, lesion type, size; each scored 1-3, total /12): ≥9 → prophylactic fixation before the bone breaks; 8 → borderline/individualise; ≤7 → radiotherapy and analgesia. A peritrochanteric, lytic lesion >2/3 of the cortex with functional pain scores the maximum 12.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Orthopaedic Oncology: metastatic bone disease
BOOS / BOA (BOAST) guidance on metastatic bone disease and pathological fractures
Mirels H. Metastatic disease in long bones: a proposed scoring system for diagnosing impending pathologic fractures. Clin Orthop Relat Res. 1989;(249):256-264

stem: Theme: principles of staging and biopsy in musculoskeletal oncology. For each scenario, select the single most appropriate next investigation or principle from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: MRI of the whole involved bone, including the joints above and below the lesion

options.label: B
options.text: CT of the chest

options.label: C
options.text: Image-guided core-needle biopsy performed by (or in conjunction with) the specialist sarcoma centre, with the tract placed in line with the planned resection

options.label: D
options.text: Excisional biopsy in primary care

options.label: E
options.text: Technetium-99m bone scintigraphy (whole-body bone scan)

options.label: F
options.text: Transverse skin incision over the most prominent part of the mass

options.label: G
options.text: Serum protein electrophoresis with serum free light chains and urinary Bence-Jones protein

options.label: H
options.text: No biopsy required; proceed directly to wide resection

emiLeads.stem: A high-grade bone sarcoma is suspected on plain films of the femur. Before any biopsy, which single imaging investigation best defines the intraosseous and soft-tissue extent of the primary tumour and identifies any skip lesions for local staging?
The same patient now needs systemic staging. Bone sarcomas metastasise principally to the lungs. Which single investigation is most important for detecting distant metastatic disease?
A tissue diagnosis is required for the suspected primary bone sarcoma in the femur. Which option correctly describes how the biopsy should be obtained so as not to compromise subsequent limb salvage?

A 70-year-old man has multiple lytic 'punched-out' lesions in the skull and pelvis, normocytic anaemia and renal impairment, with a near-normal bone scan despite extensive radiographic disease. Which single investigation is most likely to establish the diagnosis?

explanation: Local staging of a primary bone sarcoma uses MRI of the entire involved bone (joint above to joint below) to show marrow and soft-tissue extent and to detect skip metastases. Systemic staging targets the lungs (the dominant site of haematogenous spread for sarcoma) with CT chest. The biopsy is the highest-risk step: it must be an image-guided core-needle biopsy planned with the sarcoma centre, with a longitudinal tract sited so it can be excised en-bloc at definitive surgery — never a transverse incision, never an excision/biopsy outside the centre, and never skipped, because a poorly executed biopsy seeds tumour and can convert a limb-salvageable case into an amputation. The elderly patient with multiple punched-out lytic lesions, anaemia, renal impairment and a characteristically near-normal/negative bone scan points to multiple myeloma: because myeloma is osteoclast-driven with suppressed osteoblastic activity, bone scintigraphy (which depends on osteoblastic uptake) frequently misses lesions. Diagnosis rests on serum protein electrophoresis, serum free light chains and urinary Bence-Jones protein, supported by bone marrow biopsy (and whole-body low-dose CT or MRI rather than a bone scan for skeletal assessment).
learningPoint: Stage the bone (whole-bone MRI) and the chest (CT) before touching the tumour; the biopsy belongs to the sarcoma centre and its tract must lie in the planned resection line. Myeloma classically shows lytic lesions with a false-negative bone scan (osteoblast suppression) — diagnose with protein electrophoresis, free light chains and Bence-Jones, plus marrow biopsy.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Orthopaedic Oncology: principles of staging and biopsy
BOOS guidance — biopsy of suspected bone and soft-tissue tumours at specialist centres
Enneking WF. Surgical staging system for musculoskeletal sarcoma

stem: A 28-year-old man is brought in after a high-speed motorcycle collision. Primary survey shows a patent airway, respiratory rate 28, heart rate 130, blood pressure 88/60, and he is anxious and confused. He has an obviously deformed, swollen right thigh and an unstable pelvis on gentle examination. After applying high-flow oxygen, what is the most appropriate immediate intervention to control haemorrhage?

options.label: A
options.text: Apply a pelvic binder centred over the greater trochanters

options.label: B
options.text: Take the patient immediately to CT for a trauma series

options.label: C
options.text: Apply skeletal traction to the femur in the resuscitation room

options.label: D
options.text: Perform a diagnostic peritoneal lavage

options.label: E
options.text: External fixation of the pelvis in the emergency department

explanation: This is a haemodynamically unstable polytrauma patient (Class III shock) with a likely unstable pelvic ring injury, which can cause major venous and arterial bleeding into the retroperitoneum. The immediate, non-invasive manoeuvre to reduce pelvic volume and tamponade bleeding is a correctly positioned pelvic binder, centred over the greater trochanters (not the iliac crests, where it is biomechanically ineffective and may worsen an open-book injury). Resuscitation precedes imaging: an unstable patient should not be taken to CT until haemorrhage control and resuscitation are under way. The femoral fracture is a contributory bleeding source, but the binder is the single fastest control for the higher-volume pelvic bleed; skeletal traction and ED external fixation take time and are not the first response to exsanguinating pelvic bleeding. DPL is largely historical and does not control pelvic haemorrhage.
learningPoint: In the shocked trauma patient with an unstable pelvis, the first step is a correctly placed pelvic binder centred over the greater trochanters — resuscitate before imaging.
references: ATLS (10th ed.) — shock and pelvic trauma
BOAST — The Management of Patients with Pelvic Fractures
Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Trauma: pelvic ring injuries

subtopic: ATLS and the polytrauma patient — damage control
stem: A 35-year-old polytrauma patient with a femoral shaft fracture, chest injury and a closed head injury remains physiologically unstable in the operating theatre. The anaesthetist reports a temperature of 34.5°C, pH 7.18, base deficit -9 and a rising lactate. The fracture has not yet been definitively stabilised. Which strategy is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Application of a temporary spanning external fixator (damage control orthopaedics)

options.label: B
options.text: Reamed intramedullary nailing of the femur on this list

options.label: C
options.text: Open reduction and plate fixation of the femur

options.label: D
options.text: Skin traction and return to theatre in two weeks

options.label: E
options.text: Bilateral plaster casts and conservative management

explanation: The patient displays the lethal triad — hypothermia, acidosis and (by inference) coagulopathy — and is physiologically unstable. The principle of damage control orthopaedics is rapid temporary stabilisation (a spanning external fixator) to control the 'second hit', limit blood loss and inflammatory load while resuscitation continues, deferring definitive fixation until the patient is physiologically optimised. Reamed nailing or open plating in an unstable patient adds a major systemic insult and risks worsening ARDS/multi-organ failure, particularly with the associated chest and head injuries. Traction and casting do not adequately control a femoral shaft fracture in polytrauma.
learningPoint: Physiologically unstable polytrauma (lethal triad) → damage control orthopaedics with a temporary external fixator; convert to definitive fixation once resuscitated.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Trauma: polytrauma and damage control orthopaedics

stem: A 42-year-old sustains an isolated open tibial shaft fracture in a low-energy fall. The wound is clean, 3 cm long, with no significant periosteal stripping, and the limb is neurovascularly intact with a viable soft-tissue envelope. According to current UK (BOAST / NICE NG37) guidance, which of the following is the most appropriate statement about timing and antibiotics?

options.label: A
options.text: Intravenous antibiotics as soon as possible (ideally within 1 hour of injury), with debridement and fixation on a planned ortho-plastic list, generally within 24 hours for this low-energy injury

options.label: B
options.text: Immediate wound washout and definitive internal fixation within 6 hours to prevent infection

options.label: C
options.text: Oral antibiotics and discharge with outpatient follow-up given the low Gustilo grade

options.label: D
options.text: Debridement delayed beyond 72 hours has no influence on outcome

options.label: E
options.text: Antibiotics should be withheld until intra-operative cultures have been taken

explanation: BOAST and NICE NG37 emphasise that intravenous antibiotics should be given as soon as possible, ideally within 1 hour of injury, and not delayed for cultures. The historical '6-hour rule' for emergency debridement has been superseded: debridement should be immediate for highly contaminated wounds, within 12 hours for high-energy injuries, and within 24 hours for low-energy injuries — performed on a scheduled combined ortho-plastic list by senior surgeons. This low-energy isolated injury without contamination, vascular compromise or compartment syndrome therefore falls within the 24-hour window. Immediate emergency surgery is reserved for gross contamination (e.g. marine/agricultural), devascularisation or compartment syndrome. Open fractures are admitted, not discharged on oral antibiotics.
learningPoint: Open fracture: IV antibiotics ideally within 1 hour; debridement on a combined ortho-plastic list — immediate if contaminated/devascularised/compartment syndrome, ≤12 h high-energy, ≤24 h low-energy.
references: NICE NG37 — Fractures (complex): assessment and management
Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Trauma: open fractures

stem: Theme: Gustilo–Anderson classification of open fractures. For each clinical description, select the single most appropriate Gustilo–Anderson grade. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Grade I

options.label: B
options.text: Grade II

options.label: C
options.text: Grade IIIA

options.label: D
options.text: Grade IIIB

options.label: E
options.text: Grade IIIC

options.label: F
options.text: Closed fracture, Tscherne grade 0

emiLeads.stem: A tibial fracture with a clean 1 cm wound, minimal contamination, a simple fracture pattern and no significant soft-tissue injury.
A high-energy tibial fracture with extensive periosteal stripping and a soft-tissue defect that will require a free flap for coverage, with intact distal pulses.
A segmental tibial fracture with an associated transected posterior tibial artery requiring vascular repair to maintain limb viability.
A wound 6 cm long with moderate soft-tissue damage and contamination but adequate local soft tissue for coverage without a flap.

explanation: Gustilo–Anderson grades open fractures by wound size, contamination, soft-tissue injury and vascular status. Grade I: wound <1 cm, clean, low energy. Grade II: 1–10 cm with moderate soft-tissue damage and adequate coverage. Grade III: high-energy/extensive soft-tissue injury, subdivided into IIIA (adequate soft-tissue coverage despite extensive injury), IIIB (soft-tissue loss/periosteal stripping requiring flap coverage) and IIIC (any open fracture with an arterial injury requiring repair, irrespective of wound size). The grade is most reliably assigned at the time of surgical debridement, not in the emergency department.
learningPoint: IIIB = needs flap coverage; IIIC = arterial injury requiring repair (regardless of wound size). Grade definitively at debridement.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Trauma: open fractures and classification

stem: A 9-year-old boy is in a backslab following closed reduction of a displaced supracondylar humeral fracture. Four hours later he has increasing forearm pain requiring escalating analgesia and severe pain when the fingers are passively extended. The radial pulse is palpable and capillary refill is 2 seconds. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Split the cast and all dressings down to skin and reassess promptly

options.label: B
options.text: Reassure the family that a present radial pulse excludes compartment syndrome

options.label: C
options.text: Arrange an urgent CT angiogram of the upper limb

options.label: D
options.text: Give further opioid analgesia and review in the morning

options.label: E
options.text: Elevate the limb above the heart and continue observation only

explanation: Pain out of proportion and pain on passive stretch are the earliest and most reliable signs of acute compartment syndrome; a palpable pulse and normal capillary refill do NOT exclude it, because the compartment pressure that causes muscle ischaemia is well below arterial pressure. The immediate bedside step for a child in a cast is to release all circumferential dressings down to skin and reassess. If signs persist after splitting the cast, urgent fasciotomy is indicated. Pulselessness is a late sign; relying on it risks a Volkmann's ischaemic contracture. In children the classic clinical signs are unreliable, and an increasing analgesia requirement (the '3 A's' — increasing analgesia, anxiety, agitation) is an important early warning sign.
learningPoint: A present pulse never excludes compartment syndrome; split the cast to skin first, and in children rising analgesia requirement is a key red flag.
references: BOAST — Diagnosis and Management of Compartment Syndrome of the Limbs
Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Paediatrics: supracondylar fractures

subtopic: Principles of external fixation — construct stiffness
stem: During application of a monolateral external fixator to a tibial fracture, which single adjustment would most increase the stiffness of the fixator construct?

options.label: A
options.text: Reducing the distance between the bone and the connecting rod (side bar)

options.label: B
options.text: Narrowing the spacing between the two pins within each fragment

options.label: C
options.text: Using pins of a smaller diameter to reduce stress concentration

options.label: D
options.text: Positioning the connecting rod further from the bone to ease pin-site care

options.label: E
options.text: Removing one rod from a double-bar (stacked) configuration

explanation: External fixator rigidity is governed by predictable mechanical principles. Bringing the connecting rod closer to the bone shortens the cantilever ('rod offset') and markedly increases construct stiffness — it is the single most effective lever in a monolateral frame. The other options all REDUCE stiffness: narrowing the pin spread within a fragment, using smaller-diameter pins (stiffness rises with the 4th power of pin radius), increasing the rod-to-bone offset, and removing a connecting bar each lower rigidity. (Other legitimate ways to increase stiffness, not listed here, include widening the pin spread within each fragment, placing the near pins close to the fracture, adding a second bar and reducing the working length.)
learningPoint: Stiffen a monolateral fixator by bringing the bar to the bone, using larger pins, widening pin spread within each fragment, placing near pins close to the fracture, adding a bar and shortening the working length.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Basic Science: external fixation biomechanics

stem: A 30-year-old right-hand-dominant manual labourer sustains a closed mid-shaft clavicle fracture. Radiographs show complete displacement with 2.5 cm of shortening and a vertical intermediate fragment, but the skin is intact and the limb is neurovascularly normal. He is keen to return to manual work quickly. After shared decision-making, which is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Open reduction and plate fixation

options.label: B
options.text: Broad-arm sling and routine fracture-clinic review

options.label: C
options.text: Figure-of-eight bandage for 6 weeks

options.label: D
options.text: Intramedullary pinning followed by 6 weeks of immobilisation

options.label: E
options.text: Corticosteroid injection and early mobilisation

explanation: Most mid-shaft clavicle fractures are managed non-operatively.
