However, a completely displaced fracture with significant shortening (commonly cited as >2 cm) in an active adult is associated with higher rates of symptomatic non-union and malunion; meta-analyses of randomised trials show operative fixation reduces non-union and symptomatic malunion and improves early function — making plate fixation the best of the listed options for this displaced, shortened pattern in a manual worker who wants early return. Routine fixation of all displaced fractures is not supported, so the decision is shared; the FRCS answer hinges on recognising the high-risk displaced/shortened pattern. A figure-of-eight bandage offers no advantage over a sling and is poorly tolerated.
learningPoint: Displaced mid-shaft clavicle fracture with >2 cm shortening in an active adult: discuss fixation — it lowers non-union/malunion and can speed recovery; decision is shared, not automatic.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Trauma: clavicle fractures
Canadian Orthopaedic Trauma Society. JBJS 2007 — operative vs non-operative displaced mid-shaft clavicle fracture

stem: An 80-year-old independent woman with osteoporosis sustains a 4-part fracture-dislocation of the proximal humerus, with the articular head dislocated and devoid of soft-tissue attachments. She has no pre-existing rotator cuff arthropathy. Which operative option offers the most reliable functional outcome?

options.label: A
options.text: Reverse total shoulder arthroplasty

options.label: B
options.text: Open reduction and locking-plate fixation

options.label: C
options.text: Hemiarthroplasty

options.label: D
options.text: Closed reduction and collar-and-cuff immobilisation

options.label: E
options.text: Intramedullary nailing of the humerus

explanation: In an elderly patient with a 4-part fracture or fracture-dislocation where the head is non-reconstructable and at high risk of avascular necrosis, arthroplasty is indicated. Reverse total shoulder arthroplasty has become the preferred arthroplasty over hemiarthroplasty in the elderly because outcomes are far less dependent on tuberosity healing and rotator cuff function, giving more reliable and predictable forward elevation. Locking-plate fixation of a devascularised 4-part head risks screw cut-out and AVN. Hemiarthroplasty outcomes hinge on tuberosity union, which is unreliable in osteoporotic elderly bone. Non-operative treatment of a true head dislocation in an active patient gives poor results.
learningPoint: Non-reconstructable 4-part proximal humerus fracture in the elderly → reverse total shoulder arthroplasty; outcomes are less reliant on tuberosity healing than hemiarthroplasty.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Shoulder: proximal humerus fractures
PROFHER trial (context for operative vs non-operative); PROFHER-2 (RSA vs hemiarthroplasty vs non-operative)

subtopic: Humeral shaft fracture and radial nerve
stem: A 45-year-old sustains a closed spiral fracture of the distal third of the humeral shaft. On examination there is a complete wrist drop with loss of finger and thumb metacarpophalangeal extension, and numbness over the dorsal first web space. Distal pulses are normal. Radiographs confirm a closed Holstein–Lewis pattern fracture. What is the most appropriate initial management of the nerve injury?

options.label: A
options.text: Apply a functional humeral brace and observe the nerve, expecting recovery

options.label: B
options.text: Immediate surgical exploration of the radial nerve

options.label: C
options.text: Nerve conduction studies in the first week to guide urgent grafting

options.label: D
options.text: Tendon transfers as the primary treatment

options.label: E
options.text: Below-elbow cast and discharge

explanation: A radial nerve palsy associated with a CLOSED humeral shaft fracture is most often a neurapraxia, and the great majority recover spontaneously over weeks to months. Standard management is to treat the fracture non-operatively (a functional humeral brace gives high union rates) and observe the nerve, monitoring clinically and with nerve conduction studies if there is no recovery by around 3 months, at which point exploration ± reconstruction is considered. Immediate exploration is reserved for an OPEN fracture, a palsy that develops after manipulation/fixation, or an associated vascular injury. Tendon transfers are a salvage option if the nerve fails to recover, not first-line.
learningPoint: Closed humeral shaft fracture with radial nerve palsy: brace and observe — most recover; explore if open, new after manipulation, or no recovery by ~3 months.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Trauma: humeral shaft fractures and radial nerve palsy

stem: A 33-year-old man falls and sustains an isolated, displaced fracture of the distal third of the radial shaft. Radiographs show widening of the distal radio-ulnar joint with dorsal prominence of the ulnar head. What is the most appropriate definitive management?

options.label: A
options.text: Open reduction and plate fixation of the radius, then assess and stabilise the distal radio-ulnar joint

options.label: B
options.text: Above-elbow cast in supination for 6 weeks

options.label: C
options.text: Intramedullary nailing of the radius alone

options.label: D
options.text: Closed reduction and a functional forearm brace

options.label: E
options.text: Isolated open reduction and pinning of the distal radio-ulnar joint, leaving the radius

explanation: This is a Galeazzi fracture — a distal radial shaft fracture with disruption of the distal radio-ulnar joint (DRUJ). In adults it is inherently unstable (a 'fracture of necessity') and requires anatomical open reduction and rigid plate fixation of the radius. Once the radius is restored to length and alignment the DRUJ is reassessed: if stable, early mobilisation; if reducible but unstable, immobilise in supination (± transfixion K-wires); if irreducible, open the DRUJ (the extensor carpi ulnaris tendon is the most common block). Cast treatment of an adult Galeazzi fails because of recurrent displacement and DRUJ subluxation.
learningPoint: Galeazzi = radial shaft fracture + DRUJ injury — plate the radius anatomically, then assess and stabilise the DRUJ (supination if reducible/unstable; open if irreducible, ECU often the block).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Trauma: forearm fractures (Galeazzi)

stem: A fit, independent 72-year-old woman sustains a closed, dorsally displaced extra-articular distal radius fracture. After closed reduction and a moulded cast, check radiographs at one week show dorsal angulation of 25°, 4 mm of radial shortening and loss of radial inclination. She is keen to maintain her function. Based on current UK (BOAST / BSSH) guidance, what is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Surgical fixation, as the reduction has failed to maintain acceptable alignment

options.label: B
options.text: Accept the position and continue cast immobilisation, as outcome is independent of alignment at this age

options.label: C
options.text: Repeat closed manipulation in the fracture clinic and re-cast

options.label: D
options.text: Convert to a removable splint and begin immediate mobilisation

options.label: E
options.text: Refer for a corticosteroid injection into the radiocarpal joint

explanation: The fracture has redisplaced beyond accepted radiological limits (broadly: dorsal tilt >10°, radial shortening >2–3 mm, intra-articular step >2 mm). BOAST/BSSH guidance is that loss of an acceptable reduction in a patient for whom function matters warrants definitive surgical stabilisation rather than repeated manipulations, which rarely hold in osteoporotic metaphyseal bone. While minor malunion is well tolerated in lower-demand elderly patients, this woman is fit, independent and symptomatic of malalignment, so redisplacement past threshold values is an indication to fix. A second closed manipulation of a fracture that has already slipped is unlikely to hold.
learningPoint: Distal radius fracture that redisplaces past acceptable limits (dorsal tilt >10°, shortening >2–3 mm, articular step >2 mm) in a functioning patient → fix, don't re-manipulate repeatedly.
references: BOAST — The Management of Distal Radius Fractures
BSSH — Best Practice for Management of Distal Radial Fractures
Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Trauma: distal radius fractures

stem: A 26-year-old falls from height onto an outstretched hand. He has a swollen, painful wrist and median nerve paraesthesia. A lateral wrist radiograph shows the lunate tilted and displaced volarly with a 'spilled teacup' appearance, while the capitate is aligned with the radius. What is the diagnosis and the most appropriate definitive management?

options.label: A
options.text: Volar lunate dislocation requiring urgent reduction and operative ligament repair/fixation

options.label: B
options.text: Isolated scapholunate ligament sprain managed in a wrist splint

options.label: C
options.text: Distal radius fracture treated by closed reduction and casting

options.label: D
options.text: Kienböck's disease managed with a radial shortening osteotomy

options.label: E
options.text: Dorsal perilunate dislocation treated with a removable brace alone

explanation: The 'spilled teacup' sign on the lateral view — the lunate tipped and displaced volarly while the capitate sits in line with the radius — denotes a volar lunate dislocation, the end stage (Mayfield IV) of progressive perilunate instability. These are high-energy injuries frequently associated with acute median nerve compression. Management is urgent closed reduction to decompress the nerve, followed by definitive open reduction with repair of the scapholunate (and lunotriquetral) ligaments and K-wire/screw stabilisation; a missed or under-treated injury leads to chronic instability, carpal collapse and arthritis. Splinting or bracing alone is inadequate. These injuries are commonly missed on initial assessment because the AP film can look deceptively normal — always scrutinise the lateral.
learningPoint: 'Spilled teacup' = volar lunate dislocation (Mayfield IV) — urgent reduction (decompress median nerve) then operative ligament repair and fixation; always scrutinise the lateral film.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: carpal instability and perilunate injuries

stem: Theme: acetabular fracture patterns. For each clinical and radiographic description of an acetabular fracture, select the single most likely Judet-Letournel pattern from the list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Posterior wall fracture

options.label: B
options.text: Anterior column fracture

options.label: C
options.text: Transverse fracture

options.label: D
options.text: Posterior column fracture

options.label: E
options.text: Both-column fracture (associated with the 'spur sign')

options.label: F
options.text: T-shaped fracture

options.label: G
options.text: Anterior column plus posterior hemitransverse fracture

emiLeads.stem: A 35-year-old sustains a dashboard injury with a posterior hip dislocation. After reduction, CT shows a fragment carrying the posterior rim of the acetabulum and the hip is unstable in flexion. The iliopectineal and ilioischial lines are intact.

An obturator-oblique Judet view shows fracture lines crossing both the iliopectineal and ilioischial lines, the obturator ring is disrupted, and on the iliac-oblique view there is a fracture extending into the iliac wing. No articular cartilage remains attached to the intact axial skeleton, and a 'spur sign' is seen on the obturator-oblique view.

A single fracture line divides the innominate bone into a superior (acetabular dome) and an inferior (ischiopubic) segment, breaking both the iliopectineal and ilioischial lines, with the obturator ring remaining intact and no spur sign.

An 80-year-old falls onto the side. CT shows the anterior column and quadrilateral plate displaced medially with a separate posterior hemitransverse component; the ilioischial line is broken but a large posterior segment of the articular surface remains in continuity with the intact ilium.

explanation: Judet-Letournel patterns are read off the AP and the two 45-degree Judet (obturator- and iliac-oblique) views plus CT. A posterior wall fracture commonly follows a dashboard/posterior dislocation and disrupts the rim without breaking the ilio-lines. A both-column fracture separates ALL articular cartilage from the intact axial skeleton ('floating acetabulum'), disrupts the obturator ring, and classically shows the spur sign on the obturator-oblique view. A transverse fracture breaks both ilio-lines but, critically, the obturator ring is intact and there is no spur sign (this distinguishes it from both-column and T-shaped patterns). The anterior column plus posterior hemitransverse pattern is the typical fragility-fracture pattern in older patients, with medialisation of the quadrilateral plate and anterior column.
learningPoint: Use the ilio-lines plus the obturator ring on Judet views: transverse breaks both lines with an INTACT obturator ring (no spur); both-column separates all cartilage from the axial skeleton with a disrupted ring and a spur sign.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Trauma: acetabular fractures (Judet-Letournel)
Letournel & Judet, Fractures of the Acetabulum

stem: A 64-year-old man has a 3-year history of progressive right groin pain that is worse on weight-bearing and limits his walking distance to half a mile. He has tried activity modification, weight loss, regular analgesia and a course of physiotherapy without lasting benefit. Examination shows an antalgic gait and painful, restricted internal rotation of the hip. Weight-bearing radiographs show joint-space narrowing, subchondral sclerosis and osteophytes consistent with osteoarthritis. According to current UK guidance, what is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Refer for consideration of total hip arthroplasty

options.label: B
options.text: Arrange an MRI of the hip before any further decision

options.label: C
options.text: Offer an intra-articular hyaluronic acid injection

options.label: D
options.text: Refer for arthroscopic debridement of the hip

options.label: E
options.text: Continue conservative management and review in 12 months

explanation: NICE NG226 frames the core (non-surgical) management of osteoarthritis as information/education, therapeutic exercise, weight loss where relevant and appropriate analgesia. Referral for joint replacement should be considered when symptoms (pain, stiffness, reduced function) substantially affect quality of life and have not responded to non-surgical treatment, and should not be delayed until there is prolonged or severe limitation. This patient has exhausted appropriate conservative measures, so referral for THA is correct. The diagnosis of hip OA is clinical and radiographic — MRI is not required. NICE explicitly advises 'do not offer intra-articular hyaluronan' for OA, and arthroscopy has no role in degenerate hip OA without a true mechanical block.
learningPoint: Refer for THA when hip OA refractory to core non-surgical care affects quality of life — do not wait for severe disability, and do not offer intra-articular hyaluronan or arthroscopy.
references: NICE NG226 — Osteoarthritis in over 16s: diagnosis and management (2022)
Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Adult Reconstruction: hip osteoarthritis

stem: A 79-year-old woman with osteoporosis is listed for an elective total hip arthroplasty for end-stage osteoarthritis. Her femoral canal is wide with thin cortices and a "stovepipe" (Dorr type C) morphology on the templating radiograph. According to current UK registry evidence and guidance, which femoral fixation strategy is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: A cemented femoral stem

options.label: B
options.text: An uncemented proximally porous-coated femoral stem

options.label: C
options.text: An uncemented fully hydroxyapatite-coated tapered stem

options.label: D
options.text: Hip resurfacing arthroplasty

options.label: E
options.text: An uncemented modular fluted tapered stem

explanation: In older patients — particularly elderly women with osteoporotic, capacious Dorr type C femora — cemented femoral fixation gives more reliable immediate fixation and a substantially lower risk of periprosthetic fracture than uncemented stems. National Joint Registry data and the Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) programme show fewer early periprosthetic fractures and good survivorship with cement in this group, and cemented or hybrid constructs are favoured in older patients with poor bone quality. Uncemented stems rely on press-fit/ingrowth into good-quality bone and carry a higher intra- and post-operative fracture risk in thin, wide cortices. Resurfacing is contraindicated in older osteoporotic women (poor femoral bone and metal-on-metal bearing concerns).
learningPoint: Elderly osteoporotic patient with a wide Dorr type C femur → cemented stem: lower periprosthetic fracture risk and reliable survivorship per the NJR/GIRFT.
references: National Joint Registry (NJR) annual report — implant survivorship and periprosthetic fracture by fixation and age
GIRFT Orthopaedics — cemented fixation in older patients
Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Adult Reconstruction: femoral fixation and Dorr classification

stem: A fit and active 55-year-old man with primary hip osteoarthritis is to undergo total hip arthroplasty. He has good bone stock and is a high-demand patient. Of the options below, which bearing couple has the lowest in-vivo volumetric wear while producing biologically inert debris and avoiding the soft-tissue and systemic risks associated with metal-on-metal articulation?

options.label: A
options.text: Ceramic-on-ceramic

options.label: B
options.text: Metal-on-metal

options.label: C
options.text: Metal-on-conventional (non-cross-linked) polyethylene

options.label: D
options.text: Metal-on-highly-cross-linked polyethylene

options.label: E
options.text: Ceramic-on-conventional (non-cross-linked) polyethylene

explanation: Modern hard-on-hard ceramic-on-ceramic bearings have the lowest in-vivo volumetric wear rates of the available couples and produce biologically inert wear debris, which is attractive in a young, high-demand patient with good bone stock. They avoid the adverse reaction to metal debris (ALVAL/pseudotumour) and systemic ion release associated with metal-on-metal articulation, which is no longer recommended for primary THA. Their drawbacks are cost, a small risk of ceramic fracture and audible squeaking. Ceramic-on-highly-cross-linked polyethylene (a very common contemporary choice) and metal-on-highly-cross-linked polyethylene are reasonable alternatives but generate more wear than ceramic-on-ceramic; conventional (non-cross-linked) polyethylene wears fastest and drives osteolysis, so options C and E are inferior. The stem specifically asks for the lowest wear with inert debris, which uniquely identifies ceramic-on-ceramic.
learningPoint: For lowest wear with inert debris in a young, active patient: ceramic-on-ceramic. Avoid metal-on-metal and conventional polyethylene; ceramic- or metal-on-highly-cross-linked polyethylene are acceptable second choices.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Adult Reconstruction: bearing surfaces and tribology
MHRA — Metal-on-metal hip implants: updated advice
NJR annual report — bearing survivorship by patient age

stem: A 60-year-old woman undergoes primary total hip arthroplasty through a direct anterior approach. In the early post-operative period she reports numbness and burning dysaesthesia over the anterolateral aspect of the thigh, but full motor power including normal knee extension. Which structure has most likely been injured?

options.label: A
options.text: Lateral femoral cutaneous nerve

options.label: B
options.text: Femoral nerve

options.label: C
options.text: Superior gluteal nerve

options.label: D
options.text: Sciatic nerve

options.label: E
options.text: Obturator nerve

explanation: The lateral femoral cutaneous nerve is the structure most at risk during the direct anterior (Hueter) approach, where the interval is developed lateral to sartorius; it is purely sensory and supplies the anterolateral thigh, so injury produces numbness/dysaesthesia (meralgia paraesthetica) with no motor deficit. The femoral nerve would cause weak knee extension and quadriceps weakness; the superior gluteal nerve (at risk with the anterolateral/Hardinge approach) supplies the abductors and produces a Trendelenburg gait; the sciatic nerve is most at risk in the posterior approach and would cause a foot drop and sensory loss below the knee; the obturator nerve supplies the adductors and the medial thigh.
learningPoint: Direct anterior approach → lateral femoral cutaneous nerve (purely sensory, anterolateral thigh). Posterior → sciatic; Hardinge → superior gluteal.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Adult Reconstruction / Anatomy: surgical approaches to the hip
Hoppenfeld — Surgical Exposures in Orthopaedics: the hip

stem: A 72-year-old man sustains a posterior dislocation of a primary total hip replacement that was implanted via a posterior approach 6 weeks ago. It is reduced closed and is stable on examination. Post-reduction radiographs show a well-fixed, well-sized acetabular component with inclination of 42° and anteversion of 15°, and the leg lengths and offset are restored. There is no evidence of impingement or component loosening. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Hip precautions with a period of abduction bracing, then mobilise

options.label: B
options.text: Revise the acetabular component to increase anteversion

options.label: C
options.text: Revise to a constrained acetabular liner

options.label: D
options.text: Convert to a dual-mobility bearing

options.label: E
options.text: Exchange the femoral head for a smaller diameter head

explanation: This is a first-time, early dislocation of a THA in which the components are well-fixed and well-orientated (the cup lies within the Lewinnek safe zone — inclination 40°±10°, anteversion 15°±10°) with restored offset/length and no impingement. The great majority of such early dislocations relate to soft-tissue healing/laxity and a transient deficit of the posterior soft-tissue envelope after a posterior approach; they are managed non-operatively with closed reduction, hip precautions and often a brace, and most do not recur. Revision surgery (cup re-orientation, dual-mobility or a constrained liner) is reserved for recurrent instability or where a clear, correctable cause (malposition, impingement, abductor or component failure) is identified. Reducing the head size would shorten the jump distance and increase instability.
learningPoint: First-time early THA dislocation with well-positioned, well-fixed components and no identifiable cause → treat conservatively; reserve revision for recurrent instability or a correctable cause.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Adult Reconstruction: instability after THA
Lewinnek et al. — acetabular component safe zone (concept)

stem: A 68-year-old man has deep aching pain in a total hip replacement that has never felt right since it was implanted 14 months ago. There is no sinus. CRP is mildly raised at 18 mg/L. A radiologically guided hip aspiration, performed after an appropriate antibiotic-free period, yields fluid with a synovial white-cell count of 5,200 cells/µL and 88% polymorphonuclear neutrophils; culture is awaited. Using the European Bone and Joint Infection Society (EBJIS) 2021 definition, how should this result be interpreted?

options.label: A
options.text: The synovial fluid findings confirm periprosthetic joint infection

options.label: B
options.text: Infection is excluded because there is no sinus and the CRP is only mildly raised

options.label: C
options.text: The result is non-diagnostic until two positive cultures are obtained

options.label: D
options.text: The values indicate aseptic loosening rather than infection

options.label: E
options.text: Histology of five intra-operative samples is required before infection can be diagnosed

explanation: Under the EBJIS 2021 definition, a synovial fluid leucocyte count of >3,000 cells/µL or a polymorphonuclear percentage of >80% is, on its own, a confirmatory criterion for periprosthetic joint infection. This aspirate (5,200 cells/µL, 88% PMN) exceeds both thresholds, so PJI is confirmed regardless of the culture result, the CRP value, or the absence of a sinus. (For reference, in the EBJIS three-tier system a count <1,500 cells/µL and PMN <65%, with no other positive feature, makes infection unlikely.) A sinus communicating with the prosthesis is also confirmatory when present, but its absence does not exclude infection. Serum CRP/ESR are screening tests, not exclusion tests. Cultures and histology add information but are not required once a confirmatory synovial-fluid threshold is met.
learningPoint: EBJIS 2021: synovial WBC >3,000/µL or PMN >80% confirms PJI on its own — a near-normal CRP or absent sinus does not exclude it.
references: McNally M et al. The EBJIS definition of periprosthetic joint infection. Bone Joint J 2021
Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Adult Reconstruction: prosthetic joint infection

stem: Four weeks after a primary total hip arthroplasty, a previously well patient develops a hot, swollen, painful hip with wound discharge and a fever. Inflammatory markers are markedly raised and aspiration confirms infection with Staphylococcus aureus. Radiographs show well-fixed, well-positioned components with no loosening or osteolysis. What is the most appropriate surgical management?

options.label: A
options.text: Debridement, antibiotics and implant retention (DAIR) with modular component (head and liner) exchange

options.label: B
options.text: One-stage (single-stage) exchange arthroplasty

options.label: C
options.text: Two-stage revision with an antibiotic-loaded cement spacer

options.label: D
options.text: Long-term suppressive antibiotics without surgery

options.label: E
options.text: Excision (Girdlestone) arthroplasty

explanation: This is an acute early post-operative infection (within roughly 4–6 weeks of implantation) with a stable, well-fixed prosthesis, short symptom duration and a known, treatable organism — the classic indication for DAIR. Thorough open debridement with exchange of the modular parts (femoral head and acetabular liner) to access and clean the joint, combined with organism-directed antibiotics, can eradicate infection while retaining the fixed components and gives a good functional outcome when performed promptly. Single- or two-stage revision is reserved for chronic infection, loose components, a sinus, or difficult/resistant organisms. Suppression alone is for patients unfit for or unwilling to have surgery, and excision arthroplasty is a salvage option.
learningPoint: Acute (early post-operative or haematogenous), short-duration PJI with well-fixed components → DAIR with modular head/liner exchange; chronic or loose components → staged revision.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Adult Reconstruction: management of prosthetic joint infection
EBJIS / BOA — treatment principles for acute versus chronic PJI

stem: A 58-year-old man underwent total hip arthroplasty for osteoarthritis secondary to a previous acetabular fracture. He has ankylosing spondylitis and developed extensive heterotopic ossification around the contralateral hip after its replacement. He is now being planned for the second hip. Which intervention is most effective at reducing the risk of clinically significant heterotopic ossification in this high-risk patient?

options.label: A
options.text: A peri-operative course of an NSAID such as indometacin

options.label: B
options.text: A course of oral bisphosphonate started post-operatively

options.label: C
options.text: Early aggressive passive hip mobilisation by physiotherapy

options.label: D
options.text: Prophylactic warfarin for six weeks

options.label: E
options.text: Post-operative immobilisation of the hip in a brace

explanation: Effective prophylaxis against heterotopic ossification has two evidence-based options: a peri-operative course of an NSAID (classically indometacin), which inhibits the prostaglandin-driven differentiation of mesenchymal cells towards osteoblasts, or a single fraction of low-dose radiotherapy given shortly before or after surgery. In high-risk patients (previous HO, ankylosing spondylitis, diffuse idiopathic skeletal hyperostosis, hypertrophic OA, extensive surgery for acetabular trauma) one of these should be used. Bisphosphonates only delay mineralisation and HO recurs once they are stopped, so they are not used for prophylaxis. Anticoagulants and bracing do not prevent HO, and forced passive mobilisation of an inflamed hip may promote ectopic bone.
learningPoint: HO prophylaxis in a high-risk hip = peri-operative NSAID (e.g. indometacin) or single-dose radiotherapy; bisphosphonates only delay mineralisation.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Adult Reconstruction: heterotopic ossification (Brooker classification)
Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Basic Science: ectopic ossification and the prostaglandin pathway

subtopic: Revision THA — bone loss and reconstruction
stem: Theme: reconstruction strategies in revision total hip arthroplasty. For each scenario, select the single most appropriate reconstruction option from the list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Cemented acetabular component with impaction bone grafting

options.label: B
options.text: Hemispherical uncemented (porous) acetabular shell with supplementary screws

options.label: C
options.text: Custom triflange / cup-cage construct

options.label: D
options.text: Extensively porous-coated or modular fluted tapered (distally fixing) femoral stem

options.label: E
options.text: Proximal femoral replacement (megaprosthesis)

options.label: F
options.text: Dual-mobility acetabular bearing

options.label: G
options.text: Girdlestone excision arthroplasty

options.label: H
options.text: Tantalum (trabecular metal) augment with a hemispherical shell

emiLeads.stem: At acetabular revision there is a contained cavitary defect but the acetabular rim and both columns are intact, giving more than 50% host-bone contact and good initial stability of a press-fit shell.

There is complete loss of acetabular bone stock with pelvic discontinuity, so that the superior and inferior halves of the pelvis move independently and a hemispherical shell cannot gain stable fixation.
At femoral revision the proximal femur is deficient and ectatic but there is at least 4 cm of intact diaphyseal bone available distally for fixation.
A segmental, uncontained superolateral acetabular rim defect leaves a stable medial wall, and the gap needs to be filled to restore the hemisphere and support a press-fit cup.
Revision is performed for recurrent dislocation in an elderly patient with deficient abductors but well-fixed, well-positioned components and no significant bone loss.

explanation: Acetabular reconstruction is driven by the remaining host bone (Paprosky classification). A contained cavitary defect with a supportable rim and >50% host contact is reliably managed with a hemispherical porous shell and screws (B). A segmental but uncontained rim defect with an intact medial wall is restored with a modular porous-metal (tantalum) augment plus a hemispherical shell (H). Pelvic discontinuity, where the columns are disconnected, requires a construct that bridges the two halves — a cup-cage or custom triflange (C). On the femoral side, distal diaphyseal fixation with an extensively porous-coated or modular fluted tapered stem (D) bypasses a deficient proximal femur when adequate diaphysis remains; a proximal femoral replacement (E) is reserved for catastrophic proximal bone loss with no usable diaphysis. Recurrent instability with deficient abductors but preserved bone stock is an ideal indication for a dual-mobility bearing (F).
learningPoint: Match revision reconstruction to host bone: contained cavitary → porous shell + screws; segmental rim → augment; pelvic discontinuity → cup-cage/triflange; deficient proximal femur with diaphysis → distal-fixation stem; instability with poor abductors → dual mobility.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Adult Reconstruction: revision THA and the Paprosky classification
BOA / British Hip Society — revision hip arthroplasty principles

stem: A 27-year-old recreational footballer has a 12-month history of activity-related groin pain and a positive anterior impingement test (pain on flexion, adduction and internal rotation). Plain radiographs show an aspherical femoral head-neck junction with an alpha angle of 68 degrees and a crossover sign; the joint space is preserved with no arthritic change. He has failed activity modification and a structured physiotherapy programme. What is the most appropriate next management step?

options.label: A
options.text: Arthroscopic femoral osteochondroplasty and acetabular rim trimming with labral repair

options.label: B
options.text: Periacetabular osteotomy

options.label: C
options.text: Total hip arthroplasty

options.label: D
options.text: Intra-articular corticosteroid injection and continued observation

options.label: E
options.text: Surgical hip dislocation with proximal femoral derotation osteotomy

explanation: This is mixed (cam plus pincer) femoroacetabular impingement: the raised alpha angle (>55-60 degrees) indicates a cam lesion and the crossover sign indicates focal acetabular retroversion/over-coverage (pincer). In a young patient with preserved joint space who has failed conservative care, the joint-preserving procedure that addresses the underlying morphology is arthroscopic (or open) femoral osteochondroplasty with rim recession and labral preservation/repair. The UK FASHIoN RCT (Griffin et al., Lancet 2018) showed arthroscopic hip surgery gave a greater improvement in hip-related quality of life (iHOT-33) than best conservative/personalised hip therapy at 12 months. PAO treats acetabular under-coverage (dysplasia), not over-coverage. THA is for established arthritis. An injection is diagnostic/temporising, not definitive, after failed rehab.
learningPoint: Symptomatic FAI failing rehab with preserved joint space is treated by correcting the morphology (osteochondroplasty/rim trim) with labral preservation, not by arthroplasty.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Adult Reconstruction / Sports: femoroacetabular impingement
Griffin DR et al. UK FASHIoN trial, Lancet 2018
BOA / British Hip Society guidance on hip preservation

stem: A 24-year-old woman has lateral and groin hip pain worse on prolonged standing. A weight-bearing AP pelvis radiograph shows a lateral centre-edge angle (of Wiberg) of 18 degrees, an increased acetabular index (Tonnis angle) and a break in Shenton's line. The joint is congruent and concentrically reducible with no established arthritis. What is the most appropriate definitive operative treatment?

options.label: A
options.text: Bernese (Ganz) periacetabular osteotomy

options.label: B
options.text: Arthroscopic labral repair alone

options.label: C
options.text: Femoral head-neck osteochondroplasty

options.label: D
options.text: Total hip arthroplasty

options.label: E
options.text: Valgus proximal femoral osteotomy

explanation: A lateral centre-edge angle below 20-25 degrees with an increased acetabular index and a broken Shenton's line indicates acetabular dysplasia (under-coverage). In a young adult with a congruent, concentric joint and no established osteoarthritis, the procedure that reorients the acetabulum to restore femoral head coverage and offload the rim/labrum is the Bernese (Ganz) periacetabular osteotomy. Isolated labral repair or arthroscopy does not correct the bony under-coverage that drives the labral overload and tends to fail. Femoral osteochondroplasty addresses impingement (over-coverage/cam), the opposite problem. THA is reserved for established arthritis. A valgus femoral osteotomy worsens lateral under-coverage.
learningPoint: Symptomatic acetabular dysplasia (low CEA, broken Shenton's line) in a young adult without arthritis is treated by reorienting the socket with a periacetabular osteotomy, not by arthroscopy alone.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Adult Reconstruction: hip dysplasia
Ganz R et al. The Bernese periacetabular osteotomy, Clin Orthop Relat Res 1988
BOA / British Hip Society hip preservation principles

stem: A 45-year-old man with a history of high alcohol intake has 3 months of groin pain. An AP radiograph of the hip demonstrates a subchondral radiolucent line beneath the anterolateral femoral head (the 'crescent sign'). The overall sphericity of the femoral head is maintained and the joint space is preserved, with no acetabular involvement. Using the classic Ficat-Arlet classification, which stage does this represent?

options.label: A
options.text: Stage III

options.label: B
options.text: Stage I

options.label: C
options.text: Stage IIA

options.label: D
options.text: Stage IIB

options.label: E
options.text: Stage IV

explanation: In the classic Ficat-Arlet system the crescent sign (subchondral fracture) defines Stage III: there is established subchondral collapse but the femoral head still retains its overall sphericity and the joint space is preserved. Stage I has normal radiographs (changes only on MRI/biopsy). Stage II shows sclerosis and/or cystic change in the pre-collapse head without a subchondral fracture. Stage IV is the end stage with loss of sphericity (frank head flattening/collapse), joint-space narrowing, osteophytes and acetabular (degenerative) involvement. Recognising the crescent sign matters because it marks the point at which the head is mechanically failing and head-preserving surgery becomes much less reliable.
learningPoint: In the Ficat-Arlet classification the crescent sign (subchondral fracture with a still-spherical head and preserved joint space) is Stage III and signals imminent collapse; Stage IV is loss of sphericity with secondary arthritis.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Adult Reconstruction: osteonecrosis of the femoral head
Ficat RP, Arlet J — staging of femoral head osteonecrosis
Choi HR et al. Osteonecrosis of the femoral head: diagnosis and classification systems (review)

stem: A 52-year-old man with steroid-induced osteonecrosis has worsening pain and a limp. Radiographs show flattening and collapse of the femoral head with joint-space narrowing and small osteophytes (Ficat-Arlet stage IV). He is otherwise fit with high functional demands. What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Total hip arthroplasty

options.label: B
options.text: Core decompression with bone grafting

options.label: C
options.text: Free vascularised fibular graft

options.label: D
options.text: Rotational (transtrochanteric) femoral osteotomy

options.label: E
options.text: Protected weight-bearing and bisphosphonate therapy

explanation: Once the femoral head has collapsed with secondary arthritic change of the joint (Ficat stage III-IV), head-preserving options no longer work reliably because the articular surface and acetabular cartilage are already damaged. Total hip arthroplasty is the procedure of choice and gives reliable pain relief and function. Core decompression and vascularised grafts are pre-collapse (Ficat I-II) procedures aimed at preserving the native head. Rotational osteotomy is occasionally used in selected younger patients with a small, well-localised necrotic segment and an intact area that can be rotated into the weight-bearing zone, but is not appropriate once there is established joint arthrosis. Bisphosphonates/protected weight-bearing do not reverse established collapse.
learningPoint: Femoral head collapse with secondary arthritis (Ficat III-IV) is treated with total hip arthroplasty; head-preserving surgery is for the pre-collapse hip.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Adult Reconstruction: osteonecrosis treatment by stage
Mont MA et al. — management of osteonecrosis of the femoral head

stem: A 34-year-old woman in the third trimester of pregnancy develops progressively disabling left hip and groin pain over 4 weeks with an antalgic gait, but no preceding trauma and no fever. Plain radiographs show diffuse osteopenia of the femoral head and neck with a preserved joint space and no subchondral crescent. MRI shows extensive, homogeneous bone-marrow oedema throughout the femoral head and neck with a joint effusion but no focal subchondral 'double-line' sign or necrotic segment. What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Protected weight-bearing, analgesia and reassurance, with planned interval review as it is self-limiting

options.label: B
options.text: Core decompression of the femoral head

options.label: C
options.text: Urgent hip arthroscopy and synovial biopsy

options.label: D
options.text: Total hip arthroplasty

options.label: E
options.text: Long-term high-dose oral corticosteroids

explanation: The picture (third-trimester pregnancy, atraumatic, diffuse marrow oedema of the head AND neck on MRI without a focal necrotic/double-line lesion, and a preserved joint with no crescent) is transient osteoporosis of the hip / bone-marrow oedema syndrome. It is self-limiting, typically resolving over 6-12 months. Management is supportive: protected weight-bearing to reduce the risk of insufficiency fracture, analgesia and reassurance. The key distinction from osteonecrosis is that the oedema is diffuse and reversible with no focal subchondral necrotic segment; core decompression is a treatment for osteonecrosis and is not first-line here (it is occasionally used to shorten symptoms but is not standard). Arthroplasty and steroids are inappropriate; corticosteroids could even precipitate osteonecrosis.
learningPoint: Atraumatic diffuse femoral head/neck marrow oedema without a focal necrotic segment in late pregnancy is transient osteoporosis of the hip — self-limiting; treat with protected weight-bearing and analgesia.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Adult Reconstruction: transient osteoporosis / bone marrow oedema syndrome

stem: An asymptomatic 60-year-old man attends routine follow-up 6 years after a large-diameter (>=36 mm) metal-on-metal total hip replacement. He has no pain and a well-functioning hip. According to current UK MHRA surveillance guidance, which statement best describes appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Annual clinical review for the life of the implant, with blood cobalt/chromium measurement and cross-sectional imaging (MARS MRI or ultrasound) if he becomes symptomatic, his Oxford Hip Score deteriorates, or ion levels are raised or rising

options.label: B
options.text: Discharge him from follow-up because he is asymptomatic

options.label: C
options.text: List him for revision arthroplasty on the basis of the bearing alone

options.label: D
options.text: Measure blood ions only if a plain radiograph shows osteolysis

options.label: E
options.text: Routine annual review is unnecessary; review only every 10 years as for other bearings

explanation: MHRA guidance singles out large-head (>=36 mm) stemmed metal-on-metal total hips (and recalled designs such as the DePuy ASR) as higher risk for adverse reaction to metal debris and recommends annual surveillance for the life of the implant. Asymptomatic patients are still followed because adverse soft-tissue reactions and rising ions can precede symptoms. Investigation is escalated (blood cobalt and chromium plus MARS MRI or ultrasound) when the patient becomes symptomatic, when the Oxford Hip Score deteriorates, or when ion levels are elevated (the MHRA uses 7 ppb, ~119 nmol/L) or rising. Imaging findings (effusion, pseudotumour, soft-tissue/bone destruction) carry more weight than isolated ion levels. The bearing alone is not an indication for revision: MHRA states there is no threshold value that mandates revision. Small-head MoM and metal-on-polyethylene hips are surveyed as for any conventional arthroplasty.
learningPoint: Large-head (>=36 mm) MoM total hips need lifelong annual surveillance per MHRA; investigate with ions plus MARS MRI/ultrasound if symptomatic, OHS deteriorates, or ions are raised/rising (7 ppb), and revise on the whole picture, not the bearing alone.
references: MHRA Medical Device Alert / guidance on all metal-on-metal hip replacements — updated follow-up advice (2017)
Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Adult Reconstruction: bearing surfaces and MoM
NJR (National Joint Registry) outcomes for MoM bearings

stem: Theme: clinical tests in examination of the painful hip. For each described provocation/clinical test, select the single structure or condition it is designed to assess. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Anterior femoroacetabular impingement / anterosuperior labrum

options.label: B
options.text: Hip abductor (gluteus medius/minimus) function

options.label: C
options.text: Fixed flexion deformity of the hip

options.label: D
options.text: Iliotibial band / tensor fascia lata tightness

options.label: E
options.text: Posterior wall / posterior labral pathology

options.label: F
options.text: Sacroiliac joint

options.label: G
options.text: Rectus femoris (two-joint hip flexor) tightness

options.label: H
options.text: Leg-length discrepancy

emiLeads.stem: With the patient supine, the examiner passively flexes the hip and knee, then adducts and internally rotates the hip; reproduction of the patient's groin pain is a positive test.

The patient stands and lifts one foot off the ground, balancing on the contralateral (stance) leg; the pelvis on the side of the lifted leg drops rather than rises, indicating weakness on the stance-side hip.

The patient lies supine and maximally flexes the opposite hip to flatten the lumbar lordosis; the hip under examination then rises off the couch into flexion and cannot be fully extended, with the residual angle measured.

The patient lies in the lateral decubitus position; the examiner abducts and extends the uppermost hip with the knee flexed, then releases it, and the thigh remains abducted instead of falling to the table.
With the patient prone, the knee is passively flexed; the ipsilateral buttock/hip rises off the couch as the hip is drawn into flexion, indicating tightness of a two-joint anterior thigh muscle.

explanation: These are core hip examination manoeuvres. (1) Flexion-adduction-internal rotation reproducing groin pain is the anterior impingement (FADIR) test for anterosuperior labral/cam impingement. (2) The single-leg stance (Trendelenburg) test assesses abductor function: the pelvis dipping on the side of the lifted leg means the stance-leg abductors are weak. (3) The Thomas test unmasks a fixed flexion deformity of the hip by abolishing compensatory lumbar lordosis. (4) The Ober test assesses iliotibial band/TFL tightness — a tight band keeps the thigh abducted. (5) The Ely test (prone knee flexion lifting the buttock) demonstrates rectus femoris (a two-joint hip flexor) tightness. Unused distractors include posterior labral tests, sacroiliac joint provocation and leg-length assessment, which use different manoeuvres.
learningPoint: Know the named hip tests and exactly what each isolates: FADIR (impingement/labrum), Trendelenburg (abductors), Thomas (fixed flexion), Ober (ITB/TFL), Ely (rectus femoris).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Adult Reconstruction: clinical evaluation of the hip
BOA / British Hip Society clinical examination standards

stem: A 30-year-old keen runner has mechanical groin pain and clicking with a positive impingement test. Plain AP pelvis and lateral radiographs are normal, with a normal alpha angle and centre-edge angle. He has no red-flag features. You suspect an acetabular labral tear. Which is the most appropriate next imaging investigation to confirm the diagnosis?

options.label: A
options.text: Magnetic resonance arthrography of the hip

options.label: B
options.text: Technetium-99m bone scan

options.label: C
options.text: CT of the pelvis with three-dimensional reconstruction

options.label: D
options.text: Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA)

options.label: E
options.text: Plain ultrasound of the hip

explanation: For a suspected acetabular labral tear with normal radiographs, magnetic resonance arthrography (intra-articular gadolinium) is the most sensitive and specific test: the contrast distends the joint and tracks into a labral tear or paralabral cyst. It also allows an intra-articular local anaesthetic to be given, so relief of pain helps confirm an intra-articular source. A standard (non-arthrographic) MRI is less sensitive for subtle labral tears, particularly on lower field strengths. A bone scan is non-specific. CT is excellent for bony morphology (cam/pincer, version) but poor for the labrum and cartilage and adds radiation in a young patient. DEXA assesses bone density, not the labrum. Ultrasound cannot reliably visualise the intra-articular labrum.
learningPoint: Suspected labral tear with normal radiographs: MR arthrography is the investigation of choice, with the option of a diagnostic intra-articular anaesthetic.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Adult Reconstruction / Sports: hip imaging and labral pathology
BOA / British Hip Society imaging guidance for the young adult hip

stem: A 58-year-old man has a 2-year history of medial-sided right knee pain. Radiographs show isolated medial compartment joint-space narrowing with a 6° varus mechanical axis. He has a stable knee, a full range of movement, a BMI of 29 and has not yet tried any structured non-operative treatment. Which is the most appropriate first-line management?

options.label: A
options.text: A supervised exercise and weight-management programme with analgesia

options.label: B
options.text: Arthroscopic washout and debridement

options.label: C
options.text: Medial unicompartmental knee arthroplasty

options.label: D
options.text: Opening-wedge high tibial osteotomy

options.label: E
options.text: Intra-articular hyaluronic acid injection

explanation: NICE NG226 makes therapeutic exercise (a supervised, tailored programme) the core first-line treatment for osteoarthritis, offered to everyone irrespective of age, weight or severity, alongside weight loss where relevant and appropriate analgesia. Surgery (osteotomy or arthroplasty) is considered only after non-surgical measures have failed to control symptoms and quality of life. NICE NG226 explicitly recommends against arthroscopic lavage/debridement for osteoarthritis (no exception for mechanical symptoms) and against intra-articular hyaluronan, which gives only short-lived relief.
learningPoint: Knee OA: optimise supervised therapeutic exercise, weight and analgesia before any surgical referral; under NICE NG226 neither arthroscopic washout nor intra-articular hyaluronan is indicated for OA.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Adult Reconstruction: knee osteoarthritis

stem: A 44-year-old manual labourer has activity-related medial knee pain with isolated medial compartment osteoarthritis and a correctable varus deformity. He has full extension, flexion to 130°, intact ligaments and no patellofemoral or lateral compartment symptoms, and non-operative treatment has failed. Which patient factor most strongly favours a high tibial osteotomy over a medial unicompartmental knee arthroplasty?

options.label: A
options.text: His young age combined with heavy manual occupation

options.label: B
options.text: A fixed-flexion deformity of 15°

options.label: C
options.text: A body mass index of 36

options.label: D
options.text: Tricompartmental radiographic changes

options.label: E
options.text: An incompetent anterior cruciate ligament with instability

explanation: A valgus-producing HTO offloads the arthritic medial compartment while preserving the native joint, making it well suited to the young, high-demand patient who wishes to remain in heavy manual or impact work, where an arthroplasty would carry a higher risk of early wear/loosening and would impose activity restriction. A large fixed-flexion deformity, marked obesity, tricompartmental disease and instability are all relative contraindications to HTO. (HTO can be combined with ACL reconstruction for the unstable varus knee, but instability itself does not favour osteotomy over UKA.)
learningPoint: HTO suits the young, active, heavy-demand patient with isolated medial OA, correctable varus, good motion and a stable knee; obesity, stiffness and multicompartment disease push away from it.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Adult Reconstruction: high tibial osteotomy
BASK / BOA guidance on osteotomy around the knee

stem: A 67-year-old woman with anteromedial osteoarthritis is being considered for a mobile-bearing (Oxford-type) medial unicompartmental knee arthroplasty. The lateral compartment cartilage is preserved at full thickness. Which single finding is the strongest contraindication to this procedure?

options.label: A
options.text: A correctable 8° varus deformity

options.label: B
options.text: A body mass index of 31

options.label: C
options.text: Anterior cruciate ligament rupture with rotational instability

options.label: D
options.text: Partial-thickness chondral wear behind the patella

options.label: E
options.text: Asymptomatic patellofemoral osteophytes

explanation: A functioning ACL is fundamental to the Oxford-type mobile-bearing UKA: it provides the anteroposterior stability that keeps the bearing well-tracked and protects the lateral compartment, so ACL deficiency with instability is a classic contraindication, predisposing to bearing dislocation and lateral compartment progression. A correctable varus, a modestly raised BMI, mild patellofemoral chondral wear and asymptomatic patellofemoral osteophytes are not contraindications in modern practice. (Full-thickness lateral compartment loss is also a contraindication, but the lateral cartilage is intact in this knee, which is otherwise well suited to UKA.)
learningPoint: Mobile-bearing UKA depends on an intact ACL; instability from ACL deficiency is a key contraindication, along with full-thickness disease in the other compartment.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Adult Reconstruction: unicompartmental knee arthroplasty

stem: During a cruciate-retaining total knee arthroplasty using a measured-resection technique, the trial components show a flexion gap that is appropriately tight but an extension gap that is too tight, so the knee cannot reach full extension. Both gaps are symmetric medially and laterally and posterior osteophytes have already been removed. Which is the most appropriate corrective step?

options.label: A
options.text: Resect additional bone from the distal femur

options.label: B
options.text: Resect additional bone from the proximal tibia

options.label: C
options.text: Increase the tibial polyethylene insert thickness

options.label: D
options.text: Upsize to a larger femoral component

options.label: E
options.text: Release the posterior cruciate ligament

explanation: A symmetric gap mismatch where extension is tight but flexion is satisfactory isolates the problem to the extension gap. Distal femoral resection raises (proximalises) the joint line and enlarges the extension gap without affecting the flexion gap, restoring full extension. Resecting more tibia loosens both gaps equally and would leave the knee unstable in flexion; thickening the insert tightens both gaps and worsens the flexion contracture. Posterior capsular contracture and osteophytes should be addressed first, but with those already cleared the gap-specific manoeuvre is distal femoral resection.
learningPoint: Tight extension gap with a satisfactory flexion gap → resect more distal femur; tibial resection changes both gaps equally.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Adult Reconstruction: TKA gap balancing
BASK — Best practice in primary knee arthroplasty

stem: A 70-year-old man develops a hot, swollen, painful knee 10 days after a primary total knee arthroplasty. The wound is healing without a sinus, components are well fixed, and he is systemically well. Joint aspiration confirms infection with a single antibiotic-sensitive organism. Which is the most appropriate surgical management?

options.label: A
options.text: Debridement, antibiotics and implant retention with exchange of modular parts

options.label: B
options.text: Single-stage revision with all components exchanged

options.label: C
options.text: Two-stage revision with an antibiotic spacer

options.label: D
options.text: Arthroscopic washout and a course of oral antibiotics

options.label: E
options.text: Long-term suppressive antibiotics with no surgery

explanation: This is an early acute (post-operative) periprosthetic joint infection: short symptom duration (well within ~4 weeks of implantation), well-fixed components, no sinus and a single sensitive organism. The appropriate strategy is prompt open debridement, antibiotics and implant retention (DAIR) with exchange of the modular parts (polyethylene insert) and thorough synovectomy/lavage, followed by targeted antibiotics. One- or two-stage revision is reserved for chronic infection, loose components, sinus tracts or failed/late presentations. Arthroscopic washout is inadequate (it cannot exchange the liner or fully debride), and suppression alone will not clear an acute infection.
learningPoint: Acute early PJI with well-fixed components and a sensitive organism → prompt DAIR with modular-part (liner) exchange; chronic infection → staged revision.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Adult Reconstruction: periprosthetic joint infection
BOA / BASK guidance on prosthetic joint infection; MSIS criteria

subtopic: Revision TKA — bone loss and constraint
stem: A 74-year-old woman is undergoing revision total knee arthroplasty for aseptic loosening. Intra-operatively the medial collateral ligament is found to be incompetent and cannot be reconstructed, producing global coronal instability, and there is contained metaphyseal bone loss managed with metaphyseal cones and stems. Which implant constraint is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: A rotating-hinge prosthesis

options.label: B
options.text: A cruciate-retaining implant

options.label: C
options.text: A posterior-stabilised (cruciate-substituting) implant

options.label: D
options.text: A varus–valgus constrained (condylar constrained) implant

options.label: E
options.text: A mobile-bearing unconstrained implant

explanation: Constraint should be the minimum required to restore stability. A condylar (varus–valgus) constrained implant relies on a competent collateral sleeve to control coronal stability — it tolerates mild laxity but not a globally deficient or irreparable collateral. With a truly incompetent, non-reconstructable MCL and resultant global instability, a rotating-hinge prosthesis is required because it provides the coronal and sagittal stability the soft tissues can no longer supply. CR, PS and unconstrained implants all depend on intact collaterals and would be unstable here.
learningPoint: Match constraint to the soft-tissue deficit: deficient/irreparable collateral with global instability → rotating hinge; isolated mild coronal laxity with competent collaterals → condylar constrained.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Adult Reconstruction: revision TKA and the constraint ladder
BASK revision knee principles; AORI bone-loss classification

stem: A 35-year-old man feels a sudden pop in the front of his knee while jumping. He cannot perform a straight-leg raise, there is a palpable gap below the patella, and the patella sits high (patella alta) on the lateral radiograph. Which is the most appropriate definitive management?

options.label: A
options.text: Prompt primary surgical repair of the patellar tendon

options.label: B
options.text: A cylinder cast in extension for 6 weeks

options.label: C
options.text: A hinged knee brace with early range-of-motion exercises

options.label: D
options.text: Physiotherapy and quadriceps strengthening only

options.label: E
options.text: Patellectomy and extensor mechanism reconstruction

explanation: An acute, complete patellar tendon rupture — loss of active extension/straight-leg raise, a palpable infrapatellar gap and patella alta — disrupts the extensor mechanism and requires prompt primary surgical repair (transosseous sutures or suture anchors, protected with a tension band or cerclage as needed) to restore continuity and patellar height. Non-operative treatment is reserved for partial tears with an intact extensor mechanism and preserved active extension. Patellectomy is not appropriate for a repairable acute tendon rupture.
learningPoint: Complete extensor mechanism disruption (no straight-leg raise + palpable gap + patella alta) → prompt primary repair; only partial tears with preserved extension are treated non-operatively.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Sports Medicine / Knee: extensor mechanism injuries
BOAST principles for acute soft-tissue knee injury referral

stem: Theme: anterior knee and patellofemoral problems. For each clinical scenario, select the single most appropriate management option from the list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Physiotherapy targeting vastus medialis and core/hip control

options.label: B
options.text: Medial patellofemoral ligament (MPFL) reconstruction

options.label: C
options.text: Tibial tubercle distalisation (with or without medialisation)

options.label: D
options.text: Lateral retinacular release in isolation

options.label: E
options.text: Primary surgical repair of the quadriceps tendon

options.label: F
options.text: Arthroscopic lateral release plus extensive medial reefing

options.label: G
options.text: Reassurance and activity modification

options.label: H
options.text: Revision of a malrotated total knee arthroplasty

emiLeads.stem: A 19-year-old woman has had four traumatic lateral patellar dislocations. Imaging shows a tear of the medial soft-tissue restraint, a normal tibial-tubercle-to-trochlear-groove (TT–TG) distance and no significant trochlear dysplasia or patella alta.
A 22-year-old with recurrent patellar instability has marked patella alta and a TT–TG distance of 22 mm, with maltracking driven by the high, lateralised tubercle position.
A 58-year-old man could not actively extend his knee after a fall; there is a palpable gap above the patella, the patella sits low (patella baja), and he cannot hold a straight-leg raise.
A 16-year-old has bilateral anterior knee pain that is worse on stairs and after prolonged sitting, with no instability, effusion or mechanical symptoms and normal radiographs.
A patient has persistent anterior knee pain and maltracking after a total knee arthroplasty; CT shows combined internal rotation of the femoral and tibial components as the cause.

explanation: Recurrent patellar instability with a torn medial restraint but normal bony parameters (normal TT–TG, no significant dysplasia/alta) is best treated by MPFL reconstruction. When instability is driven by bony malalignment — patella alta with an elevated TT–TG (typically >20 mm) — a tibial tubercle osteotomy (distalisation ± medialisation) addresses the underlying cause. A complete quadriceps tendon rupture (no active extension, suprapatellar gap, patella baja) needs prompt primary repair. Adolescent anterior knee pain (patellofemoral pain syndrome) without instability or mechanical symptoms is managed with targeted physiotherapy (VMO, hip and core control). Anterior knee pain after TKA from combined internal rotation of the components is a malrotation problem corrected by revising the malrotated components; isolated lateral release and aggressive medial reefing are now largely historical and do not address these underlying causes.
learningPoint: Treat patellar instability by its cause: soft-tissue (normal bony parameters) → MPFL reconstruction; bony maltracking (alta / high TT–TG) → tubercle osteotomy; component malrotation after TKA → revise the components.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Knee: patellofemoral instability and extensor mechanism
BASK / BOA guidance on patellofemoral disorders

subtopic: Total knee arthroplasty complications and instability
stem: Theme: the painful or unstable total knee arthroplasty. For each post-operative presentation, select the single most likely diagnosis or mechanism from the list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Flexion instability (oversized flexion gap)

options.label: B
options.text: Patellar clunk syndrome

options.label: C
options.text: Aseptic loosening

options.label: D
options.text: Chronic periprosthetic joint infection

options.label: E
options.text: Component malrotation (combined internal rotation)

options.label: F
options.text: Arthrofibrosis (stiffness)

options.label: G
options.text: Extensor mechanism (patellar tendon) rupture

options.label: H
options.text: Periprosthetic fracture

emiLeads.stem: Eighteen months after a posterior-stabilised TKA, a patient reports recurrent effusions, a sense of giving way going down stairs, and difficulty on slopes. Radiographs show well-fixed, well-aligned components, and examination reveals increased anteroposterior laxity at 90° of flexion with a stable knee in extension.

A patient with a posterior-stabilised TKA describes a painful clunk as the knee actively extends from deep flexion to around 30–40°, caused by a fibrous nodule at the proximal pole of the patella engaging the intercondylar box.

Three years post-TKA a patient has progressive activity-related pain and new-onset effusions. Inflammatory markers are normal and aspiration is sterile with a low cell count, but serial radiographs show progressive radiolucent lines and a change in component position.
Two years post-TKA a patient has persistent pain, recurrent effusions, a chronic discharging sinus and raised CRP/ESR, with aspiration showing a high neutrophil percentage and positive cultures.

A patient has persistent anterior knee pain, an effusion and patellar maltracking after TKA; CT scan shows the femoral and tibial components are both internally rotated relative to their reference axes.

explanation: Flexion instability after a PS knee gives AP laxity at 90° with a stable, well-aligned, well-fixed knee and symptoms of giving way on stairs/slopes — from an oversized flexion gap. Patellar clunk syndrome is a fibrous suprapatellar nodule that catches in the intercondylar box of a PS femoral component, producing a painful clunk on extension from deep flexion. Aseptic loosening shows progressive radiolucencies/component migration with normal inflammatory markers and a sterile aspirate. Chronic PJI is the picture with a sinus, raised CRP/ESR, a high synovial neutrophil percentage and positive cultures — distinguishing it from aseptic loosening. Combined internal rotation of the components (malrotation) causes patellar maltracking and anterior knee pain, confirmed on CT (e.g. by the Berger protocol).
learningPoint: Localise the failing TKA by the data: AP laxity at 90° = flexion instability; clunk on extension = patellar clunk; migrating radiolucencies with normal bloods = aseptic loosening; sinus + raised markers + positive aspirate = chronic infection; internal component rotation on CT = malrotation.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Adult Reconstruction: the painful TKA and modes of failure
National Joint Registry (NJR) — reasons for knee revision; BASK revision knee guidance

stem: A 26-year-old motorcyclist sustained a direct blow to the front of the flexed tibia in a low-speed fall. He has a moderate effusion and a posterior sag with an isolated grade II posterior drawer (about 8 mm of increased posterior translation), correcting fully at 90 degrees. MRI confirms an isolated PCL injury with intact menisci, collaterals and posterolateral corner. What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.label: A
options.text: Quadriceps-focused rehabilitation with a dynamic PCL brace and protected weightbearing

options.label: B
options.text: Arthroscopic single-bundle PCL reconstruction within two weeks

options.label: C
options.text: Open primary repair of the PCL with suture anchors

options.label: D
options.text: Hinged knee brace locked in 90 degrees of flexion for six weeks

options.label: E
options.text: Hamstring-strengthening programme with early unrestricted return to sport

explanation: Isolated grade I-II PCL injuries (up to about 10 mm of increased posterior translation, reducible at 90 degrees) are managed non-operatively. The cornerstone is quadriceps strengthening, because dynamic quadriceps action draws the tibia forward and resists posterior sag, combined with a dynamic anterior-drawer (PCL) brace and protected weightbearing in relative extension to off-load the healing ligament. Bracing locked in 90 degrees of flexion is wrong because gravity and the flexed position worsen posterior sag and lengthen the healing PCL. Reconstruction is reserved for high-grade (grade III) injuries, combined ligament injuries or persistent symptomatic instability after rehabilitation; isolated hamstring loading is counterproductive because the hamstrings are PCL antagonists.
learningPoint: Isolated grade I-II PCL injury: quadriceps rehabilitation in relative extension and a dynamic PCL brace, not flexion bracing or early reconstruction.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Sports Medicine: PCL and posterior knee injuries
BASK consensus on knee ligament injury management

stem: A 30-year-old has a varus, hyperextension injury to the knee. Examination shows a varus thrust on walking, increased external rotation of the tibia at 30 but not 90 degrees (asymmetric dial test), a positive posterolateral drawer and an associated complete PCL rupture. There is no common peroneal nerve deficit and the limb is well perfused. Imaging confirms a complete posterolateral corner injury. He presents 10 days after injury. What is the most appropriate definitive surgical strategy?

options.label: A
options.text: Anatomical reconstruction of the posterolateral corner together with reconstruction of the cruciate within about three weeks of injury

options.label: B
options.text: Isolated PCL reconstruction now, leaving the posterolateral corner to heal in a brace

options.label: C
options.text: Long-leg cast in full extension for six weeks then reassess

options.label: D
options.text: Lateral closing-wedge high tibial osteotomy as the primary procedure

options.label: E
options.text: Delay all surgery for six months and reconstruct only if symptomatic instability persists

explanation: An increase in external rotation at 30 degrees that disappears at 90 degrees localises the injury to the posterolateral corner; a varus thrust and posterolateral drawer with a torn PCL indicate a combined high-grade injury. Combined cruciate and PLC injuries do poorly if the corner is left untreated, and an unaddressed PLC is a leading cause of cruciate graft failure through residual rotational and varus laxity. Acute high-grade (grade III) PLC injuries are best managed by anatomical reconstruction of the key structures (popliteus, popliteofibular ligament and lateral collateral ligament), ideally within roughly three weeks while tissue planes are still definable, performed together with cruciate reconstruction. Casting risks stiffness and does not restore stability. Osteotomy is reserved for chronic PLC laxity with varus malalignment, where it is staged before or with ligament reconstruction, not as the primary acute procedure.
learningPoint: Combined PCL and posterolateral corner injuries need the corner addressed (repair or reconstruction) early and concurrently; a missed PLC is a classic cause of cruciate graft failure.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Sports Medicine: posterolateral corner injuries
BASK / BOA knee ligament injury guidance

stem: A 19-year-old rugby player sustained a valgus blow to the knee. He has medial tenderness and valgus laxity with a soft endpoint at both 30 degrees and in full extension. The Lachman and posterior drawer tests are normal and MRI shows an isolated complete (grade III) superficial MCL rupture with no cruciate or posteromedial corner injury and no displaced distal avulsion. What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Hinged knee brace and a progressive functional rehabilitation programme

options.label: B
options.text: Urgent open MCL reconstruction with tendon autograft

options.label: C
options.text: Arthroscopic MCL repair within 48 hours

options.label: D
options.text: Above-knee cast in full extension for six weeks

options.label: E
options.text: Immediate combined MCL and ACL reconstruction

explanation: The MCL has an excellent blood supply and healing potential, so the great majority of MCL injuries, including isolated grade III tears, are treated non-operatively with a hinged brace and early protected motion and rehabilitation. Valgus laxity in full extension implies a more extensive injury but, when truly isolated, still heals well with bracing. Surgery is reserved for specific situations: a displaced distal (tibial-sided) avulsion that has flipped superficial to the pes anserinus (a Stener-like lesion) and so cannot heal, combined ligament injuries (for example a symptomatically lax MCL after ACL reconstruction), or chronic symptomatic valgus instability. Casting in extension causes stiffness and is inferior to functional bracing.
learningPoint: Even isolated grade III MCL tears usually heal with a hinged brace and rehabilitation; reserve surgery for displaced distal (Stener-like) avulsions, combined injuries or chronic valgus instability.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Sports Medicine: medial collateral ligament injuries
BASK consensus on collateral ligament injuries

stem: An 11-year-old competitive gymnast has a complete ACL rupture with recurrent giving way that has failed bracing and rehabilitation. She has substantial growth remaining (Tanner stage 1, with widely open distal femoral and proximal tibial physes on imaging). Her parents want her to return to sport. What is the most appropriate surgical option?

options.label: A
options.text: Physeal-sparing ACL reconstruction using a soft-tissue graft

options.label: B
options.text: Standard transphyseal reconstruction with a bone-patellar tendon-bone autograft and interference screws across the physis

options.label: C
options.text: Defer all surgery and brace until skeletal maturity

options.label: D
options.text: Primary synthetic ligament (artificial graft) reconstruction

options.label: E
options.text: Extra-articular tenodesis alone without intra-articular graft

explanation: In a very young patient with widely open physes and significant growth remaining, the priority is to avoid iatrogenic growth disturbance (limb-length discrepancy or angular deformity) from drilling and rigid fixation across the physis. A physeal-sparing technique using soft-tissue graft (for example a combined intra- and extra-articular iliotibial band reconstruction, or all-epiphyseal tunnels) restores stability while protecting the growth plates; bone blocks and metal fixation straddling the physis are specifically avoided in this age group. Leaving a symptomatic, unstable ACL-deficient knee untreated risks progressive meniscal and chondral damage, so prolonged deferral is not ideal once rehabilitation has failed. Synthetic ligaments have an unacceptable failure and synovitis profile and are not recommended; extra-articular tenodesis alone does not adequately control anteroposterior laxity.
learningPoint: In the markedly skeletally immature knee, use a physeal-sparing soft-tissue ACL reconstruction; avoid bone blocks and hardware crossing open physes.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Paediatric Sports Medicine: ACL injuries in skeletally immature patients
BASK / BOA guidance on paediatric ACL injury

stem: A 45-year-old recreational runner felt a pop after a deep squat and has posteromedial joint-line pain. There is no mechanical locking and the knee is otherwise stable with only early, minor chondral changes. MRI shows a radial tear at the posterior attachment of the medial meniscus with a 'ghost sign' (non-visualised root) on the sagittal images and 4 mm of meniscal extrusion on the coronal images. Alignment is neutral. What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Arthroscopic transtibial pull-out repair of the meniscal root

options.label: B
options.text: Arthroscopic partial meniscectomy of the posterior horn

options.label: C
options.text: Total meniscectomy

options.label: D
options.text: Meniscal allograft transplantation

options.label: E
options.text: Intra-articular corticosteroid injection and discharge

explanation: A posterior meniscal root tear destroys the meniscus's hoop-stress (circumferential) function, so the meniscus extrudes and behaves biomechanically as if it had been largely removed, leading to rapidly progressive degenerative change and subchondral insufficiency fracture if left untreated. In a relatively young patient with minimal arthritis and acceptable (neutral) alignment, the root should be re-anchored, most commonly by an arthroscopic transtibial pull-out repair, to restore hoop tension and slow joint degeneration. Meniscectomy (partial or total) removes more functional tissue and accelerates arthritis. Allograft transplantation is considered for the previously meniscectomised, symptomatic compartment, not for an acute repairable root. Injection alone neglects a reconstructable, joint-preserving lesion.
learningPoint: A posterior meniscal root tear functionally behaves like loss of the meniscus through loss of hoop stress; repair it (transtibial pull-out) in the young knee with preserved cartilage and acceptable alignment.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Sports Medicine: meniscal root tears

stem: Theme: meniscal anatomy, vascularity and repair decisions. For each clinical scenario, select the single most appropriate statement or management option from the list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Peripheral red-red zone tear - high healing potential, repair

options.label: B
options.text: Central white-white zone tear - poor healing potential, partial meniscectomy if symptomatic

options.label: C
options.text: Ramp lesion of the posterior horn of the medial meniscus

options.label: D
options.text: Discoid lateral meniscus

options.label: E
options.text: Degenerate horizontal cleavage tear - non-operative management first

options.label: F
options.text: Displaced bucket-handle tear - reduce and repair

options.label: G
options.text: Meniscal allograft transplantation
