emiLeads.stem: A 20-year-old has a 9 mm longitudinal tear located within the outer third of the medial meniscus at the meniscocapsular junction, found during ACL reconstruction. Choose the description and management that best fit this tear.

During ACL reconstruction in a 24-year-old, probing reveals a tear at the posteromedial meniscocapsular/meniscotibial junction that was not clearly seen on the standard anterior arthroscopic views and is often missed without a dedicated posteromedial inspection. Select the lesion.

A 13-year-old has lateral-sided snapping and a 'clunk' on extension, with a block to full extension and a meniscus that appears abnormally large and slab-like, filling the lateral compartment on MRI. Select the diagnosis.

A 58-year-old has a several-month history of medial joint pain with no trauma or true locking; MRI shows a horizontal cleavage pattern with early chondral wear. Select the most appropriate initial management category.

A 27-year-old who underwent a previous subtotal lateral meniscectomy now has lateral compartment overload pain, with preserved alignment and only minor chondral change isolated to that compartment. Select the most appropriate option.

explanation: Meniscal vascularity runs from the periphery inward: the outer third (red-red) zone is vascular and heals well, so peripheral longitudinal tears in young patients are repaired; the inner third (white-white) is avascular and a symptomatic central tear is usually treated with limited partial meniscectomy. A ramp lesion is a tear of the posterior horn of the medial meniscus at the meniscocapsular/meniscotibial junction, strongly associated with ACL ruptures and easily missed unless the posteromedial compartment is specifically inspected (for example via a trans-notch or posteromedial portal view). A discoid meniscus (usually lateral) is an abnormally broad meniscus that can cause snapping and, when symptomatic, is treated by saucerisation, repairing any unstable peripheral rim. A degenerate horizontal cleavage tear in an older patient is essentially early osteoarthritis and is managed non-operatively first. Meniscal allograft transplantation is offered to the symptomatic, previously meniscectomised compartment with preserved alignment and cartilage.
learningPoint: Repair potential follows meniscal blood supply (red-red good, white-white poor); always look posteromedially for a ramp lesion with an ACL tear, and reserve allograft for the symptomatic post-meniscectomy knee with preserved alignment and cartilage.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Sports Medicine: meniscal anatomy and tears
BASK guidance on meniscal preservation and transplantation

stem: A 16-year-old girl has had a single, first-time traumatic lateral patellar dislocation that reduced spontaneously. Radiographs and MRI show a medial patellofemoral ligament injury, no osteochondral fragment, no trochlear dysplasia, and normal patellar height and tibial-tubercle-to-trochlear-groove (TT-TG) distance. The effusion has settled. What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Structured physiotherapy focusing on quadriceps and core control, with progressive return to activity

options.label: B
options.text: Medial patellofemoral ligament reconstruction

options.label: C
options.text: Tibial tubercle transfer (medialisation osteotomy)

options.label: D
options.text: Trochleoplasty

options.label: E
options.text: Lateral retinacular release as an isolated procedure

explanation: A first-time patellar dislocation without an osteochondral fragment requiring fixation and without major anatomical risk factors is managed non-operatively, with rehabilitation aimed at quadriceps strength, neuromuscular control and gradual return to function. Surgery is escalated for recurrent instability, or at first dislocation when there is a displaced osteochondral fragment or loose body needing fixation. MPFL reconstruction is the workhorse for recurrent instability where bony anatomy is favourable. Tibial tubercle transfer is added when the TT-TG distance is increased (commonly cited threshold of around 20 mm) or there is patella alta. Trochleoplasty is reserved for high-grade trochlear dysplasia. Isolated lateral release does not address medial-sided instability and is not an appropriate stabilising procedure on its own.
learningPoint: First-time patellar dislocation with no loose osteochondral fragment and no major bony risk factors is treated non-operatively; surgery is for recurrent instability or a fragment needing fixation.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Sports Medicine: patellofemoral instability
BASK / BOA guidance on patellar instability

stem: A 22-year-old has had five lateral patellar dislocations and now dislocates with everyday activities. Examination shows a grossly positive apprehension test and a J-sign. Imaging demonstrates high-grade (Dejour type B or D) trochlear dysplasia with a crossing sign and supratrochlear spur, a TT-TG distance of 14 mm, and normal patellar height. The cartilage is well preserved. Which operative plan most directly addresses the principal underlying abnormality?

options.label: A
options.text: Sulcus-deepening trochleoplasty combined with MPFL reconstruction

options.label: B
options.text: Isolated MPFL reconstruction alone

options.label: C
options.text: Large tibial tubercle medialisation osteotomy alone

options.label: D
options.text: Isolated arthroscopic lateral retinacular release

options.label: E
options.text: Patellofemoral joint replacement

explanation: The dominant abnormality here is high-grade trochlear dysplasia: a flat or convex trochlea with a crossing sign and supratrochlear spur (Dejour type B or D) is the strongest anatomical driver of recurrent instability. With a normal TT-TG (14 mm is within normal limits) and normal patellar height, a tubercle osteotomy would not address the problem, and an isolated MPFL reconstruction grafted onto a dysplastic, non-containing trochlea carries a higher risk of failure. The procedure that directly corrects the deficient groove is a sulcus-deepening trochleoplasty, which should be combined with MPFL reconstruction to restore the medial soft-tissue checkrein. Isolated lateral release does not stabilise the patella. Patellofemoral replacement is an arthritis procedure and is inappropriate in a 22-year-old with preserved cartilage.
learningPoint: Match the operation to the anatomical abnormality: high-grade trochlear dysplasia (supratrochlear spur, Dejour B or D) with a normal TT-TG calls for trochleoplasty plus MPFL reconstruction, not a tubercle osteotomy.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Sports Medicine: patellofemoral instability and trochlear dysplasia (Dejour classification)
BASK guidance on complex patellofemoral instability

subtopic: Articular cartilage lesions and osteochondritis dissecans
stem: Theme: management of articular cartilage and osteochondritis dissecans (OCD) lesions of the knee. For each scenario, select the single most appropriate management option from the list. Assume neutral alignment, a stable knee and intact menisci unless stated. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Non-operative management (activity modification and supervised rehabilitation)

options.label: B
options.text: Arthroscopic microfracture / marrow stimulation

options.label: C
options.text: Osteochondral autograft transfer (OAT / mosaicplasty)

options.label: D
options.text: Autologous chondrocyte implantation (ACI / MACI)

options.label: E
options.text: Fixation of the osteochondral fragment in situ

options.label: F
options.text: Unicompartmental knee arthroplasty

options.label: G
options.text: Removal of loose body and debridement only

emiLeads.stem: An 11-year-old with open physes has knee pain and an MRI showing a stable juvenile OCD lesion of the medial femoral condyle: intact overlying cartilage, no high-signal fluid undermining the fragment and no loose body. Select the most appropriate initial management.

A 28-year-old has a symptomatic, well-demarcated 1.5 cm-squared full-thickness chondral defect on the medial femoral condyle with no significant subchondral bone loss, found at arthroscopy. Choose the most appropriate single procedure for this small contained defect.

A 32-year-old athlete has a symptomatic 5 cm-squared full-thickness chondral defect of the femoral condyle with no major subchondral bone loss and no previous cartilage repair surgery; a durable hyaline-like repair is desired. Select the most appropriate option.

A 16-year-old presents acutely after a sporting injury with a locked knee. Imaging shows an unstable OCD fragment that has partially separated but remains in its bed with good-quality subchondral bone attached, amenable to anatomical reduction. Select the most appropriate management.

explanation: Lesion size, fragment stability and skeletal maturity drive cartilage and OCD management. A stable juvenile OCD lesion (open physes, intact cartilage, no fluid undermining the fragment) has a good prognosis and is treated non-operatively first with activity restriction. Small contained full-thickness chondral defects (broadly up to about 2 cm-squared) respond well to marrow stimulation/microfracture, which produces fibrocartilage. Larger symptomatic defects (broadly above about 2 to 3 cm-squared) are better served by cell-based resurfacing such as ACI/MACI, which yields more durable hyaline-like cartilage; in UK practice NICE (TA508) supports ACI for defects over 2 cm-squared when there is minimal osteoarthritis and no previous cartilage repair surgery. OAT/mosaicplasty suits small-to-moderate lesions but is limited by donor-site morbidity and graft-host congruity for very large defects. An unstable but reducible OCD fragment with viable bone should be anatomically reduced and fixed in situ to preserve the native osteochondral surface rather than excised. Unicompartmental arthroplasty is for established compartmental arthritis, not isolated focal defects in young patients.
learningPoint: Cartilage/OCD treatment follows size, stability and maturity: stable juvenile OCD - rehabilitate; small defect - microfracture; large defect - ACI/MACI (NICE TA508: over 2 cm-squared, minimal OA, no prior cartilage repair); salvageable unstable OCD fragment - fix in situ.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Sports Medicine: articular cartilage injury and osteochondritis dissecans
NICE TA508 autologous chondrocyte implantation for knee cartilage defects

stem: A 58-year-old woman has a symptomatic hallux valgus that has failed shoe modification. Weightbearing radiographs show a hallux valgus angle of 38 degrees and an intermetatarsal angle of 20 degrees. The first metatarsophalangeal joint is congruent with no arthritic change, and the distal metatarsal articular angle is normal. Which operative procedure is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Distal chevron (Austin) osteotomy

options.label: B
options.text: Proximal/basal first metatarsal osteotomy with distal soft-tissue release

options.label: C
options.text: Akin phalangeal osteotomy alone

options.label: D
options.text: Keller excision arthroplasty

options.label: E
options.text: First metatarsophalangeal joint arthrodesis

explanation: Correction must match deformity severity. A distal chevron reliably corrects mild-to-moderate deformity (IMA up to roughly 13-15 degrees) because the translation achievable at the metatarsal head is limited; translating more than about one third to one half of the head width risks instability and avascular necrosis. With a severe deformity (HVA ~38, IMA 20) a more powerful proximal/basal osteotomy (or a Lapidus first TMT fusion) combined with a distal soft-tissue procedure is needed to reduce the large intermetatarsal angle. An Akin alone corrects only the phalangeal component and does not address the IMA. Keller is reserved for low-demand elderly patients; arthrodesis is for arthritic, severe degenerative, or revision cases rather than this congruent non-arthritic joint.
learningPoint: Match the osteotomy to the IMA: distal osteotomies for mild-moderate deformity, proximal/basal (or Lapidus) for severe deformity with a large IMA.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, 8th ed — Foot & Ankle: hallux valgus
BOFAS (British Orthopaedic Foot & Ankle Society) hallux valgus guidance

stem: A 62-year-old man has end-stage hallux rigidus with global joint-space loss, pain throughout the arc of motion (not just at terminal dorsiflexion), and marked first metatarsophalangeal joint stiffness. He is an active hill walker. Non-operative measures have failed. What is the most appropriate surgical management?

options.label: A
options.text: First metatarsophalangeal joint arthrodesis

options.label: B
options.text: Dorsal cheilectomy

options.label: C
options.text: Moberg dorsal closing-wedge proximal phalangeal osteotomy

options.label: D
options.text: Keller excision arthroplasty

options.label: E
options.text: First metatarsophalangeal joint silastic interposition replacement

explanation: First MTPJ arthrodesis is the gold standard for end-stage (high-grade) hallux rigidus, giving reliable, durable pain relief and a stable platform that tolerates demanding activity such as hill walking. Cheilectomy (with or without a Moberg osteotomy) is effective for early/intermediate disease where pain is mainly dorsal and terminal and a good plantar joint surface remains; it is unreliable once there is global mid-range arthritis. Keller produces a weak, unstable hallux with transfer-lesion risk, so it is reserved for low-demand elderly patients. First MTPJ replacement has higher failure and revision rates and is not the standard in an active patient.
learningPoint: End-stage hallux rigidus with mid-range pain in an active patient: arthrodesis. Cheilectomy is for early/dorsal disease.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, 8th ed — Foot & Ankle: hallux rigidus

stem: A 60-year-old woman has a painful second-toe deformity with a fixed flexion contracture at the proximal interphalangeal joint and a hyperextended, dorsally subluxed metatarsophalangeal joint that does not reduce passively. The plantar plate is incompetent on examination and imaging. Which operative plan best addresses the deformity?

options.label: A
options.text: Flexor-to-extensor (Girdlestone-Taylor) transfer alone

options.label: B
options.text: PIP joint resection/fusion plus MTPJ release with plantar plate repair (and bony shortening if needed)

options.label: C
options.text: Isolated extensor tenotomy

options.label: D
options.text: Syndactylisation to the adjacent toe

options.label: E
options.text: Weil osteotomy alone

explanation: This is a fixed (rigid) hammer toe with a dislocated MTPJ and plantar plate insufficiency, so a single soft-tissue procedure is inadequate. Correction requires addressing each level: a rigid PIP deformity needs bony correction (resection arthroplasty or fusion), and a dislocated MTPJ with plantar plate failure needs a dorsal release/reduction with plantar plate repair, often combined with a Weil shortening osteotomy to relieve tension and allow reduction. A flexor-to-extensor transfer is most useful for a flexible deformity; isolated tenotomy treats only flexible/early disease; syndactylisation does not correct the bony deformity or restore the joint; a Weil osteotomy alone leaves the PIP contracture and plantar plate uncorrected.
learningPoint: Flexible lesser-toe deformity is treated with soft-tissue procedures; a fixed/dislocated toe needs combined bony correction, MTPJ reduction and plantar plate repair.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, 8th ed — Foot & Ankle: lesser-toe deformities and plantar plate

stem: A 24-year-old man has a progressive cavovarus foot with clawing of the toes and recurrent lateral ankle sprains. On the Coleman block test (lateral foot and heel on a block with the first ray allowed to drop over the edge) the hindfoot varus fully corrects to neutral. Which statement best describes what this result indicates and its principal management implication?

options.label: A
options.text: The hindfoot varus is forefoot-driven (a plantarflexed first ray) and the subtalar joint is still flexible, so a hindfoot fusion is not required

options.label: B
options.text: The hindfoot varus is rigid and structural, mandating a calcaneal osteotomy or hindfoot arthrodesis

options.label: C
options.text: There is a fixed forefoot supination requiring a midfoot osteotomy as the sole procedure

options.label: D
options.text: The deformity is purely neuromuscular and contraindicates any bony surgery

options.label: E
options.text: The tibialis posterior is the primary deforming force and should be lengthened

explanation: The Coleman block test isolates the hindfoot by placing the heel and lateral foot on a block while letting the first ray hang over the edge. If the hindfoot varus corrects, the deformity is forefoot-driven (typically a plantarflexed first ray, often from a peroneus longus that overpowers a weak tibialis anterior in conditions such as CMT) and the subtalar joint is still flexible. Correction can then be directed at the forefoot (e.g. dorsiflexion first-metatarsal osteotomy) with tendon transfers/balancing, without committing to a hindfoot arthrodesis. A hindfoot that does not correct (rigid) is what would require a calcaneal osteotomy or fusion. The other options misattribute the deforming forces or wrongly exclude surgery.
learningPoint: A correcting Coleman block test = flexible, forefoot-driven hindfoot varus, so treat the forefoot (and balance tendons) and avoid an unnecessary hindfoot fusion.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, 8th ed — Foot & Ankle / Paediatrics: pes cavus (cavovarus) and the Coleman block test

stem: A 56-year-old man with long-standing diabetes and peripheral neuropathy presents with a hot, swollen, erythematous midfoot after a minor twisting injury. He is systemically well and there is no skin breach or ulcer. The affected foot is about 3-4 degrees C warmer than the other side. Inflammatory markers are mildly raised and radiographs are reported as near-normal. What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Immobilisation and offloading in a total-contact cast with urgent referral to the multidisciplinary diabetic foot service

options.label: B
options.text: Reassure, allow normal weightbearing, and review in 6 weeks

options.label: C
options.text: Start a long-term oral bisphosphonate and continue full weightbearing

options.label: D
options.text: Proceed to immediate midfoot arthrodesis

options.label: E
options.text: Below-knee amputation

explanation: A hot, swollen, red, neuropathic foot with a raised skin-temperature gradient and normal/near-normal early radiographs is acute Charcot neuroarthropathy until proven otherwise, even before bony changes appear. The priority is immediate offloading and immobilisation (total-contact cast) with non-weightbearing or protected weightbearing and urgent referral to a multidisciplinary diabetic foot service, in line with NICE NG19, to halt progression to fixed deformity. Continued weightbearing accelerates collapse. Bisphosphonates are not a substitute for offloading and are not standard first-line. Surgical reconstruction is not undertaken in the active inflammatory phase, and amputation is not indicated for an intact foot. Infection must be excluded, but the management of suspected acute Charcot is to offload and refer.
learningPoint: A hot, swollen neuropathic foot with normal early X-rays is acute Charcot until proven otherwise: offload in a total-contact cast and refer urgently to the MDT.
references: NICE NG19 — Diabetic foot problems: prevention and management
Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, 8th ed — Foot & Ankle: Charcot neuroarthropathy

stem: A 64-year-old woman with long-standing rheumatoid arthritis has a painful forefoot with severe hallux valgus, dorsally dislocated lesser metatarsophalangeal joints, fixed clawing of the lesser toes, and painful plantar metatarsal-head callosities so that she feels she is 'walking on pebbles'. Non-operative measures have failed. Which operative plan is the most appropriate reconstruction?

options.label: A
options.text: First MTPJ arthrodesis with lesser metatarsal head excision (resection) arthroplasty

options.label: B
options.text: Isolated Keller excision arthroplasty of the hallux

options.label: C
options.text: Distal chevron osteotomy of the first metatarsal alone

options.label: D
options.text: Lesser-toe flexor-to-extensor transfers only

options.label: E
options.text: Transmetatarsal amputation

explanation: The classic reconstruction for the severely destroyed rheumatoid forefoot is a first MTPJ arthrodesis combined with excision arthroplasty of the lesser metatarsal heads (a Hoffmann/Clayton-type forefoot reconstruction). Fusing the first ray provides a stable, durable medial column that maintains correction and offloads the lesser rays, while resecting the dislocated, eroded lesser metatarsal heads relieves the painful plantar keratoses and allows the toes to be realigned. A Keller alone, or an isolated first-ray osteotomy, leaves the dislocated lesser joints and metatarsalgia uncorrected; soft-tissue transfers cannot reduce fixed dislocations; amputation is not warranted for a reconstructable foot.
learningPoint: Severe rheumatoid forefoot reconstruction = first MTPJ arthrodesis + lesser metatarsal head excision arthroplasty for a stable, pain-free forefoot.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, 8th ed — Foot & Ankle: rheumatoid forefoot

stem: Theme: staging and management of tibialis posterior (posterior tibial tendon) dysfunction / adult acquired flatfoot deformity. For each clinical picture, select the single most appropriate management from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Orthoses, physiotherapy and activity modification (non-operative)

options.label: B
options.text: FDL tendon transfer with medialising calcaneal osteotomy (joint-preserving reconstruction)

options.label: C
options.text: Triple arthrodesis (hindfoot fusion)

options.label: D
options.text: Tibiotalocalcaneal fusion or a pantalar procedure (addressing the ankle)

options.label: E
options.text: Isolated first MTPJ arthrodesis

options.label: F
options.text: Lateral column lengthening performed in isolation

emiLeads.stem: A 50-year-old has medial ankle pain and tendon tenderness with a normal foot shape and no deformity; the heel still inverts on a single-heel-rise test (stage I).
A 55-year-old has a planovalgus foot with hindfoot valgus and forefoot abduction that fully corrects passively but has failed bracing (flexible stage II).
A 60-year-old has a rigid hindfoot valgus that no longer corrects passively, with subtalar arthritic change, but a normal, well-aligned ankle joint (stage III).
A 65-year-old has a fixed hindfoot deformity with a valgus talar tilt and arthritic change within the ankle joint itself (stage IV).

explanation: Tibialis posterior dysfunction is staged (Johnson & Strom, with the Myerson stage IV modification) to guide treatment. Stage I (tenosynovitis, no deformity) is managed non-operatively. Stage II is a flexible (passively correctable) flatfoot treated with joint-preserving reconstruction — typically an FDL transfer with a medialising calcaneal osteotomy, adding a lateral column lengthening for significant forefoot abduction (lengthening is an adjunct, not a stand-alone procedure, so option F is wrong). Stage III is a rigid hindfoot deformity requiring a hindfoot (triple) arthrodesis. Stage IV involves the ankle (valgus talar tilt from deltoid/spring ligament insufficiency, with tibiotalar arthritis); the ankle must be addressed — a tibiotalocalcaneal/pantalar fusion (correctable variants may instead allow deltoid reconstruction with hindfoot fusion). Isolated first MTPJ arthrodesis (option E) plays no role in this deformity.
learningPoint: PTTD staging drives treatment: I non-operative, II flexible → joint-preserving reconstruction, III rigid → triple fusion, IV ankle involved → address the ankle.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, 8th ed — Foot & Ankle: posterior tibial tendon dysfunction (Johnson & Strom staging, Myerson modification)

stem: A 60-year-old man with diabetes and dense peripheral neuropathy has a 2 cm full-thickness neuropathic ulcer under the first metatarsal head. The ulcer probes to bone, with surrounding erythema and purulent discharge. Foot pulses are palpable. Which factor is the single most important determinant of healing in his plantar neuropathic ulcer and the cornerstone of management?

options.label: A
options.text: Mechanical offloading of plantar pressure (e.g. total-contact cast / removable cast walker)

options.label: B
options.text: A prolonged course of oral antibiotics alone

options.label: C
options.text: Tight glycaemic control alone, without altering weightbearing

options.label: D
options.text: Daily antiseptic soaks and dressings without pressure relief

options.label: E
options.text: Immediate ray amputation of the first ray

explanation: Plantar neuropathic ulcers are caused and perpetuated by repetitive pressure on an insensate foot, so redistributing/offloading mechanical load is the single most important factor for healing — the total-contact cast is the reference standard, with removable cast walkers as alternatives, alongside good wound care, infection control and vascular assessment. A positive probe-to-bone test in this infected ulcer raises the likelihood of osteomyelitis, which must be investigated and treated, but antibiotics, glycaemic control and dressings will not heal the wound if the pressure is not relieved. Amputation is not the first-line response to a reconstructable, perfused, infected ulcer; debridement, infection control and offloading come first (NICE NG19).
learningPoint: The cornerstone of healing a plantar diabetic neuropathic ulcer is offloading (total-contact cast); a positive probe-to-bone test suggests osteomyelitis.
references: NICE NG19 — Diabetic foot problems: prevention and management
Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, 8th ed — Foot & Ankle: the diabetic foot

stem: A 42-year-old recreational badminton player felt a sudden blow to the back of the ankle while pushing off, with an audible snap, and now walks with a flat-footed limp. There is a palpable gap 4 cm proximal to the calcaneal insertion. Which clinical finding most reliably confirms a complete Achilles tendon rupture?

options.label: A
options.text: Absent plantarflexion when the calf is squeezed with the patient prone and the knee flexed (Simmonds/Thompson test)

options.label: B
options.text: Inability to actively plantarflex the ankle against resistance

options.label: C
options.text: A positive Mulder click on forefoot compression

options.label: D
options.text: Pain on passive dorsiflexion of the ankle

options.label: E
options.text: Weakness of great toe flexion

explanation: The calf-squeeze (Simmonds/Thompson) test is the most reliable bedside sign: with the patient prone and the foot hanging free, squeezing the calf normally produces plantarflexion via the intact gastrocnemius-soleus-tendon unit; loss of this movement indicates a complete rupture. Active plantarflexion can be preserved by the long toe flexors, tibialis posterior and the peroneals, so its presence does NOT exclude rupture and makes option B a classic trap. Mulder's click is a sign of Morton's neuroma. Ultrasound is used when the clinical picture is equivocal. Diagnosis is essentially clinical: a palpable gap plus a positive calf-squeeze test (with reduced resting tension/altered ankle resting angle) is sufficient.
learningPoint: Active plantarflexion can be intact in a complete Achilles rupture — rely on the calf-squeeze (Simmonds/Thompson) test plus a palpable gap; reserve ultrasound for equivocal cases.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Foot & Ankle: Achilles tendon disorders
BOA/BOFAS guidance on acute Achilles tendon rupture

stem: A fit and well 35-year-old sustains an acute complete mid-substance Achilles tendon rupture playing five-a-side football. He presents on the same day; ultrasound confirms tendon-end apposition with the ankle in equinus. He is keen to understand his options. According to current UK practice, which statement best guides management?

options.label: A
options.text: Functional rehabilitation in a removable boot/brace with the ankle in equinus and early weightbearing is an appropriate first-line option, with re-rupture rates comparable to surgery

options.label: B
options.text: Surgical repair is mandatory in all patients under 40 to prevent re-rupture

options.label: C
options.text: A rigid below-knee plaster in plantigrade (neutral) position for 10 weeks is the standard non-operative regimen

options.label: D
options.text: Non-operative treatment is contraindicated because it carries an unacceptably high re-rupture rate

options.label: E
options.text: Percutaneous repair eliminates the risk of sural nerve injury

explanation: Current UK practice is early functional rehabilitation — a removable orthosis/boot holding the ankle in equinus with progressive weightbearing and graduated reduction of the heel wedge towards plantigrade. The UKSTAR RCT showed that early weightbearing in a functional brace is safe and gives outcomes equivalent to traditional plaster casting (re-rupture 5% vs 6%); separate RCTs and meta-analyses (e.g. Willits, Nilsson-Helander, Soroceanu) show that accelerated functional rehabilitation yields re-rupture rates comparable to surgery while avoiding wound and sural nerve complications. Surgery is therefore selective — reserved for delayed presentation with a persistent gap, certain high-demand/elite athletes, or informed patient preference — and is not mandatory under 40. Modern non-operative protocols immobilise in equinus, not neutral, so option C is wrong, and percutaneous repair reduces but does not eliminate sural nerve injury.
learningPoint: For acute Achilles rupture, early functional rehabilitation in equinus with progressive weightbearing matches surgery for re-rupture and is a standard UK first-line option; UKSTAR confirms functional bracing is equivalent to plaster casting, and surgery is selective rather than mandatory.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Foot & Ankle: Achilles tendon rupture
UKSTAR RCT (Lancet 2019); BOFAS / BOA position on acute Achilles tendon rupture

stem: A 26-year-old skier reports a snapping sensation on the lateral aspect of the ankle with recurrent episodes of the tendons 'flicking' over the bone, provoked by resisted dorsiflexion–eversion. There is retromalleolar swelling and tenderness but no instability of the ankle joint itself. CT shows a flat (convex) fibular groove. Symptoms persist despite physiotherapy and bracing. What is the most appropriate definitive management?

options.label: A
options.text: Superior peroneal retinaculum repair with fibular groove deepening

options.label: B
options.text: Lateral ligament reconstruction (modified Broström)

options.label: C
options.text: Peroneus brevis to longus tenodesis

options.label: D
options.text: Subtalar arthrodesis

options.label: E
options.text: Continued bracing and activity modification

explanation: The history is recurrent peroneal tendon dislocation/subluxation: the tendons subluxate out of the retromalleolar groove because of an incompetent superior peroneal retinaculum (SPR), classically associated with a shallow, flat or convex fibular groove (the normal groove is concave). Definitive surgical treatment addresses both pathologies — SPR repair/reconstruction combined with fibular groove deepening. This is distinct from lateral ligament (ankle joint) instability, so a Broström (option B) treats the wrong structure. Tenodesis is reserved for irreparable longitudinal tears, and subtalar arthrodesis is irrelevant to this problem. Non-operative care has already failed.
learningPoint: Recurrent peroneal tendon subluxation with a shallow/convex fibular groove is treated by SPR repair plus groove deepening — it is a tendon-retinaculum problem, not lateral ligament instability.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Foot & Ankle: peroneal tendon disorders

stem: A 29-year-old has recurrent giving way of the ankle on uneven ground three years after an inversion injury. Examination shows a positive anterior drawer and increased talar tilt, with no generalised ligamentous laxity. Weightbearing radiographs are normal and there is no significant hindfoot varus. A structured 3-month proprioceptive and peroneal strengthening programme has failed. What is the most appropriate surgical procedure?

options.label: A
options.text: Anatomical repair of the lateral ligament complex (modified Broström–Gould)

options.label: B
options.text: Non-anatomical tenodesis using the whole peroneus brevis (Chrisman–Snook)

options.label: C
options.text: Lateral closing-wedge calcaneal osteotomy

options.label: D
options.text: Ankle arthrodesis

options.label: E
options.text: Total ankle replacement

explanation: For chronic mechanical lateral ankle instability that has failed conservative treatment, the standard procedure is an anatomical repair of the ATFL/CFL — the modified Broström, reinforced by the Gould modification (mobilising the inferior extensor retinaculum over the repair). Non-anatomical tenodesis reconstructions (e.g. Chrisman–Snook) sacrifice peroneus brevis and restrict subtalar motion, so they are reserved for revision, high-demand, or poor-tissue cases (where a free tendon graft anatomical reconstruction is an alternative). A calcaneal osteotomy is added only when hindfoot varus is driving the instability (absent here). Arthrodesis and replacement are for end-stage arthritis, not isolated instability with a normal joint.
learningPoint: Chronic lateral ankle instability failing rehab → anatomical modified Broström–Gould repair; reserve non-anatomical tenodesis (or graft reconstruction) for revision/high-demand/poor-tissue cases and add a calcaneal osteotomy only for hindfoot varus.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Foot & Ankle: ankle instability

stem: A 30-year-old has deep medial ankle pain and intermittent swelling persisting 12 months after an ankle sprain, with no mechanical instability. MRI shows an osteochondral lesion of the medial talar dome measuring 18 mm in diameter with a 9 mm subchondral cyst beneath an unstable cartilage flap; non-operative treatment has failed. What is the most appropriate surgical option?

options.label: A
options.text: Restoration of the osteochondral unit — autologous osteochondral transfer (osteochondral autograft/mosaicplasty) or a scaffold-based repair (e.g. AMIC) with bone grafting of the cyst

options.label: B
options.text: Arthroscopic microfracture (bone marrow stimulation) alone

options.label: C
options.text: Ankle arthrodesis

options.label: D
options.text: Total ankle replacement

options.label: E
options.text: Continued non-operative management with an offloading brace

explanation: Lesion size and the presence of a subchondral cyst drive the choice. Small lesions (broadly under ~1.5 cm² / 10–15 mm) without a significant cyst respond well to arthroscopic microfracture/bone marrow stimulation. This lesion is large (18 mm) with a substantial (9 mm) cyst and an unstable flap — features that predict poor results from microfracture alone — so it requires restoration of the osteochondral unit: autologous osteochondral transfer (OAT/mosaicplasty), or a scaffold/membrane-based repair (e.g. AMIC) with bone grafting of the cyst. Arthrodesis and replacement are for end-stage arthritis, not a focal lesion in a young joint, and conservative care has already failed. (Medial dome lesions characteristically cause deep medial/posteromedial ankle pain.)
learningPoint: Large (>~1.5 cm²/10–15 mm) or significantly cystic osteochondral talar lesions respond poorly to microfracture alone — restore the osteochondral unit with autograft (OAT) transfer or scaffold/graft techniques plus cyst bone grafting.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Foot & Ankle: osteochondral lesions of the talus
BOFAS — osteochondral lesions of the talus

stem: A 52-year-old labourer has end-stage post-traumatic isolated tibiotalar osteoarthritis following an old pilon fracture. He has severe pain, a stiff but well-aligned ankle, and intact subtalar and midfoot joints. He does heavy manual work and wishes to return to it. Non-operative measures are exhausted. Which is the most appropriate definitive surgical treatment?

options.label: A
options.text: Ankle (tibiotalar) arthrodesis

options.label: B
options.text: Total ankle replacement

options.label: C
options.text: Tibiotalocalcaneal (hindfoot) arthrodesis

options.label: D
options.text: Supramalleolar osteotomy

options.label: E
options.text: Arthroscopic debridement and microfracture

explanation: Ankle arthrodesis remains the standard durable option for end-stage isolated tibiotalar arthritis in a young, heavy-demand manual worker — it provides reliable pain relief and tolerates high loads, with progressive adjacent (subtalar/midfoot) arthritis as the long-term trade-off. Total ankle replacement is preferred in lower-demand, older patients with good bone stock and alignment and avoids adjacent-joint overload, but is less suited to heavy manual labour and has higher revision rates in young, high-demand patients. A hindfoot (TTC) fusion needlessly sacrifices the healthy subtalar joint. A supramalleolar osteotomy is for malaligned, asymmetric arthritis with a preserved joint surface, and arthroscopic debridement does not address end-stage disease.
learningPoint: End-stage isolated ankle arthritis in a young, heavy-demand patient → ankle arthrodesis (durable standard); reserve total ankle replacement for lower-demand, older, well-aligned ankles with good bone stock.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Foot & Ankle: ankle arthritis and arthrodesis

stem: Theme: causes of foot and ankle pain. For each clinical presentation, select the single most likely diagnosis from the list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Morton's (interdigital) neuroma

options.label: B
options.text: Anterior ankle impingement

options.label: C
options.text: Posterior ankle impingement (os trigonum syndrome)

options.label: D
options.text: Peroneal tendon tear

options.label: E
options.text: Osteochondral lesion of the talus

options.label: F
options.text: Tarsal tunnel syndrome

options.label: G
options.text: Insertional Achilles tendinopathy

options.label: H
options.text: Syndesmotic (high ankle) sprain

emiLeads.stem: A 45-year-old woman who wears narrow high-heeled shoes describes burning pain and numbness radiating into the third and fourth toes, relieved by removing the shoe and rubbing the forefoot. Squeezing the metatarsal heads while pressing the third web space reproduces the pain with a palpable click.

A 24-year-old professional footballer has anterior ankle pain and a sensation of blockage at the front of the ankle on deep dorsiflexion (e.g. lunging). Radiographs show tibial and talar neck osteophytes; range of dorsiflexion is reduced.

A 19-year-old ballet dancer has deep posterior ankle pain that is worst on extreme plantarflexion (en pointe). There is tenderness behind the lateral malleolus and a positive forced-plantarflexion test; a lateral radiograph shows an os trigonum.

A 35-year-old runner reports a several-month history of lateral retromalleolar pain and swelling with weakness of eversion, having had recurrent ankle sprains; there is tenderness along the tendons coursing behind the lateral malleolus.

A 28-year-old presents with medial heel and arch pain with burning and tingling on the plantar surface; a Tinel's sign behind the medial malleolus reproduces the symptoms and there is no forefoot deformity.

explanation: Morton's neuroma: forefoot burning radiating into adjacent toes (most commonly the 3rd web space) with a positive Mulder's click, classically aggravated by tight/narrow or high-heeled footwear. Anterior ankle impingement: dorsiflexion-limited anterior pain with anterior tibiotalar osteophytes (footballer's ankle). Posterior ankle impingement / os trigonum syndrome: plantarflexion-provoked posterior ankle pain in dancers and footballers, with a positive forced-plantarflexion test. Peroneal tendon tear: chronic lateral retromalleolar pain/swelling with eversion weakness after recurrent sprains. Tarsal tunnel syndrome: posterior tibial nerve compression behind the medial malleolus giving plantar/medial burning with a positive Tinel's sign. UK first-line management of Morton's neuroma is footwear modification with a wide toe box, a metatarsal dome/offloading and activity advice, progressing to image-guided corticosteroid injection and then excision/decompression for refractory cases.
learningPoint: Localise foot/ankle pain by the provoking position: deep dorsiflexion = anterior impingement, extreme plantarflexion = posterior (os trigonum) impingement, forefoot burning + Mulder's click = Morton's neuroma, medial plantar burning + positive Tinel's = tarsal tunnel.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Foot & Ankle: impingement, nerve entrapment and forefoot disorders
BOFAS — Morton's neuroma and ankle impingement

stem: A 24-year-old rugby player has had four traumatic anterior shoulder dislocations. CT with 3D reconstruction of the en-face glenoid shows 22% anteroinferior glenoid bone loss and an off-track Hill-Sachs lesion. There is no glenohumeral arthritis. Which is the most appropriate surgical procedure?

options.label: A
options.text: Arthroscopic soft-tissue Bankart repair alone

options.label: B
options.text: Open Latarjet (coracoid transfer) procedure

options.label: C
options.text: Arthroscopic Bankart repair with remplissage as a stand-alone solution

options.label: D
options.text: Capsular shift with thermal capsulorrhaphy

options.label: E
options.text: Humeral head resurfacing

explanation: With glenoid bone loss in the subcritical-to-critical range (roughly 20% or more) and an off-track Hill-Sachs lesion, isolated soft-tissue Bankart repair has unacceptably high recurrence rates because the glenoid arc cannot contain the humeral head. Bony reconstruction is required: the Latarjet transfers the coracoid to restore the glenoid arc and adds the dynamic sling ('triple-block') effect of the conjoint tendon, which also converts an off-track lesion to on-track. Remplissage fills the Hill-Sachs defect but does not restore glenoid bone, so it is an adjunct rather than a stand-alone fix for this degree of glenoid loss. Thermal capsulorrhaphy is obsolete and risks capsular necrosis and axillary nerve injury; resurfacing is for arthritis.
learningPoint: Subcritical/critical glenoid bone loss (roughly 20% or more) or an off-track Hill-Sachs lesion mandates bony reconstruction (Latarjet), not soft-tissue Bankart alone.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Shoulder & Elbow: glenohumeral instability
BESS/BOA Patient Care Pathway: Traumatic Anterior Shoulder Instability

stem: A 19-year-old soldier presents with deep posterior shoulder pain and a sense of instability when pressing weight forward, such as during press-ups and bench press. There is no history of a frank dislocation. Which clinical test most specifically reproduces his symptoms?

options.label: A
options.text: Jerk test (axial load with horizontal adduction of the internally rotated, forward-flexed arm)

options.label: B
options.text: Apprehension test in abduction and external rotation

options.label: C
options.text: Sulcus sign at the side

options.label: D
options.text: Hawkins-Kennedy impingement test

options.label: E
options.text: O'Brien active compression test

explanation: The history (posterior pain, instability when loading the arm in flexion and internal rotation, no overt dislocation) is typical of posterior instability, which is frequently atraumatic or related to repetitive loading. The jerk test applies a posteriorly directed force vector and reproduces posterior subluxation and pain. The abduction-external-rotation apprehension test screens for anterior instability; the sulcus sign indicates inferior laxity and is associated with multidirectional instability; Hawkins-Kennedy is for subacromial impingement; O'Brien targets the AC joint and SLAP lesions.
learningPoint: Posterior instability presents with pain on forward-loading activities; the jerk test is the key provocative manoeuvre.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Shoulder & Elbow: posterior instability

stem: A 58-year-old undergoes arthroscopic double-row repair of a 2 cm full-thickness supraspinatus tear. At 12-month follow-up she has good pain relief and strength, but MRI shows a recurrent full-thickness defect at the footprint. Which factor is most strongly associated with this structural failure (re-tear)?

options.label: A
options.text: Use of a double-row rather than single-row construct

options.label: B
options.text: Higher Goutallier fatty infiltration grade of the muscle at the time of surgery

options.label: C
options.text: Repair performed arthroscopically rather than open

options.label: D
options.text: Patient age under 60 years

options.label: E
options.text: A traumatic rather than degenerate aetiology

explanation: Structural healing after cuff repair is governed mainly by tendon and muscle quality and by tear size. Advanced fatty infiltration (high Goutallier grade) and muscle atrophy predict poor healing and high re-tear rates and are largely irreversible. Larger tears, increasing age (over 60-65) and degenerate tears also worsen healing. Double-row constructs improve footprint contact and lower re-tear in larger tears; arthroscopic and open repairs have comparable healing; younger age and traumatic tears generally heal better.
learningPoint: Fatty infiltration (Goutallier grade) and muscle atrophy are dominant predictors of cuff repair failure - assess them before offering repair.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Shoulder & Elbow: rotator cuff repair & healing
BESS/BOA: Subacromial Shoulder Pain & rotator cuff tears

stem: A 62-year-old reports difficulty combing her hair and pain reaching to a back pocket. On examination there is a positive lift-off test and increased passive external rotation compared with the other side. Which rotator cuff muscle is principally affected?

options.label: A
options.text: Supraspinatus

options.label: B
options.text: Infraspinatus

options.label: C
options.text: Subscapularis

options.label: D
options.text: Teres minor

options.label: E
options.text: Deltoid (axillary nerve)

explanation: The lift-off test (along with the belly-press and bear-hug tests) assesses subscapularis, the principal internal rotator. A positive lift-off combined with an increase in passive external rotation (loss of the anterior restraint) indicates subscapularis insufficiency. Supraspinatus is tested by the empty-can/Jobe test, and infraspinatus and teres minor by resisted external rotation and the external rotation lag and Hornblower's signs.
learningPoint: A positive lift-off/belly-press with increased passive external rotation indicates a subscapularis tear.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Shoulder & Elbow: rotator cuff examination

stem: An independent 74-year-old has a painful pseudoparalytic shoulder. He cannot actively elevate the arm above 60 degrees, but passive movement is full and pain-free above that point. Radiographs show superior migration of the humeral head with acetabularisation of the acromion and glenohumeral joint-space loss. Which is the most appropriate definitive treatment?

options.label: A
options.text: Reverse total shoulder arthroplasty

options.label: B
options.text: Anatomic total shoulder arthroplasty

options.label: C
options.text: Humeral hemiarthroplasty

options.label: D
options.text: Arthroscopic rotator cuff repair

options.label: E
options.text: Glenohumeral arthrodesis

explanation: This is rotator cuff (cuff-tear) arthropathy with pseudoparalysis. Reverse total shoulder arthroplasty medialises and lowers the centre of rotation, tensioning and improving the lever arm of the deltoid so it can substitute for the deficient cuff and restore active elevation - it is the treatment of choice in an older, lower-demand patient. Anatomic TSA is contraindicated without a functioning cuff, owing to eccentric glenoid loading and 'rocking-horse' glenoid loosening. Hemiarthroplasty can relieve pain but does not reliably restore elevation. Cuff repair is futile in an irreparable, arthritic shoulder; arthrodesis is a salvage option, not first-line.
learningPoint: Cuff-tear arthropathy with pseudoparalysis in an older patient is the classic indication for reverse TSA; anatomic TSA needs an intact, functioning cuff.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Shoulder & Elbow: cuff-tear arthropathy & arthroplasty

stem: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes has a 5-month history of progressively painful shoulder stiffness. Active and passive external rotation are equally and markedly reduced with the arm at the side, and plain radiographs are normal. Which statement best reflects appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: It is usually self-limiting; a glenohumeral corticosteroid injection plus physiotherapy is reasonable first-line treatment

options.label: B
options.text: Urgent MR arthrography is required to exclude a structural cause before any treatment

options.label: C
options.text: Early arthroscopic capsular release is the first-line treatment of choice

options.label: D
options.text: It is unrelated to diabetes and resolves fully within 6 weeks in all patients

options.label: E
options.text: Long-term oral corticosteroids should be prescribed to abolish the disease

explanation: Equal loss of active and passive external rotation with normal radiographs is the hallmark of adhesive capsulitis (frozen shoulder). It is typically self-limiting over months to a couple of years; first-line management is reassurance, analgesia, physiotherapy and an intra-articular corticosteroid injection, which is most effective in the painful 'freezing' phase. MRI is not mandatory to make the diagnosis. Capsular release or manipulation under anaesthetic is reserved for cases failing conservative treatment. Diabetes is a strong association and predicts a more refractory course; long-term systemic steroids are not indicated.
learningPoint: Equal restriction of active and passive external rotation with normal X-rays indicates adhesive capsulitis; treat conservatively first and escalate to release only if it fails.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Shoulder & Elbow: adhesive capsulitis

stem: A 30-year-old manual labourer falls onto the point of his shoulder. Radiographs show the lateral clavicle grossly elevated relative to the acromion with a 130% increase in coracoclavicular distance compared with the normal side. Clinically there is marked superior prominence of the clavicle with tenting of the overlying skin, and the deformity is irreducible. He is right-hand dominant and keen to return to heavy work. Which Rockwood grade and management are most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Rockwood type III - non-operative management with sling and rehabilitation

options.label: B
options.text: Rockwood type V - surgical coracoclavicular reconstruction

options.label: C
options.text: Rockwood type I - simple analgesia and early mobilisation

options.label: D
options.text: Rockwood type II - non-operative management with reassurance

options.label: E
options.text: Rockwood type VI - closed reduction and broad-arm sling alone

explanation: Gross superior displacement of the lateral clavicle with a large increase in coracoclavicular distance (more than 100%, here 130%) and skin tenting indicates a high-grade type V injury, in which the clavicle has buttonholed superiorly through the deltotrapezial fascia. High-grade injuries, particularly type V in a young heavy-demand worker, are generally managed surgically with coracoclavicular ligament reconstruction. Type I and II are sprains treated non-operatively. Type III is the controversial 'grey zone' usually trialled non-operatively first. Type IV is posterior displacement of the clavicle through (or into) trapezius and Type VI is rare inferior subcoracoid displacement - neither matches the superior-displacement picture here.
learningPoint: Gross irreducible superior clavicle displacement with more than 100% coracoclavicular widening and skin tenting is a Rockwood V - reconstruct, especially in young high-demand patients.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Shoulder & Elbow: acromioclavicular joint injuries
BOAST / BESS guidance on ACJ injuries

stem: A 70-year-old is being consented for elective anatomic total shoulder arthroplasty for primary glenohumeral osteoarthritis. Preoperative CT shows posterior glenoid wear with a biconcave glenoid and posterior subluxation of the humeral head (Walch type B2). Which finding would most strongly favour reverse rather than anatomic total shoulder replacement?

options.label: A
options.text: A small, well-contained osteophyte on the inferior humeral head

options.label: B
options.text: Severe posterior glenoid bone loss with static posterior humeral head subluxation and a deficient/insufficient rotator cuff

options.label: C
options.text: Preserved passive external rotation

options.label: D
options.text: Concentric (Walch type A1) glenoid wear

options.label: E
options.text: Patient age of exactly 70 years

explanation: Anatomic TSA depends on a functioning rotator cuff and a glenoid that can be reliably resurfaced. Severe posterior glenoid erosion with fixed (static) posterior subluxation, and any cuff insufficiency, predisposes an anatomic glenoid component to eccentric loading and early loosening; a reverse prosthesis (often with an augmented or bone-grafted baseplate) is more reliable in this setting. A small humeral osteophyte, preserved external rotation and concentric (A1) wear all favour a standard anatomic replacement, and age of 70 alone does not dictate the implant.
learningPoint: Severe posterior glenoid wear with static subluxation and/or cuff insufficiency favours reverse over anatomic TSA.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Shoulder & Elbow: glenohumeral OA & arthroplasty (Walch classification)
National Joint Registry (NJR) - shoulder arthroplasty report

stem: Eight weeks after an uncomplicated reverse total shoulder arthroplasty, a 76-year-old notices the arm sits in internal rotation and she cannot actively externally rotate it past neutral, although elevation is good. Which is the most likely explanation?

options.label: A
options.text: Teres minor and infraspinatus deficiency

options.label: B
options.text: Axillary nerve palsy causing deltoid paralysis

options.label: C
options.text: Loosening of the glenoid baseplate

options.label: D
options.text: Acromial stress fracture

options.label: E
options.text: Subscapularis over-tensioning

explanation: A reverse prosthesis recruits the deltoid for elevation, but active external rotation still depends on the posterior cuff (infraspinatus and teres minor). When these are deficient, patients retain elevation but lack active external rotation and may show a positive external rotation lag or Hornblower's sign. Deltoid paralysis would abolish elevation; baseplate loosening usually presents later with pain and radiographic change; an acromial stress fracture causes pain and loss of deltoid efficiency; subscapularis over-tensioning limits external rotation passively and is not the classic cause of this early, isolated active external rotation deficit.
learningPoint: After reverse TSA, preserved elevation but absent active external rotation reflects posterior cuff (teres minor/infraspinatus) deficiency - consider a tendon transfer if function is critical.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Shoulder & Elbow: reverse shoulder arthroplasty

stem: During a governance review of a shoulder arthroplasty service, the team is reminded that certain implants have been the subject of a UK regulatory alert after the National Joint Registry flagged higher-than-expected revision rates. Which body issues the formal UK safety alerts that trigger such device recalls and surveillance actions?

options.label: A
options.text: Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA)

options.label: B
options.text: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE)

options.label: C
options.text: British Elbow and Shoulder Society (BESS)

options.label: D
options.text: Care Quality Commission (CQC)

options.label: E
options.text: British Orthopaedic Association (BOA)

explanation: In the UK the MHRA is the competent authority for medical devices: it issues device safety alerts and Field Safety Notices and coordinates recalls, frequently acting on outlier signals identified by the National Joint Registry (NJR). NICE produces clinical guidance and technology appraisals but does not issue device safety alerts; BESS and the BOA are professional bodies that publish standards and pathways; the CQC regulates the quality and safety of care providers, not devices.
learningPoint: The NJR detects outlier implants; the MHRA issues the formal UK device safety alerts and recalls.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Shoulder & Elbow: arthroplasty outcomes
National Joint Registry (NJR) annual report; MHRA device safety alerts

stem: A 55-year-old recreational golfer has anterior shoulder pain with clicking. Arthroscopy confirms a type II SLAP (superior labrum anterior-posterior) lesion with detachment of the biceps-labral anchor, and there is fraying of the long head of biceps tendon. He has good passive range and no instability. Which is the most appropriate surgical option for this age group?

options.label: A
options.text: Biceps tenodesis

options.label: B
options.text: Arthroscopic SLAP repair with suture anchors

options.label: C
options.text: Open Latarjet procedure

options.label: D
options.text: Reverse total shoulder arthroplasty

options.label: E
options.text: Capsular plication

explanation: In patients over about 40 years, arthroscopic SLAP repair gives less predictable results, with higher rates of residual stiffness and persistent pain, particularly when the biceps tendon itself is degenerate. Biceps tenodesis (or tenotomy) addresses the symptomatic biceps-labral complex, reliably relieves pain and allows quicker rehabilitation, and is the preferred option in this older patient with a frayed biceps. SLAP repair is better reserved for younger overhead athletes with a healthy anchor. Latarjet and capsular plication treat instability, and arthroplasty is irrelevant in the absence of arthritis.
learningPoint: In older patients (over about 40) or where the tendon is degenerate, treat a symptomatic type II SLAP with biceps tenodesis/tenotomy rather than labral repair.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Shoulder & Elbow: SLAP lesions & biceps pathology
BESS guidance: biceps and SLAP lesions

stem: Theme: shoulder clinical signs. For each clinical scenario, select the single most likely diagnosis from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Anterior glenohumeral instability

options.label: B
options.text: Multidirectional instability

options.label: C
options.text: Subacromial (rotator cuff) impingement

options.label: D
options.text: Acromioclavicular joint pathology

options.label: E
options.text: Adhesive capsulitis

options.label: F
options.text: Cuff-tear arthropathy

options.label: G
options.text: Subscapularis tear

options.label: H
options.text: Long head of biceps / SLAP lesion

emiLeads.stem: A 19-year-old swimmer with no history of trauma has bilateral shoulder discomfort. There is a positive sulcus sign that does not correct with external rotation, and generalised joint hypermobility (Beighton score 7).

A 63-year-old has chronic shoulder pain and weakness. Radiographs show superior migration of the humeral head with the head articulating against the undersurface of the acromion, and active elevation is severely limited while passive movement is preserved.
A 45-year-old painter has a painful arc between 60 and 120 degrees of abduction with a positive Hawkins-Kennedy test, but full passive range and no radiographic arthritis.

A 40-year-old reports pain localised to the top of the shoulder, worse on cross-body adduction, with tenderness directly over the acromioclavicular joint. The O'Brien test reproduces pain felt 'on top' of the shoulder rather than deep inside.
A 50-year-old has anterior shoulder pain and positive belly-press and bear-hug tests, with increased passive external rotation on the affected side.

explanation: A non-correcting sulcus sign with generalised hypermobility points to multidirectional instability. Static superior head migration with acetabularisation and loss of active (but not passive) elevation is cuff-tear arthropathy. A painful arc with a positive Hawkins-Kennedy sign and preserved passive range is classic subacromial impingement. Pain localised over the AC joint, worse on cross-body adduction, with a positive O'Brien test producing pain felt 'on top' (rather than deep, which would suggest a SLAP lesion), indicates AC joint pathology. Positive belly-press and bear-hug tests with increased passive external rotation indicate a subscapularis tear. These provocative tests are high-yield discriminators in the FRCS clinical and written sections.
learningPoint: Match the provocative sign to the lesion: non-correcting sulcus = MDI; static superior migration = cuff-tear arthropathy; belly-press/bear-hug = subscapularis; cross-body 'top' pain with O'Brien = ACJ.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Shoulder & Elbow: examination and instability

stem: A 46-year-old right-hand-dominant office worker presents with a 5-month history of lateral elbow pain aggravated by gripping and lifting a coffee cup. Examination reproduces pain on resisted wrist extension and resisted extension of the middle finger, with maximal tenderness just distal and anterior to the lateral epicondyle. Plain radiographs are normal. She has already completed a structured course of activity modification, a counterforce brace and physiotherapy with eccentric loading over the past 3 months without benefit. Which structure is the primary site of the underlying pathology?

options.label: A
options.text: Origin of extensor carpi radialis longus

options.label: B
options.text: Origin of extensor carpi radialis brevis

options.label: C
options.text: Origin of extensor digitorum communis

options.label: D
options.text: Posterior interosseous nerve within the arcade of Frohse

options.label: E
options.text: Radiocapitellar joint synovial fold (plica)

explanation: Lateral epicondylitis is a degenerative tendinosis (angiofibroblastic hyperplasia, not true inflammation) of the common extensor origin, and the extensor carpi radialis brevis (ECRB) origin is the principal site involved in the overwhelming majority of cases. The middle-finger extension test loads ECRB because it inserts onto the base of the third metacarpal. ECRL originates more proximally from the supracondylar ridge and is less commonly implicated; EDC is a secondary contributor. Radial tunnel syndrome (PIN compression at the arcade of Frohse) is an important differential when tenderness lies 3-4 cm distal over the mobile wad and there is pain on resisted supination with a positive middle-finger test, but the tenderness localising to the epicondyle here points to ECRB. A symptomatic radiocapitellar plica can mimic tennis elbow but is far less common and typically produces mechanical catching.
learningPoint: Tennis elbow is an ECRB tendinosis (degenerative, not inflammatory); persistent radial-sided pain with tenderness 3-4 cm distally and pain on resisted supination should prompt consideration of radial tunnel syndrome.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Shoulder & Elbow chapter (lateral epicondylitis / ECRB tendinosis)
NICE CKS Tennis elbow (graded conservative management before intervention)

stem: A 52-year-old keen golfer reports medial elbow pain on resisted wrist flexion and pronation, with tenderness over the medial epicondyle. He also describes intermittent tingling of the little and ring fingers. Examination confirms a positive elbow flexion test and a Tinel sign over the cubital tunnel, with reproducible paraesthesiae but no fixed weakness or wasting. Which feature is most important in guiding management and prognosis?

options.label: A
options.text: The presence of concomitant ulnar nerve symptoms

options.label: B
options.text: The exact point of maximal epicondylar tenderness

options.label: C
options.text: Whether the patient is right- or left-hand dominant

options.label: D
options.text: The presence of a traction spur on the medial epicondyle

options.label: E
options.text: Grip strength measured by dynamometer

explanation: Medial epicondylitis (golfer's elbow) is a tendinosis of the common flexor-pronator origin (principally pronator teres and flexor carpi radialis). A substantial proportion of patients have associated ulnar nerve symptoms because the cubital tunnel lies immediately adjacent to the flexor origin. The presence and severity of ulnar nerve involvement is the single most important prognostic and management modifier: it predicts a poorer response to conservative treatment and to simple tendon debridement, and may necessitate concomitant nerve decompression or transposition. It is also the feature most commonly missed. The other options are either non-discriminating (dominance, grip strength) or describe findings that do not materially alter the treatment algorithm in the way ulnar nerve status does.
learningPoint: Always examine the ulnar nerve in suspected golfer's elbow — coexisting cubital tunnel symptoms worsen prognosis and may need to be addressed surgically.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Elbow chapter (medial epicondylitis, flexor-pronator origin)
BSSH ulnar nerve / cubital tunnel guidance

stem: A 39-year-old man falls from a ladder and sustains a closed injury to his dominant elbow. CT confirms a 'terrible triad': a posterior elbow dislocation with a comminuted radial head fracture (more than three fragments, not reconstructable) and a small transverse coronoid tip fracture (Regan-Morrey type I-II). He is taken to theatre. Working from the deepest structure outward, which sequence best reflects the recommended order of repair?

options.label: A
options.text: Lateral collateral ligament repair, then radial head replacement, then coronoid fixation

options.label: B
options.text: Coronoid/anterior capsule, then radial head replacement, then lateral collateral ligament repair, then assess and address medial collateral ligament/hinged external fixator if still unstable

options.label: C
options.text: Radial head replacement first, then lateral collateral ligament, then coronoid

options.label: D
options.text: Medial collateral ligament repair first, then coronoid, then radial head

options.label: E
options.text: Lateral collateral ligament repair, then coronoid, then radial head replacement

explanation: The accepted reconstruction sequence for the terrible triad works from deep to superficial. The coronoid (and anterior capsule) is addressed first because access is lost once the radial head is reconstructed or replaced; small transverse tip fractures may be repaired with a suture lasso, screws, or left if the elbow is stable after the radial head and lateral ligament are restored. The radial head is then fixed or, if non-reconstructable (more than three fragments), replaced with a metallic implant — it must NOT simply be excised in this setting because it is a critical secondary stabiliser against valgus and posterolateral rotatory instability when the ligaments are compromised. The lateral collateral ligament complex (LUCL) is then repaired to its isometric point on the lateral epicondyle, usually with suture anchors. Stability is reassessed; only if the elbow remains unstable (typically persistent subluxation, especially in extension) is the medial collateral ligament repaired and/or a hinged external fixator applied. Note that the anteromedial facet pattern is associated with varus posteromedial rotatory instability rather than the terrible triad, in which the coronoid fracture is characteristically a small transverse tip fragment.
learningPoint: Terrible triad repair proceeds deep-to-superficial — coronoid/anterior capsule, then radial head (replace if non-reconstructable, never excise), then LUCL, then reassess and address MCL/hinged ex-fix only if still unstable. The coronoid fragment is typically a small transverse tip (Regan-Morrey I-II).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Elbow trauma chapter (terrible triad protocol)
BOAST: peri-articular and complex injury management principles (UK)

stem: A 28-year-old woman complains of recurrent painful clicking and a sensation of the elbow 'giving way' when pushing up from a chair, two years after a simple elbow dislocation that was treated non-operatively. Examination shows apprehension as the elbow is brought from flexion towards extension while the forearm is held in supination with a valgus and axial load applied, reproducing her symptoms. Which structure is the essential lesion responsible for this instability pattern?

options.label: A
options.text: Anterior bundle of the medial (ulnar) collateral ligament

options.label: B
options.text: Lateral ulnar collateral ligament (LUCL)

options.label: C
options.text: Annular ligament

options.label: D
options.text: Radial (lateral) collateral ligament proper

options.label: E
options.text: Posterior bundle of the medial collateral ligament

explanation: The clinical picture is posterolateral rotatory instability (PLRI), the commonest pattern of chronic elbow instability and a recognised sequel of simple dislocation. The essential lesion is incompetence of the lateral ulnar collateral ligament (LUCL), which runs from the lateral epicondyle (isometric point near the centre of the capitellum) to the supinator crest of the ulna and resists external rotation/supination of the ulna away from the humerus. The described manoeuvre is the lateral pivot-shift test of O'Driscoll (supination, valgus and axial load with the elbow moved between flexion and extension). The anterior bundle of the MCL is the primary restraint to valgus stress (relevant to throwers), not PLRI. The annular ligament stabilises the proximal radioulnar joint, and the radial collateral ligament proper contributes but is not the key lesion. Symptomatic PLRI is treated by LUCL repair or reconstruction (e.g. with tendon graft).
learningPoint: Chronic instability after a simple elbow dislocation is usually PLRI from LUCL incompetence — diagnosed with the lateral pivot-shift test and treated by LUCL repair/reconstruction.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Elbow chapter (PLRI, O'Driscoll pivot-shift)

stem: A 44-year-old labourer feels a sudden painful 'pop' in the front of his dominant elbow while lifting a heavy load with the elbow flexed. He has anterior elbow bruising, a palpable defect in the antecubital fossa, a positive hook test, and proximal retraction of the muscle belly ('reverse Popeye'). He presents 6 days after injury and is otherwise fit and active. Which is the most appropriate management to optimise functional recovery?

options.label: A
options.text: Non-operative management with early mobilisation and acceptance of strength loss

options.label: B
options.text: Anatomic surgical reattachment of the distal biceps to the radial tuberosity

options.label: C
options.text: Tenodesis of the distal biceps to the brachialis muscle

options.label: D
options.text: Reattachment of the tendon to the ulna

options.label: E
options.text: Delay surgery until 12 weeks to allow inflammation to settle

explanation: Complete distal biceps rupture in an active patient is best managed by early (ideally within around 2-3 weeks) anatomic reattachment to the radial tuberosity, because the biceps is the principal supinator of the forearm and a strong flexor. Non-operative treatment leaves roughly 40-50% loss of supination strength and around 30% loss of flexion strength with reduced endurance, which is unacceptable for a manual worker, so it is reserved for low-demand or unfit patients. Reattachment must be to the radial tuberosity (not the ulna or brachialis) to restore the supination moment arm. Excessive delay leads to tendon retraction, scarring and the potential need for grafting, so waiting 12 weeks is wrong. Fixation options include cortical button, suture anchors, or interosseous screws via a single anterior or two-incision (Boyd-Anderson) approach; the main approach-related risks are PIN injury (single anterior incision) and heterotopic ossification/proximal radioulnar synostosis (classically associated with the two-incision technique).
learningPoint: Acute complete distal biceps ruptures in active patients should be reattached anatomically to the radial tuberosity early, to preserve supination strength; non-op management leaves significant supination weakness.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Elbow chapter (distal biceps rupture; hook test; two-incision vs single-incision)

stem: A 55-year-old man has a 10-month history of numbness in the little and ring fingers and difficulty crossing his fingers. Examination shows first dorsal interosseous and hypothenar wasting, a positive Froment sign and clawing of the ulnar two digits. Nerve conduction studies confirm focal slowing of the ulnar nerve across the elbow. He has failed activity modification and night splinting. There is no bony deformity, prior fracture, or arthritis at the elbow, and the nerve is stable in the groove on flexion. Which surgical procedure is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: In situ decompression of the ulnar nerve at the cubital tunnel

options.label: B
options.text: Anterior subcutaneous transposition of the ulnar nerve

options.label: C
options.text: Anterior submuscular transposition of the ulnar nerve

options.label: D
options.text: Medial epicondylectomy

options.label: E
options.text: Decompression at Guyon's canal in the wrist

explanation: For idiopathic cubital tunnel syndrome with a nerve that is stable in the groove and no bony deformity or prior trauma, simple in situ decompression is the procedure of choice and is the UK/BSSH default. It achieves comparable clinical outcomes to transposition with less surgical morbidity, while avoiding the additional dissection and devascularisation risk of moving the nerve. Anterior transposition (subcutaneous or submuscular) is reserved for situations where the nerve subluxates or dislocates over the medial epicondyle, where there is cubitus valgus or post-traumatic deformity, or in revision surgery. Medial epicondylectomy is an alternative in selected cases but risks valgus instability and flexor-origin disruption. Guyon's canal decompression treats distal (wrist) ulnar compression, which is excluded here because NCS localises the lesion to the elbow and there is dorsal sensory involvement (the dorsal cutaneous branch arises proximal to Guyon's canal). The clinical signs (intrinsic wasting, Froment, clawing) confirm advanced motor involvement warranting surgery.
learningPoint: For uncomplicated idiopathic cubital tunnel syndrome with a stable nerve, in situ decompression is the first-line operation; reserve transposition for a subluxating nerve or post-traumatic/valgus anatomy.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Peripheral nerve / Elbow chapters (cubital tunnel; sites of compression)

stem: A 31-year-old woman falls onto an outstretched hand and sustains an isolated radial head fracture. CT shows the fracture involves a single fragment comprising approximately 30% of the articular surface, depressed and displaced by 3 mm, with a mechanical block to forearm rotation on examination under local anaesthetic. There is no associated ligamentous instability or other fracture. What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Sling immobilisation for 6 weeks then mobilise

options.label: B
options.text: Aspiration of the haemarthrosis and early mobilisation only

options.label: C
options.text: Open reduction and internal fixation of the radial head fragment

options.label: D
options.text: Radial head excision

options.label: E
options.text: Radial head arthroplasty

explanation: This is a Mason type II fracture (partial articular, displaced) with a mechanical block to rotation. A displaced partial-articular fragment that is reconstructable and causes a true mechanical block should undergo ORIF (e.g. with headless compression screws or a low-profile plate placed in the 'safe zone' — the approximately 90-110-degree non-articulating arc, which lies straight lateral with the forearm in neutral rotation, to avoid impinging on the proximal radioulnar joint). Non-displaced or minimally displaced fractures without a block (Mason I) are treated non-operatively with early mobilisation, often after aspiration for pain relief — so options A and B undertreat this fracture. Radial head excision is contraindicated acutely when there is any instability and is generally avoided in younger patients because of late valgus/proximal migration; here it would also be unnecessary as the fragment is reconstructable. Arthroplasty is reserved for comminuted, non-reconstructable Mason III fractures (typically more than three fragments) or as part of complex instability — not for a single reconstructable fragment.
learningPoint: A displaced reconstructable radial head fracture with a mechanical block to rotation (Mason II) is treated by ORIF, with hardware placed in the safe zone; reserve replacement for non-reconstructable comminuted (Mason III) heads.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Elbow trauma chapter (Mason classification; radial head safe zone)
BOAST principles for peri-articular fracture management (UK)

stem: A 71-year-old woman with longstanding rheumatoid arthritis underwent a linked (semi-constrained) total elbow replacement 6 years ago for end-stage destruction. She returns with a 3-month history of increasing pain and a new lytic area around the ulnar component on radiographs. There is no fever and inflammatory markers are equivocal. Beyond excluding infection, which factor is the most important lifelong determinant of the long-term survival of her implant?

options.label: A
options.text: Strict adherence to a permanent lifting restriction

options.label: B
options.text: Maintaining a flexion arc greater than 100 degrees

options.label: C
options.text: Avoidance of impact and contact sport only

options.label: D
options.text: Regular ultrasound surveillance of the soft tissues

options.label: E
options.text: Routine antibiotic prophylaxis for all dental work
