explanation: Total elbow replacement has the poorest survivorship and the highest complication rate of the major joint arthroplasties, and a principal cause of long-term failure is polyethylene wear and aseptic loosening driven by mechanical load (the ulnar component is most at risk). Patients must therefore observe a permanent lifting restriction — commonly cited as no single lift greater than around 2.3 kg (5 lb) and no repetitive lifting of heavier loads — for the life of the implant. This load limitation is the single most important modifiable factor governing implant longevity and is emphasised at consent. A good functional flexion arc (option B, roughly 30-130 degrees) is desirable but is an outcome, not a determinant of survival. Sport avoidance alone (C) is too narrow — everyday loading matters most. Routine ultrasound surveillance (D) is not standard. Whilst infection prophylaxis matters, current UK guidance does not mandate routine antibiotic cover for dental work in most arthroplasty patients, so E is not the best answer. The presentation of progressive pain with periprosthetic lysis should prompt investigation for both infection and aseptic loosening/osteolysis.
learningPoint: Total elbow replacements fail mainly through wear and aseptic loosening — a strict lifelong lifting limit (around 2 kg) is the key modifiable factor for implant survival, and TER has the highest complication rate of the major arthroplasties.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Elbow reconstruction chapter (TER survivorship; lifting restrictions; loosening)
BESS lifting-limitation survey; NJR data on elbow arthroplasty outcomes (UK)

stem: A 34-year-old man sustained a closed head injury and an elbow fracture-dislocation 5 months ago. His fractures have united but he now has a painless flexion arc limited to 60-90 degrees with a hard end-feel, and radiographs show mature-appearing bridging heterotopic ossification anteriorly and posteriorly. His CT confirms the bone is well corticated and the joint surface is congruent. He is keen to regain function. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Continue aggressive passive stretching and dynamic splinting only

options.label: B
options.text: Manipulation under anaesthesia

options.label: C
options.text: Surgical excision of the heterotopic ossification combined with capsular release once the lesion is mature

options.label: D
options.text: A 6-week course of indomethacin to reverse the established ossification

options.label: E
options.text: Total elbow replacement

explanation: This is established post-traumatic heterotopic ossification (HO) causing a bony block (hard end-feel) with a functionally inadequate arc (the functional arc is approximately 30-130 degrees of flexion and roughly 50-50 of pronation-supination). When mature HO causes a mechanical block, the treatment is surgical excision combined with capsular release / open arthrolysis. Historically excision was delayed 12-18 months, but contemporary practice operates once the HO is radiologically mature and clinically stable (often from around 3-6 months, judged on well-corticated margins rather than relying on bone scan), as earlier intervention in suitably selected cases limits secondary cartilage damage and stiffness. Aggressive stretching/splinting and MUA are ineffective and potentially harmful against a bony block, and MUA risks fracture. Indomethacin (and single-dose radiotherapy) is for PROPHYLAXIS against HO formation — it cannot reverse mature bone. TER is inappropriate in a young man with congruent articular surfaces; the problem is extrinsic bone and contracture, treated by excision and release, with HO prophylaxis (indomethacin and/or radiotherapy) given postoperatively to reduce recurrence. The ulnar nerve must be identified and protected, particularly with posteromedial HO.
learningPoint: Mature post-traumatic HO causing a bony block to elbow motion is treated by excision plus capsular release once the lesion is mature/stable; NSAIDs and radiotherapy prevent HO but do not reverse it.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Stiff elbow / HO chapter (functional arc 30-130; HO management)
BOA/BOAST rehabilitation and contracture management principles

subtopic: Distal humerus and complex elbow fractures
stem: Theme: Definitive management of elbow and distal humeral fractures in adults. For each clinical scenario, select the SINGLE most appropriate definitive management from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all. Answers should reflect current UK practice.

options.label: A
options.text: Open reduction and internal fixation with dual (orthogonal or parallel) plating

options.label: B
options.text: Total elbow replacement

options.label: C
options.text: Radial head arthroplasty (metallic)

options.label: D
options.text: Non-operative management with early mobilisation

options.label: E
options.text: Tension-band wire fixation

options.label: F
options.text: Distal humeral hemiarthroplasty

options.label: G
options.text: Fragment excision

explanation: Bicolumnar distal humeral fractures in younger patients with reconstructable surfaces and good bone are managed with anatomical reduction and dual-column plating (orthogonal or parallel constructs), typically via an olecranon osteotomy or triceps-sparing/-reflecting approach (A). In the elderly low-demand patient with a comminuted, non-reconstructable intra-articular distal humeral fracture, total elbow replacement gives more predictable early function than attempted fixation of poor bone (B) — a recognised indication for primary TER. A displaced transverse simple olecranon fracture with a gap and an intact triceps is classically treated by tension-band wiring, which converts the distractive pull of triceps into compression at the articular surface (E); plate fixation is preferred for comminuted or oblique patterns. A non-reconstructable comminuted radial head (more than three fragments) within a terrible triad requires metallic radial head replacement to restore the lateral buttress and stability — excision is contraindicated in this unstable setting (C). An undisplaced olecranon fracture with a preserved extensor mechanism (able to extend against gravity) is managed non-operatively with early mobilisation (D). Distractors: distal humeral hemiarthroplasty (F) is used only in highly selected isolated capitellar/trochlear patterns and is not standard for bicolumnar fractures; olecranon fragment excision (G) is reserved for very small avulsions in low-demand patients.
learningPoint: Match the construct to the pattern and patient: dual-column plating for reconstructable bicolumnar distal humeral fractures, primary TER for comminuted distal humeral fractures in the low-demand elderly, tension-band wiring for simple displaced olecranon fractures, radial head replacement for non-reconstructable heads in instability, and non-op with early motion for undisplaced olecranon fractures with an intact extensor mechanism.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Elbow trauma chapter (distal humerus, olecranon, radial head algorithms)
BOAST: management of peri-articular fractures and early mobilisation principles (UK)

emiLeads.stem: A 38-year-old man sustains a closed, displaced intra-articular bicolumnar (AO type C) fracture of the distal humerus. The articular surface is reconstructable and bone quality is good.

An independent but frail 82-year-old woman with osteoporotic bone sustains a comminuted intra-articular distal humeral fracture (AO type C) that is not amenable to stable internal fixation; she has low functional demands.
A 55-year-old woman has an isolated, transverse, displaced olecranon fracture with a 4 mm gap, an intact triceps mechanism proximally, and no comminution.

A 29-year-old man has a comminuted radial head fracture with more than three articular fragments that cannot be reconstructed, occurring as part of a terrible-triad injury requiring a stable radiocapitellar buttress.
A 60-year-old man sustains an undisplaced olecranon fracture with an intact extensor mechanism and is able to extend the elbow against gravity.

stem: A 72-year-old independent woman falls onto an outstretched hand and sustains a dorsally displaced extra-articular distal radius fracture. After closed reduction and a below-elbow cast, check radiographs show 8° of dorsal tilt, 3 mm of radial shortening and a congruent radiocarpal joint. She has no median nerve symptoms. According to current UK guidance, which is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Continue cast immobilisation and monitor with serial radiographs

options.label: B
options.text: Open reduction and internal fixation with a volar locking plate

options.label: C
options.text: Closed manipulation and percutaneous K-wire fixation

options.label: D
options.text: Bridging external fixation across the wrist

options.label: E
options.text: Immediate removal of cast and early mobilisation

explanation: NICE NG38 supports non-operative management when the reduced position is acceptable. Commonly used radiographic thresholds for an acceptable reduction are dorsal tilt within roughly 10° of neutral, radial shortening under about 3 mm and a congruent joint. With 8° of dorsal tilt, borderline shortening and a congruent joint in an independent but elderly patient, the reduction is acceptable, so cast immobilisation with surveillance radiographs (typically at around 1 and 2 weeks to detect late displacement) is appropriate; surgery is reserved for unacceptable alignment or later displacement. K-wires, a volar plate or external fixation would all be over-treatment of an acceptably reduced fracture.
learningPoint: An acceptably reduced distal radius fracture (tilt ~<10°, shortening <~3 mm, congruent joint) is managed in cast with surveillance radiographs — not routine surgery.
references: NICE NG38 — Fractures (non-complex): assessment and management
BOAST — The Management of Distal Radius Fractures
Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: distal radius fractures

stem: When assessing a true postero-anterior and lateral wrist radiograph of an uninjured adult, which set of values best represents the normal distal radius parameters used as the reference for judging a reduction?

options.label: A
options.text: Radial inclination ~22°, radial height ~11 mm, volar (palmar) tilt ~11°

options.label: B
options.text: Radial inclination ~5°, radial height ~2 mm, dorsal tilt ~25°

options.label: C
options.text: Radial inclination ~45°, radial height ~20 mm, volar tilt ~30°

options.label: D
options.text: Radial inclination ~11°, radial height ~22 mm, volar tilt ~22°

options.label: E
options.text: Radial inclination ~22°, radial height ~11 mm, dorsal tilt ~11°

explanation: The classic normal distal radius reference values are radial inclination of about 22° (range ~21–25°), radial height (length) of about 11–12 mm on the PA view, and a volar (palmar) tilt of about 11° (range ~10–12°) on the lateral view. The articular surface normally tilts volarly, so a dorsal tilt indicates malalignment (the typical deformity after a dorsally displaced fracture). Option E reverses the tilt direction; option D transposes inclination and height; the remaining options are non-physiological. Knowing the normal values underpins judging whether a reduction is acceptable.
learningPoint: Normal distal radius: ~22° radial inclination, ~11 mm radial height, ~11° volar tilt — the articular surface tilts volarly, not dorsally.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: distal radius radiographic parameters
BOAST — The Management of Distal Radius Fractures

stem: A 22-year-old manual worker has a CT-confirmed acute fracture through the proximal pole of the scaphoid with 2 mm of displacement and no humpback deformity. He is keen to return to work quickly. Which management offers the best balance of union and early return to function for this fracture pattern?

options.label: A
options.text: Percutaneous or open headless compression screw fixation

options.label: B
options.text: Below-elbow cast including the thumb for 6 weeks

options.label: C
options.text: Above-elbow scaphoid cast for 12 weeks

options.label: D
options.text: Wrist splint and early mobilisation as pain allows

options.label: E
options.text: Proximal row carpectomy

explanation: Displaced scaphoid fractures (displacement ≥1 mm) and proximal pole fractures have high non-union rates with cast treatment because of the retrograde, predominantly distal-to-proximal blood supply, which leaves the proximal pole vulnerable. Cast nonunion rates for displaced fractures may approach 50%, whereas headless compression screw fixation achieves union in the region of 90–99% with earlier return to function. Internal fixation with a headless compression screw is therefore indicated for displaced and for proximal pole fractures. Casting suits truly undisplaced waist fractures. Proximal row carpectomy is a salvage procedure for established non-union with collapse or arthritis, not an acute injury.
learningPoint: Displaced (≥1 mm) or proximal pole scaphoid fractures → fix with a headless compression screw; cast is reserved for undisplaced waist fractures.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: scaphoid fractures

stem: A 30-year-old presents 2 weeks after a fall with radial-sided wrist pain and a positive Watson (scaphoid shift) test. A PA radiograph shows a 4 mm gap between the scaphoid and lunate; the lateral view shows a scapholunate angle of 80°. There is no fixed deformity or arthritis and the ligament injury is felt to be repairable. What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Open repair of the scapholunate interosseous ligament, often with dorsal capsulodesis and temporary K-wire stabilisation

options.label: B
options.text: Four-corner fusion with scaphoid excision

options.label: C
options.text: Wrist splintage and analgesia alone

options.label: D
options.text: Total wrist arthrodesis

options.label: E
options.text: Proximal row carpectomy

explanation: A scapholunate gap >3 mm (Terry-Thomas sign) with a scapholunate angle >70° (here 80°) indicates scapholunate dissociation with a dorsal intercalated segment instability (DISI) pattern, in which the unopposed lunate extends. In an acute or subacute repairable tear without fixed deformity or arthritis, direct ligament repair — frequently augmented with a dorsal capsulodesis and protected with temporary K-wires — is the procedure of choice to restore carpal alignment. Salvage procedures (four-corner fusion, proximal row carpectomy, total wrist fusion) are reserved for chronic irreparable injury or established SLAC arthritis. Splinting alone will not correct the instability and risks progression to a fixed DISI and SLAC wrist.
learningPoint: SL gap >3 mm + SL angle >70° = scapholunate dissociation (DISI); repair acutely if reparable, salvage only once the wrist is arthritic (SLAC).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: carpal instability

stem: A 24-year-old punches a wall and sustains a fracture of the neck of the fifth metacarpal with 35° of apex-dorsal angulation. There is no rotational deformity, no scissoring of the fingers on flexion, and the skin is intact. What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Neighbour strapping or a buddy/splint and early mobilisation

options.label: B
options.text: Open reduction and plate fixation

options.label: C
options.text: Closed reduction and crossed K-wire fixation

options.label: D
options.text: Intramedullary screw fixation

options.label: E
options.text: Below-elbow cast in full extension for 6 weeks

explanation: Fifth metacarpal neck fractures ('boxer's fractures') tolerate considerable angulation because the mobility of the fourth and fifth carpometacarpal joints compensates functionally. Up to around 40–50° of apex-dorsal angulation at the little finger metacarpal neck is generally accepted if there is no malrotation and no functional deficit. With no rotation and intact skin, non-operative treatment with strapping and early movement gives excellent results; immobilising in full extension is unnecessary and poorly tolerated. The key surgical indication is malrotation (scissoring), not the angle alone. Any wound over the knuckle should raise suspicion of a 'fight bite', which requires exploration, washout and antibiotics.
learningPoint: Boxer's fracture: apex-dorsal angulation up to ~40° is acceptable if there is no rotation — treat with strapping/early motion; rotation (scissoring) is the operative indication.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: metacarpal fractures

subtopic: DRUJ instability with distal radius fracture
stem: A 35-year-old has a distal radius fracture that is anatomically reduced and stabilised with a volar locking plate. Intra-operatively, after fixation, the distal radioulnar joint is grossly unstable in supination and pronation, and there is a displaced fracture of the base of the ulnar styloid. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Assess DRUJ stability through a range of forearm rotation and fix the ulnar styloid base fracture or repair the TFCC to restore stability

options.label: B
options.text: Ignore the DRUJ as radial fixation alone restores stability

options.label: C
options.text: Excise the ulnar styloid fragment

options.label: D
options.text: Perform a Darrach distal ulna excision

options.label: E
options.text: Apply a bridging external fixator

explanation: The foveal insertion of the triangular fibrocartilage complex (TFCC) onto the base of the ulnar styloid is the primary stabiliser of the DRUJ. Most small ulnar styloid tip fractures need no treatment, but a displaced fracture at the styloid base that leaves the DRUJ unstable after radial fixation should be addressed — fixation of the styloid base fragment (restoring the TFCC foveal attachment) or direct TFCC repair, often with temporary immobilisation in supination. Excision of the styloid or a Darrach procedure would remove the very attachment needed for stability and are inappropriate acutely. The instability is soft-tissue/foveal, so a bridging external fixator does not address it.
learningPoint: After fixing the radius, always test the DRUJ: a basilar ulnar styloid fracture with an unstable DRUJ disrupts the TFCC fovea — fix the styloid base or repair the TFCC.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: DRUJ and TFCC
BOAST — The Management of Distal Radius Fractures

stem: Theme: wrist and carpal injuries. For each clinical and radiographic description, select the single most likely diagnosis from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Scapholunate dissociation (DISI)

options.label: B
options.text: Volar (palmar) lunate dislocation

options.label: C
options.text: Perilunate dislocation

options.label: D
options.text: Volar intercalated segment instability (VISI)

options.label: E
options.text: Hook of hamate fracture

options.label: F
options.text: Triquetral dorsal avulsion fracture

options.label: G
options.text: Acute TFCC (foveal) tear

options.label: H
options.text: Kienböck disease

emiLeads.stem: A patient falls onto a hyperextended wrist. The lateral radiograph shows the capitate displaced dorsally relative to the lunate, but the lunate remains aligned with the distal radius (the radius–lunate relationship is maintained).

After a high-energy hyperextension injury, the lateral radiograph shows the lunate tipped and displaced volarly with the capitate aligned with the radius, giving the classic 'spilled teacup' appearance.
A golfer has persistent ulnar-sided palmar wrist pain and tenderness over the hypothenar eminence; pain is reproduced when gripping a club, and a carpal tunnel (or CT) view confirms the bony injury.
A patient has chronic ulnar-sided wrist pain after a fall. The lateral view shows the lunate flexed (tilted volarly) with a scapholunate angle below 30°, and the scaphoid is relatively extended.

A young adult has progressive central dorsal wrist pain with no clear injury; MRI shows reduced signal and sclerosis within the lunate consistent with osteonecrosis, and there is negative ulnar variance.

explanation: Perilunate dislocation: the lunate stays aligned with the radius while the rest of the carpus (capitate) displaces, usually dorsally. Volar lunate dislocation: the lunate itself is extruded volarly ('spilled teacup' on the lateral, 'piece of pie' on the PA), the end of the same spectrum of greater-arc/lesser-arc injury and a cause of acute median nerve compression. Hook of hamate fracture: hypothenar pain in racquet/club/bat sports, seen on a carpal tunnel view or CT. VISI: lunate flexed volarly with an SL angle <30° (versus DISI where the lunate extends and the SL angle >70°). Kienböck disease: lunate osteonecrosis, classically associated with negative ulnar variance.
learningPoint: Perilunate = capitate displaces off a lunate still aligned with the radius; lunate dislocation = the lunate itself spills volarly. DISI lunate extends (SL >70°), VISI lunate flexes (SL <30°).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: carpal instability and dislocations

stem: A 40-year-old stubs the finger and cannot actively extend the distal interphalangeal joint, which rests in about 30° of flexion. A lateral radiograph shows an avulsion fracture involving roughly one-third of the dorsal articular base of the distal phalanx, but the DIP joint is congruent and not volarly subluxed. What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Continuous extension splinting of the DIP joint for 6–8 weeks

options.label: B
options.text: Open reduction and internal fixation

options.label: C
options.text: Extension-block K-wiring of the DIP joint

options.label: D
options.text: DIP joint arthrodesis

options.label: E
options.text: Buddy strapping and immediate free mobilisation

explanation: This is a bony mallet injury. The decision to operate depends mainly on joint congruity and volar subluxation of the distal phalanx, not simply the size of the fragment. With a congruent, non-subluxed joint, the great majority of bony mallets — even with a sizeable fragment — do well with continuous DIP extension splinting (e.g. a Stack or thermoplastic splint) for 6–8 weeks, keeping the PIP joint free to move. Surgery (extension-block wiring or ORIF) is considered when there is volar subluxation of the distal phalanx or a large fragment with an incongruent joint. Arthrodesis is a late salvage option for symptomatic post-traumatic arthritis, not first-line.
learningPoint: Bony mallet with a congruent, non-subluxed DIP → extension splinting; operate mainly for volar subluxation/joint incongruity, not fragment size alone.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: phalangeal fractures and mallet injuries

stem: A 54-year-old right-hand-dominant woman has a 9-month history of nocturnal paraesthesia in the thumb, index and middle fingers, now with constant numbness and early wasting of the thenar eminence. Nerve conduction studies confirm severe median nerve compression at the wrist. A trial of night splinting and a corticosteroid injection have failed. She asks what to expect from surgery. Which statement best reflects the evidence on outcome after carpal tunnel decompression in this situation?

options.label: A
options.text: Nocturnal symptoms typically resolve quickly, but established thenar wasting and constant sensory loss may only partially recover

options.label: B
options.text: Endoscopic release gives a significantly lower long-term recurrence rate than open release

options.label: C
options.text: Routine internal neurolysis of the median nerve improves recovery in severe cases

options.label: D
options.text: Flexor retinaculum reconstruction after division reduces post-operative pillar pain

options.label: E
options.text: Concurrent flexor tenosynovectomy is required in idiopathic disease to prevent recurrence

explanation: In severe long-standing carpal tunnel syndrome with axonal loss (thenar wasting, constant numbness, low-amplitude responses on NCS), decompression reliably relieves the irritative symptoms — night pain and intermittent paraesthesia — but recovery of established motor wasting and fixed sensory deficit is often incomplete and may take many months; patients must be counselled accordingly. Endoscopic and open release give equivalent symptom relief and recurrence; the endoscopic route offers only a faster early return to function at higher cost, not lower recurrence (B). Internal neurolysis (C) adds risk without benefit. Routine retinaculum reconstruction (D) and tenosynovectomy in idiopathic disease (E) are not indicated; simple complete division of the transverse carpal ligament is the standard.
learningPoint: Decompression abolishes night symptoms reliably, but fixed wasting and constant numbness recover slowly and incompletely — counsel before surgery.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: compressive neuropathies
BSSH guidance on the management of carpal tunnel syndrome

stem: A 63-year-old man has Dupuytren disease of the right ring finger with a 45-degree fixed flexion contracture at the metacarpophalangeal (MCP) joint and a 35-degree contracture at the proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joint from a well-defined cord. He is otherwise fit and wants the most durable correction. Hand therapy and splinting alone have not helped. Which factor most strongly predicts a higher risk of recurrence after treatment?

options.label: A
options.text: Onset before the age of 50 with a strong family history and ectopic disease

options.label: B
options.text: A predominantly MCP rather than PIP joint contracture

options.label: C
options.text: Disease confined to a single ray

options.label: D
options.text: Correction achieved by limited fasciectomy rather than needle fasciotomy

options.label: E
options.text: Absence of skin pitting over the cord

explanation: A strong Dupuytren diathesis — early onset (typically before 50), positive family history, bilateral disease, ectopic deposits (Garrod pads, plantar/Ledderhose or penile/Peyronie involvement) and male sex — predicts aggressive disease and higher recurrence whatever the treatment. MCP contractures correct more completely and recur less than PIP contractures, so B is protective, not adverse. Single-ray disease (C) is favourable. Recurrence is generally lower after limited fasciectomy than after needle fasciotomy, so D is the wrong direction. Skin pitting (E) is an isolated local sign; the full diathesis cluster in A is the strongest recurrence predictor.
learningPoint: Dupuytren diathesis (early onset, family history, bilateral/ectopic disease) is the dominant predictor of recurrence — counsel and choose intervention accordingly.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: Dupuytren disease

stem: A 28-year-old labourer lacerates the flexor tendons of his index finger in zone 2 with a clean knife. He undergoes primary repair within 24 hours using a four-strand core suture with a circumferential epitendinous suture. Which statement about the repair and its rehabilitation best reflects current best practice?

options.label: A
options.text: An early active motion protocol is appropriate because a four-strand repair has sufficient strength to tolerate it

options.label: B
options.text: The hand should be immobilised in a cast for four weeks before any movement is begun

options.label: C
options.text: Reducing the core suture from four to two strands improves the repair's resistance to gap formation

options.label: D
options.text: The A2 and A4 pulleys must both be completely divided to allow tendon glide

options.label: E
options.text: The epitendinous suture contributes nothing to repair strength and is purely cosmetic

explanation: Repair strength is roughly proportional to the number of core suture strands crossing the repair, plus a peripheral epitendinous suture. A modern multi-strand (four- to six-strand) core repair with an epitendinous suture is strong enough to permit controlled early active motion, which reduces adhesions and improves the ultimate range compared with prolonged immobilisation — so A is correct and B is outdated. More strands increase, not decrease, resistance to gapping; going from four to two strands weakens the repair, so C is reversed. The A2 and (especially) A4 pulleys should be preserved where possible to prevent bowstringing; venting a small part of a pulley is acceptable but complete division of both is avoided (D). The epitendinous suture adds meaningful strength and reduces gapping (E is false).
learningPoint: A multi-strand core repair plus an epitendinous suture is strong enough for controlled early active motion, which minimises adhesions in zone 2.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: flexor tendon injuries
BSSH flexor tendon repair and rehabilitation guidance

stem: A 60-year-old woman has painful basal thumb osteoarthritis affecting pinch and grip. Examination shows a positive grind test at the thumb carpometacarpal (CMC) joint; radiographs show isolated trapeziometacarpal joint-space loss with osteophytes but a preserved scaphotrapeziotrapezoid (STT) joint (Eaton stage II–III). She has had activity modification, a thumb spica splint and an intra-articular steroid injection with only temporary relief, and now wants surgery. Which statement best reflects the evidence guiding her surgical options?

options.label: A
options.text: Trapeziectomy alone gives outcomes equivalent to trapeziectomy with ligament reconstruction and tendon interposition, with fewer complications

options.label: B
options.text: Adding ligament reconstruction and tendon interposition (LRTI) reliably improves long-term grip strength over simple trapeziectomy

options.label: C
options.text: CMC arthrodesis is the procedure of choice for a low-demand patient seeking maximal mobility

options.label: D
options.text: Total joint (implant) arthroplasty has the lowest revision rate of all options at ten years

options.label: E
options.text: The presence of pantrapezial arthritis is an absolute contraindication to any form of trapeziectomy

explanation: Multiple randomised trials and systematic reviews (including a Cochrane review) show no clinically important difference in pain, function or strength between simple trapeziectomy and trapeziectomy with LRTI or with tendon interposition, while the more complex procedures add operating time and complications — so simple trapeziectomy remains a reasonable default and A is correct. B overstates LRTI (no reliable strength advantage). Arthrodesis sacrifices motion and is reserved for high-demand manual workers, not a patient wanting mobility (C). Implant arthroplasty can restore strength and motion quickly but does not have the lowest long-term revision rate (D). Pantrapezial disease may modify the choice (it makes STT involvement relevant) but is not an absolute contraindication to trapeziectomy (E).
learningPoint: For basal thumb OA, simple trapeziectomy matches LRTI/interposition outcomes with fewer complications — added soft-tissue procedures have not proven superior.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: thumb CMC arthritis
Cochrane review — surgery for thumb (trapeziometacarpal) osteoarthritis

stem: A 34-year-old man presents 36 hours after a clenched-fist injury sustained in a fight, with a 6 mm laceration over the dorsum of the right middle-finger MCP joint. The finger is swollen and painful with pain on movement, and there is a small amount of purulent discharge. Which is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Surgical exploration and debridement of the MCP joint with washout, plus broad antibiotics covering oral flora including Eikenella corrodens

options.label: B
options.text: Primary closure of the wound in the emergency department with oral flucloxacillin and outpatient review

options.label: C
options.text: A short course of oral co-amoxiclav and a wound check in 48 hours, avoiding surgery

options.label: D
options.text: Tetanus prophylaxis and a simple dressing, with reassurance that dorsal MCP wounds rarely become infected

options.label: E
options.text: Aspiration of the joint in clinic followed by an intra-articular antibiotic injection

explanation: A laceration over the dorsum of the MCP joint after a punch is a 'fight bite' until proven otherwise: the extensor hood and joint capsule are breached with the finger flexed, inoculating the joint with human oral flora. With established infection and pus, this needs formal surgical exploration, extensor/joint inspection, debridement and washout, the wound left open, and broad antibiotics covering oral anaerobes and Eikenella corrodens (co-amoxiclav first-line; in true penicillin allergy, doxycycline plus metronidazole, with microbiology advice for severe/IV cases) — so A is correct. These wounds must never be primarily closed (B). Antibiotics alone without surgical exploration risk a septic joint and osteomyelitis (C). Dismissing the injury (D) or relying on aspiration/intra-articular antibiotics (E) is unsafe. Always also obtain radiographs to look for a retained tooth fragment or fracture.
learningPoint: Any wound over the dorsal MCP after a punch is a fight bite — explore, washout, leave open, and cover Eikenella; never close primarily.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: hand infections
BSSH guidance on hand infections / 'fight bite' injuries
NICE NG184 — Human and animal bites: antimicrobial prescribing

subtopic: Compression neuropathies of the upper limb
stem: Theme: upper-limb compression neuropathies. For each clinical picture, select the single most likely site or syndrome of nerve compression from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Carpal tunnel syndrome (median nerve at the wrist)

options.label: B
options.text: Cubital tunnel syndrome (ulnar nerve at the elbow)

options.label: C
options.text: Guyon's canal compression (ulnar nerve at the wrist)

options.label: D
options.text: Posterior interosseous nerve / radial tunnel syndrome

options.label: E
options.text: Anterior interosseous nerve syndrome

options.label: F
options.text: Pronator syndrome (median nerve in the proximal forearm)

options.label: G
options.text: Wartenberg syndrome (superficial radial nerve)

options.label: H
options.text: Thoracic outlet syndrome (lower trunk / C8–T1)

emiLeads.stem: A 45-year-old cyclist has numbness of the little and ring fingers and weakness of the intrinsic muscles, but sensation over the dorsum of the ulnar hand and the hypothenar eminence is normal. Froment's sign is positive.

A 50-year-old keyboard worker has aching in the proximal volar forearm and median-type sensory symptoms that include numbness over the thenar eminence (palmar cutaneous territory), with no night waking and a negative Tinel's at the wrist.

A 30-year-old presents with sudden inability to flex the interphalangeal joint of the thumb and the distal interphalangeal joint of the index finger, producing an abnormal pinch, with no sensory loss whatsoever.

A 38-year-old manual worker has deep aching pain over the dorsoradial forearm a few centimetres distal to the lateral epicondyle, reproduced by resisted supination and resisted long-finger extension, with no sensory loss and no true weakness of wrist or finger extension.
A young woman with a cervical rib has aching and paraesthesia along the medial forearm and little finger, intrinsic wasting, and symptoms reproduced by overhead positioning of the arm.

explanation: Guyon's canal lesions spare the dorsal ulnar sensory branch (which arises proximal to the wrist) and the palmar cutaneous territory, distinguishing them from cubital tunnel syndrome. Pronator syndrome involves the median nerve in the forearm and characteristically affects the palmar cutaneous branch (thenar/palmar sensation) and lacks night symptoms, unlike carpal tunnel syndrome where the palmar cutaneous branch is spared. Anterior interosseous syndrome is purely motor (FPL and FDP to the index — the abnormal 'OK' sign) with no sensory loss. Radial tunnel / PIN syndrome causes pain on resisted supination and middle-finger extension without sensory change; pain-predominant radial tunnel has no motor loss, whereas a true PIN palsy produces finger-drop. Lower-trunk thoracic outlet syndrome (often a cervical rib) gives C8–T1 symptoms with medial forearm involvement and intrinsic wasting.
learningPoint: Localise ulnar and median lesions by which sensory branches are spared — the dorsal ulnar and palmar cutaneous branches arise proximal to the wrist and distinguish wrist-level from elbow/forearm-level compression.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: compressive neuropathies
BSSH guidance on upper-limb compression neuropathies

stem: Theme: tendon transfers for irrecoverable peripheral nerve palsy in the hand. For each deficit and reconstructive goal, select the single most appropriate tendon transfer from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Extensor indicis proprius (EIP) to abductor pollicis brevis (opponensplasty)

options.label: B
options.text: Pronator teres to extensor carpi radialis brevis

options.label: C
options.text: Flexor carpi radialis (or FCU) to extensor digitorum communis

options.label: D
options.text: Palmaris longus to rerouted extensor pollicis longus

options.label: E
options.text: Flexor digitorum superficialis (ring) to the lateral bands or A1/A2 pulley (Zancolli lasso) for intrinsic clawing

options.label: F
options.text: Brachioradialis to flexor pollicis longus

options.label: G
options.text: Extensor carpi radialis longus to flexor digitorum profundus

options.label: H
options.text: Abductor digiti minimi transfer (Huber)

emiLeads.stem: A patient with a low median nerve palsy has lost thumb opposition (cannot abduct and rotate the thumb to the small finger) but has preserved finger flexors and extensors. The aim is to restore opposition.

A patient with a high radial nerve palsy after a healed humeral shaft fracture has wrist drop and finger drop with no recovery at 6 months. To restore wrist extension as part of the standard transfer set.
In the same high radial nerve palsy, to restore extension of the metacarpophalangeal joints of the fingers.

A patient with a low ulnar nerve palsy has clawing of the ring and little fingers with hyperextension at the MCP joints and poor coordinated flexion. To correct the claw and restore integrated MCP flexion.
A patient with an isolated high median nerve palsy has lost active flexion of the thumb interphalangeal joint (paralysed flexor pollicis longus). To restore thumb IP flexion.

explanation: Low median palsy abolishes thumb opposition; the EIP opponensplasty (routed around the ulnar border of the wrist to abductor pollicis brevis) is a standard restoration (a Huber/ADM transfer is used mainly in congenital thumb hypoplasia). The classic radial-nerve transfer set restores wrist extension with pronator teres to ECRB, finger MCP extension with FCR (or FCU) to EDC, and thumb extension with palmaris longus to a rerouted EPL. For low ulnar palsy with clawing, an FDS-based Zancolli 'lasso' or transfer to the lateral bands corrects MCP hyperextension and restores coordinated flexion. In high median palsy the FPL is paralysed and brachioradialis to FPL restores thumb IP flexion. General principles: a transferred muscle must be expendable, of adequate excursion and power, ideally synergistic, with supple joints, through a suitable line of pull, and dedicated to one function.
learningPoint: Learn the standard radial-palsy transfer set (PT→ECRB, FCR/FCU→EDC, PL→EPL) and EIP opponensplasty for low median palsy — alongside the rules of tendon transfer.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: tendon transfers
BSSH guidance on tendon transfers for nerve palsy

stem: A 64-year-old man reports several months of increasing clumsiness doing up buttons, an unsteady wide-based gait and two falls. Examination shows a positive Hoffmann sign bilaterally, hyperreflexia at the knees, an upgoing right plantar and inverted supinator jerks. MRI demonstrates multilevel cervical canal stenosis with cord signal change at C5/6. Which feature is the single strongest indication that surgical decompression rather than continued observation is appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Axial neck pain refractory to analgesia

options.label: B
options.text: Progressive functional decline with objective myelopathic signs and cord signal change

options.label: C
options.text: Radiological canal diameter less than 13 mm at one level

options.label: D
options.text: A single episode of Lhermitte phenomenon

options.label: E
options.text: Reduced cervical range of movement on flexion–extension

explanation: The decision to operate in degenerative cervical myelopathy is driven by the clinical syndrome — progressive, or moderate-to-severe, functional impairment (e.g. mJOA decline) supported by objective long-tract signs — far more than by any single radiological number. Surgery aims to halt progression: established progressive deficits with intramedullary signal change predict ongoing deterioration if left untreated and form the accepted operative trigger. Axial neck pain, an isolated Lhermitte episode, a static canal measurement and reduced range of movement are each individually weak or non-specific and do not by themselves justify decompression. Genuinely mild, non-progressive myelopathy may reasonably be observed with structured follow-up, which is exactly why demonstrated progression carries the weight.
learningPoint: Operate for cervical myelopathy on the basis of progressive/moderate-to-severe functional impairment with objective signs — not on a canal-diameter number alone.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Spine: cervical degenerative disease/myelopathy
BASS/SBNS UK consensus on degenerative cervical myelopathy

stem: A 47-year-old woman has a 6-week history of severe pain radiating from the neck into the lateral forearm and thumb, with paraesthesia of the thumb and index finger. Examination shows a depressed biceps reflex and weakness of elbow flexion and wrist extension; the rest of the neurological examination is normal and there are no myelopathic signs. Which nerve root is most likely compressed?

options.label: A
options.text: C5

options.label: B
options.text: C6

options.label: C
options.text: C7

options.label: D
options.text: C8

options.label: E
options.text: T1

explanation: The pattern — pain to the thumb, paraesthesia of the thumb/index, weak elbow flexion and wrist extension, and a diminished biceps (and brachioradialis) reflex — localises to the C6 root, typically from a C5/6 disc or foraminal lesion. C5 affects deltoid and shoulder abduction with the regimental-badge area. C7 affects triceps, wrist flexion and finger extension and the middle finger, with a reduced triceps reflex. C8 affects the finger flexors and intrinsic function with little-finger sensory change. T1 affects the intrinsics (interossei) with medial forearm sensation. Reflex level is the most reliable discriminator between C6 (biceps/brachioradialis) and C7 (triceps).
learningPoint: C6 radiculopathy = thumb/index sensory change, weak elbow flexion and wrist extension, depressed biceps/brachioradialis reflex (usually C5/6).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Spine: cervical radiculopathy/dermatomal mapping

stem: A 38-year-old man presents to the emergency department with 18 hours of severe low back pain after lifting, now with bilateral leg pain, difficulty initiating micturition and a sensation of 'sitting on cotton wool'. Examination reveals reduced sensation around the anus and perineum and a lax anal tone. He last passed urine 9 hours ago and the bladder is palpable. What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Admit for 48 hours of bed rest and reassess neurology in the morning

options.label: B
options.text: Arrange an urgent (emergency) MRI of the whole spine and refer to spinal surgery for consideration of decompression

options.label: C
options.text: Request outpatient MRI within 2 weeks and start gabapentin

options.label: D
options.text: Insert a urinary catheter and discharge with safety-netting advice

options.label: E
options.text: Arrange urgent nerve conduction studies before any imaging

explanation: This is cauda equina syndrome: bilateral radiculopathy, saddle anaesthesia, reduced anal tone and bladder dysfunction, with a palpable bladder and difficulty voiding signalling impending retention. UK standards mandate emergency MRI — of the whole spine, as the compressive level cannot be assumed — and immediate referral to the on-call spinal service, with decompression undertaken as an emergency once confirmed. Delaying for bed rest, requesting an 'urgent outpatient' scan, or proceeding to surgery without imaging is unsafe, and nerve conduction studies have no role in the acute pathway. Catheterising and discharging abandons the emergency (a post-void residual may be measured, but only as part of admission, not as a substitute for it). BASS/SBNS standards support decompression as soon as practicable to maximise recovery of bladder, bowel and sexual function, and once painless retention with overflow (CES-R) supervenes the prognosis worsens.
learningPoint: Suspected cauda equina = emergency whole-spine MRI and immediate spinal referral for urgent decompression; never observe or send home.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Spine: lumbar disc herniation/cauda equina
BASS & SBNS Standards of Care for Cauda Equina Syndrome
NICE NG59 Low back pain and sciatica; GIRFT spinal pathway

stem: A 70-year-old man describes bilateral buttock and calf aching with heaviness on walking 100 metres, relieved by sitting or leaning forward over a supermarket trolley. He can cycle without symptoms. Distal pulses are normal. MRI shows multilevel degenerative central canal narrowing without spondylolisthesis. After a trial of physiotherapy and analgesia his walking distance remains limiting. Which is the most appropriate surgical option?

options.label: A
options.text: Instrumented posterolateral fusion of the stenotic levels

options.label: B
options.text: Decompression alone (e.g. laminectomy/undercutting decompression) of the symptomatic levels

options.label: C
options.text: Lumbar total disc replacement

options.label: D
options.text: Microdiscectomy at the most stenotic level

options.label: E
options.text: Vertebroplasty at the affected levels

explanation: This is neurogenic claudication from degenerative lumbar stenosis: positional (flexion-relieved) symptoms, preserved cycling tolerance and normal pulses distinguish it from vascular claudication. With stenosis but no instability or significant deformity/spondylolisthesis, decompression alone gives good results; the Försth Swedish Spinal Stenosis Study (NEJM 2016) showed that adding fusion confers no reliable clinical benefit while increasing cost and morbidity in this group. (By contrast the SLIP trial found fusion beneficial specifically in degenerative spondylolisthesis, which is absent here.) Fusion is therefore reserved for accompanying instability, significant deformity or degenerative spondylolisthesis with mechanical back pain. Disc replacement, isolated microdiscectomy and vertebroplasty do not address multilevel central canal stenosis.
learningPoint: For lumbar stenosis without instability or deformity, decompression alone is the procedure of choice (Försth) — adding fusion is not routinely justified; reserve fusion for instability/spondylolisthesis (SLIP).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Spine: lumbar spinal stenosis
Försth et al. NEJM 2016 (Swedish Spinal Stenosis Study); Ghogawala et al. SLIP trial NEJM 2016

stem: A 14-year-old competitive gymnast has several months of activity-related low back pain that is worse on lumbar extension. There is no leg pain and the neurological examination is normal. Plain radiographs are unremarkable. Which imaging investigation is most appropriate to confirm an active (acute) pars interarticularis stress lesion?

options.label: A
options.text: Oblique lumbar radiographs to look for the 'Scottie dog' collar

options.label: B
options.text: MRI of the lumbar spine (with attention to the pars/pedicles)

options.label: C
options.text: Standing flexion–extension radiographs

options.label: D
options.text: Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DXA)

options.label: E
options.text: Whole-spine EOS imaging

explanation: In an adolescent athlete with extension-related back pain and a suspected pars stress reaction, MRI is the preferred next investigation: it detects pre-lytic marrow/pedicle oedema (an early, potentially reversible stress reaction) without ionising radiation, which matters in a young patient, and also shows an established defect. Oblique radiographs are no longer recommended (added dose, poor sensitivity, and the 'Scottie dog' collar appears only once a defect is established). Flexion–extension films assess listhetic instability, not lysis activity. DXA assesses bone density and EOS is for deformity/alignment — neither answers the question. Where MRI is equivocal, CT best defines the bony anatomy and SPECT/CT can confirm metabolic activity, but MRI is the appropriate first cross-sectional study.
learningPoint: Young athlete with extension-related back pain and normal X-rays → MRI first to catch an active (oedematous, reversible) pars stress reaction.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Spine/Paediatrics: spondylolysis

stem: A 12-year-old girl with adolescent idiopathic scoliosis has a right thoracic curve measuring 28 degrees by the Cobb method. She is premenarchal, Risser grade 0 and Tanner stage 2, and a repeat radiograph 5 months later shows the curve has progressed to 33 degrees. There is no pain and her neurological examination is normal. What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Reassure and repeat radiographs once skeletally mature

options.label: B
options.text: Prescribe a full-time thoracolumbosacral orthosis (brace)

options.label: C
options.text: Refer for posterior spinal instrumented fusion now

options.label: D
options.text: Scoliosis-specific physiotherapy exercises alone

options.label: E
options.text: Anterior vertebral body tethering

explanation: Bracing is indicated for a skeletally immature patient with substantial remaining growth (premenarchal, Risser 0, Tanner 2) and a curve in the 25–40 degree range, particularly when documented progression has occurred. The BrAIST trial showed bracing significantly reduces progression to the surgical threshold, with a dose–response relationship to hours worn. Observation alone is appropriate below ~25 degrees in a growing child but not for a progressing curve already past 30 degrees. Surgery is generally reserved for curves exceeding ~45–50 degrees (or progressive curves that fail bracing). Exercises alone do not reliably prevent progression at this magnitude, and vertebral body tethering is a growth-modulation option with maturing evidence — not the standard first-line for this curve.
learningPoint: Skeletally immature AIS with a 25–40 degree progressing curve → brace (BrAIST: benefit is dose-dependent on hours worn).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Paediatric spine: AIS

stem: Theme: lumbar disc prolapse and nerve-root localisation. For each clinical picture below, select the single nerve root most likely to be compressed. A standard posterolateral (paracentral) disc herniation typically affects the traversing root. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: L2 root

options.label: B
options.text: L3 root

options.label: C
options.text: L4 root

options.label: D
options.text: L5 root

options.label: E
options.text: S1 root

options.label: F
options.text: S2 root

options.label: G
options.text: Conus medullaris

options.label: H
options.text: Lumbar sympathetic chain

emiLeads.stem: A 40-year-old man has pain radiating to the dorsum of the foot and great toe, weak great-toe extension (extensor hallucis longus) and weak ankle dorsiflexion, with a normal ankle jerk. Imaging shows a paracentral L4/5 disc herniation.
A 45-year-old woman has calf and posterolateral leg pain radiating to the sole and little toe, weak ankle plantarflexion noted on single-leg heel raises, and an absent ankle jerk.
A 55-year-old man has anterior thigh and medial leg pain, weak knee extension, a reduced knee jerk and a positive femoral nerve stretch test; MRI shows a paracentral L3/4 herniation.

A 60-year-old man has an L5 radicular syndrome (dorsum of foot, weak great-toe and ankle dorsiflexion, preserved ankle jerk), but the only abnormality on MRI is a large far-lateral (foraminal) disc herniation at the L5/S1 level. Which root is compressed?

explanation: At the lumbar levels a posterolateral (paracentral) disc spares the exiting root and compresses the traversing root, which is the exiting root of the level below: an L4/5 paracentral disc therefore hits L5 (dorsum of foot/EHL weakness, intact ankle jerk), and an L5/S1 paracentral disc hits S1 (sole/little toe, weak plantarflexion, absent ankle jerk). An L3/4 paracentral disc compresses the traversing L4 root (medial leg, weak knee extension, reduced knee jerk, positive femoral stretch). A far-lateral (foraminal) herniation is the exception — it compresses the exiting root at that level, so a far-lateral L5/S1 disc compresses L5 (not S1), reproducing an L5 pattern despite the lower disc level. Recognising the paracentral-versus-foraminal rule is the discriminator tested here.
learningPoint: Paracentral lumbar disc = traversing (lower) root; far-lateral/foraminal disc = exiting (same-level) root — so a far-lateral L5/S1 disc gives an L5, not S1, syndrome.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Spine: lumbar disc herniation and root-level localisation

stem: A 68-year-old woman with adult degenerative lumbar scoliosis is being assessed for corrective surgery for progressive stooping and back pain. Standing full-length EOS radiographs show a pelvic incidence of 60 degrees, lumbar lordosis of 20 degrees, a pelvic tilt of 32 degrees and a sagittal vertical axis of +9 cm. Which statement best explains her clinical presentation and the principal surgical aim?

options.label: A
options.text: Her alignment parameters are within normal limits, so her symptoms are unrelated to sagittal balance

options.label: B
options.text: There is a large pelvic-incidence–lumbar-lordosis mismatch with positive sagittal imbalance, and she is compensating by pelvic retroversion; the surgical aim is to restore lumbar lordosis to match pelvic incidence

options.label: C
options.text: The high pelvic tilt indicates anteversion of the pelvis and the aim is to increase thoracic kyphosis

options.label: D
options.text: Coronal Cobb angle is the only validated predictor of disability in adult deformity, so sagittal parameters can be disregarded

options.label: E
options.text: The priority is maximal coronal curve correction; sagittal vertical axis does not influence health-related quality of life

explanation: In adult spinal deformity, sagittal alignment drives disability more than coronal Cobb angle. Ideal lumbar lordosis approximates pelvic incidence (PI − LL within roughly ±10 degrees); here PI 60 with LL only 20 gives a PI–LL mismatch of about 40 degrees. To keep the head over the pelvis she retroverts the pelvis (raised pelvic tilt ~32 degrees; normal <20) and the trunk still drifts forward (SVA +9 cm; aim <5 cm). These map onto the SRS–Schwab sagittal modifiers, which correlate with health-related quality of life. The operative goal is to restore lordosis so that PI–LL, pelvic tilt and SVA are normalised — commonly requiring osteotomy and/or interbody work — not to chase coronal correction while ignoring the sagittal plane. High pelvic tilt reflects compensatory retroversion, not anteversion.
learningPoint: In adult deformity, aim to match lumbar lordosis to pelvic incidence (PI−LL ≈ 0) and normalise pelvic tilt and SVA — sagittal balance, not coronal Cobb angle, predicts disability.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Spine: adult spinal deformity/sagittal balance

subtopic: Cervical spine trauma - imaging and clearance
stem: A 34-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after a high-speed motorcycle collision. He is alert (GCS 15), haemodynamically stable and complaining of central neck pain. He has no focal neurological deficit, but reports paraesthesia in both hands that has now resolved. He cannot actively rotate his neck 45 degrees to either side because of pain. There is no distracting injury. According to current UK guidance, what is the most appropriate next imaging investigation of his cervical spine?

options.label: A
options.text: Three-view plain radiographs of the cervical spine

options.label: B
options.text: CT of the cervical spine

options.label: C
options.text: MRI of the cervical spine as the first-line investigation

options.label: D
options.text: Flexion-extension radiographs of the cervical spine

options.label: E
options.text: No imaging; clinically clear the cervical spine and remove the collar

explanation: NICE NG41 recommends the Canadian C-spine rule to determine whether imaging is needed in alert, stable adults with suspected cervical spine injury. Paraesthesia in the extremities is itself a high-risk criterion that mandates imaging regardless of the other findings; in addition, this patient cannot actively rotate his neck 45 degrees left and right. He therefore fails the rule and requires imaging. NICE specifies that when imaging is indicated in adults, CT is the first-line modality - it has superseded plain radiographs for the traumatic adult cervical spine because of its higher sensitivity for fractures. Plain radiographs are reserved for children under 16 in this pathway, not adults. MRI is added after CT if there is a neurological abnormality attributable to the cord, if ligamentous injury is suspected, or to assess cord/soft-tissue injury - it is not the first-line study. Flexion-extension views have no role in the acute clearance of the obtunded or acutely injured neck and risk provoking displacement. The transient bilateral hand paraesthesia mandates a low threshold and cannot be ignored even though it has resolved.
learningPoint: In alert adults who fail the Canadian C-spine rule, CT - not plain radiography - is the NICE NG41 first-line investigation for the cervical spine; add MRI after CT if there are neurological signs or suspected ligamentous/cord injury. Paraesthesia in the extremities is a high-risk criterion that on its own requires imaging.
references: NICE NG41 - Spinal injury: assessment and initial management (Canadian C-spine rule; CT first-line in adults)
Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Spine Trauma chapter (cervical spine clearance)
BOAST - Spinal Clearance in the Trauma Patient

stem: A 42-year-old woman falls from a horse and sustains an L1 burst fracture. She is neurologically intact. CT shows retropulsion of bone with 40% canal compromise, 20 degrees of segmental kyphosis and loss of 45% of anterior vertebral body height. MRI demonstrates disruption of the posterior ligamentous complex with high signal in the interspinous space and a widened interspinous distance. Which single factor in this case most strongly indicates the need for surgical stabilisation rather than non-operative management?

options.label: A
options.text: The 40% canal compromise from bony retropulsion

options.label: B
options.text: The disruption of the posterior ligamentous complex

options.label: C
options.text: The 45% loss of anterior vertebral body height

options.label: D
options.text: The 20 degrees of segmental kyphosis

options.label: E
options.text: The fact that the fracture is at the thoracolumbar junction

explanation: The integrity of the posterior ligamentous complex (PLC) is the pivotal determinant of mechanical stability in thoracolumbar injuries and is weighted heavily in both the Thoracolumbar Injury Classification and Severity Score (TLICS) and the AO Spine system. In TLICS, PLC disruption scores 3 points; combined with a burst morphology (2 points) this gives a total of 5, where 4 or more favours surgery. An intact PLC allows even a significant burst fracture in a neurologically intact patient to be managed non-operatively (for example in a brace or cast), as supported by randomised data. By contrast, the degree of canal compromise correlates poorly with neurological outcome in the intact patient and remodels over time; isolated thresholds of kyphosis or height loss are weaker, more controversial predictors of instability and do not by themselves mandate fixation. Although the thoracolumbar junction is biomechanically vulnerable, location alone does not dictate surgery. Here, MRI-confirmed PLC failure converts a fracture that might otherwise be braced into an unstable injury requiring stabilisation.
learningPoint: Posterior ligamentous complex integrity - best assessed on MRI - is the key swing factor between operative and non-operative management of thoracolumbar burst fractures; canal compromise alone in a neurologically intact patient is not.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Thoracolumbar Spine Trauma (TLICS, AO Spine classification)
Vaccaro et al. - Thoracolumbar Injury Classification and Severity Score (TLICS)
BOAST - The Management of Patients with Spinal Column Injury

stem: A 48-year-old man presents to the emergency department at 22:00 with a 2-day history of severe low back pain radiating into both legs, new difficulty initiating micturition, and a sensation that his perineum feels numb when wiping. Examination reveals reduced perianal sensation and a patulous anal tone. The on-call orthopaedic registrar is in theatre. What is the single most appropriate immediate management step?

options.label: A
options.text: Arrange an emergency whole-spine MRI now, without waiting to discuss with the spinal team

options.label: B
options.text: Insert a urinary catheter and measure the post-void residual volume before requesting any imaging

options.label: C
options.text: Admit for analgesia and arrange an MRI on the next available routine list in the morning

options.label: D
options.text: Request plain lumbar spine radiographs to look for a destructive or fracture lesion first

options.label: E
options.text: Transfer the patient immediately to the regional spinal centre before any imaging is performed

explanation: This patient has clinical features of cauda equina syndrome (CES) - bilateral radicular pain, urinary hesitancy, saddle sensory disturbance and reduced anal tone. The BASS/SBNS national standards and the GIRFT cauda equina pathway state that any patient with suspected CES should undergo emergency MRI of the whole spine as soon as possible (and certainly without delay for office hours), and crucially that imaging should not be delayed pending discussion with, or review by, the spinal surgical team. Emergency MRI should be available 24/7 at the admitting hospital. A scan-positive result then triggers urgent referral and surgical decompression as soon as is safely possible, because delay correlates with worse continence and neurological recovery. Catheter residual volume is useful supportive information but must not delay the definitive investigation. Plain films do not exclude CES and waste time. Transferring an unimaged patient is inappropriate - the diagnosis is confirmed by MRI at the admitting unit, and transfer without imaging risks moving a patient who does not need transfer or delaying one who does.
learningPoint: Suspected cauda equina syndrome mandates emergency whole-spine MRI at the admitting hospital without waiting for the spinal team - imaging first, discussion in parallel.
references: BASS / SBNS Standards for the Management of Cauda Equina Syndrome
GIRFT national cauda equina syndrome pathway
NICE NG59 - Low back pain and sciatica (red flags)

subtopic: Metastatic spinal cord compression - acute management
stem: A 67-year-old man with known prostate carcinoma presents with 1 week of progressive thoracic back pain and 24 hours of bilateral leg weakness, now unable to stand. Examination shows a sensory level at T6, grade 3/5 power in both legs with hyperreflexia and bilateral extensor plantar responses. Whole-spine MRI confirms an epidural soft-tissue mass at T5-T6 causing cord compression, with no other significant deposits. He had been ambulant until today. Which single statement best reflects optimal initial UK management?

options.label: A
options.text: Give a loading dose of at least 16 mg dexamethasone now and arrange definitive treatment (surgery or radiotherapy) ideally within 24 hours

options.label: B
options.text: Withhold corticosteroids until a tissue diagnosis is obtained, then start radiotherapy within 1 week

options.label: C
options.text: Nurse the patient sitting upright to relieve venous congestion and start oral morphine, deferring definitive treatment until the next multidisciplinary meeting

options.label: D
options.text: Commence palliative radiotherapy as the definitive treatment in all cases; surgery has no role in metastatic disease

options.label: E
options.text: Give intravenous methylprednisolone according to the NASCIS protocol to improve neurological recovery

explanation: This is metastatic spinal cord compression (MSCC). NICE NG234 (2023, which superseded CG75) advises that any patient with neurological signs or symptoms of MSCC should receive a loading dose of at least 16 mg of dexamethasone as soon as possible after assessment, continued at 16 mg daily while definitive treatment is planned, then tapered after surgery or commencement of radiotherapy (with gastric and glycaemic protection). Definitive treatment - surgical decompression and stabilisation, or radiotherapy - should be delivered before any further neurological deterioration and ideally within 24 hours of the diagnosis of MSCC in patients suitable for treatment. Surgery is preferred over radiotherapy in selected patients with a single area of compression, good performance status, a reasonable prognosis, and mechanical instability or a radioresistant tumour; the Patchell trial supports decompressive surgery plus radiotherapy in such patients, so option D is wrong. Steroids should not be withheld in a patient with neurology while awaiting biopsy. The patient should be nursed flat with neutral spinal alignment until the spine is confirmed stable, not sat upright. High-dose methylprednisolone (NASCIS) applies, controversially, to acute traumatic spinal cord injury, not to MSCC.
learningPoint: In MSCC with neurology, give at least 16 mg dexamethasone immediately and deliver definitive treatment (surgery or radiotherapy) ideally within 24 hours - nurse flat until the spine is shown to be stable.
references: NICE NG234 - Spinal metastases and metastatic spinal cord compression (2023, supersedes CG75)
Patchell et al. Lancet 2005 - decompressive surgery plus radiotherapy for MSCC
Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Spine Tumours / metastatic disease

stem: A 58-year-old man with type 2 diabetes presents with 6 weeks of progressively worsening, unremitting thoracolumbar back pain that is worse at night, with intermittent fevers and night sweats. He is neurologically intact and haemodynamically stable. Inflammatory markers are raised (CRP 110 mg/L). MRI of the spine shows T1-hypointense, T2-hyperintense signal in the L3 and L4 vertebral bodies with destruction of the intervening disc and a small paravertebral collection. Two sets of peripheral blood cultures are negative. What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Start empirical broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics immediately to cover Staphylococcus aureus

options.label: B
options.text: Perform an image-guided biopsy of the affected disc/vertebra to obtain a microbiological diagnosis before starting antibiotics

options.label: C
options.text: Refer urgently for surgical debridement and instrumented fusion of L3-L4

options.label: D
options.text: Commence empirical anti-tuberculous therapy on the basis of the imaging appearance

options.label: E
options.text: Start a tapering course of oral corticosteroids to settle the inflammatory response

explanation: This is pyogenic vertebral osteomyelitis / discitis. The patient is neurologically intact and haemodynamically stable, so there is no immediate indication to start antibiotics blindly. UK and international (IDSA) guidance is that in a stable patient with negative blood cultures, an image-guided (or if necessary open) biopsy should be obtained before commencing antimicrobials, because identifying the causative organism and its sensitivities critically guides several weeks of targeted therapy and improves cure rates. Staphylococcus aureus is the commonest pathogen, but starting empirical antibiotics first risks rendering subsequent cultures negative and leaving the patient on inappropriate prolonged therapy. Empirical antibiotics without a sample are reserved for the septic or neurologically deteriorating patient who cannot wait. Surgery is indicated for neurological deficit from cord/cauda compression, significant deformity or instability, a large or progressive abscess, or failure of medical therapy - none of which apply yet here. Anti-tuberculous therapy should not be started on imaging alone; TB spondylitis (Pott disease) requires microbiological or histological confirmation, and the disc is classically relatively spared in TB. Corticosteroids are contraindicated and would worsen the infection.
learningPoint: In a neurologically intact, haemodynamically stable patient with suspected discitis/vertebral osteomyelitis and negative blood cultures, obtain an image-guided biopsy BEFORE starting antibiotics to secure the organism and guide targeted therapy.
references: IDSA Clinical Practice Guideline - Native Vertebral Osteomyelitis
Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Spine Infection chapter
BASS guidance on the management of spinal infection

stem: A 64-year-old man with long-standing ankylosing spondylitis and a fixed kyphotic cervical spine falls and strikes his forehead on the pavement. He now reports new neck pain. Initial lateral cervical radiographs are reported as showing no fracture. He is neurologically intact on arrival but two hours later develops progressive weakness in all four limbs. Which single statement best reflects correct management of cervical spine injury in ankylosing spondylitis?

options.label: A
options.text: A normal radiograph reliably excludes a cervical fracture in ankylosing spondylitis, so the deterioration must have another cause

options.label: B
options.text: Any new neck pain after trauma should be treated as an unstable fracture until proven otherwise, with CT (and usually MRI) of the whole spine and immobilisation in the patient's pre-injury position

options.label: C
options.text: The patient should be immobilised flat on a hard surface with the neck forced into the neutral anatomical position to protect the cord

options.label: D
options.text: These fractures are inherently stable because of the ankylosed, fused spinal column and can be managed in a soft collar

options.label: E
options.text: Closed traction with progressively increasing weights should be applied to realign the cervical spine before any imaging

explanation: The ankylosed spine in advanced ankylosing spondylitis (AS) behaves like a long bone: it is rigid, brittle and osteoporotic, so even low-energy trauma can produce highly unstable, often transverse three-column (extension) fractures, frequently through the lower cervical spine. These fractures are easily missed on plain radiographs - the abnormal baseline anatomy, osteopenia and overlying structures obscure them - and a 'normal' film must never reassure. Hence any new neck pain in an AS patient after trauma is treated as an unstable fracture until proven otherwise, with CT of the whole spine (plus MRI to assess the cord, an epidural haematoma and the posterior elements). A common and catastrophic error is forcing the neck into the standard neutral position: because these patients have a fixed kyphosis, neutral positioning distracts the fracture and can cause or worsen cord injury, so they must be immobilised and managed in their pre-injury (deformed) position with support under the head. Soft-collar treatment of these unstable injuries is dangerous, and blind closed traction without imaging risks over-distraction across the rigid column. The delayed tetraparesis here is classic of an evolving unstable fracture, often with an epidural haematoma.
learningPoint: Treat any new neck pain after trauma in ankylosing spondylitis as an unstable three-column fracture until proven otherwise - image the whole spine with CT/MRI and immobilise in the patient's pre-injury deformed position, never forced into neutral.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Spine Trauma / Inflammatory spondyloarthropathy
BOAST - Spinal Clearance in the Trauma Patient
Caron et al. - Spine fractures in patients with ankylosing spinal disorders (Spine, 2010)

subtopic: Spinal cord injury syndromes - pattern recognition
stem: Theme: Incomplete and complete spinal cord injury syndromes. For each clinical description below, select the single most likely diagnosis from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Central cord syndrome

options.label: B
options.text: Anterior cord syndrome

options.label: C
options.text: Brown-Sequard syndrome

options.label: D
options.text: Posterior cord syndrome

options.label: E
options.text: Complete (ASIA A) spinal cord injury

options.label: F
options.text: Conus medullaris syndrome

options.label: G
options.text: Cauda equina syndrome

explanation: These vignettes test recognition of the classic cord syndromes. Central cord syndrome - the most common incomplete cord syndrome - is the textbook picture of an elderly patient with cervical spondylosis sustaining a hyperextension injury who has weakness predominantly affecting the upper limbs (hands worst) more than the lower limbs, with variable sensory loss and sometimes urinary retention; it is caused by injury to the central cord where the upper-limb motor fibres are arranged somatotopically, and generally has a favourable prognosis although hand function recovers least. Brown-Sequard syndrome follows a hemisection (often penetrating) injury and produces ipsilateral loss of motor function and dorsal-column sensation (proprioception/vibration) with contralateral loss of pain and temperature a level or two below; it carries the best prognosis of the incomplete syndromes. Anterior cord syndrome, from a flexion/axial-load injury or anterior spinal artery infarction, produces loss of motor function and pain/temperature with preserved proprioception and vibration (dorsal columns spared) and has the worst prognosis of the incomplete syndromes. A complete (ASIA A) injury shows absent motor and sensory function below the level with no sacral sparing (no perianal sensation, no voluntary anal contraction) once spinal shock has resolved. Conus medullaris syndrome arises from injury at the T12-L1 (conus) level and produces symmetrical saddle anaesthesia, early and marked bladder/bowel dysfunction and relatively symmetrical but mild lower-limb weakness; it is distinguished from cauda equina syndrome, which tends to produce asymmetrical radicular pain and lower motor neurone leg weakness. Posterior cord syndrome (isolated dorsal column loss) is rare.
learningPoint: Recognise the cord syndromes by their motor/sensory dissociation: central cord (arms > legs, hyperextension in spondylosis), Brown-Sequard (ipsilateral motor/proprioception, contralateral pain/temperature, best prognosis), anterior cord (motor and pain/temperature lost, proprioception spared, worst prognosis); sacral sparing distinguishes an incomplete injury from a complete (ASIA A) injury.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Spinal Cord Injury syndromes
ASIA International Standards for Neurological Classification of Spinal Cord Injury
NICE NG41 - Spinal injury: assessment and initial management

emiLeads.stem: A 72-year-old man with cervical spondylosis falls forwards and strikes his forehead. He has weakness affecting both hands and forearms markedly more than his legs, with patchy sensory loss in the upper limbs and some urinary urgency. CT shows no fracture.

A 26-year-old man is stabbed in the back to the right of the midline at the mid-thoracic level. He has lost motor power and joint-position sense in the right leg, and lost pain and temperature sensation in the left leg below the umbilicus.

A 30-year-old woman sustains a flexion-distraction injury with anterior cord contusion. She has dense bilateral leg weakness and loss of pain and temperature sensation below the lesion, but vibration sense and proprioception in the feet are preserved.

A 22-year-old man with a C6 fracture-dislocation has no motor or sensory function below the clavicles. There is no perianal sensation and no voluntary anal contraction, and spinal shock has resolved with return of the bulbocavernosus reflex but no recovery of sacral function.

A 40-year-old man with an L1 burst fracture has symmetrical saddle anaesthesia, early painless urinary retention with overflow and faecal incontinence, but only mild and symmetrical weakness of the lower limbs.

stem: A healthy term female infant is reviewed at her newborn examination. Barlow and Ortolani tests are negative and the hips feel clinically stable. She was in a breech presentation at 37 weeks' gestation but turned and was delivered cephalic by spontaneous vaginal delivery. There is no family history of hip problems. According to the current UK NIPE screening programme, what is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Reassure and discharge, as the clinical examination is normal and she was delivered cephalic

options.label: B
options.text: Arrange a hip ultrasound scan to be performed at around 4 to 6 weeks of age

options.label: C
options.text: Arrange an immediate hip ultrasound within 72 hours of birth

options.label: D
options.text: Arrange a pelvic radiograph at 4 to 6 weeks of age

options.label: E
options.text: Repeat clinical examination only at the 6 to 8 week check, with no imaging

explanation: The UK uses a selective (risk-factor based) ultrasound screening model for DDH rather than universal screening. Breech presentation is a major risk factor that mandates ultrasound even when the clinical examination is normal. The current NIPE standard defines the qualifying risk factor as breech presentation at or after 36 weeks of gestation, irrespective of presentation at birth or mode of delivery, so an infant who was breech at 37 weeks but turned and delivered cephalic still qualifies.
