For babies born at or after 34 weeks the scan is performed at around 4 to 6 weeks of age, because earlier scanning generates false positives from physiological immaturity that resolve spontaneously. Ultrasound (Graf) is the modality of choice under 6 months because the femoral head is largely cartilaginous and not visible on radiograph; plain films become useful once the ossific nucleus appears (around 4 to 6 months).
learningPoint: Breech at or after 36 weeks mandates a 4 to 6 week hip ultrasound regardless of presentation at birth or delivery mode, even with a normal newborn examination.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pediatric Orthopaedics (Hip: DDH)
UK NIPE Screening Programme Standards (valid from April 2024)
NICE CKS — Developmental dysplasia of the hip

stem: A 7-week-old girl is diagnosed with a dislocated left hip (Graf type IV) and started in a Pavlik harness. At review after 3 weeks of harness treatment, ultrasound shows the hip remains dislocated. The parents report she has stopped kicking the left leg and seems to keep the knee straight. What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.label: A
options.text: Continue the harness for a further 3 weeks and re-scan, as reduction can take time

options.label: B
options.text: Tighten the anterior straps to increase hip flexion and improve the chance of reduction

options.label: C
options.text: Abandon the Pavlik harness and plan examination under anaesthesia, arthrogram and closed (or open) reduction with spica

options.label: D
options.text: Switch to a rigid abduction (von Rosen) splint and continue for a further 6 weeks

options.label: E
options.text: Continue the harness but reduce the abduction to relieve pressure on the femoral nerve

explanation: Two problems coexist here. First, the Pavlik harness has failed: a hip that remains dislocated after about 3 weeks of harness treatment is unlikely to reduce with continued harness use, and persisting risks 'Pavlik harness disease' — posterolateral erosion of the acetabulum by the dislocated head that worsens the dysplasia and can compromise later reduction. Most protocols abandon the harness if reduction is not achieved by about 3 weeks. Second, the description of a leg that has stopped kicking and is held in extension is classic of femoral nerve palsy, the commonest neurological complication of the harness, caused by excessive hip flexion. The correct course is to discontinue the harness and proceed to examination under anaesthesia with arthrogram and closed reduction (open if not concentrically reducible), holding in a hip spica. Tightening straps or increasing flexion would worsen the nerve palsy; simply continuing risks structural acetabular damage.
learningPoint: A hip still dislocated after ~3 weeks in a Pavlik harness should prompt abandonment (to avoid Pavlik harness disease) and progression to EUA/arthrogram and reduction; a leg held in extension that has stopped kicking signals femoral nerve palsy from excess flexion.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pediatric Orthopaedics (DDH: Pavlik harness, complications)
BSCOS / British paediatric orthopaedic practice on DDH treatment

stem: A 9-year-old boy presents with a 4-month history of right hip and knee pain and a limp. Radiographs confirm Legg-Calvé-Perthes disease in the fragmentation stage, with more than 50% femoral head involvement and a lateral pillar (Herring) group B/C border. On examination he has lost abduction and internal rotation with a flexion-abduction-external-rotation contracture. Which single factor most strongly favours surgical containment in this child?

options.label: A
options.text: His age of 9 years (skeletal age over 8) combined with the hip stiffness

options.label: B
options.text: The presence of referred knee pain

options.label: C
options.text: Male sex

options.label: D
options.text: The 4-month duration of symptoms

options.label: E
options.text: Lateral pillar group A involvement

explanation: Prognosis in Perthes is driven principally by age at onset, the extent of head involvement (lateral pillar / Herring classification), and the degree of femoral head deformity and containment. Younger children (especially under 6, with more remodelling potential) generally do well regardless of treatment, whereas older children have a worse natural history. UK consensus (BSCOS, Leo et al. BJJ 2024) supports non-surgical, self-management-based care for the majority and recommends against routine containment surgery in children diagnosed before age 6. The decision-analysis literature (e.g. Joseph; the Lateral Pillar/Herring studies) identifies older skeletal age (over 8 years) combined with reduced hip movement as the key determinants favouring containment surgery (femoral or pelvic osteotomy), with female sex, age over 6 and over 50% collapse predicting poorer radiological outcome. In this older, stiff boy with a B/C border and extensive involvement, his age plus stiffness is the strongest single indication for surgical containment. Referred knee pain and symptom duration carry no prognostic weight; male sex and lateral pillar A would, if anything, favour a better outcome.
learningPoint: In Perthes, older skeletal age (over 8) with hip stiffness is the strongest driver toward surgical containment; children diagnosed under 6 generally do well with non-operative, self-management-based care (UK/BSCOS consensus).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pediatric Orthopaedics (Legg-Calvé-Perthes disease; lateral pillar/Herring)
Leo DG et al. — Clinical consensus recommendations for the non-surgical treatment of children with Perthes' disease in the UK, BJJ 2024 (BSCOS)

stem: A 12-year-old boy with a BMI on the 98th centile presents with 6 weeks of left thigh and knee pain and a limp. For the last 3 days the pain has been much worse, and today he is unable to bear any weight on the leg even with crutches. The hip lies in external rotation. Radiographs confirm a slipped capital femoral epiphysis. Using the Loder classification, how is this slip best categorised and what is the principal clinical implication?

options.label: A
options.text: Stable slip — low risk of avascular necrosis, can be managed semi-electively

options.label: B
options.text: Unstable slip — high risk of avascular necrosis, requires urgent treatment

options.label: C
options.text: Chronic stable slip — prognosis depends only on the slip angle

options.label: D
options.text: Pre-slip — treat with protected weight-bearing and serial radiographs

options.label: E
options.text: Acute-on-chronic slip — AVN risk is the same as a stable slip

explanation: The Loder classification is functional and is the strongest predictor of avascular necrosis. A slip is 'unstable' when the child cannot bear weight even with crutches, and 'stable' when weight-bearing is possible (with or without aids) — it is defined by weight-bearing ability, not by symptom duration or radiographic chronicity. AVN risk in unstable slips is high (commonly quoted around 20 to 50%), versus low (close to 0 to 10%) in stable slips. This boy cannot weight-bear, so the slip is unstable and carries a high AVN risk, mandating urgent management. The distractors conflate the temporal (acute/chronic) classification with the functional one: an 'acute-on-chronic' radiographic pattern can still be unstable, and stability — not chronicity — governs AVN risk. Standard treatment is in situ fixation (single cannulated screw); the role and timing of urgent reduction in unstable slips remains debated.
learningPoint: Loder stability (can the child weight-bear, even with crutches?) — not acute/chronic timing — is the key predictor of AVN in SCFE; unstable slips carry a high AVN risk and need urgent treatment.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pediatric Orthopaedics (SCFE: Loder classification)
Loder RT et al. — Acute slipped capital femoral epiphysis: the importance of physeal stability, JBJS

stem: A 9-year-old boy presents with a stable slipped capital femoral epiphysis. He is on the 25th centile for weight but the 2nd centile for height, and his mother notes he has been slow to grow and feels the cold. Which is the most appropriate management consideration in this particular child?

options.label: A
options.text: Screen for an underlying endocrinopathy (e.g. hypothyroidism) and consider prophylactic fixation of the contralateral hip

options.label: B
options.text: Proceed with single-screw fixation of the symptomatic hip alone and give routine reassurance about the other side

options.label: C
options.text: Manage non-operatively with protected weight-bearing because he is not obese

options.label: D
options.text: Arrange urgent open reduction because atypical slips are always unstable

options.label: E
options.text: Refer for growth-hormone therapy before any surgical fixation

explanation: Most SCFE occurs in overweight peri-pubertal children with no identifiable systemic cause. An 'atypical' slip should be suspected when the child is outside the usual demographic — markedly young (under about 10) or old (over about 16), or notably short/underweight rather than obese. Such children have a substantially higher rate of underlying endocrine or metabolic disease: hypothyroidism (this boy's short stature, poor growth and cold intolerance are suggestive), growth hormone deficiency or GH treatment, panhypopituitarism, and renal osteodystrophy. These atypical/endocrine cases also carry a higher risk of bilateral and sequential slips, so endocrine work-up plus consideration of prophylactic contralateral pinning is appropriate, alongside fixation of the affected hip. Body habitus does not change the need for fixation, the slip is stable (not automatically unstable), and growth-hormone treatment is not a prerequisite to stabilising an at-risk physis.
learningPoint: An atypical SCFE (very young/short/underweight rather than obese) should trigger endocrine screening and a lower threshold for prophylactic contralateral fixation, as bilateral disease is commoner in these children.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pediatric Orthopaedics (SCFE: atypical/endocrine slips, bilaterality)

stem: A newborn boy has an idiopathic right clubfoot and is undergoing Ponseti casting. After five weekly above-knee casts the forefoot adduction and cavus have corrected and the foot abducts well, but there is residual fixed equinus with the ankle unable to reach plantigrade. Examination shows good abduction of the foot (thigh-foot abduction reaching about 60 degrees). What is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Continue weekly serial casting until the equinus corrects passively

options.label: B
options.text: Perform a percutaneous Achilles tenotomy, then apply a final cast for about 3 weeks

options.label: C
options.text: Proceed directly to a posteromedial soft-tissue release

options.label: D
options.text: Begin foot-abduction (boots-and-bar) bracing immediately without further casting

options.label: E
options.text: Perform a tibialis anterior tendon transfer to hold the correction

explanation: The Ponseti sequence corrects the deformity components in order, remembered by CAVE: Cavus first (by supinating the forefoot to align with the hindfoot), then Adductus and Varus are corrected together by abducting the foot around a laterally placed talar head — never by pronating. Equinus is corrected last. Once the foot is well abducted (typically around 60 to 70 degrees of thigh-foot abduction, indicating the calcaneus and forefoot have rotated out from under the talus) the cavus, adductus and varus are corrected, but fixed equinus remains in the majority because of a tight tendo-Achilles. The standard final step is a percutaneous Achilles tenotomy followed by a final cast (about 3 weeks) to hold dorsiflexion while the tendon heals. Continued casting will not overcome true fixed equinus and risks a rocker-bottom deformity by breaking through the midfoot. Open posteromedial release is the historical approach Ponseti was designed to avoid. Boots-and-bar bracing maintains correction after the tenotomy cast, and tibialis anterior transfer is reserved for later dynamic supination relapse in the walking child (around 2.5 to 4 years), not initial correction.
learningPoint: In Ponseti the deformity corrects in CAVE order with equinus last; residual fixed equinus after the foot is well abducted is treated by percutaneous tendo-Achilles tenotomy and a final 3-week cast, not by more casting.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pediatric Orthopaedics (Clubfoot: Ponseti method, CAVE)
Ponseti IV — Congenital Clubfoot: Fundamentals of Treatment

stem: A 4-year-old boy has spastic diplegic cerebral palsy and is classified GMFCS level IV. He is enrolled in a hip surveillance programme. His most recent pelvic radiograph shows a Reimer's migration percentage of 38% on the left, increasing from 22% twelve months earlier, with no fixed adduction contracture and a normal acetabulum. What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Continue routine annual surveillance, as a migration percentage under 40% is acceptable at any age

options.label: B
options.text: Refer for consideration of soft-tissue (adductor +/- psoas) surgery to address the progressive hip displacement

options.label: C
options.text: Proceed directly to femoral and pelvic (reconstructive) osteotomies

options.label: D
options.text: Perform a Castle (salvage) proximal femoral resection

options.label: E
options.text: Discharge from surveillance, as displacement in non-ambulant children does not progress after age 4

explanation: Hip displacement is common in cerebral palsy and its risk rises steeply with GMFCS level (lowest in level I, highest in level V); a GMFCS IV child has a high risk and requires structured radiographic hip surveillance. The Reimer's migration percentage (the proportion of the femoral head lying lateral to Perkins' line) is the key surveillance metric. A migration percentage that is progressing and has crossed roughly 30 to 40%, or that is rising rapidly year on year, indicates a hip at risk and warrants intervention rather than continued watchful waiting. When the femoral head and acetabulum are still well-formed (as here — normal acetabulum, no fixed deformity), early soft-tissue surgery (adductor release, often with psoas lengthening) can slow or reverse migration and may avert later bony reconstruction. Reconstructive femoral/pelvic osteotomy is reserved for established subluxation/dysplasia or failed soft-tissue surgery, and salvage procedures (e.g. Castle, McHale) are for the painful, chronically dislocated, degenerate hip. Surveillance must continue throughout growth — displacement certainly progresses beyond age 4.
learningPoint: In CP hip surveillance, a rising Reimer's migration percentage approaching/exceeding ~30 to 40% in a still-congruent hip warrants soft-tissue surgery (adductor +/- psoas); reconstructive osteotomy and salvage are for later, more displaced or degenerate hips.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pediatric Orthopaedics (Cerebral palsy: hip, Reimer's migration percentage)
UK CP hip surveillance pathway (CPIPS) / Australian Hip Surveillance Guidelines

subtopic: Angular deformity — Blount vs physiological bowing
stem: A 3-year-old boy is referred with persistent bow legs that his parents feel are worsening rather than improving. He is above the 95th centile for weight and started walking at 10 months. Standing alignment radiographs show a varus deformity with the apex at the proximal tibia, a metaphyseal-diaphyseal (Drennan) angle of 16 degrees, and beaking of the medial proximal tibial metaphysis. Which statement best reflects the correct interpretation and management?

options.label: A
options.text: This is physiological genu varum; reassure, as it will spontaneously resolve by age 7 to 8

options.label: B
options.text: Findings indicate infantile (early-onset) Blount disease; the metaphyseal-diaphyseal angle and medial metaphyseal changes warrant bracing or surgery rather than reassurance

options.label: C
options.text: The most likely diagnosis is rickets, and the priority is vitamin D and phosphate assays before any orthopaedic treatment

options.label: D
options.text: Observation alone is appropriate because a metaphyseal-diaphyseal angle below 20 degrees always indicates physiological bowing

options.label: E
options.text: Immediate proximal tibial osteotomy is required regardless of age because Blount disease never responds to bracing

explanation: The key clinical task is distinguishing physiological genu varum (which is maximal around 12 to 18 months and resolves spontaneously, evolving toward physiological valgus by about age 3 and settling by 7 to 8) from infantile Blount disease (a growth disturbance of the posteromedial proximal tibial physis). The risk factors here are classic for Blount: obesity and early walking. The Drennan metaphyseal-diaphyseal angle helps differentiate: below about 10 degrees is usually physiological, above about 16 degrees strongly suggests Blount, with an intermediate grey zone. Crucially this boy also has structural changes — medial metaphyseal beaking/depression — and apparent progression, which point firmly away from benign physiological bowing. Infantile Blount is age- and stage-dependent (Langenskiöld): early stages in young children may be treated with bracing (KAFO), while progression or later presentation requires proximal tibial valgus (and derotational) osteotomy, ideally before age 4 to optimise outcome. Physiological bowing would not show medial metaphyseal beaking; rickets typically causes symmetrical, generalised metaphyseal cupping/fraying and other systemic features rather than isolated posteromedial proximal tibial disease, though it remains a differential to exclude.
learningPoint: Obesity, early walking, an elevated metaphyseal-diaphyseal (Drennan) angle and medial metaphyseal beaking distinguish infantile Blount disease from self-resolving physiological genu varum and mandate treatment (bracing early, osteotomy if progressive — ideally before age 4).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pediatric Orthopaedics (Blount disease; metaphyseal-diaphyseal angle; Langenskiöld stages)
Drennan JC — metaphyseal-diaphyseal angle in tibia vara

subtopic: Paediatric lower limb — gait, torsion and leg-length variants
stem: Theme: Rotational/angular variants and leg-length discrepancy. For each child described below, select the SINGLE most appropriate diagnosis or management step from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Reassurance — benign condition expected to resolve spontaneously with growth

options.label: B
options.text: Femoral derotation osteotomy

options.label: C
options.text: Internal tibial torsion

options.label: D
options.text: Increased femoral anteversion (medial femoral torsion)

options.label: E
options.text: Metatarsus adductus

options.label: F
options.text: Contralateral distal femoral epiphysiodesis, timed using the Green-Anderson / Moseley (straight-line graph) or multiplier method

options.label: G
options.text: Leg-length equalisation with a shoe raise and continued observation

options.label: H
options.text: Examination under anaesthesia and arthrogram

emiLeads.stem: A 4-year-old girl is brought in because she 'walks pigeon-toed'. She characteristically sits in the 'W' position. Examination shows internal rotation of the hips of about 80 degrees and external rotation of only 15 degrees, with a normal foot and a neutral thigh-foot axis. Gait shows in-toeing with inward-pointing patellae.

A 2-year-old boy in-toes. The feet point inward during gait but the patellae point straight ahead. Examination shows a markedly internal thigh-foot axis with a normal hip rotational profile and a straight lateral foot border.

An 18-month-old has a foot that curves inward at the front with a convex lateral border, but the heel is in neutral and the deformity is passively correctable to and beyond neutral. The hindfoot and ankle are normal.

A 10-year-old boy has a structural leg-length discrepancy from a previously physeal-injured right femur. Scanogram shows a 2.5 cm shortening, and the predicted discrepancy at skeletal maturity is about 4 cm. He is otherwise well with a level pelvis when the shortfall is blocked.
A 6-year-old girl has a measured leg-length discrepancy of 1.5 cm with a predicted discrepancy at maturity of under 2 cm. She has a mild compensatory gait but no functional limitation.

explanation: In-toeing has three common causes that are separated by where in the limb the torsion lies, assessed with Staheli's rotational profile. Increased femoral anteversion (medial femoral torsion) is the typical cause in the 3 to 6 year old who W-sits and has inward-pointing patellae, with hip internal rotation far exceeding external rotation; it almost always remodels spontaneously, and femoral derotation osteotomy is reserved for rare severe, functionally disabling cases persisting beyond about age 8 to 10. Internal tibial torsion is the commonest cause of in-toeing in toddlers (around 1 to 3 years): the patellae point forward but the feet point in, with an abnormal thigh-foot axis — it too resolves spontaneously. Metatarsus adductus is a forefoot adduction with a convex lateral border and neutral hindfoot; flexible (passively correctable) feet need only reassurance/observation. For leg-length discrepancy, management is dictated by the predicted discrepancy at maturity (estimated using Green-Anderson tables, the Moseley straight-line graph, or the multiplier method): roughly under 2 cm is treated with observation +/- a shoe raise; about 2 to 5 cm is commonly managed by well-timed contralateral epiphysiodesis; larger discrepancies (greater than ~5 cm) generally require lengthening or combinations of procedures. The 10-year-old with a predicted ~4 cm discrepancy is the classic candidate for appropriately timed epiphysiodesis, whereas the 6-year-old with under 2 cm predicted needs only a raise and observation.
learningPoint: Localise in-toeing by the rotational profile (femoral anteversion = inward patellae + W-sitting; internal tibial torsion = forward patellae, inward feet; metatarsus adductus = forefoot only) — almost all resolve spontaneously; LLD management hinges on the predicted discrepancy at maturity (<2 cm raise/observe, ~2 to 5 cm epiphysiodesis, >5 cm lengthening).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pediatric Orthopaedics (rotational profile; in-toeing; leg-length discrepancy: Green-Anderson, Moseley, multiplier)
Staheli LT — rotational problems in children

stem: A 3-month-old infant is seen following a difficult vaginal delivery of a macrosomic baby complicated by shoulder dystocia. At birth the right arm was held adducted and internally rotated with the forearm pronated and wrist flexed. There is no Horner syndrome. At this review the infant still has no antigravity elbow flexion (cannot bring the hand to the mouth against gravity). Which feature is the single most important determinant of whether this child should be referred for microsurgical nerve reconstruction?

options.label: A
options.text: Presence of an associated clavicle fracture on the birth radiograph

options.label: B
options.text: Failure to recover antigravity biceps (elbow flexion) function by around 3 months of age

options.label: C
options.text: The classic 'waiter's tip' posture being present at birth

options.label: D
options.text: A positive Horner syndrome alone irrespective of motor recovery

options.label: E
options.text: Persistence of an asymmetric Moro reflex at 6 weeks

explanation: Most brachial plexus birth palsies are upper-trunk (Erb) lesions that recover spontaneously. The single best validated prognostic indicator and the standard trigger for considering microsurgical reconstruction is recovery of antigravity biceps function: infants who regain it by roughly 3 months have a good outcome, whereas failure of biceps recovery by ~3 months (some centres extend the window) predicts incomplete recovery and is the indication to refer for nerve surgery. A clavicle fracture, the initial posture and an asymmetric Moro are diagnostic/clinical features but do not by themselves dictate surgery. Horner syndrome signals a more severe (often pan-plexal/avulsion) injury and worse prognosis, but management is still driven by motor (biceps) recovery, not the Horner sign in isolation.
learningPoint: In brachial plexus birth palsy, failure to recover antigravity biceps function by about 3 months is the key indication to refer for microsurgical reconstruction.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pediatric Orthopaedics: brachial plexus birth palsy

subtopic: Brachial plexus birth palsy — late sequelae
stem: A 4-year-old child with a history of an upper-trunk brachial plexus birth palsy that recovered partially has a persistent internal rotation and adduction contracture of the shoulder. MRI shows posterior subluxation of the humeral head with a flattened, increasingly retroverted (more negative version) and dysplastic glenoid. Which muscle imbalance is the principal driver of this progressive glenohumeral deformity?

options.label: A
options.text: Weak deltoid overpowered by an intact supraspinatus

options.label: B
options.text: Strong internal rotators (subscapularis/pectoralis major) unopposed by weak external rotators (infraspinatus/teres minor)

options.label: C
options.text: Overactivity of the long head of biceps causing anterior translation

options.label: D
options.text: Weak latissimus dorsi with a strong teres minor

options.label: E
options.text: Isolated serratus anterior palsy producing scapular winging

explanation: After upper-trunk injury the external rotators (infraspinatus, teres minor — suprascapular/axillary supply) typically recover less well than the internal rotators (subscapularis, pectoralis major). The resulting unopposed internal-rotation/adduction pull holds the arm internally rotated, and in the growing shoulder this drives progressive posterior humeral head subluxation with glenoid retroversion (increasingly negative version) and dysplasia. Management addresses this imbalance — botulinum toxin, soft-tissue rebalancing (e.g. subscapularis/pectoralis lengthening with latissimus dorsi/teres major transfer to restore external rotation) and reduction, with glenoid correction if needed. The other options misattribute the deforming force.
learningPoint: Glenohumeral dysplasia after Erb palsy results from strong internal rotators unopposed by weak external rotators; rebalancing restores external rotation and reduces the head.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pediatric Orthopaedics: brachial plexus birth palsy sequelae
Waters: glenohumeral deformity in brachial plexus birth palsy

subtopic: Congenital upper-limb difference — radial longitudinal deficiency
stem: A neonate is noted to have bilateral radial longitudinal deficiency (radial club hand) with absent thumbs and radially deviated, short forearms. The paediatric team should be most alert to which potentially life-threatening systemic association, and arrange the corresponding investigation?

options.label: A
options.text: Thrombocytopenia from Fanconi anaemia / TAR — arrange full blood count and chromosomal breakage testing

options.label: B
options.text: Congenital diaphragmatic hernia — arrange urgent chest radiograph only

options.label: C
options.text: Developmental dysplasia of the hip — arrange hip ultrasound only

options.label: D
options.text: Congenital hypothyroidism — arrange thyroid function tests only

options.label: E
options.text: Tracheo-oesophageal fistula in isolation — arrange a contrast swallow only

explanation: Radial longitudinal deficiency is frequently syndromic and mandates a systemic work-up (the classic VACTERL screen plus haematology). The most important life-threatening associations are haematological: Fanconi anaemia (chromosomal breakage, progressive marrow failure; thumb usually absent/hypoplastic) and thrombocytopenia–absent radius (TAR) syndrome (thrombocytopenia with bilateral absent radii but the thumbs characteristically present). A full blood count and chromosomal breakage testing are therefore essential before any surgery. Cardiac (Holt–Oram), renal and other VACTERL anomalies also occur and are screened for (echocardiography, renal ultrasound, spine imaging), but the missed diagnosis with the greatest mortality risk is the marrow-failure/bleeding group, making the FBC and breakage study the priority answer.
learningPoint: Every radial longitudinal deficiency needs a systemic screen; check an FBC and chromosomal breakage test for Fanconi anaemia/TAR before surgery.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pediatric Orthopaedics: congenital upper-limb differences (radial deficiency)
BSSH guidance on congenital hand anomalies

stem: A 14-month-old child has a hypoplastic thumb. On examination the thumb is smaller with a narrowed first web space and absent thenar (intrinsic) muscles, but the carpometacarpal (CMC) joint is stable and present. Using the modified Blauth classification, which feature determines whether this thumb should be reconstructed versus ablated and replaced by pollicisation, and what is the correct management here?

options.label: A
options.text: Stability of the CMC joint; here it is stable (Blauth IIIA), so reconstruct (web release, opponensplasty, collateral ligament reconstruction)

options.label: B
options.text: Presence of a nail; as a nail is present, always reconstruct regardless of CMC status

options.label: C
options.text: Length of the thumb alone; as it is short, proceed directly to pollicisation

options.label: D
options.text: Stability of the CMC joint; here it is unstable (Blauth IIIB), so pollicise the index finger

options.label: E
options.text: Quality of the flexor pollicis longus only; reconstruct if any FPL excursion is present

explanation: The modified Blauth classification of thumb hypoplasia hinges chiefly on the basal joint: the watershed is within type III, where IIIA has a stable CMC joint (reconstructable) and IIIB has an unstable/deficient CMC joint (not reconstructable). Types I–IIIA are reconstructed (first web-space deepening, opponensplasty, ulnar collateral ligament reconstruction, ± extrinsic tendon work). Types IIIB, IV (floating thumb / pouce flottant) and V (absent thumb) are best treated by thumb ablation and index pollicisation, because an unstable basal joint cannot provide a useful post for pinch. This child has a present, stable CMC joint with absent intrinsics — IIIA — so reconstruction is correct. Nail, length and FPL excursion alone do not decide reconstruction versus pollicisation.
learningPoint: In thumb hypoplasia the stability of the CMC joint is decisive: stable (Blauth ≤IIIA) reconstruct; unstable (IIIB and above) pollicise.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pediatric Orthopaedics / Hand: thumb hypoplasia (modified Blauth)
BSSH guidance on thumb hypoplasia and pollicisation

stem: A 12-year-old girl is referred with a right thoracic curve. She is premenarchal. Standing PA radiograph shows a single right thoracic curve with a Cobb angle of 28 degrees and a Risser grade of 1. Neurological examination is normal and the curve pattern is typical for adolescent idiopathic scoliosis. What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Reassurance and discharge — curves under 30 degrees never progress

options.label: B
options.text: Bracing (full-time wear with the recommended daily hours) aiming to prevent progression toward the surgical threshold

options.label: C
options.text: Posterior spinal fusion now

options.label: D
options.text: Vertebral body tethering as first-line for a 28-degree curve

options.label: E
options.text: Physiotherapy alone with no further radiographic surveillance

explanation: Progression risk in adolescent idiopathic scoliosis is highest in skeletally immature patients with larger curves. This girl is at high risk: premenarchal, low Risser grade (1) and a curve already in the 25–40 degree range. The evidence-based intervention in this window (notably the BrAIST trial) is bracing, which significantly reduces the chance of progression to the surgical threshold (around 45–50 degrees), with benefit related to hours of wear. Curves below ~25 degrees are observed; surgery (typically posterior fusion) is reserved for curves that reach/exceed ~45–50 degrees or that progress despite bracing. Discharge, immediate fusion, and abandoning surveillance are all inappropriate here; vertebral body tethering is an emerging growth-modulation option but is not the standard first-line for this curve in routine UK practice.
learningPoint: A skeletally immature patient (low Risser, premenarchal) with a 25–40 degree idiopathic curve should be braced to reduce progression toward the surgical threshold.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pediatric Orthopaedics: adolescent idiopathic scoliosis
BrAIST bracing trial; BSCOS / BOA paediatric spine guidance

stem: A 3-year-old child has congenital scoliosis. Imaging confirms a unilateral unsegmented bar on the concavity of the curve with a contralateral hemivertebra at the same levels. The curve has progressed measurably over 9 months. Which statement best reflects the prognosis and management of this anomaly?

options.label: A
options.text: This is the most benign congenital pattern and can simply be observed indefinitely

options.label: B
options.text: A unilateral unsegmented bar with a contralateral hemivertebra has the worst progression risk and warrants early surgical intervention

options.label: C
options.text: Bracing reliably corrects the bony deformity and surgery is rarely needed

options.label: D
options.text: A block (fully segmented and fused) vertebra carries the highest progression risk of all patterns

options.label: E
options.text: Progression risk is independent of the type of vertebral anomaly and depends only on the child's age

explanation: Progression in congenital scoliosis is governed mainly by the type of vertebral anomaly and the growth imbalance it creates. A unilateral unsegmented bar tethers growth on one side; when combined with a contralateral hemivertebra (active growth on the convex side opposite the tether) the result is the most rapidly progressive and worst-prognosis pattern, justifying early surgery (e.g. hemivertebra excision, in-situ or convex growth arrest, or growth-friendly instrumentation) before a severe rigid deformity develops. By contrast, a fully segmented block vertebra has balanced (minimal) growth and is the most benign. Bracing does not correct a fixed bony anomaly. Documented progression in this child mandates surgical management. Congenital scoliosis also requires screening for VACTERL, intraspinal (e.g. tethered cord, diastematomyelia) and renal/cardiac anomalies.
learningPoint: A unilateral unsegmented bar with a contralateral hemivertebra is the most progressive congenital scoliosis pattern and needs early surgery; a block vertebra is the most benign.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pediatric Orthopaedics: congenital scoliosis
BSCOS / BOA paediatric spinal deformity guidance

subtopic: Paediatric septic arthritis of the hip
stem: A 4-year-old child presents with a 2-day history of refusal to weight-bear on the right leg, holding the hip flexed, abducted and externally rotated. Temperature is 38.9 degrees C, CRP is 85 mg/L, and the child will not bear weight. Ultrasound shows a hip effusion. Hip radiograph is normal. What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.label: A
options.text: Start empirical oral antibiotics and review in clinic in 48 hours

options.label: B
options.text: Urgent ultrasound-guided or open arthrotomy with joint aspiration/washout and synovial fluid for Gram stain, culture and cell count, then IV antibiotics

options.label: C
options.text: MRI of the pelvis first and defer any intervention until the result is available

options.label: D
options.text: Reassure as transient synovitis and discharge with simple analgesia

options.label: E
options.text: Commence IV antibiotics immediately before obtaining any joint fluid sample

explanation: This child meets multiple Kocher criteria (non-weight-bearing, fever >38.5 C) with a markedly raised CRP — CRP >20 mg/L is the strongest single independent predictor in the Caird modification — and a confirmed effusion, giving a very high probability of septic arthritis. Septic arthritis of a child's hip is a surgical emergency: chondrolysis and avascular necrosis can occur within hours. The correct step is urgent joint aspiration/arthrotomy and washout, sending synovial fluid for urgent Gram stain, culture and white cell count, with IV antibiotics started immediately after the sample is obtained. Oral antibiotics with clinic review, treating as transient synovitis, or delaying for MRI all risk irreversible joint destruction. Giving antibiotics before any joint sample (option E) is inferior because it can render cultures negative and delays the definitive source control — the priority is to obtain fluid and decompress/washout the joint.
learningPoint: Suspected paediatric hip septic arthritis is a surgical emergency: aspirate/wash out and sample the joint, then give IV antibiotics — do not delay or treat as synovitis.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pediatric Orthopaedics: septic arthritis (Kocher/Caird criteria)
BOAST — The Management of Acute Bone and Joint Infection in Children

stem: A 2-year-old child presents with bowing of the legs (genu varum), widening of the wrists and a history of delayed walking. Radiographs show metaphyseal cupping, splaying and fraying with a widened physis. Blood tests show low/low-normal calcium, low phosphate, raised alkaline phosphatase, raised PTH and low 25-hydroxyvitamin D. What is the single most likely diagnosis?

options.label: A
options.text: Nutritional (vitamin D-deficiency) rickets

options.label: B
options.text: X-linked hypophosphataemic rickets

options.label: C
options.text: Hypophosphatasia

options.label: D
options.text: Renal osteodystrophy from chronic kidney disease

options.label: E
options.text: Physiological genu varum (Blount disease excluded)

explanation: The biochemical signature points to the cause. Nutritional vitamin D-deficiency rickets shows low 25-hydroxyvitamin D with secondary hyperparathyroidism (raised PTH) driving low/low-normal calcium, low phosphate (renal phosphate wasting from PTH) and a high alkaline phosphatase, with the classic rachitic radiographic changes (metaphyseal cupping, fraying, splaying, physeal widening). X-linked hypophosphataemic rickets is characterised by isolated renal phosphate wasting (FGF23-mediated) with low phosphate but a normal 25-hydroxyvitamin D and typically normal/only mildly altered PTH, so the low vitamin D here makes it less likely. Hypophosphatasia has a LOW alkaline phosphatase (the opposite of this picture). Renal osteodystrophy would feature renal failure with hyperphosphataemia. Physiological varum has normal biochemistry and no rachitic changes. Management is vitamin D and calcium replacement, with public-health vitamin D supplementation advised for at-risk children.
learningPoint: Low vitamin D with high PTH, high ALP, low phosphate and rachitic metaphyses indicates nutritional rickets; a normal vitamin D with isolated phosphate wasting suggests X-linked hypophosphataemia, and a LOW ALP suggests hypophosphatasia.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pediatric Orthopaedics / Metabolic bone disease: rickets
NICE guidance on vitamin D supplementation in at-risk children

stem: Theme: genetics of skeletal dysplasias and osteogenesis imperfecta. For each clinical description, select the single most likely underlying gene or molecular defect from the list.

options.label: A
options.text: FGFR3 gain-of-function mutation

options.label: B
options.text: Defect in type I collagen (COL1A1 / COL1A2)

options.label: C
options.text: Defect in type II collagen (COL2A1)

options.label: D
options.text: X-linked SEDL / TRAPPC2 (sedlin) gene defect

options.label: E
options.text: Deficiency of tissue-nonspecific alkaline phosphatase (ALPL)

options.label: F
options.text: Defect in the sulphate transporter (DTDST / SLC26A2)

options.label: G
options.text: EXT1 / EXT2 mutation

emiLeads.stem: A child has rhizomelic short-limb dwarfism with a normal-length trunk, frontal bossing, a trident hand and (on imaging) decreasing interpedicular distance from L1 to L5 and a characteristic 'champagne-glass' pelvis. Inheritance is autosomal dominant.
An infant has multiple fractures from minimal trauma, blue sclerae, ligamentous laxity and later develops dentinogenesis imperfecta and progressive hearing loss.

A boy presents in mid-childhood with disproportionate short trunk, a barrel chest, premature hip osteoarthritis and platyspondyly; the trait affects males and is transmitted by carrier females (X-linked), with otherwise normal-length limbs.

A child has multiple firm, painless bony lumps near the ends of the long bones that point away from the joint, with associated forearm deformity and reduced growth; there is a small lifetime risk of malignant transformation to chondrosarcoma.

A neonate has severe deformity with profoundly low serum alkaline phosphatase, poorly mineralised bone and seizures responsive to pyridoxine; radiographs show 'tongues' of radiolucency projecting into the metaphysis.

explanation: These map the commonly examined dysplasia genotypes. Achondroplasia is an FGFR3 gain-of-function mutation (A) producing rhizomelic dwarfism, trident hands, frontal bossing, a champagne-glass pelvis and the classic narrowing interpedicular distance caudally (predisposing to spinal stenosis). Osteogenesis imperfecta is most commonly a type I collagen defect (B) — fragile bones, blue sclerae, dentinogenesis imperfecta and conductive/sensorineural hearing loss. Spondyloepiphyseal dysplasia tarda is X-linked, caused by the SEDL gene (now designated TRAPPC2, encoding sedlin) (D), causing short-trunk dwarfism with platyspondyly and early hip arthritis in boys; the congenita form is a COL2A1 (type II collagen) defect (C), which is the distractor here. Multiple hereditary exostoses (osteochondromatosis) results from EXT1/EXT2 mutations (G), with metaphyseal osteochondromas pointing away from the joint and a small chondrosarcoma risk. Hypophosphatasia is ALPL deficiency (E) with characteristically LOW alkaline phosphatase, defective mineralisation, metaphyseal radiolucent tongues and pyridoxine-responsive seizures. DTDST/SLC26A2 (F) underlies diastrophic dysplasia (hitchhiker thumb, rigid clubfeet, cauliflower ears) and is the remaining distractor.
learningPoint: Know the molecular signatures: achondroplasia = FGFR3; OI = type I collagen; SED congenita = COL2A1 vs SED tarda = X-linked SEDL/TRAPPC2; hereditary multiple exostoses = EXT1/2; hypophosphatasia = low ALP (ALPL).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pediatric Orthopaedics: skeletal dysplasias and osteogenesis imperfecta
BSCOS resources on paediatric skeletal dysplasia

stem: A 24-year-old motorcyclist arrives in the emergency department after a high-speed collision. He is haemodynamically unstable with a heart rate of 130 and a systolic blood pressure of 78 mmHg despite 1 litre of crystalloid. An AP pelvic radiograph shows a wide pubic symphysis diastasis of 6 cm with vertical displacement of the left hemipelvis. A pelvic binder has been applied at the level of the greater trochanters and the major haemorrhage protocol is running. After a further unit of blood his systolic pressure remains 80 mmHg. What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.label: A
options.text: Immediate transfer to the operating theatre for definitive anterior plating of the symphysis

options.label: B
options.text: CT angiogram of the abdomen and pelvis to localise the bleeding source

options.label: C
options.text: Pelvic packing and/or angioembolisation as part of damage control resuscitation

options.label: D
options.text: Application of skeletal traction to the left femur to reduce the vertical shear

options.label: E
options.text: Repositioning the binder higher over the iliac crests to improve compression

explanation: This is an unstable APC/vertical shear pelvic injury with ongoing haemodynamic instability despite a correctly applied binder and resuscitation. UK ATLS and BOAST guidance dictate that a transient or non-responder to resuscitation needs immediate haemorrhage control, not imaging. Pre-peritoneal pelvic packing (often combined with external fixation) and/or angioembolisation are the damage-control options to arrest pelvic bleeding. A CT angiogram is inappropriate in a patient who is not responding, as it delays definitive haemorrhage control. The binder is already correctly positioned at the greater trochanters.
learningPoint: In a haemodynamically unstable pelvic fracture not responding to resuscitation, proceed to haemorrhage control (packing/embolisation), not CT.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pelvis and Acetabulum chapter
BOAST: The Management of Patients with Pelvic Fractures (BOA)

stem: A 52-year-old man sustains an isolated acetabular fracture in a road traffic collision. Judet and CT imaging demonstrate a fracture involving both the iliopectineal (anterior) and ilioischial (posterior) columns, with the articular surface entirely detached from the intact portion of the ilium, producing the characteristic 'spur sign' on the obturator oblique view. The femoral head follows the medially displaced fragments. Which acetabular fracture pattern does this describe?

options.label: A
options.text: Associated both-column fracture

options.label: B
options.text: T-shaped fracture

options.label: C
options.text: Anterior column with posterior hemitransverse fracture

options.label: D
options.text: Transverse fracture

options.label: E
options.text: Posterior column and posterior wall fracture

explanation: The defining feature of an associated both-column fracture in the Judet-Letournel classification is that no part of the articular surface remains in continuity with the intact axial skeleton (the ilium). The pathognomonic 'spur sign' on the obturator oblique view represents the intact posterior ilium projecting beyond the medially displaced articular fragments. This is the only pattern that can produce 'secondary congruence', where the head and fragments displace together. A T-shaped or anterior-column/posterior-hemitransverse pattern retains some articular continuity with the ilium, and a transverse fracture does not separate the two columns from each other in this manner.
learningPoint: The 'spur sign' on the obturator oblique view is pathognomonic of an associated both-column acetabular fracture, where no articular surface remains attached to the ilium.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pelvis and Acetabulum chapter
Letournel and Judet, Fractures of the Acetabulum

stem: A 68-year-old woman who is independent and cognitively intact sustains a displaced intracapsular fracture of the femoral neck (Garden III) after a fall. She mobilises outdoors without aids and has no significant comorbidities. According to current NICE guidance, which operation should be offered?

options.label: A
options.text: Cannulated screw fixation

options.label: B
options.text: Cemented total hip replacement

options.label: C
options.text: Uncemented hemiarthroplasty

options.label: D
options.text: Dynamic hip screw

options.label: E
options.text: Cemented hemiarthroplasty

explanation: NICE CG124 recommends total hip replacement (rather than hemiarthroplasty) for patients with a displaced intracapsular hip fracture who were able to walk independently outdoors with no more than the use of a stick, are not cognitively impaired, and are medically fit for the procedure and anaesthesia. This patient meets all three criteria. NICE also recommends cemented implants for arthroplasty. Internal fixation (screws or DHS) is reserved for undisplaced intracapsular or extracapsular fractures, and uncemented implants are not the default choice.
learningPoint: NICE offers THR for displaced intracapsular hip fractures in patients who were independently mobile outdoors, cognitively intact, and medically fit.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Hip Trauma chapter

stem: A 30-year-old man sustains a closed mid-shaft femoral fracture as an isolated injury in a fall from height. He is physiologically stable with a normal lactate and base deficit and no chest or head injury. He is scheduled for definitive fixation. Which is the most appropriate definitive treatment?

options.label: A
options.text: Antegrade reamed intramedullary nailing

options.label: B
options.text: Spanning external fixation followed by delayed conversion

options.label: C
options.text: Open reduction and lateral plate fixation

options.label: D
options.text: Skeletal traction until the fracture unites

options.label: E
options.text: Retrograde unreamed intramedullary nailing

explanation: For an isolated closed diaphyseal femoral fracture in a physiologically stable adult, reamed antegrade intramedullary nailing is the gold-standard definitive treatment, providing high union rates and allowing early mobilisation. Reaming improves union rates in isolated injuries. Temporary external fixation (damage control orthopaedics) is reserved for the physiologically deranged or polytrauma patient who is not stable enough for early definitive surgery. Plating and traction are not first-line for a diaphyseal femoral fracture in a fit adult.
learningPoint: Reamed antegrade IM nailing is the definitive treatment of choice for an isolated closed femoral shaft fracture in a physiologically stable patient.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Femur chapter

stem: A 44-year-old man sustains a closed Schatzker VI bicondylar tibial plateau fracture in a motorcycle crash. On arrival the leg is markedly swollen with fracture blisters developing over the proximal tibia, and the compartments are soft and non-tender. Distal pulses and sensation are intact. What is the most appropriate initial surgical management?

options.label: A
options.text: Immediate open reduction and dual-plate internal fixation through medial and lateral approaches

options.label: B
options.text: Application of a spanning knee external fixator with delayed definitive fixation once soft tissues settle

options.label: C
options.text: Closed reduction and application of a long-leg cast

options.label: D
options.text: Primary arthrodesis of the knee

options.label: E
options.text: Skeletal traction through a calcaneal pin until union

explanation: A high-energy Schatzker VI fracture with marked swelling and fracture blisters has a soft-tissue envelope that will not tolerate immediate open surgery; early definitive ORIF in this setting carries a high risk of wound breakdown and deep infection. The correct strategy is staged management: a temporary spanning external fixator restores length and alignment and allows the soft tissues to recover, with definitive internal fixation deferred (typically 1-2 weeks) until skin wrinkling returns and blisters re-epithelialise. Immediate ORIF through this compromised envelope is contraindicated, and casting or traction will not adequately control this unstable bicondylar injury.
learningPoint: High-energy tibial plateau fractures with a compromised soft-tissue envelope are best managed with a temporary spanning external fixator and staged delayed ORIF.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Knee and Tibial Plateau chapter
BOAST: Open fractures and soft-tissue handling principles

stem: A 28-year-old man sustains a closed mid-shaft tibial fracture playing football. Six hours after a reamed intramedullary nail is inserted he develops severe, disproportionate pain in the leg requiring escalating opioid doses, with pain markedly worsened by passive dorsiflexion of the toes. The anterior and deep posterior compartments feel tense. Dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses are palpable and capillary refill is brisk. What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Elevate the limb above the heart and reassess in two hours

options.label: B
options.text: Urgent CT angiogram to exclude vascular injury

options.label: C
options.text: Immediate four-compartment fasciotomy of the leg

options.label: D
options.text: Split the dressings, withhold opioids and observe overnight

options.label: E
options.text: Measure compartment pressures and only operate if the absolute pressure exceeds 50 mmHg

explanation: This is acute compartment syndrome, a clinical diagnosis, in a high-risk scenario (young man, tibial diaphyseal fracture, post-nailing). The cardinal early sign is pain out of proportion and pain on passive stretch of the muscles in the affected compartment. Palpable pulses and normal capillary refill do NOT exclude compartment syndrome, as arterial inflow is typically preserved until very late. Per BOAST, when the diagnosis is clinically evident, the limb requires emergency open four-compartment fasciotomy without delay; waiting for pressure measurements or imaging, or simply elevating the limb, risks irreversible muscle necrosis. Elevation to heart level (not above) is appropriate only as a holding measure while preparing for theatre.
learningPoint: Acute compartment syndrome is a clinical diagnosis (pain out of proportion, pain on passive stretch); preserved pulses do not exclude it, and treatment is emergency fasciotomy.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Tibia chapter
BOAST: Diagnosis and Management of Compartment Syndrome of the Limbs

stem: A 35-year-old woman sustains a closed rotational ankle injury. Radiographs show a spiral fracture of the fibula beginning at the level of the ankle joint line and a transverse fracture of the medial malleolus. There is talar shift on the mortise view. Intraoperatively, after fixation of both malleoli, an external rotation stress test (hook test) demonstrates lateral talar displacement and widening of the medial clear space. What does this finding indicate?

options.label: A
options.text: An occult posterior malleolar fracture requiring fixation

options.label: B
options.text: Syndesmotic instability requiring stabilisation

options.label: C
options.text: Inadequate medial malleolar fixation requiring revision

options.label: D
options.text: A deltoid ligament rupture that mandates primary repair

options.label: E
options.text: A normal physiological finding requiring no further action

explanation: A positive intraoperative external rotation stress (or lateral hook) test after malleolar fixation indicates residual syndesmotic instability, which requires stabilisation (syndesmotic screw or suture-button device). The fibular fracture pattern described (Weber B / SER) frequently involves the syndesmosis, and intraoperative stress testing is the standard method to confirm it once the malleoli are fixed. Talar shift persisting after solid bimalleolar fixation localises the instability to the syndesmosis rather than the fixed bony components, and routine primary deltoid repair is not required when the medial malleolus has been fixed.
learningPoint: A positive intraoperative stress test after malleolar fixation indicates syndesmotic instability and the need for syndesmotic stabilisation.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Foot and Ankle Trauma chapter

stem: A 26-year-old footballer reports midfoot pain and inability to weight-bear after a forced plantarflexion injury. There is plantar bruising over the midfoot and tenderness across the tarsometatarsal joints. Weight-bearing AP radiographs show widening between the bases of the first and second metatarsals with a small bony fragment in the interval, and loss of alignment between the medial border of the second metatarsal and the medial border of the middle cuneiform. Which is the most appropriate definitive management of this displaced injury?

options.label: A
options.text: Below-knee non-weight-bearing cast for six weeks

options.label: B
options.text: Closed reduction and percutaneous Kirschner-wire fixation alone

options.label: C
options.text: Open reduction and stable internal fixation (or primary arthrodesis) of the tarsometatarsal joints

options.label: D
options.text: Functional bracing with early weight-bearing as tolerated

options.label: E
options.text: Immediate weight-bearing in a stiff-soled shoe

explanation: The findings (plantar ecchymosis, first-second metatarsal base diastasis, the 'fleck sign' representing a Lisfranc ligament avulsion, and malalignment of the second metatarsal with the middle cuneiform) confirm a displaced/unstable Lisfranc injury. Any displacement is an indication for anatomical reduction and stable fixation, as residual incongruity leads to post-traumatic arthritis and poor function. Options are open reduction with internal fixation (screws/bridge plates) or primary partial arthrodesis. Non-operative treatment is reserved only for genuinely stable, non-displaced injuries, and K-wires alone do not provide adequate stability for the displaced first/second ray.
learningPoint: Any displacement in a Lisfranc injury mandates anatomical open reduction with stable internal fixation or primary arthrodesis.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Foot and Ankle Trauma chapter
BOAST: Foot and ankle injury principles

stem: A 32-year-old man sustains a displaced fracture of the talar neck with subluxation of the subtalar joint after a road traffic collision (Hawkins type II). He is treated with anatomical open reduction and internal fixation. At six weeks, a mortise radiograph shows a subchondral lucent band in the talar dome. What does this radiographic sign most likely indicate?

options.label: A
options.text: Early avascular necrosis of the talar body

options.label: B
options.text: Preserved vascularity of the talar body, a favourable sign

options.label: C
options.text: Post-traumatic subtalar osteoarthritis

options.label: D
options.text: Nonunion of the talar neck fracture

options.label: E
options.text: Stress fracture of the talar dome

explanation: The Hawkins sign is a subchondral radiolucent band visible in the talar dome on an AP/mortise radiograph at 6-8 weeks. It results from subchondral bone resorption, a process that requires intact blood supply, and therefore its presence is a reliable indicator of PRESERVED vascularity and predicts against avascular necrosis. Its absence, or a sclerotic (relatively radiodense) talar dome, raises concern for AVN. The risk of AVN rises with the Hawkins grade because of the tenuous, largely retrograde blood supply to the talus.
learningPoint: A positive Hawkins sign (subchondral lucency at 6-8 weeks) indicates preserved talar vascularity and argues against avascular necrosis.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Foot and Ankle Trauma chapter (talus)
Hawkins LG, Fractures of the neck of the talus

stem: An 80-year-old woman with a previous cemented total hip replacement falls and sustains a periprosthetic fracture of the femur. Radiographs and intraoperative assessment confirm a fracture around the stem with a definitely loose femoral component but good remaining proximal bone stock. According to the Vancouver classification, how is this fracture classified and what is the principal treatment implication?

options.label: A
options.text: Vancouver A; treat by fixation of the trochanteric fragment

options.label: B
options.text: Vancouver B1; treat by open reduction and internal fixation alone

options.label: C
options.text: Vancouver B2; treat by revision of the femoral stem to a long-stem implant

options.label: D
options.text: Vancouver B3; treat by proximal femoral replacement only

options.label: E
options.text: Vancouver C; treat by fixation distal to a well-fixed stem

explanation: The Vancouver classification guides management of periprosthetic femoral fractures. Type B fractures occur around or just distal to the stem and are subdivided by component fixation and bone stock: B1 (stem well-fixed), B2 (stem loose, adequate bone stock), and B3 (stem loose with poor/deficient bone stock). This fracture has a loose stem with good bone stock, making it B2, for which the standard treatment is revision to a long, often uncemented, stem that bypasses the fracture, usually with adjunctive fixation. ORIF alone (appropriate for B1) would fail because the underlying loose implant is not addressed. Type A is trochanteric and type C is well away from a well-fixed stem.
learningPoint: A Vancouver B2 periprosthetic fracture (loose stem, adequate bone stock) is treated by revision to a long stem bypassing the fracture, not ORIF alone.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Periprosthetic Fractures section

stem: A 6-year-old falls onto an outstretched hand and sustains a completely displaced (Gartland III) extension-type supracondylar fracture of the humerus. On examination the hand is well perfused and pink with a brisk capillary refill, but the radial pulse is not palpable. There is no neurological deficit. What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Urgent CT angiogram before any surgical intervention

options.label: B
options.text: Apply an above-elbow backslab in extension and admit for observation

options.label: C
options.text: Urgent closed reduction and percutaneous Kirschner-wire fixation, then reassess the perfusion and pulse

options.label: D
options.text: Immediate exploration of the brachial artery in theatre

options.label: E
options.text: Manipulate the fracture in the emergency department and discharge once a backslab is applied

explanation: A completely displaced supracondylar fracture with a pink, well-perfused but pulseless hand requires urgent surgery: prompt closed reduction and percutaneous K-wire fixation. In most cases reduction restores the pulse because the brachial artery is tented or kinked rather than transected. The perfusion is then reassessed: if the hand remains pink and perfused, observation is reasonable; if it becomes pale and pulseless (a 'white pulseless hand'), urgent vascular exploration is indicated. Pre-operative angiography wastes time and rarely changes management, immobilisation alone leaves an unstable displaced fracture unreduced, and exploration is not the first step when the hand is well perfused.
learningPoint: A pink, pulseless, displaced supracondylar fracture needs urgent reduction and K-wiring first; reassess perfusion afterwards and explore only if the hand becomes white and pulseless.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Paediatric Trauma chapter
BOAST: Supracondylar Fractures of the Humerus in Children

stem: A 5-month-old infant who is not yet walking or cruising is brought to the emergency department with a swollen thigh. Radiographs show a spiral fracture of the femoral shaft. The history of injury is vague and has changed between the parents' accounts. There are no other obvious external injuries. Which is the single most appropriate immediate course of action?

options.label: A
options.text: Apply a hip spica cast and arrange routine fracture clinic follow-up

options.label: B
options.text: Reassure the parents that femoral fractures are common at this age and discharge with analgesia

options.label: C
options.text: Treat the fracture and initiate child protection safeguarding procedures, including referral to the safeguarding team and a skeletal survey

options.label: D
options.text: Request only a serum alkaline phosphatase and vitamin D level before deciding on further action

options.label: E
options.text: Discharge the child once analgesia is given and review only if symptoms persist

explanation: A femoral fracture in a non-ambulant infant is a major red flag for non-accidental injury, compounded here by a vague and inconsistent history. The mandatory response is to treat the fracture appropriately and simultaneously activate child protection processes: involve the paediatric safeguarding/child protection team, perform a skeletal survey (and other investigations such as fundoscopy and clotting/metabolic bone screens as indicated), and ensure the child is admitted to a place of safety. Treating the fracture in isolation, discharging the child, or relying on a single metabolic test fails the safeguarding duty and may return the child to a dangerous environment.
learningPoint: A femoral (or any long-bone) fracture in a non-ambulant infant mandates safeguarding referral and a skeletal survey to exclude non-accidental injury.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Paediatric Trauma chapter (child abuse)
RCPCH Child Protection guidance / NICE NG76 (child maltreatment)

stem: A 24-year-old rugby player presents 6 weeks after an anterior shoulder dislocation with weakness of arm abduction and a coin-sized patch of altered sensation over the lateral aspect of the proximal arm (the 'regimental badge' area). Wasting of the deltoid is developing. The nerve responsible passes through a space whose boundaries you must know. Which of the following correctly defines the LATERAL boundary of the quadrangular (quadrilateral) space?

options.label: A
options.text: Long head of triceps

options.label: B
options.text: Teres minor

options.label: C
options.text: Teres major

options.label: D
options.text: Surgical neck of the humerus

options.label: E
options.text: Subscapularis

explanation: The clinical picture is an axillary nerve palsy — the commonest nerve injury after anterior glenohumeral dislocation. The axillary nerve and posterior circumflex humeral vessels traverse the quadrangular space, bounded superiorly by teres minor (subscapularis on the anterior view), inferiorly by teres major, medially by the long head of triceps, and laterally by the surgical neck of the humerus. Because the nerve hugs the surgical neck, it is also at risk from proximal humeral fractures and from inferiorly placed glenohumeral instability hardware. It supplies deltoid and teres minor (motor) and the upper lateral cutaneous nerve of the arm (the regimental badge patch).
learningPoint: The lateral wall of the quadrangular space is the surgical neck of the humerus — why the axillary nerve is injured in anterior dislocation and proximal humeral fractures.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Anatomy and surgical approaches: shoulder

stem: A surgeon performs a deltopectoral approach for fixation of a proximal humeral fracture. The cephalic vein is identified and retracted. Working medially along the conjoint tendon (short head of biceps and coracobrachialis), the surgeon is warned not to retract it forcefully. The structure most vulnerable to forceful medial retraction of the conjoint tendon, entering coracobrachialis a few centimetres distal to the coracoid, is which nerve?

options.label: A
options.text: Axillary nerve

options.label: B
options.text: Median nerve

options.label: C
options.text: Musculocutaneous nerve

options.label: D
options.text: Radial nerve

options.label: E
options.text: Suprascapular nerve

explanation: In the deltopectoral interval (deltoid = axillary nerve; pectoralis major = medial/lateral pectoral nerves), the conjoint tendon is retracted medially to expose subscapularis and the joint. The musculocutaneous nerve pierces coracobrachialis on its deep surface, classically 5–8 cm distal to the coracoid tip, and is at risk from vigorous medial retraction of the conjoint tendon. The axillary nerve is the deep structure at risk inferiorly (along the subscapularis lower border and quadrangular space), not from conjoint retraction. Protecting the musculocutaneous nerve means limiting the force and depth of medial retraction.
learningPoint: The musculocutaneous nerve enters coracobrachialis ~5–8 cm below the coracoid and is endangered by forceful medial retraction of the conjoint tendon in the deltopectoral approach.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Surgical approaches: shoulder (deltopectoral)
Hoppenfeld — Surgical Exposures in Orthopaedics: anterior approach to the shoulder

stem: During the proximal portion of a Henry (anterior) approach to the radius, the surgeon fully supinates the forearm before subperiosteally elevating muscle from the anterior surface of the proximal radial shaft. What is the principal anatomical reason that full supination is used at this stage?

options.label: A
options.text: It brings the radial artery medially, away from the field

options.label: B
options.text: It moves the posterior interosseous nerve laterally and protects it by carrying the supinator insertion away from the dissection

options.label: C
options.text: It relaxes pronator teres so its insertion can be released

options.label: D
options.text: It tightens the interosseous membrane to define the ulnar border

options.label: E
options.text: It delivers the bicipital tuberosity anteriorly for screw placement

explanation: The posterior interosseous nerve (deep branch of the radial nerve) runs within and around the supinator as it wraps the proximal radius. In the Henry approach the proximal internervous plane is between brachioradialis (radial nerve) and pronator teres (median nerve). Full supination rotates the supinator and its radial insertion — and with it the PIN — laterally/posteriorly, so that subperiosteal elevation of muscle off the anterior radius is performed away from the nerve. Pronation does the opposite and brings the PIN into harm's way; pronation is instead used more distally to expose the dorsal radius.
learningPoint: Supinate the forearm to take the PIN (and supinator insertion) laterally before elevating muscle off the proximal radius in Henry's approach; pronate distally.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Surgical approaches: forearm (Henry)
Hoppenfeld — Anterior (Henry) approach to the radius

stem: A surgeon is performing an open carpal tunnel decompression. Which one of the following structures passes THROUGH the carpal tunnel deep to the flexor retinaculum?

options.label: A
options.text: Ulnar nerve

options.label: B
options.text: Palmaris longus tendon

options.label: C
options.text: Flexor pollicis longus tendon

options.label: D
options.text: Flexor carpi radialis tendon

options.label: E
options.text: Palmar cutaneous branch of the median nerve

explanation: The carpal tunnel transmits the median nerve plus nine tendons: four flexor digitorum superficialis, four flexor digitorum profundus, and flexor pollicis longus. The ulnar nerve and artery lie in Guyon's canal, superficial and ulnar to the retinaculum. Palmaris longus lies superficial to (and partly inserts into) the retinaculum. Flexor carpi radialis runs in its own fibro-osseous tunnel within a split of the retinaculum, not the main carpal tunnel. The palmar cutaneous branch arises proximal to the tunnel and crosses superficially — its protection is why the incision is kept ulnar to the thenar crease/palmaris longus axis.
learningPoint: FPL is the only thumb flexor in the carpal tunnel; the ulnar nerve (Guyon's canal) and palmar cutaneous branch lie outside it — key to safe decompression.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand and wrist anatomy

stem: A 22-year-old falls on the outstretched hand and is tender in the anatomical snuffbox. A CT later confirms a fracture through the proximal third of the scaphoid. Knowledge of the scaphoid's vascular anatomy explains the high non-union/avascular necrosis risk of this fracture pattern. Which statement about scaphoid blood supply is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: The proximal pole is supplied by branches entering the proximal articular surface from the radial artery

options.label: B
options.text: The dominant supply enters dorsally at or distal to the waist (dorsal ridge) from the radial artery and perfuses the proximal pole in a retrograde direction

options.label: C
options.text: The supply is predominantly from the anterior interosseous artery entering the tubercle

options.label: D
options.text: The proximal pole has the richest direct arterial supply, so proximal fractures heal fastest

options.label: E
options.text: Blood enters chiefly through the volar waist from the ulnar artery

explanation: Roughly 70–80% of the scaphoid's intraosseous blood enters through dorsal ridge vessels (branches of the radial artery) at or just distal to the waist, supplying the proximal pole in a retrograde fashion. A volar branch supplies the distal pole/tubercle. Because the proximal pole has no independent direct supply and depends on retrograde flow, fractures of the proximal third (and waist) interrupt this and carry the highest rates of delayed/non-union and avascular necrosis — justifying a low threshold for fixation and careful preservation of the dorsal blood supply during surgery.
learningPoint: Scaphoid blood supply is dorsal-ridge dominant and retrograde to the proximal pole — the anatomical basis for proximal-pole non-union and AVN.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Carpus: scaphoid vascular anatomy

subtopic: Peripheral nerve courses & named lesions — upper limb
stem: Theme: Named upper-limb peripheral nerve lesions. For each clinical description, select the SINGLE most likely nerve or named lesion from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Posterior interosseous nerve

options.label: B
options.text: Anterior interosseous nerve

options.label: C
options.text: Ulnar nerve at the elbow (cubital tunnel)

options.label: D
options.text: Ulnar nerve at the wrist (Guyon's canal, deep branch)

options.label: E
options.text: Radial nerve in the spiral groove

options.label: F
options.text: Median nerve in the carpal tunnel

options.label: G
options.text: Lower trunk of the brachial plexus (T1)

options.label: H
options.text: Long thoracic nerve

emiLeads.stem: A 50-year-old presents with weakness of finger extension at the MCP joints (finger drop). Wrist extension is preserved but deviates radially when attempted, and there is no sensory loss anywhere in the limb.
A patient cannot make an 'OK' sign — there is loss of pulp-to-pulp pinch with inability to flex the IP joint of the thumb and the DIP joint of the index finger — but there is no sensory deficit.

A heavy manual worker who repeatedly uses the heel of the hand as a hammer develops weakness and wasting of the interossei with a positive Froment's sign, but completely normal sensation over the little finger and hypothenar eminence.

A patient falls asleep with the arm draped over the back of a chair and wakes with wrist drop and weakness of brachioradialis, but with a preserved triceps and a patch of numbness over the dorsal first web space.
A scapula that 'wings' medially when the patient pushes against a wall, following an axillary lymph node dissection.

explanation: PIN palsy gives finger-drop with preserved sensation (the PIN is purely motor) and radially deviated wrist extension because ECRL (innervated proximal to the PIN take-off) is spared — there is no true wrist drop. The AIN is also purely motor: it supplies FPL, the radial half of FDP and pronator quadratus, so an AIN lesion abolishes the OK sign with no sensory loss. A deep-branch ulnar lesion in Guyon's canal spares the dorsal and palmar cutaneous branches (given off proximal to the canal), so motor loss occurs with normal little-finger sensation. A radial nerve lesion in the spiral groove ('Saturday night palsy') spares triceps but affects brachioradialis/wrist extension and dorsal first web-space sensation. Long thoracic nerve injury (serratus anterior, C5–7) causes medial winging and is a recognised complication of axillary surgery.
learningPoint: Pure-motor radial-territory lesions (PIN, AIN) have no sensory loss; a PIN lesion causes finger drop with preserved (radially deviated) wrist extension, not true wrist drop. The level of an ulnar lesion is told by whether the dorsal/palmar cutaneous branches are spared.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Peripheral nerve anatomy and entrapment

stem: A posterior wall acetabular fracture is fixed through a Kocher-Langenbeck approach. The short external rotators are tagged and divided to protect the sciatic nerve and to preserve the medial circumflex femoral artery to the femoral head. Which external rotator should be preserved (not routinely divided) to help protect the main blood supply to the femoral head?

options.label: A
options.text: Piriformis

options.label: B
options.text: Superior gemellus

options.label: C
options.text: Obturator internus

options.label: D
options.text: Quadratus femoris

options.label: E
options.text: Inferior gemellus

explanation: The medial femoral circumflex artery (the dominant supply to the adult femoral head via the deep/ascending branch and retinacular vessels) runs along the upper border of quadratus femoris before passing deep to obturator externus and ascending posterior to the femoral neck. In the Kocher-Langenbeck approach the short rotators (piriformis, gemelli, obturator internus) are commonly divided 1–1.5 cm from their femoral insertion to protect both the nerve and the vessel, but quadratus femoris is preserved because dividing it close to the trochanter risks the MFCA. Tagging the rotators away from their insertion also keeps a cuff that protects the artery.
learningPoint: Preserve quadratus femoris in the Kocher-Langenbeck approach — the medial femoral circumflex artery runs at its superior border and is the femoral head's main supply.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Acetabular approaches; femoral head vascularity
Gautier/Ganz — vascular anatomy of the femoral head

stem: You are siting a guidewire near the femoral triangle and revising the relationships of its contents from lateral to medial. Within the femoral triangle, which structure lies immediately MEDIAL to the femoral vein?

options.label: A
options.text: Femoral nerve

options.label: B
options.text: Femoral artery

options.label: C
options.text: Femoral canal (containing lymphatics / Cloquet's node)

options.label: D
options.text: Lateral femoral cutaneous nerve

options.label: E
options.text: Profunda femoris artery

explanation: From lateral to medial in the femoral triangle the order is Nerve, Artery, Vein, Empty space/Canal (mnemonic 'NAVEL', or NAVY where Y = Y-fronts/lymphatics). The femoral nerve lies outside the femoral sheath, lateral to the artery; the femoral artery and vein lie within the sheath; the femoral canal (the most medial compartment of the sheath, containing fat and the deep inguinal lymph node of Cloquet) lies immediately medial to the vein and is the site of a femoral hernia. The lateral femoral cutaneous nerve lies at the lateral apex near the ASIS, and the profunda arises from the artery's posterolateral aspect a few centimetres below the inguinal ligament.
learningPoint: NAVEL lateral-to-medial: the femoral canal (Cloquet's node, femoral hernia site) lies immediately medial to the femoral vein.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hip and thigh anatomy

stem: A patient develops a foot drop with sensory loss over the dorsum of the foot and first web space after a high tibial osteotomy and a period in a poorly padded splint.
