The injured nerve is most vulnerable as it winds around a bony landmark at the knee. Around which structure does the common fibular (peroneal) nerve pass, making it susceptible at this site?

options.label: A
options.text: Medial tibial condyle

options.label: B
options.text: Neck of the fibula

options.label: C
options.text: Lateral head of gastrocnemius origin

options.label: D
options.text: Gerdy's tubercle

options.label: E
options.text: Adductor tubercle

explanation: The common fibular nerve runs along the posteromedial border of biceps femoris, crosses the posterolateral knee, and winds superficially around the neck of the fibula (under fibularis longus) before dividing into superficial and deep fibular branches. Its subcutaneous, fixed course over the fibular neck makes it the most commonly injured lower-limb nerve from compression (tight casts/splints, prolonged leg-crossing, lithotomy), traction (knee dislocation, varus injuries), or direct surgery (lateral knee approaches, proximal fibular/high tibial osteotomy). Deep fibular involvement gives foot drop and first-web-space numbness; padding over the fibular neck is a standard preventive measure.
learningPoint: The common fibular nerve is most vulnerable as it crosses the subcutaneous fibular neck — pad it after knee surgery/splinting to prevent foot drop.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Knee and leg anatomy
BOAST — Open fractures / cast and splint care (neurovascular monitoring)

subtopic: Surgical anatomy — leg (compartments / deep fibular nerve)
stem: A patient with a tibial shaft fracture develops acute compartment syndrome. The compartment most commonly involved contains the deep fibular (peroneal) nerve and the anterior tibial vessels. Loss of sensation in which area is the EARLIEST reliable sign of involvement of the nerve in this compartment?

options.label: A
options.text: Sole of the foot

options.label: B
options.text: Medial border of the foot

options.label: C
options.text: First dorsal web space

options.label: D
options.text: Lateral border of the foot / fifth toe

options.label: E
options.text: Heel pad

explanation: The anterior compartment of the leg (tibialis anterior, EHL, EDL, fibularis tertius) is the compartment most often affected in tibial fracture compartment syndrome and contains the deep fibular nerve and anterior tibial artery. The deep fibular nerve's only cutaneous territory is the first dorsal web space, so altered sensation there is the earliest reliable indicator of nerve involvement. The sole (tibial/medial+lateral plantar), lateral border (sural/superficial fibular contributions) and heel (medial calcaneal) are supplied by other nerves. Note that pain on passive stretch and a tense compartment are the key clinical findings; pulselessness and dense sensory loss are late.
learningPoint: Deep fibular nerve = first dorsal web space sensation; early loss there flags anterior compartment syndrome before late signs appear.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Leg compartments
BOAST — Diagnosis and Management of Compartment Syndrome of the Limbs

stem: A patient has burning sole pain and a positive Tinel's sign behind the medial malleolus, consistent with tarsal tunnel syndrome. The tibial nerve and its branches pass deep to the flexor retinaculum here, alongside several tendons. Which option correctly lists the contents of the tarsal tunnel from ANTERIOR (just behind the medial malleolus) to POSTERIOR?

options.label: A
options.text: Tibialis anterior, tibial nerve, flexor digitorum longus, flexor hallucis longus

options.label: B
options.text: Tibialis posterior, flexor digitorum longus, posterior tibial artery & tibial nerve, flexor hallucis longus

options.label: C
options.text: Flexor hallucis longus, tibial nerve, flexor digitorum longus, tibialis posterior

options.label: D
options.text: Tibialis posterior, posterior tibial artery, flexor hallucis longus, flexor digitorum longus

options.label: E
options.text: Flexor digitorum longus, tibialis posterior, flexor hallucis longus, tibial nerve

explanation: From anterior (just behind/below the medial malleolus) to posterior the tarsal tunnel contains: Tibialis posterior tendon, flexor Digitorum longus tendon, posterior tibial Artery and Vein with the tibial Nerve, then flexor Hallucis longus tendon — the classic 'Tom, Dick, And a Very Nervous Harry'. Within or just distal to the tunnel the tibial nerve divides into medial and lateral plantar nerves (and the medial calcaneal branch), which is why tarsal tunnel syndrome produces plantar burning/paraesthesia. FHL is the most posterior and lies in its own groove on the posterior talus/calcaneus.
learningPoint: Tarsal tunnel order (Tom, Dick, And Very Nervous Harry): tib. post., FDL, post. tibial artery+vein+tibial nerve, FHL.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Foot and ankle anatomy

stem: A child is being assessed for atlanto-axial instability. The atlanto-axial articulation depends on a ligament that holds the dens against the anterior arch of the atlas and is the primary check against anterior translation of C1 on C2. Which structure provides this primary restraint?

options.label: A
options.text: Alar ligaments

options.label: B
options.text: Apical ligament of the dens

options.label: C
options.text: Transverse ligament of the atlas (part of the cruciate ligament)

options.label: D
options.text: Tectorial membrane

options.label: E
options.text: Ligamentum flavum

explanation: The transverse ligament of the atlas — the horizontal limb of the cruciate ligament — passes behind the dens and anchors it against the anterior arch of C1, providing the primary restraint to anterior atlanto-axial translation. Its competence is assessed radiographically by the atlanto-dens interval (ADI): >3 mm in adults or >5 mm in children suggests transverse ligament insufficiency. The alar ligaments (dens to occipital condyles) are secondary restraints and limit axial rotation and lateral bending; the apical ligament and tectorial membrane (the cranial continuation of the posterior longitudinal ligament) are minor stabilisers. Transverse ligament laxity is relevant in Down syndrome, rheumatoid arthritis and certain skeletal dysplasias.
learningPoint: The transverse ligament of the atlas is the primary restraint to anterior C1–C2 translation; assess with the atlanto-dens interval (>3 mm adult / >5 mm child).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Cervical spine anatomy (craniovertebral junction)
BSCOS — Down syndrome and atlanto-axial instability (screening principles)

subtopic: Spinal osteology & arthrology — lumbar pedicle / nerve root relationships
stem: A 45-year-old has a paracentral (posterolateral) L4/5 disc prolapse. Considering the relationship of the lumbar nerve roots to the pedicles and disc in the typical pre-foraminal location, which nerve root is most likely to be compressed?

options.label: A
options.text: L3 nerve root

options.label: B
options.text: L4 nerve root

options.label: C
options.text: L5 nerve root

options.label: D
options.text: S1 nerve root

options.label: E
options.text: Both L4 and L5 equally

explanation: In the lumbar spine each nerve root exits below the pedicle of its like-numbered vertebra (e.g. the L4 root exits the L4/5 foramen, below the L4 pedicle). A posterolateral (paracentral) disc prolapse at L4/5 strikes the traversing root in the lateral recess — the L5 root — which is descending to exit at L5/S1. The exiting L4 root has already passed above the disc near the upper foramen and is spared by a paracentral herniation; it would only be caught by a far-lateral (foraminal) L4/5 prolapse. This 'traversing vs exiting root' rule is fundamental to correlating level, herniation position and the expected radiculopathy.
learningPoint: A paracentral lumbar disc compresses the TRAVERSING root (the root that will exit one level below) — L4/5 paracentral hits L5; a far-lateral prolapse would hit the exiting L4 root.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Lumbar spine anatomy and disc herniation
NICE NG59 — Low back pain and sciatica in over 16s

stem: Limb rotation during embryonic development explains adult dermatome and muscle arrangement. The upper and lower limb buds initially have the same orientation, then rotate in opposite directions. Which statement about limb rotation is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: The upper limb rotates medially so the original ventral (flexor) surface comes to face dorsally

options.label: B
options.text: The lower limb rotates medially, bringing the great toe to the midline and directing the original extensor surface anteriorly

options.label: C
options.text: Both limbs rotate laterally, which is why the thumb and great toe both lie laterally

options.label: D
options.text: Neither limb rotates; differences are due to differential muscle growth alone

options.label: E
options.text: The lower limb rotates laterally, which is why the knee comes to point posteriorly

explanation: The limb buds first project with a flexor (ventral/preaxial) surface facing the trunk. The upper limb rotates laterally (externally) by ~90°, carrying the thumb to the lateral side and the elbow to point posteriorly, so the original flexor surface faces anteriorly. The lower limb rotates medially (internally), carrying the great toe to the medial side, the knee to point anteriorly and the original extensor (dorsal) surface to face forward — which is why the hamstrings (flexors) lie posteriorly and the patellar/extensor surface lies anteriorly, and why the lower-limb dermatomes spiral. This opposite rotation is the embryological key to adult limb dermatome patterns.
learningPoint: Upper limb rotates laterally (thumb lateral, elbow back); lower limb rotates medially (great toe medial, knee forward) — the basis of adult dermatome spirals.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Embryology and limb development
Moore — The Developing Human: limb rotation

subtopic: Physeal anatomy — zones of the growth plate
stem: The Salter-Harris classification describes fracture planes through the physis. Most physeal fractures, and the slip in a slipped capital femoral epiphysis, propagate through the structurally weakest zone of the growth plate. Through which histological zone does this plane of mechanical weakness typically run?

options.label: A
options.text: Zone of resting (reserve) cartilage

options.label: B
options.text: Proliferative zone

options.label: C
options.text: Hypertrophic zone (zone of provisional calcification)

options.label: D
options.text: Zone of primary spongiosa / metaphysis

options.label: E
options.text: Perichondrial ring of LaCroix

explanation: The hypertrophic zone — particularly its zone of provisional calcification — has the largest chondrocytes, the least extracellular matrix and the lowest mechanical strength, so most physeal fractures (and the SCFE slip) propagate through it. Critically, the germinal cells lie in the resting and proliferative zones, which are perfused by the epiphyseal vessels; a fracture confined to the hypertrophic zone therefore usually spares the growth potential, whereas injuries crossing into the proliferative/resting zone or interrupting the epiphyseal blood supply (e.g. Salter-Harris IV/V, or proximal femoral epiphyseal vessel injury) threaten growth arrest. The perichondrial ring of LaCroix provides peripheral mechanical support, not the cleavage plane.
learningPoint: Physeal fractures cleave through the hypertrophic zone (zone of provisional calcification); the germinal resting/proliferative zones and their epiphyseal supply must be preserved to avoid growth arrest.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Physeal anatomy and the growth plate

stem: A surgeon uses the Kocher lateral approach to expose the radial head for fixation. The internervous plane lies between anconeus and extensor carpi ulnaris. To avoid injuring the posterior interosseous nerve as it crosses the proximal radius within supinator, which manoeuvre and which limit should the surgeon observe during distal dissection?

options.label: A
options.text: Pronate the forearm and limit distal dissection; pronation carries the PIN away from the field

options.label: B
options.text: Supinate the forearm and dissect freely distally; supination removes all risk to the PIN

options.label: C
options.text: Keep the forearm in neutral and divide the annular ligament fully to mobilise the head

options.label: D
options.text: Pronate the forearm but extend dissection 5–6 cm distal to the radial head to find the PIN

options.label: E
options.text: Supinate the forearm and release the lateral ulnar collateral ligament to expose the head

explanation: In the Kocher approach (anconeus = radial nerve via the nerve to anconeus; ECU = posterior interosseous nerve), the PIN is the structure at greatest risk distally as it spirals around the radial neck within supinator. PRONATION of the forearm moves the PIN medially and distally, away from the operative field on the radial neck — the opposite of the Henry approach, where supination protects it (the nerve sits on the anterolateral surface there). Dissection should also be kept proximal — staying within ~2 cm of the radial head — to avoid the nerve. The lateral ulnar collateral ligament must be preserved (releasing it causes posterolateral rotatory instability), so options releasing it are wrong.
learningPoint: Pronate the forearm and stay proximal in the Kocher approach to protect the PIN; preserve the LUCL to avoid posterolateral rotatory instability.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Surgical approaches: elbow (Kocher)
Hoppenfeld — Lateral approach to the elbow and radial head

subtopic: Bone structure & function — composition and hierarchy
stem: A trainee is asked at viva to describe the composition of normal cortical bone. Considering the organic matrix specifically, which single component predominates and is principally responsible for the tensile strength and toughness of bone?

options.label: A
options.text: Type II collagen

options.label: B
options.text: Type I collagen

options.label: C
options.text: Hydroxyapatite [Ca10(PO4)6(OH)2]

options.label: D
options.text: Proteoglycan aggrecan

options.label: E
options.text: Osteocalcin

explanation: Bone is a composite material. By dry weight it is roughly 60-70% inorganic mineral (hydroxyapatite), 20-30% organic matrix and the remainder water. Of the organic matrix, ~90% is type I collagen, which provides tensile strength, flexibility and toughness (resistance to crack propagation); the mineral phase confers compressive strength and stiffness. The question restricts the answer to the ORGANIC matrix, so type I collagen (B) is correct. Hydroxyapatite (C) is the dominant constituent overall but is inorganic, not organic — a deliberate trap. Type II collagen (A) is the collagen of hyaline/articular cartilage, not bone. Aggrecan (D) is the major proteoglycan of cartilage, present only in small amounts in bone. Osteocalcin (E) is the most abundant NON-collagenous bone protein and a marker of bone formation, but it is a minor component by mass. The hierarchical arrangement (tropocollagen → fibrils → lamellae → osteons) and the staggered deposition of mineral within and between collagen fibrils explain why bone outperforms either constituent alone.
learningPoint: Bone is a composite: type I collagen (the dominant ORGANIC component) gives tensile strength/toughness; hydroxyapatite gives compressive stiffness.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Basic Science: bone structure and composition

subtopic: Bone structure & function — cell biology and mechanotransduction
stem: A researcher studies how the skeleton senses mechanical load and orchestrates targeted remodelling. Which bone cell is the most abundant, lies within lacunae connected by a canalicular network, and is regarded as the principal mechanosensor that regulates the activity of osteoblasts and osteoclasts?

options.label: A
options.text: Osteoblast

options.label: B
options.text: Osteoclast

options.label: C
options.text: Osteocyte

options.label: D
options.text: Bone-lining cell

options.label: E
options.text: Marrow mesenchymal stem cell

explanation: Osteocytes are terminally differentiated osteoblasts that have become entombed within mineralised matrix, residing in lacunae and communicating through cytoplasmic processes running in canaliculi (the lacuno-canalicular system) via gap junctions. They are by far the most numerous bone cell (>90% of bone cells) and are the dominant mechanosensors: fluid shear across the canalicular network during loading is transduced into biochemical signals. Critically, osteocytes regulate both arms of remodelling — they are the principal source of SCLEROSTIN (SOST gene), which inhibits Wnt signalling and therefore restrains bone formation, and of RANKL, which drives osteoclastogenesis. Mechanical loading suppresses sclerostin, releasing the brake on formation — the rationale behind the anti-sclerostin antibody romosozumab. Osteoblasts (A) form osteoid; osteoclasts (B), of haematopoietic/monocyte lineage, resorb bone; bone-lining cells (D) are quiescent flat cells on resting surfaces; mesenchymal stem cells (E) are the osteoblast precursor, not a mechanosensor. Hence osteocyte (C).
learningPoint: Osteocytes are the most abundant bone cell and the master mechanosensor — they secrete sclerostin (brakes formation) and RANKL (drives resorption).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Basic Science: bone cells and remodelling
Bonewald — The osteocyte (J Bone Miner Res)

subtopic: Fracture healing — primary vs secondary, stages
stem: A 24-year-old woman sustains a displaced transverse mid-shaft radius fracture treated by open reduction and rigid compression plating with anatomical reduction and a lag screw. At three months radiographs show the fracture line gradually disappearing with NO external callus. Which process is most directly responsible for healing across the directly apposed cortical bone?

options.label: A
options.text: Endochondral ossification of a cartilaginous soft callus

options.label: B
options.text: Haversian remodelling by osteoclast cutting cones crossing the fracture line

options.label: C
options.text: Intramembranous bone formation under distraction (tension-stress)

options.label: D
options.text: Periosteal bridging callus driven by interfragmentary strain

options.label: E
options.text: Creeping substitution of necrotic bone graft

explanation: Anatomical reduction with interfragmentary compression (lag screw + compression plate) delivers ABSOLUTE stability, reducing interfragmentary strain to <2%. Under these conditions bone heals by PRIMARY (direct) healing with no visible callus. Where there is true contact, 'contact healing' occurs as osteoclast-led cutting cones bore directly across the fracture line, followed by trailing osteoblasts laying down new osteons — i.e. Haversian remodelling crossing the fracture (B). In areas with a gap of up to ~1 mm, gap healing fills the defect with lamellar bone oriented perpendicular to the long axis, later remodelled longitudinally; both are forms of primary healing. Endochondral ossification of a soft callus (A) and periosteal bridging callus from interfragmentary strain (D) are features of SECONDARY (indirect) healing under relative stability, which would not occur here and would actually signify inadequate fixation. Distraction osteogenesis (C) is intramembranous bone under gradual tension (Ilizarov). Creeping substitution (E) describes revascularisation/incorporation of necrotic or grafted bone, not this clinical scenario. The absence of callus is the radiographic signature of primary healing.
learningPoint: Absolute stability → primary healing (cutting cones/Haversian remodelling, NO callus); relative stability → secondary healing (callus, endochondral ossification).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Basic Science: bone injury and repair
AO Principles of Fracture Management; Perren strain theory

subtopic: Fracture healing — modes, stages and modifiers
stem: Theme: biology of fracture healing. For each clinical scenario, select the single most appropriate descriptor of the dominant healing process or the factor most responsible for the outcome described. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Secondary (indirect) healing with endochondral ossification

options.label: B
options.text: Primary (direct) healing by Haversian remodelling / cutting cones

options.label: C
options.text: Inflammatory (haematoma) phase

options.label: D
options.text: Soft callus phase

options.label: E
options.text: Hard callus phase

options.label: F
options.text: Distraction osteogenesis (intramembranous)

options.label: G
options.text: Excessive interfragmentary strain causing hypertrophic non-union

options.label: H
options.text: Impaired vascularity causing atrophic non-union

explanation: Map each scenario to the relevant phase, mode or modifier of healing. (1) A bridging plate spanning a comminuted diaphyseal fracture provides RELATIVE stability, so healing proceeds by secondary/indirect mechanism with endochondral ossification and visible callus (A). (2) A reamed locked IM nail showing abundant callus that fails to consolidate, with an 'elephant-foot' appearance and hypertrophic ends, indicates too much motion at the gap — hypertrophic non-union from excessive interfragmentary strain; the biology is intact but the mechanics are not (G). (3) An atrophic, sclerotic, tapered non-union after a heavily stripped open fracture reflects loss of blood supply/biology (H). (4) The earliest event after fracture — bleeding, fibrin clot, cytokine and growth-factor release (TNF-α, IL-1/6, BMPs) with a haematoma — is the inflammatory phase (C). (5) Gradual lengthening of a corticotomy in a circular frame producing new bone within the distraction gap WITHOUT a cartilage intermediate is distraction osteogenesis, which heals by intramembranous ossification under tension-stress (F). Distinguishing hypertrophic (mechanical — treat by improving stability) from atrophic (biological — treat by improving biology ± stability) non-union is a classic viva discriminator.
learningPoint: Hypertrophic non-union = mechanical problem (add stability); atrophic non-union = biological problem (restore vascularity/biology). Stages run haematoma → soft callus → hard callus → remodelling.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Basic Science: bone injury and repair
AO Principles of Fracture Management; BOAST — Open Fracture Management

emiLeads.stem: A comminuted mid-shaft humeral fracture is treated with a long bridging plate left deliberately under-contoured, preserving the soft tissues. Serial radiographs show progressively enlarging external callus that goes on to unite.

Nine months after a reamed locked femoral nail, an active man still has pain at the fracture. Radiographs show abundant, dense callus with widened sclerotic bone ends ('elephant foot') but a persistent lucent line and a backed-out distal locking screw.

Twelve months after a Gustilo IIIB open tibial fracture that required extensive periosteal stripping and a free flap, radiographs show a thin, tapered, sclerotic gap with rounded avascular-looking bone ends and no callus.

Within the first 48 hours of a closed fracture, histology of the fracture site shows a fibrin-rich clot infiltrated by neutrophils and macrophages, with release of TNF-alpha, interleukins and bone morphogenetic proteins.

A tibial lengthening is performed through a metaphyseal corticotomy in a circular frame. After a latency period the segment is distracted 1 mm per day in quarter-turn increments; new bone forms within the gap as longitudinal columns without an intervening cartilage phase.

subtopic: Bone metabolism — vitamin D / calcium / PTH homeostasis
stem: A patient's vitamin D metabolism is being explained at teaching. Considering where the biologically active hormone is produced, which enzyme carries out the final, tightly regulated activation step, and in which organ does this predominantly occur?

options.label: A
options.text: 25-hydroxylase in the liver

options.label: B
options.text: 1-alpha-hydroxylase in the proximal renal tubule

options.label: C
options.text: 24-hydroxylase in the kidney

options.label: D
options.text: 7-dehydrocholesterol reductase in the skin

options.label: E
options.text: 1-alpha-hydroxylase in the liver

explanation: Vitamin D is activated in two hydroxylation steps. In the skin UVB converts 7-dehydrocholesterol to cholecalciferol (vitamin D3). The first hydroxylation occurs in the LIVER by 25-hydroxylase, producing 25-hydroxyvitamin D (calcidiol) — the major circulating storage form and the metabolite measured to assess vitamin D status. The SECOND and rate-limiting activation step is performed by 1-alpha-hydroxylase (CYP27B1) in the PROXIMAL RENAL TUBULE, generating 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D (calcitriol), the active hormone (answer B). This renal step is the tightly regulated one: PTH and hypophosphataemia up-regulate it, whereas FGF-23 and calcitriol itself (negative feedback) suppress it. 24-hydroxylase (C) is a catabolic enzyme that inactivates vitamin D to 24,25-(OH)2D. Liver 25-hydroxylase (A) performs the first, not the final, step. Option E mislocates 1-alpha-hydroxylase to the liver. This explains why advanced chronic kidney disease, with loss of functioning renal 1-alpha-hydroxylase, causes calcitriol deficiency and contributes to renal osteodystrophy.
learningPoint: Active vitamin D (calcitriol) is made by renal 1-alpha-hydroxylase (CYP27B1) — the rate-limiting step, up-regulated by PTH and down-regulated by FGF-23.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Basic Science: calcium and vitamin D metabolism
NICE CKS — Vitamin D deficiency in adults

subtopic: Bone metabolism — PTH and calcium homeostasis
stem: Parathyroid hormone (PTH) is secreted in response to a fall in ionised serum calcium. Which combination of direct physiological actions of PTH correctly describes how it restores the serum calcium?

options.label: A
options.text: Increases renal calcium reabsorption, increases renal phosphate reabsorption, stimulates renal 1-alpha-hydroxylase

options.label: B
options.text: Increases renal calcium reabsorption, increases renal phosphate excretion, stimulates renal 1-alpha-hydroxylase

options.label: C
options.text: Decreases renal calcium reabsorption, increases renal phosphate excretion, inhibits renal 1-alpha-hydroxylase

options.label: D
options.text: Increases renal calcium reabsorption, increases renal phosphate excretion, acts directly on osteoclasts to drive resorption

options.label: E
options.text: Increases renal phosphate reabsorption, inhibits osteoclastic resorption, inhibits intestinal calcium absorption

explanation: PTH raises serum calcium through three coordinated actions. (1) Kidney — it increases distal tubular reabsorption of CALCIUM while reducing reabsorption of PHOSPHATE in the proximal tubule (it is phosphaturic), preventing the calcium-phosphate product from rising and precipitating. (2) Kidney (indirect on gut) — it stimulates 1-alpha-hydroxylase, increasing calcitriol, which in turn enhances INTESTINAL calcium (and phosphate) absorption; PTH has no direct gut action. (3) Bone — it increases osteoclastic resorption, but INDIRECTLY: osteoclasts lack PTH receptors, so PTH acts on osteoblasts/osteocytes (PTH1R) to up-regulate RANKL and suppress osteoprotegerin, thereby driving osteoclast activity. Option B captures the renal handling correctly. Option A wrongly retains phosphate. Option C reverses the calcium and calcitriol effects. Option D contains the classic error that PTH acts DIRECTLY on osteoclasts. Option E is essentially the opposite of PTH's actions (closer to calcitonin). Note the paradox that CONTINUOUS PTH excess (hyperparathyroidism) is catabolic, whereas INTERMITTENT PTH (teriparatide) is anabolic to bone.
learningPoint: PTH raises Ca by increasing renal Ca reabsorption, causing phosphaturia, boosting calcitriol (gut absorption) and driving osteoclasts INDIRECTLY via osteoblast RANKL.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Basic Science: bone metabolism and calcium homeostasis
Society for Endocrinology — primary hyperparathyroidism guidance

stem: A 74-year-old woman with severe osteoporosis (T-score -3.8) and two recent vertebral fragility fractures is reviewed after a further fracture despite adherent oral bisphosphonate therapy. The metabolic bone team consider romosozumab. By what mechanism does this agent increase bone mineral density?

options.label: A
options.text: Monoclonal antibody to RANK ligand, inhibiting osteoclastogenesis

options.label: B
options.text: Monoclonal antibody to sclerostin, releasing Wnt-mediated bone formation

options.label: C
options.text: Recombinant PTH(1-34) giving intermittent PTH-receptor stimulation

options.label: D
options.text: Selective oestrogen receptor modulator acting on bone oestrogen receptors

options.label: E
options.text: Inhibitor of farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase in the osteoclast mevalonate pathway

explanation: Romosozumab is a humanised monoclonal antibody against SCLEROSTIN, the osteocyte-derived inhibitor of the Wnt/β-catenin signalling pathway. By neutralising sclerostin it releases the brake on osteoblastic bone formation while also transiently reducing resorption, giving it a dual (predominantly anabolic) effect and rapid, large BMD gains — hence its use in severe disease at very high fracture risk (answer B). In the UK, NICE recommends romosozumab for postmenopausal women with severe osteoporosis at high risk after a fragility fracture, given monthly subcutaneously for 12 months and then followed by an antiresorptive to maintain the gains. Importantly it carries a cardiovascular caution and is contraindicated after recent myocardial infarction or stroke. Option A describes denosumab (anti-RANKL). Option C is teriparatide/abaloparatide (anabolic via intermittent PTH-receptor agonism). Option D is a SERM such as raloxifene. Option E is the mechanism of nitrogen-containing bisphosphonates (e.g. alendronate, zoledronate). Knowing which drugs are anabolic (teriparatide, abaloparatide, romosozumab) versus antiresorptive (bisphosphonates, denosumab, SERMs) — and the need for sequential antiresorptive cover after stopping anabolic/denosumab therapy — is core FRCS knowledge.
learningPoint: Romosozumab = anti-sclerostin antibody (dual anabolic/antiresorptive); follow it with an antiresorptive and avoid after recent MI/stroke.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Basic Science: bone metabolism (Wnt/sclerostin)
NICE TA791 — Romosozumab for treating severe osteoporosis

stem: A 79-year-old woman is admitted with a low-energy intertrochanteric hip fracture and undergoes fixation. She is otherwise on no bone-protective treatment. In line with current UK practice, which is the most appropriate approach to secondary fracture prevention?

options.label: A
options.text: No bone-protection treatment is indicated unless a subsequent DEXA T-score is below -2.5

options.label: B
options.text: Commence bone-protection (e.g. an oral or IV bisphosphonate with calcium/vitamin D as needed) as she has had a hip fragility fracture, alongside falls assessment

options.label: C
options.text: Start teriparatide as first-line treatment for all hip fragility fractures

options.label: D
options.text: Treat only with calcium and vitamin D supplementation, with no antiresorptive

options.label: E
options.text: Defer any assessment or treatment until the fracture has united

explanation: A low-energy hip fracture in an older adult is, by definition, a fragility fracture and is itself a powerful predictor of future fracture — one of the strongest clinical risk factors. UK guidance (NOGG, NICE, and the BOAST/fragility-fracture/falls-and-fracture frameworks, supported by the National Hip Fracture Database) is that such patients should be assessed for and started on bone-protection therapy without necessarily waiting for, or being gated by, a DEXA result; in those aged 75 and over with a hip or vertebral fragility fracture, treatment is generally recommended on clinical grounds. First-line is usually a bisphosphonate (oral alendronate/risedronate or IV zoledronate where oral is unsuitable), with correction of calcium/vitamin D status and a falls assessment (answer B). Option A wrongly makes DEXA a prerequisite for treatment in this high-risk group. Teriparatide (C) is reserved for severe/high-risk cases or treatment failure, not routine first-line. Calcium/vitamin D alone (D) is inadequate as monotherapy for an established fragility fracture. Deferring assessment (E) misses the key window — fracture liaison services exist precisely to capture these patients. Integrated orthogeriatric and FLS care also reduces mortality.
learningPoint: A hip or vertebral fragility fracture is itself an indication for bone protection (usually a bisphosphonate) plus falls assessment — don't gate treatment on DEXA in the over-75s.
references: NICE NG226 — Osteoporosis: assessing the risk of fragility fracture; NOGG guidance
BOAST — Care of the Older or Frail Orthopaedic Trauma Patient; National Hip Fracture Database

stem: A 4-year-old boy has bowing of the legs, short stature and widening of the wrists. Blood tests show: corrected calcium normal, phosphate LOW, alkaline phosphatase HIGH, PTH normal, and 25-hydroxyvitamin D normal. There is a strong family history affecting males and females across generations. Which is the single most likely diagnosis?

options.label: A
options.text: Nutritional (vitamin D deficiency) rickets

options.label: B
options.text: X-linked hypophosphataemic rickets

options.label: C
options.text: Hypophosphatasia

options.label: D
options.text: Renal osteodystrophy

options.label: E
options.text: Vitamin D-dependent rickets type 1 (1-alpha-hydroxylase deficiency)

explanation: The discriminating biochemistry is a LOW phosphate with NORMAL calcium, NORMAL PTH and NORMAL 25-hydroxyvitamin D, plus a high ALP and a dominant family pattern affecting both sexes across generations. This is X-linked hypophosphataemic rickets (XLH), the commonest heritable form of rickets, caused by PHEX mutations leading to excess circulating FGF-23. High FGF-23 causes renal phosphate wasting (low tubular reabsorption of phosphate) and inappropriately suppresses 1-alpha-hydroxylase, so calcitriol is low or inappropriately normal despite hypophosphataemia — hence isolated hypophosphataemia drives the rickets (answer B). Note XLH is dominantly inherited but, being X-linked, affected fathers transmit it only to daughters; the multigenerational both-sex pattern fits. Nutritional vitamin D deficiency (A) would show LOW 25-OH vitamin D, low/low-normal calcium and a HIGH PTH (secondary hyperparathyroidism) — not seen here. Hypophosphatasia (C) classically has a LOW alkaline phosphatase (ALPL mutation), the opposite of this picture. Renal osteodystrophy (D) would show a raised phosphate, raised PTH and abnormal renal function. Vitamin D-dependent rickets type 1 (E) is calcitriol deficiency with low calcium and high PTH. UK management of XLH now includes the anti-FGF-23 antibody burosumab in children, supplanting older oral phosphate/calcitriol regimens in many cases.
learningPoint: Low phosphate + normal Ca/PTH/25-OH-D + dominant family history = XLH (FGF-23 excess → renal phosphate wasting); treat with phosphate/calcitriol or burosumab.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Basic Science: metabolic bone disease (rickets)
NICE TA792 — Burosumab for treating X-linked hypophosphataemia in children and young people

stem: A 50-year-old woman of limited sun exposure presents with diffuse bone pain and proximal myopathy. A pelvic radiograph shows symmetrical lucent bands with sclerotic margins running perpendicular to the cortex across both femoral necks and the pubic rami. Which term best describes these radiographic lesions, and what is the underlying histological abnormality?

options.label: A
options.text: Looser zones (pseudofractures) — wide unmineralised osteoid seams with increased mineralisation lag time

options.label: B
options.text: Stress fractures — complete cortical disruption with periosteal callus only

options.label: C
options.text: Brown tumours — osteoclastic resorption cavities filled with fibrous tissue and haemosiderin

options.label: D
options.text: Pseudo-pseudofractures of Paget — mosaic lamellar bone with prominent cement lines

options.label: E
options.text: Bone infarcts — serpentine sclerosis from medullary necrosis

explanation: Symmetrical, transverse lucent bands with sclerotic borders at sites such as the medial femoral neck, pubic rami, ribs and scapulae are LOOSER ZONES (Milkman lines / pseudofractures), the radiographic hallmark of OSTEOMALACIA (the adult counterpart of rickets). They represent insufficiency-type stress lesions filled with unmineralised osteoid that fails to mineralise. The defining histology of osteomalacia, demonstrable on tetracycline-labelled undecalcified biopsy, is WIDE OSTEOID SEAMS covering an increased bone surface with a PROLONGED MINERALISATION LAG TIME — i.e. a qualitative mineralisation defect with a normal or increased amount of (un-mineralised) matrix (answer A). The clinical context (low sun exposure, diffuse bone pain, proximal myopathy) supports vitamin D deficiency osteomalacia, typically with low/low-normal calcium and phosphate, raised ALP and secondary hyperparathyroidism. Stress fractures (B) are not symmetrical and lack the osteoid-seam histology. Brown tumours (C) are focal lytic lesions of hyperparathyroidism. Paget (D) shows a mosaic pattern, not osteoid seams, and would have a markedly raised ALP with normal calcium/phosphate. Bone infarcts (E) give serpentine medullary sclerosis, not transverse pseudofractures.
learningPoint: Looser zones = pseudofractures of osteomalacia; biopsy shows wide unmineralised osteoid seams with prolonged mineralisation lag time (a qualitative defect).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Basic Science: osteomalacia and rickets
NICE CKS — Vitamin D deficiency in adults

stem: A 70-year-old man with long-standing polyostotic Paget disease of the femur, previously stable, develops new, severe, unremitting thigh pain over a few weeks. Repeat bloods show his alkaline phosphatase has risen sharply from its previous baseline. A radiograph shows a destructive lytic lesion with cortical breach and a soft-tissue mass at the site. Which complication is most likely?

options.label: A
options.text: Osteosarcoma arising in pagetic bone

options.label: B
options.text: Insufficiency fracture through the pagetic cortex

options.label: C
options.text: High-output cardiac failure

options.label: D
options.text: Secondary osteoarthritis of the adjacent hip

options.label: E
options.text: Giant cell (brown) tumour of hyperparathyroidism

explanation: The combination of NEW severe pain, a further SHARP RISE in alkaline phosphatase above the patient's pagetic baseline, and a destructive LYTIC lesion with cortical destruction and a soft-tissue mass is the classic presentation of SARCOMATOUS (most commonly OSTEOSARCOMATOUS) transformation of Paget disease (answer A). Although it complicates <1% of cases, Paget-associated osteosarcoma is the commonest cause of osteosarcoma in the elderly and carries a very poor prognosis; the femur, pelvis and humerus are typical sites. Urgent referral to a tertiary sarcoma unit for staging and image-guided biopsy (planned to avoid contaminating compartments) is mandated. An insufficiency/'banana' fracture (B) is a recognised pagetic complication but typically presents acutely on the convex (lateral) cortex of a bowed bone without a destructive soft-tissue mass. High-output cardiac failure (C) occurs only with very extensive disease and would not explain a focal lytic lesion. Secondary osteoarthritis (D) causes joint-line pain and space loss, not a destructive mass with rising ALP. Brown tumour (E) is a manifestation of hyperparathyroidism, not Paget, and would have abnormal calcium/PTH. Any sudden change in symptoms or biochemistry in known Paget disease must prompt exclusion of malignant transformation.
learningPoint: New pain + a destructive lytic lesion + a sharp rise in ALP in known Paget = osteosarcoma until proven otherwise — refer to a sarcoma unit.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Basic Science: Paget disease and its complications
British Sarcoma Group / BOA guidance on bone sarcoma referral

stem: A 68-year-old man with symptomatic monostotic Paget disease of the tibia has bowing pain and a markedly elevated alkaline phosphatase. He is scheduled for corrective osteotomy in three months. Regarding pharmacological management in current UK practice, which is the most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: A single intravenous infusion of zoledronic acid (potent bisphosphonate)

options.label: B
options.text: Long-term oral calcitonin as first-line disease control

options.label: C
options.text: Teriparatide to stimulate bone formation at the bowing site

options.label: D
options.text: Denosumab as the standard first-line agent for Paget disease

options.label: E
options.text: No drug treatment is ever indicated in Paget disease

explanation: Bisphosphonates are the mainstay of pharmacological treatment of Paget disease, and a SINGLE intravenous infusion of ZOLEDRONIC ACID is the most effective option, producing a rapid and durable biochemical response (normalisation/sustained suppression of ALP and bone turnover) in the majority of patients — hence it is recommended first-line for symptomatic disease in UK/specialist guidance (answer A). Recognised indications for treatment include bone pain attributable to active disease, and treatment before elective surgery on pagetic bone (as here) to reduce intra-operative hypervascular bleeding and post-operative complications; other indications include neurological compromise, hypercalcaemia of immobilisation and high-output cardiac failure. Calcitonin (B) is now rarely used and is far less effective/durable than IV bisphosphonate, reserved for the few who cannot have bisphosphonates. Teriparatide (C) is an anabolic osteoporosis drug and is inappropriate — even potentially harmful — in the high-turnover, hypervascular pagetic lesion. Denosumab (D) is not the standard agent for Paget. Stating treatment is never indicated (E) is wrong: symptomatic or pre-operative disease warrants therapy, even though asymptomatic, biochemically mild disease may simply be monitored. Adequate vitamin D/calcium repletion should precede IV bisphosphonate to avoid hypocalcaemia.
learningPoint: IV zoledronate is first-line for symptomatic Paget; treat before elective surgery on pagetic bone to reduce hypervascular bleeding (and ensure vitamin D repletion first).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Basic Science: Paget disease management
Paget's Association / Endocrine Society guideline; BNF — bisphosphonates in Paget disease

stem: A 55-year-old man on long-term haemodialysis for end-stage renal disease is reviewed for bone pain. Which set of biochemical findings is most typical of the classic high-turnover bone disease of chronic kidney disease (secondary hyperparathyroidism)?

options.label: A
options.text: Calcium low/normal, phosphate HIGH, PTH HIGH, calcitriol LOW

options.label: B
options.text: Calcium HIGH, phosphate LOW, PTH HIGH, calcitriol HIGH

options.label: C
options.text: Calcium HIGH, phosphate HIGH, PTH LOW, calcitriol HIGH

options.label: D
options.text: Calcium low/normal, phosphate LOW, PTH HIGH, calcitriol HIGH

options.label: E
options.text: Calcium normal, phosphate normal, PTH normal, alkaline phosphatase low

explanation: In chronic kidney disease the failing kidney cannot excrete phosphate or hydroxylate vitamin D. The resulting HYPERPHOSPHATAEMIA and LOW CALCITRIOL both lower serum calcium (phosphate also complexes calcium), while reduced calcitriol removes negative feedback on the parathyroids; rising FGF-23 further suppresses calcitriol. The net effect is SECONDARY HYPERPARATHYROIDISM: a LOW or low-normal calcium, HIGH phosphate, HIGH PTH and LOW calcitriol (answer A) — the classic high-turnover (osteitis fibrosa cystica) picture, with subperiosteal resorption (radial border of the phalanges), a 'rugger-jersey' spine and brown tumours. Option B (high calcium, low phosphate, high PTH, high calcitriol) fits PRIMARY hyperparathyroidism, not renal disease. Option C resembles a vitamin D-toxicity/calcitriol-excess state with suppressed PTH. Option D wrongly pairs a high PTH with a LOW phosphate and HIGH calcitriol — incompatible with renal failure. Option E (normal biochemistry with low ALP) is not the high-turnover state and would suggest the OPPOSITE end of the spectrum (adynamic bone). This is now framed within CKD-Mineral and Bone Disorder (CKD-MBD); management targets phosphate (binders, dietary restriction), with active vitamin D analogues and calcimimetics (cinacalcet) to control PTH.
learningPoint: Renal high-turnover bone disease = LOW/normal Ca, HIGH phosphate, HIGH PTH, LOW calcitriol (secondary hyperparathyroidism) — distinct from primary HPT (high Ca, low phosphate).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Basic Science: renal osteodystrophy / CKD-MBD
NICE NG203 — Chronic kidney disease: assessment and management; KDIGO CKD-MBD guideline

stem: A 60-year-old woman on peritoneal dialysis has been treated aggressively for hyperphosphataemia and secondary hyperparathyroidism with calcium-based binders and active vitamin D analogues. Her PTH is now persistently very LOW. A bone biopsy shows markedly reduced bone turnover with scanty osteoid and few osteoblasts/osteoclasts. Which form of renal bone disease has developed, and what is its principal risk?

options.label: A
options.text: Adynamic bone disease — increased risk of fracture and vascular calcification from over-suppression of turnover

options.label: B
options.text: Osteitis fibrosa cystica — high-turnover disease with brown tumours

options.label: C
options.text: Mixed uraemic osteodystrophy — combined high turnover and mineralisation defect

options.label: D
options.text: Osteomalacia from aluminium toxicity — wide osteoid seams

options.label: E
options.text: Tertiary hyperparathyroidism — autonomous PTH hypersecretion with hypercalcaemia

explanation: Renal osteodystrophy spans a spectrum from HIGH-turnover (secondary/tertiary hyperparathyroidism, osteitis fibrosa) through MIXED disease to LOW-turnover states. Aggressive suppression of PTH with calcium loading and active vitamin D can OVER-suppress the parathyroids, and because PTH is the principal driver of bone remodelling in dialysis patients, turnover collapses. The biopsy here — markedly LOW turnover, scanty osteoid, few cells, WITHOUT a mineralisation defect or aluminium staining — defines ADYNAMIC (aplastic) bone disease (answer A). Its risks are FRACTURE (brittle, poorly remodelling bone unable to repair microdamage), hypercalcaemia (bone cannot buffer a calcium load), and importantly accelerated VASCULAR/soft-tissue CALCIFICATION as the calcium has nowhere to go — a major cardiovascular hazard in dialysis. Management is to RELAX over-treatment: reduce calcium-based binders and active vitamin D and allow PTH to rise into the (higher) target range recommended for dialysis patients. Osteitis fibrosa (B) is the opposite, high-turnover, picture. Mixed disease (C) and aluminium osteomalacia (D, now rare since aluminium binders were abandoned) both involve a mineralisation/osteoid abnormality not present here. Tertiary hyperparathyroidism (E) features a HIGH autonomous PTH with hypercalcaemia, contradicting the very low PTH given.
learningPoint: Over-suppressing PTH in dialysis (calcium binders + active vitamin D) causes ADYNAMIC bone disease — low turnover with fracture, hypercalcaemia and vascular calcification risk; back off treatment.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Basic Science: renal osteodystrophy spectrum
KDIGO CKD-MBD guideline; NICE NG203 — Chronic kidney disease

stem: A child is investigated for recurrent long-bone fractures despite dense, sclerotic 'marble' bones on radiographs, alongside anaemia, hepatosplenomegaly and cranial nerve palsies. The underlying disorder is a failure of normal bone resorption. Which cellular/molecular defect is the fundamental abnormality?

options.label: A
options.text: Defective osteoblast collagen synthesis (type I collagen mutation)

options.label: B
options.text: Failure of osteoCLAST function (e.g. carbonic anhydrase II or chloride-channel/proton-pump defect) impairing bone resorption

options.label: C
options.text: Excess osteoclast activity driven by RANKL overexpression

options.label: D
options.text: A qualitative defect of matrix mineralisation due to vitamin D deficiency

options.label: E
options.text: Fibrous replacement of marrow from a GNAS-activating mutation

explanation: Osteopetrosis ('marble bone disease') results from FAILURE OF OSTEOCLASTIC BONE RESORPTION. Osteoclasts are typically present (often increased in number) but cannot resorb bone because they cannot acidify the resorption lacuna or dissolve mineral. The classic molecular lesions include deficiency of CARBONIC ANHYDRASE II (which also causes renal tubular acidosis and cerebral calcification), and mutations in the osteoclast proton pump (TCIRG1, a V-ATPase subunit) or the CLCN7 chloride channel — all of which block acid secretion across the ruffled border (answer B). The result is persistence of unresorbed, dense, brittle bone that fractures easily ('chalk-stick' fractures), encroachment on the marrow cavity causing anaemia and extramedullary haematopoiesis (hepatosplenomegaly), and failure of foraminal remodelling causing cranial nerve compression (optic atrophy, deafness, facial palsy). Severe (infantile, autosomal recessive 'malignant') forms can be treated with haematopoietic stem cell transplantation, which supplies functional osteoclasts of haematopoietic origin. Option A (defective osteoblast collagen) describes osteogenesis imperfecta. Option C (excess osteoclast activity) is the opposite of osteopetrosis and is more relevant to Paget/osteoporosis biology. Option D is osteomalacia/rickets. Option E (GNAS activation, fibrous marrow) describes fibrous dysplasia.
learningPoint: Osteopetrosis = osteoCLAST failure (carbonic anhydrase II / TCIRG1 proton pump / CLCN7) → dense brittle bone, marrow failure and cranial nerve palsies; severe forms treated by HSCT.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Basic Science: osteopetrosis and sclerosing bone dysplasias
Buckwalter — Orthopaedic Basic Science: disorders of bone resorption

stem: A 24-year-old footballer sustains a focal full-thickness chondral defect of the medial femoral condyle. You explain why isolated cartilage lesions of this type characteristically fail to heal spontaneously. Which feature of mature articular (hyaline) cartilage is the single most important reason for this poor intrinsic healing capacity?

options.label: A
options.text: It is avascular and is nourished mainly by synovial fluid diffusion

options.label: B
options.text: It contains a high density of mast cells that suppress repair

options.label: C
options.text: Its predominant collagen is type I, which cannot be remodelled

options.label: D
options.text: It has an unusually high water content compared with other connective tissues

options.label: E
options.text: Its chondrocytes divide rapidly but produce only fibrous tissue

explanation: Mature articular cartilage is avascular, aneural and alymphatic; chondrocytes derive nutrition by diffusion from synovial fluid. The absence of a blood supply means there is no inflammatory/fibrin-clot phase and no recruitment of undifferentiated marrow cells to a purely chondral (not subchondral) defect, so spontaneous repair is poor. Hyaline cartilage collagen is predominantly type II, not type I, and chondrocytes have very low mitotic activity in adults.
learningPoint: Articular cartilage is avascular and fed by synovial diffusion, which is the key reason isolated chondral defects do not heal — the basis for microfracture (deliberate marrow access) and cell-based repair.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Basic Science (articular cartilage)

stem: Theme: Structure and function of cartilage. For each statement below, select the SINGLE most appropriate molecule or structure from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Type I collagen

options.label: B
options.text: Type II collagen

options.label: C
options.text: Aggrecan (with hyaluronan)

options.label: D
options.text: Type X collagen

options.label: E
options.text: Superficial (tangential) zone

options.label: F
options.text: Calcified cartilage zone

options.label: G
options.text: Tidemark

options.label: H
options.text: Type VI collagen

explanation: Type II collagen is the dominant collagen of hyaline articular cartilage and provides tensile strength. Aggrecan, a large aggregating proteoglycan bound to hyaluronan, carries the negatively charged glycosaminoglycans whose fixed-charge density draws in water and generates compressive (osmotic/swelling) resistance. The superficial zone has collagen fibres arranged parallel to the surface and the highest collagen content, resisting shear and tensile forces. Type X collagen is the marker of hypertrophic chondrocytes and is found in the calcified zone and growth-plate hypertrophic zone. The meniscus, in contrast, is predominantly type I collagen arranged circumferentially to resist hoop stresses.
learningPoint: Type II collagen + aggrecan are the two load-bearing building blocks of hyaline cartilage; type I dominates the meniscus and type X marks hypertrophic/calcified cartilage.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Basic Science (cartilage matrix and zones)

emiLeads.stem: The collagen type that forms the bulk of normal hyaline articular cartilage and provides its tensile strength.
The proteoglycan-based macromolecule chiefly responsible for cartilage's resistance to compressive load through its high fixed negative charge and water retention.
The collagen type characteristically produced by hypertrophic chondrocytes and found in the calcified cartilage zone and growth-plate hypertrophic zone.
The zone of articular cartilage with collagen fibres oriented parallel to the surface, best resisting shear and tensile forces.
The predominant collagen of the meniscus, arranged circumferentially to resist hoop stresses.

stem: A 30-year-old has a primary flexor digitorum profundus repair in zone II and starts an early controlled-motion hand therapy programme. With respect to the biology of tendon healing, which statement best describes the early remodelling phase (from roughly 3 weeks onwards)?

options.label: A
options.text: Type III collagen is progressively replaced by type I collagen, with fibres reorganising along the line of mechanical stress

options.label: B
options.text: Type I collagen is replaced by type III collagen as the tendon matures

options.label: C
options.text: The repair gains no mechanical benefit from loading and is strongest if completely immobilised for 6 weeks

options.label: D
options.text: Healing is entirely intrinsic, with no contribution from the tendon sheath or surrounding tissues

options.label: E
options.text: Tendon strength peaks at 2 weeks and then plateaus permanently

explanation: Tendon healing passes through inflammatory, proliferative/reparative and remodelling phases. Early granulation tissue is rich in type III collagen; during remodelling this is gradually replaced by stronger, better cross-linked type I collagen, and controlled mechanical loading stimulates fibroblasts to align collagen along the loading axis, improving tensile strength and reducing adhesions. Healing has both intrinsic (tenocyte) and extrinsic (sheath/synovial) contributions. The repair is at its weakest around days 7-10, not strongest, which is why protected early motion rather than rigid immobilisation is favoured.
learningPoint: Tendon remodelling = type III collagen replaced by aligned type I collagen, and controlled loading drives that alignment — the rationale for early protected motion after repair.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Basic Science (tendon healing)

stem: During teaching you are asked about the histology of a tendon insertion. At a typical fibrocartilaginous (direct) enthesis such as the supraspinatus footprint, which sequence of four zones is correctly ordered from tendon to bone?

options.label: A
options.text: Tendon → fibrocartilage → mineralised fibrocartilage → bone

options.label: B
options.text: Tendon → bone → fibrocartilage → mineralised fibrocartilage

options.label: C
options.text: Tendon → hyaline cartilage → synovium → bone

options.label: D
options.text: Tendon → mineralised fibrocartilage → fibrocartilage → bone

options.label: E
options.text: Tendon → elastic cartilage → fibrocartilage → bone

explanation: A direct (fibrocartilaginous) enthesis has four transitional zones: dense fibrous tendon, uncalcified fibrocartilage, calcified (mineralised) fibrocartilage, and bone, with a tidemark separating the calcified from uncalcified fibrocartilage. This graded transition dissipates stress concentration. Indirect (fibrous) entheses, such as the deltoid insertion, attach via Sharpey's fibres into periosteum without this fibrocartilage transition.
learningPoint: A direct enthesis transitions tendon → uncalcified fibrocartilage → calcified fibrocartilage → bone to spread stress; this graded zone is what tendon-to-bone repairs try to recreate.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Basic Science (enthesis structure)

stem: A researcher compares the healing of an isolated grade II MCL injury with a complete ACL rupture in the same knee. The MCL injury heals well with non-operative management, whereas the intra-articular ACL has very poor intrinsic healing. Which factor best explains this difference in healing potential?

options.label: A
options.text: The intra-articular environment and synovial fluid prevent stable clot formation around the torn ACL

options.label: B
options.text: The ACL contains type I collagen whereas the MCL contains only elastin

options.label: C
options.text: The ACL has a far richer blood supply than the MCL, causing excessive bleeding and failure

options.label: D
options.text: The MCL is intra-articular and therefore better protected from movement

options.label: E
options.text: Only the ACL contains fibroblasts capable of producing collagen

explanation: The extra-articular MCL forms a stable fibrin clot/haematoma that bridges the gap and organises into scar, so it usually heals without surgery. The ACL is intra-articular: synovial fluid causes premature lysis of the provisional clot (plasmin activity) and the ruptured ends retract, so a bridging scaffold does not form and intrinsic healing is poor — the rationale for reconstruction rather than repair in most complete tears. Both ligaments are predominantly type I collagen and both contain fibroblasts.
learningPoint: The MCL heals because it forms a stable extra-articular clot; the ACL heals poorly because the intra-articular synovial environment lyses the clot — hence reconstruction over repair.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Basic Science (ligament healing)
Sports knee literature on ACL vs MCL healing

stem: A sports physiologist describes a muscle fibre population that is fatigue-resistant, rich in mitochondria and myoglobin, relies predominantly on oxidative (aerobic) metabolism and is recruited first during sustained low-load postural activity. Which fibre type is being described?

options.label: A
options.text: Type I (slow oxidative)

options.label: B
options.text: Type IIa (fast oxidative-glycolytic)

options.label: C
options.text: Type IIx / IIb (fast glycolytic)

options.label: D
options.text: Intrafusal nuclear bag fibres

options.label: E
options.text: Intrafusal nuclear chain fibres

explanation: Type I (slow-twitch, slow oxidative) fibres are small-diameter, high in mitochondria, myoglobin and capillary density, fatigue-resistant and recruited early (small motor units, per the size principle) for sustained postural/endurance work. Type IIa are fast but oxidative-glycolytic and moderately fatigue-resistant; type IIx/IIb are fast glycolytic, large, powerful and rapidly fatiguing. Intrafusal fibres are sensory components of the muscle spindle, not force-generating extrafusal fibres.
learningPoint: Type I fibres = slow, oxidative, fatigue-resistant, myoglobin-rich, recruited first for posture/endurance; type II fibres are fast and more fatigable.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Basic Science (skeletal muscle physiology)

stem: A 28-year-old sprinter feels a sudden 'pull' in the posterior thigh during acceleration. Examination and MRI confirm a hamstring strain at the myotendinous junction. Which statement about skeletal muscle strain injury and its repair is most accurate?

options.label: A
options.text: The myotendinous junction is the most common site of strain injury, and satellite cells are the key source of regenerating myofibres

options.label: B
options.text: Mature myofibres regenerate by simple mitotic division of existing nuclei

options.label: C
options.text: Strains characteristically occur in the mid-belly, far from any tendon

options.label: D
options.text: Excessive scar (fibrosis) never limits recovery after muscle injury

options.label: E
options.text: Satellite cells lie deep within the sarcoplasm beneath the myonuclei

explanation: Strains typically occur at the myotendinous junction, particularly in biarticular muscles (e.g. hamstrings, rectus femoris) loaded eccentrically. Mature multinucleate myofibres cannot divide; regeneration depends on satellite cells, which lie between the sarcolemma and the basal lamina and, when activated, proliferate as myoblasts that fuse to repair or replace fibres. Healing is a balance between regeneration and fibrosis — excessive scar can impair function and predisposes to re-injury, which is why progressive eccentric rehabilitation is emphasised.
learningPoint: Strains hit the myotendinous junction; satellite cells (sub-sarcolemmal, beneath the basal lamina) drive regeneration, and excessive fibrosis predisposes to re-injury.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Basic Science (muscle injury and satellite cells)

stem: A patient sustains a traction injury to a peripheral nerve. Clinical and imaging findings indicate the axons are disrupted but the endoneurial tubes and the surrounding connective tissue layers (perineurium and epineurium) remain in continuity. According to the Seddon classification, and equivalently the Sunderland system, how is this injury best described and what is the expected outcome?

options.label: A
options.text: Axonotmesis (Sunderland II) — good prognosis, with axonal regrowth along intact endoneurial tubes at roughly 1 mm/day

options.label: B
options.text: Neurapraxia (Sunderland I) — conduction block only, recovers within hours to days

options.label: C
options.text: Neurotmesis (Sunderland V) — complete transection requiring surgical repair

options.label: D
options.text: Axonotmesis (Sunderland IV) — endoneurium and perineurium disrupted, surgery usually required

options.label: E
options.text: Neurapraxia with associated Wallerian degeneration distal to the lesion

explanation: Loss of axonal continuity with intact endoneurial tubes corresponds to Seddon axonotmesis and Sunderland grade II. Because the endoneurial scaffold is preserved, regenerating axons are guided back to their original targets and recovery is generally good without surgery, advancing at approximately 1 mm/day (about 1 inch/month). Neurapraxia (Sunderland I) is a conduction block with intact axons and no Wallerian degeneration. Higher Sunderland grades (III endoneurium disrupted, IV perineurium disrupted, V complete transection) have progressively worse prognosis and increasing likelihood of needing surgery; Sunderland VI (Mackinnon) denotes a mixed-pattern injury.
learningPoint: Axonotmesis (Sunderland II) = axons severed but endoneurial tubes intact → good spontaneous recovery at ~1 mm/day; this scaffold is what distinguishes it from grades III-V.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Basic Science (nerve injury classification)
BOAST: documentation and assessment of peripheral nerve injury in trauma

stem: A medical student asks what happens distal to the site of a complete peripheral nerve transection in the days after injury. Which sequence best describes Wallerian degeneration and the subsequent cellular response that supports regeneration?

options.label: A
options.text: The distal axon and myelin break down and are cleared by Schwann cells and macrophages; Schwann cells then proliferate within the basal lamina to form bands of Büngner that guide regrowth

options.label: B
options.text: The proximal stump degenerates while the distal axon remains viable and simply reconnects

options.label: C
options.text: Oligodendrocytes remyelinate the distal segment and form the guiding columns

options.label: D
options.text: Axonal regeneration begins immediately with no degeneration of the distal segment

options.label: E
options.text: The distal segment calcifies and is permanently replaced by bone-forming cells

explanation: After transection (or axonotmesis), the axon and myelin distal to the lesion undergo Wallerian degeneration over several days and are phagocytosed by Schwann cells and recruited macrophages. Schwann cells proliferate within the preserved basal lamina to form longitudinal columns (bands of Büngner) that, together with neurotrophic factors, guide regenerating axonal sprouts from the proximal stump. In the peripheral nervous system Schwann cells (not oligodendrocytes, which serve the CNS) provide myelination and this regenerative scaffold, which is a major reason peripheral nerves regenerate far better than central axons.
learningPoint: Wallerian degeneration clears the distal axon/myelin (Schwann cells + macrophages), then Schwann cells form bands of Büngner to guide PNS regrowth — a capacity the CNS lacks.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Basic Science (Wallerian degeneration, nerve regeneration)

stem: A 40-year-old presents with mechanical low back pain. Discussing intervertebral disc structure, which statement correctly pairs a disc component with its dominant matrix and function?

options.label: A
options.text: The nucleus pulposus is proteoglycan-rich with type II collagen, retaining water to resist compressive load

options.label: B
options.text: The annulus fibrosus is composed mainly of randomly oriented type II collagen

options.label: C
options.text: The nucleus pulposus is dominated by tightly packed type I collagen lamellae

options.label: D
options.text: The annulus fibrosus is gelatinous and avascular at its core but cellular at its periphery only because of type II collagen

options.label: E
options.text: The nucleus pulposus has the lowest water content of any spinal structure

explanation: The nucleus pulposus is a hydrated gel rich in proteoglycan (aggrecan) and type II collagen; its high fixed-charge density retains water and resists axial compression, distributing load to the annulus. The annulus fibrosus is composed of concentric lamellae of predominantly type I collagen with alternating fibre orientation, which contains the nucleus and resists tensile/torsional forces. With ageing the nucleus loses proteoglycan and water content, reducing disc height and altering load transfer.
learningPoint: Nucleus pulposus = proteoglycan + type II collagen (resists compression by holding water); annulus fibrosus = lamellar type I collagen (resists tension/torsion).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Basic Science / Spine (intervertebral disc)

stem: Theme: Connective-tissue and nerve sheath layers. For each description, select the SINGLE most appropriate structure from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Epineurium

options.label: B
options.text: Perineurium

options.label: C
options.text: Endoneurium

options.label: D
options.text: Epitenon

options.label: E
options.text: Endotenon

options.label: F
options.text: Paratenon

options.label: G
options.text: Basal lamina (Schwann cell)

options.label: H
options.text: Periosteum

explanation: The peripheral nerve has three connective-tissue sheaths: the endoneurium surrounds individual axons; the perineurium ensheathes each fascicle and is the principal contributor to the blood-nerve barrier and to fascicular tensile strength; the epineurium is the outer sheath binding fascicles together. In tendon, the endotenon invests collagen fascicles internally and carries vessels/nerves, the epitenon is the fine sheath over the whole tendon surface continuous with endotenon, and the paratenon is the loose areolar sheath around tendons that lack a true synovial sheath. The Schwann cell basal lamina is the scaffold preserved in axonotmesis along which regenerating axons travel.
learningPoint: Know the nerve sheaths (endo/peri/epineurium — perineurium = blood-nerve barrier) and the tendon equivalents (endotenon/epitenon/paratenon); Sunderland grading is defined by which nerve layer is breached.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Basic Science (nerve and tendon microanatomy)

emiLeads.stem: The connective-tissue layer surrounding an individual fascicle of a peripheral nerve and chiefly responsible for the blood-nerve barrier.
The innermost nerve layer immediately surrounding individual axons; its tubes remaining intact defines Sunderland grade II (axonotmesis).
The outermost connective-tissue sheath of a peripheral nerve, the layer typically grasped and aligned in an epineurial repair.
The loose areolar sheath surrounding tendons that do not possess a true synovial sheath (e.g. the Achilles), important for vascular supply.
The internal connective-tissue layer that invests collagen fascicles within a tendon and conveys its blood vessels and nerves.

stem: A 22-year-old has an arthroscopically confirmed longitudinal tear in the peripheral third of the medial meniscus (the so-called red-red zone). Compared with a tear in the inner third, why does a peripheral tear have a substantially better chance of healing after repair?

options.label: A
options.text: The peripheral third receives a blood supply from the perimeniscal capillary plexus, whereas the inner third is avascular and nourished by diffusion

options.label: B
options.text: The inner third has the richest blood supply and therefore over-heals into scar

options.label: C
options.text: The peripheral meniscus is composed of type II collagen, which heals better than type I

options.label: D
options.text: The inner third is innervated and therefore heals faster

options.label: E
options.text: Meniscal blood supply is uniform throughout, so location does not affect healing

explanation: The meniscus is vascularised peripherally by the perimeniscal capillary plexus (from the geniculate vessels), supplying roughly the outer 10-30% — the red-red and red-white zones — while the inner third (white-white zone) is avascular and relies on diffusion from synovial fluid. Peripheral tears in vascular tissue can mount a healing response and are therefore preferentially repaired, whereas avascular inner tears heal poorly and may be debrided. The meniscus is predominantly type I collagen arranged circumferentially to resist hoop stresses.
learningPoint: Only the peripheral (red-red) meniscus is vascularised (perimeniscal plexus), which is why peripheral tears are repaired and avascular inner-zone tears generally are not.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Basic Science / Sports (meniscus vascularity)

stem: A 55-year-old with suspected carpal tunnel syndrome is referred for nerve conduction studies and electromyography (EMG). Regarding the timing and interpretation of these tests after an acute axonal nerve injury, which statement is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: Fibrillation potentials and positive sharp waves indicating active denervation typically appear on EMG only about 2-3 weeks after axonal injury

options.label: B
options.text: Fibrillation potentials appear within minutes of a complete nerve transection

options.label: C
options.text: EMG should be performed within 24 hours of injury to detect denervation reliably

options.label: D
options.text: Reduced recruitment of motor units is the last electrophysiological change to develop after injury

options.label: E
options.text: Nerve conduction studies cannot distinguish a conduction block (neurapraxia) from axonal loss

explanation: After an axonal (axonotmesis/neurotmesis) injury, spontaneous activity from denervated muscle fibres — fibrillation potentials and positive sharp waves — develops only after about 2-3 weeks, so EMG performed too early can be falsely reassuring. Reduced motor-unit recruitment is in fact an immediate finding. Nerve conduction studies are valuable precisely because they can help distinguish neurapraxia (conduction block across the lesion with preserved distal conduction and no Wallerian degeneration) from axonal loss (loss of distal amplitudes once Wallerian degeneration is complete). Polyphasic, long-duration reinnervation potentials are a later sign of recovery, appearing well after fibrillations.
learningPoint: Denervation changes on EMG (fibrillations/positive sharp waves) lag the injury by ~2-3 weeks, so time the study; NCS helps separate neurapraxia (conduction block) from axonal loss.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Basic Science (electrodiagnostics)
NICE NG215 / clinical guidance on carpal tunnel syndrome assessment

stem: An electrophysiologist explains the parameters measured during nerve conduction studies in a patient with a peripheral neuropathy. Which interpretation of a conduction parameter is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: Markedly slowed conduction velocity with conduction block points towards a demyelinating process, whereas reduced compound muscle action potential amplitude with relatively preserved velocity suggests axonal loss

options.label: B
options.text: Reduced conduction velocity with preserved amplitude is the hallmark of pure axonal loss

options.label: C
options.text: Demyelination characteristically reduces amplitude while leaving conduction velocity normal

options.label: D
options.text: Distal motor latency is unaffected by focal demyelination at an entrapment site

options.label: E
options.text: The compound muscle action potential amplitude reflects the degree of myelination rather than the number of functioning axons

explanation: Conduction velocity depends primarily on myelination (saltatory conduction); demyelinating lesions therefore cause marked slowing, prolonged distal latencies, temporal dispersion and conduction block, often disproportionate to amplitude loss. The compound muscle action potential (CMAP) amplitude reflects the number of functioning axons/muscle fibres, so axonal loss reduces amplitude with relatively preserved velocity. In focal entrapment (e.g. carpal tunnel) demyelination prolongs the distal motor and sensory latencies across the lesion. Distinguishing demyelinating from axonal patterns is a core use of NCS.
learningPoint: Velocity tracks myelin (slowing/block = demyelination); CMAP amplitude tracks axon number (low amplitude = axonal loss) — the key axonal-versus-demyelinating distinction on NCS.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Basic Science (nerve conduction parameters)

stem: A 58-year-old presents with early symptomatic knee osteoarthritis. Considering the biochemical changes occurring within articular cartilage at this stage, which of the following BEST characterises the matrix abnormality in early osteoarthritis?

options.label: A
options.text: Reduced water content with increased proteoglycan aggregation

options.label: B
options.text: Increased water content with reduced proteoglycan content and shorter chondroitin sulphate chains

options.label: C
options.text: Replacement of type II collagen by type I collagen with normal hydration

options.label: D
options.text: Increased keratan sulphate to chondroitin sulphate ratio with matrix dehydration

options.label: E
options.text: Reduced chondrocyte metabolic activity with matrix mineralisation

explanation: In early osteoarthritis the cartilage water content paradoxically RISES (not falls) because collagen network disruption allows the tissue to swell. Proteoglycan content falls overall, aggrecan chains are degraded, and chondroitin sulphate chains become shorter, with a relative increase in chondroitin sulphate over keratan sulphate (a more 'immature' profile). Chondrocytes are initially HYPER-metabolic, upregulating matrix metalloproteinases (notably MMP-13/collagenase-3) and aggrecanases (ADAMTS-4/5). Type II collagen remains the predominant collagen (it is degraded, not swapped wholesale for type I), and dehydration is a feature of LATE, end-stage cartilage, not early disease. This distinguishes osteoarthritis from normal ageing, where water content tends to fall.
learningPoint: Early OA cartilage swells (increased water) with reduced/shortened proteoglycans and hyperactive MMP/ADAMTS-driven chondrocytes — the opposite of the dehydrated picture of ageing.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Basic Science: articular cartilage & osteoarthritis

subtopic: Osteoarthritis pathophysiology — mediators and subchondral bone
stem: A basic science viva explores the molecular drivers of cartilage degradation in osteoarthritis. Which single enzyme is regarded as the principal collagenase responsible for cleavage of the type II collagen triple helix in osteoarthritic cartilage?

options.label: A
options.text: MMP-3 (stromelysin-1)

options.label: B
options.text: MMP-9 (gelatinase B)

options.label: C
options.text: MMP-13 (collagenase-3)

options.label: D
options.text: ADAMTS-5 (aggrecanase-2)

options.label: E
options.text: Cathepsin K

explanation: MMP-13 (collagenase-3) is the dominant collagenase cleaving the type II collagen triple helix in osteoarthritis and is highly upregulated by IL-1β and TNF-α. ADAMTS-4 and ADAMTS-5 are the key AGGRECANASES (they degrade aggrecan/proteoglycan, not collagen) — ADAMTS-5 is the more important aggrecanase in murine models. MMP-3 (stromelysin) activates other MMPs and degrades proteoglycan but is not the principal collagenase; MMP-9 is a gelatinase acting on denatured collagen. Cathepsin K is central to OSTEOCLAST bone resorption, relevant to subchondral remodelling but not to direct helical collagen cleavage. The proinflammatory cytokine IL-1β is considered the master catabolic cytokine in OA cartilage, with TNF-α amplifying the response.
learningPoint: MMP-13 is the key type II collagenase in OA; ADAMTS-4/5 are the aggrecanases — don't conflate collagen cleavage with proteoglycan cleavage.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Basic Science: cartilage degradation enzymes

stem: A 44-year-old woman has a 6-year history of seropositive rheumatoid arthritis with symmetrical MCP and wrist involvement.
