At synovectomy the joint is lined by inflamed, invasive synovial tissue. Which statement BEST describes the pathological process producing the periarticular bone erosions seen radiographically?

options.label: A
options.text: Direct dissolution of bone by synovial fluid acid hydrolases independent of cells

options.label: B
options.text: RANKL-driven osteoclast differentiation at the pannus–bone interface

options.label: C
options.text: Chondrocyte-mediated enchondral ossification of the marginal cartilage

options.label: D
options.text: Ischaemic osteonecrosis from synovial vascular occlusion

options.label: E
options.text: Deposition of immune complexes causing dystrophic calcification of bone

explanation: Rheumatoid pannus is a hyperplastic, locally invasive synovial membrane rich in activated fibroblast-like synoviocytes, macrophages and T cells. The bone erosions characteristic of RA are produced by OSTEOCLASTS at the pannus–bone interface. Inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1) and activated T cells drive expression of RANKL (receptor activator of NF-κB ligand), which binds RANK on osteoclast precursors to promote their differentiation and activation; this is the unifying mechanism of erosive bone loss. Cartilage destruction is mediated separately by MMPs from synoviocytes. RA marginal erosions occur classically at the 'bare areas' where synovium directly abuts bone unprotected by cartilage. This RANKL pathway underpins the rationale for biologic and anti-resorptive therapy.
learningPoint: RA bone erosion = osteoclasts activated via RANKL at the pannus–bone interface, driven by TNF-α/IL-6 — not direct enzymatic bone dissolution.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Basic Science: rheumatoid arthritis
NICE NG100 Rheumatoid arthritis in adults: management (2018, upd.)

stem: A 67-year-old woman with longstanding rheumatoid arthritis is listed for elective total knee replacement under general anaesthesia. She reports occasional occipital headache and transient upper-limb paraesthesiae. Which preoperative investigation is MOST important to perform before airway manipulation?

options.label: A
options.text: Erect lumbar spine radiographs

options.label: B
options.text: Flexion–extension lateral cervical spine radiographs

options.label: C
options.text: Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibody titre

options.label: D
options.text: DEXA bone densitometry

options.label: E
options.text: Nerve conduction studies of both upper limbs

explanation: Rheumatoid arthritis erodes the transverse ligament and odontoid, producing atlanto-axial instability. Neck manipulation during intubation can cause catastrophic cervical cord compression. Flexion–extension lateral cervical radiographs assess the anterior atlanto-dental interval (ADI); an ADI >3 mm suggests instability, and importantly a POSTERIOR atlanto-dental interval (PADI, space available for the cord) <14 mm correlates best with neurological risk and the need for surgical stabilisation/MRI. Vertical settling (basilar invagination/cranial settling) and subaxial subluxation should also be considered. Symptoms of occipital headache and limb paraesthesiae are red flags. Anti-CCP and DEXA inform diagnosis/fracture risk but do not protect the airway/cord; nerve conduction studies will not exclude cord-level pathology.
learningPoint: Always screen the rheumatoid cervical spine with flexion–extension views before GA; PADI <14 mm is the key marker of cord risk.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Spine: rheumatoid cervical spine

stem: A 24-year-old man has 8 months of insidious low back pain with early-morning stiffness lasting over an hour that improves with exercise but not rest, plus alternating buttock pain. Inflammatory markers are mildly raised and rheumatoid factor is negative. Which HLA association is most strongly linked to the most likely underlying diagnosis?

options.label: A
options.text: HLA-DR4

options.label: B
options.text: HLA-B27

options.label: C
options.text: HLA-DR2

options.label: D
options.text: HLA-B51

options.label: E
options.text: HLA-DQ2

explanation: The clinical picture — young man, insidious inflammatory back pain (>3 months, onset <45 years, morning stiffness >30 min, improvement with exercise not rest, alternating buttock pain) — is classic axial spondyloarthritis/ankylosing spondylitis. HLA-B27 is positive in roughly 90% of patients with ankylosing spondylitis and is the strongest genetic association. HLA-DR4 is linked to rheumatoid arthritis, HLA-DR2 to multiple sclerosis/SLE-related associations, HLA-B51 to Behçet disease, and HLA-DQ2 to coeliac disease. Note HLA-B27 is neither necessary nor sufficient (most B27-positive people never develop disease), so it supports but does not establish the diagnosis; sacroiliitis on MRI/radiograph remains central to classification.
learningPoint: Inflammatory back pain in a young adult + HLA-B27 → axial spondyloarthritis; B27 supports but does not confirm the diagnosis.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Basic Science: seronegative spondyloarthropathies

subtopic: Seronegative spondyloarthropathy — peri-operative spinal fracture risk
stem: A 55-year-old man with long-standing ankylosing spondylitis and a fixed kyphotic 'bamboo' spine sustains acute thoracolumbar pain after a low-energy fall. Plain radiographs appear unremarkable. What is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Reassure and discharge with simple analgesia

options.label: B
options.text: Arrange outpatient physiotherapy and bone densitometry

options.label: C
options.text: CT (and/or MRI) of the whole spine and treat as an unstable fracture until excluded

options.label: D
options.text: Apply a hyperextension brace and mobilise immediately

options.label: E
options.text: Start high-dose corticosteroids for a presumed flare

explanation: The ankylosed (bamboo) spine behaves like a long bone: even low-energy trauma can produce highly unstable three-column fractures, often transverse and frequently extending through a fused disc space, with a high risk of epidural haematoma and delayed/complete spinal cord injury. Plain films are notoriously unreliable in the fused, osteoporotic, kyphotic spine, so a normal radiograph does NOT exclude injury. The standard of care is urgent CT of the WHOLE spine (fractures are commonly multilevel and non-contiguous) with MRI if cord compromise or epidural haematoma is suspected, while immobilising the patient in their habitual deformed posture (NOT forcing them flat, which can displace the fracture). Bracing/early mobilisation or steroids are inappropriate and dangerous here.
learningPoint: Any spinal pain after even minor trauma in ankylosing spondylitis = unstable fracture until whole-spine CT proves otherwise; never force the deformity flat.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Spine trauma in ankylosing spondylitis
BOAST: Spinal Clearance in the Trauma Patient / Major Trauma

stem: A 52-year-old man presents with an acutely hot, exquisitely tender first metatarsophalangeal joint. Joint aspirate is examined under compensated polarised light microscopy. Which combination of crystal morphology and birefringence confirms gout?

options.label: A
options.text: Rhomboid crystals, weakly positively birefringent

options.label: B
options.text: Needle-shaped crystals, strongly negatively birefringent

options.label: C
options.text: Needle-shaped crystals, strongly positively birefringent

options.label: D
options.text: Rhomboid crystals, strongly negatively birefringent

options.label: E
options.text: Spherulitic crystals, non-birefringent

explanation: Acute gout is caused by monosodium urate (MSU) crystals, which are NEEDLE-shaped and strongly NEGATIVELY birefringent under compensated polarised light: when the crystal lies parallel to the axis of the red compensator it appears YELLOW, and blue when perpendicular ('parallel = yellow' for negative birefringence). Calcium pyrophosphate dihydrate (CPPD) crystals of pseudogout are by contrast RHOMBOID/rod-shaped and WEAKLY POSITIVELY birefringent (blue when parallel). Definitive diagnosis of crystal arthropathy is by aspiration and microscopy, which simultaneously excludes septic arthritis. Basic calcium phosphate (hydroxyapatite) crystals are non-birefringent and need special stains.
learningPoint: Gout = needle-shaped, strongly negatively birefringent MSU (parallel = yellow); pseudogout = rhomboid, weakly positively birefringent CPPD.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Basic Science: crystal arthropathies
NICE NG219 Gout: diagnosis and management (2022)

stem: A 60-year-old man has had three attacks of crystal-proven gout in the past year and has a subcutaneous tophus over the olecranon. His acute attack has now fully settled. According to current UK guidance, which statement BEST reflects appropriate urate-lowering management?

options.label: A
options.text: Urate-lowering therapy is not indicated until he has had at least five attacks

options.label: B
options.text: Offer allopurinol as first-line urate-lowering therapy, titrated to a target serum urate, with colchicine cover when starting

options.label: C
options.text: Start febuxostat first-line and avoid allopurinol because of nephrotoxicity

options.label: D
options.text: Aim only to keep him asymptomatic; serum urate targets are unnecessary

options.label: E
options.text: Begin urate-lowering therapy at full dose during the next acute attack without prophylaxis

explanation: NICE NG219 recommends offering urate-lowering therapy (ULT) to patients with recurrent flares, tophi, joint damage or renal/cardiovascular comorbidity — and this man (multiple attacks plus a tophus) clearly qualifies. ALLOPURINOL (a xanthine oxidase inhibitor) is first-line, with febuxostat as an alternative if allopurinol is not tolerated or is contraindicated. ULT is started low and titrated ('treat-to-target') to a serum urate below 360 µmol/L (and below ~300 µmol/L where there are tophi or ongoing flares) measured a month apart. Colchicine (or an NSAID) is co-prescribed as flare prophylaxis when initiating ULT, because urate-lowering can paradoxically precipitate attacks. ULT should be CONTINUED through an acute flare if already established, but is typically initiated once the acute attack has settled, as here. Allopurinol carries a risk of severe cutaneous reaction (consider HLA-B*58:01 in at-risk groups), but is not avoided routinely.
learningPoint: Recurrent gout or a tophus warrants treat-to-target urate-lowering: allopurinol first-line, titrate to urate <360 (<300 with tophi), with colchicine cover at initiation.
references: NICE NG219 Gout: diagnosis and management (2022)
Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — crystal arthropathy

stem: A previously well 4-year-old presents with a 2-day history of refusal to weight-bear, fever and a tender, warm distal femoral metaphysis. Acute haematogenous osteomyelitis is suspected. Across all age groups in the UK, which single organism is the most common cause of acute haematogenous osteomyelitis in children?

options.label: A
options.text: Kingella kingae

options.label: B
options.text: Streptococcus pyogenes (group A)

options.label: C
options.text: Staphylococcus aureus

options.label: D
options.text: Haemophilus influenzae type b

options.label: E
options.text: Streptococcus agalactiae (group B)

explanation: Staphylococcus aureus is the single most common organism causing acute haematogenous osteomyelitis (and septic arthritis) across all paediatric age groups. Age-specific exceptions are heavily examined: NEONATES are at additional risk from group B streptococcus and Gram-negatives; Kingella kingae is increasingly recognised (often PCR-detected) in pre-school children (6 months–4 years) and is fastidious on routine culture; Haemophilus influenzae type b has become rare since Hib vaccination; sickle-cell patients have a relatively increased frequency of Salmonella osteomyelitis (though S. aureus remains commonest even in them). The metaphyseal predilection reflects the sluggish blood flow in metaphyseal sinusoidal loops adjacent to the physis.
learningPoint: S. aureus is the commonest cause of paediatric osteomyelitis overall; remember Kingella in toddlers, GBS/Gram-negatives in neonates, and Salmonella's relative rise in sickle-cell disease.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Paediatrics: musculoskeletal infection
BSCOS / BOAST: management of children with acute bone and joint infection

stem: An 8-year-old presents with a 36-hour history of an irritable, painful hip, fever and refusal to bear weight. You apply the validated clinical prediction tool for distinguishing septic arthritis of the hip from transient synovitis. Which set of four variables forms that prediction algorithm?

options.label: A
options.text: Age >10, swelling, CRP, white cell count

options.label: B
options.text: Non-weight-bearing, temperature >38.5°C, ESR >40 mm/hr, white cell count >12 ×10⁹/L

options.label: C
options.text: Night pain, raised alkaline phosphatase, ESR, platelet count

options.label: D
options.text: Limp, normal CRP, low albumin, raised urate

options.label: E
options.text: Trauma history, temperature <37.5°C, normal ESR, raised D-dimer

explanation: The Kocher criteria distinguish septic arthritis from transient synovitis of the paediatric hip using four independent predictors: (1) inability/refusal to weight-bear, (2) temperature >38.5°C, (3) ESR >40 mm/hr, and (4) peripheral WBC >12 ×10⁹/L. The probability of septic arthritis rises steeply with the number of positive predictors (from ~<3% with none to >90% with all four in the original series). Many UK units incorporate CRP (>20 mg/L) as an additional/modified predictor (Caird modification). The criteria GUIDE but do not replace clinical judgement and joint aspiration, which remains definitive — aspiration showing a high synovial white cell count with a positive Gram stain/culture confirms septic arthritis and is a surgical emergency requiring washout.
learningPoint: Kocher criteria = non-weight-bearing, temp >38.5°C, ESR >40, WBC >12; they risk-stratify but aspiration remains definitive.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Paediatric hip infection (Kocher criteria)
BSCOS / BOAST: acute bone and joint infection in children

stem: A 70-year-old has a chronically painful total hip replacement at 18 months with a sinus communicating with the prosthesis. Cultures grow a coagulase-negative staphylococcus. Why are antibiotics alone, without removal or exchange of the implant, typically inadequate to eradicate this chronic prosthetic joint infection?

options.label: A
options.text: The implant induces permanent systemic immunosuppression

options.label: B
options.text: Bacteria form an extracellular polymeric biofilm on the implant, within which they enter a metabolically dormant, antibiotic-tolerant state

options.label: C
options.text: Antibiotics cannot reach therapeutic plasma concentrations in the elderly

options.label: D
options.text: Coagulase-negative staphylococci are intrinsically resistant to all systemic antibiotics

options.label: E
options.text: Metal ions directly inactivate all classes of antibiotic at the implant surface

explanation: Chronic prosthetic joint infection is fundamentally a BIOFILM disease. Bacteria (classically coagulase-negative staphylococci and S. aureus) adhere to the implant and secrete an extracellular polymeric matrix (glycocalyx) that impairs antibiotic penetration and immune-cell access. Crucially, many bacteria deep within the biofilm shift to a slow-growing/dormant 'persister' phenotype that is phenotypically tolerant to antibiotics targeting cell-wall synthesis or replication. This is why source control — implant removal or staged exchange — is generally required, and why RIFAMPICIN (which penetrates staphylococcal biofilm) is combined with a partner agent for retained-implant strategies (e.g. DAIR), never used alone owing to rapid resistance. The presence of a sinus tract is itself a confirmatory criterion for PJI.
learningPoint: PJI persistence is driven by biofilm-protected, dormant 'persister' bacteria; eradication usually needs implant removal/exchange ± rifampicin-based combinations.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Basic Science: biofilm and implant infection

stem: A 68-year-old has a painful total knee replacement. Aspiration is performed before any decision on revision. Which single finding would, on its own, be considered a CONFIRMATORY (major) criterion for prosthetic joint infection?

options.label: A
options.text: Serum CRP of 25 mg/L

options.label: B
options.text: A single synovial fluid culture growing a coagulase-negative staphylococcus

options.label: C
options.text: Two separate samples (e.g. aspirate and tissue) growing the same (phenotypically identical) organism, or a sinus tract communicating with the prosthesis

options.label: D
options.text: Synovial white cell count of 1,500 cells/µL

options.label: E
options.text: Localised radiolucency at the bone–cement interface on radiographs

explanation: Across the internationally used definitions (MSIS / EBJIS / ICM), MAJOR (confirmatory) criteria for PJI are: (1) a sinus tract communicating with the joint/visible prosthesis, OR (2) two positive periprosthetic cultures with the same phenotypically identical organism. A SINGLE positive culture — especially of a common skin contaminant such as a coagulase-negative staphylococcus — is only a MINOR criterion because of contamination risk, and must be interpreted alongside other parameters. Raised CRP, an elevated synovial WBC/neutrophil differential, positive synovial leukocyte esterase or alpha-defensin, and elevated ESR are MINOR criteria that are summed to reach a threshold. The synovial WBC thresholds used for chronic PJI are far higher (e.g. ~>3,000 cells/µL) than the 1,500 quoted, and bone–cement radiolucency indicates loosening, which may be aseptic or septic.
learningPoint: Two matching cultures or a communicating sinus = confirmed PJI; a single skin-flora culture or modestly raised CRP are only minor criteria.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Adult reconstruction: PJI definitions (MSIS/ICM)
BOAST: Prosthetic Joint Infection; EBJIS criteria

stem: A patient with an orthopaedic infection is treated with vancomycin. Which statement correctly describes its mechanism of action and a relevant pharmacological principle?

options.label: A
options.text: It inhibits the bacterial 30S ribosomal subunit and is concentration-dependent

options.label: B
options.text: It inhibits peptidoglycan cross-linking by binding the D-alanyl-D-alanine terminus, and its efficacy is best monitored by trough level/AUC (time-dependent)

options.label: C
options.text: It inhibits DNA gyrase (topoisomerase II) and is bacteriostatic

options.label: D
options.text: It binds penicillin-binding proteins via a beta-lactam ring and is rapidly bactericidal against Gram-negatives

options.label: E
options.text: It disrupts folate synthesis and requires no therapeutic drug monitoring

explanation: Vancomycin is a glycopeptide that inhibits cell-wall synthesis by binding the terminal D-alanyl-D-alanine (D-Ala-D-Ala) of peptidoglycan precursors, blocking transglycosylation/transpeptidation — a DIFFERENT site from the beta-lactams (which acylate penicillin-binding proteins). It acts on Gram-POSITIVE organisms (it cannot cross the Gram-negative outer membrane) and is the workhorse for MRSA. It exhibits TIME-dependent killing best predicted by the AUC/MIC ratio, so monitoring uses trough levels (or AUC-guided dosing) rather than peaks; this contrasts with aminoglycosides (concentration-dependent, 30S inhibitors). Vancomycin resistance in enterococci (VRE) arises by altering the terminus to D-Ala-D-LACTATE, abolishing binding. Quinolones inhibit DNA gyrase; sulphonamides/trimethoprim block folate synthesis.
learningPoint: Vancomycin blocks cell-wall D-Ala-D-Ala (Gram-positive only), is time/AUC-dependent (monitor troughs); VRE swaps the terminus to D-Ala-D-Lactate.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Basic Science: antibiotics
NICE/BNF antimicrobial guidance; UKHSA/local antimicrobial stewardship

stem: You are responsible for antibiotic prophylaxis in a routine primary total hip replacement under general anaesthesia, with no tourniquet. Which approach BEST reflects current UK practice for surgical antibiotic prophylaxis?

options.label: A
options.text: Give the first dose 2 hours before incision and continue intravenous antibiotics for 5 days postoperatively

options.label: B
options.text: Administer prophylaxis within 60 minutes before skin incision, with a single dose (repeated only for prolonged surgery or major blood loss)

options.label: C
options.text: Give antibiotics only after wound closure to avoid masking intraoperative contamination

options.label: D
options.text: Use oral antibiotics on the morning of surgery as the sole prophylaxis

options.label: E
options.text: Omit prophylaxis entirely if laminar-flow theatre ventilation is used

explanation: Effective surgical antibiotic prophylaxis requires adequate tissue concentrations at the moment of incision. UK guidance (NICE SSI guideline NG125 and antimicrobial stewardship principles) recommends giving intravenous prophylaxis within 60 minutes before skin incision (for slow-infusing agents such as vancomycin/teicoplanin, start earlier so the infusion completes before incision; when a tourniquet is used the dose should be fully delivered BEFORE inflation). A SINGLE dose is appropriate for most clean orthopaedic surgery, with an intraoperative REPEAT dose only if the operation outlasts ~2 half-lives of the agent or there is major (>1.5 L) blood loss. Prolonged postoperative courses do not reduce SSI and drive resistance and Clostridioides difficile — a core stewardship message. Laminar flow does not replace prophylaxis. Choice of agent follows local microbiology policy with MRSA screening/decolonisation as indicated.
learningPoint: Single-dose IV prophylaxis within 60 min of incision (before tourniquet inflation); repeat only for long ops/major blood loss — prolonged courses are harmful.
references: NICE NG125 Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment (2019)
Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — perioperative antibiotic prophylaxis

stem: A 38-year-old man on long-term high-dose corticosteroids develops gradually worsening groin pain. MRI of the hip shows a subchondral serpiginous low-signal line in the anterosuperior femoral head with preserved sphericity and no subchondral collapse. Regarding the pathophysiology and prognosis of femoral head osteonecrosis, which statement is MOST accurate?

options.label: A
options.text: MRI is less sensitive than plain radiographs for early disease

options.label: B
options.text: The final common pathway is interruption of the subchondral blood supply causing osteocyte death; collapse risk depends chiefly on lesion size and location, and this Ficat/ARCO pre-collapse stage may be amenable to joint-preserving surgery

options.label: C
options.text: Once the 'crescent sign' is visible the head will reliably remodel without surgery

options.label: D
options.text: Corticosteroid-induced osteonecrosis is caused by direct steroid cytotoxicity to articular cartilage

options.label: E
options.text: Lesion size and location have no bearing on the risk of collapse

explanation: Osteonecrosis (avascular necrosis) of the femoral head results from interruption of the subchondral blood supply, leading to osteocyte and marrow death; mechanisms proposed for steroid/alcohol-related disease include fat embolism, marrow adipocyte hypertrophy raising intraosseous pressure, and intravascular coagulation — NOT direct cartilage cytotoxicity. MRI is the most SENSITIVE early investigation (the double-line sign and serpiginous margin precede radiographic change). Prognosis hinges on lesion SIZE and LOCATION (large, lateral/anterosuperior, weight-bearing lesions — e.g. >30% head involvement or a large necrotic arc — collapse most readily). The case is PRE-collapse (Ficat II / ARCO II–IIIa, head still spherical), the stage where joint-preserving options such as core decompression (± bone grafting/biologics) are considered; the CRESCENT SIGN denotes subchondral fracture and IMPENDING collapse (a worse prognosis), not benign remodelling.
learningPoint: AVN = subchondral ischaemic osteocyte death; MRI is most sensitive early, and collapse risk tracks lesion size/location — the crescent sign signals subchondral fracture, not recovery.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Basic Science / Hip: osteonecrosis (Ficat & ARCO)
BOA/BHS guidance on osteonecrosis of the femoral head

stem: Theme: Osteonecrosis — sites, eponyms and risk factors. For each clinical description below, select the SINGLE most appropriate answer from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Kienböck disease (lunate osteonecrosis)

options.label: B
options.text: Preiser disease (scaphoid osteonecrosis)

options.label: C
options.text: Freiberg disease (second metatarsal head)

options.label: D
options.text: Köhler disease (tarsal navicular)

options.label: E
options.text: Legg–Calvé–Perthes disease (femoral head, paediatric)

options.label: F
options.text: Caisson disease (dysbaric osteonecrosis)

options.label: G
options.text: Osteonecrosis associated with sickle-cell disease

options.label: H
options.text: Corticosteroid-associated osteonecrosis

emiLeads.stem: A 22-year-old commercial diver presents with bilateral shoulder and hip pain years after repeated deep dives; radiographs show patchy subchondral sclerosis from nitrogen-bubble vascular occlusion.
A 25-year-old manual labourer has chronic wrist pain and reduced grip; he has a short (negative-variance) ulna, and MRI shows diffuse low signal of a carpal bone with later fragmentation.
A 13-year-old keen runner has forefoot pain and a swollen second metatarsophalangeal joint; radiographs show flattening and sclerosis of the second metatarsal head.
A 6-year-old boy has a painless limp and reduced hip abduction; AP pelvis shows a smaller, sclerotic, fragmenting femoral head ossific nucleus with a normal joint space.
A 9-year-old with known haemoglobinopathy develops acute bone pain and dactylitis, with avascular changes at multiple epiphyses on imaging.

explanation: Osteonecrosis eponyms map to specific sites: Kienböck = lunate (strongly associated with ulna-minus/negative ulnar variance, which alters lunate loading); Preiser = scaphoid; Freiberg = (typically) second metatarsal head, the only one of these classically commoner in females and adolescents; Köhler = tarsal navicular in young children. Legg–Calvé–Perthes is idiopathic childhood femoral head osteonecrosis. Systemic causes of osteonecrosis are high-yield: dysbaric/Caisson disease (nitrogen emboli in divers/compressed-air workers), sickle-cell disease (microvascular occlusion — a leading cause of femoral head AVN in affected populations), and corticosteroid/alcohol exposure (the commonest atraumatic causes overall in adults). Recognising the site–eponym pairing and the underlying ischaemic mechanism is a recurrent basic-science theme.
learningPoint: Know the osteonecrosis eponyms by site (lunate=Kienböck, scaphoid=Preiser, 2nd MT head=Freiberg, navicular=Köhler) and the systemic causes (Caisson, sickle-cell, steroids/alcohol).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Basic Science: osteonecrosis eponyms

stem: A materials scientist tests a cortical bone specimen by loading it slowly and then very rapidly to failure. Compared with slow loading, when the same bone is loaded at a high strain rate it behaves in which way?

options.label: A
options.text: It becomes more compliant and fails at a lower load

options.label: B
options.text: It becomes stiffer, absorbs more energy and fails at a higher load

options.label: C
options.text: Its Young's modulus is unchanged because bone is an isotropic material

options.label: D
options.text: It fails by creep at a constant low stress

options.label: E
options.text: Its behaviour is identical because bone is not viscoelastic

explanation: Bone is viscoelastic, so its mechanical response depends on the rate at which load is applied. At higher strain rates the bone becomes stiffer, can store and absorb more energy before failure, and ultimately fails at a higher load. This explains why high-energy trauma produces comminuted fractures (large energy absorbed and suddenly released), whereas low-energy loading tends to produce simpler fracture patterns. Bone is also anisotropic, not isotropic, so option C is wrong.
learningPoint: Bone is viscoelastic and anisotropic: faster loading makes it stiffer and stronger but releases more energy at failure, producing comminution.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Basic Science (Biomechanics)
Orthobullets Basic Science: Bone Material Properties

stem: During a viva you are shown a stress-strain curve for a cortical bone sample loaded to failure. Which statement about the region of the curve BEYOND the yield point but BEFORE ultimate failure is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: Deformation in this region is fully recoverable when load is removed

options.label: B
options.text: The slope of the curve in this region defines the Young's modulus

options.label: C
options.text: Permanent (plastic) deformation occurs and microdamage accumulates

options.label: D
options.text: Energy stored in this region is released completely on unloading

options.label: E
options.text: The material has not yet started to deform in this region

explanation: Before the yield point the material behaves elastically (deformation is recoverable and the slope equals Young's modulus). Beyond the yield point the material enters the plastic region: deformation becomes permanent, microcracks accumulate, and energy is dissipated rather than fully recovered. The area under the whole curve represents the energy (toughness) absorbed to failure. Ultimate failure occurs at the end of the plastic region.
learningPoint: Past the yield point bone deforms plastically — deformation is permanent, microdamage accrues, and energy is dissipated rather than returned.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Basic Science (Biomechanics)

stem: A 34-year-old sustains a short oblique mid-shaft humeral fracture treated with open reduction. The surgeon places a lag screw across the fracture and then applies a neutralisation plate. What is the principal mechanical purpose of the neutralisation plate in this construct?

options.label: A
options.text: To create a load-sharing bridge that promotes callus formation

options.label: B
options.text: To generate axial compression across the fracture by itself

options.label: C
options.text: To protect the lag screw from torsional, bending and shear forces

options.label: D
options.text: To act as a tension band converting tension into compression

options.label: E
options.text: To deliberately distract the fracture and encourage indirect healing

explanation: A lag screw provides interfragmentary compression and absolute stability but is mechanically weak in bending, torsion and shear. A neutralisation (protection) plate is applied to offload these forces from the lag screw, protecting the interfragmentary compression it generates. The plate itself is not the primary source of compression here. Bridging plates (option A) are used for comminuted fractures with relative stability, and a tension band (option D) is a different principle used at sites where eccentric load can be converted to compression.
learningPoint: A neutralisation plate protects a lag screw from bending, torsion and shear; the lag screw still provides the interfragmentary compression and absolute stability.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Trauma (Principles of Fracture Fixation)

stem: A surgeon is placing a 3.5 mm cortical lag screw to compress a simple oblique fracture using the 'lag by technique' method (a fully threaded screw). What must be done to the NEAR cortex to achieve interfragmentary compression?

options.label: A
options.text: Drill the near cortex to the screw core diameter so the threads grip it

options.label: B
options.text: Over-drill the near cortex to the screw thread (outer) diameter to create a gliding hole

options.label: C
options.text: Tap both cortices and engage threads in each

options.label: D
options.text: Leave the near cortex undrilled and self-tap through it

options.label: E
options.text: Under-drill the far cortex to the gliding diameter

explanation: To lag a fully threaded screw, the near (proximal) cortex is over-drilled to the outer (thread) diameter so it becomes a gliding hole that the threads do not grip. The far cortex is drilled to the core diameter (and tapped) so the threads engage only there. As the screw is tightened, the head pushes on the near fragment while the threads pull the far fragment, compressing the two together. If both cortices were threaded equally, no compression would occur — the fragments would simply hold their relative position.
learningPoint: Lag by technique: over-drill the NEAR cortex to thread diameter (gliding hole); engage threads only in the FAR cortex to generate compression.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Trauma (Lag Screw)

stem: An engineer explains the lubrication regimes of a total hip replacement bearing. In a well-functioning metal-on-polyethylene hip, a continuous film of synovial fluid does NOT fully separate the surfaces at all times. Which lubrication regime, in which surface asperities make intermittent contact through a partial fluid film, best describes this situation?

options.label: A
options.text: Fluid-film (full hydrodynamic) lubrication

options.label: B
options.text: Boundary lubrication

options.label: C
options.text: Mixed lubrication

options.label: D
options.text: Elastohydrodynamic lubrication with complete separation

options.label: E
options.text: Dry (unlubricated) sliding

explanation: In full fluid-film lubrication the surfaces are completely separated by a pressurised fluid layer, so wear is minimal. In boundary lubrication a thin adsorbed molecular layer protects surfaces but asperities are in near-continuous contact. Mixed lubrication is the intermediate, real-world situation in most metal-on-polyethylene hips: part of the load is carried by a fluid film while some asperities make intermittent contact. Hard-on-hard bearings with low surface roughness and high sphericity come closest to true (elasto)hydrodynamic full-film lubrication.
learningPoint: Most metal-on-polyethylene hips operate in mixed lubrication — partial fluid film with intermittent asperity contact; full fluid-film separation is the goal of hard-on-hard bearings.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Basic Science (Tribology)

stem: Theme: Mechanisms of wear in joint arthroplasty. For each clinical or laboratory description, select the SINGLE most appropriate wear mechanism from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Adhesive wear

options.label: B
options.text: Abrasive wear

options.label: C
options.text: Third-body wear

options.label: D
options.text: Fatigue wear

options.label: E
options.text: Fretting wear

options.label: F
options.text: Corrosive (tribocorrosion) wear

options.label: G
options.text: Erosive wear

explanation: Adhesive wear occurs when local bonds form between contacting surfaces and material is torn from the softer surface as they slide (classic in metal-on-polyethylene). Third-body wear results when loose hard particles (e.g. bone cement, bone, or metal debris) become trapped in the joint space and scratch the bearing. Fatigue wear (including delamination and subsurface pitting) follows cyclical subsurface stress and is characteristic of damaged or oxidised polyethylene, especially in knees. Fretting wear arises from small-amplitude oscillatory micromotion at junctions such as the modular head-trunnion (Morse taper) interface. Abrasive wear is a hard rough surface ploughing the softer one.
learningPoint: Match the wear mechanism to its mechanism of action: adhesion (bonding/tearing), abrasion (ploughing), third-body (trapped particles), fatigue (cyclic subsurface), fretting (micromotion at modular junctions).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Basic Science (Wear)

emiLeads.stem: A retrieved metal femoral head shows surface damage and black discolouration at the bore where it engaged the stem trunnion, with small-amplitude micromotion implicated.
Fragments of bone cement become trapped between a metal femoral head and the polyethylene liner, producing multidirectional scratches on the head.
A retrieved tibial polyethylene insert from a total knee replacement shows subsurface cracking, pitting and delamination after years of cyclical loading.
Microscopy of a metal-on-polyethylene bearing shows polyethylene material that has bonded to and been pulled away from the surface by the metal counterface during sliding.

stem: A manufacturer markets a 'highly cross-linked' ultra-high-molecular-weight polyethylene (UHMWPE) liner for total hip replacement, produced by irradiation. Which statement best summarises the trade-off introduced by increasing the cross-link density?

options.label: A
options.text: Improved fracture toughness but increased volumetric wear

options.label: B
options.text: Reduced adhesive/abrasive wear but reduced fatigue strength and fracture toughness

options.label: C
options.text: Increased fatigue strength with no change in wear performance

options.label: D
options.text: Reduced wear and increased oxidative stability without any need for remelting or annealing

options.label: E
options.text: Increased ductility and elongation to failure

explanation: Gamma or electron-beam irradiation cross-links UHMWPE, markedly reducing adhesive and abrasive wear, which is the principal advantage for hips. However, cross-linking and the free radicals generated reduce the polymer's mechanical properties — lower fatigue strength, lower fracture toughness, reduced ductility and elongation. Residual free radicals also predispose to oxidation, so highly cross-linked polyethylene is post-treated by remelting (better oxidation resistance, lower mechanical properties) or annealing (preserves mechanics, less complete radical quenching), and second-generation materials add an antioxidant such as vitamin E. The reduced fatigue strength is why early highly cross-linked liners were used cautiously in knees and thin/locking-mechanism applications.
learningPoint: Cross-linking UHMWPE cuts wear but lowers fatigue strength, toughness and ductility — hence remelting/annealing and vitamin-E stabilisation to manage free radicals and oxidation.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Basic Science (Polyethylene)

stem: A well-fixed cementless total hip replacement presents at 12 years with a peri-acetabular osteolytic lesion attributed to polyethylene wear debris. Which cell and mediator pathway is the principal driver of this osteolysis?

options.label: A
options.text: Polyethylene particles directly dissolving bone mineral by a chemical reaction

options.label: B
options.text: Macrophage phagocytosis of particles, releasing TNF-alpha and IL-1, upregulating RANKL and activating osteoclasts

options.label: C
options.text: An IgE-mediated type I hypersensitivity reaction to polyethylene

options.label: D
options.text: Osteoblast proliferation forming a rapidly expanding bone cyst

options.label: E
options.text: Direct osteocyte apoptosis caused by metal ion release from the femoral head

explanation: Sub-micron polyethylene wear particles are phagocytosed by macrophages, which become activated and release pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-1, IL-6, PGE2). This shifts the RANKL/OPG balance toward RANKL, driving osteoclast differentiation and activation and producing peri-prosthetic osteolysis — historically termed 'particle disease'. Particle size (around 0.1–1 micron is most biologically active) and total volumetric load matter. This is a macrophage-mediated foreign-body/innate response, not a chemical dissolution, an IgE reaction, or osteoblast proliferation.
learningPoint: Polyethylene osteolysis is macrophage-driven: phagocytosed particles release TNF-alpha/IL-1, tip RANKL/OPG toward RANKL and activate osteoclasts.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Basic Science (Osteolysis/Particle Disease)

stem: When selecting the metal alloy for a femoral stem, stiffness (elastic modulus) relative to bone is important in stress shielding. Which of the following correctly ranks the elastic (Young's) modulus from HIGHEST to LOWEST?

options.label: A
options.text: Cortical bone > titanium alloy > stainless steel > cobalt-chrome > ceramic (alumina)

options.label: B
options.text: Ceramic (alumina) > cobalt-chrome > stainless steel > titanium alloy > cortical bone

options.label: C
options.text: Titanium alloy > cobalt-chrome > stainless steel > ceramic > cortical bone

options.label: D
options.text: Cobalt-chrome > ceramic > titanium alloy > stainless steel > cortical bone

options.label: E
options.text: Stainless steel > cobalt-chrome > ceramic > cortical bone > titanium alloy

explanation: Approximate Young's moduli: alumina ceramic ~350–400 GPa, cobalt-chromium ~210–230 GPa, stainless steel ~190–200 GPa, titanium alloy (Ti-6Al-4V) ~110 GPa, and cortical bone ~15–20 GPa. So the correct order is ceramic > cobalt-chrome > stainless steel > titanium > cortical bone. Titanium's relatively low modulus (closest of the metals to bone) reduces stress shielding, which is one reason it is favoured for cementless stems; however, titanium has poor wear/notch behaviour and is not used as an articulating bearing surface.
learningPoint: Modulus order: ceramic > Co-Cr > stainless steel > titanium > bone. Titanium's lower modulus best matches bone and reduces stress shielding.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Basic Science (Biomaterials)

stem: A retrieved stainless-steel fracture plate that failed after several months in vivo shows, under scanning electron microscopy, a fracture surface with beach marks (striations) progressing from a surface initiation site, ending in a small region of dimpled rupture. What is the mode of failure?

options.label: A
options.text: Brittle fracture from a single overload event

options.label: B
options.text: Fatigue failure

options.label: C
options.text: Stress-corrosion cracking only, without cyclical loading

options.label: D
options.text: Creep deformation

options.label: E
options.text: Hydrogen embrittlement at the time of implantation

explanation: Beach marks (macroscopic) and striations (microscopic) advancing from an initiation site are the hallmark of fatigue: the crack propagates incrementally with each loading cycle until the remaining cross-section can no longer carry the load, when sudden final overload failure occurs (the terminal dimpled/ductile or cleavage zone). Implant fatigue is the commonest mechanical mode of plate/nail failure and is accelerated by stress risers (screw holes, scratches), non-union (the implant carries load indefinitely), and corrosion (corrosion-fatigue). A single overload would not produce striations, and creep is time-dependent deformation rather than crack propagation.
learningPoint: Beach marks/striations ending in a final overload zone = fatigue failure; non-union and stress risers accelerate it, and corrosion compounds it (corrosion-fatigue).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Basic Science (Implant Failure)

stem: A 62-year-old has a modular metal-on-metal total hip replacement and develops a painful pseudotumour with raised blood cobalt and chromium levels. Beyond bearing-surface wear, micromotion and corrosion at the modular head-neck (Morse taper) junction contribute to the metal release. Which single term most precisely describes this junctional process?

options.label: A
options.text: Galvanic corrosion alone

options.label: B
options.text: Mechanically assisted crevice corrosion (tribocorrosion / fretting-corrosion)

options.label: C
options.text: Pitting corrosion of a passive titanium surface

options.label: D
options.text: Intergranular corrosion from sensitised grain boundaries

options.label: E
options.text: Uniform (general) surface corrosion

explanation: At a modular taper, small-amplitude micromotion under cyclical load abrades the passivating oxide layer (fretting) within a confined fluid space (crevice) where oxygen is depleted and the local environment becomes acidic, preventing repassivation and accelerating corrosion. The combination is termed mechanically assisted crevice corrosion (MACC), a form of tribocorrosion/fretting-corrosion. It releases metal ions and debris (provoking adverse reaction to metal debris, ARMD/pseudotumour) and is recognised even in metal-on-polyethylene hips with large heads. Galvanic corrosion (dissimilar metals) may coexist but does not by itself capture the fretting/crevice mechanism. UK MHRA guidance mandates surveillance of metal-on-metal hips with metal ion levels and cross-sectional imaging.
learningPoint: Taper-junction metal release = mechanically assisted crevice corrosion (fretting + crevice corrosion); trigger MHRA metal-on-metal surveillance with ion levels and imaging.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Basic Science (Corrosion)
MHRA Medical Device Alert: All metal-on-metal hip replacements (surveillance)

stem: A trainee is asked to define the three biological properties a bone graft may provide. Fresh autogenous cancellous bone graft is considered the gold standard because it possesses all three. Which option correctly pairs each property with its meaning?

options.label: A
options.text: Osteoconduction = provides living osteoprogenitor cells; Osteoinduction = provides a scaffold; Osteogenesis = stimulates differentiation

options.label: B
options.text: Osteoconduction = provides a passive scaffold for ingrowth; Osteoinduction = recruits and stimulates host cells to differentiate into osteoblasts; Osteogenesis = graft contains living cells that directly form bone

options.label: C
options.text: Osteoinduction = provides a scaffold; Osteoconduction = provides growth factors; Osteogenesis = provides mineral

options.label: D
options.text: Osteogenesis = scaffold for ingrowth; Osteoconduction = living cells; Osteoinduction = mineral content

options.label: E
options.text: All three terms are synonyms describing the calcium content of the graft

explanation: Osteoconduction is the provision of a passive three-dimensional scaffold that supports vascular and cellular ingrowth (e.g. ceramics such as hydroxyapatite/tricalcium phosphate, and structural allograft). Osteoinduction is the recruitment and stimulation of host mesenchymal cells to differentiate into osteoblasts, mediated by growth factors such as the bone morphogenetic proteins; demineralised bone matrix is osteoinductive. Osteogenesis requires viable cells within the graft that directly lay down new bone — only fresh autograft (and bone marrow aspirate concentrate) provides this. Autograft uniquely combines all three, which is why it remains the gold standard.
learningPoint: Osteoconduction = scaffold; osteoinduction = growth-factor-driven recruitment/differentiation; osteogenesis = living bone-forming cells. Only autograft provides all three.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Basic Science (Bone Grafting)

stem: Theme: Bone graft and graft substitutes. For each description, select the SINGLE most appropriate graft material from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Fresh iliac crest autograft

options.label: B
options.text: Demineralised bone matrix (DBM)

options.label: C
options.text: Fresh-frozen structural allograft

options.label: D
options.text: Beta-tricalcium phosphate (beta-TCP) synthetic granules

options.label: E
options.text: Recombinant human BMP-2

options.label: F
options.text: Calcium sulphate pellets

options.label: G
options.text: Bone marrow aspirate concentrate (BMAC)

explanation: Fresh autograft is the only material that is simultaneously osteogenic, osteoinductive and osteoconductive, but it carries donor-site morbidity and limited supply. Structural (cortical) allograft provides immediate mechanical support and an osteoconductive scaffold but is essentially non-osteogenic and carries a small disease-transmission risk. Synthetic ceramics such as beta-TCP are purely osteoconductive, resorbable scaffolds with no induction or osteogenic capacity. Demineralised bone matrix is principally osteoinductive (retained BMPs) with some conduction. Recombinant BMP-2 is a potent osteoinductive growth factor used in specific licensed indications. Bone marrow aspirate concentrate supplies osteogenic/osteoprogenitor cells. Recognising which property each provides drives appropriate clinical selection.
learningPoint: Know the dominant property of each substitute: autograft (all three), allograft and ceramics (conductive/structural), DBM and BMP (inductive), BMAC (osteogenic cells).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Basic Science (Bone Graft Substitutes)

emiLeads.stem: A large segmental cortical defect after tumour resection requires immediate structural mechanical support; the surgeon accepts that the material has minimal osteogenic potential.
A purely synthetic, resorbable, osteoconductive-only scaffold is required to fill a contained metaphyseal cavity, avoiding any donor-site morbidity or disease-transmission risk.
A graft material is selected specifically for its osteoinductive growth-factor (BMP) content while providing little structural strength.
The surgeon wants the single material that is simultaneously osteogenic, osteoinductive and osteoconductive and is prepared to accept donor-site morbidity.

stem: During normal walking the gait cycle is divided into stance and swing phases. In a normal adult walking at a comfortable speed, stance phase occupies approximately what proportion of a single gait cycle, and what proportion is spent in double support?

options.label: A
options.text: Stance ~40%, double support ~40%

options.label: B
options.text: Stance ~60%, double support ~20%

options.label: C
options.text: Stance ~50%, double support ~50%

options.label: D
options.text: Stance ~80%, double support ~0% (none)

options.label: E
options.text: Stance ~60%, double support ~60%

explanation: In normal walking the stance phase (foot in contact with the ground) occupies roughly 60% of the gait cycle and the swing phase about 40%. Within stance there are two periods of double support (both feet on the ground), together making up about 20% of the cycle (two periods of ~10%). As walking speed increases, the time in double support decreases; in running, double support disappears entirely and is replaced by a float (double-float) period where neither foot is grounded. Double support is therefore a key feature distinguishing walking from running.
learningPoint: Normal gait: ~60% stance / 40% swing, with ~20% double support; double support vanishes in running (replaced by a float phase).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Basic Science (Gait)

stem: A patient with a painful right hip shifts the trunk laterally over the right (affected) leg during single-leg stance on that side. What is the primary biomechanical effect of this manoeuvre?

options.label: A
options.text: It increases the hip abductor force required to keep the pelvis level

options.label: B
options.text: It shortens the body-weight lever arm about the hip, reducing abductor force and hip joint reaction force

options.label: C
options.text: It increases the hip joint reaction force to improve joint congruency

options.label: D
options.text: It compensates for a foot drop by aiding toe clearance in swing

options.label: E
options.text: It corrects a true leg-length discrepancy on the affected side

explanation: Shifting the trunk (centre of gravity) over the painful hip during single-leg stance shortens the lever arm of body weight about the hip centre, so the abductors need to generate less force to balance the pelvis. Because the hip joint reaction force is the sum of body weight and abductor pull, reducing the abductor demand reduces the joint reaction force and therefore the pain. This trunk-lean-over-the-affected-side pattern is the abductor lurch component seen with a painful or abductor-deficient hip, and a walking stick used in the opposite (contralateral) hand offloads the joint by the same lever-arm principle.
learningPoint: Leaning over a painful hip shortens the body-weight lever arm, cutting abductor force and hip joint reaction force; a contralateral walking stick achieves the same offloading.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Basic Science (Gait & Hip Biomechanics)
Ramachandran, Basic Orthopaedic Sciences (Hip Joint Reaction Force)

stem: A comminuted distal femoral fracture is managed with a long locking plate used as an internal fixator (bridging mode), aiming for relative stability and secondary bone healing by callus. The fracture is slow to unite. Which adjustment is most consistent with the biomechanical principles of bridge plating?

options.label: A
options.text: Place screws in every hole adjacent to the fracture to maximise rigidity

options.label: B
options.text: Shorten the plate and concentrate fixation close to the fracture

options.label: C
options.text: Increase the plate working length and use a longer plate with well-spaced screws to allow controlled interfragmentary strain

options.label: D
options.text: Convert to absolute stability by lagging the comminuted zone

options.label: E
options.text: Use the shortest possible screws with unicortical purchase only throughout

explanation: Bridge plating relies on relative stability and the controlled micromotion that, under low-to-moderate interfragmentary strain (roughly 2–10%), stimulates callus (secondary bone healing). An excessively rigid construct produces strain too low for callus yet insufficient absolute stability for primary healing, risking non-union and plate fatigue. Increasing the plate's working length (the unsupported span between the nearest screws on either side of the fracture) and using a longer plate with fewer, well-distributed screws lowers construct stiffness and stress concentration, distributes load over more cortex, and permits the controlled motion that drives callus. Filling every hole or shortening the plate makes the construct stiffer and concentrates stress, the opposite of what is needed. Lagging the comminution would devascularise fragments and is biomechanically inappropriate in this pattern.
learningPoint: In bridge plating, increasing working length and using a longer plate with spaced screws lowers stiffness and stress risers, encouraging callus by controlled interfragmentary strain.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Trauma (Bridge Plating / Relative Stability)

stem: A 62-year-old woman is being investigated for osteoporosis after a low-energy distal radius fracture. A dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) scan is requested. Which statement BEST describes the physical principle underlying DEXA bone densitometry?

options.label: A
options.text: It measures the differential attenuation of two X-ray photon energies to calculate areal bone mineral density

options.label: B
options.text: It detects gamma photons emitted by a radiopharmaceutical concentrated in metabolically active bone

options.label: C
options.text: It quantifies the resonance of hydrogen protons within trabecular marrow fat

options.label: D
options.text: It reconstructs a volumetric density map from a rotating single-energy X-ray source

options.label: E
options.text: It uses the back-scatter of high-frequency sound waves to estimate cortical thickness

explanation: DEXA uses two distinct X-ray photon energies. Because soft tissue and bone attenuate the two energies differently, the soft-tissue contribution can be subtracted, allowing calculation of areal (2D, g/cm2) bone mineral density. Results are expressed as a T-score (vs young adult mean) and Z-score (age-matched). Option B describes bone scintigraphy, C describes MRI, D describes quantitative CT (a true volumetric technique), and E describes quantitative ultrasound.
learningPoint: DEXA gives areal (not volumetric) BMD by subtracting soft tissue using two X-ray energies; the T-score drives the WHO osteoporosis definition (<=-2.5).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Basic Science (imaging/bone metabolism)

stem: A 28-year-old footballer has persistent medial knee pain 3 weeks after a twisting injury. Plain radiographs are normal. You wish to assess the menisci and cruciate ligaments. Regarding the imaging physics, which property makes MRI the modality of choice for this soft-tissue assessment?

options.label: A
options.text: Differences in proton density and T1/T2 relaxation times between tissues generate high soft-tissue contrast without ionising radiation

options.label: B
options.text: The high atomic number of ligamentous collagen produces strong X-ray attenuation

options.label: C
options.text: Increased osteoblastic turnover in injured ligament concentrates technetium-99m

options.label: D
options.text: Acoustic impedance mismatch at the meniscal surface reflects ultrasound efficiently through bone

options.label: E
options.text: Iodinated contrast preferentially opacifies hyaline cartilage on delayed imaging

explanation: MRI generates contrast from intrinsic tissue properties - proton density and the T1 and T2 relaxation characteristics of hydrogen nuclei in different tissues - giving it unrivalled soft-tissue discrimination for menisci, cruciates and cartilage, with no ionising radiation. Ligaments have low water content and appear dark; tears show increased signal on fluid-sensitive sequences. Ultrasound cannot penetrate bone to image intra-articular menisci/cruciates (D is wrong), and the other options confuse MRI with CT or scintigraphy.
learningPoint: MRI soft-tissue contrast comes from proton density and T1/T2 relaxation - ideal for menisci and cruciates, with no radiation.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Basic Science (MRI physics)
BASK guidance - soft tissue knee injury

stem: A 70-year-old man with known prostate cancer has new mid-thoracic back pain. A whole-body technetium-99m MDP bone scan is performed. Which statement BEST explains what an area of increased tracer uptake ('hot spot') represents?

options.label: A
options.text: Localised increased osteoblastic activity and regional blood flow

options.label: B
options.text: Pure osteolysis with destruction of bony architecture

options.label: C
options.text: High fat content within the marrow space

options.label: D
options.text: Accumulation of iodinated contrast in a hypervascular tumour

options.label: E
options.text: Reduced proton relaxation time in cortical bone

explanation: Technetium-99m-labelled methylene diphosphonate (MDP) is adsorbed onto hydroxyapatite at sites of active bone formation; uptake reflects osteoblastic activity and local perfusion. Hence osteoblastic/sclerotic metastases (typical of prostate) light up strongly. A purely lytic lesion with little osteoblastic response (e.g. some myeloma or aggressive renal/thyroid mets) can be falsely negative ('cold'), which is a key limitation. The other options describe CT, MRI or contrast behaviour.
learningPoint: Bone scintigraphy images osteoblastic activity - sensitive for sclerotic mets but can miss purely lytic disease (e.g. myeloma).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Oncology/Basic Science (nuclear imaging)

stem: A trial reports patient-reported Oxford Hip Scores at 1 year that are markedly skewed, with most patients scoring near the ceiling and a long tail of poor outcomes. Which measure of central tendency BEST represents the 'typical' patient in this skewed distribution, and why?

options.label: A
options.text: The median, because it is resistant to the influence of extreme outlying values

options.label: B
options.text: The mean, because it uses every data point and is therefore always most accurate

options.label: C
options.text: The mode, because it identifies the single most frequent score

options.label: D
options.text: The standard deviation, because it summarises the spread of the data

options.label: E
options.text: The range, because it captures the best and worst outcomes

explanation: In a skewed distribution the mean is pulled towards the tail and misrepresents the centre, whereas the median (the middle value) is robust to outliers and better reflects the typical patient. The mode is rarely informative for continuous scores and a ceiling effect makes it unhelpful. SD and range are measures of dispersion, not central tendency. For skewed PROMs, report the median with interquartile range.
learningPoint: For skewed data, use the median (with IQR); the mean is distorted by outliers and the tail.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Research & Statistics

stem: An RCT comparing two fixation methods for ankle fractures reports a mean difference in Olerud-Molander score of 4 points (95% confidence interval -1 to 9; p = 0.12). The minimal clinically important difference is 10 points. Which interpretation is MOST correct?

options.label: A
options.text: The difference is neither statistically significant nor clinically important, and the CI excludes the MCID

options.label: B
options.text: The result proves the two methods are equivalent

options.label: C
options.text: The difference is statistically significant because the point estimate favours one group

options.label: D
options.text: A larger sample would necessarily make this difference clinically important

options.label: E
options.text: The p-value of 0.12 means there is a 12% chance the null hypothesis is true

explanation: The 95% CI crosses zero (-1 to 9), so the result is not statistically significant at p<0.05 - consistent with p=0.12. Crucially, the entire CI lies below the MCID of 10, so even the most optimistic plausible effect is not clinically important. 'Not significant' does not prove equivalence (absence of evidence is not evidence of absence), so B is wrong. The p-value is the probability of data this extreme IF the null were true, not the probability the null is true (E is a common misinterpretation).
learningPoint: Always interpret the CI against the MCID: a non-significant result whose CI excludes the MCID suggests any true effect is clinically trivial, but does not prove equivalence.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Research & Statistics
BMJ Statistics Notes (confidence intervals and p-values)

stem: In a normally distributed dataset of femoral head diameters with a mean of 48 mm and a standard deviation of 2 mm, approximately what proportion of measurements lie between 44 mm and 52 mm?

options.label: A
options.text: Approximately 95%

options.label: B
options.text: Approximately 68%

options.label: C
options.text: Approximately 99.7%

options.label: D
options.text: Approximately 50%

options.label: E
options.text: Approximately 75%

explanation: The range 44-52 mm is mean +/- 2 SD (48 +/- 4 mm). For a normal (Gaussian) distribution, the empirical rule states ~68% of values fall within +/-1 SD, ~95% within +/-2 SD, and ~99.7% within +/-3 SD. Therefore approximately 95% of measurements lie within this interval. Note this concerns the distribution of individual values (using SD), distinct from the precision of the mean (which uses the standard error).
learningPoint: Empirical rule for a normal distribution: ~68% within 1 SD, ~95% within 2 SD, ~99.7% within 3 SD.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Research & Statistics

stem: A surgeon wants the highest-level individual study evidence to decide whether a new cementless stem reduces revision rates compared with an established cemented stem. Excluding systematic reviews, which single study design provides the HIGHEST level of evidence (Oxford CEBM Level I) and best minimises selection and confounding bias?

options.label: A
options.text: A well-conducted, adequately powered randomised controlled trial

options.label: B
options.text: A prospective cohort study with a concurrent comparison group

options.label: C
options.text: A retrospective case-control study

options.label: D
options.text: A consecutive case series with historical controls

options.label: E
options.text: Expert consensus opinion based on mechanistic reasoning

explanation: An adequately powered RCT sits at Level I (alongside systematic reviews of RCTs) in the Oxford CEBM hierarchy. Randomisation balances known and unknown confounders between groups and minimises selection bias, making it the strongest single-study design for a therapy/prevention question. Prospective cohorts are Level II, case-control studies Level III, case series Level IV, and expert opinion Level V. (In practice, large national registries such as the NJR add complementary real-world revision data.)
learningPoint: For therapy questions, the RCT is the highest-level single study because randomisation balances confounders; cohort > case-control > case series > expert opinion.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Research & Statistics

stem: Investigators wish to study risk factors for a rare complication - deep periprosthetic infection occurring more than 5 years after total knee replacement. Recruiting a large prospective cohort and waiting for events would be impractical. Which study design is MOST efficient for investigating risk factors for this rare, late-onset outcome?

options.label: A
options.text: Case-control study comparing prior exposures in infected versus uninfected patients

options.label: B
options.text: Randomised controlled trial randomising patients to potential risk factors

options.label: C
options.text: Prospective cohort study following all primary TKR patients for 10 years

options.label: D
options.text: Cross-sectional survey of current infection prevalence

options.label: E
options.text: Single-surgeon case report of an index infection

explanation: For a rare outcome, a case-control design is the most efficient: you start with patients who already have the outcome (cases) and a comparable group without it (controls), then look backwards at exposures, yielding an odds ratio. A prospective cohort would require huge numbers and long follow-up to accrue enough events. Randomising patients to potential harms (e.g. obesity, diabetes) is unethical and impossible. Cross-sectional studies measure prevalence at a point in time and cannot establish temporal risk; a case report cannot quantify risk factors.
learningPoint: Case-control studies are the design of choice for rare outcomes - efficient but prone to recall and selection bias; they yield odds ratios.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Research & Statistics

stem: A new bedside test for scaphoid fracture has a sensitivity of 95% and a specificity of 60%. In a busy emergency department, a clinician wants to use it primarily to safely RULE OUT a fracture in patients with a negative result. Which property of the test most supports this use, and what is the main caveat?

options.label: A
options.text: Its high sensitivity makes a negative result useful for ruling out (SnNout), but its low specificity will generate many false positives requiring further imaging

options.label: B
options.text: Its high specificity makes a positive result useful for ruling in, with few false negatives

options.label: C
options.text: Its positive predictive value is fixed regardless of disease prevalence

options.label: D
options.text: Its low specificity means a negative result confidently excludes fracture

options.label: E
options.text: Sensitivity and specificity both increase as prevalence rises

explanation: A highly Sensitive test, when Negative, helps rule OUT disease ('SnNout') because few true cases are missed (low false-negative rate). The trade-off here is low specificity (60%), so many disease-free patients test positive (false positives), driving unnecessary further imaging. PPV and NPV depend on prevalence (so C is false), whereas sensitivity and specificity are properties of the test and do not change with prevalence (so E is false). 'SpPin' applies to highly specific tests for ruling in.
learningPoint: SnNout: a Sensitive test that is Negative rules OUT disease; sensitivity/specificity are prevalence-independent, but PPV/NPV are not.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Research & Statistics (diagnostic tests)

stem: A screening test for a condition has a sensitivity of 90% and specificity of 90%. The condition has a prevalence of 1% in the screened population. If applied to 10,000 people, which statement BEST describes the positive predictive value (PPV)?

options.label: A
options.text: PPV is low (around 8%) because at low prevalence false positives greatly outnumber true positives

options.label: B
options.text: PPV is around 90% because sensitivity and specificity are both high

options.label: C
options.text: PPV is around 99% because specificity is high

options.label: D
options.text: PPV is independent of prevalence and equals the sensitivity

options.label: E
options.text: PPV cannot be calculated without knowing the incidence rate

explanation: With 10,000 people and 1% prevalence: 100 have disease, 9,900 do not. True positives = 90% x 100 = 90. False positives = 10% (1 - specificity) x 9,900 = 990. PPV = TP / (TP + FP) = 90 / (90 + 990) = ~8.3%. Despite excellent sensitivity and specificity, low prevalence means most positives are false positives. This is the key reason screening rare conditions yields many false alarms, and why PPV is prevalence-dependent (unlike sensitivity/specificity).
learningPoint: At low prevalence, even a 90/90 test has a poor PPV because false positives swamp true positives - prevalence drives PPV.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Research & Statistics (diagnostic tests)

stem: You are consenting a 55-year-old man for elective total hip replacement. He asks specifically about the risk of leg-length discrepancy and dislocation. Following the UK legal standard for consent established by Montgomery v Lanarkshire Health Board (2015), what is your PRIMARY obligation regarding disclosure of risk?

options.label: A
options.text: Disclose any material risk - one a reasonable person in this patient's position would attach significance to, or that this particular patient would - including those he specifically asks about

options.label: B
options.text: Disclose only risks that a responsible body of surgeons would routinely mention (the Bolam standard)

options.label: C
options.text: Disclose only risks with an incidence greater than 1%

options.label: D
options.text: Withhold rare risks to avoid causing the patient undue anxiety

options.label: E
options.text: Defer all risk discussion to the pre-operative nursing checklist

explanation: Montgomery shifted UK consent from the doctor-centred Bolam test to a patient-centred standard. The surgeon must disclose 'material' risks - those to which a reasonable person in the patient's position would attach significance, OR that the doctor knows (or should know) this particular patient would. Risks the patient specifically asks about must be addressed. Materiality is not defined by a numerical threshold (so C is wrong), and therapeutic privilege to withhold information is very narrow (D is wrong). Consent is a process led by an appropriately informed clinician, not delegated to a checklist.
learningPoint: Montgomery: disclose material risks judged by what a reasonable patient (or this patient) would value - not by a Bolam 'responsible body' or an arbitrary percentage.
references: Montgomery v Lanarkshire Health Board [2015] UKSC 11

stem: During a hip arthroplasty you recognise that you have implanted an acetabular component you believe is malpositioned, which may compromise the result. The patient is otherwise stable. In line with the UK statutory and professional duty of candour, what is the MOST appropriate action?

options.label: A
options.text: Inform the patient (or their representative) of what happened, apologise, explain the likely consequences and next steps, and document this

options.label: B
options.text: Say nothing unless the patient develops a complication, to avoid unnecessary worry

options.label: C
options.text: Discuss it only with colleagues at the next morbidity and mortality meeting

options.label: D
options.text: Document it in the notes but do not tell the patient unless directly asked

options.label: E
options.text: Arrange revision surgery silently and omit the error from the records

explanation: The professional (GMC) and statutory duty of candour requires that patients be told promptly when something goes wrong with their care that has caused, or may cause, harm. The clinician should give an honest account of what happened, offer an apology (apologising is not an admission of legal liability under the Compensation Act 2006), explain the consequences and proposed remedy, and document the discussion. Concealment or selective disclosure breaches both the GMC duty and CQC Regulation 20. Honest open disclosure is mandatory, not optional.
learningPoint: Duty of candour: be open and honest when things go wrong, apologise, explain next steps, and document - concealment breaches GMC and CQC Regulation 20.
references: CQC Regulation 20 (Health and Social Care Act 2008)

stem: A 19-year-old presents with a 6-week history of a painful, enlarging distal femoral mass. Radiographs show an aggressive metaphyseal lesion with periosteal reaction and a soft-tissue component. The patient is seen in a district general hospital. Regarding biopsy of this suspected primary bone sarcoma, which principle reflects current UK (BOA/British Sarcoma Group) best practice?

options.label: A
options.text: The biopsy should be performed at, or under the direction of, the specialist sarcoma centre that will deliver definitive treatment

options.label: B
options.text: An urgent excisional biopsy at the local hospital is preferred to obtain the most tissue

options.label: C
options.text: The biopsy tract orientation is unimportant as it will not affect later surgery

options.label: D
options.text: A transverse incision gives the best access and should be used

options.label: E
options.text: Definitive surgery can proceed locally once histology confirms sarcoma

explanation: Suspected primary bone or soft-tissue sarcomas must be referred to a specialist sarcoma multidisciplinary centre, and the biopsy should be carried out by (or under the direction of) the team that will perform the definitive resection. A poorly planned biopsy contaminates tissue planes and the tract must be excised en bloc with the tumour, so a badly placed or transversely oriented biopsy can convert a limb-salvageable tumour into an amputation. The incision must be longitudinal, in line with the planned resection, with meticulous haemostasis. Excisional biopsy of a sarcoma at a non-specialist unit is contraindicated.
learningPoint: Biopsy a suspected sarcoma only at the treating specialist centre, longitudinally and in line with the planned resection - a misplaced biopsy can cost the limb.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Orthopaedic Oncology (biopsy principles)

stem: A musculoskeletal oncologist describes a high-grade extracompartmental osteosarcoma of the distal femur with no detectable metastases. Using the Enneking (MSTS) surgical staging system for malignant bone tumours, how is this lesion classified?

options.label: A
options.text: Stage IIB

options.label: B
options.text: Stage IA

options.label: C
options.text: Stage IB

options.label: D
options.text: Stage IIA

options.label: E
options.text: Stage III

explanation: The Enneking/MSTS staging system for malignant musculoskeletal tumours combines grade (G), site (T) and metastasis (M). Stage I = low grade, Stage II = high grade, Stage III = any grade with regional or distant metastases. Within stages I and II, A = intracompartmental (T1) and B = extracompartmental (T2). A high-grade (G2), extracompartmental (T2), non-metastatic (M0) lesion is therefore Stage IIB. The presence of metastasis would make it Stage III regardless of grade or compartment.
learningPoint: Enneking (MSTS): I = low grade, II = high grade, III = metastatic; A = intracompartmental, B = extracompartmental - so high-grade extracompartmental M0 = IIB.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Orthopaedic Oncology (staging)

stem: Theme: Assessment and management of metastatic bone disease and pathological fractures. For each clinical scenario, select the SINGLE most appropriate next step from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Calculate the Mirels score to assess fracture risk

options.label: B
options.text: Prophylactic intramedullary nailing of the whole bone

options.label: C
options.text: Image the entire length of the affected long bone before any surgery

options.label: D
options.text: Biopsy the lesion before fixation if no primary malignancy is known

options.label: E
options.text: Urgent MRI of the whole spine

options.label: F
options.text: Refer to the regional sarcoma/oncology MDT

options.label: G
options.text: Commence intravenous bisphosphonate therapy

options.label: H
options.text: Reassure and discharge with no further imaging

explanation: Mirels' score (site, pain, lesion type, size, each scored 1-3) stratifies impending pathological fracture risk; a score >=9 indicates prophylactic fixation, 8 is borderline, <=7 can be managed non-operatively/with radiotherapy. A solitary destructive bone lesion in a patient with no known primary must NOT be assumed metastatic - it could be a primary sarcoma, so biopsy/MDT discussion precedes fixation (intramedullary nailing of a sarcoma seeds the whole bone). Metastatic spinal cord compression presenting with new severe back pain and neurology mandates urgent whole-spine MRI (NICE). Whole-bone imaging before surgery identifies additional deposits so the construct spans all lesions.
learningPoint: Solitary bone lesion + no known primary = biopsy/MDT before fixation; Mirels >=9 = prophylactic fixation; new back pain + neurology = urgent whole-spine MRI for MSCC.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Oncology (metastatic disease)

emiLeads.stem: A 68-year-old woman with known breast cancer has a painful 3 cm lytic lesion in the subtrochanteric femur causing pain on weight-bearing; you must decide whether prophylactic surgery is indicated.

A 60-year-old man with no cancer history is found to have a single aggressive destructive lesion in the proximal humerus on radiographs taken for shoulder pain; the on-call team proposes immediate nailing.
A 72-year-old man with metastatic prostate cancer develops new severe thoracic back pain over 48 hours with bilateral leg weakness and urinary hesitancy.
A patient with a Mirels score of 10 in the femoral diaphysis is listed for surgery; before theatre you want to ensure the construct will protect the entire bone.
