stem: A 74-year-old woman with metastatic breast cancer has a painful lytic lesion in the proximal femoral diaphysis. You calculate a Mirels score of 9. According to current UK guidance, what does this score indicate about management?

options.label: A
options.text: High risk of pathological fracture - prophylactic internal fixation is indicated, ideally before radiotherapy

options.label: B
options.text: Low risk - safe to manage with analgesia and observation alone

options.label: C
options.text: The lesion is benign and requires no oncological input

options.label: D
options.text: Radiotherapy alone is sufficient and surgery is contraindicated

options.label: E
options.text: The score predicts overall survival rather than fracture risk

explanation: The Mirels scoring system grades four variables - site, nature of pain (functional pain scores highest), radiographic appearance (lytic highest) and size relative to cortical diameter (>2/3 highest) - each 1 to 3, total 4 to 12. A score of >=9 carries a high fracture risk (historically around 33% or more) and warrants prophylactic fixation, ideally before radiotherapy (which can transiently weaken bone and impair healing). A score <=7 is low risk; 8 is borderline and individualised. The score estimates fracture risk, not survival, and management should be agreed with the oncology MDT.
learningPoint: Mirels >=9 = prophylactic fixation before radiotherapy; <=7 = non-operative; 8 = individualised - the score predicts fracture, not survival.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Oncology (impending fracture)

subtopic: History & examination of the painful hip — provocation testing
stem: A 31-year-old recreational footballer reports deep anterior groin pain brought on by deep squatting and prolonged sitting. On examination the hip is flexed to 90 degrees, then adducted and internally rotated, which sharply reproduces his groin pain. Which clinical sign has been elicited?

options.label: A
options.text: Positive FABER (Patrick) test

options.label: B
options.text: Positive Stinchfield test

options.label: C
options.text: Positive anterior impingement (FADIR) test

options.label: D
options.text: Positive Trendelenburg sign

options.label: E
options.text: Positive Thomas test

explanation: Flexion, adduction and internal rotation (FADIR) drives the femoral neck against the anterosuperior acetabular rim and labrum; reproduction of deep anterior groin pain is the classic anterior impingement sign — highly sensitive (though not specific) for femoroacetabular impingement and labral pathology. FABER (flexion-abduction-external rotation) loads the posterior hip and sacroiliac joint and tends to localise pain laterally/posteriorly. Stinchfield is a resisted straight-leg raise (intra-articular loading via iliopsoas). Trendelenburg assesses abductor competence, and the Thomas test unmasks a fixed flexion deformity. The provocation described — adduction with internal rotation in flexion — is FADIR.
learningPoint: FADIR (flexion-adduction-internal rotation) reproducing deep groin pain is the anterior impingement sign — sensitive for FAI/labral pathology but not specific.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Adult reconstruction: hip, history & examination
BOAST — Diagnosis and management of femoroacetabular impingement (FAI)

subtopic: History & examination of the painful hip — gait and abductor mechanism
stem: A 58-year-old woman is reviewed two years after a primary total hip replacement performed through a direct lateral (Hardinge) approach. She complains of a limp. When she stands on the operated leg alone, the pelvis drops on the opposite (swing) side, and during walking she lurches her trunk over the operated hip to compensate. Hip movement is pain-free and radiographs show a well-fixed, well-positioned implant with equal leg lengths. What is the most likely explanation for her sign?

options.label: A
options.text: Fixed adduction deformity of the operated hip

options.label: B
options.text: Iliopsoas tendonitis from anterior impingement on the cup

options.label: C
options.text: True leg lengthening of the operated limb

options.label: D
options.text: Abductor (gluteus medius/minimus) insufficiency on the operated side

options.label: E
options.text: L3 radiculopathy causing quadriceps weakness

explanation: A positive Trendelenburg sign — contralateral (swing-side) pelvic drop on single-leg stance, with a compensatory trunk lurch toward the stance (operated) hip — indicates abductor mechanism failure on the weight-bearing side. After a direct lateral (Hardinge) approach this is most often due to abductor insufficiency: superior gluteal nerve traction/denervation, failure of the abductor repair, or muscle/tendon deficiency. The hip is pain-free with a well-fixed implant and equal leg lengths, arguing against psoas impingement, loosening or a length problem. A fixed adduction deformity produces a different (apparent shortening) gait pattern, and L3 radiculopathy would cause quadriceps rather than abductor weakness.
learningPoint: Trendelenburg-positive gait (swing-side pelvic drop, stance-side trunk lurch) localises to abductor insufficiency on the weight-bearing hip — a recognised sequela of the direct lateral (Hardinge) approach.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Gait analysis & hip examination
BOA/NJR — Primary total hip replacement outcomes

subtopic: History & examination of the painful hip — special tests
stem: Theme: Clinical examination tests of the hip and surrounding girdle. For each clinical scenario below, select the SINGLE test that is being described or that would best confirm the suspected diagnosis. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Thomas test

options.label: B
options.text: FABER (Patrick) test

options.label: C
options.text: Trendelenburg test

options.label: D
options.text: Ober test

options.label: E
options.text: Stinchfield (resisted straight-leg raise) test

options.label: F
options.text: Log-roll test

options.label: G
options.text: FADIR (anterior impingement) test

options.label: H
options.text: Galeazzi test

emiLeads.stem: A patient with suspected fixed flexion deformity of the right hip; the contralateral hip is fully flexed to flatten the lumbar lordosis, and the affected thigh is then observed to rise off the couch.

With the patient supine, the examiner gently rolls the extended lower limb internally and externally. This is regarded as the most specific bedside test for an intra-articular source of hip pain because it moves only the femoral head within the acetabulum without stressing extra-articular structures.

A runner with lateral hip pain and a palpable snap; in the lateral decubitus position the upper hip is extended and abducted then released, and failure of the limb to fall into adduction indicates a tight iliotibial band/tensor.
A patient with groin pain in whom resisted active straight-leg raise reproduces the groin pain, supporting an intra-articular cause by loading the hip joint through iliopsoas.
Bedside assessment of abductor competence in a patient with suspected superior gluteal nerve palsy after a direct lateral approach hip replacement.

explanation: The Thomas test unmasks a fixed flexion deformity by abolishing compensatory lumbar lordosis. The log-roll test moves only the femoral head within the acetabulum, so it is the most specific (though insensitive) bedside test for intra-articular pathology. The Ober test detects iliotibial band/tensor contracture in the lateral position. The Stinchfield (resisted SLR) loads the joint through iliopsoas and suggests an intra-articular source when it reproduces groin pain. Trendelenburg assesses abductor (superior gluteal nerve) function. FABER points to the SI joint/posterior hip, FADIR to anterior impingement, and Galeazzi to limb-length inequality/hip dislocation in children.
learningPoint: Match the provocation to the anatomy: log-roll = most specific intra-articular test; Thomas = fixed flexion; Ober = ITB; Stinchfield = iliopsoas-loaded intra-articular; Trendelenburg = abductors.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Physical examination of the hip

subtopic: Hip imaging — radiographic measurement of dysplasia
stem: A 24-year-old woman with chronic groin pain has a standing AP pelvis radiograph. To assess for acetabular dysplasia, the angle is measured between a vertical line through the centre of the femoral head and a line from the head centre to the lateral edge of the sourcil (weight-bearing acetabular rim). Which statement about this lateral centre-edge angle (LCEA) of Wiberg is correct in current practice?

options.label: A
options.text: Values above 25 degrees indicate dysplasia

options.label: B
options.text: A value below 5 degrees is normal in adults

options.label: C
options.text: It is measured on a frog-lateral, not an AP, radiograph

options.label: D
options.text: A value below 20 degrees is consistent with acetabular dysplasia

options.label: E
options.text: It quantifies femoral head sphericity rather than acetabular cover

explanation: The lateral centre-edge angle of Wiberg, measured on a well-aligned standing AP pelvis, quantifies superolateral acetabular cover of the femoral head. Widely used thresholds are: normal 25-39 degrees; borderline dysplasia 20-25 degrees; frank dysplasia below 20 degrees. A high LCEA (above ~40 degrees) suggests over-coverage (a feature of pincer-type FAI/coxa profunda), not dysplasia. The angle reflects acetabular coverage, not head sphericity (assessed by the alpha angle and head-neck offset), and it is an AP-radiograph measurement. An LCEA below 20 degrees is therefore the value consistent with dysplasia.
learningPoint: Lateral centre-edge angle (Wiberg) on a standing AP pelvis: <20 degrees = dysplasia, 20-25 borderline, 25-39 normal, >40 over-coverage (pincer).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hip imaging & adult dysplasia

stem: A 55-year-old man with a large-head metal-on-metal total hip replacement reports new groin discomfort. Whole-blood cobalt and chromium ion levels are raised above the MHRA threshold. To investigate for an adverse reaction to metal debris and a pseudotumour while minimising implant-related artefact, which imaging investigation is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Standard (non-MARS) 3-Tesla MRI of the pelvis

options.label: B
options.text: MRI with metal artefact reduction sequences (MARS)

options.label: C
options.text: Technetium-99m bone scintigraphy

options.label: D
options.text: Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) of the hip

options.label: E
options.text: Plain AP pelvis radiograph alone

explanation: In a symptomatic metal-on-metal hip with raised cobalt/chromium ions, MHRA guidance directs cross-sectional soft-tissue imaging to detect adverse reactions to metal debris (ARMD) and pseudotumours. MRI using metal artefact reduction sequences (MARS) — or ultrasound where MRI is unavailable or contraindicated — is the standard, because conventional MRI is degraded by susceptibility artefact around the implant and may miss or distort periprosthetic soft-tissue masses. Bone scintigraphy and DEXA do not characterise the soft-tissue ARMD lesion, and a plain radiograph cannot show a fluid-filled pseudotumour. MARS MRI (or US) is therefore the investigation of choice.
learningPoint: Symptomatic MoM hip with raised metal ions → MARS MRI (or ultrasound) to detect ARMD/pseudotumour, per MHRA surveillance guidance.
references: MHRA — Medical device alert: All metal-on-metal (MoM) hip replacements (updated guidance)
Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Bearing surfaces & metal-on-metal complications

stem: A 27-year-old male ice-hockey player has activity-related anterior groin pain and a positive FADIR test. A radial-reformat MRI of the hip is obtained to quantify femoral head-neck junction asphericity. The angle between the femoral neck axis and a line from the head centre to the point where the head contour first exits a best-fit circle is measured at 68 degrees. Which abnormality does this finding best characterise?

options.label: A
options.text: Pincer-type femoroacetabular impingement

options.label: B
options.text: Acetabular dysplasia

options.label: C
options.text: Cam-type femoroacetabular impingement

options.label: D
options.text: Femoral neck stress fracture

options.label: E
options.text: Slipped capital femoral epiphysis, acute

explanation: The angle described is the alpha angle (Nötzli). It measures asphericity of the femoral head-neck junction: an increased alpha angle (commonly cited as >55-60 degrees, with thresholds varying by sequence and view) indicates a cam deformity — a bony prominence at the anterosuperior head-neck junction that jams into the acetabulum in flexion/internal rotation, shearing the chondrolabral junction. Pincer impingement is an acetabular over-coverage problem (high LCEA, crossover sign) and does not raise the alpha angle. Dysplasia is under-coverage. A value of 68 degrees with a typical cam history therefore indicates cam-type FAI.
learningPoint: An increased alpha angle (>~55-60 degrees) on radial MRI/cross-table view defines a cam deformity — femoral-sided over-growth driving anterosuperior impingement.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Femoroacetabular impingement

subtopic: Femoroacetabular impingement — pincer morphology and management
stem: A 39-year-old female pilates instructor has groin pain. Her standing AP pelvis shows the anterior acetabular wall line crossing the posterior wall line in the upper acetabulum, and the ischial spine projects medial to the pelvic brim. There is no joint-space narrowing. Which combination best explains these radiographic signs and the underlying mechanism of her impingement?

options.label: A
options.text: Crossover sign indicating a cam lesion causing femoral-sided impingement

options.label: B
options.text: Crossover and ischial spine signs indicating acetabular retroversion causing pincer-type impingement

options.label: C
options.text: Lateral centre-edge angle below 20 degrees indicating dysplasia

options.label: D
options.text: Tönnis angle increase indicating lateral acetabular under-coverage

options.label: E
options.text: Break in Shenton's line indicating subtle hip subluxation

explanation: The crossover (figure-of-8) sign — the anterior wall line crossing the posterior wall line proximally — together with the ischial spine sign and a positive posterior-wall sign are radiographic markers of acetabular retroversion, which causes anterosuperior over-coverage and pincer-type FAI. The retroverted rim contacts the femoral neck early, crushing the labrum and producing a contre-coup posteroinferior chondral injury. A cam lesion is a femoral-side abnormality (alpha angle), not a wall-crossover. A low LCEA or increased Tönnis angle indicates dysplastic under-coverage (the opposite pattern), and a broken Shenton's line indicates subluxation/dysplasia. Hence retroversion-driven pincer impingement.
learningPoint: Crossover + ischial spine + posterior wall signs = acetabular retroversion → pincer FAI (over-coverage), the opposite radiographic picture to dysplasia.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Femoroacetabular impingement (pincer/retroversion)

subtopic: Adult acetabular dysplasia — indications for periacetabular osteotomy
stem: A 26-year-old woman has symptomatic acetabular dysplasia (lateral centre-edge angle 14 degrees) with activity-related groin pain. She is being counselled about a Bernese (Ganz) periacetabular osteotomy (PAO). Which factor is the most important determinant of a good outcome and therefore most strongly favours offering a joint-preserving PAO rather than arthroplasty?

options.label: A
options.text: Established Tönnis grade 3 osteoarthritis with subchondral cysts

options.label: B
options.text: An open triradiate cartilage

options.label: C
options.text: A markedly incongruent, aspherical joint requiring abduction to reduce

options.label: D
options.text: A congruent joint with preserved (Tönnis grade 0-1) articular cartilage and a closed triradiate cartilage

options.label: E
options.text: Body mass index above 40 kg/m squared

explanation: The Bernese (Ganz) PAO reorients the acetabulum to improve femoral head coverage and reduce contact stress in symptomatic dysplasia. The strongest predictor of durable benefit is a congruent, concentrically reducible joint with well-preserved cartilage (Tönnis grade 0-1); advanced osteoarthritis (Tönnis 2-3) predicts early conversion to arthroplasty and is a relative contraindication. The triradiate cartilage must be closed before a PAO, so an open physis precludes it (a Salter/triple osteotomy is used in the skeletally immature). Joint incongruence that needs abduction to reduce, and morbid obesity, both worsen results. A congruent joint with preserved cartilage and a closed triradiate cartilage is therefore the favourable factor.
learningPoint: PAO works best in a young patient with a congruent, reducible joint, minimal arthritis (Tönnis 0-1) and a closed triradiate cartilage; advanced OA is a relative contraindication.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Adult acetabular dysplasia & pelvic osteotomy

subtopic: Adult acetabular dysplasia — biomechanics and pathology
stem: A 30-year-old woman with developmental acetabular dysplasia develops increasing groin pain. Which statement best explains why the dysplastic hip is prone to early degeneration and labral pathology?

options.label: A
options.text: Acetabular over-coverage causes linear rim impaction of the labrum in flexion

options.label: B
options.text: A femoral cam lesion shears the anterosuperior chondrolabral junction

options.label: C
options.text: Deficient lateral acetabular coverage concentrates load over a small contact area, overloading the labrum and superolateral rim cartilage

options.label: D
options.text: Posterior wall deficiency causes recurrent posterior dislocation

options.label: E
options.text: Femoral retroversion blocks internal rotation and impinges anteriorly

explanation: In acetabular dysplasia the shallow, under-covered acetabulum (low LCEA, increased Tönnis angle, often increased femoral anteversion) transmits the joint reaction force through a reduced contact area at the deficient superolateral rim. The hypertrophic labrum is recruited to bear load and becomes overloaded, leading to labral tears, paralabral cysts and accelerated superolateral chondral wear — the typical degeneration pattern of dysplasia. Over-coverage rim impaction describes pincer FAI; a cam lesion is the femoral mechanism of cam FAI; and posterior wall deficiency/retroversion describe different entities. The under-coverage overload mechanism is correct.
learningPoint: Dysplasia = under-coverage: load is concentrated over a small superolateral contact area, overloading a hypertrophic labrum and causing early superolateral chondral wear.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hip dysplasia pathomechanics

subtopic: Osteonecrosis of the femoral head — Ficat staging and imaging
stem: A 42-year-old man on long-term corticosteroids for inflammatory bowel disease has 3 months of groin pain. Plain AP pelvis radiographs are normal. T1-weighted MRI shows a serpiginous low-signal line demarcating a segment of the anterosuperior femoral head, with preserved head sphericity and no subchondral collapse. Using the Ficat and Arlet classification, which stage best fits these findings?

options.label: A
options.text: Ficat stage 0

options.label: B
options.text: Ficat stage II

options.label: C
options.text: Ficat stage III

options.label: D
options.text: Ficat stage I

options.label: E
options.text: Ficat stage IV

explanation: In the Ficat and Arlet system, stage I osteonecrosis has normal or near-normal radiographs but a positive MRI — typically the serpiginous double-line/demarcation sign on T1/T2 — with the femoral head still spherical and no subchondral fracture. Stage 0 is the asymptomatic, radiograph- and MRI-silent hip (often the contralateral side). Stage II shows radiographic sclerosis/cysts but still a preserved spherical head (no crescent/collapse). Stage III is post-collapse with the crescent sign and head flattening, and stage IV shows secondary acetabular osteoarthritis. A normal radiograph plus a positive MRI demarcation line with a spherical head is therefore stage I.
learningPoint: Ficat I = symptomatic hip, normal radiograph but MRI-positive (demarcation/double-line sign), head still spherical and uncollapsed.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Osteonecrosis of the femoral head (Ficat-Arlet staging)
BOA — Osteonecrosis (avascular necrosis) of the hip

subtopic: Osteonecrosis of the femoral head — core decompression vs arthroplasty
stem: A 38-year-old man has corticosteroid-associated osteonecrosis of the right femoral head. MRI shows a medium-sized anterolateral necrotic lesion involving roughly 25 per cent of the head, with NO subchondral collapse and a spherical, congruent head (pre-collapse disease). He has mechanical groin pain. Which intervention is most appropriate to attempt joint preservation?

options.label: A
options.text: Primary total hip arthroplasty

options.label: B
options.text: Core decompression (with or without bone grafting/biologic augmentation)

options.label: C
options.text: Hip resurfacing arthroplasty

options.label: D
options.text: Intra-articular corticosteroid injection and continued observation only

options.label: E
options.text: Girdlestone excision arthroplasty

explanation: Joint-preserving surgery is appropriate in early, PRE-COLLAPSE osteonecrosis (Ficat I-II / pre-collapse) with a small-to-medium lesion and a still-spherical head. Core decompression — often with bone grafting or biologic augmentation — aims to reduce intraosseous pressure, relieve pain and delay or prevent collapse and conversion to arthroplasty; outcomes are best precisely in this pre-collapse, modest-lesion group. Once the head has collapsed (crescent sign/flattening) or there is secondary acetabular arthritis, core decompression fails and total hip arthroplasty is indicated. Resurfacing is less suitable with extensive head necrosis; observation alone neglects an at-risk head; Girdlestone is a salvage procedure. Core decompression is therefore the joint-preserving choice here.
learningPoint: Pre-collapse osteonecrosis with a small/medium lesion and a spherical head → core decompression (± grafting/biologics); post-collapse or secondary OA → total hip arthroplasty.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Management of femoral head osteonecrosis
BOA — Osteonecrosis of the hip (joint preservation vs arthroplasty)

subtopic: Transient osteoporosis of the hip — diagnosis and imaging
stem: A woman in the third trimester of pregnancy develops atraumatic left groin pain and an antalgic limp over several weeks. Plain radiographs show subtle osteopenia of the femoral head and neck with a preserved joint space and no subchondral fracture line. MRI shows diffuse bone-marrow oedema throughout the femoral head AND extending into the neck, with a joint effusion and no focal subchondral demarcation line. Which diagnosis is most likely?

options.label: A
options.text: Ficat stage I osteonecrosis of the femoral head

options.label: B
options.text: Septic arthritis of the hip

options.label: C
options.text: Subcapital femoral neck stress fracture

options.label: D
options.text: Transient osteoporosis of the hip (bone-marrow oedema syndrome)

options.label: E
options.text: Pigmented villonodular synovitis

explanation: Transient osteoporosis of the hip (transient bone-marrow oedema syndrome) classically affects middle-aged men and women in the third trimester of pregnancy. MRI shows DIFFUSE, homogeneous marrow oedema extending from the head into the neck and intertrochanteric region, with an effusion but WITHOUT a focal subchondral demarcation line or segmental low-signal lesion — the feature that distinguishes it from osteonecrosis, where oedema surrounds a focal necrotic segment with a double-line sign. It is self-limiting and resolves over 6-12 months. Septic arthritis would give systemic upset and joint destruction; a stress fracture shows a focal fracture line; PVNS shows nodular low-signal synovium with blooming. The diffuse oedema pattern in pregnancy is transient osteoporosis.
learningPoint: Transient osteoporosis = diffuse head-and-neck marrow oedema with effusion but NO focal subchondral line; self-limiting, classic in third-trimester pregnancy and middle-aged men.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Transient osteoporosis / bone-marrow oedema syndrome
BOA — Atraumatic hip pain in adults (differential diagnosis)

subtopic: Transient osteoporosis of the hip — natural history and management
stem: A 45-year-old man is diagnosed with transient osteoporosis of the hip after MRI shows diffuse femoral head and neck marrow oedema with no subchondral fracture. He has significant pain on weight-bearing. Which statement about the natural history and management is most accurate?

options.label: A
options.text: It is irreversible and progresses to femoral head collapse in the majority, so early total hip arthroplasty is indicated

options.label: B
options.text: It is self-limiting and usually resolves within 6-12 months; protected weight-bearing and analgesia are the mainstays, with a recognised insufficiency-fracture risk during the symptomatic phase

options.label: C
options.text: Urgent core decompression is mandatory to prevent osteonecrosis

options.label: D
options.text: It is caused by infection and requires prolonged intravenous antibiotics

options.label: E
options.text: Immediate unrestricted high-impact loading accelerates recovery

explanation: Transient osteoporosis of the hip is self-limiting: symptoms and the MRI marrow-oedema signal typically resolve spontaneously within about 6-12 months. Management is supportive — protected weight-bearing (crutches), activity modification and analgesia; the demineralised femoral neck carries a real, if uncommon, risk of an insufficiency/fragility fracture during the painful phase, so protected loading also has a protective rationale. Some clinicians use bisphosphonates or other adjuncts, but it does not require arthroplasty, core decompression or antibiotics, and it does not characteristically progress to collapse. Hence the self-limiting, protected-weight-bearing answer with awareness of fracture risk is correct.
learningPoint: Transient osteoporosis is self-limiting (resolves in 6-12 months); treat with protected weight-bearing and analgesia, and remember the insufficiency-fracture risk while the neck is demineralised.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Transient osteoporosis of the hip
BOA — Management of atraumatic hip pain

subtopic: Hip imaging — selecting the right investigation
stem: A 29-year-old woman has persistent mechanical groin pain with catching and a positive FADIR test. Standing AP pelvis and lateral hip radiographs are unremarkable. You suspect an acetabular labral tear. Which is the most appropriate next imaging investigation to confirm the labral pathology?

options.label: A
options.text: Whole-body bone scintigraphy

options.label: B
options.text: DEXA scan of both hips

options.label: C
options.text: Magnetic resonance arthrography (MR arthrogram) of the hip

options.label: D
options.text: Repeat plain radiographs in 6 months

options.label: E
options.text: Non-contrast CT of the pelvis

explanation: When radiographs are normal but the history and FADIR test suggest an intra-articular labral tear, magnetic resonance arthrography is the investigation of choice: intra-articular gadolinium distends the joint and outlines the chondrolabral junction, markedly improving detection of labral tears and cartilage delamination compared with plain MRI, and it can be combined with an intra-articular local anaesthetic challenge to confirm an intra-articular pain source. (High-resolution 3T non-contrast MRI is an increasingly used alternative where available.) CT excels at bony morphology and FAI surgical planning but is poor for the labrum; bone scintigraphy and DEXA do not assess the labrum; and watchful repeat radiography delays diagnosis. MR arthrography is therefore the best answer.
learningPoint: Suspected labral tear with normal radiographs → MR arthrogram (± intra-articular local anaesthetic) is the most sensitive test for the chondrolabral junction (high-field 3T MRI is an emerging alternative).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hip imaging (MR arthrography & labral pathology)

stem: A 68-year-old retired teacher has a 3-year history of right groin pain radiating to the knee, now waking her at night and limiting her walking distance to 200 metres. Radiographs show superolateral joint-space loss, subchondral sclerosis, osteophytes and a subchondral cyst. She has completed a structured exercise programme and a course of simple analgesia with weight-loss advice over the past 9 months without lasting benefit. According to current NICE guidance, which factor should be used to decide whether to offer her a total hip replacement?

options.label: A
options.text: Her body mass index must fall below 30 before surgery can be offered

options.label: B
options.text: Referral should be based on the degree of radiographic change rather than her symptoms

options.label: C
options.text: The decision should be based on the effect of symptoms on her quality of life, function and pain — not on age, sex, smoking or BMI in isolation

options.label: D
options.text: She must first fail a hip arthroscopy before joint replacement is considered

options.label: E
options.text: Surgery should be deferred until she is unable to walk indoors, as earlier replacement increases revision risk

explanation: NICE NG226 (osteoarthritis) recommends that referral for joint replacement be based on a discussion of the balance between the impact of the patient's symptoms (pain, function, quality of life) and the risks of surgery, and explicitly states that patient-specific factors such as age, sex, smoking, comorbidity and BMI should not be barriers to referral. Decisions are symptom- and function-led, not driven by the radiographic grade in isolation (severe radiographic OA can be relatively asymptomatic and vice versa). There is no requirement to reduce BMI below a fixed threshold before referral, although modifiable risk factors are optimised peri-operatively. Hip arthroscopy has no role in the management of established hip OA. Deferring until housebound worsens outcomes and is not advised.
learningPoint: Offer THA for hip OA on the basis of symptom severity, function and quality of life — not age, BMI or radiographic grade as standalone barriers (NICE NG226).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Adult Reconstruction: total hip arthroplasty
NICE NG226 Osteoarthritis in over 16s: diagnosis and management (2022)

subtopic: THA indications — avascular necrosis / younger patient
stem: A 34-year-old man on long-term corticosteroids for inflammatory bowel disease presents with bilateral groin pain. MRI shows a serpiginous double-line sign in both femoral heads. On the right there is a crescent sign with early articular collapse and head flattening (Ficat-Arlet stage III); the left head shows a subchondral lesion involving roughly 20% of the head with no collapse and a preserved joint space (stage II). Which statement best guides management of this patient?

options.label: A
options.text: Both hips should undergo immediate total hip replacement because of his steroid dependence

options.label: B
options.text: The right hip (post-collapse) is best treated with total hip arthroplasty, while the pre-collapse left hip may be considered for a joint-preserving procedure such as core decompression

options.label: C
options.text: Core decompression is the procedure of choice for the collapsed right hip and will reliably reverse the femoral head flattening

options.label: D
options.text: Hip resurfacing is the preferred reconstruction for the right hip because of his age

options.label: E
options.text: Bisphosphonates will restore the collapsed right femoral head and surgery should be avoided

explanation: In osteonecrosis, the single most important determinant of treatment is whether the femoral head has collapsed. Pre-collapse lesions (Ficat-Arlet I–II, especially small/medial) may be amenable to joint-preserving surgery such as core decompression (with or without bone grafting/biologic adjuncts), which aims to relieve pain and delay progression but cannot reverse established collapse. Once the articular surface has collapsed (stage III–IV) the joint-preserving options fail and arthroplasty is indicated; in a young patient a well-fixed uncemented THA with a modern ceramic-on-highly-crosslinked-polyethylene (or ceramic-on-ceramic) bearing is the usual reconstruction. Metal-on-metal resurfacing has largely fallen out of favour for AVN because of poor necrotic bone quality on the femoral side and MoM wear concerns (MHRA). Bisphosphonates do not restore a collapsed head.
learningPoint: In femoral head osteonecrosis, collapse is the watershed: pre-collapse → consider core decompression; post-collapse → arthroplasty.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Osteonecrosis of the femoral head
MHRA Medical Device Alert — metal-on-metal hip implants

stem: Theme: surgical approaches to the hip and structures at risk. For each clinical scenario, select the single most likely structure that has been injured or is most at risk. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Sciatic nerve

options.label: B
options.text: Lateral femoral cutaneous nerve

options.label: C
options.text: Superior gluteal nerve

options.label: D
options.text: Femoral nerve

options.label: E
options.text: Medial femoral circumflex artery

options.label: F
options.text: Ascending branch of the lateral femoral circumflex artery

options.label: G
options.text: Profunda femoris (deep femoral) artery

options.label: H
options.text: Obturator nerve

emiLeads.stem: After a primary total hip replacement through a posterior approach with lengthening of the limb to correct a fixed adduction deformity, the patient cannot dorsiflex the foot and has numbness over the dorsum of the foot.

A surgeon performing a direct anterior approach develops troublesome bleeding while elevating tensor fasciae latae and dividing a transverse vessel crossing the interval between sartorius/rectus femoris and tensor fasciae latae, just distal to the inter-muscular plane.

Eighteen months after a Hardinge (direct lateral) approach the patient has a persistent Trendelenburg gait and abductor weakness despite an intact, well-healed abductor repair on imaging, attributed to over-zealous proximal splitting of the abductor mass.

During a posterior approach, a deep retractor placed over the anterior acetabular wall at the 3 o'clock position (right hip) to aid exposure risks injuring a structure lying directly on the iliopsoas/anterior capsule.

A trochanteric osteotomy approach with aggressive posterior/superior dissection close to the obturator externus tendon threatens the dominant blood supply to the femoral head, relevant when this approach is used for a young adult joint-preserving procedure.

explanation: Posterior-approach sciatic nerve injury is most often due to limb lengthening or retraction, and the peroneal (common fibular) division — lateral, with fewer/larger funiculi and a tethered course at the fibular neck — characteristically fails first, giving foot drop and dorsal foot numbness. The direct anterior approach uses the Smith-Petersen interval; the ascending branch of the lateral femoral circumflex artery crosses the distal interval and must be ligated/cauterised (the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve is the nerve at risk superficially, but this lead specifies a transverse vessel needing division). Over-zealous proximal splitting in the Hardinge approach denervates the abductors via the superior gluteal nerve (which runs ~3–5 cm above the trochanteric tip), producing a lasting Trendelenburg lurch despite an intact repair. An anterior acetabular retractor at the 3 o'clock position can compress/injure the femoral nerve lying on iliopsoas. The medial femoral circumflex artery (deep branch) is the dominant blood supply to the adult femoral head and is endangered by posterosuperior dissection near obturator externus in surgical hip dislocation/joint-preserving approaches.
learningPoint: Map each hip approach to its signature structure at risk: posterior → sciatic (peroneal division), DAA → LFCN + ascending LFCA, Hardinge → superior gluteal nerve, anterior retractor → femoral nerve, surgical dislocation → MFCA.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Surgical approaches to the hip
Hoppenfeld — Surgical Exposures in Orthopaedics: the hip

subtopic: Surgical approaches — posterior approach and dislocation risk
stem: A surgeon routinely uses the posterior (Moore/Southern) approach for primary total hip replacement and wishes to minimise the higher dislocation rate historically associated with this approach. Which single intra-operative measure has the strongest evidence for reducing posterior-approach dislocation?

options.label: A
options.text: Routinely using a 22 mm femoral head to maximise range of motion

options.label: B
options.text: Meticulous repair of the short external rotators and posterior capsule to the greater trochanter (posterior soft-tissue repair)

options.label: C
options.text: Placing the acetabular component in 30 degrees of retroversion

options.label: D
options.text: Dividing the abductors to improve acetabular exposure

options.label: E
options.text: Leaving the limb 2 cm short to relax the soft tissues

explanation: The posterior approach is muscle-sparing for the abductors and gives excellent exposure, but historically carried a higher dislocation rate because the posterior capsule and short external rotators are taken down. Robust capsular and external-rotator repair back to the greater trochanter (a 'posterior soft-tissue repair', e.g. through trans-osseous drill holes) reduces the posterior dislocation rate to levels comparable with other approaches and is the measure with the best supporting evidence. Larger (not smaller) femoral heads improve stability by increasing jump distance and head-neck ratio. Acetabular retroversion promotes posterior instability and impingement. Dividing the abductors would damage the abductor mechanism and risk a Trendelenburg gait without improving stability. Limb shortening reduces soft-tissue tension and offset and increases instability.
learningPoint: Posterior soft-tissue (capsule + short external rotator) repair is the key step that brings posterior-approach THA dislocation rates down to those of other approaches.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Total hip arthroplasty: approaches and instability
National Joint Registry (NJR) Annual Report — surgical approach and revision for instability

subtopic: Cemented vs uncemented fixation — elderly patient
stem: An 82-year-old woman with osteoporotic, Dorr type C ('stovepipe') proximal femoral bone is listed for an elective primary total hip replacement for end-stage osteoarthritis. Drawing on National Joint Registry data and current best practice, which femoral fixation strategy is most appropriate and why?

options.label: A
options.text: A cemented stem, because in older patients with poor bone quality it has a lower risk of early periprosthetic fracture and lower early revision than uncemented fixation

options.label: B
options.text: An uncemented stem, because press-fit fixation is always superior in the elderly

options.label: C
options.text: An uncemented stem, because cement is contraindicated over the age of 80

options.label: D
options.text: Either, but uncemented fixation reduces the risk of bone cement implantation syndrome to zero

options.label: E
options.text: A cemented stem only if the patient has rheumatoid arthritis

explanation: In older patients, particularly women with wide, osteoporotic 'stovepipe' (Dorr C) femora, NJR and other registry data consistently show that cemented femoral fixation has a lower risk of early periprosthetic femoral fracture and lower early revision than uncemented stems, where the unsupported press-fit may split thin cortices. Uncemented fixation is not universally superior — its advantage is mainly in younger patients with good metaphyseal bone. Cement is not contraindicated by age; indeed registry trends and guidance favour cement in the frail elderly and in hip-fracture hemiarthroplasty. Bone cement implantation syndrome (BCIS) is a recognised risk of cementing but is mitigated by good cementing technique and anaesthetic vigilance, not eliminated by going uncemented. Diagnosis (RA vs OA) is not the determinant here — bone quality and age are.
learningPoint: Older patients with poor (Dorr C) bone do better with cemented femoral fixation: lower early periprosthetic fracture and revision rates per NJR.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Femoral fixation in THA
National Joint Registry (NJR) Annual Report — fixation and age-related outcomes

subtopic: Cemented fixation — bone cement implantation syndrome
stem: An 84-year-old woman is undergoing cemented hemiarthroplasty for an intracapsular hip fracture. Moments after the femoral stem is inserted onto pressurised cement, her end-tidal CO2 falls sharply, her oxygen saturation drops and her systolic blood pressure plummets. Which combination of measures best reflects appropriate prevention and the underlying mechanism of bone cement implantation syndrome (BCIS)?

options.label: A
options.text: BCIS is purely an allergic reaction to methylmethacrylate monomer; treat with adrenaline and avoid all future cement

options.label: B
options.text: BCIS is driven by embolisation and the haemodynamic effects of cementation; risk is reduced by thorough lavage, retrograde cement insertion, adequate venting/suction and good communication with the anaesthetist before cementing

options.label: C
options.text: BCIS only occurs with uncemented implants and is prevented by using cement

options.label: D
options.text: BCIS is best prevented by inserting the stem as forcefully and quickly as possible to beat the cement cure

options.label: E
options.text: BCIS is a benign, self-limiting cosmetic phenomenon requiring no anaesthetic input

explanation: Bone cement implantation syndrome presents around the time of cementation/prosthesis insertion or reduction with hypoxia, hypotension, a fall in end-tidal CO2 (reflecting embolic obstruction of pulmonary flow), arrhythmias and occasionally cardiac arrest. The accepted mechanism is multifactorial — intramedullary pressurisation forcing fat, marrow and cement debris into the venous system (embolisation), together with effects of MMA monomer and the systemic cardiovascular response; it is not a simple monomer allergy. Prevention follows published safety guidance: identify high-risk patients, thorough pulsatile lavage of the canal to remove debris, a suction/venting catheter to lower intramedullary pressure, retrograde cement insertion with a gun, avoiding excessive pressurisation, and crucially warning the anaesthetist immediately before cementing so resuscitation can be anticipated. It occurs with cement (not uncemented) implants. Rapid forceful insertion increases, not decreases, embolic load.
learningPoint: BCIS is an embolic/haemodynamic event of cementation — reduce risk with lavage, venting/suction, retrograde gun technique and a pre-cement 'time-out' with the anaesthetist.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Bone cement and cementing technique
AAGBI/safety guideline on bone cement implantation syndrome in hip surgery

stem: A cemented polished, double-tapered collarless femoral stem (a 'force-closed' design) achieves long-term fixation by a different mechanism from a roughened, composite-beam ('shape-closed') cemented stem. Which statement best describes the intended mechanical behaviour of a polished tapered cemented stem within its cement mantle?

options.label: A
options.text: It is designed to bond rigidly to the cement so that no relative movement ever occurs at the stem–cement interface

options.label: B
options.text: It is designed to subside slightly within the cement mantle, generating radial compressive (hoop) forces that maintain fixation (taper-slip)

options.label: C
options.text: It achieves fixation solely by bony ingrowth onto a porous surface

options.label: D
options.text: It relies on a collar resting on the calcar to prevent any axial load transfer to the cement

options.label: E
options.text: It requires a roughened surface and a precoat to function, otherwise it will fail by debonding

explanation: Cemented stems fall into two philosophies. Composite-beam ('shape-closed') stems are roughened/precoated and intended to bond to the cement, behaving as a single unit with the cement and bone — debonding is a failure mode. Taper-slip ('force-closed') stems are highly polished, collarless and double-tapered; they are designed to subside a controlled amount within the cement mantle, and as the taper engages it generates radial compressive (hoop) stresses on the cement and bone that maintain fixation and resist further subsidence. This is the intended mechanism, not a failure, and such stems have excellent long-term registry survivorship. A polished taper-slip stem deliberately lacks a calcar collar so that load is transferred through the taper; adding a precoat/roughening to a tapered stem (the 'worst of both worlds') has historically been associated with high failure. Bony ingrowth describes uncemented, not cemented, fixation.
learningPoint: Polished, collarless double-tapered cemented stems work by controlled subsidence ('taper-slip/force-closed'), generating hoop stresses — distinct from roughened composite-beam ('shape-closed') stems that must stay bonded.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Cemented femoral stem design
National Joint Registry (NJR) Annual Report — cemented stem survivorship

stem: A 59-year-old man has a large-diameter (50 mm head) metal-on-metal total hip replacement implanted 9 years ago. He now reports new groin pain and a sensation of the hip 'giving way'. Examination is unremarkable. In line with MHRA guidance on metal-on-metal hip implants, which investigation pathway is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Reassure and discharge, as metal-on-metal bearings do not require surveillance

options.label: B
options.text: Measure whole-blood cobalt and chromium ion levels and perform cross-sectional imaging (MARS MRI or ultrasound) to look for a pseudotumour / adverse reaction to metal debris

options.label: C
options.text: Arrange a plain radiograph only; if there is no loosening, no further action is needed

options.label: D
options.text: Proceed directly to revision without any imaging or blood tests

options.label: E
options.text: Check serum urate and inflammatory markers, as the likely diagnosis is gout

explanation: MHRA medical device alerts on metal-on-metal hips require ongoing surveillance, particularly for large-head MoM and resurfacing devices, which are at higher risk of adverse reaction to metal debris (ARMD). A symptomatic patient (new groin pain, instability) warrants whole-blood (or serum) cobalt and chromium ion measurement together with cross-sectional imaging — metal-artefact-reduction sequence (MARS) MRI or ultrasound — to detect soft-tissue masses (pseudotumours), fluid collections and muscle/tendon destruction that plain films miss. Elevated or rising metal ions (a level around 7 µg/L / 7 ppb is an investigation trigger, not an absolute cut-off) and/or a significant pseudotumour guide the decision to revise. Plain radiographs alone are inadequate because ARMD soft-tissue disease occurs with well-fixed components. Revising without work-up is inappropriate; this is not gout.
learningPoint: A symptomatic metal-on-metal hip needs cobalt/chromium ion levels plus MARS MRI or ultrasound to detect ARMD/pseudotumour — plain films are not enough (MHRA).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Bearing surfaces and adverse reaction to metal debris
MHRA Medical Device Alert — All metal-on-metal (MoM) hip replacements: updated advice

stem: A 62-year-old man with a conventional (non-crosslinked) ultra-high-molecular-weight polyethylene acetabular liner implanted 14 years ago is found on surveillance radiographs to have an eccentric femoral head within the cup and expansile, well-demarcated peri-acetabular lucencies, with a well-fixed shell. Which statement best explains the underlying biological process?

options.label: A
options.text: Submicron polyethylene wear particles are phagocytosed by macrophages, triggering release of pro-inflammatory mediators (e.g. TNF-alpha, IL-1, IL-6) and RANKL-mediated osteoclast activation, causing particle-induced osteolysis

options.label: B
options.text: The lucencies represent infection until proven otherwise and mandate immediate two-stage revision

options.label: C
options.text: Osteolysis here is caused by metal ions, since polyethylene is biologically inert and never causes a reaction

options.label: D
options.text: The findings indicate stress shielding from an excessively stiff cemented cup

options.label: E
options.text: Larger polyethylene particles (>50 micrometres) are the most biologically active and drive the osteoclastic response

explanation: Eccentric head position signals polyethylene wear, and expansile peri-prosthetic lucencies around a well-fixed implant are the radiographic hallmark of particle-induced (aseptic) osteolysis. The mechanism is biological: submicron, phagocytosable polyethylene wear debris is engulfed by macrophages, which then secrete pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-1, IL-6, PGE2) and, critically, up-regulate the RANK/RANKL pathway, driving osteoclast differentiation and peri-implant bone resorption. The most biologically active particles are in the 0.1–1 micrometre range — small enough to be phagocytosed — not large particles. Polyethylene is not biologically inert in particulate form; this is the dominant historical cause of late aseptic loosening, and highly-crosslinked polyethylene was developed specifically to reduce wear-particle generation. Infection can cause lytic change and should be excluded clinically and with markers, but the picture and chronology here are classic for wear-driven osteolysis.
learningPoint: Aseptic osteolysis is a macrophage-driven, RANKL-mediated response to phagocytosable (~0.1–1 µm) polyethylene wear particles — the rationale for highly-crosslinked polyethylene.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Wear, osteolysis and the biology of particle disease
National Joint Registry (NJR) — bearing surface and revision for aseptic loosening

subtopic: Bearing surfaces — wear and failure modes
stem: Theme: hip bearing surfaces and their characteristic problems. For each clinical scenario, select the single most likely bearing-related diagnosis or property from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Ceramic-on-ceramic bearing — audible squeaking

options.label: B
options.text: Ceramic component fracture

options.label: C
options.text: Adverse reaction to metal debris (pseudotumour) — metal-on-metal

options.label: D
options.text: Volumetric polyethylene wear with osteolysis — metal-on-conventional polyethylene

options.label: E
options.text: Stripe wear

options.label: F
options.text: Highly-crosslinked polyethylene — reduced wear but lower fracture toughness

options.label: G
options.text: Trunnionosis (taper corrosion)

emiLeads.stem: A 45-year-old with a ceramic-on-ceramic hip describes a reproducible high-pitched noise on rising from a chair and during certain arcs of walking. Radiographs show well-positioned, intact components and no loosening.

An active 50-year-old falls and immediately develops severe groin pain with a grinding sensation and inability to weight-bear, 4 years after a ceramic-on-ceramic THA. A radiograph shows fragmentation of a component with metallic-density debris in the joint.
A patient with a metal-on-metal resurfacing has rising cobalt and chromium ion levels and a fluid-filled soft-tissue mass on MARS MRI, with destruction of the abductors at revision.
A patient with a metal head on a conventional polyethylene liner, 15 years post-op, has an eccentric head on radiographs and expansile peri-acetabular lucency around well-fixed components.

A patient with a metal-on-polyethylene THA develops new groin pain and elevated metal ions, but the bearing couple is not metal-on-metal; the problem is localised to corrosion at the head–neck junction of a modular stem.

explanation: These vignettes map the signature failure modes of each modern hip bearing. Ceramic-on-ceramic offers the lowest wear and is scratch-resistant/biologically inert, but can squeak (a fluid-film/edge-loading phenomenon influenced by component position and design) and, rarely, fracture catastrophically — an acute event with grinding, inability to weight-bear and ceramic/metallic-density debris on imaging, requiring urgent revision and thorough synovectomy. Metal-on-metal produces adverse reaction to metal debris (ARMD/pseudotumour) with raised cobalt/chromium ions and soft-tissue masses (MHRA surveillance). Metal-on-conventional-polyethylene fails late by volumetric wear and particle-induced osteolysis (eccentric head, expansile lucency). Trunnionosis is taper-junction corrosion that can mimic a metal-on-metal reaction (groin pain, raised ions) even when the articulating couple is metal-on-polyethylene, because the modular head–neck junction is the source. Stripe wear (edge contact during microseparation) is a recognised ceramic wear pattern but is not the best fit for these specific stems.
learningPoint: Know each bearing's signature pitfall: CoC → squeak/fracture, MoM → ARMD/pseudotumour, MoP(conventional) → osteolysis, modular taper → trunnionosis (raised ions without a MoM articulation).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Bearing surfaces: ceramic, metal and polyethylene
MHRA Medical Device Alert — metal-on-metal hip implants

stem: A manufacturer's highly-crosslinked polyethylene (HXLPE) acetabular liner is produced by irradiating UHMWPE at a high dose and then thermally treating it. Compared with conventional UHMWPE, which statement best describes the trade-off achieved by crosslinking, and the reason for the post-irradiation heat treatment?

options.label: A
options.text: Crosslinking markedly reduces adhesive/abrasive wear; remelting or annealing is performed to quench free radicals and limit oxidative embrittlement, at the cost of some reduction in fatigue/fracture toughness

options.label: B
options.text: Crosslinking increases wear but improves toughness, and heating is done purely to sterilise the liner

options.label: C
options.text: Crosslinking has no effect on wear; the heat treatment is what reduces wear

options.label: D
options.text: Crosslinking eliminates free radicals on its own, so no thermal treatment is ever required

options.label: E
options.text: Crosslinking improves both wear resistance and fracture toughness with no downside, which is why all liners are now maximally crosslinked

explanation: Gamma or electron-beam irradiation creates crosslinks between polyethylene chains, which substantially reduces adhesive and abrasive wear and therefore the volume of osteolysis-inducing wear particles. However, irradiation also generates free radicals that, if left, react with oxygen over time and cause oxidative degradation and embrittlement. Post-irradiation thermal treatment addresses this: remelting (above the melt transition) most completely quenches free radicals but slightly lowers crystallinity and mechanical strength, whereas annealing (below the melt) preserves more strength but leaves residual free radicals (later addressed by approaches such as vitamin-E stabilisation). The price of crosslinking is a measurable reduction in fatigue/fracture toughness, which is why rim fracture is a concern with thin, highly-crosslinked liners and why crosslink dose is balanced, not maximised. It is therefore a deliberate wear-versus-toughness trade-off — not a free lunch.
learningPoint: HXLPE trades a large reduction in wear for some loss of fracture toughness; post-irradiation remelting/annealing (or vitamin-E) is needed to quench free radicals and prevent oxidative embrittlement.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Polyethylene: crosslinking and oxidation
National Joint Registry (NJR) — bearing surface outcomes

stem: You are templating a total hip replacement on a calibrated digital AP pelvis radiograph for a patient with unilateral osteoarthritis and a true leg-length discrepancy (the affected leg is 1 cm short). Which sequence best represents correct pre-operative templating?

options.label: A
options.text: Template the femur first, ignore the contralateral hip, and select the cup size to match whatever stem is chosen

options.label: B
options.text: Confirm magnification/calibration, use the normal contralateral hip as a template, restore the acetabular centre of rotation, then template the stem to reproduce offset and leg length — planning the level of the neck cut to correct the discrepancy

options.label: C
options.text: Template only the cup, because femoral templating does not affect leg length

options.label: D
options.text: Always lengthen the operated leg by 2 cm to guarantee stability, regardless of the contralateral side

options.label: E
options.text: Templating is unnecessary if intra-operative shuck and the contralateral malleoli feel equal

explanation: Systematic templating starts with confirming the radiograph's magnification/calibration (using a marker of known size or calibration ball) because uncorrected magnification leads to component-size error. The non-arthritic contralateral hip is used as the planning template where possible. The acetabular component is planned first to restore the anatomical centre of rotation (medialising to the floor/teardrop as appropriate), then the femoral stem is templated to reproduce the patient's offset and to restore leg length — the planned femoral neck osteotomy level is the principal means of correcting a true discrepancy, supplemented by head length/offset options. The goal is to equalise leg lengths and restore offset, not to deliberately lengthen by a fixed amount (over-lengthening causes patient dissatisfaction, sciatic nerve traction and abductor problems). Intra-operative assessment (shuck/telescoping test, comparing malleoli, fixed bony landmarks) complements but does not replace templating.
learningPoint: Template the cup first to restore the centre of rotation, then the stem for offset and length — using the normal contralateral hip and a calibrated film, with the neck-cut level as the main leg-length lever.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pre-operative templating in THA
British Orthopaedic Association — best practice in elective hip replacement planning

stem: Six weeks after an uncomplicated primary total hip replacement, a patient complains that the operated leg feels longer and reports low back and ipsilateral knee discomfort when walking. Standing block testing and a calibrated AP pelvis confirm a 12 mm true lengthening on the operated side, with well-positioned, well-fixed components. There is no instability and the abductors are competent. What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.label: A
options.text: Urgent revision to shorten the femoral component

options.label: B
options.text: Reassurance, explanation, and a shoe raise on the contralateral side, with time for accommodation, reserving revision for persistent severe symptoms after non-operative measures

options.label: C
options.text: Manipulation under anaesthesia to reduce the leg length

options.label: D
options.text: Immediate exchange to a shorter modular head only, performed under local anaesthetic in clinic

options.label: E
options.text: Lengthening of the contralateral leg with an osteotomy to match

explanation: Perceived and true leg-length discrepancy is one of the commonest causes of dissatisfaction and litigation after THA. For a moderate discrepancy (roughly 1–2 cm) in a patient with otherwise well-positioned, stable, well-fixed components, the correct initial approach is non-operative: clear explanation and reassurance (many patients accommodate over months), physiotherapy, and a contralateral shoe raise to balance the pelvis and relieve secondary back/knee symptoms. Revision surgery to equalise length is technically difficult, risks instability and is reserved for a true, symptomatic discrepancy that fails to settle with conservative measures — it is not a first-line or emergency manoeuvre. Manipulation does not change bony length. An in-clinic head exchange under local anaesthetic is not a real option, and operating on the normal contralateral leg is inappropriate. Prevention via accurate templating is far preferable to correction.
learningPoint: Symptomatic post-THA lengthening of ~1–2 cm with sound components is managed first with explanation and a contralateral shoe raise; revision is a last resort.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Leg-length discrepancy after THA
British Orthopaedic Association — managing complications after hip replacement

stem: Which single factor is most strongly associated with an increased risk of dislocation after primary total hip replacement?

options.label: A
options.text: Use of a 36 mm femoral head rather than a 28 mm head

options.label: B
options.text: Acetabular component placed within the conventional 'safe zone' of inclination and anteversion

options.label: C
options.text: Revision surgery and prior hip surgery

options.label: D
options.text: Restoration of the patient's native femoral offset

options.label: E
options.text: A well-repaired posterior soft-tissue envelope

explanation: Dislocation risk is multifactorial and is usefully divided into patient, surgical and implant factors. Among the options, revision surgery (and any prior hip surgery) is the strongest risk factor here, owing to compromised soft tissues, scarring, abductor deficiency and altered anatomy — instability is one of the leading indications for, and complications of, revision THA. The other options are protective or neutral: larger heads (36 mm vs 28 mm) increase the head–neck ratio and jump distance and reduce dislocation; correct cup positioning within the safe zone, restoration of offset, and a well-repaired posterior capsule/external rotators all reduce instability. Additional important risk factors not listed include neuromuscular disease, cognitive impairment, the posterior approach without repair, female sex, and component malposition (excessive or deficient anteversion/inclination).
learningPoint: Previous/revision hip surgery is among the strongest risk factors for THA dislocation; larger heads, correct cup position, restored offset and posterior repair are protective.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Instability after THA
National Joint Registry (NJR) Annual Report — revision for instability/dislocation

stem: A 74-year-old woman who had a primary total hip replacement via a posterior approach 5 weeks ago presents to the emergency department after her hip 'popped' as she bent to put on a sock. The leg is short, internally rotated and adducted. Radiographs confirm a posterior dislocation with components that appear well-positioned and well-fixed. What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.label: A
options.text: Immediate open revision of the acetabular component

options.label: B
options.text: Closed reduction under sedation or general anaesthesia, confirmation of concentric reduction and stable range, then a period of precautions; investigate component position and address recurrent instability if it recurs

options.label: C
options.text: Application of a long-leg cast in the dislocated position

options.label: D
options.text: Conversion to a constrained liner at the first dislocation regardless of cause

options.label: E
options.text: Discharge with analgesia and outpatient follow-up without reduction

explanation: A first-time, early dislocation of a THA with well-positioned, well-fixed components is managed by prompt closed reduction under adequate sedation or anaesthesia (with muscle relaxation), followed by image confirmation of a concentric reduction and clinical assessment of the stable arc. The classic posterior dislocation posture is a short, internally rotated, adducted limb. After reduction, the patient is counselled on hip precautions (and may use an abduction brace) and the cause is investigated — component position (anteversion/inclination), offset, impingement and soft-tissue competence. The majority of first dislocations do not recur after closed reduction. Surgery (revision for malposition, larger head, dual-mobility or, in selected recurrent cases with abductor insufficiency, a constrained liner) is reserved for recurrent instability or a demonstrable mechanical cause — not the first episode by default. Casting in a dislocated position and discharge without reduction are unacceptable.
learningPoint: First-time early THA dislocation with sound components = closed reduction, confirm concentric/stable reduction, precautions, then investigate cause; reserve surgery for recurrence or malposition.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Management of THA instability
British Orthopaedic Association Standards (BOAST) — emergency management of the dislocated prosthetic hip

stem: A 79-year-old woman has had four dislocations of her total hip replacement over the past year. Examination and EMG confirm an absent, irreparable abductor mechanism following previous surgery. Component analysis (CT) shows the cup and stem are well-fixed and within acceptable orientation, with no significant impingement. Which reconstructive option is most appropriate for this pattern of recurrent instability?

options.label: A
options.text: A constrained acetabular liner, accepting that it captures the head at the cost of higher forces at the bone–implant and locking-mechanism interfaces

options.label: B
options.text: Simply upsizing to a 32 mm head with the existing components

options.label: C
options.text: Repeat closed reduction and stricter precautions only

options.label: D
options.text: Excision (Girdlestone) arthroplasty as the first-line solution

options.label: E
options.text: Insertion of a unipolar hemiarthroplasty head

explanation: When recurrent instability is due to an irreparable abductor/soft-tissue deficiency rather than component malposition or impingement, the soft-tissue 'restraint' is lost and a standard larger head or dual-mobility construct may be insufficient. A constrained acetabular liner mechanically captures the femoral head within the cup and is specifically indicated for recurrent dislocation with abductor insufficiency or an otherwise unsolvable soft-tissue problem when components are well-positioned. The trade-off is that constraint transmits higher stresses to the locking mechanism and the bone–implant interfaces, with risk of liner/locking-ring failure and loosening — so it is a salvage solution, not a routine first choice, and meticulous cup fixation/position is essential. Dual-mobility is an alternative in many recurrent cases but is less reliable when the abductors are absent. A 32 mm head is a small increment unlikely to solve gross instability. Girdlestone is a last resort. A unipolar hemiarthroplasty head is not a solution to instability of a THA.
learningPoint: Recurrent THA dislocation from irreparable abductor deficiency with well-positioned components is an indication for a constrained liner (or dual-mobility) — constraint captures the head but loads the fixation/locking mechanism.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Constrained liners and dual mobility for instability
National Joint Registry (NJR) — revision for recurrent dislocation

stem: While templating a total hip replacement, you note the patient has a high native femoral offset. To reproduce this offset and optimise hip stability and abductor function without changing leg length, which option is the most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Choose a high-offset (lateralised) stem option, which increases offset and abductor lever arm without altering vertical leg length

options.label: B
options.text: Use a longer femoral head (e.g. +8 mm) to increase offset, accepting the resulting lengthening of the limb

options.label: C
options.text: Reduce offset deliberately, because lower offset improves abductor strength

options.label: D
options.text: Increase acetabular inclination to compensate for femoral offset

options.label: E
options.text: Offset has no influence on stability and need not be reproduced

explanation: Femoral offset is the perpendicular distance from the centre of rotation of the head to the long axis of the femur. Restoring (or appropriately increasing) offset tensions the abductors, lengthens their lever arm — improving abductor strength and reducing limp — increases soft-tissue tension and the bony jump distance, and so enhances stability and reduces both dislocation and bearing wear (by reducing joint reaction force and impingement). Crucially, offset can be increased independently of leg length by selecting a high-offset/lateralised stem (a stem geometry with a more varus/laterally placed neck), or by using a longer/skirted head only when lengthening is also acceptable. Choosing a longer head increases both offset and length, so it is the wrong tool when length is already correct. Reducing offset weakens the abductors and reduces stability. Acetabular inclination changes do not substitute for femoral offset, and excessive inclination causes edge loading and instability.
learningPoint: Reproduce femoral offset with a high-offset/lateralised stem to restore the abductor lever arm and stability without lengthening the limb; head length changes offset and length together.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Femoral offset, abductor mechanics and stability in THA
British Orthopaedic Association — restoring hip biomechanics in arthroplasty

stem: A patient has recurrent posterior instability after THA. CT shows the acetabular component at 45 degrees inclination but only 5 degrees of anteversion, and the femoral stem at 25 degrees of anteversion, giving a combined anteversion at the lower end of the recommended range; there is also a relatively low head–neck ratio. Which explanation best accounts for the posterior instability, and what is the principle for correcting it?

options.label: A
options.text: Insufficient acetabular (and combined) anteversion promotes posterior dislocation through anterior impingement that levers the head out posteriorly; correction aims to restore adequate combined anteversion (roughly 25–50 degrees) and improve the head–neck ratio

options.label: B
options.text: Excessive anteversion is the cause, and reducing cup anteversion further will cure it

options.label: C
options.text: Inclination of 45 degrees is the sole cause and must be increased to 60 degrees

options.label: D
options.text: The head–neck ratio is irrelevant to impingement and stability

options.label: E
options.text: Posterior instability is unrelated to component version and depends only on the surgical approach

explanation: Stability depends on the combined (acetabular + femoral) anteversion, not either component in isolation — a useful working target is combined anteversion of roughly 25–50 degrees (the 'combined anteversion' concept; cup anteversion ~15–20 degrees with appropriate stem version). With the cup under-anteverted (only 5 degrees) the combined value sits too low, so in flexion and internal rotation the femoral neck impinges anteriorly on the acetabular rim/component, which levers the head out posteriorly — a classic mechanism of posterior dislocation despite an apparently acceptable inclination. The correction is to restore adequate combined anteversion (e.g. revise/reorient the cup or adjust stem version) and to reduce impingement and increase jump distance by improving the head–neck ratio (a larger head and/or a skirtless/reduced-neck design). Increasing inclination to 60 degrees would worsen edge loading and instability. A low head–neck ratio is directly relevant because it brings impingement on at smaller arcs of motion. Approach matters but does not override component version.
learningPoint: Stability is governed by combined anteversion (~25–50°): an under-anteverted cup causes anterior impingement and posterior dislocation; correct by restoring combined version and improving the head–neck ratio.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Component orientation, combined anteversion and impingement
National Joint Registry (NJR) — revision for instability and component malposition

stem: A 71-year-old woman is being worked up for revision of a loose acetabular component 14 years after primary THA. Preoperative radiographs and a CT scan show superolateral migration of the cup ('up and out') with ischial lysis and teardrop erosion, but Kohler's (the ilioischial) line is intact and there is no pelvic discontinuity. Intraoperatively the surgeon confirms moderate-to-severe bone loss with roughly 50% of the acetabulum deficient, yet the columns remain continuous and the rim is partially supportive, so a hemispherical shell with supplementary screws and a porous metal augment can be used. According to the Paprosky classification, which type does this most likely represent?

options.label: A
options.text: Type I

options.label: B
options.text: Type IIA

options.label: C
options.text: Type IIC

options.label: D
options.text: Type IIIA

options.label: E
options.text: Type IIIB

explanation: Paprosky IIIA describes severe bone loss with superolateral cup migration (classically 'up and out'), a compromised but intact rim supporting roughly 40-60% host-bone contact, ischial and teardrop lysis, and an INTACT Kohler's (ilioischial) line with columns that remain continuous. A IIIA defect is typically reconstructable with a hemispherical shell plus screws and an augment/structural graft. By contrast, Type IIIB shows superomedial migration ('up and in') with a breached Kohler's line, less than 40% host contact and a high risk of pelvic discontinuity, often requiring a cup-cage or custom triflange. Type II defects retain a more supportive rim with less migration; Type I has minimal lysis with a fully supportive rim. The discriminator between IIIA and IIIB here is the intact Kohler's line with a superolateral (not superomedial) migration pattern.
learningPoint: Paprosky IIIA = superolateral ('up and out') migration with an INTACT Kohler's line and ~40-60% host contact (reconstruct with shell + screws/augment); IIIB = superomedial ('up and in') migration with a BREACHED Kohler's line, <40% host contact and discontinuity risk.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Adult Reconstruction (Revision Hip)

stem: Theme: Reconstruction strategy in revision THA for bone loss. For each clinical scenario, select the SINGLE most appropriate reconstruction option from the list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Cemented all-polyethylene cup into host bone with no augment

options.label: B
options.text: Uncemented hemispherical shell with multiple screws alone

options.label: C
options.text: Uncemented hemispherical shell plus a porous metal augment

options.label: D
options.text: Cup-cage construct

options.label: E
options.text: Custom triflange acetabular component

options.label: F
options.text: Impaction bone grafting with mesh and a cemented cup

options.label: G
options.text: Proximal femoral replacement (megaprosthesis)

options.label: H
options.text: Cemented long-stem revision into intact distal femoral diaphysis

options.label: I
options.text: Uncemented modular fluted tapered stem

explanation: C: A superolateral segmental defect (Paprosky IIIA) with >50% remaining host bone and a partially supportive rim is well managed by a hemispherical shell supplemented by a porous metal augment to restore the deficient column/dome and provide rim contact. D: Chronic pelvic discontinuity with a IIIB defect needs a construct that bridges and offloads the discontinuity while ingrowth occurs - a cup-cage spans ilium to ischium while allowing a cemented liner within the cage. I: Paprosky IIIB femoral loss with a deficient metaphysis but intact diaphysis is ideally reconstructed with a modular fluted tapered (cone) stem gaining diaphyseal scratch-fit fixation. G: Massive proximal femoral bone loss (Paprosky IV / circumferential loss >10 cm) with no supportive bone for a stem requires a proximal femoral replacement, particularly in the elderly or oncological/periprosthetic-fracture setting. F: Contained cavitary defects in a younger patient where bone-stock restoration is desirable suit impaction grafting with mesh containment and a cemented cup.
learningPoint: Match the reconstruction to the defect: contained vs segmental, columns intact vs discontinuity, metaphysis vs diaphysis available - augments and cup-cages for the acetabulum, tapered fluted stems vs megaprosthesis for the femur.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Revision Hip Arthroplasty

emiLeads.stem: A 68-year-old man undergoing cup revision has a superolateral segmental defect with an intact posterior column and approximately 55% host-bone contact; the rim is partially supportive.
A 74-year-old woman has a confirmed chronic pelvic discontinuity at revision, with a large medial wall defect and Kohler's line breached.
On the femoral side of a revision, the metaphysis is non-supportive but there is more than 4 cm of intact, scratch-fit diaphysis distal to the defect.
A 79-year-old with a Vancouver B3 periprosthetic fracture has circumferential proximal femoral bone loss of 12 cm with no supportive metaphyseal or proximal diaphyseal bone.
A 52-year-old active patient needs acetabular revision for a contained cavitary defect where restoring bone stock for the future is a priority.

stem: A 66-year-old man presents with a 9-month history of progressive groin and thigh pain following THA performed 3 years ago. He has a discharging sinus over the lateral thigh. Inflammatory markers are elevated and two separate joint aspirations grow methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus. He is otherwise fit. He is keen to understand why his surgeon is recommending a particular surgical strategy rather than a single operation. Which feature in this case most strongly favours a two-stage revision over a single-stage (one-stage) revision?

options.label: A
options.text: The patient being systemically fit for surgery

options.label: B
options.text: The presence of a discharging sinus together with a resistant organism

options.label: C
options.text: The organism having been identified preoperatively

options.label: D
options.text: An adequate soft-tissue envelope around the hip

options.label: E
options.text: Availability of sensitive antibiotics for the cultured organism
