explanation: Relative indications favouring two-stage revision include an unknown organism preoperatively, a difficult-to-treat or resistant organism (e.g. MRSA), a discharging sinus or poor soft-tissue envelope, severe bone loss, immunocompromise and systemic sepsis. Conversely, single-stage revision is favoured when the organism is known and sensitive to available antibiotics, the patient is medically fit, the soft tissues and bone stock are good and there is no sinus. Here the sinus plus a resistant organism (MRSA) pushes towards a two-stage approach. The other options (fit patient, known organism, good soft tissues, sensitive antibiotics) are actually criteria that would SUPPORT a single-stage revision.
learningPoint: Sinus, resistant/unknown organism, poor soft tissues, severe bone loss and immunocompromise favour two-stage; a fit patient with a known sensitive organism, intact soft tissues and no sinus may suit single-stage revision.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Periprosthetic Joint Infection

stem: A 70-year-old woman has persistent pain 18 months after THA. There is no sinus. Serum CRP and ESR are mildly raised. To establish whether this is a periprosthetic joint infection before deciding on management, a hip aspiration is performed. Using the 2018 International Consensus Meeting (ICM) / MSIS-derived framework used in current UK practice, which single finding is regarded as a MAJOR (definitive) criterion that alone confirms infection?

options.label: A
options.text: Synovial fluid white cell count above the PJI threshold

options.label: B
options.text: Raised synovial fluid polymorphonuclear (neutrophil) percentage

options.label: C
options.text: Two positive periprosthetic cultures growing the same organism

options.label: D
options.text: A positive synovial alpha-defensin (lateral flow) test

options.label: E
options.text: Elevated synovial fluid C-reactive protein

explanation: In the ICM/MSIS-derived scoring system there are two MAJOR criteria, either of which is alone definitive for PJI: (1) two positive periprosthetic cultures growing the same (phenotypically identical) organism, and (2) a sinus tract communicating with the joint or prosthesis. All the other listed findings - elevated synovial WCC, raised PMN%, positive alpha-defensin and raised synovial CRP - are MINOR criteria that contribute points towards the diagnosis but are not individually definitive. This distinction matters because a single positive culture may be a contaminant, whereas two concordant cultures confirm infection. (Note the patient has no sinus, so the only major criterion available here is the two concordant cultures.)
learningPoint: The two MAJOR (definitive) PJI criteria are a sinus tract communicating with the joint and two positive cultures of the same organism; synovial WCC, PMN%, alpha-defensin and synovial CRP are minor (scored) criteria.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - PJI diagnosis

stem: A 59-year-old man has a large-head (40 mm) metal-on-metal total hip replacement performed 8 years ago. He is asymptomatic with a good Oxford Hip Score, and routine radiographs are unremarkable. As part of MHRA-mandated surveillance, whole-blood cobalt and chromium levels are checked and one value returns at 9 µg/L (ppb). According to current MHRA / BOA-BHS guidance, what is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Reassure and discharge from surveillance

options.label: B
options.text: Continue annual surveillance but take no further imaging action now

options.label: C
options.text: Repeat the blood metal ions and arrange cross-sectional imaging (MARS MRI or ultrasound)

options.label: D
options.text: List for urgent revision THA on the basis of the ion level alone

options.label: E
options.text: Stop all follow-up as he is asymptomatic

explanation: MHRA guidance sets a whole-blood cobalt or chromium threshold of 7 µg/L (7 ppb): levels above this are a 'cause for concern' and should prompt repeat testing plus cross-sectional imaging (metal artefact reduction sequence MRI, or ultrasound) to look for adverse reaction to metal debris / pseudotumour, regardless of symptoms. A single raised value above threshold does not by itself mandate revision; the decision to revise integrates symptoms, rising or persistently elevated ions, radiographs and imaging findings (e.g. soft-tissue mass, ALVAL/pseudotumour, osteolysis). Discharging or simply continuing surveillance without imaging would miss a potentially progressive ARMD. Large-head MoM THR (rather than resurfacing) carries higher risk and warrants closer scrutiny.
learningPoint: Whole-blood Co/Cr above 7 µg/L is the MHRA 'cause for concern' threshold - repeat ions and obtain cross-sectional imaging (MARS MRI/US); ion level alone does not dictate revision.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Bearing surfaces / MoM
MHRA Medical Device Alert: metal-on-metal hip replacements

subtopic: ALVAL / adverse reaction to metal debris
stem: A 62-year-old woman with a metal-on-metal hip resurfacing presents with new groin pain and a palpable mass. MARS MRI shows a cystic-solid periarticular pseudotumour. She proceeds to revision and the periprosthetic tissue is sent for histology. Which histological description best characterises the lesion classically termed ALVAL (aseptic lymphocyte-dominated vasculitis-associated lesion)?

options.label: A
options.text: Sheets of foamy macrophages laden with polyethylene particles with a giant-cell foreign-body reaction

options.label: B
options.text: Dense neutrophil infiltrate with microabscess formation and fibrin

options.label: C
options.text: Diffuse and perivascular lymphocytic infiltration with surface necrosis, in a delayed (type IV) hypersensitivity-type pattern

options.label: D
options.text: Caseating granulomata with Langhans giant cells

options.label: E
options.text: Spindle-cell proliferation with a storiform pattern and atypical mitoses

explanation: ALVAL reflects a delayed (type IV) hypersensitivity-type response to metal debris. Histology shows perivascular and diffuse lymphocytic (T-cell predominant) infiltration, plasma cells, high endothelial venules, surface necrosis/fibrin deposition and tissue destruction (despite the historical name, it is not a true vasculitis). By contrast, the wear-particle (macrophage-dominated) response to polyethylene is characterised by foamy histiocytes/macrophages and foreign-body giant cells (option A) - the more particle-load-dependent, innate response. Neutrophilic microabscesses (B) suggest infection; caseating granulomata (D) suggest TB; storiform spindle-cell atypia (E) suggests a soft-tissue sarcoma, an important radiological mimic of pseudotumour that must not be missed.
learningPoint: ALVAL = lymphocyte-predominant, perivascular, type IV hypersensitivity-type reaction to metal debris (vs the macrophage/giant-cell foreign-body reaction seen with polyethylene wear).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Adverse reaction to metal debris

stem: A 68-year-old man with a metal-on-polyethylene THR (cobalt-chromium femoral head on a titanium-alloy stem) develops new groin pain 6 years post-op. There is no infection. Blood tests show a markedly raised cobalt level but a comparatively modest chromium level (cobalt disproportionately higher than chromium). The bearing surface itself is metal-on-polyethylene. What is the most likely source of the metal debris?

options.label: A
options.text: Wear of the polyethylene liner

options.label: B
options.text: Corrosion at the head-neck (trunnion) taper junction

options.label: C
options.text: Backside wear of the acetabular liner

options.label: D
options.text: Fretting at the stem-cement interface

options.label: E
options.text: Systemic dietary cobalt absorption

explanation: Trunnionosis is mechanically-assisted crevice corrosion (fretting corrosion) at the modular head-neck taper. In a metal-on-polyethylene bearing the articulation itself generates little metal, so a rise in serum cobalt - characteristically with a DISPROPORTIONATELY high cobalt relative to chromium (a high Co:Cr ratio) - points to taper corrosion as the source, because the cobalt-chromium head corrodes preferentially releasing cobalt. This contrasts with bearing-surface MoM wear, which tends to elevate both ions more proportionately. Polyethylene wear and backside wear produce polyethylene (not metal) debris and osteolysis, and would not raise serum cobalt. Trunnionosis can cause an ARMD/pseudotumour identical to that seen with MoM bearings.
learningPoint: A disproportionately high serum cobalt (high Co:Cr ratio) in a metal-on-poly THR suggests trunnionosis - mechanically-assisted crevice corrosion at the head-neck taper.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Modularity and trunnionosis

stem: During a departmental audit of adverse reaction to metal debris in metal-on-polyethylene THRs, several cases of trunnionosis are identified. The team reviews implant and surgical factors thought to increase the risk of mechanically-assisted crevice corrosion at the head-neck junction. Which of the following is most strongly associated with an INCREASED risk of trunnion corrosion?

options.label: A
options.text: A shorter head-neck offset (a shorter, skirtless head)

options.label: B
options.text: A larger-diameter femoral head with a dissimilar-metal (mixed-alloy) coupling

options.label: C
options.text: A ceramic femoral head

options.label: D
options.text: A monoblock (non-modular) femoral stem

options.label: E
options.text: Use of a smaller (e.g. 28 mm) cobalt-chromium head

explanation: Factors increasing trunnionosis risk include larger-diameter metal heads (greater frictional torque and a longer moment arm at the taper), mixed-alloy couplings (e.g. a CoCr head on a titanium stem promoting galvanic/mechanically-assisted crevice corrosion), longer head-neck offsets/skirted heads, smaller or damaged trunnion geometry, and higher loads. A ceramic head substantially reduces taper corrosion (and is a recognised solution at revision). A truly monoblock stem has no modular taper, so cannot suffer trunnionosis. A smaller 28 mm head and a shorter offset reduce - not increase - the moment at the taper. Therefore a larger head with a dissimilar-metal coupling is the strongest risk factor listed.
learningPoint: Trunnionosis risk rises with large-diameter metal heads, mixed-alloy couplings and long/skirted offsets; ceramic heads and smaller diameters reduce taper corrosion.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Taper junctions and modularity

stem: A 64-year-old woman undergoes a primary THR for dysplastic osteoarthritis through a posterior approach. The procedure requires significant limb lengthening to restore leg-length symmetry. In recovery she is noted to have a foot drop with weakness of ankle dorsiflexion and toe extension, and altered sensation over the dorsum of the foot, but preserved plantarflexion and inversion. Which nerve, and which division, is most commonly affected in this scenario?

options.label: A
options.text: Sciatic nerve, tibial division

options.label: B
options.text: Sciatic nerve, common peroneal (fibular) division

options.label: C
options.text: Femoral nerve

options.label: D
options.text: Superior gluteal nerve

options.label: E
options.text: Obturator nerve

explanation: The sciatic nerve is the most commonly injured nerve in THA, and the common peroneal (fibular) division is the most vulnerable portion - it is more lateral, more tethered (at the fibular neck and sciatic notch), and has fewer/larger fascicles with less connective tissue cushioning, so it tolerates traction poorly. Excessive limb lengthening (a recognised risk, particularly in dysplasia where significant lengthening may be needed) and the posterior approach are classic risk factors. The clinical picture - weak dorsiflexion and toe extension with dorsal foot sensory loss but preserved plantarflexion/inversion - is a peroneal-division pattern. Femoral nerve injury (more common with anterior/direct anterior approaches or retractor placement over the anterior column) causes quadriceps weakness and loss of the knee jerk. Superior gluteal nerve injury causes abductor weakness/Trendelenburg, and obturator injury affects the hip adductors.
learningPoint: The sciatic nerve (peroneal division) is the most commonly injured nerve in THA; excessive lengthening and the posterior approach are key risks, and it presents as a foot drop with sparing of plantarflexion/inversion.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Complications of THA (nerve injury)

stem: Theme: Nerve injury after total hip arthroplasty. For each postoperative clinical picture, select the SINGLE most likely nerve or structure injured from the list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Femoral nerve

options.label: B
options.text: Sciatic nerve - common peroneal division

options.label: C
options.text: Sciatic nerve - tibial division

options.label: D
options.text: Superior gluteal nerve

options.label: E
options.text: Obturator nerve

options.label: F
options.text: Lateral femoral cutaneous nerve

options.label: G
options.text: Inferior gluteal nerve

options.label: H
options.text: Pudendal nerve

explanation: B: A foot drop with weak dorsiflexion/eversion and dorsal foot numbness after a posterior approach with lengthening is the peroneal division of the sciatic nerve - the most vulnerable. A: Quadriceps weakness with an absent knee jerk and anteromedial thigh/medial leg sensory change, typical after a direct anterior approach or anterior column retractor injury, is the femoral nerve. F: A purely sensory patch of numbness/dysaesthesia over the anterolateral thigh (meralgia paraesthetica) after a direct anterior approach is the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve. D: Persistent abductor weakness and a Trendelenburg gait without sensory loss, classically after a direct lateral (or trans-gluteal) approach where the nerve runs ~3-5 cm above the greater trochanter, is the superior gluteal nerve. H: Perineal/genital numbness and (rarely) erectile dysfunction after prolonged traction against the perineal post on the traction table is the pudendal nerve.
learningPoint: Localise post-THA nerve lesions by approach and pattern: peroneal (foot drop, posterior approach + lengthening), femoral (quads/knee jerk, anterior approach), LFCN (anterolateral thigh sensory, DAA), superior gluteal (Trendelenburg, direct lateral), pudendal (perineal, traction post).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - THA approaches and neurovascular risk

emiLeads.stem: Foot drop with weak ankle dorsiflexion and eversion and numbness over the dorsum of the foot after a posterior-approach THR that required 3.5 cm of lengthening.
Difficulty extending the knee, a diminished knee-jerk and numbness over the anteromedial thigh and medial leg after a direct anterior approach.
An isolated patch of burning numbness over the anterolateral thigh with no motor deficit following a direct anterior approach.
A persistent Trendelenburg gait with hip abductor weakness but no sensory disturbance after a direct lateral (Hardinge) approach.
Perineal and genital numbness noted after a prolonged hip procedure performed on a traction table with a perineal post.

stem: A 58-year-old man is reviewed 9 months after a THR performed through an extensile approach for a complex acetabular reconstruction. He complains of progressive stiffness. Pelvic radiographs show bone spurs arising from both the proximal femur and the pelvis with extensive near-bridging, leaving only a thin radiolucent gap of less than 1 cm between the opposing bone surfaces; the hip is not completely fused. Which Brooker grade best describes this appearance?

options.label: A
options.text: Grade I

options.label: B
options.text: Grade II

options.label: C
options.text: Grade III

options.label: D
options.text: Grade IV

options.label: E
options.text: The Brooker system does not apply to the hip

explanation: The Brooker classification of heterotopic ossification around the hip is radiographic: Grade I = islands of bone within the soft tissues; Grade II = bone spurs from the pelvis or proximal femur with at least 1 cm between opposing bone surfaces; Grade III = bone spurs with LESS than 1 cm between opposing surfaces (near-bridging but a gap remains); Grade IV = apparent complete bony ankylosis (bridging) of the hip. This patient has extensive near-bridging bone with only a thin lucent gap (<1 cm) and is not fused - this is Grade III. Complete ankylosis with no gap would be Grade IV.
learningPoint: Brooker HO grading: I = bone islands; II = spurs with >1 cm gap; III = spurs with <1 cm gap; IV = complete bony ankylosis.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Heterotopic ossification

stem: A 60-year-old man with prior heterotopic ossification around his contralateral hip, and ankylosing spondylitis, is due for THR. The team plans HO prophylaxis given his high-risk profile. Considering both efficacy and the specific concern of avoiding any compromise to bone ingrowth into his uncemented, porous-coated implant, which single prophylactic strategy is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: A 6-week course of high-dose oral corticosteroids

options.label: B
options.text: Indometacin (or another NSAID) for around 2-6 weeks postoperatively

options.label: C
options.text: A single fraction of low-dose radiotherapy directed at the entire acetabular component including the porous ingrowth surface

options.label: D
options.text: Long-term oral bisphosphonate therapy

options.label: E
options.text: Therapeutic anticoagulation with warfarin

explanation: The two evidence-based options for HO prophylaxis are a short postoperative course of an NSAID (classically indometacin, typically 2-6 weeks) and single-fraction low-dose radiotherapy (around 7-8 Gy, given shortly before or within 72 hours after surgery). Both inhibit the differentiation of mesenchymal cells into osteoblasts. The key nuance here is the uncemented porous implant: radiotherapy can impair bony ingrowth/osseointegration if the porous surface is included in the field, so either the ingrowth surfaces must be shielded or an NSAID is preferred - making indometacin the safest choice in this specific scenario. Corticosteroids and bisphosphonates are not standard HO prophylaxis (bisphosphonates only delay mineralisation, with rebound), and warfarin has no role in HO prevention.
learningPoint: NSAIDs (e.g. indometacin) and single-dose radiotherapy both prevent HO; with uncemented porous implants prefer an NSAID or shield the ingrowth surface, as radiotherapy can impair osseointegration.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Heterotopic ossification prophylaxis

stem: A 67-year-old woman with no significant comorbidity and no bleeding risk is admitted for an elective primary THR on an enhanced-recovery pathway. There are no contraindications to pharmacological thromboprophylaxis. According to current NICE guidance (NG89) for elective total hip replacement, which of the following regimens is an explicitly recommended pharmacological prophylaxis option?

options.label: A
options.text: Aspirin 75 mg alone for 14 days

options.label: B
options.text: LMWH for 10 days followed by aspirin (75-150 mg) for a further 28 days

options.label: C
options.text: No pharmacological prophylaxis; mechanical prophylaxis (intermittent pneumatic compression) alone for the full duration

options.label: D
options.text: Therapeutic-dose warfarin (target INR 2.5) for 35 days

options.label: E
options.text: A single prophylactic dose of LMWH on the day of surgery only

explanation: NICE NG89 offers, for elective THR, a choice of: (1) LMWH for 10 days followed by aspirin (75-150 mg) for a further 28 days; OR (2) LMWH for 28 days combined with anti-embolism stockings until discharge; OR (3) a direct oral anticoagulant (rivaroxaban, apixaban or dabigatran). Total pharmacological prophylaxis is therefore extended (around 28-35 days). Mechanical prophylaxis is used alongside, not instead of, pharmacological prophylaxis when there is no contraindication. Aspirin alone for only 14 days, IPC alone, therapeutic warfarin and a single peri-operative LMWH dose are not the recommended elective-THR regimens. Option B is one of the explicitly named NICE options.
learningPoint: NICE NG89 elective THR options: LMWH 10 days then aspirin 28 days; or LMWH 28 days + stockings; or a DOAC - extended (~28-35 day) pharmacological prophylaxis with mechanical methods alongside.
references: NICE NG89 - Venous thromboembolism in over 16s
Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - VTE prophylaxis

subtopic: VTE prophylaxis - contraindications and mechanical methods
stem: A 72-year-old man is admitted with a displaced intracapsular hip fracture and is scheduled for hemiarthroplasty. He is on no anticoagulants but had a haemorrhagic stroke 3 weeks ago, so pharmacological thromboprophylaxis is considered contraindicated at present. Renal function is normal. Which is the most appropriate thromboprophylaxis approach while the bleeding risk remains high?

options.label: A
options.text: Give treatment-dose LMWH regardless, as the fracture risk outweighs bleeding

options.label: B
options.text: Provide mechanical prophylaxis (e.g. intermittent pneumatic compression) and reassess for pharmacological prophylaxis once bleeding risk reduces

options.label: C
options.text: Withhold all forms of prophylaxis until fully mobile

options.label: D
options.text: Insert a prophylactic inferior vena cava filter as first-line prophylaxis

options.label: E
options.text: Start a direct oral anticoagulant at treatment dose immediately

explanation: When pharmacological VTE prophylaxis is contraindicated (here a very recent haemorrhagic stroke), NICE advises offering MECHANICAL prophylaxis - intermittent pneumatic compression or anti-embolism stockings - on admission, and switching to or adding pharmacological prophylaxis once the bleeding risk is reduced and pharmacological agents are no longer contraindicated. Hip fracture itself is a high-VTE-risk setting, so doing nothing (C) is inappropriate. Giving full-dose anticoagulation (A, E) in the face of a 3-week-old intracranial haemorrhage is dangerous. IVC filters are not first-line prophylaxis and are reserved for specific situations (e.g. proven proximal DVT with an absolute contraindication to anticoagulation), not routine prophylaxis. Mechanical prophylaxis with planned reassessment is the correct, guideline-concordant answer.
learningPoint: If pharmacological VTE prophylaxis is contraindicated, use mechanical prophylaxis (IPC/stockings) and reassess for pharmacological cover once bleeding risk falls; IVC filters are not routine prophylaxis.
references: NICE NG89 - VTE in over 16s (mechanical prophylaxis when pharmacological contraindicated)

stem: A 64-year-old presents with a 3-year history of medial-sided knee pain, worse on walking and at the end of the day, with occasional stiffness after rest lasting a few minutes. Examination shows a varus thrust on gait, a fixed flexion deformity of 10 degrees and crepitus. Which radiographic view is most important to demonstrate the true degree of joint space loss before deciding on management?

options.label: A
options.text: Supine non-weightbearing AP radiograph

options.label: B
options.text: Weightbearing (standing) AP radiograph, ideally with the knee in slight flexion (Rosenberg view)

options.label: C
options.text: Skyline (axial) patellofemoral view alone

options.label: D
options.text: Cross-table lateral radiograph

options.label: E
options.text: MRI of the knee

explanation: Cartilage loss is best demonstrated under load. A standing AP radiograph reproduces the functional joint space; a supine film can look falsely preserved because the limb is unloaded. A flexed weightbearing (Rosenberg/schuss) view loads the posterior femoral condyles where wear often begins and is more sensitive than an extended standing AP. MRI is not first-line for established OA assessment in UK practice.
learningPoint: Assess OA joint space on weightbearing films; supine views underestimate true cartilage loss.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Adult Reconstruction: knee osteoarthritis

stem: A 58-year-old labourer with isolated medial compartment knee osteoarthritis has been referred for consideration of surgery. According to current NICE guidance, which of the following should be optimised and offered as part of core (first-line) non-surgical management before arthroplasty is considered?

options.label: A
options.text: Therapeutic exercise, weight loss where appropriate and patient education/self-management

options.label: B
options.text: Routine intra-articular hyaluronic acid (viscosupplementation) injections

options.label: C
options.text: Long-term oral glucosamine and chondroitin supplements

options.label: D
options.text: Arthroscopic washout and debridement

options.label: E
options.text: Routine use of lateral wedge insoles for medial compartment OA

explanation: NICE NG226 defines core management of knee OA as therapeutic exercise (muscle strengthening and aerobic), weight management where overweight, and education to support self-management; these should be in place for all patients. NICE specifically recommends against intra-articular hyaluronan, against glucosamine/chondroitin, and against arthroscopic lavage/debridement. Lateral wedge insoles are not recommended for knee OA.
learningPoint: Core knee OA care = exercise + weight management + education; NICE advises against viscosupplementation, glucosamine and arthroscopic washout.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Adult Reconstruction: knee OA

stem: A 47-year-old keen recreational runner has isolated medial compartment osteoarthritis with a varus mechanical axis, a stable knee, a good range of movement and a normal patellofemoral and lateral compartment. A medial opening-wedge high tibial osteotomy is planned. Where should the corrected mechanical axis of the limb pass to offload the medial compartment appropriately?

options.label: A
options.text: Through the centre of the knee (50% of tibial plateau width)

options.label: B
options.text: Through the lateral compartment at approximately 62% of the tibial plateau width (Fujisawa point)

options.label: C
options.text: Through the medial tibial spine

options.label: D
options.text: Through the lateral edge of the tibial plateau (100%)

options.label: E
options.text: Through the medial tibial plateau at approximately 30% width

explanation: The aim of a varus-correcting HTO is to shift load from the worn medial compartment to the relatively preserved lateral compartment. The classic target is the Fujisawa point - roughly 62% of the tibial plateau width measured from medial to lateral - producing slight mechanical valgus (around 3-5 degrees). Aiming for the centre under-corrects and risks recurrence; gross over-correction overloads the lateral compartment.
learningPoint: Varus-correcting HTO aims the mechanical axis through the Fujisawa point (~62% plateau width) for slight valgus overcorrection.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Adult Reconstruction: osteotomy about the knee

stem: You are counselling a 50-year-old manual worker about a medial opening-wedge high tibial osteotomy for medial compartment osteoarthritis. Which single patient or knee characteristic is the strongest relative contraindication and would most reduce the chance of a successful outcome?

options.label: A
options.text: Tricompartmental osteoarthritis with significant patellofemoral and lateral compartment involvement

options.label: B
options.text: A varus deformity originating from the proximal tibia

options.label: C
options.text: Age under 60 years

options.label: D
options.text: A pre-operative knee flexion arc of 0 to 120 degrees

options.label: E
options.text: A body mass index of 27

explanation: HTO redistributes load to other compartments, so it relies on those compartments being relatively healthy. Significant lateral and/or patellofemoral arthritis is a key contraindication because offloading the medial side simply transfers pain to already-degenerate cartilage. Good indications include a younger, active patient, a metaphyseal (tibial) deformity, a stable knee with good motion, and a non-inflammatory single-compartment problem. A varus deformity arising from the proximal tibia is in fact an ideal indication, not a contraindication.
learningPoint: HTO needs a healthy 'receiving' compartment; tricompartmental or significant lateral/PFJ OA contraindicates it.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Adult Reconstruction: knee osteotomy

stem: A 68-year-old is being considered for a mobile-bearing medial unicompartmental knee arthroplasty. Which combination of findings represents the most appropriate indication according to current evidence and UK registry experience?

options.label: A
options.text: Anteromedial osteoarthritis with bone-on-bone medial wear, a functionally intact ACL, a correctable varus deformity and minimal patellofemoral symptoms

options.label: B
options.text: Medial joint space narrowing on a supine film with a deficient ACL and a fixed varus deformity

options.label: C
options.text: Inflammatory (rheumatoid) arthritis confined to the medial compartment

options.label: D
options.text: Medial compartment chondrocalcinosis with a fixed 20-degree flexion contracture

options.label: E
options.text: Partial-thickness medial cartilage loss with significant lateral compartment full-thickness wear

explanation: The classic Oxford-type indication is anteromedial OA: bone-on-bone medial wear (confirming full-thickness loss), a functionally intact ACL (so the wear pattern stays anteromedial and the tibia does not subluxate), a varus deformity that corrects passively (intact MCL, no fixed deformity), and a preserved lateral compartment. A deficient ACL, fixed deformity, inflammatory arthritis or significant lateral/bicompartmental disease point towards TKA. Modern registry data suggest isolated patellofemoral OA, except severe lateral facet wear, is not an absolute contraindication for mobile-bearing UKA.
learningPoint: Medial mobile-bearing UKA = anteromedial OA with intact ACL, correctable varus and a preserved lateral compartment.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Adult Reconstruction: unicompartmental knee arthroplasty

stem: When consenting a patient for a medial unicompartmental knee arthroplasty and comparing it with total knee arthroplasty, which statement most accurately reflects current National Joint Registry data and the published evidence?

options.label: A
options.text: UKA has a higher revision rate than TKA but lower peri-operative morbidity/mortality, better function and faster recovery

options.label: B
options.text: UKA has both a lower revision rate and a lower complication rate than TKA

options.label: C
options.text: UKA has the same revision rate as TKA but a higher infection rate

options.label: D
options.text: UKA has a higher revision rate than TKA mainly because of a much higher deep infection rate

options.label: E
options.text: UKA and TKA have identical functional outcomes and recovery times

explanation: NJR and trial data (e.g. TOPKAT) consistently show UKA has a higher revision rate than TKA, partly because the threshold to revise a UKA is lower and because of progression of arthritis or bearing problems. However, UKA offers lower peri-operative morbidity and mortality, better proprioception and function, a more natural-feeling knee, less blood loss and faster recovery. Higher surgical volume reduces the UKA revision rate. Deep infection is actually less common after UKA than TKA, so it is not the reason for the higher revision rate.
learningPoint: UKA: higher revision rate than TKA but lower morbidity, better function and quicker recovery (NJR/TOPKAT).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Adult Reconstruction: UKA

stem: A surgeon performing a primary total knee arthroplasty uses the classic mechanical alignment technique aiming for a neutral hip-knee-ankle axis. The distal femoral resection is made in slight valgus and the proximal tibia is cut perpendicular to its mechanical axis. The femoral component rotation is then set, commonly referencing the transepicondylar axis. What is the principal purpose of setting correct femoral component rotation?

options.label: A
options.text: To create a balanced, rectangular flexion gap and ensure normal patellar tracking

options.label: B
options.text: To restore the native joint line height

options.label: C
options.text: To determine the overall coronal (varus/valgus) alignment of the limb

options.label: D
options.text: To set the tibial slope

options.label: E
options.text: To control the extension gap balance

explanation: Femoral component rotation primarily governs the flexion gap shape and patellofemoral kinematics. External rotation of the femoral component (referenced to the transepicondylar axis, Whiteside's line, or by adding external rotation to the posterior condylar axis) opens the medial flexion gap to make it rectangular and balanced, and lateralises the trochlear groove to improve patellar tracking. Internal rotation of the femoral component is a classic cause of patellar maltracking and anterior knee pain. Coronal limb alignment is set chiefly by the distal femoral and proximal tibial cuts, not femoral rotation.
learningPoint: Femoral component rotation balances the flexion gap and drives patellar tracking; internal rotation causes maltracking.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Adult Reconstruction: TKA principles and alignment

stem: Theme: total knee arthroplasty implant design and constraint. For each clinical scenario, select the single most appropriate prosthesis design from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Cruciate-retaining (CR) total knee

options.label: B
options.text: Posterior-stabilised (PS) total knee

options.label: C
options.text: Mobile-bearing total knee

options.label: D
options.text: Constrained condylar (semi-constrained, CCK) knee

options.label: E
options.text: Rotating-hinge total knee

options.label: F
options.text: Medial unicompartmental knee

options.label: G
options.text: Patellofemoral arthroplasty

options.label: H
options.text: Fixed-bearing cruciate-substituting (ultracongruent) insert

emiLeads.stem: A primary TKA in which the surgeon intends to resect the PCL and rely on a femoral cam and tibial post to substitute for posterior cruciate function and improve roll-back.
A complex revision TKA with global ligamentous incompetence and an absent, non-functioning medial collateral ligament, where coronal stability cannot be restored by a semi-constrained implant.
A revision TKA with moderate flexion-extension gap mismatch and mild-to-moderate collateral insufficiency (a functioning but lax MCL) requiring varus-valgus constraint but not a hinge.
A primary TKA where the surgeon wishes to preserve the patient's own posterior cruciate ligament to maintain proprioception and femoral roll-back.

explanation: Constraint is escalated only as far as the soft tissues demand. CR retains a competent PCL; PS substitutes for a resected/incompetent PCL via a cam-and-post mechanism. A constrained condylar (CCK) implant provides coronal (varus-valgus) stability through a tall tibial post engaging a deep femoral box, used when collaterals are lax but at least partly functioning. A rotating hinge is reserved for global instability, an absent/non-functional MCL, gross bone loss or recurvatum where lesser constraint will fail. Choosing the least constraint that confers stability protects fixation and bone.
learningPoint: Match TKA constraint to soft-tissue competence: CR/PS for primaries, CCK for collateral laxity, hinge for global instability or absent MCL.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Adult Reconstruction: TKA design and constraint

stem: During a primary total knee arthroplasty for a varus knee, after making standard bony cuts you find the knee is tight medially and balanced laterally throughout both flexion and extension. The flexion and extension gaps are otherwise equal. What is the most appropriate next step to balance the knee?

options.label: A
options.text: Perform a graduated medial soft-tissue release (e.g. of the deep MCL and posteromedial structures)

options.label: B
options.text: Recut the distal femur to remove more bone

options.label: C
options.text: Upsize the polyethylene insert

options.label: D
options.text: Release the lateral collateral ligament and popliteus

options.label: E
options.text: Increase external rotation of the femoral component

explanation: A tight medial side that is symmetrical in flexion and extension reflects a tight medial soft-tissue sleeve, the typical pattern in a varus knee. The correct manoeuvre is a graduated (pie-crust or sequential) release of the contracted medial structures - deep MCL and posteromedial capsule, then superficial MCL fibres as needed - until the medial and lateral gaps are equal. Recutting bone or changing insert thickness affects both gaps equally and will not correct an asymmetric (medial-only) tightness; releasing the lateral side would over-loosen an already-balanced compartment. Adding femoral external rotation would only address an asymmetry confined to flexion, not one present in both flexion and extension.
learningPoint: Symmetric medial tightness in a varus TKA = sequential medial release, not a bony recut.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Adult Reconstruction: TKA soft-tissue balancing

stem: In a gap-balancing total knee arthroplasty the surgeon finds the extension gap is well balanced but the flexion gap is symmetrically loose (lax in flexion, stable in extension). Which single adjustment would best correct the flexion gap without disturbing the satisfactory extension gap?

options.label: A
options.text: Use a larger (more posterior) femoral component, or augment the posterior femoral condyles

options.label: B
options.text: Resect more distal femur

options.label: C
options.text: Use a thicker polyethylene insert

options.label: D
options.text: Resect more proximal tibia

options.label: E
options.text: Release the posterior capsule

explanation: The flexion gap is influenced chiefly by the AP size/position of the femoral component and the tibial cut; the extension gap is influenced by the distal femoral and tibial cuts. To tighten an isolated loose flexion gap, increase the femoral component size (or add posterior condylar augments) so more of the posterior femur fills the flexion gap, leaving the extension gap unchanged. A thicker insert or more tibial resection alters both gaps equally; resecting more distal femur loosens the extension gap. Posterior capsular release would only widen gaps further.
learningPoint: Isolated loose flexion gap -> upsize/augment the femoral component; insert and tibial changes move both gaps together.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Adult Reconstruction: gap balancing in TKA

stem: A patient is 8 weeks following an uncomplicated primary total knee arthroplasty. Despite engaging well with physiotherapy, the knee remains stiff with a flexion arc of only 0 to 75 degrees, and progress has plateaued. Radiographs show well-fixed, appropriately sized and rotated components with no signs of infection and a normal CRP. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Manipulation under anaesthesia

options.label: B
options.text: Continue physiotherapy and review at one year

options.label: C
options.text: Open arthrolysis and quadricepsplasty

options.label: D
options.text: Single-stage revision of the components

options.label: E
options.text: Arthroscopic debridement of the suprapatellar pouch

explanation: Stiffness after TKA with well-positioned, infection-free components and a plateau in physiotherapy progress is best addressed by manipulation under anaesthesia, which is most effective in the early window (typically within about 6-12 weeks, before mature arthrofibrosis sets in). Beyond around 3 months MUA is less effective and the gain in motion diminishes. Revision or open arthrolysis is reserved for established arthrofibrosis refractory to MUA or where a correctable mechanical cause (e.g. component malrotation, oversizing) is identified. Infection must always be excluded first.
learningPoint: Early stiff TKA with good components and excluded infection -> manipulation under anaesthesia within the first ~6-12 weeks.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Adult Reconstruction: TKA complications (arthrofibrosis)

stem: A 70-year-old presents 3 years after a total knee arthroplasty with several weeks of increasing pain, an effusion and a low-grade fever. The patient is haemodynamically stable and not septic. Prosthetic joint infection is suspected. Before any antibiotics are started, which single investigation is the most important to establish the diagnosis and guide treatment?

options.label: A
options.text: Image-guided (or aseptic) knee aspiration for synovial fluid cell count, differential and culture

options.label: B
options.text: Serum CRP and ESR

options.label: C
options.text: Plain radiographs of the knee

options.label: D
options.text: Empirical broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics

options.label: E
options.text: Technetium-99m bone scan

explanation: When prosthetic joint infection is suspected, joint aspiration is the key diagnostic step. Synovial fluid white cell count with neutrophil differential, plus culture (and often alpha-defensin/leucocyte esterase), confirms infection and crucially identifies the organism and its sensitivities to direct treatment. In a stable, non-septic patient antibiotics should be withheld until adequate cultures are obtained, because premature antibiotics compromise culture yield. CRP/ESR are supportive screening tests but are non-specific; imaging cannot reliably confirm infection.
learningPoint: Suspected PJI: aspirate the joint for cell count and culture before giving antibiotics; CRP/ESR only screen.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Adult Reconstruction: periprosthetic joint infection

stem: A 66-year-old reports a recurrent sense of the knee 'giving way' and difficulty descending stairs 9 months after a posterior-stabilised total knee arthroplasty. There is no infection. Examination shows the knee is stable in full extension but demonstrates anteroposterior laxity and mid-flexion sag at around 45-90 degrees of flexion. Radiographs show well-fixed components. What is the most likely cause of this instability?

options.label: A
options.text: Flexion gap that is larger than the extension gap (flexion instability)

options.label: B
options.text: Deep infection causing component loosening

options.label: C
options.text: Genu recurvatum from an oversized extension gap

options.label: D
options.text: Patella baja

options.label: E
options.text: Medial collateral ligament rupture

explanation: Symptomatic laxity in flexion (instability descending stairs, a feeling of giving way, AP laxity and sag in mid-to-deep flexion) with stability in extension is the classic picture of flexion instability - the flexion gap exceeds the extension gap. In a PS knee this can also allow the post to disengage (cam jump/dislocation). It is typically corrected at revision by re-establishing gap balance, for example upsizing/augmenting the femoral component or adjusting insert and component position. Recurvatum presents with hyperextension; an MCL rupture causes coronal (valgus) laxity, not AP laxity.
learningPoint: Giving way and AP laxity in flexion with a stable extended knee = flexion instability (flexion gap > extension gap).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Adult Reconstruction: TKA instability

stem: A 72-year-old has a confirmed chronic prosthetic joint infection of a total knee arthroplasty with a sinus tract, caused by a multi-resistant organism. The soft-tissue envelope is adequate and the patient is fit for staged surgery. According to standard UK practice, what is the most appropriate surgical strategy?

options.label: A
options.text: Two-stage revision: removal of all components and cement, debridement, an antibiotic-loaded cement spacer, organism-directed antibiotics, then reimplantation

options.label: B
options.text: Debridement, antibiotics and implant retention (DAIR) with exchange of the polyethylene insert

options.label: C
options.text: Single-stage (one-stage) exchange irrespective of organism

options.label: D
options.text: Long-term suppressive oral antibiotics with implant retention

options.label: E
options.text: Immediate above-knee amputation

explanation: For a chronic PJI with a sinus tract and a difficult (multi-resistant) organism, two-stage revision remains the gold standard in the UK: explant all components and cement, thorough debridement, insert an antibiotic-loaded spacer, give targeted antibiotics, then reimplant once infection is controlled. DAIR is reserved for acute (early post-operative or acute haematogenous) infections with well-fixed implants and a known sensitive organism within a short symptom window - a chronic infection with a sinus is outside its remit. Single-stage exchange can succeed in selected cases but typically requires a known, sensitive organism and good soft tissues, not a multi-resistant infection with a sinus.
learningPoint: Chronic PJI with sinus + resistant organism -> two-stage revision; reserve DAIR for acute infection with well-fixed implants and a sensitive organism.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Adult Reconstruction: PJI management

stem: During a planned aseptic revision total knee arthroplasty for tibial component loosening, there is a contained metaphyseal bone defect after implant removal but the diaphysis and a stable cortical rim remain. To achieve durable fixation, the surgeon plans to use the 'zones of fixation' concept. Which fixation strategy best applies this principle for the tibial component?

options.label: A
options.text: Use a stemmed component engaging the diaphysis combined with a metaphyseal cone/sleeve to fill the metaphyseal defect, with the joint surface managed by the baseplate

options.label: B
options.text: Cement the revision baseplate directly onto the sclerotic metaphyseal surface with no stem

options.label: C
options.text: Fill the defect with morselised cancellous allograft and a short cemented stem only

options.label: D
options.text: Use a primary cementless tibial component press-fit into the metaphysis

options.label: E
options.text: Convert to a rotating hinge regardless of ligament status to bypass the defect

explanation: The 'three zones of fixation' in revision TKA are the epiphysis/joint surface (zone 1), the metaphysis (zone 2) and the diaphysis (zone 3); robust reconstruction obtains fixation in at least two zones. Here, with a contained metaphyseal defect, a metaphyseal cone or sleeve restores zone 2 and provides biological/mechanical support, while a stem engaging the diaphysis adds zone 3 fixation and offloads the deficient metaphysis. Cementing onto deficient sclerotic bone alone, or using primary components, gives inadequate durable fixation. Constraint (hinge) is dictated by ligament competence, not by the bone defect itself.
learningPoint: Revision TKA fixation = at least two of three zones; combine a diaphyseal stem with a metaphyseal cone/sleeve for metaphyseal defects.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Adult Reconstruction: revision TKA, bone loss management

stem: Six months after a primary total knee arthroplasty in which the patella was not resurfaced, a patient complains of anterior knee pain and lateral subluxation of the patella on stairs. Intra-operatively the surgeon recalls the patella tended to track laterally. Which intra-operative factor is the most common technical cause of patellar maltracking after TKA and should be looked for first?

options.label: A
options.text: Internal rotation (and/or medialisation) of the femoral and/or tibial components

options.label: B
options.text: Excessive external rotation of the femoral component

options.label: C
options.text: Use of a cruciate-retaining rather than posterior-stabilised design

options.label: D
options.text: Over-resection of the distal femur

options.label: E
options.text: A posterior tibial slope of 3 degrees

explanation: Component rotational malposition is the leading technical cause of patellar maltracking. Internal rotation of the femoral component medialises the trochlear groove, and internal rotation/medialisation of the tibial component effectively lateralises the tibial tubercle relative to the trochlea - both increase the Q-angle and drive lateral patellar tracking, tilt and subluxation. The corollary at the primary operation is to externally rotate and avoid internal rotation/medialisation of components, lateralise the patellar button if resurfacing, and only then consider a lateral retinacular release. The fix for established symptomatic component malrotation is revision.
learningPoint: Internal rotation/medialisation of femoral and tibial components is the commonest technical cause of post-TKA patellar maltracking.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Adult Reconstruction: patellofemoral complications of TKA

stem: A patient is being consented for a primary total knee arthroplasty and asks about whether the kneecap will be 'replaced'. Regarding patellar resurfacing in primary TKA, which statement best reflects the current evidence and UK registry position?

options.label: A
options.text: Selective or routine resurfacing both have advocates; resurfacing reduces the risk of secondary surgery for anterior knee pain, and resurfacing is generally advised for inflammatory arthropathy

options.label: B
options.text: Resurfacing the patella is contraindicated in all patients because it increases the infection rate

options.label: C
options.text: Leaving the patella unresurfaced abolishes the risk of anterior knee pain

options.label: D
options.text: Resurfacing eliminates any possibility of future patellofemoral complications

options.label: E
options.text: Resurfacing significantly worsens overall knee function scores compared with not resurfacing

explanation: The resurfacing debate persists: meta-analyses show that resurfacing the patella reduces the rate of reoperation related to the patellofemoral joint/anterior knee pain, though many patients with a well-designed trochlea and good tracking do well unresurfaced (selective resurfacing). Resurfacing is generally favoured in inflammatory arthropathy (e.g. rheumatoid) and where there is significant patellar cartilage loss or maltracking. Resurfacing does not eliminate all patellofemoral complications (it introduces its own, such as fracture, loosening, or 'patellar clunk'), and anterior knee pain can occur with either strategy.
learningPoint: Patellar resurfacing lowers reoperation for anterior knee pain and is favoured in inflammatory arthritis; both routine and selective approaches remain valid.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Adult Reconstruction: patellar resurfacing

stem: A 55-year-old felt a sudden pop while stepping off a kerb and now cannot actively extend the knee. Examination shows a high-riding patella (patella alta), a palpable gap just distal to the patella and an inability to perform a straight-leg raise. The radiograph confirms patella alta. What is the most likely diagnosis and the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Patellar tendon rupture - early surgical repair

options.label: B
options.text: Quadriceps tendon rupture - early surgical repair

options.label: C
options.text: Patellar fracture - internal fixation

options.label: D
options.text: Patellar dislocation - closed reduction and bracing

options.label: E
options.text: Partial quadriceps strain - physiotherapy and analgesia

explanation: Loss of active extension with a palpable gap distal to the patella and a high-riding patella (patella alta) indicates a patellar tendon rupture; the unopposed quadriceps pulls the patella proximally. (By contrast, a quadriceps tendon rupture gives a gap above the patella and patella baja.) A complete extensor mechanism disruption in an active patient should be repaired early - ideally within the first 1-2 weeks before retraction and scarring - to restore active extension, followed by protected rehabilitation. Inability to straight-leg raise confirms extensor mechanism discontinuity.
learningPoint: Gap distal to the patella + patella alta + no active extension = patellar tendon rupture -> early surgical repair.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Sports Medicine: extensor mechanism injuries

stem: Theme: disorders of the extensor mechanism of the knee. For each clinical or radiographic description, select the single most likely diagnosis from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Quadriceps tendon rupture

options.label: B
options.text: Patellar tendon rupture

options.label: C
options.text: Transverse patellar fracture

options.label: D
options.text: Tibial tubercle avulsion fracture

options.label: E
options.text: Acute lateral patellar dislocation

options.label: F
options.text: Osgood-Schlatter disease

options.label: G
options.text: Patellar tendinopathy (jumper's knee)

options.label: H
options.text: Bipartite patella

emiLeads.stem: A 60-year-old diabetic on long-term steroids loses active knee extension after a stumble; there is a palpable gap immediately above the superior pole of the patella and the radiograph shows the patella sitting low (patella baja).

A 14-year-old footballer suddenly cannot extend the knee after forced jumping; a lateral radiograph shows an avulsed bony fragment at the proximal tibial apophysis displaced proximally with the patellar tendon.

A 25-year-old basketball player has chronic anterior knee pain localised to the inferior pole of the patella, worse with jumping and on resisted extension, with no loss of active extension and normal radiographs.

A 19-year-old felt the knee 'give way' with the patella visibly displacing laterally during a twisting movement; after spontaneous reduction there is tenderness over the medial patellofemoral ligament and a positive apprehension test.

explanation: Extensor mechanism problems localise by site and age. A gap above the patella with patella baja and loss of active extension is a quadriceps tendon rupture (typical in older patients, steroids, diabetes, renal disease). A proximal tibial apophyseal avulsion in an adolescent during jumping is a tibial tubercle avulsion fracture. Chronic inferior pole pain with preserved extension is patellar tendinopathy (jumper's knee). Lateral patellar displacement on a twisting injury with MPFL tenderness and apprehension is an acute lateral patellar dislocation. Distinguishing rupture level (above vs below the patella) and complete versus partial loss of extension is the key examined point.
learningPoint: Localise extensor mechanism injury by site/age: gap above patella + baja = quadriceps rupture; apophyseal avulsion in teens = tubercle avulsion; inferior pole pain with intact extension = tendinopathy.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Sports Medicine/Paediatrics: extensor mechanism and patellofemoral disorders

stem: A 24-year-old professional rugby player sustains an isolated ACL rupture and is listed for reconstruction. He is keen to return to elite contact sport as quickly as the biology allows, and the surgeon plans an autograft. Which statement best reflects the current evidence comparing bone–patellar tendon–bone (BPTB) and quadrupled hamstring (semitendinosus/gracilis) autografts in this population?

options.label: A
options.text: Hamstring autograft has a lower graft-rupture rate than BPTB in young pivoting athletes

options.label: B
options.text: BPTB autograft is associated with a lower re-rupture rate but a higher incidence of anterior knee pain and pain on kneeling

options.label: C
options.text: BPTB and hamstring grafts have identical donor-site morbidity profiles

options.label: D
options.text: Hamstring graft reliably restores native hamstring strength by three months, making it preferable for sprinters

options.label: E
options.text: BPTB autograft carries a higher risk of late tibial-tunnel widening than hamstring graft

explanation: In young, high-demand pivoting athletes the registry and meta-analytic data (including the NJR-linked literature and large cohorts) show BPTB autograft has a modestly lower graft-failure/re-rupture rate than four-strand hamstring graft, at the cost of greater donor-site morbidity — anterior knee pain, pain on kneeling and a small risk of patellar fracture or patellar tendinopathy. Hamstring harvest spares the extensor mechanism but produces persistent (often subclinical) deep-flexion and terminal-flexion weakness, can leave a saphenous (infrapatellar branch) sensory deficit, and is more prone to tunnel widening, not less. Hamstring strength does not reliably normalise by three months, and donor-site profiles of the two grafts are clearly different. The choice in an elite contact athlete is therefore a trade-off: BPTB favours graft durability, hamstring favours the extensor mechanism and kneeling comfort.
learningPoint: In young pivoting athletes, BPTB autograft offers a lower re-rupture rate than hamstring graft but more anterior knee pain and kneeling pain — graft choice is a durability-versus-donor-morbidity trade-off.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Sports Medicine: ACL reconstruction and graft selection
BASK/BOA guidance on ACL injury management

stem: A 19-year-old footballer has a high-grade pivot shift and a contralateral ACL reconstruction that previously failed. As part of his revision single-bundle ACL reconstruction the surgeon plans to address persistent anterolateral rotatory laxity by adding a lateral extra-articular procedure. Which statement most accurately reflects current understanding of the anterolateral complex and lateral augmentation?

options.label: A
options.text: A lateral extra-articular tenodesis (LET) or anterolateral ligament reconstruction reduces graft-rupture rates in young high-risk pivoting patients

options.label: B
options.text: The anterolateral ligament is the primary restraint to anterior tibial translation at 90° of flexion

options.label: C
options.text: Adding a LET reliably worsens postoperative range of motion and is therefore contraindicated in athletes

options.label: D
options.text: The pivot-shift phenomenon is generated mainly by the posterior cruciate ligament becoming incompetent

options.label: E
options.text: Lateral augmentation should only be performed in patients over 40 with established lateral compartment osteoarthritis

explanation: The anterolateral complex (iliotibial band/Kaplan fibres, anterolateral capsule and the so-called anterolateral ligament) is a secondary restraint to internal rotation and contributes to control of the pivot shift; the ACL remains the primary restraint to anterior translation. Level-1 evidence (e.g. the STABILITY trial) shows that adding a lateral extra-articular tenodesis to a hamstring ACL reconstruction in young, high-risk pivoting patients significantly reduces graft failure and persistent rotatory laxity, which is why LET/ALL augmentation is increasingly used in revision and high-risk primary cases. It does not reliably cause stiffness, the PCL does not generate the pivot shift, and the procedure is aimed at young high-demand knees, not arthritic ones.
learningPoint: Adding a lateral extra-articular tenodesis to hamstring ACL reconstruction lowers graft-failure and residual pivot-shift rates in young high-risk pivoting patients (STABILITY trial).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Sports Medicine: anterolateral complex and rotatory instability

stem: A 28-year-old motorcyclist struck his flexed knee on the petrol tank in a low-speed collision. He has a posterior sag sign and an isolated grade II posterior drawer with the knee at 90°. Regarding the biomechanics and examination of the posterior cruciate ligament, which statement is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: The anterolateral bundle of the PCL is the largest component and is tightest in flexion

options.label: B
options.text: The PCL is the primary restraint to anterior tibial translation

options.label: C
options.text: A positive dial test at 30° but not at 90° indicates an isolated complete PCL rupture

options.label: D
options.text: The quadriceps active test reduces a posteriorly subluxed tibia by pulling it further posteriorly

options.label: E
options.text: Increased external tibial rotation at 90° compared with 30° is the hallmark of an isolated PCL tear

explanation: The PCL is the primary restraint to posterior tibial translation and has two functional bundles: the larger, stronger anterolateral bundle that tightens in flexion and the smaller posteromedial bundle that tightens in extension. This reciprocal behaviour underlies double-bundle reconstruction concepts and the choice of fixation angle. A posterior sag and grade II posterior drawer at 90° fit an isolated PCL injury. The quadriceps active test reduces (not worsens) a posteriorly sagged tibia by the patellar tendon pulling the tibia forward. Isolated PCL injury does not increase external rotation; increased external rotation at 30° suggests posterolateral corner injury, and a dial test that worsens from 30° to 90° suggests combined PCL/PLC injury — so the PLC-related options are incorrect.
learningPoint: The PCL's anterolateral bundle is the larger component and tightens in flexion; the PCL is the primary restraint to posterior tibial translation, not anterior.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Sports Medicine: PCL anatomy and biomechanics

stem: A 31-year-old sustains a dashboard injury. Radiographs and CT show a displaced bony avulsion of the PCL from its tibial insertion, with a fragment displaced by 8 mm and a posterior sag clinically. The knee is otherwise stable. What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Operative fixation of the avulsed bony fragment

options.label: B
options.text: Six weeks in a cylinder cast in full extension then mobilise

options.label: C
options.text: Acute double-bundle PCL reconstruction with hamstring autograft

options.label: D
options.text: PCL bracing in a dynamic anterior-drawer (PCL) brace with the tibia supported anteriorly for 12 weeks, non-operative

options.label: E
options.text: Immediate posterolateral corner reconstruction

explanation: A displaced bony PCL avulsion behaves differently from a mid-substance PCL tear. A displaced fragment (generally >5 mm of displacement, or a clinically unstable knee) is best treated by anatomical reduction and fixation (screw or suture/endobutton fixation, open posterior or arthroscopic), which restores PCL length and tension through bone-to-bone healing and gives far better results than treating it as a soft-tissue rupture. Mid-substance isolated grade I–II PCL tears are the ones managed non-operatively in a PCL/dynamic brace that supports the tibia anteriorly. Reconstruction is reserved for irreparable mid-substance tears or failed treatment, not for a freshly displaced bony avulsion, and there is no indication here for PLC surgery.
learningPoint: A displaced bony PCL avulsion is fixed (bone-to-bone heals well); reserve bracing for mid-substance isolated low-grade PCL tears and reconstruction for irreparable tears.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Sports Medicine: PCL injury management

stem: A 22-year-old footballer is tackled from the side and sustains a valgus injury. He has medial joint-line tenderness and pain with valgus stress at 30° of flexion, with a firm endpoint and no opening at full extension. There is no effusion and Lachman is negative. What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.label: A
options.text: Functional hinged-knee brace, early protected weight-bearing and rehabilitation

options.label: B
options.text: Urgent surgical repair of the superficial MCL

options.label: C
options.text: Above-knee cast in full extension for six weeks

options.label: D
options.text: Arthroscopy and MCL reconstruction with hamstring graft

options.label: E
options.text: Strict non-weight-bearing on crutches until the pain settles, then sport

explanation: Valgus laxity at 30° with a firm endpoint and a stable knee in full extension is a grade I–II isolated MCL injury. The MCL has excellent healing potential, and the overwhelming majority of isolated MCL injuries (including many grade III) are managed non-operatively with a hinged brace, early protected weight-bearing and progressive rehabilitation, returning to sport in weeks. Surgery/reconstruction is reserved for the rare chronic symptomatic valgus instability, distal (tibial-sided) avulsions with the end displaced superficial to pes (the Stener-like lesion that may not heal), or MCL injury as part of a multiligament knee. Casting in full extension and prolonged non-weight-bearing are outdated and risk stiffness.
learningPoint: Isolated MCL injuries — even many grade III — heal well non-operatively in a hinged brace with early motion; surgery is reserved for chronic instability, distal avulsions or multiligament knees.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Sports Medicine: MCL injury

stem: A patient with a varus knee and a hyperextension–varus mechanism has increased external rotation of the tibia at 30° that normalises at 90°, a positive reverse pivot shift and a foot drop. Which set of structures forms the primary static stabilisers of the posterolateral corner that this injury most likely involves?

options.label: A
options.text: Fibular (lateral) collateral ligament, popliteus tendon and popliteofibular ligament

options.label: B
options.text: Superficial and deep MCL and the posterior oblique ligament

options.label: C
options.text: Anterolateral ligament, iliotibial band and the lateral meniscus

options.label: D
options.text: Biceps femoris, lateral gastrocnemius and the fabella

options.label: E
options.text: Posterior cruciate ligament and the meniscofemoral ligaments of Humphrey and Wrisberg

explanation: The three principal static stabilisers of the posterolateral corner are the fibular (lateral) collateral ligament, the popliteus tendon and the popliteofibular ligament; injury to these produces increased external tibial rotation at 30° (the dial test), a positive reverse pivot shift and external-rotation recurvatum, and is frequently associated with a common peroneal nerve (foot drop) injury because the nerve lies adjacent to the fibular neck. The MCL/posterior oblique ligament stabilise the medial side; the anterolateral ligament/ITB control anterolateral rotation; biceps, gastrocnemius and the fabella are secondary; and the PCL with the meniscofemoral ligaments resists posterior translation, not posterolateral rotation. Missed PLC injury is a major cause of cruciate-graft failure, so it must be recognised and addressed.
learningPoint: The PLC's primary static stabilisers are the LCL, popliteus tendon and popliteofibular ligament; missed PLC injury (dial test positive at 30°) is a leading cause of cruciate graft failure.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Sports Medicine: posterolateral corner

stem: A 35-year-old is brought to the emergency department after a high-energy road traffic collision with an obviously deformed knee that reduces spontaneously before assessment. Distal pulses are palpable and the foot is warm. According to current UK practice for the spontaneously reduced knee dislocation (multiligament injury), what is the most appropriate next step in vascular assessment?

options.label: A
options.text: Measure the ankle–brachial pressure index; if it is ≥0.9 with a normal serial examination, the patient can avoid angiography but needs continued monitoring

options.label: B
options.text: Palpable pulses exclude arterial injury, so no further vascular assessment is required

options.label: C
options.text: Proceed directly to emergency arteriography in all spontaneously reduced knee dislocations regardless of pulses

options.label: D
options.text: Apply an above-knee backslab and review the limb at the fracture clinic in one week

options.label: E
options.text: A single normal pulse examination on arrival reliably excludes a popliteal intimal injury

explanation: Knee dislocation carries a significant risk of popliteal artery injury, and a spontaneously reduced dislocation is just as dangerous as a frank one. Palpable pulses do NOT exclude an intimal tear that can thrombose hours later, so a single normal examination is insufficient. Current practice is selective imaging guided by the ankle–brachial pressure index (ABPI) plus serial examination: an ABPI ≥0.9 with normal repeated examination has a high negative predictive value and allows avoidance of routine angiography while the patient is admitted for serial observation; an ABPI <0.9, an abnormal/asymmetrical examination or hard signs mandate urgent CT angiography or on-table arteriography and vascular involvement. Mandatory angiography for everyone is no longer standard, and discharging or splinting for clinic review risks a missed limb-threatening arterial injury.
learningPoint: After a (even spontaneously reduced) knee dislocation, use ABPI plus serial examination: ABPI ≥0.9 with a normal exam avoids routine angiography; <0.9 or hard signs mandate urgent CT angiography.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Sports/Trauma: knee dislocation
BOAST: The Management of Knee Dislocations

stem: A 27-year-old has a confirmed multiligament knee injury after a sporting collision. MRI shows complete ruptures of the ACL, PCL and the posterolateral corner, with intact medial structures and no vascular injury. Using the Schenck anatomical classification and current principles of surgical timing, which statement is most accurate?

options.label: A
options.text: This is a KD-III (lateral) pattern, and the posterolateral corner is best repaired or reconstructed within about 2–3 weeks

options.label: B
options.text: This is a KD-I pattern because only a single cruciate needs reconstruction

options.label: C
options.text: All multiligament knees should be braced for three months before any surgery to allow the PLC to heal

options.label: D
options.text: Posterolateral corner reconstruction gives equivalent results whether performed acutely or 6 months later

options.label: E
options.text: The presence of a peroneal nerve palsy is a contraindication to any ligament surgery

explanation: In the Schenck classification, KD-III denotes both cruciates plus one collateral/corner: KD-IIIM if the medial side is involved and KD-IIIL if the posterolateral corner is involved — this case (ACL + PCL + PLC, medial intact) is KD-IIIL. Surgical timing matters: posterolateral corner and other peripheral structures heal and scar quickly, so acute repair/reconstruction (commonly within about 2–3 weeks, before tissue planes obliterate) gives better rotatory control than delayed surgery, whereas results of chronic PLC reconstruction are inferior. KD-I means only one cruciate is torn (not this case). Prolonged bracing of a complete PLC rupture does not restore stability, acute and chronic PLC surgery are not equivalent, and a peroneal palsy is not a contraindication — it is documented and the nerve protected at surgery.
learningPoint: ACL + PCL + posterolateral corner with intact medial side is Schenck KD-IIIL; PLC structures scar fast, so acute repair/reconstruction (~2–3 weeks) outperforms delayed surgery.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Sports Medicine: multiligament knee injury

stem: A 30-year-old recreational runner has an irreparable radial tear involving the mid-body of the lateral meniscus and undergoes a substantial partial lateral meniscectomy. Regarding meniscal biomechanics and the consequences of meniscal loss, which statement is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: Loss of meniscal tissue increases peak contact stress on the articular cartilage and accelerates degenerative change, with the lateral compartment particularly affected

options.label: B
options.text: The menisci transmit no significant load and serve only as joint-space fillers

options.label: C
options.text: Circumferential hoop stresses are unaffected by a complete radial tear that reaches the periphery

options.label: D
options.text: The medial meniscus is more mobile than the lateral meniscus and therefore at greater risk after ACL injury

options.label: E
options.text: Total meniscectomy reduces tibiofemoral contact pressure by enlarging the contact area

explanation: The menisci transmit roughly half (and in flexion the majority) of tibiofemoral load by converting axial load into circumferential hoop stress; their wedge shape increases joint congruity and contact area, which lowers peak stress. Removing meniscal tissue shrinks the contact area and raises peak contact stress, predisposing to cartilage degeneration — a well-recognised driver of post-meniscectomy osteoarthritis, and the lateral compartment tolerates meniscal loss especially poorly (rapid lateral arthrosis). A full-thickness radial tear that extends to the periphery disrupts the circumferential collagen and abolishes hoop-stress conversion, functionally equivalent to a segmental loss. The medial meniscus is the LESS mobile of the two (more firmly anchored), and total meniscectomy increases, not decreases, contact pressure. This biomechanical penalty is the rationale for meniscal preservation/repair wherever feasible.
learningPoint: Menisci convert load into circumferential hoop stress; tissue loss raises peak contact stress and accelerates arthritis, especially in the lateral compartment — hence preserve/repair where possible.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Sports Medicine: meniscal anatomy and biomechanics

stem: A 21-year-old sustains a 12 mm longitudinal tear of the peripheral posterior horn of the medial meniscus at the time of an acute ACL rupture. Which combination of factors makes this tear most favourable for repair rather than resection?

options.label: A
options.text: A peripheral (red–red) longitudinal tear, in a young patient, repaired concurrently with ACL reconstruction

options.label: B
options.text: A central (white–white) horizontal cleavage tear in a degenerate meniscus

options.label: C
options.text: A complex, macerated flap tear in a 60-year-old with established compartment osteoarthritis

options.label: D
options.text: A short radial tear confined to the inner avascular third

options.label: E
options.text: A degenerate root tear in an obese patient with malalignment, repaired without addressing alignment

explanation: Meniscal healing depends on blood supply: the peripheral third (red–red zone, supplied by the perimeniscal capillary plexus) heals well, whereas the inner third (white–white, avascular) heals poorly. The ideal repair candidate is a peripheral, longitudinal (vertical) tear, of reasonable length, in a young patient with good tissue quality. Concurrent ACL reconstruction markedly improves meniscal repair healing rates because of the stable knee environment and the haemarthrosis/marrow factors released at the time of tunnel drilling. Central horizontal cleavage tears, complex macerated tears in arthritic knees, short radial tears in the avascular zone, and degenerate root tears in malaligned obese knees (without correcting alignment) all have poor healing and are generally not repaired in the same way.
learningPoint: The best meniscal-repair candidate is a peripheral (red–red) longitudinal tear in a young patient, ideally repaired at the same sitting as ACL reconstruction.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Sports Medicine: meniscal repair

stem: A 17-year-old girl has had three lateral patellar dislocations. Examination shows a positive apprehension test, a J-sign and patellar maltracking. CT/MRI shows a tibial tubercle–trochlear groove (TT-TG) distance of 22 mm and no significant trochlear dysplasia, with normal patellar height. Considering the anatomy of the medial patellofemoral ligament and surgical planning, which statement is most accurate?

options.label: A
options.text: The MPFL is the primary passive restraint to lateral patellar translation in the first 0–30° of flexion, and a raised TT-TG (>20 mm) is an indication to add a tibial tubercle transfer to MPFL reconstruction

options.label: B
options.text: The MPFL is most important beyond 60° of flexion, once the patella is engaged in the trochlea

options.label: C
options.text: A TT-TG distance of 22 mm is normal and requires no bony correction

options.label: D
options.text: Isolated lateral retinacular release reliably corrects recurrent dislocation in this patient

options.label: E
options.text: The MPFL originates from the adductor tubercle and inserts onto the tibial tubercle

explanation: The MPFL provides about 50–60% of the passive restraint to lateral patellar translation and is most important in early flexion (roughly 0–30°), before the patella engages the bony trochlea; beyond that, bony containment takes over. The MPFL runs from a point between the medial epicondyle and adductor tubercle to the superomedial border of the patella (not the tibial tubercle). Surgical planning is anatomy-led: an elevated TT-TG distance (commonly a threshold of about 20 mm) reflects an excessively lateralised extensor mechanism and is an indication to add a tibial tubercle (medialising) osteotomy to MPFL reconstruction, rather than relying on soft-tissue reconstruction alone. A lateral release alone does not correct dislocation and may worsen medial instability, and 22 mm is abnormal.
learningPoint: The MPFL is the main restraint to lateral patellar translation in early flexion (0–30°); a TT-TG distance >~20 mm warrants adding a tibial tubercle transfer to MPFL reconstruction.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Sports Medicine: patellofemoral instability

stem: A 19-year-old with recurrent patellar dislocations has a lateral radiograph showing the crossing sign, a supratrochlear spur (bump) and a double-contour appearance. Which statement best describes the significance of these findings and their management implications?

options.label: A
options.text: These are radiographic signs of trochlear dysplasia (Dejour classification), and high-grade dysplasia may require a trochleoplasty in addition to soft-tissue stabilisation

options.label: B
options.text: These signs indicate patella alta and are corrected by a tibial tubercle distalisation alone

options.label: C
options.text: The crossing sign represents a normal variant and has no bearing on instability

options.label: D
options.text: Trochlear dysplasia is best treated by lateral retinacular release in all cases

options.label: E
options.text: A supratrochlear spur indicates a deep trochlea that protects against dislocation
