explanation: The crossing sign (where the floor of the trochlear groove crosses the anterior femoral condylar line), the supratrochlear spur/bump and the double-contour sign are the classic lateral-radiograph features of trochlear dysplasia, graded by the Dejour A–D classification. Trochlear dysplasia is one of the principal anatomical risk factors for recurrent patellar instability because the shallow or convex trochlea fails to contain the patella. In high-grade dysplasia (particularly with a prominent supratrochlear spur), MPFL reconstruction alone may be insufficient and a sulcus-deepening trochleoplasty is considered to restore bony containment. The crossing sign is not a normal variant, a spur reflects a flat/convex (not deep) trochlea, distalisation addresses patella alta rather than dysplasia, and isolated lateral release does not treat dysplastic instability.
learningPoint: The crossing sign, supratrochlear spur and double-contour sign indicate trochlear dysplasia (Dejour); high-grade dysplasia may need trochleoplasty in addition to MPFL reconstruction.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Sports Medicine: trochlear dysplasia

stem: A 28-year-old recreational athlete has persistent pain and a 3 cm² (approximately 20 mm diameter), full-thickness (ICRS grade IV) symptomatic chondral defect on the medial femoral condyle. The knee is well-aligned and stable with intact menisci, and conservative measures have failed. Which treatment is most appropriate, and on what principle?

options.label: A
options.text: A cell-based or osteochondral resurfacing technique (e.g. autologous chondrocyte implantation/MACI or osteochondral autograft) is preferred because microfracture produces inferior fibrocartilage for defects of this size

options.label: B
options.text: Microfracture is the definitive treatment of choice for all full-thickness defects regardless of size

options.label: C
options.text: Total knee arthroplasty is indicated for an isolated chondral defect in a 28-year-old

options.label: D
options.text: The defect should be ignored because chondral lesions reliably heal with native hyaline cartilage

options.label: E
options.text: A medial unicompartmental arthroplasty is the standard treatment for an isolated femoral chondral defect at this age

explanation: Articular cartilage has essentially no intrinsic capacity to heal with hyaline cartilage because it is avascular and aneural. Treatment of a symptomatic full-thickness defect is size- and patient-dependent. Marrow-stimulation (microfracture) recruits marrow cells but produces mechanically inferior fibrocartilage; its results deteriorate for larger lesions (a commonly cited threshold is around 2 cm²). For a ~3 cm² grade IV lesion in a young, aligned, stable knee, a cell-based or osteochondral resurfacing technique — autologous chondrocyte implantation/MACI (NICE TA508 supports ACI for qualifying defects in patients without significant osteoarthritis who have not had prior cartilage surgery) or osteochondral autograft — gives more durable, hyaline-like repair. Arthroplasty (TKA or UKA) is inappropriate for an isolated chondral defect in a 28-year-old, and the lesion will not self-heal.
learningPoint: For a young, aligned, stable knee with a symptomatic full-thickness chondral defect above ~2 cm², prefer cell-based/osteochondral resurfacing (ACI/MACI per NICE TA508, or osteochondral autograft) over microfracture.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Sports Medicine: articular cartilage restoration
NICE TA508: autologous chondrocyte implantation for knee cartilage defects

subtopic: Cartilage and osteochondral lesions — treatment selection
stem: Theme: management of articular cartilage and osteochondral lesions of the knee. For each scenario, select the single most appropriate management from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all. Assume conservative measures have been tried where relevant and that alignment is corrected separately if abnormal.

options.label: A
options.text: Reassurance and activity modification / supervised rehabilitation, no surgery

options.label: B
options.text: Arthroscopic microfracture (marrow stimulation)

options.label: C
options.text: Autologous chondrocyte implantation / MACI

options.label: D
options.text: Osteochondral autograft transfer (OATS / mosaicplasty)

options.label: E
options.text: Fresh osteochondral allograft

options.label: F
options.text: In-situ fixation of the osteochondral fragment

options.label: G
options.text: Removal of loose body alone

options.label: H
options.text: Unicompartmental knee arthroplasty

explanation: Cartilage/osteochondral management is driven by the patient's age and skeletal maturity, lesion size and depth, and whether the fragment is stable, unstable but salvageable, or detached. A small (<2 cm²) symptomatic full-thickness chondral defect in a young knee is reasonably treated by marrow stimulation (microfracture) as a first-line resurfacing option (B). A large (≥2 cm²) contained chondral defect in a young, aligned, stable knee is better served by a cell-based technique such as ACI/MACI, which produces durable hyaline-like cartilage (C). A large lesion with significant subchondral bone loss (an osteochondral, not purely chondral, defect) needs bone as well as cartilage — a fresh osteochondral allograft restores both the bone and the hyaline surface in one construct (E). A juvenile osteochondritis dissecans lesion that is unstable but has a large viable osteochondral fragment should be reduced and fixed in situ to preserve the patient's own articular surface (F). A stable juvenile OCD lesion with intact overlying cartilage and open physes has a high spontaneous-healing potential and is managed non-operatively first (A). Loose-body removal alone, OATS and unicompartmental arthroplasty are distractors for these particular scenarios.
learningPoint: Match cartilage treatment to defect size/depth and fragment stability: small chondral → microfracture; large chondral → ACI/MACI; osteochondral with bone loss → allograft; salvageable unstable OCD fragment → fix in situ; stable juvenile OCD → non-operative.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Sports Medicine: cartilage restoration and osteochondritis dissecans

emiLeads.stem: A 26-year-old with a symptomatic 1.2 cm², full-thickness (ICRS IV) chondral defect on the medial femoral condyle in a well-aligned, stable knee; failed rehabilitation.
A 30-year-old with a symptomatic, contained, purely chondral 3.5 cm² defect of the medial femoral condyle; aligned, stable, menisci intact; no prior cartilage surgery.
A 34-year-old with a large femoral condyle osteochondral defect with about 8 mm of subchondral bone loss following a previous failed marrow-stimulation procedure.
A 14-year-old with an unstable juvenile osteochondritis dissecans lesion of the lateral femoral condyle; MRI shows fluid behind a large but viable osteochondral fragment that is still in its bed.
An 11-year-old with knee pain and an osteochondritis dissecans lesion of the medial femoral condyle; open physes, intact overlying cartilage and a stable fragment on MRI.

stem: A 12-year-old footballer has activity-related knee pain. MRI shows a lesion of the lateral aspect of the medial femoral condyle (the classic site) with open physes, intact overlying cartilage and no high-signal line at the fragment interface. Regarding juvenile osteochondritis dissecans, which statement is most accurate?

options.label: A
options.text: A stable lesion in a skeletally immature patient has good healing potential and is initially managed non-operatively, with activity modification and monitoring

options.label: B
options.text: Skeletally immature patients have a worse prognosis than adults with the same lesion

options.label: C
options.text: A high-signal (fluid) line completely surrounding the fragment on T2 MRI indicates a stable lesion

options.label: D
options.text: The patella is the most common site of osteochondritis dissecans in the knee

options.label: E
options.text: All juvenile osteochondritis dissecans lesions require immediate arthroscopic fixation to prevent loose-body formation

explanation: Osteochondritis dissecans most commonly affects the lateral aspect of the medial femoral condyle. Skeletal maturity is the key prognostic factor: juvenile OCD (open physes) has a far better spontaneous healing potential than adult OCD, so a stable lesion with intact overlying cartilage and no breach is managed non-operatively first — activity restriction, sometimes a period of protected weight-bearing, and serial imaging — with surgery reserved for lesions that fail to heal, become unstable, or are already detached. On MRI, signs of instability include a high-signal (fluid) line completely surrounding the fragment on T2, fluid-filled cysts beneath the fragment, and a focal cartilage defect; these indicate the fragment is loose, not stable. Not all juvenile OCD needs surgery, and the patella is an uncommon site.
learningPoint: Juvenile OCD (open physes) with a stable lesion heals well and is managed non-operatively first; a T2 fluid line fully encircling the fragment signals instability, not stability.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Sports/Paediatrics: osteochondritis dissecans

stem: A 58-year-old woman presents with a symptomatic bunion. On a weight-bearing dorsoplantar radiograph the hallux valgus angle (HVA) is 28 degrees and the intermetatarsal angle (IMA) between the first and second metatarsals is 14 degrees. Using conventional radiographic thresholds (mild HVA up to 20 degrees and IMA up to 11 degrees; moderate HVA 20-40 degrees and IMA 11-16 degrees; severe HVA above 40 degrees and IMA above 16 degrees), how is this deformity best classified?

options.label: A
options.text: Normal alignment

options.label: B
options.text: Mild hallux valgus

options.label: C
options.text: Moderate hallux valgus

options.label: D
options.text: Severe hallux valgus

options.label: E
options.text: Hallux varus

explanation: Standard thresholds are: normal HVA below 15 degrees and IMA below 9 degrees; mild deformity HVA up to about 20 degrees with IMA up to 11 degrees; moderate HVA roughly 20-40 degrees with IMA 11-16 degrees; severe HVA above 40 degrees with IMA above 16 degrees. An HVA of 28 degrees with IMA of 14 degrees therefore sits in the moderate range. Be aware that published cut-offs are heterogeneous (an IMA of 14 degrees can be graded differently depending on the system cited), so the stem states the scheme being applied. Correct grading drives osteotomy selection, since the IMA largely determines whether a distal versus more proximal/diaphyseal correction is required.
learningPoint: Moderate hallux valgus = HVA approximately 20-40 degrees and IMA 11-16 degrees; severity grading guides the level of corrective osteotomy, though published thresholds vary.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Foot & Ankle chapter

stem: A 49-year-old woman has a symptomatic hallux valgus that has failed shoe modification. Weight-bearing radiographs show a hallux valgus angle of 24 degrees, an intermetatarsal angle of 13 degrees and a congruent first metatarsophalangeal joint with no degenerative change. The distal metatarsal articular angle is within normal limits and there is no first-ray hypermobility. Which surgical procedure is the most appropriate single correction?

options.label: A
options.text: Akin osteotomy of the proximal phalanx alone

options.label: B
options.text: Distal chevron (Austin) first metatarsal osteotomy

options.label: C
options.text: Keller resection arthroplasty

options.label: D
options.text: First metatarsophalangeal joint arthrodesis

options.label: E
options.text: Lapidus procedure (first tarsometatarsal arthrodesis)

explanation: For mild-to-moderate hallux valgus with an IMA up to around 13-15 degrees and no first MTPJ arthritis, a distal metatarsal osteotomy such as the chevron (Austin) is the workhorse, as it reliably narrows the IMA within its translational limits and corrects the HVA. An Akin alone addresses phalangeal deformity (high DMAA or hallux interphalangeus) but does not correct an increased IMA. Keller and arthrodesis are reserved for arthritic or salvage situations. A Lapidus is indicated for a markedly increased IMA or demonstrable first-ray hypermobility, neither of which is present here.
learningPoint: A distal chevron osteotomy corrects mild-to-moderate hallux valgus; reserve proximal/Lapidus procedures for a large IMA or first-ray hypermobility.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Foot & Ankle chapter

stem: Theme: Selecting the corrective procedure in hallux valgus. For each clinical and radiographic scenario, select the single most appropriate procedure from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Akin osteotomy of the proximal phalanx

options.label: B
options.text: Distal chevron (Austin) metatarsal osteotomy

options.label: C
options.text: Scarf diaphyseal metatarsal osteotomy

options.label: D
options.text: Proximal (basal) first metatarsal osteotomy

options.label: E
options.text: Lapidus procedure (first tarsometatarsal arthrodesis)

options.label: F
options.text: First metatarsophalangeal joint arthrodesis

options.label: G
options.text: Keller resection arthroplasty

emiLeads.stem: A 35-year-old with hallux valgus, HVA 22 degrees, IMA 12 degrees, congruent joint and no arthritis, who has failed conservative care and wants a single bony correction at the diaphyseal level allowing early protected weight-bearing.
A 60-year-old with a severe deformity (HVA 48 degrees, IMA 20 degrees), generalised ligamentous laxity and a clinically hypermobile, mildly arthritic first tarsometatarsal joint.

A 72-year-old with established first metatarsophalangeal joint osteoarthritis, dorsal osteophytes and a moderate hallux valgus, in whom durable pain relief and a stable medial column are the priority.

A 28-year-old with a well-corrected IMA after previous surgery but a residual increased distal metatarsal articular angle (interphalangeus pattern), HVA 18 degrees, with the deformity arising distal to the joint line.
A low-demand 84-year-old with a painful arthritic bunion, poor skin and high anaesthetic risk, in whom a quick decompressive salvage that sacrifices push-off power is acceptable.

explanation: Procedure choice in hallux valgus follows deformity severity, joint congruity/arthritis and special factors. Scarf is a versatile diaphyseal osteotomy for moderate deformity. A markedly increased IMA with first-ray hypermobility or TMT arthritis is the classic Lapidus indication. Established first MTPJ arthritis with deformity is best served by arthrodesis, which corrects and stabilises. An isolated raised DMAA/interphalangeus with a normal IMA is corrected by an Akin (phalangeal) osteotomy. Keller arthroplasty remains a salvage option for low-demand, high-risk elderly patients, at the cost of push-off strength and a risk of transfer metatarsalgia.
learningPoint: Match the hallux valgus procedure to IMA magnitude, joint arthritis/hypermobility and the apex of deformity; arthrodesis for the arthritic joint, Lapidus for hypermobility, Akin for phalangeal/DMAA deformity.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Foot & Ankle chapter

stem: A 55-year-old keen runner reports a painful, stiff great toe that hurts most at the top of the joint when he pushes off. Examination shows a dorsal bony prominence, pain at terminal dorsiflexion but a relatively preserved arc of plantarflexion, and only mild loss of total range. Radiographs show dorsal osteophytes with preservation of most of the joint space. After failed non-operative treatment, which is the most appropriate first-line surgical option?

options.label: A
options.text: First metatarsophalangeal joint arthrodesis

options.label: B
options.text: Dorsal cheilectomy

options.label: C
options.text: Keller resection arthroplasty

options.label: D
options.text: Total first metatarsophalangeal joint replacement

options.label: E
options.text: Moberg dorsal closing-wedge osteotomy of the proximal phalanx alone

explanation: This is early-grade hallux rigidus with dorsal impingement, dorsal osteophytes and largely preserved joint space. Dorsal cheilectomy (excising the dorsal osteophyte and roughly the dorsal third of the metatarsal head) relieves dorsal impingement and improves dorsiflexion while preserving the joint, making it the procedure of choice for low-grade disease. Arthrodesis is the gold standard for advanced (high-grade) hallux rigidus with global joint-space loss, not early disease. Keller is a low-demand salvage. A Moberg can supplement a cheilectomy to gain dorsiflexion but is not the primary procedure for dorsal osteophytic impingement.
learningPoint: Low-grade hallux rigidus with preserved joint space and dorsal impingement is treated by cheilectomy; reserve arthrodesis for advanced global degeneration.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Foot & Ankle chapter (hallux rigidus, Coughlin & Shurnas grading)

stem: A 62-year-old labourer has end-stage hallux rigidus with severe pain throughout the arc of movement, grade 4 changes and global loss of the first metatarsophalangeal joint space on radiographs. He has high physical demands and wants a durable, reliable result. When counselling him for first metatarsophalangeal joint arthrodesis, which fusion position is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: Neutral dorsiflexion, neutral rotation and 15 degrees of valgus

options.label: B
options.text: Approximately 10-15 degrees of dorsiflexion relative to the floor, slight valgus and neutral rotation

options.label: C
options.text: Approximately 30 degrees of dorsiflexion relative to the metatarsal shaft and 20 degrees of valgus

options.label: D
options.text: Maximal plantarflexion to aid push-off, with neutral valgus

options.label: E
options.text: Neutral in all planes (no dorsiflexion, no valgus)

explanation: First MTPJ arthrodesis is the gold standard for end-stage hallux rigidus, particularly in higher-demand patients, giving reliable pain relief and a stable medial column. The optimal position is around 10-15 degrees of dorsiflexion relative to the floor (often expressed as roughly 20-25 degrees relative to the first metatarsal shaft), about 10-15 degrees of valgus, and neutral rotation. Too much dorsiflexion causes interphalangeal joint pressure and dorsal nail/shoe problems; too little (or plantarflexion) causes pulp loading and difficulty clearing the floor. Excess valgus risks impingement on the second toe.
learningPoint: Fuse the first MTPJ in about 10-15 degrees dorsiflexion to the floor, slight valgus and neutral rotation; judge dorsiflexion against a flat plate simulating the floor.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Foot & Ankle chapter (first MTPJ arthrodesis)

stem: A 64-year-old woman complains of a painful second toe. Examination shows the proximal interphalangeal joint is fixed in flexion with a painful dorsal callosity, while the distal interphalangeal joint is extended and the metatarsophalangeal joint lies in neutral or slight extension. Which lesser-toe deformity does this describe?

options.label: A
options.text: Mallet toe

options.label: B
options.text: Claw toe

options.label: C
options.text: Hammer toe

options.label: D
options.text: Curly toe

options.label: E
options.text: Crossover (second) toe from plantar plate insufficiency

explanation: A hammer toe is flexion at the proximal interphalangeal joint, typically with compensatory extension at the DIP joint and a neutral or extended MTPJ; the painful lesion is the dorsal PIPJ corn. A mallet toe is isolated DIPJ flexion. A claw toe combines MTPJ hyperextension with flexion at both the PIP and DIP joints and is often associated with neuromuscular disease. A curly toe (paediatric) shows flexion with medial rotation. Crossover toe results from plantar plate attenuation, causing medial-dorsal MTPJ deviation. Identifying the apex of deformity and whether it is fixed or flexible dictates whether a soft-tissue release, flexor-to-extensor transfer or PIPJ fusion/arthroplasty is required.
learningPoint: Hammer toe = PIPJ flexion (with neutral MTPJ); mallet = DIPJ flexion; claw = MTPJ extension plus PIP and DIP flexion.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Foot & Ankle chapter (lesser-toe deformities)

stem: A 58-year-old woman has progressive pain under the second metatarsophalangeal joint with dorsal-medial deviation of the second toe and a positive vertical Lachman (dorsal drawer) test of that joint. There is no hallux valgus crowding. Radiographs are unremarkable. Which structure has most likely failed, and what is the underlying lesion?

options.label: A
options.text: Rupture of the long flexor tendon to the second toe

options.label: B
options.text: Attenuation or tear of the plantar plate of the second metatarsophalangeal joint

options.label: C
options.text: Interdigital (Morton's) neuroma of the second web space

options.label: D
options.text: Freiberg infraction of the second metatarsal head

options.label: E
options.text: Rupture of the extensor digitorum longus to the second toe

explanation: Plantar plate insufficiency of the lesser MTPJ presents with plantar metatarsalgia, progressive dorsal and often medial (crossover) toe deviation, and a positive dorsal drawer (Lachman) test demonstrating sagittal instability. It is the commonest cause of crossover second toe. A Morton's neuroma gives radiating interdigital pain and a Mulder click but not fixed toe deviation or a positive drawer. Freiberg infraction shows MTP head flattening/sclerosis on radiographs. Flexor or extensor rupture would not produce this pattern of instability. MRI or ultrasound confirms the plantar plate tear; management ranges from taping/orthoses to plantar plate repair with a Weil osteotomy.
learningPoint: A positive dorsal drawer (Lachman) test at a lesser MTPJ with a crossover toe indicates plantar plate failure, the commonest cause of the deformity.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Foot & Ankle chapter (plantar plate / lesser MTPJ instability)

stem: A 21-year-old sprinter has localised pain and tenderness directly under the great toe at the head of the first metatarsal, worse on push-off and dorsiflexion of the hallux. A plain radiograph shows the medial (tibial) sesamoid divided into two fragments with smooth, rounded, corticated margins. Which is the most likely diagnosis?

options.label: A
options.text: Acute fracture of the tibial sesamoid

options.label: B
options.text: Bipartite tibial sesamoid

options.label: C
options.text: Osteomyelitis of the sesamoid

options.label: D
options.text: Avascular necrosis of the sesamoid

options.label: E
options.text: Turf toe (plantar plate / capsuloligamentous) injury

explanation: Smooth, rounded, corticated margins of two fragments favour a bipartite sesamoid (a normal variant, most often the tibial sesamoid, and frequently bilateral), whereas an acute fracture characteristically shows sharp, irregular, non-corticated edges that fit together. Bipartism can nonetheless become symptomatic. Comparison views of the opposite foot help, and MRI/bone scan can clarify when a stress reaction or osteonecrosis is suspected. Sesamoiditis, stress fracture, AVN and turf toe are differentials for plantar first-MTP pain but do not produce two corticated fragments.
learningPoint: Corticated, rounded fragment margins suggest a bipartite sesamoid; sharp, irregular non-corticated edges suggest an acute fracture.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Foot & Ankle chapter (sesamoid disorders)

stem: A 30-year-old recreational runner has chronic plantar first metatarsophalangeal pain localised to the tibial sesamoid. Six months of activity modification, a stiff-soled shoe and an orthosis offloading the sesamoid have failed, and MRI confirms an established symptomatic non-union/osteonecrosis confined to the tibial sesamoid with a healthy fibular sesamoid. What is the most appropriate surgical option?

options.label: A
options.text: Excision of both sesamoids

options.label: B
options.text: Isolated tibial sesamoidectomy

options.label: C
options.text: First metatarsophalangeal joint arthrodesis

options.label: D
options.text: Dorsal cheilectomy

options.label: E
options.text: Keller resection arthroplasty

explanation: For an isolated, refractory symptomatic tibial sesamoid lesion (non-union, osteonecrosis or intractable sesamoiditis) with a healthy contralateral sesamoid, isolated sesamoidectomy is the standard operation, preserving the other sesamoid and the windlass mechanism. Excising both sesamoids must be avoided because it removes the plantar restraints and risks a cock-up hallux (hallux extensus) and loss of push-off. Arthrodesis, cheilectomy and Keller address arthritic or impingement problems, not an isolated sesamoid lesion. After tibial sesamoidectomy the abductor hallucis sling should be repaired to reduce the risk of hallux valgus.
learningPoint: Excise only the diseased sesamoid; bilateral sesamoidectomy risks a cock-up hallux and loss of push-off, so never remove both.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Foot & Ankle chapter (sesamoid disorders)

stem: A 47-year-old woman has a painful prominence over the lateral aspect of the fifth metatarsal head (bunionette) that rubs in shoes. Weight-bearing radiographs show a normal fifth metatarsal head but lateral bowing of the fifth metatarsal shaft and an increased 4-5 intermetatarsal angle. Conservative measures have failed. Which surgical procedure is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Isolated lateral condylectomy (lateral eminence resection) of the fifth metatarsal head

options.label: B
options.text: Distal fifth metatarsal (chevron) osteotomy

options.label: C
options.text: Diaphyseal (oblique/scarf-type) fifth metatarsal osteotomy

options.label: D
options.text: Fifth metatarsal head resection

options.label: E
options.text: Fifth metatarsophalangeal joint arthrodesis

explanation: Bunionette deformities are classified (Coughlin) by mechanism. Type 1 is an enlarged lateral fifth metatarsal head (treated by lateral condylectomy). Type 2 is lateral bowing of the metatarsal shaft. Type 3 is a widened 4-5 IMA. When the deformity arises from shaft bowing or a wide 4-5 IMA (types 2 and 3), the correction must be made along the diaphysis, so a diaphyseal (oblique/scarf-type) osteotomy that rotates/translates the head medially is most appropriate. An isolated condylectomy suits a type 1 (enlarged head) only and would under-correct here; a distal chevron handles a modest deformity but has limited capacity to reduce a large IMA. Head resection and arthrodesis are not first-line.
learningPoint: Match the bunionette osteotomy to the Coughlin type: condylectomy for an enlarged head, distal osteotomy for mild deformity, diaphyseal osteotomy for shaft bowing or a wide 4-5 IMA.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Foot & Ankle chapter (bunionette)

stem: Bunionette (tailor's bunion) deformities are commonly grouped using the Coughlin radiographic classification, which guides treatment. Which description correctly matches a type 1 bunionette?

options.label: A
options.text: An enlarged or prominent lateral fifth metatarsal head (or dumbbell-shaped head) with a normal 4-5 intermetatarsal angle

options.label: B
options.text: Lateral bowing of the distal fifth metatarsal shaft with a normal head

options.label: C
options.text: An increased 4-5 intermetatarsal angle with a widely splayed forefoot

options.label: D
options.text: A congruent fifth metatarsophalangeal joint with medial deviation of the toe

options.label: E
options.text: Plantar keratosis under the fifth metatarsal head from a plantarflexed ray

explanation: In the Coughlin classification, a type 1 bunionette is an enlarged or prominent lateral fifth metatarsal head (or a dumbbell-shaped head/prominent lateral condyle) with an otherwise normal 4-5 intermetatarsal angle, and is amenable to lateral eminence (condylar) resection. Type 2 is lateral bowing of the distal fifth metatarsal shaft, and type 3 is an increased 4-5 IMA. Recognising the type determines whether simple condylectomy suffices or whether a distal versus diaphyseal osteotomy is needed to realign the ray.
learningPoint: Type 1 bunionette = enlarged lateral fifth metatarsal head with a normal 4-5 IMA, treatable by condylectomy.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Foot & Ankle chapter (bunionette classification)

stem: A 56-year-old overweight woman has a progressively flattening right foot with medial ankle pain. On standing she has a planovalgus foot with forefoot abduction (a positive 'too many toes' sign) and cannot perform a single-leg heel raise on that side. When you correct the hindfoot manually the heel passively returns to neutral and there is no fixed deformity, and the ankle joint is well preserved. According to the Johnson and Strom classification (as modified), which stage of tibialis posterior tendon dysfunction is this?

options.label: A
options.text: Stage I

options.label: B
options.text: Stage II

options.label: C
options.text: Stage III

options.label: D
options.text: Stage IV

options.label: E
options.text: Not classifiable; this is a congenital flatfoot

explanation: Stage II tibialis posterior dysfunction is a flexible planovalgus deformity: the patient cannot perform a single-leg heel raise (or the heel fails to invert), there is forefoot abduction with the 'too many toes' sign, but the hindfoot is still passively correctable to neutral. Stage I has a normal-shaped foot with tendinitis and an intact heel raise. Stage III is a rigid, fixed hindfoot deformity. Stage IV adds valgus tilt of the talus within the ankle mortise (ankle involvement). Reconstruction in stage II typically combines an FDL transfer with a medialising calcaneal osteotomy, with lateral column lengthening added for marked abduction.
learningPoint: Stage II PTTD is a flexible, passively correctable planovalgus foot with a failed single-leg heel raise; stage III is rigid and stage IV involves the ankle.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Foot & Ankle chapter (adult acquired flatfoot, Johnson & Strom staging)

stem: A 52-year-old man has stage II tibialis posterior tendon dysfunction with a flexible planovalgus foot, hindfoot valgus and moderate forefoot abduction. He has failed an orthosis and bracing programme. Which combination of procedures is the most appropriate joint-preserving reconstruction?

options.label: A
options.text: Isolated tibialis posterior tendon debridement

options.label: B
options.text: Triple arthrodesis (subtalar, talonavicular and calcaneocuboid fusion)

options.label: C
options.text: Flexor digitorum longus transfer combined with a medialising calcaneal osteotomy (with lateral column lengthening if abduction is marked)

options.label: D
options.text: Subtalar arthroereisis alone

options.label: E
options.text: Ankle arthrodesis

explanation: Flexible (stage II) deformity that has failed conservative care is reconstructed with joint-preserving surgery: an FDL tendon transfer to restore dynamic inversion power plus a medialising (medial displacement) calcaneal osteotomy to correct hindfoot valgus and protect the transfer. Significant forefoot abduction is addressed by adding a lateral column lengthening (Evans), and a fixed forefoot supination by a medial cuneiform (Cotton) osteotomy. A triple arthrodesis is reserved for rigid stage III disease, and ankle fusion for stage IV ankle involvement. Tendon debridement alone is inadequate once deformity is established, and arthroereisis is not the definitive adult reconstruction.
learningPoint: Stage II PTTD reconstruction = FDL transfer plus medialising calcaneal osteotomy, with lateral column lengthening for abduction; arthrodesis is for rigid (stage III) deformity.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Foot & Ankle chapter (adult acquired flatfoot reconstruction)

stem: A 19-year-old man with bilateral high-arched feet and frequent ankle sprains is found to have hindfoot varus on standing. You perform the Coleman block test: with a block placed under the lateral border of the foot (leaving the first ray to drop), the heel corrects from varus into valgus. What does this finding indicate?

options.label: A
options.text: The hindfoot varus is rigid and a calcaneal osteotomy is mandatory

options.label: B
options.text: The hindfoot is flexible and driven by a plantarflexed first ray (forefoot-driven deformity)

options.label: C
options.text: There is a fixed subtalar joint requiring triple arthrodesis

options.label: D
options.text: The deformity is primarily at the ankle joint

options.label: E
options.text: The tibialis posterior tendon is ruptured

explanation: The Coleman block test assesses whether hindfoot varus in a cavus foot is flexible and forefoot-driven. By supporting the lateral foot on a block and allowing the plantarflexed first ray to drop off the edge, the tripod effect of the first ray is removed; if the heel then swings into valgus, the subtalar joint is flexible and the varus is being driven by the plantarflexed first ray. Treatment can then focus on the forefoot, for example a dorsiflexion (closing-wedge) osteotomy of the first metatarsal with appropriate soft-tissue balancing, rather than a calcaneal osteotomy. If the heel stays in varus, the hindfoot is rigid and needs a bony hindfoot procedure such as a lateralising calcaneal osteotomy.
learningPoint: A positive Coleman block test (heel corrects to valgus) means flexible, forefoot-driven hindfoot varus, so correction targets the plantarflexed first ray.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Foot & Ankle chapter (pes cavus, Coleman block test)

stem: A 17-year-old presents with bilateral, progressive pes cavovarus, clawing of the toes, distal wasting and absent ankle reflexes; an older sibling has similar feet. Which underlying diagnosis most likely accounts for this presentation, and what is the appropriate next investigation?

options.label: A
options.text: Idiopathic flexible flatfoot; arrange weight-bearing radiographs only

options.label: B
options.text: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy); arrange neurological assessment with nerve conduction studies and genetic testing

options.label: C
options.text: Cerebral palsy; arrange an urgent brain MRI

options.label: D
options.text: Tarsal coalition; arrange a CT of the hindfoot

options.label: E
options.text: Simple ligamentous laxity; reassure and discharge

explanation: Bilateral, progressive cavovarus with toe clawing, symmetrical distal (intrinsic and peroneal) wasting, absent reflexes and a positive family history is the classic presentation of Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease, the commonest hereditary cause of pes cavus. Most adolescent and adult cavus feet have an underlying neurological cause, so a new or progressive cavus foot warrants a full neurological work-up including nerve conduction studies and genetic testing. A strictly unilateral cavus foot should additionally raise suspicion of a spinal cord lesion (e.g. tethered cord or tumour), prompting spinal MRI. Recognising the muscle imbalance (typically weak tibialis anterior and peroneus brevis with relatively strong peroneus longus and tibialis posterior) guides reconstruction.
learningPoint: A progressive/bilateral cavus foot is neurological until proven otherwise; CMT is the commonest cause, so investigate with neurology, nerve conduction studies and genetics (and spinal MRI if unilateral).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Foot & Ankle and Paediatrics chapters (pes cavus, Charcot-Marie-Tooth)

stem: A 13-year-old presents with recurrent ankle sprains and a painful, rigid flatfoot. On examination the hindfoot is in valgus and does not invert when he goes up on tiptoe, and subtalar motion is markedly reduced. Plain radiographs show a 'C sign' and talar beaking. Which is the most likely diagnosis and the single best confirmatory investigation?

options.label: A
options.text: Calcaneonavicular coalition; confirm with an oblique foot radiograph showing the 'anteater nose' sign

options.label: B
options.text: Talocalcaneal (subtalar) coalition; confirm with CT of the hindfoot

options.label: C
options.text: Tibialis posterior tendon rupture; confirm with ultrasound

options.label: D
options.text: Juvenile idiopathic arthritis of the subtalar joint; confirm with MRI of both feet

options.label: E
options.text: Vertical talus; confirm with a forced-plantarflexion lateral radiograph

explanation: A rigid peroneal-spastic flatfoot in an adolescent with loss of subtalar motion, a radiographic 'C sign' and talar beaking points to a talocalcaneal (subtalar) coalition, most often at the middle facet. CT is the best investigation to define a talocalcaneal coalition and to quantify the size of the bar (which influences whether resection or arthrodesis is appropriate), while MRI is particularly useful for non-osseous (fibrous/cartilaginous) coalitions. A calcaneonavicular coalition typically shows the 'anteater nose' sign on the oblique foot view; it is a reasonable differential but the C sign and middle-facet features here favour a talocalcaneal bar. Coalitions usually become symptomatic in early adolescence as the bar ossifies.
learningPoint: A rigid adolescent flatfoot with a C sign and reduced subtalar motion suggests a talocalcaneal coalition, best characterised by CT (MRI for fibrous/cartilaginous bars).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Paediatrics/Foot & Ankle chapter (tarsal coalition)

stem: Theme: Investigation and management of tarsal coalition. For each scenario, select the single most appropriate next step from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Oblique (45-degree) foot radiograph

options.label: B
options.text: CT of both hindfeet

options.label: C
options.text: MRI of the hindfoot

options.label: D
options.text: Coalition resection with interposition graft

options.label: E
options.text: Subtalar or triple arthrodesis

options.label: F
options.text: Initial non-operative management (immobilisation/orthosis) and activity modification

options.label: G
options.text: Forced-plantarflexion lateral radiograph

emiLeads.stem: A 12-year-old with a painful rigid flatfoot and a suspected calcaneonavicular bar on a standard lateral film; you want the single radiographic view most likely to demonstrate the 'anteater nose' sign.

A 13-year-old has clinical features of a coalition but plain films are equivocal and you suspect a non-osseous (fibrous or cartilaginous) bar; you want the investigation that best shows this and any associated marrow oedema.

A 14-year-old with a confirmed symptomatic calcaneonavicular coalition, no degenerative change in the surrounding joints, who has failed a period of immobilisation and wants to preserve hindfoot motion.
An adolescent with a large talocalcaneal coalition involving most of the posterior facet and established secondary subtalar degenerative change with persistent pain.
An 11-year-old presents with a first painful episode and a small coalition on imaging but no fixed deformity or arthritis; choose the most appropriate initial management.

explanation: Symptomatic tarsal coalition is initially managed non-operatively with immobilisation/orthoses and activity modification. Imaging is tailored to the suspected coalition: the oblique foot view best shows a calcaneonavicular bar (anteater nose sign); CT best defines a talocalcaneal bar and the proportion of the facet involved; and MRI is best for non-osseous (fibrous/cartilaginous) coalitions and associated marrow oedema. For surgery, resection with interposition (e.g. fat or extensor digitorum brevis) preserves motion when the coalition is small and the joints are not arthritic, whereas arthrodesis is indicated for a large coalition or established secondary degenerative change. A forced-plantarflexion lateral view is used to diagnose a congenital vertical talus, not a coalition.
learningPoint: Investigate coalitions by type (oblique view for calcaneonavicular, CT for talocalcaneal, MRI for fibrous bars); resect small non-arthritic bars, fuse large bars or arthritic joints, after a trial of non-operative care.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Paediatrics/Foot & Ankle chapter (tarsal coalition)

stem: A 44-year-old recreational badminton player presents 4 hours after feeling a sudden blow to the back of the right calf during a lunge, followed by difficulty pushing off. He has no significant comorbidities and is a non-smoker. On examination there is a palpable gap 4 cm proximal to the calcaneal insertion and the Simmonds-Thompson (calf squeeze) test fails to plantarflex the foot. Plain radiographs are normal. Which is the most appropriate initial management?

options.label: A
options.text: Immobilise the foot in equinus and refer to a foot and ankle service for functional rehabilitation under an agreed treatment pathway

options.label: B
options.text: Urgent open end-to-end Achilles repair within 24 hours to prevent re-rupture

options.label: C
options.text: Below-knee plaster in neutral (plantigrade) with immediate weight-bearing as tolerated

options.label: D
options.text: MRI of the Achilles tendon to confirm the diagnosis before any treatment decision

options.label: E
options.text: Percutaneous repair as a day case, as conservative care has an unacceptably high re-rupture rate

explanation: This is a clinical diagnosis of acute Achilles rupture (palpable gap plus positive calf-squeeze test); imaging is not routinely required to confirm it. Current UK practice favours non-operative functional rehabilitation as the default for most patients, immobilising the foot in equinus (a plantarflexed cast or functional orthosis with heel wedges) to appose the tendon ends, then progressing through a structured weight-bearing protocol. Meta-analyses and large RCTs show that with accelerated functional rehabilitation the re-rupture rate is comparable to surgery while avoiding wound and nerve complications. (The UKSTAR trial compared plaster cast with functional bracing, both non-operative, and found equivalent outcomes.) Casting in neutral would leave the tendon ends gapped. Surgery is reserved for selected patients after shared decision-making (e.g. late presentation, large persistent gap, certain high-demand athletes).
learningPoint: Acute Achilles rupture is a clinical diagnosis; functional rehabilitation with the foot in equinus is the default UK first-line treatment, with re-rupture rates similar to surgery.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics – Foot and Ankle (Achilles tendon)
UKSTAR trial (plaster cast vs functional brace), Lancet 2020

stem: A 58-year-old man with a BMI of 34 has had 9 months of pain and swelling 5 cm proximal to the Achilles insertion that has failed eccentric loading, activity modification and a heel raise. MRI shows fusiform thickening of the mid-substance tendon with intrasubstance degeneration affecting an estimated 60% of the cross-section, but no calcific change and no insertional involvement. He wishes to discuss surgery. Which operative strategy is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Debridement of the diseased tendon with augmentation using a flexor hallucis longus (FHL) tendon transfer

options.label: B
options.text: Insertional debridement with detachment and reattachment of the Achilles using suture anchors

options.label: C
options.text: Gastrocnemius recession (Strayer procedure) alone

options.label: D
options.text: Percutaneous longitudinal tenotomies only

options.label: E
options.text: Extracorporeal shockwave therapy as the definitive treatment

explanation: This is recalcitrant non-insertional (mid-substance) Achilles tendinopathy with extensive degeneration. When more than roughly 50% of the tendon cross-section is diseased, simple debridement leaves insufficient healthy tendon, so debridement is combined with augmentation — the FHL transfer is the workhorse, adding vascularised muscle and restoring plantarflexion power. Insertional debridement with reattachment (B) is for insertional disease/Haglund pathology, not mid-substance. Gastrocnemius recession addresses a tight gastrocnemius and may be an adjunct but does not treat the degenerate tendon segment here. Percutaneous tenotomies suit milder, more localised disease. Shockwave is a non-operative option that has effectively been bypassed by the failed conservative course and the decision to operate.
learningPoint: In non-insertional Achilles tendinopathy with >50% tendon involvement, debridement should be augmented (commonly an FHL transfer) rather than performed alone.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics – Foot and Ankle (Achilles tendinopathy)

stem: A 27-year-old footballer describes a painful snapping sensation at the lateral aspect of the ankle, behind the lateral malleolus, that occurs when he pushes off and circles the foot. It began after a forced dorsiflexion injury with the foot inverted. There is tenderness posterior to the fibula and he can reproduce a visible, palpable subluxation of the tendons over the tip of the lateral malleolus on resisted dorsiflexion and eversion. Which structure has most likely failed to produce this presentation?

options.label: A
options.text: Superior peroneal retinaculum

options.label: B
options.text: Anterior talofibular ligament

options.label: C
options.text: Calcaneofibular ligament

options.label: D
options.text: Inferior extensor retinaculum

options.label: E
options.text: Peroneus brevis muscle belly

explanation: Recurrent painful snapping of the peroneal tendons over the lateral malleolus, provoked by resisted dorsiflexion/eversion after a forced dorsiflexion injury, is the classic picture of peroneal tendon subluxation/dislocation. The superior peroneal retinaculum is the primary restraint holding the tendons in the retromalleolar groove; its avulsion or attenuation (often with a shallow or convex groove) allows the tendons to sublux anteriorly over the fibula. The ATFL and CFL are lateral ankle stabilisers and their rupture causes mechanical instability, not tendon snapping. The inferior extensor retinaculum is on the dorsum and is used in the Broström-Gould repair. The brevis muscle belly is not a restraining structure.
learningPoint: Painful snapping of the peroneal tendons over the lateral malleolus reflects superior peroneal retinaculum failure, often with a shallow retromalleolar groove.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics – Foot and Ankle (peroneal tendon disorders)

stem: A 40-year-old hill walker has chronic lateral ankle and hindfoot pain. MRI shows a longitudinal split tear of peroneus brevis with surrounding tenosynovitis, and importantly demonstrates a low-lying peroneus brevis muscle belly together with a peroneus quartus accessory muscle within the retromalleolar groove. At surgery, after debriding and tubularising the brevis split, which additional step most directly addresses the underlying mechanical cause of the tear?

options.label: A
options.text: Excision of the peroneus quartus and debulking of the low-lying muscle belly to decompress the retromalleolar space

options.label: B
options.text: Calcaneofibular ligament reconstruction with a free tendon graft

options.label: C
options.text: Lateralising (valgus-producing) calcaneal osteotomy

options.label: D
options.text: Tenodesis of peroneus brevis to peroneus longus

options.label: E
options.text: Lengthening of the gastrocnemius via a Strayer procedure

explanation: Overcrowding of the retromalleolar groove by a low-lying peroneus brevis muscle belly and/or an accessory peroneus quartus raises pressure on the tendons and is a recognised cause of attritional longitudinal brevis tears. Excising the quartus and debulking the low-lying belly decompresses the space and removes the mechanical driver, complementing tubularisation of the split. Tenodesis to longus is reserved for tears involving more than about 50% of the tendon cross-section that are not reconstructable. A calcaneal osteotomy is indicated only when hindfoot malalignment overloads the tendons (a hindfoot varus would be corrected with a lateralising/valgus-producing osteotomy), which is not the issue here. CFL reconstruction and gastrocnemius recession do not address the crowded groove.
learningPoint: A low-lying brevis muscle belly or a peroneus quartus crowds the retromalleolar groove and causes attritional brevis tears; debulking/excision decompresses the space.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics – Foot and Ankle (peroneal tendon tears)

stem: A 56-year-old woman has a 2-year history of progressive medial ankle pain and a flattening right foot. On standing there is loss of the medial arch, hindfoot valgus and forefoot abduction such that more than two toes are visible from behind ('too many toes' sign). She is unable to perform a single-limb heel raise. Critically, the hindfoot valgus passively corrects to neutral and the subtalar and transverse tarsal joints are supple and non-arthritic. Which is the most appropriate surgical reconstruction?

options.label: A
options.text: Medialising calcaneal osteotomy with flexor digitorum longus (FDL) transfer to the navicular

options.label: B
options.text: Triple arthrodesis (subtalar, talonavicular and calcaneocuboid fusion)

options.label: C
options.text: Isolated subtalar arthrodesis

options.label: D
options.text: Tibialis posterior tendon synovectomy alone

options.label: E
options.text: Tibiotalocalcaneal arthrodesis with a retrograde nail

explanation: This is a flexible (correctable), non-arthritic adult acquired flatfoot from tibialis posterior dysfunction — stage II in the Johnson and Strom classification. Joint-preserving reconstruction is indicated: a medialising calcaneal osteotomy realigns the hindfoot under the tibia and offloads the failing tendon, combined with an FDL tendon transfer to substitute for the deficient tibialis posterior. Additional procedures (e.g. lateral column lengthening, spring ligament repair, Cotton osteotomy) are added according to the deformity. Triple arthrodesis is reserved for rigid (stage III) or arthritic deformity, which this patient does not have. Synovectomy alone treats only early stage I disease. A tibiotalocalcaneal nail is for combined ankle and hindfoot arthritis/deformity, far beyond this presentation.
learningPoint: Flexible, non-arthritic tibialis posterior dysfunction (stage II) is treated with joint-preserving surgery: medialising calcaneal osteotomy plus FDL transfer.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics – Foot and Ankle (adult acquired flatfoot)

stem: A 70-year-old man presents with a 3-month history of a painless 'slapping' gait and a small lump on the front of the right ankle. He denies any acute injury. On examination he has a foot drop with weak dorsiflexion, a high-stepping gait, and a soft pseudotumour over the anterior ankle. Sensation and the remaining motor groups are intact, and the spine and peripheral nerves are normal. Which is the most likely diagnosis?

options.label: A
options.text: Spontaneous (closed) tibialis anterior tendon rupture

options.label: B
options.text: Common peroneal (fibular) nerve palsy

options.label: C
options.text: L5 radiculopathy

options.label: D
options.text: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease

options.label: E
options.text: Extensor hallucis longus rupture

explanation: Atraumatic or low-energy tibialis anterior rupture typically occurs in patients over 60 and presents with a relatively painless foot drop, a drop-foot/slapping gait and a characteristic anterior ankle pseudotumour formed by the retracted, bunched tendon stump under the extensor retinaculum. Critically, sensation is preserved and there is no other neurological deficit, which distinguishes it from a common peroneal nerve palsy (which also causes eversion weakness and sensory loss over the dorsum/first web space) and from an L5 radiculopathy (back/leg pain, dermatomal sensory change). Charcot-Marie-Tooth produces a cavovarus foot with distal wasting and is usually bilateral and longstanding. EHL rupture causes isolated weak great-toe extension, not a global foot drop.
learningPoint: Painless foot drop with an anterior ankle pseudotumour and intact sensation in an older patient is spontaneous tibialis anterior rupture, not a nerve palsy.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics – Foot and Ankle (tibialis anterior rupture)

stem: Theme: Diagnosis of foot pain with a neurological component. For each clinical presentation, select the SINGLE most likely diagnosis from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Morton (interdigital) neuroma

options.label: B
options.text: Tarsal tunnel syndrome

options.label: C
options.text: Plantar fasciitis

options.label: D
options.text: Freiberg disease

options.label: E
options.text: Metatarsal stress fracture

options.label: F
options.text: First metatarsophalangeal joint osteoarthritis (hallux rigidus)

options.label: G
options.text: Lisfranc injury

options.label: H
options.text: S1 radiculopathy

emiLeads.stem: A 45-year-old woman has burning pain radiating into the third and fourth toes, relieved by removing her shoes and massaging the forefoot. Compressing the forefoot while pressing the third web space reproduces the pain with a palpable click.

A 50-year-old runner has burning, tingling pain on the sole and medial heel that worsens with activity and at night. Tapping behind the medial malleolus reproduces paraesthesiae radiating into the sole, and there is no first-step heel pain.

A 38-year-old recreational runner has sharp, well-localised pain over the dorsum of the second metatarsal shaft after increasing mileage, with focal bony tenderness and swelling; initial radiographs are normal.

A 48-year-old man has heel pain that is worst with the first few steps in the morning and after rising from sitting, with maximal tenderness at the medial calcaneal tubercle and no neurological symptoms.

explanation: Morton neuroma gives interdigital burning pain (commonly the third web space) with a positive Mulder click on forefoot compression. Tarsal tunnel syndrome is compression of the posterior tibial nerve behind the medial malleolus, producing burning plantar/medial heel paraesthesiae, often worse at night, with a positive percussion (Tinel) sign — distinct from the mechanical first-step pain of plantar fasciitis. A metatarsal stress fracture gives focal shaft tenderness with initially normal radiographs that later show callus. Plantar fasciitis is mechanical heel pain maximal at the medial calcaneal tubercle, worst with the first steps in the morning, without neurology.
learningPoint: Localise foot pain by symptom pattern: interdigital click (Morton), positive Tinel behind the medial malleolus (tarsal tunnel), first-step medial heel pain (plantar fasciitis).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics – Foot and Ankle (nerve entrapments and heel pain)

stem: A 49-year-old woman has a clinically and ultrasonographically confirmed third web space Morton neuroma measuring 7 mm. She has already had 6 months of broad, low-heeled footwear, a metatarsal dome offloading pad and activity modification without relief, and a single ultrasound-guided corticosteroid injection gave only 4 weeks of benefit. She wishes to proceed to surgery. When counselling her about excision of the interdigital neuroma, which is the most important specific complication to discuss?

options.label: A
options.text: A permanent area of numbness in the cleft between the affected toes

options.label: B
options.text: Avascular necrosis of the metatarsal head

options.label: C
options.text: Progressive hallux valgus deformity

options.label: D
options.text: Rupture of the plantar plate of the adjacent joint

options.label: E
options.text: Claw-toe deformity of all lesser toes

explanation: Excision of an interdigital neuroma divides and removes the common digital nerve, so the inevitable and expected consequence is a permanent patch of numbness in the web/cleft and adjacent borders of the two toes supplied — this must be specifically consented for, alongside the risks of a painful recurrent (stump) neuroma and incomplete pain relief. AVN of the metatarsal head describes Freiberg disease and is unrelated. Neuroma excision does not cause hallux valgus or generalised claw-toe deformity, and it does not rupture the plantar plate. UK practice is to exhaust conservative measures (footwear, offloading, activity change, with or without injection) before excision, which this patient has done.
learningPoint: Always consent specifically for permanent web-space numbness — and the risk of a recurrent stump neuroma — before interdigital neuroma excision.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics – Foot and Ankle (Morton neuroma)
BOFAS patient information on Morton's neuroma

stem: A 35-year-old man has a 6-month history of burning pain and paraesthesiae on the sole of the foot, worse on activity, with a positive Tinel sign behind the medial malleolus. He has been advised to undergo tarsal tunnel decompression. Which investigation finding would most strongly support proceeding with surgical release rather than continuing non-operative management?

options.label: A
options.text: MRI demonstrating a ganglion arising from the subtalar joint compressing the tibial nerve within the tunnel

options.label: B
options.text: Plain radiographs showing a calcaneal spur at the plantar fascia origin

options.label: C
options.text: Weight-bearing radiographs showing a pes planus deformity

options.label: D
options.text: Ultrasound showing thickening of the plantar fascia to 6 mm

options.label: E
options.text: Nerve conduction studies that are entirely normal

explanation: Surgical release of the tarsal tunnel gives its best and most predictable results when a discrete, identifiable space-occupying lesion is compressing the tibial nerve — a ganglion, lipoma, varicosities, an accessory muscle or osteophyte. MRI demonstration of such a lesion is the strongest indication to operate, as decompression removes the cause. Idiopathic tarsal tunnel syndrome with no structural lesion responds far less reliably to surgery. A plantar calcaneal spur and a thickened plantar fascia point towards plantar fasciitis, not tarsal tunnel syndrome. Pes planus may be a contributing factor but is not itself an indication for release. Entirely normal nerve conduction studies do not support, and may argue against, a compressive neuropathy requiring decompression.
learningPoint: Tarsal tunnel decompression is most successful when imaging shows a discrete space-occupying lesion; idiopathic cases respond poorly to surgery.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics – Foot and Ankle (tarsal tunnel syndrome)

stem: A 52-year-old labourer has end-stage post-traumatic right ankle osteoarthritis following a malunited pilon fracture 15 years ago. He has severe pain, a fixed equinus contracture, and obvious varus malalignment of the hindfoot. The adjacent subtalar and midfoot joints are clinically and radiographically well preserved. He does heavy manual work and wishes to return to it. Which is the most appropriate definitive surgical option?

options.label: A
options.text: Ankle (tibiotalar) arthrodesis

options.label: B
options.text: Total ankle replacement

options.label: C
options.text: Tibiotalocalcaneal arthrodesis with a retrograde nail

options.label: D
options.text: Supramalleolar osteotomy

options.label: E
options.text: Subtalar arthrodesis

explanation: Isolated tibiotalar arthrodesis remains the benchmark for end-stage ankle arthritis in a young, heavy-manual worker, especially with significant deformity (varus, equinus) that can be corrected at fusion, and with healthy adjacent joints that can compensate for lost ankle motion. It is durable and reliably relieves pain. Total ankle replacement is relatively contraindicated by his age, high physical demand, occupation and fixed deformity, and registry data show higher early revision rates than hip or knee arthroplasty. A tibiotalocalcaneal fusion would needlessly sacrifice a healthy subtalar joint. A supramalleolar osteotomy is a joint-preserving option for asymmetric arthritis with a substantial preserved joint surface, not for end-stage global degeneration. Isolated subtalar fusion does not address the diseased ankle.
learningPoint: For end-stage ankle arthritis in a young heavy-manual worker with deformity and healthy adjacent joints, ankle arthrodesis remains the gold standard over replacement.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics – Foot and Ankle (ankle arthritis)
National Joint Registry (NJR) ankle replacement data
BOFAS guidance on end-stage ankle arthritis

stem: A 68-year-old retired teacher with primary ankle osteoarthritis is being counselled in clinic. She has low physical demands, a well-aligned plantigrade ankle with only mild coronal deformity, good bone stock, intact peripheral pulses and protective sensation, and — importantly — established arthritis of the ipsilateral subtalar and talonavicular joints. She asks why you are recommending a total ankle replacement rather than an ankle fusion. Which statement provides the best evidence-based rationale?

options.label: A
options.text: Total ankle replacement preserves ankle motion, which reduces the additional load transmitted to her already-arthritic adjacent hindfoot joints

options.label: B
options.text: Total ankle replacement has a lower revision rate than ankle arthrodesis at 10 years

options.label: C
options.text: Ankle fusion is contraindicated in anyone over 65 years of age

options.label: D
options.text: Total ankle replacement allows immediate unprotected full weight-bearing on the day of surgery

options.label: E
options.text: Ankle fusion has a higher rate of deep infection than total ankle replacement

explanation: A key argument for total ankle replacement over arthrodesis is preservation of tibiotalar motion, which limits the compensatory overload on neighbouring joints. This matters most when the subtalar and transverse tarsal joints are already arthritic — fusing the ankle would tend to accelerate degeneration and pain in those joints. This patient is also a good arthroplasty candidate: older, low-demand, well-aligned with good bone stock and intact vascularity/sensation. Replacement does not have a lower revision rate than fusion at 10 years (registry data generally show the opposite or at best equivalence), fusion is certainly not contraindicated by age alone, replacement requires a period of protected weight-bearing, and there is no reliable evidence that fusion carries a higher deep-infection rate than replacement.
learningPoint: Pre-existing adjacent hindfoot arthritis favours motion-preserving total ankle replacement over fusion, which would tend to overload and accelerate those joints.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics – Foot and Ankle (total ankle replacement)
National Joint Registry (NJR) ankle replacement outcomes

stem: A 24-year-old netball player has chronic lateral ankle instability with repeated 'giving way' on uneven ground despite a dedicated 3-month course of peroneal strengthening and proprioceptive (balance) rehabilitation. Examination confirms a positive anterior drawer and talar tilt with no generalised ligamentous laxity, normal hindfoot alignment, and radiographs show no osteochondral lesion or bony abnormality. Which is the most appropriate surgical procedure?

options.label: A
options.text: Broström-Gould anatomical repair of the lateral ligament complex with inferior extensor retinaculum reinforcement

options.label: B
options.text: Chrisman-Snook tenodesis using a split or whole peroneus brevis tendon

options.label: C
options.text: Calcaneal osteotomy to correct hindfoot varus

options.label: D
options.text: Ankle arthrodesis

options.label: E
options.text: Free tendon (allograft) anatomical reconstruction of the ATFL and CFL

explanation: For chronic lateral ankle instability that has failed a proper course of rehabilitation, the first-line operation is the anatomical Broström repair augmented by the Gould modification (mobilising and suturing the inferior extensor retinaculum over the repair). It restores normal anatomy and proprioception with low morbidity and is the standard in patients with good-quality local tissue and no major malalignment. Non-anatomical tenodeses such as Chrisman-Snook sacrifice peroneal tendon and overconstrain the subtalar joint, so are reserved for revision or poor tissue. A calcaneal osteotomy is added only when hindfoot varus is driving the instability, which is excluded here. Free tendon graft reconstruction is reserved for failed repairs, deficient tissue, generalised laxity or high-demand revision cases. Fusion has no role in this young athlete.
learningPoint: The Broström-Gould anatomical repair is first-line surgery for chronic lateral ankle instability with good tissue and neutral hindfoot alignment.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics – Foot and Ankle (ankle instability)

stem: A 30-year-old man is listed for a Broström-Gould repair for chronic lateral ankle instability. Which single preoperative finding would most strongly favour augmenting the repair with a tendon graft reconstruction (rather than performing an isolated anatomical repair)?

options.label: A
options.text: A high body mass index, generalised ligamentous hyperlaxity and a previous failed lateral ligament repair

options.label: B
options.text: A first-time inversion sprain 3 weeks ago with marked swelling

options.label: C
options.text: Pain localised to the sinus tarsi with a normal anterior drawer test

options.label: D
options.text: An incidental os trigonum on the lateral radiograph

options.label: E
options.text: A mild, fully correctable pes planus

explanation: Isolated anatomical repair relies on good-quality native ligament. Factors that compromise repair quality or increase load — high BMI, generalised hyperlaxity (e.g. a high Beighton score), and especially a previously failed repair — are the recognised indications to reinforce with a tendon graft (autograft or allograft) reconstruction of the ATFL/CFL. A first-time acute sprain is managed non-operatively, not by reconstruction. Sinus tarsi pain with a normal anterior drawer suggests subtalar/sinus tarsi pathology rather than mechanical lateral instability. An os trigonum relates to posterior ankle impingement, not lateral ligament competence. A mild correctable flatfoot does not in itself mandate graft augmentation (a varus hindfoot would more likely need a bony procedure).
learningPoint: Hyperlaxity, obesity and a failed previous repair are the key indications to augment lateral ligament surgery with a tendon graft reconstruction.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics – Foot and Ankle (lateral ligament reconstruction)

stem: A 26-year-old man has persistent deep ankle pain, swelling and occasional catching 8 months after an inversion injury that was treated as a sprain. MRI shows a 9 mm osteochondral lesion of the medial talar dome with an intact but oedematous overlying cartilage cap and no displaced fragment or subchondral cyst. He has had activity modification and physiotherapy without lasting relief. Which is the most appropriate next surgical intervention?

options.label: A
options.text: Arthroscopic debridement and microfracture (bone marrow stimulation) of the lesion

options.label: B
options.text: Osteochondral autograft transfer (OATS/mosaicplasty) from the knee

options.label: C
options.text: Autologous chondrocyte implantation

options.label: D
options.text: Tibiotalar arthrodesis

options.label: E
options.text: Total ankle replacement

explanation: For a symptomatic osteochondral lesion of the talus that has failed non-operative care, first-line surgery for a small/medium lesion (generally under about 1.5 cm squared in area, and certainly a 9 mm lesion) is arthroscopic debridement of unstable cartilage and bone marrow stimulation (microfracture/microdrilling), which recruits marrow elements to form fibrocartilage and gives good results in lesions of this size. Larger lesions, deep/cystic lesions or failed microfracture are the situations where osteochondral autograft transfer or autologous chondrocyte implantation are considered, accepting the added morbidity (including knee donor-site issues with OATS). Arthrodesis and replacement are end-stage salvage options entirely inappropriate for a focal lesion in a young patient with an otherwise healthy joint.
learningPoint: Small/medium talar osteochondral lesions that fail conservative care are best treated first with arthroscopic debridement and microfracture; reconstructive grafting is for large, cystic or failed lesions.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics – Foot and Ankle (osteochondral lesions of the talus)

stem: When characterising osteochondral lesions of the talar dome, which statement best reflects the typical location and mechanism relationship that is clinically useful?

options.label: A
options.text: Medial dome lesions are usually more posterior, deeper and cup-shaped and are often atraumatic, whereas lateral dome lesions tend to be more anterior, shallower/wafer-shaped and more clearly trauma-related

options.label: B
options.text: Lateral dome lesions are usually deeper and cup-shaped and are typically atraumatic, whereas medial lesions are shallow and always trauma-related

options.label: C
options.text: All talar osteochondral lesions occur at the centre of the dome and have an identical morphology regardless of side

options.label: D
options.text: Medial lesions are exclusively caused by acute eversion injuries and never present without trauma

options.label: E
options.text: Lateral lesions are deep, cystic and located posteriorly, and are the least likely to be associated with any injury

explanation: A useful clinical generalisation is that medial talar dome lesions are typically located more posteriorly, are deeper and cup-shaped, and are frequently atraumatic (or have only a remote injury), whereas lateral dome lesions are usually more anterior, shallower and wafer-shaped, and are more consistently associated with a clear traumatic (inversion-dorsiflexion) mechanism. This pattern helps in suspecting and locating lesions on imaging. The other options invert or overstate these relationships; in particular, talar lesions are not all central with identical morphology, and neither side is 'exclusively' or 'never' trauma-related.
learningPoint: Medial talar dome lesions tend to be posterior, deep and often atraumatic; lateral lesions tend to be anterior, shallow/wafer-shaped and trauma-related.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics – Foot and Ankle (osteochondral lesions of the talus)

stem: A 58-year-old man with a 20-year history of type 2 diabetes and established peripheral neuropathy presents with a 2-week history of a red, hot, swollen right midfoot following minor exertion. There is no skin break or ulcer. He is systemically well and afebrile, inflammatory markers are only mildly raised, and weight-bearing radiographs show early fragmentation around the tarsometatarsal joints. The foot is approximately 3°C warmer than the contralateral side. Which is the most appropriate initial management?

options.label: A
options.text: Immobilisation and offloading in a total contact cast with non-weight-bearing, urgent referral to the multidisciplinary diabetic foot service, and serial monitoring of the temperature difference

options.label: B
options.text: Urgent surgical reconstruction with midfoot arthrodesis during this acute phase

options.label: C
options.text: Intravenous broad-spectrum antibiotics for presumed osteomyelitis

options.label: D
options.text: Reassurance and normal footwear, as a warm swollen foot in diabetes is benign

options.label: E
options.text: Below-knee amputation referral

explanation: This is acute (active) Charcot neuroarthropathy: a neuropathic diabetic patient with a hot, swollen, erythematous foot, a raised skin-temperature difference and early radiographic fragmentation, but no ulcer or systemic sepsis. The cornerstone of treatment in the active phase is prompt, prolonged offloading and immobilisation — classically a total contact cast — to halt fragmentation and prevent collapse, with serial monitoring of the temperature gradient and radiographs until the foot 'cools' and consolidates. UK guidance (NICE NG19) stresses urgent referral to a multidisciplinary diabetic foot service. Reconstructive surgery is reserved for the chronic/reconstructive phase (deformity, instability or recurrent ulceration), not the acute phase. There is no ulcer or systemic sepsis to justify IV antibiotics or osteomyelitis treatment, the foot is certainly not benign, and amputation is not indicated.
learningPoint: Acute Charcot foot is treated by prompt offloading/immobilisation (total contact cast) and urgent diabetic-foot-service referral, with temperature and radiographic monitoring; reconstruction waits for the consolidated phase.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics – Foot and Ankle (Charcot neuroarthropathy)
NICE NG19 – Diabetic foot problems: prevention and management

stem: A 63-year-old man with type 2 diabetes attends with a 4-week-old neuropathic plantar ulcer beneath the first metatarsal head. The ulcer probes to bone, there is surrounding cellulitis and a foul discharge, and a plain radiograph shows cortical erosion of the metatarsal head. Pedal pulses are palpable. According to current UK practice, which is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.label: A
options.text: Urgent referral to the multidisciplinary diabetic foot service for assessment, imaging and treatment of likely osteomyelitis

options.label: B
options.text: Prescribe a 1-week course of oral flucloxacillin from primary care and review in a month

options.label: C
options.text: Apply a dressing and advise the patient to keep weight off the foot, with routine follow-up in 8 weeks

options.label: D
options.text: Arrange an outpatient MRI in 6 weeks and take no other action in the interim

options.label: E
options.text: Refer for primary below-knee amputation

explanation: A diabetic foot ulcer that probes to bone, with surrounding cellulitis and radiographic cortical erosion, is highly suggestive of osteomyelitis and represents a limb-threatening diabetic foot infection. UK guidance (NICE NG19) mandates urgent referral to the specialist multidisciplinary diabetic foot service for prompt assessment, appropriate imaging (often MRI), deep tissue/bone sampling to guide antibiotics, debridement and offloading. A single short oral antibiotic course from primary care with a month's delay, a simple dressing with distant follow-up, or merely arranging a delayed scan with no interim action all dangerously under-treat a limb-threatening infection. Amputation is not the first response when the foot is potentially salvageable, particularly with palpable pulses.
learningPoint: A probe-to-bone diabetic ulcer with cellulitis and cortical erosion is a limb-threatening infection needing urgent multidisciplinary diabetic foot service referral, not primary-care antibiotics alone.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics – Foot and Ankle (diabetic foot)
NICE NG19 – Diabetic foot problems: prevention and management

stem: A 47-year-old woman who has recently increased her running has a 5-week history of plantar heel pain that is sharp with the first few steps in the morning and after periods of sitting, then eases with walking. There is point tenderness at the medial calcaneal tubercle and pain on passive dorsiflexion of the toes (windlass test). Neurological examination is normal. Which is the most appropriate initial management?

options.label: A
options.text: Activity modification, calf and plantar fascia stretching, supportive footwear with arch support/heel cushioning, and simple analgesia

options.label: B
options.text: Early plantar fascia release surgery

options.label: C
options.text: Immediate corticosteroid injection into the plantar fascia origin

options.label: D
options.text: Below-knee cast immobilisation for 6 weeks

options.label: E
options.text: Urgent MRI to exclude a calcaneal stress fracture before any treatment

explanation: This is a typical presentation of plantar fasciitis (plantar heel pain): first-step pain, medial calcaneal tubercle tenderness and a positive windlass test. The condition is self-limiting in the large majority over 6–12 months, so first-line management is conservative — load/activity modification, plantar fascia and gastrocnemius stretching, cushioned and supportive footwear or orthoses, and simple analgesia. Surgery is a last resort after prolonged failure of non-operative treatment. Corticosteroid injection is a second-line adjunct (and carries risks of fat-pad atrophy and fascial rupture), not the initial step. Routine casting is not standard. MRI is not needed initially when the history and examination are classic and there are no red flags; the positional first-step pattern argues against a calcaneal stress fracture.
learningPoint: Plantar fasciitis is self-limiting and managed first-line with stretching, footwear/orthoses, load modification and analgesia; injection and surgery are later options.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics – Foot and Ankle (plantar heel pain)

stem: A 52-year-old man has had plantar heel pain for 14 months despite excellent compliance with stretching, orthoses, footwear modification, weight loss, two corticosteroid injections and a course of extracorporeal shockwave therapy. His symptoms persist but are atypical: the burning pain is also present at rest and at night, radiates into the medial sole, and there is a positive Tinel sign just distal and inferior to the medial malleolus. Which diagnosis should now be specifically considered as a cause of his recalcitrant heel pain?

options.label: A
options.text: Entrapment of the first branch of the lateral plantar nerve (Baxter nerve)

options.label: B
options.text: Plantar fibromatosis (Ledderhose disease)

options.label: C
options.text: Insertional Achilles tendinopathy

options.label: D
options.text: Sever disease (calcaneal apophysitis)

options.label: E
options.text: Subtalar joint osteoarthritis

explanation: When 'plantar fasciitis' fails to settle despite thorough non-operative treatment and the pain takes on neuropathic features — burning pain present at rest/at night, radiation into the sole and a positive Tinel sign over the medial heel — entrapment of the first branch of the lateral plantar nerve (Baxter nerve) should be actively considered, as it is an under-recognised cause of chronic heel pain and may require decompression. Plantar fibromatosis produces palpable nodules in the fascia rather than neuropathic pain. Insertional Achilles tendinopathy causes posterior heel pain at the tendon insertion. Sever disease is a calcaneal apophysitis of skeletally immature children. Subtalar arthritis gives deep hindfoot pain with restricted, painful inversion/eversion, not a positive medial Tinel sign.
learningPoint: Recalcitrant heel pain with neuropathic features and a positive medial Tinel sign suggests Baxter nerve (first branch of lateral plantar nerve) entrapment.
