references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics – Foot and Ankle (heel pain, nerve entrapment)

stem: A 19-year-old male rugby forward sustains his first traumatic anterior shoulder dislocation, reduced in the emergency department. He is keen to return to collision sport. Examination under anaesthetic and arthroscopy confirm a Bankart lesion with no significant glenoid bone loss. When using the Instability Severity Index Score (ISIS) to decide between an arthroscopic Bankart repair and an open bony (Latarjet) procedure, which single factor in his profile contributes the MAXIMUM number of points on the score?

options.label: A
options.text: Patient age 20 years or younger at surgery

options.label: B
options.text: Participation in contact or forced-overhead sport

options.label: C
options.text: Anterior-inferior glenoid loss of contour on the AP radiograph

options.label: D
options.text: Shoulder hyperlaxity on examination

options.label: E
options.text: A Hill-Sachs lesion visible on the AP radiograph in external rotation

explanation: The ISIS is a pre-operative 10-point score. Only two items score the maximum of 2 points each: age 20 years or younger at surgery, and participation in competitive sport. Of the options offered here, only patient age ≤20 carries 2 points. Contact/forced-overhead sport (1 point), anterior-inferior glenoid loss of contour on the AP radiograph (1 point), a Hill-Sachs visible on the external-rotation AP (1 point) and hyperlaxity (1 point) each score 1 point. A total above 6 predicts an unacceptably high recurrence rate after arthroscopic Bankart repair and favours a Latarjet.
learningPoint: On the ISIS, only age ≤20 and competitive sport score 2 points each; a total >6 steers you away from arthroscopic Bankart towards Latarjet.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Shoulder instability

stem: A 35-year-old recreational climber attends clinic 10 days after a first-time traumatic anterior glenohumeral dislocation, which was reduced closed with no associated fracture. He has no neurological deficit and a stable reduction. He asks whether having the shoulder immobilised in a position of external rotation, rather than the conventional internal-rotation sling, will reduce his risk of further dislocation. What is the most evidence-based advice to give him?

options.label: A
options.text: External-rotation bracing roughly halves recurrence and should be recommended

options.label: B
options.text: External-rotation bracing has not been shown to reliably reduce recurrence over conventional slinging, and immobilisation beyond about a week confers no clear benefit

options.label: C
options.text: He should be immobilised in internal rotation for a full 6 weeks to allow the Bankart lesion to heal

options.label: D
options.text: Immediate arthroscopic stabilisation is indicated for all first-time dislocators over 30

options.label: E
options.text: Abduction–external rotation bracing for 6 weeks reliably heals the labrum and avoids surgery

explanation: Early enthusiasm for external-rotation immobilisation (Itoi) was based on the idea that ER re-approximates the Bankart lesion to the glenoid. Subsequent randomised trials and meta-analyses have not demonstrated a consistent, clinically meaningful reduction in recurrence compared with conventional internal-rotation slinging, and compliance with ER bracing is poor. Prolonged immobilisation (beyond roughly a week, used for comfort) does not reduce recurrence; the dominant recurrence driver is young age. There is no indication for routine immediate surgery in a 35-year-old first-time dislocator without other indications. The honest, evidence-based answer is that ER bracing is not reliably superior.
learningPoint: External-rotation immobilisation after first-time anterior dislocation is not reliably superior to a comfort sling; counsel on age-related recurrence risk instead.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Shoulder instability

stem: A 48-year-old man is brought in after a generalised tonic–clonic seizure. He has shoulder pain and the arm is held in fixed internal rotation and adduction; he cannot externally rotate it past neutral. An AP radiograph looks deceptively normal but shows a 'light-bulb' sign. A CT confirms a locked posterior dislocation with an anteromedial humeral head impaction (reverse Hill-Sachs) involving an estimated 30% of the articular surface, with otherwise preserved articular cartilage. Which management option is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Closed reduction and broad-arm sling in internal rotation for 6 weeks

options.label: B
options.text: Open reduction with transfer of the lesser tuberosity with subscapularis into the defect (modified McLaughlin)

options.label: C
options.text: Reverse total shoulder arthroplasty

options.label: D
options.text: Arthroscopic posterior labral repair alone

options.label: E
options.text: Benign neglect, as posterior dislocations remodel spontaneously

explanation: Locked posterior dislocation is classically missed because the AP can look near-normal (the head is fixed in internal rotation giving the symmetrical 'light-bulb' appearance); axillary/modified-axial views or CT make the diagnosis. Management is guided by the size of the reverse Hill-Sachs (anteromedial impaction) and chronicity. For defects of roughly 25–50% of the articular surface with preserved cartilage, the standard joint-preserving option is to fill the defect — the modified McLaughlin procedure transfers the lesser tuberosity (with attached subscapularis) into the defect. Defects under ~25% may be stable after reduction; very large defects (>40–50%) or chronic dislocations with head collapse in older patients move towards arthroplasty (and reverse if the cuff is deficient). Immobilising in internal rotation would re-create the deforming position, and labral repair alone does not address the engaging bony defect.
learningPoint: Suspect locked posterior dislocation after a seizure/electrocution; a 25–50% reverse Hill-Sachs with preserved cartilage is treated by modified-McLaughlin lesser-tuberosity transfer.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Posterior shoulder instability
BOAST — the diagnosis and management of shoulder dislocation

stem: A 17-year-old swimmer complains of a painful 'clunk' and a feeling that the shoulder slips out the back when she reaches forwards and across the body, with no history of injury. She can voluntarily reproduce the subluxation, and there is no apprehension anteriorly. Imaging shows mild glenoid retroversion but no labral tear. Which initial management is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Arthroscopic posterior capsulolabral plication

options.label: B
options.text: Open posterior glenoid bone block

options.label: C
options.text: Glenoid osteotomy to correct retroversion

options.label: D
options.text: A structured, prolonged programme of rotator-cuff and scapular-stabiliser rehabilitation

options.label: E
options.text: Reverse total shoulder arthroplasty

explanation: This is atraumatic/functional posterior instability with positional or muscular-pattern subluxation (Stanmore polar group II/III), characterised by absent trauma, ability to demonstrate the instability and abnormal muscle patterning. The cornerstone of treatment is a prolonged, structured physiotherapy programme targeting the rotator cuff, deltoid and periscapular muscles to restore normal recruitment; the majority improve without surgery. Surgery (and especially bony or osteotomy procedures) has high failure and complication rates when muscle patterning has not been addressed first, and is reserved for genuine structural instability that fails dedicated rehabilitation. Reverse arthroplasty has no role in a teenager.
learningPoint: Atraumatic/positional posterior instability (Stanmore II/III) is treated first with prolonged cuff and scapular-stabiliser rehab; operate only after dedicated physiotherapy fails.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Multidirectional & posterior instability

stem: A 22-year-old female with generalised joint hypermobility describes both shoulders feeling loose 'in every direction', with discomfort carrying bags and an inferior sulcus that the examiner can reproduce by pulling down on the adducted arm. There is no history of a discrete dislocation. Which clinical finding is most specific for the diagnosis of multidirectional instability?

options.label: A
options.text: A positive anterior apprehension test in abduction–external rotation

options.label: B
options.text: A positive jerk test reproducing posterior subluxation

options.label: C
options.text: A sulcus sign that persists in external rotation

options.label: D
options.text: A positive Hawkins–Kennedy impingement sign

options.label: E
options.text: A positive Gerber lift-off test

explanation: The sulcus sign reflects inferior translation and rotator-interval competence. A sulcus sign that does NOT diminish when the arm is externally rotated (i.e. persists in ER) indicates an incompetent rotator interval and is the finding most specific for true MDI, which by definition includes a symptomatic inferior component plus laxity in more than one direction. An isolated anterior apprehension or jerk test indicates predominantly unidirectional anterior or posterior instability respectively, and Hawkins–Kennedy (impingement) and lift-off (subscapularis) tests assess different pathology.
learningPoint: A sulcus sign that persists in external rotation signifies rotator-interval incompetence and is the hallmark of multidirectional instability.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Multidirectional instability

subtopic: Shoulder instability — patterns and first-line management
stem: Theme: shoulder instability. For each clinical presentation, select the single most appropriate next step in management from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Closed reduction then conventional sling, with early structured rehabilitation

options.label: B
options.text: Open coracoid transfer (Latarjet)

options.label: C
options.text: Arthroscopic Bankart repair

options.label: D
options.text: Prolonged cuff and scapular-stabiliser rehabilitation programme

options.label: E
options.text: Modified McLaughlin lesser-tuberosity transfer

options.label: F
options.text: Arthroscopic posterior capsulolabral repair

options.label: G
options.text: Reverse total shoulder arthroplasty

options.label: H
options.text: Rotator-interval closure and inferior capsular shift

emiLeads.stem: A 16-year-old gymnast with generalised ligamentous laxity has symptomatic global laxity of both shoulders, an inferior sulcus persisting in external rotation, abnormal periscapular muscle patterning and no history of true dislocation. Imaging is structurally normal.
A 24-year-old professional rugby player has had four traumatic anterior dislocations. CT shows a 25% anterior-inferior glenoid bone defect with an off-track Hill-Sachs lesion.
A 26-year-old recreational gym-goer has recurrent traumatic anterior instability with a discrete Bankart lesion, an on-track Hill-Sachs lesion and subcritical (<10%) glenoid bone loss.
A 50-year-old man with epilepsy presents with a chronic locked posterior dislocation and a 35% anteromedial humeral head impaction defect; the head and glenoid cartilage are otherwise preserved.
A 28-year-old footballer attends 5 days after a first-time traumatic anterior dislocation that was reduced; there is no fracture, no neurovascular deficit and no engaging bony lesion.

explanation: MDI with abnormal muscle patterning and structurally normal anatomy is managed first with prolonged cuff/periscapular rehabilitation (D). Recurrent anterior instability with critical glenoid loss (~20–25%+) and an off-track Hill-Sachs requires bone restoration via Latarjet (B). Recurrent traumatic anterior instability with subcritical bone loss, an on-track Hill-Sachs and a discrete Bankart is well treated by arthroscopic Bankart repair (C). A chronic locked posterior dislocation with a 25–50% reverse Hill-Sachs and preserved cartilage is filled by a modified McLaughlin transfer (E). A first-time traumatic anterior dislocation without complicating features is reduced and rehabilitated (A); surgery is reserved for recurrence or specific risk profiles.
learningPoint: Match the instability lesion to the procedure: soft-tissue Bankart for subcritical bone loss/on-track lesions, Latarjet for critical loss/off-track, McLaughlin for the locked posterior reverse Hill-Sachs, and rehab first for atraumatic MDI.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Shoulder instability

stem: In the glenoid-track concept used to plan surgery for recurrent anterior instability, the width of the glenoid track on the humeral head is estimated as approximately 0.83 times the diameter (D) of the inferior glenoid, minus the anterior glenoid bone loss. A Hill-Sachs lesion is classified as 'off-track' (engaging) when which condition is met?

options.label: A
options.text: The Hill-Sachs interval (HSI) is smaller than the glenoid track

options.label: B
options.text: The Hill-Sachs interval (HSI) is larger than the glenoid track

options.label: C
options.text: The glenoid bone loss alone exceeds 25% of the glenoid width

options.label: D
options.text: The Hill-Sachs lesion is deeper than 5 mm regardless of width

options.label: E
options.text: The glenoid track exceeds 0.83 × D

explanation: The glenoid track is the contact zone the humeral head sweeps across the glenoid in maximal abduction and external rotation; its width ≈ 0.83 × D − (anterior glenoid bone loss), where D is the inferior glenoid diameter. The Hill-Sachs interval (HSI) is the width of the Hill-Sachs lesion plus the intact bone ('bone bridge') between the rotator-cuff attachment and the lateral margin of the lesion. If the HSI is LARGER than the glenoid track, the medial edge of the lesion falls off the medial rim of the track and the lesion engages — it is 'off-track', and a soft-tissue Bankart repair alone is at high risk of failure, favouring a bony or remplissage augmentation. If the HSI is smaller than the track, the lesion stays 'on-track'.
learningPoint: Off-track (engaging) = Hill-Sachs interval > glenoid track; this predicts soft-tissue Bankart failure and pushes you towards Latarjet ± remplissage.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Glenohumeral bone loss
Di Giacomo, Itoi & Burkhart — glenoid track / on-track off-track

stem: Quantification of anterior-inferior glenoid bone loss guides the choice between soft-tissue stabilisation and a bone-augmentation procedure in recurrent anterior instability. Based on traditional biomechanical and clinical evidence, above approximately what percentage of glenoid width has bone loss conventionally been regarded as 'critical' — the level at which an isolated arthroscopic soft-tissue Bankart repair has unacceptably high failure rates and a bony procedure is favoured?
explanation: Historic teaching used a 20–25% threshold for 'critical' glenoid bone loss based on biomechanical and clinical work (Itoi, Burkhart & De Beer). More recent data show that even 'subcritical' loss (~13.5–20%) worsens outcomes after isolated arthroscopic Bankart, so the contemporary trend is to consider augmentation at lower levels and to combine soft-tissue repair with remplissage in the subcritical zone. Nevertheless, the conventionally examined threshold at which loss is deemed clearly 'critical' — and an isolated soft-tissue Bankart is expected to fail — is around 20% of the glenoid width, with bony reconstruction (Latarjet/bone block) favoured above it. 40–50% would be a very large, advanced defect.
learningPoint: Glenoid bone loss around 20% (with 13.5–20% now flagged as 'subcritical') marks the move from isolated soft-tissue Bankart towards Latarjet/bone augmentation.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Glenohumeral bone loss
Burkhart & De Beer; Shaha et al. (subcritical bone loss)

stem: A surgeon explains the Latarjet procedure to a trainee. Beyond simply replacing lost glenoid bone, which mechanism is most responsible for the 'sling' (dynamic) component of its stabilising effect?

options.label: A
options.text: The transferred coracoid block lengthening the subscapularis tendon

options.label: B
options.text: The conjoint tendon and the inferior subscapularis acting as a dynamic sling across the antero-inferior capsule as the arm is abducted and externally rotated

options.label: C
options.text: Tenodesis of the long head of biceps to the coracoid

options.label: D
options.text: Reconstruction of the coracoacromial ligament re-tensioning the cuff

options.label: E
options.text: Capsular plication alone, with the bone block being biomechanically irrelevant

explanation: The Latarjet works by three complementary mechanisms (the 'triple effect' of Patte): (1) the bony block restores glenoid arc/width; (2) the conjoint tendon passing through a split in subscapularis, together with the lower fibres of subscapularis, forms a dynamic sling that reinforces the antero-inferior capsule precisely when the arm is in the provocative abduction–external rotation position; and (3) repair of the capsule to the coraco-acromial ligament stump. The dynamic sling is widely regarded as a key contributor to stability, which is why the procedure works even with bone loss beyond what a simple block would cover. Complications to counsel about include graft/screw problems, nerve injury (musculocutaneous, axillary), non-union/resorption and later arthropathy.
learningPoint: The Latarjet 'sling effect' — conjoint tendon plus lower subscapularis reinforcing the antero-inferior capsule in abduction/ER — is a dominant dynamic stabiliser, additional to the bony block.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Latarjet procedure
Patte; Young & Walch — Latarjet triple effect

subtopic: Rotator cuff disease — natural history & non-operative care
stem: A 62-year-old woman with a 4-month history of atraumatic lateral shoulder pain has an ultrasound showing a 1.2 cm full-thickness supraspinatus tear. She has near-full active elevation, pain at night, and no pseudoparalysis. She wishes to avoid surgery if reasonable. Which statement best reflects current evidence to guide her counselling?

options.label: A
options.text: All full-thickness tears progress rapidly and should be repaired within 6 weeks to prevent arthropathy

options.label: B
options.text: A structured physiotherapy programme is a reasonable first-line treatment, and many degenerate full-thickness tears can be managed non-operatively with good symptom control, though some tears enlarge over time

options.label: C
options.text: Subacromial corticosteroid injection is contraindicated because it guarantees tendon rupture

options.label: D
options.text: A full-thickness tear of this size in this age group is best treated with primary reverse total shoulder arthroplasty

options.label: E
options.text: Tendon tears never enlarge once symptomatic, so imaging surveillance is unnecessary

explanation: Degenerate (atraumatic) full-thickness cuff tears are common with age and many remain functionally well-compensated. Guideline-level evidence (e.g. the UKUFF trial and subsequent reviews) supports a trial of structured physiotherapy as reasonable first-line care for many such tears, particularly without pseudoparalysis or acute traumatic loss of function. Some tears do enlarge over time and a subset progress, so safety-netting and review are appropriate, and a judicious subacromial steroid injection can help symptoms (it does not 'guarantee' rupture). Acute traumatic tears in younger patients, pseudoparalysis or failure of non-operative care shift towards earlier repair. RTSA is not first-line for a small repairable tear with preserved elevation.
learningPoint: Many degenerate full-thickness cuff tears can be trialled non-operatively with physiotherapy; reserve early repair for acute traumatic tears, pseudoparalysis, or failed conservative care.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Rotator cuff disease
BESS/BOA rotator cuff tears commissioning guide

subtopic: Rotator cuff repair — predictors of healing
stem: A 58-year-old man is being consented for arthroscopic repair of a full-thickness supraspinatus tear. He asks what most influences whether the repair will actually heal on the bone. Which single factor is the strongest predictor of failure of tendon-to-bone healing after rotator cuff repair?

options.label: A
options.text: Use of a single-row rather than double-row repair construct

options.label: B
options.text: Advanced fatty infiltration (high Goutallier grade) and muscle atrophy of the cuff

options.label: C
options.text: The patient's dominant hand being affected

options.label: D
options.text: Use of metal rather than bioabsorbable suture anchors

options.label: E
options.text: Repair performed arthroscopically rather than open

explanation: Re-tear/non-healing after cuff repair is multifactorial, but the strongest and most consistently reported predictors relate to the biology and chronicity of the muscle–tendon unit: advanced fatty infiltration (Goutallier/Fuchs grade ≥2–3), muscle atrophy, tear size/retraction, increasing age and smoking. Fatty infiltration is largely irreversible and correlates strongly with both failure to heal and poorer function. Construct choice (single- vs double-row) and anchor material modestly affect structural integrity and re-tear rates but are far weaker determinants than the underlying muscle quality; arthroscopic and open repair give comparable healing. Dominance is not a healing predictor.
learningPoint: Muscle quality — advanced fatty infiltration and atrophy — is the dominant predictor of cuff-repair failure, outweighing single- vs double-row construct choice.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Rotator cuff repair

subtopic: Massive / irreparable cuff tears — reconstruction options
stem: A 60-year-old manual worker has a massive posterosuperior rotator cuff tear that is deemed irreparable at arthroscopy. He has painful loss of active elevation but maintains the ability to keep his arm elevated when it is passively placed there, and radiographs show a preserved acromiohumeral interval with no glenohumeral arthritis. Which joint-preserving reconstructive option is most appropriate to restore stability of the glenohumeral fulcrum in this active patient?

options.label: A
options.text: Reverse total shoulder arthroplasty

options.label: B
options.text: Superior capsular reconstruction (SCR)

options.label: C
options.text: Hemiarthroplasty

options.label: D
options.text: Isolated subacromial decompression and debridement

options.label: E
options.text: Glenohumeral arthrodesis

explanation: In a relatively young, active patient with an irreparable posterosuperior tear, no significant arthritis, a preserved acromiohumeral interval and no static superior escape (he can maintain elevation once positioned, i.e. no fixed pseudoparalysis), joint-preserving options are preferred. Superior capsular reconstruction restores superior restraint and recentres the head, reducing superior migration and improving pain/function while preserving the joint and bone stock; tendon transfers (e.g. lower trapezius/latissimus) are alternatives depending on the deficient tendons and goals. Reverse arthroplasty is reserved for cuff-tear arthropathy or true pseudoparalysis, generally in older or lower-demand patients, and is a poor first choice in a 60-year-old manual worker without arthritis. Isolated decompression does not restore the fulcrum, and arthrodesis is a salvage procedure.
learningPoint: For an irreparable cuff tear without arthritis or fixed pseudoparalysis in an active patient, joint-preserving SCR (or tendon transfer) is preferred over reverse arthroplasty.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Massive/irreparable cuff tears

stem: A 75-year-old woman has long-standing cuff-tear arthropathy. Her radiographs show superior migration of the humeral head with a narrowed acromiohumeral interval and acetabularisation of the coracoacromial arch (a concave, eroded acromial undersurface), but no glenohumeral joint-space arthritis and no humeral head collapse. Using the Hamada classification, which grade does this pattern best correspond to?

options.label: A
options.text: Hamada grade 1

options.label: B
options.text: Hamada grade 2

options.label: C
options.text: Hamada grade 3

options.label: D
options.text: Hamada grade 4

options.label: E
options.text: Hamada grade 5

explanation: The Hamada classification grades radiographic progression of massive cuff tears: grade 1, acromiohumeral interval (AHI) >6 mm; grade 2, AHI ≤5 mm; grade 3, AHI ≤5 mm with acetabularisation of the coracoacromial arch (concave undersurface of acromion); grade 4, glenohumeral arthritis (4a without acetabularisation, 4b with acetabularisation); grade 5, humeral head osteonecrosis/collapse. This patient has a narrowed AHI with acetabularisation of the arch but NO glenohumeral arthritis and NO head collapse, which corresponds to grade 3. Acetabularisation appears at grade 3; the addition of glenohumeral arthritis would make it grade 4.
learningPoint: Hamada grading: AHI >6 mm (1), AHI ≤5 mm (2), plus acetabularisation of the arch (3), plus glenohumeral arthritis (4), to head collapse (5). Acetabularisation without arthritis = grade 3.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Cuff-tear arthropathy

stem: A 76-year-old woman with cuff-tear arthropathy has disabling pain and pseudoparalysis (active forward elevation <60°, no anterosuperior escape), with an intact and functioning deltoid. Non-operative measures have failed. Which procedure offers the most reliable improvement in pain and active elevation?

options.label: A
options.text: Anatomic total shoulder arthroplasty

options.label: B
options.text: Reverse total shoulder arthroplasty

options.label: C
options.text: Resurfacing hemiarthroplasty

options.label: D
options.text: Arthroscopic biceps tenotomy and debridement

options.label: E
options.text: Glenohumeral arthrodesis

explanation: Reverse total shoulder arthroplasty is the procedure of choice for cuff-tear arthropathy with pseudoparalysis and a functioning deltoid. By medialising and lowering the centre of rotation, it increases the deltoid moment arm and lever, allowing the deltoid to substitute for the absent cuff and reliably restore active elevation while relieving pain. Anatomic TSA is contraindicated because the absent cuff leads to eccentric glenoid loading and early 'rocking-horse' glenoid loosening. Hemiarthroplasty/resurfacing relieves pain less predictably and does not restore active elevation in pseudoparalysis. Biceps tenotomy/debridement is only a palliative low-demand option, and arthrodesis is salvage. An intact deltoid (axillary nerve) is the key prerequisite for reverse arthroplasty.
learningPoint: Cuff-tear arthropathy with pseudoparalysis and an intact deltoid is best treated by reverse total shoulder arthroplasty; anatomic TSA fails by glenoid loosening.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Reverse shoulder arthroplasty

stem: A 52-year-old office worker has had 9 months of subacromial pain with a positive Hawkins–Kennedy sign and a painful arc, but no cuff tear on imaging. He has completed a graded physiotherapy programme and a subacromial injection with partial benefit and is asking about arthroscopic subacromial decompression. Reflecting the findings of the CSAW randomised trial and current UK guidance, what is the most appropriate advice?

options.label: A
options.text: Arthroscopic subacromial decompression reliably outperforms non-operative care and should be offered now

options.label: B
options.text: High-quality randomised evidence (CSAW) showed no clinically important benefit of arthroscopic subacromial decompression over placebo arthroscopy or active monitoring, so continued non-operative management is recommended

options.label: C
options.text: He should proceed straight to rotator cuff repair

options.label: D
options.text: Decompression is mandatory to prevent progression to a full-thickness cuff tear

options.label: E
options.text: A reverse total shoulder arthroplasty is indicated for refractory impingement

explanation: The CSAW trial (Beard et al., Lancet 2018) randomised patients with subacromial pain to arthroscopic subacromial decompression, investigational arthroscopy without decompression (placebo surgery), or active monitoring. It found no clinically important difference between decompression and placebo surgery, and only small differences over no treatment that were below the threshold of clinical importance. This, with other trials, has substantially reduced the role of ASD for non-specific subacromial pain, and UK commissioning guidance now discourages routine decompression. Appropriate management remains exercise-based rehabilitation and judicious injection. There is no role for cuff repair (no tear) or arthroplasty here, and decompression is not protective against tear progression.
learningPoint: CSAW showed arthroscopic subacromial decompression is no better than placebo surgery for non-specific subacromial pain; manage with rehabilitation, not routine decompression.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Subacromial impingement

stem: A 47-year-old woman has acute, severe atraumatic shoulder pain. Radiographs show a dense, well-defined homogeneous calcific deposit in the supraspinatus tendon (Gärtner type I). After analgesia and a period of non-operative care she has persistent symptoms. Which intervention is the most appropriate next step before considering surgical excision?

options.label: A
options.text: Ultrasound-guided barbotage (needle aspiration/lavage) of the calcific deposit

options.label: B
options.text: Immediate arthroscopic excision of the deposit with cuff repair

options.label: C
options.text: Reverse total shoulder arthroplasty

options.label: D
options.text: Open acromioplasty alone

options.label: E
options.text: Prolonged systemic bisphosphonate therapy to dissolve the deposit

explanation: Calcific tendinitis is usually self-limiting and most deposits resorb spontaneously; first-line care is analgesia, NSAIDs and physiotherapy, with subacromial injection for symptom control. When symptoms persist, image-guided barbotage (ultrasound-guided needle aspiration/lavage, often with a subacromial steroid) and/or extracorporeal shockwave therapy are the appropriate next steps and avoid surgery in most patients. Dense, well-defined homogeneous (Gärtner type I) deposits are in the formative/resting phase and are less likely to resorb quickly than fluffy, ill-defined type III deposits, making them more likely to need intervention. Surgical (usually arthroscopic) excision is reserved for failure of these measures. Bisphosphonates do not dissolve these deposits, and arthroplasty/acromioplasty are inappropriate.
learningPoint: For persistent calcific tendinitis, escalate to ultrasound-guided barbotage and/or shockwave therapy before arthroscopic excision; most deposits ultimately resorb.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Calcific tendinitis

stem: A patient with calcific tendinitis of the supraspinatus presents during the acute, most painful phase of the disease. According to Uhthoff's description of the cell-mediated evolution of calcific tendinitis, which phase corresponds to this acute presentation, and what is happening at tissue level?

options.label: A
options.text: Formative phase — orderly deposition of calcium with a chalk-like, well-defined deposit and usually only mild symptoms

options.label: B
options.text: Resorptive phase — vascular invasion and macrophage-mediated removal of a soft, toothpaste-like deposit causing raised intratendinous pressure and severe pain

options.label: C
options.text: Pre-calcific phase — fibrocartilaginous metaplasia with no calcium and no pain

options.label: D
options.text: Post-calcific (healing) phase — granulation tissue and collagen remodelling replacing the deposit

options.label: E
options.text: Degenerative dystrophic phase — calcification within a pre-existing full-thickness cuff tear

explanation: Uhthoff described calcific tendinitis as a cell-mediated, self-healing process passing through pre-calcific, calcific (formative → resting → resorptive) and post-calcific phases. Pain is typically mildest in the formative/resting phases when the deposit is dense and chalk-like. The acute, severe pain occurs in the RESORPTIVE phase, when vascular channels invade and macrophages remove the deposit, which becomes soft and toothpaste-like; this raises intratendinous pressure and may rupture into the subacromial bursa. Recognising the resorptive phase is useful because it heralds spontaneous resolution, and barbotage is often easiest then. Option E describes dystrophic/degenerative calcification, a distinct entity from this reactive, self-limiting process.
learningPoint: Acute, severe pain in calcific tendinitis marks Uhthoff's resorptive phase — a soft deposit being removed by vascular/macrophage invasion, heralding spontaneous resolution.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Calcific tendinitis
Uhthoff & Loehr — pathophysiology of calcifying tendinitis

subtopic: Glenohumeral osteoarthritis — Walch classification & glenoid morphology
stem: A 68-year-old retired joiner has primary glenohumeral osteoarthritis with an intact rotator cuff and is being planned for an anatomic total shoulder replacement. The pre-operative axial CT shows posterior glenoid wear with a clearly biconcave glenoid: there is a paleoglenoid and a neoglenoid, the humeral head is posteriorly subluxated, and the measured retroversion is 22 degrees. According to the modified Walch classification, which glenoid type is described, and why does it matter for planning?

options.label: A
options.text: Type A1 — minor central wear; a standard glenoid component can be implanted without correction

options.label: B
options.text: Type A2 — major central wear with humeral head medialisation; eccentric reaming alone restores the joint line

options.label: C
options.text: Type B2 — biconcave glenoid with posterior subluxation; uncorrected retroversion and posterior bone loss risk eccentric loading and component loosening

options.label: D
options.text: Type C — dysplastic glenoid with retroversion greater than 25 degrees of developmental rather than erosive origin

options.label: E
options.text: Type D — anterior glenoid wear with anterior humeral subluxation

explanation: The modified Walch classification describes glenoid morphology in primary glenohumeral OA. A biconcave glenoid (a preserved paleoglenoid plus an eroded posterior neoglenoid) with posterior humeral subluxation is the hallmark of a B2 glenoid. Type A is centred wear (A1 minor, A2 major with medialisation); type C is a dysplastic glenoid with retroversion greater than 25 degrees that is developmental rather than erosive; type D denotes anterior wear/anteversion with anterior subluxation. B2 matters because excessive posterior wear and retroversion produce posterior eccentric ('rocking-horse') loading of an anatomic glenoid component, a recognised cause of early glenoid loosening. Strategies include corrective/eccentric reaming, posterior augmented (stepped/wedged) glenoid components, posterior bone grafting, or conversion to a reverse arthroplasty when version or bone stock cannot be adequately corrected.
learningPoint: A biconcave glenoid with posterior humeral subluxation is Walch B2; uncorrected retroversion/posterior wear drives eccentric loading and glenoid loosening, altering implant choice.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Shoulder & elbow: glenohumeral arthritis
Walch et al. modified glenoid classification (J Arthroplasty 2016)

stem: A 52-year-old recreational climber has primary glenohumeral osteoarthritis confirmed on plain radiographs (joint space loss, inferior humeral head osteophyte, subchondral sclerosis). He has a concentric (Walch A1) glenoid, a fully intact rotator cuff and good range of motion, but persistent pain limiting his activities despite a structured course of physiotherapy, NSAIDs and one intra-articular corticosteroid injection. He wishes to remain active and is concerned about glenoid component longevity. Which surgical option offers the best combination of pain relief and preservation of glenoid bone stock for future revision in a young, high-demand patient?

options.label: A
options.text: Reverse total shoulder arthroplasty

options.label: B
options.text: Anatomic total shoulder arthroplasty with a cemented all-polyethylene glenoid

options.label: C
options.text: Humeral hemiarthroplasty (or a stemless head resurfacing) without glenoid resurfacing

options.label: D
options.text: Glenohumeral arthrodesis

options.label: E
options.text: Total shoulder arthroplasty with a metal-backed glenoid component

explanation: In a young, high-demand patient the central problem is glenoid component survivorship: polyethylene glenoid loosening is the leading cause of anatomic TSA revision, and metal-backed glenoids have historically performed worse with higher failure. Anatomic TSA gives the best pain relief overall, but in a very active patient under roughly 55-60 the glenoid is the weak link. Hemiarthroplasty (or stemless head resurfacing) preserves glenoid bone stock and avoids a glenoid implant at risk, at the cost of less reliable pain relief (glenoid-sided pain can persist). Reverse arthroplasty is not indicated with an intact cuff and good motion and sacrifices more bone. Arthrodesis is a salvage option reserved for failed reconstruction or unreconstructable deficits. Head-only replacement is therefore the bone-preserving compromise here, with TSA reserved for when symptoms cannot be controlled.
learningPoint: In young, high-demand glenohumeral OA, glenoid component loosening drives revision; hemiarthroplasty/head resurfacing preserves glenoid bone stock at the price of less predictable pain relief.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Shoulder & elbow: glenohumeral arthritis treatment

stem: Eighteen months after a Grammont-style reverse total shoulder replacement for cuff-tear arthropathy, a 74-year-old woman is comfortable but a routine AP radiograph shows a defect in the inferomedial scapular neck where the polyethylene has been impinging in adduction, graded Sirveaux–Nérot 2. Which single feature of implant design or positioning most directly increases the risk of this finding?

options.label: A
options.text: A high (superiorly placed) glenosphere with superior tilt and a relatively medialised centre of rotation

options.label: B
options.text: Lateralisation of the glenosphere centre of rotation

options.label: C
options.text: An inferiorly positioned glenosphere with inferior overhang/tilt

options.label: D
options.text: Reducing the neck-shaft angle of the humeral component from 155 to 135 degrees

options.label: E
options.text: Using a larger-diameter glenosphere

explanation: Scapular (inferior) notching is the commonest radiographic complication of Grammont-type reverse arthroplasty: in adduction the medial rim of the humeral polyethylene impinges on the inferior scapular neck, producing progressive bone loss (graded by Sirveaux–Nérot). The medialised, distalised centre of rotation of the classic Grammont design predisposes to it. Risk is increased by a high (superiorly placed) glenosphere and superior tilt because this brings the polyethylene closer to the inferior neck in adduction. The accepted countermeasures all do the opposite: place the glenosphere low with slight inferior overhang and neutral/inferior tilt, lateralise the centre of rotation (bony-increased-offset or lateralised designs), use a larger glenosphere, and adopt a less-vertical (e.g. 135-degree) neck-shaft angle. Therefore the high/medialised glenosphere with superior tilt is the feature that increases notching, and the other options are protective.
learningPoint: Scapular notching is reduced by a low glenosphere with inferior tilt/overhang, lateralised centre of rotation, larger glenosphere and a less-vertical neck-shaft angle; a high, superiorly tilted glenosphere increases it.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Reverse total shoulder arthroplasty

stem: Theme: choice of shoulder arthroplasty. For each patient being planned for shoulder replacement, select the single most appropriate procedure from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Anatomic total shoulder arthroplasty

options.label: B
options.text: Reverse total shoulder arthroplasty

options.label: C
options.text: Humeral hemiarthroplasty / stemless head resurfacing (no glenoid resurfacing)

options.label: D
options.text: Glenohumeral arthrodesis

options.label: E
options.text: Resection (excision) arthroplasty

options.label: F
options.text: Two-stage revision with interim antibiotic spacer

options.label: G
options.text: Total elbow arthroplasty

emiLeads.stem: An 80-year-old woman has cuff-tear arthropathy with pseudoparalysis: she cannot actively elevate the arm above 60 degrees, the radiograph shows superior migration of the humeral head with acetabularisation of the acromion, and the deltoid is functioning. She wants improved overhead function.
A 70-year-old man has primary glenohumeral osteoarthritis with an intact rotator cuff, a concentric Walch A1 glenoid and good passive motion, with pain refractory to non-operative care.

A 35-year-old manual labourer has end-stage post-traumatic glenohumeral arthritis with an absent, unreconstructable deltoid and a non-functioning rotator cuff after multiple operations; he needs a stable, pain-free shoulder for heavy lifting and accepts a stiff joint.

A 60-year-old man re-presents 14 months after an anatomic total shoulder replacement with a deep low-grade Cutibacterium acnes peri-prosthetic infection, loose components and adequate bone stock; organism sensitivities are known.

A 55-year-old climber has glenohumeral osteoarthritis with an intact cuff but is anxious to avoid a glenoid component because of his high physical demands and concern about polyethylene loosening; he accepts that pain relief may be less predictable.

explanation: Implant choice in shoulder arthroplasty is driven chiefly by the state of the rotator cuff and deltoid, glenoid bone stock/morphology, patient age and demand, and the presence of infection. Cuff-tear arthropathy with pseudoparalysis and a functioning deltoid is the classic indication for a reverse arthroplasty, which substitutes deltoid power for the deficient cuff. Primary OA with an intact cuff and concentric glenoid is the indication for anatomic TSA. An absent/unreconstructable deltoid removes the prerequisite for a reverse replacement, so a young heavy labourer who will trade motion for a stable pain-free limb is offered arthrodesis. A confirmed deep peri-prosthetic infection with loosening is managed by staged revision with an antibiotic spacer. A very active young patient wishing to avoid a glenoid implant is offered head-only replacement to preserve glenoid bone stock. Resection arthroplasty is reserved as a salvage when reimplantation is not feasible; total elbow replacement is a distractor.
learningPoint: Shoulder arthroplasty selection hinges on the cuff and deltoid: intact cuff = anatomic TSA; deficient cuff with working deltoid and pseudoparalysis = reverse; absent deltoid/heavy demand = arthrodesis; infected = staged revision.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Shoulder arthroplasty indications

subtopic: Reverse shoulder arthroplasty — nerve injury & complications
stem: A 73-year-old woman undergoes an uncomplicated deltopectoral reverse total shoulder replacement for cuff-tear arthropathy. On the first post-operative day she is comfortable but cannot actively extend the wrist, fingers or thumb, and there is sensory loss over the dorsal first web space; elbow flexion and deltoid contraction are preserved. Which mechanism best explains this deficit?

options.label: A
options.text: Axillary nerve transection at the quadrilateral space

options.label: B
options.text: Traction neurapraxia, predominantly of the posterior cord / radial nerve, from arm lengthening and intra-operative positioning

options.label: C
options.text: Musculocutaneous nerve injury during subscapularis mobilisation

options.label: D
options.text: Suprascapular nerve injury during glenoid baseplate fixation

options.label: E
options.text: Long thoracic nerve palsy from retractor placement

explanation: Reverse arthroplasty lengthens the arm by distalising and medialising the centre of rotation, tensioning the brachial plexus. The commonest neurological complication is a traction neurapraxia, and the posterior cord/radial distribution is characteristically affected, producing wrist drop with sensory loss in the radial nerve territory (dorsal first web space) while more proximal function (deltoid, elbow flexion) is spared. Most are transient and recover with observation. An axillary nerve transection would cause deltoid paralysis and is not described here. The musculocutaneous nerve supplies elbow flexors (intact). The suprascapular nerve supplies supraspinatus/infraspinatus and would not cause wrist drop. The long thoracic nerve causes scapular winging, not a wrist drop. Excessive lengthening should therefore be avoided and a plexus-protective tension chosen.
learningPoint: Excess lengthening in reverse shoulder arthroplasty tensions the brachial plexus; a posterior cord/radial-pattern traction neurapraxia (wrist drop) is the typical, usually transient, nerve complication.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Reverse total shoulder arthroplasty complications

stem: A 54-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes has a 5-month history of an increasingly painful, stiff right shoulder. Examination shows global restriction with markedly reduced passive external rotation with the arm at the side, and radiographs are normal. Which statement best reflects the underlying pathology and expected natural history of primary adhesive capsulitis?

options.label: A
options.text: It is a degenerative tear of the rotator interval and resolves only after surgical repair

options.label: B
options.text: It is a fibroproliferative contracture, characteristically of the rotator interval and coracohumeral ligament, that is usually self-limiting over 1–3 years; diabetes is associated with a more refractory course

options.label: C
options.text: It is caused by intra-articular crystal deposition and resolves rapidly with colchicine

options.label: D
options.text: Loss of passive external rotation is explained by a posterosuperior bony block, not capsular contracture

options.label: E
options.text: It progresses inevitably to glenohumeral osteoarthritis if not operated within 6 months

explanation: Primary (idiopathic) adhesive capsulitis is a fibroproliferative/contractile disorder of the glenohumeral capsule with myofibroblast-driven contracture, characteristically involving the rotator interval and the coracohumeral ligament; this anterosuperior contracture explains the hallmark loss of passive external rotation. It classically passes through painful 'freezing', stiff 'frozen' and 'thawing' phases and is generally self-limiting over roughly 1–3 years, although a meaningful proportion retain some residual restriction. Diabetes (and thyroid disease) are strongly associated and predict a more refractory, prolonged course. It is not a crystal arthropathy, not a degenerative cuff tear requiring repair, and does not characteristically progress to osteoarthritis; a bony block does not explain the restriction. Loss of passive external rotation with normal radiographs is the discriminating sign that points to capsular contracture rather than a mechanical/intra-articular block.
learningPoint: Adhesive capsulitis is a rotator-interval/coracohumeral capsular contracture causing loss of passive external rotation; it is usually self-limiting over 1–3 years and runs a more refractory course in diabetics.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Adhesive capsulitis
BESS/BOA patient care pathway — frozen shoulder

stem: A 49-year-old non-diabetic man has a 4-month history of a painful, progressively stiff shoulder. Passive external rotation is reduced to 10 degrees and radiographs are normal. Night pain is prominent and is his dominant complaint, and he has not had any intervention. Following current UK practice for a patient in the painful (freezing) phase, which is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Early arthroscopic capsular release before any non-operative treatment

options.label: B
options.text: Analgesia plus an intra-articular glenohumeral corticosteroid injection and a supervised physiotherapy / home stretching programme, with review

options.label: C
options.text: Manipulation under anaesthesia performed at first presentation

options.label: D
options.text: Long-term oral corticosteroids continued for 6 months

options.label: E
options.text: Reassurance with no treatment, as all cases resolve fully within 3 months

explanation: First-line management of primary adhesive capsulitis, especially in the painful freezing phase when pain dominates, is non-operative: analgesia, an intra-articular glenohumeral corticosteroid injection (most effective for pain early in the disease; hydrodistension is an alternative) and a graded physiotherapy/stretching programme, with appropriate review. Surgical options — manipulation under anaesthesia and arthroscopic capsular release — are reserved for the persistently stiff (frozen) phase that fails to improve with non-operative care, not used at first presentation. Prolonged systemic corticosteroids are inappropriate because of their adverse-effect burden and limited durable benefit. It does not resolve in all patients within 3 months, so 'no treatment' is wrong when the patient has disabling pain. The UK care pathway emphasises a stepwise approach with image-guided injection and physiotherapy before considering intervention.
learningPoint: Painful-phase frozen shoulder is treated non-operatively first — analgesia, intra-articular steroid (or hydrodistension) and physiotherapy; MUA/capsular release is reserved for the persistently stiff phase.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Adhesive capsulitis management

subtopic: Acromioclavicular joint injury — Rockwood classification & management
stem: A 27-year-old cyclist falls onto the point of the shoulder. He has a prominent, tender distal clavicle. The AP and axillary radiographs show the clavicle markedly elevated relative to the acromion with the coracoclavicular distance increased by about 130% compared with the uninjured side, and the distal clavicle is buttonholed posteriorly through the trapezius on the axillary view. Which Rockwood grade is this, and what is the usual management?

options.label: A
options.text: Rockwood III — managed non-operatively in the first instance for most patients

options.label: B
options.text: Rockwood I — sprain with intact ligaments, managed with a sling

options.label: C
options.text: Rockwood IV — posterior displacement of the clavicle into/through trapezius; usually managed operatively

options.label: D
options.text: Rockwood II — AC ligament rupture with intact coracoclavicular ligaments, managed non-operatively

options.label: E
options.text: Rockwood VI — clavicle displaced inferiorly beneath the coracoid, managed non-operatively

explanation: Rockwood classifies acromioclavicular injuries by the direction and degree of clavicular displacement. Posterior displacement of the distal clavicle, buttonholing into or through the trapezius and best seen on the axillary view, defines a type IV injury. Because the posterior position is mechanically blocked and rarely reduces or remodels, type IV (along with the grossly displaced V and the rare subcoracoid VI) is generally treated operatively. Types I (sprain) and II (AC ligament rupture, intact coracoclavicular ligaments) are treated non-operatively. Type III (both AC and CC ligaments torn, CC distance increased ~25–100%) is the controversial grade that is managed non-operatively in most patients initially, with surgery considered for high-demand or persistently symptomatic cases. The axillary view is the key film for detecting the posterior displacement that distinguishes IV from III.
learningPoint: Posterior buttonholing of the distal clavicle through trapezius on the axillary view is a Rockwood IV AC injury and is usually managed operatively; the axillary view distinguishes it from type III.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Acromioclavicular joint injuries

stem: A 19-year-old rugby player is crushed in a collapsing scrum. He presents with a painful right sternoclavicular region, the medial clavicle is less prominent than the opposite side, and he reports a sensation of difficulty swallowing with a slightly hoarse voice and mild breathlessness. Which is the single most important immediate management priority?

options.label: A
options.text: Apply a figure-of-eight bandage and discharge with a sling, reviewing in fracture clinic in two weeks

options.label: B
options.text: Recognise a likely posterior sternoclavicular dislocation, urgently assess the airway and mediastinal structures with contrast CT, and plan reduction with cardiothoracic surgery available

options.label: C
options.text: Arrange an outpatient MRI of the sternoclavicular joint before any intervention

options.label: D
options.text: Closed reduction in the emergency department under local anaesthetic without imaging or surgical support

options.label: E
options.text: Reassure that medial clavicle injuries in adolescents are benign physeal sprains and no imaging is required

explanation: Posterior sternoclavicular dislocation is uncommon but potentially life-threatening because the displaced medial clavicle can compress or injure the trachea, oesophagus, great vessels and brachial plexus behind the manubrium; dysphagia, voice change, dyspnoea, venous congestion or a diminished pulse are red-flag signs of mediastinal compromise. The priority is to recognise it, assess and protect the airway and mediastinal structures, and obtain CT (with contrast to assess the great vessels) to confirm displacement and vascular relationships. Reduction should be planned in theatre with cardiothoracic/vascular surgery on standby because closed reduction can precipitate or reveal vascular injury. In adolescents this is usually a Salter–Harris physeal injury of the medial clavicle (the epiphysis is the last to fuse), but it is managed with the same urgency. A figure-of-eight bandage with discharge, an outpatient MRI, blind ED reduction without support, or false reassurance are all unsafe.
learningPoint: Posterior sternoclavicular dislocation threatens mediastinal structures — assess airway/vessels, obtain contrast CT, and reduce in theatre with cardiothoracic support available.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Sternoclavicular joint injuries

stem: A 30-year-old overhead throwing athlete has deep shoulder pain and catching. At arthroscopy the superior labrum is detached from the glenoid both anterior and posterior to the biceps anchor, and the long head of biceps insertion is lifted off the supraglenoid tubercle with the labral fragment. Using the Snyder classification, which type of SLAP lesion is described?

options.label: A
options.text: Type I — fraying/degeneration of the superior labrum with an intact biceps anchor

options.label: B
options.text: Type II — detachment of the superior labrum and biceps anchor from the supraglenoid tubercle

options.label: C
options.text: Type III — bucket-handle tear of the superior labrum with an intact biceps anchor

options.label: D
options.text: Type IV — bucket-handle labral tear extending into the biceps tendon

options.label: E
options.text: Type V — anteroinferior Bankart lesion contiguous with a superior labral tear

explanation: Snyder classified superior labrum anterior-to-posterior (SLAP) lesions into four primary types. Detachment of the superior labrum together with the biceps anchor from the supraglenoid tubercle, destabilising the biceps origin, is a type II lesion — the commonest type treated surgically and characteristic of overhead athletes. Type I is degenerative fraying with an intact anchor; type III is a bucket-handle tear of the superior labrum with the biceps anchor still attached; type IV is a bucket-handle tear extending into the biceps tendon itself. Type V (an extension by later authors) denotes a Bankart lesion continuous with a SLAP tear. The key discriminator here is that the biceps anchor is lifted off with the labrum, which defines type II.
learningPoint: A SLAP lesion that detaches the superior labrum together with the biceps anchor from the supraglenoid tubercle is Snyder type II — the commonest operatively treated type, typical of overhead athletes.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — SLAP lesions (Snyder classification)
BESS/BOA shoulder instability and labral pathology guidance

subtopic: Biceps lesions — long head pathology & management
stem: A 58-year-old manual worker has anterior shoulder pain and reports a sudden painful 'pop' while lifting, after which the pain eased and he noticed a bulge in the lower arm ('Popeye' deformity). Examination confirms a distal bunching of the muscle belly with mild loss of supination strength but preserved elbow flexion. Which statement best guides management of this isolated rupture of the long head of biceps in this patient?

options.label: A
options.text: Urgent anatomical re-attachment of the long head to the supraglenoid tubercle is required to prevent permanent weakness

options.label: B
options.text: Most isolated long head ruptures in older patients are managed non-operatively, with cosmesis and minor supination/flexion weakness the main residual issues; surgery (tenodesis) is considered chiefly for cosmesis, cramping pain or in younger/manual patients

options.label: C
options.text: This represents a distal biceps tendon rupture and needs acute surgical repair to restore flexion power

options.label: D
options.text: It mandates MRI-guided thrombolysis of the bicipital groove

options.label: E
options.text: It always signifies an underlying SLAP lesion requiring labral repair

explanation: A proximal (long head) biceps tendon rupture in a middle-aged or older patient typically presents with a sudden pop, relief of pre-existing anterior pain, and a distally retracted 'Popeye' muscle belly; functional loss is usually modest (some loss of supination and flexion strength) because the short head and brachialis compensate. Most are managed non-operatively, with the main residual issues being cosmetic deformity and occasional cramping/fatigue. Surgery — usually a biceps tenodesis rather than re-attachment of the degenerate tendon to the supraglenoid tubercle — is reserved for cosmetic concern, persistent cramping pain, or younger/high-demand manual workers. This is distinct from a distal biceps rupture (loss of the antecubital tendon, marked supination/flexion weakness), which is generally repaired acutely. It does not mandate vascular intervention and does not invariably indicate a SLAP lesion requiring labral repair.
learningPoint: Isolated long head of biceps rupture in older patients is usually treated non-operatively; tenodesis is reserved for cosmesis, cramping pain or younger/manual patients — contrast with distal biceps rupture, which is repaired.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Long head of biceps pathology

stem: A 33-year-old volleyball player has deep, poorly localised posterior shoulder pain and weakness of external rotation. MRI shows isolated wasting of infraspinatus with a normal supraspinatus, and a paralabral cyst is seen posteriorly. At which anatomical site is the suprascapular nerve most likely being compressed to produce selective infraspinatus involvement, and what is the underlying anatomical reasoning?

options.label: A
options.text: At the suprascapular notch beneath the superior transverse scapular ligament, because branches to supraspinatus arise distal to this point

options.label: B
options.text: At the spinoglenoid (inferior transverse scapular) notch, because the branch to supraspinatus has already been given off proximally, so a lesion here spares supraspinatus and affects only infraspinatus

options.label: C
options.text: Within the quadrilateral space, sparing supraspinatus because the axillary nerve also travels there

options.label: D
options.text: At Erb's point in the posterior triangle of the neck

options.label: E
options.text: Beneath the inferior belly of omohyoid in the supraclavicular fossa

explanation: The suprascapular nerve (C5–C6, from the upper trunk) passes through the suprascapular notch beneath the superior transverse scapular ligament, supplies supraspinatus and gives sensory branches to the joint, then continues around the lateral border of the scapular spine through the spinoglenoid (inferior transverse scapular) notch to supply infraspinatus. A compressive lesion at the suprascapular notch affects both muscles, whereas a lesion at the spinoglenoid notch is distal to the supraspinatus branches and therefore selectively denervates infraspinatus — exactly the pattern here. Spinoglenoid entrapment is classically caused by a posterior paralabral (ganglion) cyst (often associated with a SLAP/labral tear) and is seen in volleyball and other overhead athletes. The quadrilateral space (axillary nerve), Erb's point and omohyoid are anatomically incorrect for this selective infraspinatus pattern.
learningPoint: Isolated infraspinatus wasting localises the suprascapular nerve lesion to the spinoglenoid notch (distal to the supraspinatus branches); a posterior paralabral cyst is the classic cause in overhead athletes.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Suprascapular nerve entrapment

stem: During mobilisation and medial release of a large, retracted supraspinatus tear, the surgeon plans medial dissection along the scapular spine and superior glenoid neck. To avoid iatrogenic suprascapular nerve injury, knowledge of which anatomical relationship is most important?

options.label: A
options.text: The nerve lies on average about 1.5–2 cm medial to the superior/posterior glenoid rim, so medial dissection beyond this risks injury

options.label: B
options.text: The nerve lies directly on the bare area of the humeral head and is endangered by lateral-row anchor placement

options.label: C
options.text: The nerve runs within the rotator interval anterior to the biceps and is at risk during biceps tenotomy

options.label: D
options.text: The nerve crosses the bicipital groove and is endangered by subpectoral tenodesis

options.label: E
options.text: The nerve lies immediately deep to the acromioclavicular joint capsule

explanation: When mobilising a large retracted cuff tear, aggressive medial release along the scapular spine and superior glenoid neck can endanger the suprascapular nerve where it lies close to bone at the suprascapular notch and as it curves around the spinoglenoid notch. Anatomical and cadaveric studies place the nerve roughly 1.5–2 cm medial to the superior/posterior glenoid rim, so medial dissection or anchor placement beyond this margin from the glenoid risks injury. Staying within a safe distance of the glenoid rim during medial-row work and medialising releases protects it. The nerve does not lie on the humeral bare area, within the rotator interval anterior to biceps, across the bicipital groove, or deep to the AC joint capsule; those distractors correspond to other structures/procedures and would mislead the dissection.
learningPoint: The suprascapular nerve lies roughly 1.5–2 cm medial to the glenoid rim; keep medial cuff releases and anchor placement within this margin to avoid iatrogenic injury.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Suprascapular nerve surgical anatomy

subtopic: Shoulder arthroplasty infection — Cutibacterium acnes diagnosis
stem: A 61-year-old man has progressive pain and stiffness 16 months after an anatomic total shoulder replacement. Inflammatory markers (CRP and ESR) are only mildly raised, plain films show some early lucency around the humeral stem, and there is no sinus or obvious swelling. Which feature is most characteristic of the organism most commonly responsible for peri-prosthetic shoulder infection, and how should it influence sampling?

options.label: A
options.text: Cutibacterium (Propionibacterium) acnes is an indolent low-virulence organism with frequently normal/near-normal inflammatory markers; multiple deep tissue/explant cultures should be held for prolonged (approximately 14–21 day) anaerobic incubation

options.label: B
options.text: Staphylococcus aureus is the usual cause and reliably produces a grossly raised CRP, so a single swab is sufficient

options.label: C
options.text: Escherichia coli is the typical pathogen and requires only short aerobic culture

options.label: D
options.text: Coagulase-negative staphylococci never cause shoulder PJI, so cultures are unnecessary

options.label: E
options.text: Candida species predominate and are excluded by a single 48-hour culture

explanation: Cutibacterium acnes (formerly Propionibacterium acnes) is the organism most characteristically responsible for peri-prosthetic shoulder infection, reflecting its abundance in the dermal sebaceous glands around the shoulder. It is an indolent, low-virulence, slow-growing anaerobe, so presentation is typically a subtle picture of pain, stiffness and loosening with normal or only mildly elevated inflammatory markers and no overt sepsis — exactly as described. Because it grows slowly, multiple deep tissue and/or explanted-component cultures should be taken and held for prolonged anaerobic incubation (around 14–21 days); a single swab is inadequate and risks a false-negative. Staphylococcus aureus produces a far more florid picture, and the other options (E. coli, 'never' for CoNS, Candida) are incorrect. Recognising the low-virulence Cutibacterium pattern is what drives the multiple-sample, extended-culture strategy.
learningPoint: Cutibacterium acnes is the classic indolent shoulder PJI organism with bland inflammatory markers; diagnosis requires multiple deep/explant cultures held for prolonged (approximately 2–3 week) anaerobic incubation.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Peri-prosthetic shoulder infection
ICM (International Consensus Meeting) shoulder PJI definitions

stem: A 64-year-old man develops a confirmed deep peri-prosthetic infection of an anatomic total shoulder replacement 18 months postoperatively, with positive deep cultures for Cutibacterium acnes, radiographic component loosening and good remaining bone stock. He is medically fit. Which is the most appropriate management to maximise infection eradication and durable function?

options.label: A
options.text: Long-term oral antibiotic suppression alone, leaving the loose components in situ

options.label: B
options.text: Debridement, antibiotics and implant retention (DAIR) with exchange of modular parts

options.label: C
options.text: Two-stage revision: removal of all components, thorough debridement, an antibiotic-loaded spacer and culture-directed antibiotics, followed by reimplantation once infection is controlled

options.label: D
options.text: Single irrigation and a five-day course of intravenous antibiotics with no implant removal

options.label: E
options.text: Immediate glenohumeral arthrodesis without addressing the infection medically

explanation: Established (chronic, greater than 3–4 week) deep peri-prosthetic shoulder infection with loose components is best treated by removing all implanted material, performing thorough debridement and culture-directed antibiotic therapy. With loosening and a chronic indolent organism, two-stage revision — explantation, an antibiotic-loaded cement spacer, a course of organism-directed antibiotics, then reimplantation (often a reverse arthroplasty) once infection is controlled — gives the most reliable eradication and durable function in a fit patient. Debridement with implant retention (DAIR) is reserved for acute infection with well-fixed implants, not chronic infection with loose components. Antibiotic suppression alone is a palliative strategy for patients unfit for revision and does not eradicate infection around loose implants. A single washout with a short antibiotic course is inadequate, and proceeding to arthrodesis without controlling the infection is inappropriate. The chronicity, loosening and organism here point clearly to staged revision.
learningPoint: Chronic deep shoulder PJI with loose components in a fit patient is best treated by two-stage revision (explant, antibiotic spacer, directed antibiotics, then reimplantation); DAIR suits only acute infection with well-fixed implants.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Management of peri-prosthetic shoulder infection

stem: A 46-year-old administrator presents with a 5-month history of lateral elbow pain, tender over the lateral epicondyle and worse on resisted wrist extension. She has had no previous treatment. She has read about steroid injections and asks whether one will give her the best long-term result. Based on current best evidence, which statement is most accurate when counselling her?

options.label: A
options.text: A single corticosteroid injection gives superior outcomes at 12 months compared with a wait-and-see or physiotherapy approach

options.label: B
options.text: Corticosteroid injection gives short-term relief but is associated with higher recurrence and worse outcomes at 12 months than physiotherapy or expectant management

options.label: C
options.text: Surgical extensor carpi radialis brevis release is the recommended first-line treatment given the duration of symptoms

options.label: D
options.text: The natural history is one of relentless deterioration, so early intervention is essential

options.label: E
options.text: Platelet-rich plasma injection has level 1 evidence of cure and should be offered before physiotherapy

explanation: Lateral epicondylitis is an angiofibroblastic tendinosis of the extensor carpi radialis brevis (ECRB) origin, not a true inflammatory 'itis'. It is self-limiting in the great majority, with most cases resolving within 12 months whatever is done. Randomised evidence (Coombes/Bisset, JAMA 2013) shows that corticosteroid injection produces better pain relief at around 6 weeks but PARADOXICALLY higher recurrence and inferior outcomes at 12 months compared with physiotherapy or a wait-and-see policy (recurrence ~72% with steroid versus ~8-9%), so repeated steroid is discouraged. First-line UK management is reassurance, activity modification, analgesia and a graded eccentric loading programme; surgery (open or arthroscopic ECRB release) is reserved for the small minority with refractory symptoms beyond 6-12 months. PRP evidence is inconsistent and does not justify displacing physiotherapy.
learningPoint: Steroid injection for tennis elbow helps at 6 weeks but worsens 12-month outcomes and recurrence - load the tendon instead.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Shoulder & Elbow: lateral epicondylitis
Coombes BK et al. JAMA 2013 - corticosteroid and physiotherapy for tennis elbow

stem: A 52-year-old keen golfer has medial elbow pain reproduced by resisted wrist flexion and forearm pronation, with maximal tenderness just distal and anterior to the medial epicondyle. He also describes intermittent paraesthesiae in the little finger. When considering open surgical debridement of the flexor-pronator origin for refractory medial epicondylitis, which structure is most at risk and therefore most important to identify and protect during the approach?

options.label: A
options.text: Posterior interosseous nerve

options.label: B
options.text: Ulnar nerve

options.label: C
options.text: Median nerve

options.label: D
options.text: Medial antebrachial cutaneous nerve only

options.label: E
options.text: Anterior interosseous nerve

explanation: Medial epicondylitis is a tendinosis of the common flexor-pronator origin (principally pronator teres and flexor carpi radialis). The ulnar nerve lies immediately posterior to the medial epicondyle in the cubital tunnel and is the structure most at risk during medial debridement; concomitant ulnar nerve symptoms (here, little-finger paraesthesiae) coexist in a substantial minority of cases and should be specifically assessed pre-operatively, as significant cubital tunnel symptoms may warrant simultaneous decompression. The posterior interosseous nerve is the nerve at risk in LATERAL/radial tunnel surgery, not medial. The medial antebrachial cutaneous nerve is encountered superficially and should be protected, but the single best answer is the ulnar nerve given both its immediate proximity and the clinical clue.
learningPoint: In medial epicondylitis the ulnar nerve is the structure at risk - examine for cubital tunnel symptoms before operating.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Shoulder & Elbow: medial epicondylitis

subtopic: Lateral elbow pain - differential and entrapment
stem: Theme: causes of lateral and posterolateral elbow pain. For each clinical scenario, select the single most likely diagnosis from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Lateral epicondylitis (ECRB tendinosis)

options.label: B
options.text: Radial tunnel syndrome (PIN compression)

options.label: C
options.text: Posterolateral rotatory instability (PLRI)

options.label: D
options.text: Plica / synovial fringe (radiocapitellar plica)

options.label: E
options.text: Osteochondritis dissecans of the capitellum

options.label: F
options.text: Posterior interosseous nerve palsy (complete)

options.label: G
options.text: Radiocapitellar osteoarthritis

options.label: H
options.text: Cervical radiculopathy (C6)

explanation: Lateral elbow pain has overlapping causes separated by the precise history and examination. Classic tennis elbow is point tenderness over the lateral epicondyle/ECRB origin reproduced by resisted wrist extension. Radial tunnel syndrome produces aching maximal 3-4 cm DISTAL to the lateral epicondyle over the mobile wad/supinator, with pain on resisted supination and resisted long-finger extension and NO motor loss (compression of the PIN before significant motor branching) - it classically coexists with, and is mistaken for, resistant tennis elbow. A symptomatic radiocapitellar plica gives lateral pain with painful clicking/snapping in the radiocapitellar joint on flexion-extension. An adolescent with activity-related lateral pain, effusion, catching and a fragmented capitellar lesion on imaging has capitellar osteochondritis dissecans (seen in throwers and in gymnasts from repetitive compressive/valgus radiocapitellar loading). Recurrent clunking apprehension as the elbow extends from a flexed, supinated position, after a prior dislocation, is PLRI from lateral ulnar collateral ligament insufficiency (positive lateral pivot-shift). A painless complete finger/wrist drop with preserved wrist extension in radial deviation and no sensory loss is a complete PIN palsy.
learningPoint: Pain 3-4 cm distal to the epicondyle with pain on resisted supination/long-finger extension and no motor loss is radial tunnel, not tennis elbow.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Shoulder & Elbow: lateral elbow pain and nerve entrapment
BSSH information resources - radial tunnel syndrome

emiLeads.stem: A 44-year-old machinist has lateral forearm aching with maximal tenderness about 4 cm distal to the lateral epicondyle over the supinator. Pain is reproduced by resisted supination and resisted extension of the middle finger. There is no weakness of finger or wrist extension. He has had two failed steroid injections for presumed tennis elbow.

A 28-year-old man had a simple elbow dislocation 18 months ago. He now reports the elbow 'giving way' and a clunk of apprehension as it is brought from a flexed, supinated position into extension while pushing up from a chair. Plain radiographs are normal.

A 14-year-old competitive gymnast has activity-related lateral elbow pain with catching and a small effusion. Radiographs show a lucent, fragmented lesion of the capitellum with subchondral sclerosis.

A 47-year-old typist has point tenderness exactly over the lateral epicondyle, pain on resisted wrist extension and on gripping, and no symptoms more distally. Power is full and there is no instability.

stem: A 39-year-old man sustains an elbow fracture-dislocation. CT confirms a 'terrible triad' injury. When reconstructing this injury through a lateral (and if needed medial) approach, which sequence of repair from deep to superficial is generally recommended to restore a stable, congruent elbow?

options.label: A
options.text: Lateral collateral ligament first, then radial head, then coronoid

options.label: B
options.text: Coronoid (and anterior capsule) first, then radial head, then lateral collateral ligament, then reassess and repair the medial collateral ligament and/or apply a hinged external fixator if still unstable

options.label: C
options.text: Radial head excision first, then coronoid, then skin closure with early mobilisation

options.label: D
options.text: Medial collateral ligament first, then coronoid, then radial head

options.label: E
options.text: Lateral collateral ligament and radial head only; the coronoid never requires fixation

explanation: The terrible triad is a posterolateral elbow dislocation with a radial head fracture AND a coronoid fracture. Reconstruction proceeds from DEEP to SUPERFICIAL: address the coronoid and anterior capsule first (suture or screw fixation - even small fragments matter as they confer anterior buttress and resist posterior subluxation), then reconstruct or replace the radial head (fix if amenable, otherwise metallic replacement - do NOT simply excise, as the radial head is a critical secondary stabiliser here), then repair the lateral collateral ligament complex (especially the LUCL) back to its isometric origin on the capitellum. Stability is then tested through a functional arc; if the elbow remains unstable, the medial collateral ligament is repaired and/or a hinged external fixator applied. Excising the radial head in this setting (option C) removes a key stabiliser and risks recurrent instability.
learningPoint: Terrible triad = repair coronoid then radial head (fix/replace, never just excise) then LCL, and reassess for MCL repair or a hinged fixator.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Shoulder & Elbow: elbow fracture-dislocations / terrible triad
BOAST - principles of elbow fracture-dislocation management

stem: During work-up of a complex elbow injury, the surgeon reviews the CT to characterise the coronoid fragment. According to the O'Driscoll classification, an anteromedial facet coronoid fracture is of particular concern because it is most strongly associated with which pattern of instability?

options.label: A
options.text: Posterolateral rotatory instability with valgus overload

options.label: B
