options.text: Varus posteromedial rotatory instability

options.label: C
options.text: Pure anterior translational instability

options.label: D
options.text: Longitudinal forearm (Essex-Lopresti) instability

options.label: E
options.text: Valgus instability from medial collateral ligament rupture alone

explanation: O'Driscoll classifies coronoid fractures by anatomical location: tip (type 1), anteromedial facet (type 2) and basal (type 3). The ANTEROMEDIAL FACET fracture is the hallmark of VARUS POSTEROMEDIAL ROTATORY INSTABILITY - a varus, axial and posteromedial rotation mechanism that also injures the lateral ulnar collateral ligament. Although the fragment can look small on plain films, loss of the anteromedial buttress allows the trochlea to subluxate and produces rapid, incongruent (often varus) joint overload and early arthrosis if left unfixed. It therefore frequently requires buttress plate fixation through a medial (over-the-top or Hotchkiss) approach plus LCL repair - in contrast to a terrible triad, which is a posterolateral pattern. Recognising the anteromedial facet on CT changes the operative plan.
learningPoint: An anteromedial facet (O'Driscoll type 2) coronoid fracture signals varus posteromedial rotatory instability and usually needs a medial buttress plate plus LCL repair.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Shoulder & Elbow: coronoid fractures (O'Driscoll)
O'Driscoll SW et al. - coronoid fracture classification and posteromedial instability

stem: A 44-year-old labourer feels a painful pop in the antecubital fossa lifting a heavy load 4 days ago, with bruising and a 'reverse Popeye' deformity; the hook test is positive. He proceeds to anatomical distal biceps repair. Which complication is most specifically associated with the anterior single-incision technique, and which structure is responsible?

options.label: A
options.text: Heterotopic ossification / radioulnar synostosis, related to extensive posterior dissection onto the ulna

options.label: B
options.text: Lateral antebrachial cutaneous nerve neuropraxia, from retraction at the anterior approach

options.label: C
options.text: Ulnar nerve injury, from the medial dissection

options.label: D
options.text: Anterior interosseous nerve transection, from drilling the bicipital tuberosity

options.label: E
options.text: Compartment syndrome of the forearm from the tourniquet

explanation: Distal biceps ruptures characteristically occur in middle-aged men with an eccentric load on a flexed, supinated forearm; loss of the lacertus fibrosus allows proximal retraction (positive hook test). Anatomical repair restores supination and flexion strength and is preferred over non-operative management in active patients. The SINGLE anterior incision is associated most specifically with injury to the LATERAL ANTEBRACHIAL CUTANEOUS NERVE (the terminal sensory continuation of the musculocutaneous nerve), which lies lateral to the tendon and is stretched by retraction - it is the single most common complication of this approach (~10%) and is usually a transient sensory neuropraxia. The two-incision (Boyd-Anderson) technique reduces cutaneous nerve injury but historically carries a higher rate of heterotopic ossification / proximal radioulnar synostosis from the posterior muscle-splitting dissection onto the ulna (option A is the classic two-incision risk). PIN injury is the feared motor complication but is less common overall than LABC neuropraxia.
learningPoint: Single-incision distal biceps repair most commonly injures the lateral antebrachial cutaneous nerve; the two-incision technique trades that for a higher HO/synostosis risk.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Shoulder & Elbow: distal biceps rupture
Grewal R et al. / meta-analysis - single- versus double-incision distal biceps repair complications

stem: A 50-year-old man on long-term oral corticosteroids falls onto a partially flexed elbow and develops posterior elbow pain, swelling and difficulty extending against gravity. A lateral radiograph shows a small avulsed bony 'fleck' off the olecranon. Which of the following best reflects correct management?

options.label: A
options.text: Triceps rupture is essentially never complete; treat all such injuries non-operatively in a sling

options.label: B
options.text: The 'fleck sign' strongly suggests triceps avulsion, and a complete rupture (no active extension against gravity) warrants surgical reattachment

options.label: C
options.text: An intact ability to extend the elbow against gravity confirms a complete tear requiring urgent surgery

options.label: D
options.text: MRI is contraindicated and the diagnosis must be made arthroscopically

options.label: E
options.text: Steroid use and an avulsion fleck are unrelated; rupture only follows direct laceration

explanation: Triceps rupture is the least common of all tendon ruptures and is over-represented in patients with risk factors for tendinopathy - systemic or local corticosteroids, anabolic steroids, renal failure/hyperparathyroidism and fluoroquinolones. The mechanism is usually a fall onto the outstretched hand causing eccentric loading. The avulsed bony 'FLECK SIGN' off the olecranon on a lateral radiograph is highly suggestive of triceps avulsion. The key clinical test is active extension against gravity (with a modified squeeze test): if the patient CANNOT extend against gravity the tear is complete and surgical reattachment (transosseous sutures or suture anchors, often augmented) is indicated, ideally early. Partial tears with preserved active extension against gravity can be managed non-operatively in a splint with graduated mobilisation. MRI or ultrasound usefully quantifies the tear when examination is equivocal.
learningPoint: An olecranon 'fleck sign' plus loss of active extension against gravity means a complete triceps rupture needing surgical reattachment - suspect steroid-related tendinopathy.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Shoulder & Elbow: triceps tendon rupture
MHRA Drug Safety Update - fluoroquinolone-associated tendon disorders

subtopic: Heterotopic ossification and the stiff elbow
stem: A 30-year-old man with a closed head injury and a complex elbow fracture-dislocation is felt to be at high risk of heterotopic ossification around the elbow. The team discusses prophylaxis. Which statement most accurately reflects current evidence-based practice for HO prophylaxis at the elbow?

options.label: A
options.text: Routine peri-operative single-dose radiotherapy to the elbow is the standard of care for all elbow trauma

options.label: B
options.text: Indomethacin (or another NSAID) is a reasonable prophylactic option, but the evidence for elbow HO prophylaxis is weaker than at the hip and the priorities are stable fixation permitting gentle early motion and avoiding repeated surgical insults

options.label: C
options.text: Bisphosphonates reliably prevent maturation of heterotopic bone and are first-line

options.label: D
options.text: Aggressive continuous passive motion forced through pain in the first week prevents HO

options.label: E
options.text: Prophylactic excision of any early HO at 3 weeks prevents recurrence

explanation: Risk factors for elbow HO include high-energy fracture-dislocations, associated head injury or burns, delayed or repeated surgery, and prolonged immobilisation. Prophylactic options extrapolated largely from hip data are NSAIDs (indomethacin) and, in selected high-risk cases, low-dose radiotherapy - but the elbow evidence base is weaker and radiotherapy is NOT routine for all elbow trauma. The most reliable practical measures are restoring a stable skeleton that permits GENTLE early active motion, avoiding repeated surgical trauma, and not forcing painful manipulation (which can worsen HO). Bisphosphonates do not prevent osteoid formation (they delay mineralisation, with rebound) and are not first-line. Established, mature, symptomatic HO causing ankylosis is treated by surgical excision once the bone is mature and inflammation has settled - early excision risks recurrence.
learningPoint: Elbow HO prophylaxis rests mainly on stable fixation, gentle early motion and avoiding repeat insults; NSAIDs/radiotherapy are adjuncts, not routine.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Shoulder & Elbow: heterotopic ossification and the stiff elbow
BOA/BOAST - principles of elbow trauma rehabilitation

stem: A 35-year-old joiner has a post-traumatic stiff elbow with a fixed flexion deformity of 40 degrees and further flexion to only 100 degrees, limiting his work. Imaging shows mature anterior and posterior capsular contracture without significant articular incongruity. When counselling him about open capsular release and the realistic target, which functional arc of elbow flexion-extension is conventionally cited as that within which most activities of daily living are achieved?

options.label: A
options.text: 0 to 145 degrees

options.label: B
options.text: 30 to 130 degrees (a 100-degree arc), together with 50 degrees each of pronation and supination

options.label: C
options.text: 60 to 90 degrees

options.label: D
options.text: 0 to 90 degrees with no rotation required

options.label: E
options.text: 45 to 110 degrees with full rotation

explanation: Morrey's classic functional-arc work established that most activities of daily living are performed within roughly 30-130 degrees of flexion-extension (a 100-degree arc) and 50 degrees each of pronation and supination (a 100-degree rotational arc). This is the rehabilitation and surgical TARGET when planning contracture release. This patient's arc (40-100 degrees) falls short at both ends and his occupation is affected, so he is a reasonable candidate for capsular release (open column procedure or arthroscopic release) when the contracture is mature and the joint surface is preserved. The ulnar nerve must be assessed and decompressed/transposed if flexion is being substantially improved, as gaining flexion can precipitate ulnar neuropathy. Restoring the joint towards the functional arc - not necessarily a fully normal arc - is the realistic, evidence-based goal.
learningPoint: The functional elbow arc is ~30-130 degrees flexion-extension and ~50/50 degrees rotation - that is the target of contracture release; assess and protect the ulnar nerve when regaining flexion.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Shoulder & Elbow: stiff elbow and contracture release
Morrey BF, Askew LJ, Chao EY - functional analysis of the elbow (J Bone Joint Surg Am)

stem: A 55-year-old manual worker has primary elbow osteoarthritis with painful terminal extension and flexion and mechanical locking from loose bodies, but a reasonable mid-range arc and no significant ulnar nerve symptoms. Radiographs show osteophytes on the olecranon and coronoid with preserved central joint space. Which procedure is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Total elbow arthroplasty

options.label: B
options.text: Ulnohumeral (Outerbridge-Kashiwagi) debridement arthroplasty - open or arthroscopic osteophyte excision and loose body removal

options.label: C
options.text: Radial head excision in isolation

options.label: D
options.text: Elbow arthrodesis in 90 degrees of flexion

options.label: E
options.text: Interposition arthroplasty with Achilles allograft

explanation: Primary OA of the elbow is typically seen in middle-aged men and manual workers and characteristically presents with IMPINGEMENT pain at the extremes of motion and mechanical symptoms from osteophytes and loose bodies, while the central radiocapitellar/ulnohumeral cartilage is relatively preserved. The procedure of choice in this scenario is a debridement arthroplasty - the OUTERBRIDGE-KASHIWAGI (ulnohumeral) procedure or its arthroscopic equivalent - removing olecranon and coronoid osteophytes, fenestrating the olecranon fossa and clearing loose bodies, which relieves impingement and improves the arc while retaining the native joint. Total elbow arthroplasty is inappropriate for a young, high-demand manual worker because of its strict lifetime lifting restriction and loosening risk. Arthrodesis leaves no functional arc and is poorly tolerated. Interposition arthroplasty is reserved for younger patients with more diffuse cartilage loss.
learningPoint: Primary elbow OA causes end-range impingement with preserved mid-range - debride osteophytes/loose bodies (Outerbridge-Kashiwagi) and avoid TEA in young manual workers.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Shoulder & Elbow: primary elbow osteoarthritis
BESS / BOA guidance - management of elbow arthritis

stem: A 58-year-old woman with long-standing seropositive rheumatoid arthritis has a painful elbow with a large synovitic effusion and radiocapitellar pain on rotation, but well-maintained ulnohumeral joint space on radiographs (early inflammatory disease) and a stable elbow. Pain and pronosupination are her dominant problems and the joint has not settled on optimal medical therapy. Which surgical option is most appropriate at this stage?

options.label: A
options.text: Total elbow arthroplasty (linked)

options.label: B
options.text: Synovectomy with radial head excision (open or arthroscopic)

options.label: C
options.text: Elbow arthrodesis

options.label: D
options.text: Distraction interposition arthroplasty

options.label: E
options.text: Lateral collateral ligament reconstruction

explanation: The rheumatoid elbow differs from primary OA: it is driven by inflammatory synovitis that causes pain, effusion and progressive cartilage/bone loss, and the radiocapitellar joint is often a major pain generator on rotation. In EARLY disease (low Mayo/Larsen grade) with preserved ulnohumeral architecture and synovitis refractory to medical therapy, SYNOVECTOMY combined with RADIAL HEAD EXCISION reliably relieves pain and improves pronosupination while preserving the joint - it is the recognised joint-preserving option at this stage. Total elbow arthroplasty is reserved for ADVANCED disease with destroyed joint surfaces and gives excellent pain relief but mandates a permanent lifting restriction and carries notable revision/complication rates; it is not first-line for early disease. Arthrodesis is poorly tolerated at the elbow. Modern DMARD/biologic therapy has reduced the need for surgical synovectomy overall, but it remains the answer for refractory early synovitis.
learningPoint: Early rheumatoid elbow with refractory synovitis and a preserved ulnohumeral joint -> synovectomy + radial head excision; reserve TEA for advanced joint destruction.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Shoulder & Elbow: rheumatoid elbow
NICE NG100 - Rheumatoid arthritis in adults: management

stem: A 49-year-old man has a 9-month history of little- and ring-finger numbness and clumsiness, worse with elbow flexion. Examination shows first dorsal interosseous and hypothenar wasting, weak finger abduction, a positive Froment's sign and clawing. Nerve conduction studies confirm a localising ulnar nerve lesion at the elbow. According to McGowan grading and current evidence, which is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Continued night-time elbow extension splinting and activity modification alone

options.label: B
options.text: Surgical decompression (in situ or with transposition) because there is established motor weakness and intrinsic wasting (McGowan grade III)

options.label: C
options.text: A corticosteroid injection into the cubital tunnel as definitive treatment

options.label: D
options.text: Medial epicondylectomy is mandatory in all cases and superior to simple decompression

options.label: E
options.text: Anterior submuscular transposition is proven superior to simple in-situ decompression and should always be performed

explanation: McGowan grades ulnar neuropathy at the elbow: grade I = sensory disturbance/paraesthesiae only; grade II = weakness with early intrinsic involvement; grade III = marked weakness with intrinsic WASTING. Conservative measures (night-time splinting in extension, avoiding sustained flexion and direct pressure) are appropriate for mild, intermittent (grade I) symptoms. This patient has established motor signs with wasting and clawing (grade III) plus confirmatory NCS, so SURGICAL DECOMPRESSION is indicated, recognising that recovery of long-standing intrinsic wasting may be incomplete. Randomised and meta-analytic evidence shows SIMPLE IN-SITU DECOMPRESSION gives clinical outcomes equivalent to anterior transposition for idiopathic cubital tunnel, with fewer surgical complications - so routine transposition or epicondylectomy is NOT mandated; transposition is reserved for a subluxating nerve, bony deformity or revision surgery. Steroid injection has no established role.
learningPoint: Cubital tunnel with motor weakness/wasting (McGowan II-III) warrants decompression; simple in-situ decompression equals transposition for idiopathic cases with fewer complications.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Shoulder & Elbow: cubital tunnel syndrome
BSSH information resources - cubital tunnel syndrome

subtopic: Ulnar nerve anatomy at the elbow
stem: When releasing the ulnar nerve in the cubital tunnel, the surgeon must decompress all potential sites of compression along its course. Which of the following is the most distal recognised site of ulnar nerve compression at the elbow that must be released to avoid a residual constriction?

options.label: A
options.text: Arcade of Struthers (proximal to the medial epicondyle)

options.label: B
options.text: Medial intermuscular septum

options.label: C
options.text: Cubital tunnel retinaculum (Osborne's band) at the epicondylar groove

options.label: D
options.text: Aponeurotic arch between the two heads of flexor carpi ulnaris (Osborne's fascia / FCU arcade)

options.label: E
options.text: Ligament of Struthers at the wrist

explanation: Along its course at the elbow the ulnar nerve can be compressed at several sites, from proximal to distal: the arcade of Struthers (a fascial band roughly 8 cm proximal to the medial epicondyle), the medial intermuscular septum, the medial epicondyle / Osborne's retinaculum at the epicondylar groove, and most DISTALLY the aponeurotic arch bridging the two heads of FLEXOR CARPI ULNARIS (Osborne's fascia / the FCU arcade), through which the nerve enters the forearm. Incomplete release of this deep FCU aponeurosis is a classic cause of persistent or recurrent symptoms after cubital tunnel surgery, so it must be divided. Option E is a distractor: the 'ligament of Struthers' is a separate, rare supracondylar process phenomenon associated with the MEDIAN nerve, not the ulnar nerve, and it is not a wrist structure. A complete decompression releases all of these structures along the nerve's path.
learningPoint: The most distal cubital tunnel compression site is the flexor carpi ulnaris aponeurotic arch (Osborne's fascia) - failure to release it causes persistent/recurrent ulnar symptoms.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Anatomy / Shoulder & Elbow: ulnar nerve compression sites
BSSH information resources - cubital tunnel anatomy

stem: A 41-year-old woman falls and sustains a comminuted radial head fracture in more than three fragments that cannot be reconstructed, with a mechanical block to forearm rotation and an associated elbow dislocation that has been reduced. There is no other bony injury. Which is the most appropriate management of the radial head?

options.label: A
options.text: Non-operative management with early mobilisation

options.label: B
options.text: Open reduction and internal fixation, accepting the comminution

options.label: C
options.text: Radial head excision alone

options.label: D
options.text: Metallic radial head replacement (arthroplasty)

options.label: E
options.text: Silastic radial head replacement

explanation: The Mason classification (with Hotchkiss and Broberg-Morrey modifications) guides radial head management: type I (undisplaced) - early motion; type II (displaced partial articular, with a block) - ORIF if amenable; type III (comminuted, not reconstructable) - fixation often fails. Crucially, when the radial head is fractured in the context of instability (here an associated DISLOCATION, making the radial head a key secondary stabiliser), it must NOT simply be excised - excision in the unstable elbow risks valgus/longitudinal instability and proximal radial migration. For an unreconstructable comminuted head (more than 3 fragments) with a rotational block and associated instability, METALLIC RADIAL HEAD REPLACEMENT restores the lateral column buttress and stability. SILASTIC implants are obsolete (silicone synovitis, fragmentation and inadequate stiffness). Isolated radial head excision remains acceptable only for an unreconstructable head in a STABLE elbow with no other injury.
learningPoint: An unreconstructable (Mason III) radial head with instability -> metallic replacement, never simple excision; silastic implants are obsolete.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Shoulder & Elbow: radial head fractures (Mason)

subtopic: Distal humerus and complex elbow fractures
stem: Theme: management of distal humerus and periarticular elbow fractures in adults. For each scenario, select the single most appropriate definitive management from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Open reduction and internal fixation with dual (orthogonal or parallel) plates

options.label: B
options.text: Primary total elbow arthroplasty

options.label: C
options.text: Non-operative management with early mobilisation (collar and cuff / functional brace)

options.label: D
options.text: Tension-band wiring

options.label: E
options.text: Olecranon osteotomy to access the joint, then articular ORIF

options.label: F
options.text: Metallic radial head replacement

options.label: G
options.text: Distal humeral hemiarthroplasty

options.label: H
options.text: Excision of fragment and triceps advancement

explanation: Adult distal humerus fractures are demanding. A displaced bicolumnar (AO type C) intra-articular fracture in a fit younger adult is treated by anatomical articular reduction and DUAL-COLUMN plating (orthogonal or parallel constructs), and an OLECRANON OSTEOTOMY is the work-horse exposure that maximises articular visualisation for the comminuted joint surface. In a low-demand ELDERLY patient with an unreconstructable comminuted intra-articular distal humerus fracture (and no active infection), PRIMARY TOTAL ELBOW ARTHROPLASTY gives more reliable early function than attempted fixation of poor bone - supported by McKee's randomised data - provided the patient accepts a permanent lifting restriction. A minimally displaced olecranon fracture in a frail, unfit patient with an intact extensor mechanism can be managed NON-OPERATIVELY with early motion, accepting a possible fibrous union. A displaced transverse olecranon fracture in an active patient is the classic indication for TENSION-BAND WIRING. Hemiarthroplasty is a niche option for selected isolated articular distal humerus fractures with intact, fixable columns.
learningPoint: Young bicolumnar distal humerus = dual-plate ORIF (often via olecranon osteotomy); unreconstructable fracture in a low-demand elderly patient = primary TEA.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Shoulder & Elbow / Trauma: distal humerus and olecranon fractures
McKee MD et al. J Shoulder Elbow Surg 2009 - RCT of TEA vs ORIF for distal humerus fractures in the elderly

emiLeads.stem: A fit 34-year-old motorcyclist has a closed, displaced intra-articular bicolumnar (AO type C3) distal humerus fracture with good bone stock and no neurovascular deficit.

An 82-year-old woman with rheumatoid arthritis and osteoporosis sustains a comminuted, unreconstructable intra-articular distal humerus fracture. She has low functional demands, no wound contamination and accepts a permanent lifting limit.
An active 45-year-old man has a displaced transverse fracture of the olecranon with loss of active elbow extension but no comminution and good bone quality.

A frail 90-year-old nursing-home resident with dementia sustains a minimally displaced olecranon fracture. The extensor mechanism is intact (she can extend against gravity) and she is unfit for anaesthesia.

subtopic: Total elbow arthroplasty - indications and restrictions
stem: A 72-year-old retired woman with end-stage inflammatory elbow arthritis is being consented for a linked (semi-constrained) total elbow arthroplasty. As part of informed consent regarding the durability of the implant, what permanent post-operative restriction is conventionally advised after total elbow arthroplasty?

options.label: A
options.text: No restriction once healed; the implant tolerates normal manual labour

options.label: B
options.text: A permanent lifting limit of approximately 2.3 kg (about 5 lb) repetitively, and avoidance of single heavy lifts (often quoted around 4.5 kg / 10 lb)

options.label: C
options.text: Permanent avoidance of all elbow flexion beyond 90 degrees

options.label: D
options.text: Lifelong non-weight-bearing through the contralateral upper limb

options.label: E
options.text: No driving for life

explanation: Total elbow arthroplasty gives excellent pain relief and restores a functional arc, but it is the least durable of the major joint replacements and is vulnerable to mechanical loosening and bushing/polyethylene wear, particularly under load. Patients are therefore counselled on a PERMANENT LIFTING RESTRICTION - typically about 2.3 kg (5 lb) repetitively and a single-lift limit around 4.5 kg (10 lb) - to protect the implant and reduce aseptic loosening. This restriction is the principal reason TEA is reserved for LOW-DEMAND patients (elderly inflammatory or post-traumatic arthritis, or unreconstructable distal humerus fractures in the elderly) rather than young manual workers. Linked semi-constrained designs resist instability but transmit more load to the cement interfaces; unlinked designs preserve bone but require competent collateral ligaments and adequate bone stock. The lifting limit must be made explicit before consent.
learningPoint: After TEA, counsel a lifelong lifting limit (~2.3 kg repetitive, ~4.5 kg single) - hence TEA is for low-demand patients only.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Shoulder & Elbow: total elbow arthroplasty
National Joint Registry (NJR) - elbow arthroplasty data and patient guidance

stem: A 75-year-old man returns 8 months after a linked total elbow arthroplasty with increasing pain, a discharging sinus over the posterior elbow and raised inflammatory markers. Aspiration grows a coagulase-negative Staphylococcus. Compared with hip and knee arthroplasty, which statement best characterises the complication profile and this scenario in total elbow arthroplasty?

options.label: A
options.text: Deep infection rates after TEA are lower than after hip and knee replacement because of the small joint volume

options.label: B
options.text: TEA has a higher deep infection rate than hip/knee arthroplasty, the thin posterior soft-tissue envelope is a key factor, and established deep infection usually requires implant removal/revision rather than antibiotics alone

options.label: C
options.text: A discharging sinus can be managed with oral antibiotics alone and implant retention is always successful

options.label: D
options.text: Ulnar neuropathy is essentially never seen after TEA

options.label: E
options.text: Triceps insufficiency is irrelevant to the surgical approach chosen

explanation: TEA carries a notably HIGHER complication burden than lower-limb arthroplasty. Deep infection rates are higher than for hip/knee replacement (often quoted around 3-5%, and higher in rheumatoid and post-traumatic cases), largely because of the THIN, poorly vascularised posterior soft-tissue envelope and frequent prior surgery/immunosuppression. A discharging SINUS with a positive aspirate signifies established deep periprosthetic infection; this is not curable by antibiotics alone, and management usually requires debridement with implant removal - staged revision, or in low-demand patients resection arthroplasty - alongside organism-directed antibiotics. Other characteristic TEA complications include ULNAR neuropathy (the nerve lies in the operative field), TRICEPS insufficiency (related to the extensor approach and its repair), bushing wear, periprosthetic fracture and aseptic loosening. Recognising the elevated infection risk and the inadequacy of antibiotic suppression alone is essential.
learningPoint: TEA has higher infection rates than hip/knee replacement (thin posterior envelope); an established sinus/positive aspirate means implant removal, not antibiotics alone.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Shoulder & Elbow: complications of total elbow arthroplasty
BOAST - Management of Prosthetic Joint Infection / NJR elbow revision data

stem: A 58-year-old right-hand-dominant woman sustains a dorsally displaced distal radius fracture after a fall. After closed manipulation under haematoma block, check radiographs show 25 degrees of dorsal angulation, 4 mm of radial shortening and 3 mm of intra-articular step at the radiocarpal surface. She is fit and active. According to current UK practice, what is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Accept the position and treat in a below-elbow cast for 6 weeks

options.label: B
options.text: Surgical fixation (most commonly volar locking plate)

options.label: C
options.text: Repeat closed manipulation and apply an above-elbow cast

options.label: D
options.text: Bridging external fixator alone with no internal fixation

options.label: E
options.text: Functional bracing with immediate wrist mobilisation

explanation: These post-manipulation parameters are all outside accepted radiological limits (dorsal tilt well beyond neutral/0-10 degrees, radial shortening >2-3 mm and intra-articular step >2 mm), indicating an unstable, malreduced fracture in a physiologically fit patient. Failure to maintain reduction in cast is an indication for surgical stabilisation, and the volar locking plate is the most commonly used construct in the UK for this dorsally displaced pattern. Re-manipulation into cast is unlikely to hold an already-failed reduction, and an above-elbow cast does not confer added stability for distal radius fractures.
learningPoint: Loss of reduction (dorsal tilt, shortening >2 mm, articular step >2 mm) in a fit patient is an indication to fix a distal radius fracture, usually with a volar locking plate.
references: Miller's — Hand & Wrist: distal radius fractures
BOAST — The Management of Distal Radius Fractures

subtopic: Distal radius fractures — assessment of reduction
stem: On a true lateral radiograph of a normal, uninjured wrist, the articular surface of the distal radius is normally tilted in which direction relative to the long axis of the radius?

options.label: A
options.text: Volar (palmar) tilt of approximately 11 degrees

options.label: B
options.text: Dorsal tilt of approximately 11 degrees

options.label: C
options.text: Neutral (0 degrees) with no tilt

options.label: D
options.text: Volar tilt of approximately 25 degrees

options.label: E
options.text: Radial inclination of approximately 22 degrees seen on the lateral view

explanation: Normal distal radius parameters are volar (palmar) tilt of about 11 degrees on the lateral view, radial inclination of about 22 degrees and radial height of about 11-13 mm on the PA view. A dorsally displaced (Colles-type) fracture reverses the volar tilt into dorsal angulation, which is the key deformity to correct at manipulation. Radial inclination is a PA-view (coronal) measurement, not a lateral-view finding.
learningPoint: Normal distal radius: ~11 degrees volar tilt, ~22 degrees radial inclination, ~11-13 mm radial height — restore these at reduction.
references: Miller's — Hand & Wrist: radiographic parameters of the distal radius

subtopic: Distal radius and forearm — fracture eponyms
stem: Theme: distal radius and forearm fracture-eponyms. For each radiographic/clinical description, select the single most appropriate eponymous diagnosis from the list.

options.label: A
options.text: Colles fracture

options.label: B
options.text: Smith fracture

options.label: C
options.text: Barton fracture

options.label: D
options.text: Chauffeur (Hutchinson) fracture

options.label: E
options.text: Galeazzi fracture-dislocation

options.label: F
options.text: Die-punch fracture

options.label: G
options.text: Essex-Lopresti injury

emiLeads.stem: An extra-articular distal radius fracture with dorsal displacement and dorsal angulation (apex-volar), producing the classic dinner-fork deformity.
A volarly displaced and volarly angulated extra-articular distal radius fracture, sometimes called a reverse Colles, producing a garden-spade deformity.
An intra-articular fracture of the volar rim of the distal radius with volar subluxation of the carpus along the displaced fragment.
An intra-articular fracture isolated to the radial styloid, often associated with scapholunate ligament injury.
A radial shaft fracture with associated disruption of the distal radioulnar joint.

explanation: Colles = extra-articular, dorsally displaced/angulated (dinner-fork). Smith = volarly displaced/angulated (reverse Colles, garden-spade). Barton = intra-articular rim fracture (volar or dorsal) with carpal subluxation along the fragment — inherently unstable and requires buttress fixation. Chauffeur/Hutchinson = radial styloid fracture, a marker for possible scapholunate injury. Galeazzi = radial shaft fracture with DRUJ disruption (operative in adults). The die-punch is a depressed lunate-facet fragment and Essex-Lopresti is a radial head fracture with longitudinal interosseous membrane disruption and DRUJ injury — distractors here.
learningPoint: Distinguish displacement direction (Colles vs Smith), intra-articular rim patterns (Barton), and associated joint injuries (Galeazzi = DRUJ).
references: Miller's — Hand & Wrist / Trauma: distal radius eponyms

subtopic: Scaphoid fracture — site and blood supply
stem: A 22-year-old man has a displaced fracture through the proximal pole of the scaphoid. Compared with a waist or distal fracture, the proximal pole is at particularly high risk of avascular necrosis and non-union because of which feature of its blood supply?

options.label: A
options.text: The scaphoid is supplied in a retrograde fashion by dorsal carpal branches of the radial artery entering distally

options.label: B
options.text: The proximal pole is supplied directly by the anterior interosseous artery

options.label: C
options.text: The proximal pole receives the dominant supply from the volar scaphoid branch entering at the tubercle

options.label: D
options.text: The proximal pole is supplied by the deep palmar arch via the ulnar artery

options.label: E
options.text: The proximal pole has its own independent nutrient artery entering proximally

explanation: The scaphoid receives most of its blood supply from dorsal carpal (and superficial palmar) branches of the radial artery that enter at or near the dorsal ridge in the distal/waist region and flow proximally in a retrograde manner. The proximal pole is therefore the last and most precariously perfused segment; a proximal pole fracture interrupts this retrograde inflow and carries the highest rates of AVN and non-union, often warranting early surgical fixation.
learningPoint: Scaphoid blood supply is retrograde from distal dorsal radial artery branches, so proximal pole fractures have the highest AVN/non-union risk and a low threshold for fixation.
references: Miller's — Hand & Wrist: scaphoid vascular anatomy

stem: A 28-year-old labourer has an undisplaced (no step, no gap, no angulation) fracture through the waist of the scaphoid confirmed on CT. He is keen to return to manual work as quickly as possible. Recent randomised evidence comparing cast treatment with early surgical fixation for undisplaced/minimally displaced waist fractures has most reliably shown which of the following?

options.label: A
options.text: Early fixation gives no clinically important difference in union or long-term function but allows earlier return to activity, at the cost of surgical complications

options.label: B
options.text: Cast treatment results in a markedly higher non-union rate, so all waist fractures should be fixed

options.label: C
options.text: Early fixation produces substantially better wrist range of motion at one year

options.label: D
options.text: Surgery has no complications and is therefore always preferred

options.label: E
options.text: Cast treatment is contraindicated in manual workers regardless of displacement

explanation: For genuinely undisplaced or minimally displaced scaphoid waist fractures, randomised trial data (e.g. the SWIFFT trial) show comparable union rates and patient-rated function between cast immobilisation and early surgical fixation, with surgery carrying its own complication burden but offering faster return to work/sport for selected patients. Displaced fractures (step/gap, angulation) and proximal pole fractures are the clearer surgical indications. The decision is therefore individualised rather than a blanket 'fix everything'.
learningPoint: Undisplaced scaphoid waist fractures heal comparably with cast or fixation; surgery mainly offers earlier return, while displacement/proximal pole drive the case for operative treatment.

subtopic: Carpal fractures — clinical and imaging clues
stem: Theme: carpal bone fractures. For each presentation, select the single carpal bone most likely to be fractured.

options.label: A
options.text: Scaphoid

options.label: B
options.text: Triquetrum

options.label: C
options.text: Hook of hamate

options.label: D
options.text: Lunate

options.label: E
options.text: Pisiform

options.label: F
options.text: Trapezium

options.label: G
options.text: Capitate

emiLeads.stem: A golfer reports ulnar-sided palm pain after the club struck the ground; there is tenderness over the hypothenar eminence and an occasional ulnar nerve symptom, best demonstrated on a carpal tunnel view or CT.

A young adult has a fall onto a dorsiflexed hand and a small dorsal avulsion flake is seen on the lateral radiograph at the dorsum of the ulnar carpus — the commonest carpal fracture after the scaphoid.
A patient develops progressive wrist pain and on imaging the relevant bone shows sclerosis, fragmentation and collapse (an osteonecrotic process associated with negative ulnar variance).
A patient has a fall onto an outstretched hand with tenderness in the anatomical snuffbox and pain on axial loading of the thumb; initial radiographs are normal.

explanation: Hook of hamate fractures occur with racquet/golf/bat sports (the handle levers against the hook) and present with hypothenar pain and possible ulnar nerve irritation in Guyon's canal — diagnosed on a carpal tunnel view or CT. The triquetrum is the second most commonly fractured carpal, classically a dorsal avulsion flake. Lunate sclerosis/collapse describes Kienbock disease (osteonecrosis, associated with negative ulnar variance). Snuffbox tenderness with normal initial films is the classic occult scaphoid presentation.
learningPoint: Match mechanism and site: hook of hamate (club/bat sports), triquetral dorsal flake (second commonest), lunate collapse (Kienbock), snuffbox = scaphoid.
references: Miller's — Hand & Wrist: carpal fractures and Kienbock disease

stem: A 30-year-old man has persistent radial-sided wrist pain after a fall on the outstretched hand. A PA radiograph shows a gap of 5 mm between the scaphoid and lunate, and the scaphoid appears foreshortened with a cortical ring. The lateral view shows the scapholunate angle increased to about 80 degrees. Which diagnosis and deformity do these findings represent?

options.label: A
options.text: Scapholunate dissociation with a DISI deformity

options.label: B
options.text: Scapholunate dissociation with a VISI deformity

options.label: C
options.text: Lunotriquetral dissociation with a VISI deformity

options.label: D
options.text: Midcarpal instability with normal intercarpal angles

options.label: E
options.text: Ulnar translocation of the carpus

explanation: A scapholunate gap of 3 mm or more (the Terry-Thomas sign), the scaphoid ring sign from a vertically rotated (flexed) scaphoid, and a scapholunate angle above 60-70 degrees indicate scapholunate ligament dissociation. With the scaphoid restraint lost, the lunate is pulled by the triquetrum into extension, giving a dorsal intercalated segment instability (DISI): on the lateral view the lunate tilts dorsally and the SL angle increases. VISI (lunate flexed) is the pattern seen with lunotriquetral dissociation.
learningPoint: SL dissociation → scaphoid flexes, lunate extends → DISI (SL angle >60-70 degrees, Terry-Thomas gap, ring sign).

stem: A patient has ulnar-sided wrist pain and a clicking sensation with ulnar deviation. The lateral radiograph shows the lunate flexed (tilted volarly) so that the scapholunate angle is reduced to about 30 degrees. A volar intercalated segment instability (VISI) pattern in this setting is most typically caused by which ligamentous injury?

options.label: A
options.text: Lunotriquetral ligament disruption

options.label: B
options.text: Scapholunate interosseous ligament disruption

options.label: C
options.text: Isolated radioscaphocapitate ligament injury

options.label: D
options.text: Dorsal radiotriquetral ligament injury alone

options.label: E
options.text: Triangular fibrocartilage complex central tear

explanation: In a VISI deformity the lunate is flexed (volarly tilted) and the scapholunate angle falls below about 30-40 degrees. With the lunotriquetral link disrupted, the scaphoid drags the lunate into flexion (the scaphoid's natural tendency) while the triquetrum extends, producing VISI. This is the mirror image of SL dissociation, in which loss of the scapholunate restraint allows the lunate to extend into DISI. A central TFCC tear causes ulnar pain but does not by itself create a static VISI malalignment.
learningPoint: LT dissociation → lunate flexes with the scaphoid → VISI (low SL angle); SL dissociation → DISI (high SL angle).

stem: Within the Mayfield classification of progressive perilunate instability, a pure scapholunate dissociation without any change in carpal height or lunate position on the lateral view is best categorised as which type of carpal instability?

options.label: A
options.text: Carpal instability dissociative (CID)

options.label: B
options.text: Carpal instability non-dissociative (CIND)

options.label: C
options.text: Carpal instability complex (CIC)

options.label: D
options.text: Carpal instability adaptive (CIA)

options.label: E
options.text: Axial carpal instability

explanation: Carpal instability dissociative (CID) describes instability within a carpal row owing to disruption of an intrinsic interosseous ligament — scapholunate or lunotriquetral dissociation are the classic examples. CIND describes instability between rows (e.g. midcarpal or radiocarpal) with the proximal row intact. CIC (or complex) combines dissociative and non-dissociative patterns, as in perilunate dislocations. Adaptive instability arises secondary to a malunited distal radius, and axial instability follows longitudinal/crush splitting of the carpus.
learningPoint: SL and LT dissociations are dissociative (CID) patterns — disruption within the proximal row; between-row problems are CIND.
references: Miller's — Hand & Wrist: classification of carpal instability

stem: A motorcyclist falls onto a forcibly dorsiflexed hand. The PA wrist radiograph shows loss of the normal carpal arcs (Gilula's lines) with overlap of the carpal bones, and the lateral view shows the lunate tipped volarly and rotated out of line with the radius like a 'spilled teacup', while the capitate lies dorsally and remains aligned with the radius. What is the diagnosis?

options.label: A
options.text: Volar lunate dislocation (the end stage of perilunate injury)

options.label: B
options.text: Dorsal perilunate dislocation with the lunate reduced

options.label: C
options.text: Isolated scapholunate dissociation

options.label: D
options.text: Distal radius fracture with dorsal angulation

options.label: E
options.text: Midcarpal (capitolunate) instability without dislocation

explanation: The 'spilled teacup' sign — the lunate tilted and displaced volarly out of the radius-capitate axis while the capitate stays aligned with the radius — is the hallmark of a volar lunate dislocation, the final (stage IV) Mayfield stage of progressive perilunate instability. By contrast, in a perilunate dislocation the lunate remains seated in the radial fossa ('aligned cup') and the capitate dislocates dorsally. Disruption of Gilula's arcs on the PA film signals significant carpal malalignment in either case.
learningPoint: Spilled-teacup lunate (volarly tipped, capitate aligned with radius) = lunate dislocation; aligned lunate with dorsal capitate = perilunate dislocation.
references: Miller's — Hand & Wrist / Trauma: perilunate injuries

stem: A 34-year-old presents 6 hours after a high-energy fall with a trans-scaphoid perilunate fracture-dislocation. The hand is swollen and there is progressive median nerve paraesthesia. After initial assessment, what is the most appropriate management strategy?

options.label: A
options.text: Urgent closed reduction, then definitive open reduction with ligament repair and scaphoid fixation (with carpal tunnel release if median symptoms persist)

options.label: B
options.text: Apply a below-elbow cast in slight flexion and review in fracture clinic in one week

options.label: C
options.text: Closed reduction alone followed by 6 weeks of cast immobilisation as definitive treatment

options.label: D
options.text: Immediate proximal row carpectomy

options.label: E
options.text: Primary wrist arthrodesis

explanation: Perilunate dislocations and fracture-dislocations are emergencies. Prompt closed reduction relieves pressure on the median nerve and restores gross alignment, but these injuries are unstable and almost always require definitive open surgery: anatomical reduction, repair/reconstruction of the scapholunate (and lunotriquetral) ligaments, and fixation of the scaphoid in a trans-scaphoid pattern. Acute carpal tunnel syndrome that does not resolve after reduction mandates carpal tunnel release. Closed treatment alone gives poor outcomes, and salvage procedures (PRC, arthrodesis) are not first-line for an acute injury.
learningPoint: Perilunate dislocation = urgent reduction then open repair/fixation; release the carpal tunnel if median symptoms persist after reduction.
references: Miller's — Hand & Wrist / Trauma: perilunate fracture-dislocations

stem: A 24-year-old man punches a wall and sustains an isolated, closed fracture of the neck of the fifth metacarpal (a boxer's fracture) with 35 degrees of apex-dorsal angulation. There is no rotational deformity (nails parallel on flexion) and no scissoring of the fingers. What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Neighbour strapping or a removable splint, with early mobilisation and reassurance

options.label: B
options.text: Open reduction and plate fixation

options.label: C
options.text: Closed reduction and percutaneous K-wiring because any angulation over 20 degrees needs surgery

options.label: D
options.text: Six weeks of rigid immobilisation in a scaphoid cast

options.label: E
options.text: Amputation of the metacarpal head

explanation: The fifth carpometacarpal joint is mobile, so the little-finger metacarpal neck tolerates considerable dorsal angulation (commonly quoted up to ~40-50 degrees) without functional loss. In the absence of malrotation or a pseudoclaw/extensor lag, conservative treatment with neighbour strapping or a functional splint and early movement gives excellent results. The crucial things that DO mandate intervention are malrotation (scissoring) and, less so, marked shortening or an open injury — not the angulation figure alone.
learningPoint: Boxer's fractures tolerate large dorsal angulation; rotation (scissoring), not angulation, is the key indication to operate.

stem: Theme: hand fracture and joint injuries. For each scenario, select the single most likely diagnosis from the list.

options.label: A
options.text: Bennett fracture-dislocation

options.label: B
options.text: Rolando fracture

options.label: C
options.text: Boxer's fracture (5th metacarpal neck)

options.label: D
options.text: Gamekeeper's / skier's thumb (ulnar collateral ligament injury)

options.label: E
options.text: Volar plate avulsion of the PIP joint

options.label: F
options.text: Seymour fracture

options.label: G
options.text: Jersey finger (FDP avulsion)

emiLeads.stem: An intra-articular fracture-dislocation at the base of the thumb metacarpal in which a small volar-ulnar fragment stays attached to the trapezium by the anterior oblique ligament while the metacarpal shaft is pulled proximally and radially by abductor pollicis longus.

A skier falls with the thumb forced into abduction; there is laxity and pain on radial stress of the thumb metacarpophalangeal joint, raising concern about an interposed adductor aponeurosis (Stener lesion).
A rugby player grabbing an opponent's shirt cannot actively flex the distal interphalangeal joint of the ring finger; a small bony fragment is seen retracted into the palm.
A child catches a fingertip in a door; there is a transverse fracture through the physis of the distal phalanx with an associated nail-bed injury and the nail plate displaced out of the fold.

explanation: Bennett = two-part intra-articular fracture-dislocation of the thumb metacarpal base, the volar-ulnar (Bennett) fragment held by the anterior oblique ligament while APL displaces the shaft (Rolando is the comminuted/T- or Y-shaped variant). Gamekeeper's/skier's thumb = MCP ulnar collateral ligament rupture, with a possible Stener lesion needing surgery. Jersey finger = FDP avulsion (commonly the ring finger) with loss of active DIP flexion. Seymour fracture = open physeal fracture of the distal phalanx in children with nail-bed injury and nail-plate displacement, which needs irrigation, nail-bed repair and antibiotics because of the high infection/growth-arrest risk.
learningPoint: Recognise the thumb-base patterns (Bennett vs Rolando), UCL injury (Stener), FDP avulsion (jersey finger) and the paediatric Seymour fracture.
references: Miller's — Hand & Wrist: metacarpal/phalangeal and paediatric hand injuries

subtopic: DRUJ instability — Galeazzi and forearm rotation
stem: A 35-year-old has a displaced fracture of the distal third of the radial shaft. You are concerned about an associated distal radioulnar joint (DRUJ) injury (a Galeazzi fracture-dislocation). After anatomical plate fixation of the radius, the DRUJ is assessed and found to be unstable. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Assess DRUJ stability through rotation and, if grossly unstable, immobilise the forearm in supination (with K-wire stabilisation of the DRUJ if it remains unstable or irreducible)

options.label: B
options.text: Leave the forearm free and mobilise immediately; the DRUJ always reduces once the radius is fixed

options.label: C
options.text: Excise the distal ulna (Darrach procedure) at the index operation

options.label: D
options.text: Immobilise the forearm in full pronation

options.label: E
options.text: Convert to a one-bone forearm

explanation: In a Galeazzi injury, anatomical reduction and rigid fixation of the radial shaft usually restores DRUJ congruity. The joint must still be tested intra-operatively. If it is stable, early motion is allowed; if it is unstable but reducible, the classic teaching is to splint in supination (which tightens the volar restraints and seats the ulnar head), often with temporary K-wire stabilisation of the reduced DRUJ. If the joint is irreducible (commonly because of interposed extensor carpi ulnaris or other soft tissue), open reduction is required. Distal ulna excision is a salvage option, not an acute solution.
learningPoint: Fix the radius anatomically, then test the DRUJ: stable → mobilise; unstable but reducible → supination cast ± K-wire; irreducible → open the joint.
references: Miller's — Hand & Wrist / Trauma: Galeazzi fracture-dislocation and DRUJ

stem: A 26-year-old gymnast has chronic ulnar-sided wrist pain with a positive fovea sign and a click on the ulnocarpal stress test. Arthroscopy shows a peripheral, ulnar-sided detachment of the triangular fibrocartilage complex from its foveal insertion, and the DRUJ is unstable. Regarding the TFCC and this injury pattern, which statement is most accurate?

options.label: A
options.text: Peripheral (Palmer 1B) tears are vascular and the foveal attachment is the key stabiliser of the DRUJ, so repair is favoured

options.label: B
options.text: The central articular disc is well vascularised, so central tears should always be repaired rather than debrided

options.label: C
options.text: The TFCC plays no role in distal radioulnar joint stability

options.label: D
options.text: All TFCC tears, central or peripheral, are best treated by immediate ulnar shortening osteotomy

options.label: E
options.text: Peripheral tears are avascular and therefore irreparable, requiring excision

explanation: The TFCC is the principal soft-tissue stabiliser of the DRUJ, and the deep (foveal) fibres of the radioulnar ligaments are critical to that stability. The peripheral (ulnar) portion of the disc is vascularised, so peripheral/foveal (Palmer class 1B) tears are repairable and repair is preferred, especially when the DRUJ is unstable. By contrast, the central avascular portion of the disc (class 1A) is typically debrided rather than repaired. Ulnar shortening osteotomy is used for ulnocarpal impaction with positive ulnar variance (often degenerate 2C lesions), not as the default for every tear.
learningPoint: TFCC is the main DRUJ stabiliser; vascular peripheral/foveal (1B) tears are repaired (especially with DRUJ instability), central avascular (1A) tears are debrided.
references: Miller's — Hand & Wrist: TFCC and DRUJ

stem: A 52-year-old woman describes nocturnal paraesthesia in the thumb, index and middle fingers that she shakes out for relief. Examination shows preserved sensation over the thenar eminence skin despite reduced sensation at the index pulp. Which anatomical fact best explains why the thenar eminence skin sensation is spared?

options.label: A
options.text: The recurrent motor branch of the median nerve carries cutaneous fibres to the thenar skin

options.label: B
options.text: The palmar cutaneous branch of the median nerve arises proximal to the flexor retinaculum and passes superficial to it

options.label: C
options.text: The thenar skin is supplied entirely by the superficial branch of the radial nerve

options.label: D
options.text: The ulnar nerve supplies the radial half of the palm via the deep branch

options.label: E
options.text: The palmar cutaneous branch travels within the carpal tunnel alongside the digital branches

explanation: The palmar cutaneous branch of the median nerve arises about 5 cm proximal to the wrist crease and runs superficial to (not through) the flexor retinaculum to supply the skin over the thenar eminence and central palm. Because it does not pass through the carpal tunnel, it is spared in carpal tunnel syndrome, whereas the digital sensory fibres (which do traverse the tunnel) are affected. This branch is also at risk in an open carpal tunnel release if the incision strays radial to the axis of the ring finger, producing a painful palmar neuroma.
learningPoint: Thenar skin sensation is preserved in CTS because the palmar cutaneous branch arises proximal to and lies superficial to the flexor retinaculum.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: compressive neuropathies
BSSH guidelines on carpal tunnel syndrome

stem: A 60-year-old man with a 9-month history of carpal tunnel syndrome has constant numbness in the median distribution, measurable two-point discrimination of 9 mm at the index pulp, and early thenar wasting. Nerve conduction studies confirm a severe lesion with absent sensory responses. A trial of night splinting has failed. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Repeat a 6-week course of night splinting

options.label: B
options.text: Corticosteroid injection into the carpal tunnel and review in 6 months

options.label: C
options.text: Carpal tunnel decompression (surgical release of the flexor retinaculum)

options.label: D
options.text: Oral gabapentin and reassurance

options.label: E
options.text: Therapeutic ultrasound and tendon-gliding exercises

explanation: Constant sensory loss, abnormal two-point discrimination, thenar wasting and neurophysiologically severe disease indicate established axonal compromise. Conservative measures (splinting, steroid injection) are appropriate for mild-to-moderate or intermittent symptoms but are inadequate once there is fixed sensory loss and motor wasting. Surgical decompression is indicated to halt progression; recovery of the wasting may be incomplete but decompression prevents further deterioration and usually relieves pain and improves sensation. NICE and BSSH support surgery for severe or persistent CTS that has not responded to non-operative treatment.
learningPoint: Thenar wasting, constant numbness or neurophysiologically severe CTS warrants surgical decompression rather than further conservative measures.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: carpal tunnel syndrome

subtopic: Compression neuropathies of the upper limb
stem: Theme: Sites and syndromes of upper-limb nerve compression. For each clinical description, select the SINGLE most likely diagnosis from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Pronator syndrome

options.label: B
options.text: Anterior interosseous nerve syndrome

options.label: C
options.text: Cubital tunnel syndrome

options.label: D
options.text: Guyon's canal compression (ulnar tunnel syndrome)

options.label: E
options.text: Radial tunnel syndrome

options.label: F
options.text: Posterior interosseous nerve syndrome

options.label: G
options.text: Wartenberg syndrome (superficial radial nerve)

options.label: H
options.text: Carpal tunnel syndrome

emiLeads.stem: A cyclist presents with weakness of the small muscles of the hand and numbness of the little and ring fingers, but with preserved sensation on the dorsum of the hand and a normal Tinel's sign at the elbow.
A patient is unable to make an 'OK' sign because of weakness of the flexor pollicis longus and the flexor digitorum profundus to the index finger, with no sensory loss.

A patient complains of deep aching pain in the proximal dorsoradial forearm, reproduced by resisted supination and tenderness 4 cm distal to the lateral epicondyle, with no motor weakness and a normal extensor function.
A patient reports forearm pain with paraesthesia in the radial three and a half digits including the thenar skin, worsened by resisted pronation, and a negative Phalen's test.
A patient develops a progressive inability to extend the fingers at the metacarpophalangeal joints with radial deviation on wrist extension but no sensory loss.

explanation: Guyon's canal compression spares the dorsal cutaneous branch of the ulnar nerve (which arises proximal to the wrist), so dorsal sensation is preserved — unlike cubital tunnel syndrome. AIN syndrome is a pure motor palsy of FPL, FDP to index and pronator quadratus producing a weak pinch and abnormal 'OK' sign with no sensory deficit. Radial tunnel syndrome is a pain syndrome (no weakness) from compression of the PIN, classically at the arcade of Frohse, with tenderness in the mobile wad. Pronator syndrome compresses the median nerve in the proximal forearm and, crucially, involves the palmar cutaneous branch so thenar skin sensation is affected (distinguishing it from CTS). PIN syndrome is a motor palsy causing finger-drop with radial wrist deviation because ECRL (radial nerve proper) is spared while the ulnar wrist extensors are weak.
learningPoint: Dorsal hand sensation distinguishes Guyon's canal (spared) from cubital tunnel; thenar skin sensation distinguishes pronator syndrome (affected) from CTS (spared).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: compressive neuropathies

stem: A histopathologist reviews tissue from a Dupuytren fasciectomy and reports a proliferative nodule rich in cells expressing alpha-smooth-muscle actin. Which cell type and matrix component are most characteristic of the active (proliferative) phase of Dupuytren disease?

options.label: A
options.text: Osteoblasts producing type I collagen

options.label: B
options.text: Myofibroblasts with a relative increase in type III collagen

options.label: C
options.text: Chondrocytes producing type II collagen

options.label: D
options.text: Mast cells producing elastin

options.label: E
options.text: Adipocytes producing type IV collagen

explanation: Dupuytren disease is a fibroproliferative disorder of the palmar fascia. The proliferative and involutional phases are dominated by myofibroblasts (fibroblasts that express alpha-smooth-muscle actin and contract), and there is a shift in collagen with a relative increase in type III (normal fascia is predominantly type I). The cytokine TGF-beta drives myofibroblast differentiation and contraction. The residual phase becomes acellular cord composed mainly of type I collagen. Understanding this biology explains both contracture formation and the rationale for collagenase (which lyses collagen in the cord).
learningPoint: Dupuytren disease is a myofibroblast-driven fibroproliferative process with a relative increase in type III collagen during the active phase.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: Dupuytren disease

stem: A 58-year-old man has a Dupuytren cord producing a 40-degree fixed flexion contracture at the little finger metacarpophalangeal (MCP) joint and a 35-degree contracture at the proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joint. He requests treatment. Which statement best reflects current expected outcomes that should guide his consent?

options.label: A
options.text: PIP joint contractures generally correct more completely and recur less often than MCP joint contractures

options.label: B
options.text: MCP joint contractures generally correct more completely than PIP joint contractures, which are more prone to incomplete correction and recurrence

options.label: C
options.text: Contractures of any joint never recur after limited fasciectomy

options.label: D
options.text: Surgery is indicated only once the contracture exceeds 90 degrees at the MCP joint

options.label: E
options.text: Digital nerve injury is impossible during limited fasciectomy because the nerves lie deep to the cord

explanation: MCP joint contractures correct reliably and tolerate even severe contracture before intervention because the collateral ligaments are lax in extension. PIP joint contractures are notoriously harder to fully correct and recur more frequently because of secondary changes in the volar plate, checkrein ligaments and collateral ligaments. A commonly used threshold for intervention is an MCP contracture of about 30 degrees or any PIP contracture, or a positive Hueston tabletop test. Spiral cords can displace the neurovascular bundle superficially and centrally, putting the digital nerve at real risk during dissection. Recurrence is a recognised outcome of all treatments, so realistic expectations are essential to consent.
learningPoint: PIP joint Dupuytren contractures correct less completely and recur more often than MCP contractures; warn patients accordingly.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: Dupuytren disease

stem: A 28-year-old chef sustains a clean laceration over the volar aspect of the middle phalanx of the ring finger and cannot flex the distal interphalangeal joint, although PIP flexion is preserved. In which flexor tendon zone is the injury, and which structure is most likely divided?

options.label: A
options.text: Zone I — flexor digitorum profundus only

options.label: B
options.text: Zone II — both flexor digitorum superficialis and profundus

options.label: C
options.text: Zone III — lumbrical origin

options.label: D
options.text: Zone IV — tendons within the carpal tunnel

options.label: E
options.text: Zone V — musculotendinous junction in the forearm

explanation: Loss of isolated DIP flexion with intact PIP flexion indicates an isolated flexor digitorum profundus (FDP) division with an intact flexor digitorum superficialis (FDS). FDS inserts on the middle phalanx and flexes the PIP, while FDP continues to the distal phalanx and flexes the DIP. Zone I lies distal to the insertion of FDS (i.e. over the middle phalanx and beyond) and contains only the FDP. Zone II (Bunnell's 'no man's land') extends from the A1 pulley/distal palmar crease to the FDS insertion and contains both tendons within the sheath. Accurate zoning guides repair technique and rehabilitation.
learningPoint: Isolated loss of DIP flexion with preserved PIP flexion localises a Zone I injury affecting FDP alone.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: flexor tendon injury

stem: A hand surgeon repairs a Zone II flexor tendon laceration and wishes to maximise repair strength to allow an early active mobilisation protocol. Which technical factor has the greatest influence on the tensile strength of a primary flexor tendon repair?

options.label: A
options.text: The calibre of the epitendinous suture relative to the core suture

options.label: B
options.text: The number of suture strands crossing the repair site

options.label: C
options.text: Complete excision of the A2 and A4 pulleys to reduce friction

options.label: D
options.text: Use of an absorbable rather than a non-absorbable core suture

options.label: E
options.text: Placing the core suture knots on the volar surface of the tendon

explanation: Repair strength is approximately proportional to the number of core suture strands crossing the repair site; a 4-strand or 6-strand core repair is substantially stronger than a 2-strand repair and is what permits modern early active motion regimens. A peripheral epitendinous suture adds 10–50% strength, tidies the repair and reduces gapping but is secondary to core strand number. The A2 and A4 pulleys are biomechanically important and should be preserved (though they can be partially vented) to prevent bowstringing. Knots placed inside the repair (or with appropriate locking configurations) and increased suture purchase also help. Gapping at the repair, not just rupture, compromises gliding and strength, so the construct must resist gap formation under early active load.
learningPoint: Increasing the number of core suture strands across a flexor tendon repair is the dominant determinant of repair strength, enabling early active mobilisation.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: flexor tendon repair biomechanics

stem: A 35-year-old man catches a ball on the end of his middle finger and develops a droop of the distal phalanx. Radiographs show no fracture and the joint is congruent. He cannot actively extend the DIP joint, which rests in about 30 degrees of flexion. What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.label: A
options.text: Continuous extension splinting of the DIP joint for 6–8 weeks

options.label: B
options.text: Open repair of the terminal extensor tendon

options.label: C
options.text: K-wire arthrodesis of the DIP joint

options.label: D
options.text: Splinting of the PIP joint in extension while leaving the DIP free

options.label: E
options.text: Immediate active range-of-motion exercises with buddy strapping

explanation: This is a soft-tissue (tendinous) mallet finger — rupture or attenuation of the terminal extensor tendon at its insertion on the distal phalanx. The standard treatment is continuous splinting of the DIP joint in full extension (or slight hyperextension) for 6–8 weeks, ensuring the joint is never allowed to flex during this period (including during splint changes), followed by a weaning period. The PIP joint should be left free to move. Most soft-tissue mallets and many small bony mallets without volar subluxation do well with splinting. Surgery is reserved for large bony fragments with volar subluxation of the distal phalanx, open injuries, or selected failures.
learningPoint: A soft-tissue mallet finger is treated with continuous full-time DIP extension splinting for 6–8 weeks, keeping the PIP free.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: extensor tendon injuries

stem: A boxer presents after a punch with painful snapping of the extensor digitorum communis tendon over the middle finger MCP joint; the tendon subluxates ulnarwards when the finger is flexed and he cannot fully initiate extension from a flexed position, though he can hold it extended once passively placed there. Which structure has most likely been injured?

options.label: A
options.text: The central slip at the PIP joint

options.label: B
options.text: The radial sagittal band at the MCP joint

options.label: C
options.text: The terminal extensor tendon

options.label: D
options.text: The juncturae tendinum

options.label: E
options.text: The A1 pulley

explanation: The sagittal bands centralise the extensor tendon over the MCP joint. Rupture of the radial sagittal band (the most commonly injured, classically the middle finger — 'boxer's knuckle') allows the extensor tendon to subluxate ulnarwards in flexion. The patient can maintain extension once the finger is passively extended but cannot actively initiate extension from full flexion because the dislocated tendon loses its extensor moment arm. Acute injuries can be treated with relative-motion (yoke) extension splinting for 4–6 weeks; chronic or irreducible cases require surgical reconstruction. This is distinct from a central slip injury (Boutonniere) which is at the PIP joint.
learningPoint: Ulnar subluxation of the extensor tendon at the MCP with an extensor lag from flexion indicates a radial sagittal band rupture (boxer's knuckle).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: extensor mechanism

stem: A patient has a high radial nerve palsy following a humeral shaft fracture that has failed to recover after 6 months despite an intact nerve on exploration and is now planned for tendon transfers to restore wrist and finger extension. In the standard transfer set, which muscle is most commonly used to restore finger extension (MCP extension)?

options.label: A
options.text: Pronator teres to extensor carpi radialis brevis

options.label: B
options.text: Flexor carpi radialis (or flexor carpi ulnaris) to extensor digitorum communis

options.label: C
options.text: Palmaris longus to extensor pollicis longus

options.label: D
options.text: Flexor digitorum superficialis to abductor pollicis brevis

options.label: E
options.text: Extensor indicis proprius to extensor pollicis longus

explanation: In radial nerve palsy the classic transfer set restores three functions: wrist extension, finger (MCP) extension and thumb extension/abduction. Wrist extension is restored by pronator teres to ECRB. Finger extension is most commonly restored by transferring a wrist flexor — flexor carpi ulnaris (FCU) in the Jones-type transfer, or flexor carpi radialis (FCR) which preserves FCU's important role in ulnar wrist stability — into extensor digitorum communis. Thumb extension/abduction is restored by palmaris longus (or FDS of the ring finger) to a rerouted extensor pollicis longus. EIP to EPL is the transfer for an isolated EPL rupture (e.g. after a distal radius fracture), not for global radial palsy.
learningPoint: In radial nerve palsy, finger MCP extension is restored by transferring a wrist flexor (FCR or FCU) to EDC.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: tendon transfers

stem: When planning a tendon transfer, a surgeon considers the principles that predict a successful outcome. A muscle is selected whose excursion and line of pull match the desired action, leaving a synergist in place. Which additional principle is essential before any tendon transfer is undertaken?

options.label: A
options.text: The donor muscle should be at least two MRC grades stronger than required because transferred muscles gain power

options.label: B
options.text: Supple, full passive range of motion of the target joints with stable soft-tissue equilibrium must be present before transfer

options.label: C
options.text: The transfer should always cross two joints to maximise the lever arm

options.label: D
options.text: The donor tendon should be passed through scarred tissue to anchor it securely

options.label: E
options.text: Sensory loss in the territory is a contraindication that cannot be overcome

explanation: Core principles of tendon transfer include: a supple joint with full passive range of motion before transfer (you cannot move a stiff joint with a transfer); adequate donor strength (a transferred muscle typically loses about one MRC grade, so the donor should be strong — not stronger because it gains power, which is incorrect); matched excursion and line of pull, ideally one tendon for one function; routing through healthy, gliding tissue (a suitable bed) rather than scar; expendable donor with a remaining synergist; and ideally synergism to ease re-education. Insensate but stable tissue can still benefit from a transfer in selected cases, so sensory loss is not an absolute contraindication. A straight line of pull through a single appropriate joint is preferred.
learningPoint: A supple joint with full passive range of motion and a healthy gliding tendon bed are prerequisites before any tendon transfer.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: principles of tendon transfer

stem: A 62-year-old woman has painful basal thumb arthritis affecting the trapeziometacarpal joint. Examination shows a positive grind test, adduction of the thumb metacarpal with compensatory hyperextension of the MCP joint, and radiographs confirming joint space loss at the trapeziometacarpal joint with sparing of the scaphotrapezial joint (Eaton stage III). Conservative measures including a splint and an intra-articular injection have failed. Which operation is the most established surgical option?

options.label: A
options.text: Trapeziectomy (with or without ligament reconstruction/tendon interposition)

options.label: B
options.text: Arthrodesis of the metacarpophalangeal joint of the thumb

options.label: C
options.text: Proximal row carpectomy

options.label: D
options.text: Wrist denervation (anterior and posterior interosseous neurectomy)

options.label: E
options.text: Silicone replacement of the scaphoid

explanation: Trapeziometacarpal (basal thumb) osteoarthritis that has failed non-operative management is most commonly and reliably treated by trapeziectomy. Adding ligament reconstruction and tendon interposition (LRTI) has not been shown in trials to be superior to simple trapeziectomy for pain or function, and simple trapeziectomy has fewer complications. Alternatives in selected patients include trapeziometacarpal arthrodesis (favoured in younger, high-demand manual workers who need strength and accept loss of motion) and arthroplasty. The associated MCP hyperextension deformity may require addressing (e.g. volar plate tightening or MCP fusion) if marked. Proximal row carpectomy and scaphoid replacement are not treatments for basal thumb arthritis.
learningPoint: Trapeziectomy is the established operation for trapeziometacarpal OA refractory to conservative treatment; adding LRTI confers no proven benefit over simple excision.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: thumb CMC arthritis

stem: A 65-year-old woman has a firm swelling on the dorsum of the DIP joint of her index finger with longitudinal grooving of the adjacent nail and underlying osteoarthritic changes. Which statement best describes this lesion?

options.label: A
options.text: It is a mucous cyst, a ganglion arising from the osteoarthritic DIP joint, often associated with an underlying osteophyte

options.label: B
options.text: It is a giant cell tumour of the tendon sheath requiring marginal excision

options.label: C
options.text: It is a glomus tumour and explains the nail change

options.label: D
options.text: It is a rheumatoid nodule indicating systemic disease

options.label: E
options.text: It is an enchondroma that has expanded the distal phalanx

explanation: A mucous cyst is a ganglion associated with DIP joint osteoarthritis (a Heberden node region). It arises from the degenerate joint and is frequently associated with an underlying osteophyte; pressure on the germinal nail matrix produces a longitudinal groove or ridge in the nail. Management includes observation if asymptomatic; if treated surgically, excising the cyst together with the osteophyte (decompressing the joint) reduces recurrence. Thinning, attenuated overlying skin risks rupture and joint sepsis, which is an indication to intervene. The other lesions occur in the hand but do not fit this classic OA-associated DIP picture with nail grooving.
learningPoint: A dorsal DIP swelling with nail grooving in an osteoarthritic finger is a mucous cyst; excise the associated osteophyte to reduce recurrence.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: small joint OA and ganglia

stem: A 30-year-old man presents 3 days after a cat bite to the volar aspect of the ring finger. The finger is held in slight flexion, is diffusely swollen ('sausage' appearance), is exquisitely tender along the flexor sheath, and passive extension causes severe pain. He is systemically well. Which set of findings is being described, and what is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Kanavel's signs of pyogenic flexor tenosynovitis — urgent surgical washout of the flexor sheath plus IV antibiotics

options.label: B
options.text: Herpetic whitlow — oral aciclovir and avoid incision

options.label: C
options.text: Simple cellulitis — oral antibiotics and outpatient review

options.label: D
options.text: Gout — joint aspiration and NSAIDs

options.label: E
options.text: Acute carpal tunnel syndrome — emergency decompression of the carpal tunnel

explanation: The four Kanavel cardinal signs of pyogenic flexor tenosynovitis are: a finger held in slight flexion, fusiform ('sausage') swelling of the whole digit, tenderness along the course of the flexor sheath, and severe pain on passive extension (often the most sensitive sign). This is a surgical emergency: untreated infection within the closed sheath causes tendon necrosis, adhesions and amputation risk. Management is urgent surgical drainage/irrigation of the flexor sheath together with empirical intravenous antibiotics (covering Staphylococcus/Streptococcus, and Pasteurella multocida given the cat bite), with cultures to guide therapy.
