Some very early cases may be trialled on IV antibiotics with close monitoring, but established PFT requires theatre.
learningPoint: Kanavel's four signs indicate pyogenic flexor tenosynovitis — a surgical emergency requiring sheath washout and IV antibiotics.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: hand infections

stem: A 24-year-old man attends with a small laceration over the dorsum of the fifth MCP joint sustained during a fist fight, two days ago. The wound is now discharging and the MCP joint is swollen and painful on movement. Which organism, in addition to common skin flora, must specifically be covered, and what is the key management principle?

options.label: A
options.text: Pasteurella multocida; close the wound primarily and discharge on oral antibiotics

options.label: B
options.text: Eikenella corrodens; assume joint penetration, explore/washout the MCP joint and leave the wound open with IV antibiotics

options.label: C
options.text: Pseudomonas aeruginosa; soak in vinegar and arrange outpatient follow-up

options.label: D
options.text: Mycobacterium marinum; start anti-tuberculous therapy

options.label: E
options.text: Clostridium tetani only; give a tetanus booster and reassure

explanation: A wound over the MCP joint from a clenched-fist injury is a human 'fight bite' and must be assumed to penetrate the extensor tendon and MCP joint capsule (the tooth strikes the joint while the fist is clenched, then the injury sits more proximally as the hand relaxes, so the skin, tendon and capsular wounds are not aligned). Eikenella corrodens is the characteristic organism of human bites and is co-amoxiclav-sensitive but not covered by first-generation cephalosporins or flucloxacillin alone. Management is surgical exploration and washout of the joint, debridement, leaving the wound open (never primary closure), and antibiotics (co-amoxiclav, or clindamycin plus a quinolone if penicillin-allergic) with tetanus prophylaxis. Pasteurella is the cat/dog bite organism, not the human bite organism.
learningPoint: Treat a fight-bite wound over the MCP joint as a penetrating joint injury (Eikenella corrodens): explore, washout, leave open, and give co-amoxiclav.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: bite injuries

stem: A 26-year-old woman has a painless, firm, well-defined swelling on the dorsum of her wrist that transilluminates and has fluctuated in size over months. It is most prominent in wrist flexion. From which structure does the most common dorsal wrist ganglion typically arise?

options.label: A
options.text: The scapholunate interosseous ligament

options.label: B
options.text: The flexor carpi radialis tendon sheath

options.label: C
options.text: The distal radioulnar joint

options.label: D
options.text: The pisotriquetral joint

options.label: E
options.text: The extensor carpi ulnaris subsheath

explanation: The most common dorsal wrist ganglion arises from the scapholunate interosseous ligament/dorsal capsule of the scapholunate region, communicating with the radiocarpal joint via a one-way valve mechanism that explains size fluctuation. Ganglia contain viscous mucin and characteristically transilluminate. The most common volar wrist ganglion arises from the radioscaphoid or scaphotrapezial region (close to the radial artery, which is relevant during excision). Most ganglia are benign and can be observed; aspiration has a high recurrence rate, and surgical excision must include the capsular stalk to minimise recurrence. Knowledge of the origin matters because incomplete excision of the stalk is the main reason for recurrence.
learningPoint: The commonest dorsal wrist ganglion arises from the scapholunate ligament/dorsal capsule; complete stalk excision reduces recurrence.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: ganglia

stem: Before excising a recurrent volar wrist ganglion, which preoperative or intraoperative consideration is most important?

options.label: A
options.text: The radial artery is closely related to the volar radial ganglion and must be identified and protected during dissection

options.label: B
options.text: Volar ganglia never recur, so wide excision of surrounding tendons is required

options.label: C
options.text: Aspiration is contraindicated because volar ganglia are always malignant

options.label: D
options.text: The ulnar nerve lies directly deep to the ganglion and is the main structure at risk

options.label: E
options.text: A tourniquet must be avoided to keep the artery visible

explanation: A volar (palmar) radial wrist ganglion typically arises near the radioscaphoid or scaphotrapezial joint and lies in intimate relation to the radial artery; the artery may be densely adherent to or wrapped around the ganglion. The most important consideration is identification and careful protection (and if necessary an Allen's test to confirm the patency of the ulnar contribution) of the radial artery during excision, as inadvertent injury can cause significant bleeding or vascular compromise. Excision should still remove the stalk to reduce recurrence, and a bloodless field (tourniquet) actually aids safe dissection rather than being avoided. Volar ganglia are benign but do recur, so the answer is about vascular safety.
learningPoint: The radial artery is intimately related to volar radial wrist ganglia and must be identified and protected during excision.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: ganglia

stem: A patient sustains a closed traction injury to a peripheral nerve. Electrophysiology and clinical examination indicate that the axons have undergone Wallerian degeneration distally but the endoneurial tubes and surrounding connective tissue (endoneurium, perineurium and epineurium) remain intact. Using the Sunderland classification, what grade of injury is this, and what recovery is expected?

options.label: A
options.text: Sunderland I (neurapraxia) — full recovery within days to weeks

options.label: B
options.text: Sunderland II (axonotmesis with intact endoneurium) — good recovery by axonal regrowth along intact tubes

options.label: C
options.text: Sunderland III (endoneurium disrupted) — recovery possible but often incomplete with some misdirection

options.label: D
options.text: Sunderland V (complete transection) — no recovery without surgical repair

options.label: E
options.text: Sunderland VI (mixed/neuroma-in-continuity) — variable, fascicle-dependent

explanation: In the Sunderland classification, grade II is axonotmesis with Wallerian degeneration of the axon but preservation of the endoneurial tubes (and therefore the perineurium and epineurium). Because the endoneurial scaffold is intact, regenerating axons grow back along their original tubes to the correct end-organs, so prognosis for recovery is good (regeneration proceeds at roughly 1 mm/day). Grade I (neurapraxia) is a conduction block with intact axons and rapid full recovery. Grade III adds endoneurial disruption, so recovery is less reliable and may be misdirected. Grade IV leaves only the epineurium intact (a neuroma-in-continuity) and grade V is complete transection — both generally need surgery. The presence of an advancing Tinel sign signals regenerating axons.
learningPoint: Sunderland II is axonotmesis with intact endoneurial tubes, allowing accurate axonal regeneration and a good prognosis.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Basic science/Hand: peripheral nerve injury

stem: A patient has a low ulnar nerve injury at the wrist. Compared with a high ulnar nerve injury at the elbow, why is the clawing of the ring and little fingers MORE pronounced with the low (distal) lesion?

options.label: A
options.text: Because the lumbricals to the ring and little fingers are spared in a low lesion

options.label: B
options.text: Because in a high lesion the FDP to the ring and little fingers is also paralysed, reducing the unopposed flexion at the DIP/PIP joints (the 'ulnar paradox')

options.label: C
options.text: Because the low lesion paralyses the extensor digitorum communis

options.label: D
options.text: Because the median nerve compensates more effectively in high lesions

options.label: E
options.text: Because the flexor carpi ulnaris is spared only in the low lesion, increasing clawing

explanation: This is the 'ulnar paradox'. Ulnar claw hand results from loss of the medial two lumbricals (and interossei) producing MCP hyperextension and IP flexion of the ring and little fingers from the unopposed long extensors and flexors. In a high ulnar lesion at the elbow, the ulnar half of flexor digitorum profundus (to ring and little fingers) is also paralysed, so there is less unopposed flexion force at the DIP/PIP joints and the claw is less marked. In a low (wrist) lesion the FDP is intact, so the flexion deformity at the IP joints is stronger and the claw is more obvious — hence the paradox that the more distal (and clinically 'lesser') lesion produces the uglier deformity. Recovery in a high lesion will also reduce the claw appearance as the FDP returns.
learningPoint: The ulnar paradox: a low ulnar lesion produces more marked clawing because the FDP to ring/little fingers is intact, unlike in a high lesion.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: ulnar nerve

stem: An on-call hand surgeon is referred several amputations. For which of the following is replantation MOST strongly indicated according to accepted principles?

options.label: A
options.text: A single finger amputated through zone II (within the flexor sheath) in an adult

options.label: B
options.text: A thumb amputation at the proximal phalanx in an otherwise healthy adult

options.label: C
options.text: An isolated distal phalanx amputation distal to the FDP insertion with a severe crush

options.label: D
options.text: A single-finger amputation in a 70-year-old with peripheral vascular disease and a 12-hour warm ischaemia time

options.label: E
options.text: A heavily contaminated, multi-level (segmental) amputation of a single border digit

explanation: Strong indications for replantation include: thumb amputations (the thumb provides about 40% of hand function and should almost always be replanted if feasible), multiple digits, amputations through the palm/wrist/forearm, almost any amputation in a child, and single digits distal to the FDS insertion. Isolated single-finger replantation through zone II in adults has historically given poor functional results (stiffness) and is a relative contraindication, although selected cases are now done. Contraindications/relative contraindications include severe crush or multi-level injury, prolonged warm ischaemia, gross contamination, and significant comorbidity such as peripheral vascular disease. Therefore a thumb amputation in a healthy adult is the strongest indication among the options.
learningPoint: Thumb amputations are among the strongest indications for replantation; isolated adult zone II single-finger amputations are a relative contraindication.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: replantation
BSSH/BOAST principles of amputation and replantation

stem: A patient sustains a forearm-level (major limb) amputation. Compared with digital amputations, why is the tolerable ischaemia time much shorter for a major limb replant, and how should the amputated part be transported?

options.label: A
options.text: Because muscle tolerates ischaemia poorly; cool the part by wrapping it in saline-moistened gauze, sealing it in a bag, and placing the bag on ice (not in direct contact with ice and never in dry ice or frozen)

options.label: B
options.text: Because bone is highly metabolically active; the part should be placed directly on dry ice

options.label: C
options.text: Because nerve recovers slowly; the part should be kept warm at body temperature in saline

options.label: D
options.text: Because skin necroses rapidly; the part should be frozen solid to preserve it

options.label: E
options.text: Because tendons swell; the part should be immersed directly in iced water for several hours

explanation: Major (proximal) amputations contain significant skeletal muscle, which tolerates ischaemia far less well than the relatively avascular tissues of a digit. Warm ischaemia of more than about 6 hours (or cold ischaemia beyond roughly 12 hours) risks irreversible muscle necrosis, myoglobinuria and reperfusion injury — so major replants are time-critical, and a fasciotomy is often required. Digits (little muscle) tolerate much longer (up to ~12 hours warm, ~24 hours cold). Correct transport: wrap the part in saline-moistened gauze, seal it in a watertight plastic bag, and place that bag on ice/iced water — the part must NOT touch ice directly (frostbite injury) and must never be frozen or placed in dry ice. This cools without freezing and preserves the tissues for transfer to the replantation centre.
learningPoint: Major limb replants have short ischaemic windows because muscle dies quickly; cool the amputated part on ice via moist gauze in a sealed bag, never frozen or in direct ice contact.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: replantation

stem: A 22-year-old woman describes her fingers turning white, then blue, then red on cold exposure, symmetrically affecting both hands, with no digital ulceration. Examination, nailfold capillaroscopy and autoantibody screen are normal. Which statement best characterises her condition?

options.label: A
options.text: This is primary Raynaud disease; first-line management is conservative (cold avoidance, gloves, smoking cessation), with calcium-channel blockers such as nifedipine if needed

options.label: B
options.text: This is secondary Raynaud phenomenon and mandates urgent digital sympathectomy

options.label: C
options.text: The presence of a triphasic colour change confirms an underlying connective tissue disease

options.label: D
options.text: Beta-blockers are the first-line pharmacological treatment

options.label: E
options.text: Abnormal nailfold capillaries are typical of primary Raynaud disease

explanation: Primary Raynaud disease is common, typically affects young women, is symmetrical, and is not associated with digital ulceration, abnormal nailfold capillaroscopy or positive autoantibodies. Management is predominantly conservative: cold and stress avoidance, hand warmers/gloves, and smoking cessation; calcium-channel blockers (e.g. nifedipine) are first-line pharmacological therapy when needed. Features suggesting SECONDARY Raynaud (and prompting a search for connective tissue disease such as systemic sclerosis) include older age of onset, asymmetry, digital ulceration or pitting, abnormal nailfold capillaries and positive autoantibodies. Beta-blockers can actually precipitate vasospasm and should be avoided. Sympathectomy and prostacyclin infusions are reserved for severe secondary disease with critical digital ischaemia, not uncomplicated primary disease.
learningPoint: Primary Raynaud disease (normal capillaroscopy/autoantibodies, no ulceration) is managed conservatively first, with nifedipine if pharmacotherapy is needed; beta-blockers are avoided.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: vascular disorders

stem: A 45-year-old mechanic who repeatedly uses the heel of his hand as a hammer develops cold intolerance and ischaemic changes of the ring and little fingers of the dominant hand, with a positive Allen's test indicating ulnar artery occlusion. Which diagnosis and underlying lesion does this represent?

options.label: A
options.text: Hypothenar hammer syndrome — aneurysm or thrombosis of the ulnar artery in Guyon's canal with distal embolisation

options.label: B
options.text: Primary Raynaud disease of the dominant hand only

options.label: C
options.text: Buerger's disease of the radial artery

options.label: D
options.text: Subclavian steal syndrome

options.label: E
options.text: Embolism from atrial fibrillation lodging in the superficial palmar arch

explanation: Hypothenar hammer syndrome results from repetitive blunt trauma to the hypothenar eminence (using the palm as a hammer, or occupational vibration), which damages the superficial ulnar artery as it crosses the hook of hamate in Guyon's canal. This causes thrombosis or a true/false aneurysm of the ulnar artery with distal thromboembolism, producing unilateral (dominant-hand) cold intolerance and ischaemia of the ulnar-sided digits, often sparing the little finger if the deep arch dominates, with a positive Allen's test. Diagnosis is confirmed with duplex or angiography. Management ranges from conservative (smoking cessation, cold avoidance, calcium-channel blockers, antiplatelet/anticoagulation) to resection of the diseased ulnar artery segment with or without vein grafting if there is ongoing embolisation or aneurysm. The occupational, unilateral pattern distinguishes it from primary Raynaud disease.
learningPoint: Unilateral ulnar-sided digital ischaemia in someone using the palm as a hammer is hypothenar hammer syndrome — ulnar artery thrombosis/aneurysm at the hook of hamate.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: vascular disorders
BSSH vascular conditions of the hand

stem: Theme: Hand masses and tumours. For each clinical description, select the SINGLE most likely diagnosis from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Giant cell tumour of the tendon sheath

options.label: B
options.text: Enchondroma

options.label: C
options.text: Glomus tumour

options.label: D
options.text: Ganglion

options.label: E
options.text: Epidermoid (epidermal inclusion) cyst

options.label: F
options.text: Squamous cell carcinoma

options.label: G
options.text: Malignant melanoma (subungual)

options.label: H
options.text: Pyogenic granuloma

emiLeads.stem: A 40-year-old presents with a pathological fracture through a lytic, well-defined expansile lesion in the proximal phalanx; it is the commonest primary bone tumour of the hand and may show stippled calcification.

A patient describes severe, pinpoint, lancinating pain at the fingertip, hypersensitivity to cold, and a tiny bluish discolouration under the nail; pain is reproduced by a cold-water or pin test and relieved by a proximal tourniquet (Hildreth's sign).

A slowly growing, firm, lobulated, painless solid mass on the volar aspect of a finger that does NOT transilluminate; it is the second most common hand mass after the ganglion and may erode adjacent bone.
An elderly patient with a longstanding pigmented streak in the nail that has widened, with pigment spreading onto the nail fold.
A firm subcutaneous nodule at the fingertip following a previous penetrating injury, lined by keratinising squamous epithelium and filled with keratin.

explanation: Enchondroma is the commonest primary bone tumour of the hand and a frequent cause of a pathological fracture in the phalanx; treatment after fracture union is usually curettage and bone grafting. Glomus tumours arise from the glomus body (often subungual) and cause the classic triad of pinpoint pain, cold hypersensitivity and point tenderness, with Love's pin test and Hildreth's tourniquet sign aiding diagnosis. Giant cell tumour of the tendon sheath (localised pigmented villonodular synovitis) is the second commonest hand mass — a firm, lobulated, non-transilluminating solid lesion that can scallop bone and has a notable recurrence rate after excision. Subungual melanoma is suggested by a widening pigmented nail streak with periungual pigment spread (Hutchinson's sign) and must not be mistaken for a benign naevus or subungual haematoma. An epidermal inclusion cyst follows implantation of epidermis after a penetrating injury and is lined by squamous epithelium.
learningPoint: Enchondroma is the commonest hand bone tumour; glomus tumour gives the pinpoint-pain/cold/tenderness triad; GCT of tendon sheath is the commonest solid soft-tissue hand mass.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: tumours of the hand

stem: A 35-year-old presents with an incidental, well-defined lytic lesion with endosteal scalloping and faint matrix calcification in the proximal phalanx, found on a radiograph taken after a sports injury. There is no associated soft-tissue mass and no cortical breach. There has been no pathological fracture. What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Observation with interval radiographs for an asymptomatic, radiologically benign enchondroma

options.label: B
options.text: Immediate wide local excision and reconstruction

options.label: C
options.text: Ray amputation

options.label: D
options.text: Radiotherapy to the lesion

options.label: E
options.text: Curettage and bone grafting irrespective of symptoms or fracture, as a matter of urgency

explanation: An incidental, asymptomatic enchondroma with classic benign radiological features (well-defined, lytic, endosteal scalloping, faint chondroid calcification, no cortical destruction or soft-tissue mass) can be safely observed with interval radiographs. Active treatment — curettage and bone grafting — is indicated if it becomes symptomatic, enlarges, or causes a pathological fracture (commonly the surgery is performed once a fracture has united). Features that should raise concern for chondrosarcoma (rare in the hand but more aggressive in this site) include pain without fracture, cortical destruction, a soft-tissue mass, or interval growth, and would prompt biopsy/referral. Wide excision, ray amputation and radiotherapy are not appropriate for a benign enchondroma.
learningPoint: An asymptomatic radiologically typical enchondroma can be observed; curettage and grafting are reserved for symptomatic lesions or after a pathological fracture.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: enchondroma

stem: A 28-year-old manual worker has chronic wrist pain. Radiographs show increased density (sclerosis) of the lunate with early loss of height but no fragmentation, and the carpal architecture and height are preserved. MRI confirms lunate avascular necrosis. Which classification is used for this condition and which factor is strongly associated with its development?

options.label: A
options.text: Lichtman classification; negative ulnar variance (ulnar minus) is a recognised association

options.label: B
options.text: Lichtman classification; positive ulnar variance is the principal cause

options.label: C
options.text: Mayfield classification; perilunate ligament injury is the cause

options.label: D
options.text: Herbert classification; a scaphoid waist fracture is the cause

options.label: E
options.text: Eaton classification; trapeziometacarpal subluxation is the cause

explanation: Kienbock disease is avascular necrosis of the lunate and is staged by the Lichtman classification: stage I (normal radiograph, abnormal MRI), stage II (lunate sclerosis without collapse), stage IIIA (lunate collapse with preserved carpal alignment), stage IIIB (lunate collapse with fixed scaphoid rotation/loss of carpal height), and stage IV (pancarpal arthritis). The case described (sclerosis with early height loss but preserved architecture) is around stage II–IIIA. Negative ulnar variance (ulna shorter than radius) increases load transmitted through the lunate and is a recognised association, which underpins the rationale for joint-levelling procedures (e.g. radial shortening osteotomy) in early disease with negative variance. The Mayfield classification describes progressive perilunate instability, and Herbert classifies scaphoid fractures — different entities.
learningPoint: Kienbock disease (lunate AVN) is staged by Lichtman and is associated with negative ulnar variance, supporting joint-levelling osteotomy in early disease.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: Kienbock disease

stem: A 30-year-old labourer has Lichtman stage IIIA Kienbock disease (lunate collapse without fixed scaphoid rotation) and neutral-to-negative ulnar variance. Non-operative measures have failed. Which surgical strategy is most consistent with current principles for this stage and variance?

options.label: A
options.text: A joint-levelling/unloading procedure such as radial shortening osteotomy (where ulnar variance permits) to offload the lunate

options.label: B
options.text: Total wrist arthrodesis as the first-line procedure

options.label: C
options.text: Trapeziectomy

options.label: D
options.text: Proximal row carpectomy is mandatory at this stage regardless of cartilage status

options.label: E
options.text: Silastic lunate replacement as the procedure of choice

explanation: For earlier Kienbock disease (stages II and IIIA) with negative or neutral ulnar variance, lunate-unloading procedures are favoured. Radial shortening osteotomy (a joint-levelling procedure) reduces load through the lunate and is appropriate when variance allows; if the wrist is ulnar-positive, a capitate-shortening or other unloading procedure is preferred rather than radial shortening. Revascularisation procedures (vascularised bone grafts) are also used in stages II–IIIA. Salvage operations such as proximal row carpectomy or partial/total wrist fusion are reserved for advanced disease (stage IIIB with fixed deformity, or stage IV arthritis). Silastic lunate replacement is largely abandoned because of silicone synovitis. Total wrist fusion is not a first-line choice for stage IIIA in a young patient.
learningPoint: Early Kienbock disease (II–IIIA) with neutral/negative ulnar variance is managed by lunate-unloading procedures such as radial shortening osteotomy; salvage fusion/PRC is reserved for advanced stages.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hand & Wrist: Kienbock disease management

stem: A 64-year-old man reports 8 months of clumsy hands, difficulty fastening buttons and an unsteady, broad-based gait. On examination he has brisk reflexes in all four limbs, a positive Hoffmann sign bilaterally, an inverted radial reflex and increased tone in the lower limbs. There are no symptoms or signs in keeping with isolated nerve-root compression. MRI shows multilevel cervical canal stenosis with cord signal change at C4/5 and C5/6. His modified Japanese Orthopaedic Association (mJOA) score is 11. Which is the single most appropriate management recommendation?

options.label: A
options.text: Trial of cervical traction and a rigid collar for 6 weeks before re-imaging

options.label: B
options.text: Surgical decompression

options.label: C
options.text: Reassurance with annual clinical and radiological surveillance

options.label: D
options.text: Image-guided cervical epidural corticosteroid injection

options.label: E
options.text: A 12-week course of supervised neck physiotherapy and review

explanation: This is severe cervical spondylotic myelopathy. The validated mJOA severity bands are mild 15-17, moderate 12-14 and severe 0-11, so an mJOA of 11 is severe. Established myelopathy with cord signal change and a moderate or severe mJOA is a surgical problem: decompression arrests progression and offers the best chance of functional improvement, and the natural history of established myelopathy is generally stepwise deterioration. Conservative measures (collar, traction, physiotherapy, epidural injection) do not relieve cord compression and risk neurological decline. Surveillance alone is reserved for genuinely mild or radiologically incidental disease.
learningPoint: mJOA bands are mild 15-17, moderate 12-14, severe 0-11. Moderate-to-severe cervical spondylotic myelopathy (or any myelopathy with progressive signs/cord signal change) should be offered surgical decompression rather than conservative treatment.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Spine chapter (cervical myelopathy)

stem: A 70-year-old woman with cervical spondylotic myelopathy has multilevel disease from C3 to C6. On standing and supine lateral radiographs her cervical spine is kyphotic, with a markedly positive C2-C7 sagittal vertical axis and loss of the normal lordosis. Dynamic flexion-extension films show the kyphosis does not correct on extension. The compression is predominantly anterior (disc-osteophyte complexes). Which surgical approach is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Posterior laminectomy alone

options.label: B
options.text: Posterior laminoplasty

options.label: C
options.text: Anterior decompression and fusion (with or without supplementary posterior fixation)

options.label: D
options.text: Single-level cervical disc arthroplasty

options.label: E
options.text: Posterior foraminotomy without fusion

explanation: Posterior-only decompression (laminectomy or laminoplasty) relies on the cord drifting backwards away from anterior compression, which only happens when cervical alignment is lordotic or neutral. With a fixed (non-correcting) kyphosis the cord is draped over the anterior structures and cannot drift back, so posterior decompression fails and laminoplasty is contraindicated. Anterior decompression and fusion directly addresses the anterior pathology and allows correction of the kyphotic deformity; long or rigid constructs are often supplemented posteriorly. Arthroplasty is contraindicated with deformity and multilevel spondylosis.
learningPoint: Fixed cervical kyphosis is a contraindication to posterior-only decompression/laminoplasty because the cord cannot drift back; anterior (or combined) surgery is required.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Spine chapter (surgical approaches to cervical myelopathy)

stem: A 45-year-old office worker describes 3 weeks of severe pain radiating from the neck into the lateral forearm and the thumb and index finger, with paraesthesia in the same distribution. On examination the biceps reflex is reduced, there is weakness of elbow flexion and wrist extension, and the Spurling test reproduces the arm pain. There are no upper motor neurone signs and no bladder symptoms. Which single nerve root is most likely involved?

options.label: A
options.text: C5

options.label: B
options.text: C6

options.label: C
options.text: C7

options.label: D
options.text: C8

options.label: E
options.text: T1

explanation: The C6 root (typically compressed by a C5/6 disc-osteophyte) supplies sensation to the lateral forearm, thumb and index finger, contributes to elbow flexion and wrist extension, and is tested by the biceps and brachioradialis reflexes. C7 (C6/7) affects the middle finger, triceps and wrist flexion/finger extension with a depressed triceps reflex. C8 affects the little finger and intrinsic/finger flexion. The thumb-and-index sensory pattern with a reduced biceps reflex localises to C6.
learningPoint: C6 radiculopathy: thumb/index sensory loss, weak elbow flexion and wrist extension, diminished biceps/brachioradialis reflex.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Spine chapter (cervical radiculopathy/dermatomes)

stem: A 50-year-old man has a 6-week history of single-level C7 radiculopathy from a posterolateral C6/7 disc herniation, confirmed on MRI. His arm pain is the dominant complaint; he has mild triceps weakness (MRC grade 4+) but no myelopathy and no progressive deficit. He has had analgesia and physiotherapy with partial improvement and wishes to know about the natural history. Which statement best reflects current evidence and best informs his management?

options.label: A
options.text: The majority of cervical radiculopathies from soft-disc herniation improve with non-operative management, so continued conservative treatment is appropriate

options.label: B
options.text: Without surgery within 6 weeks he is at high risk of progressing to myelopathy

options.label: C
options.text: Surgery produces better long-term (2-year) arm-pain outcomes than non-operative care, so he should be listed now

options.label: D
options.text: A rigid collar worn continuously for 3 months is the treatment of choice

options.label: E
options.text: Mild triceps weakness is an absolute indication for urgent decompression

explanation: Most cervical radiculopathy due to soft-disc herniation resolves with non-operative care as the disc material resorbs; progression to myelopathy is uncommon and mild motor weakness is not an absolute surgical indication. Surgery (ACDF or posterior foraminotomy) reliably relieves arm pain faster, but randomised and long-term data show outcomes converge by around 1-2 years, so early surgery is not mandated for radiculopathy alone (making option C incorrect). Surgery is indicated for a significant or progressive motor deficit, intractable pain failing conservative care, or any myelopathy.
learningPoint: Most soft-disc cervical radiculopathy resolves non-operatively; surgery is for progressive/significant deficit, intractable pain, or myelopathy, and chiefly speeds arm-pain relief rather than improving long-term outcome.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Spine chapter (natural history of cervical radiculopathy)

stem: A 38-year-old man attends the emergency department with 2 days of worsening low back pain, bilateral leg pain and difficulty passing urine since this morning. On examination there is reduced sensation over the perineum and medial thighs, and reduced anal tone. A bladder scan after he attempts to void shows a post-void residual of 380 ml. Which is the single most appropriate immediate action?

options.label: A
options.text: Arrange an outpatient MRI within 2 weeks and start neuropathic analgesia

options.label: B
options.text: Admit for 48 hours of bed rest and reassess sphincter function

options.label: C
options.text: Arrange an emergency MRI of the whole spine to be performed and reported, and refer to the on-call spinal service the same presentation

options.label: D
options.text: Request plain lumbar radiographs and refer to physiotherapy

options.label: E
options.text: Insert a urinary catheter and discharge with urology follow-up

explanation: Saddle anaesthesia, bilateral sciatica, reduced anal tone and a post-void residual >=200 ml indicate cauda equina syndrome, here with retention (CES-R). UK pathways (GIRFT/BASS/BOA, supported by RCR MRI provision standards) require emergency MRI performed and reported, ideally within hours of presentation where retention or a PVR >=200 ml is present, and immediate referral to the spinal surgical service for consideration of urgent decompression. Outpatient imaging, bed rest, plain films or discharge all risk irreversible sphincter and sexual dysfunction. Catheterisation may be needed but must not delay imaging and referral.
learningPoint: Suspected cauda equina with retention/PVR >=200 ml mandates emergency MRI (performed and reported urgently) and same-presentation spinal referral - never outpatient imaging.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Spine chapter (cauda equina syndrome)
GIRFT/BASS national CES pathway; RCR MRI provision for CES

stem: A 30-year-old woman has an 8-week history of right-sided sciatica radiating to the dorsum of the foot and great toe, with weakness of great-toe and ankle dorsiflexion (MRC grade 4) and no bladder or bowel disturbance. Straight-leg raise is positive at 40 degrees. MRI shows a right paracentral L4/5 disc herniation. Her leg pain has not responded to 8 weeks of analgesia and physiotherapy and remains disabling. Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Continue the same conservative programme for a further 6 months before reconsidering

options.label: B
options.text: Offer microdiscectomy

options.label: C
options.text: Recommend instrumented posterolateral fusion of L4/5

options.label: D
options.text: Arrange an urgent MRI of the whole spine to exclude cauda equina

options.label: E
options.text: Prescribe a lumbar corset and bed rest

explanation: A paracentral L4/5 disc typically compresses the traversing L5 root, explaining great-toe/ankle dorsiflexion weakness and dorsal-foot symptoms. Most lumbar disc herniations settle within 6-12 weeks, but persistent, disabling radicular pain that has failed an adequate course (commonly ~6-12 weeks) of conservative care, with concordant imaging, is an indication for microdiscectomy - which provides faster leg-pain relief than continued conservative care. Fusion is not indicated for a simple primary herniation without instability. There are no cauda equina features, and bed rest is not recommended.
learningPoint: Microdiscectomy is indicated for concordant lumbar disc herniation with persistent disabling radicular pain after an adequate (~6-12 week) conservative trial; it speeds recovery but is not mandatory.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Spine chapter (lumbar disc herniation; SPORT data)
NICE NG59 (low back pain and sciatica)

stem: A 68-year-old man describes aching and heaviness in both calves and thighs that comes on after walking about 200 metres and forces him to stop. The discomfort is relieved by sitting or by leaning forward over a supermarket trolley, and he can cycle without difficulty. Foot pulses are normal and symmetrical. Which feature most reliably distinguishes his neurogenic claudication from vascular claudication?

options.label: A
options.text: Symptoms are bilateral

options.label: B
options.text: Relief on stopping walking

options.label: C
options.text: Relief with lumbar flexion (sitting/leaning forward) and ability to cycle

options.label: D
options.text: Onset after a fixed walking distance

options.label: E
options.text: Presence of calf discomfort

explanation: Both neurogenic and vascular claudication can be bilateral, calf-based and reproduced by a walking distance, and both ease on stopping. The discriminating feature is posture-dependence: lumbar flexion enlarges the canal and relieves neurogenic claudication, so patients are better sitting, leaning forward or cycling (flexed) and worse standing/walking downhill (extended). Vascular claudication is load- not posture-dependent and is not relieved by cycling, which is a greater metabolic demand. Normal symmetrical pulses further support a neurogenic cause.
learningPoint: Posture/flexion-dependence (better leaning forward or cycling) distinguishes neurogenic from vascular claudication, which is effort- not posture-dependent.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Spine chapter (lumbar spinal stenosis)

stem: A 72-year-old woman has neurogenic claudication from degenerative lumbar stenosis that has failed 6 months of physiotherapy and is significantly limiting her walking. Standing radiographs show a grade I degenerative spondylolisthesis at L4/5. Flexion-extension films demonstrate 6 mm of dynamic translation at this level. MRI confirms severe central and lateral recess stenosis at L4/5 with no stenosis elsewhere. Which is the most appropriate surgical procedure?

options.label: A
options.text: Wide multilevel laminectomy from L3 to S1

options.label: B
options.text: Decompression of L4/5 with instrumented fusion

options.label: C
options.text: Isolated decompression (laminectomy) of L4/5 without fusion

options.label: D
options.text: Interspinous distraction device alone

options.label: E
options.text: Microdiscectomy at L4/5

explanation: There is symptomatic stenosis with a degenerative spondylolisthesis that is demonstrably unstable (6 mm of dynamic translation on flexion-extension, exceeding the ~4-5 mm threshold for instability). Decompression alone in the presence of mobile/unstable spondylolisthesis risks progression of the slip and recurrent symptoms; while trials such as SPORT and SLIP debate the routine addition of fusion in stable grade I slips, demonstrable dynamic instability favours decompression combined with fusion. Multilevel laminectomy is unnecessary for single-level disease and would further destabilise the spine. An interspinous device is inadequate for severe stenosis with instability, and there is no disc herniation requiring discectomy.
learningPoint: Lumbar stenosis with a demonstrably unstable (mobile) degenerative spondylolisthesis is best treated by decompression plus fusion; isolated decompression risks slip progression.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Spine chapter (degenerative spondylolisthesis; SPORT/SLIP)

stem: A 15-year-old competitive gymnast presents with several months of activity-related low back pain that is worse on lumbar extension. Examination reproduces pain on single-leg hyperextension (stork test) on the right. Plain radiographs including oblique views are equivocal. Which imaging investigation is most appropriate to confirm an active (potentially healing) pars defect?

options.label: A
options.text: Standing whole-spine EOS radiograph

options.label: B
options.text: CT of the lumbar spine alone

options.label: C
options.text: MRI of the lumbar spine

options.label: D
options.text: Technetium-99m bone scan without SPECT

options.label: E
options.text: Flexion-extension lumbar radiographs

explanation: In an adolescent athlete with suspected spondylolysis and equivocal plain films, MRI is the preferred next investigation: it avoids ionising radiation in a young patient and detects bone marrow oedema/stress reaction in the pars, identifying an early, active and potentially healing lesion before a frank defect appears. CT best characterises the bony anatomy and chronicity of an established defect but involves radiation and does not show oedema. SPECT-CT is sensitive for metabolic activity but again uses radiation; a plain bone scan lacks anatomical detail. Standing EOS and flexion-extension films assess alignment/instability, not pars activity.
learningPoint: MRI is the first-line, radiation-sparing investigation for suspected adolescent spondylolysis - it shows pars marrow oedema that flags an active, potentially healable lesion.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Spine chapter (spondylolysis)

stem: A 13-year-old girl is found to have an L5/S1 isthmic spondylolisthesis. She has back pain and bilateral hamstring tightness with an abnormal gait. Standing radiographs show a high-grade slip with a markedly vertical sacrum and kyphotic angulation of L5 on S1. Which radiographic parameter is the most important predictor of further slip progression and the need for surgical stabilisation?

options.label: A
options.text: Percentage slip (Meyerding grade) only

options.label: B
options.text: The slip angle / lumbosacral kyphosis (sagittal rotation of L5 on S1)

options.label: C
options.text: Lumbar lordosis measured from L1 to L5

options.label: D
options.text: Coronal Cobb angle of any associated scoliosis

options.label: E
options.text: Vertebral body wedging of L4

explanation: In paediatric isthmic spondylolisthesis the sagittal rotation of L5 on S1 - the slip (lumbosacral) angle, reflecting lumbosacral kyphosis - is the strongest predictor of progression and correlates with deformity, gait disturbance and outcome; a high slip angle with a high-grade slip favours surgical stabilisation/reduction. Meyerding grade describes translation but is a weaker progression predictor in isolation, and high pelvic incidence is a risk factor but the angular measure best captures the kyphotic deformity driving instability. Lumbar lordosis, coronal Cobb and L4 wedging are not the key predictors.
learningPoint: The slip angle (lumbosacral kyphosis) is the key predictor of progression in high-grade paediatric isthmic spondylolisthesis, more so than percentage slip alone.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Spine chapter (isthmic spondylolisthesis; slip angle/pelvic incidence)

stem: A 14-year-old girl is referred with a clinically obvious scoliosis. Which single clinical or radiographic feature should most strongly prompt MRI of the whole neuraxis to exclude an underlying intraspinal abnormality before treatment is planned?

options.label: A
options.text: A right-sided thoracic curve in a peri-menarchal girl

options.label: B
options.text: A left-sided thoracic curve with an abnormal abdominal reflex and rapid progression

options.label: C
options.text: A family history of adolescent idiopathic scoliosis

options.label: D
options.text: A rib hump of 7 degrees on the scoliometer

options.label: E
options.text: A flexible curve that corrects on side-bending films

explanation: Typical AIS is a right thoracic curve in an otherwise neurologically normal girl. Atypical or red-flag features - a left thoracic curve, absent or asymmetric abdominal reflexes, any neurological abnormality, early-onset or rapidly progressive curves, excessive kyphosis or severe pain - suggest a secondary (non-idiopathic) cause such as syringomyelia, Chiari malformation, tethered cord or tumour, and mandate whole-spine MRI before surgery. A right thoracic curve, a positive family history, a modest rib hump and curve flexibility are all consistent with idiopathic disease and do not by themselves require MRI.
learningPoint: Left thoracic curves, abnormal/asymmetric abdominal reflexes, neurological signs or rapid progression are red flags in scoliosis mandating whole-neuraxis MRI to exclude an intraspinal lesion.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Spine chapter (AIS red flags)

stem: A 68-year-old woman with degenerative adult spinal deformity reports difficulty standing upright and progressive fatigue; she feels she is pitching forwards and compensates by bending her knees. On full-length standing radiographs her pelvic incidence is 55 degrees, lumbar lordosis is 20 degrees, pelvic tilt is 30 degrees and the C7 sagittal vertical axis is +10 cm. Which parameter combination best explains her symptoms and represents the principal target of surgical realignment?

options.label: A
options.text: A large pelvic incidence-lumbar lordosis mismatch with compensatory pelvic retroversion and positive sagittal vertical axis

options.label: B
options.text: Isolated coronal Cobb angle of the lumbar curve

options.label: C
options.text: Increased thoracic kyphosis as the primary driver

options.label: D
options.text: A negative sagittal vertical axis from over-correction

options.label: E
options.text: Low pelvic incidence with excessive lumbar lordosis

explanation: Health-related quality of life in adult spinal deformity correlates most strongly with sagittal parameters: a high pelvic incidence-minus-lumbar lordosis mismatch (here 55 - 20 = 35 degrees, well above the ~10 degree target), high pelvic tilt (>20-25 degrees indicating compensatory pelvic retroversion) and a positive SVA (>5 cm). She has lost lumbar lordosis and is compensating by retroverting the pelvis and flexing the knees to keep the head over the pelvis, which is fatiguing. The aim of realignment surgery is to restore lordosis so PI-LL is near zero, normalise pelvic tilt and bring SVA back toward neutral. Coronal Cobb and thoracic kyphosis are not the primary drivers here.
learningPoint: In adult spinal deformity the key sagittal targets are PI-LL mismatch ~10 degrees, pelvic tilt <20-25 degrees and SVA <5 cm; loss of lumbar lordosis drives compensatory pelvic retroversion and disability.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Spine chapter (adult spinal deformity; SRS-Schwab classification)
Scoliosis Research Society sagittal alignment data

stem: When assessing global sagittal balance on a full-length standing lateral radiograph, the C7 sagittal vertical axis (SVA) is measured as the horizontal offset between a plumb line dropped from the C7 vertebral body centroid and a defined posterior landmark. Which landmark is used as the reference point for this measurement?

options.label: A
options.text: The anterosuperior corner of the L1 vertebral body

options.label: B
options.text: The posterosuperior corner of the S1 endplate

options.label: C
options.text: The centre of the C2 vertebral body

options.label: D
options.text: The midpoint of both femoral heads

options.label: E
options.text: The tip of the coccyx

explanation: The C7 SVA is the horizontal distance between the C7 plumb line and the posterosuperior corner of the S1 endplate (the posterior-superior aspect of S1). A plumb line falling more than 5 cm anterior to this point indicates significant positive sagittal imbalance. Femoral-head-referenced measures (e.g., the T1-pelvic angle or sagittal femoral-head offset) are alternative, complementary parameters but are not the definition of the classic C7 SVA. L1, C2 and the coccyx are not the reference for SVA.
learningPoint: C7 SVA = horizontal offset of the C7 plumb line from the posterosuperior corner of S1; >5 cm anterior signifies positive sagittal imbalance.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Spine chapter (spinopelvic parameters)

stem: Theme: Localisation of cervical nerve root lesions. For each clinical picture below, select the SINGLE most likely nerve root involved from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: C5 root

options.label: B
options.text: C6 root

options.label: C
options.text: C7 root

options.label: D
options.text: C8 root

options.label: E
options.text: T1 root

options.label: F
options.text: Spinal accessory nerve (CN XI)

options.label: G
options.text: Lower trunk of the brachial plexus

emiLeads.stem: A 48-year-old man has weakness of elbow extension and wrist flexion, with a depressed triceps reflex and paraesthesia over the middle finger, following a posterolateral C6/7 disc herniation.
A 55-year-old woman has weakness of shoulder abduction and elbow flexion with a reduced biceps reflex and numbness over the lateral aspect of the upper arm; sensation over the hand is normal.

A 60-year-old man has weakness and wasting of the intrinsic muscles of the hand with sensory loss along the medial border of the forearm and little finger, but no Horner syndrome and a normal chest radiograph.
A 47-year-old woman has weakness of elbow flexion and wrist extension with a reduced brachioradialis reflex and paraesthesia over the thumb and index finger, following a C5/6 disc herniation.

explanation: C5 (C4/5 disc): deltoid/biceps weakness, lateral upper-arm sensory loss, reduced biceps reflex, hand spared. C6 (C5/6 disc): elbow flexion/wrist extension weakness, thumb-index sensation, reduced biceps/brachioradialis reflex. C7 (C6/7 disc): elbow extension/wrist flexion/finger extension weakness, middle-finger sensation, depressed triceps reflex. C8 (C7/T1 disc): finger flexion and hand intrinsic weakness, little-finger and medial-forearm sensory loss; an isolated C8 radiculopathy lacks the Horner syndrome and apical chest findings that would instead point to a Pancoast tumour involving the lower trunk/T1. The cervical root number matches the LOWER vertebra of the herniated level (e.g. a C5/6 disc compresses the C6 root).
learningPoint: Cervical root level follows the lower vertebra of the herniated disc: C4/5->C5, C5/6->C6, C6/7->C7, C7/T1->C8.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Spine chapter (cervical radiculopathy/dermatomes and myotomes)

stem: Theme: Lumbar radiculopathy - root localisation. A series of patients present with sciatica from lumbar disc herniations. For each clinical picture, select the SINGLE most likely nerve root affected from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: L2 root

options.label: B
options.text: L3 root

options.label: C
options.text: L4 root

options.label: D
options.text: L5 root

options.label: E
options.text: S1 root

options.label: F
options.text: Cauda equina (multiple roots)

options.label: G
options.text: Femoral nerve (extraspinal)

emiLeads.stem: A 40-year-old man has weakness of great-toe extension and ankle dorsiflexion, sensory disturbance over the dorsum of the foot, and a normal ankle jerk, from a paracentral L4/5 disc herniation.
A 45-year-old woman has weakness of ankle plantarflexion, difficulty standing on tiptoe, sensory loss over the sole and lateral border of the foot, and an absent ankle jerk.
A 58-year-old man has weakness of knee extension with a reduced knee jerk and sensory loss over the medial leg below the knee, from a far-lateral (foraminal) L3/4 disc herniation.
A 39-year-old man develops bilateral leg pain, saddle anaesthesia, painless urinary retention and reduced anal tone over 24 hours after lifting a heavy load.

explanation: L4 (L3/4 paracentral disc, or far-lateral L4/5): knee extension weakness, medial-leg sensation, reduced knee jerk. L5 (paracentral L4/5, the commonest): great-toe and ankle dorsiflexion weakness, dorsal-foot sensation, ankle jerk preserved. S1 (L5/S1): plantarflexion/tiptoe weakness, sole and lateral-foot sensation, absent ankle jerk. Note the level mismatch: a paracentral disc compresses the traversing root (L4/5 -> L5), whereas a far-lateral/foraminal disc compresses the exiting root (L3/4 -> L3, L4/5 -> L4). Saddle anaesthesia with retention and reduced anal tone indicates cauda equina (multiple roots), a surgical emergency.
learningPoint: Paracentral lumbar discs hit the traversing root (L4/5 -> L5); far-lateral/foraminal discs hit the exiting root (L3/4 -> L3). Saddle anaesthesia + retention = cauda equina.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Spine chapter (lumbar radiculopathy; traversing vs exiting root)

stem: A 78-year-old man on apixaban falls down four stairs and strikes his forehead. He has central neck pain but is neurologically intact. CT shows a fracture through the base of the odontoid peg at the waist (Anderson and D'Alonzo type II) with no significant displacement. He has a forward stooped posture from longstanding degenerative change. Which factor most strongly predicts non-union if this fracture is managed in a rigid collar alone?

options.label: A
options.text: His age over 70 combined with the type II fracture pattern

options.label: B
options.text: The presence of central neck pain

options.label: C
options.text: His intact neurological examination

options.label: D
options.text: The use of apixaban

options.label: E
options.text: His stooped sagittal posture alone

explanation: Type II odontoid fractures pass through the waist of the dens, a watershed zone of poor vascularity, and have the highest non-union rate of the three Anderson and D'Alonzo types. Advancing age, particularly over 70, is a well-established independent risk factor for failure of non-operative treatment. In the elderly these are among the commonest cervical injuries (typically low-energy falls in osteoporotic bone) and there is a clear trend in UK practice towards surgical stabilisation or accepting a stable fibrous non-union, because rigid collars are poorly tolerated and prolonged immobilisation carries high morbidity. Neurology, anticoagulation and posture influence overall management but are not the dominant predictor of bony union.
learningPoint: Type II odontoid fractures have the worst union rate; age over 70 is the key predictor of non-union in a collar.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Spine: cervical spine trauma
BOAST — Spinal Clearance in the Trauma Patient

stem: A 24-year-old is brought in after a rugby tackle with a forced flexion-distraction injury of the subaxial cervical spine. He has weakness in both arms and legs. CT and MRI show bilateral facet dislocation at C5/6 with a disc fragment herniated anterior to the cord. Using the Subaxial Cervical Spine Injury Classification (SLIC) system, which combination of features contributes the highest weighted scores driving a decision to operate?

options.label: A
options.text: Distractive morphology, disrupted disco-ligamentous complex, and incomplete spinal cord injury

options.label: B
options.text: Compression morphology, intact disco-ligamentous complex, and intact neurology

options.label: C
options.text: Compression morphology, indeterminate disco-ligamentous complex, and nerve root injury

options.label: D
options.text: Distractive morphology, intact disco-ligamentous complex, and intact neurology

options.label: E
options.text: Distractive morphology, intact disco-ligamentous complex, and transient paraesthesia only

explanation: SLIC scores three domains: injury morphology (compression 1, burst 2, distraction 3, translation/rotation 4), disco-ligamentous complex (intact 0, indeterminate 1, disrupted 2), and neurological status (intact 0, root 1, complete cord 2, incomplete cord 3, with +1 for continuous cord compression and a deficit). A total of 5 or more favours surgery and 3 or less favours non-operative care. Bilateral facet dislocation with cord injury combines a high morphology score, a disrupted disco-ligamentous complex, and the highest neurology weighting (incomplete cord injury), reliably exceeding the operative threshold. The MRI-demonstrated disc herniation is also why many surgeons obtain pre-reduction MRI and favour an anterior or combined approach rather than blind closed reduction.
learningPoint: SLIC 5 or more favours surgery; distraction/translation morphology, disco-ligamentous disruption and incomplete cord injury are the heavy-scoring features.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Spine: subaxial cervical trauma (SLIC)

stem: Theme: upper cervical spine injuries and their classifications. For each clinical and radiological description, select the single most likely diagnosis.

options.label: A
options.text: Jefferson (C1 burst) fracture

options.label: B
options.text: Type II odontoid fracture

options.label: C
options.text: Hangman's fracture (traumatic spondylolisthesis of C2)

options.label: D
options.text: Atlanto-occipital dissociation

options.label: E
options.text: Type III odontoid fracture

options.label: F
options.text: Occipital condyle fracture

options.label: G
options.text: Unilateral C1 lateral mass fracture

options.label: H
options.text: Os odontoideum

emiLeads.stem: A restrained driver in a high-speed collision is found to have markedly widened and asymmetric atlanto-dental and basion-dental measurements on CT, with prevertebral soft-tissue swelling and cranial settling. Many such patients do not survive to hospital.

A diver strikes the bottom of a pool head-first (axial load). The open-mouth view shows symmetrical lateral displacement of both C1 lateral masses, with the combined overhang exceeding 6.9 mm (rule of Spence), implying transverse atlantal ligament rupture.

A patient is thrown forward in a collision sustaining a hyperextension-distraction injury, with CT showing bilateral fractures through the pars/pedicle region of the axis and forward subluxation of the C2 body on C3.

An elderly patient falls onto the face. CT shows a fracture line through the base of the dens extending obliquely into the cancellous body of C2 and involving a portion of the lateral masses, a pattern that generally unites well in a collar.

explanation: Atlanto-occipital dissociation is a highly unstable craniocervical injury (deranged basion-dental and condyle-C1 intervals) with very high pre-hospital mortality. A Jefferson fracture is an axial-load burst of the C1 ring; the rule of Spence (combined lateral mass overhang greater than 6.9 mm on the open-mouth view or coronal CT) implies transverse atlantal ligament incompetence and instability, although its sensitivity has been questioned and CT/MRI assessment of the ligament is now preferred. A Hangman's fracture is bilateral disruption of the C2 pars from hyperextension-distraction. Type III odontoid fractures extend into the broad cancellous C2 body and unite well non-operatively, in contrast to type II fractures through the avascular waist.
learningPoint: Rule of Spence (overhang greater than 6.9 mm) flags a potentially unstable Jefferson fracture; type III dens fractures unite better than type II because they enter cancellous body bone.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Spine: upper cervical trauma
BOAST — Spinal Clearance in the Trauma Patient

stem: A 35-year-old falls from a ladder and sustains an L1 burst fracture. He is neurologically intact. CT shows retropulsion with about 40% canal compromise and 50% loss of anterior vertebral body height; MRI shows an intact posterior ligamentous complex with no interspinous oedema or facet widening. Using the Thoracolumbar Injury Classification and Severity (TLICS) score, what is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Non-operative management with a thoracolumbar orthosis and mobilisation

options.label: B
options.text: Posterior instrumented fusion with decompression

options.label: C
options.text: Anterior corpectomy and reconstruction

options.label: D
options.text: Combined anterior and posterior stabilisation

options.label: E
options.text: Kyphoplasty of L1

explanation: TLICS sums three categories: morphology (compression 1, burst 2, translation/rotation 3, distraction 4), posterior ligamentous complex (intact 0, indeterminate 2, disrupted 3) and neurology (intact 0, root 2, complete cord/conus 2, incomplete cord/conus 3, cauda equina 3). A score of 0 to 3 favours non-operative care, 4 is equivocal, and 5 or more favours surgery. A burst (2) plus intact PLC (0) plus intact neurology (0) scores 2, so a neurologically intact burst with a competent PLC is managed non-operatively despite the canal compromise. The degree of retropulsion alone does not mandate surgery; PLC integrity and neurology drive the decision.
learningPoint: TLICS 3 or less means non-operative; an intact PLC and intact neurology make a burst fracture a non-operative injury regardless of canal compromise.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Spine: thoracolumbar trauma (TLICS)

stem: A 42-year-old unrestrained passenger in a head-on collision was wearing a lap belt. He has a tender, palpable gap between the lumbar spinous processes and bilateral lower-limb sensory disturbance. Imaging shows an L2 fracture with horizontal splitting through the body, pedicles and posterior elements, widening of the interspinous distance, and facet distraction. Which feature of this injury contributes the single highest morphology score in the TLICS system?

options.label: A
options.text: The distraction (Chance-type) morphology

options.label: B
options.text: The anterior vertebral body compression

options.label: C
options.text: The presence of a burst component

options.label: D
options.text: The associated nerve root irritation

options.label: E
options.text: The intact translational alignment

explanation: This is a flexion-distraction (Chance) injury, classically a lap-belt mechanism, with failure in tension and a horizontal splitting pattern. In TLICS, morphology is scored compression 1, burst 2, translation/rotation 3 and distraction 4, so the distraction morphology carries the highest morphology weighting (4). Combined with PLC disruption (3) and neurology, the total is well into the operative range. These are unstable injuries that require surgical stabilisation, and there is a recognised association with intra-abdominal (hollow viscus) injury that must be actively sought. Note that the nerve root irritation contributes to the neurology category, not the morphology category asked about.
learningPoint: Distraction (Chance) morphology scores 4, the highest in TLICS; lap-belt flexion-distraction injuries are unstable and linked to abdominal injury.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Spine: thoracolumbar trauma
BOAST — Spinal Clearance in the Trauma Patient

stem: A 70-year-old with cervical spondylosis falls and hyperextends his neck. He has no fracture on CT. He has marked weakness and clumsiness in both hands with relatively preserved lower-limb power, patchy loss of pain and temperature sensation in the upper limbs, and some sacral sparing. Which statement about this spinal cord injury pattern is most accurate?

options.label: A
options.text: It is the commonest incomplete cord syndrome and generally has a favourable prognosis for ambulation

options.label: B
options.text: It carries the worst prognosis of all the incomplete cord syndromes

options.label: C
options.text: It results from hemisection of the cord with ipsilateral motor and contralateral sensory loss

options.label: D
options.text: It typically follows anterior spinal artery occlusion with preserved proprioception

options.label: E
options.text: Loss of proprioception and vibration with preserved pain and temperature is its defining feature

explanation: This is central cord syndrome, the commonest incomplete spinal cord injury, typically in an older patient with pre-existing canal stenosis after a hyperextension injury and often without fracture. The corticospinal tracts are somatotopically arranged with upper-limb fibres more central, so the arms (especially the hands) are affected more than the legs, with variable sensory loss and bladder dysfunction. Prognosis for recovery of ambulation is comparatively good (worse for fine hand function and in older patients). Option C describes Brown-Sequard, D describes anterior cord syndrome, and E describes posterior cord syndrome; the worst prognosis of the incomplete syndromes is anterior cord syndrome, not central cord.
learningPoint: Central cord syndrome: elderly, hyperextension, hands worse than legs; commonest incomplete injury with a relatively good prognosis for walking.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Spine: spinal cord injury syndromes

stem: Theme: spinal cord injury syndromes and assessment. For each description, select the single best-fitting term.

options.label: A
options.text: Anterior cord syndrome

options.label: B
options.text: Brown-Sequard syndrome

options.label: C
options.text: Central cord syndrome

options.label: D
options.text: Posterior cord syndrome

options.label: E
options.text: Conus medullaris syndrome

options.label: F
options.text: Spinal shock

options.label: G
options.text: Neurogenic shock

options.label: H
options.text: Complete (ASIA A) cord injury

emiLeads.stem: After a penetrating stab injury to one side of the back, a patient has ipsilateral loss of motor power, proprioception and vibration below the lesion, with contralateral loss of pain and temperature.
Immediately after a cervical injury there is flaccid areflexic paralysis and loss of the bulbocavernosus reflex; the surgeon explains that true ASIA grading is unreliable until this reflex returns.

Following a flexion injury with anterior cord compromise, a patient loses motor function and pain/temperature sensation below the level, but light touch, proprioception and vibration are preserved, and the outlook for recovery is the poorest of the incomplete syndromes.
A polytrauma patient with a high thoracic cord injury is hypotensive and, atypically for haemorrhage, bradycardic, with warm peripheries due to loss of sympathetic tone.

explanation: Brown-Sequard (cord hemisection) gives ipsilateral motor and dorsal-column loss with contralateral spinothalamic loss, and has the best prognosis of the incomplete syndromes. Spinal shock is the transient post-injury flaccid areflexia; recovery of the bulbocavernosus reflex marks its resolution, after which an ASIA examination becomes reliable and a complete injury can be confirmed. Anterior cord syndrome (anterior two-thirds, anterior spinal artery territory) spares the dorsal columns and has the worst prognosis. Neurogenic shock is distributive hypotension with bradycardia from loss of sympathetic outflow; distinguishing it from hypovolaemic shock is critical to avoid inappropriate over-transfusion.
learningPoint: Bulbocavernosus reflex return ends spinal shock and makes ASIA grading reliable; neurogenic shock is hypotension WITH bradycardia.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Spine: spinal cord injury
ASIA International Standards for Neurological Classification of SCI

stem: A 38-year-old presents to the emergency department at 23:00 with 2 days of severe bilateral sciatica that has now eased, but she reports new difficulty initiating micturition and a numb feeling when wiping after the toilet. Bladder scan shows a post-void residual of 480 mL. MRI confirms a large central L4/5 disc prolapse compressing the cauda equina. Which statement best reflects current UK best practice for the timing of surgery?

options.label: A
options.text: Emergency decompression should be performed as soon as feasible, including out of hours, rather than waiting for the next routine list

options.label: B
options.text: Surgery can safely wait up to 7 days once retention is established

options.label: C
options.text: A urinary catheter and review on the next elective list is sufficient

options.label: D
options.text: Conservative management with analgesia and a repeat MRI in 6 weeks is appropriate

options.label: E
options.text: Decompression offers no benefit once any retention has developed, so surgery should be deferred

explanation: This is cauda equina syndrome confirmed on MRI. UK guidance (BOA/SBNS standards and the national CES pathway) is that confirmed CES is a surgical emergency requiring decompression as soon as practicable, including out of hours, because delay correlates with worse bladder, bowel and sexual outcomes. Outcomes are most clearly time-dependent in incomplete CES (CES-I), which must not be allowed to progress to painless retention (CES-R) while awaiting a daytime list, so the safe default is to catheterise, refer immediately and operate on the next available emergency list rather than defer. Options B to E all involve unsafe delay or denial of indicated surgery.
learningPoint: Confirmed CES is an emergency: catheterise, refer immediately and decompress as soon as feasible (including out of hours); do not wait for a routine list.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Spine: cauda equina syndrome
BOA/SBNS Standards for the management of Cauda Equina Syndrome
NICE NG59 — Low back pain and sciatica

stem: A 45-year-old man presents with acute low back pain and bilateral leg pain. He has no urinary symptoms, normal perineal sensation and normal anal tone, but is very anxious about cauda equina after reading online. He is neurologically intact in the legs apart from symmetrically absent ankle jerks. Which is the most appropriate management in current UK practice?

options.label: A
options.text: Document the negative cauda equina findings and give clear red-flag safety-netting advice, arranging MRI only if cauda equina symptoms or signs develop

options.label: B
options.text: Arrange an emergency out-of-hours whole-spine MRI tonight regardless of findings

options.label: C
options.text: Insert a urinary catheter prophylactically and admit for observation

options.label: D
options.text: Refer immediately for emergency surgical decompression

options.label: E
options.text: Reassure and discharge with no specific advice, as red flags are absent

explanation: There are currently no objective features of cauda equina syndrome (no bladder or bowel disturbance, intact perineal sensation, normal anal tone); bilateral leg pain with symmetrical absent ankle jerks alone does not constitute CES. UK guidance (NICE NG59 and the national CES pathway) emphasises asking about and documenting the specific CES red flags and giving clear, documented safety-netting with explicit instructions to return immediately if bladder, bowel, saddle or sexual symptoms develop. Emergency MRI is reserved for those with suspicious symptoms or signs; reflexively scanning every back-pain patient overnight is neither necessary nor sustainable, but simple reassurance without safety-netting (option E) is unsafe.
learningPoint: No CES red flags means document the negatives and give explicit return-if-worse safety-netting; scan only if CES features appear.
references: NICE NG59 — Low back pain and sciatica
BOA/SBNS Standards for the management of Cauda Equina Syndrome

stem: A 64-year-old man with known prostate cancer develops a 5-day history of progressive bilateral leg weakness and difficulty walking, with a band of altered sensation around the trunk and new urinary hesitancy. Examination shows a thoracic sensory level, brisk knee reflexes and upgoing plantars. What is the most appropriate immediate management while definitive treatment is organised?

options.label: A
options.text: Start high-dose dexamethasone, arrange an urgent whole-spine MRI, and refer to the metastatic spinal cord compression coordinator the same day

options.label: B
options.text: Arrange an outpatient MRI within 1 week and review in clinic

options.label: C
options.text: Commence palliative radiotherapy without imaging the rest of the spine

options.label: D
options.text: Request plain thoracolumbar radiographs and a bone scan before any treatment

options.label: E
options.text: Bed rest and analgesia, with neurosurgical referral only if he loses the ability to walk

explanation: This is metastatic spinal cord compression (MSCC) with an upper motor neuron picture and a thoracic sensory level. NICE guidance is to treat suspected MSCC as an emergency: start dexamethasone (with gastric protection and glucose monitoring) unless contraindicated, perform whole-spine MRI (the whole spine, because synchronous lesions at other levels are common), and involve the MSCC coordinator within 24 hours to plan surgery and/or radiotherapy. Decompressive surgery, often with stabilisation, followed by radiotherapy gives better ambulatory outcomes than radiotherapy alone in selected surgical candidates, and treatment is most effective before walking is lost, so waiting until he becomes non-ambulant is wrong.
learningPoint: Suspected MSCC means dexamethasone plus whole-spine MRI plus same-day MSCC coordinator; treat before ambulation is lost.
references: NICE NG234 — Spinal metastases and metastatic spinal cord compression
Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Spine: metastatic disease

stem: A spinal MDT is deciding on surgical strategy for a 60-year-old woman with a solitary symptomatic vertebral metastasis from breast cancer causing early cord compression and mechanical instability. The team uses one validated scoring tool to grade the degree of spinal instability and another to estimate prognosis and surgical suitability. Which pairing of scoring systems is most appropriate for this decision?

options.label: A
options.text: Spinal Instability Neoplastic Score (SINS) and the Tokuhashi (or Tomita) score

options.label: B
options.text: TLICS and the Garden classification

options.label: C
options.text: SLIC and the Mirels score

options.label: D
options.text: Frankel grade and the Pavlov ratio

options.label: E
options.text: Enneking staging and the Weinstein-Boriani-Biagini (WBB) system for benign disease

explanation: For metastatic spinal disease, the Spinal Instability Neoplastic Score (SINS) grades neoplastic instability from six components (location, pain, bone lesion quality, alignment, vertebral body collapse and posterolateral involvement), guiding the need for stabilisation. The revised Tokuhashi and the Tomita scores estimate survival and help select patients for excisional versus palliative surgery. The Mirels score applies to long-bone metastases (impending fracture), not the spine; TLICS and SLIC are trauma tools; the Garden classification is for femoral neck fractures; and Enneking/WBB staging guide en bloc resection of primary spinal tumours rather than routine metastatic decision-making. Combining SINS (instability) with a prognostic score (Tokuhashi/Tomita) is the standard MDT approach.
learningPoint: Metastatic spine decisions: SINS grades instability; Tokuhashi/Tomita estimate prognosis and surgical suitability.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Spine: metastatic tumours (SINS, Tokuhashi, Tomita)
NICE NG234 — Spinal metastases and metastatic spinal cord compression

stem: A 16-year-old presents with several months of well-localised night back pain that is dramatically and reproducibly relieved by ibuprofen. Examination reveals a mild, painful scoliosis with the apex at the painful level. CT shows a small (less than 1.5 cm) radiolucent nidus with surrounding reactive sclerosis in the posterior elements of a lumbar vertebra. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.label: A
options.text: Osteoid osteoma

options.label: B
options.text: Osteoblastoma

options.label: C
options.text: Aneurysmal bone cyst

options.label: D
options.text: Ewing sarcoma

options.label: E
options.text: Osteochondroma

explanation: This is a classic osteoid osteoma: an adolescent with night pain relieved by NSAIDs (the pain is prostaglandin-mediated), a small nidus (less than 1.5 to 2 cm) with reactive sclerosis, typically in the posterior elements, and a secondary painful scoliosis with the lesion at the concave apex. Osteoblastoma is histologically similar but larger (greater than 2 cm), more aggressive and less reliably NSAID-responsive. Aneurysmal bone cyst is an expansile, blood-filled lytic lesion; Ewing sarcoma is a destructive malignant small-round-blue-cell tumour with a permeative pattern and systemic symptoms; osteochondroma is a cartilage-capped bony outgrowth. Symptomatic osteoid osteomas are often treated with CT-guided radiofrequency ablation, though spinal lesions near neural structures may require careful patient selection or open excision.
learningPoint: Night pain relieved by NSAIDs plus a small nidus (less than 2 cm) in the posterior elements plus painful scoliosis equals osteoid osteoma.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Orthopaedic Oncology: benign bone tumours

stem: A 55-year-old man has a slow-growing, locally aggressive primary tumour of the sacrum causing deep pain and bladder dysfunction. MRI shows a midline, lobulated, T2-hyperintense destructive mass arising from the sacrococcygeal region; histology shows physaliphorous (vacuolated) cells. Which statement about optimal management is most accurate?

options.label: A
options.text: Wide en bloc surgical resection offers the best chance of local control, as the tumour is relatively radio- and chemo-resistant

options.label: B
options.text: It is highly chemosensitive, so neoadjuvant chemotherapy alone is usually curative

options.label: C
options.text: Intralesional curettage gives results equivalent to en bloc resection

options.label: D
options.text: It is a benign lesion best managed by observation

options.label: E
options.text: Conventional external-beam radiotherapy alone reliably achieves cure

explanation: The physaliphorous (bubble-bearing) cells and a midline destructive sacral mass arising from notochordal remnants indicate a chordoma. Chordomas are slow-growing but locally aggressive and relatively resistant to conventional radiotherapy and chemotherapy, so wide en bloc (negative-margin) resection offers the best chance of local control and survival. Achieving clear margins in the sacrum is difficult and may require sacrificing sacral nerve roots, with bladder, bowel and sexual consequences. High-dose particle (proton or carbon-ion) radiotherapy is increasingly used as an adjunct or for unresectable disease. Intralesional surgery has high recurrence rates, and the tumour is neither benign nor curable by chemotherapy or conventional radiotherapy alone.
learningPoint: Midline sacral destructive tumour with physaliphorous cells equals chordoma; treat with wide en bloc resection (it is radio- and chemo-resistant).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Orthopaedic Oncology: chordoma

stem: A 58-year-old man with type 2 diabetes presents with 3 weeks of worsening thoracolumbar back pain, fevers and night sweats. Inflammatory markers are markedly raised.
