MRI shows T1-hypointense, T2-hyperintense signal in two adjacent vertebral bodies with loss of the intervening disc height and an enhancing paravertebral collection. He is neurologically intact and haemodynamically stable. What is the most appropriate next step before starting treatment?

options.label: A
options.text: Obtain blood cultures and an image-guided biopsy/aspirate to identify the organism before starting targeted antibiotics

options.label: B
options.text: Start empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics immediately without any cultures

options.label: C
options.text: Proceed straight to emergency surgical debridement and instrumented fusion

options.label: D
options.text: Commence a 2-week course of oral antibiotics and review as an outpatient

options.label: E
options.text: Arrange a bone scan and defer all treatment until it is reported

explanation: This is pyogenic vertebral osteomyelitis/discitis (disc-centred destruction of two adjacent endplates with a paravertebral collection is characteristic, and the commonest organism is Staphylococcus aureus). In a neurologically intact, haemodynamically stable patient, the priority is microbiological diagnosis: send blood cultures and, if these are negative, perform image-guided biopsy/aspiration of the disc or affected bone before antibiotics, so therapy can be targeted (UK and IDSA practice). Antibiotics started blindly can render cultures negative and prolong inappropriate therapy. Surgery is reserved for neurological deficit, instability or deformity, a large or symptomatic abscess, or failure of medical treatment, not as a first step here. Empirical antibiotics should not be delayed, however, in a septic or neurologically deteriorating patient.
learningPoint: Stable discitis/osteomyelitis: obtain blood cultures and image-guided biopsy BEFORE antibiotics to target therapy; S. aureus is commonest.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Spine: spinal infection

stem: A 47-year-old man originally from South Asia has 4 months of insidious lower thoracic back pain, weight loss and low-grade fevers. MRI shows destruction of two adjacent vertebral bodies with relative sparing of the intervertebral disc, a large multiloculated paravertebral collection tracking beneath the anterior longitudinal ligament, and anterior wedging producing a kyphotic (gibbus) deformity. Which feature most strongly favours tuberculous over pyogenic spinal infection?

options.label: A
options.text: Relative preservation of the disc with a large, subligamentous, multiloculated paraspinal abscess

options.label: B
options.text: The presence of fever and raised inflammatory markers

options.label: C
options.text: Involvement of two adjacent vertebral bodies

options.label: D
options.text: Back pain that is worse at night

options.label: E
options.text: An elevated peripheral white cell count

explanation: Spinal tuberculosis (Pott's disease) tends to spare the disc relatively (the avascular disc is preserved longer because TB spreads beneath the anterior longitudinal ligament rather than via the disc), produces large, often calcified, multiloculated subligamentous and paraspinal cold abscesses, and causes anterior vertebral collapse with a gibbus deformity. By contrast, pyogenic infection is typically disc-centred with early disc destruction. Fever, raised inflammatory markers, multilevel involvement and night pain are non-discriminating. Diagnosis still hinges on biopsy/culture and molecular tests, and treatment is prolonged anti-tuberculous chemotherapy, with surgery reserved for deficit, instability or significant deformity.
learningPoint: Disc sparing plus a large subligamentous multiloculated abscess plus gibbus equals spinal TB (Pott's), versus disc-centred pyogenic infection.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Spine: tuberculous spondylitis (Pott's disease)

stem: A 3-year-old girl is referred to your clinic because her parents are concerned she walks with her feet turned inwards. She is otherwise well, meeting all developmental milestones, and walks and runs without falling more than her peers. On examination she has a foot-progression angle of approximately -10 degrees bilaterally. With the child prone and knees flexed to 90 degrees, internal rotation of the hips is 80 degrees and external rotation is 20 degrees. The thigh-foot axis and trans-malleolar axis are normal, and the feet are straight with no medial border deviation. What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Reassurance and review, with expected spontaneous resolution by skeletal maturity

options.label: B
options.text: Referral for a femoral derotation osteotomy

options.label: C
options.text: Twister cables and a Denis Browne bar to be worn at night

options.label: D
options.text: Urgent hip ultrasound to exclude developmental dysplasia of the hip

options.label: E
options.text: Serial below-knee casting of both feet in external rotation

explanation: The combination of increased hip internal rotation (80 degrees) with reduced external rotation (20 degrees) and a normal thigh-foot axis and foot shape localises the in-toeing to femoral anteversion (medial femoral torsion). This is the typical cause of in-toeing in the 3-6 year age group and almost always resolves spontaneously as anteversion decreases with growth, usually reaching adult values by 8-10 years. Bracing (twister cables, Denis Browne bars) and shoe modifications have no proven effect on the natural history. Derotation osteotomy is reserved for the rare older child with severe functional/cosmetic problems persisting beyond about 8 years. The history and examination are not suggestive of DDH, and metatarsus adductus is excluded by the straight foot.
learningPoint: Femoral anteversion is the commonest cause of in-toeing at 3-6 years; it resolves spontaneously and braces/special shoes do not alter the natural history.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Orthopaedics chapter (rotational profile / torsional variations)

stem: A 2-year-old boy is brought to clinic with bow legs that his parents feel are not improving. He is a well-grown child on the 75th centile, walking independently since 13 months, with no pain. Standing alignment shows symmetrical genu varum with an intercondylar distance of about 4 cm. There is a mild internal tibial torsion. A standing long-leg radiograph shows a metaphyseal-diaphyseal angle of 8 degrees bilaterally, with smooth, intact, symmetrical physes and no metaphyseal beaking or fragmentation. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Reassure the parents that this is physiological and arrange clinical review in 6-12 months

options.label: B
options.text: Apply a medial proximal tibial guided-growth plate immediately

options.label: C
options.text: Commence a knee-ankle-foot orthosis to correct the varus

options.label: D
options.text: Request a serum biochemistry and refer for rickets work-up as the priority

options.label: E
options.text: List for a proximal tibial valgus osteotomy

explanation: Physiological genu varum is normal up to about 2 years of age, after which alignment swings into valgus, typically peaking around 3-4 years before settling to adult values by 6-7 years. A metaphyseal-diaphyseal (Drennan) angle below 11 degrees, with symmetrical, undisturbed physes and no beaking or fragmentation, strongly favours physiological bowing over infantile Blount disease (where the MDA is typically >11-16 degrees with medial metaphyseal changes). A well, normally grown child with symmetrical, painless bowing needs reassurance and interval review, not bracing or surgery. Bracing is not effective for physiological varus. Metabolic work-up is reserved for atypical features such as short stature, asymmetry, widened painful physes, or biochemical/dietary risk.
learningPoint: A Drennan (metaphyseal-diaphyseal) angle under 11 degrees with symmetrical, undisturbed physes points to physiological genu varum, which is managed by observation.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Orthopaedics chapter (angular deformity of the lower limb)

stem: A 9-year-old girl has a leg-length discrepancy following a previous femoral fracture, with the right leg shorter. Serial Moseley straight-line graph data and bone-age assessment predict a discrepancy of 4.5 cm at skeletal maturity, with the shortening arising entirely from the right femur. Her predicted final height is within the normal range and she has otherwise normal limb alignment. Which is the most appropriate definitive management strategy?

options.label: A
options.text: Well-timed epiphysiodesis of the contralateral (left) distal femur

options.label: B
options.text: Permanent 4.5 cm shoe raise on the right with no surgery

options.label: C
options.text: Acute femoral lengthening of the right femur by 4.5 cm at a single sitting

options.label: D
options.text: Ipsilateral (right) distal femoral epiphysiodesis

options.label: E
options.text: Immediate bilateral proximal tibial guided growth

explanation: For a predicted discrepancy in the roughly 2-5 cm range in a growing child with adequate predicted height, a correctly timed epiphysiodesis of the LONGER limb is the standard, low-morbidity solution. Timing is calculated from remaining growth (e.g. Green-Anderson/Moseley/multiplier methods) so the shorter limb 'catches up' by maturity. Epiphysiodesis of the short (right) limb would worsen the discrepancy. Acute lengthening of 4.5 cm in one sitting risks neurovascular injury and is not done; gradual lengthening is reserved for larger discrepancies or where shortening the contralateral side is undesirable. A permanent large shoe raise is a non-operative compromise, not the definitive option when growth remains to allow epiphysiodesis.
learningPoint: Discrepancies of about 2-5 cm in a growing child are typically equalised by a correctly timed epiphysiodesis of the LONGER limb.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Orthopaedics chapter (limb-length discrepancy)

stem: You are assessing a 6-year-old boy with a suspected leg-length discrepancy. He stands with a tilted pelvis. When you place graduated blocks under the shorter limb until the pelvis is level, a 2 cm block restores a horizontal pelvis. To localise the level of the shortening clinically, you then perform the Galeazzi test and measure segmental lengths. Which clinical finding would most specifically indicate that the shortening lies in the TIBIA rather than the femur?

options.label: A
options.text: On the Galeazzi test (supine, hips and knees flexed, feet together), the affected knee sits more distally/lower than the other

options.label: B
options.text: On the Galeazzi test, the affected knee sits more proximally/higher than the other

options.label: C
options.text: The anterior superior iliac spine to medial malleolus distance is equal on both sides

options.label: D
options.text: The umbilicus to medial malleolus distance is shorter on the affected side

options.label: E
options.text: Hip internal rotation is reduced on the affected side

explanation: The Galeazzi (Allis) test is performed supine with hips and knees flexed and heels level. With femoral shortening the affected knee appears lower along the bed but more posterior/proximal in the thigh plane; classically a short femur drops the knee. The discriminator for level is the position of the knees: tibial shortening leaves the knee tops unequal in height such that the shorter segment's knee sits more distally/lower (foot pulled up makes the affected knee project lower), whereas a short femur makes that knee sit further back. The key teaching point assessed here is that the Galeazzi test compares knee heights to localise the discrepancy to femur versus tibia. ASIS-to-medial malleolus measures true overall length (not segmental), and umbilicus-to-malleolus measures apparent length, which is altered by pelvic obliquity/adduction rather than localising the segment. Hip rotation is unrelated to the level of shortening.
learningPoint: The Galeazzi test compares knee heights to localise true shortening to the femur or tibia; segmental tape measurements then confirm the level.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Orthopaedics chapter (clinical assessment of LLD)

stem: A 2.5-year-old boy of African-Caribbean heritage on the 95th centile for weight, who walked at 10 months, presents with progressive bowing of the legs. Standing long-leg radiographs show medial proximal tibial metaphyseal beaking and irregularity, with a metaphyseal-diaphyseal angle of 14 degrees on the right. The deformity is felt to be progressing on serial review. Which is the most appropriate initial treatment?

options.label: A
options.text: A knee-ankle-foot orthosis with the aim of unloading the medial physis

options.label: B
options.text: Immediate proximal tibial valgus osteotomy

options.label: C
options.text: Reassurance, as this represents physiological bowing in an early walker

options.label: D
options.text: Lateral proximal tibial guided-growth (tension-band) plate as the first intervention

options.label: E
options.text: Long-term vitamin D and phosphate supplementation

explanation: This is infantile (early-onset) Blount disease: risk factors (early walking, raised weight, African-Caribbean heritage), medial metaphyseal beaking, and a Drennan angle in the 11-16 degree range with documented progression. In a child under about 3-4 years with relatively early-stage disease (Langenskiold I-II), a trial of bracing (KAFO) to offload the medial physis is the recognised first-line treatment and can arrest or reverse the deformity. Osteotomy is indicated when bracing fails or for older children/advanced Langenskiold stages and is most effective before age 4. Guided growth (lateral tension-band plating) is an option for some progressive cases but is not the standard first intervention in this young, early-stage child. The picture is not physiological (beaking, progression, MDA >11 degrees), and there is no biochemical evidence of rickets.
learningPoint: Early-stage infantile Blount disease in a child under ~3-4 years is initially managed with a KAFO; osteotomy is reserved for failed bracing or advanced/older disease.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Orthopaedics chapter (Blount disease / tibia vara)

stem: A 12-year-old boy with a BMI of 34 presents with painful, progressive unilateral genu varum of the left leg. Radiographs show changes consistent with adolescent (late-onset) Blount disease, with a widened and depressed medial proximal tibial physis and a varus deformity whose CORA is located at the proximal tibia. He has approximately 2 years of growth remaining. Compared with the infantile form, which statement best reflects the management considerations for this adolescent presentation?

options.label: A
options.text: Bracing is rarely effective in adolescents, and surgical correction (e.g. guided growth or corrective osteotomy) is usually required

options.label: B
options.text: A KAFO worn until skeletal maturity is the treatment of choice and reliably reverses the deformity

options.label: C
options.text: Spontaneous resolution is expected and observation alone is appropriate

options.label: D
options.text: Bilateral disease is the rule, so contralateral prophylactic osteotomy should be planned

options.label: E
options.text: The deformity is almost always purely rotational, so a derotation osteotomy alone corrects it

explanation: Adolescent (late-onset) Blount disease is strongly associated with obesity, is more often unilateral and painful, and—unlike the infantile form—does not respond to bracing. Surgical treatment is therefore the mainstay: lateral proximal tibial guided growth (hemiepiphysiodesis) can be used when adequate growth remains and the deformity is moderate, while corrective osteotomy (often with realignment about the proximal tibial CORA, sometimes acute or gradual with a frame) is used for larger or near-mature deformities. Spontaneous resolution does not occur. Adolescent disease is more commonly unilateral than the infantile form, so routine contralateral prophylactic surgery is not indicated, and the deformity is principally a varus (frontal-plane) problem, not purely rotational.
learningPoint: Late-onset (adolescent) Blount disease is obesity-related and brace-resistant; management is surgical—guided growth if growth remains, otherwise corrective osteotomy.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Orthopaedics chapter (adolescent Blount disease)

stem: A neonate is diagnosed with bilateral idiopathic congenital talipes equinovarus and referred to your Ponseti clinic. After serial above-knee casting, the cavus, adductus and varus have corrected well, but residual fixed equinus persists with the ankle unable to reach a plantigrade position. Dorsiflexion is limited to about -10 degrees despite full abduction of the forefoot to 60-70 degrees. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Percutaneous Achilles tenotomy followed by a further cast in maximal dorsiflexion and abduction

options.label: B
options.text: Open posteromedial soft-tissue release

options.label: C
options.text: Immediate transition to the foot abduction brace without further intervention

options.label: D
options.text: Posterior tibial tendon transfer

options.label: E
options.text: Continue weekly serial casting indefinitely until equinus corrects

explanation: In the Ponseti method the deformities correct in the sequence CAVE (Cavus, Adductus, Varus, then Equinus). Once forefoot abduction of about 60-70 degrees and a corrected heel are achieved but fixed equinus remains, the great majority (around 80-90%) require a percutaneous Achilles tenotomy to gain dorsiflexion, followed by a final cast (usually ~3 weeks) holding the foot in dorsiflexion and abduction. Continued casting alone will not overcome true tendo-Achilles contracture and risks a rocker-bottom deformity. Extensive open releases (posteromedial release) are avoided in the modern Ponseti pathway because they produce stiff, weak feet; tendon transfers address later dynamic relapse, not initial equinus.
learningPoint: Persistent fixed equinus after correcting cavus/adductus/varus in Ponseti casting is treated by percutaneous Achilles tenotomy plus a final dorsiflexion cast.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Orthopaedics chapter (clubfoot / Ponseti method)

stem: The parents of a 14-month-old whose idiopathic clubfoot was successfully corrected with the Ponseti method attend for review. The foot is well corrected, but the parents admit they have struggled with the foot abduction brace and have only been applying it for a few hours each night. Regarding the bracing phase and relapse, which statement is most accurate and should guide your counselling?

options.label: A
options.text: Non-compliance with the foot abduction brace is the single strongest predictor of relapse, so adherence to the prescribed regimen should be reinforced

options.label: B
options.text: Bracing can be safely discontinued once the foot looks corrected, as relapse after correction is rare

options.label: C
options.text: The brace works by continuing to stretch the Achilles tendon and should be set in plantarflexion

options.label: D
options.text: Relapse, if it occurs, is best treated by immediate extensive posteromedial soft-tissue release

options.label: E
options.text: The foot abduction brace should be worn full-time (23 hours/day) until the child is 5 years old

explanation: After Ponseti correction the foot abduction brace (boots-and-bar, typically set around 60-70 degrees external rotation on the affected side and in dorsiflexion) is worn essentially full-time (about 23 hours/day) for the first ~3 months, then at night and naps (around 12-14 hours/day) until roughly age 4-5. Poor brace compliance is consistently the strongest risk factor for relapse, raising relapse rates several-fold. Hence counselling focuses on adherence. The brace abducts and dorsiflexes the foot; it is not set in plantarflexion. Early relapse (dynamic supination/forefoot adduction) is managed with repeat casting and, where indicated, tibialis anterior tendon transfer once the lateral cuneiform has ossified—not by reverting to major open release. Full-time 23-hour wear is only for the initial phase, not until age 5.
learningPoint: Brace non-compliance is the leading cause of clubfoot relapse; the FAB is worn near full-time initially then night/naps until ~4-5 years.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Orthopaedics chapter (clubfoot bracing and relapse)
Ponseti International Association bracing protocol; BSCOS clubfoot pathway

stem: A neonate is noted to have a rigid flatfoot with a convex (rocker-bottom) plantar surface, a prominent talar head medially, and a fixed hindfoot equinovalgus with the forefoot dorsiflexed and abducted. The deformity does not correct with passive manipulation. A lateral radiograph taken in maximal plantarflexion shows that a line through the long axis of the talus passes well below (plantar to) the first metatarsal axis, and the navicular remains dorsally dislocated on the talar neck. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.label: A
options.text: Congenital vertical talus

options.label: B
options.text: Flexible (hypermobile) pes planovalgus

options.label: C
options.text: Congenital talipes equinovarus

options.label: D
options.text: Oblique talus that fully reduces on plantarflexion

options.label: E
options.text: Calcaneovalgus foot (positional)

explanation: Congenital vertical talus (CVT) is a rigid rocker-bottom flatfoot caused by a fixed dorsal dislocation of the navicular on a vertically oriented talus, with hindfoot equinovalgus and forefoot dorsiflexion/abduction. The hallmark is irreducibility on a maximal-plantarflexion lateral radiograph: the talar axis stays plantar to the first metatarsal and the talonavicular joint does not reduce. This distinguishes CVT from an oblique talus, where the talonavicular joint REDUCES on plantarflexion. A flexible flatfoot is supple and corrects (heel goes into varus on tiptoe / Jack test), CTEV has equinoVARUS not valgus with medial deviation, and a positional calcaneovalgus foot is dorsiflexed but supple and lacks the vertical talus. CVT is frequently associated with neuromuscular conditions and syndromes/chromosomal anomalies, so a careful general examination is warranted.
learningPoint: Congenital vertical talus is an irreducible rigid rocker-bottom foot; failure of the talonavicular joint to reduce on a maximal-plantarflexion lateral film distinguishes it from an oblique talus.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Orthopaedics chapter (congenital vertical talus)

stem: An 8-week-old infant has confirmed congenital vertical talus. The treating team plans a contemporary, minimally invasive approach analogous to the Ponseti philosophy. Which best describes the modern (Dobbs) management of congenital vertical talus?

options.label: A
options.text: Serial manipulation and casting in the direction OPPOSITE to clubfoot (plantarflexion and inversion) to reduce the talonavicular joint, then percutaneous pinning of the talonavicular joint usually combined with Achilles tenotomy

options.label: B
options.text: Serial casting identical to clubfoot, holding the foot in dorsiflexion and eversion

options.label: C
options.text: Observation alone, as most cases resolve spontaneously by walking age

options.label: D
options.text: Primary talectomy in the neonatal period

options.label: E
options.text: A foot abduction brace from birth without any prior casting

explanation: The Dobbs 'reverse Ponseti' technique applies serial manipulation and casting that stretches the dorsolateral structures by holding the foot in plantarflexion and inversion (the opposite of the clubfoot direction), progressively reducing the dorsally dislocated navicular onto the talar head. Once reduced, the talonavicular joint is stabilised with a percutaneous K-wire, and a percutaneous Achilles tenotomy is performed to address the fixed hindfoot equinus. This minimally invasive approach has largely replaced extensive open releases, which produced stiff, painful feet. CVT does not resolve spontaneously, talectomy is a salvage procedure, and casting in the clubfoot direction (dorsiflexion/eversion) would worsen the deformity.
learningPoint: Modern CVT treatment is the reverse-Ponseti (Dobbs) method—casting in plantarflexion/inversion, then talonavicular pinning and Achilles tenotomy—rather than extensive open release.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Orthopaedics chapter (congenital vertical talus management)
Dobbs et al. minimally invasive technique

stem: A 12-year-old keen footballer presents with a 6-month history of recurrent ankle sprains and a deep, aching pain on the outer side of the hindfoot, worse on uneven ground. On examination he has a rigid pes planovalgus; the heel does not invert and the medial arch does not reconstitute when he stands on tiptoe, and attempted passive inversion is restricted and reproduces pain with palpable peroneal tightness. Plain radiographs, including an oblique view, are equivocal. Which is the single most appropriate investigation to confirm the suspected diagnosis and characterise it?

options.label: A
options.text: CT of both feet/hindfeet

options.label: B
options.text: Weight-bearing AP and lateral radiographs only, repeated in 3 months

options.label: C
options.text: Technetium bone scan

options.label: D
options.text: Nerve conduction studies of the common peroneal nerve

options.label: E
options.text: Diagnostic subtalar joint injection under image guidance

explanation: A rigid (non-correcting) flatfoot with restricted, painful subtalar motion and 'peroneal spastic flatfoot' in an adolescent is the classic presentation of a tarsal coalition. Talocalcaneal coalitions, which tend to present around 12-16 years, are often hard to see on plain films; CT is the investigation of choice to confirm and define the coalition (location, osseous versus non-osseous, extent and joint involvement) and to assess the contralateral foot, since up to ~50% are bilateral. (A calcaneonavicular bar, presenting earlier at ~8-12 years, is best seen on a 45-degree oblique film, and MRI is useful for fibrous/cartilaginous bars and marrow oedema.) A bone scan is non-specific, peroneal 'spasm' is secondary shortening rather than a nerve lesion, and a diagnostic injection does not characterise the anatomy.
learningPoint: Rigid adolescent flatfoot with restricted, painful subtalar motion suggests tarsal coalition; CT best confirms and characterises a talocalcaneal coalition and assesses the often-bilateral contralateral foot.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Orthopaedics chapter (tarsal coalition)

stem: A 13-year-old girl has a symptomatic calcaneonavicular coalition confirmed on imaging. She has persistent pain despite a period of activity modification, NSAIDs, and a trial of orthotic support and immobilisation. CT shows a fibro-osseous calcaneonavicular bar with no degenerative change in the surrounding subtalar or talonavicular joints, and a normal hindfoot alignment. Which is the most appropriate surgical option?

options.label: A
options.text: Resection of the coalition with interposition (e.g. extensor digitorum brevis or fat graft) to reduce reossification

options.label: B
options.text: Primary subtalar (triple) arthrodesis

options.label: C
options.text: Calcaneal lengthening osteotomy alone, leaving the bar intact

options.label: D
options.text: Below-knee arthrodesis cast for a further 6 months without surgery

options.label: E
options.text: Pantalar fusion

options.label: F
options.text: Repeat the same orthotic and immobilisation regimen for another year

explanation: For a symptomatic calcaneonavicular (or talocalcaneal) coalition that has failed non-operative care, and in the absence of established degenerative change or significant fixed hindfoot deformity, resection of the bar with interposition of tissue (classically extensor digitorum brevis, or fat/other material) to discourage reossification is the procedure of choice and preserves joint motion. Arthrodesis (subtalar/triple) is reserved for coalitions with secondary arthritis, very large talocalcaneal bars not amenable to resection, or marked malalignment. A calcaneal lengthening osteotomy may be ADDED to address significant planovalgus but does not treat the bar itself, and pantalar fusion is far too extensive. Simply repeating failed conservative measures is inappropriate when symptoms persist and anatomy is favourable for resection.
learningPoint: A symptomatic coalition without arthritis or fixed deformity is treated by bar resection with interposition to preserve motion; arthrodesis is reserved for arthritic or unresectable disease.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Orthopaedics chapter (tarsal coalition management)

stem: A 4-year-old boy with bilateral spastic cerebral palsy, classified GMFCS level IV, is enrolled in a hip surveillance programme. A standardised supine AP pelvis radiograph is reported, and the Reimers migration percentage of the right hip is measured at 38% and the left at 25%, with no break in Shenton's line and preserved acetabular morphology. According to UK hip surveillance principles, what is the most appropriate management of the right hip?

options.label: A
options.text: Continue surveillance with a repeat radiograph at a shortened interval (e.g. 6 months) to monitor for progression, alongside optimisation of physiotherapy and tone management

options.label: B
options.text: Discharge from surveillance as both hips are within normal limits

options.label: C
options.text: Proceed directly to open hip reduction and femoral/pelvic reconstruction

options.label: D
options.text: Arrange an urgent salvage procedure (e.g. proximal femoral resection)

options.label: E
options.text: Repeat the radiograph only when the child reaches skeletal maturity

explanation: Reimers migration percentage (MP) is the cornerstone radiographic measure in CP hip surveillance. An MP up to about 30% is generally considered within normal limits; 30-40% indicates 'at risk' hips warranting closer monitoring (shorter-interval films) and review of tone/posture management; and an MP exceeding ~40%, or a hip showing rapid progression, prompts consideration of surgical intervention (typically reconstructive soft-tissue and/or bony surgery). With a right MP of 38% and no other adverse features, the correct action is intensified surveillance and conservative optimisation rather than immediate reconstruction. Children at GMFCS IV-V carry the highest displacement risk and need the most frequent monitoring. Salvage surgery is for established painful dislocation, not a subluxating hip detected early.
learningPoint: In CP hip surveillance an MP of 30-40% defines an 'at-risk' hip needing shorter-interval radiographs; surgery is considered above ~40% or with rapid progression.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Orthopaedics chapter (cerebral palsy hip)
Reimers migration percentage; UK CP hip surveillance (CPIPS/CPIP, BSCOS-endorsed)

stem: An 8-year-old ambulant child with spastic diplegic cerebral palsy (GMFCS II) is assessed in a gait laboratory because of increasing difficulty walking. Instrumented gait analysis shows the knee remaining flexed throughout stance, increased anterior pelvic tilt, and—on dynamic electromyography—rectus femoris activity continuing inappropriately into swing with a reduced and delayed peak knee flexion, producing a stiff-knee gait. Hamstring length modelling shows the medial hamstrings are short and 'fast'. Which intervention most directly addresses the stiff-knee, reduced-swing-phase knee-flexion component identified on this analysis?

options.label: A
options.text: Transfer of the rectus femoris (e.g. distal rectus femoris transfer) to improve swing-phase knee flexion

options.label: B
options.text: Isolated tendo-Achilles lengthening

options.label: C
options.text: Proximal femoral varus derotation osteotomy

options.label: D
options.text: Bilateral foot abduction bracing at night

options.label: E
options.text: Selective dorsal rhizotomy of the S1 nerve roots only

explanation: Stiff-knee gait in spastic CP is characterised by reduced and delayed peak knee flexion in swing, driven by inappropriate (out-of-phase) rectus femoris activity continuing into swing. The targeted treatment is rectus femoris transfer (commonly a distal transfer), which removes its knee-extension moment during swing and improves swing-phase knee flexion and foot clearance—often performed alongside hamstring lengthening for the crouch/short hamstrings as part of single-event multilevel surgery. An isolated TAL addresses equinus, not the rectus-driven stiff knee; a proximal femoral VDRO addresses hip lever-arm/coverage problems; night-time foot abduction bracing is a clubfoot tool, not relevant here; and rhizotomy is a global tone-reduction strategy, not the specific mechanical correction for the rectus-driven stiff knee. This illustrates how instrumented gait analysis directs the specific surgical decision.
learningPoint: Stiff-knee gait from out-of-phase rectus femoris activity is treated by rectus femoris transfer to restore swing-phase knee flexion, typically within multilevel surgery.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Orthopaedics chapter (cerebral palsy gait / multilevel surgery)

stem: Theme: Diagnosis of the paediatric foot. For each clinical description, select the SINGLE most likely diagnosis from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Congenital talipes equinovarus (idiopathic clubfoot)

options.label: B
options.text: Congenital vertical talus

options.label: C
options.text: Calcaneonavicular tarsal coalition

options.label: D
options.text: Talocalcaneal tarsal coalition

options.label: E
options.text: Flexible pes planovalgus

options.label: F
options.text: Metatarsus adductus

options.label: G
options.text: Positional calcaneovalgus foot

options.label: H
options.text: Oblique talus

explanation: These vignettes separate the common paediatric foot diagnoses by rigidity, direction of deformity, age of presentation and key imaging signs. Clubfoot is a rigid equinoVARUS, cavus and adductus deformity at birth. A flexible flatfoot corrects on tiptoe/Jack test and is asymptomatic. A talocalcaneal coalition presents in early-mid adolescence with a rigid painful flatfoot and restricted subtalar motion, best shown on CT. A calcaneonavicular coalition presents a little earlier and is seen on a 45-degree oblique radiograph. Congenital vertical talus is a rigid rocker-bottom foot with an irreducible dorsally dislocated navicular (talar axis stays plantar to the first metatarsal on a maximal-plantarflexion lateral). Metatarsus adductus is an isolated, often flexible, adduction of the forefoot with a normal hindfoot and a convex lateral border.
learningPoint: Classify paediatric foot deformity by rigidity, direction (varus vs valgus), age at presentation, and the key imaging sign.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Orthopaedics chapter (paediatric foot disorders)

emiLeads.stem: A newborn has a rigid foot fixed in equinus, hindfoot varus, midfoot cavus and forefoot adductus that does not correct with manipulation.

A newborn has a rigid rocker-bottom foot with a convex sole; on a maximal-plantarflexion lateral radiograph the talar long axis lies plantar to the first metatarsal and the talonavicular joint fails to reduce.
A 9-year-old has activity-related hindfoot pain and a rigid flatfoot; a 45-degree oblique radiograph shows an elongated 'anteater nose' calcaneus bridging to the navicular.
A 14-year-old footballer has recurrent sprains, a rigid valgus hindfoot that will not invert on tiptoe, and CT confirms a bony bridge across the middle facet of the subtalar joint.
A 6-year-old has painless flat feet; the medial arch reconstitutes and the heel swings into varus when she stands on tiptoe.

stem: Theme: Management of paediatric lower-limb alignment and cerebral palsy hip. For each scenario, select the SINGLE most appropriate management from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Reassurance and observation (no orthosis or surgery)

options.label: B
options.text: Well-timed contralateral epiphysiodesis

options.label: C
options.text: Medial proximal tibial guided growth (tension-band plate)

options.label: D
options.text: Proximal tibial corrective osteotomy

options.label: E
options.text: Knee-ankle-foot orthosis (bracing)

options.label: F
options.text: Continue routine hip surveillance at standard intervals

options.label: G
options.text: Reconstructive hip surgery (soft-tissue +/- femoral/pelvic osteotomy)

options.label: H
options.text: Lateral distal femoral guided growth (tension-band plate)

explanation: These lead-ins integrate the management thresholds for the alignment and CP-hip topics. Physiological bowing/knock knees in a well child are observed. A predicted moderate leg-length discrepancy with growth remaining is treated by correctly timed epiphysiodesis of the longer limb. Persistent, pathological genu valgum with growth remaining is amenable to guided growth on the appropriate side (medial proximal tibia for tibial-based valgus; the option list also offers lateral distal femoral plating for femoral-based valgus). Early infantile Blount disease in a young child is first braced. In CP hip surveillance, a hip with MP up to ~30% and stable continues standard-interval surveillance, whereas a hip exceeding ~40% (or rapidly progressing) is referred for reconstructive surgery.
learningPoint: Match the intervention to the threshold: observe physiological variants, epiphysiodesis for moderate LLD, guided growth/osteotomy or bracing for pathological angular deformity, and escalate CP hips by migration percentage.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Orthopaedics chapter (angular deformity, LLD, CP hip)
Reimers MP thresholds; UK CP hip surveillance (CPIP/CPIPS)

emiLeads.stem: A healthy, normally grown 3.5-year-old has symmetrical genu valgum with an intermalleolar distance of 5 cm and no pain.
A 10-year-old with a previous physeal injury has a Moseley-predicted 4 cm leg-length discrepancy at maturity from femoral shortening, with normal predicted final height.
An 11-year-old has persistent, progressive idiopathic genu valgum localised to the distal femur with about 2 years of growth remaining.
A 2.5-year-old early walker with raised weight has progressive infantile tibia vara, medial metaphyseal beaking and a Drennan angle of 13 degrees (Langenskiold stage II).
A GMFCS V 5-year-old has a hip with a Reimers migration percentage of 55% and a broken Shenton's line on the surveillance radiograph.

stem: A 4.6 kg neonate is reviewed on the postnatal ward after a difficult vaginal delivery complicated by shoulder dystocia. The right arm lies adducted and internally rotated at the shoulder, extended at the elbow, pronated, with the wrist flexed. The Moro reflex is absent on the right, but the hand grasp is intact and the fingers move briskly. Sensation appears preserved over the palm and there is no ptosis or miosis. Which nerve roots are most likely involved?

options.label: A
options.text: C5-C6 (Erb-Duchenne palsy)

options.label: B
options.text: C8-T1 (Klumpke palsy)

options.label: C
options.text: C5-T1 (total/pan-plexus palsy)

options.label: D
options.text: C7 alone

options.label: E
options.text: C4-C5

explanation: The classic 'waiter's tip' posture (adduction and internal rotation of the shoulder, elbow extension, forearm pronation, wrist flexion) with an intact hand grasp and preserved finger movement localises the lesion to the upper trunk (C5-C6), i.e. Erb-Duchenne palsy. This is by far the most common pattern of obstetric brachial plexus palsy and carries the best prognosis. Klumpke (C8-T1) produces a clawed hand with intrinsic loss and is very rare in isolation; total plexus palsy produces a flail, insensate limb often with an ipsilateral Horner syndrome (ptosis, miosis), which is absent here.
learningPoint: Erb's palsy (C5-C6) gives the waiter's-tip posture with a preserved functioning hand and is the commonest, best-prognosis obstetric brachial plexus injury.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Orthopaedics chapter (brachial plexus birth palsy)
BSSH guidance on obstetric brachial plexus palsy

stem: A 3-month-old infant with an upper-trunk obstetric brachial plexus palsy is reviewed in a specialist clinic. Shoulder abduction and external rotation remain absent, and there is no antigravity elbow flexion; the biceps does not contract on examination. The parents ask about the chance of useful spontaneous recovery and whether surgery may be needed. Which single finding is the most widely used clinical indicator that primary nerve reconstruction (e.g. neuroma excision and grafting) should be considered?

options.label: A
options.text: Failure to recover antigravity elbow flexion (biceps) by around 3 months of age (with a failed 'cookie test' at 9 months in conservatively managed infants)

options.label: B
options.text: Persistent absence of the Moro reflex at 6 weeks

options.label: C
options.text: An associated fractured clavicle on the affected side

options.label: D
options.text: Presence of a torticollis on the contralateral side

options.label: E
options.text: Failure of full passive shoulder external rotation at 12 months

explanation: The most established and widely cited clinical decision point for primary nerve surgery is failure to recover antigravity biceps function (elbow flexion). Recovery of biceps by around 3 months predicts a good outcome (Gilbert); its absence at this stage, particularly with absent shoulder function, identifies infants who benefit from microsurgical reconstruction. Infants managed conservatively are commonly retested with the 'cookie test' at about 9 months, and failure is a further surgical trigger (Clarke/Curtis). An associated clavicle fracture, torticollis, or transient Moro changes are not surgical indications, and passive external rotation limitation at 12 months reflects secondary glenohumeral dysplasia rather than the timing of nerve repair.
learningPoint: Absent antigravity biceps (elbow flexion) by ~3 months is the standard trigger to consider primary nerve reconstruction in obstetric brachial plexus palsy; a failed cookie test at ~9 months is a further indication.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Orthopaedics (brachial plexus birth palsy, surgical timing)
BSSH obstetric brachial plexus palsy guidance

stem: A 3-year-old child with a history of obstetric brachial plexus palsy has recovered partial shoulder function but now has a persistent internal-rotation contracture with limited active and passive external rotation. Axial MRI of the shoulder demonstrates a retroverted, flattened glenoid with posterior subluxation of the humeral head. Which muscle imbalance is the principal driver of this progressive glenohumeral deformity?

options.label: A
options.text: Weak external rotators (infraspinatus/teres minor) overpowered by relatively preserved internal rotators (subscapularis/pectoralis major)

options.label: B
options.text: Weak deltoid overpowered by an intact supraspinatus

options.label: C
options.text: Weak biceps overpowered by an intact triceps

options.label: D
options.text: Weak serratus anterior causing scapular winging

options.label: E
options.text: Weak trapezius overpowered by the rhomboids

explanation: In upper-trunk palsy the external rotators (infraspinatus, teres minor) and abductors recover poorly, while the internal rotators (subscapularis, pectoralis major, latissimus dorsi) are relatively spared. The resulting unopposed internal rotation pull produces an internal-rotation contracture, glenoid retroversion/dysplasia and posterior humeral head subluxation/dislocation over time. Management ranges from botulinum toxin and subscapularis/pectoralis lengthening with tendon transfers (e.g. latissimus dorsi and teres major to the rotator cuff) to glenohumeral reduction, depending on age and severity.
learningPoint: Internal-rotation contracture and glenoid dysplasia after Erb's palsy result from preserved internal rotators overpowering weak external rotators.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Orthopaedics (glenohumeral dysplasia in BPBP)
BSSH obstetric brachial plexus palsy guidance

stem: A 2-year-old child is referred with a longitudinal radial deficiency (radial club hand). The forearm is short and bowed with radial deviation of the hand at the wrist. Before planning any treatment, which associated systemic condition must be actively excluded because, if missed, it carries the most serious long-term threat to life?

options.label: A
options.text: Thrombocytopenia-absent radius (TAR) syndrome

options.label: B
options.text: Holt-Oram syndrome

options.label: C
options.text: Fanconi anaemia

options.label: D
options.text: VACTERL association

options.label: E
options.text: Trisomy 18

explanation: Radial longitudinal deficiency is strongly associated with several syndromes, and a systematic work-up (FBC, renal/cardiac/spinal imaging, chromosomal breakage/DEB testing) is mandatory. Of the listed conditions, Fanconi anaemia is the most important to detect early: marrow failure (progressive aplastic anaemia) and a high malignancy risk typically develop over childhood and so are easily missed, yet early diagnosis by chromosomal breakage testing allows haematological surveillance and potentially curative bone-marrow transplantation before failure occurs. A useful discriminator is that in TAR the thumbs are always PRESENT, whereas Fanconi often has thumb hypoplasia/aplasia. TAR, Holt-Oram (cardiac) and VACTERL all require screening, but untreated Fanconi anaemia is the condition that is ultimately fatal.
learningPoint: Every radial club hand needs a syndrome work-up; Fanconi anaemia must be excluded (chromosomal breakage testing) because untreated marrow failure is fatal. In TAR the thumb is always present.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric/Hand chapter (radial longitudinal deficiency)

stem: A child is referred with a congenital hand difference and the clinic uses the modern Oberg-Manske-Tonkin (OMT) classification to categorise the anomaly. According to this classification framework, into which principal category does symbrachydactyly most appropriately fall?

options.label: A
options.text: Malformation - failure of axis formation/differentiation affecting the hand plate

options.label: B
options.text: Deformation

options.label: C
options.text: Dysplasia

options.label: D
options.text: Syndromic duplication

options.label: E
options.text: Amniotic disruption sequence

explanation: The OMT classification (adopted by the IFSSH) divides congenital upper-limb anomalies into Malformations, Deformations and Dysplasias, with malformations further subdivided by the affected axis (proximal-distal, radial-ulnar/anteroposterior, dorsal-ventral) and by whether the whole limb or the hand plate is involved. Symbrachydactyly is regarded as a malformation affecting hand-plate formation/differentiation. It must be distinguished from amniotic band (constriction ring) sequence, which is a disruption/deformation - a common examination discriminator because the two can look similar but have a different aetiology and classification.
learningPoint: OMT (IFSSH) classification sorts congenital limb anomalies into malformation/deformation/dysplasia; symbrachydactyly is a hand-plate malformation, distinct from amniotic band disruption.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Hand chapter (OMT/IFSSH classification)

stem: A 14-month-old infant is seen with thumb hypoplasia. The basal carpometacarpal (CMC) joint is the key feature that determines treatment. Using the modified Blauth classification, which broad management principle most appropriately applies to a thumb in which the CMC joint is unstable or absent (Blauth grade IIIB and above)?

options.label: A
options.text: Reconstruction of the existing thumb (web release, opponensplasty, ligament stabilisation)

options.label: B
options.text: Pollicisation of the index finger

options.label: C
options.text: Simple observation with no intervention

options.label: D
options.text: Distraction lengthening of the thumb metacarpal

options.label: E
options.text: Free toe-to-hand transfer in infancy

explanation: In the modified Blauth classification of thumb hypoplasia, the stability of the basal (CMC) joint is the critical decision point. Reconstructable thumbs (grades I, II and IIIA, all with a stable CMC joint) are treated by reconstruction: first web release, opponensplasty and ulnar collateral ligament stabilisation. Once the CMC joint is deficient/unstable (grade IIIB) or the thumb is essentially absent (grade IV 'floating thumb'/pouce flottant and grade V), reconstruction of the native thumb fails and the standard treatment is ablation of the vestigial thumb with pollicisation of the index finger.
learningPoint: CMC joint stability is the watershed in thumb hypoplasia: stable (up to IIIA) = reconstruct; unstable/absent (IIIB and above) = pollicisation.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Hand chapter (thumb hypoplasia, modified Blauth)

stem: Theme: Congenital upper-limb diagnoses. For each clinical description below, select the SINGLE most likely diagnosis from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Radial longitudinal deficiency (radial club hand)

options.label: B
options.text: Amniotic band (constriction ring) sequence

options.label: C
options.text: Apert syndrome

options.label: D
options.text: Madelung deformity

options.label: E
options.text: Poland syndrome

options.label: F
options.text: Camptodactyly

options.label: G
options.text: Ulnar longitudinal deficiency

options.label: H
options.text: Congenital trigger thumb

explanation: A: A short, bowed forearm with the hand deviated radially at the wrist and an absent/hypoplastic thumb, plus screening for anaemia and renal anomalies, points to radial longitudinal deficiency. B: Circumferential constriction grooves with distal lymphoedema, acrosyndactyly (fused tips with a proximal fenestration) and sometimes intrauterine amputation are characteristic of amniotic band sequence. E: Unilateral chest-wall hypoplasia (absent sternocostal head of pectoralis major) with ipsilateral short/webbed digits is Poland syndrome. D: A triangular/dysplastic distal radial epiphysis with volar-ulnar tilt of the lunate and a dorsally prominent ulnar head, typically in an adolescent girl and linked to Leri-Weill dyschondrosteosis (Vickers ligament tethering the volar-ulnar physis), is Madelung deformity. C: Complex (bony) syndactyly of all digits ('mitten/rosebud hand') with craniosynostosis and midface hypoplasia is Apert syndrome.
learningPoint: Pattern-recognition of congenital limb syndromes: constriction grooves = bands; chest-wall + short digits = Poland; volar-ulnar wrist tilt = Madelung; mitten hand + craniosynostosis = Apert.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Hand/Pediatrics chapters

emiLeads.stem: A 1-year-old with a short, bowed forearm, the hand deviated radially at the wrist, and an absent thumb. The infant is also being investigated for anaemia and renal anomalies.
A newborn with deep circumferential grooves around the leg and fingers, distal swelling, and fusion of the tips of two adjacent fingers with a fenestration proximally.
A boy with a flat, asymmetrical chest wall on the right (absent sternal head of pectoralis major) and short, mildly webbed fingers on the same hand.
A 13-year-old girl with progressive painful wrist deformity; radiographs show a triangular distal radial epiphysis with volar-ulnar tilt of the lunate and a dorsally prominent ulnar head.
An infant with bony fusion of all the digits into a single mass and a high, fused skull with midface hypoplasia.

stem: A 13-year-old girl is referred after a school nurse noted a rib hump on forward bending. Examination shows a right thoracic curve with a positive Adams forward-bend test and no neurological abnormality. Standing PA radiograph shows a right thoracic curve with a Cobb angle of 28 degrees and a Risser grade of 1. She remains premenarchal. According to standard UK practice for adolescent idiopathic scoliosis, what is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Reassurance and discharge

options.label: B
options.text: Bracing (e.g. thoracolumbosacral orthosis)

options.label: C
options.text: Posterior spinal fusion

options.label: D
options.text: Anterior vertebral body tethering

options.label: E
options.text: Serial casting under anaesthesia

explanation: In adolescent idiopathic scoliosis the decision to brace depends on curve magnitude and remaining growth. A curve of roughly 25-45 degrees in a skeletally immature patient (low Risser grade, premenarchal) is the classic indication for bracing, which aims to prevent progression to a surgical threshold rather than to correct the curve. Curves below about 20-25 degrees are observed; curves exceeding about 45-50 degrees (or progressing despite bracing) are considered for posterior spinal fusion. Serial casting is used for early-onset/infantile curves, not a 13-year-old with AIS, and vertebral body tethering is a growth-modulation option reserved for selected larger curves rather than first-line here.
learningPoint: Brace AIS curves of ~25-45 degrees in a skeletally immature patient; observe smaller curves and operate beyond ~45-50 degrees.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Spine (AIS)

stem: A 9-month-old infant has a left thoracic curve noted incidentally. There are no congenital vertebral anomalies on plain films and the neurological examination is normal. On the supine AP radiograph the rib-vertebra angles are measured. Which single finding most reliably predicts that this infantile idiopathic curve is likely to PROGRESS rather than resolve?

options.label: A
options.text: A rib-vertebra angle difference (Mehta angle) of about 20 degrees or more (and/or a phase-2 rib head)

options.label: B
options.text: A Cobb angle of less than 15 degrees

options.label: C
options.text: A left-sided rather than right-sided curve

options.label: D
options.text: Female sex

options.label: E
options.text: A rib-vertebra angle difference of less than 10 degrees

explanation: Most infantile idiopathic scoliosis curves resolve spontaneously, so identifying the minority that will progress is key. Mehta's rib-vertebra angle difference (RVAD) is the classic predictor: an RVAD of about 20 degrees or more, and/or a 'phase 2' rib head that overlaps the apical vertebral body, indicates a high likelihood of progression and the need for active treatment (serial Mehta/EDF casting, then bracing or growth-friendly surgery). A small RVAD with phase-1 ribs predicts resolution. Curve side and sex are not reliable predictors of progression in this context.
learningPoint: In infantile idiopathic scoliosis, a Mehta rib-vertebra angle difference of ~20 degrees or more (or a phase-2 rib head) predicts progression and prompts serial casting.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Spine (infantile/early-onset scoliosis, Mehta angle)

stem: A 4-year-old child is found to have congenital scoliosis. Radiographs show a unilateral failure of segmentation forming a bony bar on the concavity of the curve, with a contralateral fully segmented hemivertebra on the convexity at the same level. Which of the following statements about this combination is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: It carries the worst prognosis for progression of all congenital vertebral anomalies and usually requires early surgery

options.label: B
options.text: It almost always resolves spontaneously and can be observed indefinitely

options.label: C
options.text: A block vertebra at the same level would make it progress faster

options.label: D
options.text: It has the same (low) progression risk as a non-segmented hemivertebra

options.label: E
options.text: Bracing reliably corrects the structural deformity and avoids surgery

explanation: Progression risk in congenital scoliosis is governed by the type of anomaly and its location. A unilateral unsegmented bar combined with a contralateral (convex) fully segmented hemivertebra produces grossly asymmetric growth - a tethered concavity plus an active convex growth plate - and carries the highest progression rate of all congenital patterns, typically warranting early surgical intervention (e.g. hemivertebra excision/instrumented fusion or growth-friendly constructs). A block (fully segmented, bilaterally fused) vertebra grows symmetrically and is the most benign anomaly. Bracing does not control the structural bony deformity of congenital curves, though it may help manage compensatory curves.
learningPoint: Unilateral unsegmented bar PLUS contralateral hemivertebra is the most aggressive congenital scoliosis pattern and usually needs early surgery; a block vertebra is the most benign.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Spine (congenital scoliosis classification/prognosis)

stem: A 12-year-old boy with Duchenne muscular dystrophy, now a full-time wheelchair user, is reviewed. He has a progressive long C-shaped thoracolumbar curve with pelvic obliquity, and sitting balance is becoming difficult. Compared with adolescent idiopathic scoliosis, which feature most characterises the surgical management of neuromuscular scoliosis in this setting?

options.label: A
options.text: Long instrumented fusion frequently extending to the pelvis to control pelvic obliquity and maintain sitting balance

options.label: B
options.text: Short selective thoracic fusion preserving lumbar motion segments

options.label: C
options.text: Bracing alone is curative and surgery is rarely needed

options.label: D
options.text: Anterior-only surgery is the standard of care

options.label: E
options.text: Observation until skeletal maturity before any intervention

explanation: Neuromuscular curves (e.g. DMD, cerebral palsy, spinal muscular atrophy) are typically long, C-shaped collapsing curves associated with pelvic obliquity and loss of sitting balance, and they often progress even after skeletal maturity. Surgical correction usually requires long-segment posterior instrumented fusion, frequently extending to the pelvis (e.g. to S1/iliac fixation) to level the pelvis and restore a stable, comfortable seated posture. In DMD, surgery is often considered relatively early (commonly cited around 20-40 degrees with declining respiratory function) because cardiorespiratory deterioration narrows the operative window. Bracing does not control these curves, and short selective fusions are inadequate.
learningPoint: Neuromuscular scoliosis = long collapsing curve with pelvic obliquity; treatment is long fusion often to the pelvis to restore sitting balance.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Spine (neuromuscular scoliosis)

stem: A 14-year-old with cerebral palsy (GMFCS level V) is being worked up for posterior spinal fusion for a severe neuromuscular curve. Which of the following perioperative considerations is most specifically associated with this neuromuscular population compared with otherwise healthy adolescent idiopathic scoliosis patients?

options.label: A
options.text: Markedly higher rates of perioperative complications including blood loss, respiratory compromise, infection and the need for nutritional optimisation

options.label: B
options.text: A near-zero complication rate making preoperative optimisation unnecessary

options.label: C
options.text: Routine avoidance of pelvic fixation regardless of pelvic obliquity

options.label: D
options.text: Contraindication to intraoperative neuromonitoring in all cases

options.label: E
options.text: An absolute contraindication to surgery due to the underlying diagnosis

explanation: Neuromuscular scoliosis surgery carries substantially higher perioperative morbidity than AIS: greater intraoperative blood loss, higher transfusion needs, more respiratory complications, higher surgical-site infection rates, frequent need for preoperative nutritional and respiratory optimisation, and longer ICU/hospital stays. A multidisciplinary approach (respiratory, nutrition, seating, anaesthesia) and an informed discussion with carers about goals (sitting balance, ease of care, comfort) are essential. Pelvic fixation is often required (not avoided) when pelvic obliquity is significant, and surgery is not contraindicated by the diagnosis itself.
learningPoint: Neuromuscular scoliosis correction has high perioperative morbidity (blood loss, infection, respiratory, nutritional) and demands MDT optimisation and realistic goal-setting.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Spine (neuromuscular scoliosis complications)

stem: A 15-year-old boy presents with a cosmetically noticeable rounded upper back and mild thoracic pain after activity. On forward bending the kyphosis is sharp and angular and does not correct with hyperextension. Lateral radiograph shows a thoracic kyphosis of 62 degrees with anterior wedging of more than 5 degrees across three adjacent vertebrae, irregular endplates and Schmorl nodes. Which feature most reliably distinguishes Scheuermann kyphosis from postural (flexible) kyphosis?

options.label: A
options.text: A rigid, structural kyphosis that fails to correct on prone hyperextension, with anterior wedging of 5 degrees or more over 3 or more consecutive vertebrae

options.label: B
options.text: Complete correction of the curve on hyperextension

options.label: C
options.text: Absence of any radiographic vertebral changes

options.label: D
options.text: A smooth, gentle, fully flexible thoracic contour

options.label: E
options.text: Onset in patients over 30 years of age

explanation: Scheuermann disease is a structural osteochondrosis of the thoracic (or thoracolumbar) spine. The diagnostic radiographic criterion is anterior wedging of 5 degrees or more across three or more consecutive vertebrae, accompanied by endplate irregularity and Schmorl nodes; clinically the kyphosis is rigid and angular and does NOT correct with hyperextension. Postural kyphosis, by contrast, is flexible, corrects fully on prone hyperextension and shows no structural vertebral changes. This rigidity, together with the wedging criterion, is the key discriminator.
learningPoint: Scheuermann kyphosis is rigid with at least 5 degrees of anterior wedging over 3 or more consecutive vertebrae; postural kyphosis is flexible and corrects on extension.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Spine (Scheuermann disease)

stem: A 14-year-old with radiologically confirmed thoracic Scheuermann kyphosis of 55 degrees has back pain and a documented Risser grade of 2 with persisting growth. Which management option is most appropriate as first-line treatment at this stage?

options.label: A
options.text: Physiotherapy and bracing, with surgery reserved for curves that progress beyond roughly 70-75 degrees or fail conservative care

options.label: B
options.text: Immediate posterior spinal fusion

options.label: C
options.text: Anterior release and fusion as the sole first-line option

options.label: D
options.text: Vertebroplasty of the wedged vertebrae

options.label: E
options.text: No treatment is ever indicated for Scheuermann kyphosis

explanation: In a skeletally immature patient with a moderate Scheuermann curve, first-line treatment is non-operative: an extension-type brace (e.g. modified Milwaukee/TLSO) combined with physiotherapy can influence flexible curves while growth remains, alongside analgesia. Surgery (posterior, or occasionally combined, instrumented fusion) is generally reserved for severe curves - commonly quoted around 70-75 degrees or more - or for intractable pain, neurological compromise or unacceptable deformity that fails conservative management. Bracing has limited effect once growth is complete or the curve is very rigid/large.
learningPoint: Moderate Scheuermann kyphosis in a growing child is managed with bracing/physiotherapy; surgery is reserved for severe curves (around 70-75 degrees or more) or refractory symptoms.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Spine (Scheuermann management)

stem: A 10-year-old child with Down syndrome is being assessed before participating in competitive sport. Flexion-extension lateral cervical radiographs show an os odontoideum with abnormal C1-C2 translation and reduction in the space available for the cord on flexion. The child has intermittent neck pain and occasional clumsiness of the hands. What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Posterior C1-C2 stabilisation/fusion

options.label: B
options.text: Reassurance and clearance for unrestricted contact sport

options.label: C
options.text: Soft cervical collar and discharge

options.label: D
options.text: Anterior odontoid screw fixation of the os

options.label: E
options.text: Manipulation under anaesthesia

explanation: Os odontoideum produces atlantoaxial instability because the ossicle is separated from the body of C2, allowing abnormal C1-C2 translation. When there is instability with neurological symptoms/signs (here hand clumsiness suggesting early myelopathy) and reduced space available for the cord, surgical posterior C1-C2 stabilisation and fusion is indicated to protect the cord. Os odontoideum is not amenable to an anterior odontoid screw (there is no intact dens to compress and unite), and a child with symptomatic instability should not be cleared for contact sport. Down syndrome predisposes to atlantoaxial instability and to os odontoideum.
learningPoint: Symptomatic/unstable os odontoideum (especially with myelopathy or reduced cord space) is treated by posterior C1-C2 fusion, not anterior screw fixation.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Spine (os odontoideum, atlantoaxial instability)

stem: A 7-year-old child is referred with a short neck, a low posterior hairline and markedly restricted neck movement, particularly rotation and lateral flexion. Cervical radiographs show congenital fusion of two cervical vertebrae. Which additional anomaly is most commonly associated with this condition and should be specifically screened for?

options.label: A
options.text: Genitourinary anomalies (e.g. renal agenesis), with Sprengel deformity and hearing loss also recognised associations

options.label: B
options.text: Congenital hip dislocation as the dominant association

options.label: C
options.text: Pectus excavatum as the dominant association

options.label: D
options.text: Tarsal coalition as the dominant association

options.label: E
options.text: Polydactyly as the dominant association

explanation: The clinical triad of a short neck, low posterior hairline and restricted cervical motion describes Klippel-Feil syndrome (congenital cervical vertebral fusion). It is associated with multiple other anomalies that must be screened for, most notably renal/genitourinary anomalies (including unilateral renal agenesis - so a renal ultrasound is recommended), Sprengel deformity (congenital high scapula), sensorineural hearing loss and congenital scoliosis, with a risk of cervical instability and neurological compromise. Recognising these associations is a classic examination point.
learningPoint: Klippel-Feil (short neck, low hairline, restricted motion) needs screening for renal anomalies, Sprengel deformity and hearing loss.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Spine (Klippel-Feil syndrome)

stem: A 9-month-old infant with achondroplasia is referred with apnoeic episodes and developmental delay. The parents ask about the underlying cause of the condition. Which statement most accurately describes the molecular and clinical basis of achondroplasia?

options.label: A
options.text: It is an autosomal recessive disorder of type I collagen causing defective osteoid mineralisation

options.label: B
options.text: It results from a gain-of-function mutation in FGFR3 that inhibits endochondral ossification, and foramen magnum stenosis can cause cervicomedullary compression

options.label: C
options.text: It is caused by a loss-of-function mutation in the SHOX gene affecting the proliferative zone of the physis

options.label: D
options.text: It is an X-linked dominant condition with hypophosphataemia and rachitic physes

options.label: E
options.text: It is a defect in cartilage oligomeric matrix protein (COMP) causing epiphyseal fragmentation

explanation: Achondroplasia is autosomal dominant (though ~80% are sporadic new mutations, associated with advanced paternal age) and arises from a gain-of-function point mutation in FGFR3, almost always G380R. Constitutive FGFR3 signalling inhibits chondrocyte proliferation in the proliferative zone, impairing endochondral ossification while membranous ossification is spared — giving rhizomelic short limbs, a normal-width but short skull base and frontal bossing. The key infant risk is foramen magnum stenosis causing cervicomedullary compression, which presents with central/obstructive apnoea, hypotonia, hyper-reflexia and developmental delay or sudden death; this child needs polysomnography and MRI, with possible foramen magnum decompression. COMP defects cause pseudoachondroplasia/MED, SHOX deficiency causes Léri-Weill/Langer dysplasia, and type I collagen defects cause osteogenesis imperfecta.
learningPoint: Achondroplasia = autosomal dominant FGFR3 gain-of-function (G380R); the dangerous infant complication is foramen magnum stenosis causing cervicomedullary compression and apnoea.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pediatric Orthopaedics: skeletal dysplasias

stem: A 32-year-old man with achondroplasia presents with bilateral leg pain, numbness and heaviness that come on after walking 100 metres and are relieved by sitting and leaning forward. Examination between episodes is near-normal. Which feature of the achondroplastic spine best explains these symptoms?

options.label: A
options.text: Atlantoaxial instability from os odontoideum

options.label: B
options.text: Congenitally short pedicles with reduced interpedicular distance causing multilevel lumbar spinal stenosis

options.label: C
options.text: Progressive thoracic hyperkyphosis with anterior cord compression

options.label: D
options.text: Tethered cord from a low-lying conus

options.label: E
options.text: Bilateral L5 pars defects with high-grade spondylolisthesis

explanation: Adults with achondroplasia characteristically develop symptomatic lumbar spinal stenosis. The interpedicular distance normally widens from L1 to L5; in achondroplasia the pedicles are congenitally short and thick and the interpedicular distance narrows caudally, producing a constitutionally small canal that decompensates with age-related degenerative change, often compounded by a thoracolumbar kyphosis (gibbus). The history is classic neurogenic claudication — exertional bilateral leg symptoms relieved by lumbar flexion (sitting/leaning forward) — distinct from vascular claudication. Management is activity modification then wide multilevel decompression when disabling. Atlantoaxial instability and tethered cord are not the typical adult achondroplastic pattern, and a fixed gibbus would give different, more constant signs.
learningPoint: Short pedicles and a caudally narrowing interpedicular distance make multilevel lumbar stenosis and neurogenic claudication the defining adult spinal problem in achondroplasia.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pediatric Orthopaedics: dwarfism / spine

stem: A 2-year-old child has had four long-bone fractures from minor trauma, blue sclerae, mild short stature and early-onset hearing concern; the mother has similar features. Genetic testing confirms a quantitative defect in type I collagen production. Which Sillence type and inheritance pattern is most consistent with this presentation?

options.label: A
options.text: Type I — autosomal dominant, the mildest and most common form

options.label: B
options.text: Type II — autosomal dominant, perinatally lethal

options.label: C
options.text: Type III — autosomal recessive only, non-deforming

options.label: D
options.text: Type IV — X-linked recessive with white sclerae

options.label: E
options.text: Type V — autosomal recessive with hypertrophic callus and IFITM5 mutation

explanation: Classic osteogenesis imperfecta types I–IV are autosomal dominant defects of type I collagen (COL1A1/COL1A2). Type I, the commonest and mildest, is a quantitative defect (reduced normal collagen), giving fractures that decrease after puberty, distinctly blue sclerae, presenile conductive/mixed hearing loss and near-normal stature — fitting this child and an affected parent. Type II is perinatally lethal; type III is the most severe survivable, progressively deforming with greyish sclerae; type IV is dominant with white/normal sclerae and variable severity. Type V is autosomal dominant (IFITM5) with hyperplastic callus and interosseous-membrane calcification, not recessive. Recessive forms exist (e.g. types VII–VIII from CRTAP/P3H1 defects) but are not the typical mild dominant picture described.
learningPoint: OI types I–IV are autosomal dominant type I collagen disorders; type I is the mildest (quantitative defect) with blue sclerae, hearing loss and fractures that improve after puberty.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pediatric Orthopaedics: osteogenesis imperfecta

stem: A 6-year-old with type III osteogenesis imperfecta has recurrent femoral fractures and progressive bowing despite cyclical intravenous pamidronate. The team plans surgical realignment. Which statement best reflects current principles of managing the deformity and the role of bisphosphonates?

options.label: A
options.text: Bisphosphonates cure OI by restoring normal collagen structure, so surgery should be deferred indefinitely

options.label: B
options.text: Definitive fixation should use a rigidly locked plate spanning the diaphysis to neutralise all bending forces

options.label: C
options.text: Multiple osteotomies with realignment over a telescoping (growing) intramedullary rod is preferred, and long-term bisphosphonate therapy can impair osteotomy healing if surgery is poorly timed

options.label: D
options.text: Bowing should be corrected with serial casting alone, as osteotomy is contraindicated in OI

options.label: E
options.text: A single mid-shaft osteotomy fixed with lag screws gives durable correction because OI bone holds screws well

explanation: Surgery for deforming OI follows the Sofield–Millar principle: multiple osteotomies ('fragmentation') to correct the bow, realigned over an intramedullary rod. Telescoping/expandable rods (e.g. Fassier–Duval) are favoured because they elongate with growth and reduce reoperation. Load-sharing intramedullary devices suit the fragile bone far better than rigid plating, which creates stress risers and screw pull-out in osteoporotic OI bone and predisposes to peri-implant fracture. Bisphosphonates (pamidronate/zoledronate) reduce fracture rate, pain and vertebral collapse and improve BMD, but they do not correct the collagen defect; by suppressing osteoclastic remodelling they can delay osteotomy union, so dosing is generally interrupted around planned osteotomies. Casting alone will not correct established structural bowing.
learningPoint: Deforming OI is treated by multiple osteotomies over a telescoping IM rod (load-sharing, not rigid plating); bisphosphonates reduce fractures but do not fix collagen and can impair osteotomy healing.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pediatric Orthopaedics: OI surgical management

stem: An 18-month-old, exclusively breastfed child with limited sun exposure presents with bow legs, delayed walking, a 'rachitic rosary' and wrist swelling. Radiographs show physeal widening with cupping and fraying of the metaphyses. Which biochemical pattern is most typical of nutritional (vitamin-D-deficiency) rickets?

options.label: A
options.text: High calcium, high phosphate, low alkaline phosphatase, high 25-OH vitamin D

options.label: B
options.text: Low/normal calcium, low phosphate, high alkaline phosphatase, low 25-OH vitamin D, high PTH

options.label: C
options.text: Low calcium, high phosphate, low PTH, normal vitamin D

options.label: D
options.text: Normal calcium, low phosphate, normal alkaline phosphatase, low PTH (renal phosphate wasting)

options.label: E
options.text: High calcium, low phosphate, high 1,25-dihydroxy vitamin D with suppressed PTH

explanation: Nutritional rickets reflects vitamin D deficiency: reduced intestinal calcium absorption lowers serum calcium, triggering secondary hyperparathyroidism. PTH maintains calcium (so calcium is low-normal) but promotes renal phosphate wasting, giving hypophosphataemia; impaired mineralisation markedly raises alkaline phosphatase, and 25-OH vitamin D is low. The radiographic hallmarks are physeal widening with metaphyseal cupping/fraying, costochondral rosary and genu varum/valgum. Treatment is vitamin D (ergocalciferol/colecalciferol) plus adequate calcium; UK Chief Medical Officers recommend routine vitamin D supplementation for breastfed infants. Option D describes the low-PTH renal phosphate-wasting pattern of X-linked hypophosphataemic rickets, which is the key contrast.
learningPoint: Nutritional rickets: low/normal Ca, low phosphate, high ALP, low 25-OH vitamin D and high PTH — secondary hyperparathyroidism drives the phosphate wasting.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pediatric Orthopaedics: metabolic bone disease
UK CMO / NICE — vitamin D supplementation in infants

subtopic: Metabolic bone disease — X-linked hypophosphataemic rickets
stem: A 3-year-old presents with progressive genu varum and short stature. Bloods show normal calcium, low phosphate, raised alkaline phosphatase, normal 25-OH vitamin D and an inappropriately normal PTH. The condition is X-linked dominant. Which mechanism underlies this disorder and which agent specifically targets it?

options.label: A
options.text: Dietary vitamin D deficiency, corrected by colecalciferol alone

options.label: B
options.text: PHEX mutation causing excess FGF23, which drives renal phosphate wasting; burosumab (anti-FGF23 antibody) targets it

options.label: C
options.text: Renal tubular acidosis causing bicarbonate loss, corrected with citrate

options.label: D
options.text: 1-alpha-hydroxylase deficiency, corrected with alfacalcidol replacement only

options.label: E
options.text: Vitamin D receptor resistance with alopecia, requiring high-dose calcium infusions

explanation: X-linked hypophosphataemic rickets (XLH) is the commonest heritable rickets.
