Loss-of-function PHEX mutations lead to elevated circulating FGF23, a phosphatonin that downregulates renal phosphate reabsorption (reduced NaPi cotransporters) and suppresses 1-alpha-hydroxylase, so phosphate is low with inappropriately normal/low 1,25-vitamin D and a non-elevated PTH — distinguishing it from nutritional rickets. Conventional therapy is oral phosphate plus active vitamin D (alfacalcidol/calcitriol), but this is burdensome and risks nephrocalcinosis and tertiary hyperparathyroidism. Burosumab, a recombinant anti-FGF23 monoclonal antibody, directly neutralises the excess FGF23, raises phosphate and improves rickets and growth, and is NICE-recommended for paediatric XLH. Orthopaedic care addresses residual deformity with guided growth or osteotomy once disease is controlled.
learningPoint: XLH = PHEX mutation → excess FGF23 → renal phosphate wasting with normal calcium and non-raised PTH; burosumab (anti-FGF23 antibody) is the targeted therapy.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pediatric Orthopaedics: hypophosphataemic rickets
NICE TA — burosumab for X-linked hypophosphataemia

stem: A 4-year-old boy is referred with toe-walking, frequent falls, calf pseudohypertrophy and a Gowers sign. Creatine kinase is markedly elevated. Which statement best reflects current understanding and management of the most likely diagnosis?

options.label: A
options.text: It is autosomal recessive; tendo-Achilles lengthening in early childhood prevents loss of ambulation

options.label: B
options.text: It is an X-linked dystrophinopathy; corticosteroids prolong ambulation, and scoliosis risk rises sharply after wheelchair dependence

options.label: C
options.text: Diagnosis requires muscle biopsy because genetic testing is unreliable in dystrophinopathies

options.label: D
options.text: Cardiomyopathy is rare, so cardiac surveillance is unnecessary

options.label: E
options.text: Spinal fusion is contraindicated because of anaesthetic risk, so bracing is definitive for the curve

explanation: Duchenne muscular dystrophy is an X-linked recessive dystrophinopathy (Xp21 DMD gene; frameshift mutations abolishing dystrophin). It presents in boys around 3–5 years with proximal weakness, Gowers sign, calf pseudohypertrophy and very high CK; diagnosis is confirmed by genetic testing (deletion/duplication then sequencing), with biopsy reserved for ambiguous cases. Glucocorticoids (prednisolone or deflazacort) are the mainstay and prolong ambulation, delay scoliosis and preserve respiratory/cardiac function. A rapidly progressive neuromuscular scoliosis typically develops after loss of independent walking, often warranting posterior spinal fusion to the pelvis, ideally before severe curve/respiratory decline. Dilated cardiomyopathy is common and mandates cardiac (ACE inhibitor/echo) surveillance. Equinus is managed within a global programme, not by isolated early TAL.
learningPoint: DMD is X-linked loss of dystrophin (high CK, Gowers sign); steroids prolong ambulation, and neuromuscular scoliosis accelerates once the child becomes wheelchair-dependent.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pediatric Orthopaedics: muscular dystrophy
BSCOS / DMD Care Considerations — orthopaedic and spinal management

stem: A 15-year-old with hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy (Charcot-Marie-Tooth) has a progressive cavovarus foot, clawing of the toes and difficulty with uneven ground. A Coleman block test shows the hindfoot varus corrects to neutral when the first ray is allowed to drop off the block. Which statement best explains the deformity and guides surgical planning?

options.label: A
options.text: The hindfoot varus is fixed, so a calcaneal osteotomy alone is sufficient and soft-tissue balancing is unnecessary

options.label: B
options.text: Weak peroneus brevis with relatively preserved tibialis posterior, and plantarflexed first ray from strong peroneus longus against weak tibialis anterior, drives a forefoot-driven hindfoot varus that is still flexible — so a first-metatarsal dorsiflexion osteotomy with tendon transfers is appropriate

options.label: C
options.text: The cavus is purely neurogenic claw-toe deformity and correcting the toes alone will realign the hindfoot

options.label: D
options.text: A triple arthrodesis is the first-line procedure in all adolescents with CMT cavovarus

options.label: E
options.text: The deformity is caused by spasticity, so botulinum toxin to gastrocnemius is the definitive treatment

explanation: CMT cavovarus arises from a predictable muscle imbalance: peroneus longus overpowers a weak tibialis anterior (plantarflexing/pronating the first ray) while tibialis posterior overpowers a weak peroneus brevis (adducting/inverting the hindfoot), with intrinsic weakness producing claw toes. The plantarflexed first ray pronates the forefoot; to keep the foot plantigrade the hindfoot compensates into varus — a 'forefoot-driven' hindfoot varus. A positive Coleman block test (hindfoot corrects when the first ray drops off the block) confirms the hindfoot is still flexible and the deformity forefoot-driven, so joint-sparing surgery is indicated: dorsiflexion (closing-wedge) osteotomy of the first metatarsal, plantar fascia release, and tendon transfers (e.g. peroneus longus-to-brevis, tibialis posterior transfer) ± calcaneal osteotomy. Triple arthrodesis is reserved for a fixed, rigid hindfoot, not first-line in a flexible adolescent foot. CMT is flaccid, not spastic.
learningPoint: CMT cavovarus is forefoot-driven (strong peroneus longus/tibialis posterior vs weak tibialis anterior/peroneus brevis); a correcting Coleman block test signals a flexible hindfoot suitable for joint-sparing osteotomy and tendon transfers, not arthrodesis.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pediatric Orthopaedics: cavovarus / neuromuscular foot

subtopic: Paediatric septic arthritis — Kocher criteria & management
stem: A 5-year-old refuses to weight-bear and holds the right hip flexed, abducted and externally rotated. Temperature is 38.6°C, CRP 65 mg/L, ESR 50 mm/hr and WCC 13×10⁹/L; ultrasound shows a hip effusion. After resuscitation and blood cultures, which is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Discharge with oral antibiotics and review in 48 hours

options.label: B
options.text: MRI first and defer any intervention until the scan is reported

options.label: C
options.text: Urgent ultrasound-guided aspiration ± arthrotomy and washout of the hip, with synovial fluid sent for urgent Gram stain, culture and cell count before/with starting empirical IV antibiotics

options.label: D
options.text: A short course of NSAIDs alone, as transient synovitis is most likely

options.label: E
options.text: Immediate hip spica casting to rest the joint

explanation: This child meets all four Kocher predictors (non-weight-bearing, temperature >38.5°C, ESR >40, WCC >12×10⁹/L — CRP >20 is an additional strong predictor), giving a very high probability of septic arthritis of the hip. Septic arthritis is a surgical emergency: pus and bacterial enzymes destroy articular cartilage within hours and the hip is at risk of avascular necrosis and chondrolysis. The correct pathway is urgent joint aspiration to confirm (Gram stain, culture, cell count typically >50,000 with neutrophil predominance) followed by arthrotomy/arthroscopic washout, and empirical IV antibiotics (after cultures) covering Staphylococcus aureus and, in the relevant age/immunisation context, Kingella kingae. Delaying for MRI, treating as transient synovitis, or oral antibiotics with discharge would risk irreversible joint destruction. Spica casting plays no role in acute decompression.
learningPoint: Hip septic arthritis is a surgical emergency — aspirate to confirm, then washout and IV antibiotics; the Kocher criteria (plus CRP) stratify it from transient synovitis.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pediatric Orthopaedics: septic arthritis
BSCOS / BOAST — paediatric bone and joint infection

stem: A previously well 7-year-old has 4 days of fever and refusal to bear weight, with focal tenderness over the distal femoral metaphysis and CRP 80 mg/L. Acute haematogenous osteomyelitis is suspected. Which statement best reflects current UK practice for the pathophysiology and treatment of this condition?

options.label: A
options.text: The metaphysis is involved because sluggish flow in metaphyseal sinusoidal loops favours bacterial seeding; Staphylococcus aureus is the commonest organism, and early switch to oral antibiotics is appropriate once the child is improving

options.label: B
options.text: Pseudomonas is the commonest organism in healthy children, so empirical anti-pseudomonal cover is mandatory

options.label: C
options.text: Prolonged 6-week intravenous antibiotic courses are always required and oral switch is contraindicated

options.label: D
options.text: Plain radiographs reliably confirm the diagnosis within the first 48 hours

options.label: E
options.text: In neonates the physis is an absolute barrier, so septic arthritis never coexists with metaphyseal osteomyelitis

explanation: Acute haematogenous osteomyelitis in children classically localises to the metaphysis of long bones because the metaphyseal capillaries form sharp sinusoidal loops with sluggish, turbulent flow and relatively deficient phagocytosis, favouring bacterial lodgement. Staphylococcus aureus is by far the commonest pathogen across ages (with Kingella kingae important in young children and group B strep/Gram-negatives in neonates). Contemporary BSCOS/BOAST guidance and randomised evidence support a relatively short intravenous course with early switch to oral antibiotics once the child is clinically improving and inflammatory markers are falling, provided source control is achieved — not routine 6-week IV therapy. Plain films are typically normal for 7–14 days (MRI is the sensitive early test). In infants under ~18 months, transphyseal vessels persist, so metaphyseal infection can readily spread to the joint (concurrent septic arthritis), and in metaphyses that are intra-articular (e.g. proximal femur) this occurs at any age.
learningPoint: Childhood osteomyelitis seeds the metaphysis (sluggish sinusoidal loops); S. aureus predominates, MRI is the early imaging test, and an early IV-to-oral switch is supported once the child improves.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pediatric Orthopaedics: osteomyelitis
BSCOS / BOAST — bone and joint infection in children

stem: A 4-month-old, not yet mobile, is brought in after 'rolling off a sofa'. Skeletal survey shows a metaphyseal corner (bucket-handle) fracture of the distal femur and several posterior rib fractures of differing ages. Which statement best reflects the correct interpretation and response?

options.label: A
options.text: Posterior rib fractures in infants usually result from cardiopulmonary resuscitation and are reassuring

options.label: B
options.text: Classic metaphyseal lesions and posterior rib fractures of differing ages are highly specific for non-accidental injury; this needs urgent child-protection escalation, and underlying conditions such as osteogenesis imperfecta must also be excluded

options.label: C
options.text: A single skeletal survey at presentation is sufficient and a repeat survey is never indicated

options.label: D
options.text: A spiral femoral fracture in a non-ambulant infant is a normal consequence of nappy changing

options.label: E
options.text: Treatment of the fractures takes priority and safeguarding can be deferred to outpatient follow-up

explanation: Classic metaphyseal lesions (corner/bucket-handle fractures) arise from shearing/torsional forces and, together with posterior/posteromedial rib fractures, fractures of differing ages, and any significant fracture in a non-ambulant infant, carry high specificity for inflicted (non-accidental) injury. The history of a low fall is implausible for these findings, and the absence of independent mobility is a major red flag. The correct response is immediate multidisciplinary safeguarding escalation per UK child-protection pathways while treating the injuries; medical concern must not be deferred. Differential diagnoses — particularly osteogenesis imperfecta, but also rickets/metabolic bone disease and birth trauma — must be actively excluded so neither abuse nor a genuine bone disorder is missed. CPR causes anterior, not posterior, rib fractures and only rarely. A repeat skeletal survey at ~11–14 days is recommended to detect healing fractures not visible initially.
learningPoint: Metaphyseal corner lesions, posterior rib fractures and fractures of differing ages in a non-mobile infant are high-specificity NAI markers — escalate safeguarding immediately and exclude OI/metabolic bone disease.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pediatric Orthopaedics: child abuse
RCPCH / NICE NG76 — child maltreatment recognition; BSCOS safeguarding

stem: When assessing a possible inflicted skeletal injury in an infant, which single fracture pattern carries the LOWEST specificity for non-accidental injury and is therefore least useful in isolation as evidence of abuse?

options.label: A
options.text: Bilateral posterior rib fractures of differing ages

options.label: B
options.text: Classic metaphyseal lesion (bucket-handle fracture)

options.label: C
options.text: Sternal and spinous process fractures

options.label: D
options.text: A single, linear, undisplaced parietal skull fracture in a mobile toddler with a witnessed fall

options.label: E
options.text: Scapular fracture in an infant

explanation: Specificity for NAI is graded by pattern. High-specificity injuries include classic metaphyseal lesions, posterior/posteromedial rib fractures, and fractures of the scapula, sternum and spinous processes — sites rarely injured by ordinary handling and requiring unusual forces. By contrast, a simple linear, non-diastatic parietal/skull fracture, clavicle and long-bone shaft fractures are common accidental injuries; in isolation, and especially with a consistent witnessed mechanism in a mobile child, such a fracture has low specificity for abuse. The point examined is that no single 'low-specificity' fracture proves or excludes NAI — interpretation must integrate the developmental stage of the child, the plausibility and consistency of the history, the presence of multiple/differently-aged injuries, and exclusion of bone fragility disorders, within a formal safeguarding assessment.
learningPoint: Metaphyseal, posterior rib, scapular, sternal and spinous-process fractures are high-specificity for NAI; an isolated simple skull fracture with a consistent witnessed mechanism is low-specificity and non-diagnostic alone.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pediatric Orthopaedics: non-accidental injury
RCPCH child protection evidence reviews; NICE NG76

subtopic: Paediatric bone tumour — osteosarcoma presentation & staging
stem: A 14-year-old presents with several weeks of progressive knee pain and a firm, tender swelling around the distal femur, worse at night. Radiographs show an aggressive metaphyseal lesion with a mixed lytic-sclerotic pattern, periosteal sunburst and a Codman triangle. Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.label: A
options.text: Excisional biopsy of the lump in the local hospital to achieve diagnosis and treatment together

options.label: B
options.text: Reassure as likely growing pains and review in 3 months

options.label: C
options.text: Urgent referral to a specialist bone-tumour (sarcoma) centre for staging (MRI of the whole bone, CT chest, bone scan/PET) and a planned biopsy performed by the treating surgical team

options.label: D
options.text: Immediate above-knee amputation to control the tumour

options.label: E
options.text: Start empirical antibiotics for presumed osteomyelitis and arrange surgical washout

explanation: Aggressive metaphyseal lesions around the knee in an adolescent with sunburst spiculation and a Codman triangle are highly suggestive of osteosarcoma. The cardinal principle is that such lesions must be referred, staged and biopsied at a specialist sarcoma centre, with the biopsy planned and ideally performed by the team that will deliver definitive surgery — a poorly placed or contaminating biopsy can compromise later limb salvage and worsen outcome. Staging includes MRI of the entire involved bone (to define extent and skip lesions), CT chest (lungs are the commonest metastatic site) and a bone scan or PET. Modern treatment is neoadjuvant chemotherapy, wide local excision with limb salvage where feasible, and adjuvant chemotherapy — not primary amputation or local excisional biopsy. Infection is a differential but the imaging and clinical picture mandate sarcoma work-up first.
learningPoint: Suspected osteosarcoma must go to a sarcoma centre for full staging and a surgeon-planned biopsy before any intervention — a contaminating local biopsy can jeopardise limb salvage.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Musculoskeletal Oncology: osteosarcoma
BOOS / NICE — sarcoma referral and biopsy principles

subtopic: Paediatric bone tumour — benign lesion characterisation
stem: An 11-year-old with night pain in the proximal femur that is dramatically relieved by NSAIDs has a small (<1.5 cm) cortical lucent nidus surrounded by dense reactive sclerosis on CT. Which lesion is most likely and what is the typical first-line definitive treatment?

options.label: A
options.text: Osteoid osteoma — CT-guided radiofrequency ablation

options.label: B
options.text: Ewing sarcoma — neoadjuvant chemotherapy and wide excision

options.label: C
options.text: Unicameral (simple) bone cyst — curettage and bone grafting

options.label: D
options.text: Osteochondroma — observation with malignant-transformation surveillance

options.label: E
options.text: Chondroblastoma — en bloc resection of the epiphysis

explanation: The picture is classic osteoid osteoma: a benign osteoblastic lesion of children/young adults producing well-localised night pain characteristically and disproportionately relieved by NSAIDs (prostaglandin-mediated). CT shows a small radiolucent nidus (<1.5–2 cm) surrounded by dense reactive cortical sclerosis. Many resolve with NSAIDs over time, but when treatment is needed the standard definitive option is CT-guided percutaneous radiofrequency (thermal) ablation, which is minimally invasive and highly effective; open excision is reserved for sites unsafe for ablation (e.g. close to the spinal cord). The distractors are distinguishable: Ewing sarcoma is an aggressive diaphyseal lesion with a permeative pattern and onion-skin periosteal reaction; a simple bone cyst is a central metaphyseal lucency presenting with pathological fracture; osteochondroma is a surface exostosis with cortical/medullary continuity; chondroblastoma is an epiphyseal lytic lesion.
learningPoint: Osteoid osteoma = small radiolucent nidus with reactive sclerosis and NSAID-responsive night pain; first-line definitive treatment is CT-guided radiofrequency ablation.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Musculoskeletal Oncology: benign bone tumours
BOOS — management of benign bone lesions

stem: A neonate is born with multiple, symmetric, non-progressive joint contractures affecting all four limbs — typically internally rotated/adducted shoulders, extended elbows, flexed/ulnarly deviated wrists, and rigid bilateral clubfeet — with featureless, cylindrical limbs but normal cognition. Which statement best characterises this condition and its management?

options.label: A
options.text: It is a single progressive genetic muscle disease that worsens throughout childhood

options.label: B
options.text: Amyoplasia is the commonest form of arthrogryposis multiplex congenita; the contractures are non-progressive, and early physiotherapy/serial casting with selectively timed surgery aims to maximise function and independence

options.label: C
options.text: Because joints are rigid, no role exists for physiotherapy or stretching

options.label: D
options.text: Intelligence is typically severely impaired, which limits rehabilitation goals

options.label: E
options.text: The clubfeet are usually supple and correct fully with a single Ponseti cast

explanation: Arthrogryposis multiplex congenita is a descriptive term for multiple congenital joint contractures present at birth, resulting from reduced fetal movement (akinesia) of any cause. Amyoplasia is the classic and commonest form, with symmetric involvement, the characteristic limb posture described, absent/replaced muscle giving featureless tubular limbs, and — importantly — normal intelligence. The contractures are non-progressive, which underpins an optimistic, function-focused approach: early and ongoing physiotherapy and stretching, serial casting/splinting, and selectively timed surgery (e.g. for resistant clubfoot, hip/knee contractures, to improve hand position or enable ambulation) to maximise independence. Arthrogrypotic clubfeet are typically rigid and recalcitrant; while Ponseti casting is still used first-line, they often need more casts, tenotomy and a higher relapse/surgery rate than idiopathic clubfoot. The deformities do not progress and cognition is usually preserved.
learningPoint: Amyoplasia is the commonest arthrogryposis — symmetric, non-progressive contractures with normal cognition; treat with early physiotherapy, casting and selectively timed surgery to maximise function.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pediatric Orthopaedics: arthrogryposis
BSCOS — paediatric limb deformity / clubfoot principles

subtopic: Paediatric metabolic & dysplastic bone — diagnosis matching
stem: Theme: Diagnosis of paediatric metabolic and dysplastic bone disorders. For each clinical/biochemical/genetic vignette, select the SINGLE most likely diagnosis from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Nutritional (vitamin D deficiency) rickets

options.label: B
options.text: X-linked hypophosphataemic rickets

options.label: C
options.text: Multiple epiphyseal dysplasia (MED)

options.label: D
options.text: Spondyloepiphyseal dysplasia congenita (SED congenita)

options.label: E
options.text: Osteopetrosis

options.label: F
options.text: Hypophosphatasia

options.label: G
options.text: Achondroplasia

options.label: H
options.text: Osteogenesis imperfecta type I

emiLeads.stem: A 6-year-old has bilateral hip and knee pain with a waddling gait and mild short stature. Radiographs show small, fragmented, irregular epiphyses at multiple joints with a normal spine. A defect in cartilage oligomeric matrix protein (COMP) is identified, and a parent is similarly affected.

A short-statured child has disproportionate truncal shortening present from birth, a barrel chest, flat midface, myopia with retinal detachment risk, odontoid hypoplasia with atlantoaxial instability, and flattened vertebral bodies (platyspondyly). A type II collagen (COL2A1) defect is confirmed.
A toddler has bowing, a rachitic appearance and recurrent fractures, with very low alkaline phosphatase, elevated urinary phosphoethanolamine and premature loss of deciduous teeth.

An infant presents with frequent fractures, dense 'bone-within-bone' sclerotic radiographs, anaemia and thrombocytopenia from marrow crowding, and cranial-nerve palsies; the defect impairs osteoclast function.

A 3-year-old has progressive genu varum and short stature with normal calcium, low phosphate, raised alkaline phosphatase, normal 25-OH vitamin D and a non-elevated PTH; the trait is X-linked dominant.

explanation: MED (C) is an epiphyseal dysplasia from COMP (or type IX collagen/matrilin-3) defects: small, fragmented, irregular epiphyses at multiple joints with a relatively spared spine, joint pain, waddling gait and early osteoarthritis — contrast with SED. SED congenita (D) is a COL2A1 (type II collagen) disorder with congenital, predominantly truncal/spinal shortening, platyspondyly, barrel chest, midface hypoplasia, high myopia/retinal detachment and — critically for the orthopaedic surgeon/anaesthetist — odontoid hypoplasia with atlantoaxial instability requiring cervical-spine assessment. Hypophosphatasia (F) is distinguished by a paradoxically LOW alkaline phosphatase (deficient tissue-nonspecific ALP) with raised substrates (phosphoethanolamine, pyridoxal-5'-phosphate) and early tooth loss. Osteopetrosis (E) is failure of osteoclastic resorption giving dense, brittle 'bone-within-bone' sclerosis, marrow failure (pancytopenia) and cranial-nerve compression. X-linked hypophosphataemia (B) shows renal phosphate wasting — low phosphate with normal calcium and non-elevated PTH — versus nutritional rickets (A), which has secondary hyperparathyroidism (low/normal calcium, high PTH, low vitamin D).
learningPoint: MED = COMP, multiple fragmented epiphyses with a spared spine; SED congenita = COL2A1 with truncal shortening and atlantoaxial instability; low ALP flags hypophosphatasia; sclerotic 'bone-within-bone' with marrow failure flags osteopetrosis.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pediatric Orthopaedics: skeletal dysplasias and metabolic bone disease
BSCOS — skeletal dysplasia and metabolic bone disorder pathways

stem: Theme: Diagnosis of paediatric neuromuscular disorders. For each presentation select the SINGLE most likely diagnosis from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Duchenne muscular dystrophy

options.label: B
options.text: Becker muscular dystrophy

options.label: C
options.text: Spinal muscular atrophy (SMA)

options.label: D
options.text: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (HMSN)

options.label: E
options.text: Cerebral palsy (spastic diplegia)

options.label: F
options.text: Friedreich ataxia

options.label: G
options.text: Myelomeningocele (spina bifida)

emiLeads.stem: A floppy infant has profound symmetric proximal weakness, areflexia, tongue fasciculations and a paradoxical 'bell-shaped' chest with preserved alertness. The cause is degeneration of anterior horn cells due to a homozygous SMN1 deletion. A rapidly progressive collapsing neuromuscular scoliosis is anticipated.

A 4-year-old boy has proximal weakness, calf pseudohypertrophy, a Gowers sign and markedly raised creatine kinase, with absent dystrophin on testing. He is expected to lose independent ambulation around 10–12 years and to develop scoliosis thereafter.

A 13-year-old has a slowly progressive cavovarus foot, claw toes, distal wasting ('stork legs'), absent ankle reflexes and impaired proprioception/vibration, with a positive family history of a length-dependent peripheral neuropathy.

A child has an upper-motor-neuron pattern with bilateral lower-limb spasticity, brisk reflexes, a crouch/scissoring gait and a history of prematurity; the static brain insult also predisposes to hip subluxation requiring surveillance.

An adolescent presents with progressive ataxia, dysarthria, pes cavus, scoliosis and absent lower-limb reflexes with extensor plantars, plus a hypertrophic cardiomyopathy and diabetes; the cause is a GAA trinucleotide repeat expansion in the frataxin gene.

explanation: SMA (C) is an anterior-horn-cell (lower motor neuron) degeneration from homozygous SMN1 loss: a hypotonic, areflexic infant with proximal weakness, tongue fasciculations, intercostal weakness with diaphragmatic sparing (bell-shaped chest) and preserved cognition; severe early-onset forms drive a long collapsing neuromuscular scoliosis (disease-modifying therapies such as nusinersen/onasemnogene now alter the natural history). DMD (A) is the X-linked dystrophinopathy with pseudohypertrophy, Gowers sign and very high CK and loss of ambulation in the early teens. CMT/HMSN (D) is a hereditary peripheral neuropathy producing length-dependent distal wasting, cavovarus feet and lost ankle jerks with sensory loss. Spastic CP (E) is an upper-motor-neuron disorder from a static perinatal brain injury, with spasticity, hyper-reflexia, gait abnormality and a high risk of hip displacement (hence hip surveillance programmes). Friedreich ataxia (F) is an autosomal recessive GAA-repeat frataxin disorder combining spinocerebellar degeneration (ataxia, areflexia with extensor plantars), pes cavus and scoliosis with cardiomyopathy and diabetes. Distinguishing lower-motor-neuron/flaccid (SMA, CMT) from upper-motor-neuron/spastic (CP, Friedreich's mixed picture) patterns is the examiner's core discriminator.
learningPoint: Localise the lesion: anterior horn cell (SMA — areflexia, fasciculations), muscle (DMD — high CK, pseudohypertrophy), peripheral nerve (CMT — distal cavovarus, lost ankle jerks), or UMN (spastic CP — spasticity, hip surveillance).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pediatric Orthopaedics: neuromuscular disorders
BSCOS — neuromuscular scoliosis and hip surveillance

stem: A 38-year-old man sustains a closed midshaft femoral fracture and bilateral rib fractures with a small pulmonary contusion in a road traffic collision. Twelve hours after admission his lactate has fallen from 4.8 to 1.9 mmol/L, base excess is -2, he is normothermic, and his platelet count and INR are normal off vasopressors. Using the principles that distinguish damage-control orthopaedics from early appropriate care, which course of action is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Apply a spanning external fixator only, on the principle that every polytrauma patient should be managed by damage control

options.label: B
options.text: Apply a spanning external fixator and delay definitive fixation, because the pulmonary contusion makes intramedullary nailing unsafe at this stage

options.label: C
options.text: Proceed with definitive intramedullary nailing, as the trend in physiological markers indicates a resuscitated, stable patient suitable for early appropriate care

options.label: D
options.text: Delay all surgery for 5 days until the pulmonary contusion has fully resolved

options.label: E
options.text: Perform skeletal traction alone and reassess at 2 weeks

explanation: The decision between damage-control orthopaedics (temporising external fixation) and early definitive fixation is driven by the patient's physiological status, not a blanket rule. Normalising lactate (<2.0 mmol/L), a corrected base deficit, normothermia and intact coagulation define a 'stable' or successfully resuscitated patient in whom early definitive fixation ('early appropriate care') is safe and reduces complications. Damage control is reserved for the unstable or 'in extremis' physiology (persistent acidosis, coagulopathy, hypothermia). A small contusion that is not deteriorating, in an otherwise resuscitated patient, does not mandate delay.
learningPoint: DCO vs early appropriate care is a physiology-led decision: normalising lactate/base deficit, normothermia and normal coagulation flag a resuscitated patient suitable for early definitive fixation.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Polytrauma/Damage Control
BOAST: The Management of the Severely Injured Patient

stem: A 45-year-old woman presents 50 minutes after an open tibial fracture sustained in a fall from a horse. The wound is 4 cm long with moderate contamination but no vascular compromise. According to the current BOAST 'Open Fractures' standard, what is the single most important antibiotic-related action in the emergency department?

options.label: A
options.text: Withhold antibiotics until the limb has been formally debrided in theatre

options.label: B
options.text: Administer intravenous co-amoxiclav (or a cephalosporin) as soon as possible, ideally within 1 hour of injury

options.label: C
options.text: Give a single dose of oral flucloxacillin and arrange outpatient review

options.label: D
options.text: Administer high-dose gentamicin alone as monotherapy

options.label: E
options.text: Start antibiotics only once microbiology swab results are available

explanation: The current UK standard (BOAST/NICE NG38) is that intravenous antibiotics should be given as soon as possible and within 1 hour of injury for open fractures - co-amoxiclav or a cephalosporin is the usual first-line agent, continued until definitive soft-tissue closure (with the addition of an agent such as gentamicin at the time of debridement and closure). Antibiotics must not await theatre or swab results, and oral monotherapy is inadequate. Wound swabs taken in the ED are unhelpful and not recommended.
learningPoint: Open fracture = IV co-amoxiclav/cephalosporin within 1 hour of injury - do not wait for theatre, swabs or culture results.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Open Fractures

stem: A patient sustains an open tibial fracture from a farmyard injury. At debridement there is an 8 cm wound, gross contamination with soil, and after thorough excision of devitalised tissue an area of segmental periosteal stripping remains that will require a free flap; the posterior tibial artery is intact on exploration. Which Gustilo-Anderson grade most accurately describes this injury, and what is the key implication for timing of debridement?

options.label: A
options.text: Grade II; debridement is not urgent and can wait 72 hours

options.label: B
options.text: Grade IIIA; soft-tissue cover is not required

options.label: C
options.text: Grade IIIB; debridement of gross contamination should be expedited (within 12-24 hours on a planned list), with combined ortho-plastic soft-tissue cover ideally within 72 hours

options.label: D
options.text: Grade IIIC; immediate amputation is indicated

options.label: E
options.text: Grade I; primary closure in the emergency department is appropriate

explanation: Extensive soft-tissue loss with periosteal stripping requiring flap coverage, in the presence of an intact vascular supply, defines Gustilo-Anderson IIIB (IIIC requires an arterial injury needing repair). Current UK practice (BOAST) no longer mandates the historic 6-hour rule for all wounds; instead, grossly contaminated agricultural or marine wounds and ischaemic limbs warrant expedited debridement (often within 12-24 hours on a planned, daytime ortho-plastic list), with definitive soft-tissue cover ideally within 72 hours. Gross contamination is the driver for earlier surgery, not an arbitrary 6-hour cut-off.
learningPoint: Flap-requiring soft-tissue loss with intact vessels = Gustilo IIIB; expedite debridement for gross/agricultural contamination and aim for definitive ortho-plastic cover within 72 hours.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Open Fractures (Gustilo-Anderson)

stem: A 19-year-old man has a closed tibial shaft fracture treated in a backslab. Four hours later he reports escalating pain unrelieved by morphine and severe pain on passive dorsiflexion of the toes. Distal pulses are present and palpable and the calf is tense. The on-call team measures a continuous intra-compartmental pressure of 42 mmHg; the patient's diastolic blood pressure is 70 mmHg. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Reassure, elevate the limb above the heart and re-examine in 2 hours

options.label: B
options.text: Proceed to urgent four-compartment fasciotomy of the leg

options.label: C
options.text: Remove the backslab, place the limb at the level of the heart, and continue clinical monitoring only as the absolute pressure is below 45 mmHg

options.label: D
options.text: Order urgent CT angiography of the lower limb before any intervention

options.label: E
options.text: Give intravenous mannitol and repeat the pressure in 30 minutes

explanation: The diagnosis of acute compartment syndrome is primarily clinical: disproportionate pain and pain on passive stretch are the earliest and most reliable signs, and pulses are typically preserved until very late so their presence does NOT exclude it. When measured, a delta pressure (diastolic minus compartment pressure) of <30 mmHg is the accepted threshold for decompression. Here the delta pressure is 70 - 42 = 28 mmHg, below 30, in a symptomatic patient - urgent four-compartment fasciotomy is indicated. Splint release and elevation to the level of the heart are adjuncts but must not delay decompression once the diagnosis is made.
learningPoint: Use delta pressure (diastolic minus compartment) <30 mmHg, not an absolute value - and never rely on pulses, which persist until late.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Compartment Syndrome
BOAST: Diagnosis and Management of Compartment Syndrome of the Limbs

stem: An obtunded, intubated polytrauma patient in the intensive care unit has a closed tibial plateau fracture managed in a spanning external fixator. The trauma team is concerned about missed acute compartment syndrome because the patient cannot report pain. Which approach is most consistent with current UK guidance for monitoring this high-risk, unconscious patient?

options.label: A
options.text: Rely solely on twice-daily palpation of the calf by the nursing staff

options.label: B
options.text: Use continuous intra-compartmental pressure monitoring and decompress if the delta pressure remains below 30 mmHg

options.label: C
options.text: Accept that compartment syndrome cannot be diagnosed in an unconscious patient and take no specific action

options.label: D
options.text: Wait for a rise in serum creatine kinase above 5000 U/L before intervening

options.label: E
options.text: Perform prophylactic fasciotomy on every unconscious patient with a tibial fracture regardless of pressures

explanation: In the obtunded or anaesthetised patient the cardinal clinical signs (pain, pain on passive stretch) are unobtainable, so BOAST recommends a high index of suspicion combined with continuous intra-compartmental pressure monitoring in those deemed at risk. A sustained delta pressure (diastolic minus compartment pressure) below 30 mmHg warrants urgent fasciotomy. Intermittent palpation is unreliable, a rise in CK is a late marker of established muscle necrosis, and blanket prophylactic fasciotomy is not justified.
learningPoint: In unconscious at-risk patients, substitute continuous compartment pressure monitoring (delta <30 mmHg = decompress) for the unobtainable clinical signs.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Compartment Syndrome
BOAST: Diagnosis and Management of Compartment Syndrome of the Limbs

stem: A surgeon is planning a locking plate construct to bridge a comminuted distal femoral metaphyseal fracture, aiming for relative stability and secondary bone healing through callus. Which combination of construct adjustments is most likely to optimise healing by producing controlled, axial micromotion at the fracture and avoiding excessively stiff fixation?

options.label: A
options.text: Maximise the number of screws immediately adjacent to the fracture and use the shortest possible plate

options.label: B
options.text: Use a longer plate with screws spread widely, leave the screw holes nearest the gap empty, and increase the bridging working length

options.label: C
options.text: Convert all screws to bicortical lag screws across the fracture to achieve absolute stability

options.label: D
options.text: Use the stiffest available plate and fill every screw hole

options.label: E
options.text: Apply a single short plate eccentrically with unicortical screws only at one end

explanation: For bridge plating of comminuted fractures the goal is relative stability with controlled interfragmentary strain to stimulate callus. Increasing the plate working length (the distance between the innermost screws spanning the fracture) by using a longer plate, spreading the screws, and omitting the holes nearest the gap reduces construct stiffness and distributes strain, lowering peak stress at the bone-screw interface and allowing axial micromotion. Too rigid a construct or a very short working length raises interfragmentary strain locally and suppresses callus, risking non-union or implant failure. Lag screws/absolute stability would suppress the callus this strategy depends upon.
learningPoint: Bridge plating: lengthen the working length (longer plate, spread screws, skip holes by the gap) to lower stiffness and permit the micromotion that drives callus.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Biomechanics/Fracture Fixation

stem: A trainee is comparing two methods of fixing a simple transverse midshaft femoral fracture. They want the construct that produces interfragmentary compression and primary (direct) bone healing without visible callus. Which fixation principle and healing mechanism are correctly paired?

options.label: A
options.text: A bridging external fixator producing relative stability and primary bone healing

options.label: B
options.text: A reamed intramedullary nail producing absolute stability and primary bone healing

options.label: C
options.text: Compression plating producing absolute stability and primary (direct, Haversian) bone healing with little or no callus

options.label: D
options.text: A locked bridge plate producing absolute stability and abundant callus

options.label: E
options.text: Compression plating producing relative stability and secondary bone healing with abundant callus

explanation: Absolute stability, achieved by interfragmentary compression (e.g. a lag screw with a neutralisation plate, or a dynamic compression plate), reduces interfragmentary strain to under about 2% and allows primary/direct bone healing via Haversian remodelling and cutting cones, with little or no visible callus. Intramedullary nails and external fixators confer relative stability and heal by secondary (callus) formation. Pairing compression plating with callus formation, or nailing with absolute stability, is incorrect.
learningPoint: Absolute stability (interfragmentary compression, strain <2%) heals directly without callus; relative stability (nails, bridge plates, ex-fix) heals by callus.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Fracture Healing/Biomechanics

stem: A 22-year-old man with isolated closed femoral and tibial shaft fractures, awaiting theatre, becomes acutely breathless and confused 36 hours after injury. His oxygen saturation is 88% on air, he is tachycardic, and a petechial rash is noted over the conjunctivae, axillae and upper chest. CT pulmonary angiogram shows no large-vessel thrombus. Which statement best reflects the diagnosis and its management?

options.label: A
options.text: This is most likely pulmonary thromboembolism; start therapeutic anticoagulation as the priority

options.label: B
options.text: This is fat embolism syndrome; management is primarily supportive with oxygen and respiratory support, and early fracture stabilisation reduces the embolic load

options.label: C
options.text: This is fat embolism syndrome; high-dose corticosteroids are the established definitive treatment and are mandatory

options.label: D
options.text: This is an anaphylactic reaction; give intramuscular adrenaline

options.label: E
options.text: This is delirium tremens; commence a benzodiazepine regimen

explanation: The classic triad of fat embolism syndrome - respiratory distress (hypoxia), neurological dysfunction (confusion) and a petechial rash in the characteristic distribution - typically appears 24-72 hours after long-bone fractures and is supported here by a negative CTPA for large-vessel thrombus. It is largely a clinical diagnosis (Gurd and Wilson criteria). Treatment is supportive (oxygen, ventilatory support as needed); early stabilisation of long-bone fractures reduces the embolic burden and incidence. Corticosteroids may have a prophylactic role in some studies but are not an established mandatory treatment, and anticoagulation does not treat fat embolism.
learningPoint: Hypoxia + confusion + petechiae 24-72 h after long-bone fracture = fat embolism syndrome; treat supportively and stabilise fractures early - it is not PE.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Fat Embolism Syndrome

stem: A 30-year-old man sustains a through-and-through gunshot wound to the thigh from a low-velocity handgun. There is a small entry and exit wound, no expanding haematoma, palpable distal pulses, and a transverse mid-shaft femoral fracture on radiograph. He is haemodynamically stable. Which management approach is most appropriate for this low-velocity injury?

options.label: A
options.text: Radical wide surgical debridement of the entire wound tract as for a high-velocity injury

options.label: B
options.text: Local wound care (cleaning and superficial debridement of the wound edges), antibiotics and tetanus prophylaxis, with fracture stabilisation as indicated; aggressive tract excision is not routinely required

options.label: C
options.text: Immediate above-knee amputation

options.label: D
options.text: No antibiotics, as low-velocity wounds are sterile

options.label: E
options.text: Mandatory exploratory arteriography and vascular bypass despite normal pulses

explanation: Low-velocity gunshot wounds cause limited cavitation and tissue damage confined largely to the missile path. Stable through-and-through low-velocity injuries with a normal vascular examination are generally managed with local wound care, antibiotics and tetanus prophylaxis, plus appropriate fracture stabilisation - radical excision of the whole tract is reserved for high-velocity, shotgun, or grossly contaminated wounds where temporary cavitation devitalises a large soft-tissue zone. Amputation and mandatory angiography are not indicated with intact perfusion and no hard signs of vascular injury.
learningPoint: Low-velocity gunshot wounds with normal perfusion need local wound care + antibiotics + tetanus, not the radical tract debridement reserved for high-velocity injuries.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Ballistic/Gunshot Injuries

stem: A 60-year-old woman with a closed bimalleolar ankle fracture develops tense blisters over the medial malleolus 48 hours after injury, some clear and fluid-filled and others blood-filled. She is listed for open reduction and internal fixation. Which statement best guides surgical timing and soft-tissue management?

options.label: A
options.text: Operate immediately through the blistered skin, as fracture blisters do not affect wound healing

options.label: B
options.text: Fracture blisters indicate a significant soft-tissue injury; delay definitive surgery until the soft-tissue envelope recovers, and avoid incisions through blistered (especially blood-filled) skin

options.label: C
options.text: Deroof all blisters and apply a tight circumferential dressing, then operate the same day

options.label: D
options.text: Blood-filled blisters are less serious than clear blisters and can be safely incised through

options.label: E
options.text: Proceed straight to definitive plating regardless of skin condition because delay always increases non-union

explanation: Fracture blisters form from cleavage at the dermo-epidermal junction and are a marker of significant soft-tissue injury. Haemorrhagic (blood-filled) blisters indicate deeper, full-thickness epidermal loss and a more compromised dermal base than clear blisters, carrying a higher wound-complication risk. The principle is to respect the soft-tissue envelope: temporise (e.g. splint or spanning ex-fix and elevation) and delay definitive internal fixation until swelling settles and skin re-epithelialises, and to avoid placing incisions directly through blistered skin. This mirrors soft-tissue handling in high-energy pilon and calcaneal fractures.
learningPoint: Fracture blisters (especially blood-filled) signal a damaged soft-tissue envelope - temporise, elevate, and delay definitive fixation rather than cutting through compromised skin.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Soft-tissue injury/Fracture Blisters

stem: Theme: Gustilo-Anderson classification of open fractures. For each clinical description, select the SINGLE most appropriate Gustilo-Anderson grade from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Grade I

options.label: B
options.text: Grade II

options.label: C
options.text: Grade IIIA

options.label: D
options.text: Grade IIIB

options.label: E
options.text: Grade IIIC

explanation: Gustilo-Anderson grading depends on wound size, contamination, soft-tissue injury, periosteal stripping and vascular status. Grade I: wound <1 cm, clean, minimal soft-tissue damage. Grade II: 1-10 cm wound with moderate soft-tissue damage but adequate coverage. Grade IIIA: high-energy or extensive soft-tissue injury but adequate soft-tissue coverage of bone. Grade IIIB: extensive soft-tissue loss with periosteal stripping requiring flap coverage, vessels intact. Grade IIIC: any open fracture with an arterial injury requiring repair, irrespective of wound size. Note that high-energy mechanisms (segmental fractures, gross contamination) are graded type III regardless of wound length.
learningPoint: IIIC is defined by an arterial injury needing repair (any wound size); IIIB by flap-requiring soft-tissue loss with intact vessels.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Open Fractures

emiLeads.stem: A 7 cm wound over an open tibial fracture with moderate muscle bruising; after debridement the bone is fully covered by viable soft tissue and the limb is well perfused.
An open forearm fracture with a 0.5 cm clean puncture wound, minimal soft-tissue injury and no contamination.
A high-energy open tibial fracture with extensive periosteal stripping and a soft-tissue defect that will need a free flap; on exploration the posterior tibial and dorsalis pedis pulses are intact.
An open distal femoral fracture with a cool, pulseless foot; on-table angiography confirms a popliteal arterial transection requiring repair.
A 4 cm wound over an open ankle fracture with moderate contamination that is adequately covered by local soft tissue after debridement, with no neurovascular deficit.

stem: Theme: Classes of haemorrhagic shock (ATLS). For each adult trauma patient's physiological picture, select the SINGLE most appropriate ATLS class of haemorrhagic shock. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Class I

options.label: B
options.text: Class II

options.label: C
options.text: Class III

options.label: D
options.text: Class IV

options.label: E
options.text: Not consistent with haemorrhagic shock

explanation: ATLS classes (approximate blood loss in a 70 kg adult): Class I = up to 15% (<750 mL), normal vitals, minimal anxiety. Class II = 15-30% (750-1500 mL), tachycardia 100-120, narrowed pulse pressure, normal systolic pressure, mild anxiety, slightly reduced urine output. Class III = 30-40% (1500-2000 mL), heart rate 120-140, hypotension, marked tachypnoea, confusion, oliguria. Class IV = >40% (>2000 mL), heart rate >140, profound hypotension, negligible urine output, lethargic/unconscious - immediately life-threatening. Hypotension is a feature from Class III onward; Class II is typically normotensive with a narrowed pulse pressure.
learningPoint: Systolic BP stays normal until Class III - earlier blood loss shows as tachycardia and a narrowing pulse pressure, not hypotension.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, ATLS/Trauma resuscitation

emiLeads.stem: A 70 kg man with heart rate 138, systolic blood pressure 84 mmHg, respiratory rate 34, confusion and minimal urine output after a pelvic injury.
A young woman with heart rate 110, systolic blood pressure 122 mmHg, a narrowed pulse pressure, mild anxiety and slightly reduced urine output.
A man with heart rate 150, unrecordable diastolic pressure, systolic 68 mmHg, anuria and a markedly depressed conscious level requiring immediate transfusion and haemorrhage control.
A patient with heart rate 82, blood pressure 128/78 mmHg, normal respiratory rate and normal mentation after a minor laceration.

stem: A 24-year-old right-hand-dominant semi-professional rugby player sustains an isolated closed midshaft clavicle fracture after a tackle. Radiographs show complete displacement with no cortical contact and 2.2 cm of shortening. He is neurovascularly intact and the skin is not tented. He is keen to return to his sport as quickly as possible. According to current UK practice and the best available evidence, which is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Broad arm sling for comfort and non-operative management, accepting the displacement

options.label: B
options.text: Open reduction and internal fixation with a pre-contoured superior plate

options.label: C
options.text: Figure-of-eight bandage with weekly radiographs until union

options.label: D
options.text: Intramedullary nailing with an elastic titanium nail as a day case

options.label: E
options.text: Hanging cast and review at 6 weeks

explanation: This is a completely displaced midshaft clavicle fracture (no cortical contact) with >2 cm shortening in a young high-demand athlete. Pooled evidence and meta-analyses show that primary plate fixation of completely displaced midshaft fractures reduces non-union and symptomatic malunion and gives faster return to function, which matters for this patient's sporting demands. While many displaced fractures can still be managed non-operatively, the combination of complete displacement, significant shortening, and high functional demand makes operative fixation the most appropriate choice. A figure-of-eight bandage confers no benefit over a sling and risks skin/neurovascular complications.
learningPoint: Primary plate fixation is favoured for completely displaced, shortened midshaft clavicle fractures in young high-demand patients to reduce non-union/symptomatic malunion and speed functional recovery; routine non-operative care remains reasonable for minimally displaced injuries.
references: BOAST - Clavicle / shoulder girdle injury principles

stem: A 38-year-old labourer presents 7 months after a non-operatively treated displaced midshaft clavicle fracture with persistent pain, a palpable mobile non-union, and weakness on overhead lifting. CT confirms an atrophic non-union with a small intercalary fragment and no infection. What is the most appropriate surgical strategy?

options.label: A
options.text: Excision of the clavicle fragment ends and primary end-to-end suture

options.label: B
options.text: Compression plating with autologous structural/cancellous bone grafting

options.label: C
options.text: Application of a uniplanar external fixator across the non-union

options.label: D
options.text: Continued non-operative management and analgesia

options.label: E
options.text: Partial claviculectomy of the lateral fragment

explanation: An established symptomatic atrophic clavicular non-union with biological failure is best treated by stable internal fixation (compression plating, typically a superior or anteroinferior plate) combined with autologous bone grafting to restore length and biology. Atrophic non-unions are biologically inactive and require grafting in addition to mechanical stability. Excision/suture and partial claviculectomy do not restore the strut function of the clavicle and lead to weakness and shoulder girdle dysfunction. External fixation is not standard for clavicular non-union.
learningPoint: Symptomatic clavicular non-union is treated with rigid compression plating plus autologous bone graft (structural graft if a defect exists); restoring length and biology is key, and excision/claviculectomy compromises shoulder girdle function.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Shoulder/Trauma (clavicle non-union)

stem: An 82-year-old woman with a low-energy fall sustains a displaced 4-part proximal humerus fracture. CT shows a head-split component, marked comminution of the tuberosities and metaphysis, and a calcar/medial hinge that is disrupted with less than 8 mm of medial metaphyseal head extension. She has a functioning deltoid and rotator cuff clinically and modest functional demands. Which option offers the most reliable functional outcome?

options.label: A
options.text: Locking plate fixation (proximal humeral locking plate)

options.label: B
options.text: Closed reduction and percutaneous K-wire fixation

options.label: C
options.text: Reverse total shoulder arthroplasty

options.label: D
options.text: Antegrade locked intramedullary nailing

options.label: E
options.text: Non-operative management with early mobilisation

explanation: In an elderly patient with a 4-part fracture that has a head-split, gross tuberosity/metaphyseal comminution and a disrupted medial calcar (short head-shaft calcar length and disrupted medial hinge are predictors of locking-plate failure/screw cut-out and head osteonecrosis), reverse total shoulder arthroplasty gives more predictable and better functional results than locking plate fixation or hemiarthroplasty because it is less dependent on tuberosity healing. Locking plate fixation in this configuration has high rates of cut-out and AVN. Hemiarthroplasty outcomes hinge on tuberosity healing, which is unreliable here.
learningPoint: Loss of medial calcar support (short calcar length/disrupted medial hinge) and head-split predict locking-plate failure; reverse shoulder arthroplasty is the more reliable option for complex elderly 4-part proximal humerus fractures because it is independent of tuberosity healing.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Shoulder (proximal humerus)
PROFHER trial context; BESS/BOA proximal humerus guidance

stem: The PROFHER randomised controlled trial compared surgical with non-surgical treatment of displaced proximal humeral fractures involving the surgical neck in adults. Which statement best summarises its principal finding and the resulting influence on UK practice?

options.label: A
options.text: Surgery produced significantly better Oxford Shoulder Scores at 2 years, supporting routine fixation

options.label: B
options.text: There was no significant difference in shoulder function (Oxford Shoulder Score) over 2 years, supporting non-operative management for most such fractures

options.label: C
options.text: Non-operative treatment led to significantly higher rates of non-union requiring late surgery

options.label: D
options.text: Surgery significantly reduced mortality at 2 years

options.label: E
options.text: Reverse arthroplasty was superior to all other treatment arms

explanation: PROFHER found no significant difference in shoulder function (measured by the Oxford Shoulder Score) over 2 years between surgical and non-surgical management of the majority of displaced proximal humeral fractures involving the surgical neck, and surgery was not cost-effective. This has reinforced a non-operative-first approach for most of these fractures in the UK, reserving surgery for selected patterns (e.g. head-split, fracture-dislocation, certain young patients). The other options misstate the trial findings.
learningPoint: PROFHER showed no functional benefit of surgery over non-operative care for most displaced surgical-neck proximal humeral fractures, underpinning a non-operative-first UK approach with surgery reserved for selected indications.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Shoulder (proximal humerus)

stem: A 30-year-old man sustains a closed, transverse mid-third humeral shaft fracture. On presentation he has a complete wrist drop with inability to extend the wrist, thumb, and metacarpophalangeal joints, and loss of sensation over the dorsal first web space; there is no vascular deficit. The fracture is in acceptable alignment in a functional brace. What is the most appropriate management of the nerve palsy?

options.label: A
options.text: Immediate surgical exploration and repair of the radial nerve

options.label: B
options.text: Continue non-operative fracture management and observe the nerve, with a wrist extension splint and planned EMG/NCS at around 6 weeks if no recovery

options.label: C
options.text: Urgent tendon transfers to restore wrist and finger extension

options.label: D
options.text: Open reduction and internal fixation purely to decompress the nerve, regardless of fracture alignment

options.label: E
options.text: Immediate nerve grafting using sural nerve

explanation: A radial nerve palsy associated with a closed humeral shaft fracture is usually a neurapraxia/axonotmesis and the great majority recover spontaneously. Standard practice is to treat the fracture on its own merits (a closed shaft fracture in acceptable alignment can be braced) and observe the nerve, protecting the hand with a wrist-extension splint and performing nerve conduction studies/EMG at around 6 weeks (and again later) to monitor recovery; exploration is considered if there is no clinical or electrophysiological recovery by around 3-4 months. Immediate exploration is not indicated for a closed injury. Tendon transfers are a late salvage option only.
learningPoint: Radial nerve palsy with a closed humeral shaft fracture is observed (splint + serial EMG/NCS), as most recover; exploration is reserved for failure of recovery by ~3-4 months, open fractures, or palsy occurring during manipulation/fixation.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Humeral shaft / peripheral nerve
BOAST - peripheral nerve injury / open fracture principles

stem: A 45-year-old woman undergoes successful closed reduction of a displaced spiral humeral shaft fracture at the junction of the middle and distal thirds and is placed in a functional brace. She was neurologically intact before reduction. Immediately after the manipulation she develops a complete radial nerve palsy. Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Reassure and continue bracing, as all post-reduction palsies recover

options.label: B
options.text: Surgical exploration of the radial nerve with fracture stabilisation

options.label: C
options.text: Immediate tendon transfers

options.label: D
options.text: Repeat closed manipulation under anaesthesia to free the nerve

options.label: E
options.text: Arrange outpatient nerve conduction studies in 6 weeks and review

explanation: A radial nerve palsy that develops after (i.e. is caused by) manipulation/reduction is a recognised indication for exploration, because the nerve may have become entrapped or kinked at the fracture site (a particular concern at the distal third, Holstein-Lewis pattern). This differs from a palsy present at the time of injury, which is observed. The correct response is to explore the nerve and stabilise the fracture. Simply reassuring or waiting risks leaving an entrapped nerve, and tendon transfers are a late salvage measure.
learningPoint: A radial nerve palsy that appears after manipulation/reduction (or with an open fracture) warrants exploration because of possible nerve entrapment; this contrasts with an injury-time palsy, which is observed.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Humeral shaft (Holstein-Lewis, radial nerve)

stem: A 34-year-old man sustains a closed, displaced intra-articular bicolumnar (AO type C) distal humeral fracture with good bone quality. He is fit for surgery and the surgeon plans open reduction and internal fixation. Which fixation principle gives the most biomechanically stable construct for this injury?

options.label: A
options.text: Single posterolateral plate alone

options.label: B
options.text: Dual plating with the plates placed at 90 degrees or in parallel, restoring both columns

options.label: C
options.text: Crossed Kirschner wires and a tension band

options.label: D
options.text: A single lag screw across the articular surface only

options.label: E
options.text: Primary total elbow arthroplasty

explanation: A bicolumnar (AO type C) distal humeral fracture in a young patient with good bone is best stabilised by anatomical reduction of the articular surface and dual-column plating, with the plates either orthogonal (90-90) or parallel; both configurations are biomechanically sound when applied to restore both columns. Single-plate or wire constructs are insufficiently stable to allow early mobilisation and risk non-union/stiffness. Primary total elbow arthroplasty is reserved for selected elderly patients with low demand or unreconstructable articular comminution, not a young patient with reconstructable bone.
learningPoint: Bicolumnar (AO type C) distal humeral fractures need dual-column plating (orthogonal or parallel) with anatomical articular reduction to permit early motion; primary total elbow arthroplasty is reserved for selected low-demand elderly patients with unreconstructable fractures.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Elbow (distal humerus)

stem: A 78-year-old woman with rheumatoid arthritis, poor bone quality, and low functional demands sustains a comminuted intra-articular distal humeral fracture in which the articular surface is severely fragmented and not amenable to stable reconstruction. She is medically fit for surgery. Which option is most likely to give a good, predictable functional outcome?

options.label: A
options.text: Open reduction and internal fixation with dual plates

options.label: B
options.text: Linked (semi-constrained) total elbow arthroplasty

options.label: C
options.text: Non-operative management in a collar and cuff (bag-of-bones)

options.label: D
options.text: Closed reduction and percutaneous K-wiring

options.label: E
options.text: Olecranon osteotomy and screw fixation of fragments

explanation: In an elderly low-demand patient with poor bone and an unreconstructable comminuted intra-articular distal humeral fracture, a linked (semi-constrained) total elbow arthroplasty gives a more predictable, pain-free functional range than attempting fixation of bone that will not hold screws. ORIF in this setting risks fixation failure and non-union. The bag-of-bones (non-operative) approach is reserved for patients unfit for surgery and gives inferior function. A lifelong lifting restriction (typically a few kilograms) applies after total elbow arthroplasty.
learningPoint: For low-demand elderly patients with unreconstructable comminuted intra-articular distal humeral fractures, linked total elbow arthroplasty gives more reliable function than ORIF, with a permanent post-operative lifting restriction.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Elbow (distal humerus, TEA)

stem: A 40-year-old woman sustains a displaced, transverse, non-comminuted olecranon fracture at the level of the trochlear notch. The elbow is otherwise stable with intact collateral ligaments and no radial head or coronoid injury. Which fixation method is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Tension band wiring

options.label: B
options.text: Plate fixation with a contoured olecranon locking plate

options.label: C
options.text: Excision of the proximal fragment and triceps advancement

options.label: D
options.text: Intramedullary screw alone without supplementary wiring

options.label: E
options.text: Non-operative management in extension cast

explanation: A simple displaced transverse non-comminuted olecranon fracture in a stable elbow is the classic indication for tension band wiring, which converts the distracting pull of triceps into compression across the fracture during flexion. Plate fixation is preferred for comminuted, oblique distal-to-the-notch, or fracture-dislocation patterns. Fragment excision with triceps advancement is reserved for very proximal small avulsion fractures or in very low-demand patients. An IM screw alone is insufficiently stable for a transverse fracture without a tension band/plate.
learningPoint: Simple transverse non-comminuted olecranon fractures in a stable elbow are ideal for tension band wiring; comminuted or oblique/fracture-dislocation patterns require plate fixation.

stem: Tension band wiring relies on a specific biomechanical principle to achieve fracture compression at the articular surface of the olecranon during elbow movement. Which statement best describes how this principle works?

options.label: A
options.text: The triceps tensile force is converted into a compressive force across the fracture as the elbow flexes

options.label: B
options.text: The wire acts as a rigid buttress that resists axial load along the ulna

options.label: C
options.text: Compression is generated only when the elbow is in full extension

options.label: D
options.text: The K-wires provide rotational stability and the wire provides no compression

options.label: E
options.text: The construct neutralises bending forces by acting as an external fixator

explanation: The tension band principle converts the tensile (distracting) force of the triceps on the tension (dorsal/posterior) side of the olecranon into a dynamic compressive force at the articular (far) cortex when the elbow flexes. For this to work, the far cortex must be intact (so comminution at the articular surface is a contraindication) and the implant must be on the tension side. The other statements describe other implant mechanics, not the tension band principle.
learningPoint: The tension band converts triceps tensile force into articular-side compression during flexion; it requires an intact far cortex and placement on the tension (dorsal) side, so it is unsuitable for comminuted patterns.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Basic science of fracture fixation; Elbow (olecranon)

stem: A 36-year-old man falls onto an outstretched hand and sustains an isolated radial head fracture. CT shows three articular fragments that are displaced and comminuted with an associated mechanical block to forearm rotation; the fragments are not amenable to stable internal fixation, and there is no associated ligamentous instability or other fracture. Which is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Radial head excision without replacement

options.label: B
options.text: Radial head arthroplasty (metallic replacement)

options.label: C
options.text: Open reduction and internal fixation with multiple small screws

options.label: D
options.text: Non-operative management with early mobilisation

options.label: E
options.text: Aspiration of the haemarthrosis and immobilisation for 4 weeks

explanation: A comminuted radial head fracture (more than three fragments) that is not reconstructable and is causing a mechanical block is best treated with radial head arthroplasty, especially as the radial head is an important secondary stabiliser of the elbow. Attempting ORIF of more than three fragments is associated with high rates of failure and is generally not recommended. Excision without replacement can be acceptable for an isolated unreconstructable fracture in a stable elbow but risks proximal radial migration and valgus instability and is less favoured when there is any concern about stability; arthroplasty preserves the stabilising function. Non-operative care is inappropriate when there is a mechanical block.
learningPoint: Unreconstructable comminuted radial head fractures (>3 fragments) or those with a mechanical block are best managed with radial head replacement, preserving the radial head's role as a stabiliser; ORIF of >3 fragments has high failure rates.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Elbow (radial head)

stem: A 28-year-old woman attends after a fall onto an outstretched hand. Radiographs show a minimally displaced (<2 mm) radial head fracture involving less than a third of the head. There is a positive elbow joint effusion (fat-pad sign) but a full, smooth range of forearm rotation with no mechanical block on examination. What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Early mobilisation with a sling for comfort and analgesia

options.label: B
options.text: Open reduction and internal fixation with a single lag screw

options.label: C
options.text: Above-elbow cast for 6 weeks

options.label: D
options.text: Radial head excision

options.label: E
options.text: Radial head arthroplasty

explanation: A minimally displaced radial head fracture (Mason type I) with no mechanical block to forearm rotation is treated non-operatively with early mobilisation, using a sling only for comfort. Aspiration of the haemarthrosis with local anaesthetic can be used to confirm absence of a mechanical block and aid early movement, but prolonged immobilisation causes stiffness and is avoided. Surgery (ORIF, excision, arthroplasty) is not indicated for an undisplaced fracture with full rotation.
learningPoint: Mason type I radial head fractures (minimally displaced, no mechanical block) are managed with early mobilisation and a comfort sling; prolonged casting causes stiffness and surgery is unnecessary.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Elbow (radial head, Mason classification)

stem: A 44-year-old man sustains a posterolateral elbow fracture-dislocation. Imaging confirms a radial head fracture, a small transverse coronoid tip fracture, and a posterolateral rotatory mechanism with lateral collateral ligament avulsion (the terrible triad). At surgery, what is the generally recommended sequence and combination of steps to restore a stable, congruent elbow?

options.label: A
options.text: Repair/fix coronoid, then address radial head (fix or replace), then repair the lateral collateral ligament complex, assessing stability and only repairing the medial collateral ligament/applying a hinged external fixator if instability persists

options.label: B
options.text: Repair only the lateral collateral ligament and immobilise in extension

options.label: C
options.text: Excise the radial head, leave the coronoid, and brace

options.label: D
options.text: Fix the radial head only and begin immediate unprotected mobilisation

options.label: E
options.text: Apply a hinged external fixator alone without addressing the bony or ligamentous lesions

explanation: Management of the terrible triad follows a systematic deep-to-superficial approach: address the coronoid (repair or fix where feasible, as it is a key anterior buttress against posterior subluxation), then the radial head (fix if reconstructable, otherwise replace - do not simply excise, as the radial head is a vital stabiliser here), then repair the lateral collateral ligament complex. Stability is then tested; if the elbow remains unstable, the medial collateral ligament is repaired and/or a hinged external fixator is applied. Immobilising in extension or excising the radial head without replacement leaves the elbow unstable.
learningPoint: Terrible triad surgery proceeds coronoid -> radial head (fix or replace, never bare excision) -> lateral collateral ligament repair, then re-test stability and add medial collateral ligament repair/hinged external fixator only if needed.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Elbow (terrible triad)

stem: Regan and Morrey classify coronoid fractures by the height of the fragment, and the O'Driscoll classification refines this by anatomical location. A patient sustains a varus posteromedial rotatory injury of the elbow. Which coronoid fracture pattern is most characteristic of this mechanism, and why is it important to recognise?

options.label: A
options.text: A tip (Regan-Morrey type I) fracture, which is always stable and never needs fixation

options.label: B
options.text: An anteromedial facet fracture, which causes varus posteromedial rotatory instability and often requires buttress fixation despite appearing small

options.label: C
options.text: A basal (type III) fracture that only occurs with radial head replacement

options.label: D
options.text: A large body fragment that is best treated by excision

options.label: E
options.text: A lateral coronoid fracture associated with Galeazzi injuries

explanation: The anteromedial facet of the coronoid is the characteristic fracture of a varus posteromedial rotatory instability injury (often with lateral collateral ligament injury). Although the fragment can look small on plain films, the anteromedial facet is critical to ulnohumeral congruity, and failure to recognise and buttress-fix it leads to varus malalignment, articular incongruity, and rapid post-traumatic arthritis. It typically requires a medial approach and a buttress plate/anchors. This contrasts with terrible-triad tip fractures (posterolateral mechanism).
learningPoint: Anteromedial coronoid facet fractures signify varus posteromedial rotatory instability; they are deceptively small but require buttress fixation to prevent incongruity and arthritis.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Elbow (coronoid, O'Driscoll/Regan-Morrey)

stem: A 29-year-old man sustains a closed, displaced fracture of both the radial and ulnar shafts (a both-bone forearm fracture) after a fall. He is neurovascularly intact with no features of compartment syndrome. What is the standard management to restore forearm function in an adult?

options.label: A
options.text: Open reduction and internal fixation of both bones with compression plates

options.label: B
options.text: Above-elbow cast in neutral rotation for 6 weeks

options.label: C
options.text: Intramedullary nailing of both bones as the first-line treatment

options.label: D
options.text: ORIF of the ulna only, leaving the radius to remodel

options.label: E
options.text: Functional bracing once swelling settles

explanation: Displaced both-bone forearm fractures in adults are intra-articular-equivalent injuries because the radius and ulna with the proximal and distal radioulnar joints act as a functional joint; anatomical restoration of length, alignment and the radial bow is essential for forearm rotation. The standard treatment is open reduction and internal fixation of both bones with compression plating. Non-operative management (cast/brace) cannot reliably maintain alignment and the radial bow in adults and leads to malunion and loss of rotation. Intramedullary nailing is not the routine first-line adult treatment for diaphyseal both-bone fractures.
learningPoint: Adult displaced both-bone forearm fractures require ORIF of both bones with compression plating to restore length, alignment and the radial bow (key to forearm rotation); casting is inadequate in adults.

stem: A 32-year-old man is brought in after a high-energy crush injury to the forearm with a closed both-bone fracture. Over the first few hours he develops increasing pain that is severe and out of proportion, worsened markedly by passive finger extension, with a tense swollen forearm. Distal pulses are still palpable and capillary refill is present. What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Elevate the limb above the heart and reassess in 4 hours

options.label: B
options.text: Urgent fasciotomy of the forearm compartments

options.label: C
options.text: Loosen any dressings, obtain compartment pressure monitoring, and re-examine in the morning

options.label: D
options.text: Apply a backslab and arrange semi-elective fixation

options.label: E
options.text: Arrange CT angiography before any intervention

explanation: This is acute compartment syndrome of the forearm: pain out of proportion and pain on passive stretch of the muscles within the compartment are the earliest and most reliable clinical signs, and the presence of distal pulses does NOT exclude it. The treatment is urgent (emergency) fasciotomy of the forearm compartments to prevent muscle necrosis (Volkmann's ischaemic contracture).
