Compartment syndrome is a clinical diagnosis; pressure monitoring may support equivocal cases but must not delay decompression when the clinical picture is clear, and elevation only to heart level (not above) is advised while preparing for surgery.
learningPoint: Forearm compartment syndrome is a clinical diagnosis (pain out of proportion and pain on passive stretch; pulses often preserved) requiring emergency fasciotomy; do not delay for pressure monitoring or angiography when the picture is clear.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Compartment syndrome
BOAST - Diagnosis and Management of Compartment Syndrome of the Limbs

stem: A 27-year-old man presents with a displaced proximal ulnar shaft fracture. The treating clinician must not miss the associated injury that defines a Monteggia fracture-dislocation. Regarding this injury, which statement is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: It is a fracture of the distal radius with dislocation of the distal radioulnar joint, and the radial head should not be assessed

options.label: B
options.text: It is a fracture of the ulna with dislocation of the radial head (proximal radioulnar joint); in adults, anatomical ORIF of the ulna usually reduces the radial head, and a true lateral elbow radiograph is essential to detect the radiocapitellar dislocation

options.label: C
options.text: The direction of radial head dislocation has no relationship to the ulnar fracture angulation

options.label: D
options.text: In adults it is best treated non-operatively in a cast

options.label: E
options.text: The posterior interosseous nerve is never at risk in this injury

explanation: A Monteggia injury is an ulnar shaft fracture with an associated dislocation of the radial head (disruption of the proximal radioulnar/radiocapitellar relationship). The Bado classification relates the direction of radial head dislocation to the apex of the ulnar fracture angulation. In adults, treatment is anatomical ORIF of the ulna, which usually reduces the radial head; the radiocapitellar joint must be checked on a true lateral elbow film and after fixation. The posterior interosseous nerve is at risk, particularly with anterolateral (Bado I/III-type) radial head dislocations. A line drawn through the radial head and neck should always pass through the centre of the capitellum on every view.
learningPoint: Monteggia = ulnar fracture + radial head dislocation; the radiocapitellar line must align with the capitellum on every view, adults need anatomical ulnar ORIF, and the posterior interosseous nerve is at risk.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Forearm (Monteggia, Bado)

stem: A 35-year-old woman sustains a displaced fracture of the distal third of the radial shaft. There is clinical and radiographic evidence of distal radioulnar joint (DRUJ) disruption (a Galeazzi fracture-dislocation). After anatomical ORIF of the radius, the DRUJ is assessed and found to be unstable but reducible. Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Leave the DRUJ alone, as radial fixation always stabilises it

options.label: B
options.text: Reduce the DRUJ and immobilise the forearm in supination (with or without temporary K-wire stabilisation of the DRUJ)

options.label: C
options.text: Excise the distal ulna (Darrach procedure) at the index operation

options.label: D
options.text: Immobilise the forearm in full pronation

options.label: E
options.text: Apply a hinged external fixator across the wrist

explanation: In a Galeazzi injury, anatomical reduction and plating of the radius is the priority, after which the DRUJ must be specifically assessed. If the DRUJ is reduced and stable, early motion is allowed; if it is reducible but unstable, the forearm is immobilised in supination (which tightens the volar structures and stabilises the reduction), often with temporary percutaneous K-wire stabilisation of the DRUJ. If the DRUJ is irreducible, an interposed structure (commonly extensor carpi ulnaris tendon) must be removed via open reduction. Darrach excision is a salvage option, not an index procedure for an acute reducible DRUJ.
learningPoint: After radial ORIF in a Galeazzi injury, assess the DRUJ: stable -> mobilise; reducible but unstable -> immobilise in supination +/- temporary K-wire; irreducible -> open reduction to remove interposed tissue (often ECU).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Forearm (Galeazzi, DRUJ)

stem: A 48-year-old woman is reviewed 8 weeks after a volar locking plate was used to fix a distal radius fracture. She reports increasing pain and crepitus on finger flexion, and then sudden loss of active flexion of the interphalangeal joint of the thumb (inability to actively bend the thumb tip). Which complication is most likely, and what is the usual underlying cause?

options.label: A
options.text: Extensor pollicis longus rupture due to a dorsally prominent screw

options.label: B
options.text: Flexor pollicis longus attritional rupture related to a prominent volar plate placed distal to the watershed line

options.label: C
options.text: Anterior interosseous nerve palsy

options.label: D
options.text: Median nerve compression at the carpal tunnel

options.label: E
options.text: Complex regional pain syndrome

explanation: Loss of active thumb interphalangeal joint FLEXION indicates flexor pollicis longus (FPL) failure. After volar plating, attritional FPL rupture is a recognised complication when the plate is placed too distally (volar/prominent to the watershed line), causing tendon abrasion; prodromal pain and crepitus on flexion often precede rupture. This is why plates should sit proximal to the watershed line and prominent hardware should be removed if symptomatic. Extensor pollicis longus rupture causes loss of thumb extension (and is associated with dorsal screw prominence or non-displaced distal radius fractures), not flexion. The anterior interosseous nerve also affects FPL but presents differently and is not the typical late post-plating cause here.
learningPoint: Loss of active thumb IP flexion after volar plating suggests FPL attritional rupture from a plate placed distal/prominent to the watershed line; prodromal flexion pain/crepitus warrants early plate removal to prevent rupture.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Wrist (distal radius complications)

stem: A 22-year-old man falls onto an outstretched hand and complains of wrist pain. There is tenderness in the anatomical snuffbox and pain on telescoping the thumb, but initial wrist and dedicated scaphoid-view radiographs show no fracture. What is the most appropriate next step in UK practice?

options.label: A
options.text: Reassure, discharge with analgesia and no follow-up

options.label: B
options.text: Immobilise in a thumb spica/wrist splint and arrange further imaging (e.g. MRI, or repeat radiographs/clinical review) to confirm or exclude an occult scaphoid fracture

options.label: C
options.text: Proceed directly to percutaneous scaphoid screw fixation

options.label: D
options.text: Apply an above-elbow cast for 12 weeks regardless of imaging

options.label: E
options.text: Order a bone scan immediately on the day of injury

explanation: Clinical suspicion of a scaphoid fracture (snuffbox tenderness, pain on thumb axial loading) with normal initial radiographs requires treatment as a clinically suspected scaphoid fracture: immobilise and obtain further imaging to confirm or exclude an occult fracture. MRI is the investigation of choice in many UK units as it is sensitive early and avoids prolonged unnecessary immobilisation; alternatively, repeat radiographs/clinical review at around 10-14 days is used. A bone scan is sensitive but not on the day of injury (needs ~72 hours and is less specific). Missing a scaphoid fracture risks non-union and avascular necrosis because of the retrograde blood supply.
learningPoint: A clinically suspected scaphoid fracture with normal initial X-rays is splinted and further imaged (MRI preferred, or interval radiographs/review) because missed fractures risk non-union/AVN from the retrograde blood supply.
references: BOAST - scaphoid fracture / suspected scaphoid injury

stem: A 26-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after a motorcycle crash with a painful, swollen wrist. A true lateral wrist radiograph shows the capitate displaced dorsally relative to the lunate, while the lunate remains aligned with the distal radius; there is associated scaphoid widening and a fracture. The median nerve territory is paraesthetic. Which is the diagnosis and the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Lunate dislocation; treat with a wrist splint and outpatient hand clinic review

options.label: B
options.text: Perilunate (trans-scaphoid) fracture-dislocation; urgent reduction and operative repair/fixation (and carpal tunnel decompression if median nerve symptoms persist)

options.label: C
options.text: Distal radius fracture; closed reduction and cast only

options.label: D
options.text: Scapholunate sprain; early mobilisation

options.label: E
options.text: Isolated capitate fracture; percutaneous screw without reduction

explanation: Dorsal displacement of the capitate (and the carpus) relative to a lunate that remains congruent with the radius indicates a perilunate dislocation; the associated scaphoid fracture makes this a trans-scaphoid perilunate fracture-dislocation (a greater-arc injury). These are high-energy injuries that are frequently missed and are commonly associated with acute median nerve symptoms. Management is urgent closed reduction to relieve pressure on the median nerve, followed by operative repair (open reduction, scaphoid fixation and ligament repair), with carpal tunnel decompression if median nerve symptoms persist after reduction. A pure lunate dislocation (lunate tipped volarly out of line with the radius, the 'spilled teacup') is the further stage of the same spectrum and is also a surgical emergency.
learningPoint: Capitate dorsally displaced on a radius-aligned lunate = perilunate (trans-scaphoid) fracture-dislocation, a frequently missed high-energy injury needing urgent reduction, operative repair/fixation, and carpal tunnel decompression if median nerve signs persist.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Wrist (perilunate/lunate dislocation, Mayfield)
BOAST - carpal dislocation / wrist injury principles

stem: A 28-year-old motorcyclist arrives after a high-speed collision. He is GCS 15 but pale, with heart rate 128 and systolic blood pressure 82 mmHg unresponsive to 1 L of warmed crystalloid. The pelvis is clinically unstable. A pelvic binder has been correctly applied at the level of the greater trochanters and 4 units of red cells transfused via the major haemorrhage protocol, but he remains hypotensive. The trauma CT shows a complete vertical-shear injury with active contrast extravasation in the posterior pelvis and no significant intra-abdominal free fluid. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Apply external fixator to the anterior pelvic ring in the emergency department

options.label: B
options.text: Transfer to interventional radiology for angioembolisation

options.label: C
options.text: Emergency laparotomy with pelvic packing

options.label: D
options.text: Definitive posterior fixation with iliosacral screws

options.label: E
options.text: Re-apply the binder higher, over the iliac crests, and continue transfusion

explanation: This patient has an arterial (contrast blush) source of bleeding that persists despite a correctly applied binder and ongoing resuscitation, with a CT excluding a significant intra-abdominal source. In a unit with rapid access to interventional radiology, angioembolisation is the most appropriate next step to control an arterial pelvic bleed. Pre-peritoneal pelvic packing is the alternative when IR is not immediately available or the patient is too unstable to travel, and is typically combined with anterior external fixation; here the question specifies CT has already been obtained and a discrete arterial blush identified, making targeted embolisation the better-matched answer. An anterior external fixator controls venous/bony bleeding by reducing pelvic volume but does not address a named arterial blush. The binder is already correctly placed at the trochanters (not the crests), so re-siting it higher would worsen reduction.
learningPoint: A persistent arterial contrast blush in the pelvis despite a correctly applied binder is an indication for angioembolisation (or pre-peritoneal packing if IR is not immediately available).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pelvis & Acetabulum
BOAST: The Management of Patients with Pelvic Fractures (BOA)

stem: A pelvic binder is being applied in the emergency department to a patient with a suspected anteroposterior-compression (open-book) pelvic injury. At which anatomical level should the binder be centred to achieve the most effective ring reduction?

options.label: A
options.text: The iliac crests

options.label: B
options.text: The umbilicus

options.label: C
options.text: The greater trochanters

options.label: D
options.text: The anterior superior iliac spines

options.label: E
options.text: The mid-thigh

explanation: A pelvic binder is centred over the greater trochanters, not the iliac crests. At this level the binder generates internal-rotation force on the hemipelves that closes an open-book (APC) injury and reduces pelvic volume, tamponading venous bleeding. Placement too high over the crests is a common error that fails to reduce the ring and can paradoxically distract it. The legs should also be internally rotated and the knees bound together to assist closure. Binders should be released as soon as practical once haemorrhage is controlled to avoid skin necrosis.
learningPoint: Centre a pelvic binder over the greater trochanters — placement over the iliac crests is a common error that fails to reduce the ring.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pelvis & Acetabulum
BOAST: The Management of Patients with Pelvic Fractures (BOA)

stem: Theme: surgical approaches to acetabular fractures. For each elementary or associated acetabular fracture pattern, select the single most appropriate primary surgical approach from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Kocher–Langenbeck approach

options.label: B
options.text: Ilioinguinal approach

options.label: C
options.text: Anterior intrapelvic (modified Stoppa) approach

options.label: D
options.text: Extended iliofemoral approach

options.label: E
options.text: Smith–Petersen (direct anterior) approach

options.label: F
options.text: Triradiate approach

options.label: G
options.text: Non-operative management with protected weight-bearing

emiLeads.stem: An isolated displaced posterior wall fracture with a dislocated hip reduced in the emergency department; CT shows no marginal impaction and a stable concentric reduction is maintained after relocation, but the wall fragment is >40% of the wall.
An anterior-column-and-posterior-hemitransverse fracture in an elderly patient where the surgeon wishes to access the quadrilateral plate and anterior column through a single anterior corridor.

A both-column fracture in a young patient where, after assessment, the surgeon judges that satisfactory reduction of both columns can be achieved through an anterior window-based extrapelvic approach with access to the iliac fossa, pelvic brim and pubic symphysis.

An associated both-column fracture with secondary congruence on CT in a low-demand elderly patient who is a poor surgical candidate, where the femoral head and acetabular fragments maintain a congruent relationship.

explanation: Approach selection in acetabular surgery follows the column/wall involved. Posterior wall and posterior column fractures (and most transverse patterns with predominant posterior displacement) are addressed through the Kocher–Langenbeck approach. Anterior column, anterior-column-posterior-hemitransverse and many both-column fractures are reached anteriorly: the classic ilioinguinal approach uses three windows over the iliac fossa, vessels and symphysis, whereas the anterior intrapelvic (modified Stoppa) approach gives direct access to the quadrilateral plate and is increasingly favoured, particularly in the elderly. The extended iliofemoral approach gives simultaneous access to both columns but carries high morbidity and is now rarely used. 'Secondary congruence' in a both-column fracture — where the displaced columns maintain a congruent relationship with the femoral head — can justify non-operative treatment in a low-demand patient.
learningPoint: Match the acetabular approach to the column/wall: Kocher–Langenbeck for posterior, ilioinguinal or anterior intrapelvic (Stoppa) for anterior/quadrilateral plate; secondary congruence in a both-column fracture may be treated non-operatively.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pelvis & Acetabulum

stem: A 74-year-old woman who lives independently, walks to the shops without aids and has no cognitive impairment sustains a displaced intracapsular femoral neck fracture after a simple fall. She has no significant hip osteoarthritis on the radiograph and is medically fit for surgery. According to current UK national guidance, which operation should be offered?

options.label: A
options.text: Cannulated screw fixation

options.label: B
options.text: Sliding hip screw

options.label: C
options.text: Cemented hemiarthroplasty

options.label: D
options.text: Total hip replacement

options.label: E
options.text: Uncemented hemiarthroplasty

explanation: NICE (CG124/NG) recommends total hip replacement for patients with a displaced intracapsular fracture who were able to walk independently out of doors with no more than the use of a stick, are not cognitively impaired, and are medically fit for anaesthesia and the procedure. This patient meets all three criteria, so THR — which gives better functional outcomes and lower revision/reoperation rates than hemiarthroplasty in this group — is correct. Hemiarthroplasty (cemented) is the appropriate choice for displaced intracapsular fractures in patients who do not meet the THR criteria. Internal fixation (cannulated screws) is generally reserved for undisplaced/young patients, and a sliding hip screw is an extracapsular device.
learningPoint: Offer THR for a displaced intracapsular hip fracture if the patient walks independently outdoors (≤1 stick), is not cognitively impaired and is fit for surgery; otherwise a cemented hemiarthroplasty.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hip Fractures
NICE NG (Hip fracture management, formerly CG124)

subtopic: Intracapsular hip fracture — young adult timing
stem: A 32-year-old man sustains a displaced (Garden III) intracapsular femoral neck fracture in a fall from a height. He is otherwise fit. After resuscitation and exclusion of other injuries, what is the single most important principle guiding management of his hip?

options.label: A
options.text: Hemiarthroplasty to allow immediate full weight-bearing

options.label: B
options.text: Urgent anatomical reduction and internal fixation to preserve the native head

options.label: C
options.text: Non-weight-bearing traction for 6 weeks to allow the fracture to settle before fixation

options.label: D
options.text: Total hip replacement as the definitive solution

options.label: E
options.text: Delayed fixation once swelling has subsided at 7–10 days

explanation: In a young adult, the priority is to preserve the native femoral head, so a displaced intracapsular fracture is treated by urgent anatomical reduction (closed or open) and stable internal fixation rather than arthroplasty. Although the evidence that very early surgery reduces avascular necrosis is debated, the combination of urgent treatment, anatomical reduction and (where indicated) capsulotomy/decompression of the intracapsular haematoma offers the best chance of avoiding AVN and non-union, which would otherwise commit a young patient to early arthroplasty. Arthroplasty (hemi or THR) is avoided as a primary procedure in this age group when reconstruction is feasible. Prolonged traction simply delays definitive care.
learningPoint: In young adults a displaced femoral neck fracture is a relative emergency: anatomical reduction and stable fixation aim to preserve the native head and minimise AVN/non-union.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hip Fractures

subtopic: Extracapsular hip fracture — reverse oblique pattern
stem: An 80-year-old woman sustains a low-energy proximal femoral fracture. The radiograph shows a reverse-obliquity intertrochanteric fracture line running from the proximal-medial to the distal-lateral cortex. Which device gives the most biomechanically appropriate fixation?

options.label: A
options.text: Dynamic (sliding) hip screw with a two-hole side plate

options.label: B
options.text: Long cephalomedullary nail

options.label: C
options.text: Cannulated screws

options.label: D
options.text: Cemented hemiarthroplasty

options.label: E
options.text: Conservative management with early mobilisation

explanation: A reverse-oblique (and subtrochanteric/transtrochanteric) fracture is biomechanically unstable for a sliding hip screw: the obliquity of the fracture line means that controlled collapse along the DHS barrel produces medial translation and lateralisation of the shaft rather than compression, with a high failure rate. An intramedullary device (cephalomedullary nail) acts as a load-sharing implant closer to the mechanical axis and resists this displacement, and is the recommended choice. NICE specifically recommends an intramedullary nail for subtrochanteric fractures and the same principle applies to reverse-oblique patterns. A DHS remains appropriate for stable trochanteric fractures. Arthroplasty is not the standard primary treatment for extracapsular fractures.
learningPoint: Reverse-oblique and subtrochanteric fractures should be fixed with an intramedullary (cephalomedullary) nail — a sliding hip screw fails because collapse causes shaft medialisation.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hip Fractures

subtopic: Femoral shaft fracture — fat embolism syndrome
stem: A 24-year-old man with an isolated closed femoral shaft fracture, awaiting intramedullary nailing, becomes acutely breathless 36 hours after injury with oxygen saturations of 88% on air, a tachycardia, a new confusional state and a petechial rash over the chest and axillae. A chest radiograph shows bilateral patchy infiltrates. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.label: A
options.text: Pulmonary thromboembolism

options.label: B
options.text: Fat embolism syndrome

options.label: C
options.text: Aspiration pneumonia

options.label: D
options.text: Transfusion-related acute lung injury

options.label: E
options.text: Acute coronary syndrome

explanation: The triad of respiratory insufficiency (hypoxia, bilateral infiltrates), neurological dysfunction (confusion) and a petechial rash — classically over the upper trunk, axillae and conjunctivae — arising 24–72 hours after a long-bone fracture is the clinical picture of fat embolism syndrome (Gurd's criteria). It is more common in young patients with diaphyseal long-bone fractures. The petechial rash, although present in a minority of cases, is the most specific feature and helps distinguish FES from pulmonary embolism, which does not cause a petechial rash or confusion in this way. Management is largely supportive (oxygen/ventilatory support); early stabilisation of the fracture reduces the risk.
learningPoint: Hypoxia + confusion + a petechial rash 24–72 h after a long-bone fracture in a young patient is fat embolism syndrome (Gurd's criteria); the petechiae are the most specific feature.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Trauma (Femoral shaft)

subtopic: Distal femoral fracture — coronal plane (Hoffa) fragment
stem: A 45-year-old is being planned for fixation of a comminuted intra-articular distal femoral fracture. The surgeon is specifically concerned about a coronal-plane fracture of the posterior aspect of a femoral condyle (a Hoffa fragment), which is most commonly missed on plain radiographs. Which investigation is most appropriate to identify and characterise this fragment, and which condyle is most often involved?

options.label: A
options.text: MRI; medial condyle

options.label: B
options.text: CT scan; lateral condyle

options.label: C
options.text: CT scan; medial condyle

options.label: D
options.text: Stress radiographs; lateral condyle

options.label: E
options.text: Bone scan; medial condyle

explanation: A Hoffa fracture is a coronal-plane shear fracture of a femoral condyle. It is frequently missed on AP and lateral radiographs because the fragment lies in the coronal plane, so CT is the investigation of choice to detect and characterise it and to plan fixation (typically anteroposterior lag screws, often countersunk or headless, with the construct perpendicular to the fracture plane). The lateral condyle is involved far more often than the medial, reflecting the valgus mechanism and load distribution. Recognising a Hoffa fragment is critical because failure to fix it leads to intra-articular displacement and non-union.
learningPoint: Suspect and CT-image a Hoffa (coronal-plane condylar) fracture in distal femoral injuries — it is usually the lateral condyle and is easily missed on plain films.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Trauma (Distal femur)

stem: A 50-year-old sustains a transverse mid-body patellar fracture with a 4 mm articular step and is unable to perform a straight-leg raise. Operative fixation is planned using a tension-band construct. What is the fundamental biomechanical principle by which a correctly applied anterior tension band stabilises this fracture?

options.label: A
options.text: It rigidly prevents all motion at the fracture site through absolute interfragmentary compression alone

options.label: B
options.text: It converts the distracting tensile force of the extensor mechanism on the anterior surface into compression across the articular surface during knee flexion

options.label: C
options.text: It relies on the patella tendon to splint the fracture without any metalwork

options.label: D
options.text: It bridges the fracture as a load-bearing buttress that off-loads the bone entirely

options.label: E
options.text: It neutralises rotational forces while permitting controlled axial collapse

explanation: The tension-band principle exploits the eccentric loading of the patella. The extensor mechanism applies a tensile (distracting) force to the anterior cortex of the patella. A wire (or suture) placed on the tension (anterior) side converts this tensile force into dynamic compression at the articular (far) cortex when the knee is flexed, so that physiological loading actively compresses the fracture rather than distracting it. This requires intact opposite (articular) cortical contact to act as the fulcrum. It is dynamic compression, not absolute rigidity, and is the rationale for fixing transverse patellar (and olecranon) fractures with a tension band, supplemented by K-wires or screws for rotational control.
learningPoint: An anterior tension band converts the extensor mechanism's distracting force into compression at the articular surface on knee flexion — it requires intact far-cortex contact to work.

subtopic: Patellar / extensor mechanism — patella baja vs alta
stem: A 30-year-old presents after a fall onto a flexed knee with sudden loss of active knee extension and a palpable gap just distal to the patella. The lateral radiograph shows a high-riding patella (patella alta). Which structure has most likely ruptured?

options.label: A
options.text: Quadriceps tendon

options.label: B
options.text: Patellar (patellar ligament) tendon

options.label: C
options.text: Anterior cruciate ligament

options.label: D
options.text: Medial patellofemoral ligament

options.label: E
options.text: Iliotibial band

explanation: Loss of active extension with an extensor lag, a palpable infrapatellar gap and patella alta (the patella rides high because the distal tether is lost) indicate a patellar tendon rupture, which typically occurs in patients under 40. By contrast, a quadriceps tendon rupture (more common over 40) produces a suprapatellar gap and patella baja (low-riding patella) because the proximal pull is lost. Both present with inability to perform a straight-leg raise and are repaired surgically; the radiographic patella position is the key discriminator. The Insall–Salvati ratio quantifies patellar height.
learningPoint: Patella alta + infrapatellar gap = patellar tendon rupture (younger patient); patella baja + suprapatellar gap = quadriceps tendon rupture (older patient).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Knee (Extensor mechanism)

stem: A 55-year-old pedestrian struck by a car has a tibial plateau fracture. The radiograph and CT show a split fracture of the lateral plateau with associated central articular depression. According to the Schatzker classification, which type is this?

options.label: A
options.text: Schatzker I

options.label: B
options.text: Schatzker II

options.label: C
options.text: Schatzker III

options.label: D
options.text: Schatzker IV

options.label: E
options.text: Schatzker V

explanation: The Schatzker classification of tibial plateau fractures runs: I = lateral split (no depression), typically in younger patients with good bone quality; II = lateral split with depression (the most common pattern, seen in older patients); III = pure lateral central depression without a split; IV = medial plateau fracture (high-energy, associated with knee dislocation and neurovascular injury); V = bicondylar; VI = plateau fracture with metaphyseal–diaphyseal dissociation. A lateral split combined with central depression is therefore Schatzker II. CT is essential to quantify depression and split width and to plan elevation, bone grafting/substitute and buttress plating.
learningPoint: Schatzker II = lateral split + depression (the commonest plateau fracture); a medial-plateau (Schatzker IV) injury signals a high-energy mechanism with knee-dislocation/neurovascular risk.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Trauma (Tibial plateau)

subtopic: Tibial plateau fracture — associated injuries / approach
stem: A 38-year-old presents with an isolated displaced medial tibial plateau fracture (Schatzker IV) following a high-energy crush. The foot is warm with palpable pulses, but you are concerned about an associated injury given the fracture pattern. Which associated finding most warrants urgent assessment before any definitive fixation?

options.label: A
options.text: Common peroneal nerve palsy and vascular compromise from a reduced knee dislocation

options.label: B
options.text: Anterior cruciate ligament rupture requiring acute reconstruction

options.label: C
options.text: Meniscal tear requiring acute repair at the same sitting

options.label: D
options.text: Patellar tendon avulsion

options.label: E
options.text: Proximal tibiofibular joint dislocation

explanation: A Schatzker IV (medial plateau) fracture is a high-energy injury and is frequently a manifestation of a spontaneously reduced knee dislocation. The priority is to exclude limb-threatening neurovascular injury — popliteal artery disruption and common peroneal nerve palsy — because pulses may be present despite an intimal vascular injury. Serial vascular examination, ankle–brachial pressure index and a low threshold for CT angiography are mandatory before definitive surgery; missed popliteal injury risks amputation. Ligamentous and meniscal injuries are common but are not acute emergencies and are addressed after the bony and vascular issues. This contrasts with lateral split fractures (Schatzker I–II) which are lower-energy.
learningPoint: Treat a medial tibial-plateau (Schatzker IV) fracture as a reduced knee dislocation until proven otherwise — assess popliteal artery and common peroneal nerve before fixation, as pulses can be normal with an intimal injury.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Trauma (Tibial plateau / knee dislocation)
BOAST: Vascular injury / knee dislocation principles

subtopic: Tibial shaft fracture — nailing and anterior knee pain
stem: A 27-year-old footballer sustains a closed, displaced mid-shaft tibial fracture and undergoes reamed intramedullary nailing through a standard infrapatellar approach. He recovers well but at follow-up reports persistent anterior knee pain on kneeling. This is the commonest complication of this procedure. Which technical factor is most strongly associated with reducing this problem?

options.label: A
options.text: Using an unreamed nail

options.label: B
options.text: Leaving the nail proud above the cortex to ease removal

options.label: C
options.text: Ensuring the nail is buried beneath the articular cartilage / appropriate start point and seating depth

options.label: D
options.text: Removing both proximal locking screws early

options.label: E
options.text: Using a larger-diameter nail than templated

explanation: Anterior knee pain is the most common complication after antegrade tibial nailing, reported in a large proportion of patients regardless of whether a trans- or para-patellar tendon approach is used. Although its aetiology is multifactorial, a prominent (proud) nail tip is a recognised and avoidable contributor: ensuring a correct start point and that the nail is seated at or just below the cortical entry/articular surface reduces mechanical irritation. Leaving the nail proud worsens symptoms. Reaming versus no reaming and nail diameter relate to union and stability rather than to anterior knee pain. Suprapatellar nailing techniques have been developed partly to address this complication.
learningPoint: Anterior knee pain is the commonest complication of antegrade tibial nailing; a correct start point with the nail seated (not proud) at the entry point minimises it.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Trauma (Tibial shaft)

subtopic: Tibial shaft fracture — diaphyseal compartment syndrome monitoring
stem: A 22-year-old with a closed mid-shaft tibial fracture is admitted for observation prior to surgery. Four hours later he reports escalating pain disproportionate to his injury and requiring increasing opioid doses, with severe pain on passive dorsiflexion of the toes. His leg compartments feel tense. According to current UK guidance, what is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Measure compartment pressures and only proceed to surgery if the absolute pressure exceeds 30 mmHg

options.label: B
options.text: Elevate the limb above heart level and reassess in 2 hours

options.label: C
options.text: Proceed to urgent fasciotomy of all four compartments on clinical grounds

options.label: D
options.text: Apply a backslab and arrange an urgent MRI of the leg

options.label: E
options.text: Give a regional nerve block for analgesia and continue observation

explanation: Acute compartment syndrome is a clinical diagnosis. Escalating pain out of proportion, increasing analgesic requirement and pain on passive stretch are the cardinal early features, and in an alert patient with a consistent clinical picture the appropriate action is urgent fasciotomy of all four leg compartments without delay. Compartment pressure monitoring (with a delta-p/perfusion-pressure threshold rather than a single absolute value) is useful chiefly in obtunded or unreliable patients, but it must never delay surgery when the clinical diagnosis is clear. Elevating the limb to (not above) heart level is reasonable while preparing, but observation, MRI or a nerve block that masks symptoms are all inappropriate and dangerous here. Pulselessness and paralysis are late signs.
learningPoint: Acute compartment syndrome is a clinical diagnosis — escalating pain, increasing analgesia and pain on passive stretch in an alert patient mandate urgent fasciotomy without waiting for pressure measurements.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Trauma (Compartment syndrome)
BOAST: Diagnosis and Management of Compartment Syndrome of the Limbs (BOA)

stem: A 44-year-old falls from a ladder and sustains a high-energy, comminuted intra-articular distal tibial (pilon) fracture. On arrival the ankle is grossly swollen with fracture blisters beginning to form and significant soft-tissue contusion, but it is a closed injury and neurovascularly intact. What is the most appropriate initial surgical strategy?

options.label: A
options.text: Immediate definitive open reduction and internal fixation through an anteromedial approach

options.label: B
options.text: Spanning external fixation now, with delayed definitive fixation once the soft tissues have recovered

options.label: C
options.text: Primary ankle arthrodesis

options.label: D
options.text: Below-knee plaster cast and elevation until union

options.label: E
options.text: Primary hindfoot nail

explanation: High-energy pilon fractures are defined as much by the soft-tissue injury as by the bony pattern. Immediate definitive ORIF through swollen, blistered, contused skin carries a very high risk of catastrophic wound breakdown and deep infection. The accepted strategy is staged ('span, scan and plan'): apply a spanning external fixator to restore length, alignment and rotation and allow soft-tissue recovery (often with early fixation of the fibula if appropriate), obtain a CT to plan the articular reconstruction, and then carry out definitive fixation typically 10–21 days later once swelling has settled and skin wrinkles return. Primary arthrodesis and casting are not standard, and a hindfoot nail does not reconstruct the articular surface.
learningPoint: High-energy pilon fractures are managed in stages — spanning external fixator first, CT to plan, definitive fixation only once the soft-tissue envelope has recovered (the 'span, scan, plan' principle).
references: BOAST: Open Fractures / Ankle Fracture soft-tissue principles

subtopic: Ankle fracture — Weber/AO classification and syndesmosis
stem: A 35-year-old sustains an isolated fibular fracture. The radiograph shows the fracture at the level of the syndesmosis with the line running obliquely from anteroinferior to posterosuperior, and there is mild medial clear-space widening. In terms of the Lauge-Hansen mechanism, which injury pattern does this most likely represent?

options.label: A
options.text: Supination–adduction

options.label: B
options.text: Supination–external rotation

options.label: C
options.text: Pronation–abduction

options.label: D
options.text: Pronation–external rotation

options.label: E
options.text: Pronation–dorsiflexion

explanation: A spiral/oblique fibular fracture at the level of the syndesmosis (Weber B) running from anteroinferior to posterosuperior is the hallmark of the supination–external rotation (SER) mechanism, which is the commonest ankle fracture pattern. The sequence (Lauge-Hansen) progresses: anteroinferior tibiofibular ligament, then the spiral fibular fracture, then posterior malleolus/PITFL, then the medial side (deltoid rupture or medial malleolus fracture). Medial clear-space widening implies a deltoid/medial injury (a higher SER stage), making the fracture unstable. Weber A (below syndesmosis) corresponds to supination–adduction, and Weber C (above syndesmosis, e.g. Maisonneuve) corresponds to pronation–external rotation with a high syndesmotic disruption.
learningPoint: A spiral Weber B fibular fracture (anteroinferior to posterosuperior) with medial widening is a supination–external rotation injury — medial-side disruption makes it unstable.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Foot & Ankle (Ankle fractures)

stem: During open reduction and internal fixation of a Weber C fibular fracture, you have anatomically fixed the fibula and wish to determine whether the syndesmosis requires stabilisation. Which intra-operative assessment is the most reliable way to detect residual syndesmotic instability?

options.label: A
options.text: Static AP radiograph measuring the tibiofibular overlap only

options.label: B
options.text: Intra-operative external-rotation and lateral hook stress under fluoroscopy, ideally compared with the contralateral side

options.label: C
options.text: Palpation of the syndesmosis for tenderness

options.label: D
options.text: Measuring the medial clear space on a single mortise view

options.label: E
options.text: Routine syndesmotic screw insertion in every Weber C fracture regardless of testing

explanation: After fixing the fibula to length and rotation, syndesmotic stability should be assessed dynamically: a lateral 'hook' (Cotton) test pulling the fibula laterally and an external-rotation stress test under fluoroscopy, looking for widening of the medial clear space and tibiofibular clear space and reduced tibiofibular overlap, ideally compared with the uninjured side. Static single-view measurements are insensitive because instability is often only revealed under stress, and reduction in the coronal plane on a mortise view can look normal despite sagittal/rotational malreduction. Routinely screwing every Weber C without testing over-treats stable injuries; conversely, a stress test that demonstrates instability mandates stabilisation (screw or suture-button). CT is the most accurate way to confirm reduction post-operatively.
learningPoint: Assess the syndesmosis dynamically — fluoroscopic lateral hook and external-rotation stress tests (compared with the other side) detect instability that static single views miss.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Foot & Ankle (Syndesmosis)

stem: A 30-year-old is brought in after a head-on car crash with a displaced talar neck fracture. The radiograph shows the talar body subluxated at both the subtalar joint and dislocated from the ankle (tibiotalar) joint, but the talar head remains located at the talonavicular joint. What is the Hawkins classification, and what does it imply for the risk of avascular necrosis?

options.label: A
options.text: Hawkins I — minimal AVN risk

options.label: B
options.text: Hawkins II — moderate AVN risk

options.label: C
options.text: Hawkins III — high AVN risk

options.label: D
options.text: Hawkins IV — highest AVN risk

options.label: E
options.text: Hawkins II — negligible AVN risk because the blood supply is unaffected

explanation: The Hawkins classification of talar neck fractures correlates with disruption of the talar blood supply and hence AVN risk: I = undisplaced (≈0–15% AVN); II = displaced with subtalar subluxation/dislocation (≈20–50%); III = displaced with dislocation of the body from both the subtalar AND ankle joints (≈80–100%); IV = type III plus talonavicular dislocation (highest). Here the body is dislocated from both the subtalar and ankle joints while the head/talonavicular joint is preserved — this is Hawkins III, carrying a very high risk of AVN because the body's tenuous blood supply is largely disrupted. Displaced talar neck fractures require urgent anatomical reduction and stable fixation. The Hawkins sign (subchondral lucency at 6–8 weeks) indicates preserved vascularity and a lower likelihood of AVN.
learningPoint: Hawkins III (talar body dislocated from both subtalar and ankle joints) carries ~80–100% AVN risk; a positive Hawkins sign at 6–8 weeks suggests preserved vascularity.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Foot & Ankle (Talus)

stem: A 25-year-old snowboarder presents with a painful, swollen ankle after a fall in which the foot was forcibly dorsiflexed and inverted. He is tender over the lateral process of the talus, just below the tip of the lateral malleolus. Initial ankle radiographs are reported as normal, but pain persists and he cannot weight-bear. What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.label: A
options.text: Repeat plain radiographs in 2 weeks

options.label: B
options.text: CT scan of the hindfoot

options.label: C
options.text: Ultrasound of the lateral ligaments

options.label: D
options.text: Nerve conduction studies

options.label: E
options.text: Weight-bearing whole-foot radiograph only

explanation: A fracture of the lateral process of the talus ('snowboarder's fracture') is classically caused by dorsiflexion and inversion and is frequently missed on plain radiographs, where it can be mistaken for a simple lateral ligament sprain. In a patient with focal lateral-process tenderness, persistent pain and inability to weight-bear despite 'normal' films, CT is the investigation of choice to confirm the fracture and to size/displace the fragment, which determines whether it is treated non-operatively (small, undisplaced) or by fixation/excision (large or displaced). Missing it leads to subtalar arthritis and chronic pain. Repeat plain films delay diagnosis and are less sensitive than CT.
learningPoint: Lateral-process talar (snowboarder's) fractures mimic an ankle sprain and are often radiographically occult — image with CT when focal lateral-process tenderness persists.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Foot & Ankle (Talus)

subtopic: Calcaneal fracture — Böhler's angle and associated injuries
stem: A 40-year-old falls from scaffolding and lands on his heels, sustaining bilateral intra-articular calcaneal fractures. On the lateral radiograph Böhler's angle is reduced to 10 degrees. A reduced Böhler's angle indicates significant articular collapse, and the mechanism should prompt a search for associated injuries. Which associated injury must you specifically exclude?

options.label: A
options.text: Femoral neck fracture

options.label: B
options.text: Thoracolumbar (spinal) burst fracture

options.label: C
options.text: Scaphoid fracture

options.label: D
options.text: Clavicle fracture

options.label: E
options.text: Patellar fracture

explanation: Böhler's angle (normally 20–40°) is reduced when the posterior facet collapses, quantifying the severity of an intra-articular calcaneal fracture (a normal angle is roughly 20–40°; values below ~20° indicate collapse). The axial-loading mechanism of a fall from height transmits force up the skeleton, so calcaneal fractures — particularly bilateral ones — are classically associated with thoracolumbar spinal (burst) fractures, and the spine must be specifically examined and imaged. Other parts of the 'axial load' chain (tibial plateau/pilon) may also be injured, but the spinal fracture is the classic must-not-miss association. Calcaneal fractures also carry a high risk of compartment syndrome of the foot and significant soft-tissue swelling that influences the timing of surgery.
learningPoint: A fall-from-height calcaneal fracture (low Böhler's angle) mandates assessment of the thoracolumbar spine for an associated burst fracture; also watch for foot compartment syndrome.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Foot & Ankle (Calcaneus)

stem: An intra-articular calcaneal fracture is being classified on a coronal CT through the widest part of the posterior facet. The Sanders classification is based on the number and position of articular fracture lines through the posterior facet. A fracture with three fracture lines producing four articular fragments (including the constant sustentacular fragment) corresponds to which Sanders type?

options.label: A
options.text: Sanders I

options.label: B
options.text: Sanders II

options.label: C
options.text: Sanders III

options.label: D
options.text: Sanders IV

options.label: E
options.text: Sanders V

explanation: The Sanders classification is determined on the coronal CT image at the widest portion of the posterior facet and counts the articular fracture lines: type I = all non-displaced fractures (regardless of number of lines); type II = one fracture line / two fragments; type III = two lines / three fragments; type IV = three or more lines / four or more fragments, i.e. highly comminuted (and there is no Sanders V). Four articular fragments produced by three fracture lines is therefore Sanders IV, which has the worst prognosis and the highest rate of post-traumatic subtalar arthritis; some surgeons favour primary subtalar fusion for these. The constant medial sustentacular ('constant') fragment retains its soft-tissue attachments and serves as a reduction reference.
learningPoint: Sanders is classified on the coronal CT at the widest posterior facet: IV = 4+ articular fragments (most comminuted, worst prognosis); the sustentacular fragment is the constant reduction key.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Foot & Ankle (Calcaneus)

stem: A 26-year-old reports persistent midfoot pain and difficulty weight-bearing after a twisting injury with the foot plantarflexed. There is plantar bruising over the midfoot and tenderness across the tarsometatarsal joints. Non-weight-bearing radiographs appear near-normal. Which single radiographic finding, if present, is most specific for a Lisfranc injury?

options.label: A
options.text: A 'fleck sign' — a small bony avulsion in the first–second intermetatarsal space

options.label: B
options.text: A joint effusion in the ankle

options.label: C
options.text: A fracture of the fifth metatarsal base

options.label: D
options.text: Loss of the calcaneal pitch

options.label: E
options.text: An os trigonum

explanation: The Lisfranc ligament runs from the medial cuneiform to the base of the second metatarsal. A small avulsion fragment in the first–second intermetatarsal space — the 'fleck sign' — represents a bony avulsion of this ligament and is highly specific for a Lisfranc injury. Plantar midfoot ecchymosis is another classic clinical clue. Because non-weight-bearing films can look normal, weight-bearing or stress radiographs (and CT/MRI) are used to reveal diastasis between the medial cuneiform and the second metatarsal base or malalignment of the second tarsometatarsal joint. Missed Lisfranc injuries lead to midfoot collapse and post-traumatic arthritis; subtle injuries are easily overlooked.
learningPoint: The 'fleck sign' (avulsion in the 1st–2nd intermetatarsal space) and plantar midfoot bruising are highly suggestive of a Lisfranc injury; use weight-bearing/stress views as non-weight-bearing films can be normal.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Foot & Ankle (Lisfranc)

stem: A fit 34-year-old has a displaced, unstable Lisfranc injury with diastasis between the medial cuneiform and the second metatarsal base on weight-bearing films, but no significant articular comminution. What is the most appropriate definitive management?

options.label: A
options.text: Below-knee cast immobilisation and non-weight-bearing for 6 weeks

options.label: B
options.text: Anatomical open reduction with stable fixation (e.g. transarticular screws / dorsal plate) of the tarsometatarsal complex

options.label: C
options.text: Percutaneous K-wiring left in situ permanently

options.label: D
options.text: Primary complete midfoot arthrodesis of all tarsometatarsal joints

options.label: E
options.text: Functional bracing with immediate full weight-bearing

explanation: A displaced, unstable Lisfranc injury requires anatomical reduction and stable internal fixation; even small degrees of residual displacement at the tarsometatarsal joint predict post-traumatic arthritis and poor function. Standard treatment is open reduction with stable fixation of the second tarsometatarsal joint and adjacent columns (transarticular screws and/or dorsal bridge plates), restoring the alignment of the medial and middle columns. Closed treatment is reserved for genuinely stable, non-displaced injuries confirmed on stress/weight-bearing imaging. Primary arthrodesis is an accepted option particularly for purely ligamentous or highly comminuted injuries, but a displaced injury without significant articular comminution in a young patient is appropriately treated by anatomical ORIF. Permanent K-wires alone provide inadequate stability for the columns.
learningPoint: An unstable/displaced Lisfranc injury needs anatomical reduction and stable fixation — residual displacement predicts arthritis; primary fusion is favoured for purely ligamentous or highly comminuted patterns.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Foot & Ankle (Lisfranc)

stem: A 23-year-old athlete presents with pain over the lateral border of the foot. Radiographs show a transverse fracture at the metaphyseal–diaphyseal junction of the fifth metatarsal, distal to the tuberosity, within roughly 1.5 cm of the base (a Jones fracture, zone 2). Compared with an avulsion fracture of the tuberosity (zone 1), why is this zone clinically important?

options.label: A
options.text: It always requires emergency surgery within 24 hours

options.label: B
options.text: It lies in a relative watershed area and has a higher rate of delayed/non-union

options.label: C
options.text: It heals faster than a zone 1 avulsion because of the rich metaphyseal blood supply

options.label: D
options.text: It is invariably associated with a Lisfranc injury

options.label: E
options.text: It is best treated by excision of the fragment

explanation: Fifth metatarsal base fractures are described in three zones: zone 1 (tuberosity avulsion, the most common, usually heals well with symptomatic treatment), zone 2 (the Jones fracture at the metaphyseal–diaphyseal junction involving the 4th–5th intermetatarsal articulation) and zone 3 (proximal diaphyseal stress fracture). The Jones fracture lies in a relative vascular watershed, which is why it has a notably higher rate of delayed union and non-union than a zone 1 avulsion. Because of this, and especially in athletes who wish to return to sport, intramedullary screw fixation is frequently offered rather than prolonged non-weight-bearing casting. Zone 1 avulsions rarely need surgery. The fragment is not excised.
learningPoint: The Jones fracture (zone 2, metaphyseal–diaphyseal junction of the 5th metatarsal) lies in a vascular watershed and has a high non-union rate — consider screw fixation, especially in athletes; zone 1 avulsions usually heal with symptomatic care.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Foot & Ankle (Metatarsals)

stem: Theme: management of metatarsal and forefoot injuries. For each scenario, select the single most appropriate management option from the list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Symptomatic treatment in a supportive shoe / walking boot with weight-bearing as tolerated

options.label: B
options.text: Intramedullary screw fixation

options.label: C
options.text: Open reduction and plate fixation

options.label: D
options.text: Relative rest and activity modification for a stress fracture

options.label: E
options.text: Closed reduction and K-wire fixation

options.label: F
options.text: Below-knee non-weight-bearing cast for 6 weeks

options.label: G
options.text: Urgent fasciotomy of the foot compartments

emiLeads.stem: A 60-year-old with a minimally displaced isolated fracture of the third metatarsal shaft after dropping a weight on the foot; alignment is acceptable and the adjacent metatarsals are intact.
A 21-year-old elite runner with several weeks of insidious forefoot pain and a hairline fracture of the second metatarsal neck on radiographs, with no acute trauma.
A 25-year-old footballer with an acute Jones fracture (zone 2 fifth metatarsal) who wishes to return to competitive sport as quickly as possible.
A crush injury of the forefoot with multiple displaced metatarsal shaft fractures, a tensely swollen foot, pain out of proportion and pain on passive toe movement.

explanation: Most isolated, minimally displaced metatarsal shaft fractures with intact neighbouring rays are stable and heal with symptomatic weight-bearing treatment in a stiff-soled shoe or walking boot. Metatarsal stress fractures (classically the second metatarsal — a 'march' fracture) are treated with relative rest and graded return to activity. An acute Jones fracture in an athlete is commonly fixed with an intramedullary screw to reduce the non-union risk and speed return to sport. A tensely swollen, crushed forefoot with pain out of proportion and pain on passive stretch raises acute compartment syndrome of the foot, which — once diagnosed — is a surgical emergency requiring fasciotomy (note that foot compartment release remains debated, but the recognition of the clinical syndrome is the testable point). Plate fixation and K-wiring are reserved for displaced/unstable shaft or neck fractures not fitting the above.
learningPoint: Stable isolated metatarsal shaft fractures heal with symptomatic weight-bearing; second-metatarsal stress (march) fractures need rest; athlete Jones fractures favour screw fixation; a tense crushed forefoot = consider compartment syndrome.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Foot & Ankle (Metatarsals)

stem: An 82-year-old falls and sustains a periprosthetic femoral fracture around a cemented total hip replacement. Radiographs show the fracture is located around the stem tip; assessment of the implant shows the femoral stem is loose, but there is good remaining proximal bone stock. Using the Vancouver classification, how is this fracture classified and what is the principle of management?

options.label: A
options.text: Vancouver A — fix the trochanteric fragment

options.label: B
options.text: Vancouver B1 — open reduction and internal fixation alone, retaining the stem

options.label: C
options.text: Vancouver B2 — revision of the femoral stem to a longer component (± fixation)

options.label: D
options.text: Vancouver B3 — revision with proximal femoral reconstruction/allograft for deficient bone

options.label: E
options.text: Vancouver C — fix the fracture independently of the well-fixed prosthesis

explanation: The Vancouver classification of periprosthetic femoral fractures around a hip stem considers fracture location, implant stability and bone stock. Type A is around the trochanters (AG greater, AL lesser). Type B is around or just distal to the stem and is subdivided by stem fixation and bone quality: B1 = stem well-fixed (treat with ORIF, retaining the stem); B2 = stem loose with adequate bone stock (revise the stem to a longer, usually uncemented, component, with supplementary fixation as needed); B3 = stem loose with poor/deficient proximal bone (revision with proximal femoral reconstruction, allograft or a proximal femoral replacement). Type C is well distal to the stem tip (treat as a fracture, ignoring the well-fixed prosthesis). A loose stem with good bone stock around the stem is therefore B2, managed by stem revision rather than fixation alone — the most commonly examined teaching point, as treating a B2 as a B1 (ORIF of a loose stem) is a classic error.
learningPoint: Vancouver B2 = fracture around a LOOSE stem with adequate bone — treat by revising the stem, not by ORIF alone (the classic exam trap is fixing a loose-stem fracture as if it were B1).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Adult Reconstruction (Periprosthetic fractures)

stem: A 78-year-old with a well-functioning, well-fixed total knee replacement sustains a low-energy supracondylar periprosthetic distal femoral fracture above the femoral component. The prosthesis is stable and there is adequate bone stock, but the fracture is displaced. Assuming the femoral component has an 'open box' design that accepts a nail, which fixation options are most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Immediate revision of the femoral component to a hinged prosthesis

options.label: B
options.text: Distal femoral locking plate or retrograde intramedullary nail

options.label: C
options.text: Cannulated screw fixation alone

options.label: D
options.text: Long cephalomedullary nail from the hip

options.label: E
options.text: Conservative management in a cast brace

explanation: A periprosthetic supracondylar femoral fracture above a well-fixed, stable knee replacement with adequate bone stock is treated by fixation, preserving the prosthesis. The two mainstays are a distal femoral locking plate (which works regardless of component design and gives good distal fixation) or a retrograde intramedullary nail — the latter only if the femoral component is an 'open-box' design with an intercondylar notch that accommodates the nail and there is enough distal bone to lock it. Both are acceptable; the question stem specifies an open-box component, so a retrograde nail is feasible alongside plating. Revision to a hinge is reserved for a loose component or unreconstructable distal bone, and a long cephalomedullary nail from the hip will not reach/control a distal supracondylar fracture. Screws alone are insufficient for a displaced supracondylar fracture, and conservative management of a displaced fracture in this group risks malunion and immobility complications.
learningPoint: A displaced supracondylar fracture above a well-fixed TKR with good bone is fixed (not revised): distal femoral locking plate, or a retrograde nail if the component is an open-box design with adequate distal bone.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Adult Reconstruction (Periprosthetic distal femur)

stem: Theme: Salter-Harris classification of physeal injuries. For each radiographic description of a fracture involving the growth plate of a skeletally immature child, select the SINGLE most appropriate Salter-Harris type from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Salter-Harris type I

options.label: B
options.text: Salter-Harris type II

options.label: C
options.text: Salter-Harris type III

options.label: D
options.text: Salter-Harris type IV

options.label: E
options.text: Salter-Harris type V

options.label: F
options.text: Salter-Harris type VI

emiLeads.stem: A 12-year-old has a distal radius fracture in which the fracture line passes transversely through the physis and then exits proximally through a triangular fragment of the metaphysis (the Thurston-Holland fragment), with no epiphyseal involvement.
A 13-year-old sustains a fracture line that begins at the joint surface, crosses the epiphysis, traverses the physis and exits through the metaphysis, crossing all zones of the growth plate.

A 4-year-old presents with a tender, swollen wrist after a fall. Radiographs appear essentially normal apart from minimal widening of the physis; the injury is a pure transverse separation through the growth plate with no bony fragment.
A 10-year-old has an intra-articular fracture in which the line runs from the joint surface across the epiphysis to reach the physis, without any metaphyseal component.

A child develops progressive growth arrest after a high-energy axial-loading injury to the knee in which the initial radiographs showed no fracture line but a crush of the physis is suspected in retrospect.

explanation: The Salter-Harris system describes the relationship of the fracture line to the physis. Type I is a pure transverse separation through the growth plate (often radiographically subtle). Type II, the commonest, exits through a metaphyseal (Thurston-Holland) fragment, sparing the epiphysis. Type III is intra-articular through epiphysis and physis only. Type IV crosses epiphysis, physis and metaphysis (all zones), and being intra-articular and trans-physeal carries a high risk of growth arrest and articular incongruity if not anatomically reduced. Type V is a crush of the physis, often diagnosed retrospectively when growth arrest develops. The mnemonic SALTR (Slipped, Above, Lower, Through, Rammed/cRush) aids recall.
learningPoint: Salter-Harris II exits through a metaphyseal fragment (commonest); types III and IV are intra-articular and need anatomical reduction; type V is a crush diagnosed by later growth arrest.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Paediatric Trauma chapter

stem: An 11-year-old boy is reviewed 14 months after a Salter-Harris type IV fracture of the distal tibia that was treated non-operatively in a cast. He has developed a progressive varus deformity of the ankle. Imaging confirms a localised bony bridge across the medial part of the distal tibial physis, estimated on CT to involve approximately 30% of the physeal cross-sectional area, in a child with at least 2 years of growth remaining. What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Continued observation, as the deformity will spontaneously remodel with growth

options.label: B
options.text: Excision of the physeal bar (bar resection) with interposition of fat or other material

options.label: C
options.text: Immediate completion of the epiphysiodesis across the entire distal tibial physis and contralateral epiphysiodesis

options.label: D
options.text: Supramalleolar corrective osteotomy alone, leaving the bar in situ

options.label: E
options.text: Below-knee cast immobilisation to allow the deformity to correct

explanation: A symptomatic, progressive deformity from a partial physeal bar in a child with substantial growth remaining and a bar occupying less than roughly 50% of the physis is an indication for physeal bar resection with interposition (commonly fat, but also cranioplast or silicone) to attempt restoration of growth. A bar of this size with this much remaining growth will continue to cause progressive deformity, so observation is inappropriate. Completing the epiphysiodesis (with a contralateral procedure to manage limb-length discrepancy) is the alternative when the bar is too large (>50%) or growth remaining is minimal. A corrective osteotomy may be needed in addition for established deformity but, used alone with the bar left in situ, the tethering would simply recur with further growth.
learningPoint: A partial physeal bar (<~50% of the physis) with significant growth remaining is treated by bar resection and interposition; large bars or minimal remaining growth favour completion of the epiphysiodesis.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Paediatric Trauma / Growth Disturbance chapter

stem: A 7-year-old child sustains a completely displaced extension-type (Gartland III) supracondylar fracture of the humerus. On careful examination before any intervention, the child is unable to make the 'OK' sign because of inability to flex the interphalangeal joint of the thumb and the distal interphalangeal joint of the index finger, and reports no sensory loss. The hand is warm and well perfused. Which nerve is most likely to have been injured?

options.label: A
options.text: Anterior interosseous branch of the median nerve

options.label: B
options.text: Posterior interosseous branch of the radial nerve

options.label: C
options.text: Ulnar nerve

options.label: D
options.text: Superficial branch of the radial nerve

options.label: E
options.text: Main trunk of the median nerve with sensory loss over the palm

explanation: Inability to flex the IP joint of the thumb (flexor pollicis longus) and the DIP joint of the index finger (the index-finger slip of flexor digitorum profundus) with NO sensory deficit is the classic picture of an anterior interosseous nerve (AIN) palsy. The AIN is a pure motor branch of the median nerve and is the most commonly injured nerve in extension-type supracondylar fractures, where the proximal metaphyseal spike displaces anteriorly. Because it is purely motor, sensation is preserved, which distinguishes it from a main median trunk injury (which would also cause sensory loss). PIN injury would affect digital extension, and ulnar nerve injury (more associated with flexion-type fractures or medial wire placement) affects the intrinsics. Most traction neurapraxias of the AIN recover spontaneously over weeks to a few months.
learningPoint: AIN palsy (loss of FPL and index FDP, no sensory loss; the 'OK sign' cannot be made) is the commonest nerve injury in extension-type supracondylar fractures and usually recovers spontaneously.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Paediatric Trauma chapter
BOAST: Supracondylar Fractures of the Humerus in Children (BOA/BSCOS)

stem: A 5-year-old undergoes closed reduction and percutaneous Kirschner-wire fixation of a Gartland III extension-type supracondylar humeral fracture. The surgeon is choosing the wire configuration. Which statement best reflects current evidence and UK practice regarding wire configuration and the risk of iatrogenic ulnar nerve injury?

options.label: A
options.text: Crossed medial and lateral wires provide the greatest stability and carry no greater risk of ulnar nerve injury than lateral-only wires

options.label: B
options.text: Two or three divergent lateral-entry wires provide adequate stability for most fractures and avoid the ulnar nerve risk associated with a medial wire

options.label: C
options.text: A single lateral wire is biomechanically sufficient and is the standard construct

options.label: D
options.text: A medial wire should always be placed first, before any lateral wire, to maximise stability

options.label: E
options.text: Wire configuration has no influence on either construct stability or the risk of nerve injury

explanation: Two (or three) divergent lateral-entry wires that engage both the medial and lateral columns provide biomechanical stability adequate for the majority of supracondylar fractures while avoiding the iatrogenic ulnar nerve injury risk associated with a medially placed wire, since the ulnar nerve lies in close proximity to the medial epicondyle. Crossed-wire constructs are biomechanically slightly more stable in torsion but carry a recognised risk of ulnar nerve injury from the medial wire; if a medial wire is used (e.g. for a very unstable or comminuted fracture), techniques to protect the nerve (mini-open medial incision, placing the medial wire last with the elbow less flexed) are advised. A single lateral wire is generally insufficient for a completely displaced fracture.
learningPoint: Divergent lateral-entry wires engaging both columns give adequate stability for most supracondylar fractures and avoid the ulnar nerve risk of a medial wire; if a medial wire is needed, protect the nerve.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Paediatric Trauma chapter
BOAST: Supracondylar Fractures of the Humerus in Children (BOA/BSCOS)

stem: A 4-year-old falls and presents with lateral elbow swelling and tenderness. Radiographs show a fracture of the lateral humeral condyle. On an internal-oblique view the fracture gap measures 3 mm at the articular margin, and there is concern about whether the cartilaginous articular hinge is intact. Which statement about the assessment and management of this injury is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: All lateral condyle fractures can be treated in a cast regardless of displacement because they reliably remodel

options.label: B
options.text: Displacement of more than 2 mm, or evidence that the articular surface is not intact, is an indication for surgical fixation because these are intra-articular, often Salter-Harris IV, fractures prone to nonunion

options.label: C
options.text: Lateral condyle fractures are extra-articular and therefore minor, and never require fixation

options.label: D
options.text: The main complication of a missed or untreated displaced lateral condyle fracture is cubitus varus, not nonunion

options.label: E
options.text: An arthrogram is contraindicated in assessing the articular congruity of these fractures

explanation: Lateral condyle fractures are intra-articular injuries (often Salter-Harris type IV through the capitellar/trochlear cartilage) and are notorious for displacement, delayed union and nonunion because the forearm extensor origin exerts a continuous distracting pull and the fracture is bathed in synovial fluid. Minimally displaced fractures (<2 mm) with an intact articular cartilage hinge can be treated in a cast with close radiographic surveillance, but displacement greater than 2 mm, or an incongruent/incomplete articular surface (which an arthrogram can usefully demonstrate intraoperatively), is an indication for open or closed reduction and K-wire (or screw) fixation. Untreated displaced fractures characteristically progress to nonunion, which in turn can cause a progressive cubitus VALGUS deformity and tardy ulnar nerve palsy (in contrast to the cubitus varus classically associated with malunited supracondylar fractures).
learningPoint: Lateral condyle fractures are intra-articular (often SH IV) and prone to nonunion; displacement >2 mm or a non-intact articular surface mandates fixation, and untreated nonunion can cause cubitus valgus with tardy ulnar nerve palsy.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Paediatric Trauma chapter

stem: A 12-year-old sustains an elbow dislocation while wrestling, which reduces spontaneously. Post-reduction radiographs show a displaced medial epicondyle avulsion fracture. There is concern that the fragment may be incarcerated within the joint. Which clinical or radiographic feature would most strongly mandate operative intervention in this injury?

options.label: A
options.text: Any displacement of the fragment greater than 2 mm in an asymptomatic child

options.label: B
options.text: Incarceration of the epicondyle fragment within the joint, an ulnar nerve deficit, or gross valgus instability (e.g. in a throwing athlete)

options.label: C
options.text: The presence of an associated elbow dislocation that has already reduced, irrespective of fragment position

options.label: D
options.text: The age of the child being over 10 years

options.label: E
options.text: A fragment that lies over the medial epicondylar region with a congruent joint and no neurological deficit

explanation: Absolute indications for fixing a paediatric medial epicondyle fracture are an intra-articular incarcerated fragment (the avulsed epicondyle, to which the flexor-pronator mass and ulnar collateral ligament attach, can be trapped in the joint after a dislocation and must be retrieved), an ulnar nerve deficit, and gross valgus instability that would compromise an overhead-throwing athlete. The acceptable amount of displacement for non-operative treatment is debated and measurement is notoriously unreliable, so a small displacement alone in an asymptomatic child is a relative, not absolute, indication. A reduced dislocation by itself, or a congruent joint with the fragment in an acceptable position and no nerve deficit, does not in itself mandate surgery. Note that the medial epicondyle is an apophysis (a traction physis); it is the LAST ossification centre to fuse, and an apparently 'absent' epicondyle on a post-trauma film must raise suspicion of an incarcerated intra-articular fragment.
learningPoint: Operate on a medial epicondyle fracture for an incarcerated intra-articular fragment, an ulnar nerve deficit, or valgus instability in a throwing athlete; an 'absent' epicondyle after dislocation may be trapped in the joint.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Paediatric Trauma chapter

stem: A 6-year-old falls onto an outstretched hand. Radiographs of the forearm show a plastic deformation (bowing) of the ulna with an anteriorly dislocated radial head (Bado type I). The referring department has requested fixation of the ulna. On the initial radiograph the radiocapitellar line does not pass through the centre of the capitellum. Which principle is most important in the management of this paediatric Monteggia injury?

options.label: A
options.text: The radial head dislocation should be reduced and held with a trans-capitellar Kirschner wire as the primary procedure

options.label: B
options.text: Anatomical restoration of ulnar length and alignment is the key to achieving and maintaining reduction of the radial head, which usually then reduces by closed means

options.label: C
options.text: The radial head dislocation is unimportant and only the ulna needs treatment

options.label: D
options.text: The radiocapitellar line is unreliable in children and should be ignored

options.label: E
options.text: Open reduction of the radial head with annular ligament reconstruction is required in all acute paediatric Monteggia injuries

explanation: The cardinal principle of Monteggia management at any age is that the radial head dislocation is a consequence of the ulnar deformity: restoring the correct length and alignment of the ulna (here correcting the plastic deformation, by intramedullary fixation or osteotomy as required) almost always allows the radial head to reduce and, critically, to STAY reduced by closed means. The radiocapitellar line, which should pass through the centre of the capitellum on every view, is a key check for adequacy of reduction and is the most commonly missed sign when a paediatric Monteggia lesion is overlooked. Reducing the radial head while leaving the ulna malreduced will fail. Trans-capitellar wiring and acute annular ligament reconstruction are not required in the acute setting once ulnar alignment is restored and the radiocapitellar joint is congruent; ligament reconstruction is reserved for chronic/missed cases.
learningPoint: In a paediatric Monteggia injury, anatomically correct the ulna and the radial head follows; always check the radiocapitellar line on every view to avoid missing the dislocation.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Paediatric Trauma chapter

stem: A 7-year-old is referred 4 months after a forearm injury that was initially treated as an 'isolated ulna fracture'. The parents report a persistent bump at the elbow and a loss of full extension. Radiographs now show a healed, slightly malunited (apex-anterior) ulna and a chronically anteriorly dislocated radial head with early convexity (doming) of the capitellum. What is the most appropriate management of this chronic/missed Monteggia lesion?

options.label: A
options.text: Accept the deformity and observe, as a chronically dislocated radial head in a child remodels and never causes symptoms

options.label: B
options.text: Closed manipulation of the radial head in the clinic under entonox

options.label: C
options.text: Corrective (often over-correcting) osteotomy of the ulna to restore alignment, with open reduction of the radial head and annular ligament reconstruction as required

options.label: D
options.text: Excision of the radial head as the primary procedure in this skeletally immature child

options.label: E
options.text: Application of an above-elbow cast in supination for 6 weeks

explanation: A chronic (missed) Monteggia lesion in a child cannot be reduced closed because the soft tissues and ulnar malunion have remodelled. The accepted reconstructive strategy is a corrective ulnar osteotomy (frequently deliberately over-corrected/lengthened with an apex-posterior or apex-dorsal angulation to push the radial head back and keep it reduced), combined with open reduction of the radial head and, where the annular ligament is incompetent, its reconstruction (e.g. with a strip of triceps fascia). Doing nothing risks progressive radial head deformity, pain, restricted rotation, deformity and a late tardy posterior interosseous nerve palsy. Radial head excision is avoided in the growing child because it causes wrist pain, cubitus valgus and instability; it is reserved for the symptomatic skeletally mature patient. Closed manipulation and casting will not reduce a chronic dislocation.
