learningPoint: A chronic/missed paediatric Monteggia lesion is reconstructed by corrective (often over-correcting) ulnar osteotomy with open reduction of the radial head and annular ligament reconstruction; avoid radial head excision in a growing child.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Paediatric Trauma chapter

subtopic: Paediatric both-bone forearm / distal radius fracture
stem: An 8-year-old sustains a closed, displaced diaphyseal both-bone forearm fracture. After closed reduction in theatre, the angulation of both bones is reduced to within accepted limits but the surgeon is considering whether fixation is needed because of concern about re-displacement. Which statement best reflects current UK paediatric practice for diaphyseal both-bone forearm fractures?

options.label: A
options.text: Diaphyseal forearm fractures remodel as reliably as distal radius fractures, so any degree of residual angulation is acceptable

options.label: B
options.text: Diaphyseal (mid-shaft) fractures remodel poorly and rotational malalignment does not correct, so unstable or unacceptably angulated fractures are stabilised, commonly with flexible intramedullary nails

options.label: C
options.text: Rigid compression plating of both bones is the standard of care for all paediatric diaphyseal forearm fractures

options.label: D
options.text: Loss of forearm rotation is unimportant in children because they compensate at the shoulder

options.label: E
options.text: A malunited diaphyseal forearm fracture in a child cannot affect pronation or supination

explanation: Remodelling potential is greatest near an active physis and for deformity in the plane of joint motion; it is LIMITED in the mid-diaphysis and rotational malalignment does not remodel at all. Consequently, diaphyseal both-bone forearm fractures have stricter acceptance criteria than metaphyseal distal radius fractures, and an unstable fracture or one that cannot be held within acceptable angulation/rotation is stabilised, most commonly with elastic stable (flexible) intramedullary nails in this age group, which restore and hold alignment with minimal soft-tissue disruption. Rigid plating is generally reserved for older/adolescent patients or specific indications rather than being the default for every child. Forearm malunion (especially loss of the radial bow or rotational malalignment) directly restricts pronation and supination, which is functionally important and not compensated at the shoulder.
learningPoint: Mid-diaphyseal forearm fractures remodel poorly and rotation does not correct, so unstable/unacceptable ones are fixed (typically with flexible IM nails); malunion limits forearm rotation.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Paediatric Trauma chapter

stem: A 9-year-old falls and sustains a dorsally angulated greenstick fracture of the distal radial metaphysis with 25 degrees of dorsal angulation and no rotational deformity. The child has at least 4 years of growth remaining and the deformity lies in the plane of wrist flexion-extension. After discussion, which statement about remodelling and management is most accurate?

options.label: A
options.text: This metaphyseal deformity, lying in the plane of joint motion in a child with substantial growth remaining, has good remodelling potential and may be managed by cast immobilisation (with or without reduction) rather than mandatory operative fixation

options.label: B
options.text: Rotational deformity remodels better than angular deformity at this site

options.label: C
options.text: No distal radius deformity remodels, so all such fractures require anatomical surgical reduction

options.label: D
options.text: Remodelling is greatest in the mid-diaphysis and least near the physis

options.label: E
options.text: Deformity perpendicular to the plane of joint motion remodels better than deformity within the plane of motion

explanation: Three factors maximise remodelling: proximity to an active physis, the amount of growth remaining, and deformity that lies in the plane of the adjacent joint's motion. A distal radial metaphyseal fracture in a young child with years of growth left, angulated in the sagittal (flexion-extension) plane, satisfies all three and therefore has excellent remodelling potential; such fractures are frequently managed non-operatively (cast, with reduction if needed) and reliably correct. Rotational malalignment, by contrast, does NOT remodel. Remodelling is greatest near the physis (not in the diaphysis), and deformity in the plane of joint motion remodels better than deformity out of that plane. This contrasts deliberately with diaphyseal forearm fractures, where remodelling is limited and stricter criteria apply.
learningPoint: Remodelling is best near an active physis, with much growth remaining, and for deformity in the plane of joint motion; distal radius metaphyseal angulation therefore often needs only a cast, whereas rotation never remodels.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Paediatric Trauma chapter

subtopic: Paediatric femoral shaft fracture by age
stem: Theme: Age-appropriate management of the isolated, closed paediatric femoral shaft (diaphyseal) fracture. For each child, select the SINGLE most appropriate definitive management option, assuming an otherwise stable child with an isolated injury and an acceptable soft-tissue envelope. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Pavlik harness or simple gallows/traction-then-spica

options.label: B
options.text: Early hip spica cast (with or without prior traction)

options.label: C
options.text: Flexible (elastic stable) intramedullary nailing

options.label: D
options.text: Submuscular bridge plating or a lateral-entry rigid intramedullary nail

options.label: E
options.text: Antegrade reamed piriformis-entry intramedullary nail

options.label: F
options.text: Safeguarding referral and skeletal survey as the immediate priority

emiLeads.stem: A healthy 9-month-old who is not yet walking sustains a transverse mid-shaft femoral fracture; the history is consistent, witnessed and entirely plausible for the injury.
A 4-year-old sustains a closed, minimally shortened mid-shaft femoral fracture after a fall from playground equipment.
A 7-year-old of average build sustains a closed, transverse, length-stable mid-diaphyseal femoral fracture.
A 13-year-old, heavy-for-age adolescent sustains a length-UNSTABLE, comminuted mid-shaft femoral fracture.
A 10-month-old who is not yet cruising presents with a spiral femoral shaft fracture; the history is vague, has changed between carers, and is not consistent with the stated mechanism.

explanation: Management of the paediatric femoral shaft fracture is age- and weight-dependent. Infants (roughly 0-6 months and up to walking age) do well with a Pavlik harness or gallows traction then a spica, given their enormous remodelling capacity. Toddlers/young children (approximately 6 months to 5-6 years) are typically treated with early hip spica casting (with prior traction if shortened). School-age children (around 5/6-11 years) with length-stable fractures are ideally managed with flexible (elastic stable) intramedullary nails. Older/heavier children and adolescents, or those with length-UNSTABLE comminuted patterns where flexible nails would not maintain length, are better treated with submuscular bridge plating or a lateral-entry rigid intramedullary nail. A reamed PIRIFORMIS-entry nail is avoided in the growing child because of the risk of femoral head avascular necrosis, so it does not feature here. Finally, any femoral fracture in a non-ambulant infant with an inconsistent or changing history must trigger safeguarding processes and a skeletal survey BEFORE being treated as a simple isolated injury.
learningPoint: Paediatric femoral shaft management is age/weight-led: harness/traction-spica in infants, spica in toddlers, flexible nails in school-age length-stable fractures, and plating/lateral-entry nail in adolescents or length-unstable patterns; avoid piriformis-entry nails in growing children and always exclude NAI in non-ambulant infants.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Paediatric Trauma chapter
BSCOS / BOAST paediatric femoral fracture guidance

stem: A skeletal survey is performed in a 7-month-old infant in whom non-accidental injury is suspected. The radiologist is asked which findings are most specific for inflicted (non-accidental) injury. Which of the following injury patterns has the HIGHEST specificity for non-accidental injury in an infant?

options.label: A
options.text: A single linear parietal skull fracture

options.label: B
options.text: A mid-shaft transverse fracture of the clavicle

options.label: C
options.text: Classic metaphyseal lesions ('corner' or 'bucket-handle' fractures) and posterior rib fractures

options.label: D
options.text: A torus (buckle) fracture of the distal radius

options.label: E
options.text: A single non-displaced fracture of the distal tibial metaphysis presenting with a clear accidental history

explanation: Certain fracture patterns carry high specificity for inflicted injury. The classic metaphyseal lesion (CML), seen as a 'corner' or 'bucket-handle' fracture, results from shearing/torsional forces (grabbing, pulling, shaking) on the immature metaphysis and is highly specific for abuse in a non-ambulant infant. Posterior rib fractures (typically from anteroposterior chest compression during squeezing) are likewise highly specific, as the costovertebral mechanism is rarely reproduced by accidental injury or routine CPR in infants. Other high-specificity injuries include fractures of different ages (suggesting repeated trauma), sternal and scapular fractures. By contrast, a simple linear skull fracture, a clavicular fracture, and a distal radial buckle fracture are common accidental injuries with LOW specificity. Specificity must always be interpreted alongside the history, the child's developmental stage, and the overall clinical picture, in conjunction with the safeguarding team.
learningPoint: Classic metaphyseal (corner/bucket-handle) lesions and posterior rib fractures are among the most specific radiographic markers of non-accidental injury; simple skull, clavicle and buckle fractures are low-specificity.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Paediatric Trauma chapter (child abuse)
RCPCH Child Protection guidance / NICE NG76 (child maltreatment)

stem: An orthopaedic registrar is teaching foundation doctors about features in the history and examination that should raise concern for non-accidental injury when a child presents with a fracture. Which single feature should most strongly prompt activation of child-protection (safeguarding) processes?

options.label: A
options.text: A witnessed, clearly described fall from a trampoline in a 9-year-old that fully accounts for a distal radius fracture

options.label: B
options.text: A fracture in a child whose developmental stage is incompatible with the stated mechanism, or a history that is absent, vague, inconsistent or changes between accounts

options.label: C
options.text: A torus fracture of the distal radius in a 6-year-old who tripped while running in the playground

options.label: D
options.text: A toddler's (oblique tibial) fracture in a newly walking 15-month-old with a consistent history of a twisting stumble

options.label: E
options.text: Prompt presentation to hospital immediately after the injury

explanation: The strongest single red flag is a mismatch between the injury and the child: a fracture whose mechanism is incompatible with the child's developmental stage (most importantly a long-bone fracture in a non-ambulant infant), or a history that is absent, vague, internally inconsistent, or that changes between carers. Other concerning features include a delay in seeking medical attention, multiple injuries or injuries of different ages, injuries in unusual sites, and inappropriate carer behaviour or affect. By contrast, a clearly witnessed and consistent mechanism that fully explains the injury (such as a trampoline fall, a playground buckle fracture, or a classic 'toddler's fracture' in a newly walking child with a typical twisting history) is reassuring, and prompt rather than delayed presentation is also reassuring. When concern exists, the duty is to treat the injury and simultaneously involve the safeguarding team, document carefully and arrange appropriate investigation (e.g. skeletal survey).
learningPoint: The key NAI red flag is incongruity between the injury and the child's developmental stage, or a history that is absent, inconsistent or changing; a clear consistent mechanism and prompt presentation are reassuring.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Paediatric Trauma chapter (child abuse)
RCPCH Child Protection guidance / NICE NG76 (child maltreatment)

stem: A 13-year-old sustains a twisting ankle injury. Plain radiographs are difficult to interpret: the AP view appears to show a Salter-Harris III pattern (a vertical epiphyseal fracture line) while the lateral view appears to show a Salter-Harris II pattern (a posterior metaphyseal fragment). A CT scan is obtained to characterise the injury and plan treatment. Which injury does this most likely represent, and what is the key management principle?

options.label: A
options.text: A juvenile Tillaux fracture, which is always extra-articular and never needs reduction

options.label: B
options.text: A triplane fracture; CT defines the fragments (commonly two or three parts) and the size of any intra-articular step, and an articular displacement of more than about 2 mm requires anatomical reduction and fixation

options.label: C
options.text: A pure Salter-Harris I separation that can simply be casted

options.label: D
options.text: An ankle dislocation without fracture

options.label: E
options.text: A pilon fracture of adult type requiring a spanning external fixator as definitive treatment

explanation: A fracture that looks like a Salter-Harris III on the AP view and a Salter-Harris II on the lateral view is the classic description of a TRIPLANE fracture, so named because its components lie in the axial, sagittal and coronal planes. These are transitional fractures, occurring in the narrow window (around 12-15 years) as the distal tibial physis closes asymmetrically (from central to anterolateral, the medial part closing first, the anterolateral 'Tillaux' fragment last). CT is the investigation of choice to determine whether the injury is two-part or three-part and, crucially, to measure the intra-articular step: because these are intra-articular fractures in an adolescent with little remodelling potential left, an articular gap or step exceeding roughly 2 mm warrants anatomical reduction and fixation (often percutaneous screws), whereas truly minimally displaced fractures may be treated in a cast. The juvenile Tillaux fracture (a Salter-Harris III avulsion of the anterolateral epiphysis by the anterior tibiofibular ligament) is the related, two-dimensional transitional injury and is itself intra-articular and reduced if displaced, so option A is wrong.
learningPoint: A fracture that is SH III on AP and SH II on lateral is a triplane fracture; CT characterises the fragments and articular step, and >~2 mm of intra-articular displacement in these transitional injuries requires anatomical reduction and fixation.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Paediatric Trauma chapter

stem: A 14-year-old girl sustains an external rotation injury to the ankle. Radiographs and CT show an isolated Salter-Harris III fracture of the anterolateral corner of the distal tibial epiphysis, with the fragment displaced and a 4 mm intra-articular gap. Understanding the closure sequence of the distal tibial physis, which mechanism best explains this specific fragment, and how should it be managed?

options.label: A
options.text: It is avulsed by the anterior inferior tibiofibular ligament because the anterolateral physis is the last part to fuse; being intra-articular and displaced >2 mm it requires reduction and fixation

options.label: B
options.text: It is a traction injury of the Achilles tendon and should be treated in an equinus cast

options.label: C
options.text: It is avulsed by the deltoid ligament medially and never needs fixation

options.label: D
options.text: It is a purely extra-articular apophyseal avulsion that can be ignored

options.label: E
options.text: It results from the medial malleolus fusing last, so reduction is unnecessary

explanation: The juvenile Tillaux fracture is a transitional injury of late adolescence. The distal tibial physis closes asymmetrically over roughly 18 months, beginning centrally and progressing medially first, so that the ANTEROLATERAL portion of the physis is the last to fuse and is therefore the only part still 'open' and vulnerable when an external rotation force is transmitted through the anterior inferior tibiofibular ligament. That ligament avulses the anterolateral epiphyseal fragment, producing a Salter-Harris III (intra-articular) pattern. Because it is intra-articular and this patient has minimal growth remaining (limiting remodelling), a displacement or gap exceeding about 2 mm (here 4 mm) requires anatomical reduction (closed or open) and fixation, typically with an epiphyseal screw. The Achilles and deltoid mechanisms are incorrect, and the fracture is intra-articular rather than a benign extra-articular avulsion.
learningPoint: The juvenile Tillaux fracture is an anterolateral epiphyseal avulsion by the anterior inferior tibiofibular ligament (the last physeal segment to fuse); being intra-articular, displacement >2 mm needs anatomical reduction and screw fixation.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Paediatric Trauma chapter

stem: Under the current UK Newborn and Infant Physical Examination (NIPE) programme, infants without an unstable hip on clinical examination are still referred for a screening hip ultrasound on the basis of recognised risk factors. Which single feature is an accepted indication for selective ultrasound screening in an infant whose Barlow and Ortolani tests are normal?

options.label: A
options.text: Birth weight below the 10th centile

options.label: B
options.text: Breech presentation at or after 36 weeks' gestation, irrespective of mode of delivery

options.label: C
options.text: Maternal gestational diabetes

options.label: D
options.text: Delivery by elective caesarean section for a cephalic presentation

options.label: E
options.text: A second-degree relative treated for DDH in infancy

explanation: The two nationally accepted NIPE risk factors that trigger a selective screening ultrasound in a clinically stable hip are (1) breech presentation at or after 36 weeks (or breech at delivery if earlier) regardless of whether the baby was eventually delivered cephalic or by caesarean, and (2) a first-degree family history of a hip problem in early life treated by splint/harness or surgery. A multiple pregnancy is sometimes added locally. Breech at term is therefore the correct indication. Low birth weight, maternal diabetes and a second-degree (rather than first-degree) family history are not stand-alone indications under the national programme, and elective caesarean for a cephalic baby carries no extra risk. The scan is performed at around 4–6 weeks of age so that maternal hormone-related laxity has settled and Graf measurements are reliable.
learningPoint: NIPE selective hip ultrasound is triggered by breech at/after 36 weeks and a first-degree family history; clinical exam covers everyone else.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Orthopaedics (DDH)
UK NIPE Screening Programme Handbook — newborn hip screening

stem: A 5-week-old girl referred for breech presentation has a hip ultrasound. The sonographer reports a bony roof (alpha) angle of 52° and a cartilaginous roof (beta) angle of 70°, with the femoral head well covered by the bony acetabulum. The contralateral hip has an alpha angle of 59°. According to the Graf classification, how should the reported hip be categorised and managed?

options.label: A
options.text: Graf type I — normal mature hip; discharge

options.label: B
options.text: Graf type IIa — physiologically immature hip; repeat ultrasound at around 6 weeks rather than treat

options.label: C
options.text: Graf type IIc — start abduction splinting immediately

options.label: D
options.text: Graf type III — eccentric hip requiring urgent treatment

options.label: E
options.text: Graf type IV — dislocated hip requiring closed reduction

explanation: Graf typing is driven principally by the alpha (bony roof) angle. Alpha 60° or more is type I (mature). An alpha of 50–59° in an infant under 3 months is type IIa, a 'physiologically immature' hip that is age-appropriate and is managed by surveillance with a repeat scan in 4–6 weeks rather than by harness treatment, as the great majority mature spontaneously. With alpha 52° at 5 weeks and a covered head, this hip is IIa. Type IIc is alpha 43–49° (a decentring hip on the threshold of instability) and warrants abduction treatment; type III (alpha <43°, everted labrum, eccentric head) and type IV (dislocated, head displaced caudally) are clearly pathological and need active reduction/splinting. Treating a IIa hip would over-medicalise a normal variant and exposes the child to avascular necrosis risk for no benefit.
learningPoint: Graf IIa (alpha 50–59°, age <3 months) is an immature but normal hip — re-scan, do not splint.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Orthopaedics (DDH ultrasound)

subtopic: DDH — Pavlik harness positioning and complications
stem: A 9-week-old girl with a Graf type III dislocated left hip is treated in a Pavlik harness. The harness is correctly applied to hold the hips flexed and to allow gentle abduction. Three weeks later she is noted to have weak active knee extension and the left hip remains dislocated on ultrasound. Which single statement best explains the most likely complication and the correct response?

options.label: A
options.text: Excessive abduction has compromised the medial circumflex femoral artery; reduce abduction to lower the risk of avascular necrosis

options.label: B
options.text: Excessive hip flexion (beyond about 100–110°) has caused a femoral nerve palsy; reduce the degree of flexion and reassess nerve function

options.label: C
options.text: The harness has caused an obturator nerve palsy; this is harmless and the harness should be continued unchanged

options.label: D
options.text: Knee weakness indicates a femoral fracture; obtain radiographs and immobilise the limb

options.label: E
options.text: This is expected disuse weakness; continue the harness and review in a further 3 weeks

explanation: Femoral nerve palsy is a recognised Pavlik harness complication caused by excessive hip flexion, classically when the anterior straps hold the hips beyond roughly 100–110°. It presents as loss of active quadriceps (knee extension) function. Management is to relax the flexion and confirm recovery of quadriceps power before continuing; persistent palsy is a reason to abandon the harness, and an irreducible hip should not be left flexed in a non-functioning harness. The other dreaded harness complications are avascular necrosis (driven by forced/excessive ABDUCTION stretching the medial circumflex vessels — hence the harness allows, but does not force, abduction) and inferior/posterior dislocation. 'Pavlik harness disease' describes erosion of the posterior acetabular wall when a frankly dislocated hip is left in a harness that never achieves reduction — after about 3 weeks without reduction the harness should be abandoned, not continued.
learningPoint: Quadriceps weakness in a Pavlik harness = femoral nerve palsy from excess flexion; AVN comes from excess abduction. Abandon the harness if not reduced by ~3 weeks.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Orthopaedics (DDH treatment)

subtopic: DDH — closed reduction and safe zone
stem: An 11-month-old girl with an irreducible dislocated hip undergoes examination under anaesthetic and closed reduction with arthrography. At reduction the hip is concentric only when held in marked abduction, and dislocates if abduction is reduced below 60°, giving a very narrow stable arc. An arthrogram shows a medial dye pool of about 7 mm. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Apply a hip spica in maximal (around 80°) abduction to keep the hip reduced

options.label: B
options.text: Accept the closed reduction and spica at 90° flexion and 45° abduction within the safe zone

options.label: C
options.text: Proceed to open reduction, as the safe (Ramsey) zone is unacceptably narrow and the medial dye pool indicates an inadequate concentric reduction

options.label: D
options.text: Perform a percutaneous adductor tenotomy alone and re-attempt closed reduction in 6 weeks

options.label: E
options.text: Abandon any reduction and review with repeat imaging at age 2 years

explanation: A successful closed reduction needs a CONCENTRIC reduction sitting within a wide Ramsey 'safe zone' — the arc between the position of redislocation and the position of excessive abduction (beyond which avascular necrosis risk rises). Here the hip is only stable in extreme abduction and redislocates below 60°, so the safe zone is dangerously narrow; spica-ing in maximal abduction to hold it would court AVN, which is the principal complication of treatment. The arthrographic medial dye pool of about 7 mm (a widened gap between the ossific femoral head and the teardrop, with failure of the head to seat against the medial wall) signals a non-concentric reduction, often from an inverted limbus, interposed ligamentum teres/pulvinar or hourglass capsule. Both findings indicate that closed treatment will fail, so open reduction (with adductor and possibly psoas release, capsulorrhaphy and clearance of obstructing soft tissues) is the correct choice. An adductor tenotomy may widen the safe zone but does not address an obstructed concentric reduction with a medial pool.
learningPoint: Reduce concentrically inside a WIDE safe zone; a narrow arc or a large medial dye pool means open reduction, not a deeper spica.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Orthopaedics (DDH — closed/open reduction)
Ramsey safe zone; arthrographic assessment of DDH

stem: A 3-year-old girl with a previously dislocated hip has had an open reduction. Persistent acetabular dysplasia is present, with an acetabular index of 38° and a steep, deficient anterolateral acetabulum. The hip is concentrically reduced and congruent, and she has good hip range of movement. Which single pelvic procedure is most appropriate to correct the acetabular deficiency?

options.label: A
options.text: Salter (innominate) osteotomy, redirecting the acetabulum to improve anterolateral cover

options.label: B
options.text: Chiari medial displacement osteotomy

options.label: C
options.text: Shelf (slotted acetabular augmentation) procedure

options.label: D
options.text: Periacetabular (Ganz/Bernese) osteotomy

options.label: E
options.text: Schanz subtrochanteric abduction osteotomy

explanation: In a young child (roughly 18 months to 6 years) with a concentric, congruent, reducible hip and persistent dysplasia, a Salter innominate osteotomy is the standard reorientation procedure: it hinges the acetabulum through the symphysis on the pubic side and the osteotomy through the ilium, redirecting cover anteriorly and laterally, and can improve the acetabular index by roughly 10–15°. It requires a mobile symphysis and a reducible/congruent joint. The Pemberton and Dega (incomplete pericapsular) osteotomies are alternatives that reduce acetabular volume by hinging on the triradiate cartilage. Chiari and shelf procedures are salvage/augmentation operations that create fibrocartilaginous cover for an incongruent or subluxed hip and are reserved for older patients or where reorientation is not feasible. The Bernese periacetabular osteotomy is for skeletally mature patients with a closed triradiate cartilage. A Schanz osteotomy is a femoral salvage for an unreducible high hip, not an acetabular correction.
learningPoint: Young child, concentric congruent hip, dysplasia → Salter reorientation; Chiari/shelf are salvage for incongruent/older hips; PAO needs a closed triradiate.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Orthopaedics (acetabular osteotomies)

stem: Theme: surgical correction of acetabular dysplasia. For each clinical scenario, select the single most appropriate osteotomy or salvage procedure from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Salter (innominate) osteotomy

options.label: B
options.text: Pemberton pericapsular osteotomy

options.label: C
options.text: Dega osteotomy

options.label: D
options.text: Bernese periacetabular (Ganz) osteotomy

options.label: E
options.text: Chiari medial displacement osteotomy

options.label: F
options.text: Shelf (slotted acetabular augmentation)

options.label: G
options.text: Triple innominate osteotomy

options.label: H
options.text: Varus derotation femoral osteotomy

emiLeads.stem: A 28-year-old woman with symptomatic developmental dysplasia, a closed triradiate cartilage, a congruent concentric hip and no significant osteoarthritis, in whom maximal acetabular reorientation with preserved posterior column continuity is desired.

A 4-year-old with global acetabular deficiency and excessive acetabular volume after open reduction, where reduction of acetabular volume by hinging on the open triradiate cartilage is required and the symphysis is to be left undisturbed.

A 14-year-old with painful subluxation and an incongruent, non-spherical femoral head in whom joint-preserving reorientation is not possible and fibrocartilaginous medial cover with limb medialisation is the goal.

A 2-and-a-half-year-old with persistent anterolateral dysplasia, a concentric congruent reducible hip and a mobile symphysis, where redirection of the whole acetabulum to improve anterolateral cover is required.

A 7-year-old with posterolateral acetabular deficiency from neuromuscular hip displacement, where a directionally selective incomplete osteotomy hinging on the triradiate cartilage can address the deficient segment.

explanation: Reorientation procedures redirect an intact, congruent acetabulum: the Salter hinges the whole innominate on the symphysis to improve anterolateral cover in younger children, while the Bernese/Ganz PAO achieves powerful multiplanar reorientation in the skeletally mature (closed triradiate) congruent hip. Reshaping/volume-reducing procedures hinge on the open triradiate cartilage: the Pemberton bends the whole acetabular roof to reduce volume globally, and the Dega is a directionally selective incomplete osteotomy useful for the posterolateral deficiency of neuromuscular dysplasia. Salvage procedures provide non-articular cover for incongruent or subluxed hips: the Chiari medialises the limb and creates a bony shelf of fibrocartilage, while the slotted shelf augments deficient lateral cover. A varus derotation femoral osteotomy addresses the femoral side and is not an acetabular correction. Matching the procedure to triradiate status, congruency and the location of the deficiency is the core decision.
learningPoint: Reorientation (Salter/PAO) needs congruency; reshaping (Pemberton/Dega) needs an open triradiate; Chiari/shelf are salvage for incongruent hips.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Orthopaedics (pelvic osteotomies)

subtopic: Perthes — Herring lateral pillar and prognosis
stem: An 8-year-old boy has Legg-Calvé-Perthes disease in the fragmentation phase. On the anteroposterior pelvis the lateral third of the capital femoral epiphysis (the lateral pillar) is reduced in height to about 60% of the original, with no extension into the central pillar. Using the Herring lateral pillar classification, how is this hip graded and what does it imply for prognosis?

options.label: A
options.text: Group A — lateral pillar height fully maintained; uniformly good outcome

options.label: B
options.text: Group B — more than 50% of lateral pillar height maintained; outcome is strongly age-dependent

options.label: C
options.text: Group B/C border — a thin lateral pillar with about 50% maintained; outcome is poor in older children

options.label: D
options.text: Group C — less than 50% of lateral pillar height maintained; uniformly good outcome

options.label: E
options.text: The lateral pillar classification cannot be applied during the fragmentation phase

explanation: The Herring lateral pillar classification is assessed at the fragmentation stage on the AP radiograph by the height of the lateral third of the epiphysis relative to the uninvolved side: group A, no loss; group B, more than 50% retained; group B/C border, a narrow (~2–3 mm) or poorly ossified pillar with about 50% retained; group C, less than 50% retained. A pillar at ~60% with the central pillar spared is group B. Prognosis depends on the interaction of lateral pillar group and age: group A does well at any age, group C tends to do poorly, and group B (and the B/C border) outcomes hinge strongly on skeletal/chronological age — children under about 6–8 years generally do well, while older children with B or B/C border hips do markedly worse and are the group most likely to benefit from containment surgery. This age dependence is the key teaching point of the prospective Perthes trial data.
learningPoint: Herring B = >50% lateral pillar retained; its outcome is age-driven, which is why older Herring B hips are offered containment.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Orthopaedics (Perthes — Herring classification)
Herring JA — lateral pillar classification & prognosis

stem: A 9-year-old boy with Legg-Calvé-Perthes disease attends with worsening pain and a reducing range of hip movement. His radiographs show lateral extrusion of the epiphysis beyond the acetabular margin, calcification lateral to the epiphysis, a horizontal physis and a radiolucent 'V' in the lateral epiphysis and adjacent metaphysis. Which single statement best reflects the significance of these findings and the rationale for management?

options.label: A
options.text: These are Catterall 'head-at-risk' signs; with subluxation and loss of containment in an older child, containment surgery is more likely to be indicated

options.label: B
options.text: These signs indicate the disease has healed and the child can be discharged

options.label: C
options.text: Gage's sign and lateral calcification are protective features predicting an excellent outcome

options.label: D
options.text: The findings mandate immediate total hip replacement

options.label: E
options.text: These are features of slipped capital femoral epiphysis rather than Perthes

explanation: The classic Catterall head-at-risk signs are lateral subluxation/extrusion of the epiphysis, Gage's sign (a radiolucent V-shaped defect in the lateral epiphysis and adjacent metaphysis), calcification lateral to the epiphysis, a horizontal (more transverse) growth plate, and metaphyseal cysts/rarefaction. They indicate a femoral head that is escaping containment and is at risk of deformity and incongruity. The overriding principle of Perthes treatment is containment — keeping the soft, biologically plastic head deeply seated in the acetabulum so it remodels spherically. Older children (typically over about 8 years) with head-at-risk signs, loss of containment and poorer lateral pillar grades are the group in whom operative containment (femoral varus osteotomy and/or pelvic/Salter osteotomy) is most likely to be offered, whereas younger children with contained hips are often managed with movement, analgesia and observation. These are not healing or protective features, and they are not features of SCFE.
learningPoint: Head-at-risk signs (extrusion, Gage's sign, lateral calcification, horizontal physis, metaphyseal cysts) flag loss of containment — the trigger to consider containment surgery, especially in older children.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Orthopaedics (Perthes — Catterall head-at-risk)

stem: A 6-year-old boy is diagnosed with early Legg-Calvé-Perthes disease. His parents ask about the disease process. Which single statement most accurately describes the underlying pathophysiology and natural history of Perthes disease?

options.label: A
options.text: It is an idiopathic osteonecrosis of the capital femoral epiphysis that passes through avascular, fragmentation, reossification and remodelling phases

options.label: B
options.text: It is a primary infection of the femoral head requiring prolonged antibiotics

options.label: C
options.text: It is caused by a fixed dislocation of the hip present from birth

options.label: D
options.text: It is an inflammatory arthritis that typically affects both hips symmetrically and simultaneously

options.label: E
options.text: It is a malignant tumour of the proximal femoral physis

explanation: Legg-Calvé-Perthes disease is an idiopathic osteonecrosis (avascular necrosis) of the capital femoral epiphysis in a growing child, most often a boy aged roughly 4–8 years, classically of smaller stature with delayed bone age. The disrupted blood supply leads to a recognised radiographic sequence: an initial avascular/sclerotic phase, a fragmentation (resorptive) phase during which the head is mechanically weakest and most prone to deformity, a reossification phase, and finally healing/remodelling. The aim of treatment is to preserve sphericity and congruity (containment) during the plastic phases. It is typically unilateral — truly symmetrical, simultaneous bilateral involvement should prompt consideration of an epiphyseal dysplasia (e.g. multiple epiphyseal dysplasia or hypothyroidism) instead. It is neither infective, congenital dislocation, an inflammatory arthritis nor a tumour.
learningPoint: Perthes is idiopathic AVN of the capital epiphysis passing through avascular → fragmentation → reossification → remodelling; symmetrical bilateral disease suggests an epiphyseal dysplasia.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Orthopaedics (Perthes pathophysiology)

subtopic: SCFE — stable vs unstable and AVN risk
stem: A 13-year-old boy presents acutely after a fall, unable to bear any weight even with crutches, with a severely painful, externally rotated right hip. Radiographs confirm a slipped capital femoral epiphysis. Using the Loder classification and current understanding of avascular necrosis risk, which single statement is most accurate?

options.label: A
options.text: This is an unstable slip; the rate of avascular necrosis is substantially higher than for a stable slip

options.label: B
options.text: This is a stable slip because he reached hospital walking earlier in the day

options.label: C
options.text: Stability is defined by the radiographic slip angle, not by the ability to bear weight

options.label: D
options.text: Avascular necrosis risk is identical for stable and unstable slips

options.label: E
options.text: Unstable slips have a lower avascular necrosis rate because they reduce more easily

explanation: The Loder classification is functional, not radiographic: a slip is STABLE if the child can weight-bear (with or without crutches) and UNSTABLE if they cannot bear weight even with aids. Inability to weight-bear here makes this an unstable slip. The distinction matters because avascular necrosis — the most feared complication of SCFE — is strongly linked to instability: AVN rates for unstable slips are high (reported broadly in the region of 20–50%), whereas stable slips carry a very low AVN risk (close to nil). The presumed mechanism is disruption or kinking of the retinacular vessels (the posterosuperior branches of the medial circumflex femoral artery) at the moment of acute displacement, possibly compounded by tamponade from an intracapsular haematoma. Stability is judged by weight-bearing ability, not by the slip angle, and unstable slips do not have a protective lower AVN rate.
learningPoint: Loder stability is functional (can the child weight-bear?); unstable slips carry a far higher AVN risk than stable slips.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Orthopaedics (SCFE — Loder classification)
Loder RT — stability and AVN in SCFE

stem: A 12-year-old boy with a stable, moderate slipped capital femoral epiphysis is to undergo in-situ fixation with a single cannulated screw. Which single principle best describes correct screw placement to stabilise the slip while minimising complications?

options.label: A
options.text: The screw should start on the anterior femoral neck and cross the physis perpendicular to it, ending centrally in the epiphysis without penetrating the joint

options.label: B
options.text: The screw should be placed eccentrically in the superolateral quadrant of the head to maximise purchase

options.label: C
options.text: Multiple screws should always be used for a stable slip to allow continued growth

options.label: D
options.text: The screw threads should straddle the physis to act as a lag screw and compress the slip acutely

options.label: E
options.text: The screw should be aimed into the posterosuperior quadrant of the epiphysis for the strongest fixation

explanation: Because the epiphysis slips posteriorly and inferiorly, the femoral neck rotates so that the central axis of the epiphysis now points anteriorly. To place a single screw perpendicular to the physis and finishing in the centre of the epiphysis, the entry point therefore moves onto the ANTERIOR neck (the more the slip, the more anterior and proximal the start point). A central, perpendicular position resists displacement best and keeps the screw away from the joint surface. Eccentric or peripheral placement, particularly into the posterosuperior quadrant, risks the lateral epiphyseal (retinacular) vessels and AVN, and a screw tip in any quadrant that penetrates the subchondral bone risks unrecognised joint penetration and chondrolysis (the 'approach-withdraw' blind-spot phenomenon). All threads should cross the physis into the epiphysis to achieve fixation; the goal is in-situ stabilisation, not acute compression/reduction of the slip, which would endanger the blood supply.
learningPoint: In SCFE, the slipped epiphysis points anteriorly, so a single screw starts on the anterior neck and aims central and perpendicular — avoid the posterosuperior quadrant and joint penetration.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Orthopaedics (SCFE — in-situ fixation)

stem: A 10-year-old boy is diagnosed with a unilateral slipped capital femoral epiphysis and undergoes in-situ pinning. He has no identified endocrine disorder. His parents ask whether the normal hip should also be fixed. Which single factor most strongly favours prophylactic fixation of the contralateral, asymptomatic hip?

options.label: A
options.text: Young chronological age with an open triradiate cartilage and a low modified Oxford/Risser bone age

options.label: B
options.text: A body mass index below the 50th centile

options.label: C
options.text: A family history of osteoarthritis of the hip

options.label: D
options.text: Skeletal maturity with a closed triradiate cartilage

options.label: E
options.text: The slip on the affected side being stable rather than unstable

explanation: Roughly one in five to one in three unilateral slips will later slip on the other side, and the great majority of contralateral slips occur within about 18 months of the first. The strongest predictor of a future contralateral slip is skeletal IMMATURITY at presentation — a young chronological age with an open triradiate cartilage and a low bone-age score (e.g. a low modified Oxford bone score or a high posterior sloping angle). The younger and less mature the child, the higher the residual risk and the stronger the argument for prophylactic in-situ fixation of the asymptomatic hip; conversely, a child who is already near skeletal maturity has little remaining at-risk growth. An identifiable endocrinopathy (hypothyroidism, growth-hormone treatment, renal osteodystrophy, hypogonadism) also lowers the threshold for prophylactic fixation. BMI below average, a family history of osteoarthritis and the stability of the index slip are not the key drivers of contralateral slip risk.
learningPoint: Skeletal immaturity (open triradiate, low bone age) — especially with an endocrinopathy — is the main reason to fix the contralateral hip prophylactically.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Orthopaedics (SCFE — prophylactic fixation)
Modified Oxford bone score / posterior sloping angle in SCFE

stem: Theme: complications of paediatric hip disorders and their treatment. For each clinical description, select the single most likely complication from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head

options.label: B
options.text: Chondrolysis

options.label: C
options.text: Femoral nerve palsy

options.label: D
options.text: Residual acetabular dysplasia

options.label: E
options.text: Femoroacetabular (cam) impingement

options.label: F
options.text: Leg-length discrepancy from physeal arrest

options.label: G
options.text: Coxa magna

options.label: H
options.text: Re-dislocation

emiLeads.stem: Eighteen months after in-situ pinning of a stable slipped capital femoral epiphysis, a previously comfortable adolescent develops increasing stiffness and pain; radiographs show symmetrical narrowing of the joint space to under 3 mm without femoral head collapse.

A residual prominence of the anterolateral femoral head-neck junction after a moderate slip causes groin pain and a positive anterior impingement test when the hip is flexed, adducted and internally rotated in a teenager who declined corrective osteotomy.

Following closed reduction and spica for developmental dysplasia of the hip, the ossific nucleus fails to grow, the femoral head later appears fragmented and flattened, and the greater trochanter overgrows relative to a short femoral neck.
A child with healed Perthes disease has an enlarged, broadened femoral head that is wider than the contralateral side but remains congruent within an adapted acetabulum.
An infant develops sudden loss of active knee extension three weeks into Pavlik harness treatment, attributable to the position the harness is holding the hip in.

explanation: Recognising the signature complications across paediatric hip conditions is core exam material. Symmetrical joint-space narrowing after SCFE pinning, without head collapse, is chondrolysis (loss of articular cartilage) — distinct from AVN, which produces segmental head collapse and sclerosis and follows unstable slips, forced reduction or unsafe screw placement. A residual head-neck 'bump' after a slip causes cam-type femoroacetabular impingement with a positive flexion-adduction-internal-rotation test. After DDH treatment, failure of the ossific nucleus to grow followed by fragmentation/flattening and relative trochanteric overgrowth is the picture of treatment-related AVN. Coxa magna is the broad, enlarged but congruent head that commonly results from healed Perthes. A femoral nerve palsy from excessive hip flexion in a Pavlik harness presents as loss of quadriceps power. Each pattern keys off the original diagnosis and the mechanism of injury to the joint.
learningPoint: Chondrolysis = symmetric cartilage loss; AVN = segmental collapse; a residual slip 'bump' = cam impingement; quadriceps weakness in a harness = femoral nerve palsy.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Orthopaedics (complications of DDH/Perthes/SCFE)
SCFE — chondrolysis and AVN; FAI after slip

stem: A 4-year-old child presents with a painless limp and limb shortening. Radiographs show a reduced neck-shaft angle with a vertically oriented physis and a triangular fragment of bone in the inferomedial femoral neck. The Hilgenreiner-epiphyseal (HE) angle is measured at approximately 65°. Which single statement best guides management of this developmental coxa vara?

options.label: A
options.text: A Hilgenreiner-epiphyseal angle above about 60° predicts progression and is an indication for a valgus-producing proximal femoral osteotomy

options.label: B
options.text: A Hilgenreiner-epiphyseal angle above 60° reliably resolves spontaneously, so observation is sufficient

options.label: C
options.text: The triangular metaphyseal fragment confirms a healing fracture from non-accidental injury

options.label: D
options.text: First-line treatment is a varus-producing femoral osteotomy to restore the neck-shaft angle

options.label: E
options.text: The deformity is in the acetabulum, so a Salter osteotomy is the treatment of choice

explanation: Developmental (infantile) coxa vara is characterised by a decreased neck-shaft angle, a vertically inclined capital physis, a defect/triangular ossific fragment in the inferomedial metaphyseal neck (the Fairbank triangle), and shortening with a Trendelenburg gait. The key prognostic measurement is the Hilgenreiner-epiphyseal (HE) angle — the angle between Hilgenreiner's line and the capital physis. An HE angle under about 45° tends to remodel and can be observed; 45–60° is a watchful zone; an HE angle greater than about 60° (as here) predicts relentless progression and is the accepted indication for surgery. Correction is by a VALGUS (and usually derotation) proximal femoral osteotomy to restore the neck-shaft angle, reorient the physis towards horizontal (reducing the shearing forces that perpetuate the varus) and restore abductor function and length. A varus osteotomy would worsen the deformity, the pathology is femoral rather than acetabular, and the metaphyseal fragment is part of the dysplasia, not a sign of abuse.
learningPoint: In congenital coxa vara, an HE angle >60° predicts progression and is treated with a VALGUS proximal femoral osteotomy.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Orthopaedics (congenital coxa vara)

subtopic: Septic arthritis vs transient synovitis — Kocher criteria
stem: A 5-year-old child presents with a 2-day limp and reluctance to weight-bear on the left hip. Temperature is 38.7°C, the child cannot bear weight, the serum white cell count is 13.5 × 10⁹/L and the ESR is 55 mm/hr. An ultrasound shows a hip joint effusion. Applying the Kocher criteria, which single statement best describes the predicted probability of septic arthritis and the most appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: All four Kocher predictors are met, giving a very high predicted probability of septic arthritis; urgent ultrasound-guided aspiration of the hip is indicated

options.label: B
options.text: Only two predictors are met, so septic arthritis is effectively excluded and the child can be discharged

options.label: C
options.text: The effusion alone confirms septic arthritis, so proceed straight to formal washout without aspiration

options.label: D
options.text: CRP is one of the four original Kocher criteria, and it must be normal here to allow discharge

options.label: E
options.text: A normal radiograph reliably excludes septic arthritis

explanation: The four original Kocher predictors distinguishing septic arthritis of the hip from transient synovitis are: inability to weight-bear, temperature above 38.5°C, ESR above 40 mm/hr, and serum white cell count above 12 × 10⁹/L. This child meets all four (non-weight-bearing, 38.7°C, ESR 55, WCC 13.5), which in the original series corresponds to a predicted probability of septic arthritis in the high-90% range. With a confirmed effusion on ultrasound, the correct next step is urgent aspiration of the hip (typically ultrasound-guided) for Gram stain, cell count and culture before commencing antibiotics, proceeding to arthrotomy/washout if pus is obtained or the diagnosis is confirmed. CRP is NOT one of the four original Kocher variables (though it is a useful additional predictor and is included in some modified criteria); the criteria are probabilistic and never 'exclude' sepsis at low counts; an effusion is non-specific; and early plain radiographs are frequently normal in septic arthritis.
learningPoint: Kocher = non-weight-bearing, temp >38.5°C, ESR >40, WCC >12 (not CRP); all four ≈ >95% probability of septic arthritis — aspirate.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Orthopaedics (septic arthritis vs transient synovitis)
Kocher MS — clinical prediction algorithm for septic hip

subtopic: Septic arthritis of the hip — surgical urgency and microbiology
stem: A 3-year-old child with a hot, irritable hip undergoes aspiration that yields frankly purulent fluid; a diagnosis of septic arthritis of the hip is made. Which single statement best reflects current UK management principles?

options.label: A
options.text: Urgent surgical drainage (arthrotomy or arthroscopic washout) is required, because the hip is at particular risk of cartilage destruction and femoral head osteonecrosis from raised intra-articular pressure

options.label: B
options.text: Intravenous antibiotics alone are sufficient and surgery should be avoided in a child of this age

options.label: C
options.text: Antibiotics should be withheld until all culture results are finalised at 48–72 hours

options.label: D
options.text: Staphylococcus aureus is a rare cause at this age and empirical cover against it is unnecessary

options.label: E
options.text: A single dose of intra-articular antibiotic at aspiration removes the need for any systemic treatment

explanation: Septic arthritis of the hip is a surgical emergency. The hip is intracapsular and the femoral head's blood supply (lateral epiphyseal/retinacular vessels) runs within the capsule, so a tense pyarthrosis raises intra-articular pressure and, together with the chondrolytic effect of bacterial and leucocyte enzymes, threatens both the articular cartilage and the viability of the femoral head (osteonecrosis). Established pus in the hip therefore warrants prompt surgical drainage and joint lavage in addition to antibiotics. Empirical intravenous antibiotics should be started immediately AFTER samples (aspirate and blood cultures) are taken — not deferred until cultures finalise — and the regimen must cover Staphylococcus aureus, the commonest organism across childhood (with Kingella kingae important in younger children and group A streptococcus also seen; local microbiology guidance and Hib/meningococcal vaccination status modify cover). Antibiotics alone or a single intra-articular dose are inadequate for an established purulent hip.
learningPoint: A purulent septic hip needs urgent surgical drainage plus prompt empirical anti-staphylococcal IV antibiotics after cultures — delay risks cartilage loss and femoral head osteonecrosis.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Orthopaedics (septic arthritis management)
BSCOS / BOAST — paediatric bone and joint infection

subtopic: Surgical approaches — posterolateral forearm (Thompson) / internervous plane
stem: A surgeon plans open reduction of a proximal-third radial shaft fracture using the Thompson (posterior) approach. In its proximal extent, between which two muscles does the true internervous plane lie?

options.label: A
options.text: Extensor carpi radialis brevis and extensor digitorum communis

options.label: B
options.text: Extensor carpi radialis brevis and extensor pollicis longus

options.label: C
options.text: Extensor digitorum communis and extensor carpi ulnaris

options.label: D
options.text: Brachioradialis and pronator teres

options.label: E
options.text: Anconeus and extensor carpi ulnaris

explanation: The proximal internervous plane of the Thompson approach lies between extensor carpi radialis brevis (radial nerve) and extensor digitorum communis (posterior interosseous nerve, PIN). Distally the plane runs between ECRB and extensor pollicis longus. The critical structure at risk is the PIN, which pierces and runs within supinator; supination of the forearm rotates the PIN laterally/anteriorly and away, but the nerve must be identified and protected before the radius is exposed proximally. Brachioradialis–pronator teres is the superficial interval of the anterior (Henry) approach, not Thompson.
learningPoint: Thompson proximal internervous plane = ECRB (radial n.) / EDC (PIN); protect the PIN within supinator.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Anatomy & Surgical Approaches

subtopic: Surgical approaches — anterolateral hip (Watson-Jones), structures at risk
stem: During a Watson-Jones (anterolateral) approach to the hip the surgeon develops the interval between tensor fasciae latae and gluteus medius. This approach is NOT truly internervous because both muscles bordering the interval share a common innervation. Which nerve is it?

options.label: A
options.text: Femoral nerve

options.label: B
options.text: Inferior gluteal nerve

options.label: C
options.text: Superior gluteal nerve

options.label: D
options.text: Obturator nerve

options.label: E
options.text: Nerve to quadratus femoris

explanation: Both tensor fasciae latae and gluteus medius are supplied by the superior gluteal nerve, so the Watson-Jones interval is intermuscular but not internervous. Aggressive proximal/posterior retraction within the abductors therefore risks denervating TFL and the anterior abductors, contributing to abductor weakness and a Trendelenburg gait. This contrasts with the Smith-Petersen approach, whose superficial interval (sartorius–TFL) IS internervous (femoral vs superior gluteal). The superior gluteal nerve emerges above piriformis through the greater sciatic foramen.
learningPoint: Watson-Jones (TFL/glut medius) is intermuscular but NOT internervous — both are superior gluteal nerve.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Surgical Approaches to the Hip

subtopic: Surgical anatomy — posteromedial ankle / tarsal tunnel order (Tom-Dick-Harry)
stem: While exposing the posteromedial ankle behind the medial malleolus, a surgeon identifies the contents of the tarsal tunnel deep to the flexor retinaculum. Listed from anterior (closest to the malleolus) to posterior, what is the correct order of the structures?

options.label: A
options.text: Tibialis posterior, flexor digitorum longus, posterior tibial artery and tibial nerve, flexor hallucis longus

options.label: B
options.text: Flexor hallucis longus, posterior tibial artery and tibial nerve, flexor digitorum longus, tibialis posterior

options.label: C
options.text: Posterior tibial artery and tibial nerve, tibialis posterior, flexor digitorum longus, flexor hallucis longus

options.label: D
options.text: Tibialis posterior, posterior tibial artery and tibial nerve, flexor digitorum longus, flexor hallucis longus

options.label: E
options.text: Flexor digitorum longus, tibialis posterior, flexor hallucis longus, posterior tibial artery and tibial nerve

explanation: From anterior to posterior behind the medial malleolus the structures are: Tibialis posterior, flexor Digitorum longus, posterior tibial Artery, tibial Nerve, flexor Hallucis longus — the mnemonic 'Tom, Dick, and a Very Nervous Harry'. The neurovascular bundle therefore lies between FDL and FHL. Knowing this order matters for safe placement of medial malleolar fixation, tarsal tunnel release, and posteromedial approaches to the talus/pilon, where the bundle must be protected.
learningPoint: Tarsal tunnel order (anterior→posterior): Tibialis posterior, FDL, artery, nerve, FHL ('Tom, Dick, And Very Nervous Harry').
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Foot & Ankle Anatomy

subtopic: Surgical anatomy — anterior approach to the shoulder, axillary nerve
stem: A surgeon performs an extended deltopectoral approach and needs to mobilise the subscapularis. Before tenotomy, a finger is swept along the anteroinferior border of subscapularis to protect a nerve that lies immediately deep to it before passing through the quadrangular space. Which manoeuvre most reliably moves this nerve away from the operative field during subscapularis release?

options.label: A
options.text: Internal rotation and adduction of the arm

options.label: B
options.text: External rotation and adduction of the arm

options.label: C
options.text: Abduction and external rotation of the arm

options.label: D
options.text: Forward flexion and internal rotation of the arm

options.label: E
options.text: Longitudinal traction with the arm in neutral

explanation: The axillary nerve runs along the inferior border of subscapularis before entering the quadrangular space; it is the structure most at risk during subscapularis tenotomy and inferior capsular release in anterior shoulder surgery. External rotation with the arm adducted draws the lesser tuberosity and subscapularis insertion laterally/anteriorly and moves the axillary nerve medially away from the tenotomy line, protecting it. The classic protective 'finger sweep' (Bankart's bony landmark) confirms the nerve's position inferiorly. Abduction/external rotation tensions and brings the nerve closer, increasing risk.
learningPoint: Axillary nerve hugs the inferior border of subscapularis; external rotation + adduction protects it during tenotomy.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Shoulder Anatomy & Approaches

subtopic: Surgical anatomy — distal humerus posterior approach, ulnar vs radial nerve
stem: A triceps-splitting posterior approach is extended distally to fix a complex distal humeral fracture. As dissection proceeds from the spiral groove towards the elbow, two major nerves cross the operative field. Which statement about their relationship to the bone is correct and surgically relevant?

options.label: A
options.text: The radial nerve crosses posteriorly from medial to lateral at roughly the junction of the proximal and middle thirds of the humerus

options.label: B
options.text: The ulnar nerve lies on the posterior surface of the lateral epicondyle

options.label: C
options.text: The radial nerve is intact within the lateral intermuscular septum until it reaches the lateral epicondyle

options.label: D
options.text: The ulnar nerve passes anterior to the medial epicondyle before entering the forearm

options.label: E
options.text: The radial nerve becomes most vulnerable as it passes through the medial intermuscular septum just above the olecranon fossa

explanation: In the posterior approach the radial nerve crosses the back of the humerus within the spiral (radial) groove, running from medial-proximal to lateral-distal, on average at about the junction of the middle and proximal thirds (some texts: middle third), then pierces the lateral intermuscular septum roughly 10 cm proximal to the lateral epicondyle to enter the anterior compartment — so it does not remain in the septum down to the epicondyle (C is wrong). The ulnar nerve lies behind the MEDIAL epicondyle in the cubital tunnel (B and D wrong). Both nerves must be identified and protected; the radial nerve is the principal structure at risk proximally and the ulnar nerve distomedially.
learningPoint: Radial nerve crosses the posterior humerus medial→lateral and exits the lateral intermuscular septum ~10 cm above the lateral epicondyle.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Humerus & Elbow Approaches

subtopic: Surgical approaches — internervous planes (upper & lower limb)
stem: Theme: internervous planes of standard orthopaedic approaches. For each approach, select the SINGLE pair of nerves whose territories define its (superficial) internervous plane. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Femoral nerve and superior gluteal nerve

options.label: B
options.text: Radial nerve and posterior interosseous nerve

options.label: C
options.text: Radial nerve and median nerve

options.label: D
options.text: Posterior interosseous nerve and ulnar nerve

options.label: E
options.text: Musculocutaneous nerve and radial nerve

options.label: F
options.text: Superior gluteal nerve and inferior gluteal nerve

options.label: G
options.text: Axillary nerve and radial nerve

options.label: H
options.text: Median nerve and ulnar nerve

emiLeads.stem: Smith-Petersen anterior approach to the hip (superficial interval).
Henry (anterior) approach to the radius (superficial interval, proximally).
Kocher lateral approach to the elbow / radial head.
Anterior (extensile) approach to the arm between biceps and triceps in its distal extent.

explanation: Smith-Petersen superficial interval = sartorius (femoral n.) and tensor fasciae latae (superior gluteal n.) → A. Henry proximal superficial interval = brachioradialis (radial n.) and pronator teres (median n.) → C. Kocher = anconeus (radial n., via the nerve to anconeus) and extensor carpi ulnaris (PIN) → D. The distal anterior approach to the arm exploits the plane between biceps (musculocutaneous n.) and the lateral fibres of brachialis/triceps (radial n.) → E. Recognising whether a plane is truly internervous predicts which approaches can be extended without denervating muscle (e.g. Watson-Jones and the posterior forearm distally are NOT internervous).
learningPoint: Memorise the nerve PAIR for each classic interval — it predicts safe extensibility and the structure at risk.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Surgical Approaches

subtopic: Biomechanics — hip joint reaction force / abductor lever arm
stem: During single-leg stance the abductor lever arm is roughly one-third of the body-weight lever arm about the hip centre. A surgeon performs a procedure that medialises the hip centre and lateralises the greater trochanter, lengthening the abductor moment arm. Assuming body weight and its lever arm are unchanged, what is the principal mechanical effect on the abductor muscle force and the hip joint reaction force?

options.label: A
options.text: Both abductor force and joint reaction force increase

options.label: B
options.text: Both abductor force and joint reaction force decrease

options.label: C
options.text: Abductor force increases but joint reaction force is unchanged

options.label: D
options.text: Abductor force decreases but joint reaction force increases

options.label: E
options.text: Abductor force is unchanged but joint reaction force decreases

explanation: In single-leg stance the abductors must balance the moment of body weight about the hip (lever-arm ratio ~3:1, so abductor force ≈ 3× partial body weight, giving a joint reaction force of roughly 3–4× body weight). Lengthening the abductor moment arm (medialising the cup, lateralising/advancing the trochanter, or a varus-producing offset) means LESS abductor force is needed to generate the same balancing moment. Because the joint reaction force is largely the vector sum of body weight and abductor pull, reducing the required abductor force reduces the joint reaction force — improving efficiency and reducing bearing load. This is a key rationale for restoring/increasing femoral offset in THR.
learningPoint: Lengthening the abductor lever arm (more offset/medial cup) lowers BOTH abductor force and hip joint reaction force.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Biomechanics of the Hip

stem: Two identical statically locked intramedullary nails are used for comminuted mid-shaft femoral fractures. In construct X the fracture gap (and bone-contact-free segment) is short; in construct Y it is long, giving a greater 'working length' of the nail. Compared with X, what is the effect on the rigidity of construct Y in bending and torsion?

options.label: A
options.text: Greater working length increases both bending and torsional rigidity

options.label: B
options.text: Greater working length decreases both bending and torsional rigidity

options.label: C
options.text: Greater working length increases bending rigidity only

options.label: D
options.text: Greater working length increases torsional rigidity only

options.label: E
options.text: Working length has no effect on either; only nail diameter matters

explanation: Working length is the unsupported length of implant between the points that transmit load on either side of the fracture (for a nail, effectively between the most adjacent points of cortical contact / locking). For a nail, bending rigidity is proportional to the fourth power of radius and inversely related to working length, while torsional rigidity also falls as working length increases. A longer working length (more comminution, more proximal-distal lock separation, or loss of cortical fit) therefore reduces both bending and torsional stiffness and increases stress at the locking bolts — relevant to fatigue failure of bolts and the nail itself. Increasing nail diameter (radius) is the most powerful way to restore rigidity.
learningPoint: Longer nail working length lowers both bending and torsional rigidity; diameter (r⁴) dominates bending stiffness.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Implant Biomechanics / Fracture Fixation

subtopic: Biomechanics — extramedullary vs intramedullary device for hip fracture
stem: A reverse-oblique (transverse subtrochanteric, AO 31-A3) intertrochanteric fracture is being fixed. A sliding hip screw (dynamic hip screw, DHS) and a cephalomedullary nail are both considered. On mechanical grounds, why is the cephalomedullary nail preferred for this specific pattern?

options.label: A
options.text: The nail has a shorter lever arm/moment arm, reducing the bending moment on the implant

options.label: B
options.text: The DHS provides controlled fracture-line compression along the favourable obliquity, whereas in a reverse-oblique pattern sliding causes medialisation and varus

options.label: C
options.text: The nail allows greater interfragmentary sliding, increasing compression across the reverse-oblique line

options.label: D
options.text: The DHS plate has a higher fatigue endurance limit than any nail

options.label: E
options.text: Reverse-oblique fractures are intracapsular, contraindicating a nail

explanation: In a reverse-oblique pattern the fracture line slopes such that DHS sliding does NOT compress the fragments along the line; instead the distal fragment slides medially and the proximal fragment drifts into varus, leading to lag-screw cut-out and plate/screw failure. A cephalomedullary nail places the load-bearing implant closer to the mechanical axis (medial calcar), giving a shorter moment/lever arm and a lower bending moment, and buttresses the medial column — hence its mechanical superiority. So although B correctly describes the DHS problem, the question asks WHY the nail is preferred, which is the reduced lever arm/bending moment of an intramedullary device.
learningPoint: Cephalomedullary nails sit nearer the mechanical axis → shorter lever arm and lower bending moment; preferred for reverse-oblique/subtrochanteric patterns.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Hip Fracture Biomechanics

subtopic: Biomechanics — screw pull-out and locking constructs
stem: A locking plate is applied to an osteoporotic distal femoral fracture. Compared with a conventional (non-locking) plate using the same number of cortical screws in the same bone, which statement about pull-out (failure) mechanics is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: Individual locking screws resist higher axial pull-out forces because they engage thicker cortex

options.label: B
options.text: A locking construct fails by simultaneous pull-out of all screws ('all-or-none'), whereas a non-locking construct tends to fail one screw at a time ('toggle and sequential pull-out')

options.label: C
options.text: Locking screws rely on plate-to-bone friction/compression for stability

options.label: D
options.text: Non-locking screws are mechanically superior in osteoporotic bone

options.label: E
options.text: Locking plates must be precisely contoured to the bone to function

explanation: A locking plate is a fixed-angle ('internal fixator') construct: the screws are rigidly locked to the plate, so the screw-plate-bone unit must fail together — load is shared and the whole array resists pull-out simultaneously, which is advantageous in osteoporotic bone where individual screw purchase is poor. A non-locking construct depends on plate-to-bone compression/friction; under load individual screws toggle and pull out one at a time, allowing progressive loosening. Crucially the locking plate does NOT require precise contouring (it does not need to compress to bone), so E is wrong, and individual locking-screw pull-out strength is not inherently higher (A wrong).
learningPoint: Locking plates are fixed-angle 'internal fixators' — failure is all-or-none, advantageous in osteoporotic/comminuted bone; no plate-bone compression needed.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Internal Fixation Biomechanics

subtopic: Biomechanics — fatigue, endurance limit and stress concentration
stem: An empty screw hole left in the middle of a plate spanning a slow-to-unite diaphyseal fracture is a recognised site of implant breakage. On an S-N (stress versus number-of-cycles) fatigue diagram, what best explains why this construct may fail even though the peak stress during normal walking is well below the material's ultimate tensile strength?

options.label: A
options.text: The empty hole acts as a stress raiser, so local stress exceeds the endurance (fatigue) limit and crack propagation occurs over repeated cycles

options.label: B
options.text: Titanium alloys have no endurance limit, so any cyclic stress eventually fails

options.label: C
options.text: Creep deformation at body temperature reduces the ultimate tensile strength below walking loads

options.label: D
options.text: The plate yields plastically on the first walking cycle

options.label: E
options.text: Corrosion fatigue is irrelevant to surgical implants in vivo

explanation: An open (empty) screw hole over the fracture concentrates stress (a notch/stress raiser), locally elevating the cyclic stress above the material's endurance limit. With repeated loading a fatigue crack initiates and propagates, and the plate breaks at the hole — even though the nominal stress is below the ultimate tensile strength — because failure is governed by cyclic stress amplitude relative to the endurance limit, not by a single overload. Mitigation: avoid leaving empty holes at the fracture, restore medial cortical contact to reduce plate stress, and biological fixation to achieve union before the implant fails. (Steels classically show an endurance limit; some titanium/aluminium alloys may show a less distinct one, but A is the primary mechanism here.)
learningPoint: Empty plate holes are stress raisers — local cyclic stress exceeds the endurance limit → fatigue failure below UTS.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Material Properties & Fatigue

subtopic: Biomechanics — knee, screw-home and patellofemoral lever arm
stem: A tibial tubercle anteriorisation (anteromedialisation) osteotomy is performed for patellofemoral overload. Mechanically, the principal benefit of anteriorising the tibial tubercle is best explained by which change?

options.label: A
options.text: It increases the quadriceps (extensor) moment arm, reducing the quadriceps and patellofemoral joint reaction force for a given extension torque

options.label: B
options.text: It increases the Q-angle, recentring the patella

options.label: C
options.text: It shortens the patellar tendon, raising patellar height

options.label: D
options.text: It converts the patellofemoral joint to a pure rolling contact

options.label: E
options.text: It eliminates the screw-home mechanism at terminal extension

explanation: Anteriorising the tibial tubercle moves the patellar tendon insertion (and hence the line of quadriceps pull) further from the knee's flexion axis, lengthening the extensor moment arm. For a given required extension torque, a longer moment arm means LESS quadriceps force is needed, and because the patellofemoral joint reaction force is proportional to quadriceps tension, the patellofemoral contact force falls — unloading the joint. Anteromedialisation (the Fulkerson osteotomy) additionally medialises to correct lateral maltracking. Anteriorisation does not act by changing rolling/sliding kinematics or abolishing screw-home.
learningPoint: Anteriorising the tibial tubercle lengthens the extensor lever arm → less quadriceps force → lower patellofemoral joint reaction force.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Knee Biomechanics / Patellofemoral

subtopic: Orthopaedic oncology — giant cell tumour of bone, recurrence and denosumab
stem: A 32-year-old woman has a biopsy-proven giant cell tumour of bone (GCTB) of the distal radius with cortical thinning but no soft-tissue mass (Campanacci grade 2). After intralesional curettage with high-speed burr and adjuvant treatment, which adjuvant is associated with the LOWEST local recurrence when used in the cavity, and what is the role of denosumab?

options.label: A
options.text: Phenol/cement (PMMA) or cryotherapy reduce recurrence; denosumab is a RANK-ligand inhibitor used neoadjuvantly for unresectable/aggressive disease, not as a curative substitute for surgery

options.label: B
options.text: Bone graft alone gives the lowest recurrence; denosumab cures GCTB without surgery

options.label: C
options.text: Radiotherapy is the adjuvant of choice; denosumab is a bisphosphonate

options.label: D
options.text: No adjuvant reduces recurrence; denosumab is a chemotherapy agent

options.label: E
options.text: Wide en-bloc resection is mandatory for all GCTB; denosumab is contraindicated

explanation: GCTB is a locally aggressive, usually benign tumour of the epiphysis/metaphysis (often distal radius, distal femur, proximal tibia). Standard treatment is extended intralesional curettage with a high-speed burr plus a local adjuvant — PMMA cement (exothermic effect, immediate stability, easy surveillance), phenol, hydrogen peroxide, or cryotherapy — which lowers recurrence compared with curettage alone; filling with cement rather than graft is common. Denosumab is a monoclonal antibody against RANK-ligand (it inhibits the osteoclast-like giant cells); it is used neoadjuvantly to downstage unresectable or axial/aggressive lesions and for inoperable disease, but does not replace surgery and recurrence is high if it is stopped without resection. It is NOT a bisphosphonate or chemotherapy.
learningPoint: GCTB: extended curettage + local adjuvant (cement/phenol/cryo); denosumab (anti-RANKL) downstages aggressive/unresectable disease but is not curative alone.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Bone Tumours (GCTB)

subtopic: Orthopaedic oncology — chondrosarcoma vs enchondroma, why no chemo/radiotherapy
stem: A 55-year-old has a cartilage lesion of the proximal humerus with deep endosteal scalloping (>2/3 cortical thickness), cortical thickening and activity pain; biopsy suggests a low-grade chondrosarcoma. Which statement best reflects current management principles?

options.label: A
