options.text: Conventional chondrosarcoma is largely resistant to chemotherapy and radiotherapy, so adequate surgical resection (wide margin for higher grade; selected low-grade lesions by extended intralesional curettage in specialist centres) is the mainstay

options.label: B
options.text: Neoadjuvant chemotherapy as for osteosarcoma is standard for all chondrosarcomas

options.label: C
options.text: Radiotherapy alone reliably controls conventional chondrosarcoma

options.label: D
options.text: All chondrosarcomas are high grade and require amputation

options.label: E
options.text: Endosteal scalloping favours a simple enchondroma and warrants discharge

explanation: Conventional chondrosarcoma is characteristically resistant to both chemotherapy and radiotherapy, so surgery is the mainstay: wide resection for intermediate/high-grade tumours, while selected low-grade (grade 1 / atypical cartilaginous) appendicular tumours may be treated by extended intralesional curettage with adjuvant in specialist sarcoma units. Features distinguishing aggressive cartilage tumours from benign enchondroma include deep endosteal scalloping (>2/3 cortex), cortical destruction/thickening, a soft-tissue mass, and pain at rest/with activity — so E is wrong. Exceptions such as mesenchymal and dedifferentiated chondrosarcoma may receive chemotherapy, but conventional chondrosarcoma does not.
learningPoint: Conventional chondrosarcoma is chemo- and radio-resistant — adequate surgical resection is the cornerstone; deep scalloping/cortical change/rest pain flag malignancy over enchondroma.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Cartilage Tumours

subtopic: Orthopaedic oncology — biopsy principles and complications
stem: A patient with a suspected high-grade sarcoma of the distal femur is referred to a tertiary sarcoma unit, but a needle biopsy was already performed at a district hospital through a lateral approach. Which principle of musculoskeletal tumour biopsy was MOST likely violated, and what is the consequence?

options.label: A
options.text: The biopsy should be planned and performed by (or in conjunction with) the treating surgical oncology unit so the tract can be excised en bloc; an ill-sited tract contaminates compartments and may convert a limb-salvage candidate to amputation

options.label: B
options.text: Needle biopsy is never acceptable for bone tumours; only open incisional biopsy is valid

options.label: C
options.text: A transverse incision is mandatory for all extremity biopsies

options.label: D
options.text: Frozen section is contraindicated in sarcoma biopsy

options.label: E
options.text: The biopsy tract is irrelevant to subsequent surgery once chemotherapy is given

explanation: A cardinal rule is that the biopsy of a suspected primary bone or soft-tissue sarcoma should be planned and ideally performed by the definitive treating (sarcoma) centre, because the biopsy tract is considered contaminated and must be excised en bloc with the tumour at the definitive resection. A poorly placed or transverse incision, haematoma, or a tract crossing multiple compartments/neurovascular structures contaminates tissues and can preclude limb-salvage, mandating a wider resection or amputation and worsening outcome. Biopsy incisions on the limb should be longitudinal and in line with the planned resection; meticulous haemostasis is essential.
learningPoint: Sarcoma biopsies belong to the treating unit — longitudinal incision, in line with the planned resection; a bad tract can cost the limb.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Principles of Tumour Biopsy
NICE NG27 / Sarcoma IOG; BSG guidelines

stem: Using the Enneking surgical staging system for BENIGN musculoskeletal tumours (stages 1–3), a lesion that has breached its natural capsule, shows aggressive local behaviour with cortical destruction and an extraosseous component, but no metastasis, is best classified — and managed — as which of the following?

options.label: A
options.text: Stage 3 (aggressive); treated by extended intralesional or marginal/wide excision because of high recurrence

options.label: B
options.text: Stage 1 (latent); observation only

options.label: C
options.text: Stage 2 (active); intralesional curettage usually sufficient

options.label: D
options.text: Stage IIB malignant; neoadjuvant chemotherapy then wide resection

options.label: E
options.text: Stage 3 (aggressive); radiotherapy is first-line

explanation: Enneking's benign staging uses Arabic numerals 1–3: Stage 1 = latent (e.g. non-ossifying fibroma) — leave alone; Stage 2 = active (e.g. aneurysmal bone cyst, active simple bone cyst) — intralesional treatment with adjuvant; Stage 3 = aggressive (e.g. aggressive giant cell tumour) — the capsule is breached with an extracompartmental/soft-tissue component and a high recurrence rate, requiring extended intralesional excision with adjuvant or a marginal/wide margin. The MALIGNANT Enneking system uses Roman numerals with grade (low I / high II) and compartment (A intracompartmental / B extracompartmental), e.g. IIB — so D describes the malignant scheme, not benign.
learningPoint: Enneking benign stages: 1 latent (observe), 2 active (curette+adjuvant), 3 aggressive (extended/marginal excision); malignant uses Roman numerals + grade/compartment.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Enneking Staging

subtopic: Orthopaedic oncology — radiological diagnosis of bone lesions
stem: Theme: radiological and clinical diagnosis of bone lesions. For each description, select the SINGLE most likely diagnosis from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Ewing sarcoma

options.label: B
options.text: Osteosarcoma

options.label: C
options.text: Adamantinoma

options.label: D
options.text: Chordoma

options.label: E
options.text: Aneurysmal bone cyst

options.label: F
options.text: Osteoid osteoma

options.label: G
options.text: Chondrosarcoma

options.label: H
options.text: Giant cell tumour of bone

options.label: I
options.text: Fibrous dysplasia

emiLeads.stem: A 14-year-old with a febrile illness, raised inflammatory markers and a permeative, 'onion-skin' lamellated periosteal reaction in the diaphysis of the femur; biopsy shows small round blue cells with a t(11;22) translocation.
A 55-year-old with a destructive midline sacrococcygeal mass; histology shows physaliferous (bubbly, vacuolated) cells arising from notochordal remnants.

A young adult with a long-standing, painless, slightly expansile lytic lesion of the mid-tibial diaphysis (anterior cortex) with a 'soap-bubble' appearance; histology shows epithelial nests in a fibrous stroma.
A young adult with an eccentric, expansile, multiloculated lytic metaphyseal lesion with fluid-fluid levels on MRI and no malignant cells; may arise secondarily within another lesion.
A 20-year-old with a ground-glass medullary lesion of the proximal femur producing a 'shepherd's crook' varus deformity; associated café-au-lait macules in McCune-Albright syndrome.

explanation: Ewing sarcoma: diaphyseal, permeative with lamellated 'onion-skin' periosteum, systemic features, small round blue cells, EWSR1-FLI1 t(11;22) → A. Chordoma: midline (sacrum/clivus) tumour of notochordal origin with physaliferous cells → D. Adamantinoma: classically the anterior tibial diaphyseal cortex, 'soap-bubble' lytic lesion with epithelial nests (related to osteofibrous dysplasia) → C. Aneurysmal bone cyst: expansile, multiloculated, fluid-fluid levels, may be secondary within GCT/chondroblastoma/fibrous dysplasia → E. Fibrous dysplasia: ground-glass matrix, shepherd's crook deformity, McCune-Albright (café-au-lait + endocrinopathy), GNAS mutation → I.
learningPoint: Match the signature: onion-skin diaphysis = Ewing; physaliferous midline = chordoma; anterior tibial soap-bubble = adamantinoma; fluid-fluid levels = ABC; ground-glass/shepherd's crook = fibrous dysplasia.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Bone Tumours
Grainger & Allison's Diagnostic Radiology — Bone Tumours

subtopic: Orthopaedic oncology — osteochondroma and malignant transformation
stem: A 28-year-old man with hereditary multiple exostoses reports that a previously stable pelvic osteochondroma has become painful and is enlarging. Which single feature most strongly raises concern for malignant transformation to secondary (peripheral) chondrosarcoma, and what is the underlying genetics of the condition?

options.label: A
options.text: A growing cartilage cap thicker than ~1.5–2 cm in a skeletally mature patient; the condition is autosomal dominant, due to EXT1/EXT2 mutations

options.label: B
options.text: A bony stalk pointing away from the joint; the condition is X-linked, due to FBN1 mutations

options.label: C
options.text: Continuity of the lesion's medulla with the host bone; the condition is autosomal recessive, due to COMP mutations

options.label: D
options.text: Multiple lesions rather than a solitary lesion; the condition is due to FGFR3 mutation

options.label: E
options.text: Calcification within the cartilage cap; the condition is due to a GNAS mutation

explanation: Hereditary multiple exostoses (multiple osteochondromas) is autosomal dominant, caused by loss-of-function mutations in the tumour-suppressor genes EXT1 and EXT2 (heparan-sulphate biosynthesis). The hallmark of malignant transformation of an osteochondroma to secondary peripheral chondrosarcoma is growth or new pain after skeletal maturity with a thickened cartilage cap — a cap >1.5–2 cm on MRI is the key red flag (axial/proximal lesions, e.g. pelvis/shoulder girdle, carry higher risk). Medullary continuity with the host bone and a stalk pointing away from the joint are features of a BENIGN osteochondroma, not malignancy. FBN1 = Marfan, COMP = pseudoachondroplasia/MED, FGFR3 = achondroplasia, GNAS = fibrous dysplasia.
learningPoint: HME is AD (EXT1/EXT2); a cartilage cap >1.5–2 cm with new pain/growth after maturity signals transformation to secondary chondrosarcoma.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Osteochondroma / HME

subtopic: Orthopaedic oncology — Mirels score and prophylactic fixation
stem: A 70-year-old woman with metastatic breast carcinoma has a painful lytic deposit in the proximal femur. You apply the Mirels scoring system. Which combination of features gives the MAXIMUM (highest-risk) score in every category and therefore the strongest indication for prophylactic fixation before radiotherapy?

options.label: A
options.text: Lower-limb site, lytic lesion, >2/3 cortical involvement, functional (mechanical) pain

options.label: B
options.text: Upper-limb site, blastic lesion, <1/3 cortical involvement, mild pain

options.label: C
options.text: Peritrochanteric site, mixed lesion, 1/3–2/3 cortical involvement, moderate pain

options.label: D
options.text: Lower-limb site, blastic lesion, <1/3 cortical involvement, mild pain

options.label: E
options.text: Upper-limb site, lytic lesion, >2/3 cortical involvement, functional pain

explanation: Mirels scores four variables 1–3 each (max 12): SITE (upper limb 1, lower limb 2, peritrochanteric 3), LESION (blastic 1, mixed 2, lytic 3), SIZE (<1/3 cortex 1, 1/3–2/3 = 2, >2/3 = 3), and PAIN (mild 1, moderate 2, functional/mechanical 3). A score ≥8 (some use ≥9) indicates a high fracture risk and prophylactic fixation is generally recommended; ≤7 can be irradiated and observed. The maximum score in every category is peritrochanteric site, lytic, >2/3 cortex, functional pain = 12; of the options given, A scores 2+3+3+3 = 11, the highest available, hence the strongest indication. (Option A omits 'peritrochanteric' so scores 11 rather than 12, but is still the maximal choice offered.)
learningPoint: Mirels: site/lesion/size/pain, each 1–3; ≥8 → consider prophylactic fixation before radiotherapy.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Metastatic Bone Disease
Mirels, Clin Orthop 1989; BOA/BOOS Metastatic Bone Disease guidance

subtopic: Genetics — osteogenesis imperfecta, Sillence types and bisphosphonates
stem: A 4-year-old child has recurrent low-energy long-bone fractures, blue sclerae, mild short stature and a family history of fractures over three generations. Which statement about the genetics and management of the likely condition is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: The commonest forms are autosomal dominant defects of type I collagen (COL1A1/COL1A2); intravenous bisphosphonates reduce fracture rate and load-sharing intramedullary (telescoping) rods are used for deformity/recurrent fracture

options.label: B
options.text: It is caused by a defect of type II collagen and is uniformly lethal in infancy

options.label: C
options.text: It is an X-linked defect of type III collagen treated mainly by rigid plating

options.label: D
options.text: Bisphosphonates are contraindicated because they weaken bone in children

options.label: E
options.text: It is due to FGFR3 mutation with normal collagen

explanation: Osteogenesis imperfecta is, in its commonest (classical) forms, an autosomal dominant disorder of type I collagen due to mutations in COL1A1 or COL1A2; the Sillence classification recognises type I (mild, blue sclerae, AD), type II (perinatal-lethal), type III (severe, progressively deforming), and type IV (moderate). Management includes intravenous bisphosphonates (e.g. pamidronate), which reduce fracture frequency and bone pain and increase BMD in children, plus telescoping/load-sharing intramedullary rods (Fassier-Duval, Sheffield/Bailey-Dubow) to manage deformity and recurrent fracture — rigid plating is avoided because it creates stress risers at the plate ends. Type II OI involves type I collagen (not type II collagen, despite the name) and is lethal.
learningPoint: OI = AD type I collagen defect (COL1A1/2); manage with IV bisphosphonates and telescoping IM rods, not rigid plates.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Metabolic/Genetic Bone Disease

subtopic: Genetics — Ehlers-Danlos vascular type, perioperative risk
stem: A young adult with marked skin fragility, easy bruising, thin translucent skin and a history of a spontaneous bowel perforation is referred with recurrent joint instability. Which subtype of the likely connective-tissue disorder carries the greatest perioperative concern, and what is its molecular basis?

options.label: A
options.text: Vascular (classically 'type IV') Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, due to a defect of type III collagen (COL3A1); high risk of arterial/visceral rupture and poor tissue/wound healing

options.label: B
options.text: Hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, due to a fibrillin-1 (FBN1) defect; high risk of aortic dissection

options.label: C
options.text: Classical Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, due to a type II collagen defect; high risk of retinal detachment

options.label: D
options.text: Kyphoscoliotic Ehlers-Danlos, due to an FGFR3 defect; risk of spinal stenosis

options.label: E
options.text: Vascular Ehlers-Danlos, due to a COMP defect; risk of premature osteoarthritis

explanation: The vascular type of Ehlers-Danlos syndrome (formerly type IV) is the most dangerous subtype: it is caused by mutations in COL3A1 affecting type III collagen, and patients are at high risk of spontaneous arterial rupture/aneurysm, bowel perforation and uterine rupture, with extremely friable tissues and poor wound healing. Surgery should be undertaken cautiously (ideally avoided/minimally invasive), with meticulous haemostasis and tissue handling and the involvement of vascular services. Fibrillin-1 (FBN1) defects cause Marfan syndrome (aortic root dilatation); type II collagen defects cause Stickler/skeletal dysplasias; COMP defects cause pseudoachondroplasia/MED; FGFR3 causes achondroplasia.
learningPoint: Vascular EDS (COL3A1, type III collagen) = friable tissues and arterial/visceral rupture — operate only with extreme caution.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Connective-Tissue Disorders

subtopic: Genetics — skeletal dysplasias and connective-tissue disorders (gene matching)
stem: Theme: molecular basis of inherited skeletal and connective-tissue disorders. For each clinical description, select the SINGLE gene/protein defect responsible from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: FGFR3

options.label: B
options.text: FBN1 (fibrillin-1)

options.label: C
options.text: COMP (cartilage oligomeric matrix protein)

options.label: D
options.text: COL1A1/COL1A2 (type I collagen)

options.label: E
options.text: COL2A1 (type II collagen)

options.label: F
options.text: COL3A1 (type III collagen)

options.label: G
options.text: SLC26A2 (DTDST sulphate transporter)

options.label: H
options.text: Dystrophin

emiLeads.stem: Rhizomelic short stature, frontal bossing, trident hands and a narrow lumbar interpedicular distance; the commonest non-lethal skeletal dysplasia, autosomal dominant.
Tall stature, arachnodactyly, ectopia lentis (upward lens dislocation), a positive thumb/wrist sign and aortic root dilatation.

Disproportionate short-limb dwarfism with a normal face and skull, ligamentous laxity, early osteoarthritis, and epiphyseal/metaphyseal changes — a disorder that overlaps with multiple epiphyseal dysplasia.
A boy with progressive proximal muscle weakness, calf pseudohypertrophy, a Gowers sign and grossly elevated creatine kinase; X-linked recessive.
Severe short-limb dysplasia with rigid 'cauliflower' ears, a 'hitchhiker' thumb and joint contractures; autosomal recessive defect of a sulphate transporter.

explanation: Achondroplasia: gain-of-function FGFR3 mutation → rhizomelic dwarfism, trident hand, decreasing lumbar interpedicular distance, foramen magnum stenosis → A. Marfan: FBN1 (fibrillin-1) → tall, arachnodactyly, UPWARD ectopia lentis, aortic root dilatation → B. Pseudoachondroplasia/MED: COMP defect → short limbs with a normal face, laxity and early OA → C. Duchenne muscular dystrophy: X-linked dystrophin defect → Gowers sign, calf pseudohypertrophy, very high CK → H. Diastrophic dysplasia: SLC26A2 (DTDST) sulphate transporter → hitchhiker thumb, cauliflower ear, contractures → G. (Stickler syndrome would be COL2A1/E; OI is COL1A1/2 = D; vascular EDS is COL3A1 = F.)
learningPoint: Know the gene one-liners: achondroplasia FGFR3; Marfan FBN1; pseudoachondroplasia/MED COMP; OI COL1A1/2; Stickler COL2A1; diastrophic SLC26A2; DMD dystrophin.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Skeletal Dysplasias & Genetic Disorders

subtopic: Genetics — achondroplasia, neurological complications and limb realignment
stem: A 6-month-old with achondroplasia is reviewed. Beyond the characteristic rhizomelic short stature, which complication is the most important to actively screen for in INFANCY because it can be life-threatening, and what is the underlying mechanism?

options.label: A
options.text: Foramen magnum (cervicomedullary) stenosis causing central apnoea and sudden death, due to abnormal endochondral ossification narrowing the foramen magnum

options.label: B
options.text: Aortic root dilatation and dissection, due to fibrillin deficiency

options.label: C
options.text: Atlanto-axial instability, due to odontoid hypoplasia as in Down/Morquio syndrome

options.label: D
options.text: Pathological long-bone fracture, due to defective type I collagen

options.label: E
options.text: Progressive scoliosis from hemivertebrae

explanation: Achondroplasia is an autosomal dominant gain-of-function FGFR3 disorder that impairs endochondral ossification. In infancy the most important complication is foramen magnum/cervicomedullary stenosis: the abnormally small foramen magnum compresses the brainstem and upper cervical cord, causing central sleep apnoea, hypotonia and an increased risk of sudden death — hence active screening (examination, sleep studies, and MRI when indicated), with surgical foramen magnum decompression if there is symptomatic compression. Later orthopaedic issues include thoracolumbar kyphosis, genu varum (treated with realignment osteotomy/guided growth) and symptomatic spinal canal stenosis in adulthood. Aortic dilatation = Marfan; odontoid hypoplasia/atlanto-axial instability = Morquio/Down; type I collagen = OI.
learningPoint: In infants with achondroplasia, screen for foramen magnum stenosis (apnoea, sudden death) — the key early, life-threatening complication.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Skeletal Dysplasias

stem: A 58-year-old woman is referred with diffuse bone pain. Radiographs show subperiosteal bone resorption, a lytic lesion in the diaphysis of the proximal phalanx, and a 'salt-and-pepper' skull. Her corrected calcium is 2.92 mmol/L, phosphate is low, and alkaline phosphatase is raised. A lytic expansile lesion is biopsied and shows multinucleated giant cells in a fibrous stroma with haemosiderin. Which is the SINGLE most appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: En-bloc excision of the phalangeal lesion and reconstruction

options.label: B
options.text: Measure parathyroid hormone and arrange parathyroid localisation imaging

options.label: C
options.text: Commence denosumab for presumed giant cell tumour of bone

options.label: D
options.text: Curettage and cementation of the lesion

options.label: E
options.text: Radiotherapy to the lesion

explanation: The biopsy describes a brown tumour (osteitis fibrosa cystica), the giant-cell-rich reparative lesion of primary hyperparathyroidism, not a true giant cell tumour. The combination of hypercalcaemia, hypophosphataemia, raised ALP, subperiosteal resorption and a salt-and-pepper skull is diagnostic of hyperparathyroidism. The correct step is to confirm with PTH and localise the adenoma; brown tumours typically regress after parathyroidectomy, so primary surgical excision or denosumab would be inappropriate and could cause harm.
learningPoint: A giant-cell-rich lytic lesion with hypercalcaemia and hypophosphataemia is a brown tumour of hyperparathyroidism, not a giant cell tumour - check PTH before operating.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Basic Science (metabolic bone disease)

stem: A 47-year-old man on long-term haemodialysis develops progressive proximal muscle weakness and bone pain. Biochemistry shows calcium 2.10 mmol/L, phosphate 2.6 mmol/L, markedly raised PTH and raised alkaline phosphatase. A bone biopsy with double tetracycline labelling shows a high bone turnover state with increased osteoid and peritrabecular fibrosis. Which management change is most appropriate to address the underlying bone disorder?

options.label: A
options.text: Start a bisphosphonate to suppress bone turnover

options.label: B
options.text: Control hyperphosphataemia and add a calcimimetic (cinacalcet) +/- active vitamin D analogue

options.label: C
options.text: Increase the dialysate calcium concentration alone

options.label: D
options.text: Commence teriparatide

options.label: E
options.text: Restrict dietary calcium and stop all vitamin D

explanation: The picture is high-turnover renal osteodystrophy (osteitis fibrosa cystica) driven by secondary hyperparathyroidism: hyperphosphataemia and relative hypocalcaemia stimulate PTH, and reduced 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D removes negative feedback. Management targets the drivers - phosphate binders to lower phosphate, then a calcimimetic to suppress PTH directly (lowering calcium and phosphate, unlike vitamin D), with active vitamin D analogues as needed. Bisphosphonates and teriparatide are not appropriate in CKD-MBD high-turnover disease; oversuppression risks adynamic bone.
learningPoint: High-turnover renal osteodystrophy is driven by secondary hyperparathyroidism - control phosphate first, then suppress PTH with a calcimimetic.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Basic Science (renal osteodystrophy)

stem: A 62-year-old woman with end-stage renal disease on peritoneal dialysis sustains a low-energy femoral fracture. Her PTH is suppressed (well below the dialysis target range), calcium is high-normal, and alkaline phosphatase is low. Bone biopsy shows reduced osteoblastic and osteoclastic activity with no increase in osteoid and no mineralisation defect. Which factor is most likely to have contributed to this bone state?

options.label: A
options.text: Aluminium-based phosphate binders

options.label: B
options.text: Over-suppression of PTH by aggressive calcium loading and active vitamin D

options.label: C
options.text: Vitamin D deficiency

options.label: D
options.text: Uncontrolled hyperphosphataemia

options.label: E
options.text: Untreated secondary hyperparathyroidism

explanation: This is adynamic (low-turnover) bone disease: low bone formation and resorption, low ALP and over-suppressed PTH with NO excess osteoid (distinguishing it from osteomalacia). In modern UK practice the dominant cause is iatrogenic over-suppression of PTH from high calcium load (calcium-based binders, high dialysate calcium) and active vitamin D. A minimal level of PTH activity is needed to maintain bone remodelling; over-suppression renders bone unable to buffer calcium and prone to fracture. Aluminium toxicity is a classic but now rare cause in the UK.
learningPoint: Adynamic bone disease (low PTH, low ALP, no excess osteoid) is now most often caused by over-suppression of PTH, not aluminium.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Basic Science (renal osteodystrophy)

stem: A 74-year-old man with known polyostotic Paget disease of the pelvis and femur, previously stable on a bisphosphonate, presents with new severe, unremitting deep thigh pain not relieved by rest, and a recent rise in alkaline phosphatase after years of stability. Plain films show cortical destruction and a soft-tissue mass at the site of pagetic bone. Which complication is most likely?

options.label: A
options.text: Insufficiency (Looser-type) fissure fracture

options.label: B
options.text: Secondary osteosarcoma

options.label: C
options.text: High-output cardiac failure

options.label: D
options.text: Degenerate osteoarthritis of the hip

options.label: E
options.text: Bone marrow oedema from a flare of Paget activity

explanation: New unremitting pain, cortical destruction with a soft-tissue mass, and a rising ALP after stability in long-standing Paget disease are the classic red flags for sarcomatous transformation. Paget sarcoma (usually osteosarcoma, sometimes fibrosarcoma/chondrosarcoma) occurs in under 1% but carries a very poor prognosis and most often affects the femur, pelvis and humerus. Insufficiency fractures cause pain but not a destructive soft-tissue mass; high-output failure is a systemic complication of extensive disease, not a focal mass.
learningPoint: New unremitting bone pain, a soft-tissue mass and rising ALP in established Paget disease signals sarcomatous transformation until proven otherwise.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Basic Science / Oncology (Paget disease)
NICE / BSG bone and soft tissue sarcoma guidance

stem: A 45-year-old man presents with a 2-year history of generalised bone pain, proximal myopathy and several pseudofractures. Calcium and 25-hydroxyvitamin D are normal, but phosphate is persistently very low with inappropriately high renal phosphate excretion. He has no family history. Serum FGF23 is elevated, and he is later found to have a small phosphaturic mesenchymal tumour. Which is the SINGLE most appropriate definitive treatment?

options.label: A
options.text: High-dose cholecalciferol replacement

options.label: B
options.text: Wide local excision of the tumour

options.label: C
options.text: Lifelong oral phosphate and calcitriol only

options.label: D
options.text: Bisphosphonate therapy

options.label: E
options.text: Parathyroidectomy

explanation: This is tumour-induced (oncogenic) osteomalacia: a phosphaturic mesenchymal tumour secretes FGF23, causing renal phosphate wasting, low 1,25-vitamin D, hypophosphataemic osteomalacia and pseudofractures (Looser zones) - with NORMAL calcium and 25-OH vitamin D, which excludes nutritional osteomalacia. Complete excision of the tumour is curative and normalises FGF23 and phosphate. Phosphate/calcitriol supplementation is only a temporising medical measure when the tumour cannot be found or removed.
learningPoint: Acquired hypophosphataemic osteomalacia with high FGF23 and normal vitamin D suggests an FGF23-secreting tumour - resection is curative.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Basic Science (osteomalacia / FGF23)
Endocrine Society guidance on hypophosphataemic disorders

stem: An 80-year-old man develops an acutely painful, swollen, erythematous total knee replacement 8 days after primary surgery, with a discharging wound, fever and raised inflammatory markers. The implant is well-fixed. Joint aspirate and tissue cultures grow methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus. The patient is otherwise medically fit. Which is the SINGLE most appropriate surgical management?

options.label: A
options.text: Long-term suppressive oral antibiotics alone

options.label: B
options.text: Debridement, antibiotics and implant retention (DAIR) with modular component exchange

options.label: C
options.text: One-stage revision arthroplasty

options.label: D
options.text: Two-stage revision arthroplasty

options.label: E
options.text: Above-knee amputation

explanation: This is an early acute (post-operative) periprosthetic joint infection within ~4 weeks of implantation, with a well-fixed implant, a known sensitive organism and a fit patient - the precise indication for DAIR. Exchange of modular parts (e.g. polyethylene insert) improves access and biofilm clearance, while the fixed components are retained. Two-stage revision is reserved for chronic infection, loose implants, sinus tracts, difficult organisms or failed DAIR. Suppression alone is for patients unfit for surgery.
learningPoint: Acute PJI (<4 weeks), well-fixed implant, sensitive organism and a fit patient is the classic indication for DAIR with modular exchange.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Adult Reconstruction (PJI)
British Orthopaedic Association / EBJIS PJI guidance

stem: A 24-year-old man with sickle cell disease develops acute multifocal long-bone osteomyelitis. Blood cultures and bone aspirate grow a Gram-negative rod. Which organism is most characteristically associated with osteomyelitis in this specific patient group?

options.label: A
options.text: Staphylococcus aureus

options.label: B
options.text: Salmonella species

options.label: C
options.text: Pseudomonas aeruginosa

options.label: D
options.text: Kingella kingae

options.label: E
options.text: Pasteurella multocida

explanation: Although Staphylococcus aureus remains the commonest cause of osteomyelitis overall (including in sickle cell patients), the organism characteristically and disproportionately associated with osteomyelitis in sickle cell disease is Salmonella, and the stem specifies a Gram-negative rod. Bowel microinfarction allows translocation, and functional asplenia impairs clearance of encapsulated organisms. Pseudomonas is linked to puncture wounds through trainers and to IV drug use; Kingella to young children; Pasteurella to animal bites.
learningPoint: Salmonella is the characteristically associated organism in sickle cell osteomyelitis, even though S. aureus is commonest overall.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Basic Science (musculoskeletal infection)

stem: Theme: Likely causative organism in musculoskeletal infection. For each clinical scenario, select the SINGLE most likely organism from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Staphylococcus aureus

options.label: B
options.text: Pseudomonas aeruginosa

options.label: C
options.text: Eikenella corrodens

options.label: D
options.text: Pasteurella multocida

options.label: E
options.text: Neisseria gonorrhoeae

options.label: F
options.text: Kingella kingae

options.label: G
options.text: Staphylococcus epidermidis

options.label: H
options.text: Mycobacterium marinum

explanation: These are the classic setting-specific organisms. A puncture through a trainer/plimsoll classically yields Pseudomonas osteomyelitis. A clenched-fist 'fight bite' over the MCP joint is associated with Eikenella corrodens (human oral flora). A young, sexually active adult with migratory polyarthralgia, tenosynovitis and a pustular rash points to disseminated gonococcal infection. A healthy toddler with insidious septic arthritis and negative routine cultures but positive PCR points to Kingella kingae.
learningPoint: Setting dictates organism: trainer puncture = Pseudomonas, fight-bite = Eikenella, sexually active + tenosynovitis/rash = gonococcus, toddler = Kingella.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Basic Science (infection)

emiLeads.stem: A 9-year-old steps on a nail that pierces the sole of his trainer; 10 days later he develops osteomyelitis of the calcaneus.
A 25-year-old man sustains a laceration over the 3rd metacarpophalangeal joint after punching another man in the mouth; the wound becomes infected.
A 22-year-old sexually active woman presents with migratory polyarthralgia, tenosynovitis of the wrist and a few pustular skin lesions.
A previously healthy 18-month-old child presents with insidious-onset reluctance to weight-bear and a low-grade effusion; routine cultures are negative but PCR is positive.

stem: A patient is listed for elective primary total hip replacement. They have a documented history of anaphylaxis to penicillin (urticaria, angio-oedema and hypotension after amoxicillin). Local MRSA screening is negative. Which is the SINGLE most appropriate antibiotic regimen for surgical prophylaxis at induction?

options.label: A
options.text: Intravenous cefuroxime

options.label: B
options.text: Intravenous flucloxacillin

options.label: C
options.text: Intravenous co-amoxiclav

options.label: D
options.text: Intravenous teicoplanin (or vancomycin) with gentamicin

options.label: E
options.text: Oral doxycycline

explanation: True penicillin anaphylaxis is a contraindication to beta-lactams, and there is meaningful (though low) cross-reactivity with cephalosporins, so cefuroxime, flucloxacillin and co-amoxiclav are all inappropriate. The standard UK alternative for arthroplasty prophylaxis in genuine penicillin allergy is a glycopeptide (teicoplanin or vancomycin), which also covers MRSA, frequently combined with gentamicin for Gram-negative cover. Oral doxycycline is not a timed IV induction prophylactic agent for arthroplasty. Prophylaxis is mandatory for joint replacement and must be given as a timed IV dose at induction.
learningPoint: In true penicillin anaphylaxis, replace beta-lactam arthroplasty prophylaxis with a glycopeptide (teicoplanin/vancomycin) +/- gentamicin, not a cephalosporin.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Basic Science (prophylaxis)

stem: During a two-stage revision for an infected hip arthroplasty, an antibiotic-loaded cement spacer is planned. The microbiology team recommends adding an aminoglycoside to the cement. Which property of gentamicin makes it particularly suitable for incorporation into polymethylmethacrylate (PMMA) bone cement?

options.label: A
options.text: It is highly lipophilic, aiding intracellular penetration

options.label: B
options.text: It is heat-stable, water-soluble and elutes effectively from PMMA

options.label: C
options.text: It is bacteriostatic, reducing local toxicity

options.label: D
options.text: It binds irreversibly to PMMA, providing years of release

options.label: E
options.text: It is only active in anaerobic conditions typical of biofilm

explanation: Gentamicin (and vancomycin) are added to PMMA because they withstand the exothermic polymerisation reaction (heat-stable), are water-soluble so they elute as fluid penetrates the cement matrix, and have a broad relevant spectrum. Elution is highest in the first 24-48 hours then falls. Aminoglycosides are bactericidal (concentration-dependent) and act by binding the 30S ribosomal subunit causing misreading - their oxygen-dependent uptake actually makes them LESS active in anaerobic/abscess conditions, which is a limitation, not the reason for use.
learningPoint: Antibiotics for bone cement must be heat-stable and water-soluble (e.g. gentamicin, vancomycin) to survive polymerisation and elute locally.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Basic Science (biomaterials/antibiotics)

stem: A 78-year-old woman presents with an acutely hot, swollen knee. Aspiration yields cloudy fluid; polarised light microscopy shows abundant intracellular rhomboid crystals that are weakly positively birefringent. Radiographs show fine linear calcification of the meniscal cartilage. Which underlying metabolic condition is most strongly associated with this crystal arthropathy and should be screened for, particularly in younger patients?

options.label: A
options.text: Hypoparathyroidism

options.label: B
options.text: Haemochromatosis

options.label: C
options.text: Hyperthyroidism

options.label: D
options.text: Addison disease

options.label: E
options.text: Cystic fibrosis

explanation: The rhomboid, weakly positively birefringent crystals and chondrocalcinosis are diagnostic of calcium pyrophosphate dihydrate (CPPD) deposition disease (pseudogout). When CPPD presents in younger patients or is florid/polyarticular, metabolic associations must be excluded - classically the '4 Hs': Hyperparathyroidism, Haemochromatosis, Hypophosphatasia and Hypomagnesaemia. Of the listed options, haemochromatosis is the recognised association (with a characteristic hook-like osteophyte arthropathy at the 2nd/3rd MCP joints).
learningPoint: CPPD/chondrocalcinosis in a younger patient warrants screening for haemochromatosis, hyperparathyroidism, hypophosphatasia and hypomagnesaemia.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Basic Science (crystal arthropathy)

stem: A 64-year-old man with chronic kidney disease (eGFR 28) and known tophaceous gout is admitted with an acute gout flare affecting the first metatarsophalangeal joint. He is not currently on urate-lowering therapy. NSAIDs are contraindicated due to his renal impairment, and he is intolerant of colchicine. Which is the SINGLE most appropriate treatment for this acute flare?

options.label: A
options.text: Start high-dose allopurinol immediately

options.label: B
options.text: A short course of oral corticosteroids (or intra-articular steroid)

options.label: C
options.text: Start febuxostat at full dose

options.label: D
options.text: Full-dose ibuprofen with a proton pump inhibitor

options.label: E
options.text: Rasburicase infusion

explanation: In an acute gout flare where both NSAIDs (renal impairment) and colchicine (intolerance/CKD dose limits) are unsuitable, corticosteroids - oral or intra-articular - are the appropriate anti-inflammatory choice. Crucially, urate-lowering therapy (allopurinol, febuxostat) should NEVER be initiated during an acute flare as it can prolong or worsen it; if already established it should be continued. Rasburicase is reserved for tumour lysis, not routine gout. So the correct acute treatment is a steroid.
learningPoint: When NSAIDs and colchicine are contraindicated in acute gout, use corticosteroids - and never initiate urate-lowering therapy during the flare.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Basic Science (gout)
BSR Guideline for the Management of Gout

stem: A 60-year-old woman with long-standing rheumatoid arthritis affecting the cervical spine is listed for elective hand surgery under general anaesthesia. She reports occasional occipital headache and transient upper limb paraesthesiae. Which is the SINGLE most important preoperative investigation to perform before airway management?

options.label: A
options.text: Flexion-extension lateral cervical spine radiographs

options.label: B
options.text: Resting ECG

options.label: C
options.text: Chest radiograph

options.label: D
options.text: Hand and wrist radiographs

options.label: E
options.text: Serum rheumatoid factor titre

explanation: Patients with long-standing rheumatoid arthritis are at risk of atlanto-axial instability from erosion of the transverse ligament and the odontoid. Neck manipulation during intubation can precipitate cervical cord compression. Flexion-extension lateral cervical radiographs assess the anterior atlanto-dental interval and, importantly, the posterior interval (space available for the cord); subluxation guides the need for cautious/fibreoptic intubation or further MRI. This is the key safety investigation before any GA in rheumatoid patients with neck symptoms.
learningPoint: Before GA in long-standing rheumatoid arthritis, screen for atlanto-axial instability with flexion-extension cervical radiographs to protect the cord during intubation.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Basic Science / Spine (rheumatoid cervical spine)

stem: A patient with rheumatoid arthritis on methotrexate, a TNF-alpha inhibitor (adalimumab) and low-dose prednisolone is scheduled for total knee replacement. According to current UK rheumatology perioperative guidance, which medication adjustment is most appropriate around the time of surgery?

options.label: A
options.text: Stop methotrexate for 4 weeks before and after surgery

options.label: B
options.text: Continue methotrexate but withhold the TNF-alpha inhibitor, timing surgery at the end of its dosing cycle

options.label: C
options.text: Stop all immunosuppression including prednisolone abruptly on the day of surgery

options.label: D
options.text: Double the prednisolone dose indefinitely postoperatively

options.label: E
options.text: Withhold methotrexate and continue the biologic without interruption

explanation: Current UK/BSR and ACR guidance advises continuing methotrexate through major elective orthopaedic surgery - it does not increase infection or impair healing, and stopping risks a flare. Biologic DMARDs (e.g. adalimumab) are associated with increased infection risk and are conventionally withheld, with surgery planned at the end of the dosing interval and the drug resumed once the wound is healed and there is no infection (note the PERISCOPE trial is examining this). Steroids should be continued (with stress dosing if needed), not stopped abruptly, to avoid an Addisonian crisis.
learningPoint: Continue methotrexate perioperatively but withhold biologics (timing surgery at the end of the dosing cycle) and never stop long-term steroids abruptly.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Basic Science (perioperative DMARDs)
BSR/ACR perioperative management of anti-rheumatic drugs

stem: A 55-year-old man with long-standing ankylosing spondylitis and a rigid, fused, kyphotic thoracolumbar spine presents after a seemingly trivial fall onto his back. He has new severe midline back pain. Initial plain radiographs are reported as 'no acute fracture' against the background of ankylosis. Which is the SINGLE most appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Reassure and discharge with analgesia

options.label: B
options.text: CT of the whole spine (and MRI if needed), treating as an unstable fracture until excluded

options.label: C
options.text: Outpatient physiotherapy referral

options.label: D
options.text: Lumbar spine MRI only if pain persists beyond 6 weeks

options.label: E
options.text: Flexion-extension radiographs of the spine

explanation: An ankylosed spine (ankylosing spondylitis or DISH) behaves like a long bone: even low-energy trauma can cause unstable, often three-column, transverse fractures that are frequently displaced and easily missed on plain films, with a high risk of epidural haematoma and spinal cord injury. Any new spinal pain after trauma in these patients mandates CT of the entire spine (multiple non-contiguous fractures occur), with MRI for cord/ligament/haematoma assessment, and spinal precautions/immobilisation. Flexion-extension views are dangerous and contraindicated.
learningPoint: New back pain after even minor trauma in an ankylosed spine is an unstable fracture until CT of the whole spine proves otherwise.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Spine (ankylosing spondylitis fractures)

stem: A 70-year-old woman with a mechanical (metallic) mitral valve, on long-term warfarin, requires semi-elective hip hemiarthroplasty after a fracture. Which is the SINGLE most appropriate perioperative anticoagulation strategy?

options.label: A
options.text: Stop warfarin and rely on a prophylactic-dose low-molecular-weight heparin only

options.label: B
options.text: Continue therapeutic warfarin through surgery

options.label: C
options.text: Stop warfarin preoperatively and bridge with therapeutic-dose heparin (treatment-dose LMWH or IV unfractionated heparin), resuming warfarin postoperatively

options.label: D
options.text: Switch permanently to a direct oral anticoagulant (DOAC)

options.label: E
options.text: Give vitamin K and proceed with no further anticoagulation cover

explanation: A mechanical heart valve is a high thrombotic risk requiring bridging: warfarin is stopped preoperatively (INR allowed to fall to a safe level for surgery) and therapeutic-dose heparin is used to 'bridge' the period of subtherapeutic INR, withheld briefly around surgery, then warfarin is restarted with heparin continued until the INR is therapeutic again. Prophylactic-dose LMWH alone is insufficient for a mechanical valve. DOACs are contraindicated in mechanical valves. Continuing therapeutic warfarin through major surgery risks catastrophic bleeding, and vitamin K with no cover leaves the valve unprotected.
learningPoint: Mechanical heart valves are high thrombotic risk - bridge with therapeutic heparin around surgery and never use a DOAC.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Basic Science (perioperative medicine)
NICE NG89 / BSH guideline on perioperative anticoagulation

stem: A patient who received low-molecular-weight heparin thromboprophylaxis after a pelvic fracture develops, on day 7, a fall in platelet count from 250 to 70 x10^9/L together with a new deep vein thrombosis and a skin lesion at an injection site. Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia is strongly suspected. Which is the SINGLE most appropriate management step?

options.label: A
options.text: Continue LMWH and transfuse platelets

options.label: B
options.text: Stop all heparin and start a non-heparin anticoagulant (e.g. argatroban, danaparoid or fondaparinux)

options.label: C
options.text: Switch to unfractionated heparin

options.label: D
options.text: Stop all anticoagulation and observe

options.label: E
options.text: Start warfarin immediately as monotherapy

explanation: Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT type II) is an immune, prothrombotic reaction (anti-PF4/heparin antibodies) typically at days 5-10, causing a >50% platelet fall AND thrombosis - paradoxically, despite the low platelets the risk is clotting, not bleeding. Management is to STOP all heparin (including LMWH and flushes) and anticoagulate with a non-heparin agent (argatroban, danaparoid, fondaparinux). Switching to UFH is contraindicated (cross-reactivity), platelet transfusion is avoided (can fuel thrombosis), and warfarin must NOT be started until platelets recover (risk of venous limb gangrene).
learningPoint: In HIT, stop all heparin and anticoagulate with a non-heparin agent - do not give platelets or start warfarin early.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Basic Science (haematology/perioperative)

stem: A patient is to have a spinal anaesthetic for total knee replacement. They take a prophylactic-dose low-molecular-weight heparin once daily for VTE prevention. Regarding the safe timing of neuraxial block to minimise the risk of vertebral canal (epidural) haematoma, which statement is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: The block may be performed at any time as prophylactic LMWH does not affect neuraxial bleeding risk

options.label: B
options.text: Neuraxial puncture should be delayed until at least ~12 hours after a prophylactic LMWH dose

options.label: C
options.text: LMWH should be given 1 hour before the block to optimise timing

options.label: D
options.text: The next LMWH dose may be given immediately as the catheter is removed

options.label: E
options.text: Therapeutic and prophylactic LMWH have identical timing intervals

explanation: Neuraxial blockade carries a risk of vertebral canal haematoma if performed while anticoagulated. UK/AAGBI guidance advises a minimum interval of about 12 hours after a prophylactic dose of LMWH before neuraxial puncture or catheter removal (and ~24 hours after a therapeutic dose - so they are NOT identical). A subsequent LMWH dose should be delayed (commonly ~4 hours) after the block/catheter removal. Saying prophylactic LMWH has no bearing on timing is incorrect and unsafe.
learningPoint: Wait ~12 hours after prophylactic (and ~24 hours after therapeutic) LMWH before neuraxial puncture or catheter removal to avoid epidural haematoma.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Basic Science (anaesthesia)
AAGBI / Regional Anaesthesia UK guideline on anticoagulants and neuraxial block

stem: A 22-year-old man with isolated closed bilateral femoral shaft fractures, awaiting fixation, develops over 24-48 hours acute breathlessness with hypoxia, confusion and a petechial rash over the axillae and conjunctivae. Which set of criteria is most commonly used to support this clinical diagnosis?

options.label: A
options.text: Wells criteria

options.label: B
options.text: Gurd and Wilson criteria

options.label: C
options.text: Ranson criteria

options.label: D
options.text: Light criteria

options.label: E
options.text: Centor criteria

explanation: The triad of respiratory distress/hypoxia, cerebral dysfunction and a petechial rash 24-72 hours after long-bone fracture is classic fat embolism syndrome. Gurd and Wilson criteria (major: petechial rash, respiratory insufficiency, cerebral involvement; with minor features such as tachycardia, pyrexia, retinal changes, fat in urine/sputum, thrombocytopenia, raised ESR) are the recognised diagnostic framework. Wells is for VTE, Ranson for pancreatitis, Light for pleural effusions and Centor for streptococcal pharyngitis - useful distractors testing whether the eponym is correctly linked.
learningPoint: Fat embolism syndrome (respiratory, cerebral and petechial features after long-bone fracture) is supported by the Gurd and Wilson criteria.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Basic Science / Trauma (fat embolism)

stem: A new point-of-care assay for periprosthetic joint infection is evaluated. In a population with a low pre-test probability of infection, the test has a sensitivity of 95% and a specificity of 80%. The clinical team wants to use it to CONFIRM (rule in) infection in patients who test positive. Which statement best describes the limitation of using this test in this way?

options.label: A
options.text: High sensitivity means a positive result reliably confirms infection regardless of prevalence

options.label: B
options.text: In a low-prevalence setting the positive predictive value will be low, so many positives will be false positives

options.label: C
options.text: Specificity determines the negative predictive value, which is irrelevant here

options.label: D
options.text: Sensitivity and specificity change with prevalence, invalidating the test

options.label: E
options.text: A high specificity test is best for ruling out (SnNout) disease

explanation: Predictive values depend on disease prevalence, whereas sensitivity and specificity are largely intrinsic test properties. In a low-prevalence population, even a fairly specific test yields a relatively low positive predictive value, so a large proportion of positive results are false positives - limiting its use to 'rule in' disease. Mnemonics: a highly SENSitive test, when Negative, rules OUT (SnNout); a highly SPECific test, when Positive, rules IN (SpPin). Option E misstates this (it conflates specificity with ruling out). Sensitivity/specificity do not themselves change with prevalence, so D is wrong.
learningPoint: Positive predictive value falls with low prevalence - a positive result on a moderately specific test in a low-risk population is often a false positive.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Basic Science (statistics/EBM)

stem: Theme: Study design and sources of bias. For each description, select the SINGLE best matching term from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Selection bias

options.label: B
options.text: Lead-time bias

options.label: C
options.text: Recall bias

options.label: D
options.text: Confounding

options.label: E
options.text: Type II error

options.label: F
options.text: Type I error

options.label: G
options.text: Intention-to-treat analysis

options.label: H
options.text: Immortal time bias

explanation: These distinguish core EBM concepts. Apparent improvement in survival purely because earlier screening detection moves the diagnosis date back, without changing the disease course, is lead-time bias. Differential accuracy of past-exposure reporting between cases and controls in a case-control study is recall bias. Failing to detect a true difference because the study is underpowered (falsely accepting a false null) is a Type II (beta) error. Analysing randomised participants in their originally assigned groups regardless of compliance is intention-to-treat analysis, which preserves randomisation and avoids attrition bias.
learningPoint: Match the mechanism: earlier detection without changed outcome = lead-time bias; underpowered miss = Type II error; analyse-as-randomised = intention-to-treat.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Basic Science (statistics/EBM)

emiLeads.stem: A screening programme appears to improve 5-year survival, but this is entirely explained by cancers being detected earlier without any change in the time of death.
In a case-control study of NSAID use and fracture non-union, patients with non-union scrutinise and report their past drug use more thoroughly than controls.
A trial concludes a new implant shows 'no significant difference' in revision rates, but the study was too small to detect a true difference that genuinely exists.
Participants who were randomised to a surgical arm but crossed over to non-operative treatment are still analysed within the surgical group to preserve randomisation.

stem: A 41-year-old cyclist falls onto the point of the shoulder and sustains an isolated fracture of the lateral end of the clavicle. The fracture line lies medial to the coracoclavicular ligaments, and on the radiograph the medial fragment is displaced superiorly while the lateral fragment (with the acromioclavicular joint) remains aligned with the acromion. The skin is intact. Which feature of this Neer classification subtype most directly explains why it carries a high reported non-union rate if treated non-operatively?

options.label: A
options.text: The fracture is intra-articular and involves the acromioclavicular joint surface

options.label: B
options.text: The coracoclavicular ligaments are detached from the medial fragment, which is then displaced by the weight of the arm and muscle pull

options.label: C
options.text: The lateral fragment is devascularised because its only blood supply is from the acromion

options.label: D
options.text: The fracture is inherently unstable because the trapezius pulls the lateral fragment posteriorly

options.label: E
options.text: The conoid ligament remains attached to the lateral fragment, tethering it inferiorly

explanation: This is a Neer type II lateral clavicle fracture: the fracture lies medial to the coracoclavicular (CC) ligaments, so the ligaments stay with the distal fragment while the proximal (medial) fragment loses its CC restraint. The unsupported medial fragment is then pulled superiorly/posteriorly by trapezius and sternocleidomastoid and the lateral fragment/arm sags inferiorly under gravity, creating displacement and persistent motion at the fracture — hence the markedly higher non-union rate (often quoted up to ~20-30%) compared with type I (lateral to intact CC ligaments) and type III (intra-articular, CC ligaments intact). It is this CC-ligament-related instability, not articular involvement or devascularisation, that drives the non-union risk and prompts many surgeons to offer fixation in active patients. Type III is the intra-articular subtype.
learningPoint: Neer type II lateral clavicle fractures are unstable because the CC ligaments stay on the distal fragment, leaving the medial fragment unrestrained — this drives their high non-union rate.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Shoulder Trauma / Clavicle section
Neer CS, Fractures of the distal clavicle

stem: A 22-year-old is brought in after a motorcycle collision with a flail, swollen upper limb. The chest radiograph shows the entire scapula displaced laterally away from the thoracic spine compared with the contralateral side, with a widely displaced clavicle fracture. The limb is pulseless and there is a complete brachial plexus palsy. Which statement best reflects the nature and prognosis of this injury?

options.label: A
options.text: It is a closed forequarter (traumatic) amputation equivalent with a guarded limb prognosis; priority is to exclude and manage subclavian/axillary vascular injury

options.label: B
options.text: It is a floating shoulder that simply requires fixation of the clavicle to restore the superior shoulder suspensory complex

options.label: C
options.text: It is a benign radiographic appearance caused by patient rotation and needs only a repeat film

options.label: D
options.text: The lateral scapular displacement is diagnostic of an acromioclavicular joint dislocation

options.label: E
options.text: The neurological deficit will reliably recover once the clavicle is plated

explanation: Lateral displacement of the whole scapula on a non-rotated chest film, with a flail pulseless limb and a complete plexus palsy, describes scapulothoracic dissociation — a closed, internal forequarter amputation. It results from a violent traction force and is frequently associated with subclavian/axillary arterial disruption and severe (often complete avulsion) brachial plexus injury. Management priorities are ATLS resuscitation and urgent vascular assessment/repair to salvage the limb; the neurological prognosis is poor and many patients ultimately have a non-functional or amputated limb. A 'floating shoulder' (ipsilateral clavicle and scapular neck fractures disrupting the superior suspensory complex) is a different, far less catastrophic entity. The measured lateral scapular displacement distinguishes dissociation from rotation artefact.
learningPoint: Lateral scapular displacement with a flail, pulseless, plexus-palsied limb = scapulothoracic dissociation (a closed forequarter amputation) — exclude major vascular injury first; the neurological outlook is poor.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Shoulder Trauma chapter
BOAST: Peripheral nerve injury / vascular injury principles

stem: You are asked to apply the Neer classification to a proximal humeral fracture. Using Neer's criteria, a fracture fragment is counted as a separate 'part' only if it meets a defined threshold of displacement. Which of the following correctly states that threshold?

options.label: A
options.text: More than 1 cm of displacement OR more than 45 degrees of angulation between fragments

options.label: B
options.text: More than 2 cm of displacement OR more than 30 degrees of angulation

options.label: C
options.text: Any cortical discontinuity, regardless of displacement or angulation

options.label: D
options.text: More than 5 mm of displacement OR more than 20 degrees of angulation

options.label: E
options.text: More than 1 cm of displacement of the greater tuberosity only, with angulation disregarded

explanation: Neer's classification divides the proximal humerus into four anatomical segments (articular head, greater tuberosity, lesser tuberosity, shaft). A segment is only designated a displaced 'part' if it is displaced more than 1 cm OR angulated more than 45 degrees relative to the others; otherwise the fracture is considered minimally displaced ('one-part') irrespective of the number of fracture lines. This is the key examiner discriminator — counting fracture lines rather than applying the displacement threshold is the classic error. (The greater tuberosity is often treated with a lower practical surgical threshold of ~5 mm because of cuff/impingement consequences, but that is a separate clinical caveat, not the Neer definition.)
learningPoint: Neer parts are defined by displacement >1 cm OR angulation >45 degrees — fracture lines alone do not create a 'part'.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Shoulder Trauma chapter
Neer CS, Displaced proximal humeral fractures (classification)

stem: A 70-year-old woman has a displaced three-part proximal humeral fracture fixed with a proximal humeral locking plate. At her 8-week review she reports increasing shoulder pain. Radiographs show the humeral head in varus and one of the proximal locking screws now appears to project beyond the articular surface into the glenohumeral joint, although the position of the screws within the head is unchanged from the immediate post-operative films. Which mechanism best explains this finding?

options.label: A
options.text: Backing-out of the locking screws due to failure of the screw-plate interface

options.label: B
options.text: Secondary varus collapse of the head with loss of medial calcar support, causing the fixed-angle screws to perforate ('cut-through') the articular surface

options.label: C
options.text: Avascular necrosis of the humeral head with collapse of the subchondral bone

options.label: D
options.text: Deep infection causing osteolysis around the screws

options.label: E
options.text: A new fall causing a periprosthetic fracture around the plate

explanation: Because locking screws are fixed-angle and do not move relative to the plate, screws that were intra-osseous initially but later appear intra-articular indicate that the bone (head) has moved on the construct — classically secondary varus collapse from loss of the medial calcar buttress in osteoporotic bone. The screws effectively perforate the joint as the head collapses around them ('screw cut-through' or primary/secondary penetration). This is the commonest mode of locking-plate failure in elderly proximal humeral fractures and is the rationale for restoring medial support (anatomical reduction of the calcar, an inferomedial 'calcar' screw, and avoiding varus). True screw back-out is uncommon with locking constructs, and AVN, infection and periprosthetic fracture would show different radiographic features.
learningPoint: Intra-articular screw migration with unchanged screw-in-head position after locking-plate fixation means the head has collapsed into varus — a medial-calcar support failure, not screw back-out.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Shoulder Trauma chapter

stem: When deciding between head-preserving fixation and arthroplasty for a complex proximal humeral fracture, the Hertel criteria help predict humeral head ischaemia. Which combination of features is the strongest predictor that the articular segment has lost its blood supply?

options.label: A
options.text: An isolated greater tuberosity fracture with the head in valgus

options.label: B
options.text: A short calcar (less than 8 mm of metaphyseal head extension), a disrupted medial hinge, and an anatomical-neck (basicervical) fracture

options.label: C
options.text: A two-part surgical neck fracture with an intact medial periosteal hinge

options.label: D
options.text: A valgus-impacted four-part fracture with the medial hinge intact

options.label: E
options.text: A lesser tuberosity avulsion with a long calcar segment

explanation: Hertel showed that the most reliable predictors of humeral head ischaemia are: a short calcar segment (less than ~8 mm of metaphyseal head extension remaining attached to the articular fragment), a disrupted medial hinge (medial displacement of the shaft >2 mm), and an anatomical-neck fracture pattern. When all three are present the positive predictive value for ischaemia is very high. This matters because head ischaemia/AVN risk pushes the decision toward arthroplasty (or, in the elderly, reverse shoulder arthroplasty) rather than ORIF. Importantly, a valgus-impacted four-part fracture with a preserved medial hinge can retain perfusion and may be reconstructable, which is why hinge integrity and calcar length — not simply the 'number of parts' — drive the prognosis.
learningPoint: Hertel's strongest predictors of proximal humeral head ischaemia are a short calcar (<8 mm), a disrupted medial hinge, and an anatomical-neck fracture.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Shoulder Trauma chapter
Hertel R et al., Predictors of humeral head ischaemia

stem: A 50-year-old develops a radial nerve palsy in association with a closed spiral fracture at the junction of the middle and distal thirds of the humeral shaft (a Holstein-Lewis pattern). Compared with a radial nerve palsy from a mid-shaft transverse fracture, which statement is most accurate regarding this specific pattern?

options.label: A
options.text: The nerve is more likely to be entrapped or tethered at the fracture site as it passes through the lateral intermuscular septum, so a lower threshold for exploration is reasonable if recovery does not occur

options.label: B
options.text: The radial nerve is never involved at this level because it has already pierced the septum more proximally

options.label: C
options.text: It mandates immediate exploration of the nerve in all cases at the time of presentation

options.label: D
options.text: Spontaneous recovery is impossible once a Holstein-Lewis palsy occurs

options.label: E
options.text: The palsy indicates an axillary nerve injury rather than a radial nerve injury

explanation: The Holstein-Lewis fracture is a spiral fracture of the distal third of the humeral shaft where the radial nerve, which pierces the lateral intermuscular septum at this level to enter the anterior compartment, is relatively fixed and therefore more vulnerable to being trapped, contused or tethered by the displaced spike. Most closed radial nerve palsies (including many Holstein-Lewis injuries) still recover spontaneously, so initial management remains observation with a wrist-extension splint and serial examination/neurophysiology. However, because of the higher chance of mechanical entrapment at the septum, surgeons hold a somewhat lower threshold for exploration if there is no clinical or electrophysiological recovery, in addition to the standard exploration indications (open fracture, or palsy developing after manipulation/fixation).
learningPoint: A Holstein-Lewis (distal-third spiral) fracture tethers the radial nerve at the intermuscular septum, lowering the threshold for exploration if recovery stalls — though most still recover.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Humeral Shaft chapter
Holstein A, Lewis GB, Fractures of the humerus with radial nerve paralysis

stem: A 58-year-old presents 7 months after non-operative treatment of a humeral shaft fracture with a painful, mobile non-union. Reviewing why this fracture failed to unite, which fracture/patient characteristic is most strongly associated with non-union of a humeral diaphyseal fracture treated in a functional brace?

options.label: A
options.text: A transverse fracture pattern at the proximal-third/middle-third junction, particularly with distraction or interposition

options.label: B
options.text: A long spiral fracture of the mid-shaft with good bony apposition

options.label: C
options.text: Young age and dense cortical bone

options.label: D
options.text: An associated, fully recovered radial nerve palsy

options.label: E
options.text: Early functional bracing within the first week

explanation: Humeral shaft non-union is more likely with transverse fracture patterns (less surface area and less inherent rotational stability than spiral/oblique fractures), proximal-third fractures, fracture distraction or soft-tissue interposition, inadequate immobilisation, and host factors such as smoking, diabetes, osteoporosis and obesity (a large arm reduces the bracing effect). Spiral mid-shaft fractures with good apposition generally unite well in a brace; young patients with healthy bone and early appropriate bracing are favourable; and a radial nerve palsy that has recovered does not influence bony union. Recognising distraction/transverse patterns prompts earlier consideration of surgery to avoid non-union.
learningPoint: Transverse pattern, proximal-third location, fracture distraction/interposition and host factors (smoking, diabetes, obesity) predispose humeral shaft fractures to non-union.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Humeral Shaft chapter

stem: A 38-year-old falls onto an outstretched hand and sustains an isolated coronal-plane shear fracture of the capitellum. The lateral radiograph shows a 'double-arc sign', and CT confirms the fracture extends posteriorly to involve the trochlea. There is a mechanical block to elbow flexion. Which statement best guides management of this fracture type?

options.label: A
options.text: These coronal shear fractures are extra-articular and are best treated in a cast in extension

options.label: B
options.text: The 'double-arc sign' indicates trochlear involvement; the displaced articular fragment should be openly reduced and stabilised, typically with countersunk headless compression screws placed anterior-to-posterior, to restore the articular surface and allow early motion

options.label: C
options.text: Simple fragment excision gives the best long-term results regardless of fragment size

options.label: D
options.text: A lateral collateral ligament repair alone will restore stability and articular congruity

options.label: E
options.text: Total elbow arthroplasty is the treatment of choice in this young patient

explanation: Coronal-plane shear fractures of the capitellum (and trochlea) are intra-articular injuries best classified by the Dubberley system, which specifically notes posterior extension and trochlear involvement — the 'double-arc sign' on the lateral film reflects the subchondral bone of the capitellum and the lateral trochlear ridge, signalling that the trochlea is involved (a higher Dubberley type with worse prognosis). A displaced fragment with a mechanical block needs anatomical open reduction and stable fixation, classically with buried/headless compression screws inserted from anterior to posterior (or posterior-to-anterior depending on the fragment), allowing early motion to prevent stiffness. Excision is reserved for very small fragments only; casting in extension causes stiffness; LCL repair does not address the articular fragment; and TEA is inappropriate in a young patient with a reconstructable fracture.
learningPoint: A capitellar shear fracture with a 'double-arc sign' has trochlear extension (high Dubberley type) — fix the articular fragment (headless compression screws) and mobilise early.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Elbow Trauma chapter
Dubberley JH et al., Capitellar and trochlear fractures

stem: During ORIF of an intra-articular distal humeral fracture, the surgeon uses a trans-olecranon (chevron) osteotomy to access the articular surface. Which statement best reflects a key principle or recognised complication associated with this approach?

options.label: A
options.text: The osteotomy should be made through the bare area of the olecranon (the non-articular 'bare spot') to minimise articular cartilage damage, and the most frequently reported complication is osteotomy-site problems such as non-union or symptomatic hardware

options.label: B
options.text: A transverse osteotomy through the deepest part of the trochlear notch is preferred to maximise stability

options.label: C
options.text: The olecranon osteotomy reliably eliminates the risk of ulnar nerve injury

options.label: D
options.text: Osteotomy is mandatory for all extra-articular distal humeral fractures

options.label: E
options.text: The osteotomy is best fixed with a single transverse Kirschner wire without a tension band

explanation: A trans-olecranon osteotomy gives excellent exposure of the distal humeral articular surface for complex (type C) fractures. To spare articular cartilage it is made as an apex-distal chevron through the 'bare area' (the non-articular sulcus) of the trochlear notch, and it must be repaired robustly (tension-band wiring or a plate/screw construct) — osteotomy-related complications (non-union/delayed union and symptomatic/prominent hardware requiring removal) are the most commonly reported drawbacks of the approach. The ulnar nerve must still be identified and protected (the osteotomy does not remove that risk), the approach is reserved for intra-articular access (not extra-articular fractures, which can be plated without it), and a lone transverse K-wire is inadequate fixation.
learningPoint: A chevron olecranon osteotomy is made through the bare area to spare cartilage; its main downsides are osteotomy non-union and symptomatic hardware — and it does not protect the ulnar nerve.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Elbow Trauma chapter
BOA distal humeral fracture surgical principles

subtopic: Olecranon — Mayo classification and fixation choice
stem: A 67-year-old sustains a displaced, comminuted olecranon fracture with a fragment of the coronoid base and subtle ulnohumeral subluxation on the lateral radiograph. Using the Mayo classification, this corresponds to a displaced, comminuted, unstable pattern. Why is tension-band wiring inappropriate here, and what is preferred?

options.label: A
options.text: Tension-band wiring requires a simple non-comminuted fracture with an intact opposite cortex to generate compression; with comminution and instability it would shorten and malreduce the articular surface, so a contoured olecranon (dorsal) plate is preferred

options.label: B
options.text: Tension-band wiring is contraindicated only because of the patient's age; an age threshold of 60 applies

options.label: C
options.text: Fragment excision and triceps advancement is the best option for this comminuted unstable pattern

options.label: D
options.text: Tension-band wiring is ideal because comminution increases the compressive effect of the wire

options.label: E
options.text: Non-operative treatment in a cast at 90 degrees is preferred for displaced comminuted patterns

explanation: The Mayo classification grades olecranon fractures by displacement, comminution and ulnohumeral stability. Tension-band wiring works by converting triceps tension into articular-side compression, but this depends on a simple, transverse, non-comminuted fracture with an intact volar cortex to act as a buttress; in a comminuted, unstable pattern (Mayo type IIB/III) the construct would collapse and shorten across the comminution, narrowing the trochlear notch and producing articular incongruity and instability. A contoured dorsal olecranon plate (which can span comminution, capture a coronoid/proximal fragment and maintain length) is therefore preferred. Fragment excision with triceps advancement is reserved for small, non-articular, very comminuted proximal fragments in low-demand patients and is unsuitable when stability and a large articular/coronoid component are involved. Non-operative management does not address displacement or instability.
learningPoint: Comminuted, unstable olecranon fractures (higher Mayo types) require plate fixation, not tension-band wiring, because TBW shortens across comminution and distorts the trochlear notch.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Elbow Trauma chapter

stem: A 35-year-old labourer falls from a ladder and sustains a comminuted radial head fracture. He also complains of pain at the wrist. Examination reveals tenderness over the distal radio-ulnar joint, and forearm radiographs raise the suspicion of proximal migration of the radius. Which statement best describes the implication for management of this injury?

options.label: A
options.text: This is an Essex-Lopresti injury with longitudinal forearm (interosseous membrane) instability; the radial head must be preserved or replaced — never simply excised — because excision allows proximal radial migration and chronic wrist pain

options.label: B
options.text: Excision of the radial head is the treatment of choice and will relieve both the elbow and wrist symptoms

options.label: C
options.text: The wrist pain is incidental; treat the radial head fracture in isolation

options.label: D
options.text: An Essex-Lopresti injury is defined by an associated distal radius fracture

options.label: E
options.text: The interosseous membrane is irrelevant to longitudinal forearm stability once the radial head is fractured

explanation: An Essex-Lopresti injury is a comminuted radial head fracture combined with disruption of the interosseous membrane and the distal radio-ulnar joint, producing longitudinal instability of the forearm. The radial head is the critical secondary stabiliser preventing proximal migration of the radius when the interosseous membrane is incompetent; therefore it must be retained (fixed) or, if unreconstructable, replaced with a radial head prosthesis — bare excision is contraindicated because it permits proximal radial migration, ulnocarpal abutment and chronic wrist pain that is very difficult to salvage. The DRUJ must also be assessed and addressed. The wrist symptoms are the key clue and must never be dismissed; the injury is defined by interosseous membrane/DRUJ disruption (not a distal radius fracture).
learningPoint: Radial head fracture plus DRUJ/wrist pain = Essex-Lopresti; preserve or replace the radial head (never excise) because it is the last restraint to proximal radial migration.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Elbow / Forearm chapter
Essex-Lopresti P, Fractures of the radial head with distal radio-ulnar dislocation

stem: Six months after radial head replacement for a comminuted fracture, a patient has persistent lateral elbow pain, loss of terminal extension, and radiographs showing widening of the lateral ulnohumeral joint space with early erosion of the capitellum. Which technical error is the most likely cause?

options.label: A
options.text: An over-long (oversized) radial head prosthesis causing 'overstuffing' of the radiocapitellar joint

options.label: B
options.text: An undersized prosthesis that has subsided into the radial neck

options.label: C
options.text: Failure to repair the medial collateral ligament

options.label: D
options.text: Excessive forearm pronation during implantation

options.label: E
options.text: Use of a bipolar rather than a monoblock head

explanation: An over-long radial head prosthesis 'overstuffs' the radiocapitellar joint: it loads the capitellum excessively (causing pain and capitellar erosion/cartilage wear) and asymmetrically opens or alters the ulnohumeral articulation — typically producing widening of the lateral ulnohumeral joint, loss of terminal extension, and pain.
