Intra-operatively, the head should sit just proximal to the lesser sigmoid notch and reproduce native length (the contralateral side and the level of the coronoid help judge this); the lateral ulnohumeral joint should be symmetric on a true AP film. Undersizing/subsidence presents differently (proximal migration, instability), and the other options do not produce this specific overstuffing picture.
learningPoint: Lateral elbow pain, lost extension and capitellar erosion with a widened lateral ulnohumeral joint after radial head replacement indicate an over-long 'overstuffed' prosthesis.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Elbow Trauma chapter

stem: A 46-year-old sustains a fall and complains of medial elbow pain. CT shows an isolated fracture of the anteromedial facet of the coronoid with no radial head fracture. There is subtle varus malalignment of the ulnohumeral joint. Which statement is correct regarding this injury?

options.label: A
options.text: It is part of a varus posteromedial rotatory instability pattern and, despite the small fragment, usually warrants buttress fixation (often via a medial approach) with assessment of the lateral collateral ligament, because non-fixation risks ulnohumeral incongruity and rapid arthritis

options.label: B
options.text: It is always a stable injury that can be mobilised early without fixation regardless of size

options.label: C
options.text: It indicates a terrible triad and the radial head must be replaced

options.label: D
options.text: The anteromedial facet has no role in elbow stability and the fragment can be excised

options.label: E
options.text: It results from a posterolateral rotatory mechanism with valgus loading

explanation: In O'Driscoll's classification, an anteromedial facet coronoid fracture is the signature of varus posteromedial rotatory instability (a fall imparting an axial + varus + internal-rotation force, often with a lateral collateral ligament/lateral ulnar collateral ligament injury). The anteromedial facet is relatively unbuttressed by the ulna and is a key medial stabiliser of the ulnohumeral joint; even a small fragment, if left displaced, allows the trochlea to subluxate into varus, producing joint incongruity and accelerated post-traumatic arthritis. Management is buttress plate (or screw) fixation of the facet, usually through a medial approach, with repair of the lateral collateral ligament complex as needed and re-testing of stability. It is distinct from the terrible triad (which is posterolateral rotatory: radial head fracture + coronoid tip fracture + LCL injury) and must not simply be excised.
learningPoint: An anteromedial coronoid facet fracture signals varus posteromedial rotatory instability — buttress-fix even small fragments and address the LCL to prevent rapid ulnohumeral arthritis.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Elbow Trauma chapter
O'Driscoll SW et al., Coronoid fractures classification

stem: A 60-year-old woman with osteoporosis sustains a Monteggia fracture-dislocation in which the radial head is dislocated posteriorly and the ulnar fracture is angulated apex-posteriorly (Bado type II). Compared with the paediatric Bado type I injury, which statement best characterises the adult posterior (Bado II) Monteggia lesion?

options.label: A
options.text: It is the commonest adult Monteggia pattern, is frequently associated with a radial head and/or coronoid fracture, and behaves like a proximal ulna fracture-dislocation requiring precise anatomical reconstruction of the ulna (often with a contoured plate) and attention to the radiocapitellar and coronoid components

options.label: B
options.text: It is most common in young children and resolves with closed reduction and casting alone

options.label: C
options.text: The radial head is dislocated anteriorly, defining the type II pattern

options.label: D
options.text: Associated radial head or coronoid fractures essentially never occur with this pattern

options.label: E
options.text: The posterior interosseous nerve is the structure most at risk, exactly as in anterior dislocations

explanation: In the Bado classification, type II Monteggia injuries have posterior dislocation of the radial head with apex-posterior ulnar angulation; this is the most common adult Monteggia pattern (in contrast to children, where Bado type I/anterior predominates). Adult Bado II lesions are essentially proximal ulna fracture-dislocations and are frequently complicated by associated radial head and/or coronoid fractures and articular involvement of the proximal ulna. Treatment hinges on precise anatomical restoration of the ulna (length, alignment and the dorsal angulation) with a robust contoured plate, after which the radiocapitellar relationship is checked; any radial head or coronoid component must be addressed. The PIN is classically at risk in anterior (type I/III) dislocations rather than posterior ones, so option E is wrong.
learningPoint: The adult Bado type II Monteggia (posterior radial head dislocation, apex-posterior ulna) is the commonest adult pattern and often carries radial head/coronoid fractures — restore the ulna anatomically and address the associated articular injuries.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Forearm Trauma chapter

stem: A trainee describes a Galeazzi fracture as a 'fracture of necessity'. They also recall that the likelihood of distal radio-ulnar joint (DRUJ) instability rises as the radial fracture lies closer to the wrist. Which single statement most accurately captures the principle being referred to?

options.label: A
options.text: The more distal the radial shaft fracture (the closer to the DRUJ), the higher the likelihood of associated DRUJ instability; 'fracture of necessity' reflects that operative fixation of the radius is required in adults to achieve a stable, congruent result

options.label: B
options.text: 'Fracture of necessity' means the fracture necessarily heals without surgery

options.label: C
options.text: DRUJ instability is more likely the more proximal the radial fracture lies

options.label: D
options.text: The term refers to a mandatory ulnar shaft fracture accompanying the radial fracture

options.label: E
options.text: It indicates that the radial head is necessarily dislocated

explanation: A Galeazzi fracture is a distal-third radial shaft fracture with DRUJ disruption. It is termed a 'fracture of necessity' because, in adults, non-operative treatment reliably fails (the deforming forces redisplace the radius and DRUJ), so anatomical open reduction and plate fixation of the radius is necessary. The risk of DRUJ instability correlates with how distal the radial fracture is — fractures within roughly the distal 7.5 cm of the radius (closer to the DRUJ) carry a substantially higher rate of DRUJ instability (the 'critical distance' concept). After radial fixation the DRUJ is tested: if reducible and stable, mobilise; if reducible but unstable, immobilise in supination ± temporary pinning; if irreducible, open the joint (often blocked by the extensor carpi ulnaris tendon). Galeazzi involves the radius and DRUJ, not the ulnar shaft or radial head.
learningPoint: Galeazzi is a 'fracture of necessity' (adults need radial ORIF), and the more distal the radial fracture, the greater the risk of DRUJ instability.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Forearm Trauma chapter

subtopic: Distal radius fracture — eponyms and patterns
stem: Theme: Eponymous and descriptive distal radius fracture patterns. For each clinical/radiographic description, select the single most appropriate diagnosis from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Colles' fracture

options.label: B
options.text: Smith's fracture (reverse Colles')

options.label: C
options.text: Barton's fracture

options.label: D
options.text: Chauffeur's (Hutchinson) fracture

options.label: E
options.text: Die-punch fracture

options.label: F
options.text: Galeazzi fracture

options.label: G
options.text: Lunate facet ('critical corner') fragment

emiLeads.stem: A 30-year-old falls onto the palm with the wrist flexed and sustains a volarly displaced, extra-articular distal radius fracture; the hand and distal fragment are displaced anteriorly (a 'garden spade' deformity).
An intra-articular shear fracture of the volar rim of the distal radius with the carpus subluxing volarly along with the fragment; the fracture line obliquely crosses the articular surface.

A radial styloid fracture caused by direct impaction of the scaphoid against the styloid (classically from a crank-handle recoil), with the line running obliquely from the articular surface out through the styloid.
A depressed articular fragment of the lunate fossa, driven distally by axial load through the lunate, leaving a central impaction in the distal radial articular surface.
A small but mechanically critical volar-ulnar articular fragment of the distal radius which, if not captured, allows the carpus to subluxate volarly despite an otherwise well-applied volar plate.

explanation: These distinguish the common distal radius eponyms. A Smith's fracture is the volarly displaced extra-articular pattern (reverse Colles', 'garden spade' deformity) from a fall onto a flexed wrist, whereas a Colles' fracture is the dorsally displaced extra-articular injury. A Barton's fracture is an intra-articular rim shear (volar or dorsal) where the carpus subluxes with the marginal fragment — the volar Barton's being a true fracture-dislocation. The chauffeur's (Hutchinson) fracture is the radial styloid fracture from scaphoid impaction. A die-punch fracture is a depressed lunate-fossa articular fragment from axial loading through the lunate. The lunate facet or 'critical corner' fragment is the volar-ulnar fragment that, if missed, permits volar carpal subluxation even after volar plating, and often needs a specific buttress (fragment-specific) implant.
learningPoint: Know the distal radius eponyms by mechanism and displacement: Smith (volar, extra-articular), Barton (rim shear with carpal subluxation), chauffeur/Hutchinson (radial styloid), die-punch (lunate-fossa depression), and the volar-ulnar 'critical corner' fragment.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Distal Radius / Wrist chapter

stem: A trainee is asked which factors the Frykman classification of distal radius fractures takes into account. Which statement most accurately reflects what the Frykman system is based on, and a recognised limitation?

options.label: A
options.text: It is based on the involvement of the radiocarpal and/or distal radio-ulnar joint surfaces and the presence or absence of an associated ulnar styloid fracture, but it does not account for displacement, shortening or comminution

options.label: B
options.text: It grades fractures purely by the degree of dorsal angulation

options.label: C
options.text: It is based on the amount of radial shortening alone

options.label: D
options.text: It classifies fractures solely by whether they are open or closed

options.label: E
options.text: It incorporates bone mineral density and patient age as its primary variables

explanation: The Frykman classification stratifies distal radius fractures by intra-articular involvement — whether the fracture extends into the radiocarpal joint, the distal radio-ulnar joint, both, or neither — and doubles each category according to whether an associated ulnar styloid fracture is present (giving eight types, I-VIII, with higher numbers indicating more articular involvement and a worse prognosis). Its acknowledged limitation, and a common exam point, is that it ignores the parameters that actually drive treatment decisions and outcome: the direction and magnitude of displacement, radial shortening, dorsal/volar tilt and the degree of comminution. This is why the AO/OTA system (extra-articular A, partial articular B, complete articular C) is often preferred for surgical planning.
learningPoint: Frykman classifies distal radius fractures by radiocarpal/DRUJ articular involvement and ulnar styloid status, but ignores displacement, shortening and comminution.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Distal Radius chapter
Frykman G, Fracture of the distal radius (classification)

subtopic: Distal radius — EPL rupture after undisplaced fracture
stem: A 55-year-old woman is treated non-operatively in a cast for an undisplaced (minimally displaced) extra-articular distal radius fracture. Five weeks later, after the cast is removed, she notices she can no longer lift her thumb off a flat surface (loss of retropulsion) and cannot actively extend the thumb interphalangeal joint, though passive movement is full and there is no sensory loss. Which is the most likely explanation, and the appropriate treatment?

options.label: A
options.text: Attritional rupture of the extensor pollicis longus tendon at Lister's tubercle; treat with an extensor indicis proprius (EIP) tendon transfer, as direct repair is usually not possible

options.label: B
options.text: A posterior interosseous nerve palsy that will recover spontaneously and needs only observation

options.label: C
options.text: A missed scaphoid fracture causing the loss of thumb extension

options.label: D
options.text: Carpal tunnel syndrome compressing the motor branch to the thumb

options.label: E
options.text: Rupture of the flexor pollicis longus tendon

explanation: Delayed loss of active thumb IP extension and retropulsion after a distal radius fracture — classically an undisplaced or minimally displaced one treated non-operatively — is the hallmark of extensor pollicis longus (EPL) tendon rupture. The EPL is vulnerable where it hooks around Lister's tubercle in the third dorsal compartment; even without displacement, local haematoma/oedema can compromise its tenuous watershed blood supply, leading to attritional rupture typically a few weeks later. Because the ruptured ends are frayed and retracted with a fixed gap, direct repair is generally not feasible; the standard reconstruction is an extensor indicis proprius (EIP)-to-EPL tendon transfer, which restores thumb extension and retropulsion. The absence of sensory loss and intact passive motion exclude a nerve palsy; FPL rupture would impair thumb flexion, not extension.
learningPoint: Delayed loss of thumb IP extension/retropulsion after a (often undisplaced) distal radius fracture = EPL rupture at Lister's tubercle — reconstruct with an EIP-to-EPL transfer.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Distal Radius / Hand chapter

stem: A 28-year-old presents 14 months after an untreated scaphoid waist fracture with radial-sided wrist pain and stiffness. Radiographs show an established non-union with a humpback (volar flexion) deformity, sclerosis, and early radioscaphoid joint space narrowing with osteophytes at the radial styloid, but the proximal pole appears vascular and the rest of the carpus is preserved. Which term and management principle apply?

options.label: A
options.text: This is scaphoid non-union advanced collapse (SNAC) in an early stage; the priority is correcting the humpback deformity with bone grafting and stable fixation, often with a radial styloidectomy, before pan-carpal arthritis supervenes

options.label: B
options.text: This is Kienbock's disease and requires lunate-focused treatment

options.label: C
options.text: This is a normal radiological variant and needs no treatment

options.label: D
options.text: This is a SLAC wrist caused by scapholunate ligament insufficiency

options.label: E
options.text: Because arthritis has begun, the only option at any stage is a total wrist fusion

explanation: An ununited scaphoid develops a predictable pattern of degeneration termed scaphoid non-union advanced collapse (SNAC), which progresses from radial-styloid/radioscaphoid arthritis (early) to midcarpal and pancarpal involvement (later), mirroring the SLAC sequence but driven by the scaphoid fracture rather than scapholunate ligament failure. In early SNAC with a still-reconstructable, vascular scaphoid, the aim is to achieve union and correct the humpback flexion deformity (typically with a structural/wedge bone graft to restore length and alignment, plus compression screw fixation), often combined with a radial styloidectomy to address localised arthritis. Once more advanced (midcarpal/pancarpal) arthritis is established, salvage procedures (proximal row carpectomy or scaphoid excision with four-corner fusion, or total wrist fusion) become appropriate — so fusion is not the only option at every stage. This is distinct from Kienbock's (lunate) and SLAC (scapholunate ligament) disease.
learningPoint: An ununited scaphoid causes SNAC arthritis (radial styloid → midcarpal → pancarpal); treat early reconstructable non-unions with deformity-correcting grafting/fixation, and reserve salvage fusions for advanced arthritis.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Wrist / Carpus chapter

subtopic: Carpus and hand — fracture eponyms and complications
stem: Theme: Carpal and hand fracture/dislocation diagnoses. For each clinical scenario, select the single most likely diagnosis from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Hook of hamate fracture

options.label: B
options.text: Bennett's fracture-dislocation

options.label: C
options.text: Rolando fracture

options.label: D
options.text: Stener lesion (gamekeeper's/skier's thumb)

options.label: E
options.text: Kienbock's disease

options.label: F
options.text: Preiser's disease

options.label: G
options.text: Pisiform fracture

emiLeads.stem: A golfer presents with deep ulnar-sided palm pain and occasional little-finger numbness/weakness after a mistimed swing; a carpal tunnel/oblique ('carpal bridge') view and CT confirm a fracture of a hook-shaped projection on the volar ulnar carpus, and there is concern about ulnar nerve irritation in Guyon's canal.

A man punches a wall and sustains an intra-articular fracture-subluxation of the base of the thumb metacarpal in which a small volar-ulnar fragment stays attached to the trapezium while the metacarpal shaft is pulled proximally, radially and into adduction by abductor pollicis longus.

A similar axial-load injury to the thumb metacarpal base, but the radiograph shows a comminuted, T- or Y-shaped intra-articular fracture with several fragments rather than a single volar-ulnar fragment, carrying a worse prognosis.

A skier falls with the thumb forced into abduction and now has instability of the thumb metacarpophalangeal joint to radial stress; the torn ulnar collateral ligament has retracted and become trapped superficial to the adductor aponeurosis, so it will not heal without surgery.
A young adult develops insidious wrist pain and stiffness with no clear trauma; radiographs show sclerosis, fragmentation and flattening of the lunate, and the patient has negative ulnar variance.

explanation: These cover important hand/carpal eponyms and their pitfalls. A hook of hamate fracture occurs in racquet/club/bat sports from the butt of the handle, causes ulnar-sided palm pain and can irritate the ulnar nerve/artery in Guyon's canal; it is poorly seen on standard films (needs a carpal tunnel view or CT) and often goes to non-union, sometimes requiring fragment excision. A Bennett's fracture is the two-part intra-articular thumb metacarpal base fracture-dislocation where the volar-ulnar (volar oblique/anterior oblique ligament) fragment holds onto the trapezium while APL displaces the shaft — it is unstable and needs reduction and fixation. A Rolando fracture is the comminuted T/Y intra-articular variant with a worse prognosis. A Stener lesion is a complete ulnar collateral ligament tear of the thumb MCP joint that retracts above the adductor aponeurosis (so it cannot heal non-operatively and requires surgical repair). Kienbock's disease is avascular necrosis/collapse of the lunate, associated with negative ulnar variance. (Preiser's disease is idiopathic AVN of the scaphoid; the pisiform fracture is a direct-blow injury to the volar ulnar wrist.)
learningPoint: Distinguish hand/carpal eponyms: hook of hamate (sports, Guyon's canal, hard to image), Bennett (2-part) vs Rolando (comminuted) thumb base fractures, the Stener lesion (UCL trapped above adductor aponeurosis — needs surgery), and Kienbock's (lunate AVN, negative ulnar variance).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Hand and Wrist chapter
BSSH hand fracture and thumb UCL guidance

stem: A 25-year-old sustains a Bennett's fracture-dislocation of the thumb metacarpal base. When planning closed reduction and percutaneous Kirschner-wire fixation, understanding the deforming forces is essential. Which muscle is principally responsible for the proximal, radial and dorsal subluxation of the thumb metacarpal shaft, and what reduction manoeuvre counteracts it?

options.label: A
options.text: Abductor pollicis longus pulls the shaft proximally, radially (dorsally) and into adduction; reduction is achieved by axial traction, pronation, abduction and extension of the thumb with pressure on the metacarpal base

options.label: B
options.text: Flexor pollicis longus pulls the shaft dorsally; reduction is by wrist flexion

options.label: C
options.text: Adductor pollicis pulls the shaft into abduction; reduction is by thumb adduction

options.label: D
options.text: Extensor pollicis brevis pulls the shaft volarly; reduction is by supination

options.label: E
options.text: The opponens pollicis pulls the shaft proximally; reduction is by opposition

explanation: In a Bennett's fracture-dislocation, the small volar-ulnar (anterior oblique ligament) fragment remains attached to the trapezium, while the thumb metacarpal shaft is destabilised. The principal deforming force is abductor pollicis longus (inserting on the metacarpal base), which pulls the shaft proximally, dorsally/radially and into adduction (supination); adductor pollicis also contributes to the adduction/flexion deformity. The reduction manoeuvre therefore applies longitudinal traction with thumb pronation, palmar abduction and extension while pushing the metacarpal base ulnarwards/volarwards, and the position is held with percutaneous K-wires (metacarpal-to-trapezium or metacarpal-to-metacarpal); fragments large enough may be fixed with a screw. Recognising the APL force is the key applied point.
learningPoint: In Bennett's fracture, abductor pollicis longus displaces the metacarpal shaft proximally/radially/dorsally and into adduction — reduce with traction, pronation, abduction and extension, then pin.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Hand chapter

stem: A 28-year-old motorcyclist arrives haemodynamically unstable (systolic BP 80 mmHg despite 1 L crystalloid). The AP pelvic radiograph shows symphyseal diastasis of 4 cm with complete disruption of the sacroiliac joint and the hemipelvis externally rotated and 'opened out'. Which Young-Burgess pattern is most consistent, and what is the principal mechanism of haemorrhage?

options.label: A
options.text: Lateral compression I - venous bleeding from impacted cancellous sacral surfaces

options.label: B
options.text: Anteroposterior compression III - increased pelvic volume with presacral venous plexus and internal iliac arterial bleeding

options.label: C
options.text: Anteroposterior compression II - anterior SI ligament disruption with isolated venous injury

options.label: D
options.text: Vertical shear - L5 transverse process avulsion with predominantly arterial bleeding

options.label: E
options.text: Lateral compression III - windswept pelvis with contralateral open-book component

explanation: Symphyseal diastasis >2.5 cm with complete (anterior and posterior) SI disruption and external rotation defines APC III, a fully unstable hemipelvis. The increased pelvic volume reduces tamponade and permits substantial bleeding from the presacral venous plexus, often with an internal iliac arterial contribution (e.g. superior gluteal artery). Management is binder followed by angioembolisation/REBOA per major-haemorrhage protocol.
learningPoint: APC III is the open-book pattern with complete SI disruption and the highest transfusion requirement of the AP-compression group.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Pelvis & Acetabulum
BOAST: Pelvic and Acetabular Fracture Management

subtopic: Pelvic ring - LC vs binder pitfall
stem: A 41-year-old pedestrian struck from the side has a stable blood pressure. CT shows complete sacral fracture impaction with overlapping pubic rami and internal rotation of the left hemipelvis (a lateral compression injury). An over-tightened circumferential pelvic binder has been applied in the field. What is the most important specific hazard of leaving the binder firmly applied in this particular pattern?

options.label: A
options.text: Pressure necrosis over the greater trochanters within 6 hours

options.label: B
options.text: Over-reduction causing further internal rotation and visceral (bladder/rectal) injury

options.label: C
options.text: Conversion of the injury to a vertical shear pattern

options.label: D
options.text: Worsening of an associated open-book component

options.label: E
options.text: Compression of the lumbosacral plexus causing foot drop

explanation: In lateral compression injuries the hemipelvis is already internally rotated and the pelvic volume is reduced. A binder reduces volume in open-book (APC) injuries but in LC patterns excessive compression can over-reduce/over-internally-rotate the hemipelvis, potentially worsening visceral injury and over-shortening. Binders are still applied empirically pre-imaging, but once an LC pattern is confirmed and the patient is stable the binder should be loosened/removed. Pressure necrosis is a general binder risk but is time- and not pattern-specific.
learningPoint: Pelvic binders help open-book (APC) injuries but can over-reduce lateral compression patterns - reassess once the pattern is known.
references: BOAST: Pelvic and Acetabular Fracture Management

stem: A 55-year-old sustains an acetabular fracture in a road traffic collision. On the Judet oblique views the iliopectineal line is intact but the ilioischial line, posterior rim and the teardrop are all disrupted, and the obturator-oblique view shows the 'spur sign'. Which fracture pattern does the spur sign specifically indicate?

options.label: A
options.text: Associated both-column fracture

options.label: B
options.text: T-type fracture

options.label: C
options.text: Posterior column with posterior wall fracture

options.label: D
options.text: Anterior column with posterior hemitransverse fracture

options.label: E
options.text: Transverse with posterior wall fracture

explanation: The spur sign on the obturator-oblique view is pathognomonic of an associated both-column fracture. It is produced by the intact portion of the iliac wing that remains attached to the axial skeleton while both columns and the entire articular surface 'float' free (no part of the articular cartilage remains attached to the intact ilium - the basis of secondary congruence). Recognition matters because both-column fractures can sometimes be managed non-operatively if secondary congruence is satisfactory.
learningPoint: The spur sign on the obturator-oblique view is pathognomonic of an associated both-column acetabular fracture.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Acetabular Fractures

stem: Theme: Surgical approach to acetabular fractures. For each fracture pattern, select the single most appropriate operative approach. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Kocher-Langenbeck approach

options.label: B
options.text: Ilioinguinal approach

options.label: C
options.text: Anterior intrapelvic (modified Stoppa) approach

options.label: D
options.text: Extended iliofemoral approach

options.label: E
options.text: Combined anterior and posterior (sequential) approaches

options.label: F
options.text: Smith-Petersen approach

options.label: G
options.text: Watson-Jones approach

explanation: Posterior wall and posterior column fractures are reached through the Kocher-Langenbeck. Anterior column and most anterior-column-posterior-hemitransverse and both-column fractures are addressed anteriorly via the ilioinguinal or modified Stoppa (the latter giving excellent access to the quadrilateral plate). The extended iliofemoral is reserved for complex/old (>3 week) both-column or transtectal patterns needing simultaneous column exposure, accepting a higher heterotopic ossification and abductor-injury burden.
learningPoint: Match acetabular approach to column involvement: K-L for posterior, ilioinguinal/Stoppa for anterior, extended iliofemoral for complex or delayed both-column patterns.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Acetabular Surgical Approaches

emiLeads.stem: A simple posterior wall fracture with an associated marginal impaction in a 30-year-old, 4 days post-injury.
An isolated anterior column fracture with significant quadrilateral plate displacement and medial subluxation of the femoral head.
A 6-week-old neglected transtectal both-column fracture in a young patient requiring simultaneous reduction of both columns under direct vision.
An anterior column with posterior hemitransverse fracture in an elderly patient, with the main displacement and articular impaction anterior.

stem: A 38-year-old sustains a displaced subcapital femoral neck fracture; on the AP radiograph the fracture line makes an angle of approximately 70 degrees to the horizontal. The decision is made for reduction and internal fixation. Why does this Pauwels grade most influence the choice and configuration of fixation?

options.label: A
options.text: A high Pauwels angle generates predominantly compressive forces favouring cannulated screws alone

options.label: B
options.text: A high Pauwels angle generates large shear forces, favouring a fixed-angle device (e.g. sliding hip screw or medial buttress plate) to resist vertical displacement

options.label: C
options.text: A high Pauwels angle indicates a low risk of avascular necrosis so fixation choice is unimportant

options.label: D
options.text: A low Pauwels angle generates the greatest shear and mandates arthroplasty

options.label: E
options.text: The Pauwels angle predicts only nonunion risk and not the mechanical environment

explanation: Pauwels III (>50 degrees, here 70 degrees) means the fracture line is near-vertical, so physiological loading creates predominantly shear rather than compression across the fracture. Shear promotes vertical sliding and varus collapse. Parallel cannulated screws resist this poorly, so a fixed-angle construct (sliding hip screw, sometimes augmented with an anti-glide/medial buttress plate or a derotation screw) better neutralises the shear. In young patients anatomical reduction and stable fixation are prioritised over arthroplasty.
learningPoint: High Pauwels angle = high shear; favour a fixed-angle device (+/- medial buttress) over parallel screws to resist varus collapse.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Femoral Neck Fractures

stem: A fit 35-year-old presents 14 hours after a displaced intracapsular femoral neck fracture sustained in a fall from a ladder. After discussion he is listed for urgent anatomical reduction and internal fixation. Which single intra-operative factor has the strongest evidence base for reducing the risk of subsequent avascular necrosis and nonunion?

options.label: A
options.text: Performing a capsulotomy to decompress the intracapsular haematoma

options.label: B
options.text: Achieving an anatomical (or slight valgus) reduction

options.label: C
options.text: Using three rather than two cannulated screws

options.label: D
options.text: Operating within 6 hours of injury

options.label: E
options.text: Adding a fibular strut graft

options.label: label_unused
options.text: placeholder

explanation: Quality of reduction is the most important modifiable predictor of union and femoral-head survival in young femoral-neck fractures. A non-anatomical (especially varus) reduction strongly predicts failure. Capsulotomy and very early surgery have biological rationale and are commonly advocated, but the evidence that the precise hour of surgery within the first day independently alters AVN is weak and inconsistent; reduction quality is the consistent determinant. Screw number and supplementary grafting are secondary.
learningPoint: In young intracapsular hip fractures, the quality of reduction outweighs the exact timing of surgery for AVN/nonunion risk.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Femoral Neck Fractures

stem: An 82-year-old has a peritrochanteric femoral fracture. The fracture line runs obliquely from the medial proximal cortex to the lateral distal cortex (a reverse obliquity, AO/OTA 31-A3). Why is a standard sliding hip screw (dynamic hip screw) considered a poor choice for this configuration?

options.label: A
options.text: The lag screw cannot obtain purchase in the femoral head

options.label: B
options.text: Controlled collapse causes uncontrolled medial translation of the shaft and high failure/cut-out rates

options.label: C
options.text: The fracture is intracapsular so dynamic compression risks AVN

options.label: D
options.text: The plate barrel angle cannot be matched to the neck-shaft angle

options.label: E
options.text: Reverse oblique fractures unite too quickly, causing implant prominence

explanation: In a reverse oblique fracture the orientation of the fracture line means that the dynamic sliding of a hip screw, instead of producing controlled impaction, allows the shaft to translate medially (the lateral wall provides no buttress). This produces collapse, varus and a high rate of fixation failure. A cephalomedullary nail (or a fixed-angle blade plate / 95-degree device) is biomechanically superior because it provides an intramedullary buttress that resists medial translation.
learningPoint: Reverse oblique (31-A3) peritrochanteric fractures should be fixed with a cephalomedullary nail, not a sliding hip screw.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Intertrochanteric Fractures

stem: A 24-year-old with an isolated closed mid-shaft femoral fracture, splinted but not yet fixed, becomes confused and tachypnoeic 36 hours after injury with an SpO2 of 88% on air and a petechial rash over the axillae and conjunctivae. Chest radiograph shows bilateral patchy infiltrates. Which statement best reflects current understanding of this complication?

options.label: A
options.text: Definitive diagnosis requires fat globules in the urine before treatment can begin

options.label: B
options.text: It is a clinical diagnosis (e.g. Gurd criteria) and management is supportive; early stabilisation of the fracture reduces incidence

options.label: C
options.text: High-dose corticosteroids are the established definitive treatment

options.label: D
options.text: Reamed intramedullary nailing should now be avoided permanently in all long-bone fractures

options.label: E
options.text: The syndrome only occurs intra-operatively during reaming and never from the injury itself

explanation: Fat embolism syndrome is a clinical diagnosis (classically Gurd/Wilson criteria - hypoxaemia, neurological change, petechiae, with supportive minor criteria). The cornerstone is supportive care (oxygen, ventilatory support). Early fracture stabilisation reduces the incidence, which is one argument for timely fixation. Steroid prophylaxis remains controversial and is not standard definitive treatment. Reaming can contribute embolic load, but nailing is not contraindicated; in the multiply-injured/borderline patient damage-control external fixation may be chosen.
learningPoint: Fat embolism syndrome is a clinical diagnosis managed supportively; early fracture stabilisation lowers its incidence.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Femoral Shaft Fractures / Polytrauma

stem: Six weeks after antegrade intramedullary nailing of a comminuted mid-shaft femoral fracture, a patient complains that the foot 'turns out' when walking. CT rotational profile confirms a 22-degree external malrotation of the femur compared with the contralateral side. Which intra-operative technique would most reliably have prevented this complication?

options.label: A
options.text: Use of a fracture table with maximal traction

options.label: B
options.text: Comparing the lesser trochanter profile and cortical step/diameter signs intra-operatively, with reference to the uninjured side

options.label: C
options.text: Routinely locking the nail dynamically rather than statically

options.label: D
options.text: Reaming 2 mm larger than the chosen nail diameter

options.label: E
options.text: Inserting the nail retrograde rather than antegrade

explanation: Rotational malalignment is a common and under-recognised complication of femoral nailing, particularly in comminuted fractures where length and cortical contact cues are lost. The lesser trochanter shape sign (comparing the profile of the lesser trochanter with the contralateral side at matched patellar orientation) plus cortical step and diameter signs allow intra-operative judgement of rotation. Significant malrotation (>15 degrees) can cause functional problems and may warrant derotation osteotomy. Traction, locking mode, reaming and nail direction do not control rotation.
learningPoint: Femoral malrotation after nailing is prevented by intra-operative comparison with the uninjured side (lesser trochanter and cortical step signs), not by traction or locking mode.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Femoral Shaft Fractures

stem: A 78-year-old with a well-fixed posterior-stabilised total knee replacement sustains a comminuted supracondylar distal femoral fracture above the femoral component after a fall. The femoral component has an open (non-captured) intercondylar box. Which factor most strongly determines whether a retrograde intramedullary nail is a feasible fixation option?

options.label: A
options.text: The patient's bone mineral density

options.label: B
options.text: Whether the femoral component design has an open intercondylar notch that will accept the nail

options.label: C
options.text: The presence of a patellar resurfacing component

options.label: D
options.text: The time since the index arthroplasty

options.label: E
options.text: Whether the tibial insert is cruciate-retaining or posterior-stabilised

explanation: For a retrograde nail to be used in a periprosthetic distal femoral fracture, the femoral component must have an intercondylar geometry (open box / sufficient notch width) that allows nail passage; many PS and most 'closed-box' designs preclude this. Knowing the implant design is therefore the key determinant. Bone quality and comminution influence the choice between nail and locked plate but do not absolutely prevent nailing. Distal femoral locked plating is the common alternative when the box is closed.
learningPoint: Retrograde nailing of a periprosthetic distal femoral fracture is only feasible if the femoral component's intercondylar box is open enough to admit the nail.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Periprosthetic Fractures
Su classification of periprosthetic distal femur fractures

stem: A 46-year-old returns 8 weeks after tension-band wiring of a transverse patellar fracture complaining of an extensor lag and anterior knee pain. Radiographs show fragmentation at the distal pole with proximal migration of the K-wires and a 6 mm articular step. Which factor most likely contributed to this failure of the tension-band principle?

options.label: A
options.text: The fracture was too proximal for tension banding

options.label: B
options.text: Comminution at the distal pole prevented the construct from converting tensile forces into compression across an intact cortical buttress

options.label: C
options.text: The knee was immobilised for too long post-operatively

options.label: D
options.text: Stainless steel wire was used instead of titanium

options.label: E
options.text: The patient was non-weight-bearing rather than weight-bearing

explanation: The tension-band principle requires an intact cortex on the side opposite the implant so that tensile (distracting) forces generated by quadriceps pull are converted into compression across the fracture. With distal-pole comminution there is no competent buttress, the band cannot generate compression, and the construct fails with wire migration and articular step. Comminuted or distal-pole fractures are better managed with separate lag screws +/- a longitudinal plate, partial patellectomy with patellar tendon reattachment, or a mesh/cerclage technique.
learningPoint: Tension banding requires an intact opposite cortex; distal-pole comminution defeats the principle and needs alternative fixation.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Patella Fractures

stem: A 30-year-old has a medial tibial plateau fracture (Schatzker IV) following a high-energy varus injury. Beyond the bony injury, which associated finding should specifically heighten suspicion and alter early management?

options.label: A
options.text: An associated patellar dislocation

options.label: B
options.text: A knee dislocation equivalent with potential popliteal artery and common peroneal nerve injury

options.label: C
options.text: An ipsilateral femoral neck fracture

options.label: D
options.text: A Segond fracture indicating an isolated ACL tear only

options.label: E
options.text: An isolated medial meniscal tear with no vascular implication

explanation: Schatzker IV (medial plateau) fractures are frequently high-energy and behave as a knee-dislocation equivalent. They carry a disproportionately high risk of neurovascular injury - popliteal artery disruption and common peroneal nerve palsy - and of compartment syndrome. Documentation of distal pulses, ABPI and a low threshold for CT angiography are essential. Recognising the medial plateau fracture as a marker of severe injury changes early management more than the bony pattern alone.
learningPoint: A Schatzker IV (medial plateau) fracture is a knee-dislocation equivalent - actively exclude popliteal artery and peroneal nerve injury and compartment syndrome.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Tibial Plateau Fractures

stem: A bicolumnar tibial plateau fracture has a discrete posteromedial coronal fragment well demonstrated on CT. The surgeon plans a buttress plate for this fragment. Why is a standard anterolateral or anteromedial plate inadequate for this specific fragment, and which approach best addresses it?

options.label: A
options.text: It is adequately stabilised by lag screws from anterior; no specific approach is needed

options.label: B
options.text: The posteromedial fragment shears posteriorly under axial load and requires a posteromedial buttress plate via a posteromedial (or reversed L) approach

options.label: C
options.text: The fragment is best ignored as it does not affect the articular surface

options.label: D
options.text: An anterolateral plate captures it through ligamentotaxis alone

options.label: E
options.text: Only an external fixator can control this fragment

explanation: A coronal posteromedial fragment is subject to shear and tends to displace posteriorly and inferiorly under axial loading; anteriorly placed screws or plates cannot resist this shear (a buttress must be applied on the side of displacement). A dedicated posteromedial buttress plate placed via a posteromedial or reversed-L approach (often with the patient in a floating or prone position) provides antiglide support. Failure to buttress this fragment is a recognised cause of early loss of reduction.
learningPoint: Posteromedial coronal plateau fragments require a posteromedial buttress (antiglide) plate; anterior fixation cannot resist their shear displacement.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Tibial Plateau Fractures

stem: A 50-year-old has a high-energy comminuted distal tibial pilon fracture with marked swelling, fracture blisters and an intact but tense soft-tissue envelope. What is the most appropriate early management strategy?

options.label: A
options.text: Immediate definitive open reduction and internal fixation within 24 hours regardless of swelling

options.label: B
options.text: Spanning external fixation (+/- fibular fixation) now, with delayed definitive ORIF once the soft tissues settle

options.label: C
options.text: Primary ankle arthrodesis

options.label: D
options.text: Below-knee plaster and non-weight-bearing for 6 weeks then ORIF

options.label: E
options.text: Primary amputation

explanation: High-energy pilon fractures have a soft-tissue injury that drives the complication rate. The accepted staged ('span, scan, plan') protocol is to apply a spanning external fixator early to restore length and stability and protect the soft tissues, obtain CT for definitive planning, then perform definitive internal fixation once swelling has resolved and skin creases return (often 10-21 days). Early definitive ORIF through swollen tissue markedly increases wound breakdown and deep infection.
learningPoint: High-energy pilon fractures are managed in stages: spanning ex-fix first, definitive ORIF only once the soft-tissue envelope recovers.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Pilon Fractures

stem: An ankle injury is classified as supination-external rotation. Following the Lauge-Hansen sequence, the radiograph already shows an anterior tibiofibular (Chaput/Tillaux) ligament injury and a short oblique fibular fracture at the level of the syndesmosis. According to the staged sequence, which structure is injured NEXT as energy increases, and what is its clinical relevance?

options.label: A
options.text: The deltoid ligament or medial malleolus - completing the injury and rendering it unstable (bimalleolar-equivalent)

options.label: B
options.text: The anterior talofibular ligament - producing chronic lateral instability

options.label: C
options.text: The calcaneofibular ligament - causing subtalar instability

options.label: D
options.text: The spring ligament - producing acquired flatfoot

options.label: E
options.text: The Lisfranc ligament - producing midfoot instability

explanation: In supination-external rotation (the commonest pattern) the sequence is: (1) anterior inferior tibiofibular ligament, (2) spiral fibular fracture at the syndesmosis, (3) posterior inferior tibiofibular ligament or posterior malleolus, (4) medial malleolus fracture or deltoid rupture. Progression to stage 4 (medial injury) signifies a bimalleolar-equivalent unstable ankle requiring surgical stabilisation. Recognising the medial-side injury (clinically or on stress/weight-bearing views) is what distinguishes a stable SER-2 from an unstable SER-4.
learningPoint: In SER injuries, medial-side (deltoid/medial malleolus) failure is the final stage and converts a stable into an unstable, surgically treated ankle.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Ankle Fractures

stem: After fixation of a Weber C ankle fracture with syndesmotic screws, post-operative CT shows the fibula sitting too anterior and externally rotated within the incisura compared with the uninjured side. What is the most appropriate management and why?

options.label: A
options.text: Accept the position; syndesmotic malreduction has no functional consequence

options.label: B
options.text: Revise the syndesmotic reduction, because malreduction is the strongest predictor of poor functional outcome after these injuries

options.label: C
options.text: Remove all metalwork immediately to allow the fibula to spring back into place

options.label: D
options.text: Convert to a hindfoot fusion

options.label: E
options.text: Apply a below-knee cast and allow remodelling of the incisura

explanation: Syndesmotic malreduction (commonly the fibula left anterior/externally rotated or translated) is the single most important predictor of a poor outcome after syndesmotic injury. Because plain films are insensitive, intra-operative or early post-operative CT comparing with the contralateral side is increasingly used; a clearly malreduced syndesmosis should be revised. The incisura does not remodel to correct a fixed malposition, and casting will not address it.
learningPoint: Syndesmotic malreduction is the leading predictor of poor outcome - confirm reduction (ideally with CT vs the other side) and revise if malreduced.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Syndesmosis Injuries

stem: A 27-year-old underwent ORIF of a displaced talar neck fracture (Hawkins type III) 8 weeks ago. On the current AP radiograph there is a subchondral radiolucent band in the talar dome. How should this 'Hawkins sign' be interpreted?

options.label: A
options.text: It indicates established avascular necrosis and predicts collapse

options.label: B
options.text: It indicates an intact blood supply (subchondral resorption requires vascularity) and is a favourable sign against AVN

options.label: C
options.text: It indicates infection of the talar body

options.label: D
options.text: It indicates nonunion of the talar neck

options.label: E
options.text: It is an artefact with no prognostic value

explanation: The Hawkins sign is a subchondral lucency in the talar dome usually visible at 6-8 weeks. It reflects subchondral bone resorption (disuse osteopenia), a process that can only occur if the bone is vascularised - so its presence indicates a viable, perfused talar body and is a good prognostic sign that argues against avascular necrosis. Its absence is suggestive (but not diagnostic) of impaired vascularity; MRI gives definitive assessment. The higher the Hawkins fracture type, the higher the AVN risk.
learningPoint: A present Hawkins sign (subchondral lucency at 6-8 weeks) indicates a vascularised talar body and argues against AVN.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Talus Fractures

stem: A 44-year-old labourer falls from a roof and sustains a displaced intra-articular calcaneal fracture. The lateral radiograph shows the Bohler angle reduced to 5 degrees (normal 20-40 degrees). Which statement best explains the significance of this measurement and its relation to the Sanders classification?

options.label: A
options.text: A reduced Bohler angle reflects loss of calcaneal height/joint depression; the Sanders classification (CT-based) then grades the posterior facet articular comminution and guides prognosis

options.label: B
options.text: A reduced Bohler angle indicates an extra-articular fracture and excludes posterior facet involvement

options.label: C
options.text: The Bohler angle quantifies the number of articular fragments directly

options.label: D
options.text: A reduced Bohler angle predicts a better outcome with non-operative care

options.label: E
options.text: The Sanders classification is based on the lateral radiograph, not CT

explanation: Bohler angle is measured on the lateral radiograph; a reduced angle indicates collapse of calcaneal height with depression of the posterior facet and is associated with worse outcomes. The Sanders classification is based on the coronal CT image at the widest point of the posterior facet and grades the number and location of articular fracture lines (I-IV); higher Sanders grades (especially IV) predict worse results and a higher likelihood of needing primary subtalar fusion. The two are complementary - one a height/severity surrogate, the other an articular-comminution grade.
learningPoint: Bohler angle (lateral X-ray) flags loss of height; the Sanders grade (coronal CT of the posterior facet) grades articular comminution and prognosis.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Calcaneal Fractures

stem: Eighteen months after non-operative treatment of a displaced intra-articular calcaneal fracture, a patient has persistent lateral hindfoot pain, difficulty with shoe-wear and pain on eversion. Examination shows a widened heel and impingement-type pain beneath the lateral malleolus. Which late complication is most likely and what is the typical corrective procedure?

options.label: A
options.text: Subtalar arthritis only - treated by tendoachilles lengthening

options.label: B
options.text: Lateral calcaneal wall blow-out causing subfibular/peroneal impingement - treated by lateral wall exostectomy +/- subtalar fusion

options.label: C
options.text: Tarsal tunnel syndrome - treated by plantar fascia release

options.label: D
options.text: Calcaneal nonunion - treated by bone grafting alone

options.label: E
options.text: Sinus tarsi syndrome - treated by ankle arthrodesis

explanation: Malunited calcaneal fractures characteristically heal with a widened heel and a laterally displaced/blown-out lateral wall that impinges on the peroneal tendons and the lateral malleolus (subfibular impingement), causing lateral pain, peroneal tendon dysfunction and shoe-wear problems, often with loss of height and a more horizontal talus. Reconstruction typically involves lateral wall exostectomy (decompression), peroneal tendon management and subtalar (sometimes with calcaneal osteotomy) fusion to correct height/alignment.
learningPoint: Calcaneal malunion causes a widened heel with lateral wall blow-out and subfibular/peroneal impingement; treat with lateral wall decompression +/- subtalar fusion.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Calcaneal Malunion

stem: A 25-year-old footballer has midfoot pain and swelling after a twisting injury with the foot plantarflexed. Initial non-weight-bearing radiographs are reported as normal, but there is plantar bruising of the midfoot. Which single next investigation is most appropriate to avoid missing a significant injury?

options.label: A
options.text: A weight-bearing (or stress) AP foot radiograph, looking for diastasis between the medial cuneiform and the base of the second metatarsal and a fleck sign

options.label: B
options.text: An immediate MRI of the ankle

options.label: C
options.text: A bone scan at 6 weeks

options.label: D
options.text: A standing lateral spine radiograph

options.label: E
options.text: No further imaging; reassure and discharge

explanation: Plantar ecchymosis of the midfoot is a classic flag for a Lisfranc (tarsometatarsal) injury, which is frequently missed on non-weight-bearing films because the ligamentous disruption only manifests under load. A weight-bearing AP (and ideally comparison/stress) view may reveal diastasis between the medial cuneiform and the second metatarsal base or a 'fleck sign' (avulsion of the Lisfranc ligament). CT/MRI are useful adjuncts, but the key, inexpensive step that converts a 'normal' study into a diagnosis is the weight-bearing radiograph.
learningPoint: Suspect a Lisfranc injury with midfoot plantar bruising and a 'normal' non-weight-bearing film - obtain weight-bearing/stress views to reveal diastasis or the fleck sign.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Lisfranc Injuries

stem: Theme: Compartment syndrome of the lower limb. For each scenario, select the single most appropriate immediate action. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Split/remove all circumferential dressings and casts, place the limb at heart level, reassess within 30 minutes

options.label: B
options.text: Proceed directly to emergency four-compartment fasciotomy

options.label: C
options.text: Measure intracompartmental pressure and compare with diastolic blood pressure (delta-p)

options.label: D
options.text: Elevate the limb well above the heart and apply ice

options.label: E
options.text: Reassure and discharge with analgesia

options.label: F
options.text: Obtain urgent CT angiography of the leg

explanation: Acute compartment syndrome is primarily a clinical diagnosis; pain out of proportion and pain on passive stretch are the earliest signs (pulselessness and paraesthesia are late). The first step in an alert patient with a tight cast is to release everything constricting and keep the limb at heart height (elevation above the heart lowers arterial inflow and worsens perfusion pressure). If clinical signs persist after decompressing dressings, or in an obtunded/unreliable patient, continuous pressure monitoring using delta-p (diastolic minus compartment pressure) under ~30 mmHg supports the diagnosis. Established/clear compartment syndrome mandates immediate fasciotomy without waiting for monitoring.
learningPoint: Compartment syndrome: release constricting casts and keep the limb at heart level first; use delta-p monitoring in unreliable patients; proceed to fasciotomy when the diagnosis is clear.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Compartment Syndrome
BOAST: Diagnosis and Management of Compartment Syndrome of the Limbs

emiLeads.stem: A conscious patient 4 hours after closed tibial nailing with severe pain, in a tight back-slab, normal sensation, palpable pulses, pain on passive toe extension.
An intubated polytrauma patient with a tibial shaft fracture; the leg is tense and the on-call team needs an objective threshold to decide on surgery.
A patient 6 hours post-injury with a tense leg, paralysis of the toes, absent sensation in the first web space and a firm anterior compartment, dressings already removed.
A well child with a minor undisplaced fracture, comfortable in a backslab, who has been positioned with the leg propped high on three pillows and is now complaining of increasing discomfort.

subtopic: Osteolysis & aseptic loosening — biology and radiographic interpretation
stem: Theme: the biology and radiographic features of wear-mediated osteolysis and aseptic loosening around a total hip replacement. For each statement, select the single most appropriate answer from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Macrophages

options.label: B
options.text: RANKL / RANK signalling

options.label: C
options.text: Osteoprotegerin (OPG)

options.label: D
options.text: Sub-micron polyethylene particles (0.1–1.0 µm)

options.label: E
options.text: Effective joint space

options.label: F
options.text: Endotoxin (bacterial debris) adherent to particles

options.label: G
options.text: Linear (rather than volumetric) wear

options.label: H
options.text: Granulomatous expansile, well-marginated lysis around a well-fixed implant

emiLeads.stem: The primary cell type that phagocytoses wear particles and, when unable to digest them, releases the cytokine cascade (TNF-alpha, IL-1, IL-6) that drives peri-implant bone loss.
The final common pathway by which the particle-stimulated cytokine milieu promotes osteoclast differentiation and activation, tipping the balance towards bone resorption.
The particle size range that is most biologically active in stimulating the macrophage-driven osteolytic response.

The concept describing how joint fluid under cyclical pressure carries wear debris to any region communicating with the articulation, explaining lysis at sites remote from the bearing such as screw holes and the distal stem.

The characteristic radiographic pattern that distinguishes particle-disease osteolysis from infection or from simple loosening — focal, expansile, well-marginated lucency adjacent to a component that is itself still well fixed.

explanation: Wear-particle osteolysis is macrophage-driven: macrophages phagocytose particles, and the most bioactive are sub-micron (~0.1–1.0 µm) polyethylene particles. Frustrated phagocytosis releases TNF-alpha, IL-1 and IL-6, which upregulate RANKL relative to its decoy receptor OPG; RANKL–RANK signalling drives osteoclastogenesis and net resorption. The 'effective joint space' concept (Schmalzried) explains how pressurised fluid transports debris to any communicating region, producing lysis at screw holes or the stem tip far from the bearing. Radiographically, particle disease typically produces focal, expansile, well-marginated (often 'balloon-like') lucency around an otherwise well-fixed implant, in contrast to the diffuse interface lucency of loosening or the aggressive ill-defined lysis of infection.
learningPoint: Osteolysis = sub-micron PE particles → macrophage cytokines → RANKL>OPG → osteoclastic resorption; the effective joint space spreads debris, giving expansile lysis around well-fixed implants.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Adult Reconstruction (wear & osteolysis)

subtopic: Periprosthetic femoral fracture — Vancouver classification matching
stem: Theme: the Vancouver classification of periprosthetic femoral fractures around a total hip replacement. For each scenario, select the single most appropriate Vancouver category from the list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Type AG

options.label: B
options.text: Type AL

options.label: C
options.text: Type B1

options.label: D
options.text: Type B2

options.label: E
options.text: Type B3

options.label: F
options.text: Type C

options.label: G
options.text: Intra-operative, non-displaced cortical perforation

emiLeads.stem: A fracture around the stem with a definitely LOOSE stem but with adequate remaining proximal bone stock for a revision stem to gain fixation.
A fracture around the stem with a well-fixed, stable stem and good bone stock.
A fracture occurring well distal to the tip of a well-fixed stem, at the supracondylar region.
A fracture around the stem with a loose stem AND severely deficient/comminuted proximal bone unable to support a standard revision stem.
An isolated avulsion of the lesser trochanter adjacent to a well-fixed stem, which should also prompt a search for underlying osteolysis.

explanation: Vancouver classifies periprosthetic femoral fractures by location, stem fixation and bone stock. Type A = trochanteric (AG greater, AL lesser). Type B is at/around the stem and is subdivided by stem stability and bone: B1 = well-fixed stem, good bone (ORIF); B2 = loose stem, adequate bone (revise to a long stem); B3 = loose stem with deficient/poor proximal bone (revision with bone-loss reconstruction, e.g. proximal femoral replacement or allograft-prosthesis composite). Type C is distal to the stem tip and is treated as a fracture in its own right while respecting the stress riser at the stem tip. An isolated lesser-trochanter avulsion (AL) should prompt assessment for osteolysis weakening the calcar.
learningPoint: Vancouver: A = trochanteric (AG/AL); B = around the stem graded by stem fixation/bone (B1 fix, B2 revise, B3 revise + reconstruct); C = below the stem tip.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Adult Reconstruction (Vancouver classification)

subtopic: Revision THA — Paprosky femoral bone loss and reconstruction
stem: Theme: the Paprosky classification of FEMORAL bone loss at revision total hip arthroplasty and the corresponding reconstruction strategy. For each scenario, select the single most appropriate reconstruction option from the list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Primary-length proximally-coated/cemented stem (minimal bone loss)

options.label: B
options.text: Extensively porous-coated cylindrical (or modular fluted tapered) stem gaining diaphyseal scratch-fit

options.label: C
options.text: Modular fluted tapered titanium stem relying on a short distal diaphyseal segment

options.label: D
options.text: Impaction bone grafting with a cemented stem

options.label: E
options.text: Allograft–prosthesis composite or proximal femoral (mega-) replacement

options.label: F
options.text: Cemented all-polyethylene cup

emiLeads.stem: Paprosky type I: minimal metaphyseal cancellous bone loss with an intact diaphysis, as after removal of a well-fixed primary stem with little damage.
Paprosky type II / IIIA: the metaphysis is non-supportive but there remains more than ~4 cm of intact diaphysis distal to the deficiency for a scratch-fit.
Paprosky type IIIB: the metaphysis is non-supportive and only a short (less than ~4 cm) isthmic diaphyseal segment of supportive bone remains.
Paprosky type IV: both the metaphysis AND the diaphysis are non-supportive with a widened, ectatic canal, so no segment will reliably hold a press-fit or scratch-fit stem.

explanation: Paprosky femoral grading is driven by how much supportive diaphyseal bone remains for fixation. Type I (minimal loss, intact diaphysis) accepts a primary-type stem. Type II and IIIA have a non-supportive metaphysis but ≥4 cm of intact diaphysis, so an extensively porous-coated cylindrical stem or a modular fluted tapered stem can gain a diaphyseal scratch-fit (B). Type IIIB has only a short (<4 cm) diaphyseal segment, favouring a modular fluted tapered stem that achieves taper/rotational fixation on a short isthmus (C). Type IV has neither supportive metaphysis nor diaphysis (ectatic canal) and cannot hold a press-fit stem — reconstruction options are an allograft–prosthesis composite, impaction grafting in selected cases, or a proximal femoral (megaprosthesis) replacement (E). Matching the defect to the fixation strategy is the core revision principle.
learningPoint: Paprosky femoral grading hinges on remaining supportive diaphysis: I primary stem; II/IIIA diaphyseal-fixing stem (≥4 cm); IIIB modular tapered on short isthmus; IV allograft-composite/megaprosthesis.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Adult Reconstruction (revision THA, Paprosky femoral)

stem: A 9-month-old infant is referred with a unilateral radially deviated wrist. The forearm is short, the thumb is absent and the wrist sits at nearly 90 degrees of radial deviation that can be passively corrected only partway. The paediatric team is arranging further investigations because of the recognised associations of this condition. Which single investigation is the highest priority to organise before any hand surgery is planned?

options.label: A
options.text: MRI of the cervical spine

options.label: B
options.text: Renal ultrasound and echocardiogram with a full blood count

options.label: C
options.text: Nerve conduction studies of the affected limb

options.label: D
options.text: CT angiogram of the forearm vessels

options.label: E
options.text: Bone age radiograph of the contralateral wrist

explanation: Radial longitudinal deficiency (radial club hand) is strongly associated with systemic syndromes that must be excluded before elective hand surgery: VACTERL (vertebral, anal, cardiac, tracheo-oesophageal, renal, limb), Holt-Oram (cardiac septal defects), TAR (thrombocytopenia-absent radius — a low platelet count is life-threatening and contraindicates surgery) and Fanconi anaemia (which presents with later marrow failure and is screened with chromosomal breakage testing). A baseline FBC, renal tract imaging and cardiac assessment are therefore the priority. Bone age and angiography do not change the immediate work-up, and the neurological deficit is structural rather than something nerve conduction studies clarify.
learningPoint: Radial club hand is a marker for VACTERL/Holt-Oram/TAR/Fanconi — screen the heart, kidneys and platelets (and consider Fanconi testing) before operating.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Hand/Upper Limb; BSSH congenital hand guidance

stem: A 2-year-old child has a hypoplastic thumb. Examination and assessment under anaesthetic show that the carpometacarpal (trapeziometacarpal) joint is unstable with an absent or non-functional basal joint, and the thenar muscles are deficient. This corresponds to a Blauth grade IIIB thumb. Which is the most appropriate definitive surgical strategy?

options.label: A
options.text: Opponensplasty alone with first web release and ulnar collateral ligament reconstruction

options.label: B
options.text: Distraction lengthening of the thumb metacarpal

options.label: C
options.text: Pollicisation of the index finger

options.label: D
options.text: Free toe-to-hand transfer of the great toe

options.label: E
options.text: Observation until skeletal maturity, then trapeziometacarpal arthrodesis

explanation: The Blauth (modified) classification grades thumb hypoplasia by the integrity of the basal joint. The watershed is the carpometacarpal joint: a stable basal joint (grade II and IIIA) is reconstructable (web release, opponensplasty, UCL reconstruction), whereas an unstable/absent basal joint (grade IIIB and above) is not salvageable and is best treated by ablation of the remnant and pollicisation of the index finger. Distraction lengthening and toe transfer have no role for an unsalvageable basal joint, and waiting for arthrodesis ignores the absent functional CMC joint and thenar deficiency.
learningPoint: In thumb hypoplasia the carpometacarpal joint is the decision point: stable (IIIA) = reconstruct; unstable (IIIB+) = pollicisation.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Congenital Hand; Blauth/Manske classification

stem: Theme: congenital differences of the hand and their eponymous classifications. For each clinical description, select the SINGLE most appropriate classification system or term from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Wassel classification

options.label: B
options.text: Al-Qattan (constriction ring) classification

options.label: C
options.text: Oberg-Manske-Tonkin (OMT) classification

options.label: D
options.text: Blauth classification

options.label: E
options.text: Verdan/Tubiana staging

options.label: F
options.text: Seddon classification

options.label: G
options.text: Kay classification of symbrachydactyly

options.label: H
options.text: Tada classification of central deficiency

emiLeads.stem: A child has a duplicated thumb with a bifid proximal phalanx but a single, shared metacarpal. Which system grades the level of duplication?

An overarching modern framework, endorsed by the IFSSH, that organises congenital upper-limb anomalies into malformations, deformations and dysplasias based on developmental biology rather than appearance.
A newborn has a deep circumferential groove around the leg with distal lymphoedema and an associated fenestrated syndactyly; the grading runs from a simple ring to intrauterine amputation.
A hypoplastic thumb is graded from a slightly small but normal thumb (I) through to complete absence (V).

explanation: Wassel (now often Wassel-Flatt) grades thumb (radial) polydactyly by the most proximal level of skeletal duplication (I–VII); a bifid proximal phalanx sharing one metacarpal is Wassel type II/III depending on the joint level. The OMT classification is the current IFSSH-endorsed dysmorphology-based framework (malformation/deformation/dysplasia), replacing the older Swanson scheme. The Al-Qattan classification grades amniotic constriction ring (Streeter) bands from a simple ring to intrauterine amputation. Blauth grades thumb hypoplasia I–V. Kay and Tada grade symbrachydactyly and central deficiency respectively but do not fit these stems.
learningPoint: Know the eponyms: Wassel = thumb polydactyly, Blauth = thumb hypoplasia, Al-Qattan = constriction rings, OMT = the overall modern classification.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Congenital Hand; IFSSH/OMT classification

stem: A 3-month-old infant with an obstetric brachial plexus palsy is reviewed. There has been some recovery of shoulder and elbow function but the team is deciding whether microsurgical nerve reconstruction is indicated. According to the widely used guidance, which single clinical finding is the strongest indication to proceed with early plexus exploration and nerve grafting rather than continued observation?

options.label: A
options.text: Persistent Horner syndrome with absent biceps recovery at 3 months

options.label: B
options.text: An isolated waiter's-tip posture that is already improving

options.label: C
options.text: A weak hand grasp that is present from birth and unchanged

options.label: D
options.text: Absent shoulder external rotation alone at 3 months with intact elbow flexion

options.label: E
options.text: A positive Moro reflex on the affected side

explanation: In obstetric brachial plexus palsy, the presence of a Horner syndrome (ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis) signifies a very proximal/pre-ganglionic (often lower-trunk root avulsion) injury and carries a poor prognosis for spontaneous recovery; combined with failure of biceps recovery it is a strong indication for early microsurgical reconstruction. Failure of biceps (elbow flexion) recovery by around 3 months is the classic single criterion used (e.g. the cookie/Toronto test concepts), and it is markedly reinforced by a Horner sign or a flail pan-plexal limb. An improving waiter's-tip posture and isolated loss of external rotation favour observation/later secondary surgery; a positive Moro is a sign of function, not a surgical indication.
learningPoint: Horner syndrome plus absent biceps recovery at ~3 months marks a pre-ganglionic injury and is a strong indication for early nerve reconstruction in birth palsy.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Brachial Plexus/Pediatric; Narakas classification

subtopic: Brachial plexus sequelae — internal rotation contracture
stem: A 4-year-old child with a residual upper-trunk (Erb) obstetric palsy has developed a fixed internal rotation and adduction contracture of the shoulder, with secondary glenoid retroversion and posterior subluxation of the humeral head on imaging. There is reasonable hand and elbow function. Which single procedure most directly addresses the muscle imbalance driving this deformity and restores active external rotation?

options.label: A
options.text: Latissimus dorsi and teres major transfer to the rotator cuff (L'Episcopo / Hoffer transfer)

options.label: B
options.text: Glenohumeral arthrodesis

options.label: C
options.text: Proximal humeral derotation osteotomy alone

options.label: D
options.text: Open reduction with subscapularis lengthening only

options.label: E
options.text: Reverse total shoulder replacement

explanation: The deformity results from unopposed internal rotators (subscapularis, pectoralis major, latissimus, teres major) overpowering the paralysed external rotators (infraspinatus/teres minor). Transferring the latissimus dorsi and teres major posterosuperiorly onto the rotator cuff (the modified L'Episcopo / Hoffer transfer), usually combined with anterior contracture release/subscapularis slide and joint reduction, both weakens the deforming internal-rotation force and adds an active external-rotation force. A humeral derotation osteotomy repositions but adds no active external rotation and does not address glenohumeral dysplasia; arthrodesis and reverse replacement are inappropriate in a young child with usable function; subscapularis lengthening alone leaves the head without an external rotator.
learningPoint: Birth-palsy internal rotation contracture is best treated by latissimus/teres major transfer (Hoffer) to add active external rotation, not a derotation osteotomy alone.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Pediatric Upper Limb; Hoffer/L'Episcopo transfer

stem: A 30-year-old labourer catches his wedding ring on a fence and degloves the soft tissues of his ring finger. The skin is circumferentially avulsed but the bone and joints are intact, and the digit is completely ischaemic with no flow through either digital artery. Using the Urbaniak classification of ring avulsion injuries, into which class does this fall, and what does it imply for management?

options.label: A
options.text: Class I — adequate circulation; standard bone and soft-tissue treatment

options.label: B
options.text: Class II — inadequate circulation but skeleton intact; revascularisation usually succeeds and is worthwhile

options.label: C
options.text: Class III — complete degloving or complete amputation; replantation/revascularisation results are poor and amputation is often advised

options.label: D
options.text: Class III — complete degloving; emergency arthrodesis without vascular repair

options.label: E
options.text: Class II — inadequate circulation; mandatory primary amputation

explanation: The Urbaniak classification of ring avulsion injuries (as modified by Kay) grades by vascularity and skeletal/tendon integrity: Class I has adequate circulation; Class II has inadequate circulation but an intact skeleton (revascularisation worthwhile); Class III is complete degloving of the soft tissues (or complete amputation), where revascularisation/replantation outcomes are poor and ray amputation is frequently the more functional choice. A circumferential soft-tissue degloving with no arterial flow is Class III; outcomes after attempted salvage are guarded, so counselling about amputation is appropriate. Emergency arthrodesis without addressing the vascular injury is not the correct framing, and Class II implies a non-degloving pattern.
learningPoint: Urbaniak ring avulsion: Class III = complete degloving/amputation, with poor salvage outcomes — amputation is often the better functional option.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Replantation; Urbaniak/Kay ring avulsion classification

stem: Eighteen hours after a successful single-digit replantation, the finger becomes swollen and dusky with a brisk dark capillary refill of under 2 seconds and bleeding of dark blood on pinprick. The arterial Doppler signal is still present. Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Return to theatre for arterial anastomosis revision

options.label: B
options.text: Remove a few sutures, apply medicinal leeches and consider heparin-soaked gauze to relieve venous congestion

options.label: C
options.text: Elevate the hand higher and tighten the dressing to reduce swelling

options.label: D
options.text: Start a vasopressor infusion to improve perfusion pressure

options.label: E
options.text: Reassure and continue observation as the arterial signal is intact

explanation: A congested, swollen, dusky digit with rapid dark capillary refill and dark venous bleeding on pinprick, but an intact arterial signal, indicates venous outflow obstruction (the more common cause of late replant failure than arterial occlusion). First-line salvage is to relieve congestion: release some sutures, apply medicinal leeches (Hirudo medicinalis — hirudin produces local bleeding/anticoagulation; give antibiotic prophylaxis against Aeromonas) and use heparin-soaked gauze, while planning possible venous revision. A pale, cool digit with sluggish refill and loss of arterial signal would instead suggest arterial thrombosis needing re-exploration. Tightening the dressing worsens congestion, and systemic vasopressors cause peripheral vasoconstriction.
learningPoint: Congested, dusky digit with fast dark refill = venous failure: release sutures and apply leeches (with Aeromonas prophylaxis); a pale, pulseless digit = arterial failure needing re-exploration.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Replantation; BSSH replantation guidance

stem: A 28-year-old loses the volar pulp of the thumb tip with exposed distal phalanx bone over an area of about 1.5 cm, but the nail bed and most of the dorsal skin are intact. Sensate, durable, like-with-like cover that preserves length is desired. Which is the most appropriate reconstructive option?

options.label: A
options.text: Cross-finger flap from the index finger

options.label: B
