options.text: Moberg volar advancement flap

options.label: C
options.text: Full-thickness skin graft from the groin

options.label: D
options.text: First dorsal metacarpal artery (kite) flap

options.label: E
options.text: Healing by secondary intention

explanation: The Moberg volar advancement flap is particularly suited to the thumb: the thumb's dorsal skin has an independent blood supply, so a volar flap based on both neurovascular bundles can be advanced to resurface a pulp defect of up to ~1.5 cm with sensate, glabrous, like-for-like tissue while preserving length and protective sensation. A cross-finger flap is insensate initially and sacrifices a donor finger; a full-thickness graft will not survive on bare cortical bone without periosteum and gives poor pulp padding; the kite flap is better for larger dorsal thumb defects and is initially supplied by a non-thumb sensory territory; secondary intention is unsuitable with exposed bone of this size.
learningPoint: For a thumb pulp defect with exposed bone, the Moberg flap gives sensate, glabrous cover and preserves length because the thumb's dorsal skin has a separate blood supply.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Hand Soft-Tissue Coverage

subtopic: Vascular — Allen test and ulnar artery
stem: A 50-year-old carpenter has digital ischaemia and cold intolerance of the ulnar three digits. A modified Allen test shows that, on release of the ulnar artery, the hand remains pale for over 10 seconds while release of the radial artery promptly refills the hand. Which is the most likely diagnosis?

options.label: A
options.text: Radial artery thrombosis

options.label: B
options.text: Hypothenar hammer syndrome with ulnar artery occlusion/aneurysm

options.label: C
options.text: Subclavian steal syndrome

options.label: D
options.text: Buerger disease of the radial artery

options.label: E
options.text: Primary Raynaud disease

explanation: In the modified Allen test, both arteries are occluded with the hand exsanguinated, then each is released in turn; persistent pallor after releasing one vessel indicates that that vessel is not supplying the hand. Here, prolonged pallor on releasing the ulnar artery localises the obstruction to the ulnar side. In a manual worker using the hypothenar eminence as a hammer, this is classic hypothenar hammer syndrome: repetitive trauma to the ulnar artery as it crosses the hook of hamate causes thrombosis or aneurysm, embolising to the ulnar digits. Radial thrombosis would give pallor on radial release; Raynaud is episodic and symmetrical without a fixed Allen abnormality; subclavian steal and Buerger do not fit the localised ulnar Allen finding.
learningPoint: Prolonged pallor on releasing the ulnar artery during the Allen test localises ulnar inflow loss — in a manual worker think hypothenar hammer syndrome.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Vascular Disorders of the Hand

stem: A patient with severe connective-tissue-disease-associated Raynaud phenomenon has a critically ischaemic, intensely painful fingertip with an early ulcer, despite warming, smoking cessation and an optimised oral calcium-channel blocker. There is no large-vessel occlusion to bypass. Which is the most appropriate next escalation?

options.label: A
options.text: Immediate ray amputation of the digit

options.label: B
options.text: An intravenous prostacyclin analogue (e.g. iloprost) infusion, with consideration of digital (peri-arterial) sympathectomy if it fails

options.label: C
options.text: Long-term systemic warfarin anticoagulation

options.label: D
options.text: Cervicothoracic (stellate ganglion) sympathectomy as the definitive first surgical step

options.label: E
options.text: High-dose oral corticosteroids

explanation: For critical digital ischaemia in secondary Raynaud refractory to oral vasodilators, the established next step is an intravenous prostacyclin analogue (iloprost), which is a potent vasodilator and inhibits platelet aggregation and can heal ulcers and relieve rest pain. If pharmacological measures fail and there is reversible distal vasospasm, peri-arterial (digital) sympathectomy — stripping the adventitia of the digital and common digital arteries — is the surgical option of choice, being more durable and selective than proximal cervicothoracic sympathectomy (which tends to relapse). Amputation is a last resort once tissue is non-viable; warfarin and steroids do not treat the vasospastic ischaemia itself.
learningPoint: Refractory critical digital ischaemia in connective-tissue disease: escalate to IV iloprost, then digital (peri-arterial) sympathectomy — proximal sympathectomy relapses.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Vascular Hand; BSR/BHPR Raynaud guidance

stem: Theme: degenerative wrist collapse (SLAC and SNAC patterns) and stage-specific surgery. Each lettered option is a possible radiographic stage or operative choice. For each scenario select the SINGLE best answer. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Stage I — arthritis confined to the radial styloid / radioscaphoid

options.label: B
options.text: Stage II — radioscaphoid arthritis extending across the whole scaphoid facet

options.label: C
options.text: Stage III — midcarpal (capitolunate) arthritis with proximal capitate migration

options.label: D
options.text: Radial styloidectomy alone

options.label: E
options.text: Proximal row carpectomy

options.label: F
options.text: Scaphoid excision and four-corner fusion

options.label: G
options.text: Total wrist arthrodesis

emiLeads.stem: In both SLAC and SNAC collapse, which joint is characteristically spared until the latest stages, a fact that underpins the choice of motion-preserving fusions?
A 60-year-old has wrist pain; radiographs show arthritis between the radius and the entire scaphoid facet but a preserved capitolunate joint and a normal lunate fossa. What is the stage?

A patient has Stage III SLAC with degenerate articular cartilage on the proximal pole of the capitate (the lunate fossa of the radius is preserved). Which motion-preserving operation is contraindicated, making the alternative motion-preserving option preferable?
A 65-year-old manual worker with pan-carpal Stage III arthritis prioritises a pain-free, durable, strong wrist over retained movement. What is the most reliable operation?

explanation: SLAC (scapholunate advanced collapse) and SNAC (scaphoid nonunion advanced collapse) progress in a predictable pattern: I = radial styloid/radioscaphoid, II = whole radioscaphoid facet, III = capitolunate (midcarpal) involvement, while the radiolunate (lunate fossa) joint is characteristically spared until very late. Operative choice is stage- and cartilage-dependent: styloidectomy for very early disease; for stage II–III the two motion-preserving options are proximal row carpectomy (PRC) and scaphoid excision with four-corner fusion. PRC relies on a healthy capitate head articulating with the lunate fossa, so if the proximal capitate cartilage is degenerate (as can occur in stage III), PRC is contraindicated and four-corner fusion is preferred. For a heavy labourer wanting maximal durability and pain relief, total wrist arthrodesis is the most reliable.
learningPoint: SLAC/SNAC spares the radiolunate joint; PRC needs a healthy capitate head — if the proximal capitate is worn, choose four-corner fusion.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Wrist Arthritis (Watson SLAC staging)

subtopic: Wrist arthritis — proximal row carpectomy biomechanics
stem: A surgeon plans a proximal row carpectomy for SNAC wrist. When counselling the patient about how the wrist will work afterwards, which statement best describes the resulting articulation and its key prerequisite?

options.label: A
options.text: The capitate head articulates within the lunate fossa of the radius, so the cartilage of both surfaces must be preserved

options.label: B
options.text: The scaphoid is retained to articulate with the radius, so its cartilage must be intact

options.label: C
options.text: The hamate articulates with the radial styloid, so a styloidectomy must be avoided

options.label: D
options.text: A radiolunate fusion is created, so movement occurs only through the midcarpal joint

options.label: E
options.text: The triquetrum is retained as a spacer, so the lunotriquetral ligament must be repaired

explanation: Proximal row carpectomy removes the scaphoid, lunate and triquetrum, allowing the head of the capitate to drop into and articulate with the lunate fossa of the distal radius, creating a simplified single radiocapitate articulation that preserves useful motion and grip. The essential prerequisite is that the cartilage of both the capitate head and the lunate fossa is intact; if either is degenerate (e.g. proximal capitate wear in late SLAC/SNAC), PRC will fail and a four-corner fusion (or arthrodesis) is preferred. The other options misdescribe the resulting joint.
learningPoint: After PRC the capitate head articulates with the lunate fossa — the operation depends on healthy cartilage on both of those surfaces.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Wrist Arthritis/PRC

subtopic: Kienbock disease — ulnar variance and treatment
stem: A 26-year-old manual worker has Lichtman stage II Kienbock disease (sclerosis without lunate collapse) confirmed on MRI. Plain radiographs demonstrate clear ulnar-negative variance. Which surgical option is most appropriate to offload the lunate and is best supported for this configuration?

options.label: A
options.text: Radial shortening osteotomy (joint-levelling procedure)

options.label: B
options.text: Ulnar shortening osteotomy

options.label: C
options.text: Proximal row carpectomy

options.label: D
options.text: Total wrist arthrodesis

options.label: E
options.text: Scaphotrapeziotrapezoid (STT) fusion

options.label: F
options.text: Wrist denervation alone

explanation: Ulnar-negative variance increases radiolunate load and is associated with Kienbock disease. In early disease (Lichtman I–IIIA) with an ulnar-negative wrist and no fixed scaphoid rotation, a joint-levelling procedure that unloads the lunate is favoured: with ulnar-negative variance the correct levelling operation is a radial shortening osteotomy (you shorten the radius to bring it level with the ulna). An ulnar shortening osteotomy would be used to offload the ulnar side (e.g. ulnar impaction) and would worsen lunate loading here. PRC, arthrodesis and STT fusion are reserved for collapse/advanced disease; denervation addresses pain but not biomechanics. (For an ulnar-neutral/positive wrist, a non-levelling option such as capitate shortening or revascularisation is chosen instead.)
learningPoint: Early Kienbock with ulnar-negative variance: unload the lunate by shortening the long bone — a radial shortening osteotomy.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Kienbock Disease (Lichtman staging)

stem: When using the Lichtman classification of Kienbock disease, a wrist shows lunate collapse. Which single radiographic feature distinguishes stage IIIB from stage IIIA and signals a worse prognosis with altered surgical planning?

options.label: A
options.text: Fixed rotatory subluxation of the scaphoid (ring sign) with loss of carpal height

options.label: B
options.text: Increased density of the lunate without any collapse

options.label: C
options.text: Generalised radiocarpal and midcarpal degenerative arthritis

options.label: D
options.text: A linear fracture line through the lunate without sclerosis

options.label: E
options.text: Ulnar translocation of the entire carpus

explanation: In the Lichtman classification, stage III denotes lunate collapse; it is subdivided by the integrity of carpal alignment. Stage IIIA has lunate collapse with a normally aligned, stable carpus, whereas stage IIIB has additional carpal instability — fixed flexion/rotatory subluxation of the scaphoid (a positive ring sign on the PA film) and loss of carpal height as the proximal pole migrates. This breakdown of carpal alignment makes simple joint-levelling/revascularisation less reliable and pushes management towards reconstructive options (e.g. STT or scaphocapitate fusion, PRC). Stage IV is generalised arthritis; sclerosis without collapse is stage II.
learningPoint: Lichtman IIIA vs IIIB hinges on carpal alignment: IIIB = fixed scaphoid rotation (ring sign) and loss of carpal height, requiring reconstructive rather than levelling surgery.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Kienbock Disease (Lichtman staging)

stem: A 40-year-old has chronic ulnar-sided wrist pain worse on power grip and pronation. Radiographs show ulnar-positive variance with subchondral cysts in the ulnar head and lunate, and MRI confirms a central degenerative TFCC perforation (Palmer class 2C) with chondromalacia. Arthroscopy will be performed. Which is the most appropriate definitive treatment?

options.label: A
options.text: Arthroscopic debridement of the TFCC tear and an ulnar shortening osteotomy

options.label: B
options.text: Open foveal (peripheral) TFCC repair to the ulnar styloid

options.label: C
options.text: Darrach excision of the distal ulna

options.label: D
options.text: Radial shortening osteotomy

options.label: E
options.text: Sauve-Kapandji procedure

explanation: Palmer class 2 lesions are degenerative ulnar impaction (ulnocarpal abutment): a positive ulnar variance overloads the ulnar carpus, producing central TFCC wear, chondromalacia and cysts. The mechanical driver is the long ulna, so treatment must decompress the ulnocarpal joint: arthroscopic debridement of the (irreparable central) TFCC tear combined with ulnar shortening osteotomy (or an arthroscopic wafer resection) is the standard. A peripheral/foveal repair is for traumatic Palmer class 1B tears causing DRUJ instability, not central degenerative tears. Darrach and Sauve-Kapandji are salvage procedures for DRUJ arthritis/instability, usually in lower-demand patients; radial shortening would worsen ulnar-positive impaction.
learningPoint: Ulnar impaction (Palmer 2, ulnar-positive): decompress with ulnar shortening osteotomy (or wafer) plus debridement — central TFCC tears are not repaired.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, TFCC/DRUJ (Palmer classification)

stem: A 35-year-old labourer has post-traumatic distal radioulnar joint arthritis with painful, restricted forearm rotation but no ulnocarpal arthritis. A salvage procedure is planned. Compared with a Darrach distal ulna excision, which statement best explains why a Sauve-Kapandji procedure is often preferred in a young, high-demand patient?

options.label: A
options.text: It preserves the ulnar buttress and ulnocarpal support by fusing the DRUJ and creating a proximal pseudarthrosis, reducing ulnar translation/convergence and stylocarpal impingement

options.label: B
options.text: It completely removes the ulnar head and so eliminates any risk of ulnar stump instability

options.label: C
options.text: It is a soft-tissue-only procedure that does not require bony union

options.label: D
options.text: It reliably restores native DRUJ kinematics and full pronosupination strength

options.label: E
options.text: It avoids any risk of distal ulnar stump painful instability altogether

explanation: The Sauve-Kapandji procedure arthrodeses the distal radioulnar joint and creates a controlled pseudarthrosis in the distal ulna just proximal to the fusion, restoring forearm rotation through the new pseudarthrosis while keeping the ulnar head in place to support the ulnar carpus and TFCC. This preserves the ulnar buttress and is attractive in young, heavy-demand patients because it reduces the radioulnar convergence and ulnar translation that complicate a Darrach (which simply excises the distal ulna and can leave a painful unstable ulnar stump). It does not recreate native DRUJ kinematics, and the proximal stump can still become symptomatic, so option A is the only accurate comparison.
learningPoint: Sauve-Kapandji fuses the DRUJ and makes a proximal ulnar pseudarthrosis, keeping ulnocarpal support — favoured over Darrach in young, high-demand wrists.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, DRUJ Salvage

stem: A 38-year-old woman has a long history of exquisite, lancinating pain localised to one fingertip, dramatic cold intolerance and pinpoint tenderness under the nail. There is a faint bluish discolouration beneath the nail plate but no mass is visible. Which investigation is most useful to confirm the suspected diagnosis and plan excision?

options.label: A
options.text: Plain radiograph only

options.label: B
options.text: MRI of the digit (high-resolution, with contrast)

options.label: C
options.text: Nerve conduction studies

options.label: D
options.text: Bone scintigraphy

options.label: E
options.text: Arteriography of the digit

explanation: The triad of severe paroxysmal pain, marked cold sensitivity and focal tenderness (Love's pin test positive, relieved by a proximal tourniquet — Hildreth sign) in the subungual region is classic for a glomus tumour, a benign hamartoma of the glomus body (a thermoregulatory arteriovenous structure). Lesions are often tiny and not seen on plain films (which may show a faint scalloping of the dorsal phalanx only when large). High-resolution contrast-enhanced MRI is the best modality to confirm, localise and size the lesion to guide complete excision (incomplete excision causes recurrence). Nerve conduction studies, bone scan and arteriography do not characterise it.
learningPoint: Severe pinpoint subungual pain with cold intolerance = glomus tumour; confirm and localise with contrast MRI before complete excision.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Hand Tumours

stem: A 30-year-old man has a slowly enlarging, firm nodule on the volar aspect of the finger that has been present for over a year. It was previously excised elsewhere as a presumed benign lesion and has recurred, now with a small ulcerated area. It is repeatedly mistaken for a benign process or chronic infection. Which malignancy is the classic 'great mimic' that must be excluded here, and what is the appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Epithelioid sarcoma — perform an open incisional biopsy with staging before any further excision

options.label: B
options.text: Giant cell tumour of the tendon sheath — proceed straight to marginal re-excision

options.label: C
options.text: Pyogenic granuloma — cauterise and treat as infection

options.label: D
options.text: Squamous cell carcinoma — apply topical chemotherapy

options.label: E
options.text: Dupuytren nodule — observe

explanation: Epithelioid sarcoma is the most common soft-tissue sarcoma of the hand and forearm in young adults and is notorious for masquerading as a benign nodule, a wart, an ulcer or chronic infection; it characteristically recurs after inadequate excision and spreads proximally along tendon sheaths and to regional lymph nodes. A firm, persistent or recurrent hand nodule — especially one with ulceration or prior incomplete excision — must be regarded as a possible sarcoma and worked up properly: MRI, then a planned biopsy and staging through a sarcoma multidisciplinary team, before any definitive (often wide) resection. Treating it as a benign GCTTS, pyogenic granuloma or Dupuytren nodule risks under-treating a malignancy.
learningPoint: A recurrent or ulcerating 'benign' hand nodule in a young adult may be epithelioid sarcoma — image, biopsy and stage via the sarcoma MDT before re-excising.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Hand/Soft-Tissue Tumours; BOA/BSSH sarcoma referral

stem: A 45-year-old diabetic presents with a tense, fluctuant swelling of the central palm. There is loss of the normal palmar concavity, the middle and ring fingers are held abducted and slightly flexed, and passive adduction of these fingers is painful. There is no fusiform swelling of any single digit. Which is the most likely diagnosis?

options.label: A
options.text: Thenar space abscess

options.label: B
options.text: Midpalmar (deep) space abscess

options.label: C
options.text: Collar-stud abscess of the first web

options.label: D
options.text: Pyogenic flexor tenosynovitis of the middle finger

options.label: E
options.text: Parona space (distal forearm) abscess

explanation: The deep palmar spaces are the thenar and midpalmar spaces, separated by a fascial septum to the third metacarpal. A midpalmar space abscess fills the central palm: the normal palmar concavity is lost or even convex, and the overlying middle and ring fingers are held in slight flexion and abduction with pain on passive movement/adduction. A thenar space abscess instead presents with swelling of the thenar eminence and first web with the thumb held abducted. Flexor tenosynovitis would show the four Kanavel signs in one digit (fusiform swelling, semiflexed posture, pain on passive extension, tenderness along the sheath). Parona space lies in the distal forearm deep to FDP and communicates with the radial/ulnar bursae (horseshoe abscess).
learningPoint: A central palm abscess with loss of the palmar concavity and abducted middle/ring fingers is a midpalmar space collection; thenar abscess pushes the thumb into abduction.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Hand Infections (deep spaces, Kanavel)

stem: A 24-year-old dental nurse presents with a painful, swollen fingertip. There is throbbing pain and erythema but, instead of a single tense pointing abscess, the pulp shows a cluster of small clear vesicles, and a similar crop developed and resolved a few months ago. Which is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Urgent incision and drainage of the pulp space

options.label: B
options.text: Conservative management with analgesia and oral aciclovir; avoid surgical drainage

options.label: C
options.text: Wide debridement and intravenous broad-spectrum antibiotics

options.label: D
options.text: Nail plate removal to decompress a subungual abscess

options.label: E
options.text: Amputation of the distal phalanx

explanation: The picture is a herpetic whitlow: a herpes simplex infection of the fingertip seen in those exposed to oral secretions (dental and healthcare staff, children), characterised by grouped clear/honey vesicles rather than a single tense felon, with a recurrent course. It is a self-limiting viral infection; the key teaching point is that incision and drainage is contraindicated because it does not help and can cause secondary bacterial superinfection, delayed healing and viral spread. Management is supportive (analgesia, covering the lesion), with oral aciclovir useful especially if started early or for recurrent disease. Surgical options are inappropriate for this viral process.
learningPoint: Grouped vesicles on the fingertip of a dental/healthcare worker = herpetic whitlow — treat with aciclovir and do NOT incise.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Hand Infections

stem: A 59-year-old woman underwent a Weil osteotomy of the second metatarsal for an intractable plantar keratosis and dislocated second metatarsophalangeal joint. Four months later she reports that the toe no longer touches the ground and 'sticks up' when she walks, although her original pain has resolved. Examination shows the toe held in slight extension with a gap beneath the pulp on weight-bearing. Which mechanism best explains this complication?

options.label: A
options.text: Excessive plantar translation of the metatarsal head shifting the axis of the intrinsics dorsally, producing a floating toe

options.label: B
options.text: Avascular necrosis of the metatarsal head

options.label: C
options.text: Non-union of the osteotomy

options.label: D
options.text: Recurrent dislocation of the metatarsophalangeal joint

options.label: E
options.text: Iatrogenic rupture of the long flexor tendon

explanation: Floating toe is the most frequent complication of the Weil osteotomy, reported in up to a third of cases. Because the cut is made parallel to the floor, shortening the metatarsal also translates the head plantarward; this drops the centre of rotation of the metatarsophalangeal joint below the line of pull of the interossei, so the intrinsics come to act as dorsiflexors (extensors) of the proximal phalanx rather than plantar-flexors. The toe is then held in extension and fails to purchase the ground. Strategies to reduce this include a second 'derotation' bone slice to avoid plantar displacement, limiting shortening, and not over-tensioning the dorsal capsule. AVN, non-union and flexor rupture are uncommon and would not characteristically give a painless dorsally-held toe; recurrent dislocation would reproduce pain rather than relieve it.
learningPoint: Floating toe after a Weil osteotomy results from plantar head translation altering the intrinsic line of pull — minimise shortening and consider a derotation slice.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, 9th ed — Foot & Ankle: lesser-toe deformities and metatarsal osteotomies

stem: A 54-year-old woman is reviewed nine months after distal soft-tissue release and chevron osteotomy for hallux valgus. The great toe now deviates medially away from the second toe, the interphalangeal joint is flexed and the metatarsophalangeal joint hyperextends. The deformity is passively fully correctable and the joint is not arthritic. She finds shoe-fitting difficult. What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Reattachment/reconstruction of the lateral structures, e.g. an extensor hallucis longus or split EHL tendon transfer with Akin osteotomy as needed

options.label: B
options.text: First metatarsophalangeal joint arthrodesis

options.label: C
options.text: Reassurance and rigid-soled shoes only

options.label: D
options.text: Keller excision arthroplasty

options.label: E
options.text: Repeat distal metatarsal osteotomy with lateral translation

explanation: This is acquired (iatrogenic) hallux varus, classically following overzealous lateral release, excessive medial eminence resection or over-tightening of the medial capsule, sometimes compounded by removal of the fibular sesamoid. While the deformity remains flexible and the joint preserved, it can be corrected with a soft-tissue/tendon-balancing procedure — a (split) extensor hallucis longus transfer to recreate a lateral tether, combined with an Akin or release of the contracted medial structures, and addressing any IP flexion. Arthrodesis is the salvage option once the deformity is fixed or the joint is arthritic, not the first choice in a flexible, non-arthritic toe. Keller and a further metatarsal osteotomy do not address the dynamic medial pull, and conservative measures rarely satisfy a patient with shoe-wear difficulty.
learningPoint: Flexible non-arthritic iatrogenic hallux varus is balanced with a tendon transfer (e.g. split EHL); arthrodesis is reserved for fixed or arthritic deformity.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, 9th ed — Foot & Ankle: complications of hallux valgus surgery

stem: A 47-year-old woman had a modified Lapidus (first tarsometatarsal arthrodesis) for severe hallux valgus with hypermobility. At five months she has persistent pain localised to the fusion site on push-off and dorsal midfoot tenderness; weight-bearing radiographs show a persistent lucency across the joint with no bridging trabeculae and slight dorsal angulation of the first ray. Which factor is the most important technical contributor to this outcome that should be addressed at revision?

options.label: A
options.text: Inadequate preparation of the subchondral surfaces and failure to achieve rigid, compressed fixation across the first TMT joint

options.label: B
options.text: Failure to fuse the second tarsometatarsal joint at the same sitting

options.label: C
options.text: Use of an Akin osteotomy in the index procedure

options.label: D
options.text: Excessive plantarflexion of the first ray

options.label: E
options.text: Removal of the medial eminence

explanation: The first TMT is a relatively small, sclerotic, biomechanically demanding joint exposed to large dorsiflexion moments on push-off, so the Lapidus has a recognised non-union rate (historically up to ~10%, lower with modern compression constructs). The dominant modifiable technical factors are thorough cartilage removal down to bleeding cancellous bone, accurate apposition and rigid fixation that compresses the joint (crossed screws, or a plantar/medial plate with a lag screw); dorsal malreduction and poor surface contact predispose to the lucency and dorsal angulation seen here. The second TMT is not routinely included. An Akin is a phalangeal osteotomy unrelated to TMT healing, dorsal (not plantar) malposition is the typical error, and medial eminence resection does not affect fusion. Revision requires debridement to healthy bone, correction of the dorsal malalignment, bone graft and stable compression.
learningPoint: Lapidus non-union is driven chiefly by inadequate surface preparation and non-rigid fixation across a high-stress joint; revise with grafting and stable compression.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, 9th ed — Foot & Ankle: first ray procedures and arthrodesis
BOFAS hallux valgus / first ray surgery guidance

stem: A 23-year-old man with Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease has a bilateral cavovarus foot and recurrent lateral ankle instability. When he stands with the heel and lateral border of the foot on a 2.5 cm wooden block and the first ray allowed to drop over the edge, the previously varus heel swings into neutral/valgus. This finding most directly indicates that:

options.label: A
options.text: The hindfoot varus is flexible and driven by the plantarflexed first ray, so a forefoot-based (first metatarsal dorsiflexion) procedure may correct the hindfoot without a calcaneal osteotomy

options.label: B
options.text: The hindfoot varus is fixed and a calcaneal osteotomy is mandatory

options.label: C
options.text: There is an underlying tarsal coalition

options.label: D
options.text: The tibialis posterior is the primary deforming force

options.label: E
options.text: A triple arthrodesis is required

explanation: In the cavovarus foot the classic deforming pattern is a plantarflexed first ray (relatively strong peroneus longus overpowering a weak tibialis anterior), which pronates the forefoot and forces the hindfoot into compensatory varus. The Coleman block test eliminates the first ray's contribution by letting it drop off the block: if the heel then corrects to neutral/valgus, the hindfoot varus is flexible and forefoot-driven, so correction can begin with a first metatarsal dorsiflexion osteotomy (with appropriate tendon transfers) and may not need a calcaneal osteotomy. If the heel stays in varus on the block, the hindfoot is fixed and a lateralising/Dwyer calcaneal osteotomy is added. The test does not diagnose coalition, does not implicate tibialis posterior as primary, and a flexible deformity does not mandate triple fusion.
learningPoint: A correcting Coleman block test means flexible, forefoot-driven hindfoot varus — start correction at the first ray; a fixed heel needs a calcaneal osteotomy.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, 9th ed — Foot & Ankle: pes cavus and Charcot-Marie-Tooth

stem: A 26-year-old man with Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease has a flexible cavovarus foot. Examination confirms a relatively strong peroneus longus, a weak tibialis anterior with a developing drop-foot, and a weak peroneus brevis. He is undergoing reconstruction with a first metatarsal dorsiflexion osteotomy. Which tendon transfer most rationally addresses the underlying muscle imbalance?

options.label: A
options.text: Transfer of peroneus longus to peroneus brevis, and transfer of tibialis posterior (or tibialis anterior augmentation) to restore dorsiflexion

options.label: B
options.text: Transfer of tibialis anterior laterally to the cuboid (Hibbs)

options.label: C
options.text: Flexor digitorum longus transfer to the navicular

options.label: D
options.text: Peroneus brevis to peroneus longus transfer

options.label: E
options.text: Achilles tendon lengthening alone

explanation: In CMT the characteristic imbalance is a strong peroneus longus and tibialis posterior with weak tibialis anterior and peroneus brevis. Transferring peroneus longus to peroneus brevis simultaneously removes the major plantarflexing/pronating force on the first ray (helping the cavus) and augments eversion (countering varus). The associated dorsiflexion weakness is addressed by transferring tibialis posterior through the interosseous membrane to the dorsum (or augmenting tibialis anterior), restoring active dorsiflexion and reducing varus drive. A Hibbs transfer (EHL/EDL to midfoot) treats clawing and weak dorsiflexion but does not rebalance the peroneals; FDL-to-navicular is a flatfoot procedure; brevis-to-longus would worsen the deformity; and tendo-Achilles lengthening alone ignores the muscle imbalance.
learningPoint: CMT rebalancing classically transfers the over-strong peroneus longus to brevis and uses tibialis posterior transfer for drop-foot, alongside bony correction of the cavus.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, 9th ed — Foot & Ankle: cavovarus reconstruction and tendon transfers

stem: A 48-year-old man has isolated, painful post-traumatic subtalar arthritis two years after a Sanders type III calcaneal fracture treated non-operatively. He has hindfoot pain on uneven ground but a well-preserved talonavicular and calcaneocuboid joint, no significant deformity, and only mild loss of heel height. What is the most appropriate operation?

options.label: A
options.text: Isolated subtalar (talocalcaneal) arthrodesis, with a bone block if heel height is significantly lost

options.label: B
options.text: Triple arthrodesis

options.label: C
options.text: Tibiotalocalcaneal fusion

options.label: D
options.text: Total ankle replacement

options.label: E
options.text: Calcaneal osteotomy alone

explanation: When arthritis is confined to the subtalar joint with healthy transverse tarsal joints and no major deformity, an isolated subtalar arthrodesis preserves the talonavicular and calcaneocuboid motion that contributes to forefoot adaptation, and gives reliable pain relief. After a malunited calcaneal fracture with loss of height and talar declination, a distraction bone-block subtalar fusion can restore height and the talo-first metatarsal relationship. A triple fusion is reserved for multi-joint arthritis or fixed deformity and unnecessarily sacrifices healthy joints here. TTC fusion and TAR involve the ankle, which is uninvolved, and a calcaneal osteotomy alone does not treat the arthritic joint.
learningPoint: Isolated subtalar arthritis with healthy transverse tarsal joints is best treated by isolated subtalar fusion (bone-block if heel height is lost), sparing the other joints.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, 9th ed — Foot & Ankle: hindfoot arthrodesis and calcaneal malunion

stem: A 62-year-old woman is reviewed 18 months after a triple arthrodesis performed for a rigid planovalgus deformity. The fusions are solid, but she complains of lateral border foot pain, difficulty with shoe-fitting and a feeling that she is 'walking on the outside' of her foot. Standing assessment shows the heel in varus and the forefoot supinated. Which malposition has most likely occurred?

options.label: A
options.text: Hindfoot fused in varus, throwing the forefoot into compensatory supination and overloading the lateral border

options.label: B
options.text: Hindfoot fused in excessive valgus

options.label: C
options.text: Forefoot fused in excessive pronation

options.label: D
options.text: Equinus malunion of the ankle

options.label: E
options.text: Non-union of the talonavicular joint

explanation: The commonest and most poorly tolerated malposition after triple arthrodesis is hindfoot varus. A varus heel locks the transverse tarsal joints (loss of the normal 'unlocking' that accommodates the ground), forces the forefoot into compensatory supination, and shifts weight to the lateral border — producing exactly this picture of lateral overload, fifth metatarsal callosity and shoe-fitting trouble, and it predisposes to lateral ankle instability. Excessive valgus is better tolerated but risks impingement and medial overload; the heel here is clearly varus. The deformity is symptomatic malunion, not non-union (fusions are solid). Management is a corrective (often lateral closing-wedge calcaneal or midfoot) osteotomy to restore a plantigrade, slightly valgus hindfoot.
learningPoint: Hindfoot varus is the least-tolerated triple-arthrodesis malunion — it supinates the forefoot and overloads the lateral border; aim for a plantigrade, slightly valgus heel.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, 9th ed — Foot & Ankle: triple arthrodesis and its complications

stem: A 57-year-old woman had a flatfoot reconstruction for flexible stage II tibialis posterior dysfunction comprising an FDL transfer, medialising calcaneal osteotomy and a lateral column lengthening through the anterior calcaneus. Postoperatively she develops persistent pain over the dorsolateral midfoot and at the calcaneocuboid region, and finds the foot feels 'stiff and over-corrected'. Which complication is most likely?

options.label: A
options.text: Overlengthening of the lateral column causing forefoot supination, lateral column overload and calcaneocuboid joint stress

options.label: B
options.text: Under-correction of the hindfoot valgus

options.label: C
options.text: Rupture of the transferred FDL

options.label: D
options.text: Non-union of the medialising calcaneal osteotomy

options.label: E
options.text: Tarsal tunnel syndrome from the calcaneal shift

explanation: Lateral column lengthening (Evans-type osteotomy or calcaneocuboid distraction) powerfully corrects forefoot abduction but is unforgiving: too large a graft overlengthens the lateral column, producing forefoot supination, painful lateral/plantar-lateral overload, stiffness and increased pressure across the calcaneocuboid joint (a recognised cause of CC arthritis and dorsolateral pain). The described 'over-corrected, stiff, lateral pain' picture fits overlengthening rather than under-correction (which would leave residual abduction) or FDL rupture/calcaneal non-union. Tarsal tunnel symptoms are sensory and medial. Care with graft size and avoiding excessive distraction limits this complication.
learningPoint: Excessive lateral column lengthening over-corrects the flatfoot into forefoot supination with painful lateral overload and calcaneocuboid stress — graft size must be judged carefully.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, 9th ed — Foot & Ankle: adult acquired flatfoot reconstruction

subtopic: AAFD — spring ligament and deltoid in stage IV
stem: A 60-year-old woman with progressive adult acquired flatfoot now has hindfoot valgus that no longer fully corrects, plus a valgus talar tilt on a weight-bearing mortise ankle radiograph but only early tibiotalar arthritic change. Which structural failure most directly accounts for the valgus talar tilt that defines progression to a stage IV deformity?

options.label: A
options.text: Attenuation/insufficiency of the deltoid ligament (with spring ligament failure) allowing the talus to tilt into valgus within the mortise

options.label: B
options.text: Rupture of the anterior talofibular ligament

options.label: C
options.text: Peroneus brevis tear

options.label: D
options.text: Plantar fascia rupture

options.label: E
options.text: Interosseous talocalcaneal ligament tear

explanation: Stage IV (Myerson) flatfoot is defined by involvement of the ankle: as the hindfoot drifts into valgus, chronic medial overload attenuates the deltoid ligament (the superficial/deep medial complex), which together with spring ligament insufficiency lets the talus tilt into valgus within the mortise, eventually producing tibiotalar arthritis. Recognising this matters because a correctable stage IVa (no/early arthritis, flexible tilt) may be amenable to deltoid (and spring ligament) reconstruction combined with hindfoot realignment/fusion, whereas a fixed, arthritic stage IVb requires the ankle to be addressed by fusion or replacement. The lateral ligaments (ATFL), peroneal tendons, plantar fascia and interosseous ligament are not the structures responsible for medial-sided valgus talar tilt.
learningPoint: Valgus talar tilt in stage IV flatfoot reflects deltoid (and spring) ligament insufficiency; a flexible early tilt may be reconstructed rather than fused.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, 9th ed — Foot & Ankle: adult acquired flatfoot, Myerson stage IV

stem: A 49-year-old manual labourer with a heavy occupation and a BMI of 36 has end-stage post-traumatic ankle arthritis. He has a 12-degree fixed varus deformity of the ankle with incompetent medial and lateral ligaments and avascular changes in the talar body. He asks specifically for a total ankle replacement so he can keep ankle movement. Which feature is the strongest contraindication to total ankle replacement in his case?

options.label: A
options.text: Talar avascular necrosis with uncorrectable coronal malalignment and ligamentous incompetence in a young, heavy-demand patient

options.label: B
options.text: Post-traumatic (rather than primary) aetiology

options.label: C
options.text: Age under 50

options.label: D
options.text: A BMI of 36 in isolation

options.label: E
options.text: Presence of any varus deformity

explanation: The decisive contraindications to total ankle replacement are those that compromise implant support and balance: significant talar avascular necrosis (no bony bed to support the talar component), and severe, uncorrectable coronal-plane deformity with incompetent collateral ligaments — together these predict early failure and edge-loading, especially in a young, high-demand heavy worker. Post-traumatic aetiology, young age, raised BMI and a correctable varus are relative considerations that may worsen survivorship but are not absolute bars on their own; many modern series include such patients with deformity that can be balanced. For this man, arthrodesis is the more durable choice. (UK NICE/registry data show TAR has higher revision rates than hip/knee arthroplasty, so patient selection is critical.)
learningPoint: Talar AVN plus uncorrectable malalignment and ligament incompetence is a strong contraindication to ankle replacement; correctable deformity and post-traumatic aetiology are relative.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, 9th ed — Foot & Ankle: total ankle replacement indications and contraindications
NICE / National Joint Registry ankle replacement data

stem: A 55-year-old man has a solid ankle arthrodesis but complains of difficulty descending stairs and ramps, a tendency to overload the forefoot, and accelerating pain. Standing radiographs show the tibiotalar fusion in 10 degrees of equinus with neutral coronal and rotational alignment. What is the explanation for his symptoms?

options.label: A
options.text: Equinus malposition increases forefoot pressure and the knee-flexion/back-knee demand on slopes and stairs, accelerating midfoot and forefoot overload

options.label: B
options.text: Excessive dorsiflexion causing heel overload

options.label: C
options.text: Varus malalignment causing lateral overload

options.label: D
options.text: Internal rotation malalignment

options.label: E
options.text: Non-union of the fusion

explanation: The optimal position for ankle arthrodesis is neutral dorsiflexion (plantigrade), slight hindfoot valgus (~5 degrees) and external rotation matching the contralateral side, with the talus translated slightly posterior. Fusing in equinus is a classic error: it throws weight onto the forefoot, generates a back-knee (hyperextension) thrust at heel strike and makes descending stairs and slopes difficult, and it accelerates overload arthritis of the midfoot/forefoot. Excessive dorsiflexion overloads the heel; varus overloads the lateral border; internal rotation impairs gait — but the radiograph here specifically shows equinus with otherwise neutral alignment. The fusion is solid, so this is malposition, not non-union; treatment is a dorsiflexion-corrective osteotomy.
learningPoint: Ankle fusion should be plantigrade with slight valgus and external rotation; equinus malposition causes forefoot overload and a back-knee thrust on slopes and stairs.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, 9th ed — Foot & Ankle: ankle arthrodesis position and complications

subtopic: Osteochondral lesions of the talus — classifications
stem: Theme: classification of osteochondral lesions of the talus. For each description, select the single best matching staging system or grade from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Berndt and Harty stage I (subchondral compression, intact cartilage)

options.label: B
options.text: Berndt and Harty stage IV (displaced osteochondral fragment lying free in the joint)

options.label: C
options.text: Hepple MRI stage 5 (subchondral cyst formation)

options.label: D
options.text: Hepple MRI stage 1 (articular cartilage damage only)

options.label: E
options.text: Ferkel and Sgaglione CT stage IIa

options.label: F
options.text: Mintz/arthroscopic ICRS grade for an intact but soft, 'ballottable' cartilage surface

emiLeads.stem: A plain-radiograph and historical classification description of a completely detached talar dome fragment that has displaced and is lying free within the ankle joint.
The earliest grade in the original radiographic system: localised subchondral trabecular compression with an intact, congruent cartilage surface and no visible fragment.
An MRI-based grade describing a subchondral cyst beneath the lesion, indicating chronicity and a poorer prognosis on the Hepple scale.
A CT-based subclassification used to define the presence and communication of a subchondral cyst on cross-sectional imaging when MRI is not the primary modality.

explanation: Talar osteochondral lesions are described by several complementary systems. Berndt and Harty's original radiographic staging runs I (subchondral compression), II (partially detached fragment), III (completely detached, undisplaced) to IV (detached and displaced free fragment). Hepple modified this for MRI, adding cartilage-only injury (stage 1) and, importantly, subchondral cyst formation (stage 5) which carries a worse prognosis. Ferkel and Sgaglione produced a CT-based staging that characterises cysts and their communication with the joint surface. Arthroscopic/ICRS grading (Mintz) describes the cartilage surface directly. Knowing which modality underpins each system — radiograph (Berndt-Harty), MRI (Hepple), CT (Ferkel-Sgaglione), arthroscopy (ICRS/Mintz) — is the discriminating point.
learningPoint: Match the OLT classification to its modality: Berndt-Harty (X-ray), Hepple (MRI, cyst = stage 5), Ferkel-Sgaglione (CT), ICRS/Mintz (arthroscopy).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, 9th ed — Foot & Ankle: osteochondral lesions of the talus

stem: A 32-year-old recreational footballer has persistent deep medial ankle pain 14 months after an arthroscopic microfracture of a 1.8 cm² medial talar dome osteochondral lesion. MRI now shows a recurrent lesion with a 9 mm-deep subchondral cyst and surrounding oedema, and the ankle is otherwise well-aligned and stable. Which is the most appropriate next surgical option?

options.label: A
options.text: Osteochondral autograft transfer (OATS/mosaicplasty) or autologous chondrocyte-based grafting to restore the subchondral bone and cartilage

options.label: B
options.text: Repeat isolated microfracture

options.label: C
options.text: Total ankle replacement

options.label: D
options.text: Tibiotalar arthrodesis

options.label: E
options.text: Continued non-operative management with a further 12 months of bracing

explanation: Microfracture (marrow stimulation) is the recognised first-line treatment for small, contained talar lesions, but it produces fibrocartilage and works poorly for larger lesions or when there is a significant subchondral cyst. After failed microfracture with a deep cyst, the bony deficit must be restored — options that address both bone and cartilage include osteochondral autograft transfer (OATS/mosaicplasty, often harvested from the knee), structural allograft, or a cartilage-regenerative technique (e.g. AMIC or matrix-associated chondrocyte implantation) with bone grafting of the cyst. Repeat microfracture is unlikely to succeed in this setting. The joint is preserved, aligned and stable, so replacement and arthrodesis are inappropriately destructive, and further non-operative treatment is futile after a structural failure with a cyst.
learningPoint: After failed talar microfracture with a deep subchondral cyst, restore the bone — OATS/mosaicplasty or cartilage-regenerative grafting, not repeat marrow stimulation.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, 9th ed — Foot & Ankle: management of osteochondral lesions of the talus

stem: A 28-year-old skier has recurrent painful snapping of the peroneal tendons over the lateral malleolus despite a previous direct repair of the superior peroneal retinaculum. CT shows a flat (convex) retromalleolar groove and the tendons subluxing over the tip of the fibula. Which surgical strategy most directly addresses the underlying bony abnormality?

options.label: A
options.text: Fibular groove-deepening procedure (with retinacular repair/reconstruction)

options.label: B
options.text: Isolated repeat retinacular repair only

options.label: C
options.text: Peroneal tendon tenodesis (brevis to longus)

options.label: D
options.text: Broström-Gould lateral ligament reconstruction

options.label: E
options.text: Subtalar arthrodesis

explanation: Recurrent peroneal tendon instability after a failed soft-tissue repair, particularly with a shallow or convex retromalleolar groove on CT, is best treated by deepening the fibular groove to recreate a containing trough, combined with repair or reconstruction of the superior peroneal retinaculum. A second isolated retinacular repair ignores the bony cause and is likely to fail again. Tenodesis is used when one tendon is irreparably torn, not for instability with healthy tendons. A Broström-Gould treats lateral ligament (ATFL/CFL) instability, a different problem, and subtalar fusion is irrelevant to tendon containment.
learningPoint: Recurrent peroneal subluxation with a shallow/convex groove on CT needs a fibular groove-deepening procedure plus retinacular repair, not a repeat soft-tissue repair alone.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, 9th ed — Foot & Ankle: peroneal tendon disorders and instability
BOFAS peroneal tendon / lateral ankle guidance

stem: A 58-year-old man presents 10 weeks after an untreated Achilles tendon rupture with persistent weakness, an altered gait and an inability to perform a single-leg heel raise. Examination and MRI confirm a chronic rupture with a 6 cm gap and retracted, atrophic tendon ends. What is the most appropriate reconstructive option?

options.label: A
options.text: Flexor hallucis longus (FHL) tendon transfer to reconstruct the gap, with or without a turndown/V-Y advancement

options.label: B
options.text: Simple end-to-end primary repair

options.label: C
options.text: Functional bracing and physiotherapy as for an acute rupture

options.label: D
options.text: Percutaneous Achilles repair

options.label: E
options.text: Gastrocnemius recession (Strayer) alone

explanation: A chronic, retracted Achilles rupture with a large gap (here 6 cm) cannot be brought together end-to-end because the tendon ends are atrophic and the muscle is contracted. Bridging the defect requires a tendon transfer — the flexor hallucis longus is favoured because it lies adjacent, fires in phase with the triceps surae, is the next strongest plantarflexor and adds vascularity; for moderate gaps it may be combined with a V-Y lengthening or gastrocnemius turndown. Primary or percutaneous end-to-end repair and acute-style bracing are appropriate for fresh ruptures, not a 10-week retracted gap. A Strayer recession lengthens the gastrocnemius and would worsen, not restore, plantarflexion power.
learningPoint: Chronic retracted Achilles ruptures with a large gap are reconstructed (commonly FHL transfer ± turndown/V-Y), not repaired end-to-end like an acute tear.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, 9th ed — Foot & Ankle: chronic Achilles tendon rupture and reconstruction

stem: A 56-year-old man with diabetic neuropathy has a Charcot foot. Clinically the deformity is centred on the tarsometatarsal (Lisfranc) region with a 'rocker-bottom' collapse of the midfoot arch and a plantar-medial bony prominence at risk of ulceration. Using the Brodsky anatomical classification of Charcot foot, which pattern does this represent?

options.label: A
options.text: Brodsky type 1 — tarsometatarsal and naviculocuneiform joints (midfoot)

options.label: B
options.text: Brodsky type 2 — subtalar, talonavicular and calcaneocuboid joints (hindfoot)

options.label: C
options.text: Brodsky type 3A — ankle joint

options.label: D
options.text: Brodsky type 3B — calcaneal tuberosity (avulsion)

options.label: E
options.text: Brodsky type 4 — combined regions

explanation: The Brodsky classification describes Charcot neuroarthropathy by anatomical site: type 1 involves the midfoot (tarsometatarsal and naviculocuneiform joints) and is the most common pattern, classically producing the rocker-bottom deformity with a plantar bony prominence prone to ulceration — exactly as described. Type 2 affects the hindfoot (subtalar, talonavicular, calcaneocuboid); type 3A is the ankle (which is unstable and more likely to need fusion); type 3B is an avulsion of the calcaneal tuberosity; and type 4 is multi-regional. Distinguishing the level matters because hindfoot/ankle (type 2/3) disease is more unstable and more often requires arthrodesis or a hindfoot nail than midfoot disease.
learningPoint: Brodsky type 1 Charcot is midfoot (tarsometatarsal/naviculocuneiform) — the commonest pattern, giving the ulcer-prone rocker-bottom foot.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, 9th ed — Foot & Ankle: Charcot neuroarthropathy (Brodsky classification)

stem: A 60-year-old man with diabetic Charcot neuroarthropathy has a fixed, unstable midfoot deformity with a recurrent plantar ulcer that has failed total-contact casting and bracing. He is now being planned for operative reconstruction. Which set of principles best describes the modern 'superconstruct' approach to Charcot midfoot reconstruction?

options.label: A
options.text: Extend the fusion beyond the zone of injury into uninvolved joints, resect bone to shorten and realign without tension, use the strongest tolerable fixation, and apply it in the most mechanically advantageous position (e.g. intramedullary beaming/plantar plating)

options.label: B
options.text: Limit the fusion strictly to the single most deformed joint to preserve motion

options.label: C
options.text: Avoid internal fixation entirely and rely on prolonged casting

options.label: D
options.text: Use standard-strength fixation confined within the zone of injury

options.label: E
options.text: Perform a Syme amputation as the first-line reconstruction

explanation: Charcot bone is osteopenic, the soft tissues are poor and healing is prolonged, so conventional fixation often fails. The 'superconstruct' concept (Sammarco) sets out four principles to improve durability: (1) extend the fusion beyond the zone of injury to include adjacent, less-affected joints for better fixation purchase; (2) resect bone to allow adequate reduction and shorten the column so the deformity is corrected without soft-tissue tension; (3) use the strongest device the soft tissues will tolerate (e.g. intramedullary midfoot beams, solid bolts or locked plantar plates); and (4) place the fixation in the most mechanically advantageous position (plantar tension-side plating, intramedullary beaming along the columns). Limiting fixation to one joint, using standard fixation within the injury zone, or relying on casting alone all under-treat an unstable Charcot deformity. Amputation is a salvage option, not the primary reconstruction here.
learningPoint: Charcot 'superconstruct': fuse beyond the injury zone, resect to realign without tension, use the strongest fixation, and place it in the most mechanically advantageous position.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, 9th ed — Foot & Ankle: Charcot reconstruction (superconstruct principles)

stem: A 14-year-old girl with adolescent idiopathic scoliosis is being planned for posterior fusion. Standing radiographs show a proximal thoracic curve of 22 degrees, a main thoracic curve of 58 degrees and a thoracolumbar/lumbar curve of 30 degrees. On supine side-bending films the proximal thoracic corrects to 18 degrees, the main thoracic to 45 degrees and the thoracolumbar/lumbar to 8 degrees. The centre sacral vertical line passes between the pedicles of the apical lumbar vertebra. Using the Lenke system, how should this curve be classified with respect to curve type and lumbar modifier?

options.label: A
options.text: Type 1 (main thoracic), lumbar modifier A

options.label: B
options.text: Type 1 (main thoracic), lumbar modifier C

options.label: C
options.text: Type 3 (double major), lumbar modifier B

options.label: D
options.text: Type 2 (double thoracic), lumbar modifier A

options.label: E
options.text: Type 5 (thoracolumbar/lumbar), lumbar modifier C

explanation: A curve is 'structural' in Lenke if it fails to correct below 25 degrees on side-bending. Here only the main thoracic (45 degrees on bending) remains structural; the proximal thoracic (18) and TL/L (8) both correct out, making this a single structural main thoracic curve = Type 1. The lumbar modifier reflects where the CSVL falls relative to the apical lumbar vertebra: A = CSVL falls between the pedicles (medial to the apex), B = touches the apical body/pedicle, C = falls completely medial to the apex (apex translated off the line). With the CSVL between the lumbar pedicles, the modifier is A. The minor TL/L curve therefore need not be instrumented, supporting a selective thoracic fusion.
learningPoint: Lenke structurality hinges on the 25-degree side-bending threshold; the lumbar modifier (A/B/C) is set by where the CSVL sits relative to the apical lumbar vertebra and helps decide selective thoracic fusion.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Pediatric Spine/Scoliosis

stem: An 15-year-old has a Lenke 1C right main thoracic adolescent idiopathic scoliosis treated with a selective posterior thoracic fusion that stopped at the lower thoracic spine, leaving the unfused thoracolumbar/lumbar curve to spontaneously balance. Eight months later she is unhappy with her appearance: her trunk now leans noticeably to the left and the lumbar curve looks more prominent than before surgery, although the instrumented thoracic segment is well corrected. What is the most likely explanation?

options.label: A
options.text: Coronal decompensation from over-correction of the thoracic curve relative to the unfused lumbar curve

options.label: B
options.text: Proximal junctional kyphosis above the upper instrumented vertebra

options.label: C
options.text: Pseudarthrosis of the thoracic fusion mass

options.label: D
options.text: Adding-on at the distal end of the construct

options.label: E
options.text: Crankshaft phenomenon from continued anterior growth

options.label: F
options.text: Implant pull-out at the upper instrumented vertebra

explanation: Selective thoracic fusion of a 1C curve relies on the lumbar curve spontaneously compensating. If the thoracic curve is corrected more than the lumbar curve can match, the trunk shifts to the concave (left) side of the thoracic curve — coronal decompensation. This is the classic pitfall of being too aggressive with thoracic correction when the lumbar curve is large and translated (modifier C). Adding-on is distal extension of the curve below the construct (a distal selection error) and crankshaft occurs in skeletally immature patients after posterior-only fusion, but the described global truncal imbalance immediately after an over-corrected selective fusion is decompensation.
learningPoint: After selective thoracic fusion of a Lenke C curve, matching thoracic correction to the compensatory potential of the unfused lumbar curve avoids coronal decompensation.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Scoliosis surgical planning

stem: A 70-year-old woman with degenerative adult spinal deformity is having corrective surgery planned. Full-length standing radiographs give a pelvic incidence of 55 degrees, lumbar lordosis of 15 degrees, a pelvic tilt of 32 degrees and a C7 sagittal vertical axis of 95 mm. According to the SRS-Schwab classification and its associated realignment objectives, which combination of correction targets should the operation aim to restore?

options.label: A
options.text: PI−LL within ±9 degrees, pelvic tilt under 20 degrees and SVA under 50 mm

options.label: B
options.text: PI−LL of around 30 degrees, pelvic tilt under 10 degrees and SVA under 100 mm

options.label: C
options.text: Lumbar lordosis equal to thoracic kyphosis, pelvic tilt over 30 degrees and SVA of 0 mm

options.label: D
options.text: PI equal to pelvic tilt, lumbar lordosis under 20 degrees and SVA under 50 mm

options.label: E
options.text: PI−LL within ±9 degrees, pelvic tilt over 25 degrees and SVA under 95 mm

explanation: The SRS-Schwab classification grades three sagittal modifiers, each with a validated disability-linked threshold: PI−LL mismatch (0: <10, +: moderate 10–20, ++: marked >20), pelvic tilt (0: <20, +: 20–30, ++: >30) and global alignment by SVA (0: <40–50 mm, +: 40–95, ++: >95 mm). This patient is ++ on all three. The accepted realignment goals are to bring PI−LL to within roughly ±9–10 degrees, pelvic tilt below 20 degrees and SVA below 50 mm, which correlate with improved patient-reported outcomes. Under-correction leaves residual disability and predisposes to proximal junctional failure.
learningPoint: SRS-Schwab realignment aims for PI−LL ≈ ±9 degrees, PT <20 degrees and SVA <50 mm — the thresholds linked to better outcome and lower mechanical failure.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Adult Spinal Deformity

stem: A 68-year-old woman is reviewed two years after a long instrumented thoraco-pelvic fusion (upper instrumented vertebra at T10) for adult degenerative scoliosis. She has new thoracolumbar pain and a visible forward stoop. Radiographs show that the angle measured between the inferior endplate of the uppermost instrumented vertebra and the superior endplate of the vertebra two levels above it has increased from 5 degrees post-operatively to 24 degrees, with mild anterior wedging but no fracture or implant failure. Which statement best characterises this finding?

options.label: A
options.text: This is proximal junctional kyphosis; an increase of at least 10 degrees beyond the postoperative value (to >10 degrees) at the proximal junction defines it radiographically

options.label: B
options.text: This is proximal junctional failure and mandates urgent revision regardless of symptoms

options.label: C
options.text: This is distal junctional kyphosis and reflects sacro-pelvic fixation failure

options.label: D
options.text: This is within normal limits as the proximal junctional angle is expected to exceed 20 degrees

options.label: E
options.text: This represents pseudarthrosis of the construct and is diagnosed on this radiograph alone

explanation: Proximal junctional kyphosis (PJK) is defined radiographically as a proximal junctional angle (inferior endplate of the UIV to the superior endplate of the vertebra two levels cephalad) that is at least 10 degrees and at least 10 degrees greater than the corresponding preoperative/postoperative value. It is common after long deformity constructs and is often managed non-operatively when there is no neurology, fracture, implant failure or severe progression. Proximal junctional failure (PJF) is the more severe, structural form (fracture, screw pull-out, myelopathy, marked progression) that may need revision — this case is PJK without failure.
learningPoint: PJK = proximal junctional angle ≥10 degrees AND ≥10 degrees greater than baseline; distinguish from proximal junctional failure (structural compromise/neurology) which may require revision.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Deformity complications

subtopic: Subaxial cervical trauma — AO Spine classification
stem: A 30-year-old motorcyclist sustains a subaxial cervical injury. CT shows a fracture line passing through the vertebral body, both pedicles and the lamina such that the anterior and posterior elements are completely dissociated, but the two columns remain in anatomical alignment. There is no facet dislocation. Using the AO Spine subaxial cervical classification, which morphological type best describes this injury?

options.label: A
options.text: Type C — translation/dislocation injury

options.label: B
options.text: Type B — posterior or anterior tension band failure

options.label: C
options.text: Type A3 — incomplete burst fracture

options.label: D
options.text: Type A4 — complete burst fracture

options.label: E
options.text: Type F — non-displaced facet fracture

explanation: The AO Spine subaxial cervical system mirrors the thoracolumbar one. Type A = compression injuries (A0 minor, A1–A2 partial, A3 incomplete burst, A4 complete burst). Type B = tension band failures (B1 bony Chance, B2 posterior ligamentous/capsular, B3 anterior/hyperextension through the disc). Type C = any injury with translation or dislocation in any plane, i.e. complete dissociation of the cranial from the caudal segment regardless of current alignment. A fracture that completely dissociates anterior and posterior elements through body, pedicles and lamina is the cervical equivalent of a translational/dissociative injury and is graded Type C even when reduced, because the structural continuity between the segments is lost.
learningPoint: AO Spine Type C denotes complete dissociation/translation of one segment from another (most unstable) — alignment on the static scan does not downgrade it.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Cervical Spine Trauma

subtopic: Thoracolumbar trauma — AO Spine type B vs A4
stem: A 40-year-old falls from height. CT of an L1 fracture shows comminution of the vertebral body with involvement of both endplates and retropulsion (a complete burst pattern). MRI additionally shows a high-signal disruption of the supraspinous and interspinous ligaments with widening of the spinous process interval, but no translation. In the AO Spine thoracolumbar classification, what is the most accurate morphological grade?

options.label: A
options.text: B2 with A4 — posterior tension band disruption combined with a complete burst

options.label: B
options.text: A4 alone — complete burst fracture

options.label: C
options.text: A3 — incomplete burst fracture

options.label: D
options.text: C — translational injury

options.label: E
options.text: B1 — bony (Chance) tension band injury

explanation: AO Spine codes the bony injury and any tension band failure together. A complete burst (both endplates) is A4. Disruption of the posterior osteoligamentous tension band through soft tissue (interspinous/supraspinous ligament and capsules) is a B2 injury; if the tension band fails through bone it is B1 (Chance). The combination is therefore recorded as a B2 injury with an underlying A4 fracture — a meaningfully more unstable pattern than A4 alone and one that shifts management toward surgical stabilisation. C is reserved for translation/dislocation, which is absent here.
learningPoint: In AO Spine, an unstable burst with posterior ligamentous failure is coded B2/A4 — the B-modifier (tension band failure) raises instability above an isolated A4 burst.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Thoracolumbar Trauma

stem: A 28-year-old is brought in after a head-on car crash. CT shows bilateral fractures through the pars interarticularis of C2 with 4 mm of anterior translation of C2 on C3 and angulation of the C2–C3 disc space; the facet joints at C2–C3 appear disrupted. He is neurologically intact. Using the Levine–Edwards modification of the Effendi classification, which type does this represent and what is the implication for management?

options.label: A
options.text: Type II — disc and posterior ligament disruption with translation/angulation; reduction and halo or surgical stabilisation usually required

options.label: B
options.text: Type I — minimally displaced, stable; rigid collar

options.label: C
options.text: Type IIa — predominantly angulated (flexion-distraction); traction is contraindicated and it is treated by gentle extension and halo

options.label: D
options.text: Type III — bilateral facet dislocation at C2–C3 with severe displacement; open reduction and fusion

options.label: E
options.text: An os odontoideum and therefore not an acute injury

explanation: Hangman fractures (traumatic spondylolisthesis of the axis) are graded by Levine–Edwards. Type I: <3 mm translation, no angulation — stable, collar. Type II: significant translation (>3 mm) and angulation from disc and posterior ligamentous disruption — unstable, needs reduction with traction then halo, or surgery. Type IIa: marked angulation with little translation from a flexion-distraction mechanism — crucially, axial traction worsens it and is contraindicated; treated by extension/compression and halo. Type III: bilateral facet dislocation with a severely displaced body — requires open reduction and fixation. With 4 mm translation plus angulation and disc disruption, this is Type II.
learningPoint: Hangman Type IIa is the trap — it is a flexion-distraction pattern where traction is harmful; reduce by extension. Type II tolerates traction.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Upper Cervical Trauma

stem: Theme: classification systems for upper cervical (occipitocervical and C1–C2) injuries. For each clinical and radiological description below, select the SINGLE most appropriate named classification from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Anderson and Montesano classification

options.label: B
options.text: Dickman classification

options.label: C
options.text: Anderson and D'Alonzo classification

options.label: D
options.text: Levine and Edwards classification

options.label: E
options.text: Roy-Camille classification

options.label: F
options.text: Fielding and Hawkins classification

options.label: G
options.text: Powers ratio

options.label: H
options.text: Allen and Ferguson classification

emiLeads.stem: A comminuted impaction fracture of the occipital condyle with fragments displaced toward the brainstem, sustained in an axial load.

Determining whether a transverse atlantal ligament injury in a Jefferson fracture is a midsubstance ligamentous tear (type I) or a bony avulsion from the C1 lateral mass (type II), which guides operative versus halo treatment.
A fracture through the base of the odontoid process at its junction with the C2 body, with a characteristically high rate of non-union.
Grading rotatory atlantoaxial subluxation from simple rotation without anterior displacement (type I) through to posterior displacement (type IV).
Using the ratio of the distance from the basion to the posterior arch of C1 and from the opisthion to the anterior arch to detect anterior occipitoatlantal dissociation on a lateral radiograph.

explanation: Anderson–Montesano grades occipital condyle fractures (I impaction, II basilar skull extension, III avulsion — the unstable alar-ligament type). Dickman subdivides transverse atlantal ligament injuries into type I (intrasubstance, needs fusion) and type II (bony avulsion, may heal in a halo). Anderson–D'Alonzo grades odontoid fractures (II at the waist = non-union prone). Fielding–Hawkins grades atlantoaxial rotatory subluxation I–IV. The Powers ratio screens for occipitocervical dissociation. Levine–Edwards is for hangman fractures, Roy-Camille for sacral fractures and Allen–Ferguson for subaxial cervical injuries — included as distractors.
learningPoint: Match the named system to the level: condyle = Anderson-Montesano; TAL = Dickman; odontoid = Anderson-D'Alonzo; rotatory C1-2 = Fielding-Hawkins; occipitocervical dissociation screen = Powers ratio.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Cervical Spine Trauma

stem: A 25-year-old sustains a cervical spinal cord injury. At 72 hours, after the bulbocavernosus reflex has returned, examination shows no motor function below the injury but he has preserved light-touch and pinprick sensation in the perianal (S4–S5) region and is aware of deep anal pressure. There is no voluntary anal contraction. What is his ASIA Impairment Scale grade and the prognostic significance?

options.label: A
options.text: ASIA B — sensory but not motor incomplete; preserved sacral sensation indicates an incomplete injury with a better prognosis than a complete (ASIA A) injury

options.label: B
options.text: ASIA A — complete injury, because there is no motor function below the level

options.label: C
options.text: ASIA C — motor incomplete with most key muscles below the level grade <3

options.label: D
options.text: ASIA D — motor incomplete with most key muscles below the level grade ≥3

options.label: E
options.text: ASIA E — normal, as sacral function is preserved

explanation: Sacral sparing (any of: perianal sensation at S4–S5, deep anal pressure, or voluntary anal contraction) is the cardinal sign distinguishing an incomplete from a complete injury, and is only valid once spinal shock has resolved (bulbocavernosus reflex returned). Here there is preserved sacral sensation but no motor function below the level = ASIA B (sensory incomplete). Incomplete injuries (B–D) carry a substantially better neurological recovery prognosis than ASIA A (complete). ASIA C/D require motor sparing more than three levels below the injury, which is absent; ASIA E is normal.
learningPoint: Assess completeness only after spinal shock resolves; sacral sparing makes an injury incomplete (ASIA B+) and markedly improves the recovery prognosis.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Spinal Cord Injury

stem: A 32-year-old man with a complete spinal cord injury at T4 sustained eight months ago presents acutely with a pounding headache, flushing and sweating above the level of his lesion, and is found to have a blood pressure of 210/120 mmHg with a relative bradycardia. He had been catheterised but the bag is not draining. What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Sit the patient upright, loosen constrictive clothing, and identify and relieve the noxious stimulus (e.g. unblock or replace the catheter), giving a rapid-onset short-acting antihypertensive if the pressure remains dangerously high

options.label: B
options.text: Lie the patient flat and elevate the legs to maintain cerebral perfusion

options.label: C
options.text: Give intravenous fluids and atropine for the bradycardia before treating the blood pressure

options.label: D
options.text: Start a long-acting beta-blocker to control the hypertension over the next few hours

options.label: E
options.text: Arrange urgent CT head, as the headache and hypertension indicate raised intracranial pressure

explanation: Autonomic dysreflexia is a medical emergency seen in injuries at or above T6, triggered by a noxious stimulus below the lesion (commonly a blocked catheter or distended bladder, or faecal loading). Massive reflex sympathetic outflow causes severe hypertension with a baroreceptor-mediated bradycardia and flushing/sweating above the level. Management is to sit the patient up (using orthostatic drop to lower BP — the opposite of shock management), remove constricting items, and urgently find and remove the cause, most often by relieving the bladder. A rapid-onset antihypertensive (e.g. GTN, nifedipine) is given if BP remains critically high while the cause is sought.
learningPoint: Autonomic dysreflexia (lesions ≥T6): sit the patient UP and remove the trigger (usually a blocked catheter) first — uncontrolled it causes stroke, seizures and death.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Spinal Cord Injury complications

stem: A 27-year-old man sustains a penetrating knife injury to the left side of the neck. He has weakness of the left leg with a left-sided pyramidal pattern, loss of proprioception and vibration on the left below the lesion, and loss of pain and temperature sensation on the right side a couple of segments below the level. Which statement about this syndrome is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: It is a Brown-Séquard (cord hemisection) syndrome and carries the best prognosis for functional recovery and ambulation of the incomplete cord syndromes

options.label: B
options.text: It is an anterior cord syndrome and carries the worst prognosis of the incomplete syndromes

options.label: C
options.text: It is a posterior cord syndrome with isolated dorsal column loss

options.label: D
options.text: It is a central cord syndrome, typically affecting the upper limbs more than the lower limbs

options.label: E
options.text: The contralateral spinothalamic loss indicates this is a complete injury with no recovery expected

explanation: Cord hemisection produces ipsilateral motor (corticospinal) and dorsal column (proprioception/vibration) loss, with contralateral spinothalamic (pain/temperature) loss beginning a few segments below the lesion because those fibres decussate after ascending. Among the incomplete syndromes, Brown-Séquard has the most favourable prognosis: the majority of patients regain bowel and bladder control and the ability to walk. Anterior cord syndrome has the poorest prognosis; central cord syndrome classically spares the legs more than the arms and recovers in a characteristic sequence.
learningPoint: Brown-Séquard = ipsilateral motor/dorsal-column loss + contralateral spinothalamic loss, and has the best functional/ambulatory recovery of the incomplete cord syndromes (anterior cord the worst).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Cord Syndromes

stem: A spinal MDT reviews a 62-year-old with a single thoracic vertebral metastasis from renal cell carcinoma causing early epidural cord compression. Using the validated grading of epidural disease to decide whether circumferential cord decompression is needed before stereotactic radiosurgery, and a separate score that estimates whether instability requires stabilisation, which TWO tools are being applied — and which feature most strongly favours surgical decompression over radiotherapy alone for this radioresistant tumour?

options.label: A
options.text: Bilsky epidural spinal cord compression (ESCC) grade and the SINS; high-grade ESCC with cord compression from a radioresistant histology favours surgical separation/decompression then radiotherapy

options.label: B
options.text: Tokuhashi score and the Frankel grade; a low Tokuhashi favours surgery

options.label: C
