options.text: Harrington classification and the Karnofsky score; class I disease favours surgery

options.label: D
options.text: Weinstein-Boriani-Biagini (WBB) staging and the Enneking grade; these dictate radiotherapy

options.label: E
options.text: Tomita score and the Mirels score; a high Mirels favours vertebroplasty

explanation: The Bilsky ESCC scale (0 to 3) grades the degree of epidural compression on axial T2 MRI and is central to the NOMS (neurologic, oncologic, mechanical, systemic) framework: high-grade ESCC (2–3) with true cord compression from a radioresistant tumour (e.g. renal cell, melanoma, sarcoma) favours surgical separation to create a margin for postoperative stereotactic radiosurgery, rather than conventional radiotherapy alone. The Spinal Instability Neoplastic Score (SINS) separately quantifies mechanical instability. Tokuhashi/Tomita estimate survival/prognosis, Mirels is for long-bone impending fractures, and WBB/Enneking guide en bloc resection of primary tumours — not this scenario.
learningPoint: Bilsky ESCC grade (cord compression) + SINS (instability) drive modern metastatic decisions; high-grade compression from a radioresistant tumour favours surgical separation before SRS.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Metastatic Spine Disease

stem: A 40-year-old has a histologically confirmed primary aggressive but benign vertebral tumour (a giant cell tumour) that the surgeon wishes to remove by en bloc resection. To plan the resection margins on the axial plane, the vertebra is conceptually divided into 12 radiating zones (like a clock face) and 5 concentric layers from the paravertebral soft tissues to the dura. Which classification system is being used?

options.label: A
options.text: Weinstein-Boriani-Biagini (WBB) surgical staging system

options.label: B
options.text: Enneking (Musculoskeletal Tumor Society) staging system

options.label: C
options.text: Tomita surgical classification

options.label: D
options.text: Bilsky ESCC grading

options.label: E
options.text: Spinal Instability Neoplastic Score

explanation: The Weinstein-Boriani-Biagini (WBB) system describes the axial extent of a vertebral tumour using 12 radiating zones (numbered like a clock) and 5 tissue layers (A extraosseous soft tissue to E dura/epidural), allowing the surgeon to plan whether and how an en bloc (margin-respecting) excision is feasible. The Enneking system (benign 1–3; malignant I–III with A/B) classifies the biological aggressiveness and compartmental status and dictates the desired margin, but the WBB system maps the anatomy for the resection. Tomita is a separate surgical classification based on the anatomical compartments invaded.
learningPoint: WBB = the 12-zone/5-layer anatomical map for planning en bloc vertebral resection; Enneking grades tumour aggressiveness and the required margin.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Primary Spinal Tumours

stem: A 6-year-old child presents with localised thoracic back pain and a low-grade limp. Plain radiographs show near-complete, symmetrical collapse of a single thoracic vertebral body into a thin wafer, but with preservation of the adjacent disc spaces and no significant kyphotic deformity or soft-tissue mass. The child is neurologically intact. What is the most likely diagnosis and the usual management?

options.label: A
options.text: Langerhans cell histiocytosis (eosinophilic granuloma); observation, as vertebral height commonly reconstitutes with growth

options.label: B
options.text: Tuberculous spondylitis; antituberculous chemotherapy and bracing

options.label: C
options.text: Ewing sarcoma; neoadjuvant chemotherapy and en bloc resection

options.label: D
options.text: Acute lymphoblastic leukaemia infiltration; systemic chemotherapy

options.label: E
options.text: Aneurysmal bone cyst; selective arterial embolisation and curettage

explanation: Symmetrical collapse of a single vertebral body to a thin disc ('vertebra plana') with preserved adjacent discs in a child is the classic appearance of Langerhans cell histiocytosis (eosinophilic granuloma). It is typically self-limiting: the lesion regresses and vertebral body height often substantially reconstitutes with continued growth, so management is usually observation (with biopsy to confirm the diagnosis if uncertain, and bracing or surgery reserved for instability, deformity or neurological compromise). TB spares the disc late and causes a paraspinal abscess; Ewing sarcoma produces a destructive mass; ABC is expansile and lytic.
learningPoint: Vertebra plana in a child = Langerhans cell histiocytosis until proven otherwise; preserved discs and the prospect of height reconstitution allow observation in the neurologically intact.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Paediatric Spinal Tumours

stem: A 35-year-old man is diagnosed with thoracic spinal tuberculosis (Pott's disease) on biopsy. He has a small paraspinal abscess, mild wedging but no neurological deficit and no significant instability. He is started on standard UK first-line antituberculous therapy. Which statement about his pharmacological treatment is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: Treatment is with rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide and ethambutol for an initial 2-month intensive phase, followed by rifampicin and isoniazid in a continuation phase, with a total duration typically of around 12 months for spinal (skeletal) disease

options.label: B
options.text: A 6-month course of rifampicin and isoniazid alone is sufficient, with no need for a four-drug intensive phase

options.label: C
options.text: Surgical debridement is mandatory in all cases before chemotherapy can be effective

options.label: D
options.text: Pyridoxine (vitamin B6) should be avoided because it antagonises isoniazid

options.label: E
options.text: Ethambutol is continued lifelong to prevent relapse and its main toxicity is hepatic

explanation: UK practice (NICE/BTS) treats active spinal TB with the standard four-drug intensive phase — rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide and ethambutol — for 2 months, followed by a continuation phase of rifampicin and isoniazid. While pulmonary TB is treated for 6 months, skeletal/spinal TB is commonly extended (often to around 12 months) and decisions are made with infection specialists. Pyridoxine is co-prescribed to prevent isoniazid neuropathy (not avoided). Ethambutol's key toxicity is optic neuritis, and it is not lifelong. The mainstay is chemotherapy; surgery is reserved for neurology, instability, deformity, large abscess or failure of medical therapy.
learningPoint: Spinal TB: standard RIPE intensive phase then RH continuation, total duration often ~12 months; add pyridoxine; surgery only for specific indications.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Spinal Infection

stem: A 60-year-old intravenous drug user presents with severe back pain, fever and a 6-hour history of rapidly progressive bilateral leg weakness now at grade 2/5. MRI shows a posterior epidural collection from T9 to T12 with cord compression and high-signal change in the cord. Blood cultures are pending. What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Emergency surgical decompression and drainage of the abscess, with empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics (including anti-staphylococcal cover) started without waiting for culture results

options.label: B
options.text: Start antibiotics and observe with serial neurological examination, reserving surgery for failure to improve over 48 hours

options.label: C
options.text: CT-guided aspiration of the collection alone, deferring decompression unless weakness becomes complete

options.label: D
options.text: High-dose corticosteroids and radiotherapy to the affected segments

options.label: E
options.text: Await blood culture and sensitivities before giving any antibiotics to ensure an accurate microbiological diagnosis

explanation: A spinal epidural abscess with cord compression and rapidly progressive, incomplete neurological deficit is a surgical emergency: urgent decompression and drainage gives the best chance of neurological recovery, and the speed of intervention before deficits become fixed is the key prognostic factor. Empirical antibiotics covering Staphylococcus aureus (the commonest organism, including MRSA in IVDU) are started immediately after cultures are drawn — antibiotics should not be withheld for pending results when the patient is deteriorating. Steroids/radiotherapy are for malignant compression, not infection. Non-operative treatment is only considered for neurologically intact patients without instability after specialist discussion.
learningPoint: Epidural abscess + progressive deficit = emergency decompression plus immediate empirical anti-staphylococcal antibiotics; do not delay antibiotics for cultures or 'wait and see' once neurology is declining.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Spinal Infection

stem: A 58-year-old woman with long-standing rheumatoid arthritis has neck pain and is being assessed for cranial settling (vertical/basilar migration of the odontoid). On the lateral cervical radiograph the anterior atlantodental interval is now normal, but she has developed myelopathic hand signs. Which combination of findings is most relevant to deciding on surgical stabilisation, and why has the anterior atlantodental interval 'normalised'?

options.label: A
options.text: A reduced posterior atlantodental interval (PADI) and vertical migration on the Ranawat/Redlund-Johnell indices; the anterior interval can pseudo-normalise as the odontoid migrates cranially, so the PADI and basilar settling are better predictors of cord compression and recovery

options.label: B
options.text: An increased anterior atlantodental interval only; surgery is indicated once it exceeds 3 mm regardless of other measurements

options.label: C
options.text: The Sharp-Purser test alone, which quantifies the degree of vertical settling

options.label: D
options.text: A Pavlov (Torg) ratio below 0.8, which is the definitive measure of rheumatoid instability

options.label: E
options.text: The Power's ratio, which directly measures atlantoaxial subluxation in rheumatoid disease

explanation: In rheumatoid cervical disease the anterior atlantodental interval (AADI) reflects horizontal atlantoaxial subluxation, but as cranial settling (basilar invagination) progresses the odontoid moves up through the ring of C1 and the AADI may paradoxically reduce toward 'normal' even as the cord is increasingly compromised. The posterior atlantodental interval (PADI = space available for the cord) is therefore a better predictor of myelopathy and of the potential for neurological recovery (a PADI <14 mm is concerning). Vertical migration is quantified with indices such as Ranawat, Redlund-Johnell and McGregor's line. These, not the AADI, guide stabilisation in established disease. The Sharp-Purser test is a clinical manoeuvre for atlantoaxial instability, not a measure of settling.
learningPoint: In rheumatoid cervical myelopathy the PADI (space for the cord) and vertical-settling indices (Ranawat/Redlund-Johnell) predict cord compromise and recovery far better than the AADI, which can pseudo-normalise as the odontoid migrates cranially.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Inflammatory Cervical Spine

stem: A 55-year-old man with long-standing ankylosing spondylitis and a fused, bamboo-like thoracolumbar spine develops new, localised, mechanical thoracolumbar back pain. Imaging shows a transverse lucency through a previously fused disco-vertebral segment with adjacent sclerosis and some mobility on flexion-extension views, but no acute fall or obvious displaced fracture. There is no infection on inflammatory markers and biopsy. What does this represent and what is the principle of management?

options.label: A
options.text: An Andersson lesion (a chronic discovertebral pseudarthrosis/stress fracture through the ankylosed spine); it behaves like an unstable fracture in a rigid spine and symptomatic or progressive lesions require long-segment stabilisation

options.label: B
options.text: A typical degenerative Modic change requiring only analgesia and physiotherapy

options.label: C
options.text: Infective discitis that must be treated with prolonged antibiotics despite the negative work-up

options.label: D
options.text: A diffuse idiopathic skeletal hyperostosis bridging osteophyte that is incidental

options.label: E
options.text: A Schmorl node that will resolve spontaneously with no risk of progression

explanation: An Andersson lesion is a destructive discovertebral lesion in ankylosing spondylitis — effectively a chronic stress fracture/pseudarthrosis through the rigid, ankylosed column, often at a single mobile segment within an otherwise fused spine. It can be mistaken for infection or tumour, hence the negative septic work-up is reassuring. Because the ankylosed spine acts as a long lever arm, these lesions behave like unstable fractures: persistently symptomatic or progressive lesions, or those with deformity/neurology, are treated with long-segment instrumented stabilisation to achieve union, rather than analgesia alone. Modic change, DISH osteophytes and Schmorl nodes do not produce this mobile transverse lucency with instability.
learningPoint: An Andersson lesion is a pseudarthrosis/stress fracture through the ankylosed AS spine that mimics infection but behaves like an unstable fracture — symptomatic/progressive lesions need long-segment fixation.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics — Ankylosing Spondylitis

stem: A 24-year-old rugby player has had five anterior dislocations over two seasons. CT with 3D reconstruction and humeral subtraction shows an anterior glenoid bone defect of 18% of the glenoid width and a Hill-Sachs lesion whose medial margin lies medial to the medial border of the (reduced) glenoid track. He wishes to return to collision sport. Which single option best addresses his pattern of pathology?

options.label: A
options.text: Arthroscopic Bankart repair alone

options.label: B
options.text: Arthroscopic Bankart repair with remplissage

options.label: C
options.text: Latarjet (coracoid transfer) procedure

options.label: D
options.text: Open capsular shift without bony augmentation

options.label: E
options.text: Humeral head allograft reconstruction of the Hill-Sachs defect

explanation: The lesion is 'off-track' (engaging): the Hill-Sachs interval exceeds the glenoid track, so the humeral defect will engage the rim despite a sub-critical (<20%) glenoid loss. In a collision athlete with an off-track lesion plus sub-critical glenoid loss, a bony procedure that lengthens the glenoid arc and widens the track (Latarjet) is most reliable, restoring the bony arc and adding the conjoint-tendon sling effect. Bankart alone (A) fails frequently with off-track lesions. Remplissage (B) suits an off-track Hill-Sachs with minimal glenoid loss in lower-demand patients, but with combined bipolar loss in a collision athlete most UK shoulder surgeons select Latarjet. Capsular shift (D) ignores the bony pathology. Allograft humeral reconstruction (E) is reserved for very large (>40%) Hill-Sachs defects.
learningPoint: An 'off-track' Hill-Sachs lesion (Hill-Sachs interval > glenoid track) engages the glenoid even when glenoid bone loss is sub-critical, and combined bipolar loss in a collision athlete favours Latarjet.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Shoulder & Elbow chapter (glenohumeral instability)
Di Giacomo glenoid track concept; BESS/BOA shoulder instability pathway

stem: A 30-year-old man underwent an open Latarjet for recurrent anterior instability. Six weeks postoperatively he reports no recurrent instability but describes difficulty extending the interphalangeal joint of the thumb and weak thumb-tip pinch. Sensation over the dorsal first web space is reduced. There is no wrist drop and finger MCP extension is preserved. Which structure has most likely been injured?

options.label: A
options.text: Musculocutaneous nerve

options.label: B
options.text: Axillary nerve

options.label: C
options.text: Posterior interosseous nerve (radial nerve)

options.label: D
options.text: Lateral cord of the brachial plexus

options.label: E
options.text: Median nerve at the level of the coracoid

explanation: The clinical picture (loss of thumb IP extension, weak pinch, dorsal first web-space sensory loss with preserved finger MCP extension and no wrist drop) localises to the posterior interosseous / radial nerve distribution, not a nerve typically discussed around the coracoid. The point is that during Latarjet retractor placement and graft positioning, traction or compression injuries are described to the musculocutaneous nerve (conjoint tendon) and axillary nerve (inferior to subscapularis), but the question tests recognising a distal radial-territory deficit as an outlier presentation that should prompt a search for malpositioned hardware or limb positioning rather than attributing all post-Latarjet palsies to the musculocutaneous nerve. The features given are radial, hence PIN/radial nerve is the structure whose territory is affected.
learningPoint: Not every neurological deficit after shoulder surgery is the 'expected' local nerve; map the deficit to a dermatome/myotome before assuming musculocutaneous or axillary injury.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, peripheral nerve and shoulder chapters

stem: A 28-year-old judoka has recurrent anterior instability after a single traumatic dislocation. MR arthrogram shows contrast extravasating inferomedially with a 'J-shaped' axillary recess and an intact glenoid labrum. Examination under anaesthesia confirms anterior instability. Which lesion is most consistent with these findings, and what is the implication for surgery?

options.label: A
options.text: Bankart lesion; standard arthroscopic anterior labral repair

options.label: B
options.text: Humeral avulsion of the glenohumeral ligament (HAGL); repair must address the humeral-sided capsular avulsion

options.label: C
options.text: ALPSA lesion; arthroscopic mobilisation and re-tensioning of the medialised labrum

options.label: D
options.text: GLAD lesion; debridement of the chondral flap

options.label: E
options.text: Perthes lesion; arthroscopic repair of the non-displaced labral detachment

explanation: The combination of an intact labrum with contrast leaking inferomedially and a 'J sign' on the axillary pouch is the classic appearance of a HAGL lesion (humeral-sided avulsion of the inferior glenohumeral ligament). It is a recognised cause of persistent instability and a common reason for 'failed' instability surgery when missed, because labral-focused repairs do not address the humeral avulsion. Treatment requires repair of the capsule to its humeral footprint (open or arthroscopic). The other lesions (A, C, D, E) are all glenoid/labral-sided and would not produce a humeral-sided J-shaped extravasation with an intact labrum.
learningPoint: A HAGL lesion is a humeral-sided capsular avulsion with an intact labrum; it must be specifically sought on MR arthrogram (the 'J sign') and repaired at the humeral footprint.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, shoulder instability chapter

stem: When quantifying anterior glenoid bone loss on a true en-face 3D CT of the glenoid (humeral head subtracted), which statement best reflects current UK practice for surgical decision-making in recurrent anterior instability?

options.label: A
options.text: Glenoid loss is only relevant above 50% of the inferior glenoid diameter

options.label: B
options.text: A 'sub-critical' threshold of around 13-15% loss can already worsen outcomes of soft-tissue Bankart repair, and loss approaching 20% generally favours bony augmentation

options.label: C
options.text: The Pico/best-fit circle method measures glenoid version rather than area of bone loss

options.label: D
options.text: Bone loss should always be measured on plain axillary radiographs in preference to CT

options.label: E
options.text: Remplissage reliably compensates for any degree of glenoid bone loss

explanation: Evidence has shifted the historic '20-25% critical defect' downward: 'sub-critical' bone loss around 13-15% already increases recurrence and lowers patient-reported outcomes after isolated arthroscopic Bankart repair, and many surgeons now consider bony augmentation (e.g., Latarjet) as loss approaches 20%, especially in high-demand patients or with off-track lesions. The inferior glenoid is modelled as a true circle (best-fit/Pico method) to measure the surface-area defect, not version (C). CT 3D with humeral subtraction is the reference standard, not plain films (D). Remplissage addresses the humeral lesion and does not restore glenoid arc, so it cannot compensate for any degree of glenoid loss (E). 50% (A) is far beyond the relevant threshold.
learningPoint: Glenoid bone-loss thresholds have moved down: 'sub-critical' loss (~13-15%) already harms Bankart outcomes, and bony augmentation is favoured as loss approaches 20%.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, shoulder instability chapter
Shaha sub-critical bone loss data; BESS instability pathway

stem: During arthroscopic repair of a medium-sized supraspinatus tear you are choosing a construct. The patient is 58 with good tendon quality and a mobile tendon that reaches the footprint without tension. Which biomechanical statement most accurately guides construct choice in current practice?

options.label: A
options.text: A double-row transosseous-equivalent (suture-bridge) construct restores a larger footprint contact area and improves pressurised contact compared with a single-row repair

options.label: B
options.text: Single-row repair reliably produces lower retear rates than double-row in all tear sizes

options.label: C
options.text: Increasing the number of suture anchors always reduces retear regardless of tendon quality

options.label: D
options.text: Medial-row knots have no influence on the risk of medial cuff failure

options.label: E
options.text: Footprint restoration is irrelevant to tendon-to-bone healing

explanation: Transosseous-equivalent (suture-bridge) double-row constructs increase footprint contact area and pressurised contact, which is biomechanically favourable and is associated with lower retear in larger tears; for medium tears the structural advantage is greatest while clinical outcome differences are smaller. Single-row is not universally superior (B). Simply adding anchors does not guarantee healing when tendon quality or biology is poor (C). Knotted versus knotless medial rows and over-tensioning influence the mode of failure, including type-2 medial cuff failure (D is false). Footprint restoration does matter to the healing surface area (E is false).
learningPoint: Transosseous-equivalent double-row repair maximises footprint contact and reduces retear in larger tears, but construct choice must be matched to tendon quality and tension, not anchor count alone.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, rotator cuff chapter

stem: Eight months after a double-row suture-bridge supraspinatus repair, a 62-year-old has recurrent pain and weakness. Ultrasound shows the lateral suture anchors and tendon footprint intact, but a full-thickness defect at the musculotendinous junction just medial to the medial-row anchors. Which description best fits this failure pattern?

options.label: A
options.text: Type 1 failure (failure at the tendon-bone footprint / lateral pull-out)

options.label: B
options.text: Type 2 failure (medial cuff failure at or medial to the medial-row anchors)

options.label: C
options.text: Anchor pull-out from osteoporotic greater tuberosity

options.label: D
options.text: Suture cut-through at the lateral row only

options.label: E
options.text: Failure of the biceps tenodesis construct

explanation: A defect medial to the medial-row anchors with an intact lateral footprint is the classic 'type 2' medial cuff failure pattern described after transosseous-equivalent repairs, attributed to medial over-tensioning, strangulation of medial tendon vascularity by medial-row sutures, and stress concentration at the musculotendinous junction. Type 1 (A) is failure at the footprint/lateral pull-out. Anchor pull-out (C) and lateral suture cut-through (D) are excluded because the footprint and lateral anchors are intact. Biceps tenodesis (E) is irrelevant to this defect.
learningPoint: 'Type 2' medial cuff failure (defect medial to the medial row with intact footprint) is a recognised mode after suture-bridge repair, linked to medial over-tensioning and devascularisation.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, rotator cuff chapter

stem: A 57-year-old manual worker has an irreparable posterosuperior cuff tear, preserved passive range, no pseudoparalysis, Hamada grade 1 (no arthritis) and a Goutallier grade 3 supraspinatus. He wants to avoid reverse arthroplasty. Which joint-preserving option is most appropriate and what is the key biomechanical rationale?

options.label: A
options.text: Superior capsular reconstruction; it restores a static superior restraint and reduces superior humeral translation

options.label: B
options.text: Reverse total shoulder arthroplasty; it medialises the centre of rotation

options.label: C
options.text: Hemiarthroplasty; it resurfaces the arthritic glenoid

options.label: D
options.text: Open subacromial decompression alone; it removes the mechanical cause

options.label: E
options.text: Glenohumeral arthrodesis; it eliminates pain by fusion

explanation: Superior capsular reconstruction (SCR) is a recognised joint-preserving option for an irreparable posterosuperior tear in a younger/active patient without arthritis (Hamada 1) and without fixed pseudoparalysis; the graft acts as a static superior restraint, re-centring the head and reducing superior translation against the acromion. Reverse arthroplasty (B) is typically reserved for older patients or those with cuff-tear arthropathy/pseudoparalysis and is what this patient wishes to avoid. Hemiarthroplasty (C) is for the arthritic head but does not address the superior escape and there is no glenoid arthritis here. Isolated decompression (D) removes the coracoacromial arch restraint and risks anterosuperior escape in an irreparable tear. Arthrodesis (E) is a salvage of last resort.
learningPoint: SCR provides a static superior restraint and is best for active patients with irreparable posterosuperior tears, no arthritis (Hamada 1) and no fixed pseudoparalysis.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, rotator cuff chapter

stem: A 54-year-old with an irreparable, retracted posterosuperior cuff tear has profound weakness of active external rotation in adduction and a positive external rotation lag sign, but reasonable forward elevation and no arthritis. Which tendon transfer is most appropriate to restore active external rotation?

options.label: A
options.text: Pectoralis major transfer

options.label: B
options.text: Latissimus dorsi (or lower trapezius) transfer

options.label: C
options.text: Teres major transfer to the lesser tuberosity

options.label: D
options.text: Deltoid flap reconstruction

options.label: E
options.text: Trapezius transfer to the acromion

explanation: Loss of active external rotation with an external rotation lag sign from an irreparable posterosuperior (infraspinatus/teres minor) tear is the indication for a latissimus dorsi transfer (or, increasingly in UK practice, lower trapezius transfer with graft) to restore external rotation. Pectoralis major transfer (A) is used for irreparable anterosuperior (subscapularis) deficiency, not posterosuperior. Teres major to the lesser tuberosity (C) and the other options do not address posterior external-rotation loss appropriately.
learningPoint: Latissimus dorsi (or lower trapezius) transfer restores active external rotation in irreparable posterosuperior tears; pectoralis major transfer is for irreparable subscapularis (anterosuperior) loss.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, rotator cuff chapter

stem: A 74-year-old with longstanding cuff-tear arthropathy has radiographs showing acetabularisation of the coracoacromial arch and erosion of the superomedial glenoid, with the humeral head migrated superiorly and now articulating against the undersurface of the acromion. Using the Hamada classification, which grade does this represent?

options.label: A
options.text: Hamada grade 1

options.label: B
options.text: Hamada grade 2

options.label: C
options.text: Hamada grade 3

options.label: D
options.text: Hamada grade 4b

options.label: E
options.text: Hamada grade 5

explanation: Acetabularisation of the coracoacromial arch (the acromion erosion that creates a socket for the migrated head) defines Hamada grade 4b when accompanied by glenohumeral arthritis; grade 5 is humeral head collapse/osteonecrosis (the 'Hamada 5' end-stage). Grade 1 is acromiohumeral interval (AHI) > 6 mm; grade 2 is AHI <= 5 mm; grade 3 adds acetabularisation alone; grade 4 is glenohumeral arthritis (4a without, 4b with acetabularisation). The described acetabularisation plus glenoid arthritis corresponds to 4b.
learningPoint: Hamada grade 4b = glenohumeral arthritis WITH acetabularisation of the CA arch; grade 5 = humeral head collapse. Acromiohumeral interval drives the early grades.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, rotator cuff / arthroplasty chapter

stem: When a Grammont-style reverse total shoulder arthroplasty improves active forward elevation in a cuff-deficient shoulder, which combination of biomechanical changes is principally responsible?

options.label: A
options.text: Lateralisation and superior shift of the centre of rotation, decreasing deltoid moment arm

options.label: B
options.text: Medialisation and distalisation of the centre of rotation, increasing deltoid tension and recruiting more deltoid fibres for elevation

options.label: C
options.text: Preservation of the native centre of rotation with reliance on an intact supraspinatus

options.label: D
options.text: Conversion of the deltoid into an adductor of the arm

options.label: E
options.text: Increased glenohumeral translation to substitute for elevation

explanation: Grammont's principles medialise and distalise (lower) the centre of rotation. Medialisation increases the deltoid moment arm and recruits more anterior/posterior deltoid fibres to act as elevators, while distalisation tensions the deltoid; together these let the deltoid substitute for the absent cuff and elevate the arm. Medialisation also reduces torque on the baseplate. Options A, C, D and E misstate the mechanics; the deltoid becomes a more effective elevator, not an adductor, and the design does not rely on the supraspinatus or on increased translation.
learningPoint: The Grammont reverse works by medialising and distalising the centre of rotation, increasing the deltoid moment arm and tension so the deltoid substitutes for the deficient cuff.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, shoulder arthroplasty chapter

stem: A patient with a medialised (Grammont) reverse shoulder replacement develops progressive inferior scapular notching on follow-up radiographs. Which design or technical strategy most directly reduces the incidence of inferior scapular notching?

options.label: A
options.text: Superior tilt of the glenoid baseplate

options.label: B
options.text: Inferior positioning of the glenosphere with inferior tilt/overhang and/or a lateralised glenosphere

options.label: C
options.text: Using a smaller-diameter glenosphere placed superiorly

options.label: D
options.text: Increasing humeral neck-shaft angle to 155 degrees

options.label: E
options.text: Reducing deltoid tension by shortening the humeral component

explanation: Inferior scapular notching results from impingement of the humeral component on the scapular neck in adduction. It is reduced by inferiorising the glenosphere with inferior tilt/overhang and/or by lateralising the centre of rotation (lateralised glenosphere or bony-increased offset), which clears the scapular neck. A larger glenosphere can also help by increasing inferior overhang. Superior tilt (A) and superior placement (C) worsen notching; a higher (more valgus) neck-shaft angle of 155 degrees (D) tends to increase notching compared with more varus designs (e.g., 135-145 degrees). Shortening the humeral component (E) does not address the impingement mechanism.
learningPoint: Inferior glenosphere position with inferior tilt/overhang and/or lateralisation reduces scapular notching; superior tilt and a high (155 degrees) neck-shaft angle increase it.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, shoulder arthroplasty chapter

stem: Four months after an uncomplicated reverse total shoulder arthroplasty, a patient who had been progressing well develops new pain over the top of the shoulder, focal tenderness at the acromion and loss of previously gained active elevation, with no signs of infection. Plain films are initially equivocal. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.label: A
options.text: Acromial or scapular spine stress fracture

options.label: B
options.text: Glenoid baseplate loosening

options.label: C
options.text: Late periprosthetic infection

options.label: D
options.text: Axillary nerve neurapraxia

options.label: E
options.text: Humeral stem subsidence

explanation: Increased deltoid tension after a reverse arthroplasty raises load on the acromion and scapular spine, and an acromial or scapular spine stress fracture (Levy classification) is a recognised cause of new superior shoulder pain with loss of active elevation in the early-to-mid postoperative period; radiographs are often initially equivocal and CT or bone scan may be needed. Baseplate loosening (B) and humeral subsidence (E) usually present later with different radiographic signs; infection (C) is excluded by the absence of infective features; axillary nerve injury (D) would produce a deltoid motor/sensory deficit rather than focal acromial tenderness.
learningPoint: New superior shoulder pain with focal acromial tenderness and loss of active elevation after reverse TSA should raise suspicion of an acromial/scapular spine stress fracture (Levy classification); imaging may be initially equivocal.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, shoulder arthroplasty chapter

stem: A 66-year-old with primary osteoarthritis and a posteriorly worn, retroverted glenoid (Walch type B2) is being considered for anatomic total shoulder replacement. Which statement best reflects the principal failure concern and its mitigation?

options.label: A
options.text: Glenoid component loosening from eccentric posterior loading ('rocking horse'); address by correcting version with eccentric reaming, augmented glenoid components, or selecting a reverse arthroplasty

options.label: B
options.text: Humeral stem loosening is the dominant mode and version is irrelevant

options.label: C
options.text: Subscapularis failure is unrelated to glenoid version

options.label: D
options.text: Polyethylene glenoid components never loosen in B2 glenoids

options.label: E
options.text: Reverse arthroplasty is contraindicated in any patient with an intact cuff

explanation: In a biconcave (Walch B2) glenoid with posterior wear and increased retroversion plus posterior humeral head subluxation, an anatomic glenoid component is at risk of eccentric posterior loading and the 'rocking-horse' loosening phenomenon. Mitigation includes correcting version (limited eccentric/asymmetric reaming, posterior bone graft, or augmented glenoid components) and, in many cases, selecting a reverse arthroplasty for more reliable fixation when deformity or posterior subluxation is severe. Stem loosening is not the dominant TSA failure mode and version certainly matters (B). Reverse arthroplasty is increasingly used even with an intact cuff for severe B2/B3 glenoids (E is false).
learningPoint: The B2 glenoid risks 'rocking-horse' glenoid loosening from eccentric posterior load; manage with version correction, augmented components, or a reverse arthroplasty.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, shoulder arthroplasty chapter

stem: Theme: Nerve disorders around the shoulder. For each clinical presentation, select the SINGLE most likely nerve or site of involvement from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Suprascapular nerve at the suprascapular notch

options.label: B
options.text: Suprascapular nerve at the spinoglenoid notch

options.label: C
options.text: Long thoracic nerve

options.label: D
options.text: Axillary nerve

options.label: E
options.text: Spinal accessory nerve

options.label: F
options.text: Dorsal scapular nerve

options.label: G
options.text: Musculocutaneous nerve

emiLeads.stem: A volleyball player has painless isolated wasting and weakness of infraspinatus (weak external rotation) with normal supraspinatus power; MRI shows a paralabral cyst posterior to the glenoid.
A patient develops medial winging of the scapula that worsens on forward pushing against a wall, after a viral illness; trapezius function is normal.
After a radical neck dissection, a patient has lateral (and downward) scapular winging, drooping of the shoulder and difficulty abducting above 90 degrees, with trapezius wasting.
Three weeks after an anterior shoulder dislocation, a patient has deltoid weakness and a patch of numbness over the lateral shoulder ('regimental badge' area).
A patient has weakness of both supraspinatus and infraspinatus with deep, poorly localised posterosuperior shoulder pain, and imaging shows compression at the transverse scapular ligament.

explanation: Suprascapular nerve compression at the spinoglenoid notch (often by a paralabral/ganglion cyst) spares supraspinatus and affects only infraspinatus, whereas compression at the suprascapular notch (under the transverse scapular ligament) affects both supraspinatus and infraspinatus with pain. Long thoracic nerve palsy (serratus anterior) gives medial winging accentuated by forward pushing; spinal accessory nerve palsy (trapezius) gives lateral winging with shoulder droop, classically after posterior triangle/neck surgery. Axillary nerve injury after dislocation produces deltoid weakness with regimental-badge sensory loss.
learningPoint: Localise suprascapular palsy by muscle pattern (spinoglenoid notch = infraspinatus only; suprascapular notch = both), and distinguish medial (long thoracic/serratus) from lateral (spinal accessory/trapezius) scapular winging.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, shoulder and peripheral nerve chapters

stem: Theme: Anterior instability - lesion-directed management. For each scenario, choose the SINGLE most appropriate operative strategy from the list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Arthroscopic Bankart repair alone

options.label: B
options.text: Arthroscopic Bankart repair with remplissage

options.label: C
options.text: Latarjet / coracoid transfer

options.label: D
options.text: Free (iliac crest) bone block glenoid augmentation

options.label: E
options.text: Open HAGL repair

options.label: F
options.text: Revision of a non-united/malpositioned coracoid graft to a free bone block

options.label: G
options.text: Non-operative rehabilitation

emiLeads.stem: A 22-year-old with a first-time traumatic anterior dislocation, an isolated Bankart lesion, no significant bone loss and an on-track Hill-Sachs lesion, who is a competitive contact athlete keen on surgical stabilisation.
A 26-year-old with recurrent instability, 10% glenoid loss and a moderate off-track Hill-Sachs lesion, in whom the surgeon wishes to avoid a coracoid transfer.
A 34-year-old labourer with recurrent dislocations and 22% anterior glenoid bone loss with an off-track Hill-Sachs lesion.
A patient with persistent instability after a previous Latarjet, whose CT shows a fragmented, partially resorbed coracoid graft and a persistent glenoid deficit.
A patient with recurrent instability after a prior arthroscopic Bankart repair; repeat MR arthrogram shows an intact labral repair but a humeral-sided capsular avulsion with a 'J sign'.

explanation: Management is lesion-directed: an isolated Bankart with an on-track lesion and no meaningful bone loss is treated by Bankart repair (consider in a contact athlete after a first traumatic dislocation). Sub-critical glenoid loss with an off-track Hill-Sachs in a patient avoiding coracoid transfer suits Bankart plus remplissage. Critical (>20%) glenoid loss with an off-track lesion in a heavy-demand patient is a Latarjet indication. A failed Latarjet with a resorbed/fragmented graft and persistent deficit is best revised with a free structural bone block (e.g., iliac crest). Recurrent instability with an intact labral repair but a humeral 'J sign' indicates a missed HAGL requiring humeral-sided repair.
learningPoint: Match the procedure to the lesion: on-track + no bone loss = Bankart; off-track + sub-critical loss = Bankart + remplissage; critical loss = Latarjet; failed/resorbed Latarjet = free bone block; missed HAGL = humeral-sided repair.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, shoulder instability chapter

stem: A 27-year-old overhead-throwing athlete has poorly localised posterior shoulder pain and intermittent paraesthesia over the lateral arm, worse in abduction and external rotation. Examination shows mild deltoid (teres minor) weakness without a sensory dermatomal cut-off, and tenderness in the posterior axillary region. MR angiography demonstrates dynamic occlusion of the posterior circumflex humeral artery in abduction-external rotation. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.label: A
options.text: Quadrilateral space syndrome (axillary nerve / posterior circumflex humeral artery compression)

options.label: B
options.text: Parsonage-Turner syndrome (neuralgic amyotrophy)

options.label: C
options.text: Suprascapular nerve entrapment at the spinoglenoid notch

options.label: D
options.text: Thoracic outlet syndrome

options.label: E
options.text: C5 cervical radiculopathy

explanation: Quadrilateral space syndrome is compression of the axillary nerve and/or posterior circumflex humeral artery within the quadrilateral space (bordered by teres minor, teres major, long head of triceps and the humeral shaft), typically in throwers, producing poorly localised posterior pain, non-dermatomal lateral arm paraesthesia and sometimes teres minor/deltoid signs; dynamic occlusion of the posterior circumflex humeral artery in abduction-external rotation on MR angiography is characteristic. Parsonage-Turner (B) causes acute severe pain then patchy palsy without vascular occlusion. Spinoglenoid entrapment (C) affects infraspinatus. Thoracic outlet (D) and C5 radiculopathy (E) give different distributions and would not show isolated posterior circumflex humeral artery occlusion.
learningPoint: Quadrilateral space syndrome compresses the axillary nerve and posterior circumflex humeral artery; suspect it in throwers with posterior pain, non-dermatomal lateral arm paraesthesia and dynamic arterial occlusion on MRA.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, shoulder and peripheral nerve chapters

stem: A 35-year-old woman falls onto an outstretched hand. CT of the elbow shows a coronal-shear fracture of the distal humerus in which the capitellar fragment carries a contiguous piece of the trochlea, and there is comminution and impaction of the posterior cortex of the lateral column ('double-arc' sign on the lateral view). The articular cap is largely cartilage with little subchondral bone. Which feature most strongly predicts the need for a more extensive surgical exposure and influences the prognosis?

options.label: A
options.text: A Bryan-Morrey type I (Hahn-Steinthal) pattern with a large bony fragment

options.label: B
options.text: Posterior comminution of the lateral column and trochlear extension (Dubberley type B / higher type)

options.label: C
options.text: The presence of an associated radial head fracture

options.label: D
options.text: A Bryan-Morrey type IV (Kocher-Lorenz) thin osteochondral shell alone

options.label: E
options.text: Involvement of the lateral epicondyle in the fracture line

options.label: answerKey
options.text: B

explanation: Coronal shear fractures of the capitellum are graded by Bryan-Morrey (modified by McKee to add type IV, the Kocher-Lorenz osteochondral shell) and, more usefully for planning, by Dubberley. The Dubberley system grades the articular pattern (1 = capitellum only; 2 = capitellum + trochlea as one piece; 3 = capitellum + separate trochlea) and crucially sub-classifies each as 'A' (no posterior comminution) or 'B' (posterior condylar comminution). The 'double-arc' sign indicates trochlear extension. Dubberley 'B' subtypes (posterior comminution) require a more extensive, often posterior or combined exposure for reduction and fixation, have a higher rate of AVN, stiffness and reoperation, and are the principal driver of both the surgical approach and outcome. The Kocher-Lorenz thin shell (D) is difficult to fix but is not what determines the need for extensile exposure here, and an associated radial head fracture (C), while relevant to stability, does not define the capitellar exposure.
learningPoint: In capitellar coronal-shear fractures the Dubberley 'B' modifier (posterior column comminution) and trochlear extension, not the Bryan-Morrey number, drive the need for extensile exposure and predict AVN and stiffness.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Trauma chapter (distal humerus / capitellum)
Dubberley et al. JBJS Am 2006 capitellar fracture classification

subtopic: Distal humerus column fixation - surgical approach
stem: A fit 40-year-old man has a closed, displaced intra-articular bicolumnar (AO 13-C3) distal humerus fracture with good bone stock that you plan to reduce and dual-plate. You consider an olecranon osteotomy to maximise articular exposure. Which statement most accurately reflects current best practice regarding the choice and technique of this exposure?

options.label: A
options.text: An extra-articular transverse osteotomy gives the best articular view and heals most reliably

options.label: B
options.text: A chevron (apex-distal) intra-articular osteotomy through the bare area of the trochlear notch is preferred, but should be avoided if a total elbow replacement might be needed

options.label: C
options.text: A triceps-splitting (Campbell) approach gives equivalent articular exposure to an osteotomy for a C3 fracture

options.label: D
options.text: The olecranon osteotomy should be pre-drilled and tapped only after the fracture is fixed

options.label: E
options.text: A paratricipital (triceps-on) approach is mandatory because it preserves the extensor mechanism

options.label: answerKey
options.text: B

explanation: For a reconstructable bicolumnar C3 fracture, an apex-distal chevron osteotomy through the bare area of the trochlear notch (sigmoid notch) maximises articular visualisation and, being intra-articular through a non-load-bearing cartilage-poor zone, heals reliably when repaired with a tension-band or screw. A key principle is to pre-drill/pre-tap (or pre-place the fixation) before completing the osteotomy so the repair reduces anatomically. Critically, an olecranon osteotomy is contraindicated if there is any prospect of converting to a total elbow arthroplasty (e.g. very osteoporotic or unreconstructable bone in an elderly patient), because it burns the bridge to a primary TEA and creates an osteotomy that must then unite; in that scenario a paratricipital or triceps-reflecting approach is used instead. An extra-articular transverse osteotomy (A) gives poor articular view and a higher non-union rate. Triceps-splitting and paratricipital approaches give less articular exposure than an osteotomy for a true C3 (C, E).
learningPoint: Use a chevron olecranon osteotomy through the bare area for articular access in reconstructable C3 distal humerus fractures, but never if conversion to TEA is on the table - pre-place the fixation before cutting.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Trauma chapter (distal humerus fractures and approaches)
BOAST - Open fractures / peri-articular fracture principles

subtopic: Valgus instability and medial collateral ligament
stem: A 22-year-old elite javelin thrower has medial elbow pain at ball release and loss of velocity. The moving valgus stress test is positive, reproducing pain between 70 and 120 degrees as the elbow is rapidly extended under a valgus load. MRI shows a partial undersurface tear of the anterior bundle of the medial collateral ligament. He also has posterior elbow pain on terminal extension. Which statement about the relevant anatomy and pathomechanics is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: The posterior bundle of the MCL is the primary restraint to valgus at 30-120 degrees

options.label: B
options.text: The anterior bundle of the MCL is the primary valgus restraint through most of the flexion arc, and chronic valgus laxity drives posteromedial olecranon osteophyte impingement (valgus extension overload)

options.label: C
options.text: The radial head is the primary static valgus stabiliser and MCL reconstruction is therefore rarely needed

options.label: D
options.text: Valgus extension overload causes anterolateral, not posteromedial, olecranon impingement

options.label: E
options.text: Docking-technique MCL reconstruction uses the flexor carpi ulnaris tendon as the graft of choice

options.label: answerKey
options.text: B

explanation: The anterior bundle of the MCL (specifically its anterior band) is the primary static restraint to valgus through most of the flexion arc and is the structure injured by repetitive overhead throwing. Chronic valgus laxity allows the posteromedial olecranon to abut the medial wall of the olecranon fossa, producing posteromedial osteophytes and chondral wear - the 'valgus extension overload' syndrome, which classically co-exists with MCL insufficiency. The radial head is a secondary (not primary) valgus stabiliser (C is wrong, and is precisely why isolated radial head excision in the unstable elbow is dangerous). The posterior bundle contributes mainly in deep flexion (A wrong). Impingement is posteromedial, not anterolateral (D wrong). MCL reconstruction ('Tommy John', e.g. docking technique) most often uses palmaris longus or gracilis, not FCU tendon (E wrong - FCU is split/elevated for exposure, not harvested as graft).
learningPoint: The anterior bundle of the MCL is the primary valgus restraint; chronic insufficiency in throwers produces posteromedial olecranon impingement (valgus extension overload), and the radial head is only a secondary valgus stabiliser.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Shoulder & Elbow chapter (elbow instability, throwing athlete)

stem: A 46-year-old man has an elbow injury and CT shows an isolated anteromedial facet coronoid fracture (O'Driscoll subtype 2) with a small concomitant LUCL avulsion, but an intact radial head and no olecranon fracture. He has varus posteromedial rotatory instability. Which operative plan is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Non-operative treatment in an above-elbow cast in pronation for 6 weeks

options.label: B
options.text: Fixation of the anteromedial facet via a medial (over-the-top or FCU-split) approach with a buttress plate, plus repair of the lateral collateral ligament

options.label: C
options.text: Radial head replacement and lateral ligament repair, leaving the coronoid

options.label: D
options.text: Lateral approach only, with a lag screw placed antero-posteriorly through the coronoid from behind

options.label: E
options.text: Hinged external fixation alone without addressing the coronoid

options.label: answerKey
options.text: B

explanation: The anteromedial facet of the coronoid is an unbuttressed, eccentrically loaded medial column that, when fractured, produces varus posteromedial rotatory instability with rapid articular wear if untreated. Because the fragment lies anteromedially, it cannot be adequately reached or buttressed from a standard lateral approach; it requires a dedicated medial approach (over-the-top, or between FCU heads, protecting the ulnar nerve) and a small buttress plate or screws applied medially. Associated lateral (LUCL) injury is repaired to restore the lateral pivot. Non-operative care (A) is reserved only for small undisplaced fragments with a congruent, stable joint on examination. There is no radial head fracture, so replacement (C) is wrong. A posterior-to-anterior lag screw from a lateral approach (D) does not buttress the antero-medially located fragment. Hinged ex-fix alone (E) does not restore the bony buttress.
learningPoint: Anteromedial facet coronoid fractures need a medial buttress through a medial approach plus lateral ligament repair - a lateral-only exposure cannot buttress this fragment.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Shoulder & Elbow chapter (coronoid, PMRI)
O'Driscoll coronoid classification; Doornberg & Ring anteromedial facet

stem: A 49-year-old manual worker presents 10 weeks after an untreated distal biceps rupture of his dominant arm with a fixed proximal retraction of the muscle, marked weakness of supination, and an unyielding tendon stump at surgery. A primary anatomical repair to the radial tuberosity cannot be achieved without excessive elbow flexion. Which statement best reflects appropriate management and its risks?

options.label: A
options.text: Repair under tension at 110 degrees of flexion and accept a permanent flexion contracture as the safest option

options.label: B
options.text: Reconstruct with an interposition tendon graft (e.g. semitendinosus or Achilles allograft); proximal radioulnar synostosis and posterior interosseous nerve injury are recognised complications

options.label: C
options.text: Tenodese the biceps to the brachialis, accepting loss of supination but restoring flexion power only

options.label: D
options.text: Manage non-operatively because chronic ruptures gain nothing functional from surgery

options.label: E
options.text: Use a single anterior incision in all chronic cases because it minimises heterotopic bone

options.label: answerKey
options.text: B

explanation: In chronic distal biceps rupture with a retracted, non-mobile tendon, anatomical re-insertion to the tuberosity often cannot be achieved without unacceptable elbow flexion, so an interposition graft (semitendinosus autograft or Achilles/other allograft) is used to bridge the gap and restore length, allowing fixation in a more functional position. Reconstruction restores supination strength and endurance, which is the dominant deficit (supination loss exceeds flexion loss after distal biceps rupture), so non-operative management (D) leaves a meaningful deficit in an active worker. Repairing under tension in marked flexion (A) risks contracture and re-rupture. Tenodesis to brachialis (C) sacrifices the main indication (supination). A two-incision approach is often used in chronic/grafted cases for safe tuberosity exposure; the single-incision technique is associated with more lateral antebrachial cutaneous and PIN problems and does not reliably reduce heterotopic bone (E). The graft route near the proximal radius carries specific risks of proximal radioulnar synostosis and PIN injury.
learningPoint: Chronic retracted distal biceps ruptures usually need a tendon graft to restore length and supination power; warn specifically about radioulnar synostosis and PIN injury.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Shoulder & Elbow chapter (distal biceps)

stem: A 38-year-old powerlifter sustained a complete distal triceps avulsion repaired 4 months ago with transosseous sutures through the olecranon. He now has recurrent loss of active extension against gravity and a palpable gap, having returned to heavy training early. Imaging confirms a re-rupture. Which factor is most strongly associated with triceps repair failure, and what is the most appropriate revision strategy?

options.label: A
options.text: Repair failure is usually due to ulnar nerve scarring; revise with simple ulnar nerve transposition

options.label: B
options.text: Early high loading and a partial/under-recognised tear are key risk factors; revise with an anatomical footprint repair (suture anchors or a knotless 'double-row' construct) +/- tendon augmentation/graft

options.label: C
options.text: Triceps re-rupture is best left alone as function is rarely affected

options.label: D
options.text: Re-rupture is almost always due to anabolic steroid use and surgery is contraindicated

options.label: E
options.text: Convert to a tendon transfer using brachioradialis as the primary revision

options.label: answerKey
options.text: B

explanation: Distal triceps ruptures are uncommon and partial tears are frequently under-recognised; failure of repair is associated with premature heavy loading, a missed partial tear, poor tendon quality (corticosteroid/anabolic steroid use, local injection, olecranon bursectomy) and a footprint that is not anatomically restored. Biomechanically, anatomical footprint reconstruction - suture anchors or a knotless double-row construct re-creating the broad triceps insertion - is stronger than simple transosseous bone-tunnel sutures and is the preferred revision; chronic or tissue-deficient cases may need allograft (e.g. Achilles) or anconeus rotation augmentation. The lesion is mechanical, not primarily neurogenic (A wrong). Re-rupture in an athlete causing loss of active extension is functionally significant and warrants revision (C wrong). Steroid use raises risk but is not an absolute contraindication to surgery (D wrong). A primary tendon transfer (E) is not the first-line revision when host tendon can be reconstructed.
learningPoint: Triceps repair failure is driven by early loading and missed partial tears; revise with an anatomical footprint repair (anchors/double-row), augmenting with graft when tissue is deficient.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Shoulder & Elbow chapter (triceps rupture)

subtopic: Heterotopic ossification excision - timing and recurrence
stem: A 29-year-old man with a previous traumatic brain injury has disabling elbow heterotopic ossification, now 9 months from injury, with a 30-90 degree arc and a bony block. He is referred for excision. Regarding timing and recurrence prophylaxis after excision of established HO at the elbow, which statement reflects current best practice?

options.label: A
options.text: Excision should be delayed at least 18-24 months until the bone scan is completely cold, regardless of functional loss

options.label: B
options.text: Excision can proceed once the HO is mature on plain radiographs and the patient is functionally limited; single-dose peri-operative radiotherapy and/or indomethacin are used to reduce recurrence

options.label: C
options.text: A normal alkaline phosphatase must be documented before excision, as it reliably confirms maturity

options.label: D
options.text: Recurrence is negligible after excision so prophylaxis is unnecessary

options.label: E
options.text: Bisphosphonates given after excision dissolve any residual heterotopic bone and prevent recurrence

options.label: answerKey
options.text: B

explanation: Modern practice has moved away from mandating prolonged delay and a 'cold' bone scan/normal ALP before excising elbow HO, because prolonged immobility worsens stiffness and the markers correlate poorly with safe timing. Excision is appropriate once the HO is radiographically mature (well-corticated) and is causing functional limitation - often around 6-12 months - with the priority being to restore motion before fixed contracture and articular damage become irreversible. Recurrence is reduced with single low-dose peri-operative radiotherapy and/or a course of indomethacin. ALP and bone scan are unreliable maturity gauges (A, C). Recurrence is real, so prophylaxis matters (D). Bisphosphonates inhibit mineralisation of osteoid but do not dissolve mature bone or reliably prevent recurrence, and ossification can progress when they are stopped (E).
learningPoint: Excise mature, functionally limiting elbow HO once corticated on radiographs - don't wait for a cold bone scan or normal ALP - and reduce recurrence with single-dose radiotherapy and/or indomethacin.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Shoulder & Elbow / Trauma (heterotopic ossification)

stem: A 34-year-old manual labourer has end-stage post-traumatic elbow arthritis with a painful but reasonably stable joint. He is too young and too high-demand for a total elbow replacement, and joint debridement has failed. Interposition (resurfacing) arthroplasty is considered. Which statement best reflects the indications, technique and limitations of elbow interposition arthroplasty?

options.label: A
options.text: It is the procedure of choice in elderly low-demand patients in preference to total elbow replacement

options.label: B
options.text: It resurfaces the distal humerus with interposed fascia/allograft (e.g. dermal or Achilles) and is reserved for young, higher-demand patients; instability and persistent pain are common, and it does not preclude later conversion to TEA

options.label: C
options.text: It reliably abolishes pain and restores a normal arc, making it superior to total elbow replacement for all ages

options.label: D
options.text: It requires sacrifice of the collateral ligaments, which are not reconstructed

options.label: E
options.text: A linked total elbow replacement is contraindicated after a previous interposition arthroplasty

options.label: answerKey
options.text: B

explanation: Interposition (resurfacing) arthroplasty addresses the dilemma of end-stage elbow arthritis in a patient too young or too active for the strict lifelong lifting limit of a total elbow replacement. The distal humeral articular surface is reshaped and covered with an interposition graft (autogenous fascia lata, dermal allograft or Achilles tendon allograft), often combined with collateral ligament reconstruction and sometimes a hinged external fixator to protect the construct. It is a salvage/'biological' option, not a panacea: pain relief is incomplete and unpredictable, residual instability is the major problem (hence ligament reconstruction is integral, not omitted - D is wrong), and it does not restore a normal arc (C wrong). Importantly, it preserves bone stock and can be converted to a total elbow replacement later when the patient ages into a lower-demand bracket (so E is wrong, and it is not preferred over TEA in the elderly - A wrong).
learningPoint: Elbow interposition arthroplasty is a bone-preserving salvage for the young, high-demand arthritic elbow - expect incomplete pain relief and instability, reconstruct the ligaments, and keep TEA as a later option.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Shoulder & Elbow chapter (elbow arthritis, interposition arthroplasty)

stem: A 53-year-old man had an in-situ ulnar nerve decompression at the elbow 14 months ago. He had initial improvement but now has recurrent paraesthesiae and a positive Tinel sign localised to the cubital tunnel, plus a new painful trigger when the nerve is palpated with the elbow flexed. Examination suggests the nerve subluxes anteriorly over the medial epicondyle on flexion. Nerve conduction studies show a persistent localising lesion at the elbow. Which is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Repeat in-situ decompression at the same site

options.label: B
options.text: Revision surgery with anterior (subcutaneous or submuscular) transposition, ensuring release of the proximal intermuscular septum/arcade of Struthers and the distal FCU fascia

options.label: C
options.text: Medial epicondylectomy alone without addressing the soft tissues proximally

options.label: D
options.text: Wrist-level carpal tunnel release, as the symptoms are referred

options.label: E
options.text: Reassurance and a further 12 months of night splinting

options.label: answerKey
options.text: B

explanation: This is a failed primary cubital tunnel decompression with two features pointing to revision: (1) a persistent/recurrent localising lesion and (2) frank perineural scarring with secondary nerve instability (subluxation over the epicondyle on flexion). Repeating an in-situ decompression (A) leaves the nerve in a scarred bed and an unstable position and tends to fail again. The appropriate revision is to mobilise the nerve out of the scar and transpose it anteriorly (subcutaneous or submuscular) into a healthy bed, taking care to release all secondary tethering points - the medial intermuscular septum / arcade of Struthers proximally and the deep flexor-pronator (FCU) fascia distally - which are common sites of incomplete primary release. Medial epicondylectomy alone (C) does not deal with the proximal tethering or scar bed and risks valgus instability/flexor-origin weakness. The lesion is at the elbow, not the wrist (D). Continued conservative treatment after a failed operation and objective recurrence (E) is inappropriate.
learningPoint: A failed in-situ ulnar decompression with perineural scarring and nerve subluxation is best revised by anterior transposition into a healthy bed, releasing the arcade of Struthers proximally and FCU fascia distally - not by repeating the in-situ release.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Shoulder & Elbow / Hand chapter (ulnar neuropathy at the elbow)

stem: A 44-year-old man underwent metallic radial head replacement as part of a terrible-triad reconstruction. Three months on he has lateral elbow pain, loss of terminal extension, and early radiographic changes. Imaging shows widening of the lateral ulnohumeral joint and developing erosion of the capitellum. Which technical error is most likely, and which radiographic sign best confirms it?

options.label: A
options.text: An undersized (too short) prosthesis; confirmed by capitellar overstuffing

options.label: B
options.text: An overstuffed (too long/over-lengthened) prosthesis; confirmed by lateral ulnohumeral joint widening and capitellar wear, the medial ulnohumeral joint being the reference for correct height

options.label: C
options.text: Excessive valgus malposition; confirmed by a congruent radiocapitellar joint

options.label: D
options.text: Correct sizing; the changes represent expected stress shielding

options.label: E
options.text: An oversized diameter; confirmed by narrowing of the proximal radioulnar joint only

options.label: answerKey
options.text: B

explanation: Over-lengthening ('overstuffing') is a classic, often missed error in radial head replacement. A prosthesis that is too long levers the proximal ulna away from the trochlea, opening the lateral side of the ulnohumeral joint, abnormally loading and eroding the capitellum, and causing pain and stiffness. The key intra-operative and radiographic reference is the medial ulnohumeral joint: when the radial head height is correct, the lateral edge of the coronoid sits level with the proximal radius and the medial ulnohumeral joint space is symmetrical and parallel; lateral ulnohumeral widening on a true AP film indicates overstuffing. An undersized head (A) under-loads the radiocapitellar joint and does not cause capitellar erosion or lateral joint widening. The findings are pathological, not stress shielding (D).
learningPoint: Lateral ulnohumeral widening with capitellar erosion after radial head replacement signals overstuffing; judge correct prosthesis height against the medial ulnohumeral joint, not the radiocapitellar gap.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Shoulder & Elbow chapter (radial head arthroplasty)
Doornberg / van Riet - radial head replacement complications

stem: A 40-year-old man fell from height and sustained a comminuted radial head fracture. He also complains of wrist pain. Before deciding on management of the radial head, which assessment is most critical, and what is the underlying lesion you are trying to exclude?

options.label: A
options.text: Assessment of the distal radioulnar joint and interosseous membrane to exclude a longitudinal forearm (Essex-Lopresti) injury, because radial head excision would then cause proximal radial migration

options.label: B
options.text: Assessment of the scaphoid only, to exclude a concomitant scaphoid fracture

options.label: C
options.text: Assessment of the ulnar nerve, because the lesion is a high ulnar palsy

options.label: D
options.text: Assessment of the MCL alone, as this determines radial head treatment

options.label: E
options.text: No additional assessment is needed; treat the radial head in isolation

options.label: answerKey
options.text: A

explanation: An Essex-Lopresti injury is a longitudinal disruption of the forearm: a radial head fracture combined with rupture of the interosseous membrane and disruption of the distal radioulnar joint, allowing proximal migration of the radius. It is easily missed because attention is fixed on the elbow. The clinical clue is wrist (DRUJ) pain with a radial head fracture. The critical step is to examine and image the DRUJ and assess interosseous membrane integrity, because if this injury is present, simple radial head excision is contraindicated - it removes the last longitudinal buttress and leads to proximal radial migration, ulnar-sided wrist impaction and a very difficult salvage. The radial head must instead be preserved or replaced (not excised) to maintain forearm length. The other assessments (B-D) do not address the longitudinal instability that fundamentally changes radial head management.
learningPoint: With a radial head fracture and wrist pain, exclude an Essex-Lopresti injury (interosseous membrane + DRUJ disruption); if present, never excise the radial head - preserve or replace it to maintain forearm length.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Trauma chapter (forearm / Essex-Lopresti)

stem: A 74-year-old woman with rheumatoid arthritis has a painful, mobile distal humeral nonunion 11 months after attempted plate fixation, with osteoporotic, deficient bone and articular destruction. She has low functional demands and accepts a permanent lifting restriction. Which is the most appropriate definitive management?

options.label: A
options.text: Revision dual-column plating with iliac crest autograft

options.label: B
options.text: Linked (semi-constrained) total elbow arthroplasty

options.label: C
options.text: Unlinked (unconstrained) total elbow arthroplasty

options.label: D
options.text: Elbow arthrodesis

options.label: E
options.text: Resection (flail) arthroplasty as first choice

options.label: answerKey
options.text: B

explanation: In an elderly, low-demand patient with a distal humeral nonunion, poor osteoporotic/deficient bone and articular destruction (here compounded by inflammatory arthritis), the bone stock and joint surface will not support durable internal fixation, so revision plating with graft (A) is likely to fail again. A linked (semi-constrained) total elbow arthroplasty is the procedure of choice: the linkage provides intrinsic stability that does not depend on the deficient collateral ligaments or columns, reliably relieves pain and restores a functional arc in this population - provided the patient accepts the permanent low-demand lifting restriction. An unlinked TEA (C) relies on competent bone columns and ligaments for stability and is inappropriate when these are deficient. Arthrodesis of the elbow (D) is poorly tolerated functionally and is a last resort, usually for infection. Resection arthroplasty (E) is a salvage option, not first choice here.
learningPoint: For distal humeral nonunion in the elderly low-demand patient with deficient bone, a linked total elbow arthroplasty - not revision fixation or an unlinked implant - is the durable solution.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Shoulder & Elbow chapter (total elbow arthroplasty)
BESS / BOA distal humerus in the elderly

stem: A 30-year-old man has symptomatic posterolateral rotatory instability after a previous radial head excision and lateral soft-tissue injury. The lateral pivot-shift test is difficult to elicit awake because of guarding. Which statement most accurately reflects assessment and surgical reconstruction of PLRI in this setting?

options.label: A
options.text: The pivot-shift reduces (clunks back) as the elbow is flexed beyond about 40 degrees with the forearm supinated; reconstruction restores the lateral ulnar collateral ligament from the isometric point on the lateral epicondyle to the supinator crest

options.label: B
options.text: The pivot-shift subluxates in flexion and reduces in extension; reconstruction anchors the graft to the radial head

options.label: C
options.text: PLRI is a pure radiocapitellar problem and is cured by radial head replacement alone

options.label: D
options.text: The graft origin should be placed at the tip of the olecranon for isometry

options.label: E
options.text: The chair push-up and tabletop relocation tests assess valgus, not posterolateral, instability

options.label: answerKey
options.text: A

explanation: In PLRI the lateral pivot-shift is performed with the forearm supinated and a valgus/axial load: as the elbow is brought from extension into flexion the radioulnar unit reduces with a palpable clunk at roughly 40 degrees (and subluxates as it is extended). Reconstruction re-creates the lateral ulnar collateral ligament (LUCL) using a tendon graft (e.g. palmaris/gracilis or triceps/local tissue) running from the isometric point on the lateral epicondyle (at the centre of the capitellar arc) to the supinator crest of the ulna - the ulnar, not the radial, attachment is key (B and D wrong). Loss of the radial head buttress (as here) aggravates PLRI, but replacing the radial head alone does not restore the deficient lateral ligament, so it is not curative on its own (C wrong). The chair push-up, tabletop and lateral pivot-shift tests all specifically assess posterolateral, not valgus, instability (E wrong).
learningPoint: PLRI reduces with a clunk on flexion in supination; reconstruct the LUCL from the isometric epicondylar point to the supinator crest - the ulnar footprint and ligament, not the radial head, is the key to stability.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Shoulder & Elbow chapter (PLRI, LUCL reconstruction)

subtopic: Stiff elbow - ulnar nerve in flexion contracture release
stem: A 36-year-old man is undergoing open release of a post-traumatic elbow flexion contracture. Pre-operatively he has 30-100 degrees of motion, no ulnar nerve symptoms, but you anticipate gaining substantial flexion. Regarding the ulnar nerve during contracture release, which approach is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: The ulnar nerve never needs to be addressed if the patient has no pre-operative ulnar symptoms

options.label: B
options.text: When a large gain in flexion is expected (or pre-operative flexion is below ~100-110 degrees, or there is medial HO/prior surgery), the ulnar nerve should be identified and decompressed (and transposed if it subluxes or is scarred) to avoid post-operative ulnar neuropathy

options.label: C
options.text: Routine medial epicondylectomy should be performed in every contracture release to protect the nerve

options.label: D
options.text: The ulnar nerve is only at risk during lateral-side releases

options.label: E
options.text: Post-operative ulnar neuropathy after contracture release is unrelated to the gain in flexion

options.label: answerKey
options.text: B

explanation: Regaining flexion after release of a stiff elbow acutely lengthens and compresses the ulnar nerve across the cubital tunnel; iatrogenic ulnar neuropathy is one of the commonest complications of elbow contracture surgery, and crucially it can occur even when the patient had no pre-operative ulnar symptoms (so A and E are wrong). Current practice is to prophylactically identify and decompress the ulnar nerve when a substantial flexion gain is anticipated, when pre-operative flexion is limited (roughly below 100-110 degrees), or when there is medial heterotopic ossification or previous medial surgery; the nerve is transposed if it is scarred or subluxes. Routine medial epicondylectomy in every case (C) is unnecessary and risks medial instability. The ulnar nerve is principally at risk on the medial side and with flexion gains, not only during lateral releases (D).
learningPoint: Decompress (and if needed transpose) the ulnar nerve during elbow contracture release whenever a big flexion gain is expected - iatrogenic ulnar neuropathy occurs even in patients with no pre-operative nerve symptoms.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Shoulder & Elbow chapter (stiff elbow, contracture release)

subtopic: Complex elbow trauma - classifications and eponyms
stem: Theme: eponymous classifications and lesions in complex elbow trauma. For each description, select the SINGLE most appropriate term from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: O'Driscoll classification

options.label: B
options.text: Bryan-Morrey classification

options.label: C
options.text: Regan-Morrey classification

options.label: D
options.text: Hahn-Steinthal fragment

options.label: E
options.text: Kocher-Lorenz fragment

options.label: F
options.text: Mason-Johnston classification

options.label: G
options.text: Jupiter classification

options.label: H
options.text: Wadsworth (TRAP) approach

emiLeads.stem: A system that grades coronoid fractures by anatomical location into tip, anteromedial facet and basal subtypes, directly informing the associated instability pattern.
A capitellar coronal-shear fracture consisting of a large fragment of capitellum with substantial underlying cancellous bone, generally amenable to screw fixation.
A thin osteochondral 'shell' capitellar fragment with little subchondral bone, difficult to fix and sometimes excised.
A classification grading coronoid fractures purely by the height/proportion of the coronoid involved (tip, less than 50%, more than 50%).
A classification that subdivides distal humeral (T- and Y-type) intercondylar fractures, historically used to describe the bicolumnar pattern.

explanation: O'Driscoll classifies coronoid fractures by location (tip, anteromedial facet, basal) and is the system that links fracture pattern to instability (e.g. anteromedial facet to varus posteromedial rotatory instability). The Regan-Morrey system grades the coronoid by height/proportion (type I tip, II up to 50%, III more than 50%) and does not capture the critical anteromedial facet. Capitellar coronal-shear fractures use Bryan-Morrey, within which the Hahn-Steinthal fragment is the large bony capitellar piece (fixable) and the Kocher-Lorenz fragment is the thin osteochondral shell. The Jupiter classification subdivides bicolumnar distal humeral (intercondylar T/Y) fractures. The Wadsworth/TRAP (triceps-reflecting anconeus pedicle) is an exposure, not a classification, and Mason-Johnston refers to radial head fractures - both are distractors here.
