learningPoint: Coronoid: O'Driscoll = location (anteromedial facet matters), Regan-Morrey = height; capitellum: Bryan-Morrey, with Hahn-Steinthal (big, fixable) vs Kocher-Lorenz (thin shell).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics, Shoulder & Elbow / Trauma chapters (elbow classifications)

subtopic: Nerve lesions about the elbow and forearm
stem: Theme: nerve lesions about the elbow and forearm. For each clinical picture, select the single most likely nerve (or branch) injured. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Ulnar nerve at the cubital tunnel

options.label: B
options.text: Posterior interosseous nerve (PIN)

options.label: C
options.text: Anterior interosseous nerve (AIN)

options.label: D
options.text: Median nerve in the proximal forearm (pronator)

options.label: E
options.text: Radial nerve in the spiral groove

options.label: F
options.text: Musculocutaneous nerve

options.label: G
options.text: Ulnar nerve at Guyon's canal

options.label: H
options.text: Lower brachial plexus (C8-T1)

emiLeads.stem: A young adult sustains a Monteggia injury with an anteriorly dislocated radial head. He cannot extend the metacarpophalangeal joints of the fingers or thumb, but wrist extension is preserved with radial deviation and sensation is entirely normal.

Several weeks of forearm aching are followed by inability to make the 'OK' sign: the patient cannot flex the interphalangeal joint of the thumb or the distal interphalangeal joint of the index finger, and there is no sensory loss.
A spiral fracture of the distal third of the humeral shaft is complicated by a wrist drop, weak finger MCP extension and a small patch of numbness over the dorsal first web space.
An adult who had a lateral condyle non-union in childhood, with a progressive valgus elbow, develops gradual clawing of the ring and little fingers, weak pinch and numbness of the little finger.

explanation: Localise by the motor/sensory pattern. PIN palsy is purely motor: finger and thumb MCP drop with radial-deviated wrist extension and no sensory loss, classically after a Monteggia injury or radial neck pathology. The anterior interosseous nerve supplies FPL and FDP to the index, so an AIN (Kiloh-Nevin) lesion abolishes the 'OK' sign with no sensory deficit. The radial nerve in the spiral groove is tethered at the distal third of the humerus (Holstein-Lewis pattern), giving wrist/finger drop with dorsal first web-space numbness. A 'tardy' ulnar palsy follows years of cubitus valgus after a childhood lateral condyle non-union, stretching the ulnar nerve at the cubital tunnel.
learningPoint: A PIN palsy is a finger-drop with radial wrist deviation and NO sensory loss, distinguishing it from a high radial nerve lesion (wrist drop with dorsal web-space numbness).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Anatomy: peripheral nerves of the upper limb
Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Elbow and forearm trauma

subtopic: Acute knee injuries - mechanism to diagnosis
stem: Theme: the acutely injured knee. For each mechanism and examination finding, choose the single most likely structural diagnosis. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Anterior cruciate ligament rupture

options.label: B
options.text: Posterior cruciate ligament rupture

options.label: C
options.text: Medial collateral ligament injury

options.label: D
options.text: Posterolateral corner injury

options.label: E
options.text: Acute patellar dislocation

options.label: F
options.text: Bucket-handle meniscal tear

options.label: G
options.text: Quadriceps tendon rupture

options.label: H
options.text: Patellar (tendon) rupture

emiLeads.stem: A footballer changes direction without contact, feels a 'pop' and the knee swells immediately into a tense effusion. Lachman's test is positive with a soft end-point.
A front-seat passenger strikes the dashboard with a flexed knee. There is a posterior sag of the proximal tibia and a positive quadriceps active test.
After a twisting injury the knee is locked at about 15 degrees with a springy block to full extension; plain radiographs are normal and the patient can still weight-bear.
A teenage girl twists on a planted foot; the knee gives way and swells, and there is marked apprehension on lateral translation of the patella with tenderness over the medial patellofemoral ligament.

explanation: An immediate, tense haemarthrosis after a non-contact pivot with a positive Lachman is an ACL rupture until proven otherwise. A posterior sag and positive quadriceps active test after a dashboard injury indicate a PCL rupture. A knee locked short of full extension with a springy mechanical block and normal films is a displaced bucket-handle meniscal tear. Lateral patellar apprehension with MPFL tenderness after a twisting injury indicates an acute patellar dislocation.
learningPoint: A tense, immediate haemarthrosis after a non-contact pivoting injury is an ACL rupture until proven otherwise.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Knee: ligament and meniscal injuries

stem: Theme: the painful shoulder. For each presentation, select the single most likely diagnosis. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Subacromial impingement / rotator cuff tendinopathy

options.label: B
options.text: Adhesive capsulitis (frozen shoulder)

options.label: C
options.text: Calcific tendinitis

options.label: D
options.text: Acromioclavicular joint osteoarthritis

options.label: E
options.text: Long head of biceps rupture

options.label: F
options.text: Glenohumeral osteoarthritis

options.label: G
options.text: Full-thickness supraspinatus tear

options.label: H
options.text: Anterior glenohumeral instability

emiLeads.stem: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes has four months of increasing shoulder pain and stiffness. Both active AND passive external rotation are markedly reduced, and radiographs are normal.
A previously well adult develops sudden, severe atraumatic shoulder pain, and a plain film shows a globular calcific density just above the greater tuberosity.
A labourer feels a sudden give while lifting; he develops a bunched 'Popeye' bulge in the lower arm and bruising, but his strength is near-normal.
Pain is localised to the top of the shoulder, reproduced by cross-body adduction and at the very top of the abduction arc, with tenderness directly over the acromioclavicular joint.

explanation: A global loss of passive (not just active) external rotation with normal radiographs, especially in a diabetic, is adhesive capsulitis rather than a cuff tear. Sudden atraumatic severe pain with a calcific deposit above the greater tuberosity is calcific tendinitis. A 'Popeye' deformity with bruising but preserved strength after lifting indicates a long head of biceps rupture. Pain localised to the top of the shoulder with a positive cross-body adduction test and high-arc pain points to acromioclavicular joint pathology.
learningPoint: Global loss of passive external rotation (especially in a diabetic) points to adhesive capsulitis, not a rotator cuff tear.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Shoulder and elbow: degenerative and soft-tissue disorders

subtopic: Incomplete cord and cauda equina syndromes
stem: Theme: incomplete spinal cord and cauda equina syndromes. For each presentation, choose the single most likely syndrome. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Central cord syndrome

options.label: B
options.text: Anterior cord syndrome

options.label: C
options.text: Brown-Sequard syndrome

options.label: D
options.text: Posterior cord syndrome

options.label: E
options.text: Cauda equina syndrome

options.label: F
options.text: Conus medullaris syndrome

options.label: G
options.text: Complete cord transection

options.label: H
options.text: Spinal shock

emiLeads.stem: An elderly man with cervical spondylosis falls and hyperextends his neck. He has disproportionately greater weakness in the hands and arms than in the legs, with patchy sensory loss.
A stab wound hemisects the cord. Below the level there is ipsilateral loss of motor power and proprioception with contralateral loss of pain and temperature sensation.
A flexion injury compromises the anterior spinal artery: motor function and pain/temperature sensation are lost below the level, while joint-position sense and vibration are preserved.
A large central lumbar disc prolapse produces bilateral leg pain, saddle anaesthesia and painless urinary retention with reduced anal tone.

explanation: Central cord syndrome follows a hyperextension injury in a spondylotic neck and weakens the arms more than the legs ('upper limbs worse'). Brown-Sequard (cord hemisection) gives ipsilateral motor and dorsal-column loss with contralateral spinothalamic loss. Anterior cord syndrome (anterior spinal artery territory) spares the dorsal columns. Cauda equina syndrome from a large central disc causes bilateral sciatica, saddle anaesthesia and bladder dysfunction - a surgical emergency.
learningPoint: Central cord syndrome = arms worse than legs after a hyperextension injury in a spondylotic neck; cauda equina = saddle anaesthesia + retention = emergency MRI.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Spine: spinal cord injury syndromes

subtopic: Compressive neuropathies of the upper limb
stem: Theme: compressive neuropathies of the upper limb. For each picture, select the single most likely site of compression. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Median nerve at the carpal tunnel

options.label: B
options.text: Ulnar nerve at the cubital tunnel

options.label: C
options.text: Ulnar nerve at Guyon's canal

options.label: D
options.text: Median nerve in the proximal forearm (pronator)

options.label: E
options.text: Radial tunnel / posterior interosseous nerve

options.label: F
options.text: Superficial radial nerve (Wartenberg's)

options.label: G
options.text: C6 cervical radiculopathy

options.label: H
options.text: C8/T1 cervical radiculopathy

emiLeads.stem: A cyclist on a long tour develops weakness of the interossei and hypothenar muscles with numbness of the volar little finger, but sensation over the dorsal ulnar hand and the power of FCU and the little-finger FDP are preserved.
Night-time numbness of the thumb, index and middle fingers is relieved by shaking the hand; Phalen's test is positive and there is early thenar wasting.
Numbness over the dorsoradial hand and base of the thumb follows wearing a tight wristband; Tinel's sign is positive over the distal radius and there is no motor weakness.
Aching over the proximal extensor forearm and mobile wad is reproduced by resisted supination; there is no sensory loss but finger and thumb extension are weak.

explanation: A Guyon's canal (handlebar) palsy spares the dorsal ulnar sensation and FCU/ring-little FDP because those are innervated proximally, distinguishing it from a cubital tunnel lesion. Carpal tunnel syndrome gives night pain in the radial three-and-a-half digits relieved by shaking, with late thenar wasting. Wartenberg's syndrome is a purely sensory compression of the superficial radial nerve over the distal radius. Radial tunnel syndrome is pain over the mobile wad with weak finger extension and no sensory loss.
learningPoint: Guyon's canal palsy spares dorsal ulnar sensation and the FCU/ring-little FDP, separating it from a cubital tunnel (elbow) lesion.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Hand and wrist: compressive neuropathies

stem: Theme: forefoot pain. For each presentation, select the single most likely diagnosis. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Morton's (interdigital) neuroma

options.label: B
options.text: Hallux rigidus

options.label: C
options.text: Freiberg's disease

options.label: D
options.text: Plantar plate rupture of the second MTP joint

options.label: E
options.text: Sesamoiditis

options.label: F
options.text: Gout

options.label: G
options.text: Metatarsal stress fracture

options.label: H
options.text: Hallux valgus

emiLeads.stem: Burning pain radiates into the third web space with a palpable click on mediolateral compression of the forefoot (Mulder's sign) and numbness of the adjacent toe borders.
A middle-aged runner has dorsal first MTP joint pain provoked by terminal dorsiflexion, with a dorsal osteophyte and reduced dorsiflexion but a reasonably preserved mid-range arc.
An adolescent athlete develops second MTP joint pain; radiographs show flattening and sclerosis of the second metatarsal head.
The second toe gradually deviates medially and lifts dorsally; there is a positive dorsal drawer of the second MTP joint and pain under the metatarsal head.

explanation: A positive Mulder's click in the third web space localises an interdigital (Morton's) neuroma. Dorsal first MTP pain at terminal dorsiflexion with a dorsal osteophyte is hallux rigidus. An osteochondrosis flattening the second metatarsal head in an adolescent is Freiberg's disease. Progressive dorsomedial drift of the second toe with a positive MTP drawer is a plantar plate rupture.
learningPoint: A positive Mulder's click localises an interdigital (Morton's) neuroma, most often in the third web space.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Foot and ankle: forefoot disorders

stem: Theme: the limping child. For each presentation, select the single most likely diagnosis. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Developmental dysplasia of the hip

options.label: B
options.text: Perthes disease

options.label: C
options.text: Slipped upper femoral epiphysis (SUFE)

options.label: D
options.text: Septic arthritis of the hip

options.label: E
options.text: Transient synovitis

options.label: F
options.text: Juvenile idiopathic arthritis

options.label: G
options.text: Toddler's fracture

options.label: H
options.text: Osteomyelitis

emiLeads.stem: A 13-year-old overweight boy limps with knee and thigh pain; the affected leg lies externally rotated, internal rotation is limited, and the hip rolls into obligatory external rotation as it is flexed.
A 6-year-old boy has several weeks of a painless limp with reduced hip abduction and internal rotation; radiographs show fragmentation and collapse of the femoral head epiphysis.
A 3-year-old refuses to bear weight and holds the hip flexed and externally rotated; he is febrile with a raised CRP and ESR and has severe pain on any passive movement.
A 4-year-old develops a limp a week after a viral illness; he is afebrile, only mildly restricted, has near-normal inflammatory markers and settles with rest over a few days.

explanation: Obligatory external rotation on hip flexion in an overweight adolescent is the classic sign of a SUFE. A several-week painless limp in a young boy with epiphyseal fragmentation is Perthes disease. A febrile child who will not weight-bear, with raised inflammatory markers and pain on any movement, has septic arthritis (apply the Kocher criteria) until proven otherwise. A well, afebrile child who settles after a viral illness has transient synovitis - a diagnosis of exclusion.
learningPoint: An unwell, febrile child who will not weight-bear with raised inflammatory markers (Kocher criteria) has septic arthritis until proven otherwise.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Paediatric orthopaedics: the limping child

subtopic: Eponymous fractures of the wrist and hand
stem: Theme: eponymous fractures of the wrist and hand. For each description, select the single best-matching eponym. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Bennett fracture

options.label: B
options.text: Rolando fracture

options.label: C
options.text: Galeazzi fracture-dislocation

options.label: D
options.text: Monteggia fracture-dislocation

options.label: E
options.text: Colles fracture

options.label: F
options.text: Smith fracture

options.label: G
options.text: Barton fracture

options.label: H
options.text: Chauffeur (radial styloid) fracture

emiLeads.stem: An intra-articular two-part fracture of the base of the thumb metacarpal, in which abductor pollicis longus pulls the shaft proximally while the small volar-ulnar fragment stays reduced.
A fracture of the distal third of the radial shaft associated with disruption of the distal radio-ulnar joint.
An intra-articular fracture of the volar rim of the distal radius with volar subluxation of the carpus - a fracture-dislocation.
A distal radius fracture with volar displacement of the distal fragment, typically after a fall onto the back of a flexed wrist - a 'reverse Colles'.

explanation: A Bennett fracture is a two-part intra-articular fracture-subluxation of the thumb metacarpal base, destabilised by the pull of abductor pollicis longus. A Galeazzi injury is a distal radial shaft fracture with a disrupted DRUJ; the mirror-image Monteggia is a proximal ulna fracture with radial head dislocation. A Barton fracture is an intra-articular fracture-dislocation of the volar (or dorsal) rim of the distal radius. A Smith fracture is a volarly displaced distal radius fracture (reverse Colles).
learningPoint: Galeazzi = distal radial shaft fracture + DRUJ injury; Monteggia = proximal ulna fracture + radial head dislocation.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Hand and wrist: distal radius and carpal injuries

stem: Theme: collagen and connective-tissue proteins. For each description, select the single best-matching protein. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Type I collagen

options.label: B
options.text: Type II collagen

options.label: C
options.text: Type III collagen

options.label: D
options.text: Type IV collagen

options.label: E
options.text: Type X collagen

options.label: F
options.text: Fibrillin-1

options.label: G
options.text: Type IX collagen

options.label: H
options.text: Elastin

emiLeads.stem: The predominant collagen of bone, tendon and skin, which is qualitatively or quantitatively defective in osteogenesis imperfecta.
The predominant collagen of hyaline articular cartilage.
The principal collagen of the basement membrane, defective in Alport syndrome.
A glycoprotein component of the extracellular microfibril, mutated in Marfan syndrome.

explanation: Type I collagen ('bONE') is the main collagen of bone, tendon and skin and is defective in osteogenesis imperfecta. Type II ('carTWOlage') predominates in hyaline cartilage. Type IV is the basement-membrane collagen affected in Alport syndrome. Fibrillin-1 is not a collagen at all but the microfibrillar glycoprotein mutated in Marfan syndrome; including it tests whether the candidate separates collagens from other matrix proteins. Type X marks the hypertrophic zone of the physis.
learningPoint: Memory hooks: type I = bONE, type II = carTWOlage, type IV = basement membrane (Alport); fibrillin-1 (not a collagen) is mutated in Marfan syndrome.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Basic science: collagen and extracellular matrix

stem: Theme: pathological gait. For each description, select the single most likely gait pattern. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Trendelenburg gait

options.label: B
options.text: Antalgic gait

options.label: C
options.text: High-stepping (foot-drop) gait

options.label: D
options.text: Circumduction gait

options.label: E
options.text: Short-limb gait

options.label: F
options.text: Ataxic (broad-based) gait

options.label: G
options.text: Back-knee (genu recurvatum) gait

options.label: H
options.text: Festinating gait

emiLeads.stem: During single-leg stance on the affected side the pelvis drops to the opposite side because the hip abductors are weak.
To compensate for a weak quadriceps, for example after polio, the patient thrusts the knee into hyperextension during the stance phase.
Because of a common peroneal nerve palsy the patient lifts the hip and knee high to clear the toes and the foot slaps to the floor.
The patient spends as little time as possible bearing weight on the painful limb, shortening the stance phase on that side.

explanation: A Trendelenburg gait reflects hip abductor (gluteus medius / superior gluteal nerve) dysfunction, with the contralateral pelvis dropping in single-leg stance. A back-knee gait hyperextends the knee in stance to compensate for quadriceps weakness. A high-stepping gait with a foot slap is the hallmark of a foot drop from a common peroneal palsy. An antalgic gait simply shortens the painful stance phase.
learningPoint: A Trendelenburg gait reflects hip abductor weakness (gluteus medius / superior gluteal nerve), not pain.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Basic science: gait analysis

stem: A 40-year-old falls onto an outstretched hand and sustains a posterolateral elbow fracture-dislocation. CT confirms the classic 'terrible triad'. Which combination of injuries defines this pattern?

options.label: A
options.text: Radial head fracture, coronoid fracture and lateral collateral ligament disruption

options.label: B
options.text: Olecranon fracture, radial head fracture and medial collateral ligament disruption

options.label: C
options.text: Capitellum fracture, coronoid fracture and triceps avulsion

options.label: D
options.text: Radial neck fracture, olecranon fracture and lateral collateral ligament disruption

options.label: E
options.text: Coronoid fracture, medial epicondyle avulsion and radial head fracture

explanation: The terrible triad is a posterolateral rotatory dislocation of the elbow combined with a radial head fracture and a coronoid fracture, with disruption of the lateral collateral ligament complex (and often the medial collateral ligament). Stability is restored by reconstructing the lateral column: fix or replace the radial head, address the coronoid (especially the anteromedial facet), repair the LCL, and then mobilise early to avoid stiffness.
learningPoint: The terrible triad = elbow dislocation + radial head fracture + coronoid fracture; restore the radial head and lateral collateral ligament to regain a stable arc of motion.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Elbow trauma: complex instability

stem: A 45-year-old man feels a painful pop in the antecubital fossa while lifting a heavy box. He has weakness of supination more than flexion and a positive hook test. What is the most appropriate definitive management for this active patient?

options.label: A
options.text: Anatomic surgical reattachment of the distal biceps tendon to the radial tuberosity

options.label: B
options.text: Non-operative management with a sling and early mobilisation

options.label: C
options.text: Tenodesis of the distal biceps to brachialis

options.label: D
options.text: Reattachment of the tendon to the proximal ulna

options.label: E
options.text: Corticosteroid injection and physiotherapy

explanation: A complete distal biceps avulsion in an active patient is best treated by anatomic reattachment to the radial tuberosity (single- or double-incision technique), which restores supination strength and endurance more reliably than non-operative care. The hook test - the examiner cannot hook a finger under the intact tendon laterally - confirms a complete tear. Non-operative treatment is reserved for low-demand patients; key risks of repair are lateral antebrachial cutaneous nerve and PIN injury.
learningPoint: An absent hook test indicates a complete distal biceps rupture; active patients benefit from anatomic repair to the radial tuberosity to restore supination power.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Elbow: tendon injuries

stem: A 44-year-old office worker has lateral elbow pain reproduced by resisted wrist extension and gripping, with no neurological deficit. Which structure is primarily affected and what is the appropriate first-line management?

options.label: A
options.text: Origin of extensor carpi radialis brevis; activity modification and a period of non-operative treatment

options.label: B
options.text: Origin of the common flexor tendon; immediate surgical release

options.label: C
options.text: Posterior interosseous nerve; urgent decompression

options.label: D
options.text: Radial collateral ligament; reconstruction

options.label: E
options.text: Annular ligament; open repair

explanation: Lateral epicondylitis is an angiofibroblastic tendinosis of the extensor carpi radialis brevis origin. The large majority of cases settle with activity modification, eccentric loading exercises, a counterforce brace and time; surgery is reserved for symptoms refractory beyond 6-12 months. The main differential is radial tunnel syndrome, where pain is more distal over the mobile wad and there is no point tenderness at the epicondyle.
learningPoint: 'Tennis elbow' is an ECRB-origin tendinosis that is overwhelmingly self-limiting; first-line treatment is non-operative.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Elbow: lateral epicondylitis

stem: A 30-year-old sustains an isolated radial head fracture with 3 mm of displacement and no block to forearm rotation (Mason type II), with no associated injuries. What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.label: A
options.text: Early mobilisation, reserving operative fixation for a true mechanical block to rotation

options.label: B
options.text: Immediate radial head excision

options.label: C
options.text: Radial head arthroplasty

options.label: D
options.text: Cast immobilisation for six weeks

options.label: E
options.text: Open reduction and plating in all cases

explanation: A Mason II radial head fracture without a mechanical block is treated with early movement; aspirating the haemarthrosis with local anaesthetic can confirm that forearm rotation is genuinely unblocked. Open reduction and internal fixation is indicated when a displaced fracture blocks rotation but remains reconstructable. Arthroplasty is reserved for unreconstructable comminuted (Mason III) fractures, particularly with associated instability. Early acute excision is avoided where any longitudinal forearm instability is suspected.
learningPoint: A Mason II radial head fracture with no block to rotation is treated with early movement; a genuine mechanical block is the indication to operate.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Elbow trauma: radial head fractures

stem: A 14-year-old competitive gymnast has activity-related lateral elbow pain and loss of terminal extension. MRI shows an osteochondral lesion of the capitellum with a stable overlying cartilage cap. How is this best distinguished from Panner disease, and what is the initial management?

options.label: A
options.text: It is osteochondritis dissecans (older child, focal lesion that can destabilise); a stable lesion is managed with activity restriction

options.label: B
options.text: It is identical to Panner disease and always requires internal fixation

options.label: C
options.text: It represents normal ossification and needs no follow-up

options.label: D
options.text: It is a capitellar fracture requiring urgent open reduction

options.label: E
options.text: It is septic arthritis requiring washout

explanation: Capitellar osteochondritis dissecans affects adolescents (roughly 10-16 years) exposed to repetitive radiocapitellar loading, such as throwers and gymnasts; a stable lesion with an intact cartilage cap and no loose body is managed with rest and activity modification. Panner disease occurs in younger children (around 5-10 years), is a self-limiting osteochondrosis of the entire capitellar ossific nucleus, and recovers without surgery. An unstable OCD lesion with loose bodies or mechanical symptoms needs arthroscopic debridement, fixation or grafting.
learningPoint: Panner disease (age 5-10, benign, self-limiting) versus capitellar OCD (adolescent, can destabilise and form loose bodies) - age and lesion stability drive treatment.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Paediatric/sports elbow: osteochondral lesions

subtopic: High tibial osteotomy for medial OA
stem: A 48-year-old manual labourer has isolated medial compartment knee osteoarthritis with a varus mechanical axis, a stable knee and a good range of motion. Which procedure best preserves the joint and corrects the deformity?

options.label: A
options.text: Medial opening-wedge high tibial osteotomy to offload the medial compartment

options.label: B
options.text: Total knee arthroplasty

options.label: C
options.text: Patellofemoral arthroplasty

options.label: D
options.text: Arthroscopic washout and debridement

options.label: E
options.text: Knee arthrodesis

explanation: A young, active patient with isolated medial-compartment osteoarthritis, varus malalignment and a stable knee with good motion is a classic indication for a valgus-producing high tibial osteotomy (commonly a medial opening wedge), which shifts the mechanical axis laterally and preserves the native joint for a physically demanding lifestyle. Unicompartmental arthroplasty is an alternative in older or lower-demand patients. Arthroscopic debridement is not indicated for osteoarthritis alone.
learningPoint: Medial compartment osteoarthritis with varus malalignment in a young, active patient is a classic indication for a valgus high tibial osteotomy.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Knee: osteotomy and joint preservation

subtopic: Recurrent patellar instability and TT-TG distance
stem: A 19-year-old has recurrent lateral patellar dislocations. Imaging shows an elevated tibial tubercle-trochlear groove (TT-TG) distance of 22 mm and an incompetent medial patellofemoral ligament. Which statement best guides surgical planning?

options.label: A
options.text: A TT-TG distance above roughly 20 mm supports adding a tibial tubercle transfer to MPFL reconstruction

options.label: B
options.text: MPFL reconstruction is contraindicated

options.label: C
options.text: An isolated lateral release reliably prevents recurrence

options.label: D
options.text: The TT-TG distance is normal so no bony procedure is needed

options.label: E
options.text: Trochleoplasty is mandatory in every case

explanation: The medial patellofemoral ligament is the primary restraint to lateral patellar translation in early flexion and is reconstructed for recurrent instability. An elevated TT-TG distance (above approximately 20 mm) reflects a lateralised extensor mechanism and supports adding a tibial tubercle medialisation, with distalisation if there is patella alta. An isolated lateral release does not treat instability and can worsen it, and trochleoplasty is reserved for severe trochlear dysplasia.
learningPoint: Reconstruct the MPFL for recurrent patellar instability, and add a tibial tubercle osteotomy when the TT-TG distance is elevated (above ~20 mm).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Knee: patellofemoral instability

stem: A 60-year-old presents with an acutely hot, swollen, exquisitely painful knee and a fever. Aspiration yields turbid fluid. Which synovial-fluid finding most strongly indicates septic arthritis requiring urgent washout?

options.label: A
options.text: A white cell count typically above 50,000/mm3 with a high polymorph percentage and organisms on Gram stain or culture

options.label: B
options.text: Negatively birefringent crystals

options.label: C
options.text: A bloody aspirate with fat globules

options.label: D
options.text: A white cell count of 1,500/mm3

options.label: E
options.text: Positively birefringent crystals

explanation: Septic arthritis classically produces a high synovial white cell count (often above 50,000/mm3, neutrophil-predominant) with a positive Gram stain or culture, and is treated by urgent arthroscopic or open washout plus intravenous antibiotics after samples are taken. Negatively birefringent needle-shaped crystals indicate gout, and positively birefringent rhomboid crystals indicate pseudogout (CPPD); crystal arthropathy can mimic sepsis, so infection must be excluded first.
learningPoint: A hot knee with a synovial white cell count above 50,000/mm3 and organisms on Gram stain is septic until proven otherwise - urgent washout, not antibiotics alone.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Infection: septic arthritis

stem: A 24-year-old recreational footballer with a symptomatically unstable ACL-deficient knee elects reconstruction. Regarding graft choice, which statement is most accurate?

options.label: A
options.text: Both hamstring and bone-patellar tendon-bone autografts give good stability; BPTB carries more anterior knee pain while hamstring carries a higher risk of residual laxity

options.label: B
options.text: Allograft is the gold standard in young athletes because of a lower re-rupture rate

options.label: C
options.text: Synthetic grafts are now first-line

options.label: D
options.text: Graft choice has no effect on donor-site morbidity

options.label: E
options.text: The lateral collateral ligament is the usual graft source

explanation: Autograft remains the standard for ACL reconstruction in young, active patients. Bone-patellar tendon-bone gives robust bone-to-bone healing but more anterior knee pain and kneeling discomfort, whereas a hamstring autograft has less harvest-site pain but a slightly higher rate of residual laxity and some loss of deep-flexion strength. Allograft has a higher re-rupture rate in young high-demand patients, and historical synthetic grafts performed poorly.
learningPoint: ACL autograft choice is a trade-off: BPTB risks anterior knee pain, hamstring risks residual laxity - individualise to the patient.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Sports medicine: ACL reconstruction

subtopic: Localising rotator cuff tears by lag signs
stem: A 58-year-old has shoulder weakness after a fall. On examination there is a positive external rotation lag sign and weakness of resisted external rotation with the arm at the side. Which rotator cuff tendon is most likely torn?

options.label: A
options.text: Infraspinatus

options.label: B
options.text: Subscapularis

options.label: C
options.text: Supraspinatus only

options.label: D
options.text: Teres major

options.label: E
options.text: Pectoralis major

explanation: Weakness of external rotation with the arm at the side and an external rotation lag sign localise to infraspinatus (with teres minor). Subscapularis tears instead produce a positive lift-off or belly-press sign and increased passive external rotation. An isolated supraspinatus tear gives a painful arc and weak abduction (empty-can or drop-arm signs). Teres major is not part of the rotator cuff.
learningPoint: External rotation weakness and an ER lag sign localise to infraspinatus; lift-off/belly-press abnormalities localise to subscapularis.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Shoulder: rotator cuff examination

subtopic: Glenoid bone loss and recurrent instability
stem: A 23-year-old contact athlete has recurrent anterior shoulder dislocations. CT shows approximately 25% anteroinferior glenoid bone loss with an engaging Hill-Sachs lesion. Which surgical strategy is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: A bone-block procedure (for example a Latarjet coracoid transfer) to address the critical glenoid bone loss

options.label: B
options.text: Isolated arthroscopic Bankart repair

options.label: C
options.text: Rotator interval closure alone

options.label: D
options.text: Capsular plication alone

options.label: E
options.text: Non-operative rehabilitation only

explanation: Critical glenoid bone loss - commonly cited above about 20-25%, and formalised by the glenoid-track concept for engaging or 'off-track' Hill-Sachs lesions - predicts a high failure rate for an isolated soft-tissue Bankart repair. A bony augmentation such as the Latarjet procedure restores the articular arc and adds the dynamic sling of the conjoint tendon (the 'triple effect'). An off-track Hill-Sachs lesion may additionally require a remplissage.
learningPoint: With critical glenoid bone loss (above ~20-25%) or an off-track Hill-Sachs lesion, an isolated Bankart repair fails often - choose a bony procedure such as Latarjet.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Shoulder: instability and bone loss

stem: A 50-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes has five months of a painful, stiff shoulder. Active and passive external rotation are equally and severely limited, and radiographs are normal. Which statement about the natural history is most accurate?

options.label: A
options.text: It is typically self-limiting over months to a few years through 'freezing', 'frozen' and 'thawing' phases

options.label: B
options.text: It always requires early surgical release

options.label: C
options.text: It never recovers without manipulation

options.label: D
options.text: It is caused by a full-thickness rotator cuff tear

options.label: E
options.text: Radiographs usually show glenohumeral joint-space loss

explanation: Adhesive capsulitis classically passes through a painful freezing phase, a stiff frozen phase and a recovery (thawing) phase over roughly one to three years, and most cases resolve with analgesia, physiotherapy and intra-articular steroid or hydrodilatation; diabetics tend to have more refractory and bilateral disease. Equal loss of active and passive external rotation with normal radiographs distinguishes it from osteoarthritis and from a cuff tear.
learningPoint: Frozen shoulder is usually self-limiting through freezing/frozen/thawing phases; the hallmark is global loss of passive external rotation with normal radiographs.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Shoulder: adhesive capsulitis

stem: A 40-year-old with acute low back pain develops bilateral leg pain, difficulty initiating micturition and reduced sensation when wiping. Which is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Urgent MRI of the lumbosacral spine and emergency surgical decompression if cauda equina compression is confirmed

options.label: B
options.text: Routine outpatient physiotherapy referral

options.label: C
options.text: A trial of six weeks of analgesia before any imaging

options.label: D
options.text: Plain lumbar radiographs and reassurance

options.label: E
options.text: A lumbar epidural steroid injection

explanation: Suspected cauda equina syndrome - saddle sensory change, bladder or bowel dysfunction and bilateral sciatica - requires emergency MRI and urgent surgical decompression, because delay risks irreversible sphincter dysfunction. Painless urinary retention with overflow is a late, ominous sign. Always document perianal sensation, anal tone and the post-void residual volume.
learningPoint: Bladder dysfunction + saddle anaesthesia + bilateral sciatica = cauda equina syndrome - emergency MRI and decompression; do not wait.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Spine: cauda equina syndrome

stem: A 65-year-old reports deteriorating balance, clumsy hands and difficulty doing up buttons. Examination shows a positive Hoffmann's sign, hyper-reflexia and an upgoing plantar response. What is the most likely diagnosis and the definitive management for progressive disease?

options.label: A
options.text: Degenerative cervical myelopathy; surgical decompression to halt neurological decline

options.label: B
options.text: Carpal tunnel syndrome; carpal tunnel release

options.label: C
options.text: Peripheral neuropathy; observation

options.label: D
options.text: Cubital tunnel syndrome; ulnar nerve transposition

options.label: E
options.text: Lumbar canal stenosis; lumbar laminectomy

explanation: Upper-motor-neuron signs (hyper-reflexia, a positive Hoffmann's sign, an upgoing plantar response, clonus) together with gait imbalance and loss of fine hand dexterity indicate degenerative cervical myelopathy. Surgical decompression - anterior, posterior, or combined - is the mainstay for progressive or moderate-to-severe disease and aims to prevent further deterioration; severity is graded with the modified Japanese Orthopaedic Association (mJOA) score.
learningPoint: Clumsy hands + gait imbalance + UMN signs (Hoffmann, hyper-reflexia) = degenerative cervical myelopathy; progressive disease is decompressed surgically.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Spine: cervical myelopathy

stem: A teenage fast bowler has activity-related low back pain. Imaging shows a bilateral pars interarticularis defect at L5 with a Meyerding grade I slip of L5 on S1. Which statement is most accurate?

options.label: A
options.text: This is a spondylolytic (isthmic) spondylolisthesis; most low-grade slips are managed non-operatively with activity modification and physiotherapy

options.label: B
options.text: Meyerding grade I means more than 75% slip

options.label: C
options.text: The defect lies in the pedicle

options.label: D
options.text: Immediate fusion is required for all grade I slips

options.label: E
options.text: The condition occurs only in the elderly

explanation: A pars interarticularis defect (spondylolysis) can allow a vertebra to slip forward, producing an isthmic spondylolisthesis; it is common in adolescent athletes who repeatedly hyperextend the spine, such as cricket fast bowlers and gymnasts. The Meyerding system grades the slip in quartiles (grade I is under 25%). Low-grade slips are managed with activity modification, physiotherapy and sometimes bracing; surgery is reserved for high-grade, progressive or refractory disease, or neurological compromise.
learningPoint: Meyerding grades spondylolisthesis in 25% quartiles (grade I is under 25%); low-grade isthmic slips in adolescents are usually managed non-operatively.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Spine: spondylolysis and spondylolisthesis

stem: A 55-year-old has painful first MTP joint osteoarthritis (hallux rigidus) with dorsal osteophytes, pain mainly at the extremes of dorsiflexion and a reasonably preserved mid-range arc. Which procedure is most appropriate for this early-to-moderate, dorsal-impingement pattern?

options.label: A
options.text: Dorsal cheilectomy

options.label: B
options.text: First MTP joint arthrodesis

options.label: C
options.text: Keller excision arthroplasty

options.label: D
options.text: Total ankle replacement

options.label: E
options.text: Triple fusion

explanation: A dorsal cheilectomy - removing the dorsal osteophyte and the dorsal third of the metatarsal head - relieves dorsal impingement in early-to-moderate hallux rigidus where joint space is reasonably preserved. First MTP arthrodesis is the gold-standard for advanced, global joint arthritis. Keller excision arthroplasty is reserved for low-demand elderly patients. Severity can be graded with the Coughlin and Shurnas classification.
learningPoint: Early hallux rigidus with dorsal impingement is treated by cheilectomy; advanced global joint arthritis is best treated by first MTP arthrodesis.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Foot and ankle: hallux rigidus

stem: A 40-year-old badminton player feels a sudden blow to the back of the ankle. There is a palpable gap in the tendo-Achilles and a positive Simmonds-Thompson (calf-squeeze) test. Which statement about management is most accurate?

options.label: A
options.text: Both functional non-operative rehabilitation and surgical repair are accepted; surgery lowers re-rupture but adds wound and sural-nerve risks, and early functional rehabilitation improves outcomes either way

options.label: B
options.text: Surgery is mandatory in all patients

options.label: C
options.text: It should be treated in a below-knee cast in full dorsiflexion

options.label: D
options.text: A positive calf-squeeze test excludes a rupture

options.label: E
options.text: The sural nerve is never at risk during repair

explanation: An acute Achilles rupture can be managed either by functional bracing in equinus with accelerated rehabilitation or by surgical repair; modern functional non-operative protocols achieve re-rupture rates approaching those of surgery while avoiding wound complications and sural-nerve injury. A positive Simmonds-Thompson test (loss of passive plantarflexion on squeezing the calf) confirms the rupture, and the limb is immobilised in equinus (plantarflexion), not dorsiflexion.
learningPoint: A positive Simmonds-Thompson test confirms Achilles rupture; both surgical and functional non-operative pathways are valid, surgery lowering re-rupture at the cost of wound/sural-nerve risk.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Foot and ankle: Achilles tendon rupture

stem: A patient has midfoot pain and swelling after a twisting fall. There is plantar bruising of the midfoot and a small bony fragment in the first web space on the radiograph (the 'fleck sign'). What is the diagnosis and the key next step?

options.label: A
options.text: A Lisfranc (tarsometatarsal) injury; obtain weight-bearing or stress views and treat unstable injuries with anatomic reduction and stable fixation

options.label: B
options.text: A simple ankle sprain; discharge with an elastic support

options.label: C
options.text: A calcaneal fracture; CT and non-weight-bearing

options.label: D
options.text: A Jones fracture; intramedullary screw fixation

options.label: E
options.text: A talar neck fracture; urgent reduction

explanation: Plantar midfoot ecchymosis with a 'fleck sign' (an avulsion at the Lisfranc ligament between the medial cuneiform and the second metatarsal base) signals a tarsometatarsal injury. Subtle instability is easily missed on non-weight-bearing films, so weight-bearing or stress radiographs (or CT) are essential. Unstable injuries are treated with anatomic open reduction and rigid internal fixation, with primary fusion considered for selected severe patterns.
learningPoint: Plantar midfoot bruising and a 'fleck sign' indicate a Lisfranc injury; non-weight-bearing films can look normal, so use weight-bearing/stress views and fix unstable injuries anatomically.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Foot and ankle: Lisfranc injuries

subtopic: Scaphoid blood supply and AVN risk
stem: A 22-year-old falls onto an outstretched hand and has anatomical snuffbox tenderness. Which statement about the scaphoid blood supply explains the risk of proximal pole avascular necrosis?

options.label: A
options.text: The scaphoid is supplied mainly by retrograde flow from dorsal branches of the radial artery entering distally, so proximal pole fractures risk avascular necrosis and non-union

options.label: B
options.text: The proximal pole has the richest direct blood supply

options.label: C
options.text: The scaphoid is supplied entirely by the ulnar artery

options.label: D
options.text: Blood enters at the proximal pole and flows distally

options.label: E
options.text: The scaphoid has no intra-osseous blood supply

explanation: The dorsal carpal branch of the radial artery enters the scaphoid distally, at and around the dorsal ridge, and perfuses the proximal pole in a retrograde direction. Consequently proximal pole fractures and displaced waist fractures are prone to avascular necrosis and non-union. A clinically suspected scaphoid fracture with normal initial radiographs is immobilised and re-imaged (repeat films, MRI or CT) because early films are frequently negative.
learningPoint: The scaphoid's retrograde blood supply (distal entry from the radial artery) means proximal pole fractures are at high risk of avascular necrosis and non-union.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Hand and wrist: scaphoid fractures

stem: A 62-year-old man has a firm cord in the palm tethering the ring finger, with a 40-degree metacarpophalangeal joint flexion contracture and a positive tabletop test. Which statement best guides management?

options.label: A
options.text: Intervention is generally indicated for an MCP contracture of about 30 degrees or more, or for any significant PIP contracture, with PIP contractures being harder to correct

options.label: B
options.text: Surgery should be offered as soon as a painless nodule appears

options.label: C
options.text: Dupuytren disease primarily affects the extensor tendons

options.label: D
options.text: The condition is cured by a single corticosteroid injection

options.label: E
options.text: A positive tabletop test indicates carpal tunnel syndrome

explanation: Dupuytren disease is a fibroproliferative disorder of the palmar fascia that forms nodules and cords. Function-limiting contractures are treated - typically a metacarpophalangeal joint contracture of around 30 degrees or more, or any fixed proximal interphalangeal joint contracture, which corrects less reliably and recurs more often. Options include needle aponeurotomy, collagenase injection and open fasciectomy. Isolated painless nodules are simply observed.
learningPoint: Treat Dupuytren contractures, not nodules - an MCP contracture of about 30 degrees or a fixed PIP contracture (a positive tabletop test) is the usual threshold; PIP contractures correct less reliably.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Hand: Dupuytren disease

stem: After closed reduction of a dorsally displaced distal radius fracture in an active adult, which set of parameters most suggests an acceptable reduction that can be managed non-operatively?

options.label: A
options.text: Restored radial height, radial inclination around 22 degrees, dorsal tilt corrected to neutral or slight volar tilt, and a congruent articular surface

options.label: B
options.text: Dorsal tilt of 30 degrees, radial shortening of 8 mm and a 4 mm intra-articular step

options.label: C
options.text: Volar tilt of 40 degrees with carpal subluxation

options.label: D
options.text: Any position, because distal radius alignment does not affect outcome

options.label: E
options.text: Complete loss of radial inclination

explanation: Broadly accepted parameters for distal radius alignment are a radial inclination of about 22 degrees (loss of less than about 5 degrees), radial length within roughly 2-3 mm of the contralateral side, dorsal tilt corrected to neutral or slightly volar (and certainly within about 5-10 degrees), and an articular step of less than 2 mm. Failure to hold these, particularly in a younger or more active patient, favours fixation, usually with a volar locking plate.
learningPoint: An acceptable distal radius reduction restores radial height and inclination (~22 degrees) and corrects dorsal tilt to neutral with an articular step under 2 mm; loss of these favours fixation.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Wrist: distal radius fractures

stem: A 12-year-old has a stable (able to weight-bear) slipped upper femoral epiphysis. What is the most widely accepted surgical management?

options.label: A
options.text: In-situ fixation with a single cannulated screw, avoiding forceful reduction

options.label: B
options.text: Open anatomical reduction and plate fixation

options.label: C
options.text: Closed manipulation under anaesthetic and a hip spica

options.label: D
options.text: Observation until skeletal maturity

options.label: E
options.text: Immediate total hip replacement

explanation: A stable slipped upper femoral epiphysis is treated by in-situ percutaneous single-screw fixation to prevent further slippage; forceful reduction sharply increases the risk of avascular necrosis. Prophylactic fixation of the other hip is considered in higher-risk groups (younger children, open triradiate cartilage, or an underlying endocrinopathy). Unstable slips carry a much higher avascular necrosis rate. Disruption of Klein's line on the AP film aids diagnosis.
learningPoint: A SUFE is fixed in situ with a single cannulated screw; avoid forceful reduction because of the high risk of avascular necrosis.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Paediatrics: slipped upper femoral epiphysis

stem: A 6-year-old falls and has a completely displaced extension-type supracondylar humeral fracture (Gartland III). The hand is well perfused but there is weakness of the 'OK sign' (anterior interosseous nerve). What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Urgent closed reduction and percutaneous K-wire fixation, with spontaneous recovery of the nerve palsy expected in most cases

options.label: B
options.text: A long-arm cast in full flexion and discharge home

options.label: C
options.text: Immediate surgical exploration of the anterior interosseous nerve

options.label: D
options.text: Overnight observation before any intervention

options.label: E
options.text: A below-elbow backslab and routine clinic follow-up

explanation: A Gartland III supracondylar fracture is treated with urgent closed reduction and percutaneous K-wire fixation. An associated anterior interosseous nerve (or other) traction neurapraxia usually recovers spontaneously over weeks to months and is not by itself an indication for nerve exploration. After reduction, a pink but pulseless hand that remains well perfused can be observed, whereas a white, poorly perfused pulseless hand needs urgent vascular exploration; watch closely for compartment syndrome.
learningPoint: Displaced supracondylar fractures (Gartland III) need urgent reduction and K-wiring; an associated AIN palsy typically recovers spontaneously and does not mandate exploration.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Paediatric trauma: supracondylar humeral fractures

stem: A neonate has idiopathic congenital talipes equinovarus. Using the Ponseti method, in what sequence are the components of the deformity corrected, and which is corrected last?

options.label: A
options.text: Cavus, then adductus and varus, with equinus corrected last (often needing a percutaneous Achilles tenotomy)

options.label: B
options.text: Equinus first, then varus, with cavus last

options.label: C
options.text: All components are corrected simultaneously by open surgery

options.label: D
options.text: Adductus is corrected last by tendon transfer

options.label: E
options.text: The deformity self-corrects without intervention

explanation: Ponseti correction follows the sequence summarised by 'CAVE': first the cavus is corrected by supinating and aligning the forefoot with the hindfoot, then the adductus and varus are corrected with serial casts using counter-pressure on the lateral talar head, and the equinus is corrected last - usually requiring a percutaneous tendo-Achilles tenotomy. A foot-abduction orthosis (boots and bar) is then worn to prevent relapse, which is the commonest cause of failure.
learningPoint: Ponseti correction follows CAVE - cavus, adductus, varus, then equinus last (commonly via Achilles tenotomy) - with boots-and-bar bracing to prevent relapse.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Paediatrics: congenital talipes equinovarus

stem: A patient with a large-head metal-on-metal total hip replacement presents with new groin pain and a soft-tissue mass on imaging. Blood tests show raised cobalt and chromium ion levels. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.label: A
options.text: An adverse reaction to metal debris (pseudotumour / ALVAL) from the metal-on-metal bearing

options.label: B
options.text: Deep periprosthetic infection only

options.label: C
options.text: Aseptic loosening of a cemented stem unrelated to the bearing

options.label: D
options.text: Stress shielding

options.label: E
options.text: Trochanteric bursitis

explanation: Metal-on-metal bearings can shed metal wear debris that provokes an adverse reaction to metal debris - a spectrum including pseudotumour, aseptic lymphocyte-dominated vasculitis-associated lesion (ALVAL) and soft-tissue necrosis - accompanied by raised serum cobalt and chromium ions. Assessment uses cross-sectional imaging (metal-artefact-reduction MRI or ultrasound), serial ion levels and an infection screen; symptomatic disease or rising ions usually warrants revision, after excluding infection.
learningPoint: Groin pain + a soft-tissue mass + raised cobalt/chromium ions around a metal-on-metal hip indicates an adverse reaction to metal debris (pseudotumour) and usually warrants revision.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Adult reconstruction: bearing surfaces and metal-on-metal

subtopic: Femoral neck fracture in the young patient
stem: A 35-year-old sustains a displaced intracapsular femoral neck fracture in a high-energy road traffic collision. What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Urgent anatomical reduction and internal fixation to preserve the native femoral head

options.label: B
options.text: Cemented hemiarthroplasty

options.label: C
options.text: Total hip replacement

options.label: D
options.text: Non-weight-bearing mobilisation without surgery

options.label: E
options.text: Girdlestone excision arthroplasty

explanation: In a young patient a displaced intracapsular femoral neck fracture is a surgical emergency for head preservation: urgent anatomical reduction (closed or open) and internal fixation, accepting the risks of avascular necrosis and non-union. The quality of reduction is the key modifiable factor for avascular necrosis, and capsular decompression may help. Arthroplasty is reserved for older or physiologically older patients.
learningPoint: A displaced intracapsular hip fracture in a young patient is treated by urgent anatomical reduction and fixation to preserve the head, not by arthroplasty.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Trauma: femoral neck fractures in the young

subtopic: Initial management of an open fracture
stem: A patient arrives with an open tibial fracture and a 6 cm wound. According to current open-fracture principles, which initial step is most important alongside resuscitation?

options.label: A
options.text: Early intravenous antibiotics, removal of gross contamination, wound photography and a saline-soaked dressing, with timely combined orthoplastic debridement

options.label: B
options.text: Immediate bedside scrubbing and primary closure in the emergency department

options.label: C
options.text: Delaying antibiotics until the operating theatre

options.label: D
options.text: Leaving the wound exposed without any dressing

options.label: E
options.text: Reduction without any antibiotic cover

explanation: Modern open-fracture care (along BOAST/BAPRAS principles) involves prompt intravenous antibiotics and tetanus cover, a neurovascular assessment, removal of gross contamination, a single clinical photograph and a saline-soaked dressing, and splintage, followed by formal debridement on a planned combined orthoplastic list. Immediate bedside scrubbing or wound closure is discouraged, and the definitive Gustilo-Anderson grade is best assigned at the time of debridement.
learningPoint: Open fractures need early IV antibiotics, a photograph and saline-soaked dressing, and splintage, with planned orthoplastic debridement - not bedside scrubbing or immediate closure.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Trauma: open fracture management

stem: A patient with a tibial shaft fracture has pain out of proportion to the injury, worsened by passive stretch of the toes. Which statement about acute compartment syndrome is most accurate?

options.label: A
options.text: It is primarily a clinical diagnosis; a delta pressure (diastolic minus compartment pressure) below about 30 mmHg supports urgent fasciotomy

options.label: B
options.text: Loss of distal pulses is an early and reliable sign

options.label: C
options.text: Compartment pressures must exceed 80 mmHg before fasciotomy is justified

options.label: D
options.text: Pain on passive stretch is a late and unreliable sign

options.label: E
options.text: The treatment is elevation well above the heart and observation

explanation: Acute compartment syndrome is mainly a clinical diagnosis: escalating pain, pain on passive stretch of the muscles in the compartment, and tense compartments, with rapidly rising analgesic requirements. Pulses are typically preserved until very late. When pressures are measured, a delta pressure (diastolic blood pressure minus compartment pressure) below about 30 mmHg supports emergency fasciotomy. The limb is kept at heart level, constricting dressings are removed, and the compartments are decompressed urgently.
learningPoint: Acute compartment syndrome is a clinical diagnosis (pain on passive stretch, escalating analgesia); a delta pressure under 30 mmHg supports urgent fasciotomy, and pulses stay present until late.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Trauma: acute compartment syndrome

stem: Which cell is primarily responsible for bone resorption?

options.label: A
options.text: Osteoclast

options.label: B
options.text: Osteoblast

options.label: C
options.text: Osteocyte

options.label: D
options.text: Chondrocyte

options.label: E
options.text: Fibroblast

explanation: Osteoclasts are large multinucleated cells of haematopoietic (monocyte-macrophage) lineage that resorb bone through a ruffled border, secreting hydrogen ions and cathepsin K into a sealed acidic compartment. Osteoblasts lay down osteoid, and osteocytes are embedded mechanosensors. The balance of osteoclast activity is governed by the RANK / RANKL / osteoprotegerin signalling system.
learningPoint: Osteoclasts resorb bone and osteoblasts build it; their balance is controlled by the RANK/RANKL/OPG system.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Basic science: bone biology

stem: Which nerve is most commonly injured in association with a surgical neck of humerus fracture or an anterior shoulder dislocation, causing weak abduction and numbness over the lateral shoulder (the 'regimental badge' area)?

options.label: A
options.text: Axillary nerve

options.label: B
options.text: Radial nerve

options.label: C
options.text: Musculocutaneous nerve

options.label: D
options.text: Ulnar nerve

options.label: E
options.text: Median nerve

explanation: The axillary nerve (C5-6) winds around the surgical neck of the humerus and is therefore vulnerable in proximal humeral fractures and anterior glenohumeral dislocations. It supplies deltoid (a key abductor) and teres minor, and carries sensation over the 'regimental badge' area of the lateral shoulder. Its function should be documented both before and after any reduction.
learningPoint: The axillary nerve is at risk with shoulder dislocations and surgical neck fractures - always test deltoid power and regimental-badge sensation.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Anatomy: brachial plexus and shoulder

subtopic: Primary restraint to anterior tibial translation
stem: Which structure is the primary restraint to anterior translation of the tibia on the femur?

options.label: A
options.text: Anterior cruciate ligament

options.label: B
options.text: Posterior cruciate ligament

options.label: C
options.text: Medial collateral ligament

options.label: D
options.text: Lateral collateral ligament

options.label: E
options.text: Patellar tendon

explanation: The anterior cruciate ligament is the primary restraint to anterior translation of the tibia on the femur, assessed clinically with the Lachman and anterior drawer tests. The posterior cruciate ligament resists posterior translation, and the collateral ligaments resist valgus and varus angulation. The ACL's anteromedial and posterolateral bundles tension reciprocally through the flexion arc.
learningPoint: The ACL is the primary restraint to anterior tibial translation, tested with the Lachman test.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Knee: ligamentous anatomy and biomechanics

stem: Theme: bone tumours. For each clinical and radiological description, select the single most likely diagnosis. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Osteosarcoma

options.label: B
options.text: Ewing sarcoma

options.label: C
options.text: Giant cell tumour

options.label: D
options.text: Osteochondroma

options.label: E
options.text: Enchondroma

options.label: F
options.text: Osteoid osteoma

options.label: G
options.text: Chondrosarcoma

options.label: H
options.text: Aneurysmal bone cyst

emiLeads.stem: An adolescent has a painful metaphyseal mass around the knee; the radiograph shows an aggressive lesion with a 'sunburst' pattern and a Codman triangle, with new bone in the adjacent soft tissues.
A child has a painful diaphyseal long-bone lesion with a lamellated 'onion-skin' periosteal reaction; he is febrile with raised inflammatory markers, mimicking infection.
A skeletally mature young adult has an eccentric lytic lesion abutting the articular surface of the distal radius, with a 'soap-bubble' appearance and no marginal sclerosis.
A teenager has night pain that is dramatically relieved by NSAIDs; imaging shows a small (under 1.5 cm) lucent nidus surrounded by dense reactive sclerosis.

explanation: Osteosarcoma is the classic aggressive metaphyseal lesion around the knee in adolescents, with a sunburst spiculated pattern and a Codman triangle. Ewing sarcoma is a diaphyseal small-round-blue-cell tumour with an onion-skin periosteal reaction that can mimic osteomyelitis and is associated with the t(11;22) translocation. A giant cell tumour is an epiphyseal/metaphyseal 'soap-bubble' lytic lesion in skeletally mature patients. An osteoid osteoma causes night pain strikingly relieved by NSAIDs and shows a small nidus with surrounding sclerosis.
learningPoint: Night pain relieved by NSAIDs + a small nidus = osteoid osteoma; a metaphyseal 'sunburst' around the knee in a teenager = osteosarcoma.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Orthopaedic oncology: bone tumours

subtopic: Nerve injuries around the shoulder girdle
stem: Theme: nerve injuries around the shoulder girdle. For each presentation, select the single most likely nerve involved. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Axillary nerve

options.label: B
options.text: Suprascapular nerve

options.label: C
options.text: Long thoracic nerve

options.label: D
options.text: Spinal accessory nerve

options.label: E
options.text: Musculocutaneous nerve

options.label: F
options.text: Upper trunk of the brachial plexus

options.label: G
options.text: Lower trunk of the brachial plexus

options.label: H
options.text: Dorsal scapular nerve

emiLeads.stem: After an anterior shoulder dislocation a patient has weak abduction and numbness over the lateral deltoid (the 'regimental badge' area).
Following an axillary lymph-node clearance the patient develops medial winging of the scapula, made worse by pushing against a wall.
After a lymph-node biopsy in the posterior triangle of the neck the patient cannot shrug the shoulder and has lateral scapular winging worse on abduction.
A volleyball player with a ganglion at the spinoglenoid notch has isolated weakness of external rotation with infraspinatus wasting and no sensory loss.

explanation: The axillary nerve (deltoid and teres minor, regimental-badge sensation) is injured in anterior dislocations. The long thoracic nerve supplies serratus anterior, so its palsy causes medial scapular winging worse on forward pushing. The spinal accessory nerve supplies trapezius, so its injury (often iatrogenic in the posterior triangle) causes inability to shrug and lateral winging. A spinoglenoid-notch lesion of the suprascapular nerve affects infraspinatus alone, giving isolated external-rotation weakness.
learningPoint: Medial scapular winging = long thoracic nerve (serratus anterior); lateral winging with inability to shrug = spinal accessory nerve (trapezius).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Shoulder: neurological injuries

stem: Theme: orthopaedic biomaterials. For each property, select the single best-matching material. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Cobalt-chromium alloy

options.label: B
options.text: Titanium alloy (Ti-6Al-4V)

options.label: C
options.text: Ultra-high-molecular-weight polyethylene (UHMWPE)

options.label: D
options.text: Polymethylmethacrylate (PMMA bone cement)

options.label: E
options.text: Ceramic (alumina/zirconia)

options.label: F
options.text: Stainless steel (316L)

options.label: G
options.text: Tantalum

options.label: H
options.text: Hydroxyapatite

emiLeads.stem: An alloy whose elastic modulus is closest to that of bone, with excellent osseointegration and corrosion resistance, but which performs poorly as an articulating bearing surface.
A polymer used as the bearing surface on the acetabular or tibial side, whose wear debris is the principal driver of periprosthetic osteolysis and which is highly cross-linked to reduce wear.
A material that does not bond chemically to bone but provides immediate fixation by mechanical interlock, undergoing an exothermic polymerisation as it sets.
A very hard, scratch-resistant bearing with the lowest wear rates, whose main drawback is brittleness and a small risk of fracture in vivo.

explanation: Titanium alloy has an elastic modulus closer to bone than other metals (reducing stress shielding), osseointegrates well and resists corrosion, but is a poor bearing surface. UHMWPE is the workhorse bearing polymer; its wear particles drive osteolysis, which highly cross-linked polyethylene mitigates. PMMA bone cement is a grout that interlocks mechanically rather than bonding chemically and cures exothermically. Ceramic bearings have the lowest wear but are brittle and can fracture.
learningPoint: Titanium's low elastic modulus (near bone) reduces stress shielding and osseointegrates but makes a poor bearing; ceramics wear the least but can fracture.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Basic science: biomaterials and bearing surfaces

stem: Theme: common hand and wrist soft-tissue conditions. For each presentation, select the single most likely diagnosis. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Trigger finger (stenosing flexor tenosynovitis)

options.label: B
options.text: De Quervain's tenosynovitis

options.label: C
options.text: Carpal tunnel syndrome

options.label: D
options.text: First carpometacarpal (thumb basal) joint osteoarthritis

options.label: E
options.text: Mallet finger

options.label: F
options.text: Ganglion cyst

options.label: G
options.text: Dupuytren's disease

options.label: H
options.text: Boutonniere deformity

emiLeads.stem: A new mother has radial-sided wrist pain localised over the first dorsal compartment, with a positive Finkelstein test.
A diabetic notices a finger catching and locking in flexion, with a tender palpable nodule at the A1 pulley.
After a ball strikes the end of the finger the patient cannot actively extend the distal interphalangeal joint, which rests in flexion, and there is no fracture.
An older woman has pain at the base of the thumb with a positive grind test and squaring of the thumb base.

explanation: De Quervain's tenosynovitis affects the first dorsal compartment (abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis) and gives a positive Finkelstein test. Trigger finger is stenosis at the A1 pulley with a catching nodule. A mallet finger is disruption of the terminal extensor mechanism at the DIP joint, which then droops. Thumb basal (CMC) joint osteoarthritis produces a positive grind test and squaring of the thumb base.
learningPoint: A positive Finkelstein test = De Quervain's (first dorsal compartment); a positive grind test = thumb basal joint osteoarthritis.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Hand: tendon and degenerative conditions

stem: Theme: ankle injuries. For each description, select the single best-matching diagnosis or classification. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Weber A (infrasyndesmotic) fibular fracture

options.label: B
options.text: Weber B (transsyndesmotic) fibular fracture

options.label: C
options.text: Weber C (suprasyndesmotic) fibular fracture

options.label: D
options.text: Maisonneuve fracture

options.label: E
options.text: Isolated medial malleolus fracture

options.label: F
options.text: Pilon fracture

options.label: G
options.text: Stable lateral ligament sprain

options.label: H
options.text: Talar neck fracture

emiLeads.stem: A rotational ankle injury presents with medial-sided pain and a widened mortise; a fracture is then found at the proximal fibula near the knee.
A fibular fracture lies above the level of the syndesmosis, which is disrupted, with a tendency to mortise instability.
A high-energy axial load drives the talus into the distal tibial articular surface, producing a comminuted intra-articular distal tibial fracture.
An avulsion-type fibular fracture lies below the level of the ankle joint line, with the syndesmosis intact and the mortise stable.

explanation: The Weber classification describes the level of the fibular fracture relative to the syndesmosis: type A (below) is usually stable, type B is at the level, and type C (above) implies syndesmotic disruption and potential instability. A Maisonneuve injury is a proximal fibular fracture combined with a medial/syndesmotic injury, easily missed unless the proximal fibula is palpated. A pilon fracture is an axial-load intra-articular fracture of the distal tibia.
learningPoint: Always palpate the proximal fibula in an ankle injury - a Maisonneuve fracture is easily missed; the Weber level predicts syndesmotic disruption and stability.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Foot and ankle trauma: malleolar fractures

stem: Theme: atraumatic osteonecrosis of the femoral head. For each scenario, select the single most likely contributing cause. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Long-term corticosteroid therapy

options.label: B
options.text: Excess alcohol intake

options.label: C
options.text: Sickle cell disease

options.label: D
options.text: Dysbarism (decompression sickness)

options.label: E
options.text: Gaucher's disease

options.label: F
options.text: Systemic lupus erythematosus

options.label: G
options.text: Idiopathic

options.label: H
options.text: Radiotherapy

emiLeads.stem: A patient on several months of high-dose prednisolone for a flare of inflammatory bowel disease develops bilateral groin pain, with subchondral femoral head changes on MRI.
A young Afro-Caribbean patient with recurrent painful crises and a haemoglobinopathy develops femoral head collapse.
A commercial diver with a history of rapid ascents develops osteonecrosis affecting both the hip and the shoulder.
A patient with hepatosplenomegaly and a lysosomal storage disorder (glucocerebrosidase deficiency) develops femoral head osteonecrosis.

explanation: Corticosteroid use and excess alcohol are the two commonest causes of atraumatic femoral head osteonecrosis. Sickle cell disease causes marrow infarction; dysbarism produces nitrogen emboli; and Gaucher's disease infiltrates the marrow (glucocerebrosidase deficiency). The classic associations are often remembered with mnemonics such as 'PLASTIC RAGS' or 'ASEPTIC'.
learningPoint: Corticosteroids and alcohol are the two commonest causes of atraumatic femoral head osteonecrosis; remember sickle cell, dysbarism and Gaucher's as classic associations.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Adult hip: osteonecrosis

stem: Theme: heritable skeletal disorders in children. For each description, select the single most likely diagnosis. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Achondroplasia

options.label: B
options.text: Osteogenesis imperfecta

options.label: C
options.text: Marfan syndrome

options.label: D
options.text: Multiple hereditary exostoses (diaphyseal aclasis)

options.label: E
options.text: Cleidocranial dysplasia

options.label: F
options.text: Osteopetrosis

options.label: G
options.text: Mucopolysaccharidosis (e.g. Morquio)

options.label: H
options.text: Ehlers-Danlos syndrome

emiLeads.stem: A child has rhizomelic short stature, a trident hand, frontal bossing and spinal canal stenosis; inheritance is autosomal dominant due to an FGFR3 mutation.
A child has recurrent low-energy fractures, blue sclerae and early-onset hearing loss, with a defect of type I collagen.
A child has multiple bony lumps arising from the metaphyses near the ends of the long bones, with a small lifetime risk of malignant transformation to chondrosarcoma.
A child has dense, brittle 'marble bone' on radiographs from defective osteoclast function, with anaemia from marrow crowding and cranial-nerve compression.

explanation: Achondroplasia (an FGFR3 mutation) causes rhizomelic dwarfism, a trident hand and spinal stenosis. Osteogenesis imperfecta is a type I collagen defect causing bone fragility, blue sclerae and hearing loss. Multiple hereditary exostoses produces numerous metaphyseal osteochondromas with a small risk of chondrosarcoma. Osteopetrosis is a failure of osteoclast resorption, producing dense but brittle bone and marrow failure.
learningPoint: Achondroplasia = FGFR3 (rhizomelic dwarfism); osteopetrosis = failed osteoclast resorption ('marble bone'); osteogenesis imperfecta = type I collagen defect.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Paediatrics: skeletal dysplasias

stem: Theme: low back pain. For each red-flag pattern, select the single most likely underlying cause. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Vertebral osteomyelitis / discitis

options.label: B
options.text: Metastatic spinal disease

options.label: C
options.text: Osteoporotic vertebral compression fracture

options.label: D
options.text: Ankylosing spondylitis

options.label: E
options.text: Cauda equina syndrome

options.label: F
options.text: Mechanical (non-specific) low back pain

options.label: G
options.text: Spinal epidural abscess

options.label: H
options.text: Multiple myeloma

emiLeads.stem: A young man has months of insidious low back pain with morning stiffness lasting over an hour that improves with exercise; he is HLA-B27 positive with radiographic sacroiliitis.
An intravenous drug user has escalating back pain, fevers and night sweats with a raised CRP, and MRI shows disc-space and vertebral endplate destruction.
An elderly woman develops sudden severe back pain after a trivial stumble; imaging shows vertebral body wedging with no associated soft-tissue mass.
A patient with previously treated breast cancer has progressive night-time back pain and a destructive lesion involving the vertebral body and pedicle.

explanation: Inflammatory back pain (young patient, insidious onset, morning stiffness over an hour, improvement with activity, HLA-B27, sacroiliitis) points to ankylosing spondylitis. Fever, raised inflammatory markers and endplate/disc destruction in an at-risk patient indicate discitis/osteomyelitis. Sudden pain after minimal trauma in an older person with vertebral wedging is an osteoporotic fracture, whereas a destructive lesion of the body and pedicle with a cancer history suggests metastasis.
learningPoint: Inflammatory back pain (young, morning stiffness over an hour, better with activity, HLA-B27) suggests ankylosing spondylitis; fever + raised CRP + endplate destruction suggests discitis.
