references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Spine: red flags and infection/tumour

stem: Theme: anterior knee pain. For each presentation, select the single most likely diagnosis. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Patellofemoral pain syndrome

options.label: B
options.text: Osgood-Schlatter disease

options.label: C
options.text: Patellar tendinopathy (jumper's knee)

options.label: D
options.text: Sinding-Larsen-Johansson syndrome

options.label: E
options.text: Prepatellar bursitis

options.label: F
options.text: Chondromalacia patellae

options.label: G
options.text: Bipartite patella

options.label: H
options.text: Quadriceps tendinopathy

emiLeads.stem: A 13-year-old footballer has pain and a tender bony prominence at the tibial tubercle that is worse with activity and jumping.
A basketball player has well-localised pain at the inferior pole of the patella, worse on jumping and landing.
A carpet-fitter develops a fluctuant, tender swelling directly over the front of the patella after prolonged kneeling.
A young woman has diffuse anterior knee pain that is worse on stairs and after prolonged sitting (the 'cinema sign'), with no effusion and a normal radiograph.

explanation: Osgood-Schlatter disease is a traction apophysitis of the tibial tubercle in adolescents. Patellar tendinopathy ('jumper's knee') localises to the inferior pole of the patella in jumping athletes. Prepatellar bursitis ('housemaid's/carpet-fitter's knee') is a swelling in front of the patella from repetitive kneeling. Patellofemoral pain syndrome causes diffuse anterior pain worse on stairs and prolonged sitting (the cinema sign).
learningPoint: Tibial tubercle tenderness in an adolescent = Osgood-Schlatter; inferior patellar pole pain in a jumper = patellar tendinopathy; the 'cinema sign' = patellofemoral pain.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Knee: anterior knee pain

stem: Theme: the paediatric elbow. For each presentation, select the single most likely diagnosis. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Supracondylar fracture

options.label: B
options.text: Lateral condyle fracture

options.label: C
options.text: Medial epicondyle avulsion

options.label: D
options.text: Radial head/neck fracture

options.label: E
options.text: Pulled elbow (radial head subluxation)

options.label: F
options.text: Monteggia injury

options.label: G
options.text: Olecranon fracture

options.label: H
options.text: Normal ossification (no fracture)

emiLeads.stem: A 2-year-old will not use the arm after being pulled up by the hand; the arm is held pronated and slightly flexed with no swelling, and a supination-flexion manoeuvre restores normal use.
After an elbow dislocation a child has medial-sided pain; the medial epicondyle ossification centre is not seen in its expected position and may be trapped within the joint.
A child has lateral elbow swelling after a fall; the fracture passes through the cartilaginous epiphysis and is prone to non-union and growth disturbance, often requiring fixation.
A child has a markedly swollen elbow after a fall onto an outstretched hand; the lateral radiograph shows a posterior fat-pad sign and a disrupted anterior humeral line.

explanation: Pulled elbow (radial head subluxation) follows an axial pull in a toddler and reduces with supination-flexion. After a dislocation the medial epicondyle apophysis can be avulsed and trapped intra-articularly; the CRITOE ossification sequence (capitellum, radial head, internal/medial epicondyle, trochlea, olecranon, external/lateral epicondyle) helps spot a displaced centre. Lateral condyle fractures often need fixation to avoid non-union, and a displaced supracondylar fracture shows a posterior fat pad with a broken anterior humeral line.
learningPoint: Use CRITOE to judge paediatric elbow ossification; a 'missing' medial epicondyle after a dislocation may be trapped in the joint, and lateral condyle fractures often need fixation.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Paediatric elbow trauma

stem: Theme: brachial plexus and nerve-root lesions. For each presentation, select the single most likely level of injury. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Upper trunk (C5-6) - Erb's palsy

options.label: B
options.text: Lower trunk (C8-T1) - Klumpke's palsy

options.label: C
options.text: Posterior cord

options.label: D
options.text: Long thoracic nerve

options.label: E
options.text: Axillary nerve

options.label: F
options.text: C7 nerve root

options.label: G
options.text: Whole plexus (pan-plexus injury)

options.label: H
options.text: Musculocutaneous nerve

emiLeads.stem: A newborn after a difficult delivery with shoulder dystocia holds the arm adducted, internally rotated and pronated - the 'waiter's tip' posture.
A traction injury produces an intrinsic-minus 'claw hand' with weak small muscles of the hand, sometimes accompanied by a Horner's syndrome.
A lesion weakens the deltoid, triceps and wrist/finger extensors, with sensory loss over the dorsum of the hand - the muscles supplied by the axillary and radial nerves.
After an anterior shoulder injury a patient has weak elbow flexion and supination with numbness over the lateral forearm, but normal hand function.

explanation: Erb's palsy (C5-6 / upper trunk) gives the 'waiter's tip' posture. Klumpke's palsy (C8-T1 / lower trunk) gives a claw hand with intrinsic weakness and may include a Horner's syndrome from T1 sympathetic involvement. The posterior cord supplies the axillary and radial nerves (deltoid, triceps, wrist and finger extensors). The musculocutaneous nerve supplies the elbow flexors and the lateral forearm sensation.
learningPoint: Erb's palsy (C5-6) = 'waiter's tip'; Klumpke's (C8-T1) = claw hand with possible Horner's; the posterior cord supplies the axillary and radial nerve muscles.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Anatomy: brachial plexus

stem: Theme: the acute monoarthritis. For each presentation and synovial-fluid finding, select the single most likely diagnosis. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Gout

options.label: B
options.text: Pseudogout (CPPD)

options.label: C
options.text: Septic arthritis

options.label: D
options.text: Rheumatoid arthritis

options.label: E
options.text: Reactive arthritis

options.label: F
options.text: Haemarthrosis

options.label: G
options.text: Osteoarthritis

options.label: H
options.text: Haemochromatosis arthropathy

emiLeads.stem: A middle-aged man has a hot, exquisitely painful first metatarsophalangeal joint; aspiration shows needle-shaped, negatively birefringent crystals.
An elderly woman has an acutely swollen knee; the radiograph shows chondrocalcinosis and aspiration reveals rhomboid, positively birefringent crystals.
A patient has a hot, swollen knee with fever; the synovial white cell count is very high with neutrophil predominance and organisms are seen on Gram stain.
A young man develops an asymmetrical lower-limb arthritis a few weeks after an episode of urethritis, with associated conjunctivitis.

explanation: Gout produces negatively birefringent, needle-shaped urate crystals and classically affects the first metatarsophalangeal joint (podagra). Pseudogout (calcium pyrophosphate deposition) produces positively birefringent rhomboid crystals with chondrocalcinosis. Septic arthritis gives a very high neutrophil-predominant synovial cell count with organisms on Gram stain and is an emergency. Reactive arthritis follows a urethritis or enteritis with the triad of arthritis, conjunctivitis and urethritis.
learningPoint: Gout = negatively birefringent needles; pseudogout = positively birefringent rhomboids with chondrocalcinosis; always exclude sepsis first in an acute hot joint.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Basic science: crystal arthropathy and infection

stem: Theme: complications after total hip replacement. For each presentation, select the single most likely complication. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Prosthetic joint infection

options.label: B
options.text: Aseptic loosening

options.label: C
options.text: Recurrent dislocation

options.label: D
options.text: Periprosthetic fracture

options.label: E
options.text: Leg-length discrepancy

options.label: F
options.text: Sciatic nerve injury

options.label: G
options.text: Venous thromboembolism

options.label: H
options.text: Adverse reaction to metal debris

emiLeads.stem: Six months post-operatively the patient has increasing pain at rest and at night, a discharging sinus, raised CRP and ESR, and a joint aspirate with a high neutrophil percentage.
There are repeated episodes of the hip giving way and 'popping out', often when bending to put on shoes; radiographs reduced in the emergency department show component malposition.
Immediately after surgery the patient has a foot drop with numbness of the lateral leg and dorsum of the foot; the injury followed a limb-lengthening procedure.
Several years post-operatively there is gradually worsening activity-related thigh and groin pain with progressive radiolucent lines around the implant and no features of infection.

explanation: Rest and night pain with a sinus and raised inflammatory markers, confirmed on aspiration, is a prosthetic joint infection. Recurrent dislocation reflects component malposition or soft-tissue insufficiency. The peroneal division of the sciatic nerve is most vulnerable, particularly after lengthening. Late, progressive radiolucency with activity-related pain and no infection indicates aseptic loosening - but infection must always be excluded first.
learningPoint: Night/rest pain + raised CRP/ESR + a sinus after THA is a prosthetic joint infection until excluded; the peroneal division of the sciatic nerve is most at risk with lengthening.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Adult reconstruction: complications of hip arthroplasty

subtopic: Clinical examination tests of the knee
stem: Theme: clinical tests of the knee. For each test, select the single structure it best assesses. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Anterior cruciate ligament

options.label: B
options.text: Posterior cruciate ligament

options.label: C
options.text: Medial meniscus

options.label: D
options.text: Posterolateral corner

options.label: E
options.text: Medial collateral ligament

options.label: F
options.text: Patellofemoral joint

options.label: G
options.text: Lateral meniscus

options.label: H
options.text: Extensor mechanism

emiLeads.stem: Lachman's test and the pivot-shift test.
The posterior drawer test, the quadriceps active test and a posterior sag sign.
The dial test showing increased external rotation at 30 degrees that normalises at 90 degrees.
McMurray's test producing a painful click on tibial rotation with the knee flexed.

explanation: Lachman's test and the pivot-shift assess the anterior cruciate ligament. The posterior drawer, quadriceps active test and posterior sag assess the posterior cruciate ligament. The dial test localises the posterolateral corner when external rotation is increased at 30 degrees but not 90 degrees (increased at both implies a combined PCL and PLC injury). McMurray's test assesses the menisci.
learningPoint: The dial test localises a posterolateral corner injury when external rotation is increased at 30 degrees but normal at 90 degrees.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Knee: clinical examination

stem: Theme: nerve-root lesions. For each motor, sensory and reflex pattern, select the single nerve root involved. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: C5

options.label: B
options.text: C6

options.label: C
options.text: C7

options.label: D
options.text: C8

options.label: E
options.text: L3/L4

options.label: F
options.text: L5

options.label: G
options.text: S1

options.label: H
options.text: L2

emiLeads.stem: Weakness of great-toe dorsiflexion (extensor hallucis longus) and foot dorsiflexion, with numbness over the dorsum of the foot and normal reflexes.
Weak plantarflexion with a reduced ankle jerk and numbness of the sole and lateral border of the foot.
Weak elbow flexion and wrist extension, a reduced biceps/brachioradialis reflex, and numbness of the thumb.
Weak elbow extension (triceps), a reduced triceps reflex and numbness of the middle finger.

explanation: The L5 root supplies extensor hallucis longus and foot dorsiflexion with sensation over the dorsum of the foot and no reflex change. The S1 root supplies plantarflexion and the ankle jerk, with sensation over the sole and lateral foot. C6 corresponds to the thumb and the biceps/brachioradialis reflex, and C7 to triceps and the middle finger.
learningPoint: L5 = weak great-toe extension with preserved reflexes; S1 = reduced ankle jerk with weak plantarflexion.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Spine: radiculopathy and nerve-root assessment

stem: Theme: finger and thumb deformities and injuries. For each description, select the single most likely diagnosis. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Mallet finger

options.label: B
options.text: Jersey finger (FDP avulsion)

options.label: C
options.text: Swan-neck deformity

options.label: D
options.text: Boutonniere deformity

options.label: E
options.text: Trigger finger

options.label: F
options.text: Dupuytren's contracture

options.label: G
options.text: Sagittal band rupture

options.label: H
options.text: Camptodactyly

emiLeads.stem: Loss of active extension of the distal interphalangeal joint, which rests flexed, after a forced flexion injury to the fingertip.
Inability to actively flex the distal interphalangeal joint after a flexed finger was forcibly extended while gripping a jersey, most often the ring finger.
Proximal interphalangeal joint flexion with distal interphalangeal hyperextension after a central slip injury, with a positive Elson test.
Proximal interphalangeal joint hyperextension with distal interphalangeal flexion, seen in rheumatoid disease or after an untreated mallet injury.

explanation: A mallet finger is a terminal extensor disruption leaving the DIP joint dropped. A jersey finger is an avulsion of flexor digitorum profundus so the DIP cannot actively flex. A boutonniere deformity follows a central slip injury (PIP flexion, DIP hyperextension, positive Elson test), and a swan-neck deformity is the reverse pattern (PIP hyperextension, DIP flexion).
learningPoint: Boutonniere (central slip) = PIP flexion + DIP hyperextension; swan-neck is the reverse; a positive Elson test confirms central slip rupture.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Hand: tendon injuries and deformities

stem: Theme: paediatric foot conditions. For each presentation, select the single most likely diagnosis. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Congenital talipes equinovarus (clubfoot)

options.label: B
options.text: Congenital vertical talus

options.label: C
options.text: Tarsal coalition

options.label: D
options.text: Flexible flatfoot

options.label: E
options.text: Kohler disease

options.label: F
options.text: Juvenile hallux valgus

options.label: G
options.text: Cavovarus foot

options.label: H
options.text: Metatarsus adductus

emiLeads.stem: A rigid, painful flatfoot in an adolescent with limited subtalar motion and recurrent ankle sprains; a calcaneonavicular bar is suspected.
A painless flatfoot in a young child whose arch reforms on tiptoe standing and on great-toe dorsiflexion (Jack's test).
A rigid 'rocker-bottom' flatfoot in a neonate with a dorsally dislocated navicular and a vertical talus on radiographs.
A progressively worsening high-arched foot, which should prompt a search for an underlying neurological cause such as Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease.

explanation: A rigid, painful adolescent flatfoot with restricted subtalar motion suggests a tarsal coalition. A flexible flatfoot reconstitutes its arch on tiptoe and is benign. A congenital vertical talus is a rigid rocker-bottom foot in the neonate. A progressive cavovarus foot should always prompt a neurological work-up (commonly Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease).
learningPoint: A flexible flatfoot reforms its arch on tiptoe (benign); a rigid painful adolescent flatfoot suggests tarsal coalition; a cavovarus foot warrants a neurological work-up.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Paediatrics: the paediatric foot

subtopic: Diagnostic test statistics and study design
stem: Theme: diagnostic test statistics and study design. For each description, select the single best-matching term. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Sensitivity

options.label: B
options.text: Specificity

options.label: C
options.text: Positive predictive value

options.label: D
options.text: Negative predictive value

options.label: E
options.text: Type I error

options.label: F
options.text: Type II error

options.label: G
options.text: Randomised controlled trial

options.label: H
options.text: Number needed to treat

emiLeads.stem: The proportion of patients WITH the disease who test positive; a very high value means a negative result is good at ruling the disease OUT.
The proportion of patients WITHOUT the disease who test negative; a very high value means a positive result is good at ruling the disease IN.
Rejecting the null hypothesis when it is in fact true - a false-positive conclusion, whose risk is set by the alpha (p-value) threshold.
Failing to reject the null hypothesis when it is in fact false - a false-negative conclusion, related to inadequate statistical power.

explanation: Sensitivity is the true-positive rate (SnNOut: a Sensitive test, when Negative, rules a diagnosis OUT). Specificity is the true-negative rate (SpPIn: a Specific test, when Positive, rules it IN). A type I error is a false-positive (set by alpha), and a type II error is a false-negative (its probability is beta, with power equal to 1 minus beta).
learningPoint: SnNOut and SpPIn; a type I error is a false-positive (alpha), a type II error is a false-negative (related to power).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Basic science: research and statistics

subtopic: Nerve injuries with specific orthopaedic injuries
stem: Theme: nerve injuries associated with specific orthopaedic injuries. For each injury, select the single nerve most at risk. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Sciatic nerve

options.label: B
options.text: Axillary nerve

options.label: C
options.text: Radial nerve

options.label: D
options.text: Common peroneal nerve

options.label: E
options.text: Ulnar nerve

options.label: F
options.text: Anterior interosseous nerve

options.label: G
options.text: Median nerve

options.label: H
options.text: Posterior interosseous nerve

emiLeads.stem: A posterior dislocation of the hip.
A fracture of the mid-to-distal humeral shaft.
A traumatic knee dislocation or a fracture of the proximal fibula.
An anterior dislocation of the glenohumeral joint.

explanation: A posterior hip dislocation threatens the sciatic nerve (its peroneal division most). A humeral shaft fracture threatens the radial nerve in the spiral groove. A knee dislocation or proximal fibular fracture threatens the common peroneal nerve - and the popliteal artery, which must always be assessed. An anterior shoulder dislocation threatens the axillary nerve.
learningPoint: Posterior hip dislocation threatens the sciatic nerve; a knee dislocation threatens the common peroneal nerve (and the popliteal artery - always check the vascular status).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Trauma: nerve injuries

stem: Theme: fracture fixation. For each scenario, select the single best-matching principle or device. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Lag screw (interfragmentary compression)

options.label: B
options.text: Locking plate (fixed-angle, bridging)

options.label: C
options.text: Tension band

options.label: D
options.text: Intramedullary nail (load-sharing)

options.label: E
options.text: External fixator

options.label: F
options.text: Neutralisation plate

options.label: G
options.text: Antiglide plate

options.label: H
options.text: Cerclage wire

emiLeads.stem: Converting the distractive tensile force of a muscle pull into compression across a fracture, as used for a transverse olecranon or patella fracture.
Achieving absolute stability and primary bone healing by compressing a simple oblique fracture, with the screw placed perpendicular to the fracture plane.
A fixed-angle construct that preserves the periosteal blood supply and works well in osteoporotic metaphyseal bone or as a bridging device over comminution.
A load-sharing device passed down the medullary canal that gives relative stability and callus (secondary) healing, ideal for a diaphyseal long-bone fracture.

explanation: A tension band converts a tensile (distractive) muscle force into compression across the fracture (olecranon, patella). A lag screw gives interfragmentary compression, absolute stability and primary bone healing. A locking plate is a fixed-angle construct useful in osteoporotic bone and for bridging comminution while preserving blood supply. An intramedullary nail is load-sharing, giving relative stability and secondary (callus) healing.
learningPoint: A tension band converts tensile muscle force into compression (olecranon, patella); a lag screw gives absolute stability and primary healing, an IM nail relative stability and callus.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Basic science: principles of fracture fixation

stem: A patient whose total hip replacement was done five years ago presents with a three-week history of an acutely painful, swollen hip and fevers, having been entirely well in between. Aspiration confirms infection with a virulent organism and the implant is radiographically well fixed. What is the most appropriate management of this acute haematogenous infection?

options.label: A
options.text: Debridement, antibiotics and implant retention (DAIR) with exchange of modular components

options.label: B
options.text: Immediate one-stage revision in all cases

options.label: C
options.text: Long-term antibiotic suppression alone with no surgery

options.label: D
options.text: Girdlestone excision arthroplasty as first line

options.label: E
options.text: Observation

explanation: An acute infection (short symptom duration, generally under three to four weeks) with a well-fixed implant and a treatable organism is managed with debridement, antibiotics and implant retention (DAIR), exchanging the modular head and liner and giving targeted antibiotics. Chronic infection or a loose implant requires one- or two-stage revision. Suppression alone is reserved for patients unfit for surgery.
learningPoint: Acute prosthetic joint infection with a well-fixed implant and short symptom duration is treated with DAIR plus modular component exchange; chronic infection needs staged revision.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Adult reconstruction: periprosthetic joint infection

stem: A 28-year-old footballer has activity-related groin pain and a positive anterior impingement test (pain on flexion, adduction and internal rotation). Imaging shows an aspherical femoral head-neck junction with an alpha angle of 65 degrees. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.label: A
options.text: Cam-type femoroacetabular impingement

options.label: B
options.text: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head

options.label: C
options.text: Septic arthritis

options.label: D
options.text: Perthes disease

options.label: E
options.text: Lumbar radiculopathy

explanation: A positive flexion-adduction-internal-rotation (FADIR) impingement test with an aspherical head-neck junction and a raised alpha angle indicates cam-type femoroacetabular impingement, where the aspherical femoral head shears the labrum and chondral surface. Pincer-type impingement is acetabular over-coverage (for example a retroverted or deep socket). Management is activity modification and physiotherapy, with arthroscopic osteochondroplasty and labral repair if refractory.
learningPoint: Groin pain with a positive FADIR test and a raised alpha angle (aspherical head-neck junction) indicates cam-type femoroacetabular impingement.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Hip: femoroacetabular impingement

stem: A patient sustains an isolated posterior cruciate ligament injury with grade I-II laxity on posterior drawer and no other ligament involvement. What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.label: A
options.text: A functional rehabilitation programme focused on quadriceps strengthening

options.label: B
options.text: Immediate PCL reconstruction in all cases

options.label: C
options.text: Long-leg casting in flexion

options.label: D
options.text: Knee arthrodesis

options.label: E
options.text: Above-knee amputation

explanation: Most isolated low-grade posterior cruciate ligament injuries are managed non-operatively, with quadriceps-focused rehabilitation; the quadriceps dynamically compensates for posterior cruciate deficiency. Reconstruction is reserved for high-grade, symptomatic injuries and for combined injuries, particularly when the posterolateral corner is also involved.
learningPoint: Isolated low-grade PCL injuries are usually managed with quadriceps-focused rehabilitation rather than reconstruction.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Sports medicine: posterior cruciate ligament

stem: A patient has cubital tunnel syndrome with persistent ulnar nerve symptoms and intrinsic weakness despite six months of non-operative measures, with confirmatory nerve conduction studies. Which is an appropriate surgical option?

options.label: A
options.text: In-situ decompression of the ulnar nerve at the cubital tunnel, with or without anterior transposition

options.label: B
options.text: Carpal tunnel release

options.label: C
options.text: Wrist arthrodesis

options.label: D
options.text: Excision of the radial head

options.label: E
options.text: Tendon transfer as the first-line procedure

explanation: Cubital tunnel syndrome that fails non-operative treatment, with confirmatory electrophysiology, is treated by ulnar nerve decompression at the elbow - either simple in-situ release or anterior transposition (subcutaneous or submuscular), sometimes with a medial epicondylectomy. A carpal tunnel release addresses the median nerve at the wrist and is the wrong site.
learningPoint: Refractory cubital tunnel syndrome is treated by ulnar nerve decompression at the elbow (in-situ release, with or without anterior transposition).
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Elbow: cubital tunnel syndrome

stem: A 75-year-old with cuff-tear arthropathy (glenohumeral arthritis with an irreparable rotator cuff tear) has pain and pseudoparalysis with poor active elevation. Which arthroplasty is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Reverse total shoulder arthroplasty

options.label: B
options.text: Anatomic total shoulder arthroplasty

options.label: C
options.text: Hemiarthroplasty with rotator cuff repair

options.label: D
options.text: Glenohumeral arthrodesis

options.label: E
options.text: Hemiresurfacing

explanation: A reverse total shoulder arthroplasty is indicated for cuff-tear arthropathy with pseudoparalysis: it medialises and lowers the centre of rotation so that the deltoid can elevate the arm without a functioning rotator cuff. An anatomic total shoulder replacement requires an intact, functioning cuff; without one it fails through eccentric glenoid loading (the 'rocking-horse' glenoid).
learningPoint: A reverse total shoulder arthroplasty lets the deltoid power elevation without a functioning cuff - the procedure of choice for cuff-tear arthropathy; an anatomic TSA needs an intact cuff.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Shoulder: arthroplasty

stem: A 35-year-old has acute sciatica from an L5-S1 disc prolapse with a mild S1 sensory disturbance, but no motor weakness and no bladder symptoms. What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.label: A
options.text: Non-operative treatment with analgesia, activity modification and physiotherapy, as most settle over weeks

options.label: B
options.text: Immediate discectomy

options.label: C
options.text: Urgent surgical decompression within 24 hours

options.label: D
options.text: Lumbar spinal fusion

options.label: E
options.text: Strict bed rest for six weeks

explanation: Most lumbar disc herniations causing radiculopathy without a significant or progressive neurological deficit, and without cauda equina syndrome, settle within roughly six to twelve weeks with non-operative care: analgesia, staying active, physiotherapy and sometimes a nerve-root injection. Microdiscectomy is offered for persistent or severe radiculopathy or a progressive deficit. Prolonged bed rest is harmful.
learningPoint: Uncomplicated sciatica from a disc prolapse is managed non-operatively first; surgery is for a progressive deficit, cauda equina, or persistent severe radiculopathy.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Spine: lumbar disc herniation

stem: A volar finger laceration divides the flexor tendons within the fibro-osseous sheath between the distal palmar crease and the FDS insertion ('zone II'). Which statement about management is most accurate?

options.label: A
options.text: Primary repair of the flexor tendon(s) with an early controlled mobilisation protocol gives the best results; zone II is technically demanding ('no man's land')

options.label: B
options.text: The tendon ends should be left unrepaired

options.label: C
options.text: Immediate arthrodesis of the finger is preferred

options.label: D
options.text: Zone II injuries never require surgery

options.label: E
options.text: The finger should be immobilised rigidly for eight weeks

explanation: Zone II flexor tendon injuries ('no man's land') are repaired primarily with a core suture and an epitendinous suture, followed by an early controlled mobilisation protocol that reduces adhesions while protecting the repair. The zone is technically demanding because the flexor digitorum superficialis and profundus run together within a tight sheath. Rigid prolonged immobilisation produces stiffness and adhesions.
learningPoint: Zone II ('no man's land') flexor tendon injuries need primary repair with early controlled mobilisation to balance tendon healing against adhesion formation.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Hand: flexor tendon injuries

stem: A 10-week-old infant has developmental dysplasia of the hip confirmed on ultrasound, with a dislocatable but reducible hip. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?

options.label: A
options.text: A Pavlik harness holding the hips flexed and abducted

options.label: B
options.text: Immediate closed reduction and hip spica under anaesthetic

options.label: C
options.text: Open reduction

options.label: D
options.text: Femoral shortening osteotomy

options.label: E
options.text: Observation until walking age

explanation: In an infant under about six months with a reducible developmental hip dysplasia, a Pavlik harness holding the hips flexed and abducted is first-line; maintaining concentric reduction allows the acetabulum to develop. Forced or excessive abduction is avoided because of the risk of avascular necrosis. Closed or open reduction is reserved for older infants or for hips that fail harness treatment.
learningPoint: DDH in an infant under six months is treated first with a Pavlik harness (flexion and abduction); avoid forced abduction to limit the risk of avascular necrosis.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Paediatrics: developmental dysplasia of the hip

stem: Theme: surgical approaches to the hip. For each description, select the single best-matching approach. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Posterior (Moore / Southern)

options.label: B
options.text: Direct lateral (Hardinge)

options.label: C
options.text: Anterolateral (Watson-Jones)

options.label: D
options.text: Anterior (Smith-Petersen)

options.label: E
options.text: Medial (Ludloff)

options.label: F
options.text: Trochanteric osteotomy

options.label: G
options.text: Ilioinguinal

options.label: H
options.text: Kocher-Langenbeck

emiLeads.stem: An internervous approach between sartorius and rectus femoris (femoral nerve) and tensor fasciae latae and gluteus medius (superior gluteal nerve), used for the direct anterior hip replacement and open reduction of paediatric DDH; the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve is at risk.

The commonest approach for a hemiarthroplasty; it splits gluteus maximus and detaches the short external rotators, puts the sciatic nerve at risk and has a higher dislocation rate than the anterior approach.
An approach that splits gluteus medius and detaches part of the abductor mechanism, risking the superior gluteal nerve and a postoperative limp but giving a low dislocation rate.
An extensile approach to the anterior column and quadrilateral plate of the acetabulum through three windows, used for acetabular fracture fixation.

explanation: The Smith-Petersen (anterior) approach is internervous between the femoral and superior gluteal nerves and risks the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve. The posterior (Moore) approach splits gluteus maximus and releases the short external rotators, with the highest dislocation rate and risk to the sciatic nerve. The Hardinge (direct lateral) approach divides the abductors, risking the superior gluteal nerve and a limp. The ilioinguinal approach exposes the anterior acetabular column.
learningPoint: The Smith-Petersen approach is internervous (femoral / superior gluteal nerves); the posterior approach has the highest dislocation rate and risks the sciatic nerve.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Anatomy: surgical approaches to the hip

stem: Theme: drugs affecting bone. For each description, select the single best-matching agent. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Bisphosphonate

options.label: B
options.text: Denosumab

options.label: C
options.text: Teriparatide

options.label: D
options.text: Romosozumab

options.label: E
options.text: NSAID

options.label: F
options.text: Calcium and vitamin D

options.label: G
options.text: Strontium ranelate

options.label: H
options.text: Raloxifene

emiLeads.stem: An anti-resorptive that binds hydroxyapatite and is taken up by osteoclasts, inhibiting farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase and inducing osteoclast apoptosis; rare associations include atypical femoral fractures and osteonecrosis of the jaw.
A monoclonal antibody against RANKL that blocks osteoclast formation and activity; rebound vertebral fractures can occur if it is stopped without a follow-on agent.
A recombinant parathyroid hormone analogue that, given intermittently, is ANABOLIC and stimulates new bone formation.
A drug that inhibits cyclo-oxygenase and prostaglandin synthesis; in orthopaedics it can impair fracture healing and is used to reduce heterotopic ossification.

explanation: Bisphosphonates are anti-resorptive, taken up by osteoclasts where they inhibit farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase and trigger apoptosis (rarely causing atypical femoral fractures and osteonecrosis of the jaw). Denosumab is an anti-RANKL antibody with a rebound-fracture risk on cessation. Teriparatide is intermittent PTH and is anabolic. NSAIDs inhibit cyclo-oxygenase, reducing heterotopic ossification but potentially impairing fracture healing.
learningPoint: Bisphosphonates and denosumab are anti-resorptive; teriparatide (intermittent PTH) is anabolic; NSAIDs reduce heterotopic ossification but can impair fracture healing.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Basic science: metabolic bone disease and pharmacology

stem: Theme: pelvic ring injuries (Young-Burgess). For each description, select the single best-matching pattern or principle. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Anteroposterior compression (APC)

options.label: B
options.text: Lateral compression (LC)

options.label: C
options.text: Vertical shear (VS)

options.label: D
options.text: Combined mechanism

options.label: E
options.text: Stable isolated pubic rami fracture

options.label: F
options.text: Acetabular fracture

options.label: G
options.text: Pelvic binder

options.label: H
options.text: Sacral insufficiency fracture

emiLeads.stem: A head-on motorcycle crash produces an 'open-book' pelvis with external rotation of the hemipelvis and symphyseal diastasis; the pelvic volume increases, raising the risk of major bleeding.
A side-impact injury causes internal rotation of the hemipelvis with horizontal pubic rami fractures and an impacted sacral fracture.
A fall from height drives one hemipelvis vertically upward, disrupting the symphysis and the entire posterior sacroiliac complex with cephalad displacement.
The immediate device applied at the level of the greater trochanters to reduce pelvic volume and tamponade bleeding in an unstable open-book injury.

explanation: An anteroposterior-compression ('open-book') injury externally rotates the hemipelvis and increases pelvic volume, raising the haemorrhage risk; a pelvic binder applied over the greater trochanters closes the ring and tamponades bleeding. Lateral compression internally rotates the hemipelvis with transverse rami fractures and sacral impaction. Vertical shear displaces a hemipelvis cephalad with complete posterior disruption.
learningPoint: An APC 'open-book' pelvis increases volume and bleeding - a binder at the greater trochanters closes it; lateral compression internally rotates the hemipelvis.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Trauma: pelvic ring injuries

subtopic: Tendon disorders of the hindfoot and ankle
stem: Theme: tendon and soft-tissue disorders around the hindfoot and ankle. For each presentation, select the single most likely structure involved. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Tibialis posterior

options.label: B
options.text: Peroneal tendons

options.label: C
options.text: Tibialis anterior

options.label: D
options.text: Flexor hallucis longus

options.label: E
options.text: Achilles tendon

options.label: F
options.text: Plantar fascia

options.label: G
options.text: Extensor digitorum longus

options.label: H
options.text: Posterior tibial nerve

emiLeads.stem: A middle-aged woman develops a progressive unilateral flatfoot with hindfoot valgus, a 'too-many-toes' sign and an inability to perform a single-leg heel raise.
Lateral ankle pain with a sensation of tendons subluxing over the lateral malleolus after an inversion injury, worse on resisted eversion.
Posteromedial ankle pain in a ballet dancer on pushing into pointe ('dancer's tendinitis'), sometimes with triggering of the great toe.
Sharp inferior heel pain that is worst on the first steps in the morning and on dorsiflexion of the toes, with tenderness at the medial calcaneal tubercle.

explanation: Tibialis posterior dysfunction causes a progressive adult-acquired flatfoot with hindfoot valgus, a 'too-many-toes' sign and failure of single-leg heel rise. Peroneal tendon subluxation gives lateral ankle pain after an inversion injury. Flexor hallucis longus tendinopathy ('dancer's tendinitis') causes posteromedial pain with great-toe triggering. Plantar fasciitis causes inferior heel pain worst on the first morning steps.
learningPoint: Tibialis posterior dysfunction causes a progressive adult-acquired flatfoot with a 'too-many-toes' sign and failure of single-leg heel rise.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Foot and ankle: tendon disorders

stem: Theme: shoulder trauma. For each presentation, select the single most likely diagnosis. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Neer one-part proximal humerus fracture

options.label: B
options.text: Neer four-part fracture

options.label: C
options.text: Anterior shoulder dislocation

options.label: D
options.text: Posterior shoulder dislocation

options.label: E
options.text: Greater tuberosity fracture

options.label: F
options.text: Clavicle midshaft fracture

options.label: G
options.text: Acromioclavicular joint dislocation

options.label: H
options.text: Sternoclavicular dislocation

emiLeads.stem: After an epileptic seizure the arm is locked in internal rotation and cannot be externally rotated; the AP radiograph shows a 'light-bulb' sign.
A comminuted proximal humeral fracture in which the head, shaft and both tuberosities are all displaced; the articular segment is at high risk of avascular necrosis.
A proximal humeral fracture in which no segment is displaced more than a centimetre or angulated beyond 45 degrees, managed in a sling with early movement.
A young adult after a fall holds the arm in slight abduction and external rotation, with loss of the deltoid contour and a palpable sulcus below the acromion.

explanation: A posterior shoulder dislocation (classically after a seizure or electric shock) locks the arm in internal rotation and shows a 'light-bulb' sign; it is frequently missed. A Neer four-part fracture displaces all segments and threatens the head's blood supply. A Neer one-part fracture is minimally displaced and treated non-operatively. An anterior dislocation holds the arm abducted and externally rotated with loss of the deltoid contour.
learningPoint: A posterior shoulder dislocation (after a seizure or shock) locks the arm in internal rotation with a 'light-bulb' sign and is easily missed.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Shoulder: trauma

stem: Theme: articular cartilage defects of the knee. For each scenario, select the single most appropriate treatment. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Microfracture

options.label: B
options.text: Autologous chondrocyte implantation (ACI)

options.label: C
options.text: Osteochondral autograft transfer (OATS)

options.label: D
options.text: Osteochondral allograft

options.label: E
options.text: Non-operative management

options.label: F
options.text: Total knee replacement

options.label: G
options.text: Meniscal transplant

options.label: H
options.text: Realignment osteotomy

emiLeads.stem: A small (under 2 cm2) contained chondral defect in a young patient is treated with a marrow-stimulation technique that recruits mesenchymal cells to form fibrocartilage.
A large (over 2-4 cm2) symptomatic chondral defect is treated by harvesting and culturing the patient's own chondrocytes, then reimplanting them under a membrane in a two-stage procedure.
A small focal osteochondral defect is resurfaced with cylindrical plugs of bone and hyaline cartilage taken from a non-weight-bearing part of the same knee.
A large osteochondral defect with subchondral bone loss in a young patient is resurfaced with a size-matched cadaveric graft of bone and cartilage.

explanation: Cartilage treatment is driven by defect size and whether subchondral bone is involved. Microfracture suits small contained chondral lesions (forming fibrocartilage). Autologous chondrocyte implantation suits large chondral defects (a two-stage, more hyaline-like repair). Osteochondral autograft (OATS) resurfaces small osteochondral lesions with the patient's own plugs, and an osteochondral allograft addresses large defects with bone loss. Co-existing malalignment is corrected with an osteotomy.
learningPoint: Defect size and bone involvement drive cartilage treatment - microfracture for small contained lesions, ACI for large chondral defects, osteochondral grafts when bone is involved.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Sports medicine: articular cartilage

stem: A 55-year-old has a displaced simple transverse fracture of the olecranon with an intact articular surface and no comminution; the triceps pulls the proximal fragment apart. Which fixation best neutralises this distractive force?

options.label: A
options.text: Tension-band wiring, converting the triceps' distractive pull into compression across the fracture

options.label: B
options.text: A single lag screw alone

options.label: C
options.text: Excision of the proximal fragment in all cases

options.label: D
options.text: Cast immobilisation in full extension

options.label: E
options.text: External fixation

explanation: A simple transverse olecranon fracture is ideally treated with tension-band wiring: as the elbow flexes, the tensile pull of triceps is converted into compression across the articular surface. Comminuted or oblique patterns are better treated with a plate. Excision of the fragment with triceps advancement is reserved for small, very comminuted fragments in low-demand patients.
learningPoint: A simple transverse olecranon fracture is fixed with a tension band that turns the triceps pull into fracture compression; comminuted patterns need a plate.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Elbow trauma: olecranon fractures

subtopic: Displaced intracapsular hip fracture in the elderly
stem: An independent, mobile and cognitively intact 80-year-old sustains a displaced intracapsular femoral neck fracture (Garden III-IV) after a simple fall. Which is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Arthroplasty - a total hip replacement for this fit, active patient (hemiarthroplasty for the frailer/less active)

options.label: B
options.text: Internal fixation with cannulated screws

options.label: C
options.text: Non-operative management with bed rest

options.label: D
options.text: Dynamic hip screw

options.label: E
options.text: Girdlestone excision arthroplasty

explanation: A displaced intracapsular fracture disrupts the femoral head blood supply, so internal fixation carries high rates of avascular necrosis and non-union in older patients; arthroplasty is preferred. A total hip replacement is favoured for patients who were independently mobile and cognitively intact, while hemiarthroplasty suits the frailer or less active. (Undisplaced fractures and young patients are managed differently.)
learningPoint: Displaced intracapsular hip fractures in older patients are treated by arthroplasty - total hip replacement for the fit and active, hemiarthroplasty for the frailer.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Trauma: femoral neck fractures in the elderly

stem: An active 68-year-old sustains a minimally displaced proximal humeral fracture, with no segment displaced more than a centimetre or angulated beyond 45 degrees. What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Non-operative management with a sling and early supervised mobilisation

options.label: B
options.text: Open reduction and locking-plate fixation

options.label: C
options.text: Reverse total shoulder arthroplasty

options.label: D
options.text: Hemiarthroplasty

options.label: E
options.text: Shoulder arthrodesis

explanation: Most proximal humeral fractures are minimally displaced (Neer one-part) and do well with non-operative care: a short period in a sling followed by early movement to prevent stiffness. Surgery is reserved for significantly displaced patterns - locking-plate fixation for reconstructable fractures, or arthroplasty (reverse, in the elderly) for unreconstructable head-splitting or four-part fractures.
learningPoint: Most proximal humeral fractures are minimally displaced and managed non-operatively with early mobilisation; surgery is for significant displacement.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Shoulder: proximal humeral fractures

stem: A 55-year-old woman has a progressive flatfoot with hindfoot valgus and cannot perform a single-leg heel raise; the hindfoot is still flexible and passively correctable. What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Orthoses/bracing and physiotherapy first, with joint-sparing reconstruction (tendon transfer and calcaneal osteotomy) if these fail

options.label: B
options.text: Immediate triple arthrodesis

options.label: C
options.text: Below-knee amputation

options.label: D
options.text: Reassurance with no treatment

options.label: E
options.text: Total ankle replacement

explanation: A flexible (stage II) tibialis posterior tendon dysfunction - a correctable flatfoot with an inability to single-leg heel-rise - is managed first with orthoses or bracing and physiotherapy. If non-operative measures fail, joint-sparing reconstruction (a flexor digitorum longus transfer with a medial calcaneal osteotomy) is offered. A rigid (stage III) deformity requires arthrodesis.
learningPoint: A flexible (stage II) tibialis posterior dysfunction is managed with orthoses first, then joint-sparing reconstruction; a rigid deformity needs fusion.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Foot and ankle: adult acquired flatfoot

stem: A 13-year-old girl with adolescent idiopathic scoliosis and substantial remaining growth (Risser 1) has a thoracic curve of 30 degrees (Cobb) that has progressed. What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Bracing to prevent curve progression during the remaining growth

options.label: B
options.text: Immediate posterior spinal fusion

options.label: C
options.text: Observation with no intervention

options.label: D
options.text: Physiotherapy alone, as bracing has no role

options.label: E
options.text: Anterior vertebral body tethering in all cases

explanation: In a skeletally immature patient (open growth, low Risser grade) with a curve of roughly 25-45 degrees, bracing aims to halt progression during the remaining growth. Surgery (typically posterior spinal fusion) is generally considered for curves beyond about 45-50 degrees, or for documented progression despite bracing. Curves under about 25 degrees are observed.
learningPoint: In skeletally immature adolescent idiopathic scoliosis, curves of ~25-45 degrees are braced and curves beyond ~45-50 degrees are considered for fusion.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Spine: adolescent idiopathic scoliosis

stem: A patient on long-term bisphosphonate therapy presents with thigh pain and a transverse fracture of the lateral subtrochanteric/diaphyseal femoral cortex with focal cortical thickening ('beaking'). Which statement is most accurate?

options.label: A
options.text: This is an atypical femoral fracture associated with prolonged bisphosphonate use; the contralateral femur should also be assessed

options.label: B
options.text: This is a typical osteoporotic fracture unrelated to the medication

options.label: C
options.text: Bisphosphonates work by stimulating osteoclast activity

options.label: D
options.text: The fracture should never be fixed surgically

options.label: E
options.text: This finding proves the patient has metastatic bone disease

explanation: Atypical femoral fractures are transverse or short-oblique fractures of the lateral subtrochanteric or diaphyseal cortex, occurring with minimal trauma and showing focal cortical hypertrophy ('beaking'). They are linked to prolonged suppression of bone turnover by bisphosphonates (and denosumab). Because they are frequently bilateral, the opposite femur must be assessed; displaced fractures are fixed with a cephalomedullary nail and the drug is reviewed.
learningPoint: An atypical femoral fracture (transverse lateral cortex, 'beaking', minimal trauma) is linked to long-term bisphosphonates and is often bilateral - always assess the other femur.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics - Basic science: bisphosphonates and atypical fractures

stem: A 24-year-old is referred with a suspected primary bone sarcoma of the distal femur. Definitive imaging is complete and a tissue diagnosis is required before surgery. Which single principle should most strongly govern how the biopsy is performed?

options.label: A
options.text: A generous transverse incision should be used so adequate tissue can be obtained

options.label: B
options.text: The biopsy tract must lie within the planned resection field and be placed by, or in agreement with, the treating tumour unit

options.label: C
options.text: An open incisional biopsy is preferred to image-guided needle biopsy in all cases

options.label: D
options.text: The biopsy should be taken from the radiologically central part of the lesion to maximise yield

options.label: E
options.text: Raising fasciocutaneous flaps gives the safest exposure of the lesion

explanation: An ill-considered biopsy can compromise an otherwise curable limb. The cardinal rule is that the entire biopsy tract becomes contaminated and must be excised en bloc with the tumour, so it has to be sited (longitudinally) within the future resection bed and ideally performed at the definitive sarcoma centre. Image-guided core needle biopsy is highly accurate and is preferred to open biopsy; transverse incisions, flap elevation and sampling the often-necrotic centre are all errors that increase seeding or give non-diagnostic tissue.
learningPoint: Plan the biopsy as part of the definitive operation: longitudinal tract, no flaps, sited and ideally done by the tumour unit.

stem: A 68-year-old man presents with a destructive lytic lesion of the proximal femur and a metabolic screen is being arranged. Which group of primary cancers most commonly accounts for skeletal metastases in adults?

options.label: A
options.text: Stomach, colon, pancreas, ovary and bladder

options.label: B
options.text: Breast, lung, kidney, prostate and thyroid

options.label: C
options.text: Melanoma, glioma, hepatocellular carcinoma and testis

options.label: D
options.text: Oesophagus, larynx, cervix and endometrium

options.label: E
options.text: Adrenal, parathyroid, pituitary and pineal tumours

explanation: Secondary deposits are far more frequent than primary bone tumours in adults, and five carcinomas dominate the picture: breast, bronchus (lung), kidney (renal), prostate and thyroid. Remembering this short list directs the metastatic screen (for example PSA, breast and chest evaluation) and the search for an unknown primary. Renal and thyroid deposits are characteristically very vascular, which is relevant to surgical planning.
learningPoint: The classic bone-seeking primaries are breast, bronchus, kidney, prostate and thyroid.

stem: A woman with known breast cancer has a painful lytic deposit in the subtrochanteric femur measuring more than two-thirds of the bone width, and she reports severe pain. Using the Mirels scoring system, what is the most appropriate management of this lesion?

options.label: A
options.text: Reassurance and simple analgesia alone

options.label: B
options.text: Radiotherapy with no consideration of surgery

options.label: C
options.text: Prophylactic fixation of the whole bone

options.label: D
options.text: Six weeks of protected weight-bearing then reassessment

options.label: E
options.text: Bisphosphonate infusion as the sole intervention

explanation: Mirels scores four variables (site, size, radiographic nature and pain) from one to three each. A peritrochanteric site (3), lesion over two-thirds width (3), lytic pattern (3) and severe pain (3) give a maximum of 12, well above the threshold of 9 at which fracture risk is around a third and prophylactic stabilisation of the entire bone is advised. Fixing prophylactically is technically easier and gives better function than fixing after a pathological fracture; radiotherapy is a useful adjunct but does not remove the mechanical risk.
learningPoint: A Mirels score of 9 or more indicates prophylactic fixation of the whole bone before the lesion fractures.

stem: A 16-year-old boy describes deep aching pain in his thigh that is worst at night and is reliably abolished by aspirin. Radiographs show a small lucent nidus in the femoral cortex surrounded by dense reactive sclerosis. Which feature best explains the dramatic response to aspirin?

options.label: A
options.text: The lesion is highly vascular and aspirin reduces local blood flow

options.label: B
options.text: The nidus produces prostaglandins, and aspirin inhibits their synthesis

options.label: C
options.text: Aspirin dissolves the calcified matrix of the nidus

options.label: D
options.text: The pain is purely psychological and responds to any analgesic

options.label: E
options.text: Aspirin reverses the reactive scoliosis that causes the pain

explanation: Osteoid osteoma classically affects young people (male predominance) and gives night pain that responds well to aspirin or other NSAIDs because the nidus secretes prostaglandins that mediate the pain. The radiographic hallmark is a small lucent nidus within marked cortical sclerosis. Many burn out with time, so non-operative treatment is reasonable; if intervention is needed, CT-guided radiofrequency ablation or curettage of the nidus is used. Aspirin is avoided in young children because of Reye's syndrome.
learningPoint: Osteoid osteoma = cortical nidus with sclerosis, night pain relieved by NSAIDs (prostaglandin-mediated).

stem: A 30-year-old man with a long-standing bony lump near the knee, present since childhood, reports that it has recently started to enlarge again and has become painful. MRI of a known osteochondroma shows a cartilage cap measuring 2 cm in thickness. What does this finding most strongly suggest?

options.label: A
options.text: Normal maturation of the lesion after skeletal maturity

options.label: B
options.text: Malignant transformation to a secondary chondrosarcoma

options.label: C
options.text: An associated overlying bursitis only

options.label: D
options.text: Reactivation due to a recent fracture through the stalk

options.label: E
options.text: Conversion to an osteosarcoma

explanation: An osteochondroma should stop growing once the physes close, so new growth or pain in an adult is a warning sign. A cartilage cap thicker than roughly 1.5 to 2 cm on MRI raises strong concern for malignant transformation, which is to a secondary (low-grade) chondrosarcoma rather than osteosarcoma. The overall risk of transformation in a solitary lesion is small (around 1%) but is higher in multiple hereditary exostoses, so growth after maturity warrants referral and imaging.
learningPoint: Growth after skeletal maturity or a cartilage cap over ~1.5 cm in an osteochondroma suggests secondary chondrosarcoma.

stem: A 28-year-old woman has an expansile lytic lesion in the distal radius that is eccentrically placed and extends right up to the subchondral bone of the articular surface, in a skeletally mature patient. Which diagnosis is most consistent with these features?

options.label: A
options.text: Chondroblastoma

options.label: B
options.text: Giant cell tumour of bone

options.label: C
options.text: Simple (unicameral) bone cyst

options.label: D
options.text: Osteoid osteoma

options.label: E
options.text: Fibrous cortical defect

explanation: Giant cell tumour characteristically arises in the skeletally mature patient, begins in the metaphysis and extends across the closed physis to lie eccentrically right up to the subchondral surface. Around half occur about the knee, with the distal radius and proximal humerus also typical. It is a locally aggressive benign lesion with a notable recurrence rate and a small risk of lung deposits, so it is usually treated by extended curettage with cement or graft. Chondroblastoma is the analogous epiphyseal lesion but occurs in the immature skeleton.
learningPoint: GCT: skeletally mature, eccentric metaphyseal lesion reaching the subchondral bone; locally aggressive with recurrence risk.

stem: A 10-year-old sustains a low-energy fracture through a centrally placed, well-defined lytic lesion with a thin sclerotic rim in the proximal humerus. A fragment of cortex is seen lying within the lucent cavity. What is this radiographic sign and the most likely diagnosis?

options.label: A
options.text: Codman triangle, indicating osteosarcoma

options.label: B
options.text: Fallen fragment sign, indicating a simple (unicameral) bone cyst

options.label: C
options.text: Fluid-fluid levels, indicating an aneurysmal bone cyst

options.label: D
options.text: Onion-skin reaction, indicating Ewing sarcoma

options.label: E
options.text: Ground-glass matrix, indicating fibrous dysplasia

explanation: A simple bone cyst typically occurs in the proximal humerus or proximal femur of a skeletally immature child and shows benign features: a central, well-circumscribed lucency with a thin sclerotic rim and a narrow zone of transition. When it fractures, a piece of cortex can drop into the fluid-filled cavity, producing the pathognomonic fallen fragment sign. Many heal after fracture or with observation; aspiration and steroid or marrow injection and curettage with graft are options. The fallen fragment confirms the lesion is hollow.
learningPoint: The fallen fragment sign is pathognomonic of a fluid-filled simple bone cyst.

stem: A patient with multiple enchondromas of the hands is found also to have multiple soft-tissue vascular malformations. Compared with a solitary enchondroma, what is the most important implication of this combination?

options.label: A
options.text: It is Ollier disease, with essentially no increased malignancy risk

options.label: B
options.text: It is Maffucci syndrome, carrying a very high lifetime risk of malignant transformation

options.label: C
options.text: It is McCune-Albright syndrome, with precocious puberty

options.label: D
options.text: It is multiple hereditary exostoses, with forearm deformity

options.label: E
options.text: It is hereditary and the lesions always regress at puberty

explanation: A solitary enchondroma, usually in a phalanx, is generally benign. Multiple enchondromas alone constitute Ollier disease, which carries an appreciable (around 30%) risk of malignant change. When multiple enchondromas occur together with soft-tissue vascular malformations the diagnosis is Maffucci syndrome, in which the risk of malignant transformation approaches very high levels, and there is also an increased risk of visceral and CNS malignancies. These patients need long-term surveillance, sometimes including whole-body MRI.
learningPoint: Multiple enchondromas = Ollier (~30% malignancy); with vascular malformations = Maffucci (near-total malignancy risk).

stem: A 14-year-old presents with knee pain and a lytic lesion confined to the epiphysis of the proximal tibia, with patchy calcification, in an open-physis skeleton. Which lesion does this most likely represent?

options.label: A
options.text: Giant cell tumour of bone

options.label: B
options.text: Chondroblastoma

options.label: C
options.text: Non-ossifying fibroma

options.label: D
options.text: Osteosarcoma

options.label: E
options.text: Enchondroma

explanation: Chondroblastoma is the classic epiphyseal lesion of the immature skeleton, most often around the knee or proximal humerus, and roughly half show internal calcification. It is benign but locally aggressive with a high recurrence rate, treated by curettage (often with an adjuvant such as cryotherapy) and grafting. Its epiphyseal, open-physis setting distinguishes it from giant cell tumour, which characteristically occurs after physeal closure.
learningPoint: An epiphyseal lytic lesion in an immature skeleton, often calcified, is chondroblastoma.

stem: A 15-year-old has a biopsy-proven high-grade osteosarcoma of the distal femur with no detectable metastases. Within a specialist unit, what is the standard sequence of definitive treatment?

options.label: A
options.text: Immediate wide surgical resection followed by radiotherapy alone

options.label: B
options.text: Neoadjuvant chemotherapy, then restaging and limb-salvage surgery, then further chemotherapy

options.label: C
options.text: Radiotherapy first, reserving surgery for non-responders

options.label: D
options.text: Primary amputation with no chemotherapy

options.label: E
options.text: Surgery alone, as osteosarcoma is chemoresistant

explanation: High-grade osteosarcoma is managed by a multidisciplinary team with neoadjuvant chemotherapy (regimens including agents such as doxorubicin, cisplatin and methotrexate) to shrink the tumour and treat micrometastases, followed by restaging and wide local resection with limb salvage wherever possible, then further (adjuvant) chemotherapy. The percentage tumour necrosis (kill rate) in the resected specimen is an important prognostic marker, with high necrosis being favourable. Osteosarcoma is relatively radioresistant, so radiotherapy is not the mainstay. Five-year survival in this setting is around half to two-thirds.
learningPoint: Osteosarcoma pathway: neoadjuvant chemo, restage, wide resection (limb salvage), adjuvant chemo; necrosis rate is prognostic.

stem: A 12-year-old has a diaphyseal femoral lesion with a layered onion-skin periosteal reaction and a large soft-tissue mass. Histology shows a small round blue cell tumour. Which genetic abnormality is most characteristically associated with this diagnosis?

options.label: A
options.text: A t(11;22) translocation

options.label: B
options.text: Loss of the retinoblastoma (RB1) gene

options.label: C
options.text: A t(X;18) translocation

options.label: D
options.text: Amplification of MDM2

options.label: E
options.text: A BCR-ABL fusion

explanation: The picture of a diaphyseal lesion with multilayered (onion-skin) periosteal reaction, a prominent soft-tissue component and small round blue cell histology in a child under 20 is typical of Ewing sarcoma, a tumour of primitive neuroectodermal origin. It is classically associated with the t(11;22) translocation. Management combines neoadjuvant and adjuvant chemotherapy with local control by surgery, and Ewing is notably radiosensitive, so radiotherapy is reserved for surgically inaccessible sites. The t(X;18) translocation is seen in synovial sarcoma.
learningPoint: Ewing sarcoma: small round blue cells, diaphyseal onion-skin reaction, t(11;22); radiosensitive.

stem: An elderly woman has multiple punched-out lytic lesions through the skull and a midshaft humeral lesion, with anaemia and back pain. Which single investigation is most likely to confirm the underlying diagnosis?

options.label: A
options.text: A radionuclide bone scan

options.label: B
options.text: Serum and urine protein studies, including Bence-Jones protein

options.label: C
options.text: Serum prostate-specific antigen

options.label: D
options.text: Thyroid function tests

options.label: E
options.text: An MRI of the affected humerus alone

explanation: Multiple punched-out lytic lesions with anaemia and bone pain in an older patient point to myeloma, a malignancy of plasma cells. Diagnosis rests on demonstrating a paraprotein, including Bence-Jones (light chain) protein in urine, together with marrow and blood findings. Because myeloma lesions are typically not osteoblastic, a conventional bone scan is often falsely negative, so a skeletal survey (or whole-body MRI) is used to map disease. Treatment is largely medical (multi-agent chemotherapy) with surgical stabilisation and radiotherapy for symptomatic or high-risk lesions; prognosis is generally poor.
learningPoint: Myeloma: punched-out lytic lesions, paraprotein and Bence-Jones protein; bone scan often negative, use skeletal survey.

stem: A patient with a solitary renal cell carcinoma metastasis in the proximal femur is being planned for surgery. Which feature of renal metastases most directly influences the surgical strategy?

options.label: A
options.text: They are typically sclerotic and very hard to drill

options.label: B
options.text: They are highly vascular, so preoperative embolisation and en bloc resection are often favoured

options.label: C
options.text: They are radiosensitive, so surgery is rarely needed

options.label: D
options.text: They almost never cause pathological fracture

options.label: E
options.text: They respond completely to bisphosphonates alone

explanation: Renal cell (and thyroid) metastases are characteristically very vascular, so intralesional surgery can provoke dramatic haemorrhage. Preoperative embolisation reduces blood loss, and because a solitary renal deposit may behave favourably, en bloc excision with reconstruction (rather than simple curettage and fixation) can offer better local control and is best managed through the tumour unit. Renal metastases are relatively radioresistant, so radiotherapy is not a reliable substitute for surgery.
learningPoint: Renal (and thyroid) metastases are hypervascular: consider preoperative embolisation and en bloc excision for solitary deposits.

subtopic: Tumour identification by age, site and radiology
stem: For each radiological vignette, select the single most likely diagnosis from the option list.

options.label: A
options.text: Osteoid osteoma

options.label: B
options.text: Giant cell tumour of bone

options.label: C
options.text: Chondroblastoma

options.label: D
options.text: Simple (unicameral) bone cyst

options.label: E
options.text: Osteosarcoma

options.label: F
options.text: Ewing sarcoma

options.label: G
options.text: Enchondroma

options.label: H
options.text: Aneurysmal bone cyst

emiLeads.stem: A 28-year-old woman has an eccentric, expansile lytic lesion in the distal radius that extends to the subchondral bone in a skeletally mature skeleton.
A 15-year-old has a destructive lesion about the knee with a permeative pattern, cortical breach, Codman triangle and sunray spiculation.
A 13-year-old has a lytic lesion confined to the epiphysis of the proximal humerus, with patchy internal calcification and open physes.
A 10-year-old fractures through a central, well-defined lucency with a thin sclerotic rim in the proximal humerus, with a fragment of cortex lying in the cavity.

explanation: These lesions are separated by age, location within the bone and radiographic character. Giant cell tumour is the mature, eccentric, subchondral metaphyseal lesion; osteosarcoma shows aggressive permeative destruction with Codman triangle and sunray spiculation about the knee; chondroblastoma is the calcified epiphyseal lesion of the immature skeleton; and the simple bone cyst is the central, benign-looking proximal humeral cyst with the fallen fragment sign. Matching age plus precise site within the bone is the key discriminator.
learningPoint: Localise within the bone (epiphysis vs metaphysis vs diaphysis) and use patient age to narrow the tumour differential.

stem: For each clinical description, select the single most appropriate associated syndrome or condition.

options.label: A
options.text: Maffucci syndrome

options.label: B
options.text: Ollier disease

options.label: C
options.text: McCune-Albright syndrome

options.label: D
options.text: Multiple hereditary exostoses

options.label: E
options.text: Secondary osteosarcoma in Paget disease

options.label: F
options.text: Maffucci with visceral malignancy

options.label: G
options.text: Mazabraud syndrome

options.label: H
options.text: Li-Fraumeni syndrome

emiLeads.stem: A young girl has polyostotic fibrous dysplasia together with precocious puberty and cafe-au-lait skin patches.
A patient has multiple cartilage tumours in the hands together with several soft-tissue vascular malformations, carrying a near-total lifetime malignancy risk.
An autosomal dominant condition producing numerous bony juxtaphyseal outgrowths, often with forearm deformity.
An elderly man with longstanding pagetoid bone develops a new aggressive permeative lesion and rising alkaline phosphatase.

explanation: Recognising these named associations is examinable. Polyostotic fibrous dysplasia with precocious puberty and cafe-au-lait pigmentation is McCune-Albright syndrome; multiple enchondromas with vascular malformations is Maffucci syndrome (very high malignancy risk), whereas multiple enchondromas alone is Ollier disease. Multiple hereditary exostoses is the autosomal dominant osteochondroma syndrome that can cause forearm deformity, and a new aggressive lesion in pagetoid bone signals secondary osteosarcoma.
learningPoint: Link the lesion to its syndrome: enchondromas + vascular malformations = Maffucci; fibrous dysplasia + precocious puberty = McCune-Albright.

stem: For each described radiographic appearance, select the single best matching term or interpretation.

options.label: A
options.text: Codman triangle

options.label: B
options.text: Onion-skin (multilayered) periosteal reaction

options.label: C
options.text: Ground-glass matrix

options.label: D
options.text: Fallen fragment sign

options.label: E
options.text: Rings-and-arcs (popcorn) chondroid matrix

options.label: F
options.text: Wide (poorly defined) zone of transition

options.label: G
options.text: Narrow (sharp) zone of transition

options.label: H
options.text: Sunray spiculation

emiLeads.stem: An elevated periosteum at the margin of an aggressive lesion forms a triangle of new bone where it lifts off the cortex.
A cartilage-forming lesion shows stippled curvilinear calcification described as rings and arcs.
A hazy, homogeneous matrix is seen within an expansile femoral lesion producing a shepherd's crook deformity.
A lesion blends imperceptibly into adjacent normal bone over a broad ill-defined margin, implying aggressive behaviour.

explanation: Describing the film systematically earns marks: the periosteal response and the zone of transition indicate biological aggressiveness. A Codman triangle marks periosteum lifted by an aggressive process; rings-and-arcs (popcorn) calcification denotes a cartilage matrix as in enchondroma or chondrosarcoma; a ground-glass matrix with a shepherd's crook deformity suggests fibrous dysplasia; and a wide zone of transition implies a fast-growing, often malignant lesion, whereas a narrow sharp margin suggests an indolent one.
learningPoint: Periosteal pattern and zone of transition gauge aggressiveness; matrix type points to the tissue of origin.

stem: For each presentation of joint or soft-tissue pathology, select the single most likely diagnosis.

options.label: A
options.text: Pigmented villonodular synovitis

options.label: B
options.text: Synovial chondromatosis

options.label: C
options.text: Soft-tissue sarcoma

options.label: D
options.text: Ochronosis (alkaptonuria)

options.label: E
options.text: Septic arthritis

options.label: F
options.text: Gout

options.label: G
options.text: Giant cell tumour of tendon sheath

options.label: H
options.text: Complex regional pain syndrome

emiLeads.stem: A young adult has recurrent atraumatic knee swelling with a rust-coloured effusion; the synovium is brown and stains for haemosiderin, with juxta-articular erosions on X-ray.
An adult has pain and reduced range of movement with multiple calcified loose bodies of similar size seen within the joint on radiographs.
At arthrotomy of a stained black-pigmented joint, the cause is excess homogentisic acid deposition from a defect of amino-acid metabolism, and the urine darkens on standing.
A patient develops disproportionate burning pain, swelling, stiffness, skin changes and patchy osteopenia after a minor injury, out of keeping with the original insult.

explanation: These conditions are distinguished by their fluid, synovium and history. Pigmented villonodular synovitis gives a rust-coloured haemosiderin-laden synovium with juxta-articular erosions; synovial chondromatosis produces multiple calcified loose bodies of roughly equal size from cartilaginous metaplasia; ochronosis blackens the joint through homogentisic acid in alkaptonuria; and complex regional pain syndrome causes disproportionate pain with autonomic and bony changes after trivial injury. Black synovium has a short differential of ochronosis versus PVNS.
learningPoint: Rust synovium = PVNS; equal-sized calcified loose bodies = synovial chondromatosis; black joint = ochronosis or PVNS.

subtopic: Staging, fracture risk and surgical margins
stem: For each statement about staging, fracture risk or surgical management of bone tumours, select the single best matching concept.

options.label: A
options.text: Enneking stage IIB

options.label: B
options.text: Enneking stage III

options.label: C
options.text: Mirels score indicating prophylactic fixation

options.label: D
options.text: Intralesional (debulking) margin

options.label: E
options.text: Wide (en bloc) margin

options.label: F
options.text: Marginal margin

options.label: G
options.text: Enneking stage IA

options.label: H
options.text: Radical (whole-compartment) margin

emiLeads.stem: A high-grade sarcoma that has breached its compartment but has no detectable distant spread.
Any malignant bone tumour, of whatever grade or local extent, once distant metastases are present.
A resection that removes the tumour together with a continuous cuff of surrounding normal tissue, giving the lowest local recurrence.
A painful, mainly lytic peritrochanteric deposit occupying more than two-thirds of the bone width, scoring 9 or more.

explanation: The Enneking (musculoskeletal tumour society) system grades malignant lesions by grade (low G1 or high G2), local extent (intracompartmental T1 or extracompartmental T2) and the presence of metastases; a high-grade extracompartmental tumour without spread is IIB, and any tumour with distant metastases becomes stage III. Surgical margins range from intralesional (high recurrence) through marginal to a wide en bloc excision with a normal tissue cuff (low recurrence). Independently, the Mirels score quantifies fracture risk in a metastatic lesion, with 9 or more indicating prophylactic fixation.
learningPoint: Enneking grades by grade/compartment/metastasis; wide margins minimise recurrence; Mirels 9+ means prophylactic fixation.

stem: A previously well 2-year-old boy is referred with a curve of the spine noticed by his parents. By the conventional age-based grouping of idiopathic scoliosis, into which category does this child fall?

options.label: A
options.text: Infantile

options.label: B
options.text: Juvenile

options.label: C
options.text: Adolescent

options.label: D
options.text: Congenital

options.label: E
options.text: Neuromuscular

explanation: Idiopathic scoliosis is conventionally divided by age at presentation: infantile under 3 years, juvenile from 3 to 10 years, and adolescent over 10 years. A 2-year-old therefore falls into the infantile group. Congenital and neuromuscular scoliosis are separate aetiological categories, not age bands, and must be excluded clinically before a curve is labelled idiopathic.
learningPoint: Idiopathic scoliosis age bands: infantile <3 yr, juvenile 3-10 yr, adolescent >10 yr.

stem: An 11-year-old girl with adolescent idiopathic scoliosis is being counselled about the likelihood of her curve worsening. Which single combination of features carries the HIGHEST risk of further curve progression?

options.label: A
options.text: Larger curve, younger age and premenarchal status with a low Risser grade

options.label: B
