options.text: Small curve in a postmenarchal girl with a high Risser grade

options.label: C
options.text: Moderate curve in a boy who has completed his growth spurt

options.label: D
options.text: Small curve with a closed triradiate cartilage

options.label: E
options.text: Moderate curve two years after menarche

explanation: The strongest predictors of curve progression in idiopathic scoliosis are a larger curve magnitude, younger chronological age, marked skeletal immaturity (Risser 0-1) and being premenarchal. These factors cluster in the patient with the most remaining spinal growth. Conversely, a small curve in a skeletally mature, postmenarchal patient has little tendency to progress.
learningPoint: Progression risk rises with curve size, young age, premenarchal status and skeletal immaturity (Risser 0-1).

stem: When assessing skeletal maturity on a standing radiograph of a child with scoliosis, the Risser sign is based on the ossification and fusion of which structure?

options.label: A
options.text: The iliac crest apophysis

options.label: B
options.text: The vertebral ring apophysis

options.label: C
options.text: The triradiate cartilage of the acetabulum

options.label: D
options.text: The proximal femoral physis

options.label: E
options.text: The ischial tuberosity apophysis

explanation: The Risser sign grades ossification of the iliac crest apophysis from grade 0 to grade 5 as it progresses from the anterolateral crest to the posteromedial crest and then fuses. Grade 0 corresponds to the curve-acceleration phase of growth, whereas higher grades indicate that little spinal growth remains. The vertebral ring apophysis and triradiate cartilage are separate maturity markers; closure of the triradiate cartilage in particular signals that the adolescent growth spurt is near its peak.
learningPoint: The Risser grade (0-5) reflects ossification then fusion of the iliac crest apophysis; grade 0 marks the curve-acceleration phase.

stem: While measuring a scoliotic curve using the Cobb method, you must first identify the end (transitional) vertebrae. Which statement best describes the end vertebra of a curve?

options.label: A
options.text: The vertebra at the top and bottom of the curve that is most tilted into the curve and least rotated

options.label: B
options.text: The most rotated and most laterally displaced vertebra of the curve

options.label: C
options.text: The vertebra showing the greatest wedging on the convex side

options.label: D
options.text: The vertebra lying at the apex of the curve with the least tilt

options.label: E
options.text: The first non-rotated vertebra caudal to the pelvis

explanation: The end (transitional) vertebrae are the most tilted vertebrae at the upper and lower limits of the curve and are the least rotated and least displaced. Lines are drawn along the superior endplate of the upper end vertebra and the inferior endplate of the lower end vertebra, and the Cobb angle is the angle subtended between them. The apical vertebra, by contrast, is the most rotated and most laterally displaced vertebra and shows the least tilt.
learningPoint: End vertebrae are the most tilted, least rotated vertebrae; the apical vertebra is the most rotated and displaced with least tilt.

stem: A 13-year-old is diagnosed with idiopathic-appearing thoracic scoliosis. Which single feature should most strongly prompt MRI of the whole spine to exclude an underlying neural axis abnormality?

options.label: A
options.text: A left-sided (levoscoliotic) thoracic curve

options.label: B
options.text: A right-sided thoracic curve apex at T8

options.label: C
options.text: A Risser grade of 4 at presentation

options.label: D
options.text: A Cobb angle of 18 degrees

options.label: E
options.text: A positive family history of mild scoliosis

explanation: Typical adolescent idiopathic scoliosis produces a right thoracic curve. An atypical left thoracic (or thoracolumbar) curve is a recognised red flag for an intraspinal abnormality such as a syrinx, tethered cord or tumour and warrants MRI. Other red flags include juvenile onset, rapid progression, any abnormal neurology, excessive apical kyphosis and associated congenital syndromes.
learningPoint: Atypical (left) thoracic curves, neurology, juvenile onset, rapid progression and apical kyphosis are red flags mandating MRI.

stem: Compared with adolescent idiopathic scoliosis, which feature is MOST characteristic of a neuromuscular curve (for example in a boy with Duchenne muscular dystrophy)?

options.label: A
options.text: A long curve involving many segments that may continue to progress after skeletal maturity and is associated with pelvic obliquity

options.label: B
options.text: A short curve with well-developed compensatory curves that stabilises at maturity

options.label: C
options.text: A right thoracic curve with normal pulmonary function

options.label: D
options.text: A curve that reliably arrests once the iliac apophysis has fused

options.label: E
options.text: An absence of any associated bony or cervical deformity

explanation: Neuromuscular curves are typically long, involve more vertebrae, have fewer compensatory curves and tend to progress more rapidly and even continue after skeletal maturity. They are commonly associated with pelvic obliquity, other bony deformities, cervical involvement and worse pulmonary compromise. These features, together with poor bone quality and a delayed bone age, also explain the higher complication rates of surgery in this group.
learningPoint: Neuromuscular curves are long, multi-segment, pelvic-obliquity-associated and may progress beyond skeletal maturity.

stem: A neonate is found to have a hemivertebra producing congenital scoliosis. Which group of systems is most important to screen because of recognised associated anomalies?

options.label: A
options.text: Genitourinary tract, heart and the neural axis (intraspinal dysraphism)

options.label: B
options.text: Liver, spleen and pancreas

options.label: C
options.text: Thyroid, adrenal and pituitary glands

options.label: D
options.text: Inner ear, eye and facial skeleton only

options.label: E
options.text: Lower limb arteries and peripheral nerves only

explanation: Congenital vertebral anomalies arise during the same embryonic window as several other organ systems, so associated defects are common: genitourinary anomalies in around a quarter of cases, cardiac anomalies in roughly one in ten, and intraspinal (dysraphic) anomalies in about a quarter, most often diastematomyelia. For this reason renal tract imaging, echocardiography and spinal MRI are appropriate. MRI of the neural axis is also mandatory before any corrective surgery.
learningPoint: Screen congenital scoliosis for renal (~25%), cardiac (~10%) and dysraphic (~25%, often diastematomyelia) anomalies.

stem: Which single congenital vertebral anomaly carries the worst prognosis, producing the most rapid and relentless curve progression?

options.label: A
options.text: A unilateral unsegmented bar with a contralateral hemivertebra

options.label: B
options.text: An incarcerated hemivertebra

options.label: C
options.text: A non-segmented (block) hemivertebra

options.label: D
options.text: A solitary, fully segmented hemivertebra

options.label: E
options.text: A partially segmented hemivertebra

explanation: Progression in congenital scoliosis is governed by the balance of growth across the deformed segment. A unilateral unsegmented bar tethers one side while a contralateral hemivertebra adds unopposed growth on the other, giving the most rapid and relentless deterioration and usually warranting early posterior fusion. By contrast, an incarcerated or non-segmented hemivertebra has little growth potential and progresses minimally, often justifying observation.
learningPoint: A unilateral unsegmented bar with contralateral hemivertebra is the worst congenital anomaly; non-segmented/incarcerated types progress least.

stem: In congenital kyphosis, which type carries the greatest risk of progression and of neurological deficit, making it the type most likely to cause paraplegia?

options.label: A
options.text: Type I - failure of vertebral body formation

options.label: B
options.text: Type II - failure of segmentation

options.label: C
options.text: Type III - mixed defect

options.label: D
options.text: Postural kyphosis

options.label: E
options.text: Scheuermann's kyphosis

explanation: Congenital kyphosis is classified by the embryological defect: type I is failure of formation of the vertebral body, type II is failure of segmentation and type III is mixed. Type I has the worst prognosis because the sharp, progressive angular kyphos directly threatens the cord, carrying the highest risk of paraplegia, so a significant type I deformity is an indication for early surgery. Neurofibromatosis is the second most common cause of a progressive kyphosis with neurological risk.
learningPoint: Congenital kyphosis type I (failure of formation) has the worst prognosis and highest paraplegia risk; treat early.

stem: A 15-year-old competitive fast bowler complains of activity-related low back pain made worse by arching backwards. A pars interarticularis stress lesion is suspected. Which statement is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: It is a stress fracture of the pars typically caused by repetitive lumbar hyperextension, and a pars defect without slip is initially managed non-operatively

options.label: B
options.text: It is a congenital absence of the pedicle and always requires immediate fusion

options.label: C
options.text: It only occurs once a high-grade slip is already present

options.label: D
options.text: Plain flexion-extension views are diagnostic and cross-sectional imaging is never helpful

options.label: E
options.text: It invariably progresses to spondyloptosis if untreated

explanation: Spondylolysis is a stress (fatigue) fracture of the pars interarticularis, classically seen in adolescents undertaking repetitive lumbar hyperextension such as fast bowling, gymnastics or batting. CT best defines the bony defect. A pars lesion without vertebral slip is initially treated non-operatively with activity modification; if symptoms persist, direct pars repair is favoured at L4 or above while L5 lesions are usually managed by fusion.
learningPoint: Spondylolysis is a pars stress fracture from repetitive hyperextension; image with CT and treat without slip conservatively first.

stem: On a standing lateral radiograph a child's L5 vertebral body has translated forwards on S1 by approximately 90 percent of the sacral endplate width. Using the Meyerding classification, what grade is this slip?

options.label: A
options.text: Grade 4

options.label: B
options.text: Grade 1

options.label: C
options.text: Grade 2

options.label: D
options.text: Grade 3

options.label: E
options.text: Grade 5 (spondyloptosis)

explanation: The Meyerding system divides the percentage anterior translation of the upper vertebra on the one below into quartiles: grade 1 is 0-25 percent, grade 2 is 25-50 percent, grade 3 is 50-75 percent and grade 4 is 75-100 percent. A slip of about 90 percent is therefore grade 4. Complete translation so that the vertebra drops off the front of the sacrum is grade 5, or spondyloptosis.
learningPoint: Meyerding grades slip by 25% bands; grade 4 = 75-100%, grade 5 = spondyloptosis.

stem: A 14-year-old boy presents with a poor posture and a rigid thoracic hump that does not correct on extension, with pain over the apex. Examination shows notably tight hamstrings. Which finding most supports a diagnosis of Scheuermann's kyphosis rather than postural round-back?

options.label: A
options.text: A fixed (non-correctable) hyperkyphosis with anterior vertebral wedging and Schmorl's nodes on radiographs

options.label: B
options.text: A smoothly correctable kyphosis that reverses fully on hyperextension

options.label: C
options.text: Normal vertebral endplates with no wedging

options.label: D
options.text: An acute angular kyphos due to a single absent vertebral body

options.label: E
options.text: A left thoracic scoliosis with intraspinal cyst

explanation: Scheuermann's kyphosis is a structural, rigid thoracic hyperkyphosis that, unlike postural round-back, does not correct on extension. Radiographs show anterior wedging of consecutive apical vertebrae together with endplate irregularity and Schmorl's nodes. Affected adolescents, more often boys, characteristically have tight hamstrings and apical or lumbar pain; significant neurological problems from disc herniation or extradural cysts are rare.
learningPoint: Scheuermann's = rigid thoracic kyphosis with anterior wedging, Schmorl's nodes and tight hamstrings; postural round-back corrects and lacks wedging.

stem: A 5-year-old who fell from a low wall has a lateral cervical radiograph showing about 3 mm of anterior translation of C2 on C3. He has minimal pain, no prevertebral soft-tissue swelling and the deformity reduces on extension. Which test best confirms this is benign physiological pseudosubluxation?

options.label: A
options.text: The posterior cervical (Swischuk) line passes within about 2 mm of the spinolaminar point of C2

options.label: B
options.text: The atlanto-dens interval exceeds 5 mm

options.label: C
options.text: There is marked widening of the prevertebral soft-tissue shadow

options.label: D
options.text: The translation increases on extension and fails to reduce

options.label: E
options.text: The basion-dens interval is increased

explanation: Pseudosubluxation of C2 on C3 (and sometimes C3 on C4) is a normal finding in children under about 8 years owing to horizontally oriented facet joints and ligamentous laxity. It is benign when the posterior cervical (Swischuk) line, drawn between the spinolaminar points of C1 and C3, passes within roughly 2 mm of the spinolaminar cortex of C2, the alignment is otherwise maintained, prevertebral swelling is absent and the displacement reduces on extension. Disruption of this line, an increased atlanto-dens interval or prevertebral swelling instead suggests a true injury.
learningPoint: Pseudosubluxation is benign if the Swischuk line is intact, there is no prevertebral swelling and the slip reduces on extension.

stem: For each described congenital vertebral anomaly, select the single most appropriate description of its natural history or initial management. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Rapid, relentless progression - the highest-risk pattern

options.label: B
options.text: Steady progression typically treated by hemivertebra excision or anterior and posterior fusion

options.label: C
options.text: Little or no progression - observation appropriate

options.label: D
options.text: Rapid progression from a one-sided growth tether without an added growth focus

options.label: E
options.text: Slow possible progression with observation usually appropriate

options.label: F
options.text: A failure-of-formation kyphos with the highest risk of paraplegia

options.label: G
options.text: A benign age-related radiographic appearance needing no treatment

options.label: H
options.text: An intraspinal bony cleft requiring resection before deformity correction

emiLeads.stem: A unilateral unsegmented bar with a contralateral hemivertebra on the opposite side of the same segment.
A solitary, fully segmented hemivertebra with open disc spaces on both sides.
A non-segmented (block) hemivertebra fused to the vertebrae above and below.
A unilateral unsegmented bar alone, without a contralateral hemivertebra.

explanation: Congenital scoliosis progresses according to the growth imbalance across the deformed segment. A unilateral unsegmented bar combined with a contralateral hemivertebra produces the worst, most rapid deterioration. A bar alone tethers one side and also progresses quickly, though without the additional convex growth focus. A fully segmented hemivertebra has open physes on both sides and progresses steadily, often treated by excision or fusion, whereas a non-segmented hemivertebra has minimal growth potential and can be observed.
learningPoint: Rank congenital anomalies by growth balance: bar plus contralateral hemivertebra (worst) > bar alone > fully segmented hemivertebra > non-segmented/incarcerated (least).

stem: For each clinical or radiographic scenario involving the paediatric cervical spine, select the single most likely diagnosis. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Klippel-Feil syndrome

options.label: B
options.text: Atlantoaxial instability

options.label: C
options.text: Os odontoideum

options.label: D
options.text: Physiological pseudosubluxation

options.label: E
options.text: Basilar invagination

options.label: F
options.text: Intervertebral disc calcification

options.label: G
options.text: Diastematomyelia

options.label: H
options.text: Postlaminectomy kyphosis

emiLeads.stem: A child with a short webbed neck, low posterior hairline and restricted neck movement; radiographs show congenital fusion of several cervical vertebrae.
A child with bony deformity at the skull base; sagittal MRI shows the odontoid migrating up into the foramen magnum and indenting the brainstem, with limb weakness and hydrocephalus.

A previously well child with neck pain, reduced movement and low-grade fever; radiographs show calcification within the annulus of a cervical disc without bony erosion, and symptoms settle without surgery.
A young child after minor trauma with about 3 mm forward slip of C2 on C3 that reduces on extension, an intact Swischuk line and no prevertebral swelling.

explanation: Klippel-Feil syndrome is congenital cervical vertebral fusion, classically with a short webbed neck, low hairline and reduced movement. Basilar invagination is cephalad migration of the dens into the foramen magnum from a skull-base deformity, compressing the brainstem and sometimes causing hydrocephalus. Cervical disc calcification is a self-limiting condition with annular calcification and no erosion. Pseudosubluxation is a benign physiological C2-on-C3 slip in young children that reduces on extension with a preserved Swischuk line.
learningPoint: Distinguish paediatric C-spine entities: Klippel-Feil (fusion), basilar invagination (dens in foramen magnum), disc calcification (self-limiting), pseudosubluxation (benign, reduces on extension).

stem: Each child presents with low back pain. From the list, select the single most likely diagnosis. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Spondylolysis

options.label: B
options.text: Discitis / vertebral osteomyelitis

options.label: C
options.text: Osteoid osteoma

options.label: D
options.text: Herniated nucleus pulposus

options.label: E
options.text: Scheuermann's kyphosis

options.label: F
options.text: Eosinophilic granuloma

options.label: G
options.text: Ewing sarcoma

options.label: H
options.text: Tethered cord

emiLeads.stem: A 14-year-old gymnast with several months of activity-related back pain that is worse on lumbar extension; CT shows a defect in the pars interarticularis of L5 without any vertebral slip.
A 3-year-old who refuses to walk and is reluctant to bend, with a low-grade fever, raised inflammatory markers and disc-space narrowing on imaging.
A 12-year-old with constant night-time back pain that is dramatically relieved by ibuprofen; CT shows a small lucent nidus with surrounding sclerosis in a posterior element.
A 15-year-old with a rigid thoracic hump, apical pain and tight hamstrings, whose radiographs show anterior wedging of consecutive vertebrae with Schmorl's nodes.

explanation: Spondylolysis is a pars stress fracture in an adolescent doing repetitive hyperextension, with a pars defect but no slip. Discitis or vertebral osteomyelitis in a young child causes refusal to walk, fever, raised inflammatory markers and disc-space narrowing. The night pain relieved by NSAIDs with a lucent nidus is the classic picture of osteoid osteoma. A rigid wedged thoracic kyphosis with Schmorl's nodes and tight hamstrings indicates Scheuermann's disease. Persistent or painful back symptoms in children must always be investigated rather than dismissed.
learningPoint: Key paediatric back pain causes: spondylolysis (pars, no slip), discitis (toddler, fever), osteoid osteoma (night pain, NSAID-responsive), Scheuermann's (wedged kyphosis).

stem: Match each described feature used in the assessment of scoliosis to the single best term. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Apical vertebra

options.label: B
options.text: End (transitional) vertebra

options.label: C
options.text: Risser grade

options.label: D
options.text: Rib-vertebral angle difference (RVAD)

options.label: E
options.text: Triradiate cartilage closure

options.label: F
options.text: Cobb angle

options.label: G
options.text: Lenke classification

options.label: H
options.text: Vertebral ring apophysis

emiLeads.stem: The vertebra that is most rotated and most laterally displaced from the midline, lying at the centre of the curve with the least tilt.
A grading of ossification of the iliac crest apophysis used to estimate remaining spinal growth.
The angle subtended between lines drawn along the superior endplate of the upper end vertebra and the inferior endplate of the lower end vertebra.
A measurement at the apex comparing the rib angles on the concave and convex sides, used to predict whether an infantile curve will progress.

explanation: The apical vertebra is the most rotated and displaced vertebra at the curve centre with the least tilt, whereas the end vertebrae define the curve limits. The Risser grade estimates remaining growth from ossification of the iliac apophysis. The Cobb angle is measured between lines along the end-vertebra endplates. The rib-vertebral angle difference, measured at the apex, helps predict progression of infantile curves. Closure of the triradiate cartilage is a separate maturity marker indicating that the growth spurt is near its peak.
learningPoint: Know the core scoliosis terms: apical vs end vertebra, Risser grade, Cobb angle and the RVAD for infantile-curve progression.

stem: For each described situation in paediatric spinal deformity, select the single most appropriate management concept or technique. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Growing-rod construct lengthened periodically

options.label: B
options.text: Combined anterior and posterior fusion to prevent the crankshaft phenomenon

options.label: C
options.text: Galveston pelvic fixation

options.label: D
options.text: Resect the intraspinal bar before correcting the deformity

options.label: E
options.text: Observation with serial radiographs

options.label: F
options.text: Direct pars repair

options.label: G
options.text: Modified Milwaukee brace

options.label: H
options.text: Early stabilisation with anterior and posterior fusion after the procedure

emiLeads.stem: A 4-year-old with a progressive early-onset curve in whom continued controlled spinal growth must be preserved while the curve is partially corrected and adjusted every six months.
A skeletally very immature child undergoing posterior-only fusion for scoliosis in whom continued anterior vertebral growth could rotate the spine back into deformity.
A child with congenital scoliosis and a bony spur splitting the cord (diastematomyelia) who is scheduled for deformity correction.
A child who has undergone a multilevel cervical or thoracic laminectomy for an intraspinal tumour, who is at high risk of a severe progressive kyphosis.

explanation: Growing-rod constructs span an early-onset curve and are lengthened periodically to allow continued spinal growth before definitive fusion. In a very immature child a posterior-only fusion risks the crankshaft phenomenon, where ongoing anterior growth re-creates rotational deformity, so anterior plus posterior fusion is added. A diastematomyelia bar must be resected before any corrective manoeuvre to avoid cord injury. After multilevel laminectomy the spine is prone to a severe post-laminectomy kyphosis, so early stabilisation and fusion is advocated.
learningPoint: Match technique to problem: growing rods (early-onset, preserve growth), A/P fusion (prevent crankshaft), resect bar before correcting diastematomyelia, stabilise after laminectomy.

stem: During a posterior approach to the hip, the structure that most reliably identifies the level at which the sciatic nerve emerges from the pelvis is which muscle?

options.label: A
options.text: Obturator internus

options.label: B
options.text: Piriformis

options.label: C
options.text: Quadratus femoris

options.label: D
options.text: Gluteus minimus

options.label: E
options.text: Superior gemellus

explanation: In the overwhelming majority of people the sciatic nerve leaves the greater sciatic foramen immediately beneath the lower edge of piriformis, making this muscle the key surgical landmark when protecting the nerve during a posterior approach. The superior gluteal nerve and vessels, by contrast, run above piriformis. Knowing this relationship allows the surgeon to develop the short external rotators safely and to position retractors away from the nerve.
learningPoint: Piriformis is the landmark for the sciatic nerve, which typically exits just below its inferior border.

stem: A surgeon plans an anterior (Smith-Petersen) approach to the hip and wishes to protect the lateral cutaneous nerve of the thigh. Where is this nerve most consistently found?

options.label: A
options.text: Roughly two centimetres distal and medial to the anterior superior iliac spine

options.label: B
options.text: Immediately lateral to the femoral artery at the groin crease

options.label: C
options.text: Crossing superficial to the pubic tubercle

options.label: D
options.text: Deep to the rectus femoris on the anterior capsule

options.label: E
options.text: Two centimetres proximal and lateral to the anterior superior iliac spine

explanation: The lateral cutaneous nerve of the thigh arises from the lumbar plexus (L2-3), crosses iliacus and runs close to the anterior superior iliac spine, characteristically about two centimetres distal and medial to it, before passing beneath the inguinal ligament and over sartorius. It is at particular risk during the anterior approach and during anterior iliac crest bone graft harvest. Injury produces meralgia paraesthetica with altered sensation over the anterolateral thigh.
learningPoint: The lateral cutaneous nerve of thigh lies about 2 cm distal and medial to the ASIS and is vulnerable in anterior hip surgery.

stem: In a healthy adult, which vessel provides the dominant blood supply to the femoral head?

options.label: A
options.text: Artery of the ligamentum teres

options.label: B
options.text: Lateral femoral circumflex artery

options.label: C
options.text: Medial femoral circumflex artery

options.label: D
options.text: First perforating branch of profunda femoris

options.label: E
options.text: Inferior gluteal artery

explanation: Throughout adult life the principal supply to the femoral head is the retinacular (capsular) system derived from the medial femoral circumflex artery, whose ascending branch reaches the head via the posterosuperior retinacular vessels. The artery of the ligamentum teres contributes only a small and highly variable amount in adults, and the metaphyseal contribution has disappeared. This explains why complete division of quadratus femoris in the posterior approach and displaced intracapsular fractures threaten head viability.
learningPoint: The medial femoral circumflex artery (via posterosuperior retinacular vessels) is the main adult femoral head supply.

stem: Why is a child aged around 5 to 9 years vulnerable to Perthes-type avascular changes of the femoral head?

options.label: A
options.text: The ligamentum teres vessels have not yet developed

options.label: B
options.text: Transphyseal vessels freely cross the physis at this age

options.label: C
options.text: Metaphyseal vessels still dominate and bypass the epiphysis

options.label: D
options.text: The growth plate acts as a barrier and supply depends largely on the lateral epiphyseal (retinacular) vessels

options.label: E
options.text: The capsular vessels have not yet formed

explanation: After the first year the physis acts as a barrier preventing metaphyseal vessels from crossing into the epiphysis, and by mid-childhood the metaphyseal contribution has regressed so that the head relies heavily on the lateral epiphyseal vessels carried in the posterosuperior retinaculum. With the ligamentum teres contribution still minimal at this stage, any compromise of these lateral epiphyseal vessels can render the head avascular, the situation seen in Perthes disease. This watershed period explains the age distribution of the condition.
learningPoint: Between roughly 4 and 9 years the head depends mainly on lateral epiphyseal vessels, predisposing to Perthes.

stem: A patient with a painful hip is advised to use a walking stick. To gain the greatest reduction in joint reaction force, in which hand should the stick be held?

options.label: A
options.text: The hand on the same side as the affected hip

options.label: B
options.text: The hand on the opposite side to the affected hip

options.label: C
options.text: Either hand, with no biomechanical difference

options.label: D
options.text: Held centrally in front using both hands

options.label: E
options.text: The dominant hand regardless of the affected side

explanation: A stick in the contralateral hand acts at a long moment arm across the body and generates an upward force that supplements the hip abductors, so the abductor muscle force required for single-leg stance falls and with it the joint reaction force. Holding it on the same side would shorten the lever and be far less effective. Other strategies that unload the joint include weight loss, increasing femoral offset, medialising the hip centre and carrying a load in the ipsilateral hand to balance the body moment.
learningPoint: A walking stick reduces hip joint reaction force most when held in the hand opposite the affected hip.

stem: Femoral offset is best defined as which of the following?

options.label: A
options.text: The vertical distance from the centre of the femoral head to the tip of the greater trochanter

options.label: B
options.text: The perpendicular distance from the centre of rotation of the femoral head to the long axis of the femoral shaft

options.label: C
options.text: The angle between the femoral neck and shaft

options.label: D
options.text: The distance between the two femoral heads on an AP pelvis

options.label: E
options.text: The length of the femoral neck along its own axis

explanation: Offset is the perpendicular distance between the centre of rotation of the femoral head and the longitudinal axis of the femoral shaft. Restoring or increasing it lengthens the abductor lever arm, which lowers the abductor force and the joint reaction force, and it tensions the soft tissues to reduce dislocation risk without altering leg length. Excessive reduction of offset can lead to impingement, instability and a limp, while over-lengthening of offset increases torsional stress on the implant.
learningPoint: Offset is the perpendicular distance from head centre to the femoral shaft axis; restoring it optimises the abductor lever arm and stability.

stem: For reliable bony ingrowth into a porous-coated uncemented femoral stem, the gap between the implant surface and host bone and the degree of micromotion should ideally remain below which limit?

options.label: A
options.text: Around 50 micrometres of gap and under 150 micrometres of micromotion

options.label: B
options.text: Around 500 micrometres of gap and under 1000 micrometres of micromotion

options.label: C
options.text: Around 5 micrometres of gap and under 10 micrometres of micromotion

options.label: D
options.text: Around 2 millimetres of gap and under 5 millimetres of micromotion

options.label: E
options.text: Gap and micromotion are not relevant to ingrowth

explanation: Bony ingrowth requires pore sizes of approximately 50 to 150 micrometres, implant-bone gaps under about 50 micrometres and micromotion limited to roughly 150 micrometres or less; beyond this threshold fibrous tissue forms instead of bone and the construct loosens. Initial stability is achieved by a press-fit that generates hoop stresses, or by a line-to-line technique with supplementary screws, while hydroxyapatite coatings are osteoconductive and encourage faster ingrowth. Cortical seating gives more reliable fixation than relying on weaker cancellous bone.
learningPoint: Successful osseointegration needs gaps under ~50 micrometres and micromotion under ~150 micrometres, otherwise fibrous fixation occurs.

stem: A well-fixed distally coated uncemented stem shows proximal femoral osteopenia on follow-up radiographs. This finding is best explained by which mechanism?

options.label: A
options.text: Particle-induced osteolysis from polyethylene wear

options.label: B
options.text: Low-grade infection

options.label: C
options.text: Stress shielding because load is transferred distally, unloading the proximal bone

options.label: D
options.text: Aseptic loosening of the stem

options.label: E
options.text: Heterotopic ossification of the abductors

explanation: When a stiff stem is solidly fixed distally (the spot-welded appearance), physiological load bypasses the proximal femur and is transmitted to the diaphysis, so by Wolff's law the unloaded proximal bone resorbs and becomes osteopenic. The effect is governed by implant stiffness (elastic modulus, where cobalt-chrome exceeds stainless steel which exceeds titanium, and which rises steeply with stem radius), by the extent and location of the coating and by the alloy used. Proximal porous coating concentrates fixation in the metaphysis and helps preserve proximal bone.
learningPoint: Distal fixation of a stiff stem unloads the proximal femur, causing stress-shielding osteopenia.

stem: Using the acetabular quadrant system (a line from the ASIS to the centre of the acetabulum and a second perpendicular to it), which quadrant is safest for placing supplementary acetabular fixation screws?

options.label: A
options.text: Anterosuperior quadrant

options.label: B
options.text: Anteroinferior quadrant

options.label: C
options.text: Posterosuperior quadrant

options.label: D
options.text: Posteroinferior quadrant

options.label: E
options.text: Screws may be placed safely in any quadrant

explanation: The posterosuperior quadrant offers the deepest, most reliable bone stock and is the preferred zone for acetabular screws. The anterosuperior quadrant is the so-called zone of death because screws here threaten the external iliac vessels, while both inferior quadrants risk the obturator neurovascular structures. The surgeon must also remember that elevating the hip centre during revision can bring the sciatic nerve into the posterosuperior quadrant, and that in a high hip centre the structures normally anterior may be displaced posteriorly.
learningPoint: The posterosuperior acetabular quadrant is the safe zone for screws; the anterosuperior quadrant endangers the external iliac vessels.

stem: When considering metal-on-polyethylene bearings, why is a 28 mm femoral head often chosen as a compromise between a 22 mm and a 32 mm head?

options.label: A
options.text: Larger heads reduce volumetric wear while smaller heads reduce dislocation risk

options.label: B
options.text: A larger head increases volumetric wear through greater sliding distance, while a smaller head increases linear (penetration) wear and reduces stability

options.label: C
options.text: Head size has no effect on wear, only on cost

options.label: D
options.text: Smaller heads always reduce both linear and volumetric wear

options.label: E
options.text: Larger heads are mandatory below a 40 mm cup

explanation: A larger head travels a greater sliding distance per cycle and therefore generates more volumetric wear debris, whereas a smaller head produces higher contact stress and greater linear penetration (penetration is inversely related to the square of the radius) and is also less stable. The 28 mm head balances these competing effects, providing acceptable wear with reasonable stability and an adequate polyethylene thickness. Where the cup is 40 mm or smaller a 22 mm head may be needed to preserve at least about 8 mm of polyethylene.
learningPoint: Bigger heads raise volumetric wear, smaller heads raise linear wear and reduce stability; 28 mm balances both for metal-on-poly.

stem: Highly cross-linked polyethylene was developed to improve bearing performance in total hip replacement. Compared with conventional polyethylene, what is its principal advantage and the reason it is packaged carefully?

options.label: A
options.text: It is stiffer, and is vacuum packed to keep it sterile only

options.label: B
options.text: It has substantially lower wear, but cross-linking generates free radicals that can cause oxidative degradation unless controlled

options.label: C
options.text: It is cheaper to manufacture and needs no irradiation

options.label: D
options.text: It eliminates osteolysis entirely and never fractures

options.label: E
options.text: It increases wear but improves toughness

explanation: Gamma irradiation promotes cross-linking of the polymer chains, markedly improving wear resistance, with reported reductions of the order of 60 per cent compared with standard polyethylene in clinical wear studies. However irradiation also creates free radicals that can drive oxidative degradation over time, so the material is processed to limit oxidation, for example by irradiating in an inert atmosphere and vacuum packing, since oxidation and cross-linking are competing reactions. Early head penetration largely reflects creep, with true wear dominating after the first year.
learningPoint: Highly cross-linked polyethylene roughly halves wear but free radicals risk oxidation, so it is processed to limit degradation.

stem: Which statement about polymethylmethacrylate bone cement is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: Setting is an endothermic reaction that cools the surrounding bone

options.label: B
options.text: Barium sulphate or a similar salt is added to make the cement radio-opaque

options.label: C
options.text: The liquid monomer is the radio-opaque constituent

options.label: D
options.text: Cement acts as a true adhesive, chemically bonding to bone

options.label: E
options.text: Benzoyl peroxide is added to inhibit polymerisation

explanation: A radio-opacifying salt such as barium sulphate is incorporated into the powder so the cement mantle can be assessed on radiographs. Polymerisation is an exothermic addition reaction initiated by benzoyl peroxide and accelerated by the activator in the liquid monomer, passing through sandy, stringy, doughy and hard phases, and cement works as a grout that interlocks mechanically rather than as a chemical adhesive. Modern third-generation technique uses vacuum mixing to reduce porosity together with pressurisation and a stem centraliser.
learningPoint: PMMA sets by an exothermic addition reaction, contains a radio-opacifier, and grouts rather than glues.

stem: Which feature most characterises modern third-generation cementing technique for the femoral component?

options.label: A
options.text: Finger packing of cement without canal preparation

options.label: B
options.text: Use of a distal cement restrictor and a cement gun introduced for the first time

options.label: C
options.text: Vacuum mixing to reduce porosity, with pressurisation and a stem centraliser

options.label: D
options.text: Avoidance of pulsatile lavage to preserve the endosteum

options.label: E
options.text: Hand mixing in an open bowl with no pressurisation

explanation: Third-generation cementing introduced vacuum or centrifuge mixing to lower cement porosity, combined with pressurisation of the cement and a centraliser to ensure an even mantle. First-generation technique relied on finger packing with no canal preparation, while second-generation added pulsatile lavage, a canal restrictor and a cement gun. These refinements improve the cement-bone interlock and reduce the risk of early aseptic loosening.
learningPoint: Third-generation cementing = vacuum mixing (low porosity) plus pressurisation and a centraliser.

stem: What is the fundamental biological mechanism underlying periprosthetic osteolysis around a total hip replacement?

options.label: A
options.text: Direct chemical corrosion of bone by the metal implant

options.label: B
options.text: A macrophage-driven inflammatory response to submicron wear particles that releases osteolytic mediators

options.label: C
options.text: An autoimmune reaction against the patient's own collagen

options.label: D
options.text: Bacterial enzymatic digestion of bone in all cases

options.label: E
options.text: Heat necrosis of bone from the cement reaction

explanation: Submicron polyethylene particles (typically around 0.5 to 5 micrometres) are phagocytosed by macrophages, which become activated and release osteolytic mediators including cytokines such as the interleukins, prostaglandins, free radicals and acid phosphatase, driving bone resorption around the implant. The resulting macromotion generates further debris in a self-perpetuating cycle, and raised intra-articular pressure pumps particles to the limits of the effective joint space. Aseptic loosening from this process is the commonest indication for revision surgery.
learningPoint: Osteolysis is a macrophage response to submicron wear debris releasing osteolytic mediators, the leading cause of aseptic loosening.

stem: On follow-up radiographs of a cemented total hip replacement, the acetabular component is divided into three zones and the femoral component into seven. Which classifications describe these respectively?

options.label: A
options.text: DeLee and Charnley for the acetabulum, Gruen for the femur

options.label: B
options.text: Gruen for the acetabulum, DeLee and Charnley for the femur

options.label: C
options.text: Brooker for the acetabulum, Vancouver for the femur

options.label: D
options.text: Crowe for the acetabulum, Trueta for the femur

options.label: E
options.text: Harris for both components

explanation: The acetabular component is assessed using the three DeLee and Charnley zones, while the femoral component is divided into the seven Gruen zones. Progressive radiolucent lines or a change in component position suggest loosening; a cup is regarded as loose if there are lines exceeding about 2 mm in all three zones, progressive lucency in zones 1 and 2, or migration. Gruen also described patterns of femoral loosening such as the pistoning, calcar pivot windscreen-wiper and cantilever bending modes.
learningPoint: Acetabular zones = DeLee and Charnley (3); femoral zones = Gruen (7).

stem: An elderly patient sustains a femoral fracture at the level of a well-fixed hip stem with good surrounding bone stock. Which Vancouver type is this, and what is the most appropriate treatment?

options.label: A
options.text: Type A, treated with protected weight bearing

options.label: B
options.text: Type B1, treated with open reduction and internal fixation

options.label: C
options.text: Type B2, treated with revision to a long stem

options.label: D
options.text: Type B3, treated with proximal femoral replacement

options.label: E
options.text: Type C, treated with a distal locking plate independent of the stem

explanation: A fracture at the level of the stem with the prosthesis well fixed and adequate bone stock is Vancouver type B1, best managed by open reduction and internal fixation, for example a spanning locked plate with cerclage and unicortical screws around the stem. A loose stem with good bone is B2 and requires revision to a stem bypassing the fracture, while a loose stem with deficient bone is B3 and may need proximal femoral reconstruction. Type A fractures involve the trochanters and type C lie well below the stem tip.
learningPoint: Vancouver B1 = fracture at a well-fixed stem with good bone, managed by ORIF; B2 (loose stem) needs revision.

stem: What acetabular cup orientation is generally recommended to minimise the risk of dislocation after total hip replacement?

options.label: A
options.text: Approximately 15 degrees of anteversion and 40 degrees of inclination

options.label: B
options.text: Approximately 40 degrees of anteversion and 15 degrees of inclination

options.label: C
options.text: Neutral version and 70 degrees of inclination

options.label: D
options.text: 30 degrees of retroversion and 60 degrees of inclination

options.label: E
options.text: Anteversion and inclination have no bearing on stability

explanation: A commonly cited target is around 15 degrees of cup anteversion and about 40 degrees of inclination, each with a tolerance of roughly ten degrees, often described as a safe zone for stability. Excessive inclination (a vertical, open cup) predisposes to edge loading and dislocation, while malversion in either direction encourages impingement and instability. Stability also depends on restoring offset, balancing soft tissues, head-to-neck ratio and avoiding impingement from retained osteophytes.
learningPoint: A typical safe cup target is ~15 degrees anteversion and ~40 degrees inclination.

stem: On a postoperative hip radiograph there is ectopic bone with a complete bony bridge spanning from the femur to the pelvis. Which Brooker grade is this, and what prophylaxis could have reduced the risk?

options.label: A
options.text: Brooker grade I, preventable with bisphosphonates

options.label: B
options.text: Brooker grade II, preventable with high-dose long-term steroids

options.label: C
options.text: Brooker grade IV, where indomethacin or low-dose perioperative radiotherapy reduces risk

options.label: D
options.text: Brooker grade III, treated only by immediate excision

options.label: E
options.text: Brooker grade IV, for which no prophylaxis exists

explanation: A complete bony ankylosis between femur and pelvis is Brooker grade IV, the most severe category (grade I is isolated islands of bone, grade II leaves a gap greater than 1 cm, grade III a gap under 1 cm). Effective prophylaxis includes a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory such as indomethacin or a single dose of low-dose radiotherapy given within about 72 hours of surgery. Established symptomatic heterotopic ossification is managed by surgical excision, ideally with adjuvant prophylaxis to limit recurrence.
learningPoint: Brooker IV is complete bony bridging; prophylaxis is indomethacin or perioperative low-dose radiotherapy.

stem: A radiograph shows the femoral head migrated medial to the ilioischial (Kohler) line. Which statement about this condition is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: It is defined by the head lying lateral to Kohler's line

options.label: B
options.text: Primary protrusio classically affects middle-aged women and may be bilateral

options.label: C
options.text: At arthroplasty the femoral head autograft should never be used

options.label: D
options.text: The thin medial wall should be deliberately perforated to medialise the cup

options.label: E
options.text: It is never associated with rheumatoid arthritis or Paget disease

explanation: Protrusio acetabuli is medial migration of the femoral head past the ilioischial line, and the primary form characteristically affects middle-aged women and is bilateral in roughly a third of cases. Secondary causes include rheumatoid and other inflammatory arthritis, Paget disease, osteomalacia, trauma and previous infection. At arthroplasty the surgeon must avoid breaching the often paper-thin medial wall, should restore a lateralised hip centre, and can use the patient's own femoral head as autograft to reconstitute medial bone stock, aiming for a good peripheral rim fit.
learningPoint: Protrusio = head medial to Kohler's line; primary form affects middle-aged women, often bilateral; restore the hip centre laterally with autograft.

stem: The Crowe classification grades developmental hip dysplasia by the degree of femoral head subluxation. Which combination is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: Crowe I is less than 50 per cent subluxation; Crowe IV is more than 100 per cent (high dislocation)

options.label: B
options.text: Crowe I is more than 100 per cent subluxation; Crowe IV is less than 50 per cent

options.label: C
options.text: Crowe grading is based on the centre-edge angle rather than subluxation

options.label: D
options.text: Crowe II indicates more than 100 per cent subluxation

options.label: E
options.text: Crowe III describes a perfectly congruent joint

explanation: The Crowe system grades the proximal migration or subluxation of the femoral head relative to the pelvis: grade I is under 50 per cent, grade II is 50 to 75 per cent, grade III is 75 to 100 per cent and grade IV exceeds 100 per cent and represents a high dislocation, often with a false acetabulum. Higher grades make arthroplasty technically demanding because of a small deficient acetabulum, excessive femoral anteversion, a narrow canal and the risk of sciatic nerve injury, so that lengthening beyond about 4 cm or a subtrochanteric shortening osteotomy must be considered. Understanding the grade guides component selection and the need for special small or modular implants.
learningPoint: Crowe I to IV grade femoral head subluxation (<50 per cent up to >100 per cent / high dislocation).

stem: A young adult has activity-related groin pain. Examination shows reduced internal rotation and abduction in flexion with a positive impingement test on flexion, adduction and internal rotation. A lateral radiograph reveals a bony prominence at the anterior femoral head-neck junction. Which type of femoroacetabular impingement is most likely?

options.label: A
options.text: Cam-type impingement

options.label: B
options.text: Pincer-type impingement

options.label: C
options.text: Posterior impingement from retroversion

options.label: D
options.text: Ischiofemoral impingement

options.label: E
options.text: Subspine impingement

explanation: An aspherical femoral head-neck junction with a bony bump anteriorly is the hallmark of cam-type impingement, in which the abnormal contour is driven into the acetabulum during flexion and internal rotation, shearing the labrum and chondral surface. Pincer impingement instead arises from acetabular overcoverage, and a mixed pattern is common. The mechanical labral and chondral damage is thought to contribute to early osteoarthritis in young patients, and management starts with non-operative measures before considering arthroscopic or open debridement and osteochondroplasty.
learningPoint: Cam impingement = aspherical head-neck junction; positive impingement on flexion-adduction-internal rotation; a cause of early OA.

stem: During hip arthroscopy, prolonged traction is applied to distract the joint. Which nerve is at greatest risk from this traction?

options.label: A
options.text: Pudendal nerve

options.label: B
options.text: Femoral nerve

options.label: C
options.text: Obturator nerve

options.label: D
options.text: Superior gluteal nerve

options.label: E
options.text: Lateral cutaneous nerve of thigh

explanation: Distraction of the hip for arthroscopy is achieved against a perineal post, and excessive or prolonged traction can compress or stretch the pudendal nerve, a recognised traction injury of the procedure. Indications for hip arthroscopy include labral tears, removal of loose bodies and synovitis, and the technique is demanding with anterior, anterolateral and posterolateral portals. Limiting traction time and using adequate padding reduce the risk of neuropraxia.
learningPoint: Traction during hip arthroscopy puts the pudendal nerve at risk via the perineal post.

stem: A man with hip protrusio and bilateral sacroiliac changes is suspected of having ankylosing spondylitis. Which statement is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: It is a seropositive arthropathy strongly linked to rheumatoid factor

options.label: B
options.text: The earliest radiographic feature is bamboo spine

options.label: C
options.text: It is a seronegative spondyloarthropathy strongly associated with HLA-B27, and the earliest change is sacroiliitis

options.label: D
options.text: It affects women roughly twice as often as men

options.label: E
options.text: Reduced chest expansion excludes the diagnosis

explanation: Ankylosing spondylitis is a seronegative spondyloarthropathy with a strong association with HLA-B27 and a male predominance, and the earliest radiographic feature is sacroiliitis, typically beginning on the iliac side, before vertebral squaring, syndesmophytes and ultimately a bamboo spine. The underlying pathology is an enthesopathy with synovitis and progressive ossification leading to ankylosis, and reduced chest expansion is a useful clinical sign rather than an exclusion. It belongs to the same family as reactive (Reiter) arthritis, psoriatic arthritis and the arthropathy of inflammatory bowel disease.
learningPoint: Ankylosing spondylitis is HLA-B27-associated and seronegative; earliest radiographic sign is sacroiliitis.

stem: A young manual labourer undergoes hip arthrodesis. What is the recommended position of fusion?

options.label: A
options.text: About 20 degrees of flexion, neutral abduction-adduction and neutral rotation

options.label: B
options.text: Full extension, 30 degrees of abduction and external rotation

options.label: C
options.text: About 45 degrees of flexion, marked adduction and internal rotation

options.label: D
options.text: Neutral flexion, 20 degrees of adduction and 30 degrees of external rotation

options.label: E
options.text: 90 degrees of flexion and neutral rotation

explanation: The hip is usually fused in approximately 20 degrees of flexion with neutral abduction-adduction and neutral rotation, a position that balances sitting comfort with an efficient gait. The abductor mechanism should be preserved to allow later conversion to a total hip replacement, and adequate abductor function (assessed by examination and electromyography) is essential before such conversion because a deficient abductor mechanism risks failure and instability. Arthrodesis gives durable pain relief but increases the energy cost of walking and alters the mechanics of the knee, lumbar spine and contralateral hip.
learningPoint: Hip arthrodesis is fused in about 20 degrees flexion, neutral abduction and neutral rotation, preserving abductors for later conversion.

stem: Match each clinical scenario to the nerve most likely responsible or at risk.

options.label: A
options.text: Femoral nerve

options.label: B
options.text: Sciatic nerve

options.label: C
options.text: Lateral cutaneous nerve of the thigh

options.label: D
options.text: Superior gluteal nerve

options.label: E
options.text: Pudendal nerve

options.label: F
options.text: Obturator nerve

options.label: G
options.text: Nerve to quadratus femoris

options.label: H
options.text: Inferior gluteal nerve

emiLeads.stem: Following a posterior hip dislocation a patient has foot drop and reduced sensation below the knee.
After anterior iliac crest bone graft harvest a patient reports burning numbness over the anterolateral thigh with no motor loss.
Damage to this nerve during a direct lateral approach denervates gluteus medius and minimus, producing a positive Trendelenburg sign.
Prolonged traction against the perineal post during hip arthroscopy threatens this nerve.

explanation: The sciatic nerve, exiting below piriformis, is classically injured by posterior dislocation or by posterior wall fractures and gives a foot drop. The lateral cutaneous nerve of the thigh is purely sensory and is vulnerable during anterior approaches and iliac crest harvest, producing meralgia paraesthetica. The superior gluteal nerve runs above piriformis to supply the abductors, so its injury in a lateral approach causes a Trendelenburg gait, while the pudendal nerve is the classic traction casualty of hip arthroscopy.
learningPoint: Know the signature nerve injuries around the hip: sciatic (posterior dislocation), LCNT (anterior approach/graft), superior gluteal (lateral approach), pudendal (arthroscopy traction).

stem: For each description, select the most appropriate classification system.

options.label: A
options.text: Vancouver classification

options.label: B
options.text: Crowe classification

options.label: C
options.text: Brooker classification

options.label: D
options.text: Gruen zones

options.label: E
options.text: DeLee and Charnley zones

options.label: F
options.text: Trueta description of head vascularity

options.label: G
options.text: Wiberg centre-edge angle

options.label: H
options.text: AAOS acetabular bone defect classification

emiLeads.stem: Grades the severity of heterotopic ossification around a hip replacement from islands of bone to complete bony bridging.
Classifies a femoral fracture occurring around a hip stem according to its level and whether the stem is loose.
Grades the degree of femoral head subluxation in developmental dysplasia of the hip.
Divides the femoral component into seven regions to describe the pattern of loosening.

explanation: Brooker grades heterotopic ossification from isolated bone islands through to complete ankylosis, and the Vancouver system classifies periprosthetic femoral fractures by level and stem stability to guide fixation versus revision. The Crowe classification quantifies femoral head subluxation in dysplasia, while Gruen divides the femoral component into seven zones and DeLee and Charnley divide the acetabulum into three for assessing loosening. Recognising which system applies to which problem is a recurring viva theme.
learningPoint: Match the system to the problem: Brooker (HO), Vancouver (periprosthetic fracture), Crowe (dysplasia), Gruen (femoral zones).

subtopic: Femoral head blood supply with age
stem: For each patient, select the vascular factor that best explains the clinical risk.

options.label: A
options.text: Transphyseal vessels still crossing the open physis

options.label: B
options.text: Lateral epiphyseal (retinacular) vessels as the sole significant supply

options.label: C
options.text: Sluggish flow in metaphyseal sinusoids

options.label: D
options.text: Posterosuperior retinacular vessels from the medial femoral circumflex artery

options.label: E
options.text: Artery of the ligamentum teres as the dominant source

options.label: F
options.text: Cruciate anastomosis at the posterior pelvis

options.label: G
options.text: Lateral femoral circumflex artery as the dominant head supply

options.label: H
options.text: Nutrient (intraosseous) diaphyseal system

emiLeads.stem: A neonate develops septic arthritis of the hip spreading from adjacent metaphyseal osteomyelitis across the growth plate.
A 6-year-old is at risk of Perthes disease because the metaphyseal contribution has regressed.
An adult undergoing the posterior approach risks head ischaemia if quadratus femoris is completely divided, jeopardising these vessels.
An infant is prone to metaphyseal osteomyelitis owing to this haemodynamic feature.

explanation: In the neonate, vessels still cross the physis so infection can spread between metaphysis and epiphysis, whereas after about a year the physis becomes a barrier. By mid-childhood the head depends largely on the lateral epiphyseal vessels, explaining Perthes vulnerability, and in the adult the dominant supply is the posterosuperior retinacular branch of the medial femoral circumflex artery, threatened by the posterior approach. Sluggish flow in metaphyseal sinusoids predisposes the infant to metaphyseal osteomyelitis.
learningPoint: The femoral head's vascular supply shifts with age, driving the age-specific risks of sepsis, Perthes and surgical AVN.

stem: For each presentation of a possibly infected hip replacement, select the most appropriate management strategy.

options.label: A
options.text: Aggressive debridement and washout with implant retention, possible head exchange, and antibiotics

options.label: B
options.text: Two-stage revision with an antibiotic-loaded cement spacer

options.label: C
options.text: Single-stage (one-stage) revision

options.label: D
options.text: Long-term antibiotic suppression with retention of the prosthesis

options.label: E
options.text: Girdlestone excision (resection) arthroplasty

options.label: F
options.text: Ultrasound-guided aspiration for culture before any surgery

options.label: G
options.text: Immediate exchange of the polyethylene liner only

options.label: H
options.text: Observation with serial inflammatory markers alone

emiLeads.stem: An acute deep infection within a few weeks of surgery with a solidly fixed, well-positioned implant.
A chronic infection of several months in a fit patient; the surgeon's preferred definitive treatment for clearing infection and reconstructing.
A frail, unfit patient with chronic infection in whom repeated major surgery is not feasible and the priority is keeping the limb functional without eradication.
A patient with persistent infection, very poor bone stock and failed revisions in whom no further reconstruction is viable.

explanation: Early acute infection with a stable implant may be salvaged by thorough debridement and washout with retention and antibiotics, sometimes exchanging the modular head. Chronic infection is generally best treated by a two-stage revision using an antibiotic spacer, taking multiple deep samples and clearing infection before delayed reconstruction. In a patient unfit for major surgery, antibiotic suppression with retention is a compromise, while a Girdlestone resection is reserved for unreconstructable or refractory cases as a salvage.
learningPoint: Match infection chronicity and host factors to strategy: early/stable = DAIR, chronic = two-stage, unfit = suppression, unreconstructable = Girdlestone.

stem: For each statement, select the material or bearing it best describes.

options.label: A
options.text: Ultra-high molecular weight polyethylene

options.label: B
options.text: Highly cross-linked polyethylene

options.label: C
options.text: Polymethylmethacrylate cement

options.label: D
options.text: Ceramic-on-ceramic bearing

options.label: E
options.text: Metal-on-metal bearing

options.label: F
options.text: Hydroxyapatite coating

options.label: G
options.text: Cobalt-chrome alloy

options.label: H
options.text: Titanium alloy

emiLeads.stem: An osteoconductive ceramic surface applied to uncemented implants to accelerate bony ingrowth.
A bearing prized for very low wear but associated with rare audible squeaking and the catastrophic risk of head fracture.
A grouting agent that polymerises exothermically and contains a radio-opacifier.
A polymer whose wear rate is reduced by roughly 60 per cent through gamma-induced cross-linking, at the cost of free-radical formation.

explanation: Hydroxyapatite is a bioactive osteoconductive coating that speeds ingrowth onto uncemented implants. Ceramic-on-ceramic bearings have extremely low wear but can squeak and, very rarely, fracture catastrophically. Polymethylmethacrylate is a grout that sets by an exothermic reaction and carries a radio-opacifier, while highly cross-linked polyethylene gains markedly improved wear resistance from cross-linking but generates free radicals that must be managed to avoid oxidative degradation.
learningPoint: Distinguish the bearing and material properties tested in the hip viva: HA coating, ceramic-on-ceramic, PMMA and cross-linked polyethylene.

subtopic: Strategies to reduce hip joint reaction force
stem: A free body diagram of the hip is used to discuss unloading the joint. For each strategy, select the biomechanical principle it relies upon.

options.label: A
options.text: Supplementing the abductors with an external upward force

options.label: B
options.text: Reducing body weight (the load)

options.label: C
options.text: Lengthening the abductor lever arm

options.label: D
options.text: Shortening the body weight lever arm by medialising the hip centre

options.label: E
options.text: Adding a counterbalancing moment on the same side

options.label: F
options.text: Increasing the stiffness of the implant

options.label: G
options.text: Raising the hip centre superiorly

options.label: H
options.text: Increasing femoral head diameter

emiLeads.stem: Holding a walking stick in the opposite hand.
Increasing the femoral offset of the prosthesis.
Carrying a heavy suitcase in the hand on the same side as the affected hip.
Deepening and medialising the acetabular socket.

explanation: A contralateral stick adds an external upward force that does part of the abductors' work, reducing the muscle force and hence the joint reaction force. Increasing offset lengthens the abductor lever arm so less abductor force is needed, and medialising the hip centre shortens the body weight lever arm to the same end. Carrying a load on the affected side adds a counterbalancing moment about the joint, and simply losing weight reduces the load that must be balanced.
learningPoint: The joint reaction force falls by reducing load, shortening the body weight arm, lengthening the abductor arm, or adding external/counterbalancing forces.

stem: During a teaching session a registrar is asked which structural feature best describes the anterior cruciate ligament. Which statement is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: It is an extra-articular ligament lying entirely outside the joint capsule

options.label: B
options.text: It sits inside the joint but outside the synovial envelope and is made mostly of type I collagen

options.label: C
options.text: Its main blood supply comes from the anterior tibial artery

options.label: D
options.text: It consists of three discrete fibre bundles named after their tibial insertions

options.label: E
options.text: Its principal role is to resist posterior movement of the tibia beneath the femur

explanation: The ACL lies within the joint cavity yet remains outside the synovial lining (intra-articular but extra-synovial) and is composed predominantly of type I collagen. It is formed of two bundles, anteromedial and posterolateral, and receives its supply largely from the middle genicular artery together with the fat pad. Its dominant function is restraint of anterior tibial translation, the anteromedial fibres being tightest in flexion.
learningPoint: The ACL is intra-articular but extra-synovial, two-bundled, mostly type I collagen, fed by the middle genicular artery.

stem: A 28-year-old motorcyclist sustains a high-energy knee dislocation that has spontaneously reduced before arrival. Palpable distal pulses are present. What is the most appropriate next step regarding the vascular assessment?

options.label: A
options.text: Reassure and discharge as palpable pulses exclude arterial injury

options.label: B
options.text: Measure the ankle-brachial pressure index and arrange further imaging if it is reduced

options.label: C
options.text: Proceed straight to arthroscopy to assess intra-articular damage

options.label: D
options.text: Apply a long leg cast and review in fracture clinic in one week

options.label: E
options.text: Immediately amputate if any pulse asymmetry is detected

explanation: Popliteal arterial injury complicates roughly a third of knee dislocations and a normal-feeling pulse does not exclude it, because collateral flow may mask an intimal tear. An ankle-brachial pressure index below about 0.9 mandates urgent vascular review and angiography. Early arthroscopy is avoided in the first fortnight owing to the risk of fluid extravasation and compartment syndrome.
learningPoint: A present pulse never excludes vascular injury after knee dislocation; check ABPI and image if it is low.

stem: A radial split through the body of the medial meniscus is found at arthroscopy. Which mechanical consequence most directly explains why this pattern accelerates joint degeneration?

options.label: A
options.text: Loss of the circumferential fibre continuity abolishes conversion of load into hoop stress

options.label: B
options.text: The meniscus stops producing synovial fluid for the joint

options.label: C
options.text: The tear removes the meniscal contribution to active knee extension

options.label: D
options.text: Radial tears sever the principal nerve supply to the tibial plateau

options.label: E
options.text: The defect prevents the cruciate ligaments from functioning

explanation: The deep circumferential collagen fibres of the meniscus capture axial compression and redistribute it as hoop (circumferential) stress, spreading load over a wide contact area. A complete radial tear interrupts that ring, so the meniscus can no longer generate hoop stress and load becomes concentrated on a small region of cartilage, hastening osteoarthritis. Load transmission is regarded as the single most important meniscal function.
learningPoint: Radial meniscal tears destroy hoop-stress conversion, causing point loading and early osteoarthritis.

stem: A young footballer has a longitudinal tear located within 3 mm of the peripheral capsular attachment of the meniscus. Why is repair rather than excision favoured here?

options.label: A
options.text: This region is avascular and heals best when left undisturbed

options.label: B
options.text: The peripheral red-red zone has a good blood supply and strong healing potential

options.label: C
options.text: Tears in this zone never become symptomatic

options.label: D
options.text: The inner white-white zone has the richest vascularity

options.label: E
options.text: Repair is chosen only because resection is technically impossible here

explanation: Blood enters the meniscus peripherally from the perimeniscal capillary plexus fed by the genicular vessels, so the outer third (red-red zone, within roughly 3-4 mm of the rim) is well vascularised and has favourable healing capacity. The mid (red-white) zone is borderline and the inner (white-white) zone is essentially avascular. A peripheral longitudinal tear in healthy tissue is therefore an ideal candidate for preservation by repair.
learningPoint: Peripheral red-red meniscal tears heal well and should be repaired; inner white-white tears do not.

stem: A rugby player is tackled from the side and develops medial knee pain. On valgus stress at 20 degrees of flexion the joint opens up but a firm end point is felt. How is this injury best classified and treated?

options.label: A
options.text: Complete (grade III) tear requiring early surgical repair

options.label: B
options.text: Partial (grade II) tear managed non-operatively with bracing and rehabilitation

options.label: C
options.text: Simple sprain (grade I) requiring no follow-up and immediate return to play

options.label: D
options.text: Bony avulsion requiring internal fixation

options.label: E
options.text: Chronic injury best treated by reconstruction

explanation: Laxity on valgus testing at around 20 degrees with a definite firm end point indicates a partial (grade II) medial collateral injury; complete tears open up with no discernible end point. Isolated MCL injuries, including grade III, generally heal with non-operative care comprising a hinged brace, analgesia and physiotherapy. It remains essential to exclude associated cruciate, meniscal or posterolateral corner damage.
learningPoint: Valgus laxity with a firm end point = grade II MCL; isolated MCL injuries are managed non-operatively.

stem: A patient with a multiligament knee injury shows increased external rotation of the tibia when the dial test is performed at 30 degrees of flexion, but symmetrical rotation at 90 degrees. Which structure is most likely injured?

options.label: A
options.text: Posterior cruciate ligament in isolation

options.label: B
options.text: Posterolateral corner

options.label: C
options.text: Anterior cruciate ligament

options.label: D
options.text: Medial collateral ligament

options.label: E
options.text: Anteromedial joint capsule

explanation: The dial (tibial external rotation) test compares rotation at 30 and 90 degrees. Increased external rotation at 30 degrees alone points to an isolated posterolateral corner injury, whereas an increase at both 30 and 90 degrees implies combined posterolateral corner and PCL disruption. Common peroneal nerve function must always be documented, as the nerve is at risk in this region.
learningPoint: Increased dial-test external rotation at 30 but not 90 degrees indicates an isolated posterolateral corner injury.

stem: When performing a primary total knee replacement, why is the distal femoral cut set at approximately 5 to 7 degrees of valgus relative to the anatomical axis?

options.label: A
options.text: To deliberately leave the limb in residual varus

options.label: B
options.text: To produce a cut perpendicular to the mechanical axis of the femur

options.label: C
options.text: To raise the joint line and increase flexion

options.label: D
options.text: To compensate for an intentionally internally rotated femoral component

options.label: E
options.text: To match the natural posterior tibial slope

explanation: The anatomical (intramedullary) axis of the femur diverges from its mechanical axis by about 6 degrees of valgus. Cutting the distal femur at roughly 5-7 degrees of valgus relative to that intramedullary reference therefore renders the resection perpendicular to the mechanical axis, restoring a neutral limb alignment. The goal of arthroplasty is to recreate the mechanical axis and balance load between the medial and lateral compartments.
learningPoint: The TKR valgus cut angle (5-7 degrees) makes the distal femoral resection perpendicular to the mechanical axis.

stem: Intra-operatively during a TKR the knee is found to be tight in flexion but well balanced in extension. Which corrective manoeuvre is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Resect more from the distal femur

options.label: B
options.text: Release the posterior capsule

options.label: C
options.text: Downsize the femoral component using the anteroposterior sizing jig

options.label: D
options.text: Remove additional bone from the proximal tibia

options.label: E
options.text: Increase the polyethylene insert thickness

explanation: An isolated flexion-gap tightness with a satisfactory extension gap is corrected by reducing the size of the femoral component (recutting with the AP jig to a smaller size), which selectively opens the flexion gap. Taking more off the tibia would loosen both gaps, and distal femoral resection or posterior capsular release addresses extension tightness instead. Recognising which gap is tight in which plane is fundamental to balancing.
learningPoint: Tight in flexion only during TKR is corrected by downsizing the femoral component.

stem: A rheumatoid patient with a fixed valgus knee is undergoing total knee replacement. Which approach and soft-tissue strategy is most appropriate for the deformity?

options.label: A
options.text: Medial parapatellar approach with sequential release of the medial structures

options.label: B
options.text: Lateral parapatellar approach with release of the tight lateral structures

options.label: C
options.text: Release of the medial collateral ligament as the first step

options.label: D
options.text: No soft-tissue release because valgus knees are intrinsically balanced

options.label: E
options.text: Routine division of the common peroneal nerve to allow correction

explanation: In the fixed valgus knee the lateral structures (lateral capsule, iliotibial band, popliteus, lateral collateral ligament) are contracted while the medial side is attenuated, so releases target the tight lateral side. A lateral parapatellar approach gives direct access and avoids further medial stretching. The common peroneal nerve is vulnerable as the deformity corrects, so postoperative palsy is watched for and managed by releasing the dressings and flexing the knee.
learningPoint: Valgus knees are tight laterally; release the lateral structures and protect the peroneal nerve.

stem: On the first postoperative day after correction of a valgus knee with TKR, a patient is found to have a foot drop. After removing the dressing and confirming no compartment syndrome, what is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Take the patient straight back to theatre for nerve exploration

options.label: B
options.text: Flex the knee, apply a foot-drop splint and observe, arranging nerve conduction studies if no recovery

options.label: C
options.text: Reassure that no action is needed as palsies always recover fully

options.label: D
options.text: Begin anticoagulation reversal and re-explore the wound for haematoma regardless of findings

options.label: E
options.text: Immediately revise the prosthesis to a constrained design

explanation: A peroneal nerve palsy after valgus correction is initially managed by removing constrictive dressings, flexing the knee to about 30-40 degrees to relieve traction, and excluding compartment syndrome (evacuating a large haematoma if present). A foot-drop splint protects function while nerve conduction studies are arranged at around four to five weeks. Surgical exploration with neurolysis is reserved for failure to improve by around three months.
learningPoint: Post-TKR peroneal palsy: release dressings, flex the knee, splint, then NCS; explore only if no recovery by 3 months.

stem: Which finding represents a genuine contraindication to mobile-bearing unicompartmental knee replacement for anteromedial osteoarthritis?

options.label: A
options.text: Patient age under 60 years

options.label: B
options.text: Deficiency of the anterior cruciate ligament with posterior tibial wear on the lateral radiograph

options.label: C
options.text: Coexisting patellofemoral joint osteoarthritis

options.label: D
options.text: A high activity level

options.label: E
options.text: Body weight above average

explanation: A functioning anterior cruciate ligament is essential for mobile-bearing unicompartmental replacement; posterior wear on the tibia seen on the lateral film signals cruciate insufficiency with subluxation and predicts early polyethylene wear, making the patient unsuitable. By contrast, young age, high activity, raised weight, chondrocalcinosis and isolated patellofemoral changes are no longer regarded as true contraindications. Inflammatory arthritis and a non-correctable deformity remain genuine bars.
learningPoint: ACL deficiency (with lateral-film posterior tibial wear) contraindicates UKR; age, weight and activity do not.

stem: A patient develops a hot swollen knee two weeks after total knee replacement. The organism is sensitive, there is no sinus or prolonged drainage, and radiographs show a well-fixed implant. Which treatment strategy is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Long-term antibiotic suppression with retention of the prosthesis

options.label: B
options.text: Debridement with exchange of the polyethylene and retention of the fixed components

options.label: C
options.text: Single-stage revision of all components

options.label: D
