options.text: Immediate above-knee amputation

options.label: E
options.text: Arthrodesis with an intramedullary nail

explanation: An acute infection of short duration (broadly within about two to four weeks of onset) with a susceptible organism, no sinus or prolonged drainage and a well-fixed implant is amenable to debridement, antibiotics and implant retention with exchange of the modular polyethylene. Suppression alone is a last resort for unfit hosts with low-virulence organisms. Resection, arthrodesis and amputation are reserved for failed control, poor soft tissues or resistant pathogens.
learningPoint: Acute, short-duration TKR infection with a sensitive organism and fixed implant warrants debridement and liner exchange.

stem: A patient presents with a chronically infected total knee replacement diagnosed several months after surgery, with a loose implant. The chosen plan is a two-stage revision. Which feature of the first stage is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: The prosthesis is retained and only the joint is washed out

options.label: B
options.text: The prosthesis is removed, deep samples are taken and an antibiotic-loaded cement spacer is inserted

options.label: C
options.text: A plain cement spacer without antibiotic is used to avoid resistance

options.label: D
options.text: Definitive revision components are implanted at the first sitting

options.label: E
options.text: Antibiotics are continued uninterrupted up to the moment of reimplantation

explanation: The first stage of a two-stage revision removes all implants, thoroughly debrides bone, takes multiple separate deep tissue samples for microbiology and histology, and inserts an antibiotic-impregnated cement spacer; the cement is always antibiotic-loaded. Targeted antibiotics follow, and a drug-free interval before reimplantation allows assessment of infection clearance through clinical review and serial inflammatory markers. Bone stock and constraint requirements are planned before the second stage.
learningPoint: First-stage revision: remove implant, multiple deep samples, antibiotic-loaded spacer, then antibiotic-free interval.

stem: A 14-year-old presents with vague knee pain and intermittent catching. Imaging suggests osteochondritis dissecans. At which site is the lesion most commonly found in the knee?

options.label: A
options.text: Lateral aspect of the lateral femoral condyle

options.label: B
options.text: Posterolateral aspect of the medial femoral condyle

options.label: C
options.text: Central tibial plateau

options.label: D
options.text: Anterior surface of the patella

options.label: E
options.text: Intercondylar eminence

explanation: The classic and most frequent location for knee osteochondritis dissecans is the lateral (posterolateral) portion of the medial femoral condyle, accounting for the large majority of cases. The lateral femoral condyle and patella are affected far less often, and the condition is bilateral in a substantial minority. Prognosis depends heavily on skeletal maturity, lesions in patients with open physes generally faring better.
learningPoint: OCD of the knee most often affects the lateral part of the medial femoral condyle; prognosis depends on physeal status.

stem: A microfracture procedure is performed for a contained full-thickness chondral defect. What is the principal biological outcome of this technique?

options.label: A
options.text: Regeneration of normal hyaline cartilage identical to the original surface

options.label: B
options.text: Filling of the defect with fibrocartilage derived from marrow mesenchymal cells

options.label: C
options.text: Permanent sealing of the defect with fibrous scar containing no cartilage

options.label: D
options.text: Stimulation of the superficial chondrocytes above the tidemark to divide

options.label: E
options.text: Complete prevention of any future osteoarthritic change

explanation: Microfracture breaches the subchondral plate so that marrow elements enter the defect; undifferentiated mesenchymal cells then form a fibrocartilaginous repair tissue rather than true hyaline cartilage. Superficial lesions above the tidemark are avascular and do not heal, which is why the technique deliberately converts a shallow defect into a deeper one. Randomised comparison with autologous chondrocyte implantation has shown no clear difference in medium-term clinical scores, with osteoarthritic changes appearing in both groups.
learningPoint: Microfracture yields fibrocartilage, not hyaline cartilage, by recruiting marrow mesenchymal cells.

stem: A 65-year-old woman has sudden severe medial knee pain without trauma. Radiographs show sclerosis and flattening confined to the weight-bearing surface of the medial femoral condyle. Which is the most likely diagnosis?

options.label: A
options.text: Spontaneous osteonecrosis of the knee

options.label: B
options.text: Pigmented villonodular synovitis

options.label: C
options.text: Gouty arthropathy

options.label: D
options.text: Septic arthritis

options.label: E
options.text: Rheumatoid arthritis

explanation: Spontaneous osteonecrosis of the knee characteristically affects an older patient with abrupt unilateral medial pain and radiographic sclerosis and subchondral collapse limited to the weight-bearing zone of the medial femoral condyle. MRI is the most sensitive early investigation. Initial management protects weight bearing with analgesia and physiotherapy, with arthroplasty (unicompartmental or total) reserved for progressive disease, idiopathic cases doing better than secondary osteonecrosis.
learningPoint: SPONK = older patient, sudden medial pain, sclerosis/collapse of the weight-bearing medial femoral condyle; MRI is key.

stem: A 16-year-old with recurrent lateral patellar dislocation and an intoeing gait is being assessed. Cross-sectional imaging shows a tibial tubercle to trochlear groove distance of 24 mm. What does this finding indicate?

options.label: A
options.text: A normal value that excludes a bony cause of instability

options.label: B
options.text: An abnormally increased value consistent with lateralising malalignment

options.label: C
options.text: Evidence of patella baja

options.label: D
options.text: A direct measure of trochlear cartilage thickness

options.label: E
options.text: A measurement only valid following surgery

explanation: The tibial tubercle to trochlear groove distance quantifies the lateral pull on the patella; a value greater than about 20 mm is abnormal and supports a bony contribution to instability. In this patient an abnormal rotational profile should be characterised and corrected before, or alongside, any patellar realignment, because operating on the patella in isolation may not resolve maltracking. Lateral retinacular release alone is a recognised cause of persistent symptoms.
learningPoint: A TT-TG distance above ~20 mm is abnormal; assess and correct the rotational profile before realigning the patella.

stem: Six months after a non-contact ACL rupture, a patient still lacks full extension and has anterior knee pain, having had no surgery. Which is the most appropriate next step before considering reconstruction?

options.label: A
options.text: Proceed immediately to ACL reconstruction to restore stability

options.label: B
options.text: Optimise range of movement with focused physiotherapy and consider causes such as a cyclops lesion or arthrofibrosis

options.label: C
options.text: Apply a long leg cast for six weeks

options.label: D
options.text: Commence long-term opioid analgesia and review in one year

options.label: E
options.text: Perform an above-knee amputation

explanation: Operating on a stiff, inflamed knee risks worsening arthrofibrosis, so reconstruction is deferred until a good range of motion and a settled joint are restored, typically through physiotherapy. A persistent extension deficit raises the possibility of a cyclops lesion, arthrofibrosis or complex regional pain syndrome, which should be sought and addressed. Regaining full extension preoperatively is a key determinant of a good surgical outcome.
learningPoint: Restore range of motion before ACL reconstruction; a fixed extension deficit may signal a cyclops lesion or arthrofibrosis.

stem: For each described primary restraining role, select the single most appropriate structure from the list.

options.label: A
options.text: Anterior cruciate ligament

options.label: B
options.text: Posterior cruciate ligament

options.label: C
options.text: Medial collateral ligament

options.label: D
options.text: Lateral collateral ligament

options.label: E
options.text: Posterolateral corner

options.label: F
options.text: Iliotibial band

options.label: G
options.text: Popliteus

options.label: H
options.text: Anterior horn of the medial meniscus

emiLeads.stem: The primary check against anterior translation of the tibia on the femur, most effective at around 30 degrees of flexion.
The primary restraint to posterior tibial translation, resisting most strongly near 90 degrees of flexion.
The primary restraint to valgus angulation of the knee and the ligament most often injured by a lateral blow.
The primary restraint to external rotation of the tibia when the knee is flexed to about 30 degrees.

explanation: The ACL is the chief restraint to anterior tibial translation and is tightest around 30 degrees, while the PCL is the chief restraint to posterior translation, working hardest near 90 degrees. The MCL is the primary stabiliser against valgus stress and the commonest ligament injured by a valgus force, whereas the posterolateral corner is the principal restraint to tibial external rotation at 30 degrees, the basis of the dial test. Secondary stabilisers such as the iliotibial band and meniscal horns contribute but are not primary.
learningPoint: Match each motion to its primary restraint: anterior=ACL, posterior=PCL, valgus=MCL, external rotation at 30 degrees=PLC.

stem: For each radiographic description of a knee, select the single most likely underlying diagnosis.

options.label: A
options.text: Osteoarthritis

options.label: B
options.text: Rheumatoid arthritis

options.label: C
options.text: Gout

options.label: D
options.text: Spontaneous osteonecrosis

options.label: E
options.text: Pigmented villonodular synovitis

options.label: F
options.text: Ochronotic arthropathy

options.label: G
options.text: Septic arthritis

options.label: H
options.text: Charcot (neuropathic) joint

emiLeads.stem: Asymmetric joint-space narrowing with marginal osteophytes, subchondral sclerosis and subchondral cysts.
Periarticular osteopenia, symmetrical joint-space loss and juxta-articular bony erosions beneath ligament attachments.
Well-defined periarticular erosions with sclerotic overhanging edges, yet preserved bone density and joint space early on.
Juxtacortical bony erosions with sclerotic margins associated with a rust-coloured, haemosiderin-laden synovium.

explanation: Osteoarthritis shows the classic tetrad of asymmetric joint-space loss, osteophytes, subchondral sclerosis and cysts. Rheumatoid disease causes periarticular osteopenia with symmetrical narrowing and marginal erosions at ligament insertions, whereas gouty erosions are punched-out with sclerotic overhanging margins and relatively spared bone density and joint space early on. Pigmented villonodular synovitis produces juxtacortical erosions with sclerotic rims and a characteristic haemosiderin-stained synovium.
learningPoint: Distinguish OA (osteophytes, sclerosis, cysts), RA (osteopenia, symmetrical erosions), gout (overhanging sclerotic erosions) and PVNS.

stem: For each clinical consideration in selecting an ACL graft, choose the single most appropriate graft option.

options.label: A
options.text: Bone-patellar tendon-bone autograft

options.label: B
options.text: Hamstring (gracilis and semitendinosus) autograft

options.label: C
options.text: Quadriceps tendon autograft

options.label: D
options.text: Allograft

options.label: E
options.text: Synthetic graft

options.label: F
options.text: Iliotibial band (extra-articular) reconstruction

options.label: G
options.text: Contralateral patellar tendon autograft

options.label: H
options.text: Direct primary ligament repair

emiLeads.stem: Favoured for robust bone-to-bone healing and rapid return to sport, but associated with more anterior knee and kneeling pain.
Chosen to minimise anterior knee pain, accepting concerns about fixation strength and possible bone-tunnel widening.
Generally reserved for revision surgery and carries a small but real risk of viral disease transmission.
Largely abandoned for primary reconstruction because of unacceptably high failure rates.

explanation: Bone-patellar tendon-bone autograft offers strong bone-plug fixation and quick rehabilitation but more anterior knee and kneeling pain, and is relatively contraindicated after Osgood-Schlatter disease. Hamstring autograft reduces anterior knee pain at the cost of fixation concerns, possible loss of flexion strength and tunnel widening. Allograft is mainly used in revision settings and carries a transmission risk, while synthetic grafts have been discarded for primary use because of high failure rates.
learningPoint: Patellar-tendon graft = strong fixation but anterior knee pain; hamstring = less pain; allograft for revision; synthetics fail.

stem: For each description of a cartilage treatment technique, select the single best matching option.

options.label: A
options.text: Microfracture

options.label: B
options.text: Mosaicplasty (osteochondral autograft)

options.label: C
options.text: Autologous chondrocyte implantation

options.label: D
options.text: Osteochondral allograft

options.label: E
options.text: Glucosamine supplementation

options.label: F
options.text: Viscosupplementation

options.label: G
options.text: High tibial osteotomy

options.label: H
options.text: Total knee replacement

emiLeads.stem: Cylindrical full-thickness plugs are harvested from a non-weight-bearing area and transplanted into the defect.
Cartilage cells are harvested, expanded in culture over several weeks, then reimplanted beneath a cover, eventually forming hyaline-like cartilage.
An intra-articular injection intended to replace lost hyaluronic acid, supported by weak and conflicting evidence.
Mainly used to fill very large defects following tumour resection, with a recognised risk of disease transmission.

explanation: Mosaicplasty transfers osteochondral cylinders from a non-articulating donor site into the lesion, whereas autologous chondrocyte implantation cultures harvested chondrocytes and reimplants them under a periosteal or membrane cover, producing hyaline-like cartilage over roughly two years. Viscosupplementation aims to restore joint hyaluronic acid but rests on low-quality, publication-biased data. Osteochondral allograft is chiefly used for massive defects such as those after tumour excision and carries an infection-transmission risk.
learningPoint: Know the cartilage ladder: marrow stimulation, osteochondral autograft/allograft, ACI, and the role of injections.

stem: Each patient presents with knee swelling. Select the single most likely diagnosis for each scenario.

options.label: A
options.text: Septic arthritis

options.label: B
options.text: Gout

options.label: C
options.text: Anterior cruciate ligament rupture

options.label: D
options.text: Complex regional pain syndrome

options.label: E
options.text: Rheumatoid flare

options.label: F
options.text: Patellar (prepatellar) bursitis

options.label: G
options.text: Haemophilic haemarthrosis

options.label: H
options.text: Spontaneous osteonecrosis

emiLeads.stem: A 22-year-old develops an immediate tense, bloody effusion within an hour of a non-contact twisting football injury, with a feeling of the knee giving way.
A previously well adult has a single hot, exquisitely tender, swollen knee over 24 hours with no injury; aspiration must exclude infection before any other label.
Several months after a knee injury a patient has disproportionate burning pain, swelling, stiffness, skin discolouration and patchy osteopenia on radiographs.
A localised fluctuant swelling sits directly over the front of the patella in someone who kneels frequently at work.

explanation: An immediate tense haemarthrosis after a non-contact pivoting injury with instability is highly suggestive of ACL rupture. An atraumatic, acutely hot, single swollen joint must be treated as septic arthritis until aspiration proves otherwise, because infection rapidly destroys cartilage. Delayed disproportionate burning pain with swelling, stiffness, skin change and disuse osteopenia indicates complex regional pain syndrome, while a discrete swelling confined to the front of the patella in a habitual kneeler is prepatellar bursitis.
learningPoint: Match the swollen-knee history: rapid bloody effusion=ACL, atraumatic hot joint=sepsis, delayed burning pain=CRPS, front-of-patella lump=bursitis.

stem: For each patient with isolated medial compartment knee disease, select the single most appropriate surgical option.

options.label: A
options.text: High tibial osteotomy

options.label: B
options.text: Unicompartmental knee replacement

options.label: C
options.text: Total knee replacement

options.label: D
options.text: Knee arthrodesis

options.label: E
options.text: Arthroscopic debridement and chondroplasty

options.label: F
options.text: Autologous chondrocyte implantation

options.label: G
options.text: Non-operative management

options.label: H
options.text: Patellofemoral replacement

emiLeads.stem: A 42-year-old manual labourer with isolated medial osteoarthritis, a correctable varus deformity, intact cruciates and high physical demands who wishes to keep working heavily.
A 68-year-old with anteromedial osteoarthritis, intact anterior cruciate ligament, a correctable deformity and good bone stock who meets all the prerequisites for a partial replacement.
A 72-year-old with tricompartmental osteoarthritis, fixed varus deformity and a deficient anterior cruciate ligament.
A young patient with end-stage post-infective destruction of the joint, disruption of the extensor mechanism and a poor soft-tissue envelope.

explanation: High tibial osteotomy is bone-preserving and suits a younger, high-demand patient with correctable isolated medial disease who wants to defer arthroplasty, realigning load away from the worn compartment. Unicompartmental replacement fits the older patient who satisfies the prerequisites of an intact ACL, a correctable deformity and good bone stock. Tricompartmental disease with a fixed deformity and ACL deficiency is an indication for total knee replacement, whereas arthrodesis is reserved for the young patient with a destroyed joint, extensor mechanism loss or a hostile soft-tissue envelope.
learningPoint: Match medial-compartment disease to treatment: young/high-demand=HTO, older with prerequisites=UKR, tricompartmental=TKR, salvage=arthrodesis.

stem: A patient sustains a displaced talar neck fracture. Disruption of which single vessel is the dominant cause of avascular necrosis of the talar body?

options.label: A
options.text: Artery of the tarsal canal, a branch of the posterior tibial artery

options.label: B
options.text: Artery of the tarsal sinus, a branch of the peroneal artery

options.label: C
options.text: Dorsalis pedis directly supplying the talar head

options.label: D
options.text: First plantar metatarsal artery

options.label: E
options.text: Medial calcaneal branch of the posterior tibial artery

explanation: The talus has no muscular attachments and relies on an anastomotic network from the posterior tibial, anterior tibial and peroneal arteries. The artery of the tarsal canal arising from the posterior tibial artery enters from the medial side and supplies the majority of the talar body, so its disruption in a displaced neck fracture is the principal driver of body AVN. The tarsal sinus vessels (from the anterior tibial and peroneal arteries) chiefly perfuse the head and lateral structures.
learningPoint: The posterior-tibial-derived artery of the tarsal canal is the main supply to the talar body and the key vessel lost in displaced talar neck fractures.

stem: In the development of a hallux valgus deformity, what best explains why the sesamoids appear laterally subluxed on a weight-bearing radiograph?

options.label: A
options.text: The sesamoids migrate laterally by eroding through the adductor hallucis tendon

options.label: B
options.text: The sesamoids stay tethered to the second metatarsal by the intermetatarsal ligament while the first metatarsal head slides medially off the crista

options.label: C
options.text: The sesamoids are pulled laterally by an overactive abductor hallucis

options.label: D
options.text: The sesamoids dislocate because the plantar plate ruptures centrally

options.label: E
options.text: The sesamoids ossify in an abnormal position during adolescence

explanation: The sesamoids themselves do not truly move; they remain anchored in their normal relationship to the second metatarsal via the deep intermetatarsal ligament. As the deformity progresses the first metatarsal head drifts medially and rides up over the crista, which gradually erodes, giving the radiographic impression of lateral sesamoid displacement. The medial capsule attenuates while the lateral structures (including adductor hallucis) contract, and abnormal pull of the long extensor and flexor adds a bowstring effect that worsens valgus.
learningPoint: In hallux valgus the metatarsal head migrates medially off relatively fixed sesamoids, eroding the crista, rather than the sesamoids themselves dislocating.

stem: A 52-year-old woman has symptomatic hallux valgus that has failed footwear modification. Radiographs show a hallux valgus angle of 34 degrees and an intermetatarsal angle of 18 degrees with a congruent joint and no arthritis. Which surgical option is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Distal chevron osteotomy alone

options.label: B
options.text: Isolated McBride distal soft tissue procedure

options.label: C
options.text: Scarf or proximal first metatarsal osteotomy with a distal soft tissue release

options.label: D
options.text: Keller excision arthroplasty

options.label: E
options.text: First metatarsophalangeal joint arthrodesis

explanation: This is a moderate-to-severe deformity by angle (hallux valgus angle in the 30s, intermetatarsal angle approaching 20 degrees). A distal osteotomy such as a chevron has limited correction power and is generally reserved for intermetatarsal angles up to roughly 13 degrees; an angle of this size needs a more proximal correction (scarf or basal osteotomy) combined with a lateral soft tissue release. Keller and fusion are reserved for arthritic, elderly or salvage situations rather than a congruent non-arthritic joint in a younger patient.
learningPoint: Larger intermetatarsal angles (beyond about 13 degrees) require a powerful proximal/diaphyseal osteotomy such as scarf or a basal osteotomy, not a distal chevron alone.

stem: Six weeks after bunion correction a patient has developed a hallux varus deformity. Which intra-operative factor is LEAST likely to have contributed?

options.label: A
options.text: Excessive resection of the medial eminence

options.label: B
options.text: Over-tightening of the medial capsule

options.label: C
options.text: Release or excision of the lateral sesamoid

options.label: D
options.text: Over-correction of the intermetatarsal angle to neutral or negative

options.label: E
options.text: Failure to release the adductor hallucis tendon

explanation: Hallux varus after bunion surgery results from over-aggressive medial-side correction: taking too much medial eminence, over-plicating the medial capsule, removing the lateral sesamoid and over-reducing the intermetatarsal angle all unbalance the toe into varus. Failing to release the adductor hallucis would, if anything, leave a lateral deforming force and predispose to recurrent valgus rather than varus, so it is the odd one out.
learningPoint: Iatrogenic hallux varus is an over-correction problem (too much medial bone/capsule, lateral sesamoid loss, neutral/negative IMA); under-release of lateral structures causes recurrence, not varus.

stem: A 56-year-old man has dorsal first metatarsophalangeal joint pain on push-off. Radiographs show a large dorsal osteophyte but a reasonably preserved joint space. Non-operative measures have failed. What is the most appropriate surgical option?

options.label: A
options.text: Dorsal cheilectomy, with or without a dorsal closing-wedge (Moberg) phalangeal osteotomy

options.label: B
options.text: First metatarsophalangeal joint arthrodesis

options.label: C
options.text: Keller excision arthroplasty

options.label: D
options.text: Total joint replacement

options.label: E
options.text: Proximal first metatarsal osteotomy

explanation: Early hallux rigidus (grades 1-2) with a dorsal impinging osteophyte but maintained joint space is best treated by dorsal cheilectomy to remove the impingement, with a Moberg dorsal closing-wedge osteotomy of the proximal phalanx added to improve dorsiflexion if needed. Arthrodesis (the gold-standard salvage) is reserved for advanced disease with established arthritis and significant joint-space loss, while joint replacement is used selectively.
learningPoint: Grade 1-2 hallux rigidus with preserved joint space = cheilectomy +/- Moberg osteotomy; reserve fusion for advanced arthritic (grade 3) disease.

stem: When fusing the first metatarsophalangeal joint, which position and fixation construct is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Neutral dorsiflexion and neutral rotation, held with a single lag screw

options.label: B
options.text: Around 10 degrees of valgus and 10-15 degrees of dorsiflexion, with a lag screw and a low-profile dorsal plate

options.label: C
options.text: 20 degrees of plantarflexion and 20 degrees of varus, held with a dorsal plate alone

options.label: D
options.text: Neutral dorsiflexion and 15 degrees of varus, held with crossed K-wires

options.label: E
options.text: 30 degrees of dorsiflexion and neutral alignment, held with an external fixator

explanation: The target position for first MTPJ arthrodesis is roughly 10 degrees of valgus with about 10-15 degrees of dorsiflexion relative to the floor, allowing comfortable shoe wear and an efficient toe-off. Biomechanically the most stable fixation is a compression lag screw supplemented by a low-profile dorsal plate, which is significantly stiffer than a lag screw used in isolation. Excess dorsiflexion causes interphalangeal pressure problems and too little causes push-off pain.
learningPoint: Fuse the first MTPJ in ~10 degrees valgus and ~10-15 degrees dorsiflexion; a lag screw plus a dorsal plate gives the most stable construct.

stem: A 45-year-old woman describes burning forefoot pain radiating into adjacent toes, worse in narrow shoes, with a palpable click when the forefoot is squeezed. Which statement about this condition is most accurate?

options.label: A
options.text: It represents a true neoplasm of the digital nerve

options.label: B
options.text: It is most frequently found in the first web space

options.label: C
options.text: It is degenerative perineural fibrosis of a digital nerve, most often in the third web space

options.label: D
options.text: Maximal tenderness is classically over the metatarsal head rather than the web space

options.label: E
options.text: It is more common in men than women

explanation: Morton's neuroma is not a tumour but degenerative fibrosis affecting an interdigital nerve near its bifurcation, thought to relate to repetitive microtrauma and entrapment. It most often affects the third web space, is far commoner in women, and the elicited click on mediolateral compression is Mulder's sign. Tenderness is typically localised to the web space rather than over the metatarsal head, which helps distinguish it from metatarsalgia.
learningPoint: Morton's neuroma = perineural fibrosis (not a tumour), usually third web space, female predominance, with web-space tenderness and a Mulder's click.

stem: Regarding tarsal tunnel syndrome, which statement is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: It is compression of the deep peroneal nerve beneath the inferior extensor retinaculum

options.label: B
options.text: The roof of the tunnel is formed by the flexor retinaculum and the contents include the posterior tibial nerve

options.label: C
options.text: A positive Tinel's sign over the tunnel is highly specific and confirms the diagnosis

options.label: D
options.text: The medial calcaneal branch typically arises distal to the tarsal tunnel

options.label: E
options.text: Symptoms are classically relieved by prolonged standing

explanation: Tarsal tunnel syndrome is compression of the posterior tibial nerve or its branches in the fibro-osseous tunnel on the posteromedial ankle, roofed by the flexor retinaculum and bounded medially by the talus, sustentaculum tali and calcaneus. The nerve usually divides into medial and lateral plantar branches within the tunnel, and the medial calcaneal branch arises proximally to supply the medial heel. Tinel's sign is suggestive but not specific, so nerve conduction studies are the key confirmatory test, and symptoms are typically aggravated, not relieved, by standing.
learningPoint: Tarsal tunnel syndrome = posterior tibial nerve compression under the flexor retinaculum; confirm with nerve conduction studies as Tinel's is non-specific.

stem: A teenager with Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease has a cavovarus foot. Which combination of muscle imbalance best accounts for the plantarflexed first ray and hindfoot varus?

options.label: A
options.text: Strong tibialis anterior overpowering a weak tibialis posterior

options.label: B
options.text: Relatively preserved peroneus longus and tibialis posterior with weak tibialis anterior and weak peroneus brevis

options.label: C
options.text: Weak peroneus longus with strong peroneus brevis

options.label: D
options.text: Strong intrinsics overpowering the long extensors

options.label: E
options.text: Isolated gastrocnemius contracture with normal balance elsewhere

explanation: In CMT the classic pattern is weakness of tibialis anterior and peroneus brevis with relative preservation of their antagonists. A relatively strong peroneus longus drives the first ray into plantarflexion (forefoot-driven cavus), and with tibialis posterior unopposed by a weak peroneus brevis the hindfoot is pulled into varus. The weak tibialis anterior leads to recruitment of the long extensors, which aggravates clawing of the toes.
learningPoint: CMT cavovarus = weak tibialis anterior and peroneus brevis with relatively strong peroneus longus (plantarflexed first ray) and tibialis posterior (hindfoot varus).

stem: While assessing a cavovarus foot you perform a Coleman block test and the heel corrects from varus to neutral when the patient stands with the first ray off the block. What does this finding indicate?

options.label: A
options.text: The hindfoot varus is rigid and a calcaneal osteotomy is mandatory

options.label: B
options.text: The hindfoot is flexible and driven by the forefoot, so a hindfoot fusion is not required to correct it

options.label: C
options.text: There is a fixed subtalar coalition

options.label: D
options.text: The deformity is purely neurological and surgery is contraindicated

options.label: E
options.text: A triple arthrodesis is the only appropriate treatment

explanation: The Coleman block test offloads the plantarflexed first ray so that, if the hindfoot varus is being driven by the forefoot, the heel will swing back into neutral, demonstrating a flexible and correctable subtalar joint. This implies that addressing the forefoot (for example a dorsiflexion first metatarsal osteotomy and soft tissue balancing) can correct the hindfoot without a formal hindfoot fusion. If the heel stays in varus on the block the deformity is fixed and a calcaneal osteotomy or triple fusion is needed.
learningPoint: A correcting heel on the Coleman block means flexible, forefoot-driven hindfoot varus that does not require hindfoot fusion to realign.

stem: A 58-year-old woman has progressive flatfoot with hindfoot valgus and a 'too many toes' sign. She cannot perform a single-limb heel raise but the hindfoot is still passively correctable to neutral with no subtalar arthritis. Which surgical strategy fits this stage?

options.label: A
options.text: Tenosynovectomy of the tendon alone

options.label: B
options.text: Flexor digitorum longus transfer with a medialising calcaneal osteotomy (and Achilles lengthening as needed)

options.label: C
options.text: Isolated subtalar arthrodesis

options.label: D
options.text: Triple arthrodesis

options.label: E
options.text: Pantalar arthrodesis

explanation: Loss of single-limb heel raise with a flexible, passively correctable hindfoot and no arthritis is stage II tibialis posterior dysfunction in the Johnson classification. The reconstructive approach combines a tendon transfer (commonly flexor digitorum longus to the navicular) to restore the dynamic invertor with a bony realignment such as a medialising calcaneal osteotomy, plus Achilles lengthening for an equinus contracture. Tenosynovectomy alone suits stage I, and fusions are reserved for the fixed/arthritic stages III and IV.
learningPoint: Stage II tibialis posterior dysfunction (flexible, no arthritis, failed heel raise) is treated with tendon transfer plus realignment osteotomy, not fusion.

stem: A patient with recurrent ankle sprains is described as having 'functional' rather than 'mechanical' instability. What does this distinction mean?

options.label: A
options.text: Functional instability is abnormal talar translation demonstrable on stress radiographs

options.label: B
options.text: Functional instability is the symptom of giving way without necessarily having abnormal talar movement on stress views

options.label: C
options.text: Functional instability only occurs after surgical reconstruction

options.label: D
options.text: Mechanical and functional instability always coexist

options.label: E
options.text: Mechanical instability refers to a sensation that the ankle is about to give way

explanation: Freeman's distinction separates functional instability, the patient's subjective complaint of the ankle giving way (often linked to proprioceptive deficit and peroneal weakness), from mechanical instability, which is objectively abnormal talar movement within the mortise demonstrated on stress radiographs. The two do not always coincide: many patients with functional symptoms have normal stress films, and some with measurable laxity are asymptomatic. This is why initial management is a functional rehabilitation programme.
learningPoint: Functional instability = subjective giving way (proprioception/peroneal issues); mechanical instability = measurable abnormal talar movement on stress views; they need not coexist.

stem: A patient with chronic lateral ankle instability has failed rehabilitation and is listed for stabilisation. Which statement about the surgical options is most accurate?

options.label: A
options.text: The modified Brostrom procedure reinforces an anatomical repair using the inferior extensor retinaculum

options.label: B
options.text: The Chrisman-Snook is a non-anatomical reconstruction that sacrifices the whole tibialis posterior tendon

options.label: C
options.text: Arthroscopy has no role because intra-articular pathology is rare in these patients

options.label: D
options.text: A coexisting cavovarus hindfoot can be ignored when planning reconstruction

options.label: E
options.text: Anatomical repair is contraindicated in all revision settings

explanation: The modified Brostrom-Gould is the workhorse anatomical procedure: the attenuated ATFL/CFL are reattached and the repair is reinforced with the inferior extensor retinaculum. The Chrisman-Snook is a non-anatomical tenodesis that uses peroneus brevis (not tibialis posterior) and is generally reserved for revision or high-demand situations. Intra-articular lesions such as osteochondral defects and synovitis are common, so arthroscopy is valuable, and an underlying cavovarus deformity must be addressed or the repair will fail.
learningPoint: Modified Brostrom-Gould = anatomical repair reinforced with extensor retinaculum; Chrisman-Snook is a non-anatomical peroneus brevis tenodesis for revision/high-demand cases.

stem: An ankle fracture is classified as supination-external rotation (the commonest pattern). Which sequence of structural failure corresponds to this mechanism?

options.label: A
options.text: Transverse avulsion fracture of the lateral malleolus below the joint, then deltoid injury

options.label: B
options.text: Anterior tibiofibular ligament, then a spiral fibular fracture, then posterior tibiofibular ligament or posterior malleolus, then medial malleolus or deltoid

options.label: C
options.text: High fibular fracture first, then syndesmosis, then medial structures

options.label: D
options.text: Medial malleolus transverse fracture first, then lateral collateral ligament

options.label: E
options.text: Isolated posterior malleolar fracture with no other injury

explanation: In the supination-external rotation injury the talus rotates externally on a supinated foot and structures fail in a predictable order: first the anterior tibiofibular (syndesmotic) ligament, then a spiral fracture of the fibula at the syndesmosis level, then the posterior tibiofibular ligament or posterior malleolus, and finally the medial malleolus or deltoid ligament. This corresponds to a Weber B fibular pattern. Recognising the sequence helps predict the deep deltoid/medial injury even when the medial malleolus looks intact.
learningPoint: SER: anterior syndesmosis to spiral fibular fracture to posterior malleolus/PITFL to medial malleolus/deltoid - a Weber B equivalent and the commonest pattern.

stem: A patient falls from height and sustains an intra-articular calcaneal fracture. On the lateral radiograph Bohler's tuber angle is reduced to 10 degrees. What does this indicate, and which associated injury must be actively excluded?

options.label: A
options.text: An increase from the normal of 100-105 degrees, exclude a hip fracture

options.label: B
options.text: A decrease from a normal of about 20-40 degrees indicating posterior facet collapse, and a thoracolumbar spine fracture must be excluded

options.label: C
options.text: A normal Bohler's angle, so no further imaging is needed

options.label: D
options.text: Reduced crucial angle of Gissane, exclude a patella fracture

options.label: E
options.text: A decrease in the normal calcaneal pitch, exclude a femoral neck fracture

explanation: Bohler's tuber angle normally measures roughly 20-40 degrees; a reduced value reflects flattening of the calcaneus from depression of the posterior facet. Calcaneal fractures from axial loading carry well-recognised associated injuries, the classic being thoracolumbar spine fractures (around 10 percent), so the spine must be cleared, and the contralateral calcaneus and lower limb should also be assessed. The angle of Gissane is a separate measurement (normally about 100-105 degrees) that opens up with facet disruption.
learningPoint: Bohler's angle (normal ~20-40 degrees) drops with posterior facet collapse; always screen for an associated thoracolumbar spine fracture.

stem: Which single factor is the most important contributor to ulceration in the diabetic foot?

options.label: A
options.text: Peripheral sensory neuropathy with loss of protective sensation

options.label: B
options.text: Large-vessel atherosclerotic occlusion

options.label: C
options.text: Diabetic retinopathy

options.label: D
options.text: Autonomic neuropathy alone

options.label: E
options.text: Renal impairment

explanation: Although diabetic foot disease is multifactorial, sensory neuropathy with loss of protective sensation is the dominant cause of ulceration; the inability to perceive a 10 g (5.07) Semmes-Weinstein monofilament markedly raises the risk, and loss of sensation under the first metatarsal head is the single most predictive site. Motor neuropathy adds clawing and pressure, autonomic neuropathy causes dry, fissured skin, and ischaemia and impaired immunity worsen healing, but neuropathy is the key initiating factor.
learningPoint: Sensory neuropathy (inability to feel a 10 g monofilament) is the single most important cause of diabetic foot ulceration.

stem: A diabetic patient presents with a hot, swollen, erythematous midfoot and early radiographic fragmentation but intact skin. Which statement about management is most accurate?

options.label: A
options.text: This is the consolidation (Eichenholtz stage 3) phase and surgical fusion should proceed now

options.label: B
options.text: This is the fragmentation (Eichenholtz stage 1) phase and the priority is offloading in a total contact cast while excluding osteomyelitis

options.label: C
options.text: Immediate amputation is indicated

options.label: D
options.text: Weight-bearing should be encouraged to maintain bone density

options.label: E
options.text: The commonest site of Charcot in the foot is the ankle joint

explanation: Acute Charcot neuroarthropathy presents with a warm, swollen, red foot and early bony fragmentation; this is Eichenholtz stage 1 (destruction/fragmentation). The cornerstone of management is offloading and immobilisation in a total contact cast (or removable walker) with strict non- or protected weight-bearing, while excluding infection, which can mimic it. Reconstructive surgery, if needed, is best deferred until the process settles into the coalescence/consolidation phases; the commonest site is the tarsometatarsal region (Brodsky type 1), not the ankle.
learningPoint: Acute (Eichenholtz stage 1) Charcot is managed by total contact casting and offloading while excluding infection; the commonest site is the tarsometatarsal joints.

stem: Which patient with end-stage ankle arthritis is the BEST candidate for total ankle replacement rather than arthrodesis?

options.label: A
options.text: A 35-year-old labourer with marked varus deformity and ligamentous instability

options.label: B
options.text: A lower-demand 62-year-old with osteoarthritis, a well-aligned ankle and no instability or infection

options.label: C
options.text: A patient with active deep infection of the ankle

options.label: D
options.text: A young patient with Charcot neuroarthropathy of the ankle

options.label: E
options.text: A patient with severe uncorrectable deformity and neuromuscular imbalance

explanation: Total ankle replacement is best reserved for older, lower-demand patients with osteoarthritis or inflammatory arthritis, a reasonably well-aligned ankle, and an absence of significant deformity, instability, neuromuscular disorder or infection. The younger high-demand labourer, the infected ankle, the Charcot joint and the severely deformed neuromuscular foot are all poor candidates, in whom arthrodesis is generally preferred. Modern (third-generation) designs have improved survivorship but patient selection remains critical.
learningPoint: Ideal TAR candidate: older, lower-demand, well-aligned, stable, non-infected arthritic ankle; deformity, instability, youth, infection or neuropathy favour fusion.

stem: For each presentation of forefoot pain, select the single most likely diagnosis.

options.label: A
options.text: Morton's neuroma

options.label: B
options.text: Freiberg's disease

options.label: C
options.text: Hallux rigidus

options.label: D
options.text: Sesamoiditis / turf toe

options.label: E
options.text: Stress fracture of a metatarsal

options.label: F
options.text: Hallux valgus

options.label: G
options.text: Plantar plate rupture

options.label: H
options.text: Gout of the first MTPJ

emiLeads.stem: A 16-year-old gymnast has localised pain and stiffness of the second metatarsophalangeal joint; radiographs show flattening and collapse of the dorsal part of an unusually long second metatarsal head.
A 45-year-old woman has burning pain radiating into the third and fourth toes, relieved by removing her shoe, with a click on transverse forefoot compression.
A 55-year-old man has dorsal first MTPJ pain on push-off and a hard dorsal lump; the joint is stiff with a preserved plantar joint line and dorsal osteophyte on radiograph.
A sprinter has acute pain under the first metatarsal head after forced great-toe dorsiflexion, with point tenderness over the plantar sesamoid region.

explanation: Freiberg's disease is osteochondrosis/infraction of a lesser metatarsal head (classically the second, often relatively long) presenting in adolescence with a stiff painful joint and dorsal head collapse. Morton's neuroma gives web-space neuritic pain with a Mulder's click. Hallux rigidus produces dorsal first MTPJ osteophytes and push-off pain with a stiff joint. A hyperdorsiflexion injury damaging the plantar plate/sesamoid complex of the great toe is turf toe with sesamoid tenderness.
learningPoint: Localise forefoot pain: web space and click = neuroma; collapsed long lesser metatarsal head = Freiberg's; stiff dorsal first MTPJ osteophyte = hallux rigidus; plantar great-toe after hyperdorsiflexion = sesamoid/turf toe.

stem: Match each clinical scenario to the eponymous classification system most appropriate to describe it.

options.label: A
options.text: Johnson and Strom classification

options.label: B
options.text: Sanders CT classification

options.label: C
options.text: Lauge-Hansen classification

options.label: D
options.text: Eichenholtz classification

options.label: E
options.text: Wagner classification

options.label: F
options.text: Hawkins classification

options.label: G
options.text: Myerson classification

options.label: H
options.text: Brodsky classification

emiLeads.stem: A coronal-plane CT grading of an intra-articular calcaneal fracture based on the number and position of posterior facet fragments.
A grading of adult acquired flatfoot from tibialis posterior dysfunction, guiding treatment from tenosynovitis through to fixed hindfoot deformity.
A staging system describing the destruction, coalescence and consolidation phases of neuroarthropathy.
A grading of diabetic foot ulcers from intact skin at risk, through superficial and deep ulcers, to gangrene of the forefoot or whole foot.

explanation: The Sanders system uses coronal CT cuts to count posterior facet fragments and predict outcome of intra-articular calcaneal fractures. Johnson and Strom (with the Myerson stage IV addition) stages tibialis posterior dysfunction and guides treatment. Eichenholtz describes the temporal stages of Charcot neuroarthropathy (fragmentation, coalescence, consolidation). Wagner grades diabetic ulcer depth and gangrene. Brodsky, by contrast, describes the anatomical distribution of Charcot, and Lauge-Hansen describes ankle fracture mechanism.
learningPoint: Sanders = calcaneal CT; Johnson/Strom = tibialis posterior dysfunction; Eichenholtz = Charcot stages; Wagner = diabetic ulcer depth.

subtopic: Cutaneous nerve supply of the foot
stem: For each area of altered sensation, select the nerve most likely responsible.

options.label: A
options.text: Deep peroneal nerve

options.label: B
options.text: Superficial peroneal nerve

options.label: C
options.text: Sural nerve

options.label: D
options.text: Saphenous nerve

options.label: E
options.text: Medial plantar nerve

options.label: F
options.text: Lateral plantar nerve

options.label: G
options.text: Medial calcaneal branch of the posterior tibial nerve

options.label: H
options.text: Tibial nerve trunk

emiLeads.stem: Numbness confined to the first dorsal web space between the great and second toes after anterior ankle surgery.
Reduced sensation over the lateral border of the foot and little toe following a posterolateral ankle approach.
Altered sensation over the medial side of the heel pad in a patient with proximal posterior tibial nerve compression.
Numbness over the medial border of the foot just proximal to the great toe after saphenous vein harvest at the ankle.

explanation: The deep peroneal nerve supplies only the first dorsal web space, so isolated numbness there points to it after anterior ankle dissection. The sural nerve supplies the lateral foot and little toe and is at risk in posterolateral approaches. The medial calcaneal branch of the posterior tibial nerve serves the medial heel and arises proximal to the tarsal tunnel. The saphenous nerve supplies the medial border of the foot and accompanies the long saphenous vein at the medial ankle.
learningPoint: First web space = deep peroneal; lateral foot/little toe = sural; medial heel = medial calcaneal branch; medial foot border = saphenous.

stem: For each foot or ankle problem, select the single most appropriate surgical procedure.

options.label: A
options.text: Triple arthrodesis

options.label: B
options.text: Modified Brostrom repair

options.label: C
options.text: Medialising calcaneal osteotomy with FDL transfer

options.label: D
options.text: Dorsal cheilectomy

options.label: E
options.text: Total contact casting

options.label: F
options.text: First MTPJ arthrodesis

options.label: G
options.text: Plantar fascia release

options.label: H
options.text: Subtalar arthrodesis

emiLeads.stem: A rigid, fixed cavovarus or arthritic planovalgus hindfoot with deformity across the subtalar, talonavicular and calcaneocuboid joints requiring three-joint correction.
A young athlete with recurrent lateral ankle giving way and demonstrable laxity who has failed proprioceptive rehabilitation, with attenuated but repairable ligaments.
A flexible stage II adult acquired flatfoot from tibialis posterior dysfunction with a correctable hindfoot and no arthritis.
An acute neuroarthropathic midfoot with intact skin in the fragmentation phase requiring offloading rather than fixation.

explanation: A fixed multi-joint hindfoot deformity is corrected by a triple arthrodesis fusing the subtalar, talonavicular and calcaneocuboid joints. Recurrent lateral instability with repairable ligaments after failed rehabilitation is best managed by an anatomical modified Brostrom repair. Flexible stage II tibialis posterior dysfunction is reconstructed with a medialising calcaneal osteotomy plus FDL transfer. Acute Charcot with intact skin is treated non-operatively with total contact casting and offloading.
learningPoint: Match deformity to procedure: fixed multi-joint hindfoot = triple fusion; repairable lateral laxity = Brostrom; flexible stage II flatfoot = calcaneal osteotomy + FDL transfer; acute Charcot = total contact cast.

stem: For each patient presenting with heel or hindfoot pain, choose the single most likely diagnosis.

options.label: A
options.text: Plantar fasciitis

options.label: B
options.text: Tarsal tunnel syndrome

options.label: C
options.text: Calcaneal stress fracture

options.label: D
options.text: Insertional Achilles tendinopathy with Haglund deformity

options.label: E
options.text: Baxter's nerve entrapment

options.label: F
options.text: Retrocalcaneal bursitis

options.label: G
options.text: Sever's disease

options.label: H
options.text: Complex regional pain syndrome

emiLeads.stem: A 48-year-old has sharp inferomedial heel pain that is worst with the first few steps in the morning and eases with activity; tenderness localises to the medial calcaneal tuberosity.
A runner has posterior heel pain with a tender bony prominence at the Achilles insertion and difficulty with shoe wear; radiographs show a prominent posterosuperior calcaneal tuberosity.
A patient has burning, tingling pain in the sole with paraesthesiae aggravated by standing, and percussion behind the medial malleolus reproduces symptoms.
Six months after a Weber B ankle fracture treated in plaster, a patient has disproportionate burning pain, swelling, allodynia and a mottled, stiff foot with patchy radiographic osteopenia.

explanation: Plantar fasciitis classically causes inferomedial heel pain at the medial calcaneal tuberosity, worst with the first steps of the day. Insertional Achilles tendinopathy with a Haglund prominence gives posterior heel pain and shoe-wear difficulty. Tarsal tunnel syndrome produces neuritic plantar symptoms aggravated by standing with a positive Tinel's behind the medial malleolus. Disproportionate pain with allodynia, swelling and patchy osteopenia after a fracture is complex regional pain syndrome.
learningPoint: First-step inferomedial pain = plantar fasciitis; posterior bony prominence = Haglund/insertional Achilles; plantar paraesthesiae worse standing = tarsal tunnel; disproportionate post-fracture pain with osteopenia = CRPS.

subtopic: Surgical approaches and structures at risk
stem: For each surgical approach to the foot and ankle, select the structure most at risk.

options.label: A
options.text: Superficial peroneal nerve

options.label: B
options.text: Sural nerve and short saphenous vein

options.label: C
options.text: Posterior tibial neurovascular bundle

options.label: D
options.text: Anterior tibial artery and deep peroneal nerve

options.label: E
options.text: Saphenous nerve and long saphenous vein

options.label: F
options.text: Dorsal cutaneous branch to the great toe

options.label: G
options.text: Flexor hallucis longus tendon

options.label: H
options.text: Medial calcaneal nerve

emiLeads.stem: An anterior approach to the ankle developing the interval between extensor hallucis longus and extensor digitorum longus.
A posterolateral approach to the distal tibia and fibula between the lateral malleolus and the Achilles tendon.
A posteromedial approach to decompress the tarsal tunnel between the medial malleolus and the Achilles tendon.
A medial approach for hallux valgus surgery over the first metatarsophalangeal joint.

explanation: The anterior ankle approach places the anterior tibial artery and deep peroneal nerve, which run in the interval and travel laterally with the extensor digitorum, directly at risk. The posterolateral approach endangers the sural nerve and short saphenous vein lying between the lateral malleolus and Achilles. The posteromedial (tarsal tunnel) approach is centred on the posterior tibial neurovascular bundle. The medial approach for hallux valgus risks the dorsal cutaneous sensory branch to the great toe, which a correctly placed medial incision is designed to protect.
learningPoint: Anterior ankle = anterior tibial artery/deep peroneal nerve; posterolateral = sural nerve/short saphenous vein; tarsal tunnel = posterior tibial bundle; medial bunion approach = dorsal cutaneous branch to hallux.

stem: While performing a posterior approach to the shoulder, the surgeon must be aware of the structures traversing the quadrangular space. Which two structures pass through this interval?

options.label: A
options.text: Radial nerve and profunda brachii artery

options.label: B
options.text: Axillary nerve and posterior circumflex humeral vessels

options.label: C
options.text: Suprascapular nerve and suprascapular artery

options.label: D
options.text: Circumflex scapular vessels alone

options.label: E
options.text: Musculocutaneous nerve and brachial artery

explanation: The quadrangular space is bounded by teres minor (above), teres major (below), the long head of triceps (medially) and the humeral shaft (laterally). It transmits the axillary nerve and the posterior circumflex humeral artery. The radial nerve and profunda brachii instead run through the triangular interval, and the circumflex scapular vessels occupy the triangular space medial to the long head of triceps.
learningPoint: Quadrangular space contents: axillary nerve and posterior circumflex humeral vessels.

stem: A candidate is asked about the coracoid process. Which of the following structures does NOT attach to the coracoid?

options.label: A
options.text: Short head of biceps

options.label: B
options.text: Coracobrachialis

options.label: C
options.text: Pectoralis minor

options.label: D
options.text: Coracoclavicular (conoid and trapezoid) ligaments

options.label: E
options.text: Long head of biceps

explanation: The coracoid is the anchor for three muscles/tendons (short head of biceps, coracobrachialis, pectoralis minor) and several ligaments (conoid and trapezoid components of the coracoclavicular ligament, the coracohumeral ligament and the coraco-acromial ligament). The long head of biceps arises from the supraglenoid tubercle and superior labrum, not the coracoid, so it is the exception.
learningPoint: Long head of biceps originates from the supraglenoid tubercle, not the coracoid.

stem: During a deltopectoral approach the cephalic vein is identified and the conjoint tendon retracted medially. Which nerve lies within the substance of the conjoint tendon a few centimetres distal to the coracoid and is therefore at risk from over-vigorous medial retraction?

options.label: A
options.text: Axillary nerve

options.label: B
options.text: Musculocutaneous nerve

options.label: C
options.text: Suprascapular nerve

options.label: D
options.text: Radial nerve

options.label: E
options.text: Long thoracic nerve

explanation: The musculocutaneous nerve typically enters coracobrachialis roughly 4-6 cm distal to the coracoid, lying within the conjoint tendon. Aggressive medial retraction of the conjoint tendon places it under traction. The axillary nerve is instead at risk at the inferior border of subscapularis and along the quadrangular space.
learningPoint: The musculocutaneous nerve enters the conjoint tendon a few centimetres below the coracoid and is endangered by forceful medial retraction.

stem: Which vessel is classically described as the dominant supply to the humeral head, ascending in the bicipital groove and giving the arcuate branch?

options.label: A
options.text: Posterior circumflex humeral artery

options.label: B
options.text: Anterior circumflex humeral artery

options.label: C
options.text: Suprascapular artery

options.label: D
options.text: Thoracoacromial artery

options.label: E
options.text: Subscapular artery

explanation: The ascending branch of the anterior circumflex humeral artery runs in the intertubercular groove and continues as the arcuate artery, classically perfusing the majority of the humeral head. The posterior circumflex humeral artery supplies only a small posteroinferior region, although it provides an important collateral supply in valgus-impacted fractures. Recent work has emphasised the posterior contribution more, but the anterior circumflex remains the traditional viva answer.
learningPoint: The arcuate branch of the anterior circumflex humeral artery is the classic dominant supply to the humeral head.

stem: When planning an anatomic total shoulder replacement, which set of values best represents normal humeral head retroversion and glenoid version?

options.label: A
options.text: Humeral retroversion 30 degrees; glenoid retroversion approximately 2-7 degrees

options.label: B
options.text: Humeral anteversion 30 degrees; glenoid anteversion 20 degrees

options.label: C
options.text: Humeral retroversion 60 degrees; glenoid retroversion 25 degrees

options.label: D
options.text: Humeral retroversion 10 degrees; glenoid anteversion 15 degrees

options.label: E
options.text: Humeral neutral version; glenoid retroversion 40 degrees

explanation: The humeral head is retroverted by roughly 30 degrees relative to the transepicondylar axis and sits posteriorly offset on the shaft. The glenoid is normally retroverted only a few degrees (about 2-7 degrees) with a slight superior tilt. Excess posterior glenoid erosion increases retroversion and predisposes to posterior instability after arthroplasty.
learningPoint: Humeral head retroversion is about 30 degrees; native glenoid version is only a few degrees of retroversion.

stem: A 22-year-old rugby player has had three traumatic anterior dislocations of the same shoulder, each requiring reduction. Imaging shows a Bankart lesion. Using the traditional instability classification, how is this best described?

options.label: A
options.text: AMBRI

options.label: B
options.text: TUBS

options.label: C
options.text: Voluntary multidirectional instability

options.label: D
options.text: Muscle-patterning instability

options.label: E
options.text: Atraumatic posterior instability

explanation: TUBS describes Traumatic, Unidirectional instability with a Bankart lesion that usually requires Surgery ("torn loose"). AMBRI describes Atraumatic, Multidirectional, Bilateral instability managed by Rehabilitation, occasionally needing an Inferior capsular shift ("born loose"). A young athlete with recurrent traumatic dislocations and a Bankart lesion is the prototypical TUBS pattern.
learningPoint: TUBS = traumatic, unidirectional, Bankart, surgical; AMBRI = atraumatic, multidirectional, bilateral, rehab, inferior shift.

stem: A fit 19-year-old sustains a first-time traumatic anterior shoulder dislocation. Which statement about prognosis and management best reflects the evidence?

options.label: A
options.text: Recurrence risk is under 5 percent regardless of age

options.label: B
options.text: Recurrence is strongly age-dependent and may approach 90 percent in young athletes managed non-operatively

options.label: C
options.text: Immediate arthroplasty gives the best functional outcome

options.label: D
options.text: Recurrence risk increases with advancing age

options.label: E
options.text: Six weeks of strict immobilisation eliminates the risk of recurrence

explanation: Recurrence after a first traumatic anterior dislocation is inversely related to age: it is highest in adolescents and young athletes and may reach 80-90 percent in this group when treated non-operatively. Older patients are more likely to sustain a rotator cuff tear but are far less prone to recurrent instability. In a high-demand young patient, early arthroscopic Bankart repair reduces recurrence substantially compared with conservative care.
learningPoint: Recurrence of anterior instability falls with age; young athletes face the highest redislocation risk.

stem: A 25-year-old contact athlete with recurrent anterior instability is found to have significant anteroinferior glenoid bone loss producing an "inverted-pear" glenoid and an engaging Hill-Sachs lesion. Which intervention is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Arthroscopic soft-tissue Bankart repair alone

options.label: B
options.text: Bony reconstruction such as a coracoid transfer (Latarjet)

options.label: C
options.text: Physiotherapy and activity modification only

options.label: D
options.text: Isolated arthroscopic capsular plication

options.label: E
options.text: Reverse total shoulder replacement

explanation: Significant glenoid bone loss (the inverted-pear glenoid) and an engaging Hill-Sachs lesion are recognised predictors of failure after isolated arthroscopic soft-tissue repair. In these patients a bony procedure such as the Latarjet coracoid transfer addresses the deficiency by restoring the glenoid arc and adding the sling effect of the conjoint tendon. Soft-tissue repair alone in this setting carries a high redislocation rate.
learningPoint: Glenoid bone loss with an engaging Hill-Sachs lesion calls for a bony procedure rather than isolated arthroscopic Bankart repair.

stem: A 38-year-old swimmer reports painful-arc shoulder symptoms. Which examination test most specifically reproduces subacromial impingement by driving the greater tuberosity under the acromion?

options.label: A
options.text: Hawkins-Kennedy test

options.label: B
options.text: Gerber lift-off test

options.label: C
options.text: Scarf (cross-body adduction) test

options.label: D
options.text: Sulcus sign

options.label: E
options.text: Speed test

explanation: The Hawkins-Kennedy test forward-flexes the shoulder to 90 degrees with the elbow flexed and internally rotates the arm, driving the greater tuberosity beneath the coraco-acromial arch and reproducing impingement pain. The lift-off test assesses subscapularis, the scarf test targets the acromioclavicular joint, the sulcus sign indicates inferior laxity, and Speed test is for the long head of biceps.
learningPoint: Hawkins-Kennedy reproduces subacromial impingement by internally rotating the flexed shoulder.

stem: A reverse total shoulder replacement restores active elevation in a cuff-deficient shoulder. Which biomechanical principle best explains how it achieves this?

options.label: A
options.text: It lateralises the centre of rotation to tension the remaining cuff

options.label: B
options.text: It medialises and distalises the centre of rotation, lengthening and tensioning the deltoid

options.label: C
options.text: It relies on an intact supraspinatus to elevate the arm

options.label: D
options.text: It converts the deltoid force into a purely compressive vector

options.label: E
options.text: It restores the transverse force couple of infraspinatus and subscapularis

explanation: By fixing a convex glenosphere on the glenoid and a concave cup on the humerus, the reverse prosthesis medialises and distalises the centre of rotation. This lengthens the deltoid lever arm and tensions the muscle, allowing it to substitute for the absent rotator cuff and elevate the arm. Medialisation also reduces torque on the glenoid baseplate, though scapular notching and limited external rotation remain recognised drawbacks.
learningPoint: The reverse shoulder works by medialising and distalising the centre of rotation to tension the deltoid.

stem: A 52-year-old with diabetes has a painful, globally stiff shoulder with markedly reduced passive external rotation and normal radiographs. Which statement about the underlying pathology of this condition is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: It is a crystal-induced destructive arthropathy of the humeral head

options.label: B
options.text: It is a fibroproliferative capsular contracture, with type III collagen and myofibroblasts, centred on the rotator interval

options.label: C
options.text: It is caused by a full-thickness supraspinatus tear with superior migration

options.label: D
options.text: It is due to glenohumeral osteoarthritis with posterior glenoid wear

options.label: E
options.text: It results from an unreduced posterior dislocation

explanation: Adhesive capsulitis is a fibroproliferative contracture of the glenohumeral capsule, histologically resembling Dupuytren disease, with abundant fibroblasts and myofibroblasts laying down type III collagen and active angiogenesis concentrated in the rotator interval. It is strongly associated with diabetes and progresses through freezing, frozen and thawing phases. The loss of passive external rotation with normal radiographs is characteristic, distinguishing it from glenohumeral arthritis or a locked posterior dislocation.
learningPoint: Frozen shoulder is a myofibroblast-driven fibrotic capsular contracture centred on the rotator interval, with loss of passive external rotation and normal radiographs.

stem: A 30-year-old is brought in after a generalised seizure with a painful shoulder held in internal rotation and adduction. He cannot externally rotate the arm at all. Which radiographic view is most useful for confirming the suspected diagnosis?

options.label: A
options.text: Standard AP in internal rotation only

options.label: B
options.text: Axillary (or modified axial) view

options.label: C
options.text: Zanca view

options.label: D
options.text: Stryker notch view

options.label: E
options.text: Weight-bearing AP of both shoulders

explanation: Posterior dislocation is frequently missed because the AP film can look near-normal (the "light-bulb" sign of fixed internal rotation may be subtle). An axillary or modified axial view directly demonstrates the humeral head sitting posterior to the glenoid and any reverse Hill-Sachs (McLaughlin) lesion. The combination of a seizure, fixed internal rotation and absent external rotation should always raise suspicion of posterior dislocation.
learningPoint: Suspect posterior dislocation after a seizure with a fixed internally rotated shoulder; confirm with an axillary view.

stem: A patient develops medial winging of the scapula after a viral illness, with difficulty elevating the arm above the head. Which nerve and muscle are responsible?

options.label: A
options.text: Spinal accessory nerve supplying trapezius

options.label: B
options.text: Long thoracic nerve supplying serratus anterior

options.label: C
options.text: Dorsal scapular nerve supplying the rhomboids

options.label: D
options.text: Suprascapular nerve supplying supraspinatus

options.label: E
options.text: Axillary nerve supplying deltoid

explanation: Medial winging, where the inferior pole of the scapula moves medially and the scapula lifts off the chest wall on forward pushing, reflects serratus anterior weakness from a long thoracic nerve palsy. Lateral winging instead arises from trapezius weakness due to a spinal accessory nerve injury, classically iatrogenic after posterior triangle lymph node biopsy. A fascioscapulohumeral dystrophy is a differential and may be screened with creatine kinase.
learningPoint: Medial scapular winging = long thoracic nerve / serratus anterior; lateral winging = spinal accessory nerve / trapezius.

stem: A young patient with a flail shoulder after a brachial plexus injury is to undergo glenohumeral arthrodesis. Which position of fusion is generally accepted as functional?

options.label: A
options.text: 60 degrees flexion, 60 degrees abduction, neutral rotation

options.label: B
options.text: Approximately 20-30 degrees flexion, 20-30 degrees abduction and 40 degrees internal rotation

options.label: C
options.text: Full external rotation with the arm at the side

options.label: D
options.text: 90 degrees abduction and 90 degrees external rotation

options.label: E
options.text: Neutral in all planes

explanation: A shoulder fusion is positioned so the hand can reach the mouth, the opposite shoulder, the belt line and the back pocket. This is achieved with roughly 20-30 degrees of flexion, 20-30 degrees of abduction and about 40 degrees of internal rotation. Movement afterwards is entirely scapulothoracic, so functioning serratus anterior and trapezius and good distal limb function are prerequisites.
learningPoint: Shoulder arthrodesis position: about 20-30 degrees flexion, 20-30 degrees abduction, 40 degrees internal rotation, relying on scapulothoracic motion.

stem: A 40-year-old weightlifter has pain localised to the top of the shoulder, worse on cross-body movement and washing the opposite axilla. Which examination finding most supports acromioclavicular joint pathology?

options.label: A
options.text: Positive sulcus sign

options.label: B
options.text: Positive scarf (cross-body adduction) test with point tenderness over the joint

options.label: C
options.text: Positive apprehension at 90 degrees abduction and external rotation

options.label: D
options.text: Positive Gerber lift-off

options.label: E
options.text: Positive Hornblower sign

explanation: Acromioclavicular joint disease produces well-localised pain and tenderness directly over the joint, reproduced by the scarf (cross-body adduction) test. Plain films (Zanca view) correlate poorly with symptoms. The apprehension test indicates anterior instability, the lift-off test subscapularis dysfunction, and the Hornblower sign teres minor involvement.
learningPoint: A positive scarf test with focal ACJ tenderness points to acromioclavicular pathology.

stem: An elderly woman presents with a rapidly destructive, effusive shoulder; aspiration reveals a blood-stained effusion and basic calcium phosphate (hydroxyapatite) crystals, with radiographs showing marked humeral head and rotator cuff destruction. Before attributing this to crystal disease, which step is essential?

options.label: A
options.text: Commence bisphosphonates immediately

options.label: B
options.text: Exclude joint sepsis

options.label: C
options.text: Proceed directly to reverse arthroplasty

options.label: D
options.text: Start methotrexate for presumed rheumatoid arthritis

options.label: E
options.text: Arrange urgent CT angiography

explanation: This picture is consistent with a basic-calcium-phosphate (hydroxyapatite) destructive arthropathy, sometimes called Milwaukee shoulder, which causes rapid degradation of the cuff and head via released proteases. However, a rapidly destructive effusive joint in an elderly patient can mimic, or coexist with, infection, so septic arthritis must always be excluded before settling on a crystal diagnosis. Management is otherwise largely supportive with analgesia and activity modification.
learningPoint: Always exclude sepsis in a rapidly destructive crystal-associated shoulder arthropathy.

stem: Which feature is the principal theoretical advantage of a stemless humeral resurfacing arthroplasty (for example a Copeland) over a stemmed total shoulder replacement?

options.label: A
options.text: It guarantees a lower revision rate in all age groups

options.label: B
options.text: It preserves bone stock and avoids a diaphyseal stress riser, simplifying revision

options.label: C
options.text: It removes the need to consider humeral version

options.label: D
options.text: It restores active elevation in a cuff-deficient shoulder

options.label: E
options.text: It eliminates the risk of glenoid wear entirely

explanation: A stemless resurfacing implant conserves proximal humeral bone and avoids the diaphyseal stress riser created by a stem, which makes future revision more straightforward. Because there is no stem, problems related to neck-shaft angle and stem fixation are reduced, although the surgeon must still respect version when seating the component. Resurfacing does not address cuff deficiency and a hemiresurfacing can still erode the glenoid over time.
learningPoint: Resurfacing arthroplasty preserves bone and avoids a stem-related stress riser, easing later revision.

stem: In the concept of rotator cuff force couples that centre the humeral head on the glenoid, which pairing represents the transverse (axial) force couple?

options.label: A
options.text: Supraspinatus and deltoid

options.label: B
options.text: Infraspinatus and subscapularis

options.label: C
options.text: Teres major and latissimus dorsi

options.label: D
options.text: Pectoralis major and deltoid

options.label: E
options.text: Long head of biceps and triceps

explanation: In the transverse (axial) plane the posterior cuff (infraspinatus and teres minor) is balanced against the anterior cuff (subscapularis), keeping the head centred from front to back. In the coronal plane the vertical force couple balances the deltoid against the inferior cuff, with supraspinatus contributing to compression. Disruption of these couples, as in massive cuff tears, leads to superior escape of the head.
learningPoint: Transverse force couple = subscapularis versus infraspinatus/teres minor; coronal couple balances deltoid against the inferior cuff.

stem: A surgeon uses the Kocher lateral approach to expose the radial head. Which manoeuvre best protects the posterior interosseous nerve during this exposure?

options.label: A
options.text: Supinating the forearm and extending the elbow

options.label: B
options.text: Pronating the forearm to move the nerve away from the operative field

options.label: C
options.text: Extending dissection distal to the annular ligament

options.label: D
options.text: Releasing the medial collateral ligament first

options.label: E
options.text: Dividing the anconeus from its ulnar origin

explanation: The Kocher approach develops the plane between anconeus (radial nerve) and extensor carpi ulnaris. Pronating the forearm rotates the posterior interosseous nerve anteromedially, moving it further from the surgical field as it passes through supinator. Dissection should be kept proximal to the annular ligament and anterior dissection avoided, because extending distally endangers the PIN.
learningPoint: Pronate the forearm and stay proximal to the annular ligament during the Kocher approach to protect the PIN.

stem: According to the Horii circle concept of posterolateral elbow dislocation, which structure fails first as soft-tissue disruption proceeds from lateral to medial?

options.label: A
options.text: The anterior band of the medial collateral ligament

options.label: B
options.text: The lateral (ulnar) collateral ligament

options.label: C
options.text: The annular ligament

options.label: D
options.text: The anterior capsule

options.label: E
options.text: The brachialis insertion

explanation: In the Horii (O'Driscoll) circle, soft-tissue failure begins laterally with the lateral ulnar collateral ligament and proceeds in a circle through the anterior and posterior capsule to the medial side. The anterior band of the medial collateral ligament is the last to fail and, if intact, provides residual stability with the forearm held in pronation. This explains why posterolateral rotatory instability follows lateral-sided ligament deficiency.
learningPoint: Posterolateral dislocation begins with lateral (ulnar) collateral ligament failure and proceeds circumferentially; the anterior MCL band fails last.

stem: An elbow injury is described as the "terrible triad". Which combination of injuries does this denote?

options.label: A
options.text: Olecranon fracture, radial head fracture and distal humerus fracture

options.label: B
options.text: Elbow dislocation, radial head fracture and coronoid process fracture

options.label: C
options.text: Medial epicondyle avulsion, radial head fracture and capitellar fracture

options.label: D
options.text: Monteggia fracture, coronoid fracture and ulnar nerve palsy

options.label: E
options.text: Capitellar fracture, lateral epicondyle fracture and dislocation

explanation: The terrible triad is the combination of a posterior elbow dislocation with fractures of both the radial head and the coronoid process. It is notoriously unstable because the principal bony and ligamentous stabilisers are all compromised. Surgical management aims to restore a stable, congruent joint by addressing the radial head, the coronoid and the lateral collateral ligament, with medial repair or a hinged external fixator if instability persists.
learningPoint: Terrible triad = elbow dislocation plus radial head and coronoid fractures, an inherently unstable pattern.

stem: Which range of elbow motion is generally accepted as the functional arc needed for most activities of daily living?

options.label: A
options.text: 0 to 145 degrees of flexion with 85 degrees each of pronation and supination

options.label: B
options.text: Approximately 30 to 130 degrees of flexion with about 50 degrees each of pronation and supination

options.label: C
options.text: 0 to 90 degrees of flexion with no rotation required

options.label: D
options.text: 60 to 160 degrees of flexion with 90 degrees of supination only

options.label: E
options.text: Full extension to 100 degrees flexion with 10 degrees of rotation
