explanation: Although the normal arc is roughly 0-145 degrees of flexion with about 75-85 degrees each of pronation and supination, most daily tasks can be performed within a functional arc of approximately 30-130 degrees of flexion and a 100-degree rotational arc (about 50 degrees of pronation and 50 degrees of supination). This concept guides decisions about contracture release and acceptable stiffness after injury.
learningPoint: Functional elbow arc is about 30-130 degrees flexion with 50 degrees each of pronation and supination.

stem: A 45-year-old develops lateral elbow pain aggravated by gripping, with tenderness over the lateral epicondyle and pain on resisted wrist extension. What is the underlying pathological process?

options.label: A
options.text: Acute suppurative tendinitis of the common extensor origin

options.label: B
options.text: Angiofibroblastic tendinosis of the common extensor origin without significant inflammation

options.label: C
options.text: Compression of the posterior interosseous nerve in the radial tunnel

options.label: D
options.text: Osteochondritis dissecans of the capitellum

options.label: E
options.text: Calcific deposition within the annular ligament

explanation: Lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow) is a degenerative tendinosis, not a true tendinitis, characterised by angiofibroblastic (mucoid) degeneration of the extensor carpi radialis brevis origin with little inflammatory infiltrate, analogous to de Quervain disease. Radial tunnel syndrome is the main differential, producing more diffuse, distal forearm discomfort. Most cases settle with non-operative measures, and surgery to excise the abnormal tissue is reserved for refractory cases.
learningPoint: Tennis elbow is an angiofibroblastic tendinosis of the ECRB origin, not an inflammatory tendinitis.

stem: When counselling a patient about total elbow replacement, which statement comparing linked (semiconstrained) and unlinked (unconstrained) designs is most accurate?

options.label: A
options.text: Unlinked implants rely on bone and soft-tissue integrity and carry a higher risk of instability

options.label: B
options.text: Linked implants depend entirely on intact collateral ligaments for stability

options.label: C
options.text: Unlinked implants have a higher rate of polyethylene-wear-related loosening than linked designs

options.label: D
options.text: Linked implants are contraindicated when there is significant bone loss

options.label: E
options.text: Unlinked designs are preferred for acute distal humeral fractures in the elderly

explanation: An unlinked (unconstrained) elbow replacement has no mechanical coupling between the components and therefore depends on the shape of the articulation together with competent ligaments and adequate bone stock; its main drawback is a higher risk of instability and dislocation, making it more suitable for younger patients with good bone and minimal deformity. A linked (semiconstrained) design connects the components with a sloppy-hinge mechanism, providing inherent stability when ligaments or bone are deficient (for example late rheumatoid disease or comminuted distal humeral fractures), at the cost of greater polyethylene-wear-related loosening.
learningPoint: Unlinked elbow implants depend on bone and ligaments and risk instability; linked designs are stable but prone to wear-related loosening.

subtopic: Spaces around the shoulder and their contents
stem: For each described interval around the shoulder and arm, select the single most appropriate set of contents from the list below.

options.label: A
options.text: Axillary nerve and posterior circumflex humeral vessels

options.label: B
options.text: Radial nerve and profunda brachii artery

options.label: C
options.text: Circumflex scapular vessels

options.label: D
options.text: Suprascapular nerve and suprascapular artery

options.label: E
options.text: Musculocutaneous nerve

options.label: F
options.text: Long thoracic nerve

options.label: G
options.text: Median nerve and brachial artery

options.label: H
options.text: Thoracoacromial vessels

emiLeads.stem: An interval bounded by teres minor above, teres major below, the long head of triceps medially and the humeral shaft laterally.
A triangular interval bounded by teres major above, the long head of triceps medially and the humeral shaft laterally, used as a surgical window to the posterior humerus.
A space bounded by teres minor superiorly, teres major inferiorly and the long head of triceps laterally, lying medial to the long head of triceps.
The structure that enters coracobrachialis within the conjoint tendon a few centimetres distal to the coracoid.

explanation: The quadrangular space transmits the axillary nerve and posterior circumflex humeral vessels. The triangular interval (below teres major) carries the radial nerve and profunda brachii. The triangular space (medial to the long head of triceps) contains the circumflex scapular vessels. The musculocutaneous nerve pierces coracobrachialis within the conjoint tendon below the coracoid, a key landmark in the deltopectoral approach.
learningPoint: Distinguish the quadrangular space, triangular interval and triangular space by their borders and neurovascular contents.

stem: For each clinical finding below, select the single most appropriate examination test or sign from the list.

options.label: A
options.text: Gerber lift-off / belly-press test

options.label: B
options.text: Hornblower sign

options.label: C
options.text: Jobe (empty-can) test

options.label: D
options.text: Hawkins-Kennedy test

options.label: E
options.text: Scarf (cross-body adduction) test

options.label: F
options.text: Sulcus sign

options.label: G
options.text: Speed test

options.label: H
options.text: Apprehension test

emiLeads.stem: Used to isolate subscapularis; weakness or inability to hold the hand off the back, or to maintain pressure with the palm on the abdomen.
Reproduces subacromial impingement by internally rotating the shoulder that is forward-flexed to 90 degrees with the elbow bent.
Inability to maintain external rotation of the abducted arm, indicating teres minor deficiency.
Isolates supraspinatus by testing resisted elevation at 90 degrees in the scapular plane with the thumb pointing down.

explanation: The lift-off and belly-press tests assess subscapularis. The Hawkins-Kennedy manoeuvre reproduces subacromial impingement. The Hornblower sign reflects teres minor weakness. The Jobe (empty-can) test isolates supraspinatus by loading it in abduction, scapular-plane flexion and internal rotation. Recognising which structure each test targets allows accurate localisation of cuff pathology.
learningPoint: Match cuff tests to muscles: subscapularis (lift-off/belly-press), supraspinatus (Jobe), teres minor (Hornblower), impingement (Hawkins).

stem: For each clinical scenario, select the single most appropriate surgical option from the list below.

options.label: A
options.text: Anatomic total shoulder replacement

options.label: B
options.text: Reverse total shoulder replacement

options.label: C
options.text: Hemiarthroplasty / resurfacing

options.label: D
options.text: Glenohumeral arthrodesis

options.label: E
options.text: Arthroscopic subacromial decompression

options.label: F
options.text: Latarjet coracoid transfer

options.label: G
options.text: Open Bankart repair

options.label: H
options.text: Manipulation under anaesthesia

emiLeads.stem: A 74-year-old with end-stage cuff-tear arthropathy, pseudoparalysis and an irreparable rotator cuff, but a functioning deltoid.
A 68-year-old with primary glenohumeral osteoarthritis, an intact rotator cuff and good glenoid bone stock.
A young patient with a flail shoulder following a complete brachial plexus injury, but functioning trapezius and serratus anterior.
A 26-year-old contact athlete with recurrent anterior instability and an inverted-pear glenoid from anteroinferior bone loss.

explanation: Cuff-tear arthropathy with pseudoparalysis but a working deltoid is the classic indication for a reverse replacement, which recruits the deltoid to elevate the arm. Primary osteoarthritis with an intact cuff is well treated by an anatomic total shoulder replacement. A flail shoulder with no recoverable cuff but good scapular control is a recognised indication for arthrodesis. Recurrent instability with significant glenoid bone loss is best addressed with a bony procedure such as the Latarjet transfer.
learningPoint: Match the arthroplasty to cuff status and bone stock: reverse for cuff-deficient pseudoparalysis, anatomic TSR for OA with intact cuff, Latarjet for glenoid bone loss.

stem: Using the Snyder classification of superior labrum anterior-to-posterior (SLAP) lesions, select the single best description for each subtype.

options.label: A
options.text: Type I

options.label: B
options.text: Type II

options.label: C
options.text: Type III

options.label: D
options.text: Type IV

options.label: E
options.text: Type V

options.label: F
options.text: Type VI

options.label: G
options.text: Type VII

options.label: H
options.text: Bankart lesion

emiLeads.stem: Fraying and degeneration of the superior labrum with the biceps anchor still firmly attached; usually treated by debridement.
Detachment of the superior labrum together with the biceps anchor from the glenoid, requiring reattachment.
A bucket-handle tear of the superior labrum with an intact biceps anchor, typically debrided.
A bucket-handle labral tear that extends into the biceps tendon, managed by repair or tenodesis.

explanation: In the Snyder scheme, type I is degenerative fraying with an intact anchor (debride), type II is detachment of the labrum and biceps anchor (reattach), type III is a bucket-handle tear with the biceps intact (debride), and type IV is a bucket-handle tear extending into the biceps tendon (repair or tenodesis depending on the amount of tendon involved). Higher subtypes describe additional flap or capsular extensions. Recognising the subtype dictates whether debridement, repair or biceps tenodesis is appropriate.
learningPoint: SLAP types I-IV: fraying (debride), anchor detachment (reattach), bucket-handle biceps-intact (debride), bucket-handle into biceps (repair/tenodesis).

subtopic: Nerves at risk in elbow approaches
stem: For each elbow approach or manoeuvre, select the single nerve most at risk from the list.

options.label: A
options.text: Posterior interosseous nerve

options.label: B
options.text: Median nerve

options.label: C
options.text: Ulnar nerve

options.label: D
options.text: Radial nerve

options.label: E
options.text: Anterior interosseous nerve

options.label: F
options.text: Musculocutaneous nerve

options.label: G
options.text: Lateral antebrachial cutaneous nerve

options.label: H
options.text: Superficial radial nerve

emiLeads.stem: Endangered by distal dissection beyond the annular ligament during the Kocher lateral approach; protected by pronating the forearm.
Must be identified and protected when performing a posterior olecranon osteotomy approach to the distal humerus.
Lies medial to the biceps tendon and brachial artery and is at risk during the anterior (lazy-S) approach to the elbow.
Placed under tension in a fixed flexion deformity and must always be assessed before surgery to correct elbow contracture.

explanation: The posterior interosseous nerve is most vulnerable distal to the annular ligament in the Kocher approach and is protected by forearm pronation. The ulnar nerve must be isolated for a posterior olecranon osteotomy and is also stretched in a fixed flexion deformity, so it should be examined before contracture release. In the anterior lazy-S approach the median nerve lies medial to the biceps tendon and brachial artery.
learningPoint: Know the nerve at risk for each elbow window: PIN laterally (Kocher), ulnar nerve posteromedially, median nerve anteriorly.

stem: For each clinical vignette, select the single most likely diagnosis from the list.

options.label: A
options.text: Osteochondritis dissecans of the capitellum

options.label: B
options.text: Panner disease

options.label: C
options.text: Distal biceps tendon rupture

options.label: D
options.text: Nail-patella syndrome

options.label: E
options.text: Olecranon bursitis

options.label: F
options.text: Posterolateral rotatory instability

options.label: G
options.text: Transepiphyseal separation of the distal humerus

options.label: H
options.text: Lateral epicondylitis

emiLeads.stem: A 45-year-old labourer feels a sudden pop in the antecubital region while lifting; there is bruising and a retracted muscle belly, and the hook test is abnormal.
A 13-year-old competitive gymnast has lateral elbow pain and a capitellar osteochondral lesion on imaging, with a guarded prognosis for return to sport.

An infant under three years has a swollen elbow after a suspicious injury; radiographs show the radiocapitellar alignment preserved but the whole epiphysis displaced, raising concern about non-accidental injury.
A patient with an autosomal dominant disorder has absent or hypoplastic patellae, dysplastic radial heads limiting forearm rotation, iliac horns on pelvic radiographs and renal abnormalities.

explanation: A sudden pop with bruising, a retracted (Popeye) muscle and an abnormal hook test indicates a distal biceps rupture. A capitellar osteochondral lesion in an adolescent athlete is osteochondritis dissecans, distinct from the younger-onset osteonecrosis of Panner disease. In an infant, a displaced distal humeral epiphysis with preserved radiocapitellar alignment suggests transepiphyseal separation and should prompt non-accidental injury evaluation. The combination of nail and patellar dysplasia, radial head dysplasia, iliac horns and renal disease is nail-patella syndrome.
learningPoint: Differentiate elbow diagnoses by age and clues: distal biceps rupture (pop, hook test), adolescent capitellar OCD versus Panner disease, infant transepiphyseal separation (NAI), and the multisystem nail-patella syndrome.

stem: A 32-year-old motorcyclist arrives with an obvious deformity of the left leg and a 10 cm wound over the anteromedial tibia with bone visible in the base. Talk me through your management from the moment they arrive.
modelAnswer: I would treat this as an open long-bone fracture within an ATLS framework. After a primary survey and resuscitation I would assess the limb's neurovascular status and the soft-tissue envelope. Early steps are intravenous antibiotics and tetanus cover, a single clinical photograph of the wound, removal of gross contamination, a saline-soaked dressing, and realignment and splinting with reassessment of the neurovascular status. I would grade the injury (Gustilo-Anderson, definitively at debridement) and involve plastic surgery early as a combined orthoplastic team. Definitive care is a planned debridement on a dedicated list, skeletal stabilisation and soft-tissue cover within the timelines set out in the BOAST guidance.
followUps: How does the Gustilo-Anderson grade influence your antibiotic choice and the need for plastic surgery?
What are the current timelines for debridement and definitive soft-tissue cover?
When would you choose an external fixator over an intramedullary nail here?
How would the development of a compartment syndrome change your plan?

stem: This is the radiograph of a 28-year-old after a high-speed road traffic collision. Describe the injury and tell me how you would manage it.
modelAnswer: The film shows a displaced intracapsular fracture of the femoral neck in a young adult. Because this jeopardises the retinacular blood supply to the femoral head, in a young patient it is a surgical emergency for head preservation. As this is a high-energy injury I would first resuscitate and exclude other injuries, then aim for urgent anatomical reduction (closed or open) and stable internal fixation, accepting the risks of avascular necrosis and non-union. The quality of reduction is the principal modifiable factor for avascular necrosis, and capsular decompression may help. Arthroplasty is reserved for older or physiologically older patients.
followUps: Why does the timing of surgery matter?
Describe the blood supply to the femoral head.
How would you counsel this patient about avascular necrosis and non-union?
How would your management differ in an 80-year-old with the same fracture?

stem: A 68-year-old man has five years of progressive medial knee pain that now disturbs his sleep and limits his walking. His weight-bearing radiographs show isolated medial compartment osteoarthritis with a varus deformity. How would you assess and manage him?
modelAnswer: I would take a focused history (pain pattern, night pain, functional limitation, previous treatment, comorbidities and expectations) and examine the knee — gait and alignment, range of motion, ligamentous stability, the patellofemoral joint and the neurovascular status — with weight-bearing and skyline radiographs. Management follows a ladder from non-operative to operative: weight loss, activity modification, analgesia, physiotherapy and a walking aid, with an intra-articular injection as an adjunct. Surgical options are individualised: a unicompartmental knee replacement for isolated medial disease with an intact ACL and a correctable deformity, a total knee replacement for more diffuse disease, or a high tibial osteotomy in a young, active patient with isolated medial wear.
followUps: What are the indications and contraindications for a unicompartmental knee replacement?
How do you decide between an osteotomy and arthroplasty?
What would you counsel about the longevity and complications of a total knee replacement?
What does the evidence say about arthroscopy for osteoarthritis?

stem: A 6-year-old boy is referred with a three-month painless limp and reduced hip movement; his radiographs show fragmentation of the femoral head epiphysis. What is the diagnosis and how would you approach this child?
modelAnswer: This is Perthes disease — idiopathic osteonecrosis of the proximal femoral epiphysis. I would assess the child's age, the range of hip movement (particularly abduction and internal rotation) and the radiographic stage and extent of head involvement (for example the lateral pillar classification). The principles of treatment are to relieve pain, maintain hip movement and 'contain' the femoral head within the acetabulum so it remodels as a sphere. Younger children with limited involvement often do well with observation and physiotherapy; older children, or those with greater involvement and loss of containment, may need containment by bracing or surgery (a femoral or pelvic osteotomy). Prognosis is worse with older age at onset, greater head involvement and loss of movement.
followUps: What factors determine the prognosis?
Explain the principle of containment and how it is achieved.
How would you distinguish this from a slipped upper femoral epiphysis?
What is your differential for a limping child of this age?

stem: Tell me about the bearing surfaces used in total hip replacement and how you would choose between them.
modelAnswer: The common bearings are metal-on-polyethylene, ceramic-on-polyethylene, ceramic-on-ceramic and, historically, metal-on-metal — each a trade-off between wear, fracture risk and biological response. Highly cross-linked polyethylene has markedly reduced wear and osteolysis and pairs well with a metal or ceramic head; ceramic-on-ceramic has the lowest wear but carries a small risk of fracture and squeaking; metal-on-metal has largely been abandoned because of adverse reactions to metal debris. My default in most patients is a ceramic or metal head on highly cross-linked polyethylene, reserving ceramic-on-ceramic for younger, higher-demand patients. The choice also weighs head size, cost, and the patient's age and activity.
followUps: By what mechanism does polyethylene wear debris lead to loosening?
Why was metal-on-metal largely abandoned?
What is the advantage of a larger femoral head, and its downside?
How does cross-linking change the properties of polyethylene?
references: FRCS Tr&Orth viva - Applied basic science

stem: A 58-year-old woman falls onto her outstretched right arm and sustains a comminuted intra-articular distal humerus fracture. CT confirms bicolumnar involvement with a central depression of the articular surface. She is otherwise healthy. At surgery, after achieving adequate exposure, the surgeon applies parallel plating medially and laterally. Which biomechanical principle best explains why orthogonal (90-90) plating was historically favoured over parallel plating for distal humerus fractures?

options.label: A
options.text: Orthogonal plating distributes bending loads over a greater arc of the distal humerus, improving torsional rigidity

options.label: B
options.text: A medial plate in the orthogonal configuration lies on the broader, flatter medial column, providing a larger bone-plate contact area than its lateral counterpart

options.label: C
options.text: Orthogonal plating was thought to share load across two perpendicular planes, preventing any single plate from bearing the full varus-valgus moment

options.label: D
options.text: The posterior plate in an orthogonal construct acts as a tension band, converting triceps pull into articular compression

options.label: E
options.text: Orthogonal plating avoids hardware prominence in the subcutaneous olecranon groove, reducing implant irritation

explanation: Orthogonal (90-90) plating was originally favoured because it was believed that placing plates in two perpendicular planes shared varus-valgus and flexion-extension loads between them, so neither plate bore the full moment arm alone. Contemporary biomechanical studies have since shown that parallel plating achieves comparable or superior stiffness, particularly in comminuted patterns, because both plates can engage the widest part of the epicondyles with longer screws. Option A incorrectly attributes the benefit to torsional rigidity. Option D conflates orthogonal plating with olecranon tension-band mechanics. Option E is practically reversed — the medial column groove is where implant prominence actually troubles patients.
learningPoint: Orthogonal plating distributes load across two perpendicular planes; parallel plating offers equivalent stiffness with better screw purchase in epicondylar bone.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics – distal humerus biomechanics

stem: A 34-year-old man attends the emergency department after a simple fall onto a pronated, outstretched hand. He has lateral elbow tenderness and a restricted arc of forearm rotation. Plain radiographs reveal a single crack through the radial head involving 35% of the articular surface with 2 mm of step and no comminution. There is no associated dislocation. According to the Mason classification as modified by Broberg and Morrey, which type is this fracture and what is the recommended initial management?

options.label: A
options.text: Mason I; plaster immobilisation for three weeks

options.label: B
options.text: Mason II; aspiration of the haemarthrosis, early mobilisation, and operative fixation if mechanical block persists

options.label: C
options.text: Mason II; immediate open reduction and internal fixation with a headless screw

options.label: D
options.text: Mason III; radial head arthroplasty

options.label: E
options.text: Mason I; aspiration alone with sling support for one week

explanation: Broberg-Morrey Mason II fractures have a marginal fragment exceeding 30% of the head with more than 2 mm of displacement. Non-operative management with aspiration of the haemarthrosis and early active mobilisation is recommended initially; surgery is reserved for those with a demonstrable mechanical block to forearm rotation or for associated ligamentous injury requiring fracture stability. Option C over-commits to immediate ORIF for a fracture that may settle. Option D (arthroplasty) applies to comminuted Mason III or failed fixation. Option A (Mason I) requires neither aspiration nor immobilisation beyond a brief sling.
learningPoint: Mason II radial head fractures are managed non-operatively first; ORIF is indicated only when a mechanical block to rotation persists after haemarthrosis drainage.
references: Broberg MA, Morrey BF (1987) classification modification

stem: A 45-year-old right-hand-dominant physiotherapist sustains a posterior elbow dislocation, radial head fracture, and coronoid fracture following a fall from a horse. After closed reduction, the elbow re-dislocates at 60° of extension under image intensifier assessment. Surgery is planned. Which sequence of repair most reliably restores stability and reflects current evidence-based practice?

options.label: A
options.text: Medial collateral ligament repair → lateral collateral ligament repair → radial head fixation

options.label: B
options.text: Radial head fixation or replacement → coronoid fixation → lateral ulnar collateral ligament repair, with medial repair only if instability persists

options.label: C
options.text: Coronoid fixation → medial collateral ligament repair → radial head fixation

options.label: D
options.text: Radial head excision → lateral collateral ligament repair → medial collateral ligament repair

options.label: E
options.text: External fixation alone until soft tissues settle, followed by delayed ligament reconstruction

explanation: The terrible triad repair sequence follows a lateral-to-medial logic: address the radial head first (fixation or metal replacement — excision alone is contraindicated), then coronoid if accessible from the lateral window or via a medial approach, then repair the lateral ulnar collateral ligament (LUCL). Medial collateral ligament repair is added only if the elbow remains unstable after lateral-side reconstruction. Option A reverses the sequence without evidence of superiority. Option D (radial head excision) removes the primary bony lateral stabiliser and is contraindicated in terrible triad. Option E delays definitive repair and risks progressive instability and stiffness.
learningPoint: Terrible triad surgery proceeds lateral-first: radial head, coronoid, LUCL; MCL repair is reserved for persistent instability after lateral reconstruction.
references: Pugh et al. JBJS 2004 terrible triad protocol

stem: A 38-year-old recreational tennis player reports repeated episodes of the elbow 'giving way' when pushing up from a chair. She had a posterior dislocation treated non-operatively two years ago. Examination reveals a positive posterolateral rotatory instability (PLRI) test and a normal medial side. Valgus stress testing at 30° of flexion is stable. MRI shows a complete tear of the lateral ulnar collateral ligament with an intact anterior band of the medial collateral ligament. What is the primary anatomical structure responsible for preventing posterolateral rotatory instability of the elbow?

options.label: A
options.text: Anterior band of the medial collateral ligament

options.label: B
options.text: Radial head

options.label: C
options.text: Lateral ulnar collateral ligament

options.label: D
options.text: Common extensor origin

options.label: E
options.text: Annular ligament

explanation: The lateral ulnar collateral ligament (LUCL), running from the lateral epicondyle to the supinator crest of the ulna, is the primary restraint against posterolateral rotatory instability. When it fails, the ulna — carrying the radial head — can rotate away from the trochlea. The anterior band of the MCL (Option A) is the primary valgus stabiliser and is intact here. The radial head (Option B) contributes secondarily once the LUCL is torn. The annular ligament (Option E) stabilises the radial head relative to the ulna but does not prevent PLRI. The common extensor origin (Option D) is a dynamic restraint and not the primary static stabiliser.
learningPoint: The LUCL is the primary restraint to posterolateral rotatory instability; its disruption allows the ulna and radial head to sublux posterolaterally.
references: O'Driscoll SW et al. J Bone Joint Surg 1991 PLRI
Miller's Review of Orthopaedics – elbow ligaments

stem: A 52-year-old woman sustains an elbow dislocation treated by closed reduction. Post-reduction CT reveals an isolated coronoid fracture measuring 6 mm in height involving the tip only, with the anteromedial facet fully intact. The elbow is concentrically reduced and stable through a functional arc following reduction. According to the Regan–Morrey classification and current management principles, what is the most appropriate treatment?

options.label: A
options.text: Immediate operative fixation via a medial approach using a suture lasso technique

options.label: B
options.text: Protected early mobilisation in a hinged brace, avoiding the last 30° of extension for three weeks

options.label: C
options.text: Above-elbow plaster at 90° for six weeks

options.label: D
options.text: Operative fixation via a lateral Kocher approach with a Herbert screw

options.label: E
options.text: Coronoid fragment excision to prevent impingement

explanation: A Regan–Morrey type I fracture (tip fragment ≤2 mm) or small type II tip fragment (≤50% of height) without anteromedial involvement and with a concentrically reduced, stable elbow after dislocation is treated non-operatively. A hinged brace set to block the last 30° of extension protects the repair while allowing early movement, which limits stiffness. Operative fixation (Options A and D) is reserved for type II/III fractures with anteromedial facet involvement, or those associated with persistent instability. Prolonged immobilisation (Option C) causes predictable flexion contracture. Excision (Option E) would worsen varus posteromedial instability.
learningPoint: Isolated small coronoid tip fractures with a stable, concentrically reduced elbow are treated non-operatively with early protected mobilisation.
references: Regan W, Morrey B JBJS 1989 coronoid classification

stem: A 72-year-old woman with osteoporosis slips and sustains a displaced transverse olecranon fracture at the mid-olecranon. The distal fragment has no comminution. Radiographs confirm 8 mm of displacement and an intact anterior cortex. Which fixation construct offers the best biomechanical advantage for this specific fracture pattern and patient demographic?

options.label: A
options.text: Intramedullary screw fixation alone

options.label: B
options.text: Tension band wiring using two parallel K-wires and a figure-of-eight wire loop

options.label: C
options.text: Plate fixation with a pre-contoured locking plate applied to the dorsal surface

options.label: D
options.text: Non-operative management in a long-arm cast at 90°

options.label: E
options.text: Fragment excision and triceps advancement

explanation: In osteoporotic bone, tension-band wiring (Option B) has high rates of hardware migration and symptomatic prominence requiring re-operation; K-wires cut through osteoporotic cancellous bone under cyclic loading. A pre-contoured locking plate provides angular-stable fixation that does not rely on cortical bone quality in the same way, and biomechanical studies show superior resistance to pull-out in low-density bone. Intramedullary screw alone (Option A) lacks rotational control and is better suited to comminuted or oblique patterns. Non-operative management (Option D) is inappropriate for displaced fractures in an active patient. Fragment excision (Option E) is reserved for small, proximal avulsion-type injuries.
learningPoint: Pre-contoured locking plates are preferred over tension-band wiring for transverse olecranon fractures in osteoporotic patients because angular-stable fixation resists hardware migration.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics – olecranon fixation

stem: A 9-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department after a fall from a climbing frame. His left forearm is swollen and he refuses to pronate. AP and lateral radiographs of the forearm show an apex-anterior angulation fracture of the proximal ulna. The radiograph is taken at the forearm, and the radiographer has not included the elbow joint in the image. What is the most critical next step and why?

options.label: A
options.text: Perform forearm CT to characterise the ulnar fracture pattern before any reduction

options.label: B
options.text: Obtain AP and lateral views of the elbow joint to identify a possible radial head dislocation and confirm Bado classification

options.label: C
options.text: Proceed immediately to forearm splintage in full supination as the predominant injury is an isolated ulnar shaft fracture

options.label: D
options.text: Request MRI of the forearm to exclude periosteal injury

options.label: E
options.text: Refer directly for operative fixation of the ulnar fracture without further imaging

explanation: Any child with a proximal ulnar fracture must have full-length elbow radiographs to exclude a Monteggia injury, where the radial head dislocates concurrent with the ulnar fracture. A line drawn through the long axis of the radial shaft in any projection should pass through the centre of the capitellum (the radiocapitellar line); deviation confirms dislocation. Missing this diagnosis leads to missed radial head dislocation with long-term instability and nerve palsy. CT (Option A) adds no value before plain radiographs of the correct joints are obtained. Splintage alone (Option C) would miss a dislocation requiring reduction.
learningPoint: In any child with a proximal ulnar fracture, full elbow radiographs are mandatory to check the radiocapitellar line and exclude a Monteggia lesion.

stem: A 44-year-old office administrator has had aching pain over her right lateral elbow for seven months, worsened by gripping and computer work. She has tenderness 1 cm distal and anterior to the lateral epicondyle. Grip strength is reduced on the affected side. Two courses of corticosteroid injection brought short-term relief lasting six to eight weeks each time. She is otherwise fit and well. What represents the most appropriate next management step?

options.label: A
options.text: A third corticosteroid injection targeting the extensor carpi radialis brevis origin

options.label: B
options.text: Supervised progressive loading physiotherapy programme focusing on eccentric and isometric wrist extensor exercises

options.label: C
options.text: Immediate referral for lateral release surgery

options.label: D
options.text: Platelet-rich plasma injection as first-line escalation

options.label: E
options.text: MRI to confirm the diagnosis before any further treatment

explanation: Lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow) has an underlying tendinopathic pathology; corticosteroid injections suppress pain short-term but do not address the degenerate collagen and have been shown in high-quality RCTs to produce worse long-term outcomes than physiotherapy. After two failed injections, the next step is a structured exercise programme, particularly eccentric and isometric loading of the wrist extensors, which has robust evidence for sustained improvement. A third injection (Option A) repeats a strategy that has already provided only transient relief. Surgery (Option C) is reserved for failure after at least 12 months of non-operative treatment including physiotherapy. PRP (Option D) evidence remains inconclusive and is not current first-line escalation in UK practice.
learningPoint: After failed corticosteroid injections for lateral epicondylitis, progressive loading physiotherapy is the evidence-based next step; further injections worsen long-term outcomes.

stem: A 52-year-old plumber reports medial elbow pain aggravated by resisted wrist flexion and forearm pronation. Examination reveals tenderness at the medial epicondyle and pain on resisted pronation, but ulnar nerve examination and Tinel's sign are normal. Plain radiograph shows a small calcific density adjacent to the medial epicondyle. Which structure is primarily implicated in medial epicondylitis?

options.label: A
options.text: Pronator teres and flexor carpi radialis origin

options.label: B
options.text: Flexor carpi ulnaris origin

options.label: C
options.text: Anterior band of the medial collateral ligament

options.label: D
options.text: Flexor digitorum superficialis origin

options.label: E
options.text: Palmaris longus tendon

explanation: Medial epicondylitis (golfer's elbow) principally involves degenerative change within the pronator teres and flexor carpi radialis origin at their common attachment to the medial epicondyle; this explains why resisted pronation and wrist flexion reproduce pain. The flexor carpi ulnaris (Option B) is also attached medially but is less consistently affected. The MCL (Option C) is an intra-articular structure deep to the epicondyle, not the primary structure in tendinopathy. FDS (Option D) and palmaris longus (Option E) contribute to the common flexor origin but are not the dominant structures implicated in this condition.
learningPoint: Medial epicondylitis arises from degeneration of the pronator teres and flexor carpi radialis at the medial epicondyle; distinguish from cubital tunnel syndrome by normal ulnar nerve examination.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics – medial epicondylitis

stem: A 47-year-old lorry driver presents with a six-month history of paraesthesia in his ring and little finger, medial hand weakness, and difficulty separating his fingers. Examination reveals wasting of the first dorsal interosseous and hypothenar eminence, weak finger abduction, and a positive Froment's sign. Tinel's sign is positive at the medial elbow. Nerve conduction studies confirm focal slowing across the elbow segment. Conservative management for three months has failed. Which surgical procedure is associated with the most favourable outcomes for this degree of compression?

options.label: A
options.text: Simple in situ ulnar nerve decompression without transposition

options.label: B
options.text: Medial epicondylectomy alone

options.label: C
options.text: Anterior subcutaneous transposition of the ulnar nerve

options.label: D
options.text: Anterior submuscular transposition of the ulnar nerve

options.label: E
options.text: Endoscopic cubital tunnel release

explanation: Multiple systematic reviews and RCTs have demonstrated that simple in situ decompression (neurolysis at the cubital tunnel without nerve transposition) produces equivalent or superior outcomes to transposition techniques for most patients with cubital tunnel syndrome, with fewer complications, shorter operative time, and no risk of kinking or devascularisation of the transposed nerve. Transposition techniques (Options C and D) are reserved for cases with an anatomically subluxing nerve, prior failed in situ release, or where flexion exacerbates symptoms by a mechanism of nerve tethering. Medial epicondylectomy (Option B) removes the bony prominence but carries risks of MCL destabilisation. Endoscopic release (Option E) has comparable early results but is technically demanding.
learningPoint: In situ decompression of the ulnar nerve at the cubital tunnel produces outcomes equivalent to transposition with fewer complications, and is the preferred first-line surgical option.

stem: A 14-year-old male gymnast has had six months of lateral elbow pain and intermittent locking. MRI reveals a 1.2 cm osteochondral lesion of the capitellum with a fluid signal deep to the fragment, suggesting instability, and a loose body is identified in the posterior recess. There is no radial head involvement. What is the most appropriate surgical management?

options.label: A
options.text: Arthroscopic loose body removal and drilling of the lesion bed only

options.label: B
options.text: Arthroscopic loose body removal and fixation of the unstable fragment with resorbable pins

options.label: C
options.text: Arthroscopic debridement and microfracture of the entire capitellar lesion without fragment retention

options.label: D
options.text: Open osteochondral autograft transplantation from the knee (mosaicplasty)

options.label: E
options.text: Non-operative management with activity restriction for a further six months

explanation: A 1.2 cm unstable capitellar OCD with a loose body in a skeletally maturing athlete represents a high-grade lesion (ICRS III–IV) that is too large for reliable healing with drilling or microfracture alone, and the detached fragment quality is likely too poor for viable refixation. Open mosaicplasty using osteochondral autograft from the non-weight-bearing lateral femoral condyle restores articular congruity with hyaline cartilage and has the strongest evidence for large unstable lesions. Simple drilling (Option A) is appropriate for stable, intact lesions. Fragment refixation (Option B) can be considered when the detached fragment has adequate bony architecture; the MRI description here suggests otherwise. Microfracture alone (Option C) produces fibrocartilage and is insufficient for a 1.2 cm defect in a high-demand athlete.
learningPoint: Large (>1 cm) unstable capitellar OCD lesions in young athletes are best treated with osteochondral autograft mosaicplasty to restore durable hyaline cartilage coverage.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics – capitellar OCD

stem: A 43-year-old man felt a sudden 'pop' in his antecubital fossa whilst lifting a heavy box. He now reports weakness of elbow flexion and forearm supination. Examination reveals a palpable gap distal to the antecubital crease, a positive hook test, and a positive biceps squeeze test. MRI confirms complete distal biceps tendon avulsion from the radial tuberosity with 4 cm proximal retraction. He is keen to return to manual labour. Which surgical approach carries the lowest risk of posterior interosseous nerve injury during distal biceps reattachment?

options.label: A
options.text: Two-incision technique using a dorsal muscle-splitting approach to the tuberosity

options.label: B
options.text: Single anterior approach using a transverse antecubital incision with the forearm in full supination

options.label: C
options.text: Single anterior approach with the forearm in full pronation to move the posterior interosseous nerve laterally away from the tuberosity

options.label: D
options.text: Posterior approach using the Thompson interval between ECRB and EDC

options.label: E
options.text: Medial approach through the flexor-pronator mass to access the tuberosity

explanation: During single anterior approach repair of the distal biceps, positioning the forearm in full supination rotates the radial tuberosity anteriorly and away from the posterior interosseous nerve (PIN), providing the best window to the tuberosity while keeping the PIN furthest from the surgical field. Pronation (Option C) rotates the tuberosity posteriorly, bringing it directly towards the PIN and increasing nerve risk. The Boyd two-incision technique (Option A) was designed to reduce anterior neurovascular risk by accessing the tuberosity posteriorly through extensor muscles, not for PIN protection; it carries its own risk of radioulnar synostosis if bone is stripped incorrectly. Option D describes the Thompson approach to the radius, not specifically a distal biceps approach.
learningPoint: During single anterior approach distal biceps repair, the forearm is held in full supination to rotate the radial tuberosity anteriorly, maximising distance from the posterior interosseous nerve.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics – distal biceps repair

stem: A 38-year-old powerlifter who takes anabolic steroids presents after feeling his elbow 'give way' during a heavy press. He cannot actively extend his elbow against gravity. Radiographs reveal a small flake of bone at the olecranon tip. The Thompson squeeze test of the triceps is positive. Which co-morbidity is most strongly associated with spontaneous or low-energy triceps tendon rupture?

options.label: A
options.text: Diabetes mellitus

options.label: B
options.text: Anabolic steroid use

options.label: C
options.text: Chronic renal failure treated with haemodialysis

options.label: D
options.text: Fluoroquinolone antibiotic use

options.label: E
options.text: Hyperparathyroidism

explanation: Chronic renal failure requiring haemodialysis is the systemic condition most strongly and specifically linked to spontaneous triceps tendon rupture, likely through uraemic tendinopathy, secondary hyperparathyroidism causing bone resorption at the enthesis, and aluminium-related osteodystrophy affecting the olecranon apophysis. This association is well documented in the literature and explains bilateral and recurrent ruptures. Anabolic steroids (Option B) are associated with tendon ruptures broadly, including triceps, but the strongest SPECIFIC association in published case series is renal failure. Fluoroquinolones (Option D) are classically linked to Achilles tendon rupture but triceps rupture is far less commonly reported.
learningPoint: Chronic renal failure on haemodialysis is the systemic condition most specifically associated with spontaneous triceps tendon rupture, due to uraemic tendinopathy and secondary bone changes at the olecranon enthesis.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics – triceps rupture

stem: A 36-year-old man sustained a posterior elbow dislocation with associated distal humerus fracture managed operatively 14 months ago. He now has an arc of elbow motion from 40° to 100° of flexion and cannot fully pronate or supinate. CT demonstrates a posterior capsular block and a mature heterotopic ossification bridge spanning the posterolateral gutter. He is motivated to improve his functional range. At what minimum post-injury interval should surgical excision of heterotopic ossification generally be undertaken, and what adjunct reduces recurrence?

options.label: A
options.text: Six months; no adjunct is required if complete resection is achieved

options.label: B
options.text: Twelve months; indomethacin 25 mg three times daily for six weeks post-operatively

options.label: C
options.text: Twelve to eighteen months with radiographic maturity; single-fraction low-dose radiotherapy or an NSAID course post-operatively

options.label: D
options.text: Six months; single-fraction radiotherapy pre-operatively

options.label: E
options.text: Twenty-four months; bisphosphonate therapy for one year post-operatively

explanation: Surgery for post-traumatic HO around the elbow should be delayed until the bone is radiographically mature — typically 12 to 18 months post-injury — to minimise recurrence risk; operating on immature HO is associated with high recurrence. Prophylaxis against recurrence uses either low-dose radiotherapy (single fraction of 7 Gy within 72 hours peri-operatively) or a six-week NSAID course (e.g. indomethacin); both have evidence for efficacy. Bisphosphonates (Option E) lack high-quality evidence for HO prevention in this setting. Operating at six months (Options A and D) risks excising immature bone into already-inflamed tissue, increasing the chance of recurrence.
learningPoint: HO excision around the elbow is deferred until radiographic maturity (12-18 months); prophylaxis with low-dose radiotherapy or NSAIDs post-operatively reduces recurrence.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics – stiff elbow

stem: A 68-year-old woman with longstanding seropositive rheumatoid arthritis has been referred by her rheumatologist. Her disease is well controlled on a biologic agent. She reports severe pain at rest and during daily activities, a flexion contracture of 30°, and a maximum arc of 40° of flexion. Radiographs show Larsen grade IV destruction of the radiocapitellar and ulnohumeral joints with gross deformity. Inflammatory markers are currently normal. Which is the most appropriate surgical intervention?

options.label: A
options.text: Elbow arthroscopy and debridement

options.label: B
options.text: Radial head arthroplasty alone

options.label: C
options.text: Linked (constrained) semi-constrained total elbow arthroplasty

options.label: D
options.text: Interposition arthroplasty using a fascial graft

options.label: E
options.text: Elbow fusion in functional position

explanation: Total elbow arthroplasty (TEA) is the procedure of choice for Larsen grade IV–V rheumatoid destruction with severe pain and functional loss in a patient of appropriate age and activity level. The linked (semi-constrained, 'sloppy hinge') design is preferred in rheumatoid arthritis because the soft tissues and bone stock are typically inadequate to provide the stability required for an unlinked prosthesis. Arthroscopy (Option A) is palliative at best for Larsen grade IV disease. Interposition arthroplasty (Option D) is generally considered for younger patients where TEA survivorship is a concern, but results in RA are inferior to TEA. Elbow fusion (Option E) is rarely indicated for RA given bilateral upper limb involvement and functional consequences.
learningPoint: Linked semi-constrained TEA is the standard surgical treatment for advanced rheumatoid destruction of the elbow, given the typically deficient soft-tissue envelope and bony architecture in this condition.

stem: A 55-year-old builder presents with a fluctuant, warm, erythematous swelling over the olecranon following a minor graze to that area one week ago. He is systemically well, afebrile, and his elbow joint has a full, pain-free arc of motion. WBC is 11.2 × 10⁹/L and CRP is 38 mg/L. Ultrasound confirms the fluid is entirely within the olecranon bursa with no joint effusion. Bursal aspiration yields 5 mL of turbid fluid; Gram stain shows Gram-positive cocci in clusters. What is the single most appropriate immediate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Oral flucloxacillin 500 mg four times daily, repeat aspiration if recollects, outpatient follow-up in one week

options.label: B
options.text: Urgent surgical washout of the elbow joint

options.label: C
options.text: Intravenous co-amoxiclav and inpatient admission

options.label: D
options.text: Aspiration and injection with corticosteroid into the bursa

options.label: E
options.text: Immediate bursectomy under general anaesthesia

explanation: Septic olecranon bursitis without joint involvement, no systemic sepsis, and with the Gram stain suggesting Staphylococcus aureus is appropriately managed with oral flucloxacillin (first-line anti-staphylococcal agent in the UK per BNF) alongside needle aspiration to dryness, with close outpatient review. Olecranon bursitis does not communicate with the joint in most patients; the clinical and ultrasonographic confirmation of a normal joint means urgent joint washout (Option B) is unnecessary. IV antibiotics (Option C) are escalated if the patient deteriorates or oral therapy fails. Corticosteroid into a confirmed septic bursa (Option D) is contraindicated. Bursectomy (Option E) is reserved for chronically recurrent or refractory septic bursitis.
learningPoint: Septic olecranon bursitis without systemic sepsis or joint involvement is treated with oral flucloxacillin and aspiration; corticosteroid injection into a confirmed infected bursa is contraindicated.

stem: In a biomechanical study assessing the primary stabilisers of the elbow against valgus stress at 30° of flexion, sequential sectioning of each structure is performed. Sectioning of which structure produces the greatest increase in valgus laxity and is therefore considered the primary valgus stabiliser of the elbow?

options.label: A
options.text: Radial head

options.label: B
options.text: Posterior band of the medial collateral ligament

options.label: C
options.text: Anterior band of the medial collateral ligament

options.label: D
options.text: Common flexor tendon origin

options.label: E
options.text: Lateral ulnar collateral ligament

explanation: The anterior band of the medial collateral ligament (aMCL) runs from the anterior medial epicondyle to the sublime tubercle of the ulna and is taut throughout the functional arc of elbow motion; sequential sectioning studies consistently identify it as the structure whose removal produces the largest increment in valgus laxity. The radial head (Option A) is a secondary valgus stabiliser that becomes critical when the aMCL is deficient. The posterior band (Option B) is taut in extension and contributes less in mid-arc positions. The LUCL (Option E) resists posterolateral rotation, not primarily valgus. The common flexor origin (Option D) provides dynamic but not primary static valgus support.
learningPoint: The anterior band of the MCL, attaching to the sublime tubercle, is the primary static stabiliser of the elbow against valgus stress throughout the functional arc of motion.
references: Morrey BF, An KN JBJS 1983 valgus stability
Miller's Review of Orthopaedics – elbow biomechanics

subtopic: radial head fracture classification and management
stem: Theme: Management of radial head and neck fractures. For each clinical scenario select the single most appropriate management option from the list below. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Aspiration of haemarthrosis and early active mobilisation with a sling

options.label: B
options.text: Open reduction and internal fixation with a headless compression screw

options.label: C
options.text: Radial head arthroplasty with a metallic prosthesis

options.label: D
options.text: Radial head excision alone

options.label: E
options.text: Above-elbow plaster for four weeks

options.label: F
options.text: Open reduction and internal fixation with a mini-fragment plate and screws

options.label: G
options.text: Closed reduction under image intensifier and collar-and-cuff

options.label: H
options.text: Aspiration, ORIF of radial head, coronoid repair, and LUCL reconstruction

emiLeads.stem: A 28-year-old falls and sustains a Mason I radial head fracture with 1 mm of step, no mechanical block to rotation, and no associated injuries.

A 55-year-old sustains a comminuted radial head fracture in three fragments with 50% articular involvement and a concomitant terrible triad pattern (coronoid avulsion, elbow dislocation). Stable fixation of the radial head is not achievable due to the degree of comminution.
A 40-year-old has a single-fragment Mason II radial head fracture with 3 mm of step involving 40% of the articular surface and a confirmed mechanical block to forearm rotation at 45° of pronation.

explanation: Lead 1: Non-displaced Mason I fractures without mechanical block are treated non-operatively; aspiration of the haemarthrosis relieves pain and facilitates early movement. Lead 2: Comminuted fractures not amenable to fixation within a terrible triad pattern require metallic radial head arthroplasty — excision alone (Option D) is contraindicated because the radial head is needed as a secondary valgus stabiliser. Lead 3: A single-fragment Mason II fracture with a mechanical block is the ideal indication for headless compression screw fixation, which provides interfragmentary compression without prominent hardware in the soft zone.

stem: Theme: Nerve injuries associated with elbow pathology and surgery. For each scenario select the single most likely nerve or nerve branch at risk from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Anterior interosseous nerve

options.label: B
options.text: Posterior interosseous nerve (deep branch of radial nerve)

options.label: C
options.text: Ulnar nerve

options.label: D
options.text: Medial antebrachial cutaneous nerve

options.label: E
options.text: Lateral antebrachial cutaneous nerve (terminal branch of musculocutaneous nerve)

options.label: F
options.text: Radial nerve (main trunk)

options.label: G
options.text: Median nerve

emiLeads.stem: A 35-year-old undergoes single anterior incision distal biceps reattachment to the radial tuberosity. Post-operatively he has weakness of wrist and finger extension but full sensation over the dorsum of the hand.
A patient develops tingling over the medial forearm and inner upper arm following a medial approach for coronoid fracture fixation; there is no intrinsic weakness.
A 12-year-old sustains a severely displaced supracondylar humerus fracture. Post-reduction he has weakness of thumb IP flexion and index finger DIP flexion but sensation and intrinsics are intact.

A surgeon uses a lateral (Kocher) approach between ECU and anconeus for radial head ORIF. Post-operatively the patient has finger drop and wrist drop with preserved sensation over the first web space.

explanation: Lead 1: The PIN runs through the radial tunnel close to the radial neck; during anterior distal biceps repair, especially with the forearm pronated or in a deep exposure, it is at risk. Motor loss with preserved sensation distinguishes PIN palsy from main radial nerve injury. Lead 2: The medial antebrachial cutaneous nerve crosses the medial elbow subcutaneously and is vulnerable in any medial elbow incision; injury causes sensory symptoms only. Lead 3: AIN palsy following supracondylar fracture presents with failure of the flexor pollicis longus and FDP to the index finger (no pinch) but no sensory deficit, distinguishing it from median nerve palsy. Lead 4: A Kocher approach that violates the distal extent can place the PIN at risk as it enters the arcade of Frohse; finger and wrist drop with an intact superficial radial nerve sensation confirms a PIN lesion.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics – elbow surgical approaches

stem: Theme: Classification and diagnosis of complex elbow fracture-dislocation patterns. For each clinical vignette select the single most accurate diagnosis or injury classification from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Bado type I Monteggia fracture-dislocation

options.label: B
options.text: Bado type II Monteggia fracture-dislocation

options.label: C
options.text: Essex-Lopresti injury

options.label: D
options.text: Terrible triad of the elbow

options.label: E
options.text: Varus posteromedial rotatory instability with anteromedial coronoid fracture

options.label: F
options.text: Posterior olecranon fracture-dislocation (trans-olecranon)

options.label: G
options.text: Bado type IV Monteggia fracture-dislocation

emiLeads.stem: A 48-year-old sustains a posterior elbow dislocation with an associated Mason III radial head fracture and a small coronoid tip avulsion. CT confirms the coronoid fragment is less than 2 mm and involves only the tip.

A 30-year-old falls from height onto an outstretched arm. Forearm radiographs show an apex-posterior angulated proximal ulnar fracture. The elbow radiograph reveals an anterior radial head dislocation. The PIN is intact.

A 62-year-old sustains a posterior elbow injury. CT shows an intact olecranon with a large anteromedial coronoid facet fracture and demonstrates varus malalignment of the ulnohumeral joint. The radial head is fractured on the lateral column. There is no associated elbow dislocation.

explanation: Lead 1: Terrible triad = posterior elbow dislocation + radial head fracture + coronoid fracture (any size). The tip-only coronoid fracture with Mason III radial head confirms this pattern. Lead 2: Bado type I Monteggia = apex-anterior ulnar fracture with anterior radial head dislocation; the PIN is commonly injured in this pattern but need not be. The apex here is posterior, meaning the ulna bows apex-posteriorly but the dislocation is anterior — this matches Bado I where the ulnar apex and radial head point the same direction (both anterior). Lead 3: Varus posteromedial rotatory instability characteristically involves an anteromedial coronoid facet fracture (O'Driscoll classification), lateral collateral ligament disruption, and possible radial head fracture — without complete dislocation. The ulnohumeral joint subluxes into varus, producing the described CT findings.

stem: A 38-year-old woman rolls her ankle whilst descending stairs. Radiographs reveal an oblique fibular fracture at the level of the ankle mortise with no medial-side injury and an intact mortise on weight-bearing views. She is neurovascularly intact. Which management is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Open reduction and internal fixation of the fibula

options.label: B
options.text: Functional bracing and progressive weight-bearing with clinical and radiographic follow-up

options.label: C
options.text: Below-knee plaster cast non-weight-bearing for 6 weeks

options.label: D
options.text: MRI ankle to assess syndesmosis before deciding management

options.label: E
options.text: Immediate arthroscopy to exclude osteochondral injury

explanation: This is a Weber B (Lauge-Hansen supination-external rotation stage II) isolated lateral malleolus fracture with a stable mortise on weight-bearing stress radiographs. Evidence supports functional bracing with early weight-bearing for isolated, stable Weber B fractures; outcomes are equivalent to operative fixation with fewer complications. Casting non-weight-bearing (C) is unnecessarily restrictive without instability. ORIF (A) is reserved for unstable or displaced fractures. Routine MRI (D) and arthroscopy (E) are not first-line steps when clinical and radiographic stability is confirmed.
learningPoint: Isolated stable Weber B fractures with an intact mortise on weight-bearing views are managed non-operatively with functional bracing.
references: NICE Clinical Knowledge Summary: Ankle fractures

stem: A 27-year-old male rugby player sustains a high-energy ankle injury. Radiographs show a Weber C fibula fracture 8 cm above the plafond, widening of the medial clear space to 6 mm, and a tibiofibular clear space of 8 mm on the mortise view. After ORIF of the fibula, intraoperative hook testing confirms frank syndesmotic instability. Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Close the wound and apply a below-knee plaster; re-image at 6 weeks

options.label: B
options.text: Insert a single 3.5 mm solid cortical screw through 4 cortices across the syndesmosis

options.label: C
options.text: Place a suture-button device across the syndesmosis

options.label: D
options.text: Perform primary ankle arthrodesis

options.label: E
options.text: Apply an external fixator across the ankle and plan delayed internal fixation

explanation: Current evidence and most UK practice supports suture-button (TightRope-type) fixation for syndesmotic stabilisation, as it allows physiological micromotion and has equivalent or superior functional outcomes versus solid screws without the need for routine removal. A solid 4-cortex screw (B) remains acceptable but carries a screw removal debate and risk of breakage. Closing without fixation (A) would leave instability untreated. Primary arthrodesis (D) is not indicated acutely in a young patient with repairable soft tissues. External fixator alone (E) does not address the syndesmosis definitively.
learningPoint: Confirmed syndesmotic instability after fibular fixation should be stabilised; suture-button devices provide dynamic fixation and do not require planned removal.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics (concept: syndesmotic fixation)

stem: A 52-year-old construction worker falls 4 metres onto his feet. His right ankle is severely swollen with fracture blisters; CT shows a comminuted OTA/AO 43-C3 pilon fracture. He has no neurovascular compromise. What is the preferred staged management sequence?

options.label: A
options.text: Immediate ORIF through an anteromedial approach

options.label: B
options.text: Spanning external fixator to restore length and alignment, followed by definitive ORIF once soft tissue envelope has recovered

options.label: C
options.text: Intramedullary nail of the tibia combined with lateral plate for fibula as a single-stage procedure

options.label: D
options.text: Non-operative management in a plaster cast with serial radiographs

options.label: E
options.text: Primary ankle arthrodesis as a single-stage procedure on the day of injury

explanation: High-energy comminuted pilon fractures require a staged approach: temporary spanning external fixation to restore fibular length and ankle alignment, allowing the traumatised soft tissue envelope to recover over 10–21 days before definitive plate osteosynthesis. Immediate ORIF (A) through swollen tissues carries a prohibitive wound complication rate. IM nail is not appropriate for articular pilon fractures (C). Non-operative management (D) is unsuitable for this degree of articular comminution. Primary arthrodesis (E) may occasionally be considered for heavily contaminated open injuries but not as a default for this scenario.
learningPoint: Staged management — spanning external fixation first, then delayed ORIF — is the standard for high-energy pilon fractures to protect the soft tissue envelope.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics (concept: pilon fractures)

stem: A 45-year-old man falls 2 metres off a ladder, landing on his heels. CT of the right calcaneum shows an intra-articular fracture with a Böhler's angle of 8° and a Sanders type III injury involving the posterior facet. He smokes 20 cigarettes per day and is otherwise well. What is the most important factor guiding operative versus non-operative decision-making in this patient?

options.label: A
options.text: Böhler's angle alone determines operative indication

options.label: B
options.text: Sanders classification drives the decision independently of the patient

options.label: C
options.text: Smoking status, soft tissue condition and patient factors must all be weighed; heavy smoking substantially increases wound complication risk with ORIF

options.label: D
options.text: All Sanders III fractures must be treated operatively regardless of host factors

options.label: E
options.text: Age over 40 is a contraindication to operative fixation

explanation: Intra-articular calcaneal fracture management requires balanced consideration of fracture morphology (Sanders classification, Böhler's angle) AND patient factors. Heavy smoking is the single most important modifiable risk factor for wound dehiscence and deep infection after extensile lateral approach ORIF. The UK RECUPERO trial and subsequent analyses confirm that wound complications are substantially more common in smokers. Sanders III fractures with poor soft tissue or significant smoking may be better managed non-operatively or with percutaneous/minimally invasive techniques. Neither Böhler's angle alone (A), Sanders class alone (B), nor age (E) independently dictates operative management.
learningPoint: Heavy smoking is the dominant patient-side risk factor for wound complications after calcaneal ORIF; patient factors must accompany fracture morphology in the decision.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics (concept: calcaneal fractures)

stem: A 29-year-old cyclist is brought in after a road traffic collision. He complains of midfoot pain and swelling. Weight-bearing AP radiographs show 2.5 mm diastasis between the medial cuneiform and the base of the second metatarsal, with no other bony injury visible. What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Below-knee plaster cast, non-weight-bearing for 6 weeks, then rehabilitation

options.label: B
options.text: MRI to confirm ligamentous injury, then operative stabilisation if instability is confirmed

options.label: C
options.text: Operative stabilisation of the Lisfranc joint complex

options.label: D
options.text: Functional bracing and weight-bearing as tolerated

options.label: E
options.text: Primary Lisfranc arthrodesis of all five rays

explanation: A diastasis of ≥2 mm at the Lisfranc interval on weight-bearing radiographs is the threshold for operative intervention because ligamentous Lisfranc injuries missed or under-treated lead to painful midfoot instability and post-traumatic arthritis. MRI (B) is useful when radiographs are equivocal (no diastasis on weight-bearing), not when instability is already radiographically evident. Non-operative management (A, D) is reserved for truly undisplaced, stable injuries. Primary arthrodesis of all five rays (E) is excessive; selective arthrodesis of the medial rays (1–3) versus ORIF of the lateral rays is debated, but this initial step should be stabilisation, not universal fusion.
learningPoint: A Lisfranc diastasis of ≥2 mm on weight-bearing radiographs is an operative indication; non-operative treatment risks chronic midfoot instability.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics (concept: Lisfranc injury)

stem: A 34-year-old man sustains a Hawkins type II talar neck fracture (subtalar subluxation, intact tibiotalar joint) in a motorcycle crash. ORIF is performed within 6 hours. At his 8-week postoperative radiograph, a subchondral lucency is visible beneath the talar dome on the AP ankle view. What does this sign indicate?

options.label: A
options.text: Hardware failure and need for revision fixation

options.label: B
options.text: Developing avascular necrosis with bone resorption

options.label: C
options.text: Revascularisation of the talar body, predicting a favourable outcome

options.label: D
options.text: Stress fracture of the distal tibial plafond

options.label: E
options.text: Septic arthritis of the ankle joint

explanation: The Hawkins sign is a subchondral lucency (osteoporosis) beneath the talar dome seen at 6–8 weeks after talar neck fracture. It signifies viable bone that is undergoing normal stress-related remodelling because blood flow has been re-established; its presence effectively excludes avascular necrosis (AVN) at that stage. Absence of the sign at 8 weeks raises concern for AVN (B) but does not confirm it. Hardware failure (A), tibial stress fracture (D), and septic arthritis (E) have distinct clinical and radiographic features.
learningPoint: The Hawkins sign (subchondral talar dome lucency at 6–8 weeks) indicates revascularisation and predicts against avascular necrosis following talar neck fracture.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics (concept: talar neck fractures, Hawkins sign)

stem: A 55-year-old woman presents with a painful hallux valgus deformity. Radiographs reveal a hallux valgus angle of 36°, an intermetatarsal angle of 17°, and a distal metatarsal articular angle of 12°. The first metatarsophalangeal joint shows no arthritic change. Conservative measures have failed over 18 months. Which surgical procedure best addresses this deformity?

options.label: A
options.text: Silver procedure (medial eminence excision only)

options.label: B
options.text: Mitchell osteotomy

options.label: C
options.text: Scarf osteotomy with Akin phalangeal osteotomy

options.label: D
options.text: Lapidus arthrodesis of the first tarsometatarsal joint

options.label: E
options.text: First MTPJ arthrodesis

explanation: This patient has a severe hallux valgus (HVA >35°) with a large intermetatarsal angle (IMA >16°), indicating significant proximal deformity at the first TMT joint. The Lapidus procedure (first TMT arthrodesis) corrects deformity at its origin and is best suited to high IMA deformities, especially with associated hypermobility of the first ray. A Scarf osteotomy (C) is powerful but typically suited to IMA up to ~16°; at 17° with high HVA it may not achieve sufficient correction alone. The Silver procedure (A) treats only the bony prominence without correcting alignment. Mitchell (B) is an older distal osteotomy, inadequate here. MTPJ arthrodesis (E) is reserved for arthritic or severe deformity with failed prior surgery.
learningPoint: High intermetatarsal angles (>16°) with severe hallux valgus are best addressed by a proximal corrective procedure such as the Lapidus arthrodesis.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics (concept: hallux valgus surgery)

stem: A 62-year-old man has a 3-year history of progressive pain at his left first metatarsophalangeal joint. He reports 20° of dorsiflexion (reduced from the contralateral 65°) and bony prominences on the dorsum. Radiographs show periarticular osteophytes, joint-space narrowing of approximately 50%, and subchondral sclerosis. Conservative treatment has failed. What is the most appropriate surgical option at this stage?

options.label: A
options.text: Keller's arthroplasty

options.label: B
options.text: First MTPJ arthrodesis

options.label: C
options.text: Cheilectomy (dorsal osteophyte excision with dorsal joint debridement)

options.label: D
options.text: Silastic total joint replacement

options.label: E
options.text: Percutaneous cortisone injection and orthotics only

explanation: Grade II hallux rigidus (moderate joint-space preservation with dorsal osteophytes) is well managed by cheilectomy, which removes the dorsal 20–30% of the metatarsal head together with osteophytes, reliably improving dorsiflexion and pain. Arthrodesis (B) is the gold standard for grade III/IV (severe joint-space loss) or failed cheilectomy. Keller's arthroplasty (A) is largely historical, weakens toe push-off, and is rarely performed now. Silastic implants (D) carry long-term failure and particulate synovitis concerns. Injection alone (E) has already failed in this timeline.
learningPoint: Cheilectomy is the first-line surgical option for hallux rigidus with preserved joint space (grades I–II); arthrodesis is reserved for advanced disease.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics (concept: hallux rigidus)
BOA GIRFT Foot and Ankle report

stem: A 48-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes and a BMI of 31 kg/m² has progressive medial ankle pain, hindfoot valgus and a flattened medial arch. She can single-leg heel-rise on the unaffected side but not on the affected side. MRI shows complete discontinuity of the tibialis posterior tendon at the navicular insertion with no subtalar or talonavicular arthritis. Which surgical strategy is most appropriate if conservative management fails?

options.label: A
options.text: Isolated tibialis posterior tendon repair

options.label: B
options.text: Tibialis posterior tendon reconstruction with flexor digitorum longus transfer, calcaneal osteotomy (medialising), and spring ligament repair

options.label: C
options.text: Triple arthrodesis as the primary procedure

options.label: D
options.text: Total ankle replacement

options.label: E
options.text: Subtalar arthrodesis alone

explanation: Stage II tibialis posterior dysfunction (mobile hindfoot, no fixed deformity, no arthritic change) with complete tendon rupture is addressed by a combination of FDL transfer to replace tendon function, medialising calcaneal osteotomy to correct hindfoot valgus and offload the medial column, and spring ligament reconstruction to restore the plantar calcaneonavicular support. Tendon repair alone (A) is inadequate with complete discontinuity. Triple arthrodesis (C) is reserved for stage III/IV (rigid deformity or arthritis). TAR (D) and subtalar fusion (E) do not address the tendon or the deformity in a mobile flatfoot.
learningPoint: Stage II adult acquired flatfoot with complete TPT rupture requires joint-sparing reconstruction: FDL transfer, medialising calcaneal osteotomy, and spring ligament repair.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics (concept: adult acquired flatfoot)

stem: A 42-year-old recreational tennis player hears a 'pop' mid-game and cannot plantarflex. Examination shows a palpable gap 4 cm proximal to the calcaneal insertion and a positive Simmonds-Thompson test. He is healthy with no relevant comorbidities. After counselling, he opts for non-operative management. What is the recommended rehabilitation protocol according to current UK evidence?

options.label: A
options.text: Plaster cast in equinus for 8 weeks, strictly non-weight-bearing throughout

options.label: B
options.text: Functional rehabilitation in a hinged boot with early protected weight-bearing and graduated range-of-motion exercises

options.label: C
options.text: Immediate weight-bearing in a neutral below-knee plaster cast

options.label: D
options.text: Bed rest with the ankle unsupported for 2 weeks, then physiotherapy

options.label: E
options.text: Non-operative management is contraindicated; operative repair is mandatory in active patients

explanation: The UK UKSTAR trial and subsequent meta-analyses confirm that early functional rehabilitation with a removable hinged boot and graduated weight-bearing yields re-rupture rates and functional outcomes equivalent to operative repair in patients managed in a structured protocol. Static equinus casting non-weight-bearing (A) is the older approach and associated with prolonged recovery. Immediate neutral casting (C) does not appose the tendon ends adequately. Unsupported bed rest (D) risks re-rupture. Non-operative management is a valid and widely practised option in motivated patients (refuting E).
learningPoint: Early functional rehabilitation in a hinged boot with protected weight-bearing is the evidence-based approach for Achilles tendon rupture managed non-operatively in the UK.
references: NICE Evidence review: Achilles tendon rupture

stem: A 50-year-old male distance runner presents with a 4-month history of mid-portion Achilles pain that is worst at the start of activity and eases with warm-up. Ultrasound shows fusiform tendon thickening with intratendinous hypoechoic areas. There is no full-thickness tear. First-line management should be:

options.label: A
options.text: Corticosteroid injection into the tendon substance

options.label: B
options.text: Eccentric loading programme (Alfredson protocol) for a minimum of 12 weeks

options.label: C
options.text: Open tendon debridement and repair

options.label: D
options.text: MRI and immediate operative referral

options.label: E
options.text: Extracorporeal shockwave therapy as the initial first-line intervention

explanation: Mid-portion Achilles tendinopathy responds best to structured tendon loading; the Alfredson eccentric loading protocol over at least 12 weeks is the most evidence-based first-line intervention, with good-to-excellent results in the majority of patients. Intratendinous corticosteroid injection (A) is contraindicated due to risk of tendon rupture. Operative debridement (C) and MRI-directed referral (D) are only appropriate after failure of adequate non-operative management. ESWT (E) is a second-line option after failure of the loading programme.
learningPoint: Eccentric loading (Alfredson protocol) for 12 weeks is the established first-line treatment for mid-portion Achilles tendinopathy; intratendinous steroid injection risks rupture.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics (concept: Achilles tendinopathy)

stem: A 47-year-old woman presents with a 6-week history of pain on the plantar aspect of her heel, worst with the first steps after rest. She is a teacher who stands for long periods and has a BMI of 28 kg/m². Examination reveals tenderness at the medial calcaneal tubercle. What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.label: A
options.text: Radiograph to confirm a calcaneal spur before any treatment

options.label: B
options.text: Analgesics, stretching exercises (plantar fascia and Achilles), supportive footwear advice, and insoles

options.label: C
options.text: MRI foot to exclude plantar fascia tear

options.label: D
options.text: Corticosteroid injection into the plantar fascia origin at first presentation

options.label: E
options.text: Immediate referral for surgical plantar fascia release
