explanation: Plantar fasciitis is a clinical diagnosis; initial management centres on stretching exercises (specifically plantar fascia-specific stretching and Achilles stretching), activity modification, appropriate supportive footwear, and simple analgesia. These measures resolve symptoms in the majority within 6–12 months. Plain radiographs (A) are not required routinely as a calcaneal spur is an incidental finding and does not alter management. MRI (C) is not needed at presentation. Steroid injection (D) is a second-line intervention after initial conservative treatment fails. Surgical release (E) is a last resort.
learningPoint: Plantar fasciitis is managed initially with stretching, supportive footwear, and analgesia; no imaging is required for a straightforward clinical diagnosis.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics (concept: plantar fasciitis)

stem: A 58-year-old man with a 20-year history of type 1 diabetes mellitus presents with a warm, erythematous, swollen right foot. He is afebrile and systemically well. Plain radiographs show early periarticular osteopenia and subtle subluxation of the midtarsal joints. There is no break in the skin. Plain radiographs and MRI are both reported as equivocal for early Charcot neuroarthropathy versus osteomyelitis. What is the single most important immediate management step?

options.label: A
options.text: Intravenous antibiotics covering gram-positive organisms

options.label: B
options.text: Urgent surgical debridement of the foot

options.label: C
options.text: Total contact casting and complete offloading of the foot

options.label: D
options.text: Bisphosphonate infusion as definitive treatment

options.label: E
options.text: Weight-bearing as tolerated with compression bandaging

explanation: In the acute (Eichenholtz stage 0/1) Charcot foot, immediate and absolute offloading via total contact casting (TCC) or equivalent irremovable device is the cornerstone of management to prevent progressive joint destruction. Continued weight-bearing causes irreversible bony collapse. With no skin breach, IV antibiotics (A) are not indicated; if osteomyelitis cannot be excluded, further investigation (labelled white-cell scan or bone biopsy) is needed rather than empirical antibiotics. Surgical debridement (B) is not the first step in the absence of infection. Bisphosphonates (D) may be used adjunctively but are not the primary intervention. Weight-bearing (E) risks catastrophic progression.
learningPoint: Acute Charcot neuroarthropathy requires immediate and complete offloading in a total contact cast; further weight-bearing causes irreversible midfoot collapse.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics (concept: Charcot neuroarthropathy)

stem: A 44-year-old woman reports a 6-month history of burning pain and tingling radiating into the third and fourth toes of her right foot, worse in narrow shoes and relieved by removing her footwear and rubbing the ball of her foot. Mulder's click is positive on examination. Which interspace is most commonly affected by this condition?

options.label: A
options.text: First intermetatarsal space

options.label: B
options.text: Second intermetatarsal space

options.label: C
options.text: Third intermetatarsal space

options.label: D
options.text: Fourth intermetatarsal space

options.label: E
options.text: Equal distribution across all interspaces

explanation: Morton's neuroma (interdigital perineural fibrosis) most commonly occurs in the third intermetatarsal space (between the third and fourth metatarsal heads), where the combined branch of the medial and lateral plantar nerves passes through a relatively confined space. The second interspace is the next most common location. First and fourth interspaces are rarely affected. The history and a positive Mulder's click are characteristic; ultrasound or MRI can confirm the diagnosis and guide injection.
learningPoint: Morton's neuroma most commonly affects the third intermetatarsal space; Mulder's click is a useful clinical sign.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics (concept: Morton's neuroma)

stem: A 68-year-old retired headmistress has end-stage post-traumatic ankle osteoarthritis following a Weber C fracture treated 20 years ago. She remains active, walks 3 miles daily, and has an ipsilateral hindfoot that is well-aligned with good subtalar movement. She has no inflammatory arthropathy and no significant coronal deformity. She asks about total ankle replacement versus arthrodesis. Which statement most accurately reflects current UK evidence?

options.label: A
options.text: Arthrodesis should always be preferred as it has a longer track record and lower revision rate

options.label: B
options.text: Total ankle replacement preserves adjacent joint motion, may reduce progression of subtalar and midtarsal arthritis, and is the preferred option in well-selected patients with good bone stock and alignment

options.label: C
options.text: Total ankle replacement is contraindicated after any previous fracture

options.label: D
options.text: Arthrodesis is preferred in all patients over 65 years

options.label: E
options.text: Neither procedure is appropriate; hyaluronic acid injection is the treatment of choice

explanation: For well-selected patients with end-stage ankle OA — good bone stock, well-aligned hindfoot, preserved subtalar/midtarsal mobility, and no severe deformity — modern third-generation total ankle replacement offers equivalent pain relief to arthrodesis, while preserving sagittal motion and potentially protecting adjacent joints from accelerated arthritic change. NICE TA 559 supports TAR in selected patients. Arthrodesis is preferred where deformity is large, bone stock poor, or soft tissues compromised, and has historically lower short-term revision rates but leads to adjacent joint arthritis. Age alone (D) and prior fracture (C) are not absolute contraindications to TAR.
learningPoint: Total ankle replacement is appropriate for well-selected patients with good bone stock and alignment; it preserves hindfoot motion and may protect adjacent joints compared with arthrodesis.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics (concept: ankle OA management)

stem: A 24-year-old county-level footballer inverts his ankle during a match. Radiographs show a transverse fracture through the metaphyseal-diaphyseal junction at the proximal fifth metatarsal (the fracture line begins at the distal end of the 4th–5th intermetatarsal articulation and extends into the diaphysis). He is keen to return to competitive football as quickly as possible. What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Below-knee plaster cast, non-weight-bearing for 6 weeks

options.label: B
options.text: Symptomatic treatment in a hard-soled shoe with early weight-bearing as tolerated

options.label: C
options.text: Primary intramedullary screw fixation

options.label: D
options.text: Lateral plate fixation with bone grafting

options.label: E
options.text: Amputation of the fifth ray

explanation: This describes a Jones fracture (zone II proximal 5th metatarsal fracture at the metaphyseal-diaphyseal junction), which has a high non-union rate due to poor blood supply in this region. In competitive athletes who require rapid return to sport, primary intramedullary screw fixation substantially reduces time to union and return to play compared with non-operative management. Conservative management (A, B) carries non-union rates of 25–50% in active patients. Lateral plate (D) is non-standard and is used for comminuted variants. Amputation (E) has no role here.
learningPoint: Jones fractures (zone II proximal 5th metatarsal) in competitive athletes are best managed with primary intramedullary screw fixation to minimise non-union risk and shorten return to sport.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics (concept: 5th metatarsal fractures)

stem: A 32-year-old man is referred with bilateral, asymmetric, progressive cavovarus foot deformity. He reports a history of frequent lateral ankle sprains and difficulty finding footwear. Examination reveals clawing of all toes, a high arch, and a plantarflexed first ray. The Coleman block test shows that the hindfoot valgus corrects when the first ray is elevated. What underlying diagnosis must be excluded as a priority, and what does the positive Coleman block test indicate?

options.label: A
options.text: Exclude Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease; the positive test indicates fixed hindfoot deformity requiring triple arthrodesis

options.label: B
options.text: Exclude Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease; the positive test indicates the hindfoot is flexible, with the deformity driven by the plantarflexed first ray

options.label: C
options.text: Exclude idiopathic causes; the positive test indicates a fixed subtalar joint

options.label: D
options.text: Exclude rheumatoid arthritis; the positive test confirms ankle OA

options.label: E
options.text: Exclude flat foot; the positive test indicates over-activity of tibialis anterior

explanation: Progressive bilateral (even if asymmetric) cavovarus in a young adult must prompt exclusion of Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy), the commonest neurological cause of pes cavus. A positive Coleman block test — hindfoot valgus corrects when the first ray is elevated off the block — demonstrates that the hindfoot is passively flexible and that the varus is driven by the plantarflexed first ray. This has critical surgical implications: joint-sparing procedures (first metatarsal dorsiflexion osteotomy, peroneus longus to brevis transfer, tibialis posterior transfer) can correct the deformity rather than obligating triple arthrodesis, which would be appropriate only for a fixed, arthritic hindfoot (where the block test would be negative).
learningPoint: A positive Coleman block test in cavovarus indicates a flexible hindfoot driven by a plantarflexed first ray; joint-sparing correction is possible. CMT must be excluded in progressive bilateral cavovarus.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics (concept: pes cavus, CMT)

stem: Theme: Classification of ankle fractures. For each clinical and radiographic scenario below, select the single most appropriate Lauge-Hansen fracture pattern from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Supination-adduction (SA)

options.label: B
options.text: Supination-external rotation (SER) stage II

options.label: C
options.text: Supination-external rotation (SER) stage IV

options.label: D
options.text: Pronation-abduction (PA)

options.label: E
options.text: Pronation-external rotation (PER)

options.label: F
options.text: Isolated posterior malleolus fracture

options.label: G
options.text: Pilon fracture (AO 43-C)

options.label: H
options.text: Tillaux fracture

emiLeads.stem: A 30-year-old woman trips on a kerb. Radiographs show an oblique fibular fracture at the level of the plafond with an associated medial malleolus fracture and a small posterior tibial lip fragment. The mortise is widened. There is no fibular fracture above the syndesmosis.

A 55-year-old man falls heavily. His radiograph shows a transverse fibular fracture well above the tibial plafond (at the level of the proximal fibula), with a comminuted medial malleolus fracture, and a horizontal tibial fracture proximally. The mortise is severely disrupted.

A 22-year-old presents with lateral ankle pain after an inversion injury. Radiographs show a transverse fracture of the distal fibula at or just below the level of the ankle joint with no medial injury.

explanation: Lead 1: SER IV has four components — AITFL tear/avulsion, oblique fibular fracture at plafond level, posterior malleolus fracture, and medial-side injury (medial malleolus fracture or deltoid rupture) — with the fibula fracture at the syndesmotic level. Lead 2: PER produces a fibular fracture above the syndesmosis (often proximal), disruption of all syndesmotic ligaments, and medial-side injury; the hallmark is a high fibular fracture with mortise instability. Lead 3: SA typically results in a transverse fibular fracture at or below the joint level (Weber A) from direct adduction stress; medial side may show a vertical medial malleolus fracture due to impaction.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics (concept: Lauge-Hansen classification)

stem: Theme: Diagnosis of hindfoot and ankle pain. For each clinical scenario below, select the single most likely diagnosis from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Plantar fasciitis

options.label: B
options.text: Achilles tendinopathy (insertional)

options.label: C
options.text: Tarsal tunnel syndrome

options.label: D
options.text: Posterior impingement syndrome

options.label: E
options.text: Sever's disease (calcaneal apophysitis)

options.label: F
options.text: Peroneal tendon subluxation

options.label: G
options.text: Os trigonum syndrome

options.label: H
options.text: Subtalar osteoarthritis

emiLeads.stem: A 16-year-old male cross-country runner reports bilateral heel pain at the back of both heels that is worst during and just after training sessions. He is at Tanner stage 3. Tenderness is elicited by squeezing the posterior calcaneal apophysis laterally.

A 28-year-old female ballet dancer describes deep posterior ankle pain during forced plantarflexion, specifically when performing en pointe. The pain is reproduced by passive plantarflexion of the ankle and there is tenderness posterior to the flexor hallucis longus tendon.

A 45-year-old man reports burning pain, tingling and numbness radiating along the sole of his foot after standing all day. Tinel's sign is positive posterior and inferior to the medial malleolus. Nerve conduction studies confirm delayed sensory conduction.

explanation: Lead 1: Calcaneal apophysitis (Sever's disease) is the commonest cause of heel pain in adolescents at peak growth, characterised by lateral apophyseal squeeze tenderness. Lead 2: Os trigonum syndrome causes posterior ankle pain in dancers during forced plantarflexion; passive plantarflexion and direct posterior tenderness reproduces pain from compression or motion at the trigonal process. Lead 3: Tarsal tunnel syndrome — entrapment of the posterior tibial nerve and its branches beneath the flexor retinaculum — presents with plantar burning and positive Tinel's posterior to the medial malleolus, confirmed by neurophysiology.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics (concept: posterior ankle impingement, tarsal tunnel)

stem: Theme: Management of the diabetic foot. For each clinical scenario below, select the single most appropriate immediate management from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Total contact casting and complete non-weight-bearing

options.label: B
options.text: Urgent surgical debridement and IV antibiotics

options.label: C
options.text: Outpatient wound dressing and oral antibiotics

options.label: D
options.text: Vascular surgical referral for revascularisation before wound management

options.label: E
options.text: Elective referral to a diabetic foot clinic within 4 weeks

options.label: F
options.text: Bisphosphonate infusion as the primary intervention

options.label: G
options.text: MRI foot and multidisciplinary team review within 24 hours

options.label: H
options.text: Urgent below-knee amputation

emiLeads.stem: A 67-year-old with type 2 diabetes and peripheral neuropathy presents to the emergency department with a warm, erythematous, grossly swollen foot. She is afebrile, blood glucose is 11 mmol/L, CRP is 8 mg/L, and WBC is normal. No skin ulceration is visible. X-ray shows early fragmentation of the navicular.

A 70-year-old man with poorly controlled type 1 diabetes attends his GP with a 1-week history of a heel ulcer that has become malodorous. He is now febrile at 38.6°C, heart rate 108 bpm, WBC 18 × 10⁹/L, CRP 210 mg/L. Probing of the ulcer reaches bone.

A 60-year-old woman with long-standing type 2 diabetes presents with a new neuropathic plantar forefoot ulcer that is superficial (Wagner grade 1), clean, and painless. She has palpable pedal pulses and no systemic features of infection.

explanation: Lead 1: Acute Charcot neuroarthropathy with no infection signs requires immediate offloading by total contact cast; no antibiotics are indicated. Lead 2: Bone-probing positivity, systemic sepsis, and foul discharge indicate deep infection with probable osteomyelitis — this is a surgical emergency requiring urgent debridement and IV antibiotics per NICE NG19. Lead 3: A clean superficial neuropathic ulcer in a patient with preserved perfusion should be assessed by the multidisciplinary diabetic foot team (NICE NG19 mandates within 24 hours for new active diabetic foot problems) — escalation beyond standard outpatient dressing is required but urgent surgery is not.
references: NICE NG19: Diabetic foot problems in hospital (2015, updated 2019)
Miller's Review of Orthopaedics (concept: Charcot foot, diabetic foot)

stem: A 24-year-old rugby player falls on an outstretched hand. Radiographs show no fracture. He has tenderness localised to the anatomical snuffbox and pain on axial compression of the thumb. MRI performed at 48 hours confirms a non-displaced waist fracture of the scaphoid. Which management is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Below-elbow cast excluding the thumb for 6 weeks

options.label: B
options.text: Percutaneous headless compression screw fixation

options.label: C
options.text: Open reduction and Herbert screw fixation via dorsal approach

options.label: D
options.text: Short-arm thumb spica cast for 12 weeks with repeat radiograph at 6 weeks

options.label: E
options.text: Reassurance and analgesia; repeat MRI at 6 weeks

explanation: Current evidence (BSSH guidelines, multiple RCTs) supports percutaneous fixation of non-displaced waist fractures in active patients, offering faster return to sport and lower non-union rates than cast immobilisation. A below-elbow cast excluding the thumb (option A) is insufficient immobilisation for scaphoid injury. Twelve weeks in cast (option D) is long and carries higher non-union risk in an active young patient. Open fixation (option C) adds surgical morbidity unnecessarily when percutaneous technique is feasible. Reassurance alone (option E) risks non-union and AVN given the confirmed fracture.
learningPoint: Non-displaced scaphoid waist fractures in active patients are best managed with percutaneous compression screw fixation to minimise non-union risk and enable early return to activity.

stem: A 35-year-old man presents with a 14-month history of radial-sided wrist pain following a missed scaphoid fracture. CT shows a proximal pole non-union with fragmentation. MRI demonstrates complete loss of signal in the proximal pole on T1-weighted sequences, consistent with avascular necrosis. What is the most appropriate surgical option?

options.label: A
options.text: Radial styloidectomy and prolonged cast immobilisation

options.label: B
options.text: Non-vascularised iliac crest bone grafting with Herbert screw

options.label: C
options.text: Vascularised bone graft from the distal radius (1,2-ICSRA pedicle) with internal fixation

options.label: D
options.text: Scaphoidectomy and four-corner fusion

options.label: E
options.text: Total wrist arthrodesis

explanation: Proximal pole AVN with non-union requires a blood supply to achieve union; vascularised bone graft (most commonly the 1,2-intercompartmental supraretinacular artery pedicle from the distal radius) provides this and is the treatment of choice in the absence of wrist arthritis. Non-vascularised graft (option B) has unacceptably low union rates when the proximal pole is avascular. Radial styloidectomy and cast alone (option A) will not heal an established non-union with AVN. Scaphoidectomy and four-corner fusion (option D) and total wrist fusion (option E) are salvage procedures for SNAC arthritis, not the preferred first step when the wrist joint is still preserved.
learningPoint: Proximal pole scaphoid non-union with AVN requires vascularised bone graft; the 1,2-ICSRA pedicle from the distal radius is the most widely used source.

stem: A 58-year-old woman sustains a distal radius fracture. Post-reduction radiographs show radial shortening of 4 mm, dorsal tilt of 18°, and an intra-articular step of 2 mm at the radiocarpal joint. She is otherwise fit and has an active lifestyle. According to accepted radiographic criteria for acceptable reduction, which statement best describes her current alignment?

options.label: A
options.text: Alignment is acceptable; below-elbow cast for 6 weeks is appropriate

options.label: B
options.text: Radial shortening alone is unacceptable; proceed to volar locking plate fixation

options.label: C
options.text: All three parameters are outside acceptable limits; operative fixation is indicated

options.label: D
options.text: Only the articular step exceeds acceptable limits; arthroscopic reduction alone is sufficient

options.label: E
options.text: Dorsal tilt alone is unacceptable; dorsal plating is the preferred fixation method

explanation: Widely cited acceptable radiographic thresholds after distal radius fracture reduction are: radial shortening ≤3 mm, dorsal tilt ≤10° (or palmar tilt ≤20°), and intra-articular step ≤1–2 mm. This patient exceeds all three limits (4 mm shortening, 18° dorsal tilt, 2 mm step), making operative fixation appropriate. Volar locking plate fixation is the preferred construct for this pattern and patient profile. Dorsal plating (option E) is now less favoured due to extensor tendon complications. Arthroscopic reduction alone (option D) does not address the shortening or overall tilt.
learningPoint: Acceptable post-reduction thresholds for distal radius fractures are ≤3 mm shortening, ≤10° dorsal tilt, and ≤1–2 mm intra-articular step; exceeding these in an active patient indicates operative fixation.

stem: A 30-year-old squash player complains of ulnar-sided wrist pain, clicking, and weakness of forearm rotation following a distal radius fracture treated conservatively 10 months ago. Examination reveals a positive piano-key sign and pain on resisted pronation. CT scan shows sigmoid notch dysplasia and 3 mm dorsal translation of the ulnar head relative to the radius in full pronation. The DRUJ is classified as unstable with no articular cartilage damage. What is the most appropriate surgical treatment?

options.label: A
options.text: Ulnar shortening osteotomy

options.label: B
options.text: Darrach resection of the distal ulna

options.label: C
options.text: Arthroscopic debridement of the DRUJ

options.label: D
options.text: TFCC repair via foveal reattachment

options.label: E
options.text: Adams-Berger DRUJ ligament reconstruction using a strip of flexor carpi ulnaris

explanation: Chronic DRUJ instability with sigmoid notch dysplasia and no articular damage in a young active patient is best addressed by ligament reconstruction rather than joint-sacrificing or soft-tissue-only procedures. The Adams-Berger procedure reconstructs the volar and dorsal radioulnar ligaments using an FCU or palmaris longus tendon strip, restoring joint stability. Ulnar shortening osteotomy (option A) addresses ulnar variance but not ligamentous instability. Darrach resection (option B) is a salvage procedure for arthritic DRUJs in lower-demand patients and causes convergence instability. TFCC foveal repair (option D) is appropriate for acute or subacute peripheral TFCC tears without bony instability. Arthroscopic debridement (option C) does not restore mechanical stability.
learningPoint: Chronic DRUJ instability in a young active patient without articular damage is best reconstructed with the Adams-Berger ligament reconstruction procedure.
references: Adams BD, Berger RA. J Hand Surg Am 2002

stem: A 28-year-old mechanic has persistent dorsal wrist pain 8 weeks after a fall. Radiographs show a scapholunate gap of 4 mm on the PA view and a scapholunate angle of 72° on the lateral. The Watson scaphoid shift test is positive with a clunk. There is no articular cartilage damage on MRI. Which finding on the lateral radiograph is most consistent with a dorsal intercalated segment instability (DISI) pattern?

options.label: A
options.text: Capitolunate angle of less than 30°

options.label: B
options.text: Lunate tilted in a volar direction relative to the radius

options.label: C
options.text: Scapholunate angle greater than 60° with dorsal tilt of the lunate

options.label: D
options.text: Radiolunate angle of negative 15°

options.label: E
options.text: Lunotriquetral angle less than 14°

explanation: DISI is defined by dorsal tilt of the lunate (positive radiolunate angle >15°) and an increased scapholunate angle (normal 30–60°, pathological >60–70°) on the lateral radiograph. The lunate rotates into extension (dorsiflexion) as the scaphoid flexes, creating the classic intercalated segment deformity. Volar lunate tilt (option B) and a negative radiolunate angle (option D) describe VISI, associated with lunotriquetral instability. A capitolunate angle under 30° (option A) and lunotriquetral angle under 14° (option E) are normal values and not diagnostic of DISI.
learningPoint: DISI on lateral radiograph is characterised by dorsal lunate tilt and a scapholunate angle exceeding 60°, reflecting disruption of the scapholunate ligament.

stem: A 52-year-old manual worker presents with a 3-year history of wrist pain and stiffness following a neglected scapholunate tear. Radiographs show Stage III SLAC (scapholunate advanced collapse), with arthritis at the radiolunate, capitolunate, and radioscaphoid joints. The lunate fossa of the radius appears intact. He has 40° wrist flexion-extension arc and grip strength of 55% compared with the contralateral side. What is the best surgical option?

options.label: A
options.text: Scapholunate ligament reconstruction with tendon graft

options.label: B
options.text: Proximal row carpectomy

options.label: C
options.text: Scaphoidectomy and four-corner fusion

options.label: D
options.text: Total wrist arthroplasty

options.label: E
options.text: Radial styloidectomy and prolonged splinting

explanation: Stage III SLAC wrist involves arthritis at the capitolunate joint in addition to the radioscaphoid articulation. The lunate fossa of the radius remains unaffected in Stage III, preserving a cartilaginous surface for the lunate. Scaphoidectomy and four-corner fusion (lunate, triquetrum, capitate, hamate) eliminates the arthritic joints while preserving motion through the intact radiolunate articulation, making it the procedure of choice. Proximal row carpectomy (option B) requires an intact proximal capitate and lunate fossa; Stage III disease usually has capitolunate arthritis that makes PRC unsuitable. Ligament reconstruction (option A) is futile with established arthritis. Total wrist arthroplasty (option D) has durability concerns in a manual worker. Radial styloidectomy alone (option E) will not address Stage III collapse.
learningPoint: Stage III SLAC wrist (with capitolunate arthritis but intact lunate fossa) is best treated by scaphoidectomy and four-corner fusion, preserving motion at the radiolunate joint.

stem: A 26-year-old office worker presents with progressively worsening central wrist pain. Plain radiographs show increased density of the entire lunate without collapse. MRI confirms Stage II Kienbock's disease (AVN, no collapse). Radial and ulnar lengths are equal (neutral ulnar variance). What is the most appropriate initial surgical option?

options.label: A
options.text: Lunate excision and silastic implant

options.label: B
options.text: Radial shortening osteotomy

options.label: C
options.text: Ulnar lengthening osteotomy

options.label: D
options.text: Capitalhamate arthrodesis for load redistribution

options.label: E
options.text: Core decompression of the lunate

explanation: When ulnar variance is neutral (as opposed to negative), radial shortening osteotomy (option B) is not indicated because it risks converting to positive ulnar variance. Similarly, ulnar lengthening (option C) is used in negative variance. For neutral variance, joint-levelling is not appropriate. Capitalhamate arthrodesis redirects compressive forces away from the lunate via the scaphotrapeziotrapezoid and capitate, reducing lunate loading, and has good evidence as a motion-preserving unloading procedure in neutral-variance Kienbock's. Lunate excision with silastic (option A) is a salvage procedure with poor long-term outcomes. Core decompression (option E) remains experimental with limited evidence at FRCS-relevant level.
learningPoint: In Kienbock's disease with neutral ulnar variance, joint-levelling procedures are contraindicated; capitalhamate arthrodesis is the preferred unloading procedure.

stem: A 34-year-old tennis player develops ulnar-sided wrist pain after a fall with axial load and forearm rotation. The fovea sign is positive. MRI arthrogram shows a peripheral tear of the TFCC at its ulnar attachment (Palmer Class 1B). There is no DRUJ instability. What is the best management at 10 weeks from injury with failure of conservative treatment?

options.label: A
options.text: Ulnar shortening osteotomy

options.label: B
options.text: Arthroscopic debridement of the central disc

options.label: C
options.text: Arthroscopic or open foveal reattachment of the TFCC

options.label: D
options.text: Immobilisation in long-arm cast for a further 6 weeks

options.label: E
options.text: Darrach resection of the distal ulna

explanation: Palmer Class 1B TFCC tears are peripheral ulnar-sided avulsions, often with the potential to heal given a vascular periphery. When conservative measures fail, arthroscopic or open foveal reattachment restores the biomechanical anchor of the TFCC and is well supported by evidence. Debridement of the central disc (option B) is appropriate for Palmer Class 1A (central disc) tears, which are avascular and do not heal; it is incorrect for a peripheral 1B tear. Ulnar shortening (option A) is used for ulnar impaction (Palmer Class 2 degenerative tears). Further immobilisation at 10 weeks (option D) is unlikely to be effective with persistent symptoms. Darrach resection (option E) is a salvage procedure unsuitable for this young active patient.
learningPoint: Palmer Class 1B TFCC tears (peripheral, foveal avulsion) failing conservative management require foveal reattachment, not central debridement.
references: Palmer AK. J Hand Surg Am 1989 (classification)

subtopic: thumb CMC OA and Bennett's fracture
stem: A 62-year-old woman has a 3-year history of pain and weakness at the base of the thumb. Examination shows a positive grind test, a positive hyperextension stress test of the CMC joint, and wasting of thenar eminence muscles. Plain radiograph confirms Stage III trapeziometacarpal OA with near-complete joint space loss but no trapeziotrapezoid involvement. Conservative management has failed. Which operative technique best combines pain relief, functional restoration, and durability evidence?

options.label: A
options.text: Silicone trapezium implant arthroplasty

options.label: B
options.text: Trapeziectomy alone without ligament reconstruction

options.label: C
options.text: Trapeziectomy with ligament reconstruction and tendon interposition (LRTI)

options.label: D
options.text: Thumb CMC joint arthrodesis

options.label: E
options.text: Thumb CMC joint replacement with a cobalt-chrome prosthesis

explanation: Trapeziectomy with LRTI (ligament reconstruction and tendon interposition, typically using flexor carpi radialis) has the most extensive evidence base in the UK and has been the NICE-reviewed standard for Stage III–IV trapeziometacarpal OA. NICE guidance (2010, updated surveillance) found no clinically meaningful difference between LRTI and trapeziectomy alone, but LRTI remains widely practised for thumb stability. Silicone implants (option A) have fallen from favour due to synovitis. Trapeziectomy alone (option B) is supported by some RCTs but risks proximal migration. CMC arthrodesis (option D) is suitable for younger heavy-labour patients but reduces wrist mobility in post-menopausal women. Prosthetic replacement (option E) carries dislocation and loosening concerns with currently available UK-approved implants.
learningPoint: Trapeziectomy with LRTI is the most widely performed and evidenced procedure for Stage III–IV thumb CMC OA in the UK, though trapeziectomy alone achieves similar functional results.

stem: A 67-year-old Scottish man has a fixed flexion deformity of 45° at the MCP joint and 30° at the PIP joint of his ring finger due to Dupuytren's contracture. He is fit and well. He is keen to have the most durable treatment available. He has no prior treatment. What is the most appropriate intervention?

options.label: A
options.text: Needle fasciotomy (percutaneous needle aponeurotomy)

options.label: B
options.text: Collagenase clostridium histolyticum (CCH) injection

options.label: C
options.text: Selective fasciectomy

options.label: D
options.text: Dermofasciectomy with full-thickness skin grafting

options.label: E
options.text: Continued splinting and physiotherapy

explanation: Selective (partial) fasciectomy carries the most favourable evidence for durable correction of MCP and PIP joint contractures in fit patients with established Dupuytren's disease. Although recurrence occurs, it is lower than with needle fasciotomy or CCH injection. The CORD I trial showed CCH (option B) is effective for MCP contractures but has higher recurrence than fasciectomy for PIP involvement. Needle fasciotomy (option A) has the highest recurrence rate of all three approaches. Dermofasciectomy with skin grafting (option D) is reserved for recurrent or particularly aggressive disease. Splinting alone (option E) does not correct established contracture.
learningPoint: Selective fasciectomy offers the best durability for MCP and PIP Dupuytren's contractures in fit patients; needle fasciotomy and CCH have higher recurrence rates.

stem: A 48-year-old woman has a 6-month history of numbness and tingling in the thumb, index, and middle fingers, worse at night. Tinel's sign is positive at the wrist and the Phalen test is positive at 30 seconds. Nerve conduction studies confirm moderate median neuropathy at the wrist with preserved motor amplitude. Conservative treatment with wrist splints and corticosteroid injection has provided only temporary relief. What is the next most appropriate step?

options.label: A
options.text: Repeat corticosteroid injection and review in 3 months

options.label: B
options.text: Surgical decompression of the carpal tunnel

options.label: C
options.text: EMG to confirm axonal loss before proceeding

options.label: D
options.text: Gabapentin for neuropathic pain

options.label: E
options.text: Referral for ultrasound-guided hydrodissection

explanation: NICE (NG240 2023) and BSSH guidelines recommend carpal tunnel decompression when symptoms are persistent and disabling after failed conservative treatment, which is met here. NCS confirms moderate CTS with preserved motor amplitude, indicating there is still axonal function to preserve by timely surgery. Further corticosteroid injection (option A) is unlikely to provide durable relief after one injection has already failed. Additional EMG (option C) is not required; NCS already establishes the diagnosis. Gabapentin (option D) is not recommended for CTS by NICE. Hydrodissection (option E) lacks sufficient evidence at guideline level to be preferred over established surgical decompression.
learningPoint: Carpal tunnel decompression is indicated when symptoms persist after adequate conservative treatment; NCS confirmation of moderate CTS with preserved motor response supports proceeding to surgery.

stem: A 55-year-old woman with well-controlled type 2 diabetes presents with a 4-month history of triggering and locking of her right ring finger in flexion. The A1 pulley is tender on palpation. Corticosteroid injection 2 months ago gave only 3 weeks of relief. She has no other finger or thumb involvement. What is the most appropriate next management?

options.label: A
options.text: Repeat corticosteroid injection up to a total of four injections before considering surgery

options.label: B
options.text: Surgical A1 pulley release

options.label: C
options.text: Ultrasound-guided injection of hyaluronic acid

options.label: D
options.text: Percutaneous needle release of the A1 pulley

options.label: E
options.text: Splintage of the finger in extension for 6 weeks

explanation: After a single corticosteroid injection that gives only short-term relief, open A1 pulley release is the definitive treatment and is recommended by BSSH guidance. In a diabetic patient, response to corticosteroid is less predictable, and multiple injections carry increasing risk of tendon weakening; guidelines do not mandate repeated injections before surgery when the first has failed. Percutaneous needle release (option D) is an acceptable alternative to open surgery in experienced hands, but open release is the most widely practised and definitive approach. Hyaluronic acid (option C) has no established evidence base for trigger finger. Splinting (option E) alone is effective only in mild early cases, not in established locking.
learningPoint: After a failed corticosteroid injection for trigger finger, open A1 pulley release is the definitive treatment; in diabetics, surgical threshold is lower given poorer injection response.

stem: A 22-year-old man lacerates his right index finger on a broken glass at the proximal interphalangeal joint crease level. Examination confirms absent active PIP flexion with preserved distal joint flexion. The wound is clean. He attends the emergency department 5 hours after injury. What is the optimal management of the tendon injury?

options.label: A
options.text: Primary repair of FDS within 24 hours; FDP left alone as it is intact

options.label: B
options.text: Primary repair of both FDP and FDS within 24 hours using a core suture technique with epitendinous suture

options.label: C
options.text: Wound closure only; delayed primary tendon repair at 3 weeks (in the no-man's land)

options.label: D
options.text: Primary repair of FDP only; FDS excision to reduce tendon bulk in Zone II

options.label: E
options.text: Primary repair of FDS and FDP at 6 weeks once swelling has settled (secondary repair)

explanation: Zone II flexor tendon injuries (no-man's land, between the proximal and middle flexor pulleys) should undergo early primary repair of both FDS and FDP within 12–24 hours of a clean laceration. Modern surgical teaching rejects the historic avoidance of Zone II primary repair. The loss of PIP flexion with intact DIP flexion indicates FDS division (FDP is intact at the DIP). However, because FDS contributes significantly to grip and pinch, both tendons should be repaired where technically feasible. A minimum four-strand core suture augmented by epitendinous suture is recommended to allow early active mobilisation protocols. Excising FDS (option D) and delayed repair (options C and E) are outdated practices that compromise outcomes.
learningPoint: Clean Zone II flexor tendon lacerations require primary repair of both FDS and FDP within 24 hours using a four-strand-minimum core suture technique to facilitate early active rehabilitation.

stem: A 19-year-old cricketer sustains a hyperflexion injury to the tip of his right middle finger. Examination reveals a 35° extensor lag at the DIP joint with passive correction to neutral. Radiograph shows a small avulsion flake at the base of the distal phalanx involving less than 30% of the articular surface and no volar subluxation. What is the first-line treatment?

options.label: A
options.text: Continuous DIP extension splinting for 6–8 weeks, PIP joint left free

options.label: B
options.text: Percutaneous K-wire fixation of the DIP joint in extension

options.label: C
options.text: Open reduction and internal fixation of the avulsion fragment

options.label: D
options.text: DIP joint fusion

options.label: E
options.text: Stack splint applied to both DIP and PIP joints for 4 weeks

explanation: Closed bony mallet injuries with a fragment under 30% of the articular surface and no joint subluxation are managed non-operatively with continuous DIP extension splinting for 6–8 weeks, keeping only the DIP joint splinted and leaving the PIP joint free to prevent PIP stiffness. This achieves good outcomes in the majority of patients. K-wire fixation (option B) and open reduction (option C) are reserved for large articular fragments (>30%), persistent joint subluxation, or failure of conservative management. DIP fusion (option D) is a salvage procedure. Splinting both joints (option E) adds unnecessary PIP stiffness without additional benefit.
learningPoint: Closed mallet fractures with a fragment less than 30% of the joint surface and no subluxation are treated with continuous DIP extension splinting for 6–8 weeks; the PIP joint is left free.

stem: A 38-year-old man presents 36 hours after a puncture wound to the volar aspect of his right ring finger. He is febrile at 38.2°C. Examination reveals the four Kanavel cardinal signs: a finger held in semiflexion, fusiform swelling, exquisite tenderness along the flexor sheath, and pain on passive extension. What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Oral co-amoxiclav and review in 24 hours

options.label: B
options.text: IV antibiotics, elevation and splinting; defer surgical washout to allow antibiotics to work for 48 hours

options.label: C
options.text: Urgent surgical drainage and washout of the flexor sheath under general or regional anaesthesia

options.label: D
options.text: Aspiration of the flexor sheath under local anaesthesia in the emergency department

options.label: E
options.text: IV antibiotics alone as first-line; surgery only if there is no response within 72 hours

explanation: All four Kanavel signs are present, confirming established pyogenic flexor tenosynovitis. Urgent surgical drainage and irrigation of the flexor sheath is required to prevent tendon necrosis, digital ischaemia, and contiguous spread to adjacent deep spaces. Delay materially worsens outcomes. IV antibiotics alone or oral antibiotics (options A, B, E) are insufficient when all Kanavel signs are present; antibiotics are adjuncts to surgery. Aspiration in the ED (option D) is inadequate for established tenosynovitis with systemic signs.
learningPoint: All four Kanavel signs indicate established pyogenic flexor tenosynovitis requiring urgent surgical sheath drainage; delay increases risk of tendon necrosis and digital amputation.

stem: A 35-year-old right-hand-dominant factory worker arrives in the emergency department after a guillotine amputation of his right thumb at the level of the proximal phalanx. The amputated part is viable, having been wrapped in a damp swab and stored in a sealed bag placed on ice. Warm ischaemia time is 2.5 hours. He is otherwise fit with no significant comorbidities and no other injuries. What is the most appropriate management of the amputated thumb?

options.label: A
options.text: Replantation is contraindicated at this ischaemia time; proceed to revision amputation and prosthetic fitting

options.label: B
options.text: Replantation should be attempted given the functional importance of the thumb and acceptable ischaemia time

options.label: C
options.text: Pollicisation of the index finger is preferable to replantation for proximal phalangeal thumb amputations

options.label: D
options.text: Osteoplastic thumb reconstruction with rib graft is the preferred first-line approach

options.label: E
options.text: Transfer the patient to the nearest hospital; replantation is not indicated for thumb amputations in adults

explanation: The thumb contributes approximately 40–50% of hand function; its replantation is always considered when technically feasible. Indications for thumb replantation are broad and include all clean guillotine amputations in healthy individuals regardless of digit level. A 2.5-hour warm ischaemia time is well within the accepted threshold (generally up to 6 hours for digits), and the digit has been appropriately cooled. Replantation should proceed at a specialist centre. Pollicisation (option C) is reserved for congenital thumb absence or when replantation has definitively failed. Osteoplastic reconstruction (option D) is a secondary procedure. Prosthetic fitting without an attempt at replantation (options A and E) is inappropriate given the viable part and acceptable ischaemia time.
learningPoint: Thumb replantation is indicated for all clean amputations in fit adults with warm ischaemia under 6 hours and a viable part; the thumb's unique functional importance makes every attempt worthwhile.

stem: A 44-year-old keen cyclist presents with a 6-week history of weakness of intrinsic muscles of the right hand affecting all ulnar-innervated intrinsics, with no sensory symptoms. Examination confirms weakness of all dorsal interossei, hypothenar muscles, and adductor pollicis. Sensation over the little finger and ulnar palm is entirely normal. Tinel's sign is positive at the wrist on the ulnar side. EMG/NCS confirms ulnar nerve compression at the wrist. Where is the most likely site of compression within Guyon's canal?

options.label: A
options.text: Zone 1: proximal to the bifurcation of the ulnar nerve, affecting both motor and sensory branches

options.label: B
options.text: Zone 2: around the hook of hamate, affecting the deep motor branch only

options.label: C
options.text: Zone 3: distal to the bifurcation, affecting the superficial sensory branch only

options.label: D
options.text: Proximal forearm (cubital tunnel), with sparing of the dorsal cutaneous branch

options.label: E
options.text: Distal palm, affecting the common digital nerve to the fourth web space

explanation: The pattern of pure motor deficit affecting all ulnar-innervated intrinsics with completely normal sensation in the ulnar-supplied palm and little finger localises to Zone 2 of Guyon's canal, at or around the hook of the hamate, where only the deep motor branch of the ulnar nerve runs. Zone 1 compression (option A) would produce combined motor and sensory deficit. Zone 3 (option C) causes purely sensory loss. Cubital tunnel compression (option D) would give sensory symptoms in the little/ring finger and the dorsum of the hand (dorsal cutaneous branch arises proximal to Guyon's), plus forearm flexor weakness in severe cases. Distal palmar compression (option E) affects only specific digital nerves.
learningPoint: Pure intrinsic motor loss with normal ulnar sensation at the wrist localises compression to Zone 2 of Guyon's canal (around the hook of hamate), where only the deep motor branch travels.

stem: Theme: Management of fingertip and nail-bed injuries. For each scenario select the single most appropriate management from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Primary closure of the nail fold with preservation of the nail plate as a biological dressing

options.label: B
options.text: Sterile paraffin gauze dressing and allow secondary intention healing

options.label: C
options.text: Nail-bed repair with 6-0 absorbable sutures under loupe magnification, nail plate replaced or synthetic substitute used

options.label: D
options.text: Replantation at a specialist microsurgery centre

options.label: E
options.text: V-Y advancement flap for volar soft-tissue coverage

options.label: F
options.text: Amputation revision with primary closure

options.label: G
options.text: Composite graft reattachment of the amputated fingertip

emiLeads.stem: A 7-year-old girl has a crushing injury to the tip of her left index finger with a door. There is a 1.5 cm soft-tissue defect on the volar pulp without exposed bone. The wound is clean. She is brought to the emergency department within 2 hours.
A 29-year-old man has a guillotine injury to the right index finger. The amputated tip (distal to the DIP joint) is available and viable. He is fit and well. Warm ischaemia time is 1.5 hours.

A 35-year-old woman sustains a crush injury to the right ring finger. There is a stellate laceration of the nail bed with a large haematoma, partial loss of the nail plate, and an underlying tuft fracture visible on radiograph.
A 22-year-old man has a volar pulp soft-tissue defect of 1.8 cm at the tip of the index finger with 3 mm of exposed distal phalanx bone. The amputated fragment is not available.

explanation: Lead 1: In children, fingertip injuries up to 1.5–2 cm without exposed bone heal reliably by secondary intention under moist dressings; surgery is not required. Lead 2: Composite graft reattachment of amputated fingertips is appropriate in adults for clean distal amputations with viable parts when full microsurgical replantation infrastructure is not required; success rates are acceptable for small tips. Lead 3: Nail-bed lacerations with haematoma and partial nail loss require formal repair under magnification with an absorbable suture, and the nail plate (or synthetic substitute) is replaced to maintain the fold and allow new nail growth. Lead 4: A volar pulp defect with exposed bone requires local flap coverage; the V-Y advancement flap provides sensate local tissue without the morbidity of a distant flap.

subtopic: hand and wrist ganglia and soft-tissue tumours
stem: Theme: Soft-tissue swellings of the hand and wrist. For each scenario select the single most likely diagnosis from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Dorsal wrist ganglion

options.label: B
options.text: Volar wrist ganglion

options.label: C
options.text: Giant cell tumour of tendon sheath (GCTTS)

options.label: D
options.text: Mucous cyst

options.label: E
options.text: Lipoma

options.label: F
options.text: Palmar fascial fibroma (Dupuytren's nodule)

options.label: G
options.text: Glomus tumour

options.label: H
options.text: Epidermal inclusion cyst

emiLeads.stem: A 52-year-old woman has a firm, non-tender swelling overlying the DIP joint of her right index finger. The overlying skin is thin and slightly translucent. Nail ridging is present. Radiograph shows DIP joint osteophytes.

A 38-year-old seamstress presents with a slow-growing, firm, lobulated, non-transilluminable swelling on the volar aspect of her left index finger at the level of the proximal phalanx. It is not tender and does not transilluminate. Ultrasound shows a solid hypoechoic mass tightly applied to the flexor tendon sheath.

A 27-year-old woman has an 8-month history of exquisite point tenderness under her right ring finger nail. Symptoms are dramatically worse in cold weather. Radiograph shows a small scalloped erosion on the dorsal surface of the distal phalanx.

explanation: Lead 1: A thin translucent cyst at the DIP joint associated with nail ridging and underlying OA is pathognomonic of a mucous cyst, arising from the DIP joint synovium. Lead 2: A solid, lobulated, non-transilluminable mass tightly adherent to a flexor tendon sheath in a middle-aged woman is the classic presentation of GCTTS (also called pigmented villonodular synovitis of the tendon sheath); it is the most common solid soft-tissue tumour of the hand. Lead 3: Subungual point tenderness, cold hypersensitivity, and a bony erosion on plain film are the classic triad of a glomus tumour arising from the neurovascular glomus body beneath the nail.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics (soft-tissue tumours of hand)

subtopic: Bennett's fracture and thumb CMC injuries
stem: Theme: Injuries and conditions of the thumb metacarpal base and CMC joint. For each scenario select the single most appropriate management from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Closed reduction and thumb spica cast for 4–6 weeks

options.label: B
options.text: Closed reduction and percutaneous K-wire fixation

options.label: C
options.text: Open reduction and internal fixation with mini-fragment screws

options.label: D
options.text: Trapeziectomy and LRTI

options.label: E
options.text: Thumb CMC joint arthrodesis

options.label: F
options.text: Non-operative management with buddy strapping to the index finger

options.label: G
options.text: MRI of the CMC joint and delayed surgical review

emiLeads.stem: A 24-year-old boxer has a Bennett's fracture-dislocation of the right thumb. The volar beak fragment involves 20% of the articular surface. Closed reduction is achieved under image intensifier but the joint is unstable and re-displaces on releasing traction.

A 32-year-old motorcyclist sustains a Rolando fracture (comminuted intra-articular fracture of the thumb metacarpal base) with three main articular fragments. CT shows a Y-shaped fracture pattern with 3 mm step. He is fit and young.

A 58-year-old retired teacher has Stage IV trapeziometacarpal OA with involvement of the trapeziotrapezoid joint. She has failed all conservative measures and requests surgical intervention for disabling pain.

explanation: Lead 1: A Bennett's fracture that is unstable after closed reduction (the most common scenario, because APL pulls the metacarpal shaft proximally and radially) is ideally stabilised with percutaneous K-wire fixation transfixing the base of the first metacarpal to the second or to the trapezium; open reduction is reserved for large fragments or irreducible dislocations. Lead 2: A Rolando fracture with CT-confirmed significant articular displacement in a young patient warrants open reduction and internal fixation to restore articular congruity, accepting that outcomes are still generally inferior to Bennett's fractures even with perfect reduction. Lead 3: Stage IV trapeziometacarpal OA extends to involve the trapeziotrapezoid joint; simple trapeziectomy would leave residual pain from the trapeziotrapezoid joint, so trapeziectomy (removing the trapezium completely) with LRTI addresses both joints and is the preferred surgical option.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics (thumb CMC injuries)

stem: A 24-year-old football player twists his right knee during a tackle. He hears a pop, the knee swells within two hours, and he is unable to continue playing. Examination the following day reveals a positive Lachman test and an anterior pivot shift. MRI confirms a complete mid-substance ACL tear with no other structural injury. After six weeks of physiotherapy the knee remains functionally unstable. Which graft source offers the lowest re-rupture rate at five years according to current evidence?

options.label: A
options.text: Hamstring tendon autograft (quadruple-strand semitendinosus)

options.label: B
options.text: Bone-patellar tendon-bone autograft

options.label: C
options.text: Quadriceps tendon autograft (full-thickness with bone block)

options.label: D
options.text: Peroneus longus allograft

options.label: E
options.text: Synthetic ligament augmentation device

explanation: Bone-patellar tendon-bone (BPTB) autograft remains the benchmark for ACL reconstruction with the largest body of long-term evidence showing the lowest re-rupture rates, particularly in young male athletes. Hamstring autografts have higher re-rupture risk in young active males and graft diameter below 8 mm significantly worsens outcome. Quadriceps tendon is an emerging graft with promising early data but lacks equivalent long-term evidence. Allografts carry higher failure rates in young patients, especially when irradiated. Synthetic devices have high complication and failure rates and are not recommended for primary reconstruction.
learningPoint: BPTB autograft has the lowest re-rupture rate at five years in young active males; graft diameter below 8 mm is the strongest modifiable predictor of hamstring graft failure.

stem: A 30-year-old motorcyclist sustains a dashboard injury to his right knee. He complains of posterior knee pain. On examination there is 10 mm of posterior tibial sag compared with the contralateral side on the posterior drawer test, with a firm endpoint. Radiographs show no fracture. Which structure is most commonly isolated injured in this mechanism and what is the initial management of choice?

options.label: A
options.text: Posterior cruciate ligament; operative reconstruction within two weeks

options.label: B
options.text: Posterior cruciate ligament; supervised physiotherapy with quadriceps strengthening

options.label: C
options.text: Posterolateral corner; primary repair within three weeks

options.label: D
options.text: Posterior cruciate ligament; immobilisation in a plaster cylinder for six weeks

options.label: E
options.text: Anterior cruciate ligament; arthroscopic reconstruction at three months

explanation: The dashboard mechanism and positive posterior drawer test with firm endpoint indicate an isolated PCL rupture (grade II, 6–10 mm). Isolated PCL injuries in the absence of instability or combined ligament injury are treated conservatively with intensive quadriceps rehabilitation, which restores function in the majority of patients. Operative reconstruction is reserved for grade III injuries (> 10 mm), symptomatic instability after rehabilitation, or combined ligament injuries. Plaster immobilisation is not evidence-based and leads to quadriceps atrophy. Posterolateral corner injury typically presents with external rotation and varus laxity.
learningPoint: Isolated grade I–II PCL tears are managed non-operatively with quadriceps strengthening; surgery is reserved for grade III or combined ligament injuries.

stem: A 28-year-old recreational runner develops medial knee pain after a twisting injury while playing squash two weeks ago. MRI shows a vertical longitudinal tear of the posterior horn of the medial meniscus measuring 25 mm, situated entirely within the red-red zone. The knee has full range of motion and no locking. Which management strategy best preserves long-term knee function?

options.label: A
options.text: Arthroscopic partial meniscectomy of the posterior horn

options.label: B
options.text: Six weeks of physiotherapy followed by repeat MRI before any surgical decision

options.label: C
options.text: Arthroscopic meniscal repair using an all-inside technique

options.label: D
options.text: Total meniscectomy via open arthrotomy

options.label: E
options.text: Corticosteroid injection into the joint and return to sport at six weeks

explanation: A vertical longitudinal tear in the red-red zone (peripheral vascular territory) in a young patient is highly amenable to repair; this location has the best healing potential due to its blood supply. Preserving meniscal tissue reduces long-term risk of compartmental osteoarthritis. Partial meniscectomy sacrifices tissue unnecessarily and accelerates joint degeneration. Total meniscectomy is wholly inappropriate for a young patient with a repairable tear. Conservative management alone is unlikely to heal a 25 mm tear. Corticosteroid injection does not address the structural injury.
learningPoint: Vertical longitudinal tears in the vascular (red-red) zone in young patients should be repaired rather than excised to protect the articular cartilage long-term.
references: NICE MTG guidance on meniscal repair

stem: A 17-year-old girl presents after a third episode of traumatic patellar dislocation. She has patella alta, a tibial tuberosity to trochlear groove (TT-TG) distance of 18 mm on CT, and a trochlear dysplasia classified as Dejour type B. MPFL is confirmed ruptured on MRI. The distal femoral physis is closed. Which surgical procedure addresses the primary static stabiliser that is deficient in this condition?

options.label: A
options.text: Tibial tuberosity anteromedialisation (Fulkerson osteotomy)

options.label: B
options.text: Medial patellofemoral ligament reconstruction

options.label: C
options.text: Lateral retinacular release in isolation

options.label: D
options.text: Trochleoplasty alone

options.label: E
options.text: Medial reefing and vastus medialis obliquus advancement

explanation: The MPFL is the primary static restraint to lateral patellar displacement, accounting for approximately 50–60% of medial stabilising force. Its reconstruction is the cornerstone of surgical management for recurrent patellar instability with a ruptured MPFL. TT-TG of 18 mm is borderline (threshold for osteotomy typically > 20 mm), so tibial tuberosity transfer is not the priority here. Isolated lateral release is contraindicated as primary treatment and worsens instability in many cases. Trochleoplasty is reserved for severe trochlear dysplasia causing impingement. Soft-tissue reefing alone has high recurrence rates.
learningPoint: MPFL reconstruction is the primary operation for recurrent patellar instability with a confirmed MPFL tear; lateral release alone is insufficient and may worsen the condition.
references: BSCOS consensus statement on patellar instability

stem: A 45-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after a high-energy road traffic collision. His right knee is grossly unstable in all planes, with the tibia displaced anteriorly. The limb is warm distally on arrival, pulses are present, and ABI is 0.82. Which is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.label: A
options.text: Proceed to theatre for immediate multiligament reconstruction

options.label: B
options.text: Apply a hinged knee brace and obtain an MRI within 24 hours

options.label: C
options.text: Obtain CT angiography of the popliteal artery urgently

options.label: D
options.text: Apply a spanning external fixator and discharge for outpatient follow-up

options.label: E
options.text: Reassure that pulses are present and arrange vascular review in 48 hours

explanation: An ABI below 0.9 after knee dislocation indicates a significant probability of vascular injury and mandates urgent CT angiography even when pulses are palpable, because intimal tears can produce a normal flow initially before thrombosing. The classic teaching of 'warm foot = safe' is unreliable; missed popliteal artery injury leads to limb loss. The immediate priority is vascular assessment, not ligament reconstruction or imaging of soft tissues. A spanning external fixator is appropriate to stabilise the joint once vascular status is confirmed, not as the primary response.
learningPoint: An ABI < 0.9 after knee dislocation requires urgent CT angiography regardless of the presence of distal pulses — intimal injury may not cause immediate ischaemia.

stem: A 72-year-old woman underwent a cemented total knee arthroplasty eighteen months ago. She presents with progressive knee pain, a CRP of 6 mg/L, and an ESR of 22 mm/hr. Plain radiographs show a well-fixed femoral component but a radiolucent line of 2 mm around the entire tibial baseplate without change from the post-operative film. Aspiration reveals 1,200 white cells/mm³ with 70% polymorphs. Which diagnosis is most consistent with these findings?

options.label: A
options.text: Periprosthetic joint infection

options.label: B
options.text: Aseptic loosening of the tibial component

options.label: C
options.text: Adverse reaction to metal debris

options.label: D
options.text: Complex regional pain syndrome

options.label: E
options.text: Referred pain from ipsilateral hip osteoarthritis

explanation: Aseptic loosening is the most consistent diagnosis. The synovial fluid cell count of 1,200 cells/mm³ is below the ICM 2018 threshold of 3,000 cells/mm³ for TKA infection. The CRP (< 10 mg/L) and ESR are within normal limits for the early post-operative period, making periprosthetic infection unlikely. The radiolucent line is stable from the post-operative film, suggesting it is a non-progressive mantle defect rather than progressive loosening — however the clinical picture overall fits aseptic aetiology. Metal debris reaction is uncommon in cemented all-polyethylene or standard implants. CRPS produces normal inflammatory markers and no radiological change.
learningPoint: Synovial fluid cell count > 3,000 cells/mm³ with > 80% polymorphs is the ICM 2018 threshold for TKA periprosthetic infection; counts below this in conjunction with normal serum markers favour aseptic aetiology.

stem: A 61-year-old man with isolated medial compartment osteoarthritis is being assessed for unicompartmental knee arthroplasty (UKA). Which combination of factors represents the strongest contraindication to a medial UKA?

options.label: A
options.text: BMI 32 kg/m² and age under 65 years

options.label: B
options.text: Anterior knee pain and patellofemoral crepitus on clinical examination

options.label: C
options.text: Full-thickness chondral loss in the medial compartment with an intact ACL and correctable varus deformity

options.label: D
options.text: Inflammatory arthropathy and fixed flexion deformity greater than 15°

options.label: E
options.text: Occupational requirement for kneeling and a range of motion of 120 degrees

explanation: Inflammatory arthropathy is an absolute contraindication to UKA because the disease process affects all compartments and synovium, making single-compartment replacement inappropriate. A fixed flexion deformity exceeding 15° is also a contraindication as it indicates pan-compartmental involvement or significant soft-tissue contracture that cannot be corrected by a single-compartment implant. BMI and age were previously quoted contraindications but Oxford data and subsequent registry analysis show acceptable outcomes in higher BMI and younger patients when other criteria are met. Patellofemoral symptoms are no longer an absolute contraindication with modern devices. Good ROM and ACL integrity actually favour UKA.
learningPoint: Inflammatory arthropathy and fixed flexion deformity > 15° are firm contraindications to UKA; patellofemoral symptoms and younger age/higher BMI are no longer absolute contraindications.

stem: A 42-year-old physiotherapist has symptomatic medial compartment osteoarthritis with 7 degrees of varus alignment of the mechanical axis. She wishes to remain active and delay arthroplasty. Cartilage loss is confirmed as Outerbridge grade III in the medial compartment only, with an intact lateral compartment and ACL. Which osteotomy technique is most commonly used to achieve mechanical axis correction in this scenario and what is the target post-operative alignment?

options.label: A
options.text: Lateral closing-wedge high tibial osteotomy; neutral mechanical axis (0 degrees)

options.label: B
options.text: Medial opening-wedge high tibial osteotomy; 3–5 degrees of valgus of the mechanical axis

options.label: C
options.text: Distal femoral osteotomy; 2 degrees of valgus of the mechanical axis

options.label: D
options.text: Medial opening-wedge tibial osteotomy; exact neutral axis

options.label: E
options.text: Lateral closing-wedge high tibial osteotomy; 8 degrees of anatomical valgus

explanation: Medial opening-wedge high tibial osteotomy (MOWHTO) has largely superseded lateral closing-wedge HTO in most UK centres because it avoids fibular osteotomy, preserves bone stock for future arthroplasty, and is technically straightforward with locking plates. The target correction is to shift the weight-bearing line to the Fujisawa point (approximately 62% of the tibial plateau width from medial), corresponding to 3–5 degrees of mechanical valgus overcorrection, thereby unloading the medial compartment. Exact neutral axis is insufficient to offload the diseased compartment. Distal femoral osteotomy is used for lateral compartment disease with valgus deformity.
learningPoint: Medial opening-wedge HTO targets 3–5 degrees of mechanical valgus overcorrection (Fujisawa point) to unload medial compartment osteoarthritis.

stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with anterior knee pain worsened by prolonged sitting, stair descent, and squatting. She has no history of patellar dislocation. Examination reveals positive Clarke's sign, J-sign on active extension, and tenderness along the medial patellar facet. Lateral retinacular tightness is noted. Plain radiographs show no fracture; lateral view shows patella alta. MRI confirms lateral patellar tilt but intact MPFL and cartilage. Which is the most appropriate first-line intervention?

options.label: A
options.text: Arthroscopic lateral retinacular release

options.label: B
options.text: Tibial tuberosity anteromedialisation

options.label: C
options.text: Physiotherapy focusing on VMO strengthening and lower limb kinetic chain rehabilitation

options.label: D
options.text: MPFL reconstruction

options.label: E
options.text: Intra-articular corticosteroid injection followed by arthroscopic debridement

explanation: Patellofemoral pain syndrome without dislocation or instability is initially managed with supervised physiotherapy, targeting VMO and hip abductor strengthening and lower limb kinetic chain correction, which has the strongest evidence base for this presentation. Lateral retinacular release as a primary procedure has unpredictable results and is not first-line. Tibial tuberosity osteotomy is reserved for structural malalignment unresponsive to rehabilitation. MPFL reconstruction addresses instability, which is absent here. Corticosteroid injection and debridement have limited evidence for patellofemoral pain without structural pathology.
learningPoint: Patellofemoral pain syndrome is treated first with structured physiotherapy (VMO + kinetic chain); surgery is reserved for failure of adequate conservative management.

stem: A 16-year-old male footballer has a six-month history of activity-related medial knee pain. MRI shows a 2 cm × 2 cm osteochondral dissecans lesion on the medial femoral condyle, with a T2 high-signal line deep to the fragment but no breach of the articular surface and no loose body. The fragment appears stable on gradient-echo sequences. Which management is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Arthroscopic fragment excision and microfracture of the base

options.label: B
options.text: Non-operative management with activity modification and a follow-up MRI at 3–6 months

options.label: C
options.text: Autologous chondrocyte implantation as first-line treatment

options.label: D
options.text: Mosaicplasty osteochondral transfer from the lateral trochlear ridge

options.label: E
options.text: Retroarticular drilling and bone grafting via arthroscopy

explanation: In a skeletally immature patient with an open physis and a stable OCD lesion (no loose body, articular surface intact, no definitive MRI instability signs), non-operative management with activity restriction and serial MRI surveillance is the standard of care. The majority of juvenile OCD lesions heal with conservative treatment given the inherent biological healing potential of the growing skeleton. Surgical intervention — whether drilling, fixation, or resurfacing — is reserved for failed conservative management, unstable fragments, or loose bodies. ACI and mosaicplasty are second-line salvage procedures.
learningPoint: Stable juvenile OCD with an intact articular surface and open physes should be managed conservatively with activity restriction; surgery is reserved for instability or failure to heal.

stem: A 55-year-old man with type 2 diabetes and a BMI of 34 kg/m² slips on wet stairs and is unable to straighten his right knee. Examination reveals a palpable gap just above the superior patellar pole, a high-riding patella, and inability to perform a straight leg raise. Plain radiographs confirm patella alta on lateral view with no fracture. Which investigation will best confirm the suspected diagnosis and define the extent of injury?

options.label: A
options.text: Ultrasound of the anterior knee structures

options.label: B
options.text: CT of the knee in full extension

options.label: C
options.text: MRI of the knee

options.label: D
options.text: Diagnostic arthroscopy

options.label: E
options.text: Stress radiograph with weight-bearing

explanation: MRI is the investigation of choice for suspected quadriceps tendon rupture when clinical findings are equivocal or when the extent of injury (complete vs. partial, associated retinacular tears) needs definition before surgical planning. Ultrasound is a reasonable first-line dynamic tool in experienced hands but is operator-dependent and provides less information about the retinacular contribution and bone marrow. CT does not visualise soft tissue detail adequately. Arthroscopy is invasive and unnecessary for diagnosis. Stress radiographs add little beyond the clinical examination and plain films.
learningPoint: MRI is the preferred imaging modality for quantifying quadriceps tendon ruptures and guiding surgical planning; ultrasound is a useful adjunct but is operator-dependent.

stem: A 38-year-old pedestrian is struck by a vehicle at low speed. Radiographs reveal a split-depression fracture of the lateral tibial plateau. CT confirms 12 mm of articular depression and 4 mm of lateral condylar widening involving less than one third of the plateau width. The limb is neurovascularly intact. Which Schatzker classification type and treatment is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Schatzker II; open reduction, elevation of the depressed segment, bone grafting, and lateral buttress plating

options.label: B
options.text: Schatzker I; closed reduction and casting in a long leg plaster for six weeks

options.label: C
options.text: Schatzker IV; medial plate fixation via posteromedial approach

options.label: D
options.text: Schatzker II; spanning external fixation and early mobilisation

options.label: E
options.text: Schatzker VI; dual-plate fixation via two separate incisions

explanation: A split-depression pattern of the lateral tibial plateau is Schatzker type II. Surgical thresholds include articular depression ≥ 3 mm (some use 5–10 mm in young active patients, typically 10 mm used in practice) or condylar widening ≥ 5 mm. With 12 mm depression, operative treatment is clearly indicated. The technique involves subarticular elevation of the depressed fragment, bone grafting to support the reduction, and lateral buttress plate fixation to prevent re-collapse. Schatzker I is a pure split without depression. Medial plate fixation addresses Schatzker IV (medial plateau). Dual plating is for bicondylar (Schatzker V–VI) injuries.
learningPoint: Schatzker II (split-depression lateral plateau) with > 10 mm articular depression requires elevation, bone graft, and lateral buttress plating to restore congruity.
references: BOAST guideline on tibial plateau fractures

subtopic: Septic arthritis of the native knee
stem: A 68-year-old woman with rheumatoid arthritis on methotrexate and adalimumab presents with a two-day history of a hot, swollen, painful right knee. Temperature is 38.4°C, CRP 187 mg/L, WBC 14 × 10⁹/L. Joint aspiration yields 45 mL of turbid fluid; Gram stain shows Gram-positive cocci in clusters. What is the most appropriate immediate surgical management?

options.label: A
options.text: Repeat aspiration every 24 hours and intravenous flucloxacillin

options.label: B
options.text: Arthroscopic washout and debridement with IV antibiotics

options.label: C
options.text: Open arthrotomy and joint washout with IV antibiotics

options.label: D
options.text: Intra-articular corticosteroid injection with IV antibiotics

options.label: E
options.text: Oral antibiotics and urgent rheumatology referral

explanation: Septic arthritis of the native knee requires urgent surgical lavage to remove purulent material, debris, and proteolytic enzymes that destroy articular cartilage within hours to days. Arthroscopic washout and debridement is the preferred technique in most UK centres as it allows thorough irrigation, synovial biopsy, and is less morbid than open arthrotomy — both are acceptable. In a patient on biologics, Staphylococcus aureus is the most likely organism (Gram-positive cocci in clusters), and IV flucloxacillin (or vancomycin if MRSA risk) should accompany surgery. Repeated needle aspiration alone is inadequate for pyarthrosis of this severity. Corticosteroid injection is contraindicated in suspected infection.
learningPoint: Native knee septic arthritis requires urgent arthroscopic or open washout combined with IV anti-staphylococcal antibiotics; aspiration alone is insufficient for established pyarthrosis.

stem: A 32-year-old rugby player sustains a varus and hyperextension injury to his left knee. Examination reveals varus laxity at 0° and 30°, external rotation asymmetry of 15° at 30° flexion on the dial test, and a positive reverse pivot shift. MRI confirms disruption of the fibular collateral ligament, popliteus tendon, and popliteofibular ligament. There is no PCL or ACL injury. When is surgical repair or reconstruction most likely to yield the best outcome?

options.label: A
options.text: Delayed reconstruction at three to six months once swelling has resolved

options.label: B
options.text: Primary repair or reconstruction within the first two to three weeks of injury

options.label: C
options.text: Arthroscopic reconstruction at six months using a synthetic graft

options.label: D
options.text: Conservative management with a functional brace and physiotherapy for 12 weeks

options.label: E
options.text: Repair at time of ACL reconstruction only if instability persists

explanation: Grade III posterolateral corner injuries do not heal reliably with conservative management and the surgical window for primary repair (superior tissue quality, anatomical restoration) closes rapidly — most authorities advocate repair or reconstruction within two to three weeks before tissue becomes friable and contracted. Beyond this window, reconstruction using tendon graft is required. Delayed reconstruction at three to six months has higher failure rates. Conservative management for complete PLC disruption leads to persistent instability and accelerated medial and ACL graft failure if not addressed. Waiting for ACL reconstruction timing is a common error that results in missed repair opportunity.
learningPoint: Complete PLC injuries should be repaired or reconstructed within two to three weeks; delayed surgery requires tendon graft reconstruction and has higher failure rates.

stem: A 58-year-old man has a two-month history of a soft, fluctuant swelling in the popliteal fossa. It increases in size after prolonged standing and partially reduces on knee flexion. He denies trauma or fever. Ultrasound shows a 4 cm anechoic fluid collection in the medial popliteal fossa with a narrow neck communicating with the joint between the medial gastrocnemius and semimembranosus tendons. What is the most important next step in his management?

options.label: A
options.text: Aspiration of the cyst under ultrasound guidance

options.label: B
options.text: Immediate surgical excision to prevent rupture

options.label: C
options.text: MRI of the knee to identify the underlying intra-articular cause

options.label: D
options.text: Doppler ultrasound to exclude deep vein thrombosis

options.label: E
options.text: Reassurance and review in six months without further investigation
