explanation: Baker's (popliteal) cysts in adults are almost always secondary to intra-articular pathology — most commonly a medial meniscal tear or osteoarthritis — that generates excess synovial fluid. Treating the cyst in isolation without identifying and addressing the underlying cause leads to high recurrence rates. MRI of the knee will identify meniscal tears, cartilage loss, or other structural pathology that is the primary target for treatment. Aspiration alone is effective only temporarily. Surgical excision without addressing intra-articular disease has a high recurrence rate. DVT is in the differential but ultrasound already confirmed a cyst with characteristic appearance and no thrombosis features.
learningPoint: Adult Baker's cysts are secondary to intra-articular pathology; MRI is required to identify the underlying cause before directing treatment.

stem: A 29-year-old female distance runner undergoes arthroscopy for a symptomatic chondral lesion on the medial femoral condyle, found to be an Outerbridge grade IV full-thickness defect measuring 3.8 cm². The margins are stable, the underlying bone is not sclerotic, and she has normal alignment and an intact meniscus. She has not had any prior cartilage surgery. Which first-stage biological treatment is most appropriate, and what is the current preferred second-line option if this fails?

options.label: A
options.text: Microfracture as first-line; autologous chondrocyte implantation (ACI) if microfracture fails

options.label: B
options.text: ACI as first-line; osteochondral allograft if ACI fails

options.label: C
options.text: Mosaicplasty as first-line; microfracture if mosaicplasty fails

options.label: D
options.text: Microfracture as first-line; total knee replacement if microfracture fails

options.label: E
options.text: Intra-articular platelet-rich plasma injections as first-line; microfracture if this fails

explanation: For a first-time full-thickness chondral defect of this size (< 4 cm²) in a young patient, microfracture remains an appropriate first-line biological treatment, producing fibrocartilage repair tissue with acceptable functional results. NICE technology appraisal TA477 recommends ACI (now predominantly matrix-associated ACI, MACI) as the preferred second-line option in patients for whom microfracture has failed or is considered inappropriate. For defects > 4 cm², some guidelines advocate first-line ACI. Mosaicplasty is typically reserved for smaller defects (< 2–3 cm²) due to donor site morbidity. TKR is not appropriate as first-line in a 29-year-old. PRP is not recommended for structural chondral repair.
learningPoint: Microfracture is first-line for isolated chondral defects < 4 cm²; NICE TA477 recommends ACI (MACI) as the preferred option when microfracture fails.

stem: A 74-year-old woman is reviewed 14 months after a cemented primary TKA. She has had increasing knee pain for the past two months. CRP is 32 mg/L, ESR 58 mm/hr. Joint aspiration yields 5,800 cells/mm³ with 85% polymorphonuclear cells; culture grows coagulase-negative Staphylococcus after five days. The components are well-fixed on radiograph with no osteolysis. She is fit for surgery. Which is the most appropriate surgical strategy?

options.label: A
options.text: DAIR (debridement, antibiotics, and implant retention) with exchange of all modular components

options.label: B
options.text: Single-stage revision total knee arthroplasty

options.label: C
options.text: Two-stage revision: explant + cement spacer, followed by reimplantation after six weeks of targeted IV antibiotics

options.label: D
options.text: Long-term suppressive antibiotics with rifampicin combination without surgery

options.label: E
options.text: Arthroscopic washout and synovectomy with IV vancomycin

explanation: Two-stage revision is the gold-standard treatment for chronic periprosthetic joint infection (defined as > 4–6 weeks from index surgery or symptom onset), as recommended by the ICM 2018 consensus for cases with a virulent or difficult-to-treat organism. At 14 months with a two-month symptom history, this is chronic PJI; the organism (coagulase-negative Staphylococcus) can form biofilm. DAIR is appropriate only for acute haematogenous or early post-operative infection (< 3–4 weeks of symptoms) with well-fixed components and no sinus tract — the chronicity here exceeds this window. Single-stage revision requires confident organism identification pre-operatively and soft-tissue health that favours this approach; it is increasingly used but two-stage remains the standard for biofilm-forming organisms. Antibiotics alone without surgery are not curative.
learningPoint: Two-stage revision is the standard for chronic TKA PJI (> 4–6 weeks symptoms); DAIR is reserved for acute infection (< 3–4 weeks) with well-fixed components and no sinus tract.

subtopic: Knee ligament anatomy and injury diagnosis
stem: Theme: Knee ligament injury diagnosis. For each clinical scenario select the single most likely diagnosis from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Anterior cruciate ligament rupture

options.label: B
options.text: Posterior cruciate ligament rupture

options.label: C
options.text: Medial collateral ligament sprain (grade II)

options.label: D
options.text: Posterolateral corner injury

options.label: E
options.text: Combined ACL and posterolateral corner injury

options.label: F
options.text: Knee dislocation with multiligament disruption

options.label: G
options.text: Lateral collateral ligament avulsion

options.label: H
options.text: Arcuate ligament complex injury

emiLeads.stem: A 22-year-old woman decelerates and pivots during netball. She hears a pop, the knee swells over two hours, and a haemarthrosis is confirmed on aspiration. Lachman test shows grade 2 laxity with a soft endpoint. Valgus and varus stress tests are negative at 0° and 30°.

A 35-year-old motorcyclist sustains a direct blow to the anterior tibia in a collision. He has a positive posterior drawer test (10 mm, firm endpoint), varus laxity at 30° of flexion, external rotation asymmetry of 12° at 30° on the dial test, and a reverse pivot shift. Anterior drawer and Lachman are negative.

A 40-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after a road traffic collision. His knee is grossly unstable in all planes, limb alignment is grossly abnormal, and dorsalis pedis pulse is absent. Radiographs show no fractures.

explanation: Lead 1: haemarthrosis + soft-endpoint Lachman with no collateral laxity = isolated ACL rupture. Lead 2: posterior drawer with firm endpoint (PCL intact or partial) but concurrent varus laxity and positive dial test localise injury to the posterolateral corner structures (FCL, popliteus, popliteofibular ligament) without cruciate involvement. Lead 3: gross multi-plane instability with absent pulse is a knee dislocation (multiligament disruption) until proven otherwise — vascular assessment is the immediate priority.

stem: Theme: Meniscal tear management. For each clinical scenario select the single most appropriate management strategy from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Arthroscopic partial meniscectomy

options.label: B
options.text: Arthroscopic meniscal repair (all-inside technique)

options.label: C
options.text: Supervised physiotherapy and activity modification

options.label: D
options.text: Meniscal allograft transplantation

options.label: E
options.text: Total meniscectomy

options.label: F
options.text: MRI-guided aspiration and corticosteroid injection

options.label: G
options.text: Arthroscopic repair combined with concurrent ACL reconstruction

options.label: H
options.text: Observation with repeat MRI at three months

emiLeads.stem: A 19-year-old female athlete has a traumatic medial knee injury during football training. MRI shows a 3 cm vertical longitudinal tear of the posterior horn of the medial meniscus in the red-red zone. The knee has full extension and no mechanical symptoms. ACL is intact.

A 45-year-old man has medial knee pain with intermittent locking episodes for four months. MRI shows a complex horizontal cleavage tear of the medial meniscus body and posterior horn extending into the white-white zone. There is no acute trauma, and the knee is otherwise structurally normal.

A 23-year-old rugby player undergoes ACL reconstruction. Intraoperative arthroscopy identifies a fresh bucket-handle tear of the medial meniscus that reduces anatomically with a probe and is situated in the red-white zone.

explanation: Lead 1: peripheral red-red zone tears in young patients have excellent healing potential and should be repaired to preserve meniscal tissue and protect the joint long-term. Lead 2: complex horizontal cleavage tears in the white-white (avascular) zone have negligible healing potential; partial meniscectomy removing the unstable fragment relieves mechanical symptoms. Lead 3: concomitant meniscal repair at the time of ACL reconstruction is strongly advocated because the haemarthrosis and growth factors released during bone tunnel preparation enhance healing; the ACL restores joint stability which further protects the repair.
references: NICE MTG guidance on meniscal repair

subtopic: Total knee arthroplasty — cause of pain
stem: Theme: Cause of pain after total knee arthroplasty. For each clinical scenario select the single most likely diagnosis from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Periprosthetic joint infection

options.label: B
options.text: Aseptic loosening

options.label: C
options.text: Patellofemoral maltracking with lateral patellar subluxation

options.label: D
options.text: Medial collateral ligament imbalance with instability

options.label: E
options.text: Stiffness due to arthrofibrosis

options.label: F
options.text: Tibial component malalignment with internal rotation

options.label: G
options.text: Stress fracture of the distal femur

options.label: H
options.text: Referred pain from ipsilateral hip osteoarthritis

emiLeads.stem: A 68-year-old woman has persistent anterior knee pain two years after TKA. The knee has full range of motion and no swelling. Radiographs show lateral tilt of the patella on a skyline view. CRP and ESR are normal. There is no radiolucency around the components.

A 71-year-old man has recurrent giving-way of his TKA knee at six months post-operatively, worse when descending stairs. He reports a feeling of the knee 'sliding'. Radiographs show the components are well positioned and fixed. CRP 4 mg/L. Joint aspiration: 900 cells/mm³, no growth on culture.

A 74-year-old woman has progressive aching pain in her TKA knee at rest and at night, worsening over six months from the three-year post-operative mark. CRP 28 mg/L, ESR 54 mm/hr. Radiographs show a progressive radiolucent line at the tibial cement-bone interface compared with the one-year film.

explanation: Lead 1: anterior knee pain with lateral patellar tilt and normal inflammatory markers two years post-TKA indicates patellofemoral maltracking (often related to component rotation or overstuffing) rather than infection or loosening. Lead 2: giving-way with a 'sliding' sensation and normal inflammatory markers at six months points to ligamentous imbalance — specifically MCL insufficiency producing medial-sided instability; there is no radiological or infective cause. Lead 3: elevated CRP and ESR with a progressive tibial radiolucent line beyond the honeymoon period (onset at three years) is chronic periprosthetic joint infection until proven otherwise; further aspiration/culture and staged revision planning are required.

stem: A 62-year-old right-handed plumber presents with a 6-month history of right shoulder pain and weakness on abduction. MRI confirms a 3 cm full-thickness supraspinatus tear with no retraction beyond the glenoid rim and preserved muscle bulk (Goutallier grade 1). He has failed 4 months of physiotherapy and subacromial steroid injection. Which is the most appropriate next management step?

options.label: A
options.text: Repeat MRI in 6 months to reassess tear size

options.label: B
options.text: Arthroscopic rotator cuff repair

options.label: C
options.text: Reverse total shoulder arthroplasty

options.label: D
options.text: Superior capsule reconstruction

options.label: E
options.text: Subacromial steroid injection repeated at 3-month intervals

explanation: A symptomatic full-thickness supraspinatus tear with good muscle quality (Goutallier 1) and failed conservative measures is a strong indication for arthroscopic repair. Repair is most durable when retraction is limited and fatty infiltration is low. Reverse arthroplasty and superior capsule reconstruction are reserved for irreparable or massive tears with arthropathy. Repeated injections beyond an initial course are not recommended as they risk further cuff degeneration.
learningPoint: Arthroscopic repair is indicated for full-thickness supraspinatus tears failing conservative treatment when muscle quality is good (Goutallier 0-1).

stem: A 45-year-old office worker presents with right shoulder pain on overhead activity. Examination reveals a painful arc between 60° and 120° of abduction, positive Hawkins-Kennedy sign, and normal rotator cuff power. Plain radiographs show no abnormality. She has had no prior treatment. What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.label: A
options.text: Subacromial corticosteroid injection alone

options.label: B
options.text: Subacromial decompression via arthroscopy

options.label: C
options.text: Supervised physiotherapy focusing on rotator cuff and scapular stabilisation

options.label: D
options.text: MRI of the shoulder and subsequent management based on findings

options.label: E
options.text: Oral prednisolone 30 mg daily for 2 weeks

explanation: Current evidence (including the UK CSAW trial) supports supervised physiotherapy as the first-line treatment for subacromial impingement, with outcomes equivalent to arthroscopic decompression. Corticosteroid injection may provide short-term benefit but is not the primary initial treatment in isolation. Arthroscopic decompression should not precede an adequate trial of conservative therapy. Oral prednisolone is not indicated here.
learningPoint: Supervised physiotherapy targeting rotator cuff strengthening and scapular control is first-line management for subacromial impingement syndrome.

stem: A 22-year-old rugby prop has had three anterior shoulder dislocations over 18 months, all reduced in the emergency department. CT-arthrogram demonstrates a Bankart lesion with 20% anterior glenoid bone loss and an engaging Hill-Sachs defect. Examination reveals a positive load-and-shift test and apprehension at 90° abduction and external rotation. Which operative procedure addresses both the glenoid bone loss and the engaging Hill-Sachs most effectively?

options.label: A
options.text: Arthroscopic Bankart repair alone

options.label: B
options.text: Open Bankart repair with capsular shift

options.label: C
options.text: Latarjet coracoid transfer procedure

options.label: D
options.text: Eden-Hybinette free iliac crest bone graft

options.label: E
options.text: Remplissage with arthroscopic Bankart repair

explanation: With 20% glenoid bone loss and an engaging Hill-Sachs, the Latarjet procedure is the preferred option in contact sport athletes. It restores the glenoid arc, provides a dynamic sling effect from the conjoined tendon, and simultaneously addresses the engaging Hill-Sachs by extending the anterior arc of contact. Arthroscopic Bankart repair alone has unacceptably high recurrence rates beyond 15% glenoid loss. Remplissage is an option for an off-track Hill-Sachs with minimal bone loss. Eden-Hybinette is an alternative where coracoid is unavailable.
learningPoint: Latarjet coracoid transfer is the procedure of choice for anterior instability with significant (≥15-20%) glenoid bone loss in contact athletes.

stem: A 28-year-old competitive weightlifter reports posterior shoulder pain and a sensation of 'giving way' during the bench press and push-press. Examination reveals posterior joint line tenderness and a positive posterior load-and-shift test. MRI shows a posterior labral tear without reverse Hill-Sachs lesion or posterior glenoid bone loss. He has not tried structured rehabilitation. What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.label: A
options.text: Arthroscopic posterior labral repair immediately

options.label: B
options.text: Posterior bone block procedure

options.label: C
options.text: Minimum 6 months of supervised physiotherapy emphasising posterior capsule stretching and scapular strengthening

options.label: D
options.text: Posterior capsular plication alone via open approach

options.label: E
options.text: Avoidance of all overhead activity for 12 months

explanation: Posterior shoulder instability, particularly without significant bony deficit, responds well to structured rehabilitation in a substantial proportion of patients. At least 6 months of physiotherapy addressing posterior capsule flexibility and periscapular muscle control should be trialled before surgery is considered. Arthroscopic posterior labral repair is appropriate when conservative measures fail. Posterior bone block is reserved for significant posterior glenoid bone loss.
learningPoint: Posterior shoulder instability without bone loss should have at least 6 months of structured rehabilitation before surgical intervention is considered.

stem: A 74-year-old woman sustains a proximal humerus fracture after a fall. Radiographs show a 4-part fracture with significant displacement and head-splitting involvement. She is physiologically fit with good pre-injury function and bone quality on DXA (T-score -1.8). Which management option gives the most predictable functional outcome?

options.label: A
options.text: Collar-and-cuff immobilisation with early physiotherapy

options.label: B
options.text: Open reduction and internal fixation with a locking plate

options.label: C
options.text: Hemiarthroplasty

options.label: D
options.text: Reverse total shoulder arthroplasty

options.label: E
options.text: Intramedullary humeral nail

explanation: In elderly patients with a displaced 4-part or head-splitting proximal humerus fracture, reverse total shoulder arthroplasty (rTSA) provides more reliable functional outcomes than hemiarthroplasty, which depends on tuberosity healing that is unpredictable in older bone. ORIF with a locking plate carries a high rate of complications including avascular necrosis and screw cut-out in osteopaenic bone. Non-operative management is appropriate for undisplaced or minimally displaced fractures. The PROFHER trial informed non-operative vs. surgical decision-making for 2- and 3-part fractures but not specifically 4-part head-splitting injuries.
learningPoint: Reverse TSA is preferred over hemiarthroplasty for displaced 4-part or head-splitting proximal humerus fractures in physiologically fit elderly patients due to more predictable functional recovery.

stem: A 30-year-old male cyclist falls onto his right shoulder. He presents with localised AC joint tenderness and a visible step deformity. Radiographs show a coracoclavicular distance of 18 mm on the injured side compared to 12 mm on the contralateral side (50% increase). Stress views confirm no improvement with weight-bearing. According to the Rockwood classification, which type is this, and what is the standard initial management?

options.label: A
options.text: Rockwood type II — operative reconstruction

options.label: B
options.text: Rockwood type III — sling immobilisation and physiotherapy

options.label: C
options.text: Rockwood type III — immediate surgical stabilisation

options.label: D
options.text: Rockwood type V — sling immobilisation and physiotherapy

options.label: E
options.text: Rockwood type IV — closed manipulation and casting

explanation: A 50% increase in coracoclavicular distance (from 12 to 18 mm represents exactly a 50% increase) is consistent with a Rockwood type III injury, in which both AC and CC ligaments are disrupted but the deltotrapezial fascia remains intact. Consensus guidelines support initial non-operative management for type III injuries; the majority of patients achieve satisfactory function with sling immobilisation and physiotherapy. Operative intervention is reserved for chronic symptomatic cases or high-demand athletes after failed conservative treatment. Types IV, V, and VI have additional displacement or periosteal disruption and generally require surgery.
learningPoint: Rockwood type III AC joint injuries are best managed initially with sling immobilisation and rehabilitation; surgery is reserved for failure of conservative treatment.

stem: A 52-year-old woman with well-controlled type 2 diabetes presents with an 8-week history of progressive right shoulder pain and stiffness. Active and passive external rotation at the side is reduced to 10° (contralateral 55°). Forward flexion is limited to 80°. There is no history of trauma. Plain radiograph is normal. Which statement best describes the natural course of her condition?

options.label: A
options.text: The condition is self-limiting but can take 1-3 years to fully resolve, with some patients retaining mild residual restriction

options.label: B
options.text: Without intervention, permanent severe stiffness is the expected outcome

options.label: C
options.text: Full painless recovery invariably occurs within 6 months

options.label: D
options.text: The condition progresses inexorably in diabetic patients and surgical release is always required

options.label: E
options.text: Night pain resolves first, followed by loss of external rotation, then loss of abduction

explanation: Adhesive capsulitis (frozen shoulder) is a self-limiting condition that typically evolves through freezing, frozen, and thawing phases over a period of 12 to 36 months. The majority of patients recover satisfactorily, though a minority—especially those with diabetes—may retain mild residual restriction. It is not invariably permanent or invariably complete in its resolution. Diabetic patients have higher rates and slower resolution but still generally improve. The symptom sequence is not as described in option E.
learningPoint: Adhesive capsulitis is self-limiting over 1-3 years; most patients recover well, though diabetics have a longer and more symptomatic course.

stem: A 40-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with acute severe right shoulder pain of 2 hours' duration, following no specific injury. She is unable to lift the arm. A plain radiograph reveals a large calcific deposit overlying the supraspinatus tendon. She is systemically well. What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Urgent surgical needling and washout under general anaesthetic

options.label: B
options.text: Oral non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug, application of ice, and early physiotherapy

options.label: C
options.text: Intravenous morphine and hospital admission for 48 hours

options.label: D
options.text: Extracorporeal shock wave therapy the same day

options.label: E
options.text: Immediate ultrasound-guided aspiration of the calcific deposit

explanation: Acute calcific tendinitis presents with intense pain due to resorption of the calcium deposit. Initial management is analgesia with NSAIDs (if not contraindicated), ice, and encouragement of gentle movement. Most episodes settle within days to weeks. Ultrasound-guided needling is an elective procedure for chronic or recurrent symptomatic disease unresponsive to conservative measures, not an emergency intervention. ESWT is likewise a planned outpatient procedure. Hospital admission is not warranted for an otherwise well patient.
learningPoint: Acute calcific tendinitis is managed with NSAIDs and physiotherapy; ultrasound-guided needling and ESWT are reserved for chronic refractory cases.

stem: A 38-year-old overhead throwing athlete describes deep anterior shoulder pain that is worst during the late-cocking phase of throwing. The Speed test is weakly positive, and the O'Brien active compression test is positive with the thumb pointing downward but negative when the palm faces up. MRI arthrogram demonstrates contrast undermining the superior labrum from anterior to posterior with no cuff tear. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.label: A
options.text: Subscapularis full-thickness tear

options.label: B
options.text: SLAP type II lesion

options.label: C
options.text: Anterior Bankart lesion

options.label: D
options.text: Subacromial bursitis

options.label: E
options.text: Posterior labral tear

explanation: The combination of overhead athletic activity, late-cocking phase pain, positive O'Brien test (thumb-down positive, palm-up negative), and MRI arthrogram showing contrast tracking beneath the superior labrum from anterior to posterior is characteristic of a SLAP type II lesion — a detachment of the superior labrum and biceps anchor from the supraglenoid tubercle. A Bankart lesion involves the anteroinferior labrum. Subscapularis tears produce different clinical findings (lift-off, belly-press tests). Subacromial bursitis does not produce these arthrographic findings.
learningPoint: SLAP type II lesion presents in overhead athletes with late-cocking pain, positive O'Brien test, and MRI arthrogram showing superior labral detachment.

stem: A 71-year-old man with a long-standing massive irreparable rotator cuff tear presents with painful pseudoparalysis — he cannot actively elevate beyond 40° though passive movement is nearly full. Radiographs show superior humeral head migration with erosion of the greater tuberosity and acetabularisation of the undersurface of the coracoacromial arch (Hamada grade 3). He has tried 12 months of conservative treatment. Which surgical option is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Arthroscopic subacromial decompression and debridement

options.label: B
options.text: Conventional anatomical total shoulder arthroplasty

options.label: C
options.text: Reverse total shoulder arthroplasty

options.label: D
options.text: Latissimus dorsi tendon transfer

options.label: E
options.text: Superior capsule reconstruction using dermal allograft

explanation: Reverse TSA is the definitive treatment for cuff tear arthropathy presenting with pseudoparalysis (Hamada grade 3+). By medialisng the centre of rotation it allows the deltoid to generate elevation, compensating for the absent rotator cuff force couple. Anatomical TSA is contraindicated because an intact cuff is required for the glenoid component to function without rapid loosening. Arthroscopic debridement may provide temporary pain relief but does not restore elevation. Latissimus dorsi transfer and superior capsule reconstruction are options for irreparable tears without arthritis but not at Hamada grade 3.
learningPoint: Reverse TSA is the treatment of choice for cuff tear arthropathy with pseudoparalysis; it uses the deltoid to restore active elevation independently of the rotator cuff.

stem: A 25-year-old male triathlete sustains a mid-shaft clavicle fracture after a cycling fall. Radiographs show 100% displacement, 2 cm of shortening, and comminution of a middle-third fragment. He is keen to return to competitive sport as soon as possible. Which management approach optimises functional recovery in this patient?

options.label: A
options.text: Figure-of-eight bandage for 6 weeks followed by physiotherapy

options.label: B
options.text: Broad arm sling for 6 weeks with no fixation

options.label: C
options.text: Primary plate fixation with a pre-contoured clavicle plate

options.label: D
options.text: Intramedullary elastic nail fixation

options.label: E
options.text: External fixator application

explanation: Meta-analyses of randomised trials (including the Canadian Orthopaedic Trauma Society study) show that operative fixation of displaced mid-shaft clavicle fractures with significant shortening reduces non-union, malunion, and time to return to sport compared to conservative treatment. Pre-contoured plate fixation provides stable fixation allowing early mobilisation. In a young competitive athlete with 100% displacement and 2 cm shortening, primary plate fixation is justified. Intramedullary nailing is an option but provides less rotational control and has higher implant prominence. Figure-of-eight bandage offers no superiority over sling.
learningPoint: Displaced mid-shaft clavicle fractures with significant shortening (>2 cm) in active patients have lower non-union rates and faster return to sport with primary plate fixation.

stem: A 55-year-old woman presents after a fall with pain over the lateral clavicle. Radiographs show a distal clavicle fracture with the fracture line medial to the coracoclavicular ligaments. The coracoclavicular distance is markedly increased compared to the contralateral side. What Neer type is this, and what is the clinical implication?

options.label: A
options.text: Neer type I — stable, managed non-operatively

options.label: B
options.text: Neer type IIA — both CC ligaments are intact; stable fracture; managed non-operatively

options.label: C
options.text: Neer type IIB — one or both CC ligaments disrupted; unstable fracture with higher non-union risk

options.label: D
options.text: Neer type III — intra-articular AC joint fracture; operative fixation required

options.label: E
options.text: Neer type V — periosteal sleeve fracture; conservative management appropriate

explanation: Neer type IIB distal clavicle fractures occur medial to the intact coracoid ligament with disruption of the trapezoid ligament (and sometimes the conoid), rendering the proximal fragment unstable and the fracture at high risk of non-union (reported up to 22-30%). The increased CC distance on radiograph reflects ligamentous disruption. Type IIA has both ligaments attached to the distal fragment, making it more stable. The increased non-union risk in type IIB means operative fixation is frequently recommended, particularly in active patients.
learningPoint: Neer type IIB distal clavicle fractures are unstable with high non-union rates due to CC ligament disruption; operative management is often indicated.

stem: A 19-year-old male presents to the emergency department after a direct blow to the anterior chest during a rugby scrum. He complains of central chest pain, mild dysphagia, and a hoarse voice. Examination reveals asymmetry over the medial clavicle with the medial end appearing to be behind the sternum. Oxygen saturations are 97%. What is the most important next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Attempted closed reduction in the emergency department using sedation

options.label: B
options.text: CT scan of the chest and sternoclavicular joints before any reduction attempt

options.label: C
options.text: Immediate anterior thoracotomy

options.label: D
options.text: Immobilisation in a broad arm sling and physiotherapy referral

options.label: E
options.text: MRI of the sternoclavicular joint

explanation: Posterior sternoclavicular dislocation is a limb- and life-threatening injury. The medial clavicle can compress mediastinal structures including the trachea, oesophagus, and great vessels. Dysphagia and hoarseness indicate mediastinal compression. CT of the chest should be obtained immediately to confirm posterior dislocation and assess vascular injury before any reduction attempt. Closed reduction should be performed with cardiothoracic surgical backup available. Blind reduction attempts in the emergency department without imaging and surgical standby are hazardous.
learningPoint: Posterior sternoclavicular dislocation causing mediastinal symptoms requires CT chest first and reduction only with cardiothoracic surgical support ready.

stem: A 35-year-old male beach volleyball player presents with posterior shoulder pain, weakness on external rotation, and wasting of the infraspinatus. MRI shows an isolated ganglion cyst at the spinoglenoid notch. EMG confirms denervation of infraspinatus with normal supraspinatus function. Which statement correctly describes the anatomy explaining isolated infraspinatus involvement?

options.label: A
options.text: A lesion at the suprascapular notch affects only the infraspinatus branch

options.label: B
options.text: The suprascapular nerve passes through the spinoglenoid notch before supplying the infraspinatus, so a cyst here spares the supraspinatus branch which has already departed

options.label: C
options.text: The infraspinatus receives its motor supply from the axillary nerve at the quadrilateral space

options.label: D
options.text: A lesion at the spinoglenoid notch typically causes combined supraspinatus and infraspinatus wasting

options.label: E
options.text: The spinoglenoid notch is medial to the suprascapular notch in the course of the nerve

explanation: The suprascapular nerve enters the supraspinous fossa via the suprascapular notch (under the transverse scapular ligament), supplies the supraspinatus, then continues around the lateral scapular spine through the spinoglenoid notch to supply the infraspinatus. A ganglion cyst at the spinoglenoid notch compresses the nerve distal to the supraspinatus branch, resulting in isolated infraspinatus denervation and wasting, as the supraspinatus branch has already been given off. This anatomy explains why spinoglenoid lesions spare the supraspinatus.
learningPoint: Spinoglenoid notch compression of the suprascapular nerve causes isolated infraspinatus palsy; supraspinatus is spared because its branch is given off more proximally at the suprascapular notch.

stem: A 24-year-old woman notices prominence of her right scapula after a viral illness 3 months ago. On examination, pushing against a wall causes medial winging of the right scapula with the inferior angle rotating medially. Serratus anterior strength testing confirms weakness. Which nerve and its injury level best explains this presentation?

options.label: A
options.text: Spinal accessory nerve (CN XI) — injury at the posterior triangle of the neck

options.label: B
options.text: Long thoracic nerve — injury along its course on the lateral thoracic wall

options.label: C
options.text: Dorsal scapular nerve — injury at the scalenus medius

options.label: D
options.text: Thoracodorsal nerve — injury in the axilla

options.label: E
options.text: Lower trunk of the brachial plexus — injury at the thoracic outlet

explanation: Long thoracic nerve palsy causes serratus anterior weakness, manifesting as medial winging — the inferior angle of the scapula rotates medially when the arm is pushed forward against resistance. The long thoracic nerve (C5-C7) is vulnerable to traction, viral neuritis, or direct injury along the lateral chest wall. Spinal accessory nerve palsy causes trapezius weakness, producing a different pattern of lateral winging on shoulder abduction. Dorsal scapular nerve injury affects rhomboids and levator scapulae, producing a subtler winging. Thoracodorsal nerve injury does not cause winging.
learningPoint: Long thoracic nerve palsy causes serratus anterior weakness and medial scapular winging; the inferior angle rotates medially when pushing against a wall.

stem: A 58-year-old former professional rower presents with persistent localised pain at the acromioclavicular joint unresponsive to 4 months of physiotherapy and two ultrasound-guided corticosteroid injections. Cross-body adduction and the O'Brien test reproduce the pain. Radiographs confirm degenerative AC joint changes with inferior osteophytes but the glenohumeral joint is normal. What is the most appropriate next management step?

options.label: A
options.text: Glenohumeral joint injection under ultrasound guidance

options.label: B
options.text: MRI of the shoulder to exclude a rotator cuff tear

options.label: C
options.text: Arthroscopic distal clavicle excision (Mumford procedure)

options.label: D
options.text: Total shoulder arthroplasty

options.label: E
options.text: Open AC joint reconstruction with ligament transfer

explanation: Isolated AC joint osteoarthritis that has failed conservative measures including physiotherapy and corticosteroid injection is appropriately treated with arthroscopic distal clavicle excision (Mumford procedure). This reliably reduces mechanical impingement at the AC joint. Total shoulder arthroplasty addresses glenohumeral disease, which is absent here. AC joint reconstruction with ligaments is indicated for instability, not isolated OA. A glenohumeral injection would not address AC joint pathology that is clinically and radiographically confirmed.
learningPoint: Arthroscopic distal clavicle excision (Mumford procedure) is the surgical treatment of choice for isolated AC joint OA refractory to conservative management.

stem: A 67-year-old woman presents with a visible 'Popeye' deformity of her right arm following a pop when lifting a heavy shopping bag. She has mild bruising at the anterior arm but minimal pain now and good elbow flexion strength. Her shoulder examination reveals a full-thickness supraspinatus tear already known on MRI. Which statement best guides management of the proximal long head of biceps rupture in isolation?

options.label: A
options.text: Emergency surgical repair of the proximal biceps tendon is mandatory within 72 hours

options.label: B
options.text: Non-operative management is appropriate; she will retain satisfactory function with a small loss of supination strength and the cosmetic deformity

options.label: C
options.text: Biceps tenodesis is mandatory to prevent further rotator cuff damage

options.label: D
options.text: Biceps tenotomy should be performed urgently to prevent tendon retraction

options.label: E
options.text: She will lose more than 50% of elbow flexion strength without surgical repair

explanation: Proximal long head of biceps tendon rupture in an older, low-demand patient is best managed non-operatively. The short head of biceps remains intact and accounts for the majority of biceps function, so elbow flexion strength is only modestly reduced (approximately 20%) and supination strength by around 30%. The Popeye deformity is permanent but does not impair function significantly. Surgical repair (tenodesis or tenotomy) may be considered in younger, high-demand patients or when operated alongside cuff repair, but is not mandated in this scenario.
learningPoint: Proximal LHB rupture in older low-demand patients is treated non-operatively; the short head preserves most elbow flexion and strength loss is modest.

stem: Theme: Surgical procedures for shoulder instability. For each clinical scenario select the single most appropriate surgical procedure from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Arthroscopic Bankart repair

options.label: B
options.text: Latarjet coracoid transfer

options.label: C
options.text: Remplissage (posterior capsulodesis into Hill-Sachs)

options.label: D
options.text: Arthroscopic posterior labral repair

options.label: E
options.text: Open capsular shift (posterior)

options.label: F
options.text: Eden-Hybinette free iliac crest bone graft

options.label: G
options.text: Putti-Platt subscapularis overlap procedure

options.label: H
options.text: Reverse Hill-Sachs impaction grafting (McLaughlin procedure)

emiLeads.stem: A 20-year-old first-time anterior dislocator who is a non-contact athlete. CT arthrogram shows a soft-tissue Bankart lesion with 8% anterior glenoid bone loss. No Hill-Sachs defect. He has failed physiotherapy.
A 26-year-old rugby hooker with recurrent anterior dislocations. CT shows 25% anterior glenoid bone loss and an engaging on-track Hill-Sachs. Prior arthroscopic Bankart repair failed 18 months ago.

A 32-year-old male presents with posterior shoulder instability after two posterior dislocations. CT shows no posterior glenoid bone loss and the posterior labrum is torn. He has completed 9 months of posterior capsule rehabilitation without success.
A 55-year-old man sustains a posterior shoulder dislocation after a seizure. On reduction, CT reveals a large reverse Hill-Sachs defect occupying 35% of the humeral head articular surface.

explanation: Lead 1: Isolated soft-tissue Bankart with <15% bone loss in a non-contact athlete is well suited to arthroscopic Bankart repair with good recurrence rates. Lead 2: Recurrent anterior instability with >20% bone loss and a prior failed Bankart repair demands a bony procedure; Latarjet is preferred over Eden-Hybinette in the UK because coracoid transfer simultaneously provides a dynamic sling. Lead 3: Posterior labral tear without bone loss failing rehabilitation is treated with arthroscopic posterior labral repair. Lead 4: A large reverse Hill-Sachs (>25% articular surface) after posterior dislocation requires the McLaughlin subscapularis or infraspinatus tendon transfer/bone grafting into the defect to prevent re-dislocation.
references: Burkhart & De Beer glenoid bone loss guidelines

stem: Theme: Neer classification and management of proximal humerus fractures. For each scenario select the single most appropriate Neer classification or management from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Neer 1-part (minimally displaced) — collar-and-cuff + early mobilisation

options.label: B
options.text: Neer 2-part surgical neck — closed reduction and intramedullary nail

options.label: C
options.text: Neer 2-part greater tuberosity — operative fixation if displaced >5 mm

options.label: D
options.text: Neer 3-part — open reduction and locking plate fixation

options.label: E
options.text: Neer 4-part valgus impacted — consider operative fixation with preservation of head

options.label: F
options.text: Neer 4-part displaced — reverse total shoulder arthroplasty in elderly low-demand patient

options.label: G
options.text: Neer 4-part displaced — open reduction and locking plate fixation in a young patient

options.label: H
options.text: Head-splitting fracture — primary anatomical total shoulder arthroplasty

emiLeads.stem: An 80-year-old woman falls in a care home. Radiographs show a comminuted 4-part proximal humerus fracture with complete head displacement and varus angulation. She lives independently with a walking frame and has good pre-injury function. Her bone quality is poor.

A 35-year-old motorcyclist sustains a proximal humerus fracture. Radiographs show a 2-part greater tuberosity fracture displaced 8 mm posterosuperiorly. He is right-hand dominant and an active tradesman.
A 72-year-old man has radiographs showing a 4-part proximal humerus fracture with valgus impaction of the humeral head. The medial calcar is intact. He is fit with good pre-injury function.

explanation: Lead 1: Displaced 4-part fractures in elderly patients with poor bone quality have high ORIF complication rates; rTSA provides reliable pain relief and function. Lead 2: Greater tuberosity displacement of >5 mm is associated with persistent pain and restricted external rotation if left unreduced; operative fixation is indicated, especially in dominant-arm manual workers. Lead 3: Valgus-impacted 4-part fractures carry a lower avascular necrosis risk than widely displaced ones because the medial calcar hinge preserves some head vascularity, making head-preserving ORIF a reasonable option in physiologically fit patients.

stem: Theme: Nerve injuries around the shoulder. For each clinical scenario select the single most likely nerve injury from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Axillary nerve

options.label: B
options.text: Long thoracic nerve

options.label: C
options.text: Spinal accessory nerve (CN XI)

options.label: D
options.text: Suprascapular nerve — suprascapular notch level

options.label: E
options.text: Suprascapular nerve — spinoglenoid notch level

options.label: F
options.text: Musculocutaneous nerve

options.label: G
options.text: Dorsal scapular nerve

options.label: H
options.text: Thoracodorsal nerve

emiLeads.stem: A 45-year-old man undergoes a left-sided modified radical neck dissection for squamous cell carcinoma. Post-operatively he develops drooping of the left shoulder, difficulty abducting the arm beyond 90°, and lateral winging of the scapula that is most prominent when the arm is abducted in the coronal plane.
A 32-year-old volleyball player presents with posterior shoulder pain, weakness on external rotation only, and isolated infraspinatus wasting. MRI shows a ganglion cyst at the spinoglenoid notch.

A 20-year-old sustains an anterior glenohumeral dislocation reduced in the emergency department. Immediately after reduction he reports paraesthesia over a 'badge' area of the lateral upper arm and weakness lifting the arm away from his side. Deltoid bulk is reduced on the affected side.

explanation: Lead 1: The spinal accessory nerve traverses the posterior cervical triangle and is vulnerable during neck dissection. Its injury causes trapezius palsy with drooping shoulder and lateral winging maximal on arm abduction. Lead 2: Spinoglenoid notch level suprascapular nerve compression spares the supraspinatus (whose branch has already been given off) and causes isolated infraspinatus palsy — confirmed by selective wasting and external rotation weakness. Lead 3: Axillary nerve injury is the most common neurological complication of anterior shoulder dislocation, causing deltoid weakness and sensory loss over the lateral shoulder (regimental badge area).
references: Anatomy (axillary nerve, suprascapular nerve, spinal accessory)

stem: A 38-year-old man presents at 02:00 with a 3-day history of severe low back pain radiating to both legs. He reports difficulty initiating micturition and reduced perineal sensation noticed in the shower. Bladder scan shows 480 mL residual urine. MRI lumbar spine reveals a large L4/5 central disc prolapse causing complete cauda equina compression. What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.label: A
options.text: Arrange urgent MRI of the whole spine to exclude multilevel disease before operating

options.label: B
options.text: Administer high-dose intravenous dexamethasone and reassess in 6 hours

options.label: C
options.text: Proceed to emergency decompressive surgery within the same operating session

options.label: D
options.text: Insert a urinary catheter and list for elective microdiscectomy within 2 weeks

options.label: E
options.text: Commence physiotherapy and reassess neurological status in the morning

explanation: Cauda equina syndrome with urinary retention (complete CES-R) requires emergency surgical decompression, ideally within 24 hours, to maximise chance of bladder and bowel recovery. The imaging is already diagnostic; delaying for further imaging or steroids is not justified. Catheterisation alone addresses the retention but does not treat the underlying compression. Physiotherapy has no role in the acute setting.
learningPoint: CES with urinary retention demands emergency decompression — do not delay for additional imaging or a steroid trial.
references: NICE Clinical Knowledge Summary: Cauda equina syndrome

stem: A 67-year-old retired teacher reports a 9-month history of progressive clumsy hands, difficulty doing up shirt buttons, and a wide-based unsteady gait. She has no bladder symptoms. Examination reveals hyperreflexia at the knees and ankles, positive Hoffman's sign bilaterally, and a mJOA score of 13/18. MRI shows multilevel cervical spondylosis with cord signal change at C5/6. She is otherwise medically fit. What management is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Conservative treatment with physiotherapy and review in 6 months

options.label: B
options.text: Surgical decompression with or without fusion

options.label: C
options.text: Epidural steroid injection at C5/6

options.label: D
options.text: Cervical collar immobilisation for 12 weeks then reassess

options.label: E
options.text: Repeat MRI in 6 months to determine rate of progression before deciding on surgery

explanation: Moderate-to-severe cervical spondylotic myelopathy (mJOA ≤14) with cord signal change on MRI carries a significant risk of further neurological deterioration without intervention. Evidence from RCTs (including the AOSpine CSM-NA/International studies) demonstrates that surgical decompression halts progression and produces meaningful neurological recovery in the majority. Physiotherapy alone or conservative observation is appropriate only for mild, non-progressive disease. Epidural steroids and cervical collars have no proven disease-modifying role in established myelopathy.
learningPoint: Moderate CSM (mJOA ≤14) with cord signal change warrants surgical decompression; conservative management risks irreversible deterioration.

stem: A 24-year-old motorcyclist is admitted following a high-speed collision. He has complete loss of motor and sensory function below T10, including no anal sensation and no voluntary anal contraction. Sacral sensation (S4–5 dermatomes) is absent. The bulbocavernosus reflex is present. How is this injury classified on the ASIA Impairment Scale?

options.label: A
options.text: ASIA A

options.label: B
options.text: ASIA B

options.label: C
options.text: ASIA C

options.label: D
options.text: ASIA D

options.label: E
options.text: Cannot be classified until spinal shock has resolved

explanation: ASIA A denotes a complete spinal cord injury: no sensory or motor function is preserved in sacral segments S4–S5, including no anal sensation, no light touch at the anal mucocutaneous junction, and no voluntary anal sphincter contraction. The return of the bulbocavernosus reflex confirms that spinal shock has ended, making classification reliable. ASIA B would require preservation of sacral sensory function with absent motor function. The injury level is T10.
learningPoint: ASIA A = complete injury: no S4–S5 sensory or motor function; the returned bulbocavernosus reflex confirms spinal shock has resolved and the grade is valid.
references: ASIA/ISCoS International Standards for Neurological Classification of SCI 2019
Miller's Review of Orthopaedics (spinal cord injury)

stem: A 35-year-old construction worker falls 4 metres and sustains an L1 burst fracture. CT shows 45% vertebral body height loss, 40% canal compromise, intact posterior ligamentous complex (no interspinous widening, no facet joint distraction on MRI). He has full motor power and normal sensation in all limbs. Using the TLICS scoring system, what is his score and the recommended management?

options.label: A
options.text: TLICS 1 — non-operative management in a thoracolumbar orthosis

options.label: B
options.text: TLICS 2 — non-operative management in a thoracolumbar orthosis

options.label: C
options.text: TLICS 4 — either operative or non-operative management acceptable

options.label: D
options.text: TLICS 5 — surgical stabilisation recommended

options.label: E
options.text: TLICS 7 — urgent surgical decompression and stabilisation required

explanation: TLICS scores three domains: injury morphology (burst = 2), posterior ligamentous complex integrity (intact = 0), and neurological status (intact = 0), giving a total of 2. A score ≤3 supports non-operative management. Although the canal compromise is significant, neurological status is intact and the PLC is preserved; TLICS does not penalise for canal percentage alone. A score of 4 represents the equipoise zone; 5 or above favours surgery.
learningPoint: TLICS = morphology + PLC + neurology; an intact PLC and intact neurology with a burst fracture scores 2, supporting non-operative treatment.
references: Vaccaro et al. TLICS classification, Spine 2005

stem: An 82-year-old woman with osteoporosis falls from standing height. She complains of neck pain and occipital headache. Plain radiographs are non-diagnostic. CT reveals a fracture through the base of the dens with 6 mm posterior displacement of the odontoid relative to the body of C2. She has no neurological deficit. What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Philadelphia collar for 12 weeks with serial radiographs

options.label: B
options.text: Halo vest immobilisation for 12 weeks

options.label: C
options.text: Anterior odontoid screw fixation

options.label: D
options.text: Posterior C1–C2 arthrodesis

options.label: E
options.text: Rigid cervicothoracic orthosis and outpatient physiotherapy

explanation: This is an Anderson and D'Alonzo Type II odontoid fracture — the pattern with the highest non-union rate, particularly in elderly patients with osteoporosis, posterior displacement >5 mm, and comminution. Non-union rates with halo vest immobilisation approach 20–40% in this population and are compounded by halo-related complications in elderly patients. Anterior screw fixation is technically feasible only with an intact transverse ligament and a favourable fracture trajectory (anterior-to-posterior), neither of which may apply here. Posterior C1–C2 arthrodesis (e.g. Harms technique) offers reliable union and is the preferred surgical option.
learningPoint: Elderly Type II dens fracture with >5 mm displacement carries very high non-union risk with collar or halo alone; posterior C1–C2 fusion is preferred.

stem: A 34-year-old nurse presents with a 6-week history of right-sided low back pain and shooting pain down the right posterior thigh, lateral calf, and into the dorsum of the foot. She has 4/5 weakness of right foot dorsiflexion. Reflexes are intact. MRI confirms a right-sided posterolateral L4/5 disc prolapse compressing the right L5 nerve root. Conservative treatment with analgesia and physiotherapy has not produced improvement. Which nerve root territory matches her dorsiflexion weakness?

options.label: A
options.text: L3

options.label: B
options.text: L4

options.label: C
options.text: L5

options.label: D
options.text: S1

options.label: E
options.text: S2

explanation: The L5 nerve root supplies tibialis anterior (ankle dorsiflexion), extensor hallucis longus, and extensor digitorum longus; its cutaneous territory covers the lateral calf and dorsum of the foot. L4 compression produces knee extension weakness and an impaired knee jerk. S1 compresses to cause calf weakness, absent ankle jerk, and pain in the sole and posterior leg. The clinical picture here — dorsiflexion weakness, lateral calf and dorsal foot symptoms, and intact reflexes — is classical L5.
learningPoint: L5 root compression causes weakness of ankle dorsiflexion and extensor hallucis longus, with sensory loss over the lateral calf and dorsum of the foot; reflexes are typically intact.

stem: A 72-year-old retired postman presents with a 2-year history of bilateral leg pain, heaviness, and weakness brought on reliably after walking 200 metres, and relieved within 5 minutes of sitting or leaning forward over a shopping trolley. He has no pain at rest and peripheral pulses are intact. MRI shows severe multilevel lumbar canal stenosis from L2 to L5. Eighteen months of physiotherapy and one epidural steroid injection have provided no sustained benefit. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Repeat epidural steroid injection

options.label: B
options.text: Posterior lumbar interbody fusion L2–L5

options.label: C
options.text: Surgical decompression (laminectomy or laminotomy)

options.label: D
options.text: Ankle-brachial pressure index measurement and vascular surgery referral

options.label: E
options.text: Long-term opioid analgesia and mobility aids

explanation: The history is classic neurogenic claudication from lumbar canal stenosis: posture-dependent bilateral leg symptoms relieved by spinal flexion (sitting, leaning forward), distinguishing it from vascular claudication where the pain is calf-dominant and relieved by standing still. Intact pulses and a trolley sign make vascular disease unlikely. After failure of adequate conservative treatment (≥12 weeks physiotherapy plus injection), surgical decompression is indicated and produces sustained improvement in walking distance and quality of life. Fusion is reserved for cases with concomitant instability or degenerative spondylolisthesis with dynamic instability.
learningPoint: Neurogenic claudication from lumbar stenosis failing conservative measures warrants surgical decompression; fusion is not routinely added unless instability is present.
references: NICE NG59 (low back pain and sciatica)

stem: A 16-year-old county-level gymnast presents with a 4-month history of low back pain, worse with hyperextension and relieved by rest. Examination reveals paraspinal muscle tenderness and pain reproduced by the single-leg hyperextension test on the left. Plain radiographs show a defect in the left L5 pars interarticularis. MRI reveals marrow oedema at the pars without significant slip. What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.label: A
options.text: Immediate surgical repair of the pars defect

options.label: B
options.text: Activity modification, physiotherapy focusing on core stabilisation, and a trial off sport for 3–6 months

options.label: C
options.text: Rigid thoracolumbar orthosis for 12 months with no activity restrictions

options.label: D
options.text: Pedicle screw fixation and posterolateral fusion at L5/S1

options.label: E
options.text: Bone scan to establish fracture age, then continue gymnastics with a corset brace

explanation: An acute spondylolysis with bone marrow oedema (indicating biological activity) in an adolescent athlete should initially be managed non-operatively with relative rest, cessation of provocative sport, and a structured rehabilitation programme targeting core and lumbar stabilisers. Most acute stress reactions and early spondylolysis heal or become asymptomatic with this approach. Rigid bracing is optional and evidence of added benefit over activity modification alone is limited. Surgery is reserved for failure after 6 months of adequate conservative treatment or for high-grade slips.
learningPoint: Acute spondylolysis with marrow oedema in an adolescent athlete: first-line treatment is activity modification and core stabilisation, not immediate surgery or prolonged rigid bracing.

stem: A 62-year-old man with known prostate cancer presents to the emergency department with a 2-week history of progressive thoracic back pain, bilateral leg weakness (3/5 proximal), and difficulty voiding urine over the past 24 hours. MRI whole spine confirms epidural disease at T6 causing 70% cord compression with no bony instability. His ECOG performance status is 1. What is the most appropriate initial treatment according to NICE guidance?

options.label: A
options.text: Urgent radiotherapy to T6 alone, commencing within 24 hours

options.label: B
options.text: High-dose dexamethasone, followed by urgent surgical decompression then postoperative radiotherapy

options.label: C
options.text: High-dose dexamethasone and referral to a spine surgeon and oncologist for a multidisciplinary decision, aiming to treat within 24 hours

options.label: D
options.text: Systemic chemotherapy for hormone-refractory prostate cancer as the primary treatment

options.label: E
options.text: Palliative care referral and nursing management of bladder dysfunction

explanation: NICE guideline NG91 (Metastatic Spinal Cord Compression) mandates that patients with suspected MSCC receive high-dose dexamethasone immediately (16 mg loading dose) and that the case is discussed by a specialist multidisciplinary team within 24 hours to decide between surgery and radiotherapy. The Patchell trial showed that surgery followed by radiotherapy was superior to radiotherapy alone in ambulatory patients with a single level of compression, good performance status, and a predicted survival of at least 3 months. However, the decision requires MDT input; direct referral for radiotherapy alone, or to palliative care, without MDT discussion is not compliant with NICE guidance.
learningPoint: NICE NG91: start dexamethasone immediately in suspected MSCC and convene urgent MDT within 24 hours to decide surgery versus radiotherapy.

stem: A 58-year-old man with type 2 diabetes and a recent urinary tract infection presents with 3 weeks of severe thoracic back pain, fever (38.7°C), and elevated CRP (210 mg/L) and ESR (98 mm/hr). WBC is 15 × 10⁹/L. MRI shows T8/9 disc space destruction with adjacent vertebral end-plate irregularity and a small anterior epidural collection. He has no neurological deficit. Blood cultures are pending. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Start empirical broad-spectrum IV antibiotics immediately before culture results

options.label: B
options.text: Await blood culture results (up to 72 hours) before prescribing antibiotics to ensure a targeted regimen

options.label: C
options.text: Urgent surgical debridement and stabilisation given the epidural collection

options.label: D
options.text: CT-guided biopsy of the disc space, then await culture and sensitivity results before commencing antibiotics

options.label: E
options.text: Commence oral co-amoxiclav and arrange MRI follow-up in 6 weeks

explanation: In a neurologically intact patient with suspected spinal discitis and no sepsis or haemodynamic instability, the priority is to establish a microbiological diagnosis before committing to antibiotics, as empirical treatment substantially reduces culture yield and may condemn the patient to prolonged empirical treatment. CT-guided disc biopsy (or repeated blood cultures) should be performed promptly. If the patient deteriorates neurologically or becomes septic, antibiotics take precedence immediately. The small epidural collection without neurological compromise does not mandate emergency surgery at this stage. Oral antibiotics alone are insufficient for vertebral discitis.
learningPoint: In neurologically intact spinal discitis without sepsis, secure microbiological diagnosis (disc biopsy or blood cultures) before starting antibiotics to guide the prolonged treatment course.
references: British Spine Registry guidelines on spinal infection

stem: A 13-year-old girl is referred with a right thoracic curve measuring 34° Cobb angle on standing radiograph. Her Risser sign is 0 and she had her menarche 6 months ago. She has no back pain, normal pulmonary function, and no neurological signs. What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Observation and repeat radiograph in 12 months

options.label: B
options.text: Scoliosis-specific physiotherapy (PSSE/Schroth method) as the sole treatment

options.label: C
options.text: Full-time rigid thoracolumbar sacral orthosis (TLSO) bracing

options.label: D
options.text: Posterior spinal fusion

options.label: E
options.text: Anterior vertebral body tethering

explanation: The BrAIST RCT demonstrated that thoracolumbar bracing worn ≥18 hours per day significantly reduces the risk of curve progression to surgical threshold in skeletally immature adolescents with AIS curves of 25–40°. This patient has a 34° curve, Risser 0 (significant remaining growth), and recent menarche (early in the growth spurt), placing her at high progression risk. Observation alone is appropriate for curves under 25° or skeletally mature patients. Physiotherapy as sole treatment has insufficient evidence for curves in this range. Surgery is reserved for curves ≥45–50° or rapidly progressive curves. Vertebral body tethering is experimental and not standard practice.
learningPoint: AIS 25–45° with significant skeletal immaturity (Risser 0–2): full-time TLSO bracing is the evidence-based intervention to prevent progression to surgical threshold.
references: Weinstein et al. BrAIST trial, NEJM 2013

stem: A 68-year-old woman presents with a 5-year history of progressive low back pain and right-sided leg pain worse on walking, with a 30-metre walking tolerance. Standing radiographs show a 42° lumbar degenerative scoliosis with 4 cm coronal imbalance. Her sagittal profile shows loss of lumbar lordosis with a positive sagittal vertical axis of 9 cm. MRI demonstrates multilevel foraminal stenosis on the concave right side. She has failed 2 years of conservative treatment. What factor is the strongest predictor of a poor outcome if she undergoes deformity correction surgery?

options.label: A
options.text: Cobb angle greater than 40°

options.label: B
options.text: Age over 65 years

options.label: C
options.text: Positive sagittal vertical axis greater than 5 cm

options.label: D
options.text: Number of lumbar levels with foraminal stenosis

options.label: E
options.text: Female sex

explanation: Sagittal malalignment — specifically a positive sagittal vertical axis (SVA) — is the parameter most strongly associated with health-related quality of life impairment and with failure of surgical correction in adult spinal deformity. The SRS-Schwab classification and subsequent validation studies demonstrate that SVA >5 cm (and pelvic incidence minus lumbar lordosis mismatch >10°) are the key radiographic predictors of disability and revision risk. Coronal Cobb angle and age are important but secondary considerations. Sex is not an independent outcome predictor in adult deformity surgery.
learningPoint: Sagittal malalignment (SVA >5 cm) is the dominant predictor of disability and surgical outcome in adult degenerative scoliosis; restoration of sagittal balance is the primary goal of deformity surgery.
references: SRS-Schwab classification of adult spinal deformity

stem: A 71-year-old man with known ankylosing spondylitis falls from his bicycle. He presents with severe neck pain after a low-energy fall. His GCS is 15 and neurological examination reveals no deficit. Plain radiographs appear unremarkable. What is the single most important investigation to request?

options.label: A
options.text: MRI cervical spine with STIR sequences

options.label: B
options.text: CT cervical spine from occiput to T1

options.label: C
options.text: Bone scintigraphy to identify hyperaemia at fracture sites

options.label: D
options.text: Flexion-extension plain radiographs of the cervical spine

options.label: E
options.text: Serum CRP, ESR, and HLA-B27 testing to confirm disease activity

explanation: The ankylosed spine behaves mechanically as a long bone; even minor trauma can cause highly unstable fractures that run through the disc or vertebral body and are frequently missed on plain radiographs due to the already-abnormal bony architecture. CT of the entire cervical spine (occiput to T1 minimum) is the mandatory first-line investigation in this population after any significant pain following trauma. The BOAST guidelines for cervical spine injuries emphasise CT in all high-risk patients. Flexion-extension radiographs carry a risk of cord injury in an unstable fracture and are contraindicated before fracture has been excluded. MRI is complementary but CT identifies the bony disruption rapidly.
learningPoint: Any spinal pain after trauma in ankylosing spondylitis demands immediate CT of the relevant region; plain radiographs routinely miss fractures in the fused spine.

stem: A 76-year-old woman with a DEXA T-score of −3.2 at the lumbar spine presents with acute-onset severe thoracolumbar pain after bending to lift her shopping. CT confirms an acute T12 wedge fracture with 30% height loss and no posterior element involvement or neurological deficit. What pharmacological treatment should be commenced for her underlying bone disease?

options.label: A
options.text: Calcium and vitamin D supplementation alone

options.label: B
options.text: Alendronic acid 70 mg once weekly plus calcium and vitamin D

options.label: C
options.text: Teriparatide (recombinant PTH 1-34) subcutaneous daily injection

options.label: D
options.text: Strontium ranelate 2 g daily

options.label: E
options.text: Hormone replacement therapy

explanation: A fragility fracture in a patient with a T-score of −3.2 confirms established osteoporosis and mandates treatment. NICE TA161 and the NOGG guidelines support oral bisphosphonates (alendronic acid) as the first-line treatment for postmenopausal osteoporosis in the UK, with concurrent calcium and vitamin D supplementation. Teriparatide is reserved for patients with very low bone density who have experienced further fractures on bisphosphonate therapy or who cannot tolerate oral bisphosphonates. Strontium ranelate is no longer recommended by NICE due to cardiovascular risk. HRT is not used primarily for fracture prevention in this age group. Calcium and vitamin D alone are insufficient as the sole pharmacological intervention.
learningPoint: Following a fragility fracture with confirmed osteoporosis, first-line UK treatment is alendronic acid plus calcium and vitamin D (NICE TA161/NOGG); teriparatide is second-line.

stem: A 28-year-old rugby player sustains a tackle injury and presents with neck pain and bilateral upper limb paraesthesia that resolved within 2 hours. CT cervical spine reveals a unilateral C5/6 facet joint dislocation (perched facet) without fracture. MRI shows no disc herniation at that level and no cord signal change. What is the most appropriate sequence of treatment?

options.label: A
options.text: Cervical collar immobilisation for 6 weeks, then reassess with CT

options.label: B
options.text: Urgent closed reduction under sedation or general anaesthesia, followed by surgical posterior stabilisation

options.label: C
options.text: Urgent anterior cervical discectomy and fusion before any attempted reduction

options.label: D
options.text: Halo vest application and definitive management in 72 hours

options.label: E
options.text: Posterior foraminotomy at C5/6 without reduction of the facet joint

explanation: A unilateral facet dislocation is mechanically unstable and will not spontaneously reduce or stabilise in a collar. With no disc herniation on MRI (which is the critical exclusion — a herniated disc anterior to the spinal cord can be pushed further into the cord by closed reduction), urgent closed reduction under careful neurological monitoring is safe and appropriate. Following successful reduction, posterior surgical stabilisation is required due to disruption of the posterior ligamentous complex. If MRI had shown a herniated disc, anterior discectomy prior to reduction would be necessary. A halo vest does not reliably maintain reduction of a dislocated facet.
learningPoint: Subaxial facet dislocation: perform urgent MRI to exclude disc herniation, then proceed to closed reduction if the disc is clear; follow with posterior fixation for PLC disruption.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics (subaxial cervical trauma)

stem: A 29-year-old man presents with a 10-week history of left-sided leg pain radiating to the sole of the foot, associated with 4/5 weakness of plantar flexion and an absent left ankle jerk. He has no bladder or bowel symptoms. MRI lumbar spine shows a left postero-lateral L5/S1 disc prolapse compressing the left S1 nerve root. He has not improved with a course of NSAIDs and physiotherapy. Which treatment option has the most robust evidence for faster symptom resolution?

options.label: A
options.text: Microdiscectomy

options.label: B
options.text: Transforaminal epidural steroid injection at L5/S1

options.label: C
options.text: Intradiscal electrothermal therapy (IDET)

options.label: D
options.text: Transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation (TENS)

options.label: E
options.text: Prolonged bed rest for 4 weeks

explanation: Multiple RCTs and systematic reviews, including the Spine Patient Outcomes Research Trial (SPORT), confirm that microdiscectomy produces faster leg pain relief and functional improvement than continued non-operative treatment for lumbar disc prolapse with radiculopathy. However, long-term outcomes at 2 years are similar between surgical and non-surgical groups in patients without neurological deficit. With progressive motor weakness (4/5 plantar flexion) present for 10 weeks and failure of conservative treatment, microdiscectomy is the most appropriate next step. Epidural steroids can provide short-term pain relief but do not address motor deficit or produce durable outcomes equivalent to surgery in this scenario. IDET, TENS, and bed rest lack evidence for disc-related radiculopathy.
learningPoint: Microdiscectomy provides faster symptom relief than ongoing conservative treatment for lumbar disc prolapse with radiculopathy failing 6–12 weeks of non-operative management, particularly with progressive neurological deficit.

stem: A 45-year-old woman undergoes emergency L4/5 microdiscectomy for cauda equina syndrome with urinary retention. Six hours after surgery she reports complete inability to void urine, persisting perineal numbness, and absent anal tone. Pre-operatively she had 3/5 motor power in the right leg and partial saddle numbness. Post-operatively her right leg power has improved to 5/5 and her acute disc fragment was confirmed decompressed on intraoperative inspection. The surgeon wishes to counsel her on prognosis for bladder recovery. Which pre-operative finding most strongly predicts a worse bladder outcome?

options.label: A
options.text: Duration of urinary retention greater than 24 hours before surgery

options.label: B
options.text: Female sex

options.label: C
options.text: Pre-operative residual motor power in the lower limbs

options.label: D
options.text: Level of disc prolapse (L4/5 versus L5/S1)

options.label: E
options.text: Residual urine volume greater than 400 mL at presentation

explanation: The duration of urinary retention prior to surgical decompression is the strongest independent predictor of bladder recovery in CES-R. Multiple observational series and systematic reviews demonstrate that delays exceeding 24–48 hours from onset of retention to decompression are associated with significantly worse rates of complete bladder recovery, likely reflecting irreversible ischaemic damage to the sacral nerve roots. Pre-operative motor power in the limbs reflects motor root recovery potential and tends to recover better than bladder function regardless. Sex and disc level are not established independent predictors. A large residual volume reflects the severity of retention but is confounded by duration.
learningPoint: In cauda equina syndrome with retention, duration of retention before decompression is the dominant prognostic factor for bladder recovery — the longer the delay, the worse the outcome.
references: Todd NV, Dickson RA. BMJ review on CES prognosis

stem: Theme: ASIA Impairment Scale Classification. For each clinical scenario below, select the single most appropriate ASIA Impairment Scale grade from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: ASIA A — complete: no sensory or motor function in sacral segments S4–S5

options.label: B
options.text: ASIA B — sensory incomplete: sensory but not motor function preserved below the neurological level, including S4–S5

options.label: C
options.text: ASIA C — motor incomplete: motor function preserved below neurological level; more than half of key muscles below NLI grade <3

options.label: D
options.text: ASIA D — motor incomplete: motor function preserved below neurological level; at least half of key muscles below NLI grade ≥3

options.label: E
options.text: ASIA E — normal: sensory and motor function normal; patient may have spinal cord syndrome

options.label: F
options.text: Unclassifiable — spinal shock not yet resolved; bulbocavernosus reflex absent

emiLeads.stem: A 21-year-old man sustains a C5 cervical spine dislocation. Forty-eight hours later the bulbocavernosus reflex has returned. He has no movement or sensation below C5, no perianal sensation, and no voluntary anal contraction.

A 55-year-old woman suffers a central cord injury at C4. She has 4/5 power in both biceps and 3/5 in both wrist extensors. She can feel light touch at the perianal region and has voluntary anal sphincter contraction. Lower limbs have 2/5 power throughout. More than half of key muscles below C4 score below grade 3.

A 38-year-old man has a T6 burst fracture with epidural haematoma. Three days post-injury he has no motor function below T6 but can detect pinprick at the perianal skin and feels the urge to void urine. The bulbocavernosus reflex is present.

explanation: Lead 1: Absent sacral motor and sensory function with resolved spinal shock (BCR present) = ASIA A complete. Lead 2: Motor and sensory preserved below the NLI including S4–S5; key muscles below NLI present but majority <3 = ASIA C motor incomplete. Lead 3: No motor function below T6 but preserved sacral sensation (perianal pinprick + urge) with no motor = ASIA B sensory incomplete.
references: Miller's Review of Orthopaedics (spinal cord injury classification)

stem: Theme: Causative Organisms in Spinal Infection. For each clinical scenario below, select the single most likely causative organism from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Staphylococcus aureus

options.label: B
options.text: Mycobacterium tuberculosis

options.label: C
options.text: Brucella melitensis

options.label: D
options.text: Escherichia coli (gram-negative bacillus)

options.label: E
options.text: Candida albicans

options.label: F
options.text: Pseudomonas aeruginosa

options.label: G
options.text: Streptococcus milleri group

options.label: H
options.text: Coagulase-negative Staphylococcus (e.g. S. epidermidis)

emiLeads.stem: A 48-year-old Somali man presents with a 4-month history of insidious thoracic back pain, night sweats, and a 6 kg weight loss. MRI shows T8/9 disc space destruction, anterior vertebral body erosion forming a 'wedge', paraspinal soft-tissue mass, and no posterior element involvement. CXR shows apical calcification.

A 72-year-old woman develops lumbar discitis 6 weeks after treatment for a Klebsiella urinary tract infection. Blood cultures taken on admission to the spinal unit grow a gram-negative bacillus on extended antibiotic sensitivity testing consistent with a urinary source pathogen. The most common organism in haematogenous discitis originating from a urinary tract source is asked for.

A 35-year-old intravenous drug user presents with a 2-week history of lumbar pain, fever, and a developing paraparesis. MRI shows L2/3 discitis with a posterior epidural abscess. Blood cultures and disc aspirate both grow the same organism, which is the commonest cause of haematogenous pyogenic spinal infection.

explanation: Lead 1: Subacute onset, constitutional symptoms, immigrant background, apical CXR calcification, anterior vertebral destruction with preserved posterior elements and a paraspinal abscess are hallmarks of Pott's disease (spinal tuberculosis). Lead 2: Gram-negative discitis following urinary tract infection is most commonly caused by Escherichia coli or other Enterobacteriaceae that seed the disc haematogenously from the urinary tract. Lead 3: Staphylococcus aureus is responsible for approximately 50–60% of all pyogenic haematogenous spinal infections and is the organism most frequently isolated in IV drug users with spinal epidural abscess.
