references: British Infection Association guidelines on bone and joint infection

stem: Theme: Surgical Indications in Spinal Deformity. For each scenario, select the single most appropriate management strategy from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Observation with repeat standing radiograph in 4–6 months

options.label: B
options.text: Full-time rigid TLSO bracing (≥18 hours per day)

options.label: C
options.text: Posterior spinal fusion with instrumentation

options.label: D
options.text: Growing rod or magnetically controlled growing rod (MCGR) system

options.label: E
options.text: Vertebral body tethering (fusionless surgical correction)

options.label: F
options.text: Scoliosis-specific physiotherapy alone (Schroth/PSSE)

options.label: G
options.text: Anterior spinal release followed by posterior instrumented fusion

options.label: H
options.text: Casting (Mehta/Cotrel) followed by bracing

emiLeads.stem: A 5-year-old boy has progressive idiopathic early-onset scoliosis with a 42° curve confirmed on two radiographs 6 months apart. He is otherwise fit and healthy. The aim is to allow thoracic growth while controlling curve progression.
A 15-year-old boy has a right thoracic AIS curve of 22° Cobb angle. His Risser sign is 4 and he achieved his growth spurt 3 years ago. He is asymptomatic.

A 14-year-old girl has a right thoracic AIS curve of 52° Cobb angle, Risser sign 1, and has had menarche 8 months ago. She has failed a trial of bracing. She is neurologically intact with no pulmonary compromise.

explanation: Lead 1: Early-onset scoliosis (EOS) in a young child with a progressive curve: posterior spinal fusion would sacrifice thoracic height and lung growth. A growth-preserving system (MCGR or traditional growing rods) controls the curve while permitting thoracic development. Lead 2: A 22° curve in a near-skeletally-mature male (Risser 4, post-peak growth) has very low progression risk. No active intervention beyond clinical surveillance is warranted. Lead 3: AIS >50° in a skeletally immature teenager who has failed bracing carries a high risk of further progression in adulthood. Posterior instrumented spinal fusion is the standard of care at this stage.

stem: A 42-year-old male plasterer presents to the emergency department with sudden antecubital pain after lifting a heavy bag of plaster. He felt a 'pop' and noticed immediate weakness. On examination there is bruising in the antecubital fossa, a palpable gap just proximal to the radial tuberosity, and a positive hook test. Grip strength is preserved but forearm supination is markedly weak. Which of the following best describes the most appropriate definitive management?

options.label: A
options.text: Physiotherapy and a hinged elbow brace for 6 weeks

options.label: B
options.text: Surgical repair via a single anterior incision using cortical button fixation within 2–3 weeks of injury

options.label: C
options.text: PRP injection into the antecubital fossa followed by supervised rehabilitation

options.label: D
options.text: Surgical repair deferred until 6 weeks post-injury to allow swelling to settle

options.label: E
options.text: Long-term bracing is sufficient as the lacertus fibrosus maintains adequate function

explanation: Complete distal biceps tendon rupture in an active manual worker warrants early surgical repair; delay beyond 4–6 weeks significantly increases the technical difficulty due to tendon retraction and fibrosis. Single-incision anterior approaches using cortical button (e.g., EndoButton) or suture anchor fixation restore supination strength and endurance reliably. Non-operative management (A, E) results in approximately 40% loss of supination strength and 30% loss of flexion strength — unacceptable in a manual labourer. Deferred repair at 6 weeks (D) risks a technically demanding reconstruction requiring tendon graft. PRP injection (C) has no role in complete rupture.
learningPoint: Complete distal biceps rupture in an active patient requires early (within 2–3 weeks) surgical repair; the hook test is the most sensitive clinical sign.
references: Morrey: The Elbow and Its Disorders

stem: A 38-year-old woman is referred to the upper-limb clinic 10 weeks after she sustained an injury to her dominant elbow lifting a child. She describes ongoing aching with overhead activity. Examination reveals a visible 'Popeye' deformity of the upper arm, weak supination, and a positive hook test. MRI confirms complete distal biceps avulsion with 4 cm of tendon retraction. What is the most important surgical consideration at this stage?

options.label: A
options.text: Primary repair can be performed as if acute with no additional technical steps

options.label: B
options.text: The repair will likely require tendon graft augmentation due to the degree of retraction

options.label: C
options.text: Repair is contraindicated after 8 weeks and non-operative management should be offered

options.label: D
options.text: Synthetic ligament augmentation is the preferred option at this stage

options.label: E
options.text: Allograft tendon is preferred to autograft because it avoids donor site morbidity

explanation: After 6–10 weeks, significant tendon retraction and scarring make primary direct re-attachment impossible or under excessive tension. A tendon graft (semitendinosus autograft or Achilles allograft) is frequently required to bridge the gap to the radial tuberosity. Primary repair without graft (A) risks early re-rupture under tension. Repair is not contraindicated at 10 weeks (C) — it is technically more demanding but still appropriate in symptomatic patients. Synthetic augmentation (D) is not established practice. The choice between auto- and allograft (E) depends on surgeon preference and patient factors; no strong evidence mandates allograft.
learningPoint: Chronic distal biceps ruptures with significant retraction frequently require tendon graft reconstruction rather than primary repair.
references: Journal of Shoulder and Elbow Surgery

stem: A 55-year-old man falls onto his outstretched hand from standing height. Plain radiographs demonstrate a posterior elbow dislocation with a Mason type II radial head fracture involving 30% of the articular surface and a coronoid tip fracture (Regan–Morrey type I). Under anaesthesia the elbow is grossly unstable through the arc of motion. Which operative sequence most reliably restores stability?

options.label: A
options.text: Repair the lateral ulnar collateral ligament (LUCL) alone; radial head and coronoid fractures rarely require fixation

options.label: B
options.text: Fix the radial head fracture, fix or excise the coronoid fragment, then repair the LUCL; address the medial collateral ligament (MCL) only if instability persists after lateral-side reconstruction

options.label: C
options.text: Excise the radial head acutely and repair only the MCL to allow early mobilisation

options.label: D
options.text: Perform total elbow arthroplasty as the primary procedure to allow immediate rehabilitation

options.label: E
options.text: Apply a hinged external fixator and defer internal fixation for 2 weeks

explanation: Terrible triad (posterior dislocation + radial head fracture + coronoid fracture) is addressed in a systematic lateral-first sequence: fix or replace the radial head (which is the primary valgus and axial stabiliser), address the coronoid (even small type I fragments have a soft-tissue sleeve worth fixing if accessible), then repair the LUCL. This sequence restores stability in most cases without needing MCL repair. Isolated LUCL repair (A) ignores the bony stabilisers. Acute radial head excision (C) markedly destabilises the elbow and is contraindicated in this context. TEA (D) is reserved for elderly low-demand patients with unreconstructable fractures. Deferred fixation with external fixator (E) is a salvage strategy, not a primary approach.
learningPoint: In terrible triad injuries, the operative sequence is: radial head fixation/replacement → coronoid fixation → LUCL repair, with MCL addressed only if residual instability persists.

stem: During open repair of a terrible triad elbow injury, you fix the radial head and repair the LUCL. On fluoroscopy-guided range-of-motion testing the elbow re-subluxes posterolaterally beyond 40° of flexion. You extend the incision medially and identify the anterior band of the MCL, which is avulsed from the medial epicondyle. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Apply a hinged external fixator spanning the elbow and close — MCL repair is unnecessary

options.label: B
options.text: Repair the MCL with suture anchors to the medial epicondyle, then reassess stability

options.label: C
options.text: Convert to total elbow arthroplasty

options.label: D
options.text: Augment with a dynamic external fixator only and leave the MCL unrepaired

options.label: E
options.text: Perform an ulnar nerve transposition as the priority before addressing the MCL

explanation: When elbow instability persists after radial head fixation and LUCL repair in a terrible triad, residual instability usually indicates MCL disruption and should be addressed directly. Suture anchor repair of the avulsed MCL to the medial epicondyle restores the medial constraint and is the correct next step. An external fixator (A, D) is a fallback when soft tissue repair fails or is not feasible, not a substitute for primary repair. TEA (C) is inappropriate in a 55-year-old with reconstructable anatomy. Ulnar nerve transposition (E) may be indicated later but is not the priority when instability is present.
learningPoint: Persistent elbow instability after lateral-side reconstruction in a terrible triad injury indicates MCL disruption requiring direct repair.

stem: A 30-year-old physiotherapist falls on an outstretched hand and attends the emergency department with lateral elbow pain. Radiographs reveal a non-displaced Mason type I radial head fracture. There is no block to forearm rotation and the Essex-Lopresti test is negative. What is the recommended management?

options.label: A
options.text: Operative fixation with headless compression screws to prevent late displacement

options.label: B
options.text: Aspiration of the haemarthrosis, early mobilisation, and analgesia — no immobilisation required

options.label: C
options.text: Long-arm plaster cast for 4 weeks followed by physiotherapy

options.label: D
options.text: Radial head excision to allow immediate pain-free movement

options.label: E
options.text: Posterior slab for 2 weeks followed by gradual mobilisation

explanation: Mason type I fractures (non-displaced, no mechanical block) are managed non-operatively with aspiration of the haemarthrosis to improve comfort, immediate active range-of-motion exercises, and analgesia. Early mobilisation is key to preventing stiffness. Long-arm casting (C) or prolonged splinting (E) risks elbow stiffness and is not indicated. Operative fixation (A) is not warranted for a non-displaced fracture. Radial head excision (D) is inappropriate in an isolated non-displaced fracture in a young patient.
learningPoint: Mason type I radial head fractures are treated with haemarthrosis aspiration and immediate mobilisation — no immobilisation is needed.

stem: A 48-year-old woman sustains a Mason type III radial head fracture with comminution of the entire head in three fragments following a fall. There is no associated elbow dislocation or forearm injury. Attempts at ORIF are judged to be unreliable given the degree of comminution. Which of the following statements best supports metal radial head arthroplasty over primary radial head excision in this isolated injury?

options.label: A
options.text: Radial head excision reliably restores elbow kinematics without later complications

options.label: B
options.text: Metal arthroplasty provides an axial load-sharing constraint that reduces the long-term risk of capitellar wear and proximal radial migration

options.label: C
options.text: Excision is the technique of choice when there is no associated ligamentous injury

options.label: D
options.text: Silicone arthroplasty is preferred to metal implants due to better long-term wear characteristics

options.label: E
options.text: Both excision and arthroplasty produce identical long-term functional outcomes in isolated fractures

explanation: The radial head is the primary axial load transmitter across the radiocapitellar joint, sharing approximately 60% of longitudinal forearm load. Metal arthroplasty maintains this load-sharing function, reducing the risk of progressive valgus instability, proximal radial migration, cubitus valgus, and capitellar erosion that follow excision. Excision (A, C) can produce satisfactory short-term results in elderly low-demand patients but is associated with late complications in active individuals. Silicone prostheses (D) are no longer recommended due to fragmentation and synovitis. Long-term outcomes are not equivalent (E).
learningPoint: Metal radial head arthroplasty is preferred over excision for irreparable Mason type III fractures because it preserves axial load sharing and prevents late elbow instability.
references: Morrey: The Elbow and Its Disorders

stem: A 75-year-old woman with osteoporosis sustains a comminuted Mayo type IIB olecranon fracture involving the proximal 60% of the articular surface after a low-energy fall. She is independently mobile and has good elbow function prior to injury. Which fixation strategy offers the best outcomes for this pattern?

options.label: A
options.text: Tension-band wiring using 1.6 mm Kirschner wires and 18-gauge wire loop

options.label: B
options.text: Olecranon fragment excision and triceps advancement

options.label: C
options.text: Locking plate fixation along the dorsal cortex of the proximal ulna

options.label: D
options.text: Intramedullary screw fixation alone

options.label: E
options.text: Posterior slab immobilisation for 6 weeks followed by physiotherapy

explanation: Mayo type IIB fractures are displaced and unstable (comminuted), making tension-band wiring (A) inappropriate — it requires cortical contact on the compression side and is unreliable in comminuted patterns, with high rates of hardware failure and wire migration. Locking plate fixation provides stable multi-plane fixation and is the construct of choice for comminuted displaced olecranon fractures, particularly in osteoporotic bone. Olecranon excision with triceps advancement (B) is reserved for elderly low-demand patients with non-reconstructable fractures distal to the coronoid. Intramedullary screw alone (D) does not control rotational instability in comminuted fractures. Non-operative management (E) is not appropriate for a displaced, independently mobile patient.
learningPoint: Comminuted displaced olecranon fractures (Mayo type IIB/IIIB) require locking plate fixation; tension-band wiring is unsuitable when cortical comminution is present.

stem: A 67-year-old retired teacher sustains a simple Mayo type IA olecranon fracture — non-displaced, stable — after slipping on ice. She has no prior elbow pathology. The fracture is confirmed stable under fluoroscopy with active triceps contraction. Which management is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Tension-band wiring to accelerate rehabilitation

options.label: B
options.text: Long-arm cast with the elbow at 90° for 4–6 weeks, followed by graduated mobilisation

options.label: C
options.text: Active mobilisation in a collar-and-cuff within days, avoiding full extension loading for 4–6 weeks

options.label: D
options.text: Immediate full active-use without any restriction

options.label: E
options.text: Locking plate fixation to prevent late displacement in osteoporotic bone

explanation: Non-displaced, stable olecranon fractures (Mayo type I) can be managed non-operatively. Early controlled mobilisation within a collar-and-cuff, avoiding resisted extension loading (e.g., pushing up from a chair), allows fracture healing while minimising stiffness. Prolonged immobilisation in a long-arm cast (B) is associated with elbow stiffness and is not recommended. Immediate unrestricted use (D) risks displacement. Operative intervention (A, E) introduces surgical risk without benefit in a non-displaced stable pattern.
learningPoint: Non-displaced stable olecranon fractures (Mayo type I) are treated with early collar-and-cuff mobilisation, not prolonged casting.

stem: A 44-year-old male tennis coach presents with a 9-month history of lateral elbow pain. Clinical examination reveals tenderness 1 cm distal to the lateral epicondyle, positive Cozen's test, and pain on resisted wrist extension. He has completed a course of physiotherapy, two steroid injections, and a 6-month wait. He is keen to return to full coaching. MRI shows focal high signal and partial-thickness tearing at the extensor carpi radialis brevis (ECRB) origin. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: A third corticosteroid injection into the ECRB origin

options.label: B
options.text: Platelet-rich plasma (PRP) injection as a first-line intervention

options.label: C
options.text: Surgical debridement and repair of the ECRB origin (open or arthroscopic)

options.label: D
options.text: Extracorporeal shockwave therapy as a final non-operative option before any surgery is considered

options.label: E
options.text: Continuous ultrasound therapy for a further 6 months

explanation: After failure of comprehensive non-operative treatment including physiotherapy and two steroid injections over more than 6 months, with MRI-confirmed structural tearing, surgical debridement and repair of the ECRB origin is appropriate. A third steroid injection (A) carries increased risk of tendon weakening and has no additional benefit after two failed injections. PRP (B) may have a role earlier in the treatment pathway but is not the priority at this stage of failure. Shockwave therapy (D) may be considered earlier in the treatment pathway but at 9 months post-failure of multiple modalities, surgery is now indicated. Ultrasound therapy (E) has limited evidence in established tendinopathy.
learningPoint: Surgical ECRB debridement is indicated after failure of at least 6 months of non-operative management including physiotherapy and two corticosteroid injections.

stem: A 50-year-old golf course manager presents with medial elbow pain worsened by gripping and wrist flexion. He is tender directly over the common flexor origin at the medial epicondyle. Tinel's test at the cubital tunnel is negative and there is no ulnar nerve sensory disturbance. He has had physiotherapy for 4 months without improvement. What is the first-line treatment at this point?

options.label: A
options.text: Urgent surgical release of the flexor-pronator mass

options.label: B
options.text: Ultrasound-guided corticosteroid injection into the common flexor origin combined with further physiotherapy

options.label: C
options.text: EMG/nerve conduction studies to exclude cubital tunnel syndrome before any further treatment

options.label: D
options.text: Ulnar nerve anterior transposition as medial epicondylitis always involves cubital tunnel syndrome

options.label: E
options.text: Total elbow arthroplasty to eliminate the painful medial compartment

explanation: Medial epicondylitis (golfer's elbow) unresponsive to 4 months of physiotherapy is appropriately treated with an ultrasound-guided corticosteroid injection to the common flexor origin combined with continued physiotherapy. Tinel's test is negative here, ruling out significant cubital tunnel involvement, so EMG (C) is not mandatory at this stage. Surgery (A) is reserved for failure after 6–12 months of adequate non-operative treatment. Ulnar nerve transposition (D) is not indicated in the absence of cubital tunnel syndrome. TEA (E) has no role in epicondylitis.
learningPoint: Medial epicondylitis unresponsive to initial physiotherapy is treated with ultrasound-guided corticosteroid injection; surgery is reserved for failure after 6–12 months.

stem: A 47-year-old office worker presents with intermittent numbness and tingling in the ring and little fingers, worst at night and when the elbow is held flexed for prolonged periods. Tinel's sign is positive over the cubital tunnel, and there is mild weakness of the first dorsal interosseous muscle. Nerve conduction studies confirm ulnar neuropathy at the elbow with a motor conduction velocity of 38 m/s across the elbow segment. Which is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Wrist splinting in neutral to protect the ulnar nerve

options.label: B
options.text: Elbow extension splinting at night and avoidance of prolonged elbow flexion, combined with nerve gliding exercises

options.label: C
options.text: Immediate surgical decompression as nerve conduction studies show significant slowing

options.label: D
options.text: Corticosteroid injection into the cubital tunnel

options.label: E
options.text: Carpal tunnel decompression — the symptoms suggest a double-crush phenomenon

explanation: Mild-to-moderate cubital tunnel syndrome without significant muscle wasting or fixed weakness (first dorsal interosseous weakness present but mild) should first be managed non-operatively with nocturnal elbow extension splinting, activity modification to avoid prolonged flexion, and nerve gliding exercises. NCS slowing alone does not mandate immediate surgery if symptoms are not severe. Wrist splinting (A) would target carpal tunnel, not the elbow. Surgery (C) is reserved for failure of non-operative measures or severe/progressive neuropathy. Steroid injection (D) has limited evidence and risks nerve injury. Double-crush (E) is a diagnosis of exclusion and carpal tunnel surgery would be inappropriate as first management.
learningPoint: Mild cubital tunnel syndrome should initially be managed with nocturnal extension splinting, activity modification, and nerve gliding exercises before surgery is considered.

stem: A 55-year-old surgeon presents with wasting of the intrinsic muscles of the hand, persistent clawing of the ring and little fingers, and complete loss of sensation in the ulnar one and a half fingers. He has had 6 months of non-operative treatment. NCS shows absent sensory nerve action potential and markedly reduced compound muscle action potential amplitude at the FCU. He elects to proceed with surgical decompression. Which additional procedure should be considered at the time of in-situ decompression?

options.label: A
options.text: No additional procedure — simple in-situ decompression is always sufficient

options.label: B
options.text: Medial epicondylectomy is always performed as a routine adjunct to decompression

options.label: C
options.text: Anterior subcutaneous transposition if the nerve subluxes over the medial epicondyle or there is significant perineural fibrosis/abnormal anatomy

options.label: D
options.text: Ulnar nerve repair with nerve graft given the severity of the motor loss

options.label: E
options.text: Ulnar nerve block with 20 mL of bupivacaine at the wrist to provide intraoperative analgesia

explanation: In-situ decompression of the ulnar nerve at the cubital tunnel is effective for most cases. However, if intraoperative assessment reveals that the nerve subluxes anteriorly over the medial epicondyle with elbow flexion, or if there is significant perineural scar tissue or anomalous anatomy (e.g., arcade of Struthers, anconeus epitrochlearis), anterior transposition of the nerve (subcutaneous or submuscular) addresses the anatomical abnormality and reduces re-compression. Routine medial epicondylectomy (B) is not universally performed and has specific indications. Primary nerve repair/grafting (D) is for transection injuries, not compression. Wrist-level block (E) is an anaesthetic consideration unrelated to surgical decision-making.
learningPoint: Anterior ulnar nerve transposition should be considered at cubital tunnel decompression if the nerve subluxes over the medial epicondyle, there is perineural fibrosis, or anatomy is anomalous.

stem: A 32-year-old soldier sustained a distal humeral fracture treated with ORIF 18 months ago. He now has elbow flexion of 70° and extension deficit of 40°, with a functional arc of only 30°. CT scan shows exuberant heterotopic ossification (HO) spanning the posterior capsule and olecranon fossa, with radiographically mature bone (normal cortication). He wishes to regain elbow function. Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment?

options.label: A
options.text: Radiotherapy alone at this stage as the HO is mature

options.label: B
options.text: Surgical excision of the HO combined with posterior capsular release when bone scan shows quiescence — indomethacin prescribed post-operatively to reduce recurrence

options.label: C
options.text: Indomethacin alone for 6 months to cause regression of established HO

options.label: D
options.text: Manipulation under anaesthesia to forcefully restore the arc of motion

options.label: E
options.text: Await a further 6 months as functional arc may spontaneously improve

explanation: When mature HO causes a functionally limiting arc (<100°), surgical excision combined with capsular release is appropriate. Maturity of HO is confirmed clinically (stable arc, no warmth), radiographically (corticated margins), and ideally on bone scan (quiescence) to reduce recurrence risk. Post-operative indomethacin (or low-dose radiotherapy) is prescribed to inhibit HO recurrence. Radiotherapy alone (A) has a role in prophylaxis, not regression of established HO. NSAIDs such as indomethacin (C) prevent formation but cannot resorb mature HO. Manipulation under anaesthesia (D) risks fracture through areas of HO without addressing the underlying mass. Spontaneous improvement of established bridging HO is unlikely (E).
learningPoint: Surgical excision of mature functionally limiting HO is performed once radiographic and bone scan quiescence is confirmed, with post-operative NSAIDs or radiotherapy to reduce recurrence.
references: Morrey: The Elbow and Its Disorders

stem: A 28-year-old motorcyclist undergoes ORIF of a posterior Monteggia fracture-dislocation. Post-operatively, which intervention most effectively reduces the risk of heterotopic ossification formation around the elbow?

options.label: A
options.text: Immediate post-operative immobilisation in a cast for 6 weeks

options.label: B
options.text: Post-operative indomethacin 25 mg three times daily for 6 weeks, with early mobilisation

options.label: C
options.text: Single-dose intraoperative methylprednisolone

options.label: D
options.text: Continuous passive motion machine for 24 hours per day

options.label: E
options.text: Hyaluronic acid injection into the elbow joint at 2 weeks

explanation: NSAIDs — particularly indomethacin — are the most evidence-based pharmacological prophylaxis against HO formation after elbow trauma and surgery, acting by inhibiting arachidonic acid-mediated prostaglandin pathways that drive mesenchymal stem cell differentiation into osteoblasts. A 6-week course at therapeutic doses is the recommended protocol. Early mobilisation complements pharmacological prophylaxis by reducing capsular contracture. Prolonged casting (A) increases stiffness without reducing HO. Intraoperative steroids (C) have no established role in HO prophylaxis. Continuous CPM (D) is not standard care for HO prevention. Hyaluronic acid (E) has no role in HO prophylaxis.
learningPoint: Indomethacin for 6 weeks post-operatively is the pharmacological mainstay of HO prophylaxis after elbow trauma surgery.

stem: A 36-year-old woman describes recurrent episodes of lateral elbow pain and a 'giving way' sensation when pushing up from a chair with the elbow extended. She underwent an elbow dislocation 2 years ago treated non-operatively. Examination is normal with the elbow at rest but the lateral pivot-shift test performed under sedation provokes posterior subluxation of the radial head followed by a clunk on reduction. Which structure is primarily deficient?

options.label: A
options.text: Anterior band of the medial collateral ligament

options.label: B
options.text: Radial collateral ligament (RCL) proper

options.label: C
options.text: Lateral ulnar collateral ligament (LUCL)

options.label: D
options.text: Annular ligament

options.label: E
options.text: Interosseous membrane of the forearm

explanation: Posterolateral rotatory instability (PLRI) results from insufficiency of the lateral ulnar collateral ligament (LUCL), which runs from the lateral epicondyle to the supinator crest of the ulna and is the primary restraint against posterolateral subluxation of the ulnohumeral and radiocapitellar joints. The lateral pivot-shift test (O'Driscoll) demonstrates pathognomonic posterior subluxation of the radial head with valgus and supination stress. The MCL anterior band (A) is the primary valgus stabiliser. The RCL proper (B) contributes to PLRI but is not the primary deficient structure. The annular ligament (D) stabilises the radial head against the proximal ulna. The interosseous membrane (E) is relevant in Essex-Lopresti injuries.
learningPoint: Posterolateral rotatory instability is caused by LUCL insufficiency; the lateral pivot-shift test under sedation is the key diagnostic manoeuvre.
references: Morrey: The Elbow and Its Disorders

stem: A 40-year-old man with confirmed posterolateral rotatory instability secondary to iatrogenic LUCL injury during a lateral epicondylitis release has failed 6 months of physiotherapy. He proceeds to surgical reconstruction. Which graft and technique combination is most commonly used?

options.label: A
options.text: Semitendinosus autograft looped through bone tunnels in the lateral epicondyle and supinator crest of the ulna (docking technique)

options.label: B
options.text: Palmaris longus graft sutured end-to-end to the native LUCL remnant

options.label: C
options.text: Synthetic LARS ligament via an isometric point on the capitellum

options.label: D
options.text: MCL reconstruction using the docking technique (same as Tommy John surgery)

options.label: E
options.text: Radial head arthroplasty to create a bony block against subluxation

explanation: LUCL reconstruction for PLRI typically uses a free tendon graft — commonly palmaris longus, gracilis, or semitendinosus — routed through bone tunnels in the lateral epicondyle (isometric point) and the supinator crest of the ulna, reconstructing the LUCL footprint. The docking technique achieves secure graft tensioning. A simple end-to-end repair of a degenerate remnant (B) is not reliable. Synthetic ligaments (C) are not standard. MCL reconstruction (Tommy John, D) addresses medial instability, not PLRI. Radial head arthroplasty (E) does not address the ligamentous deficiency.
learningPoint: LUCL reconstruction for PLRI uses a free tendon graft through bone tunnels at the lateral epicondyle and supinator crest; the docking technique provides secure fixation.

stem: A 15-year-old male gymnast presents with a 6-month history of lateral elbow pain and intermittent locking. MRI shows a 10 mm osteochondral defect on the capitellum with a partially detached fragment. Plain radiographs confirm a radiolucent lesion with a subchondral halo. He trains six sessions per week. Which management is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Continue gymnastics training and reassess in 3 months as the growth plate will heal spontaneously

options.label: B
options.text: Activity restriction and non-operative management for 3–6 months given the open physis

options.label: C
options.text: Arthroscopic assessment with debridement and microfracture or fragment fixation based on stability of the lesion

options.label: D
options.text: Immediate open osteochondral allograft transplantation

options.label: E
options.text: Corticosteroid injection into the radiocapitellar joint to reduce inflammation

explanation: A partially detached OCD lesion (unstable by imaging criteria — subchondral halo, partial detachment) with mechanical symptoms (locking) requires arthroscopic assessment. If the fragment is stable and viable, internal fixation (headless compression screws, bioabsorbable pins) is attempted. If unstable and non-viable, debridement and marrow stimulation (microfracture) is performed. Continued sport without restriction (A) risks complete detachment and loose body formation. Non-operative management (B) is appropriate only for stable lesions without mechanical symptoms. Open osteochondral allograft (D) is a salvage option for large defects after failed primary surgery. Steroid injection (E) is contraindicated in active OCD and could hasten articular cartilage damage.
learningPoint: Unstable or symptomatic (locking) OCD of the capitellum requires arthroscopic surgery — fixation if viable or debridement/microfracture if non-viable.

stem: A 17-year-old female elite badminton player is found to have a Iwasaki type III OCD lesion of the capitellum (loose body in joint, crater >15 mm diameter). She has had debridement and microfracture 18 months earlier but reports return of locking and pain. What is the most appropriate salvage option?

options.label: A
options.text: Repeat debridement and microfracture of the same site

options.label: B
options.text: Radial head resection to offload the radiocapitellar joint

options.label: C
options.text: Osteochondral autograft transplantation (OATS) from a low-weight-bearing area of the knee

options.label: D
options.text: Total elbow arthroplasty as the lesion is not reconstructable

options.label: E
options.text: Corticosteroid injection followed by 6 months of sport cessation

explanation: After failed microfracture and debridement for a large capitellar OCD, osteochondral autograft transplantation (OATS/mosaicplasty) — using plugs typically harvested from the posterior non-weight-bearing femoral condyle or trochlea — provides hyaline cartilage restoration and is the preferred salvage for lesions >10 mm in active young patients. Repeat microfracture (A) is unlikely to succeed after prior failure. Radial head resection (B) would destabilise the elbow in a young patient. TEA (D) is contraindicated in a 17-year-old athlete. Steroid injection (E) does not address the structural deficiency.
learningPoint: OATS/mosaicplasty using autograft from the knee is the preferred salvage for large capitellar OCD lesions failing microfracture in young patients.

stem: A 72-year-old woman with long-standing rheumatoid arthritis has severe pain, gross elbow instability, and radiological Larsen grade V changes. She has failed 12 months of optimised DMARD therapy and intra-articular steroid injection. She is offered semi-constrained total elbow arthroplasty (TEA). Which restriction is most important to counsel her about post-operatively?

options.label: A
options.text: No driving for 6 weeks

options.label: B
options.text: Permanent lifting restriction to 1 kg single-lift and 0.5 kg repetitive loads

options.label: C
options.text: Avoid contact sports for 6 months only

options.label: D
options.text: No return to manual work after 3 months

options.label: E
options.text: Elbow immobilisation in a plaster cast for 3 months post-operatively

explanation: Semi-constrained TEA implants (e.g., Coonrad-Morrey) have a lifetime lifting restriction: single lifts should not exceed 1 kg (approximately a filled teacup) and repetitive loads should not exceed 0.5 kg. This is a permanent restriction, not temporary, and is the critical counselling point that differentiates TEA from hip or knee arthroplasty. Failure to observe this leads to early bushing wear and aseptic loosening. Driving restriction (A) is a temporary post-operative concern. Contact sports advice (C) understates the permanent nature of the restriction. A 3-month return to manual work (D) fails to address the lifelong nature of the restriction. Prolonged casting (E) is not standard post-TEA management.
learningPoint: Patients must be counselled pre-operatively that TEA carries a permanent lifting restriction of ≤1 kg single and ≤0.5 kg repetitive loads for life.

stem: A 68-year-old retired farmer undergoes semi-constrained TEA for post-traumatic elbow arthritis. At 3 years post-surgery he re-presents with progressive elbow pain, warmth, and a new sinus discharging serous fluid from the posterior elbow. CRP is 56 mg/L and WBC is normal. Aspiration grows Cutibacterium acnes (Propionibacterium acnes). What is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: A 6-week course of oral co-amoxiclav and re-aspiration at 6 weeks

options.label: B
options.text: Single-stage revision TEA with intraoperative culture-directed antibiotic cement

options.label: C
options.text: Two-stage revision: debridement with implant removal and antibiotic spacer, followed by re-implantation after 6–12 weeks of culture-directed IV antibiotics and infection clearance

options.label: D
options.text: Suppressive long-term oral antibiotics as definitive treatment

options.label: E
options.text: Above-elbow amputation given the established deep implant infection

explanation: Confirmed deep periprosthetic joint infection of a TEA with a discharging sinus and elevated CRP warrants two-stage revision: staged debridement with implant removal and antibiotic-loaded cement spacer to achieve infection control, followed by re-implantation after confirmation of eradication (normalised inflammatory markers, negative aspiration). Cutibacterium acnes is a recognised low-virulence PJI organism responsive to prolonged antibiotic courses but cannot be eradicated with the implant in situ. Oral antibiotics alone (A, D) cannot treat established biofilm on an implant. Single-stage revision (B) may be considered in selected low-virulence organisms in experienced centres but is not the standard pathway. Amputation (E) is a final salvage reserved for failed repeated revisions.
learningPoint: Established periprosthetic joint infection of TEA requires two-stage revision — implant removal, antibiotic spacer, culture-directed IV antibiotics, then reimplantation.

stem: A 35-year-old woman sustains a bicolumn distal humerus fracture (AO/OTA type C3) after a road traffic collision. CT reveals multi-fragmentary articular comminution with three separate articular fragments and medial and lateral column fractures. She is fit for surgery. Which surgical approach provides the best visualisation of both columns and the articular surface?

options.label: A
options.text: Lateral Kocher approach only

options.label: B
options.text: Medial approach via the flexor-pronator mass

options.label: C
options.text: Transolecranon approach via chevron olecranon osteotomy

options.label: D
options.text: Posterior approach with triceps splitting (Bryan-Morrey)

options.label: E
options.text: Anterior approach via the antecubital fossa

explanation: The transolecranon (olecranon osteotomy) approach provides unparalleled visualisation of the distal humeral articular surface and both columns by reflecting the olecranon superiorly with the intact triceps. It is the approach of choice for complex intra-articular distal humerus fractures requiring accurate articular reconstruction. The Kocher approach alone (A) is too limited for bicolumn fractures. The medial approach (B) does not adequately expose the lateral column. Triceps splitting (Bryan-Morrey, D) provides access but less optimal articular exposure than olecranon osteotomy. An anterior approach (E) does not expose the posterior distal humeral articular surface.
learningPoint: The chevron olecranon osteotomy approach gives the best articular exposure for complex bicolumn distal humerus fractures (AO/OTA type C3).

stem: An 82-year-old woman with moderate rheumatoid arthritis sustains an AO/OTA type C3 distal humerus fracture. She was independent prior to injury with an elbow arc of 20–110°. CT confirms severe comminution with osteoporotic bone quality, making anatomical reconstruction very challenging. What is the most appropriate definitive surgical management?

options.label: A
options.text: ORIF with parallel plating using locking plates

options.label: B
options.text: Non-operative 'bag of bones' technique with early mobilisation

options.label: C
options.text: Primary total elbow arthroplasty

options.label: D
options.text: Distal humeral hemiarthroplasty to preserve the ulna-sided articulation

options.label: E
options.text: Kirschner wire fixation and cast for 6 weeks

explanation: Primary TEA is the recommended treatment for severely comminuted intra-articular distal humerus fractures in elderly patients (typically >65–70 years) with osteoporotic bone where anatomical ORIF is not achievable and the pre-injury functional demand is low. It provides predictable and rapid return of painless function. ORIF (A) is appropriate in younger patients with reconstructable fractures but is unreliable in osteoporotic severely comminuted patterns. The 'bag of bones' technique (B) can give acceptable results in the most frail patients unfit for surgery but leaves residual pain and deformity. Hemiarthroplasty (D) addresses only one side and does not provide the stability of total elbow replacement. K-wire fixation (E) cannot stabilise complex articular comminution.
learningPoint: Primary TEA is the treatment of choice for unreconstructable intra-articular distal humerus fractures in elderly patients with osteoporosis.

stem: During ORIF of a distal humerus fracture via an olecranon osteotomy approach, you identify an anomalous ulnar nerve that courses superficially in the cubital tunnel and appears at risk. Which is the most appropriate management of the ulnar nerve during this procedure?

options.label: A
options.text: Proceed without ulnar nerve identification to minimise iatrogenic risk

options.label: B
options.text: Routinely identify and protect the ulnar nerve throughout; anterior subcutaneous transposition if the nerve is at tension or kinking over hardware

options.label: C
options.text: Divide the ulnar nerve deliberately to improve medial column exposure, then repair at the end

options.label: D
options.text: Ligate the ulnar nerve if it prevents adequate medial plate placement

options.label: E
options.text: Post-operative splinting in full extension to protect the nerve — intraoperative identification is not required

explanation: The ulnar nerve should be identified and protected at the outset of any posterior elbow approach for distal humerus ORIF. Anterior subcutaneous transposition is recommended when the nerve is tethered by scar, at risk from kinking over medial column hardware, or subluxing over the medial epicondyle. Routine transposition is advocated by many surgeons even when the nerve appears free, to prevent late cubital tunnel syndrome. Proceeding without identification (A) risks inadvertent neurotmesis. Deliberate division (C) and ligation (D) are unacceptable. Post-operative splinting alone (E) does not protect the nerve intraoperatively.
learningPoint: The ulnar nerve must be identified and protected during all posterior approaches to the distal humerus; anterior transposition is performed if the nerve is at risk from hardware or tethering.

stem: A 52-year-old male former manual worker presents with primary elbow osteoarthritis. He has pain primarily at the end ranges of motion (extension and full flexion), osteophytes on radiograph at the tip of the olecranon and the coronoid fossa, and an arc of motion of 30–115°. His pain is well localised and he has failed 12 months of activity modification, analgesia, and an intra-articular steroid injection. He is keen to return to golf. Which surgical procedure is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Total elbow arthroplasty for complete pain relief

options.label: B
options.text: Ulnohumeral arthroplasty (Outerbridge-Kashiwagi procedure) — olecranon fossa fenestration and osteophyte excision, arthroscopic or open

options.label: C
options.text: Radial head excision to reduce lateral compartment loading

options.label: D
options.text: High ulnar osteotomy to unload the medial compartment

options.label: E
options.text: Interposition arthroplasty using a fascia lata graft

explanation: Primary elbow OA with end-range pain and osteophyte impingement in an active 52-year-old is best managed with the Outerbridge-Kashiwagi (or arthroscopic equivalent) procedure — debridement, osteophyte excision, and olecranon fossa fenestration to improve the arc of motion. This effectively addresses the impingement pattern of primary OA (pain at extremes of motion rather than mid-arc) and is well tolerated with rapid recovery. TEA (A) is contraindicated in a 52-year-old active male due to the strict permanent lifting restriction. Radial head excision (C) is not indicated in primary OA without radial head pathology. Ulnar osteotomy (D) has no role in primary elbow OA. Interposition arthroplasty (E) is a salvage option for younger patients with diffuse articular destruction.
learningPoint: Ulnohumeral arthroplasty (Outerbridge-Kashiwagi or arthroscopic debridement) is the procedure of choice for primary elbow OA in active patients too young for TEA.

stem: A 40-year-old woman presents 48 hours after an elbow injury with severe wrist pain in addition to lateral elbow pain. Radiographs show a Mason type II radial head fracture. Wrist examination reveals marked tenderness over the distal radioulnar joint (DRUJ) and the distal interosseous membrane. The radius appears proximally migrated on radiograph. Which injury pattern does this most likely represent?

options.label: A
options.text: Isolated Mason type II radial head fracture with referred wrist pain

options.label: B
options.text: Essex-Lopresti lesion — disruption of the radiocapitellar joint, interosseous membrane, and DRUJ

options.label: C
options.text: Galeazzi fracture-dislocation

options.label: D
options.text: Triangular fibrocartilage complex (TFCC) tear from a separate wrist injury

options.label: E
options.text: Monteggia fracture variant with proximal radius instability

explanation: An Essex-Lopresti lesion is an axial forearm instability pattern comprising radial head fracture (usually comminuted), rupture of the interosseous membrane (IOM), and disruption of the DRUJ with proximal radial migration. This explains the combined lateral elbow and wrist pathology. It must be excluded in any radial head fracture presenting with wrist symptoms, as acute radial head excision without IOM repair or arthroplasty in this setting leads to severe proximal migration and chronic forearm instability. Isolated Mason II fracture with referred pain (A) would not cause DRUJ tenderness and radial migration. Galeazzi (C) involves a distal radius fracture, not a radial head fracture. TFCC tear (D) does not cause proximal radial migration. Monteggia (E) involves the proximal ulna.
learningPoint: Essex-Lopresti lesion (radial head fracture + IOM rupture + DRUJ disruption) must be excluded in any radial head fracture with concurrent wrist pain; acute radial head excision is contraindicated.

stem: When performing single-incision anterior repair of a distal biceps tendon to the radial tuberosity using cortical button fixation, which structure is most at risk of injury during the drilling of the radial tuberosity and passage of the button?

options.label: A
options.text: Median nerve in the antecubital fossa

options.label: B
options.text: Posterior interosseous nerve (PIN) as it passes around the radial neck through the radial tunnel

options.label: C
options.text: Brachial artery bifurcation proximal to the elbow

options.label: D
options.text: Radial nerve in the proximal forearm

options.label: E
options.text: Lateral antebrachial cutaneous nerve

explanation: The posterior interosseous nerve (deep branch of the radial nerve) is the structure most at risk during anterior single-incision distal biceps repair. It winds around the neck of the radius through the radial tunnel, lying close to the posterior cortex of the radial tuberosity. Drilling and cortical button passage through the posterior cortex can stretch or lacerate the PIN. Keeping the forearm in maximum supination during drilling moves the PIN away from the operative field and reduces this risk. The median nerve (A) and brachial artery (C) lie medially and are not at immediate risk at the tuberosity. The main radial nerve trunk (D) divides proximal to this level. The lateral antebrachial cutaneous nerve (E) is at risk in the antecubital skin incision but not at the tuberosity.
learningPoint: The posterior interosseous nerve (PIN) is the most at-risk structure during single-incision distal biceps repair; maximum supination during drilling reduces this risk.
references: Morrey: The Elbow and Its Disorders

stem: Theme: Management of radial head fractures. For each clinical scenario, select the single most appropriate management from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Aspiration of haemarthrosis and early active mobilisation

options.label: B
options.text: Open reduction and internal fixation (headless compression screws)

options.label: C
options.text: Radial head arthroplasty

options.label: D
options.text: Radial head excision

options.label: E
options.text: Hinged external fixator application

options.label: F
options.text: Closed reduction and long-arm cast

options.label: G
options.text: Aspiration, ORIF radial head and LUCL repair

emiLeads.stem: A 25-year-old falls from a bicycle and has lateral elbow pain. Radiographs show a single fragment, non-displaced crack in the radial head involving 20% of the articular surface. Forearm rotation is full and pain-free.

A 38-year-old sustains a posterior elbow dislocation that reduces spontaneously. Radiographs reveal a displaced Mason type II fracture (35% articular surface, 3 mm step) with no other bony injury. The elbow is stable through a full arc under fluoroscopy after aspiration.

A 55-year-old presents with a highly comminuted Mason type III radial head fracture with four articular fragments after a fall. The elbow was dislocated (now reduced). The joint is unstable through the arc under anaesthesia despite aspiration.

A 78-year-old retired librarian with rheumatoid arthritis sustains an irreparable Mason type III comminuted radial head fracture. She has low functional demands and the elbow is stable after reduction. She is on disease-modifying anti-rheumatic drugs.

explanation: Non-displaced type I fractures (lead 1) require only aspiration and mobilisation. Displaced but repairable type II fractures (lead 2) in a stable elbow are treated with ORIF. In the context of a terrible triad pattern (lead 3), the correct sequence is ORIF of the radial head plus LUCL repair (and coronoid if accessible). In elderly low-demand patients (lead 4) with an irreparable head, arthroplasty restores the load-sharing function of the radial head and is preferable to excision even in low-demand patients.

stem: Theme: Ulnar nerve pathology at the elbow. For each clinical scenario, select the single most appropriate next management step from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Nocturnal elbow extension splint and activity modification

options.label: B
options.text: Nerve conduction studies and EMG

options.label: C
options.text: In-situ surgical decompression of the ulnar nerve at the cubital tunnel

options.label: D
options.text: Anterior subcutaneous ulnar nerve transposition

options.label: E
options.text: Carpal tunnel decompression

options.label: F
options.text: MRI of the cervical spine

options.label: G
options.text: Ulnar nerve neurolysis and anterior submuscular transposition

emiLeads.stem: A 40-year-old desk worker reports 2 months of intermittent numbness in the ring and little fingers when resting the elbow on a desk. Clinical examination is entirely normal. Tinel's test is equivocal.

A 52-year-old electrician has a 9-month history of ulnar-sided hand numbness, weakness of the first dorsal interosseous, and a positive Tinel's sign at the cubital tunnel. He has failed 4 months of nocturnal splinting and activity modification.

A 48-year-old who previously dislocated her elbow reports ulnar nerve paraesthesiae worsening over 2 years. NCS confirms ulnar neuropathy at the elbow. Intraoperatively the nerve is found to sublux over the medial epicondyle with elbow flexion.

A 35-year-old baseball pitcher has failed in-situ decompression 12 months ago and presents with recurrent ulnar neuropathy. MRI shows perineural fibrosis tethering the nerve in the cubital tunnel. Nerve conduction studies confirm persistent ulnar slowing.

explanation: Early mild cubital tunnel syndrome without objective deficit (lead 1) warrants conservative management first. When conservative management fails with objective signs, NCS/EMG is the next step to quantify severity and guide surgical planning (lead 2). A subluxing nerve (lead 3) is best managed with anterior transposition rather than in-situ decompression. Failed in-situ decompression with perineural fibrosis (lead 4) requires neurolysis and submuscular transposition to move the nerve away from the scarred bed.

stem: Theme: Classification and pattern of elbow instability. For each clinical scenario, select the single most likely instability pattern or diagnosis from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Posterolateral rotatory instability (PLRI)

options.label: B
options.text: Medial collateral ligament insufficiency (valgus instability)

options.label: C
options.text: Terrible triad injury

options.label: D
options.text: Varus posteromedial rotatory instability

options.label: E
options.text: Simple posterior elbow dislocation with no bony injury

options.label: F
options.text: Essex-Lopresti lesion

options.label: G
options.text: Chronic medial epicondyle avulsion nonunion

emiLeads.stem: A 19-year-old javelin thrower presents with medial elbow pain and a painful arc when throwing. Moving valgus stress test is positive. Examination is otherwise normal and radiographs are unremarkable. NCS is normal.

A 50-year-old woman describes her elbow 'slipping out' when she pushes up from a chair with the forearm supinated. She had a lateral epicondylitis release 3 years ago. The lateral pivot-shift test reproduces a 'clunk'.

A 44-year-old man falls from a ladder onto an outstretched hand and sustains an elbow dislocation. Post-reduction films show a radial head fracture and a small coronoid avulsion. Under fluoroscopy the elbow re-dislocates posteriorly at 30° of flexion.

A 38-year-old woman sustains a varus-directed fall onto the lateral elbow. Radiographs show a large anteromedial coronoid facet fracture and lateral collateral ligament avulsion without radial head fracture. The elbow subluxes posteromedially.

explanation: Overhead athletes with medial pain and positive moving valgus stress test have MCL valgus instability (lead 1). Iatrogenic LUCL damage after lateral epicondylitis surgery causing subluxation on push-up is classic PLRI (lead 2). Posterior dislocation + radial head fracture + coronoid fracture = terrible triad (lead 3). Varus mechanism with anteromedial coronoid facet fracture and LCL disruption causing posteromedial subluxation is the posteromedial rotatory instability (PMRI/varus PMR) pattern — distinct from terrible triad (lead 4).

stem: Theme: Management of distal humerus fractures in adults. For each scenario, select the single most appropriate treatment from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: ORIF with parallel double-plating via olecranon osteotomy

options.label: B
options.text: Primary total elbow arthroplasty

options.label: C
options.text: Collar-and-cuff 'bag of bones' technique with early mobilisation

options.label: D
options.text: ORIF with single lateral column plate

options.label: E
options.text: Hinged external fixator

options.label: F
options.text: Skeletal traction through the olecranon

options.label: G
options.text: Closed reduction and long-arm cast for 6 weeks

emiLeads.stem: A 38-year-old male construction worker sustains an AO/OTA type C2 bicolumn distal humerus fracture with two discrete articular fragments. CT confirms reconstructable anatomy. He is otherwise fit and healthy.

A 74-year-old woman with severe osteoporosis sustains an AO/OTA type C3 comminuted distal humerus fracture after a low-energy fall. CT shows the articular surface is in more than five fragments. She was independent pre-injury.

A 91-year-old care home resident with multiple medical comorbidities, moderate dementia, and prior dependent function sustains a comminuted distal humerus fracture. The surgical team judges her unfit for general anaesthesia. She can cooperate with supervised exercises.
A 45-year-old woman sustains an AO/OTA type A2 extra-articular distal humerus fracture (transcondylar) without articular extension. CT confirms no articular involvement. She is otherwise healthy.

explanation: Reconstructable bicolumn and extra-articular distal humerus fractures in fit patients are treated with ORIF using parallel double-plating (leads 1 and 4) — this is the biomechanically superior construct. Primary TEA is the appropriate choice for irreconstructable multi-fragmentary fractures in osteoporotic elderly patients who can comply with the lifelong lifting restriction (lead 2). The 'bag of bones' technique (lead 3) is reserved for the most frail patients unfit for any surgery, where early mobility with gravity-assisted reduction is preferable to prolonged immobilisation.

stem: A 24-year-old rugby player falls onto an outstretched hand and attends the emergency department 2 hours later. He has anatomical snuffbox tenderness and pain on axial loading of the thumb. Plain radiographs are reported as normal. Which is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.label: A
options.text: Discharge with a tubular bandage and review if symptoms persist beyond 6 weeks

options.label: B
options.text: Apply a below-elbow thumb spica cast and arrange MRI wrist within 1 week

options.label: C
options.text: Apply a below-elbow thumb spica cast and repeat radiographs at 10–14 days

options.label: D
options.text: Immediate CT wrist to exclude occult fracture

options.label: E
options.text: Arrange bone scintigraphy at 72 hours

explanation: BSSH and BOA guidance recommends immobilisation in a below-elbow thumb spica and early MRI (within 1 week) for clinically suspected scaphoid fractures with normal initial radiographs, as MRI has the highest sensitivity and specificity and is cost-effective by avoiding unnecessary prolonged immobilisation. Repeat radiographs at 10–14 days (option C) were the traditional approach but miss fractures and delay definitive care. CT is faster but less sensitive for undisplaced fractures and bone bruising. Bone scan requires a 72-hour wait and has lower specificity. Discharge without imaging or immobilisation risks avascular necrosis and non-union.
learningPoint: Suspected scaphoid fractures with normal plain films should be immobilised immediately and investigated with early MRI per BSSH guidelines.

stem: A 38-year-old carpenter presents with a 2-year history of radial-sided wrist pain after an untreated fall. CT confirms a proximal-pole scaphoid non-union with a humpback deformity (intrascaphoid angle 52°) but no avascular necrosis on MRI. The most appropriate surgical management is:

options.label: A
options.text: Percutaneous headless compression screw without bone graft

options.label: B
options.text: Open reduction, interposition vascularised bone graft from 1,2-ICSRA, and headless compression screw

options.label: C
options.text: Iliac crest cortico-cancellous wedge graft (Fisk–Fernandez technique) and headless compression screw

options.label: D
options.text: Proximal row carpectomy

options.label: E
options.text: Four-corner fusion with scaphoid excision

explanation: A humpback deformity with increased intrascaphoid angle (normal <45°) requires correction of the volar intercalated segment instability pattern; this is achieved with a structural wedge graft (Fisk–Fernandez) to restore length and alignment, fixed with a headless compression screw. Vascularised graft (option B) is reserved for established avascular necrosis of the proximal pole. Percutaneous screw alone (option A) does not address the deformity or the non-union gap. PRC and four-corner fusion are salvage procedures appropriate once radiocarpal arthritis (SNAC wrist) has developed.
learningPoint: Scaphoid non-union with humpback deformity but no AVN is best treated with structural cortico-cancellous wedge graft to restore alignment and length.

stem: A 72-year-old right-handed retired teacher sustains a left distal radius fracture after a trip on a kerb. Initial radiographs show 18° dorsal tilt, 4 mm radial shortening and comminuted intra-articular extension. A satisfactory reduction is achieved under haematoma block restoring neutral tilt and 1 mm shortening. Postreduction radiographs at 1 week show redisplacement to 15° dorsal tilt. BSSH/BOA guidelines for the next management step favour:

options.label: A
options.text: Repeat closed reduction and application of a moulded plaster cast

options.label: B
options.text: Bridging external fixation

options.label: C
options.text: Volar locking plate fixation

options.label: D
options.text: Dorsal plating

options.label: E
options.text: Acceptance of position and continued cast immobilisation

explanation: Per BSSH/BOAST guidance, redisplacement following an initially adequate reduction — particularly with intra-articular comminution in a patient with high functional demands or bone that will not hold reduction — is an indication for surgical stabilisation. Volar locking plate fixation is now the predominant surgical technique for displaced distal radius fractures in adults because it achieves stable fixation with reliable restoration of volar tilt, allowing early mobilisation. Repeat manipulation has very high re-displacement rates. Bridging external fixation is a valid alternative but is less favoured in this demographic. Accepting 15° of dorsal tilt in an intra-articular fracture in a 72-year-old woman risks malunion and functional limitation.
learningPoint: Redisplacement of a distal radius fracture following closed reduction is an indication for volar locking plate fixation rather than repeat manipulation.

stem: In the Frykman classification of distal radius fractures, which feature upgrades a fracture from an even to an odd number?

options.label: A
options.text: Intra-articular extension into the radiocarpal joint

options.label: B
options.text: Intra-articular extension into the distal radioulnar joint

options.label: C
options.text: Associated ulnar styloid fracture

options.label: D
options.text: Dorsal comminution

options.label: E
options.text: Volar cortex involvement

explanation: In the Frykman classification, types are paired (I/II, III/IV, V/VI, VII/VIII) where each odd number describes the radiocarpal/DRUJ involvement pattern and the corresponding even number adds an associated ulnar styloid fracture. Thus the presence of an ipsilateral ulnar styloid fracture upgrades any type to the next higher even number. Intra-articular extension into the radiocarpal joint distinguishes types III/IV from I/II; DRUJ involvement distinguishes V/VI from III/IV. Dorsal comminution and volar involvement are not primary Frykman criteria.
learningPoint: In the Frykman classification, associated ulnar styloid fracture upgrades the fracture type from odd to the next even number.

stem: A 52-year-old hospital administrator complains of 9 months of nocturnal tingling in her right thumb, index and middle fingers, with morning clumsiness. Phalen's test is positive at 30 seconds. Nerve conduction studies confirm median nerve sensory latency prolonged at 4.2 ms (normal <3.5 ms) with a preserved compound motor action potential. She has already completed a 6-week trial of nocturnal splintage with partial relief. According to NICE and BSSH guidance, what is the single best next management option?

options.label: A
options.text: Repeat nerve conduction studies in 3 months

options.label: B
options.text: Ultrasound-guided steroid injection into the carpal tunnel

options.label: C
options.text: Continue nocturnal splintage for a further 6 weeks

options.label: D
options.text: Carpal tunnel decompression

options.label: E
options.text: MRI wrist to exclude space-occupying lesion

explanation: This patient has electrodiagnostically confirmed carpal tunnel syndrome that has failed a trial of conservative treatment (splinting). BSSH guidelines support offering surgical decompression after a failed non-operative trial in confirmed CTS. A steroid injection (option B) may provide temporary benefit and can be offered, but is typically used before or instead of surgery as a second-line measure if splinting fails — in established moderate NCS-confirmed CTS with functional limitation, decompression is the definitive treatment and is cost-effective. Repeating NCS or continuing splinting further delays effective treatment. MRI is not indicated without red-flag features.
learningPoint: Electrodiagnostically confirmed CTS that fails conservative management is an indication for carpal tunnel decompression.

stem: Following open carpal tunnel release, a 46-year-old nurse develops worsening pain, weakness of thumb opposition and inability to pinch. Examination reveals flattening of the thenar eminence and sensory loss confined to the lateral aspect of the ring finger and all of the middle, index and thumb. Which nerve structure was most likely injured intraoperatively?

options.label: A
options.text: Ulnar nerve deep branch

options.label: B
options.text: Median nerve main trunk

options.label: C
options.text: Recurrent (thenar) branch of the median nerve

options.label: D
options.text: Palmar cutaneous branch of the median nerve

options.label: E
options.text: Superficial branch of radial nerve

explanation: The recurrent (thenar) motor branch arises from the median nerve just distal to the flexor retinaculum, usually in an extraligamentous or subligamentous position, and is vulnerable during carpal tunnel release, particularly if the incision is carried too radially. Its injury causes loss of thenar muscle function (opponens pollicis, abductor pollicis brevis, superficial head of flexor pollicis brevis) and thenar wasting. The sensory loss described is the standard median nerve territory and results from the pre-existing compression, not a new injury. The palmar cutaneous branch would cause sensory loss over the proximal thenar eminence but no motor deficit. Ulnar nerve injury would cause intrinsic weakness and ring/little finger sensory loss.
learningPoint: The recurrent thenar motor branch of the median nerve is the structure most at risk of iatrogenic injury during carpal tunnel release, causing thenar wasting and loss of thumb opposition.

stem: A 64-year-old male solicitor has a 4-year history of progressive Dupuytren's contracture affecting the right ring finger. He now has a fixed flexion deformity of 40° at the metacarpophalangeal joint and 30° at the proximal interphalangeal joint, preventing him from placing his palm flat on a table. He is otherwise fit and well with no significant co-morbidities. Which of the following is the single most appropriate treatment?

options.label: A
options.text: Night splinting and physiotherapy

options.label: B
options.text: Collagenase Clostridium histolyticum (CCH) injection

options.label: C
options.text: Percutaneous needle fasciotomy

options.label: D
options.text: Limited fasciectomy

options.label: E
options.text: Dermofasciectomy with full-thickness skin graft

explanation: Limited (selective) fasciectomy remains the standard surgical treatment for Dupuytren's contracture that meets treatment criteria (positive table-top test, MCP contracture ≥30° or any PIP joint contracture). It provides good correction and a low recurrence rate. CCH (Xiapex) is an option but has been withdrawn from the UK/European market and has higher recurrence rates at the PIP joint. Needle fasciotomy has higher recurrence and is better suited to cord-type MCP contracture in patients unfit for surgery. Dermofasciectomy is reserved for recurrent disease or skin involvement. Night splinting is ineffective for established contracture.
learningPoint: Limited fasciectomy is the standard treatment for Dupuytren's contracture meeting table-top test criteria (MCP ≥30° or any PIP contracture).

stem: During limited fasciectomy for Dupuytren's disease affecting the little finger, the surgeon encounters a spiral cord displacing the digital nerve radially and superficially. Which anatomical component does NOT contribute to the formation of a spiral cord?

options.label: A
options.text: Pretendinous band

options.label: B
options.text: Spiral band

options.label: C
options.text: Lateral digital sheet

options.label: D
options.text: Grayson's ligament

options.label: E
options.text: Cleland's ligament

explanation: The spiral cord is formed by the pretendinous band, spiral band, lateral digital sheet, and Grayson's ligament spiralling around the neurovascular bundle, displacing it centrally, superficially and proximally — making it vulnerable to injury. Cleland's ligament is a dorsal cutaneous ligament that does not become diseased in Dupuytren's and does NOT contribute to the spiral cord. This anatomical distinction is surgically important: the digital nerve lies on the palmar aspect of Cleland's ligament but on the dorsal aspect of a spiral cord.
learningPoint: The spiral cord is composed of pretendinous band, spiral band, lateral digital sheet and Grayson's ligament; Cleland's ligament does not contribute.

stem: A 22-year-old chef lacerates his right index finger on a knife blade at the level of the proximal phalanx. In the emergency department he cannot flex the distal interphalangeal joint actively but can weakly flex the PIP joint. The wound is clean and 4 hours old. In which flexor tendon zone has this injury occurred, and what is the recommended management?

options.label: A
options.text: Zone I; primary repair of flexor digitorum profundus tendon within 24 hours

options.label: B
options.text: Zone II; primary repair of both FDS and FDP tendons within 24 hours

options.label: C
options.text: Zone II; primary repair of FDP only, sacrificing FDS to reduce adhesions

options.label: D
options.text: Zone III; primary repair within 24 hours

options.label: E
options.text: Zone I; conservative management with splintage for 6 weeks

explanation: An injury at the level of the proximal phalanx with intact (albeit weak) FDS and absent FDP function is in Zone II (Bunnell's 'no-man's land') — between the proximal palmar crease and the middle of the middle phalanx. Modern evidence and BSSH guidelines support primary repair of both FDS and FDP tendons in clean lacerations because preserving FDS improves digit stability and pulley nutrition. Primary repair within 24 hours (ideally in a specialist centre) allows the best anatomical repair before oedema sets in. The historic practice of sacrificing FDS has been abandoned. Zone I injury would involve only FDP (distal to FDS insertion).
learningPoint: Zone II flexor tendon injuries should undergo primary repair of both FDS and FDP tendons within 24 hours by a trained hand surgeon.

stem: A 4-strand core suture repair of a flexor tendon has been performed using a modified Kessler technique supplemented by a running epitendinous suture. The resistance to gapping of the repair at 2 kg is satisfactory. Which property of the repair does the epitendinous suture primarily enhance?

options.label: A
options.text: Overall tensile strength of the repair

options.label: B
options.text: Reduction of friction as the tendon glides through the sheath

options.label: C
options.text: Vascularity of the tendon ends

options.label: D
options.text: Prevention of tendon sheath adhesions

options.label: E
options.text: Resistance to gap formation under cyclic loading

explanation: The epitendinous (circumferential) suture smooths the repair surface and primarily reduces friction between the tendon and the flexor sheath, facilitating gliding. It contributes approximately 10–50% of overall strength depending on technique, but its principal functional role is smoothing the bulky repair site. Vascularity is maintained by the intrasynovial fluid and the intact vincular system — this is not the primary function of the epitendinous suture. Prevention of adhesions is multifactorial and driven primarily by early controlled mobilisation protocols rather than the suture itself.
learningPoint: The epitendinous suture primarily reduces repair-site friction within the flexor sheath to facilitate tendon gliding.

stem: A 55-year-old woman with well-controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus presents with a 5-month history of triggering of her right middle finger. She describes painful snapping when straightening the finger each morning, which is now occasionally locked in flexion. Clinical examination confirms a palpable nodule at the A1 pulley. She declines surgery. Which single management option has the highest evidence for sustained relief?

options.label: A
options.text: Continuous splintage of the MCP joint in extension for 6 weeks

options.label: B
options.text: Corticosteroid injection into the flexor tendon sheath at the A1 pulley

options.label: C
options.text: Physiotherapy and tendon gliding exercises

options.label: D
options.text: Oral NSAIDs for 4 weeks

options.label: E
options.text: Ultrasound-guided hyaluronic acid injection

explanation: Corticosteroid injection into or adjacent to the A1 pulley flexor sheath is the most effective non-surgical treatment for trigger finger, with high short-term resolution rates. BSSH guidelines support this as first-line treatment before surgery. In diabetic patients the effect may be less durable and blood glucose should be monitored for 48 hours post-injection, but it remains the best non-operative option. Splintage alone is less effective and poorly tolerated. Tendon gliding exercises and NSAIDs have weak evidence. Hyaluronic acid has no established evidence base for this indication.
learningPoint: Corticosteroid injection at the A1 pulley is the most effective non-surgical treatment for trigger finger, with awareness that diabetic patients may have a shorter duration of benefit.

stem: A 28-year-old male office worker presents with 18 months of central wrist pain and stiffness. MRI demonstrates diffuse low signal on T1 and T2 sequences throughout the entire lunate with no articular collapse and no carpal height loss. Plain radiographs show no bony change. His ulnar variance is −1.5 mm. What is the Lichtman stage and most appropriate intervention?

options.label: A
options.text: Stage I; activity modification and splinting with review

options.label: B
options.text: Stage II; joint levelling procedure (radial shortening osteotomy)

options.label: C
options.text: Stage IIIA; proximal row carpectomy

options.label: D
options.text: Stage IIIB; four-corner fusion

options.label: E
options.text: Stage IV; wrist arthrodesis

explanation: MRI changes (diffuse avascular signal) with no collapse or articular change on plain film indicates Lichtman Stage II (sclerosis on plain film would confirm this; Stage I is MRI-only changes with normal plain films). Negative ulnar variance (−1.5 mm) is associated with Kienböck's disease and makes radial shortening osteotomy appropriate to unload the lunate by equalising ulnar variance. Stage I may be managed conservatively. Proximal row carpectomy (Stage IIIA) and four-corner fusion (Stage IIIB with fixed scaphoid rotation) are reserved for later stages with carpal collapse. Wrist arthrodesis is a Stage IV salvage option.
learningPoint: Kienböck's Stage II with negative ulnar variance is best managed with radial shortening osteotomy to unload the lunate.

stem: A patient with established Kienböck's disease has lunate fragmentation with fixed rotary subluxation of the scaphoid but no radiocarpal or midcarpal arthrosis on plain films. According to Lichtman staging, what stage is this and what does it imply for surgical decision-making?

options.label: A
options.text: Stage IIIA — scaphoid rotation can be corrected; joint levelling or limited intercarpal fusion may be appropriate

options.label: B
options.text: Stage IIIB — scaphoid rotation is fixed; reconstructive options include four-corner fusion or scaphocapitate fusion

options.label: C
options.text: Stage IIIB — indicates wrist arthrodesis as the only viable option

options.label: D
options.text: Stage IV — pancarpal arthritis; salvage required

options.label: E
options.text: Stage II — sclerosis only; radial shortening osteotomy is indicated

explanation: Lichtman Stage IIIB is defined by lunate collapse and fragmentation WITH fixed rotary subluxation of the scaphoid (as opposed to IIIA where the scaphoid rotation is correctable). This distinction is critical because once the scaphoid rotation is fixed, ulnar-variance correcting osteotomies are ineffective. Four-corner fusion (capitate-hamate-lunate-triquetrum after scaphoid excision or lunate excision) is the preferred reconstructive procedure at Stage IIIB without arthrosis. Wrist arthrodesis is reserved for Stage IV with pancarpal arthritis.
learningPoint: Lichtman Stage IIIB (fixed scaphoid rotation) cannot be corrected by joint-levelling surgery; four-corner fusion is the preferred reconstructive option.

stem: A 35-year-old tennis coach describes chronic dorsal wrist pain and clicking for 3 years after a hyperextension injury. Radiographs show a scapholunate gap of 4 mm on the AP view and a Terry Thomas sign. The scapholunate angle on lateral view is 75°. MRI-arthrography confirms complete SL ligament tear. The cartilage is intact. Which surgical option is most appropriate at this stage?

options.label: A
options.text: Dorsal capsulodesis alone

options.label: B
options.text: Partial wrist denervation

options.label: C
options.text: Scapholunate ligament reconstruction (e.g. RASL procedure or 3-ligament tenodesis)

options.label: D
options.text: Proximal row carpectomy

options.label: E
options.text: Total wrist arthrodesis

explanation: This patient has a chronic, complete SL ligament disruption with DISI deformity (SL angle >60° = abnormal) but preserved cartilage, placing it as a Stage II/III SL dissociation (Garcia-Elias classification) with dynamic to static instability but no arthritis. Ligament reconstruction (such as the RASL procedure with reduction-association of the scapholunate using a cannulated screw, or the three-ligament tenodesis technique) is appropriate for chronic static SL instability with intact cartilage. Dorsal capsulodesis alone does not adequately restore ligament competence in static instability. PRC and total wrist fusion are reserved for SLAC wrist arthritis (Stage III–IV).
learningPoint: Chronic static SL instability with intact cartilage warrants ligament reconstruction; salvage procedures are reserved for SLAC arthritis.
