stem: A 50-year-old man with a long-standing SLAC wrist (Stage III — midcarpal arthritis, radioscaphoid arthritis, but preserved radiolunate joint) reports debilitating wrist pain limiting his work as a painter. The most appropriate surgical reconstruction that preserves wrist motion is:

options.label: A
options.text: Proximal row carpectomy

options.label: B
options.text: Total wrist arthrodesis

options.label: C
options.text: Four-corner fusion with scaphoid excision

options.label: D
options.text: Radioscapholunate fusion

options.label: E
options.text: Scaphoid excision alone

explanation: Stage III SLAC (or SNAC) wrist with midcarpal arthritis but a preserved radiolunate joint is the ideal indication for four-corner fusion (capitate, hamate, lunate, triquetrum) combined with scaphoid excision. This preserves the radiolunate articulation, allowing approximately 40–50% of normal wrist flexion-extension through this residual joint. PRC (option A) is suitable for Stage I–II SLAC where the capitate articular cartilage is intact; Stage III SLAC with capitate involvement contraindicates PRC. Total wrist fusion sacrifices all motion and is reserved for failed reconstructions or Stage IV. Radioscapholunate fusion sacrifices motion at the midcarpal joint.
learningPoint: Four-corner fusion with scaphoid excision is the motion-preserving procedure of choice for Stage III SLAC/SNAC wrist with an intact radiolunate joint.

stem: A 62-year-old retired music teacher has severe pain at the base of the right thumb, aggravated by pinching and jar opening, for 3 years. Grind test is positive. Radiographs show Stage III Eaton–Littler trapeziometacarpal OA with subluxation of the first metacarpal. She has failed 12 months of splinting, physiotherapy and two intra-articular steroid injections. The most appropriate surgical option is:

options.label: A
options.text: Metacarpophalangeal joint fusion

options.label: B
options.text: Trapeziectomy alone

options.label: C
options.text: Trapeziectomy with ligament reconstruction and tendon interposition (LRTI)

options.label: D
options.text: Total trapeziometacarpal joint replacement

options.label: E
options.text: Metacarpal extension osteotomy

explanation: Trapeziectomy with ligament reconstruction and tendon interposition (LRTI, commonly using the flexor carpi radialis tendon) has been the most widely performed procedure for Stage III–IV thumb CMC OA in the UK and has a long track record of pain relief and functional improvement. However, BSSH GANNET trial evidence and Cochrane reviews suggest trapeziectomy alone gives equivalent functional outcomes with a simpler operation — so either option B or C may be defensible. In an FRCS examination context, LRTI remains the most commonly cited 'gold standard' answer for symptomatic Stage III/IV CMC OA. MCP joint fusion addresses hyperextension deformity, not the primary arthrosis. Metacarpal osteotomy is for early/Stage II disease.
learningPoint: Trapeziectomy ± LRTI is the standard surgical treatment for symptomatic Stage III/IV trapeziometacarpal OA failing conservative management.

stem: Which anatomical structure, when ruptured, leads to the Z-deformity (zigzag collapse) seen in advanced thumb carpometacarpal osteoarthritis?

options.label: A
options.text: Extensor pollicis brevis tendon

options.label: B
options.text: Anterior oblique (beak) ligament of the trapeziometacarpal joint

options.label: C
options.text: Ulnar collateral ligament of the MCP joint

options.label: D
options.text: Flexor pollicis longus tendon

options.label: E
options.text: Adductor pollicis muscle insertion

explanation: The anterior oblique ligament (volar beak ligament) is the primary stabiliser of the trapeziometacarpal joint, preventing dorsoradial subluxation of the first metacarpal base. Its attenuation or rupture allows dorsal subluxation, leading to metacarpal adduction and compensatory hyperextension of the MCP joint — producing the characteristic Z-deformity (adducted metacarpal + hyperextended MCP + flexed IP). The UCL of the MCP joint may also become stretched but is not the primary initiator. The extensor pollicis brevis, FPL and adductor are secondary contributors to the deformity once alignment is lost.
learningPoint: The anterior oblique (beak) ligament is the primary stabiliser of the trapeziometacarpal joint; its failure initiates the Z-deformity of thumb CMC OA.

stem: A 30-year-old physiotherapist fell on a twisted outstretched hand 3 months ago. She now has ulnar-sided wrist pain with painful forearm rotation and a positive piano-key sign. MRI-arthrography shows a peripheral detachment of the TFCC at its ulnar (foveal) attachment with no central perforation. Which classification and treatment best apply?

options.label: A
options.text: Palmer Class 1A (central perforation); arthroscopic debridement

options.label: B
options.text: Palmer Class 1B (peripheral ulnar tear); arthroscopic or open foveal repair

options.label: C
options.text: Palmer Class 1D (radial attachment tear); arthroscopic repair to sigmoid notch

options.label: D
options.text: Palmer Class 2 (degenerative); arthroscopic wafer procedure

options.label: E
options.text: Palmer Class 1C (distal tear); repair of ulnocarpal ligaments

explanation: The Palmer classification divides TFCC tears into traumatic (Class 1) and degenerative (Class 2). Class 1B denotes a peripheral ulnar-sided (foveal) detachment, which destabilises the DRUJ. This is the tear pattern responsible for the piano-key sign (DRUJ instability) and painful forearm rotation. Treatment is arthroscopic or open foveal reattachment, which restores DRUJ stability. Class 1A (central perforation) causes pain but not DRUJ instability and is managed with debridement. Class 1D involves radial attachment to the sigmoid notch. Class 2 tears are degenerative and often associated with positive ulnar variance.
learningPoint: Palmer Class 1B (foveal peripheral TFCC tear) causes DRUJ instability and is treated by foveal reattachment, not debridement.

stem: A patient requires a Sauvé-Kapandji procedure for severe DRUJ arthritis. Which statement most accurately describes this operation and its principal complication?

options.label: A
options.text: The distal ulna head is excised (Darrach procedure); the principal complication is convergence of the radius and ulna stump

options.label: B
options.text: The distal radioulnar joint is fused and a pseudarthrosis created in the ulna shaft; the principal complication is ulnar stump instability

options.label: C
options.text: The sigmoid notch is deepened and the TFCC reattached; the principal complication is restricted supination

options.label: D
options.text: The ulnar head is replaced with a prosthesis; the principal complication is implant loosening

options.label: E
options.text: A vascularised fibula graft reconstructs the distal ulna; the principal complication is donor-site morbidity

explanation: The Sauvé-Kapandji procedure fuses the DRUJ (eliminating painful radioulnar articulation) while creating a proximal pseudarthrosis in the ulna shaft to preserve forearm rotation. This distinguishes it from the Darrach procedure (distal ulna excision, option A). The principal complication of Sauvé-Kapandji is instability and impingement of the proximal ulnar stump on the radius, causing pain and mechanical symptoms during forearm rotation. This is more problematic in younger, physically active patients and in those with inflammatory arthritis.
learningPoint: Sauvé-Kapandji fuses the DRUJ and creates an ulnar pseudarthrosis; the main complication is proximal ulnar stump instability.

stem: A 40-year-old car mechanic presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of a painful, swollen right index finger following a puncture wound from a screw. On examination there is: fusiform swelling of the entire finger, semiflexed posture at rest, tenderness along the flexor sheath, and extreme pain on passive extension of the finger. Which of the following is the single most important next intervention?

options.label: A
options.text: Oral co-amoxiclav and wound closure in the emergency department

options.label: B
options.text: Intravenous antibiotics alone for 48 hours with daily review

options.label: C
options.text: Urgent surgical washout and decompression of the flexor sheath

options.label: D
options.text: Aspiration of the flexor sheath under ultrasound guidance

options.label: E
options.text: MRI finger to confirm diagnosis before any intervention

explanation: This patient displays all four of Kanavel's cardinal signs of acute pyogenic flexor tenosynovitis: fusiform swelling, semi-flexed posture, tenderness along the flexor sheath, and pain on passive extension. This is a surgical emergency requiring urgent irrigation and decompression of the flexor tendon sheath, ideally within hours of diagnosis, to prevent tendon necrosis and permanent stiffness. Oral antibiotics alone (option A) or IV antibiotics alone (option B) are insufficient and will lead to tendon and pulley destruction. Imaging should not delay theatre. Aspiration is inadequate for established tenosynovitis.
learningPoint: All four Kanavel's signs constitute a surgical emergency; immediate surgical flexor sheath washout is required to prevent tendon destruction.

stem: A 65-year-old patient with poorly controlled diabetes presents with a dorsal hand abscess that when incised reveals pus tracking palmarwards between the central and radial bursa of the palm. Proximal extension of infection into the forearm via Parona's space involves which anatomical plane?

options.label: A
options.text: Between flexor digitorum superficialis and flexor digitorum profundus

options.label: B
options.text: Between the pronator quadratus and the flexor tendons

options.label: C
options.text: Between flexor carpi ulnaris and flexor digitorum profundus

options.label: D
options.text: Between flexor pollicis longus and the carpal bones

options.label: E
options.text: Between the carpal tunnel contents and the flexor retinaculum

explanation: Parona's space is the potential fascial space in the distal forearm situated between the overlying flexor tendons (FDP and FPL) and the deep surface of the pronator quadratus. It communicates with the radial (FPL) and ulnar bursae of the palm and provides a pathway for proximal spread of palmar infections into the forearm — a so-called 'horseshoe abscess' when both bursae are involved. Surgical drainage of Parona's space requires separate forearm incisions in addition to palmar decompression.
learningPoint: Parona's space lies between the flexor tendons and pronator quadratus; infection tracks here from palmar bursae to produce the horseshoe abscess pattern.

stem: A 48-year-old construction worker sustains a penetrating laceration at the medial epicondyle level and presents unable to cross his fingers, with clawing of the ring and little fingers and numbness over the hypothenar eminence and little finger. Froment's sign is positive. Which of the following intrinsic muscles is SPARED in this injury?

options.label: A
options.text: First dorsal interosseous

options.label: B
options.text: Adductor pollicis

options.label: C
options.text: Abductor digiti minimi

options.label: D
options.text: First and second lumbricals

options.label: E
options.text: Flexor digiti minimi

explanation: The first and second lumbricals are supplied by the median nerve (not the ulnar nerve) and are therefore spared in an isolated ulnar nerve lesion. The ulnar nerve supplies the third and fourth lumbricals, all seven interossei, the hypothenar muscles (abductor/flexor/opponens digiti minimi), and adductor pollicis. Clawing is more marked in the ulnar two digits (ring and little) because the lumbricals (median-supplied) prevent clawing in the radial two digits — this is the 'ulnar paradox' where a high ulnar lesion claws less because FDP to ring/little is also paralysed. Froment's sign reflects adductor pollicis weakness (ulnar nerve).
learningPoint: The first and second lumbricals are median nerve-supplied and are spared in ulnar nerve injury; the third and fourth lumbricals are ulnar-innervated.

stem: A 19-year-old woman sustains a mid-humeral shaft fracture in a road traffic collision. Three months later she has wrist drop and inability to extend the fingers and thumb at the MCP joints, but sensation over the first dorsal web space is preserved. Electrodiagnostic studies show absent radial nerve motor conduction to the extensor digitorum communis. Which factor most accurately indicates that nerve exploration and repair should be performed at this stage rather than waiting further?

options.label: A
options.text: Absence of Tinel's sign advancing distally over 3 months

options.label: B
options.text: No clinical or NCS evidence of reinnervation at 3 months, with injury mechanism (fracture) suggesting possible entrapment

options.label: C
options.text: Preserved sensation in the first dorsal web space confirming a complete lesion

options.label: D
options.text: A closed mid-shaft spiral fracture pattern (Holstein-Lewis)

options.label: E
options.text: Age under 25 predicting poor spontaneous recovery

explanation: Radial nerve palsy associated with humeral shaft fracture is usually a neurapraxia or axonotmesis that recovers spontaneously in 70–90% of cases within 3–4 months. However, absence of any clinical recovery AND no advancing Tinel's sign AND no NCS evidence of reinnervation at 3 months, combined with a fracture mechanism that may have caused nerve entrapment (e.g. in the fracture callus or by metalwork), are indications for surgical exploration. The Holstein-Lewis fracture (distal spiral) is classically associated with radial nerve injury but spontaneous recovery still occurs; it is not by itself an indication for immediate exploration. Preserved sensation in the first dorsal web space (superficial branch) indicates an incomplete lesion, which paradoxically supports observing longer — making option C incorrect as a surgical trigger.
learningPoint: Radial nerve palsy after humeral fracture warrants observation for 3 months; absence of reinnervation AND suspected entrapment (not just fracture pattern) are the primary indications for exploration.

stem: A 58-year-old woman with long-standing seropositive rheumatoid arthritis presents with sudden-onset inability to extend the right ring and little fingers. There is no pain and the deficit appeared overnight. Passive extension at the MCP joints is full and there is no swelling. The most likely underlying cause is:

options.label: A
options.text: Rupture of the extensor digitorum communis tendons to ring and little fingers over Lister's tubercle

options.label: B
options.text: Acute posterior interosseous nerve compression at the radial tunnel

options.label: C
options.text: Rupture of extensor tendons over the distal ulna (caput ulnae syndrome)

options.label: D
options.text: Dislocation of the extensor tendons into the inter-metacarpal valleys

options.label: E
options.text: MCP joint subluxation causing mechanical extensor lag

explanation: Painless sequential extensor tendon rupture over the eroded distal ulna (caput ulnae syndrome / Vaughan-Jackson syndrome) is the classic presentation in rheumatoid arthritis. Synovitis erodes the distal ulna, producing a sharp spike that attenuates and then ruptures the extensor tendons from ulnar to radial (little finger first, then ring, then middle). Painlessness and sudden onset are characteristic. This differs from rupture over Lister's tubercle (more often EDM/EPL). PIN palsy would cause weak extension but not complete absence, and active extension at the wrist would be affected. MCP subluxation causes volar dislocation with extensor lag, not sudden complete loss.
learningPoint: Vaughan-Jackson syndrome is sequential painless extensor tendon rupture (ulnar to radial) caused by attrition over the eroded distal ulna in rheumatoid arthritis.

stem: A 62-year-old woman with rheumatoid arthritis has severe ulnar drift of all fingers at the MCP joints with volar subluxation of the MCP joints but no significant MCP joint destruction on radiograph. The wrist shows Stage III rheumatoid wrist changes. Which deformity correction should be prioritised first when planning staged reconstruction?

options.label: A
options.text: MCP joint silicone arthroplasty first

options.label: B
options.text: Wrist stabilisation or arthrodesis first

options.label: C
options.text: PIP joint correction first

options.label: D
options.text: Tendon rebalancing at MCP joint first without bony procedure

options.label: E
options.text: Simultaneous single-stage correction of wrist and all MCP joints

explanation: In rheumatoid hand reconstruction, the principle is to correct the proximal deformity before the distal one. The wrist is the proximal deformity driving MCP ulnar drift; if the wrist is not stabilised first, any MCP realignment will recur as the deforming forces re-act on the corrected joints. Wrist stabilisation (arthrodesis or arthroplasty) corrects the supination/ulnar translation that contributes to finger ulnar deviation. Only after a stable wrist platform is established should MCP arthroplasty or soft-tissue rebalancing be undertaken.
learningPoint: In rheumatoid hand reconstruction, wrist stabilisation precedes MCP joint correction because proximal deformity drives distal recurrence.

stem: A 35-year-old right-hand-dominant electrician amputates his right thumb at the level of the proximal phalanx in a circular saw injury. The amputated part is clean and has been wrapped in moist gauze and placed in a bag on ice. Which statement most accurately reflects the UK replantation guidelines regarding this injury?

options.label: A
options.text: Thumb replantation at this level is a relative indication only; outcome rarely justifies the operative risk

options.label: B
options.text: Thumb replantation is a strong indication regardless of mechanism; the patient should be transferred to a regional replantation centre

options.label: C
options.text: Circular saw injuries are an absolute contraindication to replantation due to avulsion

options.label: D
options.text: Replantation should not be attempted beyond 4 hours from injury regardless of cooling

options.label: E
options.text: The patient's dominance is irrelevant; bilateral amputations are the only indication for thumb replantation

explanation: BSSH and international consensus lists thumb amputation as a strong indication for replantation because the thumb contributes approximately 40–50% of hand function; even a stiff replanted thumb outperforms most prostheses and secondary reconstruction options. The patient should be transferred urgently to a regional replantation centre. Mechanism (circular saw) is a relative consideration — sharp/tidy amputations replant better than avulsions, but a clean circular saw cut can still produce a tidy amputation. With appropriate cooling (as here), ischaemia time of up to 12 hours (digits) or 6 hours (ray-containing) is acceptable. Dominance and bilateral involvement are relevant factors but thumb amputations in working-age adults are strong indications regardless.
learningPoint: Thumb amputation in a working-age adult is a strong indication for replantation and mandates urgent transfer to a regional replantation centre.

stem: A 26-year-old boxer strikes a wall and sustains a Bennett's fracture-dislocation of the right thumb. Radiographs show a 3 mm intra-articular fragment at the volar-ulnar base of the first metacarpal with radial and proximal subluxation of the metacarpal shaft. Closed reduction under fluoroscopy achieves acceptable alignment. The single best method of definitive fixation is:

options.label: A
options.text: Below-elbow thumb spica cast for 6 weeks

options.label: B
options.text: Percutaneous K-wire fixation, pinning the first metacarpal to the second metacarpal and/or trapezium

options.label: C
options.text: Open reduction and lag screw fixation of the volar-ulnar fragment

options.label: D
options.text: First CMC joint arthrodesis

options.label: E
options.text: External fixation spanning the first CMC joint

explanation: Bennett's fracture-dislocation is inherently unstable because the pull of abductor pollicis longus subluxes the metacarpal shaft proximally and radially, leaving the volar-ulnar fragment (held by the anterior oblique ligament) in place. Once closed reduction achieves acceptable alignment, percutaneous K-wire fixation — typically first metacarpal to second metacarpal and/or trapezium — is the standard treatment to maintain reduction without the additional morbidity of open surgery. Cast alone is insufficient to prevent re-subluxation. Open reduction with lag screw (option C) is used when the fragment is large (>25–33% of articular surface) or when closed reduction cannot be achieved. CMC arthrodesis and external fixation are rarely indicated.
learningPoint: Bennett's fracture that achieves acceptable closed reduction is stabilised by percutaneous K-wire fixation (metacarpal to second metacarpal/trapezium).

stem: A 19-year-old student punches a wall and sustains an extra-articular neck fracture of the little finger metacarpal (boxer's fracture) with 45° of volar angulation. There is no rotational deformity and no skin breakdown. According to current BSSH and UK practice guidelines, the most appropriate management is:

options.label: A
options.text: Closed reduction and strapping to the ring finger for 3 weeks

options.label: B
options.text: Closed reduction under haematoma block, intrinsic-plus splint, and buddy strapping

options.label: C
options.text: Operative fixation with K-wires

options.label: D
options.text: Open reduction and plate fixation

options.label: E
options.text: No reduction needed; neighbour strapping and early mobilisation

explanation: The little (fifth) finger metacarpal neck can tolerate significantly more volar angulation than the radial digits because the fourth and fifth CMC joints have approximately 20–30° of compensatory flexion. BSSH guidance and current evidence indicate that up to 50–70° of angulation in the little finger metacarpal neck is functionally acceptable without formal reduction, provided there is no rotational deformity. Simple neighbour-strapping and early mobilisation result in outcomes equivalent to reduction. Operative fixation is reserved for fractures with rotation, skin compromise, or involvement of border rays requiring greater precision.
learningPoint: Little finger metacarpal neck fractures tolerate up to 50–70° of volar angulation; neighbour strapping and early mobilisation are appropriate without formal reduction.

stem: A 45-year-old man undergoes an elective carpal tunnel decompression. Two weeks postoperatively he develops new onset of weakness of thumb abduction and opposition that was not present before the procedure. On examination, there is a positive Tinel's sign at the carpal tunnel incision site, weakness of abductor pollicis brevis and opponens pollicis, and preserved sensation. The most likely diagnosis is:

options.label: A
options.text: Iatrogenic division of the median nerve trunk

options.label: B
options.text: Iatrogenic injury to the recurrent motor (thenar) branch of the median nerve

options.label: C
options.text: Postoperative neuroma-in-continuity of the palmar cutaneous branch

options.label: D
options.text: Reflex sympathetic dystrophy causing pseudoparesis

options.label: E
options.text: Delayed haematoma compressing the median nerve in the carpal tunnel

explanation: Isolated loss of thenar muscle function (APB and opponens) with preserved sensation and a positive Tinel's at the wound 2 weeks post-CTR is most consistent with iatrogenic injury to the recurrent thenar motor branch. The palmar cutaneous branch supplies sensation to the proximal thenar skin (not the same as median sensory territory) and its injury would cause a painful neuroma without motor loss. Division of the median trunk would cause combined sensory and motor loss. Haematoma compression would not explain isolated motor deficit with intact sensation. This pattern is a recognised, albeit uncommon, complication of carpal tunnel release.
learningPoint: Isolated postoperative thenar weakness with preserved sensation after carpal tunnel release suggests iatrogenic injury to the recurrent thenar motor branch.

stem: A 32-year-old marathon runner sustains an undisplaced waist scaphoid fracture confirmed on MRI. She is keen to return to sport as quickly as possible and asks about internal fixation versus casting. Which statement is most accurate when counselling her?

options.label: A
options.text: Percutaneous headless compression screw fixation results in higher union rates compared with cast immobilisation

options.label: B
options.text: Percutaneous fixation allows earlier return to sport but non-union rates are similar between fixation and casting in undisplaced waist fractures

options.label: C
options.text: Casting alone is always preferred in undisplaced fractures; surgery is only indicated for displaced fractures

options.label: D
options.text: Percutaneous fixation doubles the risk of avascular necrosis compared with casting

options.label: E
options.text: Cast immobilisation requires a minimum of 16 weeks to achieve reliable union

explanation: Evidence from RCTs and systematic reviews (including UK studies) shows that union rates for undisplaced scaphoid waist fractures are high (>90%) with both casting and percutaneous headless screw fixation. The primary advantage of surgical fixation is faster return to work and sport (approximately 6–8 weeks vs 12 weeks in cast). Non-union and AVN rates are not significantly different between modalities in undisplaced waist fractures. Patients with occupational or sporting demands may therefore benefit from discussing operative fixation. Casting is not always preferred; current BSSH guidance supports shared decision-making taking patient priorities into account.
learningPoint: For undisplaced scaphoid waist fractures, operative fixation and casting have equivalent union rates; surgery offers faster return to activity.

stem: Theme: Nerve injuries of the hand and wrist. For each clinical scenario, select the single most likely nerve injured from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Anterior interosseous nerve (AIN)

options.label: B
options.text: Posterior interosseous nerve (PIN)

options.label: C
options.text: Ulnar nerve at the wrist (Guyon's canal)

options.label: D
options.text: Ulnar nerve at the medial epicondyle

options.label: E
options.text: Median nerve at the wrist (carpal tunnel)

options.label: F
options.text: Recurrent thenar branch of median nerve

options.label: G
options.text: Superficial radial nerve

emiLeads.stem: A 50-year-old man with a ganglion at the hook of hamate presents with weakness of intrinsic muscles of the little and ring fingers, preserved sensation over the little finger dorsum but absent sensation over the little finger palm.

A 28-year-old woman develops inability to make an 'OK' sign after a forearm laceration. She cannot flex the distal phalanx of the index finger or the interphalangeal joint of the thumb but grip strength is preserved.

A 38-year-old man presents with wrist drop after a tight plaster cast is applied for a Monteggia fracture-dislocation. He has weakness of wrist extension and complete inability to extend the fingers at the MCP joints. Sensation in the first dorsal web space is reduced.

A 55-year-old woman with rheumatoid arthritis has new onset isolated weakness of thumb opposition and abductor pollicis brevis after steroid injection near the distal flexor retinaculum. All sensation is intact.

explanation: Scenario 1: A Guyon's canal lesion distal to the superficial sensory branch affecting only the deep motor branch spares dorsal ulnar sensation (supplied by the dorsal ulnar cutaneous branch arising above the wrist) but ablates palmar sensation and all intrinsics. Scenario 2: AIN is a purely motor branch (no sensory) supplying FDP to index and middle, FPL and pronator quadratus; its palsy produces inability to flex the index DIP and thumb IP — hence the failure of the 'pinch/OK' sign. Scenario 3: PIN (deep branch of radial nerve) passes through the radial tunnel and arcade of Frohse; its palsy produces finger extensor weakness with variable wrist extension (ECRL preserved as it branches before PIN), with possible superficial radial nerve sensory involvement explaining the reduced web space sensation. Scenario 4: The recurrent thenar branch is susceptible to direct trauma or injection injury at the distal retinaculum; its isolated injury causes thenar weakness without sensory change.

stem: Theme: Flexor tendon injury zones. For each clinical scenario, select the single most accurate flexor tendon zone of injury from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Zone I (distal to FDS insertion)

options.label: B
options.text: Zone II (proximal palmar crease to FDS insertion)

options.label: C
options.text: Zone III (distal edge of carpal tunnel to proximal palmar crease)

options.label: D
options.text: Zone IV (within the carpal tunnel)

options.label: E
options.text: Zone V (proximal to carpal tunnel)

options.label: F
options.text: Zone T-I (IP joint of thumb to FPL insertion)

options.label: G
options.text: Zone T-II (MCP joint to IP joint of thumb)

emiLeads.stem: A 35-year-old chef lacerates his palm at the base of the little finger in the distal palmar crease. He cannot flex the MCP or PIP joint of the little finger and has loss of FDP and FDS function.
A 16-year-old rugby player has a 'jersey finger' injury of the ring finger, unable to flex the DIP joint with intact PIP flexion.
A 25-year-old woman sustains a laceration at the midpoint of the proximal phalanx of the middle finger. She cannot flex the DIP joint at all and PIP flexion is reduced but present.
A 40-year-old bricklayer cuts his forearm 4 cm proximal to the wrist crease. He has combined FDP and FDS function loss at the index finger with median nerve sensory loss.

explanation: Zone III (palm) injury: at the distal palmar crease, the flexor sheath has just begun; lacerations here involve both FDS and FDP as they pass through the palm before the fibro-osseous tunnel. Zone I (jersey finger): avulsion of FDP at its insertion into the distal phalanx — only DIP flexion is lost, PIP is maintained by the intact FDS. Zone II: between the start of the flexor sheath at the proximal palmar crease and the FDS insertion at the base of middle phalanx — the classic 'no man's land'; both tendons run in close proximity here. Zone V: proximal to the carpal tunnel, in the forearm; injuries here commonly involve multiple tendons and neurovascular structures together.

stem: Theme: Diagnosis of hand and wrist conditions. For each clinical scenario, select the single most likely diagnosis from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Scapholunate dissociation

options.label: B
options.text: Kienböck's disease

options.label: C
options.text: Lunate dislocation

options.label: D
options.text: Perilunate dislocation

options.label: E
options.text: Triquetrolunate instability

options.label: F
options.text: TFCC tear with DRUJ instability

options.label: G
options.text: Midcarpal instability

emiLeads.stem: A 22-year-old motorcyclist is brought to the emergency department after a high-speed collision. He has a swollen wrist. Lateral radiograph shows the lunate displaced volarly, adopting a 'spilled teacup' configuration. The capitate lies in the radius socket.

A 34-year-old gymnast has chronic central wrist pain and MRI shows diffuse low T1 signal throughout the lunate with partial collapse on sagittal views. Plain radiographs show increased sclerosis and early flattening of the lunate.

A 28-year-old carpenter has a 6-month history of ulnar-sided wrist pain after twisting his wrist forcefully. Examination reveals a painful 'clunk' elicited by ulnar-to-radial deviation, described as the wrist catching and snapping. Plain radiographs are normal.
A 30-year-old woman has a widened scapholunate gap on PA radiograph following a fall. The lateral radiograph shows a DISI pattern (SL angle 75°, lunocapitate angle 30°). No carpal dislocation.

explanation: Lunate dislocation: the 'spilled teacup' or 'piece of pie' sign on lateral view reflects the volar-dislocated lunate rotating; the capitate sits in the lunate fossa of the radius — distinguishing it from perilunate dislocation where the lunate stays aligned with the radius but the capitate dislocates dorsally. Kienböck's: MRI is the gold standard for early diagnosis showing avascular changes before radiographic collapse; MRI flattening with T1 low signal indicates Stage II/III. Midcarpal instability: characterised by a painful 'catch-up clunk' during ulnar deviation as the proximal row snaps from flexion to extension; plain films are normal, diagnosis is clinical and confirmed by fluoroscopy. Scapholunate dissociation: the Terry-Thomas/gap sign plus DISI on lateral view — SL angle >60° (normal 30–60°) confirms dorsal intercalated segment instability.

stem: Theme: Management of distal radius fractures. For each clinical scenario, select the single most appropriate management from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Below-elbow cast in neutral for 6 weeks; no reduction needed

options.label: B
options.text: Closed reduction and below-elbow cast; review at 1 week

options.label: C
options.text: Volar locking plate fixation

options.label: D
options.text: Bridging external fixation

options.label: E
options.text: Non-bridging external fixation

options.label: F
options.text: Percutaneous K-wire fixation and plaster

options.label: G
options.text: Dorsal plating

emiLeads.stem: An 80-year-old woman with moderate dementia sustains a minimally displaced extra-articular distal radius fracture (Colles-type) with only 5° dorsal tilt and 1 mm shortening. She is independently mobile with a walking frame.

A 42-year-old plasterer sustains a displaced intra-articular distal radius fracture with 20° dorsal tilt, 4 mm shortening, and a two-part articular split. Closed reduction achieves 2° dorsal tilt and 1 mm shortening.

A 28-year-old woman has an extra-articular metaphyseal distal radius fracture that is easily reduced but re-displaces to 15° dorsal tilt and 3 mm shortening on a check radiograph at 5 days. Her bone quality is good.

A 70-year-old retired farmer has a severely comminuted intra-articular distal radius fracture that cannot be reduced adequately by closed means, with gross shortening of 10 mm. Volar locking plate is planned but the wrist is too swollen for immediate plating. Definitive surgery will be in 5–7 days.

explanation: Minimally displaced fracture in an elderly low-demand patient with cognitive impairment: cast without reduction is appropriate — attempting reduction is unlikely to succeed long-term and the functional outcome is equivalent to surgery in low-demand elderly patients. Intra-articular displaced fracture in a working-age adult: volar locking plate provides the stable anatomical fixation necessary for articular reduction and early mobilisation. Extra-articular fracture in a young patient that re-displaces in cast: K-wire fixation maintains reduction without the additional dissection of open plating, appropriate for a good-quality bone metaphyseal fracture. Grossly comminuted fracture with planned delayed surgery: bridging external fixation temporarily maintains length and alignment via ligamentotaxis until definitive plating, preventing further soft-tissue damage.

stem: A 28-year-old male recreational footballer presents with a 14-month history of right groin pain, worse with prolonged sitting and hip flexion. Examination reveals pain at 90° flexion with internal rotation (positive FADIR). AP pelvis radiograph shows a crossover sign on the right and an aspherical femoral head-neck junction with an alpha angle of 68°. Which morphological classification best describes this patient's pathoanatomy?

options.label: A
options.text: Pure pincer-type FAI

options.label: B
options.text: Pure cam-type FAI

options.label: C
options.text: Mixed cam-pincer FAI

options.label: D
options.text: Acetabular dysplasia

options.label: E
options.text: Coxa valga with retroversion

explanation: This patient demonstrates both cam morphology (alpha angle >55° indicating aspherical head-neck junction) and pincer morphology (crossover sign indicating focal acetabular retroversion). When both are present, the classification is mixed cam-pincer FAI, which is actually the most common presentation. Pure cam (B) would lack the crossover sign; pure pincer (A) would have a normal alpha angle. Acetabular dysplasia (D) causes under-coverage (low centre-edge angle) rather than over-coverage. Coxa valga (E) is a separate deformity of the femoral neck-shaft angle.
learningPoint: Mixed cam-pincer FAI is the most common presentation; alpha angle >55° defines cam morphology, and the crossover sign indicates focal acetabular retroversion (pincer).
references: Ganz R et al. FAI classification. JBJS 2003

stem: A 24-year-old female gymnast undergoes hip arthroscopy for cam FAI with an alpha angle of 72°. Intraoperatively the surgeon identifies a Outerbridge grade 3 chondral lesion on the anterosuperior acetabulum measuring 15 mm × 12 mm, and a bucket-handle tear of the anterosuperior labrum. Which intraoperative finding is most strongly associated with inferior long-term outcome after femoroplasty?

options.label: A
options.text: Labral tear requiring partial resection

options.label: B
options.text: Synovial hypertrophy

options.label: C
options.text: Full-thickness (grade 4) acetabular chondral loss

options.label: D
options.text: Alpha angle greater than 70°

options.label: E
options.text: Contralateral hip with asymptomatic cam morphology

explanation: Full-thickness (Outerbridge grade 4 / ICRS grade 4) acetabular cartilage loss is the strongest predictor of poor outcome and early conversion to THR after hip arthroscopy for FAI. Labral tears (A) should ideally be repaired rather than resected, but a repaired labrum does not preclude good outcomes. Synovial hypertrophy (B) is a minor finding. A larger alpha angle (D) correlates with extent of cam but is not independently predictive of outcome once femoroplasty is performed. Contralateral asymptomatic morphology (E) is irrelevant to the index hip's outcome.
learningPoint: Full-thickness (grade 4) acetabular cartilage loss at the time of hip arthroscopy for FAI is the single strongest predictor of poor long-term outcome and early conversion to THR.
references: Byrd JWT. Hip arthroscopy outcomes. AJSM 2009

stem: During templating for hip arthroscopy, you review the radiograph of a 31-year-old woman with groin pain. The lateral centre-edge angle (LCEA) on the AP pelvis is 38°, the alpha angle on the Dunn view is 48°, and the acetabular index (Tönnis angle) is 2°. The crossover sign is absent. Which single radiographic feature most strongly suggests pincer-type over-coverage in isolation?

options.label: A
options.text: Alpha angle of 48°

options.label: B
options.text: Lateral centre-edge angle of 38°

options.label: C
options.text: Tönnis angle of 2°

options.label: D
options.text: Absence of the crossover sign

options.label: E
options.text: Femoral head sphericity index within normal limits

explanation: An LCEA >40° indicates global acetabular overcoverage (coxa profunda / protrusio type pincer), and 38° is borderline high. However, of the findings listed, the elevated LCEA (B) is the direct radiographic correlate of over-coverage in global pincer. The Tönnis angle of 2° (C) also suggests relative horizontal acetabulum, but is a supporting rather than defining feature. The alpha angle of 48° (A) is within normal limits (<55°) and argues against significant cam. Absence of crossover sign (D) argues against focal retroversion, not against global overcoverage. Femoral head sphericity (E) is about cam, not pincer.
learningPoint: An LCEA >40° defines global acetabular over-coverage (global pincer); the crossover sign specifically denotes focal acetabular retroversion.
references: Tönnis D. Congenital Dysplasia and Dislocation of the Hip. Springer 1987

subtopic: hip OA and total hip replacement
stem: A 67-year-old woman with symptomatic right hip osteoarthritis undergoes an uncemented total hip replacement via a posterior approach. Intraoperatively, the acetabular component is placed at 45° abduction and 20° anteversion. On day 1 post-operatively she develops acute right hip pain while being helped out of a chair. A radiograph confirms posterior dislocation. Which intraoperative factor most likely contributed to this event?

options.label: A
options.text: Excessive cup abduction angle

options.label: B
options.text: Insufficient cup anteversion

options.label: C
options.text: Posterior capsular repair not performed

options.label: D
options.text: Use of a 28 mm femoral head

options.label: E
options.text: Uncemented fixation of the acetabular component

explanation: The posterior approach carries the highest dislocation rate of standard approaches, and failure to repair the posterior capsule and short external rotators substantially increases instability risk. The cup position described (45° abduction, 20° anteversion) is within the Lewinnek safe zone (abduction 40°±10°, anteversion 15°±10°), so cup malposition is not the primary factor here. Excessive abduction (A) increases edge-loading risk but does not directly cause posterior dislocation. Insufficient anteversion (B) would predispose to posterior dislocation, but 20° is adequate. Small head size (D) lowers the head-neck ratio but is a contributing, not primary, factor when capsule is not repaired. Fixation mode (E) does not affect dislocation risk.
learningPoint: Failure to repair the posterior capsule and external rotators after a posterior approach to the hip significantly increases the risk of posterior dislocation.

subtopic: hip OA and total hip replacement
stem: A 73-year-old man presents to pre-assessment clinic for elective right THR. He reports a 10-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus (HbA1c 74 mmol/mol on metformin), BMI 36 kg/m², and takes warfarin for atrial fibrillation (INR 2.4). Which modifiable risk factor has the STRONGEST evidence for reduction of periprosthetic joint infection if optimised pre-operatively?

options.label: A
options.text: Reduction of BMI below 30 kg/m²

options.label: B
options.text: Bridging anticoagulation with low molecular weight heparin

options.label: C
options.text: Optimisation of HbA1c to below 53 mmol/mol

options.label: D
options.text: Preoperative iron supplementation to achieve Hb >130 g/L

options.label: E
options.text: Switching from warfarin to a direct oral anticoagulant

explanation: Poorly controlled diabetes (HbA1c >53 mmol/mol, approximately 7%) is one of the most consistently modifiable risk factors for PJI, with an HbA1c of 74 mmol/mol representing a substantially elevated risk. Many units use 69 mmol/mol (8.5%) as a threshold for deferral. While BMI >40 is a risk factor, the evidence for requiring pre-op weight loss to below 30 (B is incorrect label — this is answer A) is less robust and practically difficult. Bridging anticoagulation (B) actually increases bleeding risk and is rarely indicated. Preoperative anaemia correction (D) reduces transfusion risk but has weaker evidence for PJI specifically. DOAC switching (E) is a perioperative management issue, not a PJI risk factor.
learningPoint: Optimising HbA1c to <53 mmol/mol pre-operatively is one of the most evidence-based interventions for reducing PJI risk in diabetic patients undergoing THR.

subtopic: hip OA and total hip replacement — surgical approaches
stem: A consultant is performing a direct anterior approach (DAA) total hip replacement on a 61-year-old woman with primary hip OA. While retracting the anterior soft tissues to visualise the acetabulum, the patient develops persistent bleeding from a small arterial vessel running along the anterior capsule. Which vessel is most likely responsible?

options.label: A
options.text: Ascending branch of the lateral femoral circumflex artery

options.label: B
options.text: Medial femoral circumflex artery

options.label: C
options.text: Superior gluteal artery

options.label: D
options.text: Obturator artery

options.label: E
options.text: Inferior epigastric artery

explanation: The ascending (transverse) branch of the lateral femoral circumflex artery (LFCA) runs across the anterior hip capsule and is the vessel most commonly encountered and ligated during the DAA. It lies in the interval between tensor fasciae latae and rectus femoris and must be controlled to obtain adequate exposure. The medial femoral circumflex artery (B) supplies the femoral head posteriorly and is at risk in posterior approaches. The superior gluteal artery (C) is at risk during the lateral/Hardinge approach. The obturator artery (D) is at risk during acetabular reconstruction, not routine anterior retraction. The inferior epigastric artery (E) is an anterior abdominal wall vessel, not relevant here.
learningPoint: The ascending branch of the lateral femoral circumflex artery crosses the anterior hip capsule and must be identified and ligated during the direct anterior approach.

subtopic: avascular necrosis of the femoral head
stem: A 38-year-old male renal transplant recipient on long-term prednisolone 10 mg/day presents with a 6-week history of right hip pain. MRI shows a subchondral crescent sign in the anterosuperior femoral head with no collapse. Plain radiograph of the right hip is normal. According to the ARCO classification, what stage is this?

options.label: A
options.text: ARCO Stage I

options.label: B
options.text: ARCO Stage II

options.label: C
options.text: ARCO Stage III

options.label: D
options.text: ARCO Stage IV

options.label: E
options.text: ARCO Stage 0

explanation: The crescent sign on MRI (or plain film) represents subchondral fracture with separation of the necrotic bone from the overlying articular cartilage, which defines ARCO Stage III — structural failure without flattening of the femoral head contour on the plain radiograph. ARCO Stage I has a normal radiograph and MRI shows marrow oedema/necrosis without crescent sign. Stage II shows radiographic sclerosis/cysts without crescent sign or collapse. Stage IV involves flattening/collapse of the femoral head with joint space narrowing. Stage 0 is histological diagnosis only with no imaging changes.
learningPoint: The crescent sign (subchondral fracture) on MRI defines ARCO Stage III AVN of the femoral head — this represents structural failure and is a critical decision point for joint-preserving versus replacement surgery.

subtopic: avascular necrosis of the femoral head
stem: A 41-year-old woman with systemic lupus erythematosus on hydroxychloroquine and prednisolone presents with bilateral hip pain. MRI confirms bilateral AVN: right femoral head ARCO Stage IIB (25% necrotic area, anterosuperior location, no collapse), left ARCO Stage I. She is otherwise well with good hip range of movement bilaterally. Which treatment for the right hip is most supported by current evidence to delay progression to collapse?

options.label: A
options.text: Protected weight-bearing alone for 3 months

options.label: B
options.text: Core decompression with bone marrow aspirate concentrate (BMAC) augmentation

options.label: C
options.text: Total hip replacement

options.label: D
options.text: Rotational transtrochanteric osteotomy

options.label: E
options.text: Hyperbaric oxygen therapy

explanation: For pre-collapse ARCO Stage I and II lesions of small-to-medium size, core decompression is the best-supported joint-preserving procedure to reduce intraosseous pressure and delay collapse. Augmentation with BMAC (or other osteogenic adjuncts) is increasingly used with evidence of improved collapse-free survival compared to core decompression alone. Protected weight-bearing (A) alone has no randomised evidence of benefit. THR (C) is the correct answer for failed or advanced disease (Stage III-IV) but is not appropriate as a first-line joint-preserving option in a 41-year-old at Stage II. Rotational osteotomy (D) requires specific expertise and is a reasonable option for larger lesions but is not first-line in the UK. Hyperbaric oxygen (E) has insufficient evidence for routine recommendation.
learningPoint: Core decompression, ideally augmented with BMAC, is the most evidence-supported joint-preserving intervention for pre-collapse (ARCO I-II) AVN of the femoral head.

stem: A 78-year-old woman falls from standing height 3 years after a cemented Exeter-Spectron right THR and sustains a periprosthetic fracture around the femoral stem. The radiograph shows a fracture at the tip of the cemented stem with no cement mantle fracture, and a 3 cm distal spiral component. Fluoroscopic stress views confirm the stem is well fixed. What is the Vancouver classification, and what is the recommended management?

options.label: A
options.text: Vancouver A — trochanteric wiring

options.label: B
options.text: Vancouver B1 — open reduction and internal fixation with cerclage wires and a plate

options.label: C
options.text: Vancouver B2 — revision to a long-stem femoral component

options.label: D
options.text: Vancouver B3 — structural allograft and revision stem

options.label: E
options.text: Vancouver C — open reduction and internal fixation distal to the stem

explanation: A fracture at or around the tip of the stem with a well-fixed prosthesis is classified as Vancouver B1. Because the stem is well fixed, revision is not required; ORIF with a locking plate (and often cerclage wires proximal to the plate) is the treatment of choice. Vancouver B2 (C) applies when the stem is loose and fracture is around the stem — requiring revision to a long distally fixed stem. Vancouver B3 (D) is B2 with poor proximal bone stock requiring structural allograft. Vancouver C (E) is a fracture entirely distal to the stem tip — also treated with ORIF but with a plate extending proximal to the tip. Vancouver A (A) is a trochanteric or calcar fracture.
learningPoint: Vancouver B1 periprosthetic femoral fractures (fracture at/around stem, well-fixed prosthesis) are treated with ORIF using a locking plate and cerclage; revision is not required.
references: Duncan CP, Masri BA. Vancouver classification. AAOS 1995

stem: A 71-year-old man with a right uncemented THR inserted 8 years ago is referred after a Vancouver B2 periprosthetic femoral fracture. Intraoperatively the surgeon finds the proximal femoral bone stock to be Paprosky Type 2B. Which revision femoral component is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Impaction bone grafting with a polished tapered cemented stem

options.label: B
options.text: Extensively porous-coated long cementless stem bypassing the fracture by at least two cortical diameters

options.label: C
options.text: Proximal femoral replacement (tumour endoprosthesis)

options.label: D
options.text: Short metaphyseal-fitting cementless stem

options.label: E
options.text: Cement-within-cement revision of the existing stem

explanation: Vancouver B2 with Paprosky 2B bone loss (metaphyseal damage but intact diaphysis) is best managed with a distally-fixed, extensively porous-coated or fluted/tapered titanium long cementless stem that bypasses the fracture by at least two cortical diameters to achieve diaphyseal fixation. This provides immediate stability while the fracture heals. Impaction bone grafting (A) requires an intact cortical tube and is more suited to cemented revision in B2/B3 with specific expertise. Proximal femoral replacement (C) is reserved for Paprosky 3B/4 with very poor proximal bone stock. A short metaphyseal stem (D) cannot span the fracture. Cement-within-cement (E) is for well-fixed cemented stems, not applicable here.
learningPoint: Vancouver B2 periprosthetic fractures with Paprosky 2B bone loss are best treated with a distally-fixed long cementless stem bypassing the fracture by ≥2 cortical diameters.

stem: A 69-year-old woman has a second episode of posterior dislocation of her right THR 18 months post-operatively. The initial surgery was via a posterior approach. Radiographs confirm cup abduction of 47° and anteversion of 12° (within Lewinnek safe zone). CT of the pelvis confirms combined femoral anteversion of 10°. Her muscle bulk on CT appears reduced. What is the most important next investigation before planning revision surgery?

options.label: A
options.text: Aspiration of the hip joint under fluoroscopy for culture and cell count

options.label: B
options.text: Dynamic fluoroscopic assessment of hip impingement and instability

options.label: C
options.text: MRI of the pelvis to assess abductor muscle integrity

options.label: D
options.text: Nuclear medicine bone scan to detect loosening

options.label: E
options.text: Measurement of serum cobalt and chromium levels

explanation: Recurrent dislocation with components in acceptable position and low combined anteversion (10°) points strongly to abductor insufficiency as the underlying cause. MRI of the pelvis to assess gluteus medius and minimus integrity, tendon tears, and muscle atrophy is the most important next step to guide whether abductor repair/reconstruction is needed alongside any revision. Aspiration (A) is important to exclude PJI but is not the single most important investigation when the clinical story points to instability rather than infection. Dynamic fluoroscopy (B) can demonstrate impingement patterns but MRI provides direct tissue assessment. Bone scan (D) is low-yield in a clinically mobile implant. Cobalt/chromium (E) would be relevant if a metal-on-metal bearing was in situ.
learningPoint: Recurrent THR dislocation with correctly positioned components and low combined anteversion should prompt MRI evaluation of abductor muscle and tendon integrity before revision planning.

stem: A 74-year-old man with a history of previous right hip arthroscopy and labral repair presents with an acute dislocation 6 weeks after right THR via a direct anterior approach. Cup anteversion is 22° and abduction 44° on CT. Combined anteversion (cup + stem) is 48°. He dislocates again 3 days after closed reduction, this time anteriorly. Which corrective strategy addresses the most likely cause of recurrent anterior dislocation?

options.label: A
options.text: Increase head size from 36 mm to 40 mm

options.label: B
options.text: Revision of acetabular component to reduce cup anteversion

options.label: C
options.text: Revision of femoral stem to reduce femoral anteversion

options.label: D
options.text: Constrained acetabular liner

options.label: E
options.text: Dual-mobility cup implantation

explanation: Combined anteversion of 48° is excessive (target ~35°±10°) and, combined with a DAA approach (which itself does not breach posterior structures), predisposes to anterior dislocation as the head levers out anteriorly in extension and external rotation. Cup anteversion of 22° is the primary contributor if stem anteversion is approximately 26°. Revising the cup to reduce anteversion to ~15° addresses the root cause. Larger head (A) increases head-neck ratio and helps marginally but does not correct the fundamental malposition. Revising the stem alone (C) would also help if stem anteversion is the greater contributor, but typically cup position is revised first. Constrained liner (D) is a salvage option with high complication rates. Dual-mobility cup (E) is an excellent option for instability but does not address the malposition and risks anterior impingement remaining.
learningPoint: Anterior THR dislocation with combined anteversion >45° is most likely caused by excessive cup or stem anteversion; revision to correct the outlier component is the primary solution.

stem: A 65-year-old woman re-presents 14 months after right THR with increasing hip pain, stiffness, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 68 mm/hr, CRP 32 mg/L, and serum white cell count 9.4 × 10⁹/L. Radiographs show lucency at the cement-bone interface. Hip aspiration yields 6 mL of cloudy fluid with a synovial white cell count of 4,800 cells/µL (78% polymorphonuclear). Culture grows Cutibacterium acnes (Propionibacterium acnes) after 12 days of incubation. According to EBJIS/ICM 2018 criteria, what is the infection likelihood category?

options.label: A
options.text: Not infected

options.label: B
options.text: Inconclusive

options.label: C
options.text: Infected (confirmed)

options.label: D
options.text: Infected (probable)

options.label: E
options.text: Colonisation only — no treatment required

explanation: A positive culture from hip aspiration is a major criterion for PJI under EBJIS/ICM criteria, and a single positive culture of a recognised pathogen (including C. acnes, which is a known PJI organism despite slow growth) combined with elevated synovial WCC (>3,000 cells/µL for chronic PJI) and elevated inflammatory markers establishes confirmed (definite) PJI. C. acnes is not merely a contaminant in this context — prolonged incubation of ≥14 days is specifically recommended to detect it. 'Inconclusive' (B) arises when criteria are partially met. 'Colonisation only' (E) is not a clinical category in EBJIS. 'Not infected' (A) requires all criteria to be negative.
learningPoint: Cutibacterium acnes is a recognised PJI pathogen requiring prolonged culture (≥14 days); a positive aspiration culture combined with elevated synovial WCC and inflammatory markers meets EBJIS confirmed PJI criteria.

stem: A 58-year-old immunocompetent man is diagnosed with acute haematogenous PJI of his right THR 3 years post-implantation. He develops symptoms 10 days ago following a dental extraction. The implants appear well-fixed on radiographs. His CRP is 145 mg/L. He is taken to theatre promptly. Which surgical strategy offers the best chance of implant retention while eradicating infection?

options.label: A
options.text: Irrigation and debridement with retention of all components

options.label: B
options.text: DAIR (Debridement, Antibiotics, and Implant Retention) with modular component exchange

options.label: C
options.text: Single-stage revision with new implants and high-dose local antibiotic cement

options.label: D
options.text: Two-stage revision with antibiotic spacer

options.label: E
options.text: Resection arthroplasty (Girdlestone procedure)

explanation: DAIR with exchange of all modular components (liner, head) is the recommended strategy for acute haematogenous PJI with a short symptom duration (<3-4 weeks), well-fixed implants, and no sinus tract. The modular exchange removes the biofilm-colonised surfaces while retaining the well-fixed stem and cup. Irrigation and debridement without modular exchange (A) has significantly worse outcomes as biofilm persists on the liner and head. Single-stage revision (C) and two-stage revision (D) are preferred for chronic infections or loosened components. Girdlestone (E) is a salvage procedure for failed revision or uncontrolled infection.
learningPoint: DAIR with modular component exchange is the treatment of choice for acute haematogenous PJI with symptom duration <3-4 weeks, well-fixed implants, and no sinus tract.

subtopic: greater trochanteric pain syndrome and abductor tears
stem: A 52-year-old female recreational runner presents with a 4-month history of lateral hip pain radiating to the lateral thigh, worse on walking upstairs and lying on the affected side. Ober's test is positive. There is no groin pain. Hip range of movement is full and pain-free. MRI reveals a high-grade partial-thickness tear of the gluteus medius tendon at the trochanteric footprint with surrounding tendinopathy. What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.label: A
options.text: Corticosteroid injection into the trochanteric bursa

options.label: B
options.text: Platelet-rich plasma injection under ultrasound guidance

options.label: C
options.text: Load management, hip abductor strengthening programme, and iliotibial band stretching

options.label: D
options.text: Open abductor repair with trochanteric reattachment

options.label: E
options.text: Trochanteric bursectomy

explanation: Current evidence (including LEAP trial data) shows that an education-based load management programme with progressive hip abductor and external rotator strengthening is superior to corticosteroid injection alone and equivalent or superior to PRP for greater trochanteric pain syndrome (GTPS) / gluteus medius tendinopathy. Corticosteroid injection (A) provides short-term relief but is inferior at 12 months and may harm the tendon with repeated use. PRP (B) has equivocal evidence and is not first-line. Surgical repair (D) is reserved for full-thickness tears with failed conservative treatment >6 months. Bursectomy (E) is rarely indicated and addresses the secondary, not primary, pathology.
learningPoint: A structured load management and progressive hip abductor strengthening programme is the first-line treatment for greater trochanteric pain syndrome / gluteus medius tendinopathy.
references: Grimaldi A. Scand J Med Sci Sports 2017

subtopic: greater trochanteric pain syndrome and abductor tears
stem: A 59-year-old woman presents with a 2-year history of lateral hip pain and a Trendelenburg gait that has worsened despite 9 months of physiotherapy. MRI shows a complete full-thickness tear of both gluteus medius and gluteus minimus tendons with moderate fatty infiltration (Goutallier grade 2) in gluteus medius and severe atrophy (Goutallier grade 3) in gluteus minimus. Trochanteric footprint is bare. Which factor most strongly predicts inferior outcome from surgical repair?

options.label: A
options.text: Patient age >55 years

options.label: B
options.text: Duration of symptoms >12 months

options.label: C
options.text: Fatty infiltration of gluteus medius ≥ Goutallier grade 3

options.label: D
options.text: Tearing of the gluteus minimus in addition to gluteus medius

options.label: E
options.text: BMI >28 kg/m²

explanation: High-grade fatty infiltration (Goutallier grade 3 or 4) of the gluteus medius on pre-operative MRI is the strongest predictor of poor outcome after abductor repair, as severely fatty-infiltrated muscle cannot regenerate functional tissue even after anatomical reattachment. This is analogous to the prognostic role of fatty infiltration in rotator cuff surgery. Gluteus minimus involvement (D) adds complexity but a functional medius can compensate. Age (A) and symptom duration (B) are less discriminating than muscle quality. BMI (E) affects rehabilitation but not repair integrity as strongly as muscle quality.
learningPoint: Fatty infiltration of gluteus medius ≥ Goutallier grade 3 on pre-operative MRI is the strongest predictor of poor outcome after surgical abductor tendon repair.
references: Grimaldi A. Scand J Med Sci Sports 2017

subtopic: adult acetabular dysplasia and periacetabular osteotomy
stem: A 26-year-old woman presents with right hip pain on exertion. AP pelvis radiograph shows a lateral centre-edge angle (LCEA) of 16°, a Tönnis angle of 16°, and a Tönnis osteoarthritis grade of 1. The sourcil is laterally sloped. MRI shows a labral tear at the anterosuperior chondrolabral junction with no chondral delamination. She has a full, pain-free range of movement. What is the most appropriate treatment?

options.label: A
options.text: Hip arthroscopy with labral repair only

options.label: B
options.text: Bernese periacetabular osteotomy (PAO) with concurrent labral repair

options.label: C
options.text: Salter innominate osteotomy

options.label: D
options.text: Total hip replacement

options.label: E
options.text: Physiotherapy and intra-articular steroid injection

explanation: This patient has symptomatic acetabular dysplasia (LCEA <20°, Tönnis angle >13°, Tönnis OA grade ≤1) with a concurrent labral tear, which is a classic indication for Bernese PAO. The PAO reorients the acetabulum to provide stable femoral head coverage, and the labral tear should be addressed concurrently (either arthroscopically or as an open procedure). Labral repair alone (A) without correcting the underlying dysplasia will fail because the labrum is being overloaded by the inadequate bony coverage. Salter osteotomy (C) is inappropriate in a skeletally mature adult. THR (D) is indicated for Tönnis grade 2-3 OA or failed joint preservation. Physiotherapy alone (E) will not halt dysplasia-driven joint deterioration.
learningPoint: Symptomatic acetabular dysplasia (LCEA <20°) with Tönnis OA grade ≤1 and concurrent labral pathology is best treated with PAO, addressing both the bony coverage and the labrum simultaneously.

subtopic: adult acetabular dysplasia and periacetabular osteotomy
stem: A 29-year-old man with bilateral hip dysplasia undergoes right Bernese PAO. At 6-week follow-up his AP radiograph shows LCEA 28°, Tönnis angle 6°, and the acetabular fragment appears well positioned without loss of fixation. However, he complains of numbness along the lateral thigh. Which nerve is most likely injured?

options.label: A
options.text: Femoral nerve

options.label: B
options.text: Lateral femoral cutaneous nerve of the thigh (LFCN)

options.label: C
options.text: Sciatic nerve

options.label: D
options.text: Obturator nerve

options.label: E
options.text: Superior gluteal nerve

explanation: Lateral thigh numbness is the hallmark of lateral femoral cutaneous nerve of the thigh (LFCN) injury, which is the most common neurological complication of PAO, occurring in 10-15% of cases. It is typically a traction or entrapment neuropraxia from retraction during the ilioinguinal approach. Femoral nerve (A) injury causes quadriceps weakness and anterior thigh numbness. Sciatic nerve (C) injury causes posterior leg symptoms. Obturator nerve (D) injury causes medial thigh numbness and adductor weakness. Superior gluteal nerve (E) injury causes abductor weakness and Trendelenburg gait.
learningPoint: Lateral femoral cutaneous nerve (LFCN) injury, presenting as lateral thigh numbness, is the most common neurological complication of PAO, occurring in 10-15% of procedures.

stem: During templating for a right THR, you note the pre-operative AP pelvis radiograph was taken with both legs internally rotated 15°. The measured pre-operative femoral offset is 42 mm. The true clinical offset on a properly calibrated radiograph should be approximately what value, and what error has the internal rotation introduced?

options.label: A
options.text: True offset is approximately 42 mm; internal rotation does not affect offset measurement

options.label: B
options.text: True offset is greater than 42 mm; internal rotation foreshortens the projected offset

options.label: C
options.text: True offset is less than 42 mm; internal rotation magnifies the projected offset

options.label: D
options.text: True offset is approximately 42 mm but leg-length discrepancy will be overestimated

options.label: E
options.text: True offset is greater than 42 mm; internal rotation magnifies the projected offset

explanation: With the femoral neck in anteversion (typically 10-15°), internal rotation of the leg profiles the neck in its true length on AP projection, which is the standard positioning for templating (15° internal rotation is actually the recommended position for hips with normal anteversion). However, if the femoral neck is already anteverted and the leg is internally rotated beyond neutral, the projected offset is shortened (foreshortened) because the neck appears more perpendicular to the beam axis. Internal rotation projects the greater trochanter posteriorly, reducing the apparent offset. Therefore, the true offset is greater than the measured 42 mm. This is a key templating pitfall that can lead to under-offsetting the reconstruction.
learningPoint: Internal rotation of the leg during AP pelvis radiography foreshortens the projected femoral offset; the true offset is greater than measured, and this must be accounted for during templating.

stem: A 68-year-old woman undergoes right THR for OA with pre-operative templating confirming a 12 mm leg-length discrepancy (right leg short). Post-operatively she reports persistent ipsilateral limb lengthening sensation and on examination the right leg is clinically 10 mm longer than the left. Radiographs show the hip centre is 8 mm medial and 5 mm superior to the pre-templated position. Which intraoperative manoeuvre most likely contributed to the relative lengthening?

options.label: A
options.text: Use of a larger femoral head trial than planned

options.label: B
options.text: Medialisation of the acetabular cup beyond the templated position

options.label: C
options.text: Superior placement of the acetabular cup

options.label: D
options.text: Selection of a lower neck cut than planned

options.label: E
options.text: Use of a cemented rather than uncemented acetabular component

explanation: Superior placement of the acetabular cup raises the hip rotation centre, which shortens the effective limb length (leg appears shorter) — but combined with a high neck cut or high offset stem this can result in net lengthening if the component placement is not compensated at the femoral side. However, superior cup placement specifically produces a higher hip centre, and if the femoral neck cut is not lowered to compensate, the leg is effectively lengthened. Medialisation (B) reduces offset but does not lengthen the leg. A larger head trial (A) affects jump distance but not limb length. A lower neck cut (D) shortens the leg. Fixation mode (E) is irrelevant to limb length.
learningPoint: Superior placement of the acetabular cup raises the hip centre, and without a corresponding lower neck cut, produces relative ipsilateral leg lengthening.

stem: A 55-year-old man with a right uncemented THR implanted 11 years ago with a conventional polyethylene liner presents with increasing right hip pain. Radiographs show a 6 mm eccentric medial joint space narrowing and expansile osteolytic lesions in both the acetabular and femoral zones (Zones 1, 2, and 7 on the femoral side). Which mechanism is primarily responsible for the osteolysis?

options.label: A
options.text: Corrosion products from a metal-on-metal bearing

options.label: B
options.text: Cement particle disease from a cemented stem

options.label: C
options.text: Macrophage-mediated bone resorption in response to ultra-high-molecular-weight polyethylene (UHMWPE) wear particles

options.label: D
options.text: Stress shielding from the uncemented femoral stem

options.label: E
options.text: Loosening of the acetabular cup due to inadequate primary press-fit

explanation: Conventional UHMWPE generates micron-sized and sub-micron polyethylene wear particles at a rate of approximately 50-150 mg/year. These particles are phagocytosed by macrophages, triggering a cytokine cascade (TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6, RANKL) that activates osteoclasts, causing periprosthetic bone resorption and expansile osteolytic lesions — the classic particle disease pathway. Eccentric joint space narrowing confirms the polyethylene wear as the source. This occurs with conventional UHMWPE but is markedly reduced with highly cross-linked polyethylene (HXLPE). Metal-on-metal corrosion (A) produces a different picture (ARMD, pseudotumour). Cement disease (B) would require cemented components. Stress shielding (D) produces proximal cortical thinning, not expansile osteolysis. Cup loosening alone (E) does not explain femoral osteolysis.
learningPoint: Expansile periprosthetic osteolysis with eccentric joint space narrowing on THR radiographs is the hallmark of UHMWPE wear particle disease, driven by macrophage-mediated cytokine activation and osteoclast bone resorption.

stem: A 48-year-old male construction worker is being counselled for left THR for end-stage hip OA secondary to Legg-Calvé-Perthes disease. He is 172 cm tall, weighs 94 kg (BMI 32), and is very active. The surgeon wishes to minimise wear and maximise longevity. Which bearing combination offers the best evidence for low wear rates in this clinical scenario?

options.label: A
options.text: Large-diameter metal-on-metal (MoM) 40 mm bearing

options.label: B
options.text: Conventional UHMWPE on a cobalt-chrome femoral head

options.label: C
options.text: Highly cross-linked polyethylene (HXLPE) on a 36 mm ceramic (alumina composite) femoral head

options.label: D
options.text: Ceramic-on-ceramic (CoC) alumina-zirconia composite bearing

options.label: E
options.text: Ceramic-on-conventional-polyethylene bearing

explanation: For a young, active, high-demand male patient, ceramic-on-ceramic (CoC) using modern alumina-zirconia composite (e.g., Delta ceramic) has the lowest in-vitro and in-vivo wear rates of any current bearing couple and is the most durable option. Modern CoC has a fracture rate well below 0.05% and virtual elimination of osteolysis. HXLPE on ceramic (C) is an excellent and widely used alternative with near-zero measurable wear and is often preferred for its lower noise risk. Large-diameter MoM (A) is contraindicated in the UK following MHRA/NICE guidance due to ARMD risk. Conventional UHMWPE (B) has unacceptably high wear for a young active patient. Ceramic on conventional PE (E) reduces wear vs CoCr on PE but significantly less than HXLPE or CoC.
learningPoint: For young, active patients, ceramic-on-ceramic (alumina composite) and HXLPE on ceramic are the most wear-resistant bearing options; large-diameter MoM is contraindicated under current UK (MHRA/NICE) guidance.

stem: A 62-year-old woman attends a hip MoM surveillance clinic 7 years after bilateral large-diameter (48 mm) metal-on-metal THR. She is asymptomatic bilaterally. Her right serum cobalt is 4.8 µg/L and chromium is 3.2 µg/L. Left serum cobalt is 2.1 µg/L and chromium 1.9 µg/L. MARS MRI of the right hip shows a 25 mm periprosthetic fluid collection with no solid component and no evidence of muscle necrosis. What is the most appropriate next step for the right hip?

options.label: A
options.text: Discharge — pseudotumour is small and patient is asymptomatic

options.label: B
options.text: Increase MRI surveillance to 6-monthly intervals

options.label: C
options.text: Aspiration of the pseudotumour for cytology and metal ion levels

options.label: D
options.text: Refer for consideration of revision surgery given elevated ions and fluid collection

options.label: E
options.text: Prescribe oral vitamin E supplementation and repeat MRI in 12 months

explanation: Under MHRA 2017 guidance, patients with large-diameter MoM THR and serum cobalt or chromium >4 µg/L, or patients with either ion between 2-4 µg/L AND a symptomatic pseudotumour or imaging abnormality, should be referred for revision consideration. This patient has cobalt >4 µg/L AND an MRI-confirmed fluid collection — both meeting the threshold for referral, even though she is currently asymptomatic. Asymptomatic pseudotumours can progress rapidly. Discharge (A) is inappropriate given elevated ions and imaging findings. More frequent surveillance (B) delays appropriate action. Aspiration (C) may be performed pre-revision but is not the primary next management step. Vitamin E (E) has no evidence in this context.
learningPoint: MHRA 2017 guidance: MoM THR with serum cobalt >4 µg/L, or cobalt 2-4 µg/L with imaging abnormality, requires referral for revision consideration even in asymptomatic patients.

stem: A 55-year-old man underwent a metal-on-metal hip resurfacing (MoM HRA) 9 years ago. At annual surveillance he reports exertional breathlessness and fatigue. Blood tests reveal: Hb 96 g/L (normocytic), serum cobalt 11.2 µg/L, serum chromium 9.8 µg/L, thyroid function normal, serum ferritin 320 µg/L. Echocardiogram shows dilated cardiomyopathy with ejection fraction 32%. Which systemic manifestation of metal ion toxicity is most likely responsible for the cardiomyopathy?

options.label: A
options.text: Chromium-induced polycythaemia

options.label: B
options.text: Cobalt-induced cardiomyopathy (cobalt toxicity)

options.label: C
options.text: Titanium particle embolisation

options.label: D
options.text: Iron deficiency secondary to chronic blood loss

options.label: E
options.text: Autoimmune myocarditis triggered by cobalt sensitisation

explanation: Cobalt toxicity (cobaltism) causes a syndrome that includes dilated cardiomyopathy, hypothyroidism, polycythaemia, and peripheral neuropathy. The cardiac manifestation is a direct toxic cardiomyopathy (not autoimmune) caused by cobalt's interference with mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation in cardiomyocytes. Historical cases of cobalt cardiomyopathy were documented in beer drinkers (cobalt added as foam stabiliser) before recognition in MoM patients. The serum cobalt of 11.2 µg/L is markedly elevated, consistent with causation. Chromium-induced polycythaemia (A) is not a recognised syndrome. Titanium embolisation (C) applies to titanium implants, not cobalt-chromium bearings. Iron deficiency (D) would show low ferritin, not 320 µg/L. Autoimmune myocarditis (E) is not the mechanism of cobalt-related cardiomyopathy.
learningPoint: Cobalt toxicity (cobaltism) from MoM implants can cause systemic toxicity including dilated cardiomyopathy via direct mitochondrial toxicity; serum cobalt >7 µg/L requires urgent cardiology review.

subtopic: hip OA and total hip replacement
stem: A 70-year-old woman is listed for elective left THR. She has a history of a previous left total knee replacement (TKR) through a midline incision. Post-operative VTE prophylaxis is being planned. She has no history of VTE, bleeding disorder, or renal impairment (eGFR 78 mL/min). What is the recommended duration of pharmacological VTE prophylaxis post-THR according to NICE guidance?

options.label: A
options.text: 10 days with low molecular weight heparin (LMWH)

options.label: B
options.text: 14 days with rivaroxaban

options.label: C
options.text: 28-35 days with LMWH or rivaroxaban

options.label: D
options.text: 6 weeks with warfarin (target INR 2-3)

options.label: E
options.text: 90 days with aspirin 75 mg

explanation: NICE NG89 (updated 2018) recommends extended pharmacological VTE prophylaxis of 28-35 days following THR using either LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin) or rivaroxaban (or other approved DOACs). This extended duration reflects the prolonged thrombotic risk following hip arthroplasty compared to knee (10-14 days). Mechanical prophylaxis (anti-embolism stockings, foot pumps) should also be used. Ten days (A) is the recommendation for TKR. Fourteen days (B) under-treats THR risk. Warfarin (D) is no longer recommended as first-line. Aspirin alone (E) has insufficient evidence for THR in standard-risk patients per NICE NG89, although it is used in some ERAS protocols for low-risk patients at some UK centres.
learningPoint: NICE NG89 recommends 28-35 days of pharmacological VTE prophylaxis (LMWH or DOAC) following THR; this is distinct from the 10-14 day recommendation for TKR.

stem: A 52-year-old woman underwent THR with a ceramic-on-ceramic (CoC) bearing 6 years ago.
