She returns with a 4-month history of a squeaking noise from the right hip, especially when standing up from a seated position. The hip is not painful and is clinically stable. Radiographs show the components are well fixed with no osteolysis. What is the most important initial investigation to determine the cause of the noise?

options.label: A
options.text: MARS MRI of the hip

options.label: B
options.text: Serum cobalt and chromium levels

options.label: C
options.text: CT of the hip to assess cup and stem anteversion

options.label: D
options.text: Ultrasound-guided aspiration for synovial WCC and culture

options.label: E
options.text: Fluoroscopic assessment of hip stability

explanation: Squeaking in CoC THR is a recognised phenomenon with an incidence of 0.5-10% depending on series. The most important modifiable cause is malposition of the acetabular cup (typically excessive abduction >50° or insufficient anteversion) causing edge loading of the ceramic, which generates stripe wear and squeaking. CT assessment of cup and stem anteversion is the most important initial investigation because it identifies a correctable cause. MARS MRI (A) is used to detect ARMD/pseudotumour in metal implants and is not the primary investigation here. Cobalt/chromium (B) is irrelevant for a ceramic bearing. Aspiration (D) would be indicated if infection were suspected but there is no clinical suggestion here. Fluoroscopy (E) assesses instability, not bearing noise.
learningPoint: Squeaking in ceramic-on-ceramic THR is most commonly caused by cup malposition (excess abduction or insufficient anteversion); CT assessment of combined anteversion is the most important initial investigation.

stem: Theme: Morphological Features and Classification in Hip Preservation. For each scenario, select the single most likely radiological or morphological finding from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Alpha angle >55° on Dunn 45° view

options.label: B
options.text: Lateral centre-edge angle <20°

options.label: C
options.text: Crossover sign with positive posterior wall sign

options.label: D
options.text: Coxa valga (NSA >140°) with lateral head uncoverage

options.label: E
options.text: Pistol-grip deformity of the femoral head-neck junction

options.label: F
options.text: Acetabular protrusio with Tönnis angle <0°

options.label: G
options.text: LCEA >40° without crossover sign

options.label: H
options.text: Tönnis osteoarthritis grade 3 with joint space <1 mm

emiLeads.stem: A 23-year-old female university hockey player presents with groin pain on flexion. AP pelvis shows a crossover sign with the posterior wall passing medial to the centre of the femoral head. Her LCEA is 34° and alpha angle is 50°.

A 27-year-old male rugby player has a 9-month history of deep groin pain and restricted internal rotation. Dunn view shows a flattening of the anterosuperior femoral head-neck junction with alpha angle 74°. AP pelvis is otherwise unremarkable with LCEA 28°.

A 32-year-old woman presents with bilateral hip pain. AP pelvis shows bilateral acetabular floors medial to the ilioischial line. Tönnis angles are -4° bilaterally. LCEA is 48° bilaterally. No crossover sign.

A 19-year-old woman with neonatal dysplasia treated conservatively presents with activity-related groin pain. Her AP pelvis shows LCEA 15°, Tönnis angle 18°, and a sloped laterally oriented sourcil with a normal femoral head-neck junction.

explanation: C: The crossover sign (retroversion) combined with the posterior wall sign (posterior wall medial to femoral head centre) indicates focal retroversion with insufficient posterior coverage — classic focal pincer. A: Alpha angle 74° on Dunn view defines cam morphology without pincer element. F: Bilateral floors medial to ilioischial line = protrusio acetabuli; Tönnis angle <0° = horizontal or horizontal-retrograde acetabulum — global overcoverage of protrusio type. B: LCEA 15° (< 20°) and Tönnis angle 18° (>13°) define acetabular dysplasia.

subtopic: periprosthetic fracture and periprosthetic joint infection
stem: Theme: Periprosthetic Fracture Classification and Management. For each scenario, select the most appropriate Vancouver classification and management from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Vancouver AG — tension-band wiring of greater trochanter

options.label: B
options.text: Vancouver AL — cable-plate fixation of calcar fracture

options.label: C
options.text: Vancouver B1 — ORIF with locking plate and cerclage wires

options.label: D
options.text: Vancouver B2 — revision to a long distally-fixed cementless stem

options.label: E
options.text: Vancouver B3 — structural allograft and megaprosthesis

options.label: F
options.text: Vancouver C — ORIF with plate extending proximal to the stem tip

options.label: G
options.text: Vancouver B2 — impaction bone grafting with a cemented long stem

options.label: H
options.text: Non-operative management with protected weight-bearing

emiLeads.stem: A 74-year-old woman sustains a periprosthetic fracture of her right THR following a low-energy fall. Radiographs show a fracture at the level of the stem tip of a cemented Exeter stem; the cement mantle is intact. Stress fluoroscopy confirms the stem is stable. Bone stock is adequate.

An 80-year-old man with a right uncemented THR inserted 12 years ago falls and sustains a spiral fracture entirely distal to the stem tip, which ends at the mid-femoral diaphysis. The implant appears well fixed. Bone stock is good.

A 69-year-old woman with a right uncemented THR presents after a fall. Radiographs show a fracture around the stem with significant proximal femoral comminution and segmental bone loss (Paprosky 3B). The stem is loose on stress views. Previous revision surgery has been performed twice.

A 76-year-old woman with a right THR presents with an undisplaced fracture of the greater trochanter identified on post-operative radiograph after a minor stumble on day 7 post-primary surgery. The trochanter fragment is minimally displaced (2 mm). The stem appears well fixed.

explanation: C: Fracture at stem tip with well-fixed cemented stem = Vancouver B1; ORIF is appropriate. F: Fracture entirely distal to the stem tip = Vancouver C; ORIF with plate bridging proximal to the stem is required. E: Loose stem with Paprosky 3B bone loss = Vancouver B3; structural allograft with megaprosthesis or tumour prosthesis is required. H: Minimally displaced greater trochanteric fracture (Vancouver AG) with <5mm displacement can be managed non-operatively with protected weight-bearing and hip precautions.

stem: Theme: Risk Factors and Staging of Avascular Necrosis of the Femoral Head. For each clinical scenario, select the most appropriate ARCO stage from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: ARCO Stage 0 — normal imaging, histological diagnosis only

options.label: B
options.text: ARCO Stage I — normal plain radiograph, MRI/bone scan positive

options.label: C
options.text: ARCO Stage II — radiographic sclerosis/cysts, no crescent sign or collapse

options.label: D
options.text: ARCO Stage III — crescent sign present, no flattening of femoral head contour

options.label: E
options.text: ARCO Stage IV — flattening/collapse of femoral head with joint space narrowing

options.label: F
options.text: ARCO Stage V — bilateral advanced collapse with protrusio

emiLeads.stem: A 45-year-old man with a history of heavy alcohol use (>30 units/week) presents with a 2-week history of hip pain. Plain AP radiograph and lateral radiograph of the hip are entirely normal. MRI shows a low-signal band on T1-weighted imaging in the anterosuperior femoral head with a high-signal inner border ('double-line sign') on T2-weighted imaging.

A 38-year-old woman on long-term prednisolone for inflammatory bowel disease develops right hip pain. AP hip radiograph shows a subchondral lucent crescent in the weightbearing dome of the femoral head. The femoral head contour appears preserved on radiograph.

A 52-year-old man presents with right hip pain. His radiograph shows mixed sclerosis and cysts in the anterosuperior femoral head with no crescent sign and a preserved femoral head sphericity. Joint space is maintained.

A 60-year-old woman with sickle cell disease presents with severe bilateral hip pain. AP pelvis shows flattening of both femoral heads with superior joint space narrowing bilaterally and secondary OA changes.

explanation: B: Normal radiograph with MRI showing the double-line sign (T1 low signal band, T2 inner high signal) is diagnostic of Stage I AVN — pre-radiographic, positive on MRI. D: The subchondral crescent sign with preserved femoral head contour = Stage III; crescent indicates subchondral fracture but no collapse yet. C: Radiographic sclerosis and cysts without crescent sign = Stage II. E: Flattening/collapse with joint space narrowing = Stage IV (bilateral involvement is not a separate ARCO stage — ARCO does not have a Stage V).

stem: Theme: Diagnosis and Investigation of Periprosthetic Joint Infection after THR. For each scenario, select the single most appropriate next investigation or diagnostic criterion from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Serum CRP and ESR

options.label: B
options.text: Hip aspiration for synovial WCC, differential, and culture

options.label: C
options.text: MARS MRI of the hip

options.label: D
options.text: Labelled white cell nuclear medicine scan

options.label: E
options.text: Intraoperative frozen section (≥5 PMNs per high-power field in ≥5 fields)

options.label: F
options.text: Synovial fluid alpha-defensin lateral flow assay

options.label: G
options.text: Periprosthetic tissue culture (≥2 of ≥5 samples growing same organism)

options.label: H
options.text: Plain radiograph series (AP and lateral)

emiLeads.stem: A 67-year-old man re-presents 3 years after right THR with worsening hip pain and a 3-week history of hip swelling. CRP is 94 mg/L and ESR 72 mm/hr. The surgeon wishes to confirm PJI and identify the causative organism prior to planning revision surgery.

A 61-year-old woman is undergoing revision THR for presumed aseptic loosening. Intraoperatively, the surgeon has extracted the femoral stem and collected tissue samples. She wants real-time intraoperative confirmation that infection is or is not present before proceeding.

A 58-year-old man with a right THR has elevated CRP 18 mg/L at 8 weeks post-operatively and ongoing hip pain. Hip aspiration synovial WCC is 2,800 cells/µL. The culture from aspiration has grown no organisms at 5 days. The surgeon is uncertain whether infection is present and wants a further rapid biomarker test on residual synovial fluid.

A 72-year-old woman with bilateral THR undergoes revision of the right hip. Intraoperatively, 5 tissue samples are taken. At 14 days, 3 of the 5 samples grow coagulase-negative Staphylococcus epidermidis. The clinical team are unsure whether this represents true infection or contamination.

explanation: B: Aspiration for synovial WCC, differential, and culture is the key pre-operative investigation to confirm PJI and identify the organism. E: Intraoperative frozen section with ≥5 PMN/HPF in ≥5 fields is a validated real-time intraoperative diagnostic criterion for PJI (ICMS/ICM 2018). F: Alpha-defensin lateral flow assay is an antimicrobial peptide test on synovial fluid with high sensitivity and specificity for PJI; useful when culture is negative/inconclusive. G: The EBJIS/ICM criterion for culture-based confirmed PJI requires ≥2 of ≥5 periprosthetic tissue samples growing the same organism — 3/5 positive for S. epidermidis meets this threshold and constitutes confirmed PJI.

stem: A 6-week-old girl attends her routine neonatal check. She was born at term by normal vaginal delivery and is the first child of a mother with a known history of hip dysplasia. On examination, the Ortolani test is positive on the left. Ultrasound shows a Graf Type IIa hip on the left with an alpha angle of 52°. What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Discharge with routine 6-month developmental check

options.label: B
options.text: Commence Pavlik harness treatment immediately

options.label: C
options.text: Repeat ultrasound in 6 weeks with observation only

options.label: D
options.text: Refer for MRI hip to confirm diagnosis

options.label: E
options.text: Proceed to closed reduction and spica casting

explanation: A Graf Type IIa hip in infants under 3 months with an alpha angle of 50–59° represents physiological immaturity and is expected to remodel spontaneously. Repeat ultrasound in 6 weeks is appropriate to confirm natural progression. Pavlik harness (B) is indicated for Graf IIb or worse (alpha <50°) or confirmed instability. Discharge (A) would be inappropriate given the risk factors and positive Ortolani. MRI (D) is not first-line for this age group; ultrasound is the gold standard. Spica casting (E) is reserved for failed harness treatment or late-presenting dislocation.
learningPoint: Graf Type IIa hips (alpha 50–59°) in infants under 3 months are physiologically immature and warrant surveillance ultrasound rather than immediate Pavlik harness.

stem: A 14-month-old girl is brought by her parents who have noticed she has been walking since 11 months but with a limp and a waddling gait. She was born in a breech position at 38 weeks. Examination reveals a positive Trendelenburg sign on the right, shortening of the right leg, and limited abduction. Plain radiograph shows a dislocated right hip with the femoral head lying superior and lateral to the acetabulum. The Perkin's line and Hilgenreiner's line confirm dislocation. What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.label: A
options.text: Immediate Pavlik harness

options.label: B
options.text: Traction followed by closed reduction and hip spica under general anaesthetic

options.label: C
options.text: Open reduction via medial approach without prior traction

options.label: D
options.text: Observation and repeat imaging at 18 months

options.label: E
options.text: Abduction splint (Denis Browne) for 3 months

explanation: In a walking child over 12 months with established dislocation, Pavlik harness (A) is ineffective and risks AVN. The standard approach is a period of skin or skeletal traction to bring the femoral head down to the level of the triradiate cartilage, followed by closed reduction under general anaesthetic with arthrogram and hip spica. Open reduction (C) may ultimately be needed but traction first reduces soft tissue tension and AVN risk. Observation (D) is inappropriate with confirmed dislocation. Denis Browne splint (E) has no role in established dislocation.
learningPoint: Late-presenting DDH in walking children (>12 months) requires traction to lower the femoral head before attempted closed reduction under anaesthesia, to reduce the risk of avascular necrosis.

stem: A 6-year-old boy presents with a 3-week history of left hip pain and a limp. He is afebrile and systemically well. Examination shows reduced internal rotation and abduction of the left hip. Plain radiographs reveal increased density of the femoral head with a subchondral crescent sign. ESR and CRP are normal. What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.label: A
options.text: Urgent joint aspiration under general anaesthetic

options.label: B
options.text: CT scan of the pelvis

options.label: C
options.text: MRI of the hip

options.label: D
options.text: Technetium bone scan

options.label: E
options.text: Hip ultrasound for effusion guided aspiration

explanation: The clinical and radiographic picture is consistent with Perthes disease. MRI provides the most accurate assessment of femoral head viability, extent of avascular necrosis, and articular congruency without radiation, making it the preferred next investigation to stage and guide treatment. Joint aspiration (A) is indicated when septic arthritis is suspected; normal inflammatory markers effectively exclude this. CT (B) provides detail of bony architecture but uses radiation and does not assess cartilage. Bone scan (D) was historically used but is superseded by MRI. Ultrasound-guided aspiration (E) is inappropriate when septic arthritis is not suspected.
learningPoint: MRI is the investigation of choice in Perthes disease to determine the extent of avascular necrosis and guide containment decisions.

stem: A 9-year-old girl is diagnosed with Perthes disease of the right hip. MRI confirms total head involvement (Catterall Group 4) and the Herring lateral pillar is classified as Group C. She has limited abduction (30°) and internal rotation (10°). What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Observation with analgesia and activity restriction only

options.label: B
options.text: Physiotherapy and non-weight-bearing with crutches for 6 months

options.label: C
options.text: Femoral varus derotation osteotomy or Salter pelvic osteotomy for containment

options.label: D
options.text: Core decompression of the femoral head

options.label: E
options.text: Total hip replacement

explanation: This girl is aged over 8 years (the most important prognostic factor), has Herring Group C disease (worst prognosis), total head involvement, and reduced range of movement — all high-risk features. Surgical containment via femoral varus derotation osteotomy or Salter innominate osteotomy improves femoral head congruency and long-term outcome in this group. Observation alone (A) is acceptable only for younger children with Group A or B disease. Non-weight-bearing (B) does not achieve containment. Core decompression (D) is used in adult AVN, not Perthes. THR (E) is not appropriate in a 9-year-old.
learningPoint: Children over 8 years with Herring Group C Perthes disease have poor prognosis with conservative management and benefit from surgical containment osteotomy.

stem: A 13-year-old obese boy presents with a 6-week history of left groin and knee pain. He walks with an antalgic gait with the left foot externally rotated. On examination, passive flexion of the hip results in obligatory external rotation. AP and frog-lateral radiographs confirm a posterior–inferior slip of the left capital femoral epiphysis. He can bear weight. What is the correct classification and initial management?

options.label: A
options.text: Unstable SCFE; immediate bed rest and traction then in-situ pinning within 24 hours

options.label: B
options.text: Stable SCFE; urgent in-situ single cannulated screw fixation

options.label: C
options.text: Stable SCFE; Pavlik harness immobilisation for 6 weeks

options.label: D
options.text: Stable SCFE; open reduction and valgus osteotomy

options.label: E
options.text: Unstable SCFE; immediate MRI then physiotherapy

explanation: The ability to bear weight classifies this as a stable SCFE (Loder classification). Stable SCFE carries a low AVN risk and is treated with urgent in-situ single cannulated screw fixation to prevent further slip. Unstable SCFE (A) refers to inability to bear weight even with crutches and carries significantly higher AVN risk. Pavlik harness (C) has no role in SCFE. Open reduction (D) risks AVN and is generally avoided in acute SCFE. MRI then physiotherapy (E) would be inappropriate for confirmed SCFE.
learningPoint: Stable SCFE (weight-bearing possible) is treated with urgent in-situ single cannulated screw fixation; Loder classification guides urgency and AVN risk stratification.

stem: A 12-year-old girl presents to the emergency department unable to walk following acute onset of severe left hip pain. She was complaining of intermittent left knee pain for the past 4 weeks. Radiographs confirm an unstable left SCFE with a slip angle of 55° on the lateral view. Her contralateral right hip shows a slip angle of 15° on the lateral. What is the best management strategy?

options.label: A
options.text: Urgent in-situ pinning of left hip only; right hip observation

options.label: B
options.text: Urgent in-situ pinning of left hip; prophylactic pinning of right hip at same sitting

options.label: C
options.text: Urgent open reduction and internal fixation of left hip; prophylactic pinning of right hip

options.label: D
options.text: Traction for 6 weeks then in-situ pinning of both hips

options.label: E
options.text: Subcapital osteotomy of left hip; prophylactic pinning of right hip

explanation: For an unstable SCFE, urgent in-situ pinning is recommended to prevent AVN; open reduction (C, E) significantly increases AVN risk and is not standard for unstable slips. The right hip has a 15° slip on the lateral, confirming a contralateral pre-slip or mild slip. Given the risk of contralateral slip (especially in younger patients, girls, and those with endocrine associations), prophylactic fixation of the right hip at the same sitting is recommended. Traction (D) is no longer a first-line temporising measure. Observation of a contralateral slip (A) risks further progression and future avascular necrosis.
learningPoint: Unstable SCFE requires urgent in-situ pinning; prophylactic contralateral pinning is recommended when a contralateral slip is confirmed or the risk of progression is high.

stem: A 7-year-old boy falls from playground equipment onto his outstretched hand. He attends the emergency department with a swollen elbow. Plain radiographs confirm a Gartland Type III supracondylar fracture of the humerus with complete posteromedial displacement. On examination his radial pulse is absent, the hand is pink and warm, and capillary refill time is 1 second. What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Urgent open reduction and internal fixation with exposure of the brachial artery

options.label: B
options.text: Immediate closed reduction and percutaneous Kirschner wire fixation; reassess vascularity

options.label: C
options.text: Above-elbow backslab immobilisation and admission for 24-hour vascular observation

options.label: D
options.text: Urgent vascular surgery referral before any orthopaedic intervention

options.label: E
options.text: Collar and cuff with outpatient review in 5–7 days

explanation: This is a Gartland III fracture with a pulseless but perfused (pink, warm) hand — the 'pink pulseless' scenario. Per BOAST guidelines, the correct management is urgent closed reduction and K-wire fixation; the pulse returns in the majority of cases once the fracture is reduced. If the hand remains pulseless after anatomical reduction, vascular exploration is then indicated. Open exploration before reduction (A, D) is premature when the hand remains viable. Backslab alone (C) or collar and cuff (E) are wholly inappropriate for a displaced Gartland III.
learningPoint: In a 'pink pulseless' hand with a Gartland III supracondylar fracture, urgent closed reduction and K-wire fixation restores the pulse in most cases; vascular exploration is reserved for a pulseless cold hand or persistent pulselessness after reduction.

stem: Following K-wire fixation of a Gartland Type II supracondylar fracture in a 5-year-old girl, you are reviewing the post-operative radiograph. On the lateral view, the anterior humeral line passes anterior to the middle third of the capitellum. On the AP view there is 5° of varus. The wires are in satisfactory position. What does this indicate?

options.label: A
options.text: Acceptable reduction; no further action needed

options.label: B
options.text: Hyperextension deformity suggesting posterior tilt of the distal fragment that should be corrected

options.label: C
options.text: Normal anatomical variant; the anterior humeral line position is within acceptable range

options.label: D
options.text: Cubitus valgus requiring corrective osteotomy now

options.label: E
options.text: Malrotation requiring immediate wire removal and re-fixation

explanation: Normally the anterior humeral line bisects the middle third of the capitellum. When it passes anterior to the capitellum, the distal fragment is tilted posteriorly (hyperextension deformity), which is a sign of inadequate reduction and may lead to cubitus varus and loss of function if not corrected. Additionally, 5° of varus is potentially significant in a child this age. This combination warrants return to theatre for repeat reduction. This is not a normal variant (C). The varus (5°) does not warrant immediate osteotomy (D) but does indicate poor reduction. Malrotation (E) would appear differently on radiograph.
learningPoint: The anterior humeral line should bisect the middle third of the capitellum on a true lateral; passage anterior to it indicates posterior tilt of the distal fragment and inadequate reduction.

stem: A 3-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department with a 2-day history of fever and refusal to weight bear on the right leg. Temperature is 38.8°C. He holds the right hip in flexion, abduction, and external rotation. Blood tests show WBC 16.2 × 10⁹/L, CRP 78 mg/L, ESR 52 mm/hr. Ultrasound confirms a hip effusion. How many of the Kocher criteria does this child satisfy?
explanation: The four original Kocher criteria are: fever >38.5°C (YES), non-weight-bearing (YES), ESR >40 mm/hr (YES, 52), and WBC >12 × 10⁹/L (YES, 16.2). All four criteria are met giving a >99% probability of septic arthritis according to Kocher's original series. CRP (Caird modification) would add a 5th criterion. With 4/4 Kocher criteria, urgent joint washout under general anaesthetic is mandatory without delay.
learningPoint: All four Kocher criteria (fever, non-weight-bearing, ESR >40, WBC >12) predict >99% probability of septic arthritis; urgent surgical washout is required.

stem: A 9-year-old girl with sickle cell disease presents with a 3-day history of right leg pain and a temperature of 38.2°C. She is not weight bearing. CRP is 92 mg/L, WBC 13.4 × 10⁹/L. MRI of the right tibia shows a periosteal reaction, bone marrow oedema, and a subperiosteal collection of 7 mm. There is no joint involvement. Blood cultures are pending. What is the most likely causative organism?

options.label: A
options.text: Staphylococcus aureus

options.label: B
options.text: Streptococcus pyogenes

options.label: C
options.text: Salmonella species

options.label: D
options.text: Haemophilus influenzae

options.label: E
options.text: Escherichia coli

explanation: In children with sickle cell disease, Salmonella species are the most common cause of osteomyelitis, accounting for approximately 50–80% of cases in some series, owing to intestinal mucosal damage from sickling allowing gut translocation. Staphylococcus aureus (A) remains the most common cause of osteomyelitis in the general paediatric population and also occurs in sickle cell patients but is less frequent than Salmonella in this context. Streptococcus pyogenes (B) and E. coli (E) are less common. Haemophilus influenzae (D) is now rare since the Hib vaccine.
learningPoint: Salmonella species are the most common pathogen in osteomyelitis complicating sickle cell disease, distinguishing this group from the general paediatric population where Staphylococcus aureus predominates.

stem: A male infant is born at 39 weeks with bilateral talipes equinovarus. Examination confirms rigid deformity of both feet. The Pirani score is 5 bilaterally. The family live 80 miles from the treating centre. What is the first-line treatment?

options.label: A
options.text: Posteromedial soft tissue release at 3 months of age

options.label: B
options.text: Denis Browne bar and boots from birth

options.label: C
options.text: Serial Ponseti manipulation and casting starting within the first 2 weeks of life

options.label: D
options.text: Physiotherapy and stretching exercises by parents for 6 weeks before reassessment

options.label: E
options.text: French functional physiotherapy method with taping and splinting

explanation: The Ponseti method is the globally accepted first-line treatment for idiopathic CTEV and has become the standard of care in the UK, endorsed by BSCOS. Serial manipulation and long leg casting should start as early as possible (ideally within the first 2 weeks of life) to take advantage of the plasticity of neonatal connective tissue. Posteromedial release (A) was historically used but leads to stiff feet with worse long-term outcomes and is now largely abandoned as primary treatment. Denis Browne boots without prior casting (B) is the maintenance phase after correction. Physiotherapy alone (D) is insufficient for rigid CTEV. The French functional method (E) is used in some centres but requires very frequent specialist physiotherapy and is not standard NHS practice.
learningPoint: The Ponseti method (serial manipulation and casting commenced in the first two weeks of life) is the UK standard of care for idiopathic congenital talipes equinovarus.

stem: A 5-month-old boy is completing his Ponseti casting programme. After 5 casts, the foot is fully abducted and the cavus and adductus are corrected, but the equinus persists and the foot cannot be dorsiflexed beyond neutral. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Continue serial casting for a further 4 weeks

options.label: B
options.text: Prescribe Denis Browne bar and boots at 60–70° abduction and begin weaning

options.label: C
options.text: Percutaneous Achilles tenotomy under local anaesthetic followed by a further 3 weeks in cast

options.label: D
options.text: Open Z-lengthening of the Achilles tendon under general anaesthetic

options.label: E
options.text: Physiotherapy stretching programme for 6 weeks

explanation: Approximately 80–90% of Ponseti-treated clubfeet require a percutaneous Achilles tenotomy to achieve full dorsiflexion beyond neutral. This is performed under local anaesthetic in the clinic setting at the end of the casting programme. A further 3 weeks in a cast allows the tendon gap to fill with new tendon before transition to boots and bar. Continued casting without tenotomy (A) will not correct a tight Achilles tendon. Denis Browne bar (B) is the correct maintenance but must only follow correction. Open Z-lengthening (D) is reserved for failures and carries greater morbidity. Physiotherapy (E) is ineffective for residual equinus in this context.
learningPoint: Residual equinus after Ponseti casting is treated by percutaneous Achilles tenotomy (usually clinic-based under local anaesthetic) before transition to boots and bar.

stem: A 4-year-old boy with spastic diplegic cerebral palsy (GMFCS Level III) attends his annual review. Pelvic radiograph shows a migration percentage (Reimer's index) of 38% on the right and 25% on the left. He has no hip pain. What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Discharge from surveillance; repeat only if symptoms develop

options.label: B
options.text: Botulinum toxin injection to adductor muscles bilaterally and repeat radiograph in 12 months

options.label: C
options.text: Urgent referral for bilateral total hip arthroplasty

options.label: D
options.text: Bilateral adductor release with or without femoral varus derotation osteotomy; continue hip surveillance

options.label: E
options.text: Repeat radiograph in 6 months only

explanation: A migration percentage of ≥30% in a child with cerebral palsy represents hip displacement that is at high risk of progression to dislocation. BSCOS/CPUP guidelines recommend surgical intervention — typically adductor release (possibly combined with psoas release) ± femoral varus derotation osteotomy, and in some cases pelvic osteotomy — to prevent progressive displacement and painful dislocation. Botulinum toxin (B) may be used at lower migration percentages (<30%) as a temporising or adjunctive measure but is insufficient at 38%. Discharge (A) and 6-month watch (E) are inappropriate at this migration percentage. THR (C) is inappropriate in a 4-year-old.
learningPoint: In children with CP, a Reimer's migration percentage ≥30% indicates high risk of dislocation and warrants surgical intervention (adductor release ± VDRO) rather than observation alone.

stem: A 7-year-old girl with spastic quadriplegic cerebral palsy (GMFCS Level V) presents with a painful dislocated right hip. Radiograph shows a completely dislocated hip with secondary acetabular dysplasia. She receives personal care from parents including perineal hygiene and seating. She has no functional ambulation. What is the priority surgical goal in this case?

options.label: A
options.text: Restoration of walking ability

options.label: B
options.text: Pain relief and improved perineal access/seating

options.label: C
options.text: Prevention of scoliosis

options.label: D
options.text: Improvement of upper limb function

options.label: E
options.text: Prevention of contralateral hip dislocation

explanation: In a GMFCS Level V child with a painful dislocated hip, the primary surgical goals are pain relief and facilitation of care (seating, perineal hygiene). Restoration of walking (A) is not possible or relevant for GMFCS V. Prevention of scoliosis (C) and contralateral dislocation (E) are secondary concerns. Upper limb function (D) is unrelated to hip surgery. Surgical options include femoral head resection (Castle procedure), total hip arthroplasty, or reconstructive procedures, chosen based on age, anatomy, and care needs.
learningPoint: In non-ambulatory children with CP (GMFCS Level V) and a painful dislocated hip, the surgical priority is pain relief and facilitation of care rather than functional restoration.

stem: A 10-year-old boy falls from a bicycle and sustains a left forearm injury. Radiographs show a fracture of the proximal third of the ulna with volar (anterior) angulation. The radial head appears to be dislocated anteriorly. The injury occurred 48 hours ago and was initially missed. What type of Monteggia fracture-dislocation is this and what is the significance of the delay?

options.label: A
options.text: Type I Monteggia; delay does not affect management

options.label: B
options.text: Type I Monteggia; delay increases risk of irreducible radial head and may require open reduction

options.label: C
options.text: Type II Monteggia; delay requires immediate open ulna plating

options.label: D
options.text: Type III Monteggia; treat with isolated radial head excision

options.label: E
options.text: Type IV Monteggia; treat conservatively with above-elbow cast

explanation: Anterior dislocation of the radial head with a volar-angulated proximal ulna fracture is the classic Type I Monteggia (Bado classification). A 48-hour delay is clinically significant because the annular ligament and surrounding soft tissues begin to contract and organise, making closed reduction of the radial head increasingly difficult or impossible. Chronic or missed Monteggia fractures often require open reduction of the radial head plus ulna osteotomy to restore radiocapitellar alignment. Type II involves posterior radial head dislocation, Type III lateral, and Type IV both-bone fractures with radial head dislocation.
learningPoint: Type I Monteggia (volar ulna angulation + anterior radial head dislocation) is the most common type; delays >48 hours increase the risk of an irreducible radial head requiring open reduction and possible ulna osteotomy.

stem: An 11-year-old girl sustains an ankle injury during football. Radiographs reveal a fracture line that traverses the physis and exits through the epiphysis only; the metaphysis is not involved. The fracture is undisplaced. What is the Salter-Harris classification and the recommended management?

options.label: A
options.text: Salter-Harris Type I; operative fixation to prevent growth arrest

options.label: B
options.text: Salter-Harris Type II; below-knee cast for 6 weeks

options.label: C
options.text: Salter-Harris Type III; below-knee cast if undisplaced, operative fixation if >2 mm displacement

options.label: D
options.text: Salter-Harris Type IV; operative fixation in all cases

options.label: E
options.text: Salter-Harris Type V; cast immobilisation and long-term growth monitoring

explanation: A fracture through the physis that exits through the epiphysis (without involving the metaphysis) is a Salter-Harris Type III injury. These intra-articular fractures have a risk of growth arrest and articular incongruity. Undisplaced (<2 mm) fractures can be managed with cast immobilisation, but displacement of ≥2 mm (or in some guidelines 1–2 mm) requires anatomical reduction and internal fixation to restore the articular surface. SH Type I is through the physis only; SH Type II extends through the metaphysis (most common); SH Type IV involves metaphysis, physis, and epiphysis; SH Type V is a crush injury of the physis.
learningPoint: Salter-Harris Type III fractures are intra-articular physeal injuries; displacement ≥2 mm requires operative fixation to restore articular congruency and minimise growth disturbance.

stem: A 9-year-old boy injures his distal radius in a fall. Radiographs show a fracture line passing through the metaphysis, across the physis, and through the epiphysis, effectively splitting the epiphysis. There is 4 mm of displacement at the joint surface. What is the Salter-Harris classification and optimal management?

options.label: A
options.text: SH Type II; closed reduction and cast

options.label: B
options.text: SH Type III; arthroscopic reduction

options.label: C
options.text: SH Type IV; open reduction and internal fixation

options.label: D
options.text: SH Type V; cast immobilisation only

options.label: E
options.text: SH Type I; percutaneous K-wire fixation

explanation: A fracture traversing the metaphysis, physis, and epiphysis is a Salter-Harris Type IV injury. These are the most complex of the original SH classification types in terms of management, as the fracture crosses all three zones and is intra-articular. With 4 mm displacement, open reduction and internal fixation is required to restore both articular congruency and physeal alignment; failure to do so carries high risk of growth arrest and angular deformity. SH II (A) extends only through the physis into the metaphysis, not the epiphysis. SH III (B) is epiphysis and physis only. SH V (D) is a crush injury.
learningPoint: Salter-Harris Type IV fractures cross the metaphysis, physis, and epiphysis; displacement requires open reduction and internal fixation to prevent growth arrest and articular incongruity.

stem: A 3-year-old boy of East African origin, primarily breastfed with limited outdoor sun exposure, presents with increasing bowing of both lower limbs. His mother gives a history of delayed walking (18 months). Serum investigations show calcium 2.1 mmol/L, phosphate 0.8 mmol/L, ALP 620 IU/L, 25-hydroxyvitamin D 12 nmol/L, PTH 14.2 pmol/L (elevated). Radiographs show widening and cupping of the distal femoral and proximal tibial metaphyses bilaterally. What is the most likely diagnosis and first-line treatment?

options.label: A
options.text: Blount disease; tibial osteotomy

options.label: B
options.text: Hypophosphataemic rickets (X-linked); oral phosphate and alfacalcidol

options.label: C
options.text: Nutritional vitamin D deficiency rickets; oral vitamin D and calcium supplementation

options.label: D
options.text: Renal osteodystrophy; haemodialysis

options.label: E
options.text: Physiological bow legs; observation and reassurance

explanation: The combination of low 25-hydroxyvitamin D (12 nmol/L, severely deficient), elevated PTH, low-normal calcium, low phosphate, raised ALP, metaphyseal widening/cupping, and the clinical context (dark skin, breastfeeding, limited sunlight) is diagnostic of nutritional vitamin D deficiency rickets. First-line treatment is oral vitamin D (cholecalciferol) and calcium supplementation, with orthopaedic correction of residual deformity considered only after biochemical normalisation. Blount disease (A) causes medial tibial bowing with characteristic Langenskiöld changes but normal biochemistry. X-linked hypophosphataemic rickets (B) features normal 25-OHD and low phosphate refractory to vitamin D alone. Physiological bow legs (E) are normal in toddlers but do not cause biochemical abnormalities.
learningPoint: Nutritional rickets presents with low 25-OHD, elevated PTH, raised ALP, and metaphyseal changes; treatment is oral vitamin D and calcium supplementation before considering any bony correction.
references: British Paediatric and Adolescent Bone Group Guidelines

stem: A 10-year-old obese girl presents with progressive medial bowing of the left tibia noted since age 3. She started walking at 11 months. She is otherwise healthy with normal biochemistry. AP radiograph of the left leg shows a medial tibial metaphyseal beak, medial epiphyseal depression, and a Langenskiöld Stage V appearance. What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Observation and weight management programme

options.label: B
options.text: Medial plateau elevation and corrective tibial osteotomy with lateral epiphysiodesis consideration

options.label: C
options.text: Bilateral tibial osteotomies and external fixation

options.label: D
options.text: Intramedullary nailing of the tibia

options.label: E
options.text: Knee-ankle-foot orthosis (KAFO) for 12 months

explanation: Langenskiöld Stage V Blount disease in an older child represents advanced physeal involvement with medial epiphyseal depression and a physieal bar. Treatment requires elevation of the medial tibial plateau to restore joint congruency, combined with proximal tibial valgus osteotomy to correct alignment. Lateral epiphysiodesis may be considered if significant growth remains to prevent recurrence of varus. Observation (A) is appropriate only for Stages I–II in young children. Bilateral osteotomies (C) are not required if only one side is affected. Intramedullary nailing (D) does not address the intra-articular component. KAFO (E) has no proven efficacy in established Blount disease at this stage.
learningPoint: Advanced Blount disease (Langenskiöld Stage V–VI) requires medial plateau elevation and tibial osteotomy to address both articular depression and angular deformity.
references: Langenskiöld A - Classification of Blount Disease

stem: A 14-year-old boy presents with bilateral hindfoot pain and stiffness that is worse after sports. He has a rigid flat foot bilaterally with limited subtalar motion. AP and lateral foot radiographs are unremarkable, but a 45° oblique view shows a bridge of bone medially between the calcaneum and navicular on the right. CT scan confirms a complete calcaneonavicular coalition. He has failed 6 months of conservative management. What is the surgical treatment of choice?

options.label: A
options.text: Triple arthrodesis

options.label: B
options.text: Resection of the coalition with fat or extensor digitorum brevis interposition

options.label: C
options.text: Subtalar arthrodesis alone

options.label: D
options.text: Calcaneal osteotomy for flat foot correction

options.label: E
options.text: Arthroscopic subtalar debridement

explanation: Calcaneonavicular coalition is the most common type of tarsal coalition and in adolescents who fail conservative management, resection of the coalition with interposition of fat or the belly of extensor digitorum brevis gives excellent results, preserving subtalar motion. Triple arthrodesis (A) sacrifices all hindfoot motion and is reserved for adult patients with established arthritic change or failed previous resection. Subtalar arthrodesis (C) addresses the wrong joint for calcaneonavicular coalitions. Calcaneal osteotomy (D) addresses shape but not the cause. Arthroscopic debridement (E) is not standard for osseous coalition.
learningPoint: Calcaneonavicular coalition failing conservative treatment is treated by surgical resection with fat or EDB interposition, preserving hindfoot motion in the adolescent.

stem: A 9-year-old boy presents with insidious-onset pain in the medial arch of his right foot, worse with running and relieved by rest. Examination reveals tenderness directly over the navicular. Plain radiograph shows a dense, fragmented navicular that is flattened. There are no other bony abnormalities. What is the most likely diagnosis and appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Stress fracture of the navicular; non-weight-bearing in cast for 8 weeks

options.label: B
options.text: Tarsal coalition; CT and resection

options.label: C
options.text: Kohler disease; activity modification and analgesia with or without short-term immobilisation

options.label: D
options.text: Accessory navicular syndrome; excision of ossicle

options.label: E
options.text: Reactive arthritis; NSAIDs and rheumatology referral

explanation: Kohler disease is an osteochondrosis of the tarsal navicular presenting in children aged 4–10 years (more commonly in boys) with medial foot pain, and the radiograph classically shows a dense, flattened, and fragmented navicular. It is a self-limiting condition that remodels with time. Management is symptomatic: activity modification, analgesia, and possibly short-term cast immobilisation for pain relief. It does not progress to significant long-term disability. Navicular stress fracture (A) occurs in older athletes. Tarsal coalition (B) typically presents with rigid flat foot and limited subtalar motion. Accessory navicular (D) is a separate ossicle adjacent to the navicular, not fragmentation of the navicular itself. Reactive arthritis (E) is inflammatory and not confined to the navicular.
learningPoint: Kohler disease (osteochondrosis of the tarsal navicular) in a child presents with a dense fragmented navicular on radiograph and is self-limiting; symptomatic management is all that is required.

stem: A 7-month-old girl is brought to the emergency department by her father with a swollen left thigh. He states she fell from a settee. Radiographs show a spiral fracture of the left femoral shaft. Examination also reveals a healing bruise over the right cheek and a frenulum tear. A full skeletal survey is requested. Which of the following additional findings would most strongly support a diagnosis of non-accidental injury?

options.label: A
options.text: Distal radius buckle (torus) fracture in various stages of healing

options.label: B
options.text: Classic metaphyseal lesions (corner fractures) of the distal tibia

options.label: C
options.text: Midshaft clavicle fracture

options.label: D
options.text: Toddler's fracture of the tibia

options.label: E
options.text: Bilateral subperiosteal new bone formation on the femora only

explanation: Classic metaphyseal lesions (CMLs), also known as corner or bucket-handle fractures, are planar fractures through the immature metaphysis produced by torsional or traction forces. They are virtually pathognomonic of non-accidental injury in infants as they require forces beyond normal handling. Their presence on a skeletal survey in a pre-mobile infant should prompt immediate child protection proceedings. A distal radius torus fracture (A) is common with normal falls. Midshaft clavicle fracture (C) is the most common birth-related fracture and can follow minor trauma. Toddler's fracture (D) is a spiral tibial fracture from low-energy twisting in ambulant children and is a common accidental injury. Subperiosteal new bone (E) can be physiological in infancy.
learningPoint: Classic metaphyseal lesions (corner/bucket-handle fractures) in a pre-mobile infant are virtually pathognomonic of non-accidental injury and mandate immediate child protection referral.
references: Kemp AM - Arch Dis Child NAI Fractures

stem: An 8-month-old boy presents with irritability and not using his left arm. The mother reports he 'rolled off the sofa.' Radiographs show a posterior rib fracture at the right 6th rib. A head CT shows bilateral thin subdural haematomas. Ophthalmology confirms bilateral retinal haemorrhages. The fractures appear to be at different stages of healing on the skeletal survey. What is the most appropriate immediate action?

options.label: A
options.text: Discharge to parents with GP follow-up and advice on safe sleep

options.label: B
options.text: Admit under paediatrics, notify the designated safeguarding lead, and make a referral to children's social care

options.label: C
options.text: Repeat skeletal survey in 6 weeks and then decide

options.label: D
options.text: Arrest the parents and call police before any further clinical management

options.label: E
options.text: Transfer to tertiary centre for DEXA scan to exclude osteogenesis imperfecta before child protection referral

explanation: This clinical picture — posterior rib fractures (high specificity for NAI), bilateral subdural haematomas, retinal haemorrhages (the triad of non-accidental head injury), and injuries at multiple healing stages in a pre-mobile infant — constitutes a high-risk presentation of non-accidental injury. The immediate response is hospital admission under paediatrics, notification of the designated safeguarding lead, and a referral to children's social care as per RCPCH NAI guidelines and Working Together to Safeguard Children. Discharge (A) is unsafe. Police referral and arrest (D) is the role of police/social care, not the clinician's. DEXA (E) to exclude OI is a secondary investigation that must not delay safeguarding referral. The repeat skeletal survey in 6 weeks (C) is a standard additional investigation but must not delay immediate safeguarding action.
learningPoint: A triad of posterior rib fractures, bilateral subdural haematomas, and retinal haemorrhages in an infant requires immediate hospital admission, safeguarding lead notification, and children's social care referral — clinical management and safeguarding run in parallel.
references: Working Together to Safeguard Children (HM Government)

stem: A 3-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department after her father lifted her by the wrist to prevent her stepping into a puddle. She is holding the left arm in slight elbow flexion and pronation, refusing to use it. She is apyrexial and not distressed. Radiographs of the elbow are reported as normal. What is the most likely diagnosis and management?

options.label: A
options.text: Radial head fracture; above-elbow backslab

options.label: B
options.text: Nursemaid's elbow (pulled elbow); supination-flexion or hyperpronation manoeuvre

options.label: C
options.text: Septic arthritis of the elbow; urgent aspiration under GA

options.label: D
options.text: Toddler's fracture of the radius; below-elbow cast for 4 weeks

options.label: E
options.text: Brachial plexus injury; physiotherapy and observation for 6 weeks

explanation: Pulled elbow (nursemaid's elbow / radial head subluxation) is extremely common in children under 5 and follows a longitudinal traction force on the forearm. The annular ligament slips over the radial head, causing the characteristic posture of elbow slight flexion and pronation. Normal radiographs are expected. Management is by closed reduction: either the traditional supination-flexion technique or the hyperpronation method (which evidence suggests has a higher first-attempt success rate). A click or clunk indicates reduction and the child typically begins using the arm within minutes. No immobilisation is required after successful reduction. The mechanism (traction not fall) and the normal radiograph exclude fracture (A, D). Afebrile presentation excludes septic arthritis (C). Brachial plexus injury (E) is not consistent with this mechanism.
learningPoint: Pulled elbow in a child under 5 follows axial traction; treatment is by supination-flexion or hyperpronation reduction manoeuvre, with no immobilisation required after a successful reduction.
references: Macias CG et al - Pediatrics 1998 Hyperpronation

stem: A 12-year-old boy sustains a both-bone forearm fracture in mid-shaft after a fall from a skateboard. Radiographs show 20° of angulation in the sagittal plane and 10° of malrotation. The fracture is closed. The child is skeletally mature (Tanner Stage 4). What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Manipulation under anaesthetic and above-elbow cast; accept up to 20° angulation in a 12-year-old

options.label: B
options.text: Manipulation under anaesthetic and cast; if residual angulation >10°, proceed to operative fixation

options.label: C
options.text: Operative fixation with flexible intramedullary nails (elastic stable intramedullary nailing, ESIN)

options.label: D
options.text: Rigid plating of both radius and ulna

options.label: E
options.text: Above-elbow cast without manipulation; remodel will correct the deformity

explanation: In a skeletally mature 12-year-old (Tanner Stage 4), remodelling potential is very limited and adult thresholds for acceptable alignment apply (<10° angulation, minimal malrotation). With 20° angulation and 10° malrotation at Tanner 4, closed reduction alone is unlikely to achieve or maintain acceptable alignment. Elastic stable intramedullary nailing (ESIN) is the standard operative treatment for paediatric both-bone forearm fractures in this age group, providing stability while preserving growth plates. Plating (D) is generally avoided in skeletally immature patients to avoid physeal damage, though it may be used in near-skeletal maturity. Accepting 20° angulation (A) is appropriate only in younger children (<8 years). Casting without reduction (E) would result in malunion and loss of forearm rotation.
learningPoint: Both-bone forearm fractures in children approaching skeletal maturity (>10 years, Tanner 4) with >10° angulation or significant malrotation require operative fixation, typically with ESIN.

stem: A 4-year-old boy is referred after his parents noticed a limp and leg length discrepancy. Examination confirms 15 mm of apparent shortening on the right, limited abduction, and a positive Trendelenburg sign. Pelvic radiograph shows a high-riding femoral head on the right with a false acetabulum and dysplastic true acetabulum. The acetabular index is 38°. Arthrogram under general anaesthetic shows a concentric reduction is achievable. What surgical procedure should accompany the soft tissue releases and open reduction at this age?

options.label: A
options.text: Femoral shortening and derotation osteotomy alone

options.label: B
options.text: Open reduction with femoral shortening/derotation osteotomy and Salter innominate or Pemberton pelvic osteotomy

options.label: C
options.text: Hip spica cast alone without osteotomy

options.label: D
options.text: Chiari osteotomy as primary procedure

options.label: E
options.text: Total hip arthroplasty in the next 6 months

explanation: In a 4-year-old with high-riding DDH, a staged or combined procedure at open reduction is required. Femoral shortening osteotomy reduces the pull on the reduced femoral head and decreases AVN risk; derotation corrects the excessive anteversion. The markedly dysplastic acetabulum (acetabular index 38°) requires pelvic osteotomy — either Salter (reorients the acetabulum) or Pemberton (reduces acetabular volume) — to provide adequate coverage of the femoral head after reduction. Femoral osteotomy alone (A) does not address acetabular dysplasia. Hip spica alone (C) without bony stabilisation will result in re-dislocation. Chiari osteotomy (D) is a salvage procedure for older patients and is not primary treatment. THR (E) is entirely inappropriate at age 4.
learningPoint: Open reduction of high DDH in a child aged 2–6 years requires combined femoral shortening/derotation osteotomy and pelvic osteotomy to achieve stable concentric reduction and adequate acetabular coverage.

stem: Theme: Paediatric Hip Disorders. For each clinical scenario below, select the single most likely diagnosis from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Developmental dysplasia of the hip (DDH)

options.label: B
options.text: Perthes disease

options.label: C
options.text: Slipped capital femoral epiphysis (SCFE)

options.label: D
options.text: Septic arthritis of the hip

options.label: E
options.text: Transient synovitis (irritable hip)

options.label: F
options.text: Avascular necrosis following trauma

options.label: G
options.text: Juvenile idiopathic arthritis (JIA)

emiLeads.stem: A 5-year-old boy presents with 4 weeks of right hip pain and a limp. He is afebrile. ESR is 24 mm/hr and CRP is 4 mg/L. Radiograph shows increased density and mild flattening of the right femoral head with subchondral fragmentation. Hip ultrasound shows a small effusion.

A 13-year-old girl with a BMI of 31 presents with left groin pain radiating to the knee for 3 weeks. She walks with the left leg externally rotated. On flexion of the hip, the thigh moves into external rotation. Radiographs show postero-inferior displacement of the capital epiphysis on the lateral view.

A 2-year-old girl is noted at her developmental check to have asymmetric skin creases, apparent shortening of the right leg, and limited abduction of the right hip. She was born by breech delivery at 36 weeks. AP pelvic radiograph shows lateral and superior displacement of the right femoral head relative to the acetabulum.

A 4-year-old boy presents with 24 hours of fever (39.1°C), severe right hip pain, and refusal to weight bear. He holds the hip in flexion, abduction, and external rotation. WBC is 18 × 10⁹/L and CRP is 112 mg/L. Ultrasound confirms a large hip effusion.

A 6-year-old boy presents with a 3-day history of left hip pain and limp following a recent upper respiratory tract infection. He is afebrile. CRP is 6 mg/L and WBC is 9.2 × 10⁹/L. Ultrasound confirms a small effusion. He can partially weight bear.

explanation: B (Perthes): insidious onset, normal inflammatory markers, classic radiographic changes of avascular necrosis. C (SCFE): obese adolescent, obligatory external rotation on flexion, posterior slip on frog-lateral. A (DDH): missed at birth, breech risk factor, asymmetric skin creases and limited abduction in toddler. D (Septic arthritis): high fever, very high inflammatory markers, large effusion, refusal to weight bear — meets Kocher criteria. E (Transient synovitis): post-viral, afebrile, low inflammatory markers, small effusion, partial weight bearing — classic irritable hip.

stem: Theme: Paediatric Fracture Classification and Nerve Injury. For each clinical scenario below, select the single most appropriate answer from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Anterior interosseous nerve (AIN) palsy

options.label: B
options.text: Radial nerve palsy

options.label: C
options.text: Ulnar nerve palsy

options.label: D
options.text: Median nerve palsy

options.label: E
options.text: Brachial artery injury

options.label: F
options.text: Salter-Harris Type II fracture

options.label: G
options.text: Salter-Harris Type IV fracture

emiLeads.stem: A 6-year-old girl sustains a Gartland Type III supracondylar fracture. Post-operatively she cannot make the 'OK' sign (opposition of thumb and index fingertip) but sensation over the hand is intact.
A 10-year-old boy fractures his lateral condyle of the humerus. Radiograph shows a fragment involving the capitellum crossing through the metaphysis, physis, and epiphysis with 4 mm of displacement.

An 8-year-old boy sustains a distal radius fracture. Radiograph shows a fracture through the metaphysis with a small triangular metaphyseal fragment; the epiphysis is displaced. The growth plate itself appears widened.

A 7-year-old girl undergoes K-wire fixation for a Gartland III supracondylar fracture. Post-operatively she has weakness of wrist extension and loss of sensation over the dorsal web space between thumb and index finger.

explanation: AIN (A): the AIN is the most commonly injured nerve in supracondylar fractures; it is a pure motor branch of the median nerve supplying FPL and FDP to index, tested by the 'OK' sign — no sensory loss. SH IV (G): lateral condyle fractures in children cross from metaphysis through physis into epiphysis — this is a SH Type IV injury requiring ORIF. SH II (F): distal radius fractures with a small metaphyseal fragment (Thurston-Holland fragment), widened physis, and epiphyseal displacement are classic SH Type II. Radial nerve (B): injury to the radial nerve causes wrist drop and loss of sensation in the first dorsal web space.

stem: Theme: Paediatric Foot and Developmental Conditions. For each clinical scenario below, select the single most likely diagnosis from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Idiopathic congenital talipes equinovarus (CTEV)

options.label: B
options.text: Positional talipes

options.label: C
options.text: Talipes calcaneovalgus

options.label: D
options.text: Metatarsus adductus

options.label: E
options.text: Flatfoot (flexible pes planus)

options.label: F
options.text: Tarsal coalition

options.label: G
options.text: Vertical talus (rocker-bottom foot)

emiLeads.stem: A newborn girl has a right foot that is dorsiflexed and everted. The foot is easily passively corrected to neutral and beyond with gentle manipulation. There is no family history of note and the baby was in a normal cephalic presentation.
A newborn boy has bilateral rigid equinovarus foot deformity. The feet cannot be passively corrected. Pirani score is 5.5 bilaterally. He has no other anomalies and there is no family history.
A 6-week-old girl is noticed to have adduction of the forefoot. The hindfoot is in neutral and the heel bisector line passes through the 3rd toe. The deformity is partially correctable passively.

A 15-year-old boy presents with painful rigid flat feet, limited subtalar motion, and peroneal spasm. CT scan shows a bony bridge between the middle facet of the calcaneum and the sustentaculum tali of the talus.

explanation: Talipes calcaneovalgus (C): dorsiflexed, everted, fully correctable foot — positional from uterine packaging, resolves spontaneously. Idiopathic CTEV (A): bilateral rigid equinovarus, high Pirani score, no neurological cause — classic idiopathic clubfoot requiring Ponseti treatment. Metatarsus adductus (D): isolated forefoot adduction, hindfoot neutral, heel bisector through 3rd toe, partially correctable — usually resolves; observe or gentle stretching. Tarsal coalition (F): rigid flat foot with peroneal spasm in adolescent, CT shows talocalcaneal coalition at middle facet/sustentaculum tali.

stem: Theme: Skeletal Injury — NAI versus Pathological Bone. For each clinical scenario below, select the single most likely explanation from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Non-accidental injury

options.label: B
options.text: Osteogenesis imperfecta

options.label: C
options.text: Nutritional rickets

options.label: D
options.text: Physiological periosteal new bone formation

options.label: E
options.text: Accidental toddler's fracture

options.label: F
options.text: Birth-related fracture

options.label: G
options.text: Scurvy

emiLeads.stem: A 3-month-old boy is found to have bilateral posterior rib fractures in different stages of healing on skeletal survey. He also has a right parietal skull fracture. The parents report no trauma. He is otherwise healthy, blue sclerae are absent, and hearing is normal.

An 18-month-old girl presents with a spiral fracture of the right tibia. The mother states she was running and fell, twisting her ankle. She has just started walking independently at 14 months. There are no other injuries on skeletal survey and no bruising.

A 3-year-old boy of South Asian background presents with a femur fracture after minimal trauma (falling from sitting). ALP is 780 IU/L, calcium is 2.0 mmol/L, phosphate is 0.7 mmol/L, and 25-OHD is 9 nmol/L. Radiographs show fraying of the metaphyses.

A 4-month-old girl is noted on routine radiographs to have smooth periosteal new bone on both femoral diaphyses. She is thriving, afebrile, and has no tenderness. Inflammatory markers are normal. There are no other fractures.

A 2-year-old boy has sustained multiple fractures of varying ages: right humerus, left tibia, and two right ribs. His mother is very anxious and states he fractures easily. He has blue sclerae, dentinogenesis imperfecta, and his maternal grandmother uses a wheelchair due to frequent fractures.

explanation: A (NAI): bilateral posterior rib fractures at different healing stages with skull fracture and no explanation are high-specificity features of NAI. E (Toddler's fracture): spiral tibial fracture in a recently-walking toddler from a low-energy twisting fall, isolated injury, no other features of concern. C (Nutritional rickets): fracture after minimal trauma with markedly elevated ALP, low calcium, phosphate, and 25-OHD in a high-risk demographic confirms nutritional rickets. D (Physiological periosteal reaction): smooth bilateral femoral periosteal new bone in a thriving infant without fever or tenderness is a normal finding at 1–4 months of age. B (Osteogenesis imperfecta): blue sclerae, dentinogenesis imperfecta, family history, and multiple fractures at different stages = OI (most likely Type III/IV).
references: British Paediatric and Adolescent Bone Group - Rickets

stem: A 54-year-old woman presents with a painful right hallux valgus deformity. Weight-bearing radiographs show a hallux valgus angle (HVA) of 34°, an intermetatarsal angle (IMA) of 16°, and a distal metatarsal articular angle (DMAA) of 8°. The first metatarsophalangeal (MTP) joint appears congruent. She has failed 18 months of conservative management including wide-fitting footwear and orthotics. Which surgical procedure is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Distal soft tissue release alone (McBride procedure)

options.label: B
options.text: Distal chevron osteotomy

options.label: C
options.text: Proximal closing wedge osteotomy with distal soft tissue release

options.label: D
options.text: First MTP joint arthrodesis

options.label: E
options.text: Scarf osteotomy with Akin phalangeal osteotomy

explanation: A Scarf osteotomy can correct IMA up to approximately 18–20° and is the procedure of choice for moderate-to-severe hallux valgus (HVA 30–40°, IMA 13–20°). Adding an Akin osteotomy fine-tunes residual hallux pronation or valgus. Distal chevron is limited to mild deformity (IMA <13°). Proximal osteotomy is reserved for IMA >20°. First MTP arthrodesis is for severe deformity with joint degeneration or neuromuscular disease. Soft-tissue release alone is insufficient for structural deformity of this magnitude.
learningPoint: Scarf osteotomy ± Akin is the workhorse procedure for moderate-to-severe hallux valgus with IMA 13–20° and a congruent joint.

stem: A 29-year-old female dancer has a hallux valgus angle of 28°, intermetatarsal angle of 11°, and an elevated DMAA of 18°. The MTP joint is congruent on radiograph. She wishes to remain professionally active. Which procedure best addresses the underlying deformity while minimising MTP stiffness?

options.label: A
options.text: Chevron osteotomy alone

options.label: B
options.text: Scarf osteotomy with medial capsule reefing

options.label: C
options.text: Double osteotomy: chevron with rotational Akin phalangeal osteotomy

options.label: D
options.text: Proximal metatarsal osteotomy with lateral release

options.label: E
options.text: First MTP arthrodesis

explanation: An elevated DMAA (>10°) means the articular surface is oblique; simply translating the distal fragment (chevron alone) does not correct this and may leave residual deformity. A rotational chevron combined with an Akin corrects both the IMA and the DMAA while preserving joint motion, which is critical in a professional dancer. Scarf carries a risk of stiffness from extensive soft-tissue stripping. Proximal osteotomy is overcorrection for an IMA of 11°. Arthrodesis is inappropriate in a young active patient without joint degeneration.
learningPoint: An elevated DMAA with a congruent MTP joint requires a rotational element (e.g., rotational chevron ± Akin) rather than simple translation to avoid residual deformity.

stem: A 41-year-old man slips on ice and sustains an isolated fracture of the fibula at the level of the ankle mortise. The medial clear space on weight-bearing anteroposterior radiograph measures 4 mm. A stress view in external rotation widens the medial clear space to 7 mm. According to the Lauge-Hansen classification, what mechanism produced this fracture pattern?

options.label: A
options.text: Pronation-abduction stage II

options.label: B
options.text: Supination-adduction stage I

options.label: C
options.text: Supination-external rotation stage II

options.label: D
options.text: Pronation-external rotation stage II

options.label: E
options.text: Supination-external rotation stage IV

explanation: A transverse (or short oblique) fibular fracture at the level of the plafond with an intact medial side at rest is the hallmark of supination-external rotation (SER) stage II. The positive external rotation stress test revealing medial clear space widening to ≥5 mm indicates deltoid ligament incompetence, upgrading this to SER stage IV in terms of instability, but the fracture pattern itself (infrasyndesmotic or at-plafond fibula) is the SER mechanism. Pronation-external rotation produces a high fibular fracture (Weber C). Supination-adduction causes a transverse distal fibular avulsion with potential vertical medial malleolus fracture.
learningPoint: Stress-view medial clear space widening >4 mm indicates syndesmotic or deltoid disruption; a transverse fibula fracture at plafond level with this finding is SER stage IV.

stem: A 63-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes and peripheral neuropathy sustains a trimalleolar ankle fracture-dislocation. She is otherwise medically stable. Intraoperative fluoroscopy after lateral plate fixation of the fibula and posterolateral plate fixation of the posterior malleolus reveals a persistent medial clear space of 5 mm. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Accept the position and close; post-operative CT to assess

options.label: B
options.text: Place a trans-syndesmotic screw before closing

options.label: C
options.text: Open the medial side, repair or reconstruct the deltoid ligament, and assess stability

options.label: D
options.text: Apply a supplementary fibula intramedullary rod

options.label: E
options.text: Convert to primary tibiotalar arthrodesis given the diabetic foot risk

explanation: A persistent medial clear space of 5 mm after fibula and posterior malleolus fixation indicates deltoid ligament disruption with residual medial instability. In a diabetic patient with neuropathy who is at higher risk of hardware failure and malunion, restoring anatomical reduction is paramount. Direct deltoid repair or reconstruction should be performed and stability reassessed before syndesmotic screw placement, which alone does not address medial soft-tissue deficiency. Accepting the reduction risks valgus malunion. Primary arthrodesis is a salvage consideration but not the first operative step in an acute setting with reducible soft tissue.
learningPoint: Persistent medial clear space after fibula and posterior malleolus fixation mandates deltoid exploration and repair before closing, especially in high-risk patients.

stem: A 38-year-old recreational squash player felt a sudden pop in his left calf while lunging. He can walk with a limp but cannot perform a single-leg heel rise. The Simmonds-Thompson test is positive. Ultrasound confirms a complete mid-substance Achilles tendon rupture with a 4 cm gap at rest. He is fit and healthy with no comorbidities. Which management approach has the best evidence for return to sport in this patient?

options.label: A
options.text: Immediate operative repair followed by non-weight-bearing cast for 6 weeks

options.label: B
options.text: Functional brace with heel wedges and early weight-bearing rehabilitation

options.label: C
options.text: Operative repair followed by early weight-bearing in a functional brace

options.label: D
options.text: Non-operative management in plaster cast for 8 weeks, then physiotherapy

options.label: E
options.text: Percutaneous repair technique followed by immediate full weight-bearing

explanation: Current best evidence (including the UKSTAR and HACK trials) shows that operative repair combined with early functional rehabilitation (early weight-bearing in a boot) achieves lower re-rupture rates compared with non-operative functional treatment and better functional outcomes than immobilisation. In a young, active patient without comorbidities the small re-rupture risk reduction (approximately 3–4%) with surgery is clinically meaningful for return to sport. Non-operative functional treatment (option B) is an acceptable alternative but evidence favours surgery for active patients. Prolonged cast immobilisation (option D) gives inferior functional outcomes regardless of operative or non-operative route.
learningPoint: Operative repair plus early functional rehabilitation gives the lowest re-rupture rate and best functional outcome for active patients with complete Achilles tendon rupture.

stem: A 52-year-old woman presents with a 9-month history of posterior heel pain and morning stiffness. MRI shows a 60% cross-sectional area tear of the Achilles tendon 4 cm proximal to its calcaneal insertion, with surrounding peritendinous signal change. She has completed 6 months of physiotherapy-led eccentric exercise and has not improved. What is the next most appropriate intervention?

options.label: A
options.text: Injection of platelet-rich plasma into the tendon

options.label: B
options.text: Operative debridement with flexor hallucis longus (FHL) transfer

options.label: C
options.text: Extracorporeal shockwave therapy (ESWT)

options.label: D
options.text: Gastrocnemius recession alone

options.label: E
options.text: Calcaneal tendon transfer (turn-down flap)

explanation: A 60% cross-sectional tear represents substantial mid-substance degeneration (>50%). After 6 months of failed conservative physiotherapy, operative debridement with FHL tendon transfer is the recommended surgical option for large partial tears. FHL lies immediately adjacent in the deep posterior compartment and provides a well-vascularised, in-line augmentation. PRP injections into the tendon substance carry risk of further structural compromise and lack robust evidence. ESWT is appropriate before surgery for tendinopathy without major structural disruption. Gastrocnemius recession addresses tightness but not the structural deficit. A turn-down flap is used for reconstruction of larger complete-defect gaps.
learningPoint: A >50% partial Achilles tear failing conservative treatment warrants operative debridement and FHL augmentation transfer rather than further injections or lesser procedures.

stem: A 67-year-old man with a 20-year history of type 2 diabetes presents with a hot, swollen right foot and ankle that developed over 3 weeks. He is afebrile; WBC is 7.8 × 10⁹/L and CRP is 12 mg/L. Plain radiograph shows bony destruction and subluxation of the midfoot joints with soft-tissue swelling but no gas. MRI shows marrow oedema throughout the tarsal bones without a defined abscess or cortical breach. Which is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.label: A
options.text: IV antibiotics and urgent surgical debridement

options.label: B
options.text: Total contact casting (TCC) and non-weight-bearing

options.label: C
options.text: Below-knee amputation

options.label: D
options.text: Bisphosphonate therapy

options.label: E
options.text: Surgical midfoot arthrodesis

explanation: The clinical picture — acute hot swollen foot in a long-standing diabetic, near-normal inflammatory markers, no fever, no abscess, and diffuse midfoot bony destruction on MRI without cortical breach — is consistent with acute Charcot neuroarthropathy (Sanders-Frykberg pattern II/III) rather than septic arthritis or osteomyelitis. Immediate total contact casting offloads the foot, prevents further collapse, and is the cornerstone of treatment. Antibiotics and surgery are not indicated without evidence of infection. Midfoot arthrodesis is a later consideration for a deformed, ulceration-prone or unstable foot after the acute phase. Bisphosphonates have limited evidence and are not first-line.
learningPoint: Acute Charcot neuroarthropathy in a diabetic foot — near-normal inflammatory markers, no infection signs — is treated with total contact casting and non-weight-bearing, not surgery.

stem: A 59-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents with a plantar forefoot ulcer graded SINBAD 4 (deep, infected, neuropathic). X-ray shows destruction of the 2nd metatarsal head. Blood cultures are negative. A bone biopsy yields Staphylococcus aureus sensitive to flucloxacillin. Surgical debridement has been performed. What is the recommended minimum duration of antibiotic therapy from the point of last debridement?

options.label: A
options.text: 2 weeks

options.label: B
options.text: 4 weeks

options.label: C
options.text: 6 weeks

options.label: D
options.text: 12 weeks

options.label: E
options.text: Lifelong suppressive therapy

explanation: NICE NG19 and IDSA diabetic foot infection guidelines recommend 6 weeks of targeted antibiotic therapy for confirmed diabetic foot osteomyelitis after surgical debridement, provided no residual infected bone remains. Shorter courses (2–4 weeks) are associated with higher relapse rates when bone involvement is confirmed on biopsy. If residual infected bone is left in situ, some guidelines extend to 12 weeks, but this patient had debridement. Lifelong suppression is not standard practice for a sensitive organism after adequate debridement.
learningPoint: Confirmed diabetic foot osteomyelitis managed with surgical debridement requires 6 weeks of targeted antibiotic therapy from last debridement per NICE NG19.

stem: A 48-year-old woman presents with medial ankle pain and progressive flatfoot deformity over 2 years. She can perform a single-leg heel rise on the right with pain, but cannot complete one on the left. Examination shows too many toes sign bilaterally, worse on the left; the subtalar joint is passively correctable. MRI demonstrates a grade III posterior tibial tendon tear. She has failed orthotics and physiotherapy for 12 months. What is the most appropriate surgical treatment?

options.label: A
options.text: Posterior tibial tendon debridement alone

options.label: B
options.text: Posterior tibial tendon repair and medialising calcaneal osteotomy

options.label: C
options.text: FDL transfer, medialising calcaneal osteotomy, and spring ligament repair

options.label: D
options.text: Subtalar arthrodesis alone

options.label: E
options.text: Triple arthrodesis

explanation: Stage IIB adult acquired flatfoot deformity (AAFD) with a flexible subtalar joint and a substantially torn posterior tibial tendon (grade III) requires tendon reconstruction (FDL transfer is the workhorse), bony realignment (medialising calcaneal osteotomy to shift the Achilles vector medially), and addressing the spring ligament deficiency which is a primary restraint of the medial column.
