Debridement alone is insufficient for a grade III tear. Repair without osseous correction fails to address the mechanical deformity. Isolated subtalar arthrodesis or triple arthrodesis sacrifices motion and is reserved for rigid fixed deformity (stage III/IV).
learningPoint: Stage IIB AAFD with a flexible deformity and substantial PTT tear is treated with FDL transfer + medialising calcaneal osteotomy + spring ligament repair — a combined soft-tissue and bony procedure.

stem: A 55-year-old man has a rigid planovalgus deformity of the right foot with lateral impingement pain. Subtalar and transverse tarsal joints show no correction on clinical stress testing. Standing AP radiograph demonstrates a talo-first metatarsal (Meary's) angle of 22° and subtalar arthritic change on CT. What operative procedure is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Medialising calcaneal osteotomy and FDL transfer

options.label: B
options.text: Lateral column lengthening

options.label: C
options.text: Subtalar arthrodesis alone

options.label: D
options.text: Triple arthrodesis (subtalar, talonavicular, calcaneocuboid)

options.label: E
options.text: Total ankle replacement

explanation: A rigid flatfoot with established subtalar arthritic change on CT that cannot be passively corrected represents Johnson and Strom stage III AAFD (or Myerson stage IIIA). The treatment is triple arthrodesis to correct the multiplanar deformity and address the arthritic joints. Isolated subtalar arthrodesis leaves the transverse tarsal deformity uncorrected. Osteotomies and tendon procedures require a flexible deformity. Total ankle replacement addresses the tibiotalar joint and is not indicated here.
learningPoint: Rigid flatfoot with established subtalar arthritis (Stage III AAFD) is treated with triple arthrodesis; soft-tissue and osteotomy procedures are only for flexible deformity.

stem: A 46-year-old teacher presents with 8 months of inferior heel pain, worst on first steps in the morning. BMI is 31. Tenderness is maximal at the medial calcaneal tuberosity. X-ray shows a small calcaneal spur but no stress fracture. Physiotherapy and heel insoles for 6 months have not helped. Which intervention has the strongest evidence as the next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Corticosteroid injection into the plantar fascia origin

options.label: B
options.text: Extracorporeal shockwave therapy (ESWT)

options.label: C
options.text: Plantar fasciotomy

options.label: D
options.text: MRI to exclude occult calcaneal stress fracture

options.label: E
options.text: Platelet-rich plasma injection

explanation: NICE and BOFAS endorse ESWT as the evidence-based second-line intervention for chronic plantar fasciitis (>6 months) after failed conservative measures. Multiple RCTs and systematic reviews demonstrate superiority over placebo and corticosteroid injection for longer-term pain reduction. Corticosteroid injection (option A) provides short-term relief but risks plantar fascia rupture and fat pad atrophy with repeated use, and effect fades by 3 months. Surgical fasciotomy is a last resort after failed ESWT. MRI is not required when clinical diagnosis is secure. PRP has lower-quality evidence than ESWT at this stage.
learningPoint: After 6 months of failed conservative management, ESWT is the evidence-based next step for chronic plantar fasciitis before considering surgery.
references: NICE guidance on ESWT for plantar fasciitis

stem: During open plantar fasciotomy for recalcitrant plantar fasciitis, the surgeon inadvertently divides more than 50% of the plantar fascia. Which complication is the patient most at risk of developing?

options.label: A
options.text: Lateral plantar nerve neuritis

options.label: B
options.text: Progressive flatfoot deformity

options.label: C
options.text: Stress fracture of the second metatarsal

options.label: D
options.text: Reflex sympathetic dystrophy

options.label: E
options.text: Calcaneal stress fracture

explanation: The plantar fascia is a primary static stabiliser of the medial longitudinal arch (windlass mechanism). Complete or near-complete fasciotomy (>50% release) removes this support and can lead to progressive arch collapse and flatfoot deformity over time. Lateral plantar nerve injury is a recognised intraoperative risk but relates to dissection technique, not extent of fasciotomy. Metatarsal stress fractures are a complication of plantar fasciotomy because forces are redistributed, but this affects the lateral metatarsals more commonly with partial release. Calcaneal stress fracture is not a direct consequence of fasciotomy volume.
learningPoint: Releasing >50% of the plantar fascia disrupts the medial longitudinal arch windlass and risks progressive flatfoot — release should be limited to the medial third.

stem: A 24-year-old motorcyclist is involved in a road traffic collision. Foot radiographs appear near normal but he cannot weight-bear. A standing (or stress) radiograph shows 3 mm diastasis between the first and second metatarsal bases on the AP view. CT confirms a subtle fracture of the second metatarsal base and disruption of the Lisfranc ligament complex with no articular comminution. Which management gives the best long-term outcome?

options.label: A
options.text: Below-knee cast for 8 weeks non-weight-bearing

options.label: B
options.text: Open reduction and temporary Kirschner wire fixation

options.label: C
options.text: Open reduction and internal fixation with screws/plates

options.label: D
options.text: Primary arthrodesis of the medial three tarsometatarsal joints

options.label: E
options.text: ORIF with bioabsorbable screw fixation of the Lisfranc ligament

explanation: Evidence from the PROFIT trial and subsequent systematic reviews demonstrates that primary arthrodesis of the medial column (first, second, third TMT joints) gives superior functional outcomes compared with ORIF alone for ligamentous or predominantly ligamentous Lisfranc injuries. The purely ligamentous Lisfranc injury has a high rate of post-traumatic arthritis after ORIF, making primary fusion a rational choice. Conservative management is contraindicated for unstable injuries with >2 mm diastasis. Kirschner wires alone are biomechanically inadequate for weight-bearing joints. Bioabsorbable screws have inferior holding power compared with metal fixation.
learningPoint: For ligamentous Lisfranc injuries with >2 mm diastasis, primary arthrodesis of the medial three TMT joints gives better long-term outcomes than ORIF alone.

stem: A 32-year-old woman sustains a midfoot injury after her foot is forcibly plantarflexed in a horse-riding accident. Plain radiographs show lateral displacement of metatarsals 2–5 with fractures of the bases of the 2nd and 3rd metatarsals. The 1st TMT joint is intact. How is this Lisfranc injury classified according to the Myerson classification?

options.label: A
options.text: Type A — total incongruity, homolateral pattern

options.label: B
options.text: Type B1 — partial incongruity, medial displacement

options.label: C
options.text: Type B2 — partial incongruity, lateral displacement

options.label: D
options.text: Type C1 — divergent, partial displacement

options.label: E
options.text: Type C2 — divergent, total displacement

explanation: The Myerson classification: Type A = total incongruity (all rays displaced in same direction). Type B1 = partial incongruity medial displacement (1st ray only). Type B2 = partial incongruity lateral displacement (some lateral rays displaced with 1st TMT intact). Type C1 = divergent partial (1st ray medially, partial lateral displacement). Type C2 = divergent total. Here rays 2–5 are displaced laterally while the 1st TMT is intact — this is a Type B2 partial incongruity with lateral displacement.
learningPoint: Myerson Type B2 = lateral displacement of some rays with the 1st TMT joint intact — distinguish from Type A (all rays involved) and Type C (divergent pattern).

stem: A 61-year-old ex-rugby player has end-stage post-traumatic ankle osteoarthritis, confirmed on weight-bearing CT. He works as a secondary school PE teacher and wants to remain active. He has no significant coronal plane deformity (tibiotalar varus/valgus <5°). BMI is 26. Which surgical option gives the most durable pain relief at 10-year follow-up according to current evidence?

options.label: A
options.text: Total ankle replacement (TAR) with a third-generation three-component implant

options.label: B
options.text: Tibiotalar arthrodesis (ankle fusion)

options.label: C
options.text: Distraction arthroplasty with an external fixator

options.label: D
options.text: Supramalleolar osteotomy

options.label: E
options.text: Tibiotalar resurfacing hemiarthroplasty

explanation: Tibiotalar arthrodesis remains the gold standard for durable pain relief in end-stage ankle OA, with well-documented long-term outcomes exceeding 20 years. It is particularly preferred in younger, high-demand patients, those with significant deformity, prior infection, avascular necrosis, or insufficient bone stock for TAR. The evidence base for TAR survivorship at 10+ years is growing but still less robust than for fusion, and young active patients have higher revision rates with TAR. Distraction arthroplasty is a niche option for younger patients wishing to avoid fusion. Supramalleolar osteotomy is for early-to-moderate OA with coronal deformity, not end-stage disease.
learningPoint: Ankle arthrodesis remains the gold standard for end-stage ankle OA in active younger patients; TAR is an alternative but has lower survivorship in high-demand individuals.

stem: A 68-year-old woman with primary ankle OA undergoes total ankle replacement. Post-operative radiographs show a well-positioned talar component but the tibial tray is placed with 5° of anterior slope on the lateral radiograph. Which early complication is most directly related to this malalignment?

options.label: A
options.text: Wound dehiscence

options.label: B
options.text: Anterior impingement and decreased plantarflexion

options.label: C
options.text: Aseptic loosening of the talar component

options.label: D
options.text: Anterior subluxation of the polyethylene insert

options.label: E
options.text: Peroneal tendon impingement

explanation: Anterior slope (anterior tilting) of the tibial component directs the articular surface posteriorly, creating a ramp that promotes anterior migration and subluxation of the polyethylene bearing insert — a specific and recognised complication of tibial component malalignment in three-component total ankle replacements. Aseptic talar loosening is more related to excessive valgus/varus talar position or undersizing. Peroneal impingement relates to the fibular component or overhang. Wound issues relate to soft-tissue handling. Posterior tibial slope errors can also cause flexion instability but anterior slope primarily drives anterior insert migration.
learningPoint: Anterior tibial component slope in total ankle replacement promotes anterior polyethylene insert subluxation — proper sagittal alignment is critical.

stem: A 49-year-old man presents with stiff, painful right first MTP joint. Examination shows 15° of dorsiflexion and 5° of plantarflexion with pain at end range. Radiograph shows loss of joint space superiorly, large dorsal osteophyte, and preserved plantar joint space. This is Grade 2 Coughlin-Shurnas hallux rigidus. Which procedure best preserves motion while providing pain relief?

options.label: A
options.text: Cheilectomy (dorsal osteophyte excision)

options.label: B
options.text: Proximal phalangeal extension osteotomy (Moberg) alone

options.label: C
options.text: Cheilectomy combined with Moberg extension osteotomy

options.label: D
options.text: First MTP joint arthrodesis

options.label: E
options.text: First MTP joint hemiarthroplasty (proximal phalangeal resurfacing)

explanation: Grade 2 hallux rigidus with preserved plantar joint space is well treated by cheilectomy, which removes the dorsal 25–30% of the metatarsal head and all dorsal osteophytes, gaining functional dorsiflexion range. Adding a Moberg dorsal closing wedge extension osteotomy of the proximal phalanx reorients the arc of motion into a more functional position and improves push-off. This combination provides superior outcomes to cheilectomy alone in moderate disease. Arthrodesis (option D) is reserved for Grade 3–4 or failed cheilectomy. Hemiarthroplasty has no clear advantage and risks component failure and bone loss.
learningPoint: Grade 2 hallux rigidus with preserved plantar space is treated with cheilectomy ± Moberg osteotomy to preserve motion; arthrodesis is reserved for Grade 3–4.

stem: A 57-year-old woman has Grade 4 (Coughlin-Shurnas) hallux rigidus with global loss of joint space, subchondral cysts and <10° of painful dorsiflexion. She is a keen walker and refuses arthrodesis because she is concerned about footwear restriction. Which alternative gives the best evidence-based outcomes?

options.label: A
options.text: Cheilectomy with aggressive resection of dorsal 40% of metatarsal head

options.label: B
options.text: Silastic (Swanson) implant arthroplasty

options.label: C
options.text: Interposition arthroplasty using plantar capsule

options.label: D
options.text: First MTP total joint replacement with metallic components

options.label: E
options.text: Keller's resection arthroplasty

explanation: When patients with end-stage hallux rigidus decline arthrodesis, interposition arthroplasty (using the plantar plate or capsule as a biological spacer) is an established motion-preserving alternative with lower complication rates than implant arthroplasty. Silastic implants (Swanson) carry a significant risk of silicone synovitis and fragmentation over time. Total joint metallic replacement systems have limited evidence and higher failure rates than fusion. Aggressive cheilectomy alone (40% resection) risks metatarsal head destabilisation. Keller's resection arthroplasty weakens push-off and is associated with transfer metatarsalgia — it is now largely abandoned for active patients.
learningPoint: When arthrodesis is declined for Grade 4 hallux rigidus, interposition arthroplasty is preferred over implant or Keller's resection arthroplasty.

stem: A 43-year-old woman presents with burning pain and paraesthesia in the third web space of her left foot, radiating to the third and fourth toes, worsened by wearing narrow shoes. Mulder's click is positive. Ultrasound confirms an interdigital mass of 6 mm in the third web space. Which initial management is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Surgical excision via dorsal approach

options.label: B
options.text: Wide-fitting footwear advice, metatarsal pad, and physiotherapy

options.label: C
options.text: Ultrasound-guided corticosteroid injection

options.label: D
options.text: Ultrasound-guided alcohol sclerosant injection series

options.label: E
options.text: MRI of forefoot to confirm diagnosis

explanation: First-line management of Morton's neuroma is conservative: wide-fitting footwear to reduce forefoot compression, a metatarsal dome pad to splay the metatarsal heads, and physiotherapy. Many patients obtain sufficient relief without further intervention. US-guided corticosteroid injection is an appropriate second-line option if conservative measures fail. Alcohol sclerosant injections are a third-line option before surgery. Surgery is reserved for failed conservative and injection treatment. MRI is unnecessary when clinical diagnosis and ultrasound are concordant.
learningPoint: Morton's neuroma initial management is footwear modification and metatarsal padding; corticosteroid injection is second-line, surgery is third-line.

stem: A surgeon plans excision of a recurrent Morton's neuroma in the third web space after a previous plantar approach excision performed 2 years earlier. The nerve stump neuroma measures 9 mm on ultrasound. Which approach and technique is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Repeat plantar longitudinal approach

options.label: B
options.text: Dorsal approach with neuroma excision and proximal nerve stump burial into intrinsic muscle

options.label: C
options.text: Endoscopic resection

options.label: D
options.text: Ultrasound-guided radiofrequency ablation

options.label: E
options.text: Dorsal approach with metatarsal shortening osteotomy

explanation: For a recurrent or stump neuroma after prior plantar excision, the dorsal approach avoids the scarred plantar wound and allows adequate proximal transection of the nerve with burial of the stump into the intrinsic muscle belly to reduce symptomatic stump neuroma formation. Repeating the plantar approach through scar tissue risks wound complications and poor visualisation. Endoscopic resection has limited evidence for revision surgery. Radiofrequency ablation is not established for stump neuromas. Metatarsal shortening does not address the nerve stump.
learningPoint: Recurrent Morton's neuroma after plantar excision is best managed via a dorsal approach with proximal nerve transection and muscle burial of the stump.

stem: A 35-year-old man falls from a scaffold and sustains a closed AO/OTA 43-C3 pilon fracture with significant metaphyseal comminution and articular impaction. The soft tissues are markedly swollen with fracture blisters forming. CT is obtained. What is the recommended staged approach to definitive fixation?

options.label: A
options.text: Immediate ORIF of fibula and tibia within 6 hours of injury

options.label: B
options.text: Spanning external fixation of the ankle followed by definitive ORIF at 10–21 days when soft tissues improve

options.label: C
options.text: Fine-wire circular frame (Ilizarov) definitive fixation immediately

options.label: D
options.text: ORIF of fibula only followed by cast immobilisation for the tibia

options.label: E
options.text: Primary tibiotalar arthrodesis with locked nail

explanation: The staged protocol for high-energy pilon fractures with compromised soft tissues is well established: immediate spanning external fixation restores length and alignment, reduces swelling, and allows skin recovery. Definitive ORIF is deferred 10–21 days until the skin is supple, blisters have epithelialised, and the wrinkle sign is present. Immediate ORIF through compromised soft tissues carries a high rate of wound breakdown, infection, and osteomyelitis. Fine-wire frames can be used but are technically complex as primary management in non-specialist centres. Primary arthrodesis is a salvage option, not first-line management.
learningPoint: High-energy pilon fractures with compromised soft tissues are managed with staged protocol: spanning external fixation first, then ORIF at 10–21 days once soft tissues recover.

stem: During definitive ORIF of a pilon fracture, the surgeon notes that the anterolateral tibial plafond fragment (Chaput fragment) is displaced and involves approximately 30% of the articular surface. Which approach allows direct visualisation and fixation of this fragment?

options.label: A
options.text: Posteromedial approach

options.label: B
options.text: Anteromedial approach

options.label: C
options.text: Anterolateral approach

options.label: D
options.text: Posterolateral approach

options.label: E
options.text: Direct anterior approach through EHL

explanation: The Chaput (anterolateral) fragment of the pilon is best accessed via the anterolateral approach to the distal tibia. This approach passes between the extensor digitorum longus and peroneus tertius, providing direct visualisation of the anterolateral plafond. The anteromedial approach is used for medial column and central die-punch fragments. The posteromedial approach addresses the posterior malleolus (Volkmann fragment). The posterolateral approach targets the posterolateral and fibular fixation.
learningPoint: The Chaput (anterolateral plafond) fragment in a pilon fracture is accessed via the anterolateral approach between EDL and peroneus tertius.

stem: A 22-year-old man presents with bilateral cavovarus foot deformity causing recurrent lateral ankle sprains and peroneal tendon tears. Neurological examination reveals distal lower limb wasting, diminished ankle reflexes, and reduced sensation in the feet. The Coleman block test shows correction of the hindfoot varus with load removed from the first ray. Which underlying diagnosis should be excluded first?

options.label: A
options.text: Friedreich's ataxia

options.label: B
options.text: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy)

options.label: C
options.text: Cerebral palsy

options.label: D
options.text: Poliomyelitis sequelae

options.label: E
options.text: Residual clubfoot

explanation: Bilateral cavovarus foot with distal lower limb wasting, reduced reflexes, and reduced distal sensation in a young adult is the classic presentation of Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (CMT, hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy). CMT is the most common inherited neurological disorder and the most frequent neurological cause of cavovarus foot. All cavovarus deformities in young patients mandate neurological workup; CMT must be excluded first. Friedreich's ataxia causes cerebellar ataxia and cardiomyopathy — reflexes are often absent and there is upper limb involvement. Cerebral palsy typically presents in childhood with spasticity. Poliomyelitis is now rare in the UK.
learningPoint: Bilateral cavovarus foot with distal neurological signs in a young patient requires exclusion of Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease first; Coleman block test determines flexibility of hindfoot varus.

stem: A 19-year-old man with CMT disease has a flexible cavovarus foot correctable on Coleman block testing. He has plantarflexion deformity of the first ray driving the hindfoot into varus, clawed lesser toes, and intrinsic weakness. Ankle stability is adequate. Which combination of procedures is most appropriate for surgical correction?

options.label: A
options.text: Plantar fascia release alone

options.label: B
options.text: Calcaneal lateralising osteotomy with plantar fascia release

options.label: C
options.text: First ray dorsiflexion osteotomy, plantar fascia release, peroneus longus to brevis transfer, and calcaneal osteotomy

options.label: D
options.text: Triple arthrodesis

options.label: E
options.text: Tibialis posterior transfer to dorsum of foot

explanation: Flexible cavovarus correction in CMT targets each component of the deformity: plantar fascia release to reduce cavus; first metatarsal dorsiflexion osteotomy to correct plantarflexed first ray (the primary driver of hindfoot varus); peroneus longus to brevis transfer to remove the deforming force on the first ray and augment eversion; calcaneal lateralising osteotomy to correct residual hindfoot varus. Plantar fascia release alone is insufficient. Triple arthrodesis is reserved for rigid deformity. Tibialis posterior transfer is used for foot drop correction, not cavovarus.
learningPoint: Flexible CMT cavovarus is corrected by addressing each pathological element: plantar fascia release + first ray osteotomy + PL-to-PB transfer + calcaneal lateralising osteotomy.

stem: A 28-year-old female runner has had 12 episodes of 'giving way' of the right ankle over 2 years. Anterior drawer test is positive and talar tilt test is positive. MRI shows complete tears of the anterior talofibular ligament (ATFL) and calcaneofibular ligament (CFL). She has completed 6 months of physiotherapy with proprioceptive training without improvement. Which surgical procedure is most appropriate and has the best evidence?

options.label: A
options.text: Anatomical ATFL repair with periosteal flap (Broström-Gould procedure)

options.label: B
options.text: Non-anatomical tenodesis using peroneus brevis (Chrisman-Snook procedure)

options.label: C
options.text: Free tendon graft reconstruction (augmented Broström)

options.label: D
options.text: Arthroscopic ankle stabilisation with suture anchor repair

options.label: E
options.text: Ankle arthrodesis

explanation: The modified Broström-Gould anatomical repair — reattachment of the ATFL and CFL with reinforcement by the inferior extensor retinaculum — is the gold-standard procedure for chronic lateral ankle instability with the highest evidence base (multiple RCTs, systematic reviews). It preserves normal ankle and subtalar kinematics and allows full return to sport. Non-anatomical tenodesis (Chrisman-Snook) restricts subtalar motion and is now largely historical. Augmented Broström with tendon graft is reserved for revision or lax tissue. Arthroscopic techniques using suture anchors are an emerging alternative but current level-1 evidence still favours open Broström-Gould as the comparator standard.
learningPoint: The modified Broström-Gould anatomical repair is the gold standard for chronic lateral ankle instability after failed physiotherapy.

stem: A 35-year-old man with generalised joint hypermobility (Beighton score 7/9) and recurrent lateral ankle instability has previously had a Broström-Gould repair that failed after 18 months. MRI shows attenuation and stretching of the repaired ATFL. Which reconstruction technique offers the most biomechanically stable solution in this revision setting?

options.label: A
options.text: Repeat Broström-Gould with aggressive retinacular advancement

options.label: B
options.text: Internal brace augmentation with a synthetic ligament

options.label: C
options.text: Reconstruction with hamstring or gracilis free tendon graft in anatomical ATFL and CFL positions

options.label: D
options.text: Chrisman-Snook non-anatomical tenodesis

options.label: E
options.text: Ankle arthrodesis

explanation: In a hypermobile patient with a failed Broström-Gould repair and attenuated residual tissue, anatomical reconstruction using a free tendon graft (gracilis or hamstring) placed in the anatomical ATFL and CFL tunnels provides durable stability without sacrificing the peroneal musculotendinous unit or restricting subtalar motion. Repeat Broström on attenuated tissue is likely to fail again. Internal brace with synthetic ligament (e.g., InternalBrace) is an augmentation option but lacks long-term comparative data in hypermobile revision cases. Chrisman-Snook sacrifices peroneus brevis and restricts subtalar motion. Arthrodesis is a last resort.
learningPoint: Failed Broström-Gould in a hypermobile patient warrants anatomical free tendon graft reconstruction rather than repeat soft-tissue repair on attenuated tissue.

stem: Theme: Classification of ankle fractures. For each scenario, select the single most appropriate classification descriptor from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Weber A — fibular fracture below the ankle syndesmosis

options.label: B
options.text: Weber B — fibular fracture at the level of the syndesmosis

options.label: C
options.text: Weber C — fibular fracture above the syndesmosis

options.label: D
options.text: Lauge-Hansen supination-adduction stage I

options.label: E
options.text: Lauge-Hansen supination-adduction stage II

options.label: F
options.text: Lauge-Hansen pronation-external rotation stage IV

options.label: G
options.text: Maisonneuve fracture

options.label: H
options.text: Pilon fracture — AO 43-C type

emiLeads.stem: A 26-year-old footballer inverts his ankle during play. Radiograph shows a transverse avulsion fracture of the distal fibular tip 1 cm below the tibiotalar joint line. The medial clear space is normal.

A 52-year-old woman trips on a step. AP radiograph shows a vertical shear fracture of the medial malleolus and a transverse fibular fracture at the level of the mortise. External rotation stress views widen the medial clear space to 6 mm.

A 19-year-old man sustains a twisting injury to his ankle. Plain films show only a small medial malleolus avulsion fracture. On palpation, he has exquisite proximal fibular tenderness at the level of the neck. Widened tibiofibular clear space is noted on ankle AP.

A 45-year-old woman falls from a height of 2 metres landing on her feet. Radiograph shows severe communition of the distal tibial articular surface with extension through the metaphysis and a separate fibular fracture. There is no open wound.

explanation: Scenario 1: transverse fibular tip avulsion below the mortise is a classic Weber A / supination-adduction stage I — syndesmosis intact. Scenario 2: vertical medial malleolus fracture plus transverse fibula at joint level with positive stress test is Lauge-Hansen supination-adduction stage II (medial malleolus fracture is stage II; a positive stress test indicates medial side disruption). Scenario 3: medial malleolus avulsion with proximal fibular fracture and widened tibiofibular clear space is a Maisonneuve fracture — the energy exits through the proximal fibula. Scenario 4: high-energy axial load with articular comminution of the distal tibia is an AO 43-C pilon fracture.

stem: Theme: Management of diabetic foot conditions. For each clinical scenario, select the single most appropriate immediate management from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Total contact casting and strict non-weight-bearing

options.label: B
options.text: Emergency surgical debridement and IV antibiotics

options.label: C
options.text: Vascular surgical referral for revascularisation

options.label: D
options.text: 6 weeks of oral targeted antibiotic therapy

options.label: E
options.text: Elective midfoot stabilisation arthrodesis

options.label: F
options.text: Maggot debridement therapy and topical wound care

options.label: G
options.text: Below-knee amputation as primary treatment

emiLeads.stem: A 70-year-old man with type 2 diabetes presents with a hot, red, oedematous right foot over 3 weeks. Temperature is 37.1°C. CRP is 9 mg/L. WBC is 8.2 × 10⁹/L. X-ray shows midfoot bony fragmentation and subluxation without gas. He is neuropathic but has palpable foot pulses.

A 58-year-old woman with diabetic neuropathy presents acutely with a deep plantar forefoot wound, spreading cellulitis to the ankle, and a black necrotic third toe. Temperature 39.1°C. WBC 19 × 10⁹/L. CRP 280 mg/L. Glucose 24 mmol/L.

A 65-year-old man with type 2 diabetes and peripheral arterial disease presents with a non-healing plantar heel ulcer of 8 weeks' duration. ABPI is 0.4. Toe pressure is 18 mmHg. The wound base has no clinical signs of infection.

explanation: Scenario 1: bony destruction without systemic infection signs in a neuropathic foot with intact vasculature is acute Charcot neuroarthropathy — total contact casting is first-line. Scenario 2: spreading infection, necrosis, high inflammatory markers and fever represent a limb-threatening diabetic foot infection (SINBAD grade 5–6) requiring emergency surgical debridement and IV antibiotics. Scenario 3: a non-healing ischaemic wound with ABPI 0.4 and toe pressure 18 mmHg has critical ischaemia — the limb cannot heal without revascularisation, which takes priority over wound management.

stem: Theme: Foot and ankle tendon pathology. For each clinical scenario, select the single most likely diagnosis from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Complete Achilles tendon rupture

options.label: B
options.text: Insertional Achilles tendinopathy with Haglund's deformity

options.label: C
options.text: Posterior tibial tendon dysfunction (PTTD) stage II

options.label: D
options.text: Peroneus brevis longitudinal split tear

options.label: E
options.text: Flexor hallucis longus tenosynovitis

options.label: F
options.text: Tibialis anterior tendon rupture

options.label: G
options.text: Peroneal tendon subluxation

emiLeads.stem: A 34-year-old ballet dancer presents with posteromedial ankle pain and clicking during demi-pointe. MRI shows fluid around the flexor tendon in the fibro-osseous tunnel behind the medial malleolus and a partial longitudinal tear.

A 47-year-old woman describes a 12-month history of progressive medial ankle pain and dragging of the right foot. Examination shows a flexible flatfoot, inability to perform a single-leg heel rise, and too-many-toes sign. MRI demonstrates fusiform swelling and intrasubstance signal change in the tendon medial to the ankle.

An 82-year-old woman presents after tripping on a kerbstone. She notes she can no longer lift her foot when walking and has painless foot drop. She remembers wearing a plaster cast for an ankle injury 6 months earlier. Examination reveals weakness of dorsiflexion; first MTP dorsiflexion is preserved.

A 22-year-old snowboarder falls and twists his ankle. At the time of injury he felt a snap behind the lateral malleolus and has since had recurring 'clunking' around the lateral ankle during dorsiflexion. Ultrasound shows the peroneal tendons moving anterior to the lateral malleolus.

explanation: Scenario 1: posteromedial click with FHL tendon signal change behind medial malleolus in a dancer is FHL tenosynovitis — hallmark of ballet-associated posterior ankle impingement/FHL pathology. Scenario 2: progressive flatfoot with failed heel rise and medial ankle pain with tendon changes is classic PTTD stage II (flexible deformity, tendon degenerate but not ruptured). Scenario 3: painless foot drop after prior immobilisation in an elderly woman with preserved first MTP extension (FHL intact) points to delayed tibialis anterior rupture — distinct from peroneal nerve palsy. Scenario 4: lateral ankle snap with imaging confirming tendon displacement anterior to lateral malleolus is peroneal tendon subluxation due to superior retinaculum disruption.

stem: Theme: Operative fixation strategies for ankle and pilon fractures. For each scenario, select the single most appropriate operative fixation strategy from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Lateral fibula anatomical plate and screws only

options.label: B
options.text: Lateral fibula plate, medial malleolus screws, and trans-syndesmotic positioning screws

options.label: C
options.text: Tension band wiring of medial malleolus

options.label: D
options.text: Immediate spanning external fixation followed by staged ORIF at 2–3 weeks

options.label: E
options.text: Non-operative management in a below-knee cast

options.label: F
options.text: Retrograde tibiotalar nail (arthrodesis nail)

options.label: G
options.text: Intramedullary fibular rod (Knowles-type) and medial malleolus screws

emiLeads.stem: A 78-year-old woman with severe osteoporosis and type 2 diabetes sustains a trimalleolar ankle fracture-dislocation. She is ambulatory only indoors with a frame. Bone quality is poor. Lateral plate fixation has been performed; intraoperative testing shows persistent instability. The surgeon seeks the most durable fixation construct for this frail patient.

A 45-year-old man falls from a ladder sustaining a closed pilon fracture with a blistering anterior compartment over the tibia and a tense ankle. CT confirms AO 43-C2 injury with three articular fragments. Adequate theatre time is not available tonight.

A 50-year-old woman with end-stage ankle OA sustains a comminuted distal fibular fracture in the same ankle. She is already listed for ankle replacement and her bone quality is poor. The fracture is at the level of the syndesmosis. Surgeons decide on immediate fixation at the time of the fracture clinic.

explanation: Scenario 1: trimalleolar fracture-dislocation in a diabetic osteoporotic patient with residual intraoperative instability needs syndesmotic screw(s) in addition to fibula and medial fixation to achieve stability in poor-quality bone. Scenario 2: high-energy pilon with blistered skin and no adequate theatre time is managed with immediate spanning external fixation — definitive ORIF is deferred to allow soft-tissue recovery. Scenario 3: low-demand, poor-quality bone with an at-syndesmosis fibular fracture in an existing OA ankle (already listed for arthroplasty) is a good indication for intramedullary fibular rod fixation which provides load-sharing, low-profile fixation without bulky lateral plating that would complicate subsequent TAR.

stem: A 24-year-old female recreational footballer twists her left knee during a non-contact deceleration manoeuvre. She reports a 'pop', immediate haemarthrosis and inability to continue playing. Lachman's test is grade 2 positive with a soft end-point. She is otherwise fit and well. After 6 weeks of physiotherapy she still has episodes of giving way. She wishes to return to competitive football. Which reconstruction graft choice has the strongest evidence for lowest re-rupture rate in young active females?

options.label: A
options.text: Ipsilateral bone–patellar tendon–bone autograft

options.label: B
options.text: Ipsilateral 4-strand hamstring tendon autograft

options.label: C
options.text: Contralateral bone–patellar tendon–bone autograft

options.label: D
options.text: Quadriceps tendon autograft with bone plug

options.label: E
options.text: Tibialis anterior allograft

explanation: In young active patients — especially females under 25 — multiple registry and RCT data (STABILITY, Swedish ACL Register) show BPTB autograft carries a statistically lower re-rupture rate than hamstring autograft in high-demand pivoting sport. Hamstring graft (B) has similar functional outcomes but higher revision rates in this demographic. Contralateral harvest (C) introduces donor-site morbidity to the uninjured leg without registry evidence of benefit. Quadriceps tendon (D) and allograft (E) have insufficient high-quality comparative data to displace BPTB as first-choice in young females returning to pivoting sport.
learningPoint: BPTB autograft is preferred over hamstring autograft in young high-demand females due to lower re-rupture rates in current registry evidence.

stem: A 28-year-old male rugby player undergoes primary ACL reconstruction with 4-strand hamstring autograft. Eighteen months later he sustains a re-rupture playing competitive rugby. MRI confirms complete graft failure with an intact medial meniscus. He has a normal contralateral knee. Revision ACL reconstruction is planned. Which intraoperative finding most influences tunnel position strategy?

options.label: A
options.text: Residual soft tissue in the femoral notch

options.label: B
options.text: Tibial tunnel diameter greater than 12 mm on CT

options.label: C
options.text: Posterior tibial slope greater than 12 degrees

options.label: D
options.text: Degree of chondral wear on medial femoral condyle

options.label: E
options.text: Pre-existing tibial tunnel position within the anatomic ACL footprint

explanation: In revision ACL surgery, a tibial tunnel diameter exceeding 12 mm (lytic widening) mandates a staged approach — bone grafting first, then reconstruction at 3–6 months — because an oversized tunnel cannot provide secure graft fixation in a single stage. Residual soft tissue (A) is easily debrided. Posterior tibial slope (C) influences re-rupture risk and slope-reducing osteotomy may be indicated but does not directly determine staging. Chondral wear (D) is noted but does not change tunnel strategy. Tunnel malposition (E) may require offset reamers but is a secondary consideration to tunnel diameter.
learningPoint: A tibial tunnel diameter >12 mm in revision ACL surgery necessitates staged bone grafting before definitive reconstruction.

stem: A 32-year-old man with a chronic ACL-deficient knee is found on MRI to have a posterior tibial slope of 15 degrees, medial compartment cartilage loss (ICRS grade 3) and varus alignment of 5 degrees on long-leg films. He has daily giving-way episodes. Which adjunct procedure, if any, is most strongly supported alongside ACL reconstruction in this scenario?

options.label: A
options.text: Lateral extra-articular tenodesis

options.label: B
options.text: Medial opening wedge high tibial osteotomy

options.label: C
options.text: Anterior closing wedge proximal tibial osteotomy to reduce slope

options.label: D
options.text: Medial meniscal root repair

options.label: E
options.text: No adjunct — intra-articular reconstruction alone is sufficient

explanation: A posterior tibial slope ≥12 degrees is an independent risk factor for ACL graft failure, and slope-reducing anterior closing wedge tibial osteotomy (reducing slope to ≤8 degrees) is supported in revision/chronic settings with excessive slope. The varus alignment in this case is mild (5 degrees) and cartilage loss is medial — a medial opening wedge HTO (B) would address alignment and offload the medial compartment but would worsen tibial slope. Lateral extra-articular tenodesis (A) addresses rotational laxity but not slope, and evidence for its addition in primary cases with slope concern is limited. Medial meniscal root repair (D) is not indicated here. Intra-articular reconstruction alone (E) carries high re-failure risk with a slope of 15 degrees.
learningPoint: Posterior tibial slope >12 degrees in ACL-deficient knees should prompt consideration of slope-reducing tibial osteotomy to protect the reconstruction.

stem: A 42-year-old woman presents with 3 months of medial knee pain after a twisting injury. MRI shows a vertical bucket-handle tear of the medial meniscus with the handle fragment displaced into the intercondylar notch. She has a 10-degree extension deficit. She has no radiographic arthrosis and is active. What is the most appropriate primary surgical treatment?

options.label: A
options.text: Partial meniscectomy of the displaced fragment

options.label: B
options.text: All-inside meniscal repair

options.label: C
options.text: Reduction and inside-out meniscal repair

options.label: D
options.text: Total meniscectomy

options.label: E
options.text: Meniscal allograft transplantation

explanation: A displaced bucket-handle tear in a patient under 50 with intact articular cartilage should be repaired, not excised, wherever possible to preserve meniscal function and reduce future OA risk. Inside-out repair allows the best multi-point fixation for a large vertical bucket-handle tear and is the standard technique for mid-body tears. All-inside repair (B) suits posterior horn tears but has limited reach for a displaced handle. Partial meniscectomy (A) sacrifices meniscal tissue unnecessarily in a suitable candidate. Total meniscectomy (D) is rarely indicated. Allograft (E) is a reconstructive option after meniscal loss, not primary treatment.
learningPoint: Displaced bucket-handle meniscal tears in patients under 50 with intact cartilage should be reduced and repaired, with inside-out technique preferred for mid-body tears.

stem: A 55-year-old male runner presents acutely with medial knee pain and a feeling of the knee 'sinking'. MRI reveals a complete radial tear of the posterior root of the medial meniscus with meniscal extrusion of 5 mm. There is Kellgren-Lawrence grade 1 change in the medial compartment. He has no varus deformity. Which statement best describes the consequence of untreated medial meniscal root tear?

options.label: A
options.text: The tear will heal spontaneously in most cases without progression

options.label: B
options.text: It biomechanically converts the medial meniscus to a functionally absent state, increasing contact stress by 25%

options.label: C
options.text: Progression to end-stage medial compartment OA occurs in nearly all cases within 5 years

options.label: D
options.text: Medial compartment contact pressures increase by approximately 70–90% compared to an intact root

options.label: E
options.text: The clinical presentation mirrors that of a complete medial collateral ligament rupture

explanation: Cadaveric and computational biomechanical studies demonstrate that a complete posterior medial meniscal root tear increases medial compartment contact pressures by 70–90%, essentially equivalent to a total meniscectomy. This is because the hoop stress mechanism is abolished. Spontaneous healing (A) does not occur for radial root tears. Progression to end-stage OA (C) is rapid but not universal within 5 years; studies show ~45–65% progression at 2 years. The 25% figure (B) understates the actual pressure increase. Clinical presentation is distinct from MCL rupture (E) — the root tear presents with posterior medial pain and extrusion, not valgus instability.
learningPoint: A complete medial meniscal root tear abolishes hoop stress and raises medial contact pressures by 70–90%, equivalent to total meniscectomy — repair is indicated in active patients with preserved cartilage.

stem: A 38-year-old woman undergoes arthroscopic partial medial meniscectomy for a symptomatic degenerative horizontal cleavage tear. Three years post-operatively she develops worsening medial knee pain. Long-leg films show 4 degrees of varus with Kellgren-Lawrence grade 2 medial compartment changes. BMI is 27. She is a keen recreational cyclist. She responds well to a 3-month physiotherapy programme. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Medial unicompartmental knee replacement

options.label: B
options.text: Repeat arthroscopic washout and debridement

options.label: C
options.text: Medial meniscal allograft transplantation

options.label: D
options.text: Medial opening wedge high tibial osteotomy

options.label: E
options.text: Total knee replacement

explanation: Meniscal allograft transplantation (MAT) is indicated in a young, active, post-meniscectomy patient with compartmental pain, preserved cartilage (KL ≤ 2) and correctable or near-normal alignment. The goal is to restore meniscal function and delay or prevent OA progression before joint replacement is appropriate. UKR (A) requires more advanced arthrosis; this patient has KL grade 2 and would have a predictably poor outcome from UKR at 38. Repeat washout (B) is not evidence-based for degenerative disease. HTO (D) addresses alignment but not the missing meniscus — and with 4-degree varus and KL 2, MAT ± concurrent correction may be preferable. TKR (E) is not appropriate at this age with this degree of arthrosis.
learningPoint: Meniscal allograft transplantation is the appropriate intervention in symptomatic post-meniscectomy syndrome with KL ≤ 2 arthrosis in active patients under 50.

stem: A 30-year-old motorcyclist sustains a dashboard injury to his left knee. Examination reveals a posterior sag at 90 degrees of flexion, a positive dial test at 30 degrees only (15 degrees of increased external rotation compared with the contralateral side), and a negative dial test at 90 degrees. Valgus and varus stress tests are normal. What is the most likely anatomical injury pattern?

options.label: A
options.text: Isolated posterior cruciate ligament tear

options.label: B
options.text: PCL tear with posterolateral corner disruption

options.label: C
options.text: Isolated posterolateral corner disruption

options.label: D
options.text: PCL tear with posteromedial corner disruption

options.label: E
options.text: Bi-cruciate ligament tear

explanation: A positive dial test at 30 degrees but negative at 90 degrees indicates isolated posterolateral corner (PLC) disruption, not PCL injury. If the PCL were also torn, the dial test would remain positive at 90 degrees because the PCL is the primary restraint in deeper flexion. The posterior sag is best explained by PLC disruption creating posterior tibial subluxation in this context. PCL with PLC (B) would produce a positive dial test at both 30 and 90 degrees. Isolated PCL (A) produces a posterior sag but typically a negative dial test. Posteromedial corner injury (D) would increase internal rotation, not external. Bi-cruciate injury (E) would show laxity in multiple planes.
learningPoint: A positive dial test at 30 degrees only indicates isolated PLC disruption; positivity at both 30 and 90 degrees implicates combined PCL + PLC injury.

stem: A 26-year-old woman sustains a combined PCL and grade III PLC injury in a road traffic accident. MRI confirms disruption of the popliteus tendon, popliteofibular ligament, and lateral collateral ligament, with a grade III PCL tear. She is haemodynamically stable, neurovascularly intact, and referred 3 days post-injury. When should definitive ligament reconstruction be performed?

options.label: A
options.text: Immediately (within 48 hours of injury)

options.label: B
options.text: Within 2–3 weeks of injury while tissue planes are still identifiable

options.label: C
options.text: At 6 weeks once scar tissue has matured and planes are recoverable

options.label: D
options.text: At 3 months once full range of motion has been restored

options.label: E
options.text: After 6 months of conservative management regardless of functional result

explanation: The optimal window for acute PLC reconstruction is within 2–3 weeks of injury. Primary repair (especially of the PLC) has significantly lower success rates after 3 weeks because scar formation degrades tissue quality and tissue planes become indistinct. Operating immediately (A) risks excess swelling and poorer visualisation. At 6 weeks (C) the scar is not yet remodelled and tissue is friable, making repair unreliable — reconstruction with graft is needed instead of repair. Delayed reconstruction at 3 months (D) or 6 months (E) sacrifices the opportunity for direct tissue repair and risks irreversible varus and external rotation deformity from prolonged instability.
learningPoint: Acute PCL/PLC injuries are best addressed surgically within 2–3 weeks of injury, when direct tissue repair of the PLC is still feasible.

stem: A 17-year-old female presents following her third lateral patellar dislocation. MRI shows medial patellofemoral ligament (MPFL) rupture, a trochlear groove that appears shallow on axial cuts, and a TT-TG distance of 18 mm. She has no chondral lesions. Tibial tubercle elevation shows that the patella is in a normal vertical position (Caton-Deschamps index 1.0). What is the most appropriate surgical treatment?

options.label: A
options.text: MPFL reconstruction alone

options.label: B
options.text: MPFL reconstruction with tibial tubercle medialisation (Fulkerson osteotomy)

options.label: C
options.text: MPFL reconstruction with trochleoplasty

options.label: D
options.text: Lateral retinacular release alone

options.label: E
options.text: Trochleoplasty alone

explanation: A TT-TG distance of 18 mm sits at the upper threshold for significance (normal <15 mm; 15–20 mm borderline; >20 mm clearly abnormal), and with recurrent instability and MPFL disruption, MPFL reconstruction combined with tibial tubercle medialisation is most appropriate to address both the soft-tissue and bony contributors. MPFL reconstruction alone (A) would be preferred if TT-TG were <15 mm, but 18 mm with recurrent instability warrants combined treatment. Trochleoplasty (C or E) is reserved for high-grade trochlear dysplasia (Dejour types B/D with a supratrochlear spur or crossing sign) — shallow trochlea without these features is not an indication. Lateral release alone (D) is contraindicated for instability.
learningPoint: In recurrent patellar instability with TT-TG 16–20 mm, MPFL reconstruction combined with tibial tubercle medialisation addresses both soft-tissue and bony risk factors.

stem: A 22-year-old man has had multiple patellar dislocations since age 14. He has Dejour type D trochlear dysplasia (crossing sign, supratrochlear spur, double contour on lateral radiograph), TT-TG of 14 mm, and Caton-Deschamps index of 1.5. He has a significant chondral injury on the lateral patellar facet. Which combination of procedures is most likely to address all his anatomical risk factors?

options.label: A
options.text: MPFL reconstruction + trochleoplasty

options.label: B
options.text: MPFL reconstruction + distal tubercle medialisation

options.label: C
options.text: Trochleoplasty + distal tubercle anteromedialization + MPFL reconstruction

options.label: D
options.text: Trochleoplasty + Elmslie-Trillat procedure

options.label: E
options.text: Total patellectomy

explanation: This patient has three anatomical risk factors: severe trochlear dysplasia (Dejour D — requiring trochleoplasty), patella alta (CD index 1.5 — requiring distalisation of the tibial tubercle), and MPFL insufficiency (requiring reconstruction). A TT-TG of 14 mm is borderline but the tubercle also needs distalising, so anteromedialization (Fulkerson/AMZ) addresses both lateralisation and the alta component while the chondral injury on the lateral facet benefits from anterior unloading. Omitting trochleoplasty (A, B) leaves the most severe dysplastic risk untreated. Option D omits MPFL reconstruction and the Elmslie-Trillat alone does not address the alta or the dysplasia adequately. Total patellectomy (E) is a salvage procedure.
learningPoint: Dejour type D dysplasia with patella alta requires triple-component correction: trochleoplasty + tubercle anteromedialization/distalisation + MPFL reconstruction.

stem: A 35-year-old male pedestrian is brought to A&E following a high-speed vehicle collision. His left knee is grossly unstable. On arrival he has an ABI (ankle-brachial pressure index) of 0.82 on the affected limb. Dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses are palpable but reduced. Radiographs show no fracture. He has a multi-ligament knee injury. What is the single most important immediate management step?

options.label: A
options.text: Urgent MRI of the knee to characterise the ligamentous injury

options.label: B
options.text: CT angiography of the popliteal vessels

options.label: C
options.text: Application of a knee immobiliser and admission for observation

options.label: D
options.text: Immediate surgical ligament reconstruction

options.label: E
options.text: Diagnostic knee arthroscopy

explanation: An ABI <0.9 following knee dislocation mandates urgent vascular imaging. Up to 25–35% of knee dislocations have popliteal artery injury, which may not be clinically apparent as collateral circulation can maintain palpable distal pulses initially. A 6-hour ischaemia window exists before irreversible muscle necrosis begins. CT angiography is the investigation of choice and allows rapid identification of intimal tears, thrombosis, or transection. MRI (A) characterises ligament injury but is not the priority when vascular compromise is suspected. Observation alone (C) risks missing evolving ischaemia. Immediate ligament reconstruction (D) is premature before vascular assessment. Arthroscopy (E) should be avoided acutely in a dislocated knee.
learningPoint: ABI <0.9 following knee dislocation mandates urgent CT angiography regardless of the presence of palpable pulses, to exclude occult popliteal artery injury.

stem: A 29-year-old woman sustains a Schenck KD-III-L dislocation (ACL + PCL + PLC disruption). Vascular assessment is normal. Her injury is 10 days old. Neurological examination reveals foot drop and absent sensation over the dorsum of the foot. Nerve conduction studies confirm a complete common peroneal nerve injury. She asks about recovery. What is the most accurate statement regarding her neurological prognosis?

options.label: A
options.text: Full neurological recovery is expected in 95% of cases within 6 months

options.label: B
options.text: Recovery depends on whether the nerve injury is neuropraxia, axonotmesis, or neurotmesis, with complete CPN lesions having approximately 30–40% full recovery rate

options.label: C
options.text: Urgent nerve repair within 72 hours is the standard of care

options.label: D
options.text: The peroneal nerve almost always recovers spontaneously within 3 months without intervention

options.label: E
options.text: Early ankle fusion prevents deformity and is indicated within 3 weeks

explanation: Common peroneal nerve injury in knee dislocation carries a guarded prognosis. Approximately 30–40% of complete CPN injuries achieve full recovery; rates approach 70–80% for incomplete injuries (neuropraxia). The injury is usually a traction neuropraxia or axonotmesis; neurotmesis is less common but can occur with avulsion. NCS at 10 days may not yet distinguish between grades. Recovery is not expected in 95% (A). Urgent nerve repair within 72 hours (C) is not the standard for closed traction injuries. Spontaneous recovery within 3 months (D) is optimistic for a complete lesion. Ankle fusion (E) is a very late salvage procedure, not an early intervention.
learningPoint: Complete common peroneal nerve injury in knee dislocation has approximately 30–40% full recovery rate; incomplete injuries have a substantially better prognosis.

subtopic: knee OA — UKR vs TKR indications
stem: A 63-year-old woman presents with isolated medial compartment knee OA (Kellgren-Lawrence grade 3) causing significant pain limiting her daily activities. She has a correctable varus deformity of 8 degrees on weight-bearing films, an intact ACL on examination and MRI, and a full-thickness medial compartment on the lateral weight-bearing view. BMI is 30. She has no patellofemoral symptoms. Which of the following is most relevant to the decision between unicompartmental and total knee replacement?

options.label: A
options.text: BMI above 30 is an absolute contraindication to UKR

options.label: B
options.text: A varus deformity >5 degrees is an absolute contraindication to UKR

options.label: C
options.text: ACL integrity is the single most important prerequisite for medial UKR

options.label: D
options.text: Patient age below 60 should preclude UKR due to implant longevity concerns

options.label: E
options.text: The presence of lateral compartment chondrosis detected on MRI automatically excludes UKR

explanation: An intact ACL is the most critical prerequisite for medial UKR, because the ACL provides anteroposterior stability that the UKR relies on — ACL deficiency leads to early anteromedial escape and bearing dislocation. Modern evidence has relaxed many historical Oxford UKR criteria: BMI >30 is no longer an absolute contraindication (A — false); varus deformity up to 10–15 degrees that corrects passively is acceptable (B — false); younger patients have excellent longevity with modern UKR (D — false); asymptomatic lateral chondrosis detected incidentally on MRI does not exclude UKR (E — false) as decision should be based on clinical and radiological compartmental assessment.
learningPoint: ACL integrity is the essential prerequisite for medial UKR; the previously strict criteria for BMI, age, and deformity degree have been substantially relaxed by modern evidence.

subtopic: knee OA — UKR vs TKR indications
stem: A 72-year-old man with tricompartmental knee OA (KL4 throughout) has undergone 18 months of physiotherapy, analgesia optimisation, and two intra-articular corticosteroid injections. He still reports severe pain limiting walking to 100 metres and significant night pain. He is otherwise well. Radiographs show severe tricompartmental arthrosis with bone-on-bone contact medially and laterally. What is the most appropriate next management step?

options.label: A
options.text: Repeat intra-articular corticosteroid injection

options.label: B
options.text: Arthroscopic joint debridement and washout

options.label: C
options.text: Hyaluronic acid injection

options.label: D
options.text: Total knee replacement

options.label: E
options.text: Platelet-rich plasma injection

explanation: This patient meets NICE NG226 thresholds for TKR: tricompartmental OA with bone-on-bone change, failure of adequate conservative treatment including physiotherapy and corticosteroid injections, and significant functional limitation. TKR is the appropriate definitive intervention. Repeat corticosteroid injection (A) is unlikely to provide meaningful benefit given failure of previous injections and the degree of arthrosis. Arthroscopic washout (B) is contraindicated by NICE for knee OA — no evidence of benefit. Hyaluronic acid (C) lacks robust evidence in severe OA and is not NICE-recommended. PRP (E) has insufficient evidence in established severe tricompartmental OA.
learningPoint: Total knee replacement is indicated for severe symptomatic tricompartmental OA after failure of adequate conservative management including physiotherapy and injection therapy.

stem: A 46-year-old male construction worker has medial compartment knee OA with varus deformity. Long-leg alignment films show a mechanical axis deviation of 8 mm medial to the medial tibial spine. The medial proximal tibial angle (MPTA) is 82 degrees and the lateral distal femoral angle (LDFA) is 89 degrees. ACL is intact. Lateral compartment is preserved. He is motivated for active rehabilitation. Which type of osteotomy is most appropriate to correct the deformity to neutral mechanical axis?

options.label: A
options.text: Medial opening wedge high tibial osteotomy (MOWHTO)

options.label: B
options.text: Lateral closing wedge high tibial osteotomy (LCWHTO)

options.label: C
options.text: Distal femoral valgus osteotomy

options.label: D
options.text: Double level osteotomy (tibial + femoral)

options.label: E
options.text: Tibial slope-reducing anterior closing wedge osteotomy

explanation: The MPTA of 82 degrees (normal ~87 degrees) confirms the deformity is at the proximal tibia. With the LDFA within normal limits at 89 degrees (normal ~87 degrees), the deformity origin is tibial and correction should be at the tibia. MOWHTO is now the preferred technique due to preserved fibula and decreased risk of neurovascular injury compared to lateral closing wedge (B). An LCWHTO (B) is an alternative but carries peroneal nerve risk and hardware prominence issues. Distal femoral osteotomy (C) would be indicated if the LDFA were abnormal, suggesting femoral-origin valgus (not the case here). Double level (D) is reserved for combined tibial and femoral deformity. Slope-reducing osteotomy (E) addresses tibial slope for ACL protection, not coronal alignment.
learningPoint: The site of osteotomy for varus knee OA is determined by the MPTA and LDFA — tibial-origin deformity (low MPTA) is corrected at the tibia with MOWHTO.

stem: A 51-year-old woman undergoes medial opening wedge high tibial osteotomy for varus medial compartment OA. The target mechanical axis is set to 62–65% of the tibial plateau width from the medial edge. Six weeks post-operatively she develops lateral knee pain. Radiographs show satisfactory osteotomy position with good healing and a healed fibular osteotomy. Which structure is most likely to have been injured during the procedure?

options.label: A
options.text: Common peroneal nerve

options.label: B
options.text: Anterior tibial artery

options.label: C
options.text: Popliteal artery

options.label: D
options.text: Iliotibial band

options.label: E
options.text: Lateral collateral ligament

explanation: Common peroneal nerve injury is the most significant complication of both medial opening wedge and lateral closing wedge HTO, occurring in 5–10% of cases. The nerve is at risk when performing fibular osteotomy (for MOWHTO) or during plate positioning at the proximal fibula. Lateral knee pain post-operatively combined with the fibular osteotomy description suggests CPN neuropraxia. Anterior tibial artery injury (B) is rare but can occur at the tibial metaphysis. Popliteal artery (C) is at risk during posterior cortex cuts but would present with ischaemia, not lateral knee pain. ITB (D) and LCL (E) injuries would produce mechanical instability rather than nerve-type lateral pain.
learningPoint: Common peroneal nerve injury is the most important neurological complication of HTO, occurring at the fibular osteotomy site in 5–10% of cases.

stem: A 68-year-old woman presents 14 months after primary TKR with 6 weeks of increasing pain, warmth, and a CRP of 84 mg/L and ESR of 76 mm/hr. She had an uneventful primary procedure. Knee aspirate grows Staphylococcus aureus sensitive to flucloxacillin. Radiographs show no loosening, and the components are well-fixed. She has an adequate soft tissue envelope. According to BASK/BASK-affiliated infection treatment guidelines, which is the most appropriate intervention?

options.label: A
options.text: Long-term oral antibiotics alone

options.label: B
options.text: Debridement, antibiotics, and implant retention (DAIR) with polyethylene exchange

options.label: C
options.text: Single-stage revision

options.label: D
options.text: Two-stage revision with articulating spacer

options.label: E
options.text: Arthroscopic washout and synovectomy

explanation: DAIR is appropriate for acute haematogenous or early chronic PJI (symptoms <3–4 weeks, though some extend to 6 weeks) with stable, well-fixed components and a sensitive organism. This patient has 6 weeks of symptoms (borderline but she has a sensitive organism and well-fixed components with a good tissue envelope), making DAIR with modular component exchange the most appropriate option. Long-term antibiotics alone (A) are suppressive therapy only, not curative. Single-stage revision (C) and two-stage revision (D) are reserved for chronic PJI, failed DAIR, or resistant organisms. Arthroscopic washout (E) is inadequate — open access for thorough synovectomy and PE exchange is required. Many BASK/BASK units support DAIR up to 6 weeks of symptoms with these favourable criteria.
learningPoint: DAIR with modular component exchange is appropriate for PJI of <6 weeks' symptom duration, well-fixed components, and a sensitive organism in a patient with an adequate soft tissue envelope.

stem: A 59-year-old man is referred 8 months after primary TKR with a fixed flexion deformity of 15 degrees and maximum active flexion of 75 degrees. Radiographs show well-positioned components with no loosening, normal patellar tracking, and no obvious causes of mechanical impingement. Inflammatory markers are normal. He has undergone 6 months of supervised physiotherapy. He is keen to improve his function. What is the most appropriate next management step?

options.label: A
options.text: Revision TKR

options.label: B
options.text: Arthroscopic adhesiolysis

options.label: C
options.text: Manipulation under anaesthesia (MUA)

options.label: D
options.text: Extension dynamic splinting at night only

options.label: E
options.text: Continued physiotherapy for a further 12 months

explanation: MUA is the first-line surgical intervention for stiffness following TKR when physiotherapy has failed. The accepted window for MUA is typically within the first 12 months post-operatively — results deteriorate significantly after this. At 8 months post-TKR with failure of physiotherapy, MUA is appropriate. Revision TKR (A) is reserved for MUA failure with an identifiable mechanical cause such as component malposition or significant flexion gap imbalance. Arthroscopic adhesiolysis (B) can supplement MUA but is not first-line. Dynamic splinting alone (D) would have been used earlier in the post-operative course. Further physiotherapy (E) without intervention has diminishing returns at this stage.
learningPoint: MUA is the first-line surgical intervention for TKR stiffness within the first 12 months; results deteriorate markedly after this window.

stem: A 73-year-old woman has recurrent giving way following TKR performed 2 years ago. She reports the knee feels unstable in mid-flexion (30–60 degrees). Radiographs show well-positioned components. She has no signs of infection. Examination reveals no instability in full extension or at 90 degrees of flexion. The tibial insert is a cruciate-retaining (CR) design. What is the most likely cause of her instability?

options.label: A
options.text: Medial collateral ligament insufficiency

options.label: B
options.text: Component aseptic loosening

options.label: C
options.text: Flexion gap greater than extension gap (flexion-extension gap mismatch)

options.label: D
options.text: Insufficiency of the retained posterior cruciate ligament

options.label: E
options.text: Patellar component maltracking

explanation: Mid-flexion instability in a CR TKR with stability at full extension and 90 degrees is the classic pattern of PCL insufficiency. In CR designs the PCL acts as the primary stabiliser in the 30–60 degree range; if it is attenuated or ruptured post-operatively, instability occurs specifically in this range. Treatment is typically revision to a posterior-stabilised (PS) design. MCL insufficiency (A) causes global instability (valgus thrust) throughout range. Aseptic loosening (B) would show radiographic changes and cause pain. Flexion-extension gap mismatch (C) produces instability in flexion but typically throughout flexion, not limited to mid-flexion. Patellar maltracking (E) causes anterior knee pain and catching, not giving way in mid-flexion.
learningPoint: Mid-flexion instability isolated to 30–60 degrees in a CR-TKR is pathognomonic of PCL insufficiency and warrants revision to a posterior-stabilised design.

stem: A 58-year-old man with a 15-year history of rheumatoid arthritis on long-term prednisolone (7.5 mg/day) trips on a kerb and is unable to weight-bear. Examination reveals inability to perform a straight leg raise, a palpable suprapatellar gap, and a high-riding patella on lateral radiograph (Insall-Salvati ratio 1.6). Ultrasound confirms complete quadriceps tendon rupture 2 cm proximal to the patella. What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Immobilisation in a knee brace in extension for 6 weeks

options.label: B
options.text: Urgent surgical repair of the quadriceps tendon

options.label: C
options.text: Augmented repair using synthetic ligament (LARS)

options.label: D
options.text: Primary repair with hamstring autograft augmentation

options.label: E
options.text: Patellectomy and quadricepsplasty

explanation: Complete quadriceps tendon rupture requires urgent surgical repair to restore extensor mechanism function. Delay beyond 2–6 weeks significantly worsens outcomes due to tendon retraction and quadriceps atrophy. While augmentation (C, D) may be considered in cases of poor tissue quality (long-term steroids predispose to this), primary repair is the first-line approach and should be undertaken urgently; augmentation is added intraoperatively if tissue quality is found to be poor. Conservative management (A) is entirely inappropriate for a complete quadriceps rupture — inability to straight leg raise confirms complete disruption. Patellectomy (E) is a salvage procedure for irreparable injuries.
learningPoint: Complete quadriceps tendon rupture requires urgent surgical repair; steroid use or poor tissue quality may prompt augmentation, but primary repair should not be delayed.

stem: A 34-year-old male marathon runner lands awkwardly from a jump. He develops immediate anterior knee pain and cannot extend his knee against gravity. The patella is low-riding on lateral radiograph (Insall-Salvati ratio 0.7). There is a palpable infrapatellar gap. MRI confirms complete patellar tendon rupture at its patellar insertion. What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Immobilisation for 8 weeks followed by physiotherapy

options.label: B
options.text: Urgent surgical reattachment of the patellar tendon to the patella

options.label: C
options.text: Augmented repair with synthetic mesh

options.label: D
options.text: Hamstring allograft reconstruction

options.label: E
options.text: Patellar tendon repair at 6 weeks when swelling has settled

explanation: A complete patellar tendon rupture in a young, active patient requires urgent surgical repair, ideally within 2 weeks of injury to prevent irreversible retraction. Direct bone-tendon reattachment with transosseous sutures or suture anchors at the inferior patellar pole is the standard technique. Conservative management (A) cannot restore extensor function with a complete tear. Synthetic augmentation (C) or allograft (D) are occasionally used when primary repair is not possible (delayed presentation, tissue deficiency) but are not primary treatment in a fresh acute rupture in a young patient. Delaying to 6 weeks (E) risks significant proximal migration and quadriceps atrophy.
learningPoint: Complete patellar tendon rupture requires urgent surgical repair within 2 weeks; delay leads to tendon retraction and dramatically worsens functional outcomes.

stem: A 22-year-old male elite rugby player has a symptomatic ICRS grade 3–4 cartilage defect measuring 3.2 cm² on the central weight-bearing surface of the medial femoral condyle following a sporting injury. The surrounding articular cartilage is normal, alignment is neutral, and the menisci are intact. He has failed 6 months of rehabilitation. He wishes to return to elite rugby. What is the most appropriate surgical intervention?

options.label: A
options.text: Microfracture alone

options.label: B
options.text: Autologous chondrocyte implantation (ACI) or matrix-induced ACI (MACI)

options.label: C
options.text: Osteochondral autograft transfer (OATS)

options.label: D
options.text: Medial unicompartmental knee replacement

options.label: E
options.text: Intra-articular injection of hyaluronic acid

explanation: For a contained cartilage defect >2 cm² in a young high-demand patient, MACI/ACI is the gold-standard biological repair technique offering the best evidence for durable hyaline-like cartilage regeneration and return to sport at elite level. Microfracture (A) is appropriate for smaller defects (<2 cm²) in lower-demand patients; beyond 2 cm² fibrocartilage fill is less durable and often fails at elite sport loads. OATS (C) suits smaller defects (1–2 cm²) where a donor plug can be harvested without significant donor morbidity; at 3.2 cm² graft harvest site complications increase substantially. UKR (D) is inappropriate in a 22-year-old. Hyaluronic acid (E) has no role in full-thickness cartilage defects.
learningPoint: Contained femoral condyle cartilage defects >2 cm² in young high-demand athletes are best treated with MACI/ACI to achieve durable hyaline-like cartilage regeneration.

stem: A 15-year-old schoolboy has a 3-month history of intermittent medial knee pain and occasional locking. MRI shows an osteochondritis dissecans (OCD) lesion on the medial femoral condyle, 18 mm in diameter, with a low-signal rim at the fragment–bed interface suggesting instability. Plain radiographs show open physes. He has no loose body. What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Conservative management — cessation of sport and reassessment at 3 months

options.label: B
options.text: Arthroscopic antegrade drilling of the lesion

options.label: C
options.text: Retrograde drilling (extra-articular) to preserve articular surface

options.label: D
options.text: Fragment fixation with headless compression screws

options.label: E
options.text: Fragment excision and microfracture of the crater

explanation: An unstable OCD lesion (low-signal rim on MRI, corresponding to instability) with an intact fragment that remains in situ should be fixed rather than excised, particularly in a skeletally immature patient where preserving the articular surface is paramount. Fragment fixation with headless Herbert-type or compression screws (and bone grafting if a gap exists) gives the best chance of healing of an unstable but in-situ fragment. Conservative management (A) is appropriate for stable lesions in skeletally immature patients. Antegrade drilling (B) is for stable lesions with intact cartilage where revascularisation is needed. Retrograde drilling (C) suits stable lesions where articular cartilage is intact and unbroken; the MRI rim sign suggests impending detachment, so drilling alone is insufficient. Fragment excision (E) sacrifices viable cartilage and is a last resort.
learningPoint: Unstable OCD lesions in skeletally immature patients with an intact, in-situ fragment should undergo fixation — not excision — to preserve articular cartilage.

stem: A 78-year-old woman with a well-functioning primary TKR falls at home 4 years post-operatively. Radiographs show a Rorabeck type II distal femoral periprosthetic fracture — the femoral component is stable and well-fixed, the fracture is displaced by 5 mm with 8 degrees of angulation, and there is no varus or valgus malalignment of the limb. She is ambulatory pre-injury and has no osteoporosis-related comorbidities. What is the most appropriate surgical management?

options.label: A
options.text: Non-operative management in a cast brace

options.label: B
options.text: Retrograde intramedullary nail

options.label: C
options.text: Locking plate fixation

options.label: D
options.text: Revision TKR with distal femoral replacement

options.label: E
options.text: Antegrade intramedullary femoral nail

explanation: Rorabeck type II fractures (displaced, stable implant) are the most common periprosthetic femoral fractures and require surgical fixation. Retrograde intramedullary nail (RIN) is the treatment of choice for Rorabeck type II when the femoral component box is open (or compatible with nail entry) — it allows load sharing, good alignment, early mobilisation, and lower non-union rates than plating in osteoporotic bone. Locking plate (C) is an alternative if the box is closed or fracture pattern prevents nail entry, but has higher non-union and implant failure rates in osteoporotic bone. Non-operative management (A) is not appropriate for displaced fractures as malunion and non-union rates are unacceptably high. Revision TKR with distal femoral replacement (D) is for fractures with an unstable or failed implant (Rorabeck type III). Antegrade nailing (E) bypasses the fracture without adequate fixation at the distal fragment.
learningPoint: Rorabeck type II periprosthetic distal femoral fractures (displaced, stable implant) are preferentially treated with retrograde intramedullary nail if implant geometry allows entry.

stem: A 71-year-old man sustains a low-energy fall 7 years after primary TKR. Radiographs show a displaced proximal tibial periprosthetic fracture with loosening of the tibial baseplate (Cain type II). He is independently ambulatory and medically fit for surgery. What is the definitive management?

options.label: A
options.text: Locking plate fixation with bone graft

options.label: B
options.text: Intramedullary tibial nail

options.label: C
options.text: Revision TKR with a stemmed tibial component +/- tumour prosthesis if bone stock is inadequate

options.label: D
options.text: Non-operative treatment in a long-leg cast

options.label: E
options.text: Temporary external fixation followed by staged locking plate

explanation: A periprosthetic tibial fracture with an associated loose tibial baseplate (Cain type II) cannot be treated by internal fixation alone because the loose implant will continue to cause pain and failure regardless of fracture healing. Revision TKR with a stemmed tibial component that bypasses the fracture is the definitive treatment, with augmented reconstruction or even distal femoral/proximal tibial tumour-prosthesis if bone stock is severely compromised. Locking plate (A) or IM nail (B) would not address the loose implant and would fail. Non-operative management (D) is unsuitable given implant loosening. Staged fixation (E) delays definitive care without benefit.
learningPoint: Periprosthetic tibial fractures with a loose tibial component (Cain type II) require revision TKR with a long-stem tibial implant — internal fixation without implant revision will fail.
