stem: A 67-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes and rheumatoid arthritis on methotrexate presents with a 2-day history of hot, swollen, painful right knee and a temperature of 38.6°C. Knee aspirate yields 65,000 white cells/mm³ (95% polymorphs). Gram stain is negative. CRP is 178 mg/L. Blood cultures are pending. She is haemodynamically stable. What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.label: A
options.text: IV antibiotics only, with repeat aspiration if no improvement at 48 hours

options.label: B
options.text: Urgent surgical washout of the knee joint plus empirical IV antibiotics

options.label: C
options.text: Arthroscopic washout plus empirical IV antibiotics, guided by local microbiological protocol

options.label: D
options.text: Serial needle aspiration three times daily plus oral flucloxacillin

options.label: E
options.text: Intra-articular corticosteroid injection to reduce inflammation while awaiting cultures

explanation: Native knee septic arthritis with a WCC >50,000/mm³ and clinical features of sepsis (fever, CRP 178) in an immunocompromised patient (RA on methotrexate, diabetes) requires urgent surgical washout to reduce bacterial load, prevent cartilage destruction, and achieve tissue samples for culture. Arthroscopic washout is the preferred modality (lower morbidity, equivalent outcomes to open washout in uncomplicated cases) combined with empirical IV antibiotics guided by local protocol (typically anti-staphylococcal cover). IV antibiotics alone (A) or serial aspiration (D) are inadequate when the joint WCC is this high and risk factors for virulent infection are present. Intra-articular steroid (E) is absolutely contraindicated in septic arthritis.
learningPoint: Native knee septic arthritis with WCC >50,000/mm³ in immunocompromised patients requires urgent arthroscopic washout plus empirical IV antibiotics — serial aspiration alone is inadequate.

stem: Theme: Anatomical Risk Factors in Patellofemoral Instability. For each scenario, select the single anatomical measurement or finding that is the PRIMARY driver of instability requiring specific surgical correction from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: TT-TG distance >20 mm

options.label: B
options.text: Patella alta (Caton-Deschamps index >1.3)

options.label: C
options.text: Trochlear dysplasia Dejour type B or D

options.label: D
options.text: Medial patellofemoral ligament insufficiency

options.label: E
options.text: Generalised ligamentous laxity (Beighton score ≥6)

options.label: F
options.text: Excessive femoral anteversion (>25 degrees on CT)

options.label: G
options.text: Lateral retinacular tightness causing fixed lateral tilt

emiLeads.stem: A 20-year-old woman with recurrent patellar instability has a TT-TG of 24 mm. MRI shows a shallow trochlear groove but no supratrochlear spur. Caton-Deschamps index is 1.1. MPFL is disrupted. She is otherwise anatomically normal on CT rotation studies.

A 16-year-old male has a first-time patellar dislocation. On examination and imaging there is a crossing sign and supratrochlear spur on lateral knee radiograph. TT-TG is 13 mm and Caton-Deschamps index is 1.2. He has already had an MPFL reconstruction which failed at 18 months.

A 19-year-old dancer has recurrent bilateral patellar subluxation. Bilateral TT-TG distances are 15 mm. Both knees show normal trochlear morphology. Caton-Deschamps indices are both 1.65. Rotation CT is normal. She has hypermobile joints (Beighton 8/9).

A 24-year-old woman with recurrent left patellar dislocations has a TT-TG of 16 mm, Caton-Deschamps index of 1.15, and a Dejour type A trochlea. CT rotation study reveals femoral anteversion of 32 degrees and external tibial torsion of 18 degrees. All three previous MPFL reconstructions have failed.

explanation: Scenario 1: TT-TG 24 mm is clearly abnormal (>20 mm threshold) and is the primary bony driver in the absence of significant dysplasia or alta — tibial tubercle medialisation is indicated alongside MPFL reconstruction. Scenario 2: Dejour B/D dysplasia with a supratrochlear spur is the primary structural abnormality that caused MPFL reconstruction failure — trochleoplasty is required. Scenario 3: Both TT-TG and trochlea are normal; the elevated Caton-Deschamps index (1.65) is the dominant risk factor — distalisation of the tubercle is the specific correction needed. Scenario 4: Despite normal TT-TG and trochlea, three MPFL failures point to an unaddressed rotational abnormality; femoral anteversion of 32 degrees (normal <20 degrees) is the culprit — derotational femoral osteotomy is required.

stem: Theme: Classification and Management of Periprosthetic Joint Infection after TKR. For each clinical scenario, select the most appropriate management strategy from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: DAIR (debridement, antibiotics, implant retention) with modular component exchange

options.label: B
options.text: Single-stage revision TKR

options.label: C
options.text: Two-stage revision TKR with antibiotic-loaded articulating cement spacer

options.label: D
options.text: Long-term oral antibiotic suppression without surgery

options.label: E
options.text: Knee arthrodesis

options.label: F
options.text: Above-knee amputation

options.label: G
options.text: Irrigation, debridement and delayed primary closure with VAC dressing

emiLeads.stem: A 65-year-old man has had a TKR for 3 years. He develops fever and knee swelling 12 days after a dental extraction. Aspirate grows Streptococcus viridans sensitive to penicillin. CRP is 96 mg/L. Components are well-fixed. Soft tissues are in good condition.

A 70-year-old woman has a 9-month history of progressive knee pain and warmth after a TKR performed 4 years ago. ESR is 88 mm/hr, CRP 72 mg/L. Aspirate grows Cutibacterium acnes. Radiographs show early radiolucent lines around the tibial component. She is medically fit.

A 78-year-old frail man with severe COPD, BMI 41, and type 2 insulin-dependent diabetes has a chronic PJI with a polymicrobial infection including MRSA. He has undergone one failed DAIR and one failed single-stage revision. His knee is painful but he declines further reconstruction.

A 62-year-old woman has a single-stage revision planned for chronic low-grade PJI (methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus, CRP 28, stable components, good bone stock, well-controlled rheumatoid arthritis on hydroxychloroquine only). She is an ideal candidate for single-stage revision.

explanation: Scenario 1: Acute haematogenous PJI (symptom onset 12 days, sensitive Streptococcus, well-fixed components) — DAIR is appropriate. Scenario 2: Chronic PJI (9-month history, Cutibacterium acnes — a slow-growing low-virulence organism — with early loosening) — two-stage revision is the standard of care; the radiolucent lines suggest loosening requiring implant removal. Scenario 3: Multiple failed revisions, high-risk patient who declines further surgery — long-term antibiotic suppression (palliative/suppressive) is the appropriate management in this carefully selected scenario. Scenario 4: Ideal single-stage revision candidate — well-controlled RA, a single sensitive organism, preserved bone stock — single-stage revision is appropriate and avoids the morbidity of a two-stage approach.

stem: Theme: Classification of Knee Dislocation and Ligamentous Injury. For each scenario, select the most appropriate Schenck KD classification from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: KD-I (single cruciate + collateral)

options.label: B
options.text: KD-II (both cruciates, collaterals intact)

options.label: C
options.text: KD-III-M (both cruciates + MCL)

options.label: D
options.text: KD-III-L (both cruciates + PLC)

options.label: E
options.text: KD-IV (both cruciates + both collaterals)

options.label: F
options.text: KD-V (periarticular fracture-dislocation)

emiLeads.stem: A 38-year-old man sustains a knee injury in a high-speed motorcycle collision. MRI demonstrates complete ACL and PCL tears. The medial and lateral collateral complexes are intact on stress radiographs. There is no fracture.

A 25-year-old woman has a contact football injury resulting in complete ACL, PCL, and LCL tears, with disruption of the popliteus tendon and popliteofibular ligament. The MCL is intact. Radiographs are normal.

A 52-year-old male is involved in a road traffic accident. MRI confirms complete ACL, PCL, and MCL tears. The lateral collateral ligament and posterolateral corner structures are intact. There is no fracture.
A 44-year-old man sustains a sports injury with complete ACL, PCL, MCL, and LCL disruption. He has a tibial plateau fracture in addition to the ligamentous injuries.

explanation: KD-II involves both cruciates with intact collaterals — purely bi-cruciate injury (scenario 1). KD-III-L involves both cruciates plus the lateral/posterolateral complex (scenario 2 — PLC disruption). KD-III-M involves both cruciates plus medial structures (scenario 3). KD-V denotes any knee dislocation accompanied by periarticular fracture (scenario 4 — tibial plateau fracture upgrades the classification regardless of ligament pattern).

stem: Theme: Surgical Treatment of Articular Cartilage Defects in the Knee. For each patient scenario, select the most appropriate primary surgical treatment from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Microfracture alone

options.label: B
options.text: Osteochondral autograft transfer (OATS / mosaicplasty)

options.label: C
options.text: Matrix-induced autologous chondrocyte implantation (MACI)

options.label: D
options.text: Osteochondral allograft transplantation

options.label: E
options.text: Fragment fixation with headless compression screws and bone grafting

options.label: F
options.text: Total knee replacement

options.label: G
options.text: Medial unicompartmental knee replacement

emiLeads.stem: A 31-year-old female sprinter has a 1.5 cm² ICRS grade 4 focal cartilage defect on the medial femoral condyle, discovered incidentally at ACL reconstruction. Surrounding cartilage is normal, alignment is neutral. She wants to return to competitive sprinting.

A 26-year-old male professional basketball player has a 4.0 cm² ICRS grade 4 medial femoral condyle cartilage defect from a previous OCD fragment that was excised 3 years ago. Bone stock beneath the defect is 10 mm below the articular surface. Alignment is neutral. He is keen to return to professional sport.

A 19-year-old male has a 2.8 cm² grade 3–4 medial femoral condyle cartilage defect. Surrounding cartilage is healthy, alignment is neutral, and menisci are intact. He has failed 12 months of physiotherapy and two steroid injections. His goal is to play university rugby.

A 55-year-old woman with medial compartment arthrosis (KL grade 3, bone-on-bone contact on standing AP film), intact ACL, neutral alignment, and no patellofemoral involvement has failed conservative management. She has no full-thickness cartilage remaining on the medial tibial plateau.

explanation: Scenario 1: 1.5 cm² defect in a high-demand athlete — OATS is ideal as a single-stage procedure for smaller focal defects, giving hyaline cartilage repair with good long-term outcome in this size range. Scenario 2: 4 cm² defect with subchondral bone loss after OCD excision — requires restoration of bone stock and a large cartilage surface, which is beyond OATS donor supply; osteochondral allograft provides the bone and cartilage scaffold in a single stage. Scenario 3: 2.8 cm² defect — above the size threshold for reliable OATS and in a young high-demand patient requiring durable hyaline-like regeneration; MACI is the appropriate choice. Scenario 4: This patient has medial compartment OA (not a focal cartilage defect) with criteria met for UKR — UKR is the correct treatment category.

stem: A 22-year-old rugby prop sustains an anterior glenohumeral dislocation during a match. On plain radiographs taken after closed reduction, a notch is visible on the posterolateral aspect of the humeral head. What is the eponymous name for this lesion and its approximate prevalence after a first-time anterior dislocation?

options.label: A
options.text: Bankart lesion; 50% of cases

options.label: B
options.text: Hill-Sachs lesion; 35–40% of cases

options.label: C
options.text: Hill-Sachs lesion; 65–80% of cases

options.label: D
options.text: GLAD lesion; 20% of cases

options.label: E
options.text: Reverse Hill-Sachs lesion; 65–80% of cases

explanation: The posterolateral humeral head compression fracture produced by impaction against the anteroinferior glenoid rim during anterior dislocation is the Hill-Sachs lesion, present in 65–80% of first-time anterior dislocations and approaching 90% in recurrent instability. A Bankart lesion is the anteroinferior labral detachment, not a bony notch. A GLAD lesion is a glenolabral articular disruption. A reverse Hill-Sachs (McLaughlin lesion) is an anteromedial head defect seen with posterior dislocation.
learningPoint: A posterolateral humeral head notch after anterior dislocation is a Hill-Sachs lesion, present in 65–80% of first-time anterior shoulder dislocations.

stem: A 28-year-old recreational climber presents with his third anterior shoulder dislocation in 18 months. MR arthrogram confirms a classic Bankart lesion with no significant glenoid bone loss (bone loss <15% on CT). He is keen to return to climbing. Which management option offers the lowest recurrence rate?

options.label: A
options.text: Structured physiotherapy programme alone

options.label: B
options.text: Arthroscopic Bankart repair

options.label: C
options.text: Open Latarjet coracoid transfer

options.label: D
options.text: Inferior capsular shift (open)

options.label: E
options.text: Thermal capsulorrhaphy

explanation: For recurrent anterior instability with a soft-tissue Bankart lesion and minimal glenoid bone loss (<15%), arthroscopic Bankart repair is the evidence-based first choice with recurrence rates of 5–10% in well-selected patients. Latarjet is preferred when glenoid bone loss exceeds 20–25% or after failed Bankart repair. Inferior capsular shift is open and primarily used for multidirectional instability. Thermal capsulorrhaphy has unacceptably high failure and complication rates and is no longer recommended. Physiotherapy alone has high recurrence in young, physically active patients with structural lesions.
learningPoint: Arthroscopic Bankart repair is the treatment of choice for recurrent anterior instability with a labral lesion and glenoid bone loss <15%.

stem: CT quantification of a recurrently unstable shoulder in a 31-year-old reveals 28% loss of the inferior glenoid diameter. MR arthrogram also demonstrates a large engaging Hill-Sachs defect. Which surgical approach best addresses this combined bony deficit?

options.label: A
options.text: Arthroscopic Bankart repair with remplissage

options.label: B
options.text: Open Latarjet coracoid transfer

options.label: C
options.text: Distal tibial allograft glenoid reconstruction

options.label: D
options.text: Arthroscopic Bankart repair alone

options.label: E
options.text: Total shoulder arthroplasty

explanation: Glenoid bone loss exceeding 20–25% is the threshold at which soft-tissue Bankart repair alone yields unacceptably high recurrence rates. The Latarjet procedure transfers the coracoid process to reconstruct the glenoid arc and additionally provides a sling effect from the conjoint tendon. Remplissage fills the Hill-Sachs defect but does not address significant glenoid bone loss. Distal tibial allograft reconstruction is an alternative to Latarjet but less widely performed in the UK and typically reserved for failed Latarjet. Bankart repair alone is insufficient here. Arthroplasty is not indicated for instability in a young patient.
learningPoint: Glenoid bone loss >25% requires a bony reconstruction procedure; the Latarjet coracoid transfer is the UK standard of care.

stem: A 64-year-old retired schoolteacher presents with a 6-month history of progressive right shoulder pain and weakness. She is unable to initiate abduction beyond 20° without shrugging. MRI shows a complete tear of supraspinatus with 2 cm retraction and grade 2 fatty infiltration of the muscle (Goutallier classification). Which of the following findings most strongly predicts an inferior outcome from rotator cuff repair?

options.label: A
options.text: Patient age of 64 years

options.label: B
options.text: Tear size of 2 cm retraction

options.label: C
options.text: Duration of symptoms of 6 months

options.label: D
options.text: Goutallier grade 2 fatty infiltration

options.label: E
options.text: Goutallier grade 3 or 4 fatty infiltration

explanation: Goutallier grade 3 (>50% fat) or grade 4 (complete fatty replacement) are established predictors of poor healing and inferior functional outcomes after rotator cuff repair, as the muscle lacks regenerative capacity. Grade 2 (up to 50% fat) carries an intermediate prognosis but repair can still be worthwhile. Age alone is not a contraindication. Retraction and symptom duration influence surgical difficulty but fatty infiltration grade is the strongest structural predictor of outcome.
learningPoint: Goutallier grade 3–4 fatty infiltration of the rotator cuff muscle is the strongest predictor of poor healing and outcome after cuff repair.

stem: A 58-year-old scaffolder attends with an acute-on-chronic posterior shoulder pain after a heavy lift. Examination shows weakness of external rotation with the arm at the side but preserved internal rotation strength. MRI reveals a full-thickness tear of the infraspinatus. Which clinical test is most sensitive for detecting infraspinatus weakness?

options.label: A
options.text: Empty-can (Jobe) test

options.label: B
options.text: Lift-off (Gerber) test

options.label: C
options.text: External rotation lag sign

options.label: D
options.text: Hornblower sign

options.label: E
options.text: Bear-hug test

explanation: The external rotation lag sign tests infraspinatus and teres minor function: the examiner passively places the arm at 0° abduction and 45° external rotation then releases; inability to maintain the position indicates posterior cuff insufficiency, with high sensitivity and specificity for infraspinatus tears. The empty-can test targets supraspinatus. The lift-off test and bear-hug test assess subscapularis. The hornblower sign (dropping into internal rotation at 90° abduction) indicates a combined infraspinatus/teres minor tear, particularly relevant in massive cuff tears.
learningPoint: The external rotation lag sign is the most specific clinical test for infraspinatus tears at the FRCS level.

stem: A 72-year-old woman with a massive irreparable rotator cuff tear and cuff tear arthropathy (Hamada grade 3) presents with a pseudoparalytic shoulder. She is unable to actively elevate beyond 40°. Pre-operative CT confirms concavity compression loss with superior migration. What is the most appropriate surgical management?

options.label: A
options.text: Anatomic total shoulder arthroplasty

options.label: B
options.text: Hemiarthroplasty

options.label: C
options.text: Reverse total shoulder arthroplasty

options.label: D
options.text: Arthroscopic debridement and biceps tenotomy

options.label: E
options.text: Superior capsular reconstruction

explanation: Reverse total shoulder arthroplasty (rTSA) is the gold-standard intervention for cuff tear arthropathy with pseudoparalysis in elderly patients. By medialising and distalising the centre of rotation it recruits deltoid as the primary elevator, restoring active elevation without a functioning rotator cuff. Anatomic TSA requires an intact cuff for a stable concentric joint and performs poorly in cuff deficiency. Hemiarthroplasty gives pain relief but does not restore elevation reliably. Arthroscopic debridement is a temporising measure with limited functional gain. Superior capsular reconstruction is more suited to younger patients with irreparable tears without arthropathy.
learningPoint: Reverse total shoulder arthroplasty is the definitive treatment for cuff tear arthropathy with pseudoparalysis in older patients.

stem: A 48-year-old painter presents with a 4-month history of right shoulder pain worse with overhead work. Neer and Hawkins-Kennedy tests are positive. Plain radiographs show a Bigliani type III (hooked) acromion. After 3 months of physiotherapy and two subacromial corticosteroid injections he remains significantly symptomatic. MRI shows subacromial bursitis with no cuff tear. What is the next most appropriate step?

options.label: A
options.text: Repeat subacromial corticosteroid injection

options.label: B
options.text: Arthroscopic subacromial decompression

options.label: C
options.text: Open acromioplasty

options.label: D
options.text: Platelet-rich plasma injection

options.label: E
options.text: Ultrasound-guided hydrodilatation

explanation: Arthroscopic subacromial decompression (ASD) is appropriate after failure of at least 3–6 months of structured physiotherapy and injections in patients with structural impingement (hooked acromion, confirmed bursitis, no cuff tear). Although the CSAW trial questioned its superiority over blinded sham surgery in unselected patients, patients with a hooked acromion and persistent structural bursitis remain a cohort in whom ASD is widely supported. A third injection is unlikely to provide sustained benefit. Open acromioplasty has been largely supplanted by arthroscopic techniques. PRP and hydrodilatation are not evidence-based for isolated impingement.
learningPoint: Arthroscopic subacromial decompression is indicated after failed conservative treatment (≥3 months physio + injections) in patients with structural subacromial impingement.

stem: Which acromial morphology is most strongly associated with rotator cuff tears in patients undergoing shoulder MRI?

options.label: A
options.text: Bigliani type I (flat)

options.label: B
options.text: Bigliani type II (curved)

options.label: C
options.text: Bigliani type III (hooked)

options.label: D
options.text: Os acromiale

options.label: E
options.text: Low-lying lateral acromion (critical shoulder angle >35°)

explanation: Bigliani type III (hooked) acromion has the highest association with full-thickness rotator cuff tears, with studies reporting it in up to 70% of patients with supraspinatus tears compared to 35% with type II and <5% with type I. Os acromiale can contribute to impingement but is not the morphology most strongly linked to cuff tears. The critical shoulder angle is a separate metric reflecting combined glenoid inclination and acromion width, also associated with cuff disease, but the Bigliani type III is the classic FRCS teaching point.
learningPoint: Bigliani type III (hooked) acromion has the strongest association with full-thickness rotator cuff tears.

stem: A 54-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes presents with a 7-month history of right shoulder pain and global stiffness. Examination reveals loss of passive external rotation of 35° compared with the contralateral side with a firm end-feel. Plain radiographs are normal. Which pathological finding is characteristic of primary adhesive capsulitis on arthroscopy?

options.label: A
options.text: Subacromial bursal thickening alone

options.label: B
options.text: Synovial hyperplasia and fibrous thickening of the rotator interval and anterior capsule

options.label: C
options.text: Anterior labral tear with capsular laxity

options.label: D
options.text: Articular-sided partial rotator cuff tear

options.label: E
options.text: Loose bodies in the glenohumeral joint

explanation: The hallmark of primary adhesive capsulitis is synovial hyperplasia and fibrous thickening predominantly of the rotator interval (including the coracohumeral and superior glenohumeral ligaments) and the anterior capsule, with reduction of joint volume to as little as 5–10 mL. Subacromial bursal thickening is a feature of impingement, not adhesive capsulitis. Anterior labral tear suggests instability. Articular-side cuff tears are a separate pathology. Loose bodies are associated with osteoarthritis or osteochondral injury.
learningPoint: Adhesive capsulitis is characterised by synovial hyperplasia and fibrous contracture of the rotator interval and anterior capsule, reducing glenohumeral joint volume.

stem: A 49-year-old woman is diagnosed with primary frozen shoulder at 4 months' duration. She is in the painful inflammatory phase. She is keen for prompt symptom relief. Which of the following has the highest level of evidence for short-term pain reduction and range of motion improvement at 6 weeks in this phase?

options.label: A
options.text: Physiotherapy alone

options.label: B
options.text: Oral NSAIDs

options.label: C
options.text: Intra-articular corticosteroid injection

options.label: D
options.text: Ultrasound-guided hydrodilatation (distension arthrography)

options.label: E
options.text: Arthroscopic capsular release

explanation: Intra-articular corticosteroid injection provides the highest level of evidence for short-term (6-week) improvement in pain and range of motion in the inflammatory (freezing) phase of adhesive capsulitis, supported by multiple randomised controlled trials. Physiotherapy is beneficial but gains are slower. NSAIDs provide analgesia but without the same functional benefit. Hydrodilatation has evidence for improved ROM but mostly studied in the frozen phase; its advantage over injection alone remains debated. Arthroscopic capsular release is reserved for refractory cases (typically >12 months without improvement).
learningPoint: Intra-articular corticosteroid injection has the strongest short-term evidence for pain relief and ROM improvement in the inflammatory phase of frozen shoulder.

stem: A 26-year-old cyclist falls onto the point of his left shoulder. Examination shows a step deformity at the ACJ; the clavicle reduces with gentle downward pressure. AP and Zanca views reveal 150% superior displacement of the clavicle relative to the acromion. The coracoclavicular (CC) distance is doubled compared with the contralateral side. According to the Rockwood classification, what grade is this injury and what is the displacement pattern?

options.label: A
options.text: Rockwood grade III — superior displacement with intact CC ligaments

options.label: B
options.text: Rockwood grade IV — posterior buttonholing of the clavicle through trapezius

options.label: C
options.text: Rockwood grade V — superior displacement 100–300% of CC distance

options.label: D
options.text: Rockwood grade VI — inferior subcoracoid dislocation

options.label: E
options.text: Rockwood grade II — ACJ capsule disrupted, CC ligaments intact

explanation: Rockwood grade V is characterised by gross superior displacement of the clavicle with the CC distance increased by 100–300% (typically 2–3× normal), with disruption of both ACJ and CC ligaments plus stripping of the deltotrapezial fascia. Grade III has 25–100% CC distance increase with all ligaments disrupted but fascial attachments intact. Grade IV shows posterior displacement (clavicle buttonholes through trapezius). Grade VI is the rare inferior subcoracoid dislocation. Grade II spares the CC ligaments.
learningPoint: Rockwood grade V ACJ injury = superior clavicle displacement 100–300% of CC distance with deltotrapezial fascial disruption.

stem: A 34-year-old rugby flanker has a Rockwood grade III ACJ injury sustained 6 weeks ago. He has persistent pain and a prominent step at the ACJ but no neurological deficit. Conservative management has failed to settle his symptoms. He is considering surgery. Which of the following represents the strongest evidence-based indication to proceed with surgical stabilisation of a chronic grade III ACJ injury?

options.label: A
options.text: Visible cosmetic step deformity alone

options.label: B
options.text: Manual labour occupation with persistent functional deficit after adequate conservative treatment

options.label: C
options.text: Athlete wanting to continue contact sport

options.label: D
options.text: CC distance doubled on plain radiographs

options.label: E
options.text: Age under 40 years

explanation: The evidence base for surgery in Rockwood grade III ACJ injuries is divided. Most guidelines (BESS, ISAKOS) support initial conservative management with surgery reserved for patients who fail at least 3 months of conservative treatment AND have a genuine functional deficit, particularly those in manual or overhead labour. Cosmetic deformity alone is not a robust surgical indication. Being an athlete or being young are not independent indications without functional failure of conservative treatment. The CC distance is used for classification not as an isolated surgical threshold.
learningPoint: Surgery for grade III ACJ injury is reserved for patients with persistent functional deficit after ≥3 months of adequate conservative treatment, particularly in manual workers.

stem: A 45-year-old woman presents with sudden onset of severe right shoulder pain at rest, worse at night, after several months of milder intermittent pain. Radiographs show a fluffy, ill-defined calcium deposit within the supraspinatus tendon. According to the Gärtner radiographic classification, this appearance corresponds to which type?

options.label: A
options.text: Gärtner type I — dense, homogeneous, well-defined

options.label: B
options.text: Gärtner type II — dense but heterogeneous with well-defined margins

options.label: C
options.text: Gärtner type III — translucent/fluffy, ill-defined

options.label: D
options.text: Gärtner type IV — calcification with cortical erosion

options.label: E
options.text: Ogilvie-Harris type I — dense calcium

explanation: Gärtner type III calcific deposits are translucent or fluffy with ill-defined margins and correspond to the resorptive (toothpaste-like) phase of calcific tendinitis, which typically presents with severe acute pain as the deposit liquefies. Type I deposits are chalk-like, dense, and homogeneous (formative phase), often less painful. Type II is intermediate. The acute resorptive phase (type III appearance) is paradoxically the phase most amenable to ultrasound-guided needling/lavage. The Gärtner classification has no type IV; the Ogilvie-Harris classification uses different descriptors.
learningPoint: Gärtner type III (fluffy, ill-defined) deposits represent the resorptive phase of calcific tendinitis and correlate with severe acute pain.

stem: A 52-year-old woman with radiographically confirmed calcific tendinitis of the supraspinatus fails two subacromial steroid injections over 4 months. Symptoms persist with significant impact on quality of life. What is the most appropriate next intervention?

options.label: A
options.text: Oral corticosteroid course for 2 weeks

options.label: B
options.text: Extracorporeal shock wave therapy (ESWT)

options.label: C
options.text: Arthroscopic removal of the calcium deposit

options.label: D
options.text: Ultrasound-guided barbotage (needling and lavage)

options.label: E
options.text: Repeat subacromial injection with hyaluronidase

explanation: Ultrasound-guided barbotage (percutaneous needling and lavage) is supported by RCT evidence and meta-analyses as the preferred minimally invasive intervention after failed steroid injections in calcific tendinitis, with success rates of 60–80% at 6–12 months. ESWT is an alternative but UK availability is limited and evidence is somewhat less robust than barbotage. Arthroscopic removal is reserved for refractory cases after failed barbotage or ESWT. Oral corticosteroids are not part of standard UK management for this indication. Hyaluronidase combination injections are not standard practice.
learningPoint: Ultrasound-guided barbotage (needling and lavage) is the preferred second-line intervention for calcific tendinitis after failed steroid injections.

stem: A 78-year-old woman falls from standing height sustaining a proximal humerus fracture. Plain radiographs show displacement of both the greater and lesser tuberosities from the humeral head along with a fracture at the surgical neck. The articular segment is intact with no dislocation. According to the Neer classification, what is this fracture?

options.label: A
options.text: Two-part surgical neck fracture

options.label: B
options.text: Three-part fracture

options.label: C
options.text: Four-part fracture

options.label: D
options.text: Head-splitting fracture

options.label: E
options.text: Two-part greater tuberosity fracture

explanation: In the Neer classification, a 'part' is a segment displaced >1 cm or angulated >45°. The four anatomical segments are: the articular head, greater tuberosity, lesser tuberosity, and shaft (surgical neck). When all four segments are displaced (head, GT, LT, shaft), it constitutes a four-part fracture. A three-part fracture has three displaced segments (head + shaft + either GT or LT). A head-splitting fracture involves articular surface comminution. The scenario describes separate displacement of both tuberosities plus a surgical neck fracture with an intact but displaced articular segment — four parts.
learningPoint: A Neer four-part proximal humerus fracture involves displacement of all four segments: humeral head, greater tuberosity, lesser tuberosity, and shaft.

stem: A 76-year-old right-handed woman with good pre-injury function sustains a valgus-impacted four-part proximal humerus fracture with 40° valgus tilt. She is otherwise medically fit. What is the primary determinant of avascular necrosis risk in deciding whether to attempt fixation versus replacement?

options.label: A
options.text: Patient age

options.label: B
options.text: Degree of medial calcar comminution and medial hinge integrity

options.label: C
options.text: Number of Neer parts

options.label: D
options.text: Fracture line location relative to the bicipital groove

options.label: E
options.text: Bone mineral density (DEXA T-score)

explanation: In valgus-impacted four-part fractures the medial periosteal hinge (posterior medial calcar) is the critical determinant of residual humeral head vascularity. If the medial hinge is intact (>8 mm of medial calcar preserved) the risk of AVN falls to approximately 20–30%, making fixation a viable option. When the medial hinge is disrupted with significant comminution, AVN risk approaches 75% and hemi- or reverse arthroplasty is preferred. Age and bone density inform implant choice but are not the primary AVN predictor. Number of parts alone does not account for the valgus-impacted subtype's favourable vascular anatomy.
learningPoint: Integrity of the posterior medial calcar (medial periosteal hinge) is the key determinant of AVN risk and surgical strategy in four-part valgus-impacted proximal humerus fractures.

stem: A 68-year-old man with primary glenohumeral osteoarthritis and an intact rotator cuff is being considered for shoulder arthroplasty. Pre-operative CT shows a Walch type B2 (biconcave, retroverted) glenoid. Which factor related to this glenoid type most influences the choice between total shoulder arthroplasty (TSA) and hemiarthroplasty?

options.label: A
options.text: Patient age

options.label: B
options.text: Superior glenoid inclination

options.label: C
options.text: Glenoid retroversion and posterior bone loss

options.label: D
options.text: Size of the humeral head osteophyte

options.label: E
options.text: Presence of subchondral cysts in the glenoid

explanation: The Walch B2 glenoid has significant retroversion (typically >15°) with posterior bone erosion and posterior humeral head subluxation. Restoring version during TSA component placement is challenging — severe retroversion may necessitate augmented glenoid components or staged bone grafting. Uncorrected retroversion leads to asymmetric loading and early glenoid component loosening ('rocking horse' phenomenon). This anatomical challenge is the primary driver of implant selection and technique planning. Hemiarthroplasty avoids glenoid complications but produces inferior pain relief long-term. Age, cysts, and osteophytes are secondary concerns.
learningPoint: Walch B2 glenoid retroversion and posterior bone loss is the critical factor influencing glenoid component placement strategy in anatomic TSA.

stem: A 74-year-old woman undergoes reverse total shoulder arthroplasty. At 6-month review she complains of new onset inferior shoulder pain and crepitus, and radiographs show inferior scapular notching at the inferior scapular neck. This complication is most directly attributable to which design or technical factor?

options.label: A
options.text: Excessive humeral component retroversion

options.label: B
options.text: Superior glenosphere positioning on the glenoid

options.label: C
options.text: Inferior glenosphere positioning with infero-lateral glenoid overhang

options.label: D
options.text: Use of a large (>38 mm) glenosphere diameter

options.label: E
options.text: Insufficient humeral neck-shaft angle

explanation: Scapular notching (Sirveaux/Nerot classification) occurs when the medialised polyethylene cup impinges against the inferior scapular pillar during adduction. Positioning the glenosphere superiorly on the glenoid increases this impingement. Inferiorising the glenosphere ('inferior tilt' technique or placing it flush with or distal to the inferior glenoid rim) with inferior overhang is the accepted surgical technique to reduce notching. Larger glenospheres also reduce notching by increasing offset. Humeral retroversion and neck-shaft angle affect range of motion and dislocation risk, not primarily notching.
learningPoint: Scapular notching in reverse TSA is reduced by positioning the glenosphere inferiorly with inferior overhang at the glenoid rim rather than a superior position.

stem: A 30-year-old overhead-throwing athlete presents with deep shoulder pain, reduced throwing velocity, and a painful click on the O'Brien (active compression) test. MR arthrogram reveals a superior labral tear extending from anterior to posterior with detachment of the biceps anchor. According to the Snyder classification this is a SLAP type II lesion. What is the recommended surgical treatment?

options.label: A
options.text: SLAP repair with suture anchors

options.label: B
options.text: Biceps tenodesis

options.label: C
options.text: Biceps tenotomy

options.label: D
options.text: Open labral reconstruction

options.label: E
options.text: Conservative physiotherapy for 12 months then reassess

explanation: In a young overhead-throwing athlete a Snyder type II SLAP lesion (superior labral detachment with biceps anchor involvement) is best treated with arthroscopic SLAP repair using suture anchors, which restores the superior labral bumper and biceps anchor. Biceps tenodesis or tenotomy effectively address biceps-related pain but sacrifice the superior labral bumper and are generally preferred in older patients (>40 years) or in those who have failed prior SLAP repair. Open reconstruction is not indicated. The athlete's age, sport demands, and desire to return to throwing make repair the correct first choice.
learningPoint: Arthroscopic SLAP type II repair with suture anchors is the treatment of choice in young overhead-throwing athletes.

stem: A 46-year-old amateur tennis player has persistent shoulder pain despite 6 months of physiotherapy. Arthroscopy confirms a Snyder type II SLAP lesion and an associated partial-thickness articular-sided rotator cuff tear (PASTA lesion, <50% thickness). He is not a competitive overhead thrower. What combined treatment is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: SLAP repair + partial cuff repair

options.label: B
options.text: Biceps tenodesis + cuff debridement

options.label: C
options.text: Biceps tenodesis + convert PASTA to full-thickness and repair

options.label: D
options.text: SLAP repair + cuff debridement only

options.label: E
options.text: Debridement of both lesions without repair

explanation: In a non-competitive overhead athlete aged >40 years with a SLAP II and a PASTA lesion (<50%), biceps tenodesis plus cuff debridement is the pragmatic and evidence-supported approach. Tenodesis reliably addresses the biceps anchor pain without the risk of SLAP repair stiffness, which is a recognised complication particularly in recreational players. PASTA lesions <50% thickness do not require conversion to full-thickness for repair; debridement is appropriate. Full conversion and repair is reserved for lesions >50% thickness. SLAP repair in this age group has higher re-operation rates.
learningPoint: In recreational athletes over 40 with SLAP II, biceps tenodesis is preferred over SLAP repair to avoid stiffness; PASTA lesions <50% are managed with debridement.

stem: A 32-year-old cyclist sustains an Edinburgh type 2B1 mid-shaft clavicle fracture with 2.5 cm shortening, marked comminution, and complete displacement. He is otherwise healthy. The Edinburgh classification type 2B indicates which of the following features?

options.label: A
options.text: Displaced distal clavicle fracture with intact coracoclavicular ligaments

options.label: B
options.text: Displaced mid-shaft fracture with comminution (2B1) or a single butterfly fragment (2B2)

options.label: C
options.text: Undisplaced mid-shaft clavicle fracture

options.label: D
options.text: Proximal clavicle fracture with posterior displacement

options.label: E
options.text: Distal clavicle fracture with coracoclavicular ligament disruption

explanation: The Edinburgh classification of mid-shaft clavicle fractures divides them into: type 1 undisplaced; type 2A displaced without comminution; type 2B displaced with comminution (2B1) or a butterfly fragment (2B2). Type 2B fractures have the highest non-union rate (up to 15%) and the strongest evidence base for operative fixation. Distal fractures are classified separately in groups 3 (intact CC) and 4 (disrupted CC). Proximal fractures are group 1 in the Edinburgh system.
learningPoint: Edinburgh type 2B clavicle fractures are displaced mid-shaft fractures with comminution (2B1) or butterfly fragment (2B2), carrying the highest non-union risk.

stem: A healthy 25-year-old man sustains a displaced comminuted mid-shaft clavicle fracture (Edinburgh 2B1) with 18 mm shortening. A colleague proposes primary plate fixation. Which RCT-level evidence supports operative over non-operative management for displaced mid-shaft clavicle fractures?

options.label: A
options.text: The CACTUS trial showing earlier return to work with plate fixation

options.label: B
options.text: The Canadian Orthopaedic Trauma Society (COTS) RCT showing improved DASH scores and reduced non-union at 1 year with plate fixation

options.label: C
options.text: The SPORT trial favouring non-operative management in all groups

options.label: D
options.text: The INFIX trial showing comparable outcomes between plate and intramedullary nail

options.label: E
options.text: NICE guidance recommending surgery for all displaced fractures

explanation: The COTS RCT (2007) was the landmark study demonstrating that plate fixation of displaced mid-shaft clavicle fractures produced significantly improved DASH scores and a lower non-union rate (2% vs 15%) compared with non-operative management at 1 year. The CACTUS trial evaluated return to activity in cyclists but is not the primary RCT cited. The SPORT trial relates to spine surgery. The INFIX trial addressed pelvic fixation. NICE does not have a specific guideline mandating surgery for all displaced clavicle fractures.
learningPoint: The COTS RCT demonstrated that plate fixation of displaced mid-shaft clavicle fractures reduces non-union rates (2% vs 15%) and improves DASH scores at 1 year.

stem: A 55-year-old manual worker develops a sudden 'pop' followed by a visible bulge in the distal arm ('Popeye' deformity). He has mild aching but preserved elbow flexion strength. What structure has ruptured and what is the typical management in this age group?

options.label: A
options.text: Distal biceps tendon rupture; urgent operative repair is mandatory

options.label: B
options.text: Long head of biceps tendon rupture; conservative management with physiotherapy is appropriate for most

options.label: C
options.text: Short head of biceps rupture; surgical reattachment recommended

options.label: D
options.text: Long head of biceps tendon rupture; urgent arthroscopic SLAP repair required

options.label: E
options.text: Coracobrachialis rupture; conservative management

explanation: Spontaneous rupture of the long head of biceps tendon at or near the bicipital groove produces the characteristic Popeye deformity (distal muscle belly retraction). In patients over 50, elbow flexion strength loss is minimal (10–20%) and supination strength is relatively preserved because the distal biceps is intact. Conservative management is appropriate for most middle-aged and elderly patients, with good patient-reported outcomes. Biceps tenodesis may be offered to younger, physically demanding patients with persistent cosmetic concern or cramping pain. Distal biceps rupture (at the radial tuberosity) presents with an antecubital fossa defect, significant supination weakness, and warrants urgent surgical repair.
learningPoint: Proximal long head of biceps rupture causes Popeye deformity with minimal strength loss; conservative management is appropriate for most patients over 50.

stem: During arthroscopic shoulder surgery a surgeon notes the long head of biceps tendon has >50% longitudinal split tears and significant synovitis within the bicipital groove. The patient is 44 years old and very active. Which of the following best describes the difference between biceps tenotomy and tenodesis in this context?

options.label: A
options.text: Tenotomy preserves supination strength; tenodesis reduces it

options.label: B
options.text: Tenodesis avoids the Popeye deformity and cramping that can occur with tenotomy; tenotomy is simpler but leads to these cosmetic and functional concerns

options.label: C
options.text: Tenotomy is preferred in young active patients because it avoids hardware

options.label: D
options.text: Tenodesis is associated with higher rates of shoulder stiffness than tenotomy

options.label: E
options.text: Both procedures have identical patient-reported outcomes at 2 years

explanation: Biceps tenotomy is a quick procedure but in a young, physically active patient carries the risk of Popeye deformity (in up to 70% if young) and biceps cramping with exertion due to uncontrolled muscle retraction. Tenodesis (anchoring the tendon to the proximal humerus or subpectoral location) prevents retraction, maintains length-tension relationship, reduces cramping, and avoids cosmetic deformity, at the cost of slightly longer operative time and fixation-related complications. Tenodesis is therefore preferred in patients under approximately 50 who are active and cosmetically concerned. Both techniques address pain effectively.
learningPoint: Biceps tenodesis is preferred over tenotomy in young active patients to prevent Popeye deformity and biceps cramping.

stem: A 38-year-old volleyball player presents with insidious onset of posterior shoulder pain and wasting of the infraspinatus fossa without involvement of supraspinatus. EMG confirms denervation isolated to infraspinatus. MRI shows a para-labral ganglion cyst at the spinoglenoid notch. What anatomical relationship explains this selective infraspinatus denervation?

options.label: A
options.text: The suprascapular nerve divides into supraspinatus and infraspinatus branches before the suprascapular notch

options.label: B
options.text: Compression at the spinoglenoid notch (distal to the branch to supraspinatus) selectively denervates infraspinatus while sparing supraspinatus

options.label: C
options.text: The ganglion cyst compresses the axillary nerve near the quadrilateral space

options.label: D
options.text: The supraspinatus branch is protected by the transverse scapular ligament while infraspinatus branch is not

options.label: E
options.text: Infraspinatus is innervated by the posterior cord of the brachial plexus separately

explanation: The suprascapular nerve passes through the suprascapular notch (under the transverse scapular ligament) and gives a branch to supraspinatus before continuing around the scapular spine to pass through the spinoglenoid notch. Compression at the spinoglenoid notch (by a ganglion cyst, which commonly arises from a posterior labral tear or SLAP lesion) affects only the terminal branch supplying infraspinatus, sparing the supraspinatus branch. Compression at the suprascapular notch affects both muscles. The axillary nerve and quadrilateral space are unrelated to this anatomy.
learningPoint: Isolated infraspinatus wasting with supraspinatus sparing indicates suprascapular nerve compression at the spinoglenoid notch (distal compression), typically from a para-labral ganglion cyst.

stem: A 42-year-old woman presents with progressive bilateral posterior shoulder aching and wasting of both supraspinatus and infraspinatus. Nerve conduction studies confirm prolonged distal latencies of the suprascapular nerve bilaterally. MRI of the shoulder and cervical spine shows no compressive lesion. What diagnosis must be prominently considered?

options.label: A
options.text: Bilateral suprascapular notch compression by hypertrophied transverse scapular ligaments

options.label: B
options.text: Parsonage-Turner syndrome (neuralgic amyotrophy)

options.label: C
options.text: Bilateral para-labral ganglion cysts

options.label: D
options.text: C5 radiculopathy

options.label: E
options.text: Motor neurone disease

explanation: Parsonage-Turner syndrome (idiopathic neuralgic amyotrophy) can mimic suprascapular nerve entrapment and may be bilateral. It classically presents with severe acute-onset shoulder pain followed by weakness and wasting of one or more muscles of the shoulder girdle, including supraspinatus and infraspinatus. When MRI shows no structural compression, bilateral involvement, and NCS changes, Parsonage-Turner is a key differential. Bilateral notch compression by ligamentous hypertrophy is exceptionally rare. Bilateral para-labral cysts are unlikely to arise simultaneously. C5 radiculopathy typically shows cervical MRI changes. MND causes a wider pattern of lower motor neurone signs.
learningPoint: Bilateral suprascapular nerve dysfunction without MRI compression should raise suspicion of Parsonage-Turner syndrome (neuralgic amyotrophy).
references: van Alfen N neuralgic amyotrophy review

stem: Theme: Labral and Capsular Lesions of the Shoulder. For each clinical scenario below, select the single most likely lesion from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Bankart lesion (anteroinferior labral detachment)

options.label: B
options.text: Reverse Bankart lesion (posteroinferior labral detachment)

options.label: C
options.text: Hill-Sachs lesion

options.label: D
options.text: Reverse Hill-Sachs lesion (McLaughlin lesion)

options.label: E
options.text: SLAP type II lesion

options.label: F
options.text: GLAD lesion (glenolabral articular disruption)

options.label: G
options.text: ALPSA lesion (anterior labroligamentous periosteal sleeve avulsion)

options.label: H
options.text: Multidirectional instability with inferior capsular redundancy

emiLeads.stem: A 19-year-old gymnast presents with shoulder pain and subjective instability in multiple directions. Examination reveals a positive sulcus sign bilaterally. MR arthrogram shows a patulous inferior pouch bilaterally with no discrete labral tear.

A 24-year-old rugby player with three episodes of anterior glenohumeral dislocation. MR arthrogram shows the anteroinferior labrum is detached but the periosteum remains intact and the detached labrum has migrated medially along the glenoid neck.

A 31-year-old weightlifter presents with deep anterior shoulder pain, no history of dislocation. The O'Brien test is positive. MR arthrogram reveals a small tear of the anteroinferior glenoid articular cartilage with labral disruption but the periosteum is intact and there is no dislocation.

A 45-year-old epileptic sustained an unwitnessed seizure. He presents with a locked shoulder fixed in internal rotation. AP radiograph shows a 'lightbulb sign'. An anteromedial humeral head compression defect is visible on CT.

explanation: H: MDI presents with sulcus sign and laxity in all directions with bilateral involvement and no discrete labral tear — bilateral patulous inferior pouch is characteristic. G: The ALPSA lesion is distinguished from a classic Bankart by medial migration of the labrum along the intact periosteal sleeve — a key radiological finding on MR arthrogram that influences surgical planning. F: A GLAD lesion occurs after forced abduction-external rotation without dislocation, producing a superficial anteroinferior labral tear with adjacent articular cartilage damage — patients present with pain rather than instability. D: Seizure-related posterior dislocation causes the anteromedial humeral head impaction fracture (reverse Hill-Sachs/McLaughlin lesion); the 'lightbulb sign' on AP view indicates fixed internal rotation from posterior dislocation.

stem: Theme: Rotator Cuff and Shoulder Impingement. For each clinical scenario, select the single most likely diagnosis or pathology from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Subacromial impingement (external impingement)

options.label: B
options.text: Internal impingement (posterosuperior glenoid impingement)

options.label: C
options.text: Subscapularis tear

options.label: D
options.text: Full-thickness supraspinatus tear

options.label: E
options.text: Massive irreparable rotator cuff tear

options.label: F
options.text: Subcoracoid impingement

options.label: G
options.text: Calcific tendinitis (resorptive phase)

options.label: H
options.text: Os acromiale

emiLeads.stem: A 26-year-old county cricket fast bowler has posterior shoulder pain specifically during the late-cocking and early-acceleration phases of throwing. MR arthrogram reveals a partial articular-sided tear of the posterior supraspinatus and anterior infraspinatus at their footprint, with posterior labral fraying.

A 38-year-old woman presents with sudden-onset excruciating anterior shoulder pain at rest that woke her at night. Her shoulder is held in slight abduction. Radiograph shows a fluffy amorphous calcific deposit in the supraspinatus. She is unable to tolerate any active movement.

A 68-year-old man is unable to actively elevate his arm above 70°. He cannot hold a weight in elevation. He has marked supraspinatus and infraspinatus wasting. Radiograph shows superior migration of the humeral head with glenohumeral articular space preserved.

A 50-year-old woman has anterior shoulder pain reproduced by forced elevation with the arm in neutral rotation and internal rotation at 90° flexion — negative in external rotation. Ultrasound-guided injection anterior to the coracoid relieves the pain. MRI shows narrowing between the lesser tuberosity and the coracoid tip.

explanation: B: Internal (posterosuperior) impingement is a thrower's condition where the posterior articular-sided cuff impinges against the posterosuperior glenoid rim in the late-cocking position — it causes articular-sided posterior cuff fraying and posterior labral lesions. G: Fluffy radiograph appearance + acute severe rest pain = resorptive-phase calcific tendinitis (Gärtner type III); the acute presentation distinguishes it from chronic impingement. E: Inability to actively elevate with marked wasting and superior humeral head migration without GH joint space loss = massive irreparable cuff tear (cuff tear arthropathy has joint space loss). F: Subcoracoid impingement causes anterior pain reproduced in forward flexion with internal rotation (not external rotation) and is anatomically located between the subscapularis/lesser tuberosity and the coracoid tip; diagnostic injection anterior to the coracoid confirms it.

stem: Theme: Shoulder Girdle Fractures. For each scenario, select the single most appropriate management option from the list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Broad arm sling and analgesia (non-operative)

options.label: B
options.text: Plate osteosynthesis

options.label: C
options.text: Intramedullary nail fixation

options.label: D
options.text: Primary hemiarthroplasty

options.label: E
options.text: Primary reverse total shoulder arthroplasty

options.label: F
options.text: Tension-band wiring

options.label: G
options.text: Coracoclavicular ligament reconstruction

options.label: H
options.text: Hook plate fixation

emiLeads.stem: A 28-year-old motorcyclist sustains an Edinburgh type 2B2 mid-shaft clavicle fracture with 2 cm shortening and a single butterfly fragment. He is fit and well and keen to return to work as a plumber within 6 weeks.

A 71-year-old osteoporotic woman sustains a four-part proximal humerus fracture. CT shows disruption of the medial calcar with <4 mm of medial periosteal hinge intact, severe comminution, and posterior articular impaction. She is right-hand dominant.

A 40-year-old falls from a horse and sustains a displaced distal clavicle fracture with rupture of the coracoclavicular ligaments (Neer type II). The lateral fragment is inferior and the proximal fragment rides superiorly by 15 mm.

An 82-year-old woman with mild cognitive impairment falls and sustains an undisplaced two-part surgical neck proximal humerus fracture. She lives alone but has good social support. Radiographs show <20° angulation with intact periosteal hinge.

explanation: B: Displaced comminuted mid-shaft clavicle (2B2) in a young manual worker seeking early return — COTS RCT evidence supports plate fixation to reduce non-union and restore function; C-nail is an alternative but plate gives more reliable stability for comminuted fractures. E: Four-part fracture in an elderly patient with destroyed medial calcar (AVN risk >80%) and comminution — RTSA is preferred over hemiarthroplasty as it provides reliable active elevation without needing tuberosity healing. H: Neer type II distal clavicle fracture (CC ligament disruption, unstable) is best treated with hook plate fixation in an active 40-year-old, stabilising the distal fragment and restoring CC anatomy; hook plate is removed electively at 3–6 months. A: Undisplaced two-part surgical neck fracture in an elderly patient is managed non-operatively in a sling with early mobilisation — good outcomes in the literature for undisplaced/minimally displaced fractures.

stem: Theme: Differential Diagnosis of Shoulder Pain. For each clinical scenario, select the single most likely diagnosis from the option list. Each option may be used once, more than once, or not at all.

options.label: A
options.text: Adhesive capsulitis (frozen shoulder)

options.label: B
options.text: Glenohumeral osteoarthritis

options.label: C
options.text: Acromioclavicular joint osteoarthritis

options.label: D
options.text: Cervical radiculopathy (C5/C6)

options.label: E
options.text: Suprascapular nerve palsy

options.label: F
options.text: Avascular necrosis of the humeral head

options.label: G
options.text: Sternoclavicular joint septic arthritis

options.label: H
options.text: Pancoast tumour

emiLeads.stem: A 61-year-old man with a 20-pack-year smoking history presents with right shoulder and upper arm pain radiating to the medial forearm and ring/little finger. He has Horner syndrome on the right and wasting of the intrinsic hand muscles. Chest radiograph shows an apical soft-tissue opacity.

A 50-year-old with rheumatoid arthritis on long-term prednisolone presents with insidious onset of right shoulder pain over 6 months, worse with activity. Plain radiograph shows subchondral collapse of the superior humeral head with a crescent sign and preserved joint space.

A 47-year-old man with a recent history of intravenous drug use presents with a 10-day history of anterior chest and right shoulder pain, fever, and swelling over the medial clavicle. The area is erythematous and exquisitely tender. His CRP is 210 mg/L.

A 55-year-old woman with well-controlled hypothyroidism presents with a 5-month history of progressive global shoulder stiffness. She has lost 45° of passive external rotation and 50° of forward flexion compared with the other side. Plain radiographs are entirely normal and joint space is preserved.

explanation: H: Pancoast tumour (superior sulcus tumour) classically causes shoulder and arm pain, Horner syndrome (sympathetic chain involvement), and intrinsic hand wasting (T1 nerve root) — the apical opacity confirms the diagnosis. A careful history of smoking and systematic examination looking for Horner is vital. F: Subchondral collapse with a 'crescent sign' and preserved joint space in a patient on long-term corticosteroids strongly indicates AVN — the crescent sign represents subchondral fracture. G: Septic arthritis of the sternoclavicular joint is rare but occurs in IV drug users and immunocompromised patients; medial clavicular swelling + fever + markedly elevated CRP in an IVDU = sternoclavicular sepsis until proven otherwise; CT-guided aspiration/drainage is required. A: Global passive loss of movement with normal radiographs and preserved joint space in a middle-aged woman with a predisposing condition (hypothyroidism) = adhesive capsulitis; loss of passive external rotation is the hallmark distinguishing it from subacromial pathology.

stem: A 42-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 3-day history of severe low back pain radiating to both legs. Over the past 12 hours he has developed difficulty passing urine, reduced perianal sensation, and faecal incontinence. Straight leg raise is positive bilaterally at 40 degrees. MRI lumbar spine confirms a large central L4/5 disc prolapse with cauda equina compression. Which of the following represents the most appropriate immediate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Arrange urgent physiotherapy and reassess in 2 weeks

options.label: B
options.text: Administer high-dose intravenous methylprednisolone and admit for observation

options.label: C
options.text: Perform urgent surgical decompression within 24 hours

options.label: D
options.text: Arrange elective microdiscectomy within 6 weeks

options.label: E
options.text: Prescribe gabapentin and arrange outpatient urology review

explanation: This patient has cauda equina syndrome (CES) with incomplete presentation (CES-I) — bladder dysfunction, altered perianal sensation, and bilateral sciatica. Current consensus (BASS guidelines and UK practice) supports urgent surgical decompression, ideally within 24–48 hours of onset of neurological deficit. Steroid use (B) is not evidence-based in CES and may cause harm. Physiotherapy (A) or elective surgery (D) are wholly inappropriate with active neurological compromise. Gabapentin (E) addresses neuropathic pain but does not treat the compressive pathology.
learningPoint: CES with neurological deficit requires urgent surgical decompression; delay risks permanent bladder, bowel, and sexual dysfunction.

stem: A 55-year-old woman with a known L5/S1 disc prolapse presents with 6 hours of new-onset urinary retention requiring catheterisation, saddle anaesthesia, and bilateral foot drop. MRI confirms severe cauda equina compression. She is consented for emergency decompression. Which prognostic factor most strongly predicts a poor functional outcome for bladder recovery?

options.label: A
options.text: Age over 50 years

options.label: B
options.text: Duration of complete urinary retention exceeding 24 hours prior to surgery

options.label: C
options.text: Bilateral rather than unilateral sciatica

options.label: D
options.text: L5/S1 rather than L4/5 level of compression

options.label: E
options.text: Female sex

explanation: The duration of complete urinary retention before surgical decompression is the strongest independent predictor of poor bladder recovery in CES. Studies consistently show that CES-R (complete retention) carries a worse prognosis than CES-I (incomplete bladder dysfunction), and prolonged retention before decompression further worsens outcome. Age, level, and side of sciatica have much weaker prognostic value. Female sex (E) is not an independent predictor.
learningPoint: Prolonged complete urinary retention before decompression is the key modifiable prognostic factor in cauda equina syndrome — speed of surgery matters most in CES-R.

stem: A 67-year-old retired teacher presents with an 8-month history of progressive hand clumsiness, difficulty with fine motor tasks such as buttoning shirts, and a broad-based unsteady gait. She denies bladder symptoms. Examination reveals upper motor neurone signs in both legs, loss of vibration sense in the feet, and a positive Lhermitte's sign. Plain radiographs show multi-level cervical spondylosis. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis?

options.label: A
options.text: CT myelogram of the cervical spine

options.label: B
options.text: MRI of the cervical spine without contrast

options.label: C
options.text: Electromyography and nerve conduction studies

options.label: D
options.text: Lumbar puncture with CSF analysis

options.label: E
options.text: Technetium bone scan

explanation: MRI of the cervical spine is the investigation of choice for suspected cervical myelopathy. It directly visualises cord compression, signal change within the cord (T2 hyperintensity indicating myelomalacia), and the degree of canal stenosis without ionising radiation. CT myelogram (A) is an alternative when MRI is contraindicated but is more invasive. EMG/NCS (C) assesses peripheral nerve and muscle function and would not demonstrate cord compression. Lumbar puncture (D) and bone scan (E) are not appropriate first-line investigations here.
learningPoint: MRI cervical spine is the investigation of choice for cervical myelopathy, allowing direct visualisation of cord compression and intrinsic cord signal change.

stem: A 71-year-old man with moderate cervical spondylotic myelopathy (modified Japanese Orthopaedic Association score 11/17) has cord compression at C4/5 and C5/6 with preserved cervical lordosis on MRI. He is medically fit for surgery. Which surgical approach is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Anterior cervical discectomy and fusion at C4/5 and C5/6

options.label: B
options.text: Posterior cervical laminectomy alone at C4–C6

options.label: C
options.text: Posterior cervical laminoplasty at C3–C6

options.label: D
options.text: Cervical total disc replacement at C4/5 and C5/6

options.label: E
options.text: Conservative management with cervical collar for 6 months

explanation: For two-level anterior pathology (C4/5 and C5/6) with preserved lordosis and moderate myelopathy, anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF) provides direct decompression of the relevant levels with good neurological outcomes and is the most widely practised approach in UK centres for this scenario. Laminoplasty (C) is preferred for multi-level (≥3 segments) posterior compression. Laminectomy alone (B) risks post-laminectomy kyphosis. Disc replacement (D) is contraindicated with myelopathy and multi-level disease. Conservative management (E) is insufficient for moderate myelopathy with progressive symptoms.
learningPoint: ACDF is preferred for two-level anterior cervical myelopathy with preserved lordosis; laminoplasty suits multi-level posterior disease.

stem: A 34-year-old warehouse worker presents with a 6-week history of right-sided low back pain radiating below the knee to the dorsum of the foot. He reports weakness of great toe extension and reduced sensation over the first web space. Straight leg raise is positive at 35 degrees on the right. MRI confirms a right posterolateral L4/5 disc prolapse. Conservative management has failed. Which nerve root is most likely to be compromised?

options.label: A
options.text: L3

options.label: B
options.text: L4

options.label: C
options.text: L5

options.label: D
options.text: S1

options.label: E
options.text: S2

explanation: The L5 nerve root supplies the extensor hallucis longus (great toe extension weakness), sensation to the first web space and dorsum of the foot, and is affected by a posterolateral L4/5 disc prolapse (the L5 root exits below L5 and is compressed at the L4/5 level). L4 root (B) would cause weakness of quadriceps and knee extension with anteromedial thigh/medial leg sensory loss. S1 root (D) would affect plantar flexion, Achilles reflex, and lateral foot/sole sensation.
learningPoint: A posterolateral L4/5 disc prolapse compresses the L5 nerve root, causing great toe extensor weakness and first web space sensory loss.

stem: A 29-year-old woman presents with a 10-week history of left-sided sciatica following an episode of heavy lifting. She describes pain radiating to the lateral calf and sole of the foot with absent left ankle jerk. There is no bladder or bowel disturbance. MRI confirms a left-sided L5/S1 disc prolapse. She has trialled analgesia, physiotherapy, and a single epidural steroid injection without benefit. What is the most appropriate next management step?

options.label: A
options.text: Repeat epidural steroid injection

options.label: B
options.text: Refer for cognitive behavioural therapy

options.label: C
options.text: Refer for surgical microdiscectomy

options.label: D
options.text: Arrange discography to confirm pain generator

options.label: E
options.text: Prescribe long-term opioids and discharge to GP

explanation: After 6–12 weeks of failed conservative treatment (analgesia, physiotherapy, and at least one injection) for radiculopathy confirmed on MRI, surgical microdiscectomy is indicated and supported by NICE NG59 as an option for persistent sciatica. It provides faster symptom relief than continued conservative care. A repeat epidural (A) has little additional benefit after one failure. Discography (D) is not used to confirm disc prolapse diagnosis in this context. Long-term opioids (E) are not recommended by NICE for chronic back or leg pain.
learningPoint: Microdiscectomy is indicated after 6–12 weeks of failed conservative management for confirmed lumbar disc prolapse with radiculopathy.

stem: A 73-year-old retired postman describes bilateral leg pain and heaviness that comes on after walking approximately 150 metres. The symptoms are relieved by sitting or leaning forward and worsened when walking downhill. He has no rest pain. Peripheral pulses are normal and ABI is 1.1 bilaterally. MRI lumbar spine shows multi-level central canal stenosis at L3/4 and L4/5. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.label: A
options.text: Peripheral arterial disease

options.label: B
options.text: Diabetic peripheral neuropathy

options.label: C
options.text: Neurogenic claudication due to lumbar spinal stenosis

options.label: D
options.text: Bilateral hip osteoarthritis

options.label: E
options.text: Restless leg syndrome

explanation: Neurogenic claudication is characterised by bilateral leg pain on walking that is relieved by flexion (sitting, leaning on a trolley — 'shopping trolley sign') and worsened by extension (walking downhill). Normal ABI and pulses exclude vascular claudication (A), which characteristically is not relieved by position change alone. Diabetic neuropathy (B) causes constant symptoms not related to walking distance. Bilateral hip OA (D) would localise to the groin and be present at rest.
learningPoint: Neurogenic claudication from lumbar spinal stenosis is relieved by flexion and aggravated by extension; normal pulses and ABI differentiate it from vascular claudication.

stem: A 68-year-old woman with symptomatic L3/4 lumbar spinal stenosis has had two epidural steroid injections and 18 months of conservative care. She has significant functional limitation (Oxford Disability Score 72%) and acceptable co-morbidities. Imaging shows isolated L3/4 stenosis without instability. Which surgical procedure is most appropriate as primary intervention?

options.label: A
options.text: L3/4 posterior decompressive laminectomy alone

options.label: B
options.text: L3/4 decompression with instrumented posterolateral fusion

options.label: C
options.text: Interspinous process device insertion (e.g. X-STOP)

options.label: D
options.text: Total disc replacement at L3/4

options.label: E
options.text: Anterior lumbar interbody fusion at L3/4

explanation: For isolated lumbar spinal stenosis without spondylolisthesis or radiographic instability, decompressive laminectomy alone (with or without flavectomy) is the standard first surgical option and is supported by evidence including the SPORT trial. Routine fusion (B) adds morbidity and is not indicated without instability or spondylolisthesis. Interspinous devices (C) have shown no superiority to decompression in RCTs and are not NICE-recommended for isolated stenosis. Disc replacement (D) is for disc-mediated pain, not stenosis.
learningPoint: Isolated lumbar spinal stenosis without instability is treated surgically with decompression alone; adding fusion is not indicated and increases morbidity.

stem: A 15-year-old female gymnast is referred with a 3-month history of activity-related low back pain. She has no leg pain or neurological symptoms. Lateral lumbar radiograph shows a Grade I spondylolisthesis (Meyerding) at L5/S1. CT scan confirms bilateral L5 pars defects (spondylolysis). What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.label: A
options.text: Surgical repair of pars defect with direct screw fixation

options.label: B
options.text: Instrumented L5/S1 posterolateral fusion

options.label: C
options.text: Activity modification, physiotherapy, and a period of relative rest from gymnastics

options.label: D
options.text: Bracing with a Boston overlap brace for 6 months

options.label: E
options.text: Epidural steroid injection at L5/S1

explanation: In an adolescent with symptomatic spondylolysis and low-grade spondylolisthesis without neurological compromise, first-line management is conservative: activity modification, cessation of provocative activities (gymnastics), core strengthening physiotherapy, and relative rest. Evidence for rigid bracing (D) as superior to activity modification alone is limited. Surgical intervention (A, B) is reserved for those who fail 6 months of conservative care or who have progressive slip or neurological deficit. Epidural injection (E) has no role in isolated pars defect pain.
learningPoint: Adolescent spondylolysis with low-grade listhesis and no neurology is managed conservatively with activity modification and physiotherapy before considering surgery.

stem: A 52-year-old woman presents with 18 months of low back pain and bilateral L5 radiculopathy. Imaging confirms a degenerative Grade II L4/5 spondylolisthesis with dynamic instability on flexion-extension views and severe bilateral neural foraminal stenosis. Conservative treatment has failed after 12 months. She is medically fit. Which surgical procedure gives the best outcomes in this scenario?

options.label: A
options.text: L4/5 decompression alone

options.label: B
options.text: L4/5 decompression with instrumented posterolateral fusion

options.label: C
options.text: Interspinous process device

options.label: D
options.text: L4/5 anterior lumbar interbody fusion alone without decompression

options.label: E
options.text: L4/5 total disc replacement

explanation: Degenerative spondylolisthesis with dynamic instability and radiculopathy that has failed conservative management is a well-established indication for decompression combined with instrumented fusion. The landmark Forsth et al. (NEJM 2016) and Ghogawala et al. (NEJM 2016) trials both demonstrated superior outcomes for decompression plus fusion over decompression alone in this population. Decompression alone (A) risks progression of the slip and recurrence of symptoms. ALIF alone (D) would not decompress the neural elements.
learningPoint: Degenerative spondylolisthesis with dynamic instability and failed conservative care is best treated with decompression and instrumented fusion, not decompression alone.

stem: A 61-year-old man with known prostate cancer presents to the emergency department with a 5-day history of progressive thoracic back pain, leg weakness, and difficulty walking. Neurological examination reveals power 3/5 in both lower limbs and a sensory level at T8. MRI confirms metastatic spinal cord compression at T7/8. He can still walk with assistance. What is the most appropriate immediate management according to current UK MSCC guidance?

options.label: A
options.text: Urgent radiotherapy to the affected vertebral levels

options.label: B
options.text: High-dose dexamethasone followed by urgent specialist MSCC review within 24 hours

options.label: C
options.text: Palliative chemotherapy and analgesia only

options.label: D
options.text: Emergency decompressive surgery

options.label: E
options.text: Bisphosphonate infusion and orthopaedic review within one week

explanation: NICE guidance (NG127) on MSCC mandates immediate high-dose dexamethasone (16 mg daily, reducing) and referral to a designated MSCC coordinator for specialist review within 24 hours of MRI confirmation. Radiotherapy (A) may ultimately be the primary treatment but cannot be initiated without specialist assessment and is not the immediate first step. Emergency surgery (D) may be indicated based on specialist assessment but is not universally the first management. Bisphosphonates (E) address bone metastasis long-term but do not treat acute cord compression.
learningPoint: Immediate high-dose dexamethasone and urgent MSCC specialist referral within 24 hours are the required first steps on MRI-confirmed MSCC per NICE NG127.
references: NICE NG127 Metastatic Spinal Cord Compression

stem: A 58-year-old woman with breast cancer presents with MSCC at T5 confirmed on MRI. She has been unable to walk for 36 hours. The tumour has a good radiosensitivity profile (hormone receptor positive). She has no evidence of spinal instability on SINS scoring (score 5). What is the preferred treatment modality according to NICE NG127?

options.label: A
options.text: Emergency surgical decompression and stabilisation

options.label: B
options.text: Urgent radiotherapy as primary treatment

options.label: C
options.text: High-dose dexamethasone alone with watchful waiting

options.label: D
options.text: Stereotactic body radiotherapy (SBRT) only

options.label: E
options.text: Vertebroplasty at T5

explanation: NICE NG127 recommends urgent radiotherapy as the primary treatment for MSCC in patients with radiosensitive tumours (such as hormone receptor-positive breast cancer) and a stable spine (low SINS score). Surgery (A) is reserved for mechanically unstable spines (SINS ≥13), single-level disease with uncertain diagnosis, or rapid neurological deterioration.
